headline: Australia's Silicon Valley dream
intro: Five years ago it was possible to count the number of venture capital firms operating in Australia on one hand - and still have fingers left to operate your iPhone. Today, the picture has changed dramatically, with around 26 active funds accounting for $2.2 billion under management, according to the Australian Private Equity and Venture Capital Association.
text:
Five years ago it was possible to count the number of venture capital firms operating in Australia on one hand - and still have fingers left to operate your iPhone. Today, the picture has changed dramatically, with around 26 active funds accounting for $2.2 billion under management, according to the Australian Private Equity and Venture Capital Association.

What's changed is the number of breakout companies emerging from the Australian start-up scene. Atlassian, a business software company founded in 2002, is the poster-child, with an estimated market capitalisation of $3.3 billion. Does this mean that we're on the cusp of creating an Australian Silicon Valley?

The Sunrise - The Silicon Valley Myth Explained is a one-day conference during Vivid aimed at answering that question. According to Rick Baker, co-founder and managing director of Blackbird Ventures, an Australian VC fund, there's been lots of effort aimed at creating a down-under Silicon Valley, but very little success.

Baker said what is happening in Australia is that funds and start-ups are taking advantage of close links to the Valley to build breakout companies that have close relationships with the landmark VC firms in Silicon Valley. "What it's about is creating something that is a global market leading out of Australia, and the good news is that there's lots of momentum, and because the mining boom has settled down, there are also a lot of high-net-worth individuals wanting to provide angel or seed funds for start-up companies."

Alan Jones, chief ideas guy (a very Valley title) at venture fund BlueChilli, agrees. "Smart investors in resources and agriculture were seeing Atlassian on the cover of BRW and they would read that story about 30-year-olds becoming billionaires with a first investment round of $500,000," said Jones. "For those guys, here was an opportunity to make 100 times return supplying one-fifth of a $500,000 investment, and for them this is the same as going into a syndicate for a race horse. This has all led to the growth of Australian start-up accelerator scene."

With the growth of the start-up scene in Australia, and the associated rise in the number of funds and investors, the trend has been for the founders of early breakout companies to begin helping people just thinking about their own start-ups and looking to find investors.

"We are starting to see founders help other founders get going," said Blackbird's Baker. "They are starting to give time and put money into the ecosystem. This is what Sunrise is all about. It's trying to get these founders to help other companies in the portfolio of Australian VC firms."

One company that has emerged as dominant in its market space - executive rewards - is RedBalloon. Its founder, Naomi Simson, is also speaking at Sunrise. Her view is a little contrarian to the views from the venture capital community: she said she bootstrapped her company, rather than take money from outside investors. "We lived on Weetbix and paid ourselves nothing to make the business viable," she said.

"Would I have done things differently if I was going through all that again? Probably not. If you don't take investment you have independence, and you can take risks. Investors just want their money back."

Weetbix aside, however, Simson said existing companies can learn a lot from the way start-ups are run. She said that start-ups are streamlined and willing to take risks. "They're lean, mean and willing to give something a crack."

BlueChilli's Jones concurs with Simson, noting that many large corporates are appointing heads of innovation, or even creating entire departments aimed at pushing innovation through the organisation. "Big companies are also starting to back start-ups with cash prizes and seed funding," he said. "They have lots of customers, and they can learn to reduce the costs of servicing those customers, while still maintaining standards, by observing the way start-ups service their own customer base."
direction: bullish for Australian technology and venture capital sectors

headline: Facebook tipped to soar as TV stagnates
intro: Facebook is set for an advertising windfall which could see it top $500 million in Australian revenue this year if blue-chip advertisers achieve their plans for a dramatic shift in marketing investment to social and mobile channels.
text:
Facebook is set for an advertising windfall which could see it top $500 million in Australian revenue this year if blue-chip advertisers achieve their plans for a dramatic shift in marketing investment to social and mobile channels.

Starcom Mediavest's annual survey of spending intentions among major advertisers - now in its 30th year - spells a boom for social media operators and chilling news for TV broadcasters. Free-to-air TV is set for no growth in 2015 while pay TV will lift about 1 per cent. Advertisers say their mobile budgets will rocket 22.9 per cent this year and advertising in social channels will rise 21.8 per cent.

Media executives told The Australian Financial Review that Facebook was now the second-largest digital media business in Australia behind Google, although verification is difficult because the tech giant has won exemption from the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) to withhold full Australian financial statements because it isn't deemed to be part of a large corporation.

"Facebook was probably about a $200 million business in 2012," said one media executive who did not want to be named. "They've at least doubled since then. Next to Google it's the biggest digital business in the country. It's growing insanely fast."

The Facebook estimates were based on about $200 million in revenue booked from national advertisers and an estimated $250 million written directly through its self-serve advertising model for smaller companies.

It means the two largest and fast-growing digital media companies in Australia are now US-based and have made huge market share gains without needing to invest in or create content.

Marketing executives in the Media Futures survey predict their total advertising budgets will rise 2.7 per cent this year, although most traditional channels will not share the joy. Newspaper advertising revenue are forecast to fall 5.9 per cent and magazines are expected to drop 4.3 per cent. Radio, out-of-home and subscription TV are the only traditional media channels forecast to see a rise in 2015 (up 1.6 per cent, 2.5 per cent and 0.9 per cent).

"We are seeing the culmination of structural change in the industry so low growth is the new norm," Starcom Mediavest CEO Chris Nolan said.

"Continued disruption and transformation across the industry means low growth is not cyclical but is the real trend for traditional media."

Mr Nolan said marketers planned this year to allocate 3.5 times more of their budget growth on "owned and earned media" over paid media channels. "Social and mobile are considered channels in which advertisers leverage their owned and earned media and the combination of those two is allowing advertisers to be more agile and tailored with their campaigns to customers," he said.

"There is renewed emphasis on proximity or location-based marketing and it's primarily driven through mobile and social forums. Some of these emerging media are getting a disproportionate amount of growth in the market."

The Media Futures report also highlighted a clear trend for companies to invest significantly more in "cross-channel analytics", meaning single-channel measurement systems will lose ground if not part of a broader mix.
direction: Facebook shares to rise

headline: Telstra, CBA at cutting edge of change
intro: It says a lot about the changing face of Australian business that two of the country's biggest listed enterprises from different but equally important sectors of the economy now describe themselves as technology companies.
text:
It says a lot about the changing face of Australian business that two of the country's biggest listed enterprises from different but equally important sectors of the economy now describe themselves as technology companies.

Telstra, which announced a CEO transition on Friday from David Thodey to Andy Penn, and Commonwealth Bank of Australia, headed by CEO Ian Narev, are telling staff that technology is at the centre of their ability to deliver telecommunications and banking services.

This is not just rhetoric being handed down from on high and talked about in staff emails using convoluted language known as corporate speak.

Both Telstra and CBA are changing the way they employ people. Also, they are taking a radically different approach to staff training. They are demanding a broader range of skills. The ideal employee is collaborative and capable of working across different divisions of each company.

Over the past 15 years, Telstra has gone from being a fixed-line phone company with a mobile division to a company that not only provides telco infrastructure but also sells software, provides e-health services and invests in start-up companies.

Both Telstra and CBA have innovation centres that develop new products and then run pilot testing for niche consumers before large-scale deployment. This sort of agile software development is becoming the norm in Australian business. It is another reason why the education system should include software language writing in the curriculum.

CBA and Telstra are focusing capital investment on mobile devices and trying to anticipate consumer tastes six to 12 months before products are launched.

The outgoing Telstra CEO has said his vision for Telstra "is that we become a great global technology company". Narev at CBA has expressed similar sentiments, although he is no longer bothering to get involved in discussions about whether CBA is ahead of its rivals in its core technology investments.

CBA believes technology excellence should be the starting point for everything the company does. Also, it must continuously update its products, particularly applications on mobile devices.

The shift in thinking at two of the country's biggest companies has broader ramifications for the economy and the organisations that feed employees into the wider workforce.

Telstra employs about 32,000 people and CBA employs about 44,000.

Schools and universities need to be preparing students for a different type of workplace. Visit the innovation centres of CBA and Telstra, as Chanticleer has done, and you will discover that they are more like Silicon Valley incubators than slow-moving bureaucratic enclaves.

Management training specialists such as business schools and consulting firms need to rethink how they interact with companies which are focusing on technology rather than industry-specific skills.

For example, CBA is using its own executives to train staff at offsite events instead of calling in the usual suspects such as management gurus and fast-talking sales guys who have published the same book in 10 different forms.

If Telstra is a technology company that sells telco services and a range of software products and CBA is a technology company that sells financial services and financial wellbeing, then just about every other listed company will have to start rethinking who and what they are.

Telstra in great shape

Don't let Telstra's stunning sharemarket performance over the past three years fool you into thinking David Thodey has had an easy ride as chief executive of the country's biggest telco.

He inherited a mess from his predecessor Sol Trujillo, even though the wreckage left behind included a world-class mobile network.

Customer service was atrocious, the management ranks needed more depth and the company's largest shareholder, the Future Fund, was selling its holding into an already sceptical market.

The Future Fund added to the pressure on Telstra management by turning up the heat on the board of directors. It famously refused to support key resolutions at Telstra's annual meeting.

The Future Fund did not support resolutions for the appointment of a new director, changes to the constitution and the remuneration report.

Bad blood between shareholders and the board is bad enough, but Thodey also had to deal with poor financial performance. It seemed that every time Thodey went to market, he was forced to announce a profit downgrade.

Telstra shares were trading around $2.60 a share, which is a long way from today's heady price of $6.60.

There were some observers during those dark days who believed Thodey was one profit downgrade away from being dumped.

Thodey's consensus approach to management, learned at IBM, did not translate well to a sprawling, silo-infested organisation like Telstra. But it is a tribute to Thodey's flexibility and willingness to embrace change that he was able to take control of the situation.

He went from running the relatively benign Telstra wholesale business to suddenly having to manage many stakeholder groups. Some of those stakeholders had been severely burned by Trujillo and his team of hard men.

It took some time, but Thodey eventually responded brilliantly to the challenge of managing investors, the media, customers, politicians, staff and the board.

Repairing relations with government was a top priority and that was a joint effort with chairman Catherine Livingstone.

Thodey's performance turned up like a J curve when his "consensus-in-all-things" approach gave way to decisive actions.

Telstra's former chief financial officer, John Stanhope, once described Thodey as a "verifier", a reference to Thodey's penchant for checking everything. This drove people crazy. But Thodey changed, and his newfound willingness to delegate actually empowered people. A notable sign of his growing confidence in the CEO position was the appointment of executives who were of CEO quality, including Gordon Ballantyne and Brendon Riley.

In the middle of all this, Thodey took personal ownership of one of the most complex negotiations between business and government in Australian history.

The NBN negotiations dragged on for about a year longer than expected but, at the end of the process, Telstra was the clear winner, with future cash flows given a net present value of $11 billion.

It would be tempting to describe this deal as Thodey's greatest legacy. In fact, his greatest legacy is changing Telstra's culture from a bureaucratic, engineering-focused company into a retail and marketing business. It is not as easy as it sounds.

Thodey put himself in the customer service complaints loop. Developing a customer service culture is an endless task, but Thodey showed a relentless commitment to it.

It says a lot about his success that AMP CEO Craig Meller said this week his decision to roll out net promoter scores as the centrepiece of a company-wide strategy to lift AMP's customer service is based on advice from Telstra. A third legacy to be handed carefully by his successor Andy Penn is the Asian growth strategy. This ticks all the boxes for offshore expansion.

It has to be said there was an element of luck in Thodey's transformation of the profit and loss. Vodafone under former CEO Nigel Dews deservedly became Vodafail. That was the gift that kept on giving for three years. Also, Optus took its eye off the ball for several years and is only now showing signs of being a true challenger.

The fact that Telstra did not even bother with an international executive search when choosing a new chief executive is a tribute to Thodey and Livingstone.

The final feather in Thodey's cap is his decision not to overstay his welcome. He leaves Telstra in great shape and at the time of his choosing. If only other CEOs were as attuned to the interests of shareholders, customers and colleagues.

TONY BOYD

Twitter: @TonyBoydAFR

tony.boyd@afr.com.au
direction: bullish for Telstra and CBA

headline: Golden Mile outlet centre up
intro: Melbourne's Brand Smart Premium Outlet Centre, one the country's few outlet centres, has been put up for sale with price expectations exceeding $75 million.
text:
Melbourne's Brand Smart Premium Outlet Centre, one the country's few outlet centres, has been put up for sale with price expectations exceeding $75 million.

The Nunawading centre, being offloaded by the private Melbourne-based Commercial Property Corporation, is one of 18 outlet centres across Australia. It is in the heritage Golden Mile area in the city's eastern suburbs.

The 9317-square-metre centre is being offered for sale via an on-market expressions-of-interest (EOI) campaign through Simon Rooney of JLL and Mark Wizel of CBRE Victorian Retail Investments. The campaign closes on June 5.

Mr Rooney said the market for large and well located retail assets remained strong. "Investor interest is wide-ranging, from domestic and offshore sources and from institutional and high-net-worth investors."

There was limited investment activity in the outlet sector until 2013 and 2014, according to JLL. Recent sales include Mirvac's purchase of Birkenhead Point in Sydney for $310 million in October and Novion's of the DFO South Wharf & Homemaker Hub in Melbourne for $87 million in May.

Brand Smart's brands include Sass & Bide, Orton, Priceline, Kathmandu, Lorna Jane, and Subway.

"Following an extensive refurbishment and repositioning program which was completed in 2013, Brand Smart Premium Outlet Centre offers numerous value-enhancement opportunities going forward through further expansion and development of the asset, subject to council approval," Mr Wizel said.
direction: bullish for retail property trusts and REITs

headline: East coast faces 20pc gas shortfall
intro: The east coast of Australia faces a shortage of almost 20 per cent of its gas demand, according to research by independent advisory EnergyQuest.
text:
The east coast of Australia faces a shortage of almost 20 per cent of its gas demand, according to research by independent advisory EnergyQuest.

A shortfall by 2019 of 133 petajoules of gas challenges findings by the Australian Energy Market Operator in April.

EnergyQuest chief executive Dr Graeme Bethune took a swipe at the Australian energy regulator, which had said there would be a gas shortage.

"Federal and state governments have internationally respected geoscience agencies, but there is little sign that this expertise has been called upon by [the Australian Energy Market Operator]," Dr Bethune said.

He said its analysis should focus on "ongoing gas market risks, rather than assuming them away".

Claims of a looming gas supply crisis have been repeated by federal and state politicians as a reason to encourage the industry's development.

Federal industry minister Ian Macfarlane has criticised the NSW and Victorian governments for not encouraging local gas industries.

Manufacturing Australia has argued the gas supply crisis could cost tens of thousands of local manufacturing jobs.

The EnergyQuest analysis said that the Australian energy regulator had underestimated the demand for Queensland's LNG projects while overestimating the production from the Cooper Basin.

The Australian Energy Market Operator stood by its analysis, which has been criticised by energy suppliers such as Senex Energy and Metgasco.

"AEMO followed a rigorous consultation process to ensure the most up-to-date information was available in its 2015 gas statement of opportunities; AEMO continues to monitor the market closely," its spokesperson said.

The Australian energy regulator's outlook in April said that gas consumption in NSW would fall by 17 per cent by 2019. It said there would no longer be a gas shortage.
direction: bearish for gas producers and energy-intensive manufacturers

headline: Oil firms set to chase hard on costs
intro: As seasoned commodity market watchers are keen to point out, share prices always overreact to spot commodity prices, whether that's on the way up or on the way down.
text:
As seasoned commodity market watchers are keen to point out, share prices always overreact to spot commodity prices, whether that's on the way up or on the way down.

In the case of crude oil, no one believes that $US46 a barrel will last for ever, even if there's still no conviction yet among analysts or industry that prices have bottomed.

What is becoming clear though is the industry is set for a prolonged period where prices are well below the $US80 to $US100 a barrel range companies became accustomed to over the past few years.

That is pointing to a radical downward shift in cost structure for the services sector.

But the suggestion from Santos chief executive David Knox this week that Santos could make as much money when at $US50 a barrel oil as it would at $US100 looks optimistic.

While oil industry service costs are sure to be severely squeezed, other expenses, such as financing and labour costs, appear to offer little flexibility.

"Clearly history shows that in times of falling oil prices costs can follow them down," says Mark Samter of Credit Suisse, who has become something of a pain in Santos's side in recent weeks with his bearish assessments on the company's valuation.

"That said, a high quality LNG project might have cash costs of $US10 to $US20 a barrel. Even in a highly unlikely scenario where costs halved, it doesn't do much to offset a $US50 a barrel fall in revenues," Samter says.

It's a live debate as to by just how much costs can be cut in the petroleum services sector, but certainly, the sentiment around is there's plenty of fat to be taken out.

Deloitte's national director of oil and gas, Geoffrey Cann, believes that an expectation of a 40 per cent cost cut is not unreasonable, given the sector has not been particularly vigilant on supply chain costs in the good times over recent years.Hard lessons to be learned

The petroleum services sector is, after all, largely unfamiliar with having to manage a dramatic slump in oil prices; in contrast to the lessons learned the hard way in the coal and iron ore sectors, which have already lived through a slump in their respective commodity prices and have shed thousands of jobs as a result.

Cann points to extensive wastage of services resources, including 40 per cent of "standing-around time" on any given day among contractor workers on some petroleum projects.

He says serious moves to tackle the problems are already afoot, with a tripartite working group set up a few months ago to look at how engineering and construction engineering and construction costs can be reduced across the three adjacent LNG projects on Curtis Island in Gladstone.

But others are less bullish at possible cost improvements, with Citigroup putting the scope for reducing capital expenditure and operating costs in the local oil and gas sector at 15 per cent.

That opportunity is open primarily to new projects, rather than those such as Santos's $US18.5 billion GLNG project in Queensland which has been mostly built during a high-cost period and so have locked those in.

Citi sees a scenario where future projects could achieve similar returns in a low oil price environment to a world where prices are at $US90 a barrel, assuming a circa 15 per cent reduction in long-term oil prices can be matched by operating and capital costs savings.

In a breakdown of capex savings for a typical LNG project, cost cuts on labour are put at zero to 5 per cent, partly due to unionised local labour.

Materials and equipment savings could be in the 25 to 30 per cent range or up to 40 per cent on energy costs. Other costs such as financing could come down by 15 per cent, while engineering and construction management could fall by a quarter.

In the months ahead, oil and gas producers will be going flat out to try to achieve those savings and more.
direction: bearish for oil and gas sector

headline: Blackstone links with formidable property partner
intro: Blackstone, one of the biggest players in Australian property, has teamed up with Canada's second-largest money manager, Caisse de Depot et Placement du Quebec (CDPQ), to buy a quarter-stake in the nearly $1 billion Liberty Place skyscraper in Sydney.
text:
Blackstone, one of the biggest players in Australian property, has teamed up with Canada's second-largest money manager, Caisse de Depot et Placement du Quebec (CDPQ), to buy a quarter-stake in the nearly $1 billion Liberty Place skyscraper in Sydney.

The deal was done just over one week before Morgan Stanley Real Estate Investing announced on Wednesday that it will exit the Investa Property Group and the $9 billion of office towers which the group and its funds own and manage.

The Australian Financial Review first revealed that Blackstone was circling LaSalle Investment Management's 25 per cent stake in the 161 Castlereagh Street building in November.

But it is the first time CDPQ, whose global property arm is called Ivanhoe Cambridge, has invested in the Australian real estate space. Should the deal signal the start of a longer-term relationship, the pair would be a ferocious contender for the entire Investa platform.

CDPQ manages one of the 10 largest real estate portfolios in the world and is one of Canada's leading commercial real estate lenders.

It's best known in Australia as an infrastructure investor, having purchased a $1.4 billion stake in Port of Brisbane in 2013. It is also preparing to bid for NSW's electricity transmission and distribution assets alongside partners Hastings Funds Management and Spark Infrastructure.

Blackstone and Ivanhoe Cambridge have history together in property. Late last year Blackstone sold Ivanhoe Cambridge one of the biggest towers on Manhattan's Avenue of the Americas. At about $US2.5 billion, it was roughly the second-highest sale price of an individual office tower in US history.

Earlier in the year, Blackstone and Ivanhoe Cambridge teamed up to take what could be a 30 per cent stake in Gecina, a French residential, office and healthcare-focused real estate investment trust.

Blackstone and CDPQ's purchase comes as commercial real estate prices continue to escalate. Real estate trusts that have reported so far this earnings season have predicted further cap-rate compression which could tempt more vendors into the market. Managers are predicting a further 25-basis-point compression in capitalisation rates over the next year.

Sources told Street Talk on Thursday that agents are preparing to pitch for the sell-side mandate for Sydney's 420 George Street. Owned by Fortius Funds Management and the Lend Lease-managed Australian Prime Property Fund Commercial and Retail, the office tower could sell for more than $500 million.
direction: bullish for Australian commercial property and REITs

headline: Businesses follow bright idea
intro: The largest rooftop solar power system in Australia is now in Darwin.
text:
The largest rooftop solar power system in Australia is now in Darwin.

The installation of the 1.25-megawatt solar system at GPT Group's Casuarina Square, the largest shopping centre in the Northern Territory, reached practical completion last week.

The 4190-solar-panel rooftop has enough output to power 310 average Australian households and reduce greenhouse pollution by more than 1300 tonnes of CO2 per annum, according to GPT.

"Solar is playing a key part in GPT's strategy to move all of the base buildings it manages towards carbon neutrality," GPT's national manager sustainability Bruce Precious said.

"GPT is reviewing all of its buildings with large rooftop areas across Australia to assess whether they are suited to solar systems. In addition to Casuarina Square, we have large solar installations in several of our assets including 3 and 5 Murray Rose at Sydney Olympic Park and Rouse Hill Town Centre."

The system, with an estimated annual output of 2030 MWh (megawatt hours), has already started providing about 20 per cent of Casuarina Square's base building and common area electricity needs. The solar installation has also been granted approval to connect to the electricity distribution network that provides power to residents and businesses in the Northern Territory.

Having spread across the roofs of more than 1.4 million homes, many businesses are now jumping on the solar bandwagon.

Casuarina Square follows Stockland's massive solar project at its Shellharbour shopping centre. The property group's solar installation is 1.22 megawatts.

"Stockland is proud to be leading the sector in sustainability and investment in renewable energy, and it's great to hear that others in the industry are following our lead," Daniel Buchanan, regional retail manager at Stockland said. "We're already planning more new solar power generation installations across our portfolio to increase our use of renewable energy."

IKEA, the world's biggest furniture retailer, is another company taking big steps in renewable energy. The company wants to be energy carbon-neutral by 2020, with 100 per cent of its energy needs coming from renewable sources. Last year IKEA's Australian businesses installed 16,000 solar panels on the roofs of its six stores, including almost 4000 panels at its flagship Tempe store near Sydney airport.

Other companies such as Coles, Telstra, Google and Australia Post are also going solar.

Investment bank UBS said in a report in September that large-scale solar looks set to usurp wind power from its long-held position as the cheapest new renewable energy source in just a few years. The global utilities research team believes solar power could account for a quarter of the world's installed power generation capacity by 2050.
direction: bullish for GPT Group and renewable energy sector

headline: 'Turnbull' switch may have come too late
intro: Shanghai | Taiwan's election just got a whole lot more interesting.
text:
Shanghai | Taiwan's election just got a whole lot more interesting.

Opposition Leader Tsai Ing-wen is still odds-on favourite to win but she may have to work a bit harder for the title.

Not that it has been a boring campaign so far. The recently dumped presidential candidate for the ruling Kuomintang Party, Hung Hsiu-chu, with her bright red hair and combative style ensured there were plenty of fireworks.

But her call for the self-governing island to take the initiative and unify with China left her unelectable and ultimately such an overtly pro-Beijing stance resulted in her being ousted by the KMT in favour of New Taipei City mayor, Eric Chu.

Just three months out from the election, the switch smacks of desperation and is an attempt to ensure the Democratic Progressive Party (DPP) under Tsai doesn't win a majority in the Legislative Yuan. If it does, it would be the first time the KMT has lost outright control of Taiwan since nationalist leader Chiang Kai-shek fled to the island in 1949 after being defeated by the Communist Party on the mainland.

Even when the DPP's Chen Shui-bian held the presidency in the eight years to 2008, the KMT controlled the legislature.

So Chu, who surprised supporters by opting not to run at the outset, has been brought in to steady the ship. Monash University professor Bruce Jacobs, who wrote the 2012 book Democratising Taiwan says in terms of personalities, it is like the Coalition's decision to dump Tony Abbott in favour of the more moderate Malcolm Turnbull.

But Chu has probably left his run too late to cause an upset. Still, his entry in the race makes the election more interesting because it puts control of the legislature back in play and Tsai might now be forced to clarify her position on cross-strait relations.

Taiwanese people generally accept the importance of the island's economic relationship with China. However, the declining popularity of the KMT and its poor showing in last November's local elections indicate a growing discomfort with President Ma Ying-jeoh's efforts to establish closer relations with Beijing.

At the same time, if Tsai were to adopt a more pro-independence stance, it could unsettle Taiwan's dealings with Washington, which is in favour of stable cross-strait relations.

So far, Tsai has played it safe, insisting the DPP would maintain the "status quo". But the assurance is vague and Beijing insists Taiwan should adhere to the "one China" principle, a loose political agreement under which both governments acknowledge there is one China but interpret that very differently. It's basically an agree-to-disagree understanding which allows the economic relationship to move forward despite political differences.

This is a delicate balancing act. Tsai doesn't want to upset DPP supporters by giving in to Beijing's demands but she also doesn't want to ratchet up tensions. Taiwan faces serious economic challenges and Tsai will need to keep the island's big corporations on side.

With Hung in the race, Tsai was able to keep it vague but Chu has already pushed her on whether she will take a more pro-independent stance.

Richard Bush, an Asia expert at the Brookings Institution, says Beijing's reaction will be crucial.

If Tsai wins the presidency, the Chinese government could take a hard line restricting interaction with Taipei, through formal or informal channels, and applying diplomatic pressure to other countries like Australia to do the same. DPP supporters might then call on Tsai to respond.

"In the event of a downward spiral, both sides would seek US support," Bush wrote in a recent report.

Tsai, who is seen as pragmatic and reserved, may be able to avoid this type of scenario by focusing on domestic issues and being transparent in the way she deals with the mainland. Beijing could also adopt a more mature policy stance. Either way, the cosy relations of the past eight years are unlikely to be repeated and the next three months might tell whether the world is set for another tense period in cross-strait relations.
direction: increased political uncertainty for Taiwan-related assets

headline: Look ahead to greater choice and control
intro: The energy industry is in transition. The emergence of the digital age and the need to progressively decarbonise our economies means this transition may occur more rapidly than many people realise. As it does, a major challenge facing the electricity industry is supplying secure, reliable and cost-effective energy to increasingly energy-conscious consumers who desire cleaner sources of power from a more decentralised network.
text:
The energy industry is in transition. The emergence of the digital age and the need to progressively decarbonise our economies means this transition may occur more rapidly than many people realise. As it does, a major challenge facing the electricity industry is supplying secure, reliable and cost-effective energy to increasingly energy-conscious consumers who desire cleaner sources of power from a more decentralised network.

As a major electricity generator, and Australia's largest CO2 emitter, AGL understands we have a key role to play in helping our industry's orderly transformation to a carbon- constrained future.

For the record, on the subject of climate change, AGL accepts the scientific consensus that anthropogenic greenhouse gas emissions have increased global average temperatures. We support the Commonwealth government's commitment to work towards a global agreement that limits global warming to less than 2 degrees celsius above pre-industrial levels.

That's why we took an industry leading position by releasing our revised Greenhouse Gas Policy earlier this year. In short, we are getting out of the CO2 emissions business. Our policy provides a pathway to a more modern and lower emitting fleet. It commits us to not extending the operating life of our existing assets and to closing all existing conventional coal-fired plants by 2050. Nor will we acquire or provide financing for new conventional coal-fired plants.

Right now, AGL is continuing to build the 53 MW solar plant at Broken Hill in NSW. This plant will join the $2 billion worth of renewable generating assets that AGL has invested in, including the 102 MW Nyngan solar plant, the largest in the southern hemisphere, which has delivered 60,000 MWh of renewable energy into the National Electricity Market since it was completed in July. Last year alone our renewable portfolio was responsible for the abatement of 3.4 million tonnes of greenhouse gas emissions.

Policies that have been introduced to limit GHG emissions and promote renewable energy in Australia have had neither longevity nor stability. Wholesale energy prices are low and the trading price of renewable energy certificates has been volatile. Australia's electricity generation asset mix is largely old and emissions intensive. To modernise the energy generation fleet, the industry needs market-based policy, not government subsidy or support, to facilitate the removal of barriers to exit of ageing coal-fired power stations.

Consumers expect electricity markets to be reliable, affordable and able to adapt to distributed technologies and the changing consumer preferences for greater choice and control.

AGL's response to this has been to change our business model, which includes establishing a dedicated business unit to focus on new and emerging energy technologies.

This includes focusing on digital meters, which go a long way to help consumers achieve greater control over their energy consumption. New digital meters and in-home energy management systems enable people to monitor their energy use daily and adjust their energy habits to take advantage of peak and non-peak energy usage periods. By combining this technology with our energy app, for example, users can look at their current usage and then project what their bill could be at the end of the month if they continued their current usage pattern. This can help consumers in many ways, including avoiding bill shock. It's these kinds of digital products and advancements that we will increasingly see as markets become more competitive in a distributed energy landscape.

Continued product innovation and falling costs will enable wider sections of the community to access distributed technologies. Earlier this year AGL was the first major retailer to introduce a solar power purchase agreement to the market where residents pay zero dollars up front to have a solar rooftop system installed.

In May we were the first major retailer to introduce a battery to the Australian market. The 7.2kWh battery will be able to power a home for several hours, and now consumers can bundle our solar and battery offering.

Battery storage combined with solar systems is a game changer. It enables people to decide when and how they use the energy they have produced and when and if they send it back to the NEM. Throw electric vehicles into the mix with in-home charging stations and we will see the creation of significant arbitrage opportunities where our homes could become electricity exchange markets.

This energy future is now. In fact, in some networks, one in four households generate their own power with rooftop solar. And Australian markets now have some of the highest rates of solar penetration globally.

In this changing energy landscape, the one constant is the customer. By anticipating the needs of these customers, and developing products, services and offering flexibility that meets those needs, the energy industry will continue to evolve.
direction: bearish for AGL shares

headline: AAA backs petrol levy to fund rail
intro: Australia's premier motoring lobby has backed spending some revenue from petrol excise on public transport, arguing the latter is a vital ingredient in reducing road congestion.
text:
Australia's premier motoring lobby has backed spending some revenue from petrol excise on public transport, arguing the latter is a vital ingredient in reducing road congestion.

With the Australian Greens and the Abbott government negotiating a deal to reintroduce the twice-yearly indexation of excise to inflation, worth $23 billion over a decade, the AAA has dropped its trenchant opposition to the excise increase which was announced in the 2014 budget.

In meetings on Thursday with both the Greens and the office of Treasurer Joe Hockey, the AAA nominated three public transport projects it considered worthy of funding.

These were the $9 billion Metro Rail Tunnel in Melbourne, funding for which the Abbott government dumped after its election, as well as the $5.3 billion Brisbane Inner Rail and the $2 billion Perth Light Rail.

"Australia's motoring clubs understand that a mix of road and public transport projects are needed to help ease worsening road congestion and improve productivity and liveability," Michael Bradley, AAA's chief executive, said. "In the absence of any alternative lists, the AAA has written to the government and the Greens outlining the priority public transport projects identified as being most capable of getting our cities moving again."

When the budget measure was announced, the government promised to spend all the extra revenue on road projects. It subsequently dropped that pledge when it tried to negotiate a deal with former Greens leader Christine Milne.

New Greens leader Richard Di Natale has resumed negotiations and is prepared to do a deal so long as some of the proceeds were dedicated towards public transport.

Mr Bradley said the AAA would not accept taking funding which has already been allocated towards a specific road project and redirecting it towards public transport. But revenue not yet allocated could be used for light rail of other public transport projects.

Using momentum from this week's deal to cut pensions by $2.4 billion, both the Greens and the government are confident of securing a deal on petrol and legislating it before October.

The government introduced the tax increase by regulation but has 12 months to enshrine it in legislation of it will lapse and the money be refunded to oil companies.
direction: bearish for rail infrastructure stocks

headline: Umbers gets Myer ready for revamp
intro: Robust sales figures released by David Jones' South African owners, Woolworths, last week were a reminder of the challenges facing rival Myer as its new boss, Richard Umbers, prepares his strategy for fixing the business.
text:
Robust sales figures released by David Jones' South African owners, Woolworths, last week were a reminder of the challenges facing rival Myer as its new boss, Richard Umbers, prepares his strategy for fixing the business.

Like Myer, David Jones has been struggling with declining sales and underinvestment, but there are early signs Woolworths are turning the ship around after posting the Australian group's strongest annual sales increase in eight years.

Woolworths' efforts to replace underperforming brands, improve service and change merchandising systems provide lessons for Myer's new management team as it attempts to reverse a 20-year slide in sales and return to profit growth.

The skills of his new executive team will be the key to this and Umbers is not going to outline his game plan until later this year until they get their feet under the desk. This includes finance chief Grant Devonport who starts next week. Myer is also recruiting for a new chief digital and data officer.

He has also been hiring at the next management level down, including a new head of visual merchandising and a new head of stock planning and replenishment.

Myer's financial year ends next week and it reports full-year results in September. Umbers is unlikely to show his hand until the new recruits have had a chance to get their hands dirty.

What is clear is that the 115-year-old department store group needs major surgery. Investors say everything will be on the table, including cutting the store network, improving service and its online offering, introducing new products and rebasing costs.

The more immediate concern for investors is the risk of another earnings downgrade and a possible equity raising, which is reflected in the company's weak share price. Umbers, who started in March, also has a window of opportunity as new chief to clear the decks financially with big asset writedowns and provisions.

Deutsche Bank analyst Michael Simotas told clients last week Myer would need to deliver 3.5 to 5.5 per cent sales growth in the fourth quarter to meet earnings expectations. This compared with his forecasts for Myer's fourth quarter sales of 2.2 per cent or 1.7 per on a like-for-like basis.

"We believe the discretionary retail environment is currently challenging," he says.

Umbers was appointed chief executive in March after his long-serving predecessor, Bernie Brookes, stepped down following a year of confusion around his tenure after he quit but decided to stay on to oversee a merger with David Jones, which never got off the ground. Finance chief Mark Ashby also quit, leaving a management vacuum.

Umbers was hired six months earlier as Myer's chief information and supply chain officer.

That appointment was part of the succession planning process ahead of Brookes' retirement. His background heading big corporate transformations and his digital, customer-facing and supply chain experience made him an ideal candidate for the job despite his lack of experience running a department store.

Umbers, a former British Army officer, has plenty of retail experience though and has been working in the front line of store management since the 1990s. He has held senior roles at supermarket groups Aldi and Woolworths and headed up Australia Post's logistics and parcels division. The jury will be out on his performance until details of his strategy become clearer, but there are early indications Umbers is willing to make the tough decisions needed to address years of under-investment and declining sales growth. He also gave the company some breathing space early this month when it refinanced $600 million in debt.

After a year of distracting takeover talk and management upheaval, Umbers has also made it clear the strategic review should not get in the way of day-to-day operations. Rather than being off-site strategising, senior executives have been told to get on with the job.

There are early signs Umbers will take the knife to the company's store footprint, putting to bed Brookes' dream of a 100-store network, which is no longer realistic given the tough retail environment and increase in online shopping, even though much of the supporting supply chain infrastructure is already there. He flagged improvements to productivity per square metre at the half-year results.

Myer said last month it would close three specialty stores in Melbourne and one of its old large-format stores in the Top Ryde City shopping centre in Sydney.

Umbers is analysing Myer's customer base on a store-by-store basis, which has shown that some customers shop at another Myer store outside of their catchment area. There is also scope to negotiate with landlords to scale back on space to reduce the overall store footprint.

Myer has 67 stores but does not own its properties. Brookes planned to open 15 new stores by 2015 with longer-term plans to increase this to 100.

Analysts believe half this number is now more realistic. In Britain, leading retailer John Lewis services 60 million customers from 45 stores compared with 20 million for Myer out of 67 stores.

But tweaking the store network footprint is just one of the things Umbers will be looking at. He is expected to realign the business to a different customer base, shifting the focus away from a previous obsession with exclusive brands. There have also been suggestions there will be less focus on chasing the discount market and a greater emphasis on the premium end.

Finding the right formula will be tricky and does not come without risk.

"This is very high-risk. While cheap and still generating solid cashflow, near-term earnings risk and high levels of debt lead us to believe there are few catalysts for outperformance," UBS analyst Ben Gilbert wrote in May after Myer released third-quarter sales.

Umbers' experience with information technology suggests he will also be focused on unlocking more value from Myer's online offering and integrating this with the physical store network. He is yet to appoint a new chief information officer, who will play a key role in this strategy.

As for any new chief executive, costs will be key to a new strategy. Myer's problem is that costs have been rising quicker than revenues and analysts say Umbers should be looking to reverse that. Improving service levels but finding ways to get more out of existing staff by attracting and retaining good people will also be part of the strategic review.

One of Umbers' priorities has been building a new management team, aimed at giving Myer the stability it needs after a high turnover of senior staff in recent times. His new finance chief starts next week, making the new lineup almost complete.

Umbers joined Myer at the same time as Daniel Bracken. The chief merchandise and marketing officer is also deputy chief executive. Gary Williams, head of strategic planning and business development is also a new face after joining in July last year.

The company still has a way to go to regain the market's confidence. Chairman Paul McClintock did not manage the succession process well and shareholders were angry after Myer downgraded earnings in March just weeks after announcing Umbers' appointment.

Myer's shares are trading about half the level they were in October last year. The stock closed on Friday at $1.29, a long way from its 2009 listing price of $4.10 a share. Analysts have eight sells, five buys and one hold on the stock, according to Bloomberg.

Analysts expect Myer to post a net profit of $71 million for the 2015 financial year compared with $99 million in 2014, according to the consensus of forecasts on Bloomberg.

Figures out from South Africa's Woolworths last week showed total David Jones sales for the 52 weeks to the end of June rose 6.4 per cent following a strong second half. Like-for-like sales rose 3.7 per cent and net space grew 1.3 per cent.

MICHAEL SMITH

Michael.smith@fairfaxmedia.com.au
direction: bearish for Myer shares

headline: The smart way to avoid congestion nightmare
subhead: Technology - National Infrastructure Summit - Building a new framework for growth
intro: Smarter technology, peak tolls, carpooling and staggered work days will be needed to avert a nightmare scenario of Mexico City-style congestion in Sydney and other capitals, says Transurban chief executive Scott Charlton.
text:
Smarter technology, peak tolls, carpooling and staggered work days will be needed to avert a nightmare scenario of Mexico City-style congestion in Sydney and other capitals, says Transurban chief executive Scott Charlton.

Mr Charlton told The Australian Financial Review's National Infrastructure Summit that Australians had to accept sweeping changes to transport pricing, including more peak user charges, to make the most of strained transport routes and limited funding. "User-pays pricing models have been pursued extensively in most infrastructure and utility markets, why should roads and transport be any different?" he asked.

Mr Charlton said transport routes were under strain because more than half the new housing for major cities' surging populations was being built on the outskirts, while more than half of new jobs were being created within 10 kilometres of the central business districts.

He said building new infrastructure was only part of the answer, and the community would have to use roads and transport systems much more efficiently - spurred by charges that reflect costs - for major capitals to remain liveable.

"We simply cannot keep building our way out of congestion, and we have to change the attitudes that existing infrastructure is free," he said. "If we can make better use of the existing infrastructure, we can not only spread the load but delay the need and size of future investment."

He painted a dystopian picture of future transport in major cities without reform, saying Sydney and Melbourne already ranked alongside London, Los Angeles and New York - cities three times larger - for gridlock. "If nothing changes, by 2035 Sydneysiders will face congestion levels on par with Mexico City and will waste 110 hours a year in traffic."

But Mr Charlton said technology under development - integrated roads and public transport, driverless cars with sensors and processors that can "read" road conditions - would help us make more efficient use of transport systems.

"In 20 years we will have technology with the potential to fundamentally change the way we move."
direction: bearish for Transurban due to regulatory and pricing risks

headline: Scandals could end UK corporation tax
intro: When Labour chancellor James Callaghan introduced corporation tax in Britain in 1965, he trumpeted it as "a new landmark in our fiscal history".
text:
When Labour chancellor James Callaghan introduced corporation tax in Britain in 1965, he trumpeted it as "a new landmark in our fiscal history".

Fifty years on, enthusiasm for the tax is in short supply. It has been complicated by reams of legislation, cut back by governments wanting to appeal to investors and tarnished by repeated scandals over avoidance.

A recent revelation that Mondelez International, owner of Cadbury, the confectionery company, is paying no UK corporate tax is the latest controversy to provoke questions about the effectiveness of the tax.

Many experts are asking whether corporation tax can survive in its current form. At an Oxford conference this summer to mark the tax's 50th anniversary, Peter Harris, a professor of tax law at the University of Cambridge, said it could not be salvaged. "We are well past the point of no return," he said. "It is just going to get worse."

Not everyone agrees the tax's days are numbered. It tends to be popular with the public, as it is assumed to be progressive in nature and not fall directly on individuals.

It makes a significant contribution to revenues, bringing in £43 billion ($89 billion) this year, or just under a quarter of the amount raised by income tax.

Yet the contribution of corporation tax as a share of total taxation has fallen. Corporate tax raised 7.7 per cent of all UK taxes last year - less than the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development average of 8.5 per cent and the lowest share since 1994. In 2013, Britain's biggest companies paid more in national insurance contributions than corporation tax for the first time. Corporate tax revenues have been falling across the industrialised world since the economic crisis, according to the OECD.

Pascal Saint-Amans, its top tax official, said: "The great majority of all tax rises seen since the crisis have fallen on individuals. This underlines the urgency of efforts to ensure that corporations pay their fair share."

The many tax-efficient structures that route profits to low-tax countries in the form of interest payments and royalties have been a big drain on revenues. The OECD is behind a crackdown on "base erosion and profit-shifting" [BEPS] to safeguard the future of the corporate tax.

Its recommendations aim at closing loopholes responsible for the avoidance of up to $240bn of tax. But the new curbs on tax planning might not result in bigger revenues if they intensify competition on rates. The OECD said: "In the absence of tax planning, higher-tax rate countries ... may ultimately compete more fiercely to attract investment."

Few countries have been as enthusiastic about tax competition as the UK. Since 2010, it has pushed down rates by 8 points to 20 per cent - just half the rate when it was introduced in 1965. The Treasury defends the cut by pointing to a real-terms increase in revenue since 2010, adding that lowering corporation tax is "one of the best ways you can drive growth in your economy".

Some experts thinks tax competition is unstoppable. Michael Devereux, director of the Oxford University Centre for Business Taxation, has long predicted a race to the bottom in corporate tax rates. He is one of many economists who think it should be reformed with a new system, similar to VAT, that taxes profits, or "value added" with relief for labour costs, at the point of sales.

Another idea by the Institute of Directors is to copy the "tax-transparent" companies known in the US as "S corporations" which pass corporate income directly to shareholders, who must then report the income on their tax returns.

Yet another approach is promoted by campaign groups such as Tax Justice Network. They want to treat multinationals as single entities. This is also being pushed by Brussels, which favours a pan-European tax system.
direction: bearish for UK-listed multinational corporations and tax-sensitive sectors

headline: Encourage investment by levelling the imputation playing field
intro: Instead of lowering the tax rate in hopeless competition with the likes of Singapore, extend the imputation system to foreign investors.
text:
Instead of lowering the tax rate in hopeless competition with the likes of Singapore, extend the imputation system to foreign investors.

A clear message comes out of the Treasury's tax discussion paper. Because our company tax rate of 30 per cent is higher than that of many other countries, we should reduce it so that we are "competitive" in attracting foreign investment. But this message ignores another issue covered in the report - imputation.

Imputation was introduced in 1987 specifically to end double taxation of dividends for Australian shareholders. This is conceptually the first-best answer: the tax system ignores the legal fiction of the corporation and taxes income only once. It also avoids tax distortions between equity and debt. It encourages companies to distribute their profits to shareholders.

Of course, this gives incentive to companies with Australian shareholders to invest here rather than overseas. Company managers recognise that their investment decisions should be guided by the return to shareholders after all taxes (including the shareholders' income tax) are paid. This incentive to invest at home arises not from a fault in the Australian system, but from the distortionary double taxation embodied in foreign tax regimes.

But don't we want to attract foreign investment to this country? The short answer should be that we want to offer foreigners the same tax environment that we offer to domestic shareholders.

The Australian shareholders pay tax at their personal rate on their dividend income, so why shouldn't the foreigners make a similar contribution for the opportunity of making profits here, thanks to the services and favourable environment offered to them in Australia? We don't want foreign companies to be disadvantaged, but nor do we want to offer them a better deal than our domestic shareholders have. With this in mind, to charge the foreigners 30 per cent tax seems eminently reasonable.

Some foreign companies will take their investment to a low-tax jurisdiction. Good luck to them. We don't want to artificially encourage their activities in Australia any more than we want to offer any other form of subsidy to foreigners. If we want to subsidise investment, let's do it for everyone.

Of course, tax in a globalised world is a complex mess. The low-tax arrangements of Google, Amazon and Apple illustrate the problem of a tax system conceived in the 19th century, when it made sense to base company tax on where products were made. Now, it makes more sense to attribute profit to the country in which these types of goods are sold, where profits accrue because these goods are protected by the intellectual property laws of that country.

This issue - "base erosion and profit shifting" - is a priority topic for the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development. Whatever the answer, lowering the Australian company tax rate won't fix this.

Competing with Singapore and Ireland on company tax rates is misguided. A couple of per cent off our company tax rate isn't going to get us anywhere near these countries, which have set their rates to attract profit-shifting tax minimisation. This ploy certainly needs correction but it requires internationally concerted action.

The discussion paper makes much of the size of the Australian corporate tax take: about 20 per cent of total tax, or more than twice the OECD average. But the proper comparison of corporate tax revenue would net out imputation against the gross corporate tax receipts. This would reduce the net corporate revenue to a figure comparable with overseas tax regimes.

Then there is the further distortion from those Australians who have arranged their personal affairs so as to be treated as if they are companies. The tax report recognises this problem but doesn't offer an estimate on the loss of revenue. A reduction in company tax would offer an even greater differential between the company tax rate and the top marginal income tax. Why would we want to do this?

In a world with many tax distortions, why would you alter imputation - the one element that is first-best? Taken together with imputation, our company tax rate seems about right. Lowering it just increases the distortions.
direction: bearish for domestic equities due to potential tax burden increases

headline: China distressed assets specialist flourishes
subhead: Daily selection from THE LEX COLUMN
intro: A distressed asset does not cry out to be bought by anyone other than a specialist. Fortunately, there are a few of these in China, where bad debts are plentiful. For the brave, there is a new chance to buy into such expertise, as one of China's top four managers of bad assets lists in Hong Kong.
text:
A distressed asset does not cry out to be bought by anyone other than a specialist. Fortunately, there are a few of these in China, where bad debts are plentiful. For the brave, there is a new chance to buy into such expertise, as one of China's top four managers of bad assets lists in Hong Kong.

China Huarong Asset Management will close the books on its initial public offering shortly, selling new shares worth as much as $US2.9 billion, or 17 per cent of the enlarged share capital. The proceeds will be used to develop the company's businesses. The bulk will go to distressed asset management, Huarong's largest division, which contributes more than half of group pre-tax profit.

At one level the timing does not seem propitious. China's economy is moving from one led by exports and fixed asset investment to one more reliant on the consumer.

The process is painful: on Monday, China confirmed that third-quarter economic growth was the slowest since 2009. So the supply of bad debts has been rising.

According to Mizuho, non-performing loans in the first half at the nine Hong Kong-listed mainland banks rose by half year-on-year. This may present opportunities for Huarong to buy assets but, as the company points out, acquisition of bad debts and their recovery move in opposite cycles.

But in spite of its ability to buy cheap assets Huarong is no steal. At the top end of the range, it will float at 1.1 times book value, a premium to the 0.9 times of peer Cinda. And Cinda is not an encouraging precedent: after one month's post-IPO rally, it has underperformed the H share index last year and this. This year, pre-IPO investors Citic Capital Financial and UBS cut their stakes.

That Huarong's issuers are not entirely confident of a smooth listing is clear from the structure of the deal: at least two-thirds will be taken up by cornerstone investors, locked up for about six months.

Huarong's stock itself may then look distressed.
direction: bearish for Huarong due to high valuation and economic headwinds

headline: PwC gives staff a chance to make extra cash on the side
intro: Big four accountancy firm PwC has found a way to make loss-leading services profitable using the internet banking model, while giving staff the opportunity to make extra cash on the side.
text:
Big four accountancy firm PwC has found a way to make loss-leading services profitable using the internet banking model, while giving staff the opportunity to make extra cash on the side.

The professional services giant is trialling a crowdsourcing model that allows its team of 120 research-and-development tax specialists to earn a cash bonus if they process claims outside work hours of 8am to 6pm.

"Some people don't want to do it; others think it's great," PwC digital specialist Mark O'Neill said.

PwC Melbourne senior consultant Sam Sargood falls into the latter category. The 26-year-old has earned an extra $1000 through PwC's new overtime scheme over the last six months, and is throwing it towards a two-week snowboarding trip to Niseko, Japan, in December.

"It's awesome earning extra cash; Ican't wait to carve up Japan," Mr Sargood said.

At least half of the firm's R&D specialists are interested in engaging with the crowdsourcing model, according to Mr O'Neill. But it's not compulsory.

The benefit to PwC is twofold: demand in the tax practice fluctuates based on regulatory deadlines, and the distributed crowdsourcing model makes the cost base variable.

"From a business point of view it's great," Mr O'Neill said. But he also believes it has the potential to increase employee engagement.

Workforce utilisation is a massive concern for professional services firms across the board, particularly in disciplines such as tax and auditing which have big peaks and troughs. When firms need people, they want the best. But they don't want to pay for top talent in the off-season.

Launched in October, PwC's new self-service tax rebate tool, Nifty R&D, is challenging many traditionally held practices within the country's biggest accountancy firm. Nifty R&D lets small businesses apply for research and development tax rebates online. It was a segment of the market PwC avoided as it was unable to service it profitably in the past.

"Profitability was a driver for it but the primary motivation was customer experience," Mr O'Neill said.

While clients can call on PwC specialists for help through the process, Nifty R&D is designed to be self-directing. Jobs go into a queue, to be picked up and vetted by any R&D specialist within Australia. For its trouble, PwC charges 13.99 per cent of the total value of a claim.

"It was a departure from PwC's traditionally relationship-driven business," Mr O'Neill said. "We're asking our team members to operate differently."

PwC's research showed only a fraction of businesses eligible for R&D tax rebates - 7500 out of 20,000 companies - claimed them as the process was too complex and time consuming. This represented a big, untapped addressable market for PwC, but only if it could service them cost effectively.

Of the hundreds of Australian businesses now using Nifty R&D, 80 per cent are new clients to PwC and more than half are less than two years old. "We've got a whole lot of new revenue because we haven't serviced that segment before," Mr O'Neill said.

Uptake is growing at 10 per cent each week. Most are small and micro-businesses, along with a few medium-sized entities. "On a per customer basis, we are profitable," Mr O'Neill said. In the April peak for filing R&D rebate claims, PwC was turning around claims in two hours. "That's a phenomenal rate compared to where we were a few years ago," Mr O'Neill said.

He concedes it has taken time for people to understand what Nifty R&D was attempting to do. Articulating "why" has become critically important. "Once they realise that if we can change our own business like this so can one of our competitors, people start to see how the new service will fit into their working lives," Mr O'Neill said.

PwC plans to replicate Nifty R&D for export development grants.
direction: neutral to slightly positive for PwC shares

headline: Propertylink secures powerful backer for bid
intro: Propertylink has secured the backing of a major investment bank in its bid for the $300 million commercial property portfolio being sold by US billionaire businessman Sam Zell's Equity Commonwealth.
text:
Propertylink has secured the backing of a major investment bank in its bid for the $300 million commercial property portfolio being sold by US billionaire businessman Sam Zell's Equity Commonwealth.

Senior sources told The Australian Financial Review Goldman Sachs has linked up with the Sydney-based property fund manager.

A tie-up would make sense, given the two have partnered together in the past. Goldman Sachs was one of two key investors for Propertylink's flagship Propertylink Australian Industrial Partnership fund.

The large-scale portfolio offering, which includes a major office tower on Pitt Street in Sydney, was first flagged last year.

Earlier this year, JLL was exclusively appointed to market the portfolio, which also includes a number of industrial properties across NSW, Victoria, Queensland and Western Australia.

The US-listed Equity Commonwealth, formerly known as Commonwealth REIT, is based in Chicago. It is an internally managed and self-advised real estate investment trust. It has wanted to sell off the Australian assets for some time, it is understood.

Propertylink and Equity Commonwealth could not be reached for a comment.

However, the Financial Review reported in December Propertylink would aggressively grow its funds business next year after kicking off its second PAIP fund.

The first PAIP fund was Propertylink's largest and most successful to date. Aside from Goldman Sachs its other main investor Grosvenor. The roughly $500 million fund acquired an asset on average every 11 days last year.

Propertylink has worked with an impressive array of investment partners over the last couple of years. In addition to Goldman Sachs and Grosvenor its other investors through mandates and other funds include Chinese conglomerate Fosun, investment giant M&G Real Estate, Bahrain-based Islamic bank Al Salam, RBS and the UK-listed Balfour Beatty.

More than 3½ years ago Propertylink had $33 million of assets. The firm now has more than $2.5 billion across property and infrastructure. Managing director Stephen Day said last year that floating the business was an option but it wants to continue growing in the medium term.

It is unclear whether Propertylink is looking at the entire Equity Commonwealth portfolio, which consists of both office and industrial properties.

The most significant asset is 320 Pitt Street in Sydney. Currently an office building leased to Telstra, the tower has development potential. It last sold about five years ago for just more than $190 million.
direction: bullish for Propertylink shares

headline: Calibre to target infrastructure
intro: Calibre Group's plan to remove its stock from the Australian Securities Exchange is the "best option" for the engineering group because it buys time to diversify away from the resources industry into infrastructure projects, chairman Ray Horsburgh said.
text:
Calibre Group's plan to remove its stock from the Australian Securities Exchange is the "best option" for the engineering group because it buys time to diversify away from the resources industry into infrastructure projects, chairman Ray Horsburgh said.

Perth-based Calibre, which is majority-owned by US private equity firm First Reserve, told investors in late October that it planned to delist because its board did not believe its $39 million market capitalisation reflected the underlying value of the business and it did not make sense to keep paying the administrative and compliance costs associated with running a public company.

Calibre's stock has lost two thirds of its value over the past 12 months, dropping to 11¢ from 36¢. It floated at $1.63 a share in August 2012 in a $75 million initial public offering but its shares tumbled in April 2013 after it slashed its annual profits guidance due to weakening resources markets.

The engineering group has specialised in providing services to iron ore miners BHP Billiton, Rio Tinto and Fortescue Metals but resources-related revenue has dropped sharply as miners have cut back spending on projects. Calibre reported a net $160 million loss for fiscal 2015 compared to a $24 million profit a year earlier.

Mr Horsburgh, who has chaired Calibre since May 2012, said the resources market was unlikely to rebound in the near term.

"There's no capex being spent, Rio and BHP have told us quite clearly that they're not going to expand into new mines for some time and it may be a decade before they work out how to get more tonnes out of their existing mines at a cheaper cost," he said.

Calibre did not see any point in getting involved in the Queensland coal industry because demand from India and China was waning, and was instead targeting infrastructure projects, he added.

Calibre hired Michel Masson, the former chief executive of Melbourne trams operator Yarra Trams, in April to run a new transport and infrastructure services division. Mr Masson was recently sent to Singapore, which is expanding its rail network, to examine potential opportunities. First Reserve plans to keep its investment in Calibre for three years and then find an exit strategy, Mr Horsburgh said.

"They want to run it pretty much like a private equity play and so we're prepared to put more cash in if we need to for the right acquisitions," he said. "We've got to diversify the business, build up its income and get some value that we can't get as a listed company."

Calibre this month paid $45 million cash to buy utility services provider Diona. Calibre shareholders can choose to participate in a share buyback at 12¢ a share or keep their shares in the unlisted company.

First Reserve, which owns 59 per cent of the company, will not participate in the buyback.
direction: bearish for Calibre and resources-sector engineering stocks

headline: Let's open up our cities for redevelopment
intro: Housing bubble and burst? Then there is a shortage of free-standing homes in capital cities and Credit Suisse predicting China will buy up $60 billion worth in the next six years. For Australian-born first-home buyers, the economy, building industry and retail, the government must declare that free-standing houses of no historical or architectural significance on 750 square metre sites within 10 kilometres of a capital city may be redeveloped with two new dwellings.
text:
Housing bubble and burst? Then there is a shortage of free-standing homes in capital cities and Credit Suisse predicting China will buy up $60 billion worth in the next six years. For Australian-born first-home buyers, the economy, building industry and retail, the government must declare that free-standing houses of no historical or architectural significance on 750 square metre sites within 10 kilometres of a capital city may be redeveloped with two new dwellings.

The government should also require that these projects must only be purchased by genuine first-home buyers (to stop Baby Boomers snatching them up) and, that they cannot be bought up by speculators (negative-gearers). What does the PM and creative thinker and doer, Josh Frydenberg, have to say about that?

John Dobinson

North Balwyn, Vic
direction: bearish for residential property developers and housing sector

headline: Why even high earners lose credit card control
intro: Would you consider putting the bulk of your monthly household spend on to your mortgage and letting the interest build up?
text:
Would you consider putting the bulk of your monthly household spend on to your mortgage and letting the interest build up?

No way, I'm guessing most of you would say, figuring that would be a pretty sloppy way to handle your finances. So why do it on your credit card where interest rates are as high as 25 per cent (compared to just under 6 per cent on your home loan)?

It's a particularly hot issue at the moment because credit card issuers are under renewed fire for keeping their rates so high when interest rates generally are at all-time lows.

The point of the question is not to coerce you to spend big on your home loan because interest costs are lower, but to illustrate how expensive credit card debt is. And if you're a high earner who's not worried about carrying over your credit card debt each month "because you know you can afford to pay it off", you're one of the worst culprits when it comes to sloppy finances.

Financial planners say this is one of the biggest problems they face with executive clients.

"We see this all the time," says David Simon, managing partner with Integral Private Wealth. "They may have a debit of $20,000, but when they get their statement, it shows the minimum payment so it's very hard to determine the impact [of carrying over the debt].

"A $20,000 outstanding liability is costing $4000 a year in interest - that's equivalent to [a monthly repayment on a] $100,000 mortgage. Or the equivalent of council rates or the cost of your insurance."

Financial planner Catherine Robson, principal of Affinity Private, agrees. "It's so wasteful, but easy to do." Her first step with clients in this situation is to get them to set up an automatic payment to pay off the credit card in full each month.

"This speaks to making your financial life easy," she explains. "It means you don't put yourself in a situation where you have to remember to pay the credit card account because there's an automated payment."

Robson says getting this under control is "non-negotiable". "We do a lot of work with clients and their cash flow management. We use cloud-based Xero to help them see where they are spending."

What happens to those who don't get it together? "If we have clients who are really not prepared to commit to achieving their own goals, [we point out that] they shouldn't be paying us if they are not prepared to prioritise their own financial security."

Robson blames the "casualness" with which credit cards are made available. "When you take out a home loan, the process and loan documentation make it feel more serious and costly," she explains.

"On a credit card, even if it's just $20,000 outstanding a year, that's usually about 20 per cent interest. That adds up to real money you could be spending on more meaningful things."

Simon says it's often a wake-up call for clients when they look at the real cost of credit card negligence. "If you know it's costing you the equivalent of a small holiday or a term of school fees, it's a practical way to evaluate the actual value of what you are having to forgo [to carry over debt]."

So how much are credit card providers gouging you in terms of interest? Way too much, says Michelle Hutchison of online comparison site finder.com.

"Home loan rates have been falling since the cash rate began to fall in November 2011, but on average, credit card purchase rates have remained the same," she says.

"We calculate Australian credit card holders have paid an extra $2.1 billion on their plastic since November 2011. That's despite the cash rate falling by 2.75 percentage points to 2 per cent."
