The average standard variable rate has fallen by 1.90 percentage points since November 2011 to 5.65 per cent in April 2015. Hutchison says credit card rates range from 7.99 to 25.9 per cent, while variable-rate home loans start from about 3.98 per cent.

Now, if you're thinking about rolling your credit card debt on to a balance transfer card that's promising zero interest for a limited period, read the small print. If you don't, the whole procedure could cost much more than you bargained for - and leave you with a poor credit rating.

Peter Arnold of online comparison site www.ratecity.com.au says these are the points to watch out for:

■ Revert rates: once the "honeymoon" period is over, you are likely to get whacked with a much higher rate - sometimes the cash advance rate, which can be particularly steep. So make sure you pay off the transferred balance and don't just make the minimum repayment.

■ Annual fees: factor these in to the overall cost, as they generally get charged straight away. "Also, don't forget it will creep up on you again in month 13," Arnold says.

■ Balance transfer fees: Arnold warns of some providers charging an upfront balance-transfer handling fee. "This can be up to 2 per cent and gets charged even if you pay the balance off pretty much straight away."

■ High interest rates: these will apply to any new purchases you make with the card. "Many of the hottest deals have high interest rates for purchases, so if you start spending on the card and don't pay it off, you'll pay more than you otherwise would."

■ Credit rating: doing too many balance transfers will be a warning sign to lenders when they look at your credit rating, says Arnold. "This could cost you more next time you apply for a home loan or personal loan, as the lenders with the lowest rates may decline your application."

dcleveland@fairfaxmedia.com.au
direction: bearish for consumer discretionary and credit card issuers

headline: Aussie drops on commodity falls as greenback finds friends
intro: The Australian dollar plunged on Monday, as further greenback support and the resulting commodity price weakness reversed a week-long rally driven mainly by investors unwinding or covering bets the local unit would decline.
text:
The Australian dollar plunged on Monday, as further greenback support and the resulting commodity price weakness reversed a week-long rally driven mainly by investors unwinding or covering bets the local unit would decline.

In late local trade the Aussie was buying US71.82¢, compared with US71.92¢ at the same time on Friday, and as high as US72.49¢ over the weekend. At one stage it had lost about 1 per cent to US71.59¢, before a bounce.

Analysts attributed the abrupt descent against the greenback to mounting bets the US Federal Reserve would next month lift interest rates for the first time in almost a decade, and to selling and short positioning in the face of further declines in the price of commodities such as gold, copper and zinc.

These all began moving lower in Asian trade, suggesting intensifying jitters about China's slowdown and the affect on global demand.

Iron ore, Australia's most important commodity, has also been losing ground in recent days, being last priced late Friday Australian time down 1.17 per cent at $US44.91 a metric tonne.

Another central bank devaluation of China's yuan, to its lowest level since the end of August, has further fuelled concerns about the country's growth slowdown.

However, analysts said renewed confidence that the Fed would begin lifting interest rates at its December 16 policy meeting was driving a fresh flurry of long bets on the US currency. The Fed's board of governors was also due to meet early on Tuesday Australian time.

The US dollar this month has hit a 12-year high on a trade-weighted basis, a fact that could ultimately force the Fed to put off its initial rate increase.

The strong currency, along with the end of quantitative easing over a year ago, is a type of de facto monetary tightening that works against inflation, which remains worryingly below the bottom of the Fed's target range of 2 per cent to 3 per cent.

"The Fed's already mentioned in some recent statements that they are concerned about the impact of a very strong dollar," Lutz Karpowitz, a senior currency strategist at Commerzbank, told Bloomberg.

"That means that the moment the dollar appreciation would be too strong, the Fed would adjust its monetary policy," he said. "The rate-hike cycle could be even more cautious than the market so far expects."

However, the greenback's latest burst followed comments by San Francisco Fed president John Williams early on Sunday morning Australian time that the case for a December "lift-off" in interest rates was firming.

"Assuming that we continue to get good data on the economy, continue to get signs that we are moving closer to achieving our goals and gaining confidence getting back to 2 per cent inflation, if that continues to happen, there's a strong case to be made in December to raise rates," he said during a panel discussion at the University of California in Berkeley.

The Aussie's resistance to further commodity price falls, particularly that of the bellwether copper, had also been unsustainable, a range of currency strategists said on Monday.

"The current momentum in Australian data is expected to fade in early 2016, while we see the commodity complex as a warning sign for the Australian dollar," wrote Australia and New Zealand Banking group senior economist Joanne Masters. "Iron ore prices are close to retesting the mid-July lows, while copper presents an even more worrying picture," she said.
direction: bearish for Australian equities and commodity-linked shares

headline: MIRA lines up Dutch money for NSW assets
intro: There has been plenty written about bid groups for the great poles and wires sell-off. But so far little has been said about the world's largest infrastructure fund manager and home-town starter, Macquarie Group's Macquarie Infrastructure and Real Assets.
text:
There has been plenty written about bid groups for the great poles and wires sell-off. But so far little has been said about the world's largest infrastructure fund manager and home-town starter, Macquarie Group's Macquarie Infrastructure and Real Assets.

MIRA is understood to be lining up for NSW's electricity transmission and distribution assets, likely teaming up with Dutch giant APG as an equity partner.

Sources said the heavyweight was likely to be advised by Macquarie's own Macquarie Capital, which has only become available in recent days after the investment bank's Queensland power privatisation mandate went down with the Newman government.

It will be interesting to see MIRA at auction. The fund has been a notable absentee in Australia's recent infrastructure bidding frenzies, such as the Queensland Motorways toll roads portfolio and the ports of Botany and Kembla, preferring to focus on offshore opportunities and smaller deals such as the Port of Newcastle.

But it can swing hard for the right deal, and with three deals worth north of $20 billion slated for NSW it is likely to be a contender.

MIRA's interest comes as the stragglers finalise bidding groups ahead of the auction's expected start in April. While the deal is subject to Mike Baird winning a sale mandate at an election in March, bidders and their advisers are working hard to be ready should the auction begin.

MIRA will face off against fellow local heavyweights AustralianSuper, which has teamed up with Canadian Pension Plan Investment Board and Borealis Infrastructure, IFM Investors, QIC Ltd, AMP Capital and Hastings Funds Management, among others.

Then there are offshore bidders including State Grid and Cheung Kong Group.
direction: supportive for Macquarie Group and NSW infrastructure assets

headline: Bleak coal outlook to force consolidation: Yancoal
intro: Loss-making miner Yancoal says coal prices will remain weak in 2015 and will prompt a round of consolidation in the Australian coal sector.
text:
Loss-making miner Yancoal says coal prices will remain weak in 2015 and will prompt a round of consolidation in the Australian coal sector.

In comments that follow Glencore's decision to cut its Australian coal production by almost 15 per cent and Rio Tinto's decision to break up its coal-dominated energy division, Yancoal said it expected weak coal prices to persist through 2015 at least.

"Market over-supply is expected to continue to restrict the opportunity for significant coal price improvements in 2015. Further consolidation of operators is anticipated and may provide some relief in the metallurgical and thermal spot price markets," the miner said in a statement.

Prices for metallurgical or coking coal have fallen from more than $US300 per tonne in 2011 to be closer to $US102 in recent days, while thermal coal has more than halved to about $US61 per tonne over the same period.

Yancoal has been among the hardest hit by the downturn, and the Chinese-dominated miner posted its second successive loss over the weekend.

Aside from weak coal prices, Yancoal said take-or-pay rail and port contracts were a major factor in its $353.4 million full-year loss, continuing a trend that has been witnessed across the industry.

Take-or-pay contracts force miners to pay for rail and port capacity over many years, regardless of whether they use that capacity or not.

The contracts have become notorious in the Australian coal sector, where some miners have been forced to keep loss-making mines open because the take-or-pay contracts would make it more expensive to shut.

The financial statements published by Yancoal suggest the miner is tied to take-or-pay rail and port contracts for many more years. Yancoal noted a $10.1 million current provision for take-or-pay contracts, and a $46.1 million non-current provision for the contracts.

Both numbers were only slightly lower than the provisions published at the 2013 full-year results.

Many of the take-or-pay contracts causing pain in the Australian coal sector were struck in 2011 around the peak of coal prices.

Yancoal hopes the impact of take-or-pay contracts will be reduced in future years when it completes the expansion of the Moolarben mine in New South Wales. While more common in the coal sector, iron ore exporter Arrium Limited has also struck trouble with take-or-pay rail contracts in South Australia, where it is obligated to pay for services for another two years.
direction: bearish for coal miners and related infrastructure stocks

headline: Mayver's cashes in on star rating
intro: Healthy spread maker Healthfarm Finefoods is cashing in from adopting the controversial Health Star Rating system early.
text:
Healthy spread maker Healthfarm Finefoods is cashing in from adopting the controversial Health Star Rating system early.

Paul Raff, founder of Healthfarm Finefoods, says he put the star label on products in the mainstream category - Mayver's peanut butter and Nutella-alternative "super spread" - to target everyday consumers who do not shop in the health food aisle.

"The health stars are not for people who shop in the category anyway. It's for the people who don't know what is healthier," Raff says.

Adopting the health stars early has been a plus for the business, he says. The spread manufacturer has claimed a 5 per cent stake in the competitive peanut butter market and has doubled revenue to $20 million in the past three years. "We put health stars on our products. We were one of the first people who knew about the labels coming out and it's helping us because it shows we're not doing anything sneaky," he says.

Under the government-regulated Health Star Rating system, products are assigned a score of between half a star and five stars according to their energy, fat, sugar and salt content. Food companies have four more years to voluntarily adopt the star system and after that it might become compulsory.

Mayver's was the first manufacturer in the spread category to adopt the label, when the system was unveiled in December 2014. So far only a handful of small businesses have adopted the system, including muesli maker Food for Health, juice maker Parker's Organic and Kiwi-made Pic's Really Good Peanut Butter. Mayver's line of products have star ratings between 4 and 5 and are retailing at a premium price above their competitors. On Coles Online Mayver's peanut spread comes in at a hefty price of $6.05 for a 375-gram jar, compared with $3.74 for the same-sized jar of Sanitarium peanut butter, $4.94 for a Kraft jar and $5.16 for a Dick Smith jar.

In a report last year the Centre for International Economics (CIE) said small businesses faced particularly high costs because of their limited in-house capacity to calculate star ratings and change label design and production. The CIE estimated calculating the health stars could cost between $500 and $900 per product, label redesigning costs between $2000 and $3000 per product and reprinting costs $350 to $5000 per plate.

Raff disagreed, saying it was easy to roll out the health star system, particularly for health food manufacturers that did not have to change their recipes to get more stars.

"It's a free thing. It's basically calculating the nutrition value of the product and because our products are healthy anyway - we've got nothing to hide - we didn't have to reformulate our products," he says.

King and Wood Mallesons consumer lawyer Melissa Monks says despite the government's push for the health star system and the "name and shame" campaign run by consumer advocate CHOICE, most small businesses are waiting to see how the health star system works before adopting it. "Certainly none of my food clients are pursuing or actively considering product reformulations to enhance their rating," she says.
direction: bullish for Healthfarm Finefoods and the broader food sector

headline: Shorten's fund raises questions
intro: In his budget reply speech recently Bill Shorten announced his plan for a $500 million Smart Investment Fund - citing the spectacular growth of SEEK.com, which he said drew on Australian government investment.
text:
In his budget reply speech recently Bill Shorten announced his plan for a $500 million Smart Investment Fund - citing the spectacular growth of SEEK.com, which he said drew on Australian government investment.

Shorten is right that SEEK was funded under the Innovation Investment Fund, a program established by the Howard government in 1997.

We all want to see more Australian tech sector start-up companies grow into large, successful, employment-generating companies with a sustainable market position - like SEEK and other success stories such as Cochlear, Atlassian and REA.

But Shorten's proposal raises more questions than it answers.

To start with, he did not say where the money is going to come from. Nor was there much detail about how the fund will work, apart from that it would "partner with venture capitalists and fund managers to invest in early-stage and high-potential companies".

This sounds much like the Innovation Investment Fund, which ran from 1997 until new funding was withdrawn in last year's budget. But Shorten did not tell us how he would overcome the difficulties the IIF experienced once it fell into the hands of the Rudd-Gillard-Rudd government.

Certainly the IIF had some early successes, notably in round 1, which began in 1998 - as well as SEEK, an investment in the early internet search engine Looksmart returned $237 million from an investment of $2 million.

But even in round 1, three of the five funds generated a negative return.

By round 3, it was a real struggle to attract matching funding from private sector investors. In 2013 the Labor government announced $100 million across three private sector managers. But two of the three funds did not go ahead. This reflected the reality that venture capital as an investment class in Australia has generated poor returns over the last decade or so, making many investors reluctant to put money in.

It might be argued that the IIF was not designed to make a profit on the taxpayers' money invested - instead the aim was to build a viable venture capital sector in Australia.

If this was the aim, the outcome was mixed at best: a review in 2010 found that the IIF was "unlikely to engender by itself a viable and flourishing VC industry in Australia".

It may be possible to design a government funding scheme that catalyses a thriving private market in venture capital. This, after all, has been part of the Israeli government's strategy - with such success that a recent book about the technology sector in Israel is entitled Start Up Nation.

But it is hard to believe a Shorten Labor government would do a good job of this - particularly when you consider Labor's track record in funding technology during the Rudd-Gillard-Rudd years. We repeatedly saw hastily conceived schemes to pump billions of dollars of taxpayers' money into government-owned behemoths - such as the $43 billion National Broadband Network Company and the $10 billion Clean Energy Finance Corporation. Rather than stimulating the private sector, the aim seemed to be to drive it off the field. The real objective should be attracting private capital into the technology sector.

There are some encouraging recent developments, including a new generation of privately funded Australian venture capital funds, such as Niki Scevak's Blackbird and Daniel Petre's Airtree Ventures.

Brandon Capital Partners recently announced it had raised $200 million from superannuation funds for its third Medical Research Commercialisation Fund - a notable achievement in crafting a venture capital investment opportunity attractive to the superannuation sector.

The Coalition's policies are designed to encourage further private-sector activity. In the budget the Abbott government announced improved tax treatment for employee share schemes - to make it easier for technology start-ups to attract the talent they need to grow - and plans to remove obstacles to crowd-sourced equity funding.

We also announced changes to the Significant Investor Visa - with applicants now required to invest at least $500,000 in eligible Australian venture capital or private equity funds, investing in start-up and small private companies.

In contrast, Bill Shorten is proposing another government-administered fund - but he has failed to explain how it will help towards an objective of boosting the private venture capital market.

Paul Fletcher is parliamentary secretary to the Minister for Communications.
direction: bearish for tech sector start-ups and venture capital funds

headline: We need more than a GST scare
intro: More reform please. The latest OECD report shows that Australia has to run faster just to stand still.
text:
More reform please. The latest OECD report shows that Australia has to run faster just to stand still.

The political risks of the Turnbull government's tax reform agenda are now sinking in.

And yet it is impossible to read the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development's diagnosis of the global economic malaise without being struck by the strength of the forces running against Australia as it seeks to capitalise on the mining boom and all the other opportunities created by China's economic emergence.

The nation's politicians are fixated on just one ambitious reform - a shift in the tax mix based on an expanded GST. But while that would make an important contribution to future growth, it would hardly be transformative. It would raise gross domestic product by perhaps 1 per cent after a decade or so.

We need that and more as we face the challenge of a weakened global economy that the OECD warns will be slow to recover. The consequent problems with global trade are more acute: it has been slowing for several years, but has stagnated and, according to the OECD, may even have declined since late 2014.

China, which is struggling with the end of its investment boom, accounts for almost 30 per cent of the decline in the non-OECD imports. The deep recessions of Russia and Brazil are responsible for another 30 per cent.

Australians know all about the slowdown in China and its effect on commodity prices and our terms of trade. But they might not appreciate the extent to which Australia's growth prospects can be damaged by a malfunctioning global economy and faulty decisions in Australia.

Trade, says the OECD, is a bellwether of global output. It strengthens competition, keeps domestic firms fit, and contributes to the diffusion of new technologies and productivity growth.

Most of the advanced countries are now far below their pre-global recession trend growth paths and the OECD has again revised down its estimates of their potential growth. Why? Because their weak growth has permanently marginalised large numbers of unemployed, and their low rates of investment have stunted productivity growth. Weak growth begets weak growth.

This, the OECD says, underlines the importance of implementing productivity-raising structural policies.

Australia is one OECD's milder cases, and one of its better patients.

But outside its resources sector Australia's investment also is weak, and so is our immigration.

Australia's potential growth rate too has been slowing and per capita real national income - the key to Australian living standards - is in decline.

Are Australians really content to see the gains from the mining boom unnecessarily eroded?

If not, we will need all the productivity-raising reform we can get. We need labour market reform and the competition policy reform recommended by the Harper committee.

And, as Ian Harper says, the government also needs to reform its attempts at trade reform. And it should start with the Trans Pacific Partnership Agreement.

"This agreement has now been released, but it has not been subject to a cost-benefit analysis," Harper says of the agreement.

"There is the question of whether cost-benefit analysis should be conducted before these agreements are signed, and we recommend that that be the case."

The Committee for Economic Development of Australia (CEDA) too has weighed in with a call for a formal trade policy that sets the parameters for all negotiations coupled with a more transparent negotiation process.

"Such a trade policy should include a component that provides direction on the future of Australia's trade liberalisation measures and ensure that appropriate governance protocols are part of the FTA approval process," it says.

"Put simply, these agreements must be about trade not foreign policy."

In 2010 the Productivity Commission recommended that it report on new trade agreements before they were signed, but that was rejected by the Gillard government.

A former head of the Industries Assistance Commission, Bill Carmichael, has since proposed public inquiries by the commission in the lead-up to trade negotiations as a partial answer to the twin problems of the secrecy of the negotiations and the quality of the agreements.

There's been no shortage of reform to do, or of the need to do it - just of the necessary political will, including from Labor's smirking front bench whose contribution now is to be another GST scare campaign.
direction: bearish for Australian equities and rate-sensitive sectors

headline: Both beauty and beast
intro:
iJaguar F-Type R coupe
Price
$226,970 excluding on road costs
text:
Was there ever a more beautiful Jaguar?

Not in the modern era.

No, in my respectful submission, you'd have to go back to the 1960s to find one.

That was before the E-Type acquired its 1970s bloat (and let's not dwell on its pug-ugly XJS replacement). It was also when the XJ6 sedan was in its original, purist form.

The F-Type coupe body is what a Jag should be: a blend of beauty and dynamism.

If the designers aimed to capture the poise of a big cat about to pounce, then mission accomplished.

It is quite a different vehicle to the convertible version which came first (also a delightful looker).

The variant we are testing here is one of the freshened-up 2016 model year cars (Jaguar got in very early). It's the R, with the most powerful version of the company's 5-litre supercharged V8.

The output is a somewhat outrageous 404 kW and 680 Nm, and the top speed is 300 km/h.

Going for the doctor, so to speak, has a dramatic effect on the purchase price.

The base F-Type coupe, with a 280 kW V6 and manual gearbox, has the same exquisite bodywork but a price-tag of around $120,000 plus on-road costs.

The R (an auto only proposition) jumps to $226,970 plus on-road costs, and you'll need to allow another $16K if you want the new all-wheel drive system.

That means at one end of its range, the F-Type is competing with the Porsche Cayman coupe, at the other it is taking on the Porsche 911.

That's if many really cross-shop. In my experience most 911 buyers wouldn't consider any Jaguar, nor would the majority of Jaguar buyers opt for the more austere and Germanic option.

The compromise position for F-Type buyers is the S version, using a more highly tuned version of the standard car's 3-litre V6. It produces a "mere" 280 kW but costs $70K less than the R.

The F-Type structure is mostly aluminium, containing the weight to 1650 kg in V8 form. That's a few kilos lighter than the equivalent convertible.

There are now 12 versions, which is a mighty high number considering the modest sales achieved thus far. To be fair, though, the F-Type is competing in a tiny niche that is as much about adding a halo to the overall range as shifting metal.

And buyers in that niche want a lot of customisation.

Improved equipment levels for model year 2016 include a rear camera and front-parking sensors on all versions and electric power-assisted steering. This is precise but a little short of feedback.

The V6 S and V8 R can now be specified with an optional all-wheel drive system.

This torque-on-demand system is mainly geared to improve all-weather grip. It comes to life only when front end traction is lost; the rest of the time the Jag's wonderful rear-drive road manners are maintained.

The switchable "Active Sports Exhaust" system is standard across the range. In the R the engine roars even in the standard setting. Flick the switch to "chequered flag" mode and it will interesting to see if your neighbours are still talking to you …

Engine audiophiles might find that there is something, even better than the shriek on acceleration. It's the fireworks when the accelerator is lifted. It's almost old school Formula One, and an absolute tribute to the ability of Jaguar engineers … to circumvent strict noise regulations.

The R rides on elaborate 20 inch wheels and has quad exhausts.

This is one very quick car, as you'd expect. It will accelerate from rest to 100 km/h in 4.2 seconds and corners hard and flat and securely.

The R's standard torque vectoring system nips the brakes on the inside wheels at just the right moments to increase cornering speeds. The only exterior area that isn't all class is the rear-tail gate. There are a couple of apparent "shutlines" under the rear window, as if the plant couldn't press the metal in one piece. These are highlighted even more when the body is finished in white, as per our test car.

The interior isn't quite as dramatic as the exterior, nor as well executed as some of the competition.

A central double ridge in the centre of the dashboard looks like it is straight from an Aston Martin, and is topped with a pair of air vents that rise and fall when the car is turned on and off. The words complex and unnecessary come to mind.

The R has a bronze anodised finish on the starter button and the steering wheel-mounted gear paddles. It is meant to look premium, but to these eyes it looks unfinished.

The seats are attractively shaped and finished in leather but the bottom squab is a little hard on long runs (not helped by the choppy ride), and the general ambience isn't quite that of a quarter million dollar car.

There is no parcel shelf behind the two seats, just a small open bin, and the boot is almost comically small, at least as long as it is dominated by the spare wheel. Apparently there is a fix. Take the spare out, leave in the garage and, if you have a flat, call Jaguar's around-the-clock helpline.

Like most companies, Jaguar uses an outside supplier for its official roadside assist, but it has negotiated a special deal where they are guaranteed to always have a spare F-Type wheel with them.

Another secret is to take out the cargo cover that keeps everything visually neat, and stack your luggage up to the height of the back window. You won't be able to see anything through the central rear-view mirror - but you could never see much anyway and you will have a change of clothes on your weekend away.

Anyway, it could be worse. It could be the F-Type roadster. That has the distinction of having what might be the smallest boot that anyone has had the cheek to call a boot.

But, hey, there are plenty of cars with generous cargo arrangements, but so very few that look stunningly beautiful. L&L

iJaguar F-Type R coupe

Price

$226,970 excluding on road costs

Engine

5-litre supercharged V8 (petrol)

Power/torque

405kW/680Nm

Fuel economy

11.1L/100km combined cycle

C02

259g/km
direction: bearish for Jaguar shares due to high pricing and niche appeal

headline: Pepper plays where banks may not stray
intro: Non-bank lenders will likely pick up borrowers whom the banks drop, just as they have in Europe, if regulators in Australia raise capital levels to meet new global standards or to slow investor speculation, says the co-head of Pepper Group, Patrick Tuttle.
text:
Non-bank lenders will likely pick up borrowers whom the banks drop, just as they have in Europe, if regulators in Australia raise capital levels to meet new global standards or to slow investor speculation, says the co-head of Pepper Group, Patrick Tuttle.

The recent bank asset quality review by the European Central Bank, which found a €9.5 billion ($13.1 billion) shortfall in capital across 130 banks, is expected to lead to loan book sales and new opportunities for Pepper, which has grown quickly in the countries hardest hit by the crisis there.

"We believe there'll be a vast number of distressed loan portfolios that will begin to trade across Italy in the next couple of years," Mr Tuttle said.

"They have just completed the asset quality review across Europe and some Italian banks were found to be most wanting in terms of lack of capital to support their loan books. We think that will naturally mean loan portfolios will be up for sale, similar to what happened in Ireland and Spain."

In Australia, the financial system inquiry has recommended banks here maintain capital levels in the top quartile of banks globally and the biggest banks raise risk-weighted capital held against mortgages. If adopted by the banking regulator, this will mean our banks will need to keep up as Europe and other regions raise capital levels.

Many analysts also expect the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority to make lenders that maintain investor loan growth rates above 10 per cent hold more capital against those loans, although it is not clear whether this will be enough for them to shy away from lending to that sector.

"We expect increases in regulatory capital requirements will be announced soon for those institutions running investor mortgage books at [annual] growth above 10 per cent," JPMorgan senior economist Ben Jarman said in a note on Tuesday.

Mr Tuttle said: "To the extent that [new banking regulation] causes the banks to allocate more capital against mortgage lending that may make our ability to compete [in Australia] easier. And if there are crackdowns on investor lending, that creates an opportunity for non-bank lenders to potentially compete in that space."

Other lenders such as Firstmac have also said there could be opportunities for them to pick up new customers if APRA-regulated banks ease investor lending.

However, the Australian Securities and Investments Commission is watching the non-bank sector as well and is seeking to reduce interest-only loans that don't comply with responsible lending standards. These are the preferred type of loan for investors because they only hold properties for short periods. If these become harder to grant, it may discourage investors.

Pepper was one of a consortium that just missed out on nabbing GE's local consumer finance business, which will be sold to private equity house KKR & Co, Varde Partners and Deutsche Bank for $8.2 billion.

But it will finalise the purchase of a similar business in China in May, rumoured to be worth about $US600 million ($784 million), and is one of two left bidding for a €400 million portfolio of so-called "salary secured" personal loans in Italy.

This involves borrowers repaying the loans via a direct debt from their pre-tax salary.

Pepper has partnered with loan buyers of distressed debt in Europe as regulators there have forced banks to offload riskier borrowers after the financial crisis. Typically it acts as a "loan servicer", taking fees from banks to manage the customer service and collections from residential or commercial borrowers over several years. But the banks won't write new loans to such borrowers. So while managing the loans Pepper acquires new customers that banks now consider too risky to loan to.

It will soon begin selling mortgages in Ireland and Britain after acting as a loan servicer in those countries.

Key points

Loan book sales are expected to lead to new opportunities for Pepper.

Recommendation for banks to raise risk-weighted capital held against mortgages.
direction: negative for major banks, positive for non-bank lenders like Pepper Group

headline: Crowdfunding firm cuts out the investment middle men
intro: At least six developers will initially be competing for investors in a new crowdfunding company that is targeting commercial and retail money with projects aiming to yield more than 9 per cent.
text:
At least six developers will initially be competing for investors in a new crowdfunding company that is targeting commercial and retail money with projects aiming to yield more than 9 per cent.

CrowdfundUP, which launches on Thursday, will accept minimum investments from $100 for projects whose terms, conditions and returns vary according to the developer and project.

Jack Quigley, founder and managing director, said: "A lot of developers need to streamline their financing and are not happy with the costs and service provided by the banks."

Mr Quigley added a lot of investors are not happy with what they are told by fund managers about the quality, duration and developers behind many investment projects.

"There problem is whether they believe what a fund manager says," he said. He said his website, which can also be accessed from mobile phones, would bring together a range of developers with at least 10 years experience, a portfolio of completed projects and proven track record. The website will provide details of the projects, key management and a beauty parade of future investments, including terms, conditions and projected returns.

There will initially be about six developers.

CrowdfundUP, which is authorised by financial regulators, including Australian Securities and Investments Commission, will generate income through success fees charged to developers if they get an agreed investment. It will be using tax and financial advisory consultancy BDO Australia to help review the suitability of developers.

Crowdfunding involves funding a project or venture by raising many small amounts of money from a large number of people.

During the past three years it has grown from a worldwide turnover of about $1 billion to projected market size of more than $2.5 billion, according to research provided by CrowdfundUP.

Ideally, it provides an alternative source of capital for developers to the banks. For sophisticated investors it cuts out advisers, fund managers and other consultants.

Mr Quigley said CrowdfundUP is targeting individual and self-managed super fund investors that would like to spread their capital across a range of projects to minimise risk if an individual project collapses.

"It should appeal to SMSF investors who cannot afford the high cost of an entire investment property but would like to still participate in the property market without having to invest," he added. "It is also a viable platform for developers who would like a low cost, diversified source of funding, faster turnaround times, and increased marketing and branding exposure."
direction: bearish for traditional property developers and banks

headline: Mirvac eyes Google for lease
subhead: Commercial Campaign launched for $200m Australian Technology Park
intro: Mirvac Group is gunning hard to take home the prized $200 million-plus Australian Technology Park in inner Sydney.
text:
Mirvac Group is gunning hard to take home the prized $200 million-plus Australian Technology Park in inner Sydney.

Behind the push is Mirvac's determination to land a major tenant, such as Google or Commonwealth Bank of Australia, for the site, industry sources said.

Home to leading Australian and global technology, communications and science companies, the ATP stretches across 14 hectares on the CBD's fringe in Eveleigh. The site was put up for sale late last year as part of the state government's multi-million property divestment plan and includes four existing office buildings, vacant land, commercial spaces and conference facilities.

UrbanGrowth launched the Australian Technology Park campaign on December 4 with the expressions of interest campaign aimed at determining a fair price. However, it is believed Mirvac had approached the state government to discuss its potential prior to it being put on the market.

Mirvac declined to comment when contacted. Knight Frank's Richard Horne, James Parry and Neil Brookes, who are marketing the 100 per cent-freehold interest in ATP, also declined to comment.

First-round expressions of interest close January 30.

One of the key attractions is the three development plots that allow commercial and mixed use buildings up to 11 storeys. The total potential gross floor area is 102,450 square metres.

The ATP has drawn the interest of a number of other parties. However, Mirvac is believed to be a frontrunner. But the eventual purchaser would likely want to have the process wrapped up quickly to negotiate with tenants.

Google and CBA are currently some of the most sought-after companies in the Sydney office market. Both are reviewing their leasing situations. CBA, currently located at Homebush, is looking for about 100,000 square metres of campus accommodation in the suburbs.

Another challenger to house the big bank is the new Parramatta Square project. A Charter Hall Group and Leighton Properties joint venture and Walker Corporation are the final two contenders to build the two office towers and an apartment complex at the Parramatta site. Final bids are due in February. The office towers will comprise 100,000 square metres and has long been considered one of the most logical places for CBA to relocate to, should it choose to move.

Meanwhile, Google is also in the market for 45,000 to 60,000 square metres of office space. The Australian Technology Park has long been on the company's shortlist, given the lack of available space in the CBD.
direction: bullish for Mirvac shares

headline: Federation board chooses manager
intro: Peter Hay, the chairman of Federation Centres, knew the surprise replacement of chief executive Steven Sewell would bring an adverse reaction from investors and analysts.
text:
Peter Hay, the chairman of Federation Centres, knew the surprise replacement of chief executive Steven Sewell would bring an adverse reaction from investors and analysts.

He knows investors and analysts rate Sewell far more highly than they rate the new chief executive, Angus McNaughton.

But he, and the board, think McNaughton, not Sewell, is the executive to lead the second biggest shopping centre operation in the country and deliver the benefits promised in the merger with the Novion Property Group.

"All 10 directors eventually came to the same conclusion," he says.

"Big decisions need unanimity."

Which includes the Gandel Group's two board members agree.

Hay, is a former chief executive of Freehills, and current chairman of Newcrest Mining, who stepped up from being a director of Novion Property Group to become chairman of the merged Federation/Novion in May.

McNaughton is an experienced property executive who rose to head Colonial First State's property operation in 2011, managed the complex exit of the Commonwealth Bank, and then headed Novion through to its merger with Federation.

His skills, which Hay would have noted, are in management behind the scenes.

Sewell is a high profile executive who started in property with QIC, became a fund manager at Macquarie Group, moved to Charter Hall as chief executive of Charter Hall Retail REIT and in February 2012 took over the recapitalised Centro and turned it into Federation.

All the while, and particularly in the merger with Novion, he demonstrated both vision and a fine grasp of the sector, from the nuances of the mall floor, to the complexities of its capital needs.

The fine grasp seems to have eluded Sewell in his relations with the new board.

And the board has chosen the manager over the maverick.
direction: short-term bearish for Federation Centres shares

headline: ASX is looking attractive for anyone who doesn't live here
intro: Local fund managers might be struggling to find any value in the sharemarket as the bull market approaches its third anniversary, but offshore investors don't seem too worried.
text:
Local fund managers might be struggling to find any value in the sharemarket as the bull market approaches its third anniversary, but offshore investors don't seem too worried.

The major S&P/ASX 200 Index is up almost 45 per cent since June 2012 and some brokers think this budget has probably done enough to attract more buying from international investors.

After weeks of trading under the influence of rising bond yields, shares had a solid day on Wednesday, with some stocks doing particularly well as investors placed bets that the federal budget would help their businesses.

But, beyond the day-to-day movements, there is a consensus that not many stocks are attractively priced. In addition, some local investors are bemused that Treasurer Joe Hockey is urging Australians to get out there and have a go when interest rates are at record lows and the rate cycle is closer to the end than the beginning.

Still, if the budget means anything for the Reserve Bank of Australia, the central bank might have some more work to do on interest rates.

Credit Suisse notes the 2016 budget is a modest drag on economic growth and suspects the RBA will have to do more.

The cash rate is already at a record low 2 per cent. However, with official interest rates still high by international standards and the Australian dollar now changing hands at about US79¢, the local sharemarket is still looking attractive for anyone who doesn't live here.

Credit Suisse is also telling clients that in a slow growth environment Australia is still a very favourable destination for global capital looking for any sort of income. The budget "fails to provide support to the profits growth outlook", Credit Suisse equity analyst Hasan Tevfik says, "but it does support Australia's status as a sound destination for international capital".

Any gains in the sharemarket will probably come from an expansion in the forward price-earnings ratio, the broker says, but given that income and dividends are still very much in vogue, offshore buyers will continue to wade into the local market. The major index is up 64 per cent, including dividends, since June 2012.

In keeping with the strategy, Tevfik thinks the recent spike up in bond yields will be contained and the only risk to the strategy is if there is a change in sentiment and investors want to chase growth stocks.

That's considered unlikely at this stage, while global investors will also be emboldened by credit rating agencies giving their nod of approval to Australia following the budget, despite all the talk that we were in danger of losing it.

Australia is still one of only nine countries that has an AAA credit rating. At an annual conference in Sydney on Wednesday, Fitch Ratings said that although higher than expected spending forecasts in the budget were a disappointment, the nation's rating was safe for now.

Of course, earnings and interest rates are the two major drivers of share price moves and the aim of the federal budget is to lift economic activity - so hopefully that will flow through to the sharemarket. What is still unclear for investors is the Senate response to the budget measures. But that didn't stop some stocks from doing well after some of the announcements.

Wesfarmers was up 1.5 per cent as Officeworks was tipped to do well from the small-business tax cut and depreciation measures that would also be good for hardware stores.

CommSec chief economist Craig James notes that changes to the cost of childcare and pensions won't happen until 2016 and 2017 respectively, and that both changes need Senate approval.

Nevertheless, some companies saw their share price rise on Wednesday in anticipation. These included: G8 Education, which ended the day flat after posting some decent early gains; Affinity Education Group, up 2 per cent; Japara Healthcare, up 3 per cent; and Estia Health, also flat on the day.
direction: ASX 200 and rate-sensitive sectors supported by offshore inflows and low rates

headline: Great Scot! Cairns approached to join Woolworths board
intro: Origin Energy chairman Gordon Cairns has been sounded out for a spot on the board of the ailing Woolworths and, with his no-nonsense ways, is a potential breath of fresh air.
text:
Origin Energy chairman Gordon Cairns has been sounded out for a spot on the board of the ailing Woolworths and, with his no-nonsense ways, is a potential breath of fresh air.

The Scottish-born Cairns ran the Lion Nathan beer business in Australia for seven years until 2004 and was lauded for his short stint as chairman of department store David Jones, where shareholder value was at the forefront of all decision-making.

The approach by head-hunters is thought to be initially for one of the spots as a non-executive director at the supermarket and liquor retailer, but should it be accepted Cairns may find himself in the frame as a potential future chairman to take over from Ralph Waters.

There is talk that Waters may depart sooner rather than later, but the clamour in fund manager land for some decisive action at the ailing Woolies is getting louder.

Cairns is also a director at Macquarie Group, and this is a spot where he's been the subject of speculation about being a future chairman.

It's nice to be wanted.

A major overhaul at Woolworths, which may involve some serious corrective surgery at the loss-making Masters Home Improvement chain, is one of the biggest turnaround opportunities going around in corporate Australia.

Those who live by the adage "timing is everything" may feel like jumping in now to help restore some of the glory of the crestfallen folks at Woolworths' Bella Vista headquarters in north-western Sydney.

Cairns made his name in corporate Australia among the beer vats in an industry where duopoly is a given.

But he's also not short of some fast-moving consumer goods retailing experience through his other gig as chairman of Quick Service Restaurants, owned by private equity firm Archer Capital.

Archer bought the business from Quadrant in 2011.

The public knows the joint better by its brand names in fast food of Red Rooster, Oporto and Chicken Treat, with the corporate entity run by chief executive Rob Coombe.

Cairns also sits on Archer's broader advisory board. Archer is one of the private equity firms thought to be taking an interest in Myer, as its new chief executive Richard Umbers tries to revive the Melbourne-based chain in the full glare of the public markets.

So too is Melbourne billionaire Solomon Lew, whose winter sun tan is said to be quite something.
direction: Woolworths shares likely to rise on turnaround expectations

headline: Use of cloud gains ground
intro: Of all the forces causing digital disruption in business, one of the most significant is cloud computing.
text:
Of all the forces causing digital disruption in business, one of the most significant is cloud computing.

Initially seen as a way for large organisations to re-engineer internal IT infrastructures, cloud computing has rapidly evolved to become a vital resource for companies of all sizes.

Cloud offerings vary from large public platforms, such as those operated by Amazon and Microsoft, to on-demand services and resources from the likes of Salesforce.com and NetSuite.

The ever-increasing range of cloud providers means companies can select those that best fit their particular requirements.

Some opt to shift internal IT resources to an external cloud, while others take advantage of services on an as-required basis.

Gerd Schenkel, executive director, Telstra Digital, says cloud computing is not something companies need. Rather, they need the benefits that cloud can deliver.

"If you go to Bunnings to buy a drill bit, it's not a drill bit you need - it's a hole," he says. "Cloud is kind of the same. It's not cloud you need, it's the things associated with that model that are attractive and cloud is just one way of bringing them to life."

Schenkel says Telstra has embraced the cloud, using it internally as well as offering a portfolio of products and services to clients.

"When we migrated our digital infrastructure into the cloud, we saw immediate benefits for stability and cost of change - all those things you would expect," he says.

Adam Bennett, executive general manager for digital and direct banking at NAB, says cloud ticks boxes for businesses in a range of different ways. Resources can be scaled up and down as demands change, with an organisation only paying for what is actually used.

"I think it is important not to pursue cloud for the sake of it, but to pursue cloud based on the kinds of solutions that you want to put in place, that give you the outcomes you are looking for," he says.

Guy Holland, partner in charge, digital consulting, at KPMG, says the phrase "cloud computing" was really hot two years ago, but organisations now tend to focus more on the solutions and capabilities that it can offer.

"Organisations are very comfortable these days with the public cloud model for certain services," he says. "Lots of vendors have made some big investments in indigenous capability in Australia to service that demand. The model of delivery, how the service is delivered, is pretty mature these days."

Holland says he expects the cloud space to continue to evolve and for companies to continue to make it an important part of their overall IT infrastructures.
direction: bullish for cloud computing and IT infrastructure providers

headline: Construction boom far to run, says CSR
intro: CSR managing director Rob Sindel said it would take interest rate rises of 2 to 3 per cent to dampen Australia's home-building frenzy and there is no sign of a downturn in construction. The building products group reported a 32 per cent jump in underlying profit to $92.4 million, its seventh consecutive year of earnings growth, and its best margins in a decade.
text:
CSR managing director Rob Sindel said it would take interest rate rises of 2 to 3 per cent to dampen Australia's home-building frenzy and there is no sign of a downturn in construction. The building products group reported a 32 per cent jump in underlying profit to $92.4 million, its seventh consecutive year of earnings growth, and its best margins in a decade.

Companies p17

News Retail growth p6

Property WA worries Singapore p43

Chanticleer back page
direction: bullish for CSR and construction sector

headline: Banks to face Finance department for $4b ARTC role
intro: The federal government has started taking face-to-face pitches for the Australian Rail Track Corporation's scoping study, which will weigh options for a potential $4 billion-plus sale.
text:
The federal government has started taking face-to-face pitches for the Australian Rail Track Corporation's scoping study, which will weigh options for a potential $4 billion-plus sale.

All of the heavyweight banks and advisory firms are lining up to pitch for the lucrative role. If they miss out on the scoping study, there is still a chance of nabbing a joint lead manager role should ARTC be destined for the ASX boards.

ARTC would be the biggest government float since the $5 billion Medibank initial public offering.

Those banks that worked on Aurizon's 2010 float - Bank of America Merrill Lynch, Credit Suisse, Goldman Sachs and UBS - are expected to pitch, along with fellow bulge-bracket shops Morgan Stanley, Macquarie Capital and JPMorgan. Corporate advisory firms Rothschild, Greenhill and Luminis are also viewed as strong contenders.

The study was expected to be delivered by June 2016, although the department warned the timetable could be extended.

Elsewhere, sources said domestic orders for Costa Group's IPO had increased this week with the fruit and vegetable grower likely to price off the bottom of the range at around 15.5 times earnings. The institutional bookbuild will kick off on Tuesday with the shares to list next Friday.

Also in floats, mobile reseller Amaysim closed 5.5 per cent higher on its first day of trade. Amaysim joint lead manager Goldman Sachs handled 30.5 per cent of Wednesday's turnover, followed by fellow JLM Macquarie, which had a 19.8 per share, and Morgan Stanley at 16 per cent.

Finally, litigation funder JustKapital is expected to emerge from a trading halt this week and announce a new joint venture partner.

The company told investors earlier this week it had close to a $1 billion of cases under consideration.
direction: bullish for investment banks and advisory firms

headline: Investment grows despite threat
intro: The property investment spike which prompted the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority to flag macroprudential measures late last year has been propelled even further by the promise of ongoing cheap credit.
text:
The property investment spike which prompted the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority to flag macroprudential measures late last year has been propelled even further by the promise of ongoing cheap credit.

If investor volumes and values remain steady, the banks will still be within APRA's target of a 10 per cent cap on housing credit growth. But the February interest rate cut, and the potential for more cuts in coming months, may further stimulate the investor market and push the regulator to putting a hard word on major banks.

Late last year, APRA said macroprudential measures would be necessary if investor lending continued to inflate property prices. Their words were seen as a "speed limit" to the banks, a caution to moderate lending.

The value of investor loans was up about 18 per cent in the year to December 2014, according to the ABS home lending figures released on Tuesday. The value of loans to owner-occupiers was up more than 10 per cent for the year and loan numbers were up 2.7 per cent. Month-to-month, investors have been borrowing about $12.6 billion, compared to $12.3 billion by owner-occupiers, excluding refinancing.

The property market has ramped up for the year, following the quieter January period. There will be more than 500 properties auctioned both in Melbourne and Sydney on Saturday. In the first two weekends of February, auction volumes were much lower at 200-300. Sydney's auction clearance rates were strong but Melbourne was the softest in the past two years.

CBA analysts said consistent high lending could encourage more controls to be introduced, noting the RBA seemed ready to cut rates again given inflation risks were benign. The bank has predicted another rate cut in May.

CBA noted in its results for the half-year to December 2015, released on Wednesday, that investors were chasing solid yields through property investment. However, ongoing rises in property value mean yields have compressed- particularly in Sydney.

February's RBA statement, presented after interest rates were cut to a new record-low, noted the reserve was working with regulators to minimise risks that could flow from the housing market into the broader economy.

Analysts from JP Morgan and CoreLogic have said property investment has risen since APRA first mooted macroprudential tightening - possibly wanting to take advantage of existing lending conditions. CoreLogic RP Data senior research analyst Cameron Kusher wrote that credit growth levels were still on the regulator's watchlist.

"What's not in doubt is that the ball is now firmly in APRA's court and we are closely watching what happens next."
direction: regulatory pressure on banks and property sector

headline: AGL Energy defends 2050 coal exit date
intro: AGL Energy chief executive Andy Vesey has given green groups little to cheer about, defending the 2050 date for an exit from coal-fired power generation, and emphasising the importance to AGL customers of the controversial Gloucester coal seam gas project in NSW.
text:
AGL Energy chief executive Andy Vesey has given green groups little to cheer about, defending the 2050 date for an exit from coal-fired power generation, and emphasising the importance to AGL customers of the controversial Gloucester coal seam gas project in NSW.

AGL is facing a shareholder resolution at next month's AGM aiming to bring forward the date for the company to switch away from coal-fired power, but Mr Vesey said any earlier move away from coal-fired power risked "significant dislocations" that would increase power prices for consumers and cost jobs.

"You can do it bigger and quicker but then you risk significant dislocations," he said. "I think you risk increased prices to the consumer, I think you risk job losses, I think you risk the issues of competitiveness of certain Australian industries."

Mr Vesey signalled that just because he had met opponents of AGL's controversial Gloucester coal seam gas project in NSW, AGL shouldn't be seen as stepping back from its commitment to the venture, which is heading toward a final investment decision in 2016.

"I want to hear all voices because it can only make it a better informed decision," he said.

"We're not going to make it because of ego we're not going to make it because we think we're right, we're going to make it for the benefit of our customers and shareholders and it's going to be a business decision," he said, adding that community voices both for and against the project would feed into that process.

The comments came as AGL reported a slightly stronger than anticipated full-year profit, mostly thanks to the $1.5 billion acquisition last year of huge NSW coal-fired power producer Macquarie Generation under previous CEO Michael Fraser.

Core profit before one-time charges rose 12 per cent to $630 million, within AGL's guidance but beating consensus of $620 million. Bottom-line earnings sank 62 per cent to $218 million, dragged down by write-downs in the gas production business and charges for the MacGen deal, which AGL had already flagged.

AGL shares dipped 1.1 per cent to $16.41, modestly outperforming the broader index.

The final dividend of 34¢ also outdid the consensus of 33¢ and was 1¢ higher than a year ago. The higher payout amid increased cash flows and lower spending on growth led some analysts to question when AGL may consider capital management to further lift returns to shareholders.

"All eyes now are fixed on next month's AGM with expectations mounting for a change in dividend policy and/or capital management - pending the sale of Macarthur Wind Farm - alongside FY16 earnings guidance," JPMorgan analyst Christopher Laybutt said.

But Mr Vesey said on a conference call that AGL wanted to show "consistent performance" before any decision was made on increased dividends or buybacks.

"It's a good problem to have as we generate more strength and more cash," he said.

Mr Vesey said AGL's planned sale of its 50 per cent stake in the Macarthur wind farm, raising about $500 million, should occur by December, while the company was also making good progress on its target of $200 million cash savings by mid-2017.
direction: AGL shares face pressure from activist shareholder resolution and ESG concerns

headline: Resource stocks boost ASX but not to 6000
intro: The Australian sharemarket edged higher on Tuesday but remained stubbornly stuck just below the 6000-point barrier in a mixed day for the banks that contained some gains among miners and energy stocks.
text:
The Australian sharemarket edged higher on Tuesday but remained stubbornly stuck just below the 6000-point barrier in a mixed day for the banks that contained some gains among miners and energy stocks.

The benchmark S&P/ASX 200 rose 12.9 points, or 0.2 per cent, coming to a rest at 5969.10. The broader All Ordinaries index picked up 13.4 points, or 0.2 per cent, to close at 5934.5.

"The market is at fair value, so long as the following preconditions are met: the cash rate heads even lower and the Australian dollar doesn't rally from its current levels," George Boubouras, chief investment officer at Contango Fund Management, said.

Mr Boubouras said the political climate meant the market could not hold out for broad economic reforms.

"A lower Aussie dollar represents the lazy way for corporate Australia to repair their balance sheets. But, at this stage, we need all the help we can get because the Reserve Bank is doing a lot itself," he said.

Henry Jennings, a private client adviser at BBY, said the market would continue to rise before a volatile winter in which investors eyed a US Federal Reserve rate rise while the northern summer reduced trading volumes.

"There's so much money looking for a home," he said. "Everyone's hoping they can be smart enough that they can exit the party just before the police."

Mr Jennings said that, with quantitative easing programs in Japan and Europe, "the bubble continues to inflate in terms of equity prices and other commodity prices, houses mainly".

"If you took out the whole central bank action, the fundamentals certainly wouldn't be supporting where we are now."

The Australian dollar had strengthened to US79.03¢, its highest level since February 26, but in late afternoon trade had fallen to US78.62¢

The release of weak Chinese manufacturing data took some of the wind from both the sharemarket's and the Australian dollar's sails. The purchasing managers' index fell to an 11-month low in February, from 50.7 to 49.2.

Among the miners, BHP Billiton and Rio Tinto again went in opposite directions. BHP picked up 0.7 per cent to $31.22 while Rio sagged 1.4 per cent to $57.37.

Iron ore, for immediate delivery to the port of Qingdao, continued to wallow, fetching $US54.82. Fortescue Metals Group, which had its stock downgraded to "underweight" status by Morgan Stanley with a price target of $1.65, perked up by 1.5 per cent to reach $2.01. Arrium was the biggest winner among the top 200 stocks, surging 20 per cent to 21¢ in high volume, but without any apparent reason.

Woodside Petroleum declined 0.3 per cent to $35.41 after the company announced it would cut 300 jobs and freeze workers' pay to combat the heavy decline in oil prices.

Outdoor clothing and equipment company Kathmandu was the day's biggest loser, sinking 12.4 per cent to $1.37. The company had reported a 2 per cent fall in same-store sales in the first seven weeks of the new half.

Spotless was a major success story, picking up a hefty gain of 7.2 per cent to a record closing high of $2.36 after private equity firm Pacific Equity Partners sold $240 million worth of stock in the cleaning and contracting caterer.

Internet provider TPG rose 3.5 per cent to $9.14 after founder David Teoh announced a set of stellar half-year results, including a 59 per cent revenue rise and a profit estimate revised up by 5 per cent.

The major banks were mixed. The Commonwealth Bank of Australia slid 0.25 per cent to $95.24 and National Australia Bank slipped further, 0.7 per cent to $38.31. Meanwhile, ANZ climbed 0.25 per cent to $36.90 and Westpac lifted 0.2 per cent to $39.64.

Wesfarmers, owner of Coles, rose 0.5 per cent to $44.22 and Woolworths gained 0.3 per cent to $29.05. Telstra ticked up 0.5 per cent to $6.38.
direction: cautious near-term consolidation with downside risk

headline: Crescent Capital flips Pentair's industrial portfolio
intro: Private equity group Crescent Capital Partners has moved to divest the industrial property holdings of its recently purchased Pentair water transport business, and some potential bidders expect the assets could sell for up to $100 million.
text:
Private equity group Crescent Capital Partners has moved to divest the industrial property holdings of its recently purchased Pentair water transport business, and some potential bidders expect the assets could sell for up to $100 million.

The plan is to sell and lease back the properties in a textbook private equity move being frequently being played out in the market because of cheap capital and a growing appetite for well-leased, institutional-grade property.

Crescent Capital, which raised about $650 million last year, scooped up the Australian and Asian water transport division of Pentair in January.

Deutsche Bank was appointed by Pentair to find a trade or private equity buyer for the business last September. Crescent Capital purchased the business for a price believed to be $100 million.

Now with dozens of hungry institutional and private property investors chasing down industrial assets, Crescent Capital have pressed the button on a sale and leaseback program for the Pentair business's four major industrial properties across Australia.

They include assets in Queensland at Carole Park and Currumbin, one asset in Victoria at Somerton and another in Western Australia at Kwinana.

A number of sale and leasebacks have occurred in the past 18 months, including Green's General Foods, part-owned by private equity group CVC. Other major industrial companies including PMP, Amcor and Arrium have also undertaken sale and leasebacks.

Industrial property fund managers, such as Charter Hall, Propertylink, 360 Capital, Fife Capital and APN Property Group, are all likely to take a look at such assets, which include leases of up to 10 years.

Offshore groups such as Ascendas are also considering a major push into Australia's industrial property.

In February Cache Logistics Trust snapped up the McPhee logistics portfolio in a $70 million play marking its entry into the market. Some potential buyers have said Knight Frank's Tim Armstrong and Greg Russell had been appointed to sell the assets.

Both agents were unavailable for comment and as yet no marketing material has been distributed.

Several large industrial portfolios have hit the market or are about to.

The Government of Singapore Investment Corporation is looking to offer a $900 million Australian industrial and logistics property portfolio.
direction: bullish for industrial REITs and property trusts

headline: Security risks no judge of Chinese investment
intro: We should be boosting trade rather than demanding cuts with particular partners.
text:
We should be boosting trade rather than demanding cuts with particular partners.

Boosting trade and investment should be a core objective after Treasury downgraded its growth forecasts. Yet increasingly vocal defence commentators won't be satisfied until the contribution of China's trade and investment to Australian growth is drastically cut. Their campaign challenges Malcolm Turnbull's calm approach to foreign investment and national security.

The more alarmist critics ignore basic facts. A widely quoted Australian National University analyst Geoff Wade said on the ABC's website last Tuesday that the lease of two wharfs in Darwin to the Chinese firm Landbridge will provide Chinese naval vessels with "facilitated access to Australia". Wade did not mention that no foreign warship can visit an Australian port without government permission.

Wade further claimed "Chinese control of the port of Darwin marks just the beginning - the so-called 'dragonhead' - of China's economic domination of northern Australia". Wade said the China Australia Free Trade Agreement is the "merciless facilitator" of China's "economic primacy and control" in Australia.

Never mind that this merciless facilitator's champion, the Trade Minister Andrew Robb, makes a cogent case for welcoming Chinese investment to help reverse the decline in national income. Nor does the lease does give China control of Darwin's port.

The Defence department head, Dennis Richardson, a former head of the Australian Security Intelligence Organisation and the Foreign Affairs and Trade department, has no problem with the lease and describes claims it will facilitate spying as an "amateur hour" suggestion. Like many companies in China and the West, Landbridge has political and military connections. This has not altered Richardson's assessment.

A former navy officer, Sam Bateman, now an academic specialist in maritime security, says the lease doesn't include major parts of the port, such as the Darwin naval base.

Writing for the online Strategist, Bateman says: "The port leasing deal is a good one that will ensure the development of a major international port [that is] desperately needed." He says ports are subject to many vulnerabilities, but foreign control isn't one of them. Turnbull says the law allows the government to take control of infrastructure deemed necessary for Defence.

Existing Chinese ownership of Australian electricity assets has not created obvious problems. But several commentators, including Wade, were deeply worried the NSW government would lease its TransGrid high voltage network to Macquarie Corp's joint venture with the Chinese firm State Grid. One fear was that State Grid could sabotage the network - and Macquarie and State Grid's profits. But another consortium bid more for TransGrid.

These commentators are now unconcerned by Middle Eastern ownership of 40 per cent of the new TransGrid consortium.

The Australian Strategic Policy Institute's head, Peter Jenkins, wants far tougher screening of Chinese investment to prevent what he sees as a wide range of potential security risks. But trying to eliminate every extreme possibility could badly hurt beneficial investment. Defence is satisfied that conditions on foreign investors can mitigate many risks - after Singtel took over Optus only Australian nationals are allowed to handle phone intercepts for ASIO and the police.

Chinese investment in agricultural land is could potentially be banned by tight screening because a communications tower, underground cable, high voltage power line, military base etc is in the general vicinity.

But a Chinese tourist could video a "target", so should they all be denied visas? Meanwhile, homegrown terrorists could blow up a communications tower and so on.

The government's recent decision to block Chinese firms buying the Kidman property empire might be a rarely justifiable move - part of one property is in the Woomera Prohibited Area where advanced weapons can be tested. But intelligence gathering is often independent of asset ownership.

The US bans Chinese ownership of any of its telecommunications system, but this doesn't stop Chinese hackers accessing unprotected US computers.
direction: bearish for Chinese-linked assets and property trusts

headline: 'Offensively Australian'
intro: We once cut tall poppies, now we're cutting the cultural cringe down to size, writes Andrew Clark.
text:
We once cut tall poppies, now we're cutting the cultural cringe down to size, writes Andrew Clark.

Barry Humphries once likened living in ­Australia to "going to a party and dancing all night with your mother". American singer Bob Dylan, a sort of global muse of 1960s counterculture, said after a late '70s tour of Australia that it was like being in a room where the window was shut.

Now, according to Australian novelist, playwright, poet, librettist and critic, David Malouf, "we are much more happy in our skin". Novelist and non-fiction writer Anna Funder says, "If there is one element in ­Australia's attitude it's confidence. Mixing with Australian writers and film people, they are so direct and confident."

So how did we move over the last 30 years from cringe to self-assured? Factors abound, from the breakdown of the White Australia policy to the opening up of the domestic economy, liberating Australia from refracting every issue through a narrow Anglo­centric prism. Tertiary education became a major export industry and China emerged as our most important trading partner.

As confidence grew, so, too, did cultural tourism and a feast of new food styles, cafes, and boutique bars; plus a rising Jumbo Generation enjoying the international connectivity of globalisation and the internet.

All have played their part, but the most significant – and elusive – factor is that the question, "What is an Australian?" no longer carries the same troublesome baggage.

Sixty-five years ago, Melbourne schoolteacher and literary critic A. A. Phillips coined the term "cultural cringe". He yearned for a national culture that conceded no inferiority to Britain, and was unembarrassed to be Australian.

"Temper, democratic; bias, offensively Australian," as Phillips's hero, Joseph Furphy – author of the novel Such is Life – put it nearly half a century earlier.Contradiction in terms

At about the time Phillips coined the term, Australia was a contradiction. The cultural cringe was dominant and many budding creative artists fled the country for Europe or the United States. Paradoxically, the same period generated remarkable works of art and literature. During the 1940s, painters William Dobell, Russell Drysdale and Sidney Nolan produced their great works of The Billy Boy, The Drover's Wife and Ned Kelly. Four years after Phillips coined his "cringe" term, Patrick White wrote The Tree of Man, a towering novel about an Australian farming family.

David Malouf tells AFR Weekend that what it meant to be an Australian "was a big question for us" – that is, members of the older generation. "[But] I don't think that idea of national identity is as important as it once was. We passed out of that period in the '80s and '90s."

Author of such novels as Remembering Babylon, Malouf believes the Jumbo Generation is a significant factor in this change. He points to the huge numbers of young, educated Europeans and Asians working in Australia for up to two years on temporary visas while huge numbers of Australians live and work overseas. They move "around the world without asking whether they are Australians or not", he says.

However, some of the legacy of a tougher past remains. Old Australian values, such as mateship and egalitarianism, "were established by adversity", Malouf says. This was a period when Henry Lawson wrote short ­stories like The Drover's Wife, about survival in a harsh bush environment.

"We have now had a period of affluence of two generations. We don't know what that adversity was," Malouf says. But Australians "hold onto" those old values. Even people who come here and who have no concept of Australia's past recognise and take up ­values of mateship and egalitarianism, the Sydney-based author says.Mates and multiculturalism

At their best, Australians have grafted the better cultural values of their past into a more confident, plural present. "Multi­cultural" is a clunky word but we live in a society where to be Australian can mean many things – a transgender Asian Australian working in IT, a Brazilian-born barista living in Brisbane, a fifth-generation brumby wrangler in Cooma, NSW, a redundant car worker in Adelaide.

They are linked through core values such as fairness, which may not be always – or even often – adhered to, but remain social benchmarks. "Mate", a common term of greeting among Australian men in the bush, the worksite, or the battlefield, has morphed into a ubiquitous call sign, from one Australian to another. Now you are just as likely to hear "mate" from a university professor as from a grader driver on a country road or even, as this columnist recently experienced, from a female receptionist at a high-fee-charging Sydney dental surgery: "Make sure you have something to eat before you come mate. See ya mate. Bye, bye."

Reflecting the new looseness of being Australian, Benjamin Law, a 32-year-old Chinese Australian writer, says the changing nature of national identity is not something he's "thought that much about lately". He quickly goes on to mention that he's ­currently writing a TV script for Matchbox Pictures where 90 per cent of the cast are Asian Australians. His six-part series, titled Family Law and based on a novel he has ­published, focuses on a 14-year-old Asian Australian boy called, not that surprisingly, Benjamin, whose parents are divorcing. "It's a very Australian story which just happens to be Asian Australian," he says.

Law contrasts his project with much of the common visual symbolism of "Australianness" which, he says, generates "a sense of Australia that hasn't been seen in Australia for many years". The first sign of popular ­culture recognising many more different faces in the crowd was the arrival of the Kim family on the long-running TV series Neighbours in the 1990s. They were, pretty much stereotypically, accused by their white Australian neighbours of eating their dog.

"White Australia is grappling with their Asian Australian neighbours," Law says. At the same time he says Asian Australians are becoming attracted to a more traditionally Australian work-life balance. As an ­Australian, or an Asian Australian, you should "maintain a balance between leisure and work. You work to make your leisure time. You make sure the work you do is the work you like and its lays the foundation for the life you want."

Recently arrived Asians conditioned to kowtow to those above are also learning a "healthy disdain for authority or government, or at least an interrogation of what politicians offer you", Law says.Easy, breezy, beautiful

That breezy confidence is moving north, driven by an emerging attraction among affluent Asians for Australian style. French high-end fashion, jewellery, knick-knacks and bubbly empire, LVMH, owns, through its Singapore private equity fund L Capital Asia, a 49.9 per cent stake in bush outfitter RM Williams, 40 per cent of performance sportswear company 2XU, gourmet food chain Jones the Grocer, and 70 per cent of swimwear company Seafolly.

Indian-born Ravi Thakran who heads LVMH's south-east Asian and Middle East operations, is keen to push these Australian brands throughout Asia and the rest of the world. "Australianness is a unique phenomenon," he told AFR Weekend late last year. "It's hip and cool, it's very trendy, it's free and easy. The spirit of Australia is very different."
