This view of a "different" Australian spirit is shared by Australian writer Anna Funder, who has been living in Brooklyn, New York, with her husband and three children for the past three years, and is now published in 20 languages. Funder, author of Stasiland and All That I Am says she feels "a bit cringy" when asked about the current state of Australian nationalism.

This is because she wishes it was irrelevant. "I live in America [and] I imagine I can see Australia more clearly [compared with when she lived in Australia]. There's a phenomenal lot to admire. There is some vestige of a cringe in people as old as me," says Funder, who turns 48 this year.

However, "the facts don't support it. I can now feel in my writing about how important it is to be Australian, how Australians are open and curious and really well educated on the public purse."Cringe fades as talent grows

Contrasting with the self-imposed exile of Australian artists and writers such as Sidney Nolan, Arthur Boyd, Barry Humphries, Germaine Greer, Robert Hughes and Clive James in the 1960s, Funder points out that famous Australians who "could live anywhere – Cate Blanchett, Rachel Griffiths or Toni Collette – choose to live in Australia.

"Part of getting rid of the cringe is enthu­siasm for local talent because that enthusiasm gives the talent confidence. I reckon there are so many talented people inside and outside Australia," she says.

"I wanted to come [to New York] for a bit and it has been fantastic. There are a lot of wonderful writers and actors. [But] it's been a bit like a mirror and seeing the other side and it's not what you thought it would be."

The author is an admirer of the Australian social contract which dates back to the Federation era. She contrasts the American low wage approach with the 107-year-old Australian tradition of the basic wage, and makes a similar comparison between the skimpy healthcare for the US poor and ­Australia's Medicare system. She recalls the visit to one of her children's school by a homeless female American war veteran who had nothing, adding: "That's when you realise this place has a dead centre."

Australians not only enjoy a functioning civic society, but they can also take part in a flavoursome life. Funder acknowledges that "maybe there's an element of protesting too much [in the sense of going on and on] about coffee and lifestyle or food in Australia", but "the coffee in Australia is hands down the best in the world, cringe or no cringe".

While living in Australia, she "failed to see how politically, socially and economically successful" the country has been. That doesn't necessarily mean Australians are confident about the future, or even know what the future holds. We live in a period of butchery by Islamic State in Syria and Iraq, of the Lindt Cafe siege and killings in Sydney's Martin Place and the massacre of 12 journalists, cartoonists and police at the Charlie Hebdo satirical magazine's office in Paris. A dark pall of fear is gripping the West.

As Malouf puts it, "Like a lot of other people, Australians have no idea of where the country is going. They would have no more answer to that question than the French. Everything seems so fluid and out of control."Winners and losers

It's not just violent Muslim extremists causing insecurity. More than six years after the global financial crisis, much of the world remains in economic strife, while at home the end of the China-driven commodities boom means many of us will have to get used to living with less at a time when national politics has turned distinctly ordinary.

Australian author, playwright, comic sketch writer and speechwriter Don Watson finds the current scene confusing. "It's hard to say if Australians are more confident. Sometimes you think they don't know about anything and then they seem to be so uncanny. They seem to make sensible decisions; I don't think they're [necessarily] the right decisions but I can see the sense."

Watson, who hails from a Gippsland, Victoria, farming family, observes an optimism among those who "rise with the tide" but is also aware of "an underclass outside the supermarket". There are "bumpkins who went out and got a new vocation from the new vocation universities and made the mistake of thinking their salaries were an indication of their worth and education. There is a strata who feel very good about everything, and get into the 'right' occupations."

Ominous storm clouds on the economic front could strain incipient confidence. "If you're a reasonably competent tradie and live in a country town you're [one of the] new barons roaring around on their horses, which are Toyota Hiluxes; a sort of Tom Jones figure," he says.

But there are "a tremendous number of winners and losers. Paul Keating [one-time reforming Labor government treasurer and prime minister, whom Watson wrote speeches for] used to say, 'If you change the government you change the country.' That's not as true as saying if you change the economy you change the country," he adds.

The final word should come from novelist Peter Carey, who is also based in New York and is the winner of two prestigious Booker prizes. Asked to comment about Australia's identity, he shoots back the following email: "I have become totally sick of my own opinions. This change of heart may come as a surprise but I hope the silence is refreshing."

Once upon a time, Australia's celebrated authors complained about tall poppies. Now they cut themselves down to size. Happy Australia Day!
direction: bearish for rate-sensitive sectors

headline: Tax loss of $7m ‘was a good year’
intro: Morgan Stanley’s Australian investment bank has slashed its after-tax loss for 2014 by $30 million to just more than $7 million, after its advisory fee income rose by $26 million. The Australian arm of the investment bank also transferred its lossmaking trading desk to Hong Kong on January 1, 2014. A spokesman for the bank said 2014 was a good year for the investment bank, with combined income from mergers and acquisitions and real estate banking advice, as well as underwriting debt capital, rising 71 per cent to $63.6 million. Fee income in total rose 21.5 per cent to $82.7 million in the year ended December 31, from $68.1 million in the previous year. Morgan Stanley worked on several large deals in Australia during the year, including the $7.1 billion acquisition of Queensland Motorways by Transurban from QIC Ltd, plus its $2.3 billion equity raising to finance the deal. The bank also worked on the $1.75 billion sale of Port of Newcastle, APA Group’s $2 billion equity raising, Pacific Equity Partner’s sale of Peters Ice Cream and Westfield’s restructure and demerger. Some of its fee income also came from overseas deals. The parent company was the largest income source, contributing net income of $80.7 million, up from $66.7 million in the previous year. Total costs rose by 2.6 per cent to about $90 million. One of the biggest increases was in staff costs, which were up $16 million to $136.6 million, including $11.9 million for bonuses. Because of its loss, Morgan Stanley Australia got a tax payment of $2.4 million, compared to a tax gain of $15.8 million in 2013.

direction: Morgan Stanley shares likely to rise on improved profitability and strong fee income growth

headline: CBA, ANZ tipped to lead $28b in capital raisings
intro: As the banking regulator ratchets up the pressure on the big four banks to raise billions of dollars to make them more resilient, analysts are predicting Commonwealth Bank of Australia and ANZ Banking Group will be forced to strengthen their balance sheets to avoid slipping behind rivals.
text:
As the banking regulator ratchets up the pressure on the big four banks to raise billions of dollars to make them more resilient, analysts are predicting Commonwealth Bank of Australia and ANZ Banking Group will be forced to strengthen their balance sheets to avoid slipping behind rivals.

The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority said the big four banks would have to raise 2 percentage points of capital - around $28 billion - to make them "unquestionably strong", as recommended by the financial system inquiry led by David Murray.

The regulator was not specific about what the ultimate level of capital would be and how long the banks will have to raise it.

With APRA expected to instruct banks to increase the amount of capital they hold against mortgages in the coming weeks, one leading analyst said Commonwealth Bank, the nation's largest, may raise capital at its annual profit result on August 12.

CLSA analyst Brian Johnson said major banks changed their tune about capital adequacy over the past year, after APRA's stress test in December highlighted the risks to their home loans from an economic shock, and, more recently, flaws in underwriting standards.

"The balance of probabilities is I think CBA could raise capital at this result, because the messaging has changed," he said.

Higher levels of capital can depress returns on equity for bank shareholders but also make banks safer by creating a larger buffer in a crisis.

Each of the banks might need another $7 billion of capital, analysts said.

ANZ faces the biggest shortfall given it has the lowest capital ratio of the big banks.

The bank has flagged the potential sale of minority stakes in Asian banks to lift capital.

Chief financial officer Shayne Elliott said the bank had "a number of options for managing our capital including organic capital generation, balance sheet management and the disposal of non-core assets".

ANZ shares fell the hardest among the big four banks on Monday, losing 1.1 per cent.

The rest of the sector closed down in a lower market.

Credit Suisse analyst Jarrod Martin said a 200 basis point increase in capital was equal to an extra $28 billion for the big four banks, a target he expects would mainly be achieved through dividend reinvestment plans in coming years.

"Based on our expectation that there will be time to get to these levels, we are looking to see maybe one or two underwritten DRPs," he said.

"The only real candidate for an outright capital raising is ANZ if they are unsuccessful with asset sales."

At its half-year profit results in May, Westpac announced a $2 billion capital raising through its dividend reinvestment plan. NAB raised $5.5 billion, sending its common equity tier 1 capital ratio to a sector-leading 10 per cent.

In its first formal response to David Murray's financial system inquiry, which the federal government is expected to respond to this month, APRA said it "fully supports" the inquiry's call for Australia's banks to be unquestionably strong "as the benefits are clear for the financial system and for the Australian community".

In a win for the banks, APRA did not endorse Mr Murray's recommendation that banks should carry enough capital to put them in the top 25 per cent of international banks, only saying that relative measure would act as a useful cross-check.

"We strongly agree with the FSI proposition that Australian banks should be unquestionably strong - you don't get any disagreement on that from us," Mr Byres told Fairfax Media.

"But what we are not going to do is tie ourselves mechanically to a particular quartile by running these numbers every 12 months and re-calibrating the capital requirements just because other banks have moved in a relative sense."

Westpac, NAB and ANZ reassured investors in the wake of APRA's decision through stock exchange announcements. CBA, which is in a blackout period, did not comment.

Westpac Banking Corp chief financial officer Peter King said the bank was "pleased APRA has confirmed Australian banks are well capitalised and that the top quartile positioning is a useful 'sense check' rather than a regulatory benchmark".

Westpac reported it had 8.3 per cent tier one reserves on June 30.

"The release of the study is a further step in helping investors understand the relative position of the Australian bank capital ratios compared to international peers," he said.

NAB said having "a strong balance sheet has always been a priority", pointing to the increase in its reserves from 8.46 per cent to about 10 per cent on a proforma basis following completion of its $5.5 billion rights issue in May and other initiatives.

"As a result, NAB is well placed to respond to changes in regulatory capital requirements," the bank said.

APRA is expected to provide clarity soon about the extent to which mortgage "risk weights" will be lifted and when. Risk weights are financial models that determine the riskiness of loans and affect how much capital banks must set aside.

APRA said on Monday "at this point no decision has been made on the total magnitude of any strengthening of capital requirements nor when that strengthening would need to be completed by".

It added that any capital build would progress in an "orderly manner".

Australian Bankers' Association chief executive Steven Munchenberg said while making international comparisons is appropriate, "the ABA believes that bank capital levels need to be set in response to actual risk levels experienced by banks in Australia, within the internationally agreed Basel framework".

"The ABA agrees with APRA that no single definition of 'unquestionably strong' should be applied and rather that international comparisons should be used as an input alone," he said.
direction: bearish for CBA and ANZ shares

headline: Red card for FFA means a trip to the referees
intro: Australian football's governing body must dispel any doubts about how it has conducted business with a full, independent inquiry.
text:
Australian football's governing body must dispel any doubts about how it has conducted business with a full, independent inquiry.

Football Federation Australia has not covered itself in glory in relation to FIFA, president Sepp Blatter or Australia's bid for the World Cup.

Sports Minister Sussan Ley has said the government would not support another bid for the World Cup until FIFA has been reformed. She should have gone further.

The government should not support a bid by the federation until there has been a full, independent investigation of how it conducted the last bid and the results of that investigation have been disclosed publicly.

The FFA then needs to set out clearly that it has learnt from any mistakes made last time and describe in detail the robust processes that will be in place for any future bid, and how such bid will be implemented by people who understand integrity, compliance and anti-corruption.

This is quite separate to any inquiry that the federal police is making. That might indicate whether there are any laws that applied and were or were not broken. Even if the police say the federation has nothing to answer under our laws, that does not mean how they conducted the bid was right.

Where was the due diligence by the federation? Where were the safeguards that money paid for one purpose could not be used for other purposes? There have been suspicions and allegations about FIFA and about World Cup bids for years. The FFA was on notice.

Indeed, the FFA is not addressing the situation by stating continually it ran a "clean" bid, issuing an open letter admitting naivete or releasing selected documents. Only a full, independent investigation will remove the dark cloud surrounding it.

It is also not just a case that money ended up in the pocket of an apparently corrupt individual.

The federation needs to have transparent rules about charitable donations. A decent anti-corruption policy will address this.

Any competent compliance professional will know that a charitable donation can amount to a bribe. The American authorities have penalised many companies for just that.

Just because the recipient is a worthy cause does not make the payment "clean". If the payment is made in order to influence somebody inappropriately, then it is a bribe.

The FFA has not come out of the FIFA saga well on other fronts.

The federation has said that, while Australia's bid was clean, the whole Qatar story was definitely not.

And yet, up until the FBI made its recent arrests, the FFA seemed as if it was going to support Mr Blatter at the recent election. Unbelievable. The very man who oversaw what the federation says was not a clean process.

Why has the FFA not been campaigning for a change of leadership at FIFA for some years?

The conclusion must be that the FFA was not serious about addressing corruption in FIFA. It only changed when it realised its position would have been totally untenable to the Australian public.

In the words of Edmund Burke: "All that is necessary for the triumph of evil is that good men do nothing." The FFA should hang its head in shame that it did not actively campaign against Blatter.

I had to laugh when Mr Blatter said he could not be held responsible for the actions of a few individuals. It sounds like some bank CEOs recently complaining about rogue employees and not addressing the pervading culture in their organisations. The investigator appointed by FIFA itself resigned citing concerns about culture. The culture of an organisation is driven from the top. If there are problems with that culture, the people at the top need to do something. If the people at the top are the problem, then the shareholders (or in FIFA's case, the member associations) need to do something. The federation should have shown courage.

As I have stated in the past, no organisation should receive government contracts or government benefits, unless the organisation can show it has in place a compliance program that addresses integrity and anti-corruption, and includes an effective whistleblowing mechanism. This should apply to the FFA.

A few hasty statements and disclosures by the FFA will not address the issues surrounding it. It needs to, and can, do much more. Only then should the government consider supporting any further bids by the FFA.

Neville Tiffen is a specialist consultant in corporate governance, integrity and compliance. He is a member of the OECD secretary-general's high-level advisory group on integrity and anti-corruption, and the former global head of compliance at Rio Tinto.
direction: negative for FFA-linked equities and sports betting stocks

headline: First step on the ladder
intro: Mitchell Watson bought his first property within months of completing school and well before obtaining his driver's licence.
text:
Mitchell Watson bought his first property within months of completing school and well before obtaining his driver's licence.

Watson's generation is more switched on than its predecessors about the importance of getting into the market before being priced out.

"The Queensland property market was booming," he says about his purchase of a three-bedroom house about 10 kilometres north of Brisbane's central business district.

"I knew I needed to get into the property market," he says of his decision, which was supported financially by his parents.

Since he bought the Stafford Heights house 10 years ago, property prices in the area have nearly doubled, from $240,000 to $450,000.

Mitchell, who is a research manager for a financial services company, continued living with his parents before moving into a rental apartment closer to Brisbane.

"I put buying a house before a car," says Mitchell, who turned 30 recently.

"I'm definitely thinking of getting another house. But this time it will be my house."

Property investors are becoming younger and are more likely to own several rental properties.

The number of investors with more than five properties has increased by 35 per cent in the past 12 months, from 175,000 to 272,000, research by Digital Financial Analytics shows.

For the first time since records began, more first-time buyers are expected to be investors rather than owner-occupiers by the end of 2015, heralding a significant change in the nation's home-owning culture, the research reveals.

"I'm definitely going to keep my first property and use it as security to buy the next," Mitchell says. "It has been fruitful."

Renovations to the house included a new kitchen, carpet and painting, he says.

"It is well located near schools, shops and public transport and it has not been difficult to find tenants."

DUNCAN HUGHES
direction: bullish for property and real estate sectors

headline: THE RISE OF THE REDBACK
intro: In the 19th century sterling was king, in the 20th the greenback; now the yuan is in the ascendancy, writes Angus Grigg.
text:
In the 19th century sterling was king, in the 20th the greenback; now the yuan is in the ascendancy, writes Angus Grigg.

Each weekday morning for the past five years China's central bank made an announcement few ever noticed. At 9.15 am from its Soviet style headquarters in Beijing, the People's Bank of China released the "daily fix" - the rate at which the yuan will trade against the US dollar.

Even for most committed market watchers it was hard going, worthy only of reporting as a matter of record as the currency was tediously stable.

Then on Tuesday everything changed.

Without notice or explanation the PBOC lowered the "daily fix" by 1.9 per cent against the US dollar, the largest devaluation in 20 years. Investors responded by hitting the panic button. Without the usual softening-up period or carefully worded statements that usually preface such a change, anything exposed to China was sold off as investors believed it signalled a sharper than expected slowdown in the world's second biggest economy.

Apple shares dropped 5 per cent, the Australian dollar was hammered and oil hit a six-year low. The Aussie recovered later in the week.

The issue was not the size of the devaluation, which by Friday had run to 4.4 per cent, but that investors hadn't had time to game out the likely consequences.

"Everyone is still trying to work out what this means," said ANZ's chief economist, Warren Hogan, on Wednesday.

Even the Reserve Bank of Australia was caught off guard and seemed a little unsure of how the devaluation would affect the local economy.

Deputy governor Philip Lowe outlined three theoretical scenarios on Wednesday evening during a speech, in which he said it was a "very recent development" and wondered out loud if any of his interpretations would prove correct.

But looking beyond the short and even medium-term impact on financial markets and commodities, ANZ's Hogan and veteran China watcher Nicholas Lardy of the Peterson Institute for International Economics see this as a defining moment in Beijing's reform push.

"What we have seen this week is the start of China's full integration into the world economy," said Hogan via phone from Sydney.

"This is the start of a new era for the global financial architecture."

That's a big statement, requiring plenty of explanation. Hogan has come to this view as he believes Beijing has taken its first significant step in allowing the yuan's value to better reflect market fundamentals.

Yes, China still has a managed exchange rate - but it is less managed than on Monday. This was the technical change implemented on Tuesday by the PBOC.

"[The] market will play a bigger role in exchange rate determination," the central bank said in a statement issued 11 minutes after the abrupt policy switch.

In what was described as a "one-time adjustment", the bank said its goal was to allow the previous day's trading activity to exert greater influence over the "daily fix" the next morning. Such a change means if the yuan falls in trading on a Wednesday, for example, the "fix" on Thursday morning should be set at a lower level.

Technically, therefore, if the yuan keeps falling, Beijing will not have devalued the currency: it will have let the markets to do so.

As changes go, this one has been a long time coming, hence the excitement from those seeing the bigger reform picture.

It was only 10 years ago that Beijing officially scrapped the yuan's peg to the US dollar, but it went back on this reform by unofficially reinstating the peg three years later during the depths of the global financial crisis. The peg was finally lifted in June 2010 and over the next four years the PBOC gradually allowed the yuan to appreciate against the US dollar, hitting a record high in January last year.

At the same time, it has widened the trading band to 2 per cent either side of the "daily fix" - the currency can therefore trade in a 4 per cent range on any given day. It has also set up offshore markets for trading yuan - the main one being in Hong Kong - which don't have the same restrictions.

The so-called managed float onshore has allowed Beijing to keep the yuan relatively stable - it likes stability in all things - while muting China hawks in the US Congress who have repeatedly accused it of keeping the currency artificially low.

But as Japan and Europe devalued their currencies via Quantitative Easing (QE), the relatively high yuan was suddenly out of step with the slowing Chinese economy.

Beijing needed the currency to fall and did so by dressing it up as a market reform via a more flexible exchange rate.

It demonstrated once again the political smarts of PBOC governor Zhou Xiaochuan, who looks to have sold the reform to hardliners as a means to engineer a lower currency in the short term without drawing criticism from the US or International Monetary Fund. As it turned out, both praised the move.

This suggests Beijing's change in currency policy was an opportunistic move to address both weak growth and the yuan's lack of flexibility. There was also a hint of panic after last Saturday's horrible trade number - followed by weaker than expected industrial production and fixed asset investment data released on Wednesday.

The weakening of the currency should therefore also be seen as Beijing's latest attempt to maintain growth near 7 per cent this year, after the country's short-lived stock market rally collapsed in July.

Lardy, a long-time chronicler of China's economic reforms, labelled the move a "potentially major step" and said up until Tuesday the PBOC has been "overriding the market".

"The action taken by China, far from being a step to manipulate its currency, is actually an effort to let the yuan fluctuate according to the dynamics of the exchange markets," he wrote in an article published on the Peterson Institute's website.

"China's move is consistent with long-standing advice from the IMF and from the US Treasury, both of which have repeatedly called for China to adopt a more market-determined exchange rate policy."

For Hogan this means we are going to see and hear a lot more about the yuan, as China takes its final step of fully integrating into the world economy. "We are now entering a new global currency regime." Hogan argues that until now only China's real economy - the trading of goods and services - was integrated into the world economy, while its financial system remained closed.

He is forecasting a big rise in the amount of yuan used in trade and the amount of yuan traded in currency markets - it is already the fifth-most-traded currency and is set to overtake the yen this year and has the British pound in its sights.

It's being called "the rise of the Redback" and its ascension will be completed when the value of the yuan is mentioned on the nightly news alongside the US dollar, euro, pound and yen. That might take three years, but for the likes of Hogan and Lardy the rise is now inevitable.

"We should expect this to lead to greater volatility," wrote Lardy.

The one disclaimer to all this reform optimism is that the PBOC has reserved the right to intervene when the market is "distorted".

In a rare press conference on Thursday, the bank's deputy governor, Yi Gang, said Beijing would keep the currency at a "reasonable" and "balanced level".

This suggests geopolitics and domestic affairs, along with market forces will play a role in settings the yuan's level.

At Friday morning's fixing, which has now become a live Tweeting event, the PBOC showed its intent to drive the direction of the currency.

For the first time in three days it strengthened the yuan marginally, despite the currency trading slightly lower on Thursday.

The PBOC's action suggests markets will play a greater role in setting the yuan's level, but will remain a secondary force to the central bank for now - it has $US3.2 trillion in foreign reserves in its back pocket.

The result still means greater fluctuations in the yuan and these will now need to be considered by most multinationals, traders and anyone connected to China.

Until Tuesday most had given little thought to such risks, which is why markets reacted so violently.

By week's end the market's collective thinking, as reflected in asset prices, had changed completely.

What began as a knee-jerk reaction to sell anything exposed to China became the exact opposite. That is why the Aussie, which is often seen as a proxy for Chinese growth, had bounced off its lows by Friday.

Rather than the devaluation being bad for Chinese growth it is now being interpreted as a positive for the economy this year as the lower currency will help the underperforming export sector.

For a commodity-exporting country like Australia this should be marginally positive as Chinese manufacturers will need to buy more iron ore, coking coal and copper, which will then be re-exported in the form of mobile phones, steel and other finished products.

Treasurer Joe Hockey acknowledged as much to Parliament on Thursday.

"We need to remember that the yuan has appreciated significantly in recent times, and that has had some significant impact on Chinese exports," he said.

Hockey said the more Australia supports the growth of China and its opportunities, "the wealthier Australia will be; for every dollar we spend buying Chinese goods and services, the Chinese spent $2 buying our goods and services".

The RBA's Lowe was more cautious but still saw the benefit for Australia. "If it supports growth in our largest trading partner, it's probably good," he said. "If it's a positive sign about structural reform, a stronger commitment to markets, then that's ultimately good for China, and if it's good for China then it's probably good for us."

ANZ's Hogan agreed the devaluation should be positive for commodities like iron ore, but said the overall picture is far more complex.

Indeed, the potential ramifications reach into every corner of the world economy.

"It could be quite negative for global growth," says Hogan. That's because gains made by China may come at the expense of others like Japan, South Korea and potentially Europe. "There could be a deflationary shock."

If global growth is dented even slightly, then the US Federal Reserve's carefully planned schedule for beginning to lift interest rates may pushed back from September to later in the year.

This is the type of chain reaction the RBA's Lowe mooted on Wednesday evening. "We know that movements in exchange rates often set off a chain reaction of events which can be difficult to predict," he said.

By week's end this chain reaction was not looking too scary, but it should be remembered that China now has the infrastructure in place to resume weakening the currency at any time.

Hogan and others believe over the next few months it will fall by as much as 10 per cent, which means Beijing's daily fixing is now a must-watch event as the yuan becomes a new global currency.
direction: bearish for Australian dollar and risk assets

headline: STANDING FIRM
intro: The woman at the centre of a political storm over Australian detention centres defies the activists: 'If they want to change government policy they should engage directly with the government'. Jenny Wiggins and Jennifer Hewett report.
text:
The woman at the centre of a political storm over Australian detention centres defies the activists: 'If they want to change government policy they should engage directly with the government'. Jenny Wiggins and Jennifer Hewett report.

Diane Smith-Gander has faced plenty of difficult situations during a long and successful business career. But this might be her most difficult of all. And it has become very, very personal.

The company she chairs, Transfield Services, is at the centre of an activists' storm focused on its management of government detention centres on Nauru and Manus Island.

This is increasingly thrusting the contractor into the news, making it a target of an aggressive social media campaign and scaring shareholders, some of whom have dumped the company's stock.

Smith-Gander has shown a steel backbone in dealing with the tirade against Transfield, arguing firmly that the company's role is to be "an essential services provider", not a political campaigner on detention centres.

"We do not influence government policy in this area, so we think the activists' attention to us is misplaced," she says. "If they want to change government policy, they should engage directly with the government."

She is no stranger to tough questions on corporate governance, having spent all day Wednesday lecturing on the topic at the University of Western Australia, where she is an adjunct professor, going through a case study of Tyco International, the US conglomerate that used accounting tricks to inflate its earnings in the 1990s.

Smith-Gander likes dealing with questions from academics and students - who press her on how Transfield's board is addressing the allegations they read in the media - because it "pressure tests" her thinking, she says.

She is certainly a woman who understands crisis situations. "Going to the islands where the centres are is a very powerful experience. Refugees and asylum-seekers by their very nature are very vulnerable people and most of the people there have taken a very challenging journey to get there," she says.

But Smith-Gander shies away from being drawn into a political debate, even though she says she is under "no pressure" from the government to keep quiet. "For us it's about the business element, and I see the business element as in very safe hands.

"Graeme Hunt is a seasoned and more than competent CEO," she says of Transfield's chief. "It's the politicisation of the work that we're doing that is the challenge for us."

In reality, Transfield Services has no choice but to join what has become an intensely political campaign. It's no longer possible, it seems, for any company to just go about its lawful business as competently and fairly as it can, while ignoring a raging controversy over its activities.

Not when the line between the commercial and the political is becoming ever more blurred in an era where consumer power - and the power of social media - have changed so much so quickly.

When Transfield announced its new name, BroadSpectrum, a week ago, after losing its licence to the Transfield brand earlier in 2015, it was immediately attacked on Twitter by people opposed to the detention centres and the hashtag #BroadSpectrum started trending.

"#BroadSpectrum is quite a fitting new name for you @TransServices given your Broad Spectrum of human rights abuses towards #asylumseekers", one post said.

Add the ability to pressure large investors to divest, especially industry superannuation funds. Some of them are keen to show they are responding to member concerns, even if it is only a small minority of members who feel strongly on the issue. Industry funds like HESTA proudly announced their intention to divest from the company after pressure from some of their union members. And if a company is targeted in this way, it has to respond to the accusations, no matter how unfair or inaccurate it believes them to be.

Transfield, for example, rejects claims that any of its employees have been involved in abuses or inappropriate behaviour against asylum seekers.

"There has not been one substantiated claim of an offence committed by a Transfield Services employee relating to an asylum seeker at Nauru or Manus Province," a spokesman says, adding that no charges have been laid either.

But the company declines to answer questions on specific allegations of abuse, referring queries to the Immigration Department, and Smith-Gander does not want to discuss whether unsubstantiated claims have been made against Transfield employees.

At base, the complaints are really about government policy rather than the company's work, she says.

For those who regard the detention of asylum seekers as an abuse of human rights per se, nothing Transfield can do will ever be enough to satisfy their concerns. They just want to end the policy however they can.

Yet it's a policy supported by the government and the Labor Party, with no change likely and little opportunity to wedge either party against the other.

So that makes the most vulnerable point of attack targeting the public company contracted to carry out the work and trying to home in on any complaints. The company's reputation, in that sense, is collateral damage, while a divestment campaign is a way of piling on the pressure.

Transfield's former chairman, Tony Shepherd, who signed off on the company's first detention centre contract on Nauru in September 2012, before handing over to Smith-Gander in October 2013, says Transfield has always had a culture of "great social responsibility" and the attacks on it are "completely unreasonable".

But human rights campaigners say Transfield needs to take responsibility for alleged human rights abuses at the detention centres on Nauru and Manus Island regardless of whether its staff participated directly in those abuses.

This includes the lobby group No Business in Abuse, which is conducting a vigorous public campaign while trying to persuade investors to sell their holdings. It argues mandatory detention is automatically an abuse of human rights.

Shen Narayanasamy, who is the executive director of NBIA and the human rights campaign director for GetUp!, says if there are human rights abuses in a system, companies that engage in a business relationship with that system can be "contributing to those abuses and complicit in those abuses" without being involved directly.

She points to the United Nations' interpretive guide on the corporate responsibility to respect human rights, which cites "performing construction and maintenance on a detention camp where inmates were allegedly subject to inhumane treatment" as one example of a situation where a business might cause "adverse human rights impact".

Smith-Gander says she understands NBIA's view, but Transfield has "a different perspective". Transfield has not signed up to any UN principles on human rights, but signed its own human rights statement in June 2015.

The statement says the company recognises the importance of upholding and respecting human rights in the course of operating its business, but adds this commitment "is limited to what is within its reasonable capability and requirements of law and government policy".

Smith-Gander visited the detention centres most recently in August and is planning another trip before the end of 2015.

"When I was there, the strongest emotion I had was a real sense of pride in the work that the company and the individual Transfield employees are doing," she says.

"Because in that very challenging and complex setting, they're delivering a safe environment, and they're delivering care and welfare to these people.

"So I came away with a very strong commitment that I would make sure that the board and senior management did all things that the company needed to continue to do to have the settings in place, the monitoring, the checking, so that I would never have to go to those places and not feel pride in what we were doing."

Immigration Minister Peter Dutton says superannuation funds and fund managers and other investors should make it clear that political activism "has no place" in the Australian marketplace.

"This sort of activism has no end," he says, arguing the targets can range across the political spectrum on any number of issues and will only increase in ferocity unless challenged. That is already obvious in the activist campaigns against coal mining.

"The most important next step is for the fund managers and peak bodies of superannuation funds in this country to condemn the activities of this extreme element," Dutton says.

"That would send the clear message this political witch hunt won't succeed, won't achieve the desired outcome and that they won't divest from a company involved in lawful activity."

Yet that sentiment is unlikely to help Transfield much in today's market. Few fund managers are prone to taking up political cudgels on behalf of a company. A public defence of a company under attack is also relatively rare when most people in business increasingly try to avoid provoking activists. Instead, the growth industry in investment is known as ethical, social and governance (ESG) investing.

Heather Ridout, the chairwoman of the country's largest industry fund, AustralianSuper, has strongly supported Transfield - its record and its management.

Ridout says AustralianSuper takes the issue of ethical investing very seriously but Transfield has been transparent and responsive to any issues raised.

"Transfield Services is a great Australian company with a great history and a strong board and very good management," she says. "If Transfield were to stop managing the detention centres, someone else would have to take that over and might not do as good a job."

The head of one of Transfield's biggest institutional investors, who says he meets the company regularly to ask questions about conditions in the detention centres, says it is "extremely naive" for activists to think that if Transfield walks away from the contracts the government policy is "dead and buried".

"You're basically asking Transfield to do one of the least professional things a professional services company can do - to turn its back on a client," the fund manager says.

But there is no doubt the activists' campaign is taking a toll, as evidenced by a protest vote against Transfield chief executive Hunt's re-election to energy group AGL's board as a non-executive director at the energy group's annual meeting this week.

One AGL investor asked the meeting: "Am I right in thinking that he is the managing director of Transfield services [and] that company makes money out of Nauru and Manus Island? If the answer is yes, should he be on the board of what hopes to be a reputable company?"

And human rights issues are becoming a more important part of corporate governance for investors, making it essential for Transfield to get on the front foot.

Investment group AMP Capital, which says it has had private discussions with Transfield over the detention centres, highlighted shif0ting "societal expectations" in its September corporate governance update and stressed it was in shareholders' interest that the "companies they invest in uphold and facilitate basic human rights within their sphere of influence". It also recommended they check whether companies they invested in were guided by the UN's principles on business and human rights.

"Just as companies move to capture the business opportunities of becoming more global, some in society are also demanding companies be far more accountable for their actions," AMP Capital said. "Increasingly, investors expect companies to understand and manage the human rights risks they are exposed to, whether these risks reside in their business relationships and supply chain or their own operations."

Transfield's job of defending its role and reputation is only going to become tougher as NBIA prepares to release a report that it says will detail "Transfield's complicity in human rights abuses" and Greens Senator Sarah Hanson-Young - who predictably wants the government to strip Transfield of its contracts - plans a campaign that will target some of the company's key customers, like BHP Billiton.

This comes at a tricky time for Transfield, because it is in final negotiations to renew its contract on Nauru and Manus Island for another five years. It desperately needs the contract, worth an estimated $2.7 billion, because other parts of its business are struggling, partially because of falling demand for its maintenance services from the resources industry. Its stock price has slumped 45 per cent over the past 12 months.

Transfield's current contract expires at the end of October, and while it has been told it is the preferred tender for a new contract, it has not signed yet with the government.

NBIA has not visited the detention centres, and has not done its own investigation into alleged abuses. It still says independent investigations, such as the Moss Review initiated by former immigration minister Scott Morrison into claims of alleged sexual and other physical assaults on Nauru, have found that abuses have occurred.

The Moss Review, which covered the period between July 2013 and October 2014, found that contractors working on Nauru - which include Save the Children, Wilson Security, and International Health and Medical Services, as well as Transfield - had not been reporting incidents quickly enough. It said this had hurt the ability of contractors or the Nauru police to investigate allegations, especially sexual assault allegations.

Another 207-page report, released in September by a Senate committee investigating alleged human rights abuses on Nauru, said the performance of Wilson Security and Transfield needed to be more closely scrutinised by the Immigration Department.

"The department has effectively outsourced its accountability to Transfield Services and through them, to Wilson Security, with no penalty for non-compliance," the report said. Transfield hired Wilson Security in 2012 to provide security services on Nauru.

The Senate report, which looked at allegations since offshore processing began on Nauru in September 2012 - when Transfield started providing services - found that 30 formal allegations of child abuse had been made against contractors working on Nauru, as well as 15 allegations of sexual assault or rape and four allegations related to the exchange of sexual favours for contraband. It also said Wilson Security had released details of 11 cases in which its staff were sacked for misconduct, including alleged sexual assault and excessive uses of force.

The Liberals, who held two seats on the Senate inquiry committee, compared to three seats held by Labor and the Greens, issued a dissenting report, saying the majority of members of the committee had accepted "untested and unsubstantiated" submissions as fact.

Smith-Gander acknowledges that some of Transfield's staff working at the centres "have not met expectations" but says they have been dealt with. Several hundred staff have been terminated from Nauru since September 2012 for various reasons.

"The controls we have in place today, the controls the client has, are very different to at the start of this contract," she says.

Still, Narayanasamy, who has given presentations outlining her views of companies' responsibilities on human rights to dozens of banks and financial institutions, says Transfield is in danger of losing its "social licence to operate".

"When you have major Australian banks and global banks saying that in their credit financing risk assessment they take into account human rights abuses and complicity in those abuses, when you have corporate entities like BHP and Rio Tinto publicly saying things like human rights is hard-wired into our business, complicity in abuses is not just a moral and ethical question, it becomes a serious question for business, for access to finance and contractual partners and in terms of the intangible value of business," she says.

Dutton says "political gripes" of all kinds - from genetically modified food to the live cattle trade to Indonesia, to what medical services should be available - belong in the political arena, not the business arena.

"These issues should be left to regulators and legislators, rather than trying to force changes through the back door," he says.

But for Transfield and Smith-Gander, those attempting to force change are trying to batter down the company's front door. That's politics.
direction: Transfield Services shares likely to face downward pressure

headline: SONY MARCO POLO
intro: Our experts look at a global investment.
text:
Our experts look at a global investment.

Sony recently announced that the PlayStation 4 (PS4) has smashed through 30 million unit sales. This marks an extraordinary run for the PS4 and the fastest growth of any console in PlayStation's hardware industry.

This is good news for Sony as the PlayStation, along with the company's image sensors, is integral to a return to sustainable earnings growth.

The PS4 launched just a little over two years ago and has blitzed its prime competitor, the Xbox One, in terms of sales. These are thought to be somewhere between 15 and 18 million (Microsoft hasn't released any updated sales figures for some time).

The latest total for the PS4 is a nice jump from the 20 million unit sales as at March this year and around 25 million in September, and likely given a helping hand by price cuts in the American, European and Japanese markets.

The PS4 is available in 124 countries and regions worldwide, and still has a long way to run - we expect the life cycle of this generation to be at least six to seven years. If this plays out, it will have a chance of surpassing the PS2 (which chalked up an impressive 155 million in unit sales) as the best-selling console.

Along with the image sensor business, the PlayStation is spearheading Sony's hopes to return its core consumer electronics business to growth.

The PS experience has expanded to include streaming gaming via a subscription service and on-demand TV. Next year Sony is set to launch its virtual reality headset as the next chapter of its gaming experience.

The PS4 and related software sales have helped push revenue for Sony's game and network services segment in the most recent quarter up 16.5 per cent year-on-year to ¥360.7 billion ($4.05 billion) and operating income 9.6 per cent higher to ¥23.9 billion.

Earnings growth was only held back by the strength of the US dollar, with a relatively high ratio of US dollar denominated costs for the segment.

The overall turnaround at Sony continues to gain solid traction, and this is being recognised by the market.

Although Sony shares (listed in Tokyo and on the NYSE) have declined around 25 per cent from their highs in the middle of this year, they are still up around 20 per cent for the year to date.

We believe there remains significant scope for more improvement, with the company's mobile communications division continuing to be a big drag on group profitability, and likely to be divested if it cannot be turned around over the next 12 to 18 months.

Growth in Sony's image sensors business, meanwhile, should get a boost from the acquisition of Toshiba's sensors unit, as Toshiba works to recover after the fallout from an accounting scandal.

Sony is also, of course, getting the tailwind from a weaker yen, with further assistance here likely from the Bank of Japan. We expect to see further value unlocked with the shares trading at around 20 times earnings and on a modest 1.5 times projected book value.

For a free trial of Fat Prophets' daily market commentary please call 1300 881 177 or email info@fatprophets.com.au

Disclosure: interests associated with Fat Prophets declare a holding in Vodafone.
direction: Bullish for Sony shares

headline: Strike threat poses new risk to $73b Gorgon gas project
intro: Chevron's $US54 billion ($73 billion) Gorgon liquefied natural gas project being built off Western Australia is facing a fresh threat of industrial action after unions won official approval to ballot workers on a potential strike just months before exports from the massive venture are finally due to begin.
text:
Chevron's $US54 billion ($73 billion) Gorgon liquefied natural gas project being built off Western Australia is facing a fresh threat of industrial action after unions won official approval to ballot workers on a potential strike just months before exports from the massive venture are finally due to begin.

The latest development risks further delaying the start of production at the troubled project, Australia's biggest single resources venture, beyond its second-half 2015 target, which has already slid by more than a year from the original schedule.

Chevron Australia boss Roy Krzywosinski had issued several warnings that the lagging competitiveness of the LNG construction sector in Australia was threatening to deter about $100 billion of potential further investment in the sector, and sounded an alarm about inflexible industrial relations systems.

The Fair Work Commission on Friday ruled the Construction, Forestry, Mining & Energy Union and the Australian Manufacturing Workers' Union could hold a protected ballot of employees of CB&I, the United States engineering company carrying out a $US2.3 billion contract on mechanical, electrical and instrumentation work at the onshore LNG plant under construction on Barrow Island.

In the last month CB&I workers have several times rejected a proposed replacement enterprise agreement, driven by a desire to see a shorter work roster introduced. A pay increase included in the new agreement, which would replace an EA that expired on January 20, was on hold awaiting resolution of the dispute.

Chevron, which owned almost 50 per cent of the Gorgon venture and was the operator, declined to comment. CB&I could not be contacted.

A hard-line group of construction workers had been pushing for the work roster at the remote Gorgon site to be switched to a 20:10 cycle, consisting of 20 days of work followed by 10 days off. That compared with an existing work cycle of 28 days on and nine days off. The shorter cycle would further increase costs at the Gorgon project, which was already a substantial $US17 billion over its original $US37 billion budget.

In a post on its Facebook site on Sunday, the "20/10 Let It Begin" group fighting for the short roster highlighted the FWC's approval of protected action and called for support. "The countdown has begun!" it said. "Help us get the last remaining people in the correct union, believe us when we say there aren't that many left to join!"

Under the FWC ruling, workers would be asked if they supported "an unlimited number of stoppages on the performance of work between 30 minutes and up to and including 24 hours". They would also be asked if they supported bans on administrative work, except for work required for health and safety reasons.

The process the unions needed to go through to ballot workers on potential strike action meant any industrial stoppages were not expected until September at the earliest. Chevron was believed to have mitigation measures in place to minimise the impact of any industrial action on the start-up schedule of the project, which was 25 per cent owned by each of ExxonMobil and Royal Dutch Shell.

Chevron's latest guidance is for the first LNG cargo to be shipped from Gorgon by the end of the year. The US major was due to update investors on progress at its second-quarter earnings briefing on July 31.
direction: bearish for Chevron and the broader resources sector

headline: More need to step up on reform
intro: South Australian Labor Premier Jay Weatherill has stoked hopes that substantial reform of both the tax system and federal-state financial relations is still possible. Other political stakeholders need to similarly come to the table if they want to be considered as legitimate policy players, as opposed to self-interested occupiers of office.
text:
South Australian Labor Premier Jay Weatherill has stoked hopes that substantial reform of both the tax system and federal-state financial relations is still possible. Other political stakeholders need to similarly come to the table if they want to be considered as legitimate policy players, as opposed to self-interested occupiers of office.

Late last week, Mr Weatherill unveiled a reform package that would increase the 10 per cent GST tax to 15 per cent. The extra revenue - perhaps $34 billion a year - would go to the federal government. At the same time, the federal government would give the states a fixed 17.5 per cent share of its income tax revenue in return for abolishing its other payments to the states. Mr Weatherill suggests that, in the short term, the increased GST would give the federal government revenue to fund incentive-sharpening tax reform, including income tax cuts, and to compensate lower and middle-income earners for the increased cost of goods and services. In the longer term, the states would get direct access to the faster-growing income tax base to fund their growing healthcare spending pressures.

Mr Weatherill's numbers will need to be scrutinised. But, on the face of it, they move the nation's longest-serving Labor leader further into the camp of genuine tax reform. Along with a 15 per cent GST, Mr Weatherill has proposed expanding its base to include financial services. Moreover, he joins NSW Liberal Premier Mike Baird and the Abbott government's National Commission of Audit in tackling so-called vertical fiscal imbalance, or the excessive mismatch between what each level of government spends and what it raises in revenue. In 2014, the Commission of Audit proposed giving the states a 10 percentage point income tax surcharge, worth about $25 billion a year, that would come out of the federal income tax base. Unlike Mr Weatherill's plan, the states would be able to individually adjust their own income tax surcharge rates, injecting some competitive tension into the federalist system. Along with former Queensland Labor Treasurer Andrew Fraser, Mr Baird has been a long-term proponent of giving the states access to the income tax base.

Any such change would partly correct the fiscal centralisation and unproductive buck passing caused from when the federal government took over income tax from the states during World War II. Previous efforts to wind this back, in the 1970s and again in the early 1990s, politically crashed. The renewed ambition from Mr Weatherill, Mr Baird and perhaps the Turnbull government to try again, contrasts with the opportunism from others on both sides of politics. Queensland Labor Premier Annastacia Palaszczuk rules out both lifting the GST or selling off the state's government-owned electricity system into a hot infrastructure market. Victorian Labor Premier Daniel Andrews has little worthwhile to say on the GST while overseeing a blowout in the state's public sector wages bill. Western Australian Liberal Premier Colin Barnett needs to recognise that the west's unfairly small share of GST revenue will naturally increase as the mining boom winds down and that he is behind the curve on reforming his own state economy. Tasmanian Liberal Premier Will Hodgman should be embarrassed that even the Labor Opposition has made more productive noises about being open to tax reform.

And, federally, Labor Opposition Leader Bill Shorten grasps at piecemeal proposals to avoid grappling with serious policy reform. Instead, all federal and state leaders need to combine such tax reform with efficient reductions in government spending growth, particularly on health, as budgets prepare for the bill from the national disability insurance scheme. Increasing taxes to try to pay for more services would be counter-productive because it would hurt the economic growth required to grow the tax base in the first place.
direction: bearish for rate-sensitive sectors due to tax reform uncertainty

headline: Locals brace for auction fight fallout
intro: The reverberations from intense competition between the big international auction houses are starting to be felt in Australia.
text:
The reverberations from intense competition between the big international auction houses are starting to be felt in Australia.

Management turmoil at international art auctioneers grappling for the most profitable business could have notable implications for Australia.

Sotheby's and Christie's International have shed their chief executives and a third major player Bonhams is for sale.

The shifts do not directly affect Australia, since Sotheby's Australia is an independent local company and Christie's ceased sales in Australia in 2006. With its parent on the market, however, the future of Bonhams Australia could be under a cloud.

The changes highlight how the big auction houses are transforming themselves into art financiers and luxury goods conglomerates in the search for growth - a trend already being echoed on the local scene.

In Australia, five sizeable auction houses, mostly independents, compete to secure worthwhile art to feed more than a dozen major sales a year, in post-boom conditions where sellers are often reluctant to commit and buyers loath to spend. Contemporary art, once solely the domain of commercial galleries, is increasingly offered at auction.

Internationally, Sotheby's chief executive William Ruprecht stepped down late last year amid calls from activist shareholder and hedge fund billionaire Daniel Loeb for the sharemarket-listed auctioneer to slash costs and improve returns. Ruprecht's replacement is yet to be named. At the close of the year the privately owned Christie's also replaced its chief executive, Steven Murphy, with Patricia Barbizet, an adviser to Christie's owner, billionaire Francois Pinault.

Behind the glamour of the international art auction world, with its celebrity-studded soirees and gushing media coverage, the big auctioneers struggle to attract record-breaking artworks and lure the biggest spenders.

Global market worth $US16b

With the worldwide art market worth an estimated $US16 billion (20.5 billion) last year, according to research group Artnet, up 10 per cent on 2013, auctioneers should be doing well: they charge most vendors at least 15 per cent commission and hit buyers with a premium of up to 25 per cent. Thus an artwork fetching $10,000 would generate $4000 for the auction house. Indeed, with such seemingly generous margins, some critics ask what value buyers and sellers actually get.

The trouble is the big auctioneers have been slashing the commissions charged, and in some cases passing on to vendors part of the buyer's premium - while canny sellers play them off against each other.

Deducted from the relatively modest proceeds are big-costs - salaries, premises and marketing - plus legal costs and damages when things go wrong, as they did for Christie's recently in a case over a fake Albert Tucker sold in Australia in 2000.

Turnover for the "internationals" is impressive. Christie's last month reported sales of art and collectables equivalent to $US7.7 billion for 2014, up 12 per cent on 2013. Sotheby's reported $US6 billion, up 18 per cent.

Yet Sotheby's recorded a net loss of $US27.7 million in the three months to September on revenue of $US94.2 million, a slight improvement on the $US30.13 million loss on $US107.9 million revenue for the same quarter 2013. For the first nine months it was in the black to the tune of a modest $US43.8 million. For 2013 net earnings were $US130 million from revenue of $US853.7 million.

Sotheby's recent performance lends weight to Loeb's comment that the auctioneer is "an old master painting in desperate need of restoration".

So where are the big boys headed? In the UK's Antiques Trade Gazette, Ivan Macquisten writes that top auctioneers are turning into "multinational conglomerates specialising in luxury goods and wealth management".

Masters of reinvention

He says they have proved themselves "masters of reinvention, first with guarantees, which gave them the potential to increase their slice of the cake while keeping consignors on board, then by extending the facility to advance guarantees, spreading business to their financial service arms in the process".

Just as global banking was collapsing, Sotheby's and Christie's were in effect turning themselves into merchant banks.

Such guarantees offer vendors an assured future price for their art, a facility increasingly utilised by influential sellers. In a role akin to that of a merchant bank, the auctioneer or an associate effectively underwrites the price of an artwork. If it sells for less, they top up the price to the vendor; if it sells for more they may pocket part of the surplus.

As Macquisten writes: "Soon [the auction houses] effectively became the biggest dealers in the world, cutting a swathe through the trade as they snaffled many of the plum private-treaty sales, a business that accounts for around 10 per cent of their turnover now and remains, along with online sales, the key battleground."

Local auctioneers are under similar pressure to take an all-encompassing middleman role in a market worth about $103.73 million last year - well down on its boom-time turnover of $175.63 million in 2007 and up just 2.4 per cent on 2013.

Chief contenders for business in this much-diminished pool are the privately owned Sotheby's Australia, which uses the name under licence, plus Menzies, Deutscher and Hackett, Bonhams Australia and the smaller Mossgreen.

Vendor finance

Just like their illustrious New York and London counterparts, several local operators are now financing vendors, guaranteeing prices, securing lucrative private sales and operating their own commercial galleries selling direct to the public. One even has a restaurant.

Sotheby's Australia and Menzies say in their catalogues that some works at their sales may be subject to guarantees, while Mossgreen apparently offers similar facilities through an alliance with Assetline Capital. In Australia an unquantifiable number of works go on the market each year with their owners guaranteed a minimum price.

Frederick McCubbin's Mrs McCubbin Picking Blossom (Spring), owned by jailed businessman Rodney Adler and sold by Mossgreen in October for $1.34 million including premium, was reputedly guaranteed, but Mossgreen's Paul Sumner would not confirm it.

Clearly, where an auctioneer or a related party guarantees a vendor a price for an artwork, they effectively have an equity in it and can hardly be independent third parties.

While some of this may raise many an eyebrow - surely it's not appropriate for auctioneers to act as principals as well as agents? - the truth is they have been doing it for centuries.

Robert Lacey's book Sotheby's: Bidding for Class, relates how in the 1700s Christie's founder James Christie offered vendors cash upfront for their goods before a sale - effectively a guarantee - which was gratefully taken up by cash-strapped vendors. Even Sotheby's, which claims a 271-year history since it began as a back-street British bookseller, was not above such tactics as manipulating prices and offering the proprietors' own art for sale.

Lacey points out that the auctioneers started life as suppliers to the trade or to middlemen.

They were able to offer cash-poor wealthy families in England, and later in war-torn Europe, a way of realising on their often substantial possessions. These artworks were in turn retailed by art and antique dealers. But when such sources dried up, the auctioneers began to take over as high-profile retailers themselves, largely usurping the role of dealers on the secondary art market.

So the locals will tend to follow the lead of the international operators, just as they did when the buyer's premium was introduced overseas. Shortly afterwards, Sotheby's Australia introduced the premium in Melbourne in 1983, at 10 per cent. Before that, auction houses survived just on a commission charged to vendors.

But the locals, like the internationals, will need deep pockets if they're to turn into full-scale art financiers and luxury conglomerates. And in the meantime, the scramble for important art to sell goes on - sometimes eased by the inevitable windfalls from death and divorce.

It is worth recalling that the Christie's exit reflected the challenges of making money in the Australian art market almost a decade ago.

The question is, will 2015 see another auction operator blink?

ptrfish8@gmail.com
direction: bearish for art auction sector and luxury goods stocks

headline: Trifecta of reforms on right track
intro: Assistant Treasurer Josh Frydenberg will wait until August to release the final in a trifecta of reforms to restore confidence in the scandal-ridden financial planning industry. The Australian Financial Review understands the Abbott government's response to a parliamentary inquiry into ethics, education and professionalism in financial planning will be delayed until August.
text:
Assistant Treasurer Josh Frydenberg will wait until August to release the final in a trifecta of reforms to restore confidence in the scandal-ridden financial planning industry. The Australian Financial Review understands the Abbott government's response to a parliamentary inquiry into ethics, education and professionalism in financial planning will be delayed until August.

It is understood that various professional and industry associations, including the Australian Bankers Association, have reached common ground on how to lift ethics, education and professionalism in the sector - no easy feat given their different vested interests.

The plan is to incorporate these reforms, similar to recommendations outlined in David Fawcett's parliamentary joint committee inquiry, into a response to David Murray's financial system inquiry.

It is understood the reforms will include lifting financial planners' educational qualifications to university standard and establishing an independent body to oversee the sector.

The basic RG146 regulation required to qualify as a financial planner can take less than eight days to complete in an open-book, non-supervised environment.

The body would oversee a governance structure for the industry, education and focus on integrity and independent standards to help the financial advice industry transform into a profession as soon as possible.

Former Financial Planning Association chairman Matthew Rowe quite rightly says professional bodies must be allowed to play a vital role in shaping the behaviour, ethics and culture of financial planners. "The culture within a corporation is determined by the quality of its leadership. The culture of a profession is determined by your peers, codes of ethics, education requirements, practice standards and a collective want to act in the public interest."

The FPA has offered to give its intellectual property on education to the new body.

The government is expected to recommend a mandatory national exam for new and existing advisers set at degree standard.

To avoid gaming the system, including cheating on exams, the government is expected to outsource the marking of the national exam. This should be broadened to include continuous professional development exams.

Cheating on exams has been common practice in the industry. Some Macquarie financial advisers circulated a "Penske file" which included cheat sheets and at Commonwealth Bank of Australia's West Australian division, two experienced planners were sacked last year for getting their junior staff to sit mandatory tests for them.

More recently, an investigation into IOOF revealed that the advice research head Peter Hilton had junior staff complete Kaplan training modules on his behalf as well as compliance training. It also found members of the research team were not RG146 compliant, even though they have contact with financial planners, in some cases had direct contact with clients through presentations or dealing with them on the phone.

In the case of IOOF, documents show that Hilton was given a final warning last March and had his responsible manager status revoked.

The issue of institutionalised cheating is important as it goes to the heart of an adviser's integrity, the culture of the organisation and the attitude to customers.

The education, ethics and professionalism reforms are designed as a circuit breaker to help restore confidence after it took a hit following a series of scandals in the past two years including Commonwealth Bank, National Australia Bank, Macquarie Group and more recently IOOF Group.

It comes as the future of financial advice reforms are set to take effect on July 1, which will ban commissions on all financial products excluding life insurance and impose a best interests test on financial advisers.

Last week the Assistant Treasurer released the first of his trifecta of reforms to the industry. They were changes to life insurance and industry funds.

Life insurance reforms include capping upfront commissions at 60 per cent of a policy's premium by 2018, a cap on trailing commissions at 20 per cent, a ban on volume-based commissions (wholesale commissions) and the development of a code of conduct.

Given life insurance was carved out of the FOFA reforms, this is a good start but the clock needs to be set on when to phase out commissions, which are known to drive conflicted behaviour that can lead to inappropriate advice to clients.

The other key reform addressed the governance of superannuation funds. An exposure draft included changes to the composition of boards with more independent directors and better transparency of director fees. The draft also requires super funds to maintain a register of all relevant interests of directors, including previous roles and other board positions.

Industry funds generally have a board composed of 50 per cent union and 50 per cent employer representatives. The reforms, to be phased in over three years, include a mandate for super funds to have one-third independent directors and an independent chairperson on the board.

In 2012, Jeremy Cooper conducted a review into super which called for such changes. David Murray in his review of the financial system went even further and called for a majority of independent directors.

Industry funds have outperformed retail funds over long periods of time, but the superannuation system is far from perfect.

One of the key concerns from a corporate governance aspect is the lack of transparency which has resulted in perceptual issues of financial links between industry super funds and unions.

The Royal Commission into Trade Union Governance and Corruption looked into these perceptual issues when it drilled into the relationship between the Transport Workers Union and TWU Super, a fund that was set up in the 1980s and has more than 140,000 members and billions of dollars of assets.

The royal commission was told up to $1 million flowed from TWU Super into TWU in 2013. This included $200,000 to directors nominated by TWU, $500,000 in reimbursements for salaries and expenses of a number of superannuation liaison officers and sponsorship. Given TWU Super is a public offer fund, which means it is open to the public and therefore not everyone is a member of the TWU, such questions about the flow of money from the super fund to the union become even more relevant.

After a series of financial scandals in the past two years, it is imperative that Australians trust the system of superannuation. It is where their hard-earned retirement savings are parked.

That means creating transparency about director fees, who sits on the board and any conflicts.

The Greens lodged a motion in the Senate for a royal commission into financial services. The Greens were supported in their motion by Nationals senator John Williams, who crossed the floor. However, it didn't get support from Labor senators, and so failed, 39 to 14.

But the clock is ticking. As more and more scandals emerge, revealing misconduct and management cover-ups, pressure will mount.

The various reforms to the sector are all good, but when a sector carries a privileged place in the lives of Australians, it needs to be above reproach. It seems every time a financial institution rock is turned over, too many cockroaches crawl out.
direction: bearish for financial advice and banking stocks

headline: Corrections
intro: ■ The article "Lending curbs pointless: investors", on page 45 incorrectly calculated the gross rental yield as 7.7 per cent. It should have been 4.64 per cent.
text: ■ The article "Lending curbs pointless: investors", on page 45 incorrectly calculated the gross rental yield as 7.7 per cent. It should have been 4.64 per cent.
direction: downward pressure on the specific property trust's valuation

headline: HOMES FOR YOUR KIDS
intro: Worried about how your children are going to get into the market? Here are five ways to help, writes Larry Schlesinger.
text:
Worried about how your children are going to get into the market? Here are five ways to help, writes Larry Schlesinger.

While other 70-year-olds may be planning exotic holidays abroad, Colin McQueen, a state government vet continues to clock in at the Ararat export abattoir as he works to pay off the last of the three homes he bought in Melbourne's inner northern suburbs for his adult children.

But he is far from bitter.

He is more than happy to forgo the luxuries usually associated with a well-earned retirement. Instead, he is providing his kids with good homes in suburbs they would otherwise never be able to afford to buy into. He is also saving them hundreds of dollars in after-tax income they would have had to pay the banks every week if they had mortgages.

While the extreme generosity of McQueen and his wife, Stephanie, sets them apart, they are part of a growing pool of Baby Boomer parents who want to help their adult children buy their first property. While many of them bought their homes when suburban real estate was affordable to anyone on a decent wage, this is a luxury no longer available to new generations of home buyers.

A recent survey by REST Industry Super found that an incredible one in three retirees in Sydney, Melbourne and Perth, and one in five in Brisbane and Adelaide, want to give their adult kids money to help them get on the property ladder.

"Not only is it difficult for young people to save a deposit, when they buy property they can be paying up to $1000 per week in after-tax income as interest on their mortgage," McQueen says.

"This makes them very vulnerable to a sudden spike in interest rates, which is a likely consequence of our ponzi-like system of money creation as debt."

When McQueen was a graduate in his 20s, he could have bought a house in North Fitzroy (a fashionable suburb 4 kilometres from the city centre) for $13,000. Now the median house price in North Fitzroy is $1.4 million, according to CoreLogic RP Data.

Making it even more challenging, salaries have not kept pace with rising house prices: 40 years ago the proportion of median family income required to meet average loan repayments was 10 to 15 per cent. Now, according to the Real Estate Institute of Australia, that figure is more than 30 per cent.

For most families, unaffordable housing means adult children are living at home longer. But as the McQueen's example shows, that is not the only option. While you may not be able to go to the lengths they have gone to, there are other ways you can help your children buy their first home.

Buy it for themThe McQueens have financed their children's homes through a combination of capital gains, inheritance money and drawing down from their superannuation. The properties are in Stephanie's name, and the children are tenants, paying minimum rent.
