All three homes - a house in North Fitzroy, a three-bedroom apartment in East Brunswick, a two-bedroom apartment in Thornbury - will be inherited by children Amber, Amelia and Callum upon their deaths.

"Our children all pay rent - just under half the market rate - which covers our expenses and provides a bit of pocket money," McQueen says. "In the fullness of time, they can expect to become an owner of the property. To me, this is a great avenue for building intergenerational wealth and maintaining control over one's assets if there were to be a breakdown of the children's relationships or bankruptcy," he says.

The McQueens are building an extension at the back of their own Fitzroy home. "We plan to live there when we are elderly rather than go into an old person's home. Hopefully one of our children's family will move in."

McQueen says the arrangement has worked "brilliantly" for them, but there are potential tax and legal implications to consider, meaning the advice of an accountant, financial planner and property lawyer should always be sought.

"You may have to pay tax on the rental income," Brisbane property lawyer Paul Brennan says. "You may also have trouble getting credit and there could be complications due to divorce or if one of the parents passes away or gets dementia," he says.

In addition, as they are investment properties, annual land tax must be paid.

The McQueen children will have to pay capital gains tax (CGT) when they eventually sell the properties, although not when they inherit them, explains Adrian Raftery, tax author and senior lecturer in financial planning at Deakin University.

"Given the tremendous growth in house prices, this could be quite substantial," he says. (They may also qualify for a partial reduction in the proportion of CGT they have to pay.)

Another risk is that a future government could reintroduce death duties, McQueen says. "An adviser might recommend buying properties in a trust structure, but trusts could be targeted for tax too," he says.

Give them a cash gift One of the most popular ways to help out, as shown in the REST Industry Super survey, is to gift your children money either from super or from other investments or savings that can go towards a deposit - or, if you are really generous, cover the entire deposit.

The issue with gifting is that the money cannot be easily recovered if parents need it later in life as they grow older.

"I've seen it a lot," says Suzanne Haddan, managing director of Sydney-based BFG Financial Services. "People reach their 80s and need all sorts of medical treatments. Because they have helped the kids so much, things can be very tight." Other things to consider, Haddan says, include the potential for a relationship breakdown and the possibility that your child may pass away.

She suggests the adult child and partner enter into a binding legal agreement, which states what happens to assets in a breakdown, and also that they buy the property as "tenants in common" rather than under the more common joint tenancy arrangement.

"Under tenants in common, if you die, your share does not automatically go to your partner as it would under joint tenancy, so you can control who you leave it to," Haddan adds. "The bottom line is you should see a solicitor. You should also periodically review your will and super, [as well as] structures that hold your assets. People stuff up even after they seek legal advice. They put assets in the wrong name or tax structure."

Be wary if you gift money in retirement or as you transition into retirement.

For parents drawing a government pension, your payout could be affected if you exceed "deprivation" (or gifting) rules, which limit gifts to $10,000 a year and no more than $30,000 over a five-year period.

For those who are retiring early having reached preservation age (55 for those born before July 1, 1960) and accessing their super through a transition to retirement income stream, they can receive up to 10 per cent of their super each financial year and gift all or part of this to their children.

Provide a personal loan

Brisbane property lawyer Paul Brennan believes providing a loan, forgiven on death, is a better option than gifting the deposit, especially when providing a large sum of money that may never be recovered.

"The worry is that the property will be tied up in a divorce situation. So the loan is a tidy way of doing it - it is easier to refinance and get out before the trouble starts," he says.

This still needs to be carefully structured because the holder of the first mortgage (usually a bank) may be reluctant to provide financing or may only lend a smaller amount if a second loan or caveat is registered against the property.

Brennan advises that parents provide money through an unregistered loan secured by the property and specify the amount lent, interest and repayment date, which can be rolled over.

"The will of both parents can include a clause forgiving the loan on death, but parents should get it back when their children move up in the property world, especially if you have other kids," Brennan adds.

Be guarantor on their mortgageOne of the most popular ways parents can help out is by acting as a guarantor over part of the loan amount, by using the equity in their own home or investment properties.

This can increase their children's borrowing capacity, remove the need for paying a deposit or having to pay costly lenders' mortgage insurance (generally required when someone is borrowing more than 80 per cent of the value of the home).

This is just what 19-year-old Sunshine Coast beauty therapist Tayla Prentice turned to after finding that her $30,000 annual salary made it almost impossible to buy even a modest investment property.

Prentice, from Maroochydore, saved up $15,000 for a deposit for a one-bedroom investment unit in Mooloolaba while living at home, but because of her low income, could only borrow $240,000.

"My mortgage broker suggested my parents Tracy and Darren were an option," Prentice says.

With her parents acting as guarantor, she was able to borrow up to $300,000 and did not need a cash deposit, most of which she used to pay stamp duty.

"We were more than happy to do it and get her into the property market," her mum Tracy says. "Young kids can't save enough for the deposit they need. By the time they have saved more, prices have gone up."

Mortgage Choice broker Jonathan Lee says banks will generally do guarantor loans up to 20 per cent of the value of the mortgage with some also covering buying costs like stamp duty.

The risk is, of course, that if the child defaults, the banks can pursue the parents for up to 20 per cent of the loan amount to cover any shortfall when they sell the property. If they can't cover it, the banks can sell the parent's home to recover their costs.

For this reason, Lee says, banks generally won't accept guarantees from parents whose sole asset is the family home.

"Parents need other investments just in case something goes wrong.

"The banks don't want to put the parents out on the street," he says.

Speaking from experience in dealing with his clients, Brennan advises parents against being guarantors, especially when it is a big loan, because of the risk they may lose their home if their children default.

Also, he says, "parents usually deny that they understood that they would lose their home."

Teach them to saveNot all help needs to be financial. In some cases, being generous is a bad idea and can be disastrous for the parents.

Haddan says that before parents contemplate how much to give, they need tolook at the effect helping their kids will have on their own overall wealth position and financial goals.

"The emergency instruction you hear on an aeroplane - 'fit your own mask first, before assisting others' - applies here. You must make sure you don't create a financial headache for your own circumstances," Haddan says.

"The default position should not be 'what money can I give my kids'. It should be teaching them about the obligations of paying off a mortgage, making sure they know how to save and sticking to a budget.

"Money comes with obligations, like not doing that trip to London, and being able to manage your finances well."

And, she warns, your generosity can also hurt.

"Gifting money to your kids may allow them to buy a home, but it can also put them under great personal strain by masking their true financial circumstances."

Disclaimer

The information featured in Smart Investor Weekend is general in nature and does not take into account your objectives, financial situation or needs. You should consider the appropriateness of the

information to your own circumstances. Before making an investment, insurance or financial planning decision, you should consult a licensed professional who can advise you of whether your decision is appropriate.
direction: bearish for residential property affordability and first-home buyer demand

headline: Giles survives and thrives
intro: One of the harder-to-believe facts in Australian politics is that, as the Northern Territory brings down its budget on Tuesday, Adam Giles is still the Chief Minister.
text:
One of the harder-to-believe facts in Australian politics is that, as the Northern Territory brings down its budget on Tuesday, Adam Giles is still the Chief Minister.

You'll remember two months ago he was dumped in the middle of the night by his party - or he would have been if he hadn't locked himself in his Chief Minister office. Giles is still the boss. He even leaves the office from time to time.

Clearly Giles is a man more popular with the business set than his backbench. Just look at his newly formed advisory council. It includes Telstra's outgoing boss David Thodey, BCA chief executive Jennifer Westacott, Linfox's Peter Fox, Seven Group's Martin Ferguson, JP Morgan's Rod Eddington, former Rio Tinto boss David Peever - even Bespoke's Ian Smith.

They are advising the NT on its Northern Australia development plans.

And Giles would have added the Queen of the North, billionaire Gina Rinehart, but she's already the Territory's tourism ambassador.
direction: bullish for NT government-linked entities and infrastructure developers

headline: Bilateral bind
intro: Australia's $500 million aid to Indonesia - our largest aid recipient - may be due for a reduction regardless of the debate about the execution of drug traffickers, writes Greg Earl.
text:
Australia's $500 million aid to Indonesia - our largest aid recipient - may be due for a reduction regardless of the debate about the execution of drug traffickers, writes Greg Earl.

When Tony Abbott finally raised the t-word - tsunami - in the row with Indonesia over the death penalty this week, he opened the door on a bigger question: how much influence does Australia's aid program buy?

It is a measure of how fast the often tumultuous relationship has entered another downturn that a prime minister who came to office promising a Jakarta-first foreign policy is now threatening Australia's biggest gift to Indonesia since it turned a blind eye to the invasion of East Timor in 1975.

Abbott's political mentor, John Howard, has described the $1 billion aid boost that Australia gave Indonesia after the 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami as reaching out to a friend in a time of a genuine need.

"By the end of 2005, the bilateral relationship with Indonesia had become stronger than at any time in recent memory," he writes in his autobiography.

But in words that have a lot more resonance today, he goes on to say: "It would never be a trouble-free association. Instances of young Australians ensnared in the tough anti-drug laws of Indonesia ... although difficult, were never going to derail the association."

After days of calls to end the aid program on talkback radio, and newspaper letters in response to the looming executions of two Australian drug couriers, Abbott pulled the diplomatic lever Australia's Indonesia policymakers often wonder about at times of bilateral stress.

"Let's not forget that a few years ago when Indonesia was struck by the Indian Ocean tsunami, Australia sent a billion dollars' worth of assistance," he said, pointing out that Australia was "always there to help you and we hope that you might reciprocate in this way at this time".

Many people would be surprised to discover that years after the official end of the five-year $1 billion tsunami aid program, Australia contributed $584 million in development aid to Indonesia last year when Indonesia was actually a so-called net negative aid recipient. That means it was paying back $US107 million ($137 million) in accrued aid-related debt thanks to several years of strong economic growth.

Every prime minister over the past 10 years has basked in the way Australian aid to Indonesia has steadily risen, through the security assistance after the 2001 Bali bombing, the tsunami aid which went well beyond reconstruction, the general aid increases to meet the Millennium Development Goals and then the efforts to manage asylum seekers.

As a result, Indonesia has long been Australia's largest aid recipient, absorbing 10.8 per cent of this year's $5 billion spending. It has overtaken Australia's erstwhile main responsibility in Papua New Guinea, which gets 10 per cent.

When Abbott cut that $5 billion by 30 per cent in the December mid-year economic and financial outlook, it was widely thought that his Jakarta-first election campaign rhetoric would protect the Indonesia spending. Foreign Minister Julie Bishop appeared to confirm that this week.

Now one of Australia's most experienced aid officials and an overseer of the original tsunami spending has had an apostasy and says it is time to bite the bullet and redress the way Indonesia dominates the aid budget.

Robin Davies, who no longer works for the Australian government and is an academic at the Australian National University's Development Policy Centre, says: "This is arguably the right time to make a step-change in our aid relationship with Indonesia, based on the fact that it is an emerging middle power. It would be better to cut quickly and cleanly rather than year after year."

He says that while Australia has a degree of influence in Indonesia because of the large aid program, it is certainly not five times as much influence as it would have if the aid program was only $100 million a year.

And in a detailed analysis of how the government could manage the historically unprecedented 30 per cent aid cut due to be revealed in the May budget, Davies is suggesting cutting the Indonesian program by more than half to $234 million. This would provide almost one-third of the aid program cuts the government has committed to.

He says that Australia stands out among developed-world aid donors in having an aid program so exposed to the fortunes of only two neighbouring countries.

And the Indonesian commitment looks unusual to more distant foreign observers because the Jakarta government is well known for trying to reduce the influence that aid donors have over its activities now it has recovered from the Asian financial crisis.

"The government is less and less interested in what external donors have to say," Davies says.

This advice, from such an experienced aid official, might seem like manna from heaven for a prime minister desperate for budget savings to redeem his economic credibility and clearly alarmed about being caught out by a populist backlash against executions of Australians.

But other former experienced government officials say Abbott may have now dealt himself into a corner by playing the tsunami card.

They argue that Indonesia would have accepted a big proportionate aid cut as part of an Australian government budget shake-up but will now be more likely to react in a nationalistic way to a cut seen as punishment for executions.

"Indonesia could just reject the aid program entirely and that would leave Australia with no influence at all, which would be a very bad outcome," one observer says.

Davies agrees: "To have some influence, you don't have to spend a lot of money. But I think this is now a really difficult policy choice for the government."
direction: negative for bilateral trade and diplomatic stability

headline: Buzz builds over Harley LiveWire
intro: Iconic brand courts new riders with sleek electric model.
text:
Iconic brand courts new riders with sleek electric model.

Steadfast Group chief executive and die-hard Harley-Davidson fan Robert Kelly has this to say about the American giant's electric motorcycle prototype, LiveWire: "I would own one - and I wait with bated breath to see what they can really do with it.

"I am fascinated with technology change, but part of owning a Harley-Davidson is the distinctive noise, so it'll be interesting to see what happens," said Mr Kelly, who owns several H-D motorbikes.

It's a sentiment echoed by former Colonial First State fund manager Oliver Ansted, who rides a Fat Bob H-D. "I'd definitely be interested in riding one - Harley is reinventing itself."

Project LiveWire, H-D's prototype electric motorcycle, is so distinctly un-Harley-like in its features that it has piqued the curiosity of existing customers and a younger audience who previously might not have given H-D's products a second glance.

Instead of H-D motorbikes' signature throaty rumble, LiveWire - arguably a two-wheeled equivalent of the Tesla electric car - is suspiciously quiet at idle. It is nimble, has a more aggressive sitting position compared with its relaxed cruiser counterparts, and sounds like a toned-down jet engine taking off when the throttle is wound open. The styling is slick and the paint stealthy black - a departure from the flashy chrome often adorning other H-D machines.

H-D is keeping mum on how much the New York Stock Exchange-listed company has pumped into research and development of the prototype, which has been in the works for the past four years.

But the purpose of the project was clear: the 112-year-old motorcycling behemoth needed to reinvent itself and inject new blood into its customer base - or battle serious risks to its bottom line associated with a rapidly ageing population.

Not that Harley is struggling right now. H-D dealers sold 267,999 new motorcycles worldwide last year - up 2.7 per cent on 2013 - while retail unit sales in the Asia-Pacific region jumped 11.8 per cent, representing the largest growth area for the company. H-D grew earnings by more than 18 per cent last year, on revenue of $US6 billion, and the company's share price has risen 145 per cent over the past five years to $US63.

But Matt Levatich, who was recently promoted to become the new global boss for H-D, openly admitted last year that the group's core customers were getting old - fast - and the company needed to diversify its audience.

"It's a way of attracting younger people … who aren't currently riders to our brand," said Greg Willis, Harley Davidson's Asia-Pacific director, of the electric motorcycle.

"We want people to start thinking about Harley a bit differently. A project like LiveWire … really [is] there to hopefully make Harley-Davidson relevant to that younger audience, particularly urban young adults."

H-D brought several LiveWire motorcycles to Sepang, Malaysia, as part of its world tour in March. Mr Willis was reluctant to reveal too much technical and development information about the motorcycle, which is smaller than most conventional Harleys. There are still issues with battery and technology developments that limit the range of LiveWire, and the company is keeping quiet about production costs.

It is also important to note that, like some prototypes, H-D might never release LiveWire to the market.

"We want feedback from our customers about the bike; what they want from something like this - this is what the project is about," Mr Willis said.

H-D has been encouraging customers around the world to sign up for tests to gauge their interest. Reports suggest that the prototype, which joins other electric motorcycles such as Zero and Brammo, could cost $50,000 if it came into production - arguably a hefty price tag when compared with conventional motorcycles.

The writer travelled to Malaysia courtesy of Harley-Davidson as part of H-D's LiveWire press tour.
direction: Bullish for Harley-Davidson (H-D) shares

headline: Schools target China's $53b market
intro: Shanghai | A Perth-based private school believes the market for education services in China is bigger than iron ore, as Australian schools increasingly look to the world's second-biggest economy to supplement federal government grants.
text:
Shanghai | A Perth-based private school believes the market for education services in China is bigger than iron ore, as Australian schools increasingly look to the world's second-biggest economy to supplement federal government grants.

Perth's St Stephen's School and Melbourne's Haileybury are among those that believe money earned in China could allow them to keep fees down at home, while also offering students a more international education.

Haileybury will officially open its Beijing campus next month, while St Stephen's is set to begin a trial of its vocational training program in China next year.

"We need to look for more innovative ways to fund ourselves," said Tony George, the principal of St Stephen's. "That meant our board had to review its risk appetite."

Private schools are facing increased funding pressure as costs have risen by an average of 5 per cent a year over the past decade, while government grants have only risen by 3 per cent.

The push by both schools into China fits neatly with the vision of Trade Minister Andrew Robb, who believes Australian education exporters could be teaching 10 million overseas students a year in less than a decade.

The sector is the fourth-largest export earner worth $16.7 billion annually, but enrols well under a million students a year.

Both St Stephen's and Haileybury are not looking to bring students to Australia, rather provide services to Chinese students in China.

"This is the market which we reckon is really taking off," said Nicholas Dwyer, founding principal of the Haileybury International School in Beijing.

"We have an Australian school but it is for Chinese students."

Haileybury's Beijing campus had its "soft opening" with 200 students last September and will double in size next school year. At full capacity it will have 1200 students who will pay around $35,000 a year for boarding.

The school offers the traditional Chinese curriculum, with a heavy emphasis on English, for students up to year 9. Students then switch to Victoria's VCE curriculum for their final three years.

"This is a way we can use our expertise in China to deliver revenue back to Australia," said Mr Dwyer.

"This should allow us to keep fees down and do more with our kids."

Haileybury was approached to start the school by a property group developing a new community on the edge of Beijing. It built the campus and engaged Haileybury as the operator.

The first group of 40 Haileybury students from Melbourne will arrive at the Beijing campus next week for an exchange.

Haileybury has had a presence in China for the last 15 years when it began offering VCE programs to local schools.

St Stephen's is new to China, but like Haileybury it is targeting the Chinese market, rather than expatriates, or those wishing to come to Australia.

"There is a bigger market for education in China than iron ore," said St Stephen's principal Tony George.

That is a difficult statement to verify, but a report by Chinese internet firm Tencent found that China's primary and secondary education market was worth 255 billion yuan annually ($53 billion).

That's still less than the value of Australia's iron ore exports to China, which topped $57 billion last financial year.

But while iron ore demand is set to fall this year, spending on education is forecast to rise in line with consumer spending which is growing at around 7 per cent.

St Stephen's Mr George said a trial of its vocational education programs will begin at a school in the Inner Mongolian city of Erdos next year. The challenge then is to quickly scale up."There could be some significant revenue that comes simply because of the volume," Mr George said.

The plan is to offer courses in business management, information technology and English. They will be delivered via a blended model, where St Stephen's will provide the curriculum and train the Chinese teachers, who will then conduct the classes. Feedback and mentoring will be provided online.

"Not-for-profit organisations like us need to innovate and look for alternative sources of funding," said Mr George. "Australia's opportunity is to be an education services provider to the region."

Key points

Perth's St Stephen's School and Melbourne's Haileybury are setting up in China.

St Stephen's claims demand for education is strong enough to rival iron ore.
direction: bullish for private education providers

headline: Weatherill flags radical state tax shake-up
intro: South Australia could introduce its own personal income tax surcharge and abolish stamp duty for house purchases under options put forward by Premier Jay Weatherill for a radical restructure of the state's tax system to head off a budget crisis.
text:
South Australia could introduce its own personal income tax surcharge and abolish stamp duty for house purchases under options put forward by Premier Jay Weatherill for a radical restructure of the state's tax system to head off a budget crisis.

The Labor government, which unexpectedly won the state election in March 2014, has commissioned a review into its taxation system - a move designed to help build public support for altering what is taxed and by how much. "The state government is open to radical reform to our state taxation system," Mr Weatherill said.

"Nothing is out of bounds, be it taxes, levies or concessions."

Mr Weatherill said he didn't want the debate to focus immediately on expanding or increasing the goods and services tax, a highly divisive issue. Other options such as being able to impose a surcharge on top of income federal taxes were a better proposition, he said. The resultant "interstate tax competition" would improve efficiency because tax competition had been shown in the past to be a powerful force.

Mr Weatherill said his government was prepared to take on radical tax reform, but it needed to be balanced. Some of the options were to cut stamp duty on real estate transactions, introduce a broad-based land tax of around $1200 a year for an average value home, and overhaul insurance and gambling taxes.

Mr Weatherill and his Treasurer, Tom Koutsantonis, released a State Tax Review Discussion Paper on Wednesday. They are seeking public submissions until April 10 and Deloitte Access Economics has been appointed to undertake independent modelling of the various proposals.

The potential tax overhaul comes against the backdrop of the looming demise of the automotive industry, with General Motors Holden preparing to stop car manufacturing in Adelaide's north by 2017 at the latest, and a rapidly ageing population which is the oldest on mainland Australia. The discussion paper outlines that the number of working-aged people to those aged 65 years and over in SA is projected to fall from 3.8 people currently to 2.7 people by 2031 and 2.3 people by 2061.

The reduction in the working-age population will deliver a big hit to the tax base due to the decline in payroll tax collections and reductions in stamp duty collections because older people are generally less active in the property market, while the demand for health services will rise.

SA collected $599 million in stamp duty from residential property transactions in 2013-14, with the total stamp duty take reaching $793 million. This was 22 per cent of the total tax take. The projections for 2014-15 are for conveyancing duty and stamp duty to reach $886 million out of an expected $4.4 billion in total state taxes. But the biggest single source of revenue for the SA government is GST grants, which in 2014-15 are projected to be $4.96 billion, or 30 per cent of total revenue.

Executive director of the Property Council in SA, Daniel Gannon, said stamp duty was a barrier to investment, growth and stimulation of the economy. "South Australia's tax regime is the worst in the nation, and that's why it's time to take an axe to the anti-growth tax in stamp duty," Mr Gannon said.

In the discussion paper, one of the options floated is to move to a flat rate of annual land tax in the order of 0.6 per cent on all land. The paper says that stamp duties are expected to raise between $900 million to $1.2 billion each year over the next few year.
direction: bearish for SA property and construction stocks

headline: HARD BUSINESS
intro: Two rural women explain how to be influential when you're away from the big smoke - and you have dodgy internet access, writes Claire Stewart.
text:
Two rural women explain how to be influential when you're away from the big smoke - and you have dodgy internet access, writes Claire Stewart.

Before Georgie Somerset accepts a midweek meeting invite in Brisbane, or ponders an evening function to upgrade her skills, she weighs the benefits of attending against the 7.5-hour round trip to get there.

While long-distance travel is an accepted part of life outside urban areas, it adds a layer of complexity to building leadership and influence, particularly if that influence is to be wielded among city decision makers.

Somerset says Australia's urbanites need to hear more of the regional perspective, particularly as they're usually the ones developing the policy that affects regional Australia. But that requires more boots on the ground, more face-to-face conversations.

"You have to make an effort to engage regional Australians, because you're not as likely to encounter them incidentally if you're not seeing them at functions or at restaurants or meetings," she says. "Women know that it's difficult to be 'incidentally noticed', and even more so for regional leaders … where you need to be in the [physical] space when people are thinking, 'Who would I like on this board? Who do I want on this committee, or to be involved in this focus group?"'

Lynise Conaghan, who has spent years lobbying for change in access to early childhood resources and teacher numbers for small schools (think 25 children or fewer across eight year groups), is even more isolated than Georgie Somerset.

Out where Conaghan lives, on the inland road halfway between Rockhampton and Mackay in Queensland, there's no mobile reception and the internet is so slow it's almost unusable.

Both women accept that it's the nature of the beast to be cut off from the blow-by-blow goings-on of peers doing similar jobs or in similar leadership positions as themselves. Teleconferencing and social media engagement help, to an extent.

Somerset is selective about what to get involved in, but says technology enables her to manage an increased load. This workload includes running a successful brand in the processed beef market she built with her husband; taking on the presidency of the Queensland Rural, Regional and Remote Women's Network; her role as a board member of the Queensland Rural Adjustment Authority; and involvement in various government forums, including the regional telecommunications independent review committee.

"I have been teleconferencing for nearly 30 years," she says. "It's fantastic but at a broader level there is no substitute for being physically present and I think you have to be quite established to be able to participate remotely, effectively."

Conaghan, who is also part of a family-run beef feedlot operation, says being in a telecommunications black spot curtails her ability to use social media the way Somerset does - "like an office water cooler". She says it's becoming a critical issue.

"To a degree, we are getting further and further behind these incredible women in urban locations because of the digital divide."

Just under a third of Australia's population live in regional and remote areas. In Queensland it's higher, with nearly 40 per cent of people living outside a major city.

Part of Conaghan's frustration is with how easily people forget that regional areas have to operate differently. NAPLAN testing may soon be moved online, which is fine for people who have internet access, she says. And a government department put out a new app the other day, she says, which looks fantastic … "But guess what? We don't have mobile telephone services ..."

Conaghan hopes better representation by rural women on boards and reference groups with their urban peers will bridge that gap, because nothing can replace old-fashioned networking and lobbying.

"We got the federal funding for Building the Education Revolution. We were told we had to have an undercover area, or a library.

"I thought, 'That's really silly; we don't need more buildings because we hardly have any kids. What we do need is a pool so the kids can learn to swim, and qualify for regional competitions."'

Conaghan won. Her part of the world might not have a local store, or mail that's delivered more than twice a week, but it now has a tiny school with a brand new 25-metre pool.W
direction: neutral to slightly bearish for regional-focused equities

headline: Growing appetite leads to battle for baby bottles
intro: Supplies are rationed as demand intensifies.
text:
Supplies are rationed as demand intensifies.

For the big supermarket chains, it's as much about staff safety as supply.

Signs in Mandarin are now commonplace on Coles and Woolworths shelves around the country, advising customers on strict limits on buying tins of infant formula.

"We've had some fairly ugly scenes here," said one senior staff member at Woolworths's Airlie Beach supermarket, behind the main tourist drag.

"Chinese travellers can basically fund their entire stay by sending baby formula back to China. You get five or six broke backpackers being told 'no, you can't have 40 tins of formula' and it can get heated at the checkout. We've had some incidents."

In a town like Airlie Beach the raids can clean out the shelves of certain brands. "The deliveries aren't as regular here as Brisbane, and we don't want mums coming in and finding out we won't have their product, but head office seems to manage it pretty well."

Australia's fastest-growing infant formula brands, Bellamy's Organic and a2 Milk, are familiar with the rationing and the heated exchanges it creates.

"I have heard that happen, both to us and the vitamin industry, where semi-riots have broken out," Bellamy's chief executive Laura McBain said. "[The rationing] is there for a reason. The demand offshore, particularly from China, for our products is huge. It's just been an incredible year as a result of that demand."

Bellamy's revenue for the year to June 30 jumped 153 per cent to $131 million. Net profit meanwhile surged 617 per cent to $9.1 million.

The company, which listed on the ASX in 2014, began investigating opportunities in the Chinese market in 2007 and in 2012 established a Chinese offshoot. But Ms McBain says the grey market has been lucrative.

In the first six months of the 2015 calendar year, about $27 million worth of Bellamy's infant formula was bought in Australia and onsold to China.

"We have to be practical about this in the sense that Australia has 300,000 babies born every year. We haven't suddenly seen a spike in the birth rate and we haven't seen a spike in formula feeding rates, so a lot of growth in Australia is coming from this demand out of China. There are 20 million babies in China; 300,000 babies in Australia. You don't need to be a rocket scientist to fill in the maths on that."

This presents an opportunity for Bellamy's, which created its own global online store. Tins of baby formula can be bought from the site for about half the price than from Chinese retailers because of reduced costs associated with distribution - and it allows Bellamy's to participate in the same "cross-border transactions" as the grey market. "It's very exciting for us," Ms McBain said.

Demand for foreign infant formula has risen in China since 2008, when a Chinese infant formula was contaminated with melamine, killing six babies and hospitalising another 54,000.

Although China's economy has stumbled this year, sparking steep sell-offs on sharemarkets around the world amid fears a slowing China would dampen global growth, Ms McBain expected the demand for infant formula to continue.

"Even though there are issues within the China economy, the demand for infant formula won't change and the aspirational demand around organic, and Australian made and imported products is continuing to climb.

"Mums and dads in China are the same as mums and dads everywhere in the world. They want to give their babies the very best opportunity and the very best start in life."

Indeed, sales haven't been slowing for a2 Milk's Platinum branded formula, which launched in August 2013. It has become the second-biggest infant formula player in Australia, according to Coles' sales data compiled by research firm Nielsen. Total Platinum sales leapt 445 per cent to $41.67 million for the 12 months to June 30.

A2 chief executive Geoff Babidge said its infant formula sales were on track to triple this financial year.

"Our sales for the first two months in FY16, July and August, we estimate to be in the order of $13 million just for two months - that's just infant formula in Australia," he said when a2 released its annual financial results last month.

A2's Australian CEO Peter Nathan said Australian shoppers and Chinese consumers were fuelling that growth.

"We have had significant growth on online sites such as Alibaba, and also at retail level at grocery and pharmacy where Chinese tourists and nationals are often buying products on trips and taking it back with them."
direction: bullish for infant formula manufacturers like Bellamy's and a2 Milk

headline: BBY's Rosewall still on ASIC panel
intro: Former executive chairman of failed stockbroker BBY Glenn Rosewall remains a member of an official panel charged with taking action over breaches of the market integrity rules, despite claims BBY might have misused client funds, misled its lenders, and breached the Corporations Act.
text:
Former executive chairman of failed stockbroker BBY Glenn Rosewall remains a member of an official panel charged with taking action over breaches of the market integrity rules, despite claims BBY might have misused client funds, misled its lenders, and breached the Corporations Act.

An explosive report from administrator KPMG on Friday said there might be a shortfall of up to $10 million in BBY client money, while also alleging "misleading" information had been provided to BBY's lender.

While the claims are concerning for BBY's directors, a spokesman for the Australian Securities and Investments Commission confirmed on Sunday Mr Rosewall was still a member of ASIC's markets disciplinary panel.

The panel, made up of part-time members, is responsible for making decisions on issuing infringement notices and whether to accept enforceable undertakings over alleged breaches.

ASIC also confirmed it would consider further investigation and action regarding BBY if the administrators formally notified ASIC of breaches of the Corporations Act.

"ASIC has been in contact with the administrators throughout their administration and und6erstood that they were developing some of the concerns that have been highlighted," the spokesman said.

The disciplinary panel that Mr Rosewall is part of includes more than 20 part-time members, who are appointed by ASIC, with chairman Lisa Gay convening three-member panels to look at individual matters.

It is understood Mr Rosewall is not a member of any panel that is hearing a matter, and he will not be appointed to panels to look at new matters.

Mr Rosewall could not be contacted for comment on Sunday. Administrators were appointed to BBY last month.
direction: bearish for BBY and broader broker sector

headline: Teneo advising BHP on Brazil's Samarco disaster
intro: We've long chronicled the reliance of mining major BHP Billiton on international consulting firm Teneo Holdings. The Big Australian's chairman, Jac Nasser, is close to Teneo boss Declan Kelly, and for some time the former Ford Motor Company chief and current 21st Century Fox director had the Manhattan-based firm on a whopping monthly retainer of $600,000 (but since reduced to less than half that amount). Nice work if you can get it.
text:
We've long chronicled the reliance of mining major BHP Billiton on international consulting firm Teneo Holdings. The Big Australian's chairman, Jac Nasser, is close to Teneo boss Declan Kelly, and for some time the former Ford Motor Company chief and current 21st Century Fox director had the Manhattan-based firm on a whopping monthly retainer of $600,000 (but since reduced to less than half that amount). Nice work if you can get it.

So it's nice to hear Andrew Mackenzie is extracting some value for his money. Teneo's São Paulo office is now "providing support to BHP Billiton on the response" to the recent disaster at its (and Vale's) Samarco iron ore mine. Teneo's local expertise has been particularly helpful. Like me after several bottles of Monte Velho Branco, they speak fluent Portuguese.

In an investigation into the firm's links with the Clinton family's fundraising machine, New York Times columnist Maureen Dowd described Teneo as "a scammy blend of corporate consulting, public relations and merchant banking". It is also a favourite adviser of Darwin-born, Brisbane-educated and Bahamas-tanned Andrew Liveris, executive chairman of Dow Chemical Company. And just a fortnight after Sepp Blatter was re-elected president of FIFA on May 29 this year, Teneo was called upon to advise the scandal-ridden sporting body. What a roll call!
direction: bearish for BHP Billiton

headline: Digital highway bypasses stockmarkets
intro: Ask many of the world's brightest investors to name a book that has moulded their mind the most, and chances are they'll answer One Up on Wall Street. Peter Lynch's investment manual is littered with gems that are just as relevant for institutional giants as small-time stock pickers. In fact he emphasises the edge the average investor often has over the professional if they pay attention to the world around them.
text:
Ask many of the world's brightest investors to name a book that has moulded their mind the most, and chances are they'll answer One Up on Wall Street. Peter Lynch's investment manual is littered with gems that are just as relevant for institutional giants as small-time stock pickers. In fact he emphasises the edge the average investor often has over the professional if they pay attention to the world around them.

The behaviour of households is a powerful driver of the economy and corporate profits, so it's well worth considering our own ever-evolving consumption patterns in our investment process. At the moment an audit of what we're doing with our time doesn't bode well for many businesses on the Australian Securities Exchange.

Take the free-to-air television sector for example - it's been battered by profit warnings as eyeballs and advertising dollars flee.

Charlie Aitken of Aitken Investment Management, who recently set up his own investment fund, says it's a powerful demonstration of Lynch's mantra. "You look at your own children and they don't watch free-to-air television. Whereas you look at Apple - everything they have is an Apple product," he says.

"I'm always paying Apple. There is massive investing insight in looking at how your family behaves."

The assault on free-to-air business models as eyes shift to iPads and smartphones is one of many investment insights that we can glean from within our own homes.

For Jason Sedawie, who runs a globally focused fund, Decisive Asset Management, investing in the companies of products he may consume is more than a source of good ideas. The approach also gives comfort to investors in his fund who know that when there's macro-induced panic gripping global equity markets, the flow of dollars in their direction won't halt.

Sedawie writes a weekly investment blog on global stocks and I tapped his brain for trade ideas based on what I've seen in everyday life.

One is the rise of television shows, that we don't actually watch on TV. I prefer House of Cards but the popularity of Game of Thrones seems to exceed it. Australian households may have a notorious reputation for pirating but many would appreciate the money-spinning potential of Time Warner (TWX:NYSE), which owns HBO, the producer of the show.

Sedawie is an investor in Time Warner and believes that HBO's online presence will be a great catalyst for the company.

"Similar to Netflix, online viewing is a much better experience on demand, no ads. They also own Warner Brothers which can add more content to their programming," Sedawie says.

I'm more of a sports fan and asked about two sports-related stocks that I can't ignore.

As a diehard Arsenal fan, I'd rather short the shares of a team I despise - Manchester United (MANU:NYSE), which is listed on the New York Stock Exchange - than buy their stock.

But I can still appreciate this 113-year-old football team's brand is among the most captivating in the world.

The problem is that football teams haven't proved to be great business models. The star players command enormous salaries but once you accept, just like investment banking, the stars will capture a large share of the economic returns, then there's an investment case to be made.

In fact live sports is one of the few reasons most people still watch free-to-air television. The only reason I have a Foxtel subscription is to watch Arsenal in the wee hours.

"MANU is a play on TV sports rights.

"Love them or hate them [I hate them], MANU command more than 51 per cent of the Premier League's entire global TV audience," Sedawie says. "They are monetising their brand in Asia and in the US, where soccer is becoming more popular."

Another sports-related stock I asked about is Under Armour [UA:NYSE] - "the Nike of the new generation". They make great gear - although they do sponsor Tottenham Hotspur, the one team I hate more than Manchester United.

"Their clothes can be seen on campuses and upcoming sport stars worldwide. UA is an expensive stock, but it's still predominantly an American company at a $19 billion market cap that could eventually grow into Nike's $91.5 billion market value."

But Sedawie says at a price to earnings multiple of over 70 times, it doesn't come cheap. In just three years, UA has soared by 10 times in value.

These examples show that in a global wired world, the threats and opportunities for investors are borderless. But Credit Suisse's equity strategist Hasan Tevfik says there are a couple of examples of applying Lynch's outlook to the local equity market.

One would be to avoid Telstra in favour of its smaller, nimbler competitors like TPG, M2 and Vocus, which are capturing more revenues from the telco giant. But Tevfik agrees that many local stocks are threatened by trends imported by global businesses. For instance the retail-focused real estate investment trusts (REITs) aren't helped by the shift towards online shopping, with no real outlet on the exchange to profit from this trend.

The forces of disruption are being felt most strongly by one business that touches our lives more than any other - Woolworths - which along with Coles once accounted for 40 cents out of every dollar we spend.

But in recent months Woolworths has come under siege from ferocious low-cost competitor Aldi.

What is fascinating about this is that Aldi is a private company. One listed company is losing, but not at the expense of another. Woolworths' shareholder profits are being divided among Aldi's private owners and, fortunately, consumers who are saving money. That's good news for us but for investors it presents a challenge.

The forces of change aren't just threatening some listed companies at the expense of others, but the equity market as a whole. Tevfik is concerned that more of the opportunities we see in everyday life are no longer trickling back to investors in the form of shareholder returns but are being scooped up by private owners.

That's evident in one of his major investment themes - the rise of "selfies" - or self-managed superannuation funds. More evidence suggests rival boutique fund managers are gaining share of these assets at the expense of listed giants like AMP.

More generally, as we've seen with taxi disrupter Uber, these capital-light companies don't need equity markets to grow, just to cash out when they've created enough wealth. That's positive for households if the service or product they're providing is better or cheaper, but in deploying those savings, it's a little harder to act on Lynch's advice. One up on Wall Street indeed.
direction: bearish for free-to-air television sector

headline: Western Australia must harvest the fruits of reform
intro: The 'R' word has historically been difficult in WA because powerful vested interests have resisted change.
text:
WA's economic brains trust has a road map for change. Now the state must put vested interests to one side and trust in the rewards which will come.

Western Australian farmer Tony Galati is being sued in the Supreme Court for growing potatoes, accused of breaching a commercial agreement with the state's potato watchdog and flooding the market with starchy tubers.

You would think an organisation called the Potato Marketing Corporation would limit itself to promoting the merits of mashed over fried, but in Western Australia they control who grows potatoes, how many and what variety.

Welcome to WA's kafkaesque regulatory system, where a hardware store can sell an electrical light fitting but not a decorative light fitting and a petrol station can sell "food" but is not permitted to sell canned fruit, vegetables, meat or seafood.

It would be funny if it weren't so serious. Western Australia has long reneged on a reform agenda.

Scrapping the Marketing of Potatoes Act 1946 and the Marketing of Potatoes Regulations 1987 would benefit the economy by $44 million over 15 years through lower prices, increased production and improved quality, says the Economic Regulatory Authority (ERA), WA's independent economic adviser.

Recently, plummeting iron ore prices and further declines in WA's share of GST have left our economy vulnerable. To add insult to injury, the other states have been quick to point out our lack of deregulation and privatisation played a significant part in our current challenges.

The truth is that our black swans are really ostriches with their heads in the sand when it comes to reform.

Our recent history is littered with examples. In 1995 the Hilmer review recommended the full deregulation of trading hours across the country. WA failed at three attempts to make those changes and lost $23 million in incentive payments.

The Chamber of Commerce and Industry has included retail trading hours reform in every reform proposal presented to government, including pre-election policies in the 2008 and 2013 state elections, and most recently the ERA Microeconomic Reform inquiry.

Last year the ERA produced a 487-page, fully vetted and analysed economic reform agenda that includes 46 recommendations covering deregulation, privatisation and state taxes.

Economic reform can cost political skin and while it's easier to do nothing, that does not make it the right thing to do. Real leadership is making the tough decisions in the best interests of everyone and having the confidence to see through those reforms.

Under the Richard Court government, in which I was the premier's chief of staff from 1999 to 2001, there was a privatisation agenda with the sale of Alinta, the Dampier to Bunbury pipeline, Bankwest and a 50-year lease on the rail freight network.

In 2006, Labor's Mark McGowan reformed liquor licensing regulation and introduced so called "small bar licences" to make it easier to open and run small-scale licensed premises.

At the time, Rio Tinto's Sam Walsh described the existing system as "ridiculous" and observed that making Perth a more liveable city would help attract and retain talented people. The CCI lobbied the upper house to support the changes.

Since the legislative change in 2007, Perth's small bar scene is thriving with 88 new establishments having opened making the city more vibrant and appealing to locals and tourists.

There is a clear roadmap for a leader intent on reform, although the "r" word has historically been difficult in WA because powerful vested interests - namely unions and businesses flourishing within protected industries - have resisted change.

Modern leaders intent on reform would tackle electricity first, where taxpayer subsidies to government-owned utilities are forecast to top $1.9 billion over the next four years. CCI has long held the view that the government must structurally separate and privatise Synergy, the dominant state-owned generator/retailer and privatise Western Power, the monopoly network service provider.

Privatisation will shore up the balance sheet and free up money to be spent on hospitals or better roads.

There are other government-controlled entities that should be considered for privatisation, such as the TAB.

As the Queensland experience has shown, asset sales are not for the faint-hearted, which puts the spotlight on deregulation.

In addition to potato marketing and trading hours, the taxi industry is another sector of low-hanging fruit where the cost of plates and their limited availability artificially inflates prices and constrains capacity during peak times.

Some of these reforms will meet with resistance. It is the job of leaders to provide a broader perspective to tough issues and to bring the people with them. Western Australians can see what is happening to the state's bank balance and are sophisticated enough to support reform.

Our leaders simply need to trust them.

Deidre Willmott is the chief executive officer of the Chamber of Commerce and Industry of Western Australia.
direction: bearish for WA economy and related sectors

headline: Virgin Australia means business on loss-making international division
intro: Virgin Australia Holdings will seek to improve its loss-making international division by re-timing flights to Bali, adding business class to its trans-Tasman flights and upgrading its long-haul business class product.
text:
Virgin Australia Holdings will seek to improve its loss-making international division by re-timing flights to Bali, adding business class to its trans-Tasman flights and upgrading its long-haul business class product.

The airline reported a $10.2 million first-half underlying profit on Thursday, compared with a $45.4 million loss the prior year.

But on a bottom-line basis it reported a $47.8 million statutory loss, which was an improvement from a $74.3 million loss.

Virgin's domestic division, which includes its Velocity frequent flyer business, reported a $103.8 million profit before interest and tax, up from

$25.9 million last year.

But its smaller international division sunk to a $49.5 million earnings before interest and tax (EBIT) loss, from a $31.9 million loss the prior year.

Rival Qantas Airways is expected to report an underlying profit from its international division for the first time in years next week and an overall underlying first-half profit of $300 million to $350 million.

Virgin chief executive John Borghetti said his airline's international division was a small but important part of its business that was underperforming in the short-haul Asian market and flights to Europe via the Middle East.

But despite the difficulties, he said Virgin would keep flying to Bali and to Abu Dhabi. "You get pressures on routes every day," Mr Borghetti told The Australian Financial Review. "There are some domestic routes that we have pressure on that aren't profitable. But it doesn't mean you pull out. It doesn't mean you redeploy the aircraft. It means you adjust your model accordingly and you make it work."

Mr Borghetti said low-cost subsidiary Tigerair Australia would be "guaranteed" to begin international flights at some point but he declined to provide details amid speculation it could replace some, but not all, of Virgin's services to leisure destinations like Bali.

Macquarie Equities analyst Sam Dobson said Tigerair's second-quarter profit, the first quarter in the black for the budget airline since 2010, was a "key positive" for Virgin.

Virgin reported a 3 per cent rise in yields, or returns on fares, in the domestic market, despite weak consumer confidence. The airline announced plans last year to introduce fully-flat business seats, all with aisle access, on its A330 flights between Perth and the east coast from next month. Qantas began deploying a similar product on its A330s in December. However, Virgin's rollout has now been delayed until mid-year.

The lower fuel price is also helping Virgin, with a $50 million benefit expected in the second half, up from $3 million in the first half.

Virgin said it retained participation in 25 per cent of any further oil price falls for the rest of the half. Qantas has not updated its figure since November, when it said it retained 70 per cent participation.
direction: Virgin Australia shares face pressure due to worsening international division losses despite domestic profit growth

headline: IAG's new CEO to refocus on Asia
intro: The change in chief executive at Insurance Australia Group from Mike Wilkins to Peter Harmer will trigger a renewed focus on Asia as the company tries to break the shackles of the low growth Australian and New Zealand general insurance markets.
text:
The change in chief executive at Insurance Australia Group from Mike Wilkins to Peter Harmer will trigger a renewed focus on Asia as the company tries to break the shackles of the low growth Australian and New Zealand general insurance markets.

The expansion in higher risk emerging markets could take many years to bear fruit. That suggests shareholders not only need to be patient but they should also contemplate the possibility of being exposed to a rising level of earnings volatility.

Wilkins leaves the organisation in good shape notwithstanding the $60 million loss recorded on IAG's foray into regional insurance markets in China.

He hands over a company with solid and reliable earnings. That was not the case when he took over in 2005. At that time IAG was reeling from its high-risk expansion in the United Kingdom. Under Wilkins's leadership the company dealt with the UK problems, recapitalised the business and carved out a new strategy involving expansion in six fast growing Asian markets. The recovery in IAG's fortunes was helped by Harmer's efforts in reviving CGU, the commercial insurance business.

Chairman Brian Schwartz has followed best practice in finding an internal replacement for Wilkins. The succession planning process started soon after Wilkins took over as CEO.

The process of finding a successor included getting external advice on the company's bench strength and making sure that each potential candidate had the necessary breadth of skills.

Harmer was a beneficiary of this process. His period running the IAG Labs has given him useful insights into the challenges facing a mature company under attack from multiple competitors using disruptive, low cost business models. Some of the key lessons learned by Harmer during his time running the IAG Labs were the importance of new distribution models for personal insurance lines, the need for IAG to use analytics to better understand its customers and the imperative for IAG to build customer facing applications which can sit on top of legacy IT systems.

Of course, the world's best apps would be worth nothing unless the products are what customers want. Harmer admits that big companies like IAG used to develop a solution and then try and find the problem.

Over the past 18 months IAG has put substantial resources into "human centre design capability" which has been used to train people in the concept of customer empathy. This means trying to understand the "customer pain point" and how can customers help design the solution.

Harmer spent time in Silicon Valley where he spoke to venture capitalists and companies that had disrupted established industries. He was told that insurance, from their perspective, was unattractive because of the capital intensity and the regulatory burden. "We are not so foolish to think that disruption will come from somebody who builds another insurance company that looks like us," Harmer says. "Disruption will come in certain elements of the value chain. The area in our view that is most likely to attract disruption is distribution and our philosophy is not to defend against it but to embrace it."

That could result in IAG partnering with other companies in order to develop new ways of distributing its products. One of the threats that IAG has so far managed to deal with quite deftly was from the insurance commission churning machine called One Big Switch. IAG ended up owning the customers involved in that churning because it bought the underwriter, Wesfarmers Insurance.

Wilkins describes it in the following way: "We are happy to have customers. We just thought that type of campaign, particularly with the non-disclosure sitting around it, was not the right way to go about acquiring customers. It was an effort to shift customers almost for the sake of it."

The twin challenges facing Harmer are to respond to the threat posed by new market participants, particularly in the key motor insurance market, while convincing institutional shareholders that he can achieve growth through the deployment of precious capital in Asia.

So far, IAG has invested about $950 million in Asia. About $650 million has been invested in Malaysia and Thailand. These markets are achieving good growth and adequate returns. The IAG joint venture in India with the State Bank of India holds out the prospect of strong growth.

But the prospect of the company investing up to $1 billion in China causes some consternation among sceptical shareholders. The strategy of expanding in regional areas of China through Bohai proved a mistake but the company would have learned valuable lessons.

The Asian growth dividend is clearly not part of the short-term forecasts of insurance analysts. The analysts have rightly focused on the company's engine room of growth in Australian and New Zealand. It is under intense pressure because of a range of factors including digital disruption, excess capacity in the general insurance market and low yields in capital markets.

IAG has about $11 billion a year of gross written premiums. The problem is that premiums are coming down. The consensus earnings outlook for IAG is about 40¢ a share for the next three years. That suggests the dividend will remain flat at about 29¢ a share for the next several years.

Harmer will have to be creative as well as disciplined to restore growth to a company that doubled in value under Wilkins.

Wilkins will hand over the formal reins of leadership in mid-November but will not leave his post officially until March next year. He rejects the suggestion that Harmer will not have any clear air for about six months because he will have a "light touch" during his last four months at the company.

But that light touch will not prevent him collecting his short term incentives during the notice period. Also, Wilkins will walk away with portions of his incentive package intact which means he will pick up his equity benefits for some time provided performance hurdles are met.

Fight over coal asset

The fight for control of Resource Generation, which is listed in Australia and South Africa, has to be the most complex seen for a long time. The company, which is led by successful mining entrepreneur Paul Jury, has called in the Australian Securities and Investments Commission to investigate the links between various shareholders and their use of confidential information.

The shareholders, Altius Investment Holdings, Noble Group and Public Investment Corporation, want to sack all the directors of Resource Generation and replace them with their own representatives. Those trying to unseat the board claim it has dragged the chain on finding a funding solution for the Boikarabelo coal mine, which is located in the Waterberg region of South Africa near the ports of Maputo, Richards Bay and Durban.

The board says its funding solution from private Swiss interests is superior to the proposal put by a debt syndicate organised by Altius involving HSBC, Rand Merchant Bank, Industrial Development Corporation and Noble Resources. Adding spice to this stoush is the considerable pressure on the Noble Group which has seen its market value slump by 65 per cent this year.

Origin's incentives

The withdrawal of proposed equity incentives for two Origin Energy executive directors, Grant King and Karen Moses, appears to be the sum total of board level accountability for the company's excessive confidence about its ability to manage too much debt during a downturn in the commodity cycle.

King and Moses made it clear to Origin chairman Gordon Cairns that they both felt it was not appropriate for the company to proceed with the granting of the equity incentives given the irrefutable need for a $2.5 billion capital raising.

King is keen to stay on as CEO of Origin but after 15 years it is likely a change will probably occur sooner rather than later.

Twitter: @TonyBoydAFR

tony.boyd@afr.com.au
direction: near-term earnings volatility and uncertainty for IAG shares

headline: Marais earned reputation as 'deep-value' contrarian investor
intro: Respected contrarian investor and Allan Gray chief executive Simon Marais has died of cancer.
text:
Respected contrarian investor and Allan Gray chief executive Simon Marais has died of cancer.

"It is with deep sorrow that I announce that Simon Marais passed away this evening after a hard-fought battle with cancer," Allan Gray Australia chairman William Gray said in a statement issued late on Thursday night.

Co-portfolio manager Simon Mawhinney will take over from Mr Marais and it has been reported senior analyst Dan Abeshouse will be promoted to co-portfolio manager from July 1.

South African-born Mr Marais announced he was scaling back his duties as head of Allan Gray in December, 2014. He was hospitalised in early October, 2014. Investigations revealed cancer of the internal organs.

Over his career Mr Marais built a reputation as a "deep-value" contrarian investor, backing unloved stocks and waiting for the market to come around or agitating for change at board level.

He first captured the attention of the media during a protracted takeover bid for Spotless that lasted more than a year, before the business was ultimately sold to private equity outfit Pacific Equity Partners.

He also built positions in the struggling paper products company Paperlinx, gold miner Newcrest following the selective briefing furore and Fairfax Media, the owner of The Australian Financial Review and Smart Investor, at a time when media stocks were deeply unpopular.

Speaking on behalf of Fairfax Media, chairman Roger Corbett today said Mr Marais was unfailingly courteous and thoughtful in all his dealings with others.

"As the chief executive of Allan Gray, Simon was a great supporter of Fairfax Media during a demanding period in the company's history. We highly valued his advice and his always direct and honest feedback."

Ted Harris, deputy chairman of APN News & Media, in which Allan Gray has been an active investor, also paid tribute to Mr Marais.

"Simon had a profound interest and understanding of the media industry. His investments reflected an optimism that established media companies would adapt and succeed in a fragmenting media landscape. The determination he constantly expressed defined his ongoing success."

Mr Marais was one of six "Stock Market Hot Shots" profiled by Smart Investor in October 2014 and frequently made himself available to talk about the discipline of value investing.

The founder of Allan Gray Australia, Mr Marias joined Allan Gray South Africa in 1991 and served as head of the firm's Australian equity team, in addition to leading the firm as chief executive officer and serving as portfolio manager.

Mr Marais worked at Allan Gray and global investment manager Orbis Group for more than 20 years, having headed Orbis's research investment team in London between 2002 and 2004.

He spent 11 years with Allan Gray in South Africa, including four years as chief investment officer and executive chairman.

Before joining Allan Gray, Mr Marais studied theoretical physics at Cambridge, earning a Master of Science and Doctor of Philosophy.

Over his career he built a reputation as a "deep-value" contrarian investor, backing unloved stocks and being prepared to wait for the share price to rise to Allan Gray's expectation of its value.

The approach led Allan Gray to be involved in a number of controversial corporate deals, including the rejection of Ferrovial's $1 billion bid for Transfield Services and Horizon Oil's reverse takeover play for Roc Oil.

In December, Mr Marais told Smart Investor he would continue to be involved in "big investment decisions" at Allan Gray, as he handed over day-to-day running to co-portfolio manager Simon Mawhinney.

"Making sure the long decisions of what to buy and sell are right, that's the important part," he said at the time.

Mr Marais is survived by his wife and three sons.
direction: bearish for Fairfax Media and related media stocks

headline: China acts to stem market rout
intro: Shanghai | China's securities regulator will investigate whether there has been any market manipulation following three weeks of volatile trading, which has wiped more than 25 per cent from the country's two main exchanges.
text:
Shanghai | China's securities regulator will investigate whether there has been any market manipulation following three weeks of volatile trading, which has wiped more than 25 per cent from the country's two main exchanges.

The Shanghai Composite Index fell a further 5.8 per cent on Friday to 3686.92, despite the government rolling out a series of policies aimed at shoring up the sharemarket over the past week. These include an interest rate cut last Saturday, a reduction in transaction fees, a relaxation of margin finance regulations and new rules that will allow the state pension fund to invest in equities.

Since reaching a seven-year high on June 12, the Shanghai's benchmark index has tumbled 27 per cent, shifting from bull to bear market territory. Concern about indebted investors rushing to cover their margin positions has weighed on the market.

"The bursting of the bubble is a good but painful education in risk management for Chinese retail investors," said Haitong Securities analyst Zhang Qi.

He expects the government to announce more policies to address the sharemarket rout over the weekend when Premier Li Keqiang returns from Europe.

Zhang Xiaojun, spokesman for the China Securities Regulatory Commission, said late on Thursday that it would investigate irregularities between securities and futures trading across multiple markets, according to state-run news agency Xinhua.

Any criminal cases would be referred to police, he said.

"These are extraordinary measures for extraordinary times," Haitong's Mr Zhang said.

They were doing this to try "to stabilise the financial system".

"Some of these individual investors put their life savings into the stock market so there are also concerns about social stability," Mr Zhang said.

The Shanghai and Shenzhen markets surged 150 per cent in the 12 months to June 12, as 40 million new traders set up accounts and the state-owned press covered the bull market.

The rally was also fuelled by a surge in margin finance, which until five years ago was banned in China.

Official margin lending through securities brokeragesrose 123 per cent this year to a high of 2.3 trillion yuan on June 18, according to Macquarie Research, equal to more than 3 per cent of China's gross domestic product.

Margin lending through the "grey channel", which includes financing firms who don't do credit checks or have minimum balance requirements, is estimated to be at least another 500 billion yuan.

Key pointsShanghai's benchmark share index has fall 27 per cent in the past month.

An inquiry has begun into irregularities in securities and futures trading.
direction: bearish for Chinese equities and related global risk assets

headline: App aims to put art on your wall
intro: "Sales are moving increasingly online. Buyers are using online for research. You can't ignore it," says Karen Zadra, the former director of Marshall Arts and the developer of a new art app. Itondo is pitched as a marketplace and viewing app that gives buyers a live preview of how artworks will look hanging on their wall.
text:
"Sales are moving increasingly online. Buyers are using online for research. You can't ignore it," says Karen Zadra, the former director of Marshall Arts and the developer of a new art app. Itondo is pitched as a marketplace and viewing app that gives buyers a live preview of how artworks will look hanging on their wall.

The idea took root when Zadra realised she was spending an increasing amount of time Photoshopping artworks onto photos for her gallery clients, but she quickly decided there would be a broader audience for an app.

Before starting, users need to print a "marker" to stick to the wall. This helps the app calculate scale and angle, so that artworks can be viewed from any position in the room. (It currently only works for two-dimensional works.)

A drawback of the current release is how close you need to stand for the app to recognise the marker - inevitably too close to get a good view of the artwork. There is also no function to search by artwork size, though you can search by style (including the rather broad category of "contemporary"). But whether collectors find the app useful is more likely to depend on Zadra's success marketing to galleries and artists: users won't come if there is no art to view. For her part Zadra is confident that "itondo will gather its own community" in a space already dominated by the likes of Artsy. She has made efforts to get galleries on side. Rather than paying a flat subscription fee, they purchase credits which are deducted as users engage with their listed artworks. Itondo will take no cut of sales.

Zadra and her husband Dirk Zadra moved to Luxembourg to build the app after closing their Adelaide gallery late last year. "Luxembourg is very central to the main art markets," she says. Of the galleries currently listing artwork on the app, two are international and there is also the Zadras' own new online venture, Galerie Zadra. Melbourne's This is No Fantasy and Sydney's Janet Clayton Gallery are also taking part.
direction: cautious for art-market and tech shares

headline: AFL BOSS FUMBLES ON GOODES FIASCO
intro: Mike Fitzpatrick has finally got around to supporting Adam Goodes but it might too little too late, writes John Stensholt.
text:
Mike Fitzpatrick has finally got around to supporting Adam Goodes but it might too little too late, writes John Stensholt.

'Extraordinary ... and a bit weird," was how one AFL club president described it. Having taken more than a week to make a public comment about the Adam Goodes controversy, in which the Sydney Swans forward elected not to play as debate raged about the racist booing of him, AFL chairman Mike Fitzpatrick finally issued a written statement to the media.

But instead of getting straight to the point and defending Goodes, Fitzpatrick waffled on about his own directorships with the AFL and Rio Tinto, and boasted about his achievements with Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islanders. Then he told an anecdote about being on an AFL ground alongside Goodes, comparing him to many brilliant ruckmen of the past 50 years.

It was not until the last paragraph Fitzpatrick said Goodes had the AFL's unanimous support but it might be too little, too late for the 62-year-old chairman.

As Goodes takes to the field in Geelong on Saturday, and as Fitzpatrick and AFL chief executive Gillon McLachlan take their seats in the stands, another debate is raging in AFL circles.

Club presidents and executives who spoke to AFR Weekend are highly critical of Fitzpatrick's handling of the Goodes situation and believe he should stand down.

They have also expressed shock that AFL commissioners, including high-profile businesspeople such as Wesfarmers chief executive Richard Goyder, Virgin director Sam Mostyn and SEEK co-founder Paul Bassat are divided on whether the booing of Goodes has been racially motivated.

"I've never seen anything like that before. You might have robust discussions in the boardroom but for something to come out like that shows there's something not quite right there," says one AFL club president.

Several say a fresh face is needed to chair the league, one with a stronger public presence.

"I think a decent term is seven or eight years," says one club president when reminded Fitzpatrick has been on the AFL commission since 2007.

Fitzpatrick's public dithering is almost certainly at odds with his personal view. "Mike's very, very left wing on just about anything, so I would have thought he would have been very strong on this matter," said another club official.

Fitzpatrick, who was a champion football player, Rhodes scholar, investment banker and long-time company director, was asked for comment by AFR Weekend several times this week but declined the opportunity.

While shy publicly, Fitzpatrick has long thrown his weight around behind the scenes. He has not been afraid to leverage the AFL's fiscal and moral authority over the clubs in an effort to keep everyone under control. Club officials who have discussed Fitzpatrick's style have been extremely reluctant to do so publicly and on the record.

It is perhaps not surprising, given revelations in the Herald Sun this week of a heated and expletive-laden conversation between Fitzpatrick and former Swans chairman Richard Colless after the club recruited Lance "Buddy" Franklin in late 2013, seemingly against the AFL's wishes. "There was no warm-up, no introduction - the opening pleasantries were the 'F' bomb and the 'C' bomb," Colless said.

For a shy man who rarely speaks out publicly, Fitzpatrick is very willing and able to stick the boot in out of the spotlight. But while that style has worked for some time, the AFL community is fraying at the edges.

The controversy about Goodes, which started on the sports pages and ended up as a national debate about racism, isn't the only problem for the sport.

It is still grappling with the Essendon drugs saga, which reared its head again this week with more allegations of a banned substance found in Essendon players by the World Anti-Doping Authority.
