Fitzpatrick was also criticised earlier this year after reappointing Chris Langford to the commission when Linda Dessau departed suddenly to become the Governor of Victoria and, before that, for keeping Rio Tinto chief financial officer Chris Lynch on the commission for a year after he had moved to London.

Should Fitzpatrick leave, Perth-based Goyder looms as a popular choice, if and when he steps down from his executive role.

"Obviously it would depend on Goyder having enough time to do the job, but it would be good to have someone from outside the fishbowl of Melbourne," said one club president.
direction: negative for AFL-related equities and governance-sensitive stocks

headline: From Perth with love
intro: Western Australia's machine plant glut, as reported in The Australian Financial Review on Thursday, is obviously not good news for the former owners of diggers, front-end loaders and drilling rigs who would prefer to still be using them. But it is illustrative of how Australia has adjusted to economic shocks and changes, whereas the Greeks did not.
text:
Western Australia's machine plant glut, as reported in The Australian Financial Review on Thursday, is obviously not good news for the former owners of diggers, front-end loaders and drilling rigs who would prefer to still be using them. But it is illustrative of how Australia has adjusted to economic shocks and changes, whereas the Greeks did not.

With the cooling of the iron ore price, the dollar has dropped, and the discount diggers are being sold abroad in Asia. Meanwhile, people are leaving WA. This is a sign of an economy adjusting, with all the heartache and difficulty that implies.

The Greeks, on the other hand, refused to adapt to their changed circumstances, persisting with an inflexible economy and dirigiste regulatory environments. The past few days have shown that unless you are prepared to change, it will be forced upon you. Unhelpfully, they are also buckled into a euro currency straitjacket which will make reforms less effective.

Here, the federal system is used to smooth out state budgets over the economic cycle, with rich states subsidising poor ones, likewise the dollar cushions different sectors over the cycle. In contrast, Greece has to always deal with high German exchange rates.

The euro constraint may not help the Greeks' uncompetitive economy much, but at least while they're in it, they might be able to afford an Australian digger or two.
direction: bearish for WA mining equipment and property sectors

headline: Santos investors angry, desperate
intro: At the Santos annual meeting earlier this year the chairman Peter Coates said he could understand shareholders' anger after almost $7 billion in value had been wiped out in the previous eight months.
text:
At the Santos annual meeting earlier this year the chairman Peter Coates said he could understand shareholders' anger after almost $7 billion in value had been wiped out in the previous eight months.

If any of those investors still own the stock it's a fair bet they're still fuming, but for some they have also reached the desperate stage.

Shares in the company closed at $8.30 that day of the AGM, April 30, when the oil price was closer to $US60 a barrel.

On Friday, the oil price was hovering closer to $US41.6 a barrel, down 30 per cent, while shares in Santos were changing hands at around $3.95, a fall of about 53 per cent since the annual meeting.

That's a massive car crash in anyone's book and yet the company has only had one change at board level, with the former chief executive David Knox leaving, replaced this week by former Clough boss Kevin Gallagher.

Apart from a spike up in the oil price or a takeover bid, investors are wondering how the fortunes of the company can be turned around when the executives that saw it all unfold are still there.

The critics say Santos should have pulled the trigger much earlier on a rights issue to shore up the balance sheet, with a few finding it hard to believe that the board didn't push harder on this. Or for a break up and sell-down of all the assets.

Did they simply think the price of oil was about to go back up again?

Knowing how much supply was coming on around the world it has angered investors that the company didn't do something to cut their gearing or hedge the exposure.

Perhaps that's the real reason former chairman Ken Borda resigned in March this year.

While it might be fair for Santos to argue that investors never wanted them to hedge their exposure to the oil and gas price in the first place - that's the domain of smaller resource companies - it's also fair to say that investors never expected to be asked to stump up more cash at such a large discount to where the stock was trading a little over a year ago. But that's what happens when companies load up on debt and their revenue depends on something they have little control over. In this instance the oil price.

By taking on so much debt to fund its massive expansion, about $8.8 billion, the board exposed the company to any fall in the price of oil. Failure to hedge any of it was a massive air swing.

It's similar to a problem that Glencore, the commodities trading house, had in late September when it's own high debt levels of around $US30 billion ($42 billion) saw the company's share price plunge by 30 per cent, triggering a global sharemarket rout.

Glencore shares have since bounced back after the company confirmed it could repay its debts, but it hasn't gone unnoticed by some that the chairman of Santos, Peter Coates, is also on the board of Glencore as a non-executive director.

A look at the oil price in Australian dollar terms since the local dollar hit parity with the US dollar shows it has fallen from around $120 a barrel to closer to $65, almost half.

Yet investors have been asked to prop up the Santos balance sheet at a 75 per cent discount to when the shares were closer to their highs in the mid-teens.

For any self-managed super fund that's not quite the correlation or risk profile they would want.

While a high oil price might not hurt them much in their day to day spending they certainly take a hit when the share price falls as hard as it has. Of course, the spectacular drop in the oil price isn't the fault of Santos but investors can't help but feel frustrated that in some way the company has done its bit by adding to the increase in supply.

Some say that all the capital raisings by the property trusts during the global financial crisis and failure of many other companies to go early should also have been a warning sign to the board.

Meanwhile, for anyone looking for a dividend, there's $80 million to be shared with the 1.75 billion shares on issue, which is no where near the 15¢ they received at the half year.

But before investors can think of a dividend, there is still the issue of how to pay the ever increasing capex that is needed to replenish the reserves.

Perhaps the last word should go to the Credit Suisse analyst Mark Samter, who did urge the company to go early with a raising. This week he told clients it was still too early to pile in. He has a price target of $5 and a neutral weighting on the stock.
direction: bearish for Santos shares

headline: Morrison looks to UK politician for reform ideas
intro: Scott Morrison, the former social services minister and now Treasurer, is full of praise for Iain Duncan Smith.
text:
Scott Morrison, the former social services minister and now Treasurer, is full of praise for Iain Duncan Smith.

But who exactly is Iain Duncan Smith? He is a UK politician who, as work and pensions secretary in the Conservative government, is presiding over some pretty radical reform to the welfare system.

The changes include capping tax credits at two children to teach parents that "children cost money" and the introduction of "universal credit".

Families with a third child born after April 2017 will not qualify for the child tax credit, which amounts to up to £2780 ($5911) a year per child.

Universal credit collapses six welfare payments into one and is aimed at people on low incomes and the unemployed. Recipients will not have their benefits withdrawn as quickly so people who work more, earn more. The policies have in-principle support from the Labour opposition and are being rolled out in stages.

Mr Duncan Smith insists traditional welfare settings have discouraged people from working, trapping them in poverty. He told a Conservative Party conference in Manchester that his measures were aimed at "ending poverty, not entrenching it and restoring lives, not parking them".

"We have to raise the value of work - but not as Labour tried to do with the taxpayer subsidising wages through tax credits," he said.

"As Conservatives we don't want people to work just for tax receipts. We want people to work because it's best for them, their family and their communities ... all our reforms have a simple principle at the heart of them: to restore lives."

Asked about unemployment in Australia on ABC radio on Wednesday morning, Mr Morrison said Mr Duncan Smith had done some "excellent" work in examining the interplay between the tax and welfare systems.

Breakfast show host Fran Kelly asked: "And there seems to be a suggestion of some kind of change in, maybe tax credit in return for lower rates of pay, you lose on the penalty side but you get a better tax treatment - ideas like that?"

To which Mr Morrison responded that, "I think these are all very innovative ideas and in the UK Iain Duncan Smith has done some excellent work in looking at those sorts of systems. What you want is a tax system and a payment system that locks together and a labour market system which links in with that to ensure that you are better off working than being on welfare."
direction: pressure on welfare-dependent sectors and consumer discretionary stocks

headline: Turnbull skips crucial apprenticeship
intro: Malcolm Turnbull undoubtedly cares deeply about economic policy, which gives him an edge on Tony Abbott.
text:
Malcolm Turnbull undoubtedly cares deeply about economic policy, which gives him an edge on Tony Abbott.

Unquestionably he knows what reforms and policies the economy needs. But, if he becomes prime minister, does he have the political skill and courage necessary to put them into place?

Can he come up with a set of spending cuts that are acceptable to both a timorous Coalition party room and a populist senate in order to get the budget back into structural surplus?

Will he be prepared to push deeply into the competition policy reform that has been mapped out by the Harper review and which is essential if Australia is to rebuild its once strong productivity growth?

Will he successfully build the public support necessary for serious workplace relations reform and have the courage to face the relentless campaign that helped bring down the Howard government?

And can he successfully pick up on Abbott's tax reform, and successfully see through the required negotiations with the Liberal and Labor states?

Abbott's strategy has been to force the states to take political responsibility for more of the money they spend and then offer them a relatively easy alternative to putting up their own taxes: a federally-legislated increase in the GST.

This is a giant game of bluff that will be played out in an election year. If the Labor states refuse and services are threatened with closure, no one can be certain who the voters will blame.

The risk of failure in getting reform through the political process has grown. Once, the Howard government could negotiate its reforms through a hostile Senate by appealing to the Australian Democrats to recognise its mandate. Two Democrats' leaders suffered politically as a result.

A new generation of populist Senate crossbenchers appear to have learned from the Democrats "mistake", as Abbott and his Treasurer, Joe Hockey, have had the misfortune to discover.

That more than anything has been Abbott's undoing: had the 2014 budget gone through Parliament unscathed, Abbott almost certainly would not have been facing death by repeated leadership crises in 2015.

Political skill is even more at a premium.

As Paul Keating famously told the Parliament, even the Treasury stenographers can tell us what the economy needs; the skill required of government is to get those reforms through the political process.

Turnbull has not done anything like the life-long political apprenticeship that gave prime ministers like John Howard their political sixth sense.

That's my main reservation about Turnbull and anyone else who seeks to enter politics at the leadership level on the strength of a successful career elsewhere.

I have this vision of Turnbull losing control of the politics early and dumping principles and policies in a vain struggle to regain control. In that case, a Turnbull prime ministership could be seen as an intermediate step between Abbott and a real successor, perhaps Scott Morrison.
direction: uncertain near-term outlook for reform-sensitive sectors

headline: Nomura in the red again but deals are picking up
intro: Nomura Australia stopped short on Friday of forecasting profitability for its local operations, as the firm reported narrower losses but remained in the red for the year ended March 31.
text:
Nomura Australia stopped short on Friday of forecasting profitability for its local operations, as the firm reported narrower losses but remained in the red for the year ended March 31.

The entity reported a net loss of $12.6 million for the period, compared with a loss of $27.2 million in the prior 12 months, accounts lodged with the Australian Securities and Investments Commission showed. Fees and other income almost doubled to $10.5 million, while related party income was roughly steady at $23.2 million, helping total revenue rise 19 per cent.

The accounts do not include the Nomura Instinet equities operations, and, like those of peers, are difficult to interpret as revenues are often booked or shared with offshore entities.

Nomura operates financial markets, including fixed-income businesses in Australia, as well as investment banking.

The co-head of Nomura's local investment bank, John Hanson, shied away from predicting when the firm would be profitable, saying it was focused on growing revenue and being diligent on costs.

"We have a very focused business model here in Australia and that business model is now showing good progress," he said. "We have executed transactions in all our areas of focus and our [advisory] pipeline is now three times the size it was 12 months ago." Mr Hanson added that Nomura was committed to the Australian market and had recently extended its Sydney lease by seven years. Malaysia's CIMB this year retreated from Australia after failing to win enough business.

Expenses declined to $50 million for the year ended March 31 as employee costs were curbed. That was down from $58.7 million. Nomura Australia did not pay a dividend to Tokyo-based parent Nomura Holdings.

Mr Hanson said the firm is seeking to keep a lid on costs but may make selective hires. Among its rumoured mandates, Nomura is thought to be advising Chinese conglomerate HNA Group in its tilt at the Esanda dealer finance portfolio. Local headcount is roughly 70 employees.

Nomura is also benefiting from a decline in the Australian dollar - which boosts interest in inbound acquisitions - and trading volatility in financial markets.

Head of global markets Australia, Jon Linton, said: "We've seen a lot of volatility and had a great start to the year … there is more activity than ever coming out of Japan."

Mr Hanson said he was optimistic on deal flow in the next six months, noting inbound inquiries from Japan and broader Asia.

"There are encouraging signs."
direction: bearish for Nomura Australia shares

headline: Fintechs want a touch more disruption
intro: The financial technology industry says the innovation statement is an important first step in building a thriving start-up scene in Australia but more needs to be done, writes James Eyers.
text:
The financial technology industry says the innovation statement is an important first step in building a thriving start-up scene in Australia but more needs to be done, writes James Eyers.

As Prime Minister Malcolm Turnbull moved to the lectern in the fintech hub Stone & Chalk on Tuesday morning, an audience of about 150 start-up entrepreneurs broke into spontaneous applause.

"There has never been a more exciting time to be in fintech," Turnbull said during the launch of an investment partnership between industry super fund First State Super and the fintech venture capital fund H2 Ventures, which is based in Stone & Chalk, a collaborative working space near Circular Quay in Sydney.

"There is nowhere more in Australia that the ideas boom is under way than here in Stone & Chalk."

Turnbull, flanked by Treasurer Scott Morrison, Assistant Treasurer Kelly O'Dwyer and Assistant Innovation Minister Wyatt Roy, said the presence of his senior ministers illustrated the government's "absolute commitment to innovation and supporting all of your imagination and creativity".

Alex Scandurra, the chief executive of Stone & Chalk, whose residents have raised $12 million in the four months it has been operating, introduced the ministers to tenants, including the low-latency switch maker for high speed trading systems, Metamako; Data Republic, a platform facilitating data sharing; Piggy, which provides payments cards and software for families to teach financial literacy; and Inamo, which is manufacturing a wearable wristband that can be used to make payments.

Stone & Chalk's chairman Craig Dunn, who is also a director of Westpac, says the innovation push will give the fintech sector an injection of confidence.

"You can tell that he gets it. The PM is taking the lead and is confident we can get real traction. There is outstanding talent in this country; why can't we become the leading technology hub for Asia?"

Leaders of the burgeoning financial technology scene say the innovation statement is an important first step in building a thriving technology sector in Australia.

But they also suggest additional policy measures will be necessary to give fintech start-ups the best chance to thrive, given the specific focus on the sector in countries such as the UK and Singapore.

Beau Bertoli, joint CEO of online business lender Prospa, says it "would have been great to see some fintech specific initiatives" but that investor tax breaks "are a good way of getting money to flow into start-ups".

"The United States, Israel and the UK have investor-friendly tax laws, and this is the first step in that direction."

In an era where jurisdictions are competing for entrepreneurial talent, other countries have introduced more aggressive policies than what was revealed in Monday's statement, suggesting local policy will have to continue to be adjusted.

Andrew Corbett-Jones, head of Tyro fintech hub, also in Sydney, says the UK has gone further on tax concessions than the Turnbull government, which would limit the ability of Australia to compete against Britain. Monday's statement provided for a 20 per cent, non-refundable tax offset based on the amount of their investment capped at $200,000 an investor, per annum.

"But the government could have been bolder given the UK offers tax offsets of 50 per cent and 30 per cent for investors," Corbett-Jones says.

"This package offers 20 per cent. That's good, but we shouldn't expect the same results as the UK."

Nevertheless, he says he is optimistic that "tax incentives may lead to start-ups becoming a part of the investment portfolio of an increasing number of investors, and that would be a good outcome".

Tyro and Stone & Chalk were among the 32 fintech incubators, VC funds and start-ups who called in November for fintech to receive special attention in the statement, given the complexities of financial services regulation and the race to lure fintech talent abroad. For example, UK Trade & Investment took 10 promising Australian fintech start-ups to London in September to promote it as a place for fintech businesses.

But Corbett-Jones says he is not concerned that fintech was not dealt with specifically in the statement.

"This promises to be the start of a concerted focus on innovation and high-growth companies, and as such, it was important that the government hear from the fintech community early in the peace.

Yet Scandurra says financial services are already a key source of competitive advantage for Australia in the Asia region, and he is urging the government to consider introducing similar fintech-specific policies as the UK, where Chancellor of the Exchequer, George Osborne, has given financial technology a big push by allowing London to rise as a fintech hub of global significance.

"The wave of fintech disruption is happening now globally," Scandurra says. "Given its digital nature, this capital-light mode of innovation can provide a huge source of new jobs and wealth creation. In the UK, investment in fintech has increased eight-fold from evidence over the last four years in Britain."

Start-ups point to the relatively low caps in the innovation statement's tax policy, which provide tax incentives for investors in companies with expenditure of less than $1 million and income of less than $200,000 in the previous earnings year. Bertoli says Prospa, which employs 70 people, passed those caps many months ago.

"Unfortunately, for companies like us, we don't qualify for the investor benefits. But great leaps can come when mid-sized business - companies like us - innovate. We could have seen mid-sized companies also qualify for the tax relief," he says.

Nor is he certain the government has the necessary sense of urgency to change visa laws, given the dearth of IT skills in the workforce.

"The innovation statement says an entrepreneur visa will be established "for entrepreneurs with innovative ideas and financial backing from a third party".

"This is needed right now," Bertoli says. "If I could find 50 coders right now, I would take them, but they are not in Australia. This is a handbrake on growth for a lot of technology companies in Australia."

The start-up sector will continue to push for more attractive employee share schemes laws. Scandurra says he welcomes the reforms to employee share schemes, but "we are disappointed that they may not have gone far enough". Earlier this year, the government introduced generous tax concessions for employee share schemes and said on Monday it would limit the requirement for disclosure documents.

Bertoli says the mid-year changes to the laws were important, but they only brought Australia back up to the level of other countries. "The question needs to be how to take this further," he says. "How do we make things more enticing than the UK or the US, so we can get the people we want."

Another resident of Stone & Chalk, Mario Hasanakos, a former investment banker and now managing director of Piggy, applauds moves to tighten the use of "ipso facto" clauses in contracts.

"Every contract we have signed has the option to terminate if our business becomes insolvent," he says. "The problem is start-ups come really close to insolvency in the ordinary course of business - that's our life, it is normal, and it is not necessarily a sign of anything bad."

Hasanakos says the Prime Minister's positive rhetoric is important to inspire confidence for entrepreneurs to take the leap into running their own small business.

"I left the bank I used to work for when [fintech venture capital fund] H2 provided institutional validation of what I was doing," he says.

"When the Prime Minister of Australia says all the eggs of our economic future are in this innovation basket, people like me will say, 'This is serious, maybe I will give it a go'. It takes policy underneath it to give rhetoric credibility, but the talk also has an impact."
direction: bullish for fintech and venture capital stocks

headline: Victorian traffic woes 'will worsen'
intro: Victoria's infrastructure is falling behind and there are no shovel-ready projects to fix traffic congestion at a time when the city's population is expected to soar, say business groups responding to a highly critical report by Infrastructure Australia.
text:
Victoria's infrastructure is falling behind and there are no shovel-ready projects to fix traffic congestion at a time when the city's population is expected to soar, say business groups responding to a highly critical report by Infrastructure Australia.

They say Victoria has "lost its momentum" and is in "dire need" of a strategic infrastructure plan.

An audit by Infrastructure Australia ranked the CityLink and Eastern Freeway connection north of Melbourne as the 10th worst congestion point and estimated the delay cost per lane kilometre at $1.6 million in 2011.

Last month, the state government paid $339 million to cancel the East West Link project, which was expected to alleviate this pressure. That decision lost $500 million in sunk costs.

"Victorian infrastructure is not keeping up with the level of traffic on the road and the population increase," Australian Industry Group Victorian director Tim Piper said.

Infrastructure Australia expects Victoria's population to grow from just over 4 million in 2011 to 6 million in 2031 and 8.5 million in 2061. The report expects demand for public transport will increase 89 per cent by 2031 and fares will have to be increased for operators to continue.

Mr Piper said the government would have a hard time cutting the free trams in the city scheme. "I don't think there is a lack in confidence, but we've lost some momentum," he said. "We're going to play catch-up continually."

The report also predicts a surge in bus patronage, especially to the Melbourne airport.

When it came into government, the Andrews government scrapped plans to build an airport rail link.

The Labor state government has said it will focus on removing 50 level crossings and will progress plans for the $11 billion Metro Rail project, which will see the city loop underground train line double in size. It is in negotiations with Transurban over a $5.5 billion proposal for a toll road from CityLink to the West Gate Freeway.

None of these projects is ready to be built and some have labelled the toll road, which is similar to the planned East West Link stage two, as inferior.

Premier Daniel Andrews said the Infrastructure Australia report "pretty well confirms" the government's infrastructure priorities. He said the report highlighted that the western section of the East West corridor was the most problematic, rather than the Eastern section his government cancelled.

He said that was why the government was in negotiations with Transurban over the Western Distributor.

"This has got a very strong business case; it makes sense," he said. "We just have to work through the finer details about how it might be funded."

Financing for the toll road is reliant on $1.8 billion from the federal government, which on Thursday claimed it had not had further detail on the project and will not commit the funds.

Master Builders Association Victoria chief executive Radley De Silva said the report showed the "dire need" for strategic planning of Victoria's infrastructure.

"We cannot put our heads in the sand and pretend that population growth and the resulting congestion is not going to happen," he said.

Victorian Employers Chamber of Commerce and Industry CEO Mark Stone said the audit provided a "compelling case" that infrastructure reform must be a priority if economic competitiveness and living standards are to be raised.
direction: bearish for Victorian infrastructure and transport sector stocks

headline: Craig Emerson wrong on FTA labour claims
intro: Through our historic China Australia Free Trade Agreement, the Australian government has abandoned nothing with regard to labour market testing. Craig Emerson's claims to the contrary ("Clearing the FTA labour thicket", AFR, September 1) are simply incorrect. The truth is that the labour provisions associated with the free trade agreement operate in accordance with the requirements under the existing skilled visa program; the exact same provisions that applied under Labor.
text:
Through our historic China Australia Free Trade Agreement, the Australian government has abandoned nothing with regard to labour market testing. Craig Emerson's claims to the contrary ("Clearing the FTA labour thicket", AFR, September 1) are simply incorrect. The truth is that the labour provisions associated with the free trade agreement operate in accordance with the requirements under the existing skilled visa program; the exact same provisions that applied under Labor.

Under the labour market testing regime - which Labor introduced, by the way - there are already a large number of categories exempted.

Labor itself signed trade deals that locked in exemptions for occupations such as contractual services suppliers in the free trade agreement with Chile.

It seems it is OK with Chile, Korea or Japan but not with China, hence the racial element of the campaign against this agreement.

The Department of Immigration and Border Protection guidelines make crystal clear that labour market testing is an essential requirement for those categories that are not exempt.

For example, the Investment Facilitation Arrangement, outlined in a memorandum of understanding alongside the free trade agreement, is simply the first stage of a three-stage process - it is the registration stage of a project to be built some time in the future.

Very conveniently, Dr Emerson, Labor and the militant unions ignore the other two vital stages.

If a company reaches the final stage of wanting to bring workers in under the Investment Facilitation Arrangement, the safeguards stipulate that employers must demonstrate that Australians have been given the first opportunity for jobs through evidence of domestic recruiting efforts, that is labour market testing.

The China FTA is all about attracting critical new investment and creating tens of thousands of new jobs for Australians. Why would we do anything to deny Australians of opportunities for work? It makes no sense.

If Dr Emerson and Labor have issue with how the migration system operates, this has absolutely nothing to do with the China free trade agreement.

The real question is why didn't they fix these things that they are supposedly so concerned about now when they had their chance?

Andrew Robb

Minister for Trade and Investment Bentleigh, Victoria
direction: bearish for China-linked equities and trade-sensitive sectors

headline: One TV box unticked
intro: Adelgunde is proving to be a very difficult task mistress. As you may recall from last week, she and I are in the market for a new television set to replace the ageing Sony in our living room - a TV from the free-to-air era that is now so out of date I've had to add layer upon layer of hardware, software and hot glue to it over the years just so we can watch modern TV apps such as Catch Up TV, Kodi, Netflix and Stan.
text:
Adelgunde is proving to be a very difficult task mistress. As you may recall from last week, she and I are in the market for a new television set to replace the ageing Sony in our living room - a TV from the free-to-air era that is now so out of date I've had to add layer upon layer of hardware, software and hot glue to it over the years just so we can watch modern TV apps such as Catch Up TV, Kodi, Netflix and Stan.

(I mention Stan here, not because it's part-owned by my employer, but because it's the only service we can find that is playing re-runs of The Shield, a show to which Adelgunde and I have belatedly become addicted. If The Shield were on Netflix, we would be watching it there, believe me.)

My needs in a TV department are modest and straightforward: I want the biggest, most sophisticated TV on the market, which runs every smart TV app ever written and every app yet to be written (future-proofing is vital), can be incorporated into our fledgling home automation system and has the best picture quality known to science.

Adelgunde, on the other hand, is making unreasonable demands. She wants a TV that doesn't keep crashing on her, and ... well, that's all she wants. That, and she has to be able to finish The Shield. Women!

Anyway, the quest has brought us this week to LG's new EG960T, an Ultra HD OLED TV that ticks so many of my modest little boxes, I just want to hug its gentle curves. It's OLED, and it's Ultra HD, both!

As you probably know, Ultra HD (also called 4K) is the latest standard in TV resolution, with four times the sharpness of regular HD TV. UHD content isn't exactly commonplace, but there's now enough of it on services such as Netflix and YouTube that you can get a pretty good taste of how good it's going to be in the next few years. Ultra HD pictures are incredible.

OLED, meanwhile, is the latest and greatest display technology. Unlike LCD displays - which rely on back- or edge-lighting to generate a picture and which, as a result, can't display blacks properly - every pixel in an OLED display is its own light source, and can be turned off completely.

That means that blacks on an OLED display are 100 per cent black, which in turn means the contrast ratio on an OLED display is infinite, which in turn means that colours and whites appear dazzling and vivid. OLED pictures are incredible.

Combine Ultra HD and OLED the way the EG960T does - and it's the only TV on the market that does combine them - and the results are incredible squared. With HD or sharper sources, the EG960T has easily the best picture I've ever seen. (Unfortunately, I still can't tell you how the EG960T fares with standard-definition, free-to-air TV. Our review was conducted in a hotel room and no free-to-air sources were made available.)

If picture quality was the only consideration, the EG960T would be a simple choice. And, indeed, if Adelgunde's demand for an easy-to-use TV were the only other consideration, the EG960T would still be a simple choice. Its webOS 2.0 smart TV operating system is so easy to use, even Adelgunde says she is confident she would "no longer need [me] around" for her TV viewing - a remark I find a little worrying in the broader scheme of things, but high praise for webOS nonetheless.

But, alas, picture quality and ease of use aren't the whole box and dice. There's the matter of apps and (case in point) of watching The Shield on an app; on this front LG's new TV is a little worrisome.

Some of the big-name apps, such as Spotify and Netflix, are available on the EG960T, but many of the apps you might expect to find on the TV aren't there, and they're not in the app store, either.

There's no Plex, for instance, and there's no Kodi, meaning if you want to watch videos you have stored on computers around your house, your options will be limited. There is a third-party, Plex-compatible player known as PLAYz which might tide you over until an official app appears, however.

Another app you can find on most smart TVs, Skype, isn't in the webOS 2.0 store, either, though LG makes the point that hardly anyone actually uses Skype on a TV anyway, and deliberately didn't include it for that reason.

Now, it's still early days for webOS 2.0, and there's every likelihood that a lot of the empty shelves in its app store will be filled with time. But compared with the Sony Android TV we recently reviewed, which has barely even been released but already seems to have a very dynamic app store, LG's app store is disappointing, perhaps the only disappointment on this otherwise amazing TV. Even the remote control is great.

Needless to say there's no Stan app, either (Stan isn't on many platforms), which would be fine if the TV's web browser was more sophisticated and could use the Stan website to play shows. But unfortunately it's not and it can't. It can't play any Silverlight-based services, which rules out a lot of websites.

All of which leaves us in a bind. Should we just attach a Google Chromecast dongle to the LG, which will allow us to watch The Shield and neatly plug most of the webOS 2.0 app store holes?

Or does resorting to a Chromecast defeat the purpose of buying a new TV, denying Adelgunde her one wish in life - for a TV with which nothing can go wrong?

Maybe she has other wishes.L&L

Twitter: @DLLabs

iLG EG960T

Likes

Incredible picture quality

Extremely easy to operate

Dislikes

webOS app store is not as full of apps as we had hoped

Price

$5999 for 55-inch model

$9999 for 65-inch model
direction: neutral to slightly bearish for consumer electronics stocks

headline: Business transformed by new digital world
intro: Australia's business leaders are discovering they need to dramatically change the way they work to respond to a disruptive wave of business change, driven by digital technologies. In the United States, digital disruption has seen more than half the companies on the Fortune 500 disappear in the past 13 years.
text:
Australia's business leaders are discovering they need to dramatically change the way they work to respond to a disruptive wave of business change, driven by digital technologies. In the United States, digital disruption has seen more than half the companies on the Fortune 500 disappear in the past 13 years.

But the realisation has come late for many local companies. Last year a Frost and Sullivan study revealed that three-quarters of Australian executives did not accept there was rapid change in their industry, marking them among the most complacent in the world.

Slowly, though, the message is getting through. A global study released last week by KPMG and executive recruiter Harvey Nash drew an acknowledgement by more than half Australia's chief information officers that the disruption of business models by digital technologies was causing significant change to their businesses.

According to KPMG technology advisory partner Guy Holland, "Boards also have to evolve. Given the strategic role of technology in delivering value and growth - and the scale of impact security and cyber threats can have across organisations - boards can no longer abrogate all responsibility for the IT function to the CIO."

Holland says board members need to understand how technology enables their business strategy, the business case for using certain technologies and how to embed a digital culture in their organisation. "The advent of cloud, mobile, the internet of things and big data as key business enablers has meant far greater scrutiny on the IT function at board level."

He said the more progressive companies were elevating digital to the level of corporate strategy and enabling the function to be delivered through, or report in to either the chief strategy officer or the chief executive officer, rather than seeing digital as a marketing or purely technological function.

The speed and implications of digital disruption headlined the Daze of Disruption conference in Sydney, on May 18 and 19, which included speakers such as former Foxtel boss Kym Williams, Xero CMO Andy Lark, and Capgemini's global digital practice leader, Didier Bonnet.

Event producer Jeremy Knibbs of the Wrong Hat Group, himself a former media CEO, told The Australian Financial Review, "Being a chief executive or other leader of an incumbent company in this day and age is a very lonely role.

"You can find yourself compromised by the board and shareholder demands. And as disruption gathers pace, there are so many more variables that work against an incumbent now - especially one which can't move quickly because they are big or are public or have shareholders.

"You can make it, but you need to have a huge sense of urgency and purpose, you will usually need some help from some good outsiders, and you probably need an element of luck."

Take the legal industry, for instance. Mike Barry, executive vice-president of strategy and product at Aderant, says there is a growing belief in the sector that the middle is hollowing out and that within the decade the top of the market will be dominated by global legal brands, and at the other end, smaller boutique firms and companies offering commoditised legal services.

There is even the possibility for an Uber-like disruption to legal markets, he suggests. Companies like ZoomLegal could become huge legal brands themselves, even though they have no lawyers.

Meanwhile, company executives at the coalface of service delivery are adamant that there is nothing intrinsic about incumbency that leads to failure. They argue that the ability to deliver much better experiences to customers while improving organisational efficiencies is a boon for companies willing to make the necessary investment.

Bryan Meredith, head of digital at Rabo Bank, said both his bank's digital brand, Rabo Direct, and its traditional agricultural banking operation were able to deliver superior services due to the impact of digital technologies.

"We do have a purely digital brand that is a deposit-taking brand and we also have a very high-touch relationship management business that involves a lot of face-to-face [client] conversations, often sitting around the kitchen table ... What digital does for the business is allow for efficiency and time improvements."

Damon Robbins, a senior marketing executive in Sydney, sees digital as a game-changer. "A lot of businesses want to put the customer at the core of what they do and the CMOs are being asked to drive that. There is a lot of work to be done however, and a lot of cultural change is needed."
direction: bearish for traditional incumbents, bullish for digital enablers

headline: Primary's new CEO, board turn to UBS for strategic advice
intro: A new boss has brought about a new corporate adviser for Primary Health Care, with investment bank UBS believed to be assisting Peter Gregg as he maps out the pathology and medical centre group's future.
text:
A new boss has brought about a new corporate adviser for Primary Health Care, with investment bank UBS believed to be assisting Peter Gregg as he maps out the pathology and medical centre group's future.

It's understood Primary's board has taken pitches from a raft of investment banks in recent months, with advisers spotting an opportunity to get inside the $2.7 billion company's boardroom.

Mr Gregg took the reins on March 2 and has quickly got about to tinkering with Primary's strategy after years under Ed Bateman's leadership.

Sources said UBS was involved as a strategic adviser, although it is not known whether the bank's role stretches to a more formal mandate. UBS is also believed to be close to Primary chairman Rob Ferguson.

Street Talk has written at some length about Primary as a takeover target, with private equity firms known to have gone as far as to try to line up debt packages for a potential leveraged buyout.

It is believed an approach has not yet been made to the $2.7 billion pathology, diagnostic imaging and medical centre operator. However, the funding discussions show the potential suitor may be getting serious about a bid.

UBS's role as a strategic adviser also comes after a prescient review from health sector analyst Andrew Goodsall. Mr Goodsall's team told clients last month that the new set of eyes at Primary could lead to a few tweaks to the company's strategy and could lead to asset sales worth about $200 million.

It will be interesting to see whether UBS ends up handling any of those asset sales, which include the $180 million Health Communication Network. Deutsche Bank has previously tried to sell HCN, at the request of some powerful primary shareholders at the time. However, Primary could not turn up an adequate offer.

Elsewhere in private equity, QIC has partnered with US private equity firm Webster Capital on the $45 million acquisition of equestrian product retailer Dover Saddlery.

QIC is one of Webster's largest institutional investors and has agreed to provide financing for the cash offer of $8.50 a share.

Nasdaq-listed Dover announced it had entered into a merger agreement, whereby a company formed by Webster would acquire all of the outstanding shares.

Its shares were trading at about $5 prior to the offer.

Dover was founded in 1975 and has become a leading multichannel retailer of equestrian products.

QIC, owned by the Queensland government, has more than $US70 billion ($92 billion) in funds under management and more than 90 clients including governments, pension plans and sovereign wealth funds.
direction: upward for Primary Health Care shares

headline: Milk war rolls on but supply deals see farmers smiling
intro: Processors secure long-term retail agreements.
text:
Processors secure long-term retail agreements.

It is a war of a different kind that has lasted almost as long as World War II, and has no end in sight.

Hostilities erupted throughout the nation's supermarket aisles on Australia Day 2011, when Coles slashed the price of milk to $1 a litre.

Woolworths and Aldi quickly retaliated, raising the ire of farmers, who said they would be forced off the land as the supermarkets crunched the profit margins of dairy processors.

But on Friday, in Australia's dairy heartland, there was a ceasefire of sorts. Farmers not only stood alongside representatives from Woolworths and the world's biggest dairy exporter, Fonterra - they were smiling.

The supermarket battles are no different from other wars when it comes to driving technological advancement.

In Fonterra's case, it has come in the form of a $31 million investment at its factory at Cobden in south-west Victoria. Here, Fonterra will produce about 115,000 bottles of fresh milk for Woolworths each day for the next 10 years.

Fonterra Australia chief executive Judith Swales said the supply agreement with Woolworths had given Fonterra the confidence to build the "most technological advanced milk bottling plant in the southern hemisphere", while creating more certainty for farmers.

The factory relies heavily on automation, using robots to pack pallets of milk and drive forklifts. This keeps the cost of labour - the second biggest cost after procuring milk - low and puts a stopper on price rises at the supermarket checkout.

Rival Murray Goulburn adopted a similar theory when it built an $80 million milk bottling factory at Laverton, in Melbourne's west, after securing a 10-year supply contract with Coles in 2014. Like Fonterra, Murray Goulburn employs 50 people at its milk factory and relies heavily on automation.

"For us, this is the future," Ms Swales said. "Using European technology, the lines incorporate a state-of-the-art management system, deploying various technologies to ensure waste is minimised.

"This efficiency means this partnership is ... a win for Woolworths, a win for Fonterra and, most importantly, a win for our farmers."

Ms Swales said the plant would not only produce private label milk for Woolworths, but a Fonterra-branded premium product, the details of which will be announced soon.

Australian Dairy Farmers president Noel Campbell welcomed Fonterra's 10-year supply deal with Woolworths.

"Retail contracts with this sort of longevity provide milk processors with the security and certainty they require to invest in the industry's capability and growth," Mr Campbell said.

"The 10-year contract also gives Fonterra the option to provide longer-term contracts to their suppliers. Farmers are always more willing to invest on-farm when they have a certain destination for their product."

Victorian Agriculture Minister Jaala Pulford also supported Fonterra's partnership with Woolworths. But she said it did not completely vindicate the milk price war between the supermarkets.

"Long-term contracts are always a great development for our primary producers and people who work in the value chain, like this factory at Cobden," Ms Pulford said. "But I'm not sure I'd agree that the milk pricing forced on farmers by supermarkets is a good thing. People were put under a lot of pressure in that period."

Farmers are still facing price pressure. Many are facing the prospect of no milk price increases this season amid a somewhat souring global dairy environment. Although prices have risen at the previous two GlobalDairyTrade auctions, key commodity prices are still about half of what they were 18 months ago as the world continues to battle an oversupply of dairy, stemming from weakening Chinese demand and Russian trade sanctions.

Woolworths merchandise manager for chilled produce Ewan Shearer said the supermarket would like to increase the price of its private-label milk but it had to remain competitive.

In other words, the $1-a-litre milk - which Australian Dairy Farmers says is now more like 87¢ if you have an inflation rate of 2 or 3 per cent - won't end soon.

"We'd happily see the price increase tomorrow. But we didn't set it from memory," Mr Shearer said. "We have got to offer our customers what's competitive in the market.

"At the end of the day our shoppers demand that the price in the market is X and we are at that price."
direction: bullish for Fonterra and dairy processors

headline: BHP Billiton winds back China forecast
intro: BHP Billiton chief Andrew Mackenzie has conceded that peak steel production in China will be lower than the mining giant's previous forecasts, by between 25 million and 165 million tonnes but insists the Chinese market is not impossible to read.
text:
BHP Billiton chief Andrew Mackenzie has conceded that peak steel production in China will be lower than the mining giant's previous forecasts, by between 25 million and 165 million tonnes but insists the Chinese market is not impossible to read.

And he declared the miner remains in "resolute" commitment to its so-called "progressive" dividend policy to maintain or steadily increase its dividend, which costs at least $US6.5 billion a year.

"That resolve is shared by every man and woman who works for BHP Billiton," Mr Mackenzie said on Tuesday. "It has withstood many previous cycles and is a key differentiator relative to our peers."

BHP's second-biggest shareholder, Aberdeen Asset Management said the progressive dividend helped to underpin the valuation of BHP.

Aberdeen London-based senior investment manager Ben Ritchie said he welcomed BHP's commitment to the progressive dividend.

"BHP has invested an awful lot of shareholders' money on capital investment over the last five or six years and I think shareholders want to see a return on that investment," he told The Australian Financial Review, adding that the same logic applied to Rio Tinto.

"These companies have strong balance sheets. I wouldn't support the company paying out a dividend it cannot afford, but at current commodity prices it seems sustainable."

Mr Mackenzie said the miner was "doing everything in our power to buttress the dividend". "This has not come by compromising our balance sheet or our plans for growth."

BHP on Tuesday posted a 51 per cent fall in underlying profit for the year to $US6.4 billion, excluding South32. It was a slight miss on analyst expectations and the miner's worst result in a decade. The result is less than the productivity gains and controllable cost efficiencies the miner has made in the past two years.

BHP's underlying payout ratio is 155 per cent (meaning it paid out 155 per cent of its profit), and its dividend yield is about 6.4 per cent, after taking into account franking tax credits. Rio's is not far behind at about 6 per cent. BHP has not cut its dividend since 1988, and did not rebase it's dividend when it spun out South32 in May this year.

BHP"s shares have collapsed to $23.34, well down from the $33 they were fetching this time last year.

BHP's disciplined cost savings and productivity drive is not enough to offset the crunch commodity prices are having on its bottom line. The miner said on Tuesday it had hit its 2017 cost-cutting target of $US4 billion after 12 months. It is also cutting capital spending for 2016 for the third time since February, to $US8 billion as it moves to protect its dividend. Mr Ritchie said it would not be surprising if the guidance was cut again. Capital spending for 2017 is put at $US7 billion.

Net profit fell to a 13-year low of $US1.9 billion after the miner took writedowns in copper and shale gas.

But Mr Mackenzie insisted the company is not hurting its growth prospects by slashing investment in new and existing projects.

"These reductions do not in any way slow in any way our growth". Productivity gains would allow the miner to grow with less capital, he said.

While Glencore chief executive Ivan Glasenberg last week declared the Chinese market was "impossible to read", Mr Mackenzie disagreed.

"We don't find China impossible to read, we've been at this game for decades, and I think by and large we have made forecasts about China and we've made very sound business decisions off those forecasts that have proved to be correct. I don't agree that China has not articulated its forward strategy well. I think it's clear but you have to look deeply and you have to look across all the sectors and that's what we do."

BHP revised its Chinese peak steel forecast date to between 935 and 985 million tonnes, compared to previous guidance of 1 to 1.1 billion tonnes, but kept the target date at the mid 2020's.

Investors have been questioning the wisdom of the miner's so-called "progressive dividend" policy.

Prices for BHP's four key "pillar" commodities, of iron ore, metallurgical coal, copper and oil and gas, have collapsed into multi-year lows in the past 12 months. Potash is a possible fifth pillar.

Mr Mackenzie said the miner was "only just now beginning to reap the simplification dividend" of South 32.
direction: downward pressure on BHP Billiton shares

headline: Carlyle, Ironbridge back Prospa
intro: Online small business lender Prospa has secured $60 million in capital from a consortium of private equity investors, including The Carlyle Group and Ironbridge Capital, as revealed by Street Talk Online on Wednesday.
text:
Online small business lender Prospa has secured $60 million in capital from a consortium of private equity investors, including The Carlyle Group and Ironbridge Capital, as revealed by Street Talk Online on Wednesday.

The proceeds from the capital raising, the largest to date by an Australian online lender, will be used to help fund more Australian small businesses and support Prospa's expansion locally.

The co-investment is led by Carlyle, Ironbridge, Airtree Ventures and high profile private investor David Fite. Prospa's original funder of the first round, Entree Capital, also contributed towards the $60 million raising.

Beau Bertoli, joint CEO of Prospa said: "This strategic raising included one of the largest funds and some of the most established technology-focused investors in the world. This group of investors is a true representation of Prospa's strengths and ambitions."

Greg Ruddock, joint CEO of Ironbridge Capital said: "We have looked at many fintech businesses but Prospa stood out from the crowd as the market leader. We are very excited about investing in Prospa to enable them to continue their rapid expansion as well as help the Australian small business economy thrive. Leveraging technology is key to their growth and we are proud to be the lead investor of this financing round."

Prospa is an online small business lender with a proprietary technology platform.
direction: bullish for Prospa and the fintech sector

headline: Stock watch
intro: Mayne Pharma
text:
Mayne Pharma

Shares in Adelaide-based pharmaceutical company Mayne Pharma Group surged 7.9 per cent higher on Wednesday following the launch of its morphine sulfate extended-release tablets in the United States; a market estimated to be worth $205 million a year. The news comes immediately after Wednesday's announcement alongside IDT Australia, detailing the former's generic temozolomide brain cancer drug is also ready for lift-off in the US. Mayne Pharma has suffered recently, with the share price off 15 per cent in the last month alone, so it seems investors are willing to cheer the good news when it arrives.
direction: Mayne Pharma shares to rise

headline: Dismal economy, dreadful politics
intro: It has been a lacklustre week for the economy, and a dismal one for anyone looking for leadership from our political classes.
text:
It has been a lacklustre week for the economy, and a dismal one for anyone looking for leadership from our political classes.

Opposition Leader Bill Shorten's willingness to undermine the China free-trade agreement shows a breathtaking level of capture by vested union interests. By grandstanding over a potential handful - at the very most - of contracting jobs, Mr Shorten is happy to put at risk vastly more employment in services and agri-business through the best trade agreement with China that anyone has secured. In making militant union bosses his first priority in the China deal, Mr Shorten has publicly torched his credentials for national economic management.

But the Abbott government continues to rule by ruling out. Its only idea now seems to be defensive politics, trying to bat out to stumps. This week, a competition effects test and a bank deposit tax were pushed off the table. They follow the abandoning of any change to superannuation tax breaks, and only the most reluctant consideration of the GST and tax reform. These decisions might all have had their merits, but it's the policy retreats that stand out because of the Abbott government's inability or unwillingness to advance on any front. Two years into the job on Monday, a Coalition government has failed to prosecute the public case for budget prudence and liberalising reforms as a way of revitalising a post-boom economy, and has paralysed itself as a result.

Yet this week the economy gave its sharpest notice yet that it is not fitting in with the Abbott government's electoral requirements. The quarterly GDP figure avoided going negative only by some government spending. The national accounts showed an annualised growth figure of 2 per cent, still far from the 2.75 per cent forecast for this financial year in the budget. Net disposable income per capita, the kitchen table measure of the economy, is down 1.2 per cent, and has fallen 2.5 per cent in the past two years as the terms of trade boom that once showered money shrinks by 30 per cent from the peak.

Australia might have reached 96 quarters without a recession, but this is also the 12th quarter in a row of sub-par growth. Ross Garnaut, chief economic adviser to the reforming Hawke government, says it is time to recognise that trend and act on it. It is taking the non-mining sector far longer than hoped to pick up the slack from mining investment: the "growth gap" that Reserve Bank governor Glenn Stevens warned of in an interview with The Australian Financial Review nearly three years ago is becoming a reality.

The economy is still more akin to a slow train than a train wreck. It is not beyond speeding up. Growth figures will recover somewhat as mine exports replace mine investment. Rio Tinto and BHP Billiton are sticking to their long-term faith in Chinese iron ore demand, though it is also clear that risk of another mishap in the world economy has for the first time shifted to China. The falling dollar is helping exports, though the currency also reflects now investor worry about China.

The big lever left to us is finding the political nerve for the home-grown reforms to create incentive, push productivity, and generate growth. Having dumped the effects test, the government should have a freer hand to push any number of proposals from the huge Harper competition review that might drive us to do better. The Abbott government also needs to convince the states that some of a GST rise must to go to reforming a tax system that is unbalanced.

Nearly two weeks ago a gathering of non-politicians at the National Reform Summit, organised by The Australian Financial Review, The Australian and KPMG, recognised that reform trade-offs do exist. The summit's very existence was a rebuke to all in politics, but this week shows how much they need to listen to its message.
direction: bearish for domestic-focused shares and property sectors
