headline: Mayne Pharma raises $115m for acquisition
intro: Mayne Pharma will spend $US50 million ($64 million) to buy back the US business of its antibiotic drug Doryx in an attempt to stem a collapse in the performance of one of its main products.
text:
Mayne Pharma will spend $US50 million ($64 million) to buy back the US business of its antibiotic drug Doryx in an attempt to stem a collapse in the performance of one of its main products.

The acquisition from pharmaceutical giant Actavis will be funded by a $115 million rights issue managed by Credit Suisse and UBS. The stock has lost 14 per cent in the past 12 months and closed at 70¢ on Monday before entering a trading halt on Tuesday morning, after The Australian Financial Review's Street Talk first revealed the capital raising on afr.com.

Mayne chief executive Scott Richards said the acquisition would transform Mayne's US business, by adding specialty brands to its existing generics and contract services efforts. "We know the Doryx product well and have been manufacturing different formulations of the product for the past 30 years," he said in a statement.

The move allows Mayne, which has a market value of $431 million, to bring Doryx's US distribution back in house and arrest the decline of what historically had been one of its strongest selling drugs.

After Actavis bought Mayne's former distributor Warner Chilcott in 2013, sales of the drug fell off sharply.

Mr Richards told shareholders last November that Mayne had entered a "manufacturing holiday" for Doryx on the back of declining demand in the US, as Actavis worked through stockpiles.

To illustrate the collapse, Mr Richards told shareholders at the annual meeting that Doryx sales accounted for $7.5 million in revenue in the four months ended October 31, 2013, compared with just $100,000 in the period ended October 31, 2014.

Mayne has the support of several big name shareholders, including pubs baron Bruce Mathieson, who holds a stake of just under 7.5 per cent. Fellow BRW Rich 200 member Richard Smith is also a shareholder.

Mayne will establish a new specialty brands division in the United States that will have 66 sales staff and will be led by former Warner Chilcott executive Andy McClenaghan. The deal is expected to close on February 26, with a renewed sales effort to begin in May.

It may turn out to be a well-timed move for Mayne.

Actavis has plenty on its plate this year as it integrates a more recent acquisition. In the biggest healthcare deal of 2014, Actavis bought Botox-maker Allergan for $US66 million.

Although not commenting on the Mayne deal Actavis' local boss Ron van der Pluijm said the Allergan tie-up would allow the company, which has revenue of $150 million in Australia, to boost sales by marketing a greater range of products to doctors.

"To be a more sizeable company always helps in the healthcare industry," Mr van der Pluijm said. "You have more resources to do things and support your products."
direction: Mayne Pharma shares face downward pressure due to dilution and fundamental product decline

headline: North America poised for QBE growth spurt
intro: QBE Insurance Group's North America division was once its biggest problem child.
text:
QBE Insurance Group's North America division was once its biggest problem child. But three years of hard work and painful remediation efforts have morphed the $US5 billion ($6.8 billion) arm into a growth opportunity.

The company plans to source $US1 billion of gross written premium in the medium term from the United States' $US85 billion specialty market, which covers sectors such as cyber liability, surety and inland marine. It currently writes $US423 million worth of specialty risk or less than 10 per cent of its North American portfolio.

QBE is aiming to grow its specialty book to $US600 million this year, and branch into three new sectors over the northern hemisphere summer.

It's a plan that has QBE chief executive John Neal excited about the future, and comes three years after the division contributed to shock profit downgrades at the $15.6 billion insurer.

When Mr Neal took a seat in a meeting room at the group's new Sydney offices in 8 Chifley, his countenance was relaxed. It was Friday, and he had just finished lunch with the Sydney Swans, the AFL team QBE had supported for the past 30 years.

Flanked by outgoing QBE North America boss David Duclos and his successor Russell Johnston, Mr Neal was ready to tell the turnaround story of the group's largest and previously loss-making division, and its journey back to profitability.

"The shape of the business as it is today, is the business that we want going forward," Mr Neal told The Australian Financial Review ahead of the group's investor day on Tuesday.

"Tuesday is a really good opportunity to stand up and reinforce [that] we'll meet the targets of 2016, and we've got really good plans as to how we can manage performance in challenging market conditions."

The North American business, which includes a standard lines or commercial arm, a specialty division, crop insurance and reinsurance, produced a combined operating ratio (COR) of 99 per cent last year. It was the first time QBE's COR - a key measure of profitability - for North America had fallen below the crucial 100 per cent mark since 2013, when it blew out to 111.5 and sunk the division into the red.

QBE will outline on Tuesday that it is aiming for a low-90s COR through strict underwriting and careful expansion in its specialty lines.

"We want to make sure we maintain our underwriting discipline as we build out these new capabilities," Mr Duclos said. "We've got a good opportunity to modestly grow the business, but do it in a very profitable way."

Mr Duclos, who was retired before he was coaxed into the North America chief executive role by Mr Neal in 2013, was brought in to clean up what one analyst called a "basket-case division", saddled with loss-making businesses due to rampant expansion under the previous leadership team. A drought in 2012, a fall in corn prices and billions of dollars in claims from Superstorm Sandy compounded QBE's problems.

In three years, QBE sold underperforming parts of its portfolio, including its agency businesses and lender-placed arm. Mr Neal and Mr Duclos, the former boss of XL's global insurance operations, also spearheaded the group's increased effort to improve its risk underwriting discipline.

The company has been on a strong recruitment drive after slashing staff numbers from 8200 in 2012 to 3700.

As mergers and acquisitions pepper the North American landscape, including insurance behemoth ACE's $29.7 billion takeover of Chubb, talent leakage has turned into an opportunity for QBE. About 2100 of the company's staff have joined QBE in the past three years, as Mr Duclos sought personnel with specific underwriting expertise.

In the past year, the company has appointed key executives such as former Chubb and AIG executive Eric Shanks, ACE private risk services underwriting executive Guy Huntley, Fireman's Fund commercial insurance executive Glenn Skrynecki and Allianz Global Corporate & Specialty's Walter Grote to its leadership ranks.

"There's a significant amount of dislocation going on in the North American market today ... there are some very large mainstream companies that are going through significant change," Mr Duclos said. "We've saved a lot of money in North America when you think about going from 8200 to 3700, but we have reinvested every year to start building the business."

Despite the company's efforts, QBE's share price has dropped 17.2 per cent in the past year to $11.42 compared with the 5.6 per cent fall of the benchmark S&P/ASX 200 index.

While underlying profits have improved, the company's shares are highly leveraged to global rate movements. Mr Neal acknowledges the macroeconomic impacts on the stock price, but remains confident QBE can "at least match if not outperform our global peers in terms of value that we can generate for the shareholder".

Mr Johnston said his priority would be to "maintain underwriting discipline as we build out new capabilities".

"There's an inordinate amount of organisation momentum right now, and so my priority is to make sure our team continues to execute and deliver on that," he said.

QBE's focus on turning around North America and its improved underwriting have been key drivers for fund managers such as Fidelity's Paul Taylor to back the company.

Mr Taylor, who runs the $4.8 billion Fidelity Australian Equities Fund, started buying QBE shares in 2015 after shying away from the insurer for several years. Fidelity is now among QBE's top 10 biggest shareholders.

But he is cautious about the sustainability of QBE's turnaround, and is demanding consistently improved results over the next few profit seasons before he is convinced profit downgrades are a thing of the past. "You don't prove yourself with one result, you do it over several," he said.

It is a sentiment echoed by long-standing QBE investor, Australian Foundation Investment Company. Ross Barker, managing director of AFIC, had numerous conversations with Mr Neal about the dire US performance during 2011 to 2013, and has tracked its recovery closely.

"We've been impressed with what the management has done in getting the companies through the difficulties it was landed in after the buying binge in the US," Mr Barker said.

"Their COR and ROE [return on equity] still need to improve, but we understand these things take time."

Mr Neal is "extremely confident" in the group's ability to generate ongoing underwriting profits. "[Downgrades] are a thing of the past," he said.
direction: QBE Insurance Group shares likely to rise

headline: Walker halts $2b Collins Square sale
intro: Billionaire developer Lang Walker has abandoned a proposed sell-off of his vast Collins Square development in Melbourne after receiving only three bids for the entire $2 billion project.
text:
Billionaire developer Lang Walker has abandoned a proposed sell-off of his vast Collins Square development in Melbourne after receiving only three bids for the entire $2 billion project.

The five-tower portfolio under development at the Docklands extension to the Melbourne CBD is among the largest urban redevelopments in the Australian market.

Mr Walker, assisted by UBS, opened up the process to bids on individual assets after only three whole-portfolio bids emerged from about 25 prospective bidders.

"Tight yields of about 5 per cent were offered for the completed buildings," Mr Walker said in a statement on Thursday. "I now believe Collins Square is an asset that we should continue to hold for the long term and as a consequence we have decided to withdraw from the formal sale campaign.

"We have an excellent management team in place at Collins Square and strong interest from potential tenants for the remaining space in the development."

Mr Walker commended UBS for doing "excellent work" in soliciting strong bids for the first round.

Among the final contenders for the project, or elements of it, were LaSalle Investment Management and AMP Wholesale Office Fund, along with major investors from China and Singapore. Mr Walker's company Walker Corporation had planned to sell down Collins Square to free up capital to fund its development pipeline.

The Walker Corporation workbook includes a range of commercial and residential projects across the country including the $2 billion Parramatta Square project in Sydney and the Festival Square project in Adelaide.

But the company has now sourced equity funding for the Parramatta Square project, obviating the need for additional capital from the proposed sale of Collins Square, it said.

First-round offers had been due late last month on the massive Melbourne development after a one-month extension on the sale process for the divestment that was unveiled in November last year.

Collins Square comprises five towers and the refurbished historic Goods Shed. In all, the project will deliver 250,000 square metres of lettable area, about the same size as the Barangaroo redevelopment in Sydney.

Two of the five towers are complete and fully tenanted. Two more are due to be completed by the middle of this year and the fifth tower in 2017. The project has a blue-chip rent book, with tenants such as Marsh Mercer, Transurban, KPMG, Commonwealth Bank of Australia, Penguin Random House and Pearson, Maddocks, Link Group, Mott MacDonald and AECOM.
direction: bearish for Walker Corporation and commercial property sector

headline: Australians opting for lifestyle over cash: NAB
text:
Australians are prioritising their quality of life over the size of their pay packet, with almost one in three saying that having spare time and being able to stump up cash to help family and friends is what makes them feel wealthy.

Nearly 30 per cent of those surveyed by NAB's wealth management arm MLC said factors such as having enough money in reserve for emergencies and the financial freedom to live the way they want define wealth to them.

By comparison 33 per cent of average Australians said their income reflected how wealthy they felt, versus 24 per cent who said it was their overall net worth.

"While we still have traditional definitions of success that do refer to income and net worth, we are now starting to rate things like being able to do what we want to do when we want to do it and to have some spare money available for emergencies to pay down debt," said MLC general manager of corporate super, Lara Bourguignon.

"That sense of stability and security are as important for us now as the amount of income we see on our pay slip. We are certainly seeing that Australians are starting to view wealth as more than money".

The latest quarterly MLC Wealth Sentiment Survey reveals that, while 22 per cent of people said being debt-free was the most important lifestyle driver of wealth, 14 per cent of Australians said having time to do what they wanted made them feel rich. This comes as the inability of Australians to afford their own home shapes up as one of the most contentious political issues.

Ms Bourguignon said that, against a backdrop of the housing affordability crisis and a growing desire for a quality lifestyle, Australians may start looking beyond property as the basis of their financial foundation.

"I think what we are seeing through the research is that we are thinking about our wealth differently across the different demographics. The research shows that the younger demographic is the only one that is looking to invest more over the next quarter," she said.
direction: bearish for consumer discretionary and housing sectors

headline: Salvos to sell more sites for development
intro: The Salvation Army aims to capitalise on Parramatta's scarcity of sites for office and residential development and sell its Parramatta office and church hall.
text:
The Salvation Army aims to capitalise on Parramatta's scarcity of sites for office and residential development and sell its Parramatta office and church hall.

The three buildings, on the corner of 93-95 Phillip Street and 32 Smith Street, form a prime 2438-square-metre redevelopment site with a potential gross floor area of 28,037sq m and ideal for a mixed-use hotel or serviced apartments and commercial project.

The properties, a block away from the high-profile Parramatta Square, could fetch between $20 million and $40 million, sources said.

The demand for sites and office space in Parramatta is exemplified by Eureka Funds Management's recent $85 million purchase of the A-grade office building at 18 Smith Street.

"[The Salvos site] position as a gateway to Parramatta CBD will provide strong incentive to commercial developers and institutional backed investors due to the exceptionally low vacancy of 1 per cent in the prime CBD office market," marketing agent JLL's John Macree said. He is managing the sale with colleague Michael Binskin and Raine & Horne's John Surian.

"The focus has been on the mixed-use residential high-rise opportunities ... this has led to withdrawal of commercial stock. There is now strong demand for commercial development, the strongest I've seen it in 43 years in Parramatta," Mr Surian said.

Salvos recently offloaded its one-hectare Dee Why site Aged Care Plus Centre in Sydney's northern beaches to an apartment developer for about $25 million.
direction: bullish for commercial property and developers

headline: Fed on hold after poor jobs data
intro: Washington | A sharp slowdown in hiring by US employers last month probably snuffed out any chance of a US interest rate rise in June and catapulted the Australian dollar nearly 2 per cent to more than US73¢ on the weekend.
text:
Washington | A sharp slowdown in hiring by US employers last month probably snuffed out any chance of a US interest rate rise in June and catapulted the Australian dollar nearly 2 per cent to more than US73¢ on the weekend.

The US economy added just 38,000 jobs in May, the lowest monthly number in almost six years and well short of the consensus forecast of 160,000. Job gains previously reported for April and March were revised down by a combined 59,000 positions.

Currency traders sold off the US dollar on the disappointing news, betting the US Federal Reserve would abandon the possibility of an interest rate increase this month.

The Australian dollar surged 1.85 per cent to US73.63¢ on the weekend, more than 1¢ higher from the level of US72.45¢ just before the data was published on Friday morning US time. Shares on Wall Street finished down modestly, after staging a late afternoon comeback.

"A Fed move for June is off the table," Wells Fargo's John Silvia said.

Paradoxically, the US unemployment rate fell to a very low 4.7 per cent, from 5 per cent, due to nearly half a million people giving up the hunt for work, triggering a 0.2 percentage point decrease in the participation rate.

More positively, hourly earnings increased 0.2 per cent in May and an upwardly revised 0.4 per cent in April, to be a healthy 2.5 per cent higher over the year.

The pick up in wages and fall in the jobless rate give credence to one view that the labour market is tightening and nearing full employment.

The Federal Open Market Committee will need to weigh conflicting signs of the state of the labour market when it holds a two-day policy meeting on June 14-15. The disappointing jobs report had economists scrambling to revise their forecasts to either a July or September tightening.

After the payroll data, Fed Fund futures prices implied traders were betting there was just a 4 per cent chance of a rate increase in June, 34 per cent in July and 46 per cent in September, according to CME Group.

In recent weeks, several FOMC participants canvassed the resumption of raising rates in June or July if the economic data was strong.

Federal Reserve chairwoman Janet Yellen said last week rates would probably rise in coming months if the US economy continued to improve as she expected.

Dr Yellen is due to speak on Monday, when there will be an opportunity to update the markets on her latest thinking on the economy.

On Friday soon after the employment report was released, one of the FOMC's most dovish members, Lael Brainard, said the central bank should wait to see more economic data before lifting rates. She noted global economic risks from Britain's June 23 referendum on its future in the European Union and fragility in China.

A strike by 35,000 workers at telecommunications company Verizon contributed to the May employment softness, but this only partly explained the slower hiring.

Adding to the gloom on Friday, the US non-manufacturing ISM index fell to 52.9 in May from 55.7 in April.

"All this means is that the Fed is unlikely to raise rates in either its June or July meetings until it gets a better read on the job market," IHS chief economist Nariman Behravesh said.

On Wall Street on Friday, the blue chip Dow Jones Industrial Average 148 points, before rallying late to finish down only 31.5 points or 0.18 per cent to 17,807.06. The broader S&P 500 lost 0.29 per cent to 2100.07. The tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite dropped 0.58 per cent to 4942.52.

Over the week, the Dow lost 0.4 per cent, the S&P was flat and the Nasdaq eked out a 0.2 per cent gain.

Key points

A rise in wages and a fall in the jobless rate indicate the labour market is tightening.

Workforce participation falls as half a million people give up looking for jobs.
direction: AUD appreciation, US equities downside

headline: Charter Hall pulls IPO: lack of interest
text:
The fading appetite for yield stocks and the growing appeal of commodities stocks combined to scuttle Australia's largest ever property sector float, the $1.1 billion Charter Hall long WALE REIT.

On Wednesday, listed fund manager Charter Hall dropped its plan to float the new trust after a bookbuild failed to raise sufficient institutional support.

"The group will consider alternative options for the assets within the proposed Long WALE REIT portfolio," the company said.

The announcement was short and sharp, but the scuppering of the listing - premised on a record IPO for the sector - was much longer in the making.

By the time, the Charter Hall and its joint lead managers, UBS and JPMorgan, were putting together the institutional bookbuild on Tuesday night, the listed property sector had already been in retreat for months.

Over that period the sector had given up 10 per cent. Yield stocks, for so long a go-to destination for yield investors, are no longer a safe haven.

The anticipation of a US rate rise has hit property stocks, bonds and infrastructure. Bond yields have risen by around 50 basis points in recent weeks.

Charter Hall said there had been significant retail demand for the $1.12 billion initial public offering of the new trust.

But the institutional offer "did not attract the sufficient quality of support that the group deemed appropriate to create an orderly market," it said.

The property trust is a spin-off of office, industrial and retail assets held across Charter Hall's unlisted platform and aimed at investors looking to weather a low interest rate over the longer term.

The new trust forecast a 5.3 per cent distribution yield in the 2017 fiscal year for investors coming in on an offer price of $4.

Charter Hall had allocated $250 million to buy into the raising, while retail investors had committed to around $300 million.

Rollover investors from the existing owners of the float properties were expected to soak up close to $150 million.

The institutional allocation of around $350 million had been covered in the bookbuild but not strongly enough to guarantee a strong after-market, crucial to the float's success.

UBS property banker Tim Church remains optimistic Charter Hall will eventually relaunch the float depending on the market.

"For something of the quality of the Long WALE REIT, to issue it into a market where you don't think it will trade well in the secondary market, is the wrong thing to do. "You have have to be a good custodian of capital.

"That's not something we were prepared to risk for the benefit of the clients and the retail and institutional investors.

"It was a tough decision but it's right thing to do. Short-term pain for long-term gain."

Folkestone Maxim Asset Management managing director Winston Sammut, an early critic of the float, said the listing had been caught out by the market move away from property.

A relaunch of the float would need to address that prevailing mood among investors, he said. "If it's going to be repriced, it's got to be a reasonable move and not a token 5 cents," he said.

Charter Hall is not alone. In Europe last week, investors also baulked at the largest IPO in the property sector there this year.

The OfficeFirst float in Frankfurt, with a target IPO of close to $1.3 billion, was pulled as markets deteriorated.

The new Charter Hall spin-off was to include 66 assets that have been held in unlisted partnerships and funds that Charter Hall controls.

That portfolio was valued at $1.253 billion. A key feature of the proposed trust was the portfolio's long average lease expiry of 12.5 years.

Growth was a key theme for the fund, which was to be headed by Charter Hall's Avi Anger who has been leading the parent group's transactions team.

More property inside

Triguboff on RBA’s Lowe p36

Stethoscope on units p36

Australia’s crane index p37

Rise in home loan arrears p37

Sales wrap p40-41

JPMorgan Mortgage Industry Report p45
direction: property sector and yield stocks

headline: Apartment resale platform Aofun.com.au launches
intro: Desperate foreign buyers will be able to sell their Australian off-the-plan properties online following the launch of selling website Aofun.com.au.
text:
Desperate foreign buyers will be able to sell their Australian off-the-plan properties online following the launch of selling website Aofun.com.au.

The English and Mandarin Melbourne-based portal, founded by entrepreneur Jason Zhu, facilitates the resale of pre-settlement properties.

The website is aimed at overseas investors who have paid deposits for off-the-plan properties but have been caught out by the major Australian banks' decision to freeze lending to foreigners, Mr Zhu said.

"In some cases astute investors will be able to pick up a bargain where the seller may be willing to forgo the full 10 per cent deposit plus more. In particular, there is a real opportunity for first home buyers who may not have sufficient savings for their first home to register on the website and acquire their first home with the deposit paid," he said.

The price offers will not be available publicly - it is not a bidding system - and the seller will negotiate directly with potential buyers.

Three weeks into its launch, the platform has already mustered about 150 listings with "thousands" coming, Mr Zhu said. One apartment has already been sold and several sales are pending.

Although it would be "easier" for overseas investors to walk away from their deposit - usually 10 per cent - they would rather lose their deposit as a discount in a resale, Mr Zhu said.

He has seen discounts of up to 10 per cent on the website.

"There will be circumstances where they may be willing to sell below the original contract amount to avoid potential lawsuit from the property developer or receiving a bad credit rating," Mr Zhu said.

Pressure from apartments flooding the market needs to be eased to avoid a potential housing crash, he said.
direction: bearish for off-the-plan apartment developers and investors

headline: STILL WINNING FRIENDS
text:
Dale Carnegie's guide for dealing with other people was published in 1936. In a digital era, it still works, writes Jo Lennan.

The title makes it sound like a flatterer's handbook. How To Win Friends And Influence People was first published in the '30s, the work of a one-time travelling salesman named Dale Carnegie.

It must have spoken to a need, and it's still speaking to a need. It's a top-selling self-help title, with 30 million sales and counting. It has spawned copycats and spoofs, like the amusingly named memoir-turned-film, How To Lose Friends And Alienate People.

The book did not invent self-help or cheat sheets for living. The Stoics offered advice on achieving eudaimonia, a state usually translated as human flourishing. After them, Cicero wrote on friendship specifically. His tract On Friendship offered the notion that life is nothing without friends.

His top tips included: don't be friends with tyrants, don't look for buddies in politics, and whatever else you do, steer clear of flattery.

So is How To Win Friends a handbook for flattery? The author writes no. Carnegie is adamant he's against what he calls cheap flattery. And perhaps it's not flattery if you take on his main messages: be interested in other people, grant them importance, and practice the art of giving sincere appreciation.

Carnegie grew up on a hog farm in Missouri ("on the edge of Jesse James country," he writes). He was no relation to Andrew Carnegie, the filthy rich steel tycoon, though he changed his name from Carnegey to trade on the association, and later booked Carnegie Hall as a speaking venue.

He tried selling everything from soap to correspondence courses, then found his niche teaching public speaking to adults - initially, at the YMCA on 125th Street in New York. Unable to find a suitable text, he set about writing his own. It came out in 1936, in the eighth year of the Great Depression. Auto workers were out on strike in Flint, Michigan, and the severe dust storms that came to be known as the Dust Bowl were devastating the prairieland.

It's a little surprising, then, to find the book a jaunty read. That's down to the "brash, breezy Carnegie style", in the fitting words of the author's wife, Dorothy Carnegie. It can be read in a single sitting (or two or three if life intervenes).

Examples are drawn from the lives of great men of antiquity through to presidents and American everymen. There are stories of Abraham Lincoln and Robert E. Lee using diplomacy even in the midst of the Civil War. We get to read some cracking insults in unsent letters from Mark Twain (his wife lifted them from the post before they went off). And there are salesmen like Carnegie, hail-fellow-well-met types.

Is it "the only book you need to lead you to success"? That's the all-caps promise on the new edition's cover. Well, no. That would be absurd. But it has good tips for framing a sales pitch - or indeed any pitch. It has practical things to say about bringing people on board, even when (and this is tricky) you're contradicting them or wielding power in some way.

It's good advice for all, and it won't be lost on women. They know - and research shows - that they are judged more harshly when they fail to present as "nice".

Some of the examples aren't really recipes for success: they show already powerful people being courteous to others. That's more likely to be a correlative than a cause of their ascent to power. It isn't clear that good people skills will vault you to the top. In some sectors, sociopathy seems the surer route.

But that's to mistake, I think, what Carnegie is on about. His book is really about people and getting along with them. He quotes a magazine editor as saying that in reading a short story he could always tell whether the author liked people. "If the author doesn't like people," he said, "people won't like his or her stories."

You would think a book this old would be hopelessly out of date. The economy has changed. We're tech-happy and time-poor. You can't use these tips in dealings without a personal dimension, or where you're facing the template rules of an Apple or Amazon. It's still largely true, however, that things happen through people, and human drivers haven't changed in the last eight decades.

Towards the end, Carnegie turns to how to influence difficult people. Try giving them a good reputation to live up to, he says. Tell the sullen maid she has unlimited potential. Tell the misbehaving school kid he is a natural leader.

Diplomats know this trick. Henry Kissinger once said the best negotiator he encountered was the Egyptian President Anwar Sadat. Before talks started, Sadat would credit his opponent with a virtue that suited his agenda, like a supposedly well-known tradition of fairness. Kissinger said, "Sadat gave his opponents a reputation to live up to" - something they then did surprisingly often.

Is that flattery? Sure - but it might be all you have to work with.

The Nobel laureate Svetlana Alexievich tells a story that makes this point about influence in a dramatic way. It comes from Gafkhar Dzhurayeva, the director of a Moscow non-profit that assists Tajik workers, a group often on the receiving end of abuse. The organisation had scant resources, operating out of few small rooms.

One day, Dzhurayeva took a call from a young woman. She was calling from a car after having been picked up by a group of drunk policemen. They were driving her into the forest but she was able to give the licence plate.

Dzhurayeva called the police station. "Hello, my dear man, I've just been notified of a strange situation unfolding. Your boys are taking our girl somewhere she shouldn't be going, and they're drunk. We know their licence plate number."

The man at the other end swore at her, yet little by little the exchange turned into a conversation. Dzhurayeva relied on phrases like "my dear", "my good man", and "I have in faith in you". Words were all she had, but in this case they did the trick. Fifteen minutes later, the police turned the car around and brought the young woman back.

It's an old strategy that still works. Not always, of course, but there might be times when such techniques are all you've got in your arsenal, and the ability to persuade can make all the difference. W
direction: neutral to bearish for rate-sensitive sectors

headline: Cover-More investors question Travelex deal due diligence
intro: It has not taken long for Cover-More Group's chief executive officer Mike Emmett to face his first big test.
text:
It has not taken long for Cover-More Group's chief executive officer Mike Emmett to face his first big test.

Only two months since taking over at the travel insurance re-seller, Emmett is in front of his institutional shareholders seeking permission for a $100 million equity injection with the help of Macquarie Capital.

As revealed by Street Talk on Monday night, Emmett is believed to want the cash to help pay for UK-based Travelex Group's insurance business which would be the first major move by Emmett and Cover-More's first big deal since its ASX debut in December 2013.

However, sources said there was some push-back from Cover-More's biggest shareholders, who questioned management on whether they had done enough due diligence on the acquisition.

Cover-More had been hoping to announce the acquisition and launch the equity raising on Monday, although there was still no news as at Monday night.

Cover-More's team is expected to go back to their biggest shareholders with more details on the business and their due diligence regime.

Travellex is best known in the market as a currency exchange provider, however, the company also has a travel insurance arm. Cover-More - and Cover-More's underwriter Great Lakes Australia - has a relationship with Travelex in Australia, while the company also sells travel insurance policies offshore underwritten by the likes of Transamerica Casualty Insurance Company and Old Republic Insurance Company.

Cover-More shares were halted from trading on Monday and last changed hands at $1.44 each. The company has had a tough year, falling from above the $2 mark and only welcomed the new chief executive officer in July.

It'll be interesting to see whether the deal gets away.

The company has fallen into the sights of short-sellers in recent months, who have questioned its business model and following changes in material underwriting contracts.

Almost 10 per cent of the stock was reported short as at September 19, which is the most up-to-date data available from the Australian Securities and Investments Commission.

Elsewhere, and as first reported by Street Talk, private equity firm Ironbridge has sold its stake in Monash IVF Group in a block trade handled by Macquarie's equities desk.

It's understood Macquarie lined up a couple of institutional buyers on Monday, selling the 11 million shares for $2.355 each.

Monash IVF shares finished the day at $2.32.

It's understood the $26 million parcel of shares represented Ironbridge's final stake in the company.

The private equity firm owned a majority stake in the fertility company before listing it on the ASX at $1.85 a share in June 2014.
direction: Cover-More shares face downward pressure due to shareholder skepticism over due diligence and equity raising

headline: Stock watch
text:
Hot Copper

Shares in online stock forum Hot Copper closed 2.4 per cent lower after it announced a 10 per cent buyback of its own shares over the next year. The surprise move comes one month after it listed on the ASX at 20¢ a share. The stock has not materially moved from that price and the proposed share purchases would cost up to $2.2 million. The company "considers the buy back to be a prudent use of cash given the current and forecast strong cash flow generation of the business and the current prevailing share price." Hot Copper still has no managing director and Steve James remains the chairman. David Argyle owns 31.7 per cent of shares and Somers & Partners 46 per cent.
direction: downward pressure on Hot Copper shares

headline: Shaw in the black, despite climbing costs
intro: Shaw and Partners hasn't let a hiring drive weigh on its bottom line.
text:
Shaw and Partners hasn't let a hiring drive weigh on its bottom line.

The past 18 months have seen a string of changes at the Sydney-based firm, including a rebranding, changes to its constitution, taking on more advisers, improving technology and shaking up ownership.

But it appears the stockbroking and advisory firm has navigated the environment. Shaw remained in the black and posted a 17 per cent increase in net profit to $1.3 million for the 12 months ended June 30.

Dividends amounted to $900,000 over the year, up from $720,000 in the prior period, accounts lodged with the corporate regulator show. Revenue jumped to $62.8 million for the year from $49.7 million, buoyed by higher stockbroking, portfolio management and corporate finance revenue. Floats that Shaw helped manage include National Veterinary Care, Medlab Clinical and Shaver Shop Group.

Rivals were, however, questioning how Shaw would manage its accounts with a bigger cost base.

Over the year, the firm opened an Adelaide office and employed 35 new advisers. The net increase in advisers was 11 as some existing staff parted ways with Shaw. The accounts show that employee benefit costs climbed to $18.5 million from $13.6 million and consultancy fees (which reflect commissions paid to advisers) rose to $28.2 million from $22.8 million.

Much of the change at Shaw has been executed by former Macquarie Group executive Earl Evans who joined the firm in early 2015.

Evans became joint Shaw chief alongside Allan Zion in August, as revealed by Street Talk.

Rival Ord Minnett also posted a rise in net profit to $10.8 million last fiscal year. That is in contrast to Perth-based Patersons Securities, which remained in the red for a fifth straight year, and is undertaking cost cutting and restructuring in an attempt to stem losses.
direction: Bullish for Shaw and Partners shares

headline: Olympic Park office space sells for $80m
intro: Germany's Real I.S. Group has agreed to sell its office building leased to the Commonwealth Bank of Australia at Sydney Olympic Park for more than $80 million.
text:
Germany's Real I.S. Group has agreed to sell its office building leased to the Commonwealth Bank of Australia at Sydney Olympic Park for more than $80 million.

The property, at 10 Dawn Fraser Avenue, will be acquired by the private Sandran Property Group.

The Munich-based Real I.S. bought the building at the end of 2008 after the start of the global financial crisis and has achieved strong returns from it.

The sale was brokered on a passing yield of more than 10 per cent; reflective of the likelihood CBA will vacate the building in several years for the Australian Technology Park at Redfern.

It is understood the parties have agreed on the terms to sell but the transaction has not settled.

Vishant Narayan, managing director of Real I.S. Australia, declined to comment specifically on the sale.

"We cannot comment on any live deals but Real I.S. is a long-term investor in Australia and will consider options for exit if it is strongly aligned with our investors' interests," he said.

The building - and the changing nature of the wider Olympic Park precinct - now presents a different opportunity for private investors such as the new owner. What was a yield play for Real I.S has become a chance to add value for Sandran.

The Sydney Olympic Park Authority has a master plan, under which the area will be dramatically renewed and revitalised over the next few years.

The Sydney Olympic Park Master Plan 2030, approved by the NSW Department of Planning in 2010, has already delivered about 100,000 square metres of new commercial space and hundreds of new residences.

In one example, the NRMA is relocating 500 staff to a purpose-built centre at Murray Rose Drive, close to Real I.S's building.

The 10 Dawn Fraser Avenue property forms part of the 57,000 square metre business campus developed for CBA. The banking group is expected to shift to the new headquarters at the Australian Technology Park when it is completed in 2020.

But the NRMA's move demonstrates that, over the long term, as the precinct changes, there is interest from companies to be at Olympic Park. Another factor is the huge amount of residential development in Parramatta, forcing companies to look elsewhere in western Sydney for commercial office space.

The deal was negotiated by Colliers International's John McCann, James Girvan and Rob Gishen, who did not comment on the deal.
direction: bearish for office REITs and property trusts

headline: Cap rate compression working for TIX
intro: The acquisition of the Australian Industrial REIT portfolio has bolstered the full-year results of the Tony Pitt-managed 360 Industrial Fund (TIX).
text:
The acquisition of the Australian Industrial REIT portfolio has bolstered the full-year results of the Tony Pitt-managed 360 Industrial Fund (TIX).

Not only did the acquisition of the $330 million ANI portfolio help lift operating profit by 71 per cent to $48.2 million, in line with guidance, but it transformed the fund into the largest pure-play industrial trust on the ASX and increased its exposure to the Sydney market, the country's strongest-performing.

Delivering the annual results to analysts, fund manager James Storey said TIX was likely to also benefit from further cap rate compression, highlighting the recent acquisition by AMP Capital of a $250 million portfolio from JPMorgan Asset Management on a 6.5 per cent yield as setting a new benchmark.

"Cap rate compression is occurring across all asset classes but is particularly evident across industrial property," Mr Storey said.

During the past financial year, TIX booked a $38 million valuation uplift to its portfolio which is now worth more than $900 million, of which $400 million are assets in NSW.

Cap rates across the portfolio tightened by 50 basis points to 7.45 per cent.

Mr Storey said trading of TIX shares would also get a boost from its rising market value, with the fund targeting inclusion in the ASX 200 over the course of FY17.

The fund was a strong performer for unitholders, delivering a total return of almost 24 per cent and delivering a 7.9 per cent distribution yield paying out 21.6¢, up from 21.01¢.

Putting aside the benefits from the ANI acquisition, rental income rose 4 per cent over the reporting period for assets held for more than 12 months. Over 80 per cent of the portfolio is subject to fixed rent review averaging 3.3 per cent.

Around 10 per cent of the portfolio was leased during the year, or 68,000 square metres, with FY17 expiry falling to 13.7 per cent of the portfolio, down from 18.5 per cent.

During the year the fund renegotiated its syndicated debt facility to $420 million and repaid ANI's debt facility of $130 million.

Mr Storey said the fund had an indicative offer from an overseas institution for a $330 million, 10-year debt facility.

Were it to take up such a facility, this would result in an increase in the average weighted debt term from 1.7 years to 8.2 years.

The fund will look to sell around $50 million worth of assets and is targeting gearing of below 40 per cent.
direction: bullish for industrial REITs and TIX shares

headline: Santos needs gas, not hot air
intro: The potential that Santos boss Kevin Gallagher might celebrate his first week in office by reopening an asset sale process abandoned by his board less than three months ago is nothing less than mind-bending.
text:
The potential that Santos boss Kevin Gallagher might celebrate his first week in office by reopening an asset sale process abandoned by his board less than three months ago is nothing less than mind-bending.

Nonetheless, there is broadsheet speculation claiming knowledge that Santos has revived a sale process for, at least, a 13.5 per cent stake in PNG LNG.

For the record, company insiders say this is just not the case. "Fanciful" was one of the words used. And at any other company this sort of instant redirection of confirmed strategy by incoming management would sit very unlikely indeed. But this is Santos, and it would be fair to say stranger things have happened at South Australia's oil and gas company over very recent times.

Certainly, there is a view that Gallagher has arrived both well prepared and with a firm agenda for recovery. It is understood he has spent the past few months in unofficial discussion with executive chairman Peter Coates and the Santos senior leadership team.

And if the debt-heavy LNG punter that was recently downgraded by the rating agencies was going to sell anything, the PNG investment would be the juiciest peach in the pile. PNG LNG was, after all, what Woodside was shopping for in the rejected and then abandoned $11.6 billion merger-of-equals proposal it offered Oil Search.

For the record, the Santos view was that the Woodside bid generated a look-through value of better than $5 billion for the South Australian's share of a PNG project that sits as the region's cheaper LNG producer.

But apparently no one else quite saw it that way.

So Santos retained that investment and the $2.5 billion of non-recourse debt it supports. Ultimately the board decided to retain pretty much anything that could be described as strategic or that generated positive cash flow and, instead, infamously opted for a $2.5 billion rights issue that crushed confidence in the board and senior management of long standing.

Trust in the Santos board had already been deeply challenged by its refusal to engage with private equity from the oil kingdoms of Brunei and the UAE that come knocking with a $7 billion takeover approach that conditionally proposed a $6.88 a share offer.

Subsequent events appear to prove that rejection ill-advised and based on a very ambitious view of value.

But the killer blow for many was the decision to go with a rights issue that would have once been welcomed. Indeed, well before the oil price began its long tank in November 2014, Santos investors had consistently invited management to relieve the building debt pressure on their balance sheet by making a rights issue. Back then the price was nearly $10 a share.

But right up until November's change of heart, the board and its executives past and present stuck firmly to a public view that said a dilutive rights issue would not be indulged.

As a result, through the months of a strategic review run by briefly executive chairman Peter Coates, the market was banking on the idea that a recapitalisation would be internally funded by asset sales rather than by reaching out to existing owners.

This confidence, and the opportune arrival of Scepter, saw Santos shares rise from a September low of $3.98 a share to a post-takeover-approach high of $6.42 in October. Santos shares were still circling the $5.50-a-share level when the results of Project Solo were confirmed. Ever since, the road had been slippery. The price slipped under $2.50 a share in mid-January and is now changing hands at a squeak over $3.

To refresh, the rights issue was made at $3.85 a share, which represented a 35 per cent discount to the last price before the issue was confirmed and a 25 per cent discount to the theoretical post-rights price of $5.15. Plainly that number proved very theoretical indeed.

In the end there are likely three powerful reasons why Gallagher will not be freed to deal with still-building balance-sheet pressure through asset sales. First, even a board as deft at the back-flip with pike as the crew at Santos, it would be pretty hard to justify such a prompt and material change of heart without a coincident broad-based renewal of personnel.

There is reason to imagine that there will be change at the Santos board through 2016. Three of its longest-standing directors are due to face election this year and the word is that all of them might be contemplating retirement. But it is suggested Coates is staying and for him to countenance asset sales after the dilution he oversaw last November would seem to be a hurdle too far. A second reason that asset sales seem unlikely is that projects like PNG LNG represent the future. The returns the project will generate over the immediate term are likely to sit crucial to Santos' hopes of just sustaining cash flows through these dangerous times.

And then there is the fact that the oil price looks an even more uncertain prospect right now than it did through the back end of 2015. The trillion-dollar question in oil is whether the price has been smashed by structural or tactical change.

One very senior oil executive told me last week that he had retreated from the acquisition market because he could no longer confidently value oil and gas assets. His view was that some level of true pricing might become apparent through the fourth quarter of 2016 and that, serious opportunity aside, he would sit on his inorganic growth plans until 2017.

Interestingly, some who wandered through the Santos data rooms through the rapid-fire Project Solo process have told us that they left with firm interest in some of the periphery assets but deep doubts about the legacy businesses. Others like, say, Shell left confident about the whole but with no interest in pursuing the sum of the parts because attention was required elsewhere.

A common concern of many that kicked the Santos tyres is that it will rely so heavily on third-party gas to fill the second LNG train that is being commissioned at its Gladstone project, and that is expected to ship is first export gas some time in the second quarter.

That Santos is long sunk investment ($US20 billion) on its Gladstone export platform but short the gas to keep the chillers full for 20 years sits arguably the biggest structural problem ahead of Gallagher.

Santos has long said that it could make money at Gladstone at an oil price of $US45 a barrel. Brent currently sits at $US35.30 a barrel and that is after three weeks of stuttering recovery in the index that sets LNG prices. So it would be fair to suggest, I think, that Santos' Gladstone footprint is struggling to generate free cash flow right now. So why introduce more gas when you are not generating even cash, let alone a profit, from the tonnes you are currently producing.

One competitor suggested that if oil prices do not start trending back through the $US50 a barrel range to the $UD65 a barrel that Shell and others now imagine is the long-run price, then Santos should arguably scrap the second train altogether.

This logic is informed, in part, by Santos' resources issue. The production characteristics of coal-seam gas are the same as shale. You need to keep drilling new wells just to sustain existing production. You need to drill a whole lot more to increase it. So the unconventional producers need to generate free cash flow to just stay in business.

If Santos is not sustaining its drilling program then it will need to acquire ever more third-party gas. To get access to new and deep gas pools it will need to pay an incentive price to bring supplies to market. That means likely paying higher prices than competitors who own supplies enough to feed their own machines. At that point the cycle of pressure on margins and cash flow becomes painfully self-sustaining. Working out how to break that uncomfortable nexus should oil's depression continue into 2017 sits Gallagher's first and biggest challenge.
direction: downward pressure on Santos shares due to debt concerns and strategic uncertainty

headline: Credit Suisse buys Perth tower for $64m
intro: Credit Suisse Real Estate Investment Management has bought a Perth office tower in an off-market counter-cyclical play for $64.2 million. The deal, Perth's first major commercial property transaction of the year, demonstrates there is still investor interest for well-leased prime property despite a challenging office leasing market.
text:
Credit Suisse Real Estate Investment Management has bought a Perth office tower in an off-market counter-cyclical play for $64.2 million. The deal, Perth's first major commercial property transaction of the year, demonstrates there is still investor interest for well-leased prime property despite a challenging office leasing market.

The vendor of the premium office building at 190 St Georges Terrace was Terrace Properties.

CBRE's Andrew Woodley-Page, in conjunction with CBRE's International Investments team of Mark Hansen and Rick Butler, negotiated the off-market sale of the asset to Credit Suisse Real Estate Investment Management (REIM).

The deal was struck on a yield of 9 per cent, it is understood.

"Boasting an occupancy rate of 95 per cent, the asset offered an opportunity to acquire a secure investment in one of Perth's most sought after locations," Mr Woodley-Page said.

The deal follows the latest Property Council of Australia figures, which put Perth's office vacancy rate at well over 20 per cent.

"Various groups are recognising the counter-cyclical opportunities the Perth CBD office market affords, we expect more sales to be announced," Mr Butler said.

The St Georges Terrace asset is located close to new developments such as Elizabeth Quay and Perth City Link. The A-Grade tower comprises a 9362-sq-m office space over 12 levels. It is mostly filled with small and medium sized tenants, but is anchored by HSBC. The weighted average lease expiry exceeds four years.

Credit Suisse REIM is well accustomed to Perth's market, having repositioned the nearby 45 St Georges Terrace, which is now fully leased, Mr Hansen said.

"This sale is further testament to the strength of the Australian investment market and demonstrates that sophisticated investors are actively targeting Perth," Mr Hansen said.

There have been a number of other counter-cyclical plays in Perth's property market over the last several months.

An offshore investor associated with Singapore's ARA is expected to shortly finalise a deal to buy an office tower at 81 St Georges Terrace.

The remaining office assets in the Insurance Commission of Western Australia portfolio may also sell this year. These include the Forrest Centre at 219 and 221 St Georges Terrace, which is under negotiation with an offshore Chinese group, it is understood. Plans to sell a stake in Exchange Tower, which is co-owned by AMP and the unlisted Private Property Syndicate, also remain in play. But ICWA withdrew 167 and 141 St Georges from sale as offers were below valuation levels.
direction: supportive for prime commercial property assets

text:
Quick bites

Thinking about the summer holidays? If you're aiming for the pointy end of the plane, you'll be paying a bit more. CommSec reports a 5 per cent increase in the price of business class tickets since a year ago. It's cheaper in economy in more ways than one - these airfares are up just 2.2 per cent on a year ago.
direction: bearish for airlines, neutral for broader market

headline: RBA cuts, dismissing property bubble risk
intro: The Reserve Bank of Australia has declared the threat of a property bubble is over after cutting the official cash rate to an unprecedented low to shield the dollar against a wave of global monetary policy stimulus and money printing.
text:
The Reserve Bank of Australia has declared the threat of a property bubble is over after cutting the official cash rate to an unprecedented low to shield the dollar against a wave of global monetary policy stimulus and money printing.

Warning of fresh weakness in China, the local unemployment market and a moribund inflation outlook, the Reserve Bank cut the official cash rate to 1.5 per cent from 1.75 per cent.

Almost immediately speculation emerged over whether another reduction may be likely in coming months after three of the nation's biggest lenders - Commonwealth Bank of Australia, National Australia Bank and ANZ Bank - passed on to mortgage holders no more than half the cut. Westpac cut its home loan rate by 14 basis points.

As their failure to lower mortgage rates by the full amount triggered a fresh political battle, with shadow treasurer Chris Bowen slamming the government for going soft on banks, there was some reprieve for long-suffering savers.

All of the big four banks took the unusual step of simultaneously hiking short-term deposit rates by between 55 basis points and 85 basis points.

Financial markets are already pricing in a 52 per cent chance the cash rate will fall to 1.25 per cent by November. The meeting coincided with the benchmark 10-year bond yield tumbling to a record low 1.81 per cent, which is the level reached by the equivalent US bond in May.

The currency, which is increasingly the central bank's key focus, initially fell by around a quarter of a US cent after the decision, before strengthening by the end of the day to US75.54¢ from its open on Tuesday at US75.36¢.

The lack of a dramatic post-meeting sell-off in the Australian dollar supports the views of analysts who warned the dollar could have surged towards US80¢ had the Reserve Bank kept the cash rate on hold, not least because the US Federal Reserve is increasingly unlikely to hike in the near term.

Reserve Bank governor Glenn Stevens said in a statement after his penultimate board meeting that "the likelihood of lower interest rates exacerbating risks in the housing market has diminished".

The remark is the first official signal that the bank is almost certain it has avoided inflating a house price bubble with its 3.25 percentage points of reductions since it began the current easing cycle in late 2011, as the terms of trade peaked.

In that time, the central bank and other regulators have been forced to become increasingly active in the mortgage market, leaning on lenders to curb risky borrowing and adding to compulsory capital reserves.

"Supervisory measures have strengthened lending standards in the housing market," Mr Stevens said. "A number of lenders are also taking a more cautious attitude to lending in certain segments."

Mr Stevens emphasised that house prices have been rising "only modestly" over the past year, with a surge in supply of apartments due to hit the market in coming years, particularly in Brisbane, Sydney and Brisbane, adding to supply and pushing down prices.

The governor's assessment on the state of the property market comes around 18 months after fellow board member and Treasury secretary John Fraser warned that parts of Sydney was in the grip of a housing price bubble.

The central bank again noted that inflation - which came in at an annual 1 per cent in the June quarter, the weakest in 17 years - is likely to stay low for some time "given very subdued growth in labour costs and very low cost pressures elsewhere in the world."

Reserve Bank economists will update their inflation and growth forecasts on Friday from three months ago, when they anticipated inflation would remain below the bottom of their 2-3 per cent range until 2018, a prediction that assumed at least one more rate cut.

"The RBA does, in our view, appear to be reluctant cutters," said CBA chief economist Michael Workman. "Inflation trends are unusually low and are forcing more rate cuts, just to keep 'real' rates stable near zero".

"Another rate cut at the end of the year is also likely ... it's about the lack of wages and inflationary pressures."

Treasurer Scott Morrison rejected suggestions a cash rate of 1.5 per cent reflects a struggling economy, but added that signs of slower growth in China and falling business investment in Australia underscore the need for economic reforms such as corporate tax cuts.
direction: bearish for banks and property, bullish for miners and exporters

headline: Crossbench not sold on case for reform
intro: Passage of the Turnbull government's changes to media ownership regulation is under pressure, with just two crossbench senators committing their support for the proposed reform.
text:
Passage of the Turnbull government's changes to media ownership regulation is under pressure, with just two crossbench senators committing their support for the proposed reform.

West Australian senator Dio Wang and NSW senator David Leyonhjelm are expected to vote in favour of the reforms brought forward by Communications Minister Mitch Fifield last week to remove the reach rule, preventing networks from broadcasting to more than 75 per cent of the population, and the two-out-of-three rule, which prevents media companies from owning a TV station, radio network and newspaper in the same market.

Six of the eight crossbench senators have not decided whether they will pledge their support to the bill in its current form.

While Labor supports the removal of the reach rule, the two-out-of-three rule is more controversial.

WA senator and deputy leader of the Greens, Scott Ludlam, said removing the two rules was complicated. He was frustrated by the new bill because its focus was too narrow and limited the debate. "This isn't reform. This is tweaking and quite likely counterproductive tweaking of a really complex picture," Senator Ludlam said.

The bill has been referred to the Senate Standing Committee on Environment and Communications and will report its findings by May 12.

Senator Fifield has expressed his desire to push through the reform package in one piece rather than splitting the bill. Should the government not win support from Labor or the Greens, it will need six crossbenchers to vote in favour of the changes to achieve a majority.

South Australian senator Bob Day, who has been in favour of deregulation, said he was making no commitments in light of the government's proposed Senate voting reform.

"Prior to the 22nd of February, I voted on principle for every bill and voted 90 per cent of the time with the government," Mr Day said. "Then they threw principle out the window when they told the crossbench to leave the Senate. So my position is reserved."

SA senator Nick Xenophon said media ownership laws are outdated, but he will take part in the inquiry process before making any decisions on the proposed reforms.

He also expressed concerns about protecting local content. Senator Xenophon previously introduced legislation for a set amount of local content on regional networks after WIN Corporation cut news bulletins in south-east SA and the Riverland in 2013.

Senator Xenophon also said he would like to see licence fees slashed and local television producers given more generous tax offsets. He said the revenue shortfall could be obtained by ensuring technology giants such as Netflix, Google and Apple were paying their fair share of tax.

The government has said TV and radio licence fees will be examined in the context of the federal budget.

Lower barriers for entry into media and taxation on companies operating in Australia was highlighted by Queensland senator Glenn Lazarus.

Senator Lazarus does not have a definitive position on the media reform bill, but supports changes in principle, saying the current laws do not embrace the role of the internet.

Tasmanian senator Jacqui Lambie and Victorian senators John Madigan and Ricky Muir are considering their positions on media reform.
direction: bearish for media sector shares

headline: Coalition turns up the heat on budget repair
text:
Treasurer Scott Morrison has ruled a line under granting further concessions on the proposed superannuation crackdown, saying those advocating weakening the budget proposals were failing to come up with alternative revenue measures.

And Finance Minister Mathias Cormann has used a pre-positioning speech ahead of the start of Parliament next week to tell Labor it could win the next election if it did the right thing over budget repair.

Mr Morrison, who is locked in negotiations with the backbench, said he could not face his own children if he kept caving in to demands to go easy on wealthy superannuants.

"We can't just keep saying 'oh well, we will make a concession here we will make a concession there and make it up somewhere else'," he said.

"If someone wants to put a million bucks after tax into their super so they can pay 15 per cent on it rather than their marginal tax rate and they had already cleared the $1.6 million then I would find it pretty hard to look my kids in the eye and tell them they have got to saddle a higher debt because someone who had a very big income wanted to pay less tax."

Mr Morrison faces demands from colleagues who now want the proposed lifetime limit on non-concessional contributions lifted from $500,000 to $1 million. Last week he put on the table either lifting it to $750,000 and keeping it backdated to 2007, or making the $500,000 prospective.

He is frustrated because those pushing for change are not coming up with matching savings.

"Everyone always says they will find the savings somewhere else but they only even start thinking about it once the money is spent and the savings don't turn up," he said.

Senator Cormann told the Sydney Institute on Monday night that Labor's election campaign came unstuck when it conceded its policies would leave the budget bottom line $16.5 billion worse off over the four-year budget period. Senator Cormann said if Labor persisted with the same approach, it would find itself in the same position at the next election and could not rely on another Medicare scare campaign to save it.

"While it may have been good politics for Bill Shorten along the way, at the conclusion of the last term of Parliament it got him into a real fiscal pickle during the election campaign proper," Senator Cormann said.

"Ultimately that cost him the votes he needed to win."

Senator Cormann pointed out that during the last campaign, Labor had to make a series of embarrassing backdowns on such policies as the schoolkids bonus and changes to the pension.

"Bill Shorten went into the last election campaign unprepared for the important task of responsibly managing our economy and our public finances. That is why he lost," he said.

Senator Cormann said Mr Shorten must choose whether to hide behind the Senate crossbench or step up to the plate in budget repair.

"Or will he be like jelly on that plate, the wibble wobble wibble wobble jelly on a plate, first opposing, then supporting, then not knowing what to do?"

The Coalition's internal unrest on superannuation is undermining its budget campaign against Labor.

Next week it will present Labor with a bill containing $6.5 billion in cuts Labor said it would support either directly or in kind.

The largest measure, worth $1.34 billion, involves removing carbon tax compensation from payments to future welfare recipients.

Key pointsTreasurer draws the line on changes to superannuation treatment.

Finance Minister says Labor needs to back budget repair to win the next election.
direction: bearish for superannuation-focused financials and equities

headline: Ocado investors await something beginning with B
subhead: Daily selection from THE LEX COLUMN
intro: Jeff Bezos' famous maxim - "get big fast" - has swiftly become conventional wisdom for online retailers. Online distribution is only efficient, he and others have argued, when there is massive throughput. So at companies from Amazon to Zalando, the land grab has come first, with profits to flow later. The snags for investors are obvious: a long wait for profits and no guarantee they will materialise at all.
text:
Jeff Bezos' famous maxim - "get big fast" - has swiftly become conventional wisdom for online retailers. Online distribution is only efficient, he and others have argued, when there is massive throughput. So at companies from Amazon to Zalando, the land grab has come first, with profits to flow later. The snags for investors are obvious: a long wait for profits and no guarantee they will materialise at all.

Investors in Ocado, a British online grocery store, face just such a conundrum. In 2013, Ocado bought them some time by agreeing a deal to fulfil deliveries for Wm Morrison, a struggling British supermarket chain. Ocado shares jumped on Tuesday after reports that it was close to agreeing a similar partnership with Amazon. The companies declined to comment.

Such chatter is nothing new; the company had led investors to expect such a major partnership by the end of 2015. That nothing has yet been announced helps explain why the shares were at year lows.

Investors might prefer it if the "B" stood for bought rather than big. The main rationale for Sainsbury's proposed acquisition of Home Retail is that the latter's superior logistics will accelerate Sainsbury's' growth in non-food. Similar arguments could apply to Amazon, which for all its logistical prowess is ill-equipped to fulfil large multi-item, temperature-controlled deliveries in tight-time slots.

This is Ocado's specialty. Its dedicated fulfilment centres are far more efficient than in-store picking or the "dark stores" used by conventional supermarkets. It has turned profitable (even stripping out the Morrison contract) and it is still winning customers, partly by diversifying beyond its core customer base of middle-class southerners.

Yet it will need to grow much more before the benefits of scale really kick in and profitability takes off. Taking share in the viciously competitive British grocery market is hard; its last trading statement showed average basket value decreasing slightly.

Sales are still growing, but at a slowing rate, and margins are also contracting. Expanding abroad would be expensive and demanding. Serving as a technology provider to another big retailer would certainly deliver returns quicker. Being bought by one would accelerate them still further.
direction: Ocado shares likely to remain under pressure or volatile

headline: China imports more gas, iron ore at lower prices
intro: Shanghai | China is taking advantage of lower commodity prices to increase imports of Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) and iron ore, while politically sensitive exports of steel rose again in July.
text:
Shanghai | China is taking advantage of lower commodity prices to increase imports of Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) and iron ore, while politically sensitive exports of steel rose again in July.

Figures released on Monday showed China's overall trade picture declined last month, with both imports and exports coming in worse than the market had expected.

Imports declined by 12.5 per cent in US dollar terms in July compared with the same period last year, while exports were 4.4 per cent lower.

The fall in commodity prices partly explains the lower import figure, but for Australia this decline is being partly offset by higher volumes.

LNG has been the best performer with overall Chinese imports rising 20 per cent to 30.5 million tonnes over the first seven months of the year. Despite the pick-up in volume the price paid for these cargoes by Chinese entities was down by 20 per cent over the period.

The spot price for LNG in Asia has fallen 25 per cent this year to around $US5.60 per million British thermal unit. This is down from prices as high as $US15.

The Chinese are seen as opportunistic buyers of LNG during periods of depressed prices and have largely avoided entering into high-priced long term contracts.

Iron ore volumes have been equally strong over the first seven months of the year, rising 8 per cent to 582 million tonnes. In July, China imported 88.4 million tonnes.

The Customs Bureau does not break down where these shipments have come from, but Beijing has separately said Australian companies supply around two-thirds of its imported iron ore. "Iron ore prices have been well supported by rising steel prices in China in recent months," said Daniel Hynes, a senior commodity strategist at ANZ.

He said bad weather in parts of China had disrupted local miners and made steel mills more reliant on imported iron ore.

"However, prices [of iron ore] over $US60 a tonne could see domestic supply rebound, which could place some downward pressure on imports," Mr Hynes said.

China's coal imports dropped back 2.5 per cent in July after kicking higher in June.

There is also no let-up in China's politically sensitive steel exports, which have been blamed on mill closures in Australia, Britain and the United States.

Over the first seven months of the year China exported 67.4 million tonnes of steel, up 8.5 per cent from the same period last year.

In July steel exports were 10.3 million tonnes.

China's overall imports have now declined for 21 straight months, while exports have fallen for 12 of 13 months, helping to drag economic growth to its slowest in a quarter of a century.

"Signs of stronger manufacturing activity among many of China's key trading partners has so far failed to lift export growth," Capital Economics' China economist Julian Evans-Pritchard said in a note. "The country's export growth is likely to remain subdued for some time."
direction: bearish for Australian miners and exporters due to weak trade data and lower commodity prices

headline: A lifetime in fragrance
intro: The relationship I have with fragrance is rather like one that you have with your best friend.Jo Malone
text:
Jo Malone's autobiography literally exudes evidence of her perfume-making talents, writes Rachel Felder.

As the founder of two fragrance brands, Jo Malone has been immutably associated with perfume for a couple of decades. So it's not surprising that the print editions of her autobiography, Jo Malone: My Story, published this month in Australia by Simon & Schuster, have a fragrant twist: a page near the front is treated with Pomelo, a crisp, citrus-based scent from her Jo Loves collection, which will waft softly upwards to the reader.

The book recounts her life through a business lens, from helping her father sell paintings at a market stall at the age of eight to the founding of the Jo Malone brand and, recently, Jo Loves. She also talks about difficult times, such as being estranged from her mother and overcoming breast cancer, topics she refers to with an unflustered air.

"What I want this book to be about is the reinvention of yourself, that nothing is wasted in our life, that every single thing that happens in our life can come out for the good to build you," say Malone, 53, by telephone from her office in the Chelsea neighbourhood of London. "I really want this story to resonate and to be for people to say, 'I can build my business'."

The business she is best known for, Jo Malone London, is now owned by Estee Lauder. She sold it for an undisclosed amount in 1999, staying on as the creative director until 2006. In 2011, she introduced Jo Loves.

"The relationship I have with fragrance is rather like one that you have with your best friend," Malone says. "When you don't see your best friend for a while, you really miss them. And those five years of not creating fragrance was one of the most miserable times of my life. I just didn't know who I was. Deciding to come back and do it again was my only option."

Like Jo Malone London, Jo Loves includes perfumes and ancillary products such as scented candles and aromatic body moisturisers. But the two brands wouldn't be mistaken for one another: The original line now includes 32 fragrances and is widely available worldwide, while Jo Loves sells 12 eau de toilette scents and other items in limited distribution.

But they do share some distinguishing traits: sleek and simple bottles with unisex appeal; deliberately direct fragrance names (such as, in the case of Jo Loves, Fresh Sweet Peas and Smoked Plum & Leather, the collection's newest offering); and scents that are straightforward but often unexpected, with a quirky combination of notes that may not seem, on first whiff, obviously "perfumey".

"The similarity is that they both have the same mother," Malone says. "They both have that person that wants to push boundaries and say, 'I know it's always been like that, but what if?' I live a life of, 'What if?"'

Her venture is growing. In July, Net-a-Porter began carrying a concise selection of Jo Loves items; last month, Emirates airline added the Pomelo scent to its in-flight duty-free selection.

The brand also has a boutique on Elizabeth Street in Belgravia, London, where neighbours include an outpost of the Parisian bakery Poilâne and a shop showcasing statement hats by Philip Treacy. Malone lives nearby with her husband, Gary Willcox, with whom she owns the company, and their son, Josh, 15.

Many industry figures credit Malone's first brand with helping to pave the way for other niche fragrances and their eventual sale to bigger businesses. For example, Estee Lauder now also owns Le Labo, Editions de Parfums Frédéric Malle and By Kilian; L'Oreal acquired Atelier Cologne this year. Manzanita Capital, a private equity group in London, has invested in Byredo and Diptyque.

"She was one of the pioneers of what I think of as the braggart indie set, for the woman who didn't want to smell like any other woman in the room," says Jane Larkworthy, the executive beauty director of W magazine. "You didn't smell anything cloying. You just smelled clean and curious, and the curious part is that you were like, 'What is that?' "

Unlike many other perfumers, Malone had no formal training. While working as a facialist in London, she developed a scented lotion and body oil to give her most loyal clients; the fragrance became Nutmeg & Ginger, the first Jo Malone scent.

"That whole homegrown, homespun thing is really what created her empire," says Elizabeth Musmanno, the president of the United States division of the Fragrance Foundation, a perfume industry organisation. "She was naive and open-minded and flexible, like a true creative could be. If you aren't taught so much, you're just allowed to work differently."

While her name is highly recognisable to perfume lovers, Malone seems to be, at her core, not all that different from the

scrappy little girl who helped bring in customers to her father's outdoor booth

on cold British mornings.

"She's so down to earth," says Sophie Bottwood, the senior merchandiser for beauty and grooming at Net-a-Porter. "She speaks so passionately about her brand that you almost forget her history - that she's Jo Malone - because she is so involved in what she's doing." L&L

THE NEW YORK TIMES

Jo Malone: My Story by Jo Malone,

Simon & Schuster.
direction: neutral to slightly bearish for consumer discretionary stocks

headline: Zero rates would smash the banks
intro: Investors in Australia's major banks have been asking two big questions in recent times: What impact will the lower cash rate environment have on bank margins, a key driver of their earnings? And how low does return on equity need to fall before banks will be forced to cut back their dividends?
text:
