Investors in Australia's major banks have been asking two big questions in recent times: What impact will the lower cash rate environment have on bank margins, a key driver of their earnings? And how low does return on equity need to fall before banks will be forced to cut back their dividends?

Credit Suisse banking analysts Jarrod Martin and James Ellis have provided a verdict on both questions in two research reports published on consecutive days this week. Their answers?

First, if the cash rate fell to zero, major bank earnings would plunge by an average of 9 per cent - or $2.7 billion, based on the forecast for $30 billion of earnings by the big four banks in 2016.

Second, if return on equity (ROE) fell to 12.1 per cent (from its current average level of 14.4 per cent), the major banks would be consuming more capital than they are generating. This would be unsustainable and pressure the banks to slice their dividend payout ratios.

When the banks provided earnings updates to the market last month, they revealed net interest margins - the difference between what they receive from borrowers and pay out to depositors - are contracting. This is partly a function of the Reserve Bank of Australia's cuts to the official cash rate; because deposit interest rates are already so low, banks have not been able to reduce them by as much as cuts to mortgage rates, crimping the margin.

The earnings - and share prices - of banks in Europe have been hit hard as rates there have gone to zero. Such a setting seems less likely in Australia, especially after the strong GDP data released on Tuesday. But the market is still factoring in a 46 per cent chance of a further rate cut by the end of the year.

Mr Martin and Mr Ellis pointed out that falling interest rates can be good for banks, because they reduce the burden on customers repaying loans, keeping a lid on provisions for bad debts. Low rates can also help to spur credit growth, bolstering bank earnings.

However, the analysts said the negative impacts on margins of interest rates approaching zero are more significant. "We believe negative primary earnings impacts become more pronounced with each incremental step towards the zero bound," they said.

If the RBA did cut the cash rate to zero, banks could add 18 per cent to earnings by reducing term deposit pricing. But earnings from standard variable mortgages would fall by 41 per cent, as banks received less from borrowers. On balance, a zero cash rate would reduce National Australia Bank's annual earnings by 3 per cent, Westpac's by 9 per cent, CBA's by 10 per cent and ANZ's by 13 per cent, for an average of 9 per cent.

Last month's bank earnings reports also revealed that return on equity remains under pressure. That is a function of pressure on the net interest margins and bad debts coming off their cyclical lows. It is also a function of the de-leveraging imposed on the banks by the financial system inquiry, which forced them to raise billions of dollars in fresh equity in 2015.

Major bank ROE, which reflects their ability to generate capital, has declined from 20.7 per cent in 2007 to 14.4 per cent in 2016. Mr Martin and Mr Ellis said if this fell to 12.1 per cent - a level they describe as the ROE "tipping point" - then "banks are no longer self-funding their capital dynamics".

In order to maintain dividends, banks would need to find productivity savings. Or, they would have to push higher prices onto their customers. But "repricing" has become "politically constrained", the analysts said.
direction: downward pressure on major bank shares

subhead: MARKET MAYHEM IT CRASH HALTS ASX TRADING BEHIND THE $9.7b PORT SELL-OFF VERDICT ON THE IPHONE 7
direction: downward pressure on rate-sensitive sectors such as banks and REITs

headline: PM lifts refugee intake by one third indefinitely
text:
New York | Malcolm Turnbull has boosted his government's humanitarian refugee program as Britain began embracing elements of Australia's stance, which combines a hardline approach with compassion.

And German Chancellor Angela Merkel, who precipitated an uncontrolled surge of refugees across Europe last year with her open-ended invitation to Syrians, expressed regret after her Christian Democratic party suffered another local election defeat at the hands of right-wing, anti-migrant party Alternative for Germany. "If I could, I would turn back time for many, many years, to prepare better," she said of her comments, which led to a million refugees arriving in her country.

At the United Nations in New York early on Wednesday AEST, Australia was one of a handful of countries invited to a special summit on refugees hosted by US President Barack Obama.

Mr Turnbull used the occasion to announce the policy changes and repeat the message he has brought to New York - a government that fails to control its borders faces political destabilisation and will never win public support to take in refugees in a controlled manner, something critical with 65 million displaced people worldwide.

He told the summit Australia would make permanent a new annual humanitarian intake of 18,750 refugees, up from 13,750. His predecessor Tony Abbott announced just over a year ago the increased intake would take effect from 2018-19, but it was never guaranteed beyond that.

Mr Turnbull also promised another $130 million over the next three years to support assistance to refugees, forcibly displaced communities and host countries. And he pledged Australia would participate in the US-led program to resettle Central American refugees currently in a resettlement centre in Costa Rica.

While the measures are modest, the permanent increased intake makes Australia one of the most generous countries on a per capita basis.

But Mr Turnbull said these measures, plus Mr Abbott's pledge to take an additional one-off quota of 12,000 Syrian refugees, would not have happened if boat people were still arriving.

"Strong borders are not just about security. They are crucial to ensuring social harmony and public support for migration domestically," he said.

"Australia is a prime example; securing our borders has increased public confidence and enabled Australia to have one of the world's most generous humanitarian regimes. Australians support these actions because they have confidence that our migration system is well managed."

Opposition Leader Bill Shorten said Mr Turnbull should stop lecturing the world and clear out the camps at Manus Island and Nauru. "They need to explain to not just the rest of the world but to Australians what's really going on in Manus and Nauru, and when are they going to get those people out of indefinite detention and make regional resettlement a reality?"

Public anger over uncontrolled migration fuelled Britain's Brexit vote. At a refugee and migrant summit at the UN on Tuesday, British PM Theresa May echoed the Australian approach.

Mrs May argued better control of "unprecedented levels of population movement" ensured that public confidence in "the economic benefits of legal and controlled migration" was maintained. She called for the UN to enshrine the principal that asylum seekers should be processed as close to their home country as is safely possible.

Mrs May resisted calls to create more legal ways for asylum seekers to enter Britain by defending the nation's right to control its borders as part of a more co-ordinated response to what she described as an international "crisis".
direction: neutral to slightly bearish for rate-sensitive sectors

headline: Peer to peer lending hits puberty
intro: The eye-popping climb in the number of people attending the LendIt USA conference over the past four years reflects a real coming of age for peer-to-peer lending.
text:
The eye-popping climb in the number of people attending the LendIt USA conference over the past four years reflects a real coming of age for peer-to-peer lending.

The first, in 2013, attracted 300 delegates. The second lured in just under 1000, while 2500 attended in 2015. Last week, the number swarming the Marriott Marquis hotel in San Francisco was nudging 4000.

Peer-to-peer lending (also known as "marketplace lending" to reflect the large proportion of funds that come from institutions rather than retail investors) matches borrowers with yield-hungry investors on an internet platform.

A decade since it was born in the United States, marketplace lending is now mainstream. In the past 12 months, 24 per cent of all personal loans in the US were provided on a P2P platform, according to Lending Club, the leading player in the US market.

Lending Club was the largest provider of personal loans in the US last year: it facilitated loans of $US8.4 billion ($10.8 billion) - more than any single US bank. Lending Club has 1.4 million members (defined as borrowers and investors on the platform), and across the US, 2.5 million people are using marketplace lending platforms. P2P lending is also surging in Europe and in China. Volumes are edging higher in Australia, too. The leading player, SocietyOne, said last week it had crossed the symbolic milestone of $100 million in loans matched on its platform. This followed a surge in activity over the first quarter of this calendar year, when new loan growth for personal and agribusiness loans jumped by $30 million.

SocietyOne co-founder Greg Symons was in San Francisco for LendIt last week and says the vibe there reflects an industry that is fast maturing. While venture capital investors still hosted plush parties for the big platforms - the hottest invitation was to Victory Park Capital's exclusive shindig for 200 people in the San Francisco Press Club - Symons says the mood reflects the new reality in Silicon Valley, where financiers are much more discerning about business models. They want to see fintechs with a clear path towards profitability.

This is a stark contrast to LendIt USA 2015, which was held soon after the initial public offerings of Lending Club and OnDeck, an online business lender. Talk last year was all about breakneck growth, as platforms clambered for "unicorn" status.

"There were a lot of people drinking the Kool-Aid in 2015, and there was a big vibe on valuations," Symons says. "VC (venture capital) money was flowing and lots of investors were walking the floor at LendIt looking for opportunities to invest in these platforms. That was reasonably worrying to me. Financial services and tech is a very long game. So it has been good to see the reset this year.

"There were fewer VC funds running around and the hype wasn't there last week. The mood was more about getting down to business."

Various presenters to LendIt last week reported a shift in funding away from hedge funds towards more endowment funds and banks, as marketplace lending is recognised as an established fixed-income asset class.

The dominant theme was risk management. Speakers pointed to the importance of credit standards, data security and how to manage platforms "through the cycle". It would seem P2P is becoming more "fin" than "tech".

At each of the four LendIt events, the keynote has been delivered by Renaud Laplanche, a French entrepreneur and the founder and CEO of Lending Club. In his address last week, Laplanche told delegates: "We have started to see more headwinds - in terms of VC funding, in terms of rising interest rates, of where we are in economic cycle and the regulatory landscape." His investors are acutely aware of his predicament: in the days after its IPO in December 2014, Lending Club's shares surged past $US25 but they sagged throughout 2015, closing on Friday at $US7.31.

In its comprehensive 108-page report on fintech disruption published last month, Citi observed that marketplace lending "is yet to be tested against interest rates and credit cycles" and pointed to "concern that higher rates resulting from Fed (US Federal Reserve) tightening could negatively impact business models levered to consumer credit such as Lending Club". As platforms get bigger, Citi also said, "increasing regulatory scrutiny and requirements could increase the operating costs of such platforms".

Laplanche says Lending Club is tightening its own credit policies and raising its rates and he sought to assuage any concerns that the model wouldn't survive a higher-rate environment.

On the borrower side, the discount for refinancing debt would remain the same as rates rose, and investors should be able to expand their spreads as rates rise and bond yields remain flat, to reflect stronger underlying economic conditions, he said.

Ron Suber, the president of Prosper, the other main P2P player in the US, also used his LendIt presentation to front up to the growing number of P2P naysayers; he urges the industry to listen to and engage with the sceptics. After the US Fed's interest rate rise in December, marketplace loans had performed relatively well for investors compared with other fixed income investments, he says, but the failure of some marketplace lenders was unavoidable and a normal part of the capitalist process.

He also warns: "it's not just about origination growth, [but] about building a business that lasts … We have to be a trusted global brand, which is why we need to be transparent and have the people and capital so the next generation will trust us and use us for more than just term loans."

It is against this global backdrop that SocietyOne reported some numbers last week that should provide encouragement to its star-studded shareholder register, that marketplace lending will become a permanent feature in the Australian financial services landscape.

SocietyOne's existing investors recently tipped another $25 million of funding into the company. While its $100 million in loans is just a drop in the ocean compared with the $100 billion personal credit market in Australia still dominated by the banks, the recent surge in volume suggests more Australian borrowers are becoming aware of fintech alternatives to the incumbents.

As three of the big banks prepare to report interim profits during the first week of May, the threat of disintermediation of profitable banking business remains a concern. Personal lending remains one of the most profitable part of banking, comprising about 4 per cent of big bank balance sheets but about 16 per cent of net profit. The net interest margin on personal loans sit at about 12 per cent; on credit cards, NIMs are 15 per cent.

In a presentation to the APP Securities Disruptive Technology lunch in Sydney on Thursday, SocietyOne's chief financial officer Anna Harper pointed to originations growth of more than 10 per cent month on month for the past three years. The average size of a loan is $18,000 with an average repayment term of 2.7 years.

The rate of default is less than 2 per cent of the dollar value of the whole portfolio. This compares with about 4 per cent for credit cards and personal loans across the industry. Sixty-two per cent of SocietyOne loans are taken out for debt consolidation. Thirty-four per cent of borrowers are classified as professionals and an additional 23 per cent are managers. Eighteen per cent of borrowers have income of more than $150,000; only 5 per cent have income of less than $50,000.

On the investor side, SocietyOne is planning on bringing retail investors onto the platform this year, which is currently only available to sophisticated investors. Its loans are also being funded by eight credit unions. Since January 2013, investors have experienced an effective annualised rate of return of about 8.5 per cent and about 70 per cent of investors have chosen to re-invest their principal and interest repayments back onto the platform.

On the sidelines of LendIt, Greg Symons says he met with US institutional investors to discuss them funding on the platform; now that SocietyOne is coming up to four years, its track record will make it easier to attract foreign capital, he says.

"The banks are waking up very fast. They can't kill the sector now - it has [become] too big. It is rapidly growing."
direction: Bullish for P2P lenders like SocietyOne and Lending Club

headline: MacBank loses pragmatic dealmaker
text:
Robin Bishop was the banker you wanted in your boardroom when the CEO thought the deal would make his reputation, investors and press were clamouring for a close and billions were on the line.

The head of Macquarie Group's investment banking business in Australia, who plans to retire this year at 45, was regarded as possibly the best deal adviser in the country because he offered sensible, practical and pragmatic advice.

"One person would tell you what you wanted to hear," one of Bishop's financier clients said on Monday. "Robin would tell you how to get it done."

A classical music loving, son of a doctor from Albury on the NSW-Victoria border, Bishop spent 22 years at Macquarie broken only by a two to three-year stint at Goldman Sachs' London office.

His sojourn overseas was perhaps the least productive of his stellar career. Planning to make his name in the global financial hub and enjoying life in trendy Notting Hill, Bishop's arrival at the famed bank's Fleet Street offices coincided with a deal-drought that cost the jobs of almost an entire year's graduate intake.

Returning to Australia, Bishop was plunged into Macquarie's desperate battle for liquidity during the financial crisis. He personally served on a committee that assessed the bank's risk every day, which in 2008 was probably the most important grouping in the bank, apart from the board.

Chief executive Nicholas Moore was a mentor and was one of many older men who relied for advice on Bishop. They were attracted by his maturity, which he exhibited even as a slightly arrogant undergraduate intellectual at Melbourne University's Ormond College, where he met a future head of TPG Capital Australia, Ben Gray.

The connection was mutually beneficial. Gray, a graduate of Credit Suisse, would hire Bishop to float department store Myer in 2009 (one of the most lucrative private equity deals in the world that year), and buy and sell the Healthscope private hospital chain and Ingham Enterprises, a chicken farmer.

By his mid-forties Bishop was one of the most experienced dealmakers in Australia. Boards took him seriously, as he did himself. He knew how to be charming, but didn't waste the effort on everyone.

Wesfarmers managing director Richard Goyder delighted hearing Bishop describe negotiations with the irascible Solomon Lew.

The Melbourne clothing merchant was attached to his 6 per cent stake in Coles Myer, which Goyder wanted as a launching pad for his grand supermarket strategy.

The supremely rational Bishop convinced Lew to sever ties with the company, which he had once chaired, a step that led to Wesfarmers taking control of Coles. The deal made Bishop's name and cemented his professional friendship with Goyder.

Bishop's retirement triggered speculation that he had already lined up a job. That doesn't seem correct. Bishop's wife is returning to work full-time and he plans to spend time in Melbourne with his sons, aged 7, 5 and 3.

If anything, Bishop is keeping open the option of returning to the bank after six months' rest.

"He is too good not to do anything," Goyder said Monday. "So I am sure he will think about what he wants to do next. I think he is capable of doing a lot."

As of today, Bishop is probably the most sought-after board director in the country. And it's fair to think the companies calling Bishop first will be those looking to deal.
direction: bearish for Macquarie Group

headline: Auction clearances lowest since 2012
intro: The cooling in housing markets around Australia has been confirmed, with new data showing the combined capital city auction clearance rate fell to 62.1 per cent for the December quarter of 2015, the lowest level since 2012.
text:
The cooling in housing markets around Australia has been confirmed, with new data showing the combined capital city auction clearance rate fell to 62.1 per cent for the December quarter of 2015, the lowest level since 2012.

The quarter started well at 73.4 per cent, the CoreLogic RP Data shows.

Auction volume rose over the December quarter, with 33,604 auctions held, 15 per cent more than the September quarter. But auction volume and the clearance rate in the December quarter for 2015 were lower than the same time in 2014.

The contraction was caused largely by the big drop in auction clearances in previously hot Sydney, with Adelaide, Brisbane, Canberra and Melbourne all recording higher clearance rates than a year ago.

Sydney's housing market is cooling, with some valuers and agents calling up to a 10 per cent fall in prices. The general sentiment among experts, however, is that the biggest housing market will remain stable at a growth rate in prices of about 4 to 5 per cent.

In Sydney, the waterfront suburb Drummoyne had a near-perfect auction clearance in the last quarter of 2015, clearing 89.4 per cent. However, Sydney's auction clearance rates tumbled in the quarter, after a heady 2015 housing boom. Clearance rates fell to 59.8 per cent, from 76.3 per cent at the start of the quarter.

"Inner Western Sydney has been a popular location for many buyers, generally showing lower price points than the eastern suburbs and North Shore," Corelogic head of research Tim Lawless said.

"The high success rate of auctions at Drummoyne reflects a very competitive market, where the auction process is working very well. With many active buyers and a relatively small number of homes for sale, the competitive tension is likely one of the key factors driving such a high clearance rate over the quarter."

There were about 225 homes listed for sale in Drummoyne in 2015, representing just 3.5 per cent of all dwelling stock in the suburb.

There's plenty of water in Melbourne's Reservoir, after it topped the list with the most auctions in the last quarter of the year, RP Data Corelogic data shows.

Inner-north Reservoir had the highest number of auctions at 275 between September and December 2015 and its clearance rate was also equally impressive, at 70.6 per cent. The overall clearance rate for Melbourne was 67.8 per cent.

"Over the past year approximately 70 per cent of all Reservoir homes listed for sale were being sold by auction," Mr Lawless said.

"Considering the average across broader Melbourne is much lower, at approximately 40 per cent of all listings being taken to auction, it is clear that the suburb has an auction culture when it comes to selling homes."

The suburbs in each city with the highest volume of listings in the quarter were Sydney's Mosman at 152 auctions, Adelaide's Prospect at 30, Brisbane's Paddington at 49, Canberra's Kambah and Narrabundah at 33 each and Perth's Rivervale at 16.

Across the largest regional auction markets, Geelong topped the tables with a clearance rate of 64.2 per cent over the quarter, slightly higher than was recorded over the final quarter of the previous year.
direction: bearish for residential property and related sectors

headline: Finance braces for AI revolution
text:
Machines are learning to out-think fund managers.

In a year of extraordinary events, few in the markets would have noted the events of Saturday, March 12.

But what happened that day in the ballroom of the Four Seasons Hotel in Seoul may in time prove to be the most dramatic moment - not only for finance, but for the entire human race.

There and then, the 33-year-old champion of the ancient Chinese board game Go, Lee Sedol, was defeated for the third time in a row by a machine.

This was not meant to happen for another decade.

In 1997, IBM's Deep Blue beat chess grandmaster Gary Kasparov, but that was a triumph of brute calculating force. In Go, there are more possible moves than there are atoms in the universe. To win, a machine had to do more than process data. It had to think for itself.

Google's AlphaGo had learnt to think in second and third orders, Daniel Nadler of start-up Kensho explained at a Milken Institute gathering weeks after the historic match.

Or as he put it, AlphaGo had to think in "the same way a trader would deduce that a Chinese economic slowdown would have a knock-on effect on the cost of Australian rocks and that has a knock-on effect on Australian bank CDS [credit default spreads].

"Whether it's a Go player or a trader modelling the second and third order effects of a Chinese growth slowdown - it is actually now capable of being monitored, studied, analysed and critically replicated by machine."

Nadler's firm Kensho was created with an unashamed mission to render hundreds of thousands of high-paying investment banking jobs obsolete by teaching machines to do their jobs.

And it turns out investment management doesn't require the most "esoteric features of the human brain", David Seigel, the co-founder of $US38 billion ($52 billion) hedge fund Two Sigma, said at the Milken panel.

In fact it requires "the features that are able to be built into artificial intelligence systems today". Machines don't even have to be "that clever, just better than the overwhelmed human investment manager", Seigel says.

"The systems are getting better and better year after year and we [humans] are not getting better year after year - this is a remarkably big change."

Computers, codes and algorithms have formed part of finance for decades - with creative and destructive consequences.

We've seen the academics that taught Wall Street how to price complex securities using models adapted from the sciences, the "trend following" commodity trading advisers that found exploitable patterns; the structured credit PhDs that almost blew up the world; and the "high-frequency traders" that used extreme speed and engineering to pick off lumbering orders.

Now the finance industry is entering the next phase of the quantitative revolution where machines are training themselves to out-think the collective human wisdom of the markets.

Kris Longmore, a 2.03-metre mechanical and environmental engineer, spent the past decade using data and simulation to help Australian mining companies.

That included collecting data and installing sensors in the most remote parts of the Pilbara. He also helped crack the problem of identifying habitats of endangered species from acquired data and machine learning.

When he first got interested in applying his skills to investing he assumed the financial world "would be way ahead of the curve with this stuff".

"I figured that whatever we were doing in engineering and earth science, these guys would have been doing years ago."

He was wrong. Longmore co-founded Quantify, a consultancy advising hedge funds and financial institutions on applying artificial intelligence (AI) and machine learning.

"There are some institutions and funds that are all over this stuff, but the vast majority are struggling to find their way. Maybe I gave these guys too much credit." Longmore believes there are amazing traders out there but they are in the minority. "The rest of us rely on number crunching and pattern recognition and the human just isn't equipped to do that anywhere near as well as a computer."

One of his projects is to build machine-learning models that replicate successful human traders. "It sounds quite sinister but what we are trying to do is uncover what the really successful discretionary traders do and bottle it, because it's a commodity."

Machine learning itself is nothing new. At its core, it is simply a tool for identifying statistical relationships between variables and then using those relationships to make decisions about the future.

In fact, the simple linear regression that is taught at high school is an example of machine learning.

Deep neural networks and support vector machines aren't taught in your standard high school but Longmore says they are different tools suitable for more complex problems.

"We live in this exciting point in time where the amount of data at our fingertips is exploding and we also have the computational resources to process it," says Longmore. "At the same time, machine learning research is on an exponential trajectory, so the analytical tools are only going to get better."

The data explosion has been spectacular. One estimate, presented at a recent trading conference in Singapore, is that since 2010 the amount of structured data which is information that is readily organised to be analysed has increased by a factor of 70 to 35 zetabytes. One zetabyte is the equivalent of 152 million hours of high-resolution video.

The future powerhouses of finance will be those that can manage, store, process and model their data.

But the potentially bigger winners, says Longmore, will be those that can unearth their own data sources, not only from the universe of structured data but from the satellite images, social network feeds, voice pitch variations of chief executives and physical movements of goods and people.

A few blocks from the University of California in Berkeley - where many of the minds that have powered the tech revolution were coded - Frank Freitas and his small team of data scientists at Pluribus Labs are turning noise into signals.

"When I walk down the street, what I see is the world spinning off massive amounts of data almost every bit of which has relevance and value for financial services," Freitas, a 25-year veteran of finance, tells AFR Weekend.

Freitas's moment of truth came when he dialled in to hear the management of a major US energy company discuss its quarterly earnings with analysts.

"I could tell something was going on there but nothing I could put my finger on explicitly. It sort of smelled a little like Denmark. There was something they were trying to talk around."

A few days later the stock was sent tumbling after bad news was disclosed, validating his gut feel. But it convinced Freitas that neither he, nor anyone else, could be on every call at every moment, even without the distractions of daily life - a hangover, a divorce or a tax return.

"We can train a machine to pick out the unintended consequences of how executives speak about the outcomes of their company that are maybe not even visible or even part of their conscious processing.

"And we can systematically, dispassionately generate those same results for every company in the Russell 3000 - from 2005 to the present."

Pluribus specialises in taking unstructured data sourced from social media, devices and satellite images and organising it to find relationships that can be used to give an edge in financial markets.

Social media feeds, for instance, can provide useful insights into shifting sentiment and trading volumes in stocks. The "internet of people" which tracks movements of devices shows emerging neighbourhoods, store locations and brands, while the "internet of things" can track locations and usage of industrial equipment, giving real-time reads on economic activity.

"We are using [technology] to ask what I would call first order questions: 'How do we take data and turn it into a prediction for a company outcome?" says Freitas. "But really we could be using it for higher order questions like macro economic policy decisions, or impact investing decisions."

In time, governments and policymakers that have relied on imperfect untimely data and inaccurate models to make decisions may embrace these new tools.

For now, it's well-resourced, tech-savvy hedge funds such as Two Sigma, Citadel Bridgewater and Point 72 that are assembling their own teams of data scientists in the hedge fund havens of New York, Connecticut and London.

But this revolution is spreading beyond the world's money centres to wherever the human talent that can programme intelligence in machines exists.

About three times a year on a Saturday morning, a dozen curious coders make their way to the Tyro Fintech hub in Sydney's Clarence Street.

They're there to take part in a Hackathon hosted by Boston hedge fund Quantopian, which holds similar events around the world.

Quantopian can best be described as a crowd-sourced hedge fund that has built a platform for coders and data scientists around the world to create their own trading algorithms. Quantopian then invests real dollars in the ones it likes, paying royalties to the creators.

Their presence in Sydney is no accident.

Quantopian indexes the geography of readers and respondents to the highly technical content it publishes.

"The traffic tells us where the people are," co-founder John Fawcett tells AFR Weekend from his Boston base.

"Relative to the population, the engagement in Australia is extremely high and we have a pretty avid user community."

Quantopian is one of several trading-focused start-ups that are attracting venture capital based on the bet that an explosion of data and the advancement of machine learning has opened up a frontier of new trading possibilities.

Among their backers is Steve Cohen, the founder of Point 72, who built his hedge fund empire on combining all available information to make winning trades.

"The way markets are needing to adapt and the ascendancy of machine learning and AI in finance is a huge part of his thinking," Fawcett says, adding that Cohen also sees the industry as a fight for talent and "the fact that we are casting the net wide is resonating with him".

The world's top hedge funds would typically recruit from the top Ivy League mathematics and statistics graduates but it's a pool that is shrinking, not growing.

The tech giants like Google, Uber, Amazon and Apple are at the forefront of the AI revolution and are competing ferociously among themselves.

It's forcing the finance industry to come up with creative solutions such as sourcing talent in other parts of the world, and from outside the formal academic institutions.

Fawcett created Quantopian to solve what he describes as a production problem. A successful trading system decays over time as it gets discovered, so the key to success is to replenish the system with new ideas.

They can be found in three areas - the people with the ideas, the markets that can be traded, and the data that can be used to search for strategies, he says.

The investment industry is already in the midst of an upheaval as low-cost passive and smart beta strategies lure hundreds of billions of assets away from active fund managers that have in aggregate failed to beat the market.

Now, Freitas says, automation is coursing through the industry.

He predicts the number of hedge funds will fall sharply from the current count of 9000, and about 10 or so firms that have the scale, sophistication and computing power will come to dominate.

"They will be the centre of the new universe that is emerging for active management."

In November of this year, Raphael Arndt, the chief investment officer of Australia's Future Fund, flew to Silicon Valley and New York to meet some of the tech start-ups and hedge funds that it has backed.

The trip re-affirmed what he already knew: technology's impact on his life, his children's lives and the sector he works in is vastly under-appreciated.

Across the $125 billion portfolio he oversees are allocations to traditional asset classes like equities and bonds, real assets such as property and infrastructure, and alternative investments like hedge funds and private equity.

It's the fund's alternative investments that have given it a glimpse into the future, which will have a dramatic impact on every aspect of its portfolio and the way it is managed.

The Future Fund has $2 billion invested in early stage venture capital across the world and has more recently begun co-investing directly in technology start-ups.

"We have direct exposure to companies pioneering commercial applications for AI and machine learning, to structure and analyse big data. We can see what they are doing."

The Future Fund emerged as a surprise lead investor, along with Google Ventures, in big data analytics specialist MapR, which just filed for a public listing, and is a backer of about 10 other big data start-ups.

"We are fortunate that because of the venture capital we have a direct line of sight which has informed our thinking," Arndt says.

The Future Fund also has around $5 billion allocated to cutting-edge hedge funds that are exploiting advances in technology and quantitative processes to offer out-sized returns.

One fund it backs, London based Man AHL, sponsors AI research centres that give it access to advancements in the field.

The hedge fund industry is deploying extraordinary trading strategies to which the Future Fund is exposed.

"We have managers that are reading every sell-side broker research note in real time and trading intra day. The reports could be in any language - for instance a Chinese stock report in Mandarin. It can be read, processed and traded within seconds.

"We have managers that are scanning satellite images of car parks in shopping centres to predict earnings of department stores, and trading on that information in real time."

The Future Fund's investments in tech start-ups and hedge funds are what Arndt describes as offensive. But it's the defensive aspect that will take up more of his time.

The rise of AI threatens to destroy billions of dollars of value in global companies and even real assets.

And no manager is safe - as quantitative techniques threaten to either beat them or expose them. The Future Fund has already warned the private equity industry that it will need to do better to justify the fees they charge. Now some US managers are using quantitative techniques to scan the accounts of thousands of smaller businesses for value, and are promising better returns than the bigger players, which are fighting aggressively for the same pool of available assets.

With a portfolio of $125 billion that touches every aspect of the global economy with investments in US retailers to Australian ports and Chinese start-ups, the Future Fund will one day have the potential to piece together a picture of the micro and macro economy from the data it owns.

"The challenge is that this information has always been available but there is too much data and much of it is unstructured," Arndt says. "I wouldn't want to give the impression that we have solved this but we have investments in companies that can do this."

The Future Fund is hiring a head of investment data management as it ramps up its data management and portfolio analytic functions.

Arndt says the fund is also in the early stages of scoping a role to take responsibility for disruption risk.

"We need to talk to managers and incorporate ESG [environmental, social and governance] risks into the investment process and we want to do the same for technological disruption risk.

"Banks are on to it more than the super funds and long-term wealth funds. There is not enough creative disruption in our industry."

History has shown that the human race is able to adapt to technological changes, Arndt says.

But his optimism is moderated by the timing of this change.

The aftermath of the financial crisis has led to a reduction in policy rates, and an inflation of assets that has benefited the wealthy but also lowered expected returns on surplus capital, lowered investment in productive capacity, leading wages to fall and income disparity to widen.

A technological revolution threatens to exacerbate the discontent already evident in populist backlashes.

"These are problems our policy makers need to adapt to and embrace, but in our industry, we also have to adapt and embrace."

Future is now

Five places the machines are hunting for investment ideas

The internet of thingsTracking the movement of industrial equipment and machinery can give real time data on economic activity.

The internet of people Tracking the movement of devices can show which stores, brands and neighbourhoods are on the rise.

Earnings calls

Natural language processing detects behavioural insights from management beyond the carefully worded responses to analysts.

Social mediaSocial media feeds can help show when sentiment towards a stock shifts, which can help predict trading value and movements

Satellite imagesTracking images of places like a department store's car park can reveal trends on how it is trading.

SOURCE: FINANCIAL REVIEW
direction: bearish for traditional finance roles

headline: Malcolm and Bill clock off for a rare moment of accord
text:
Ralph E. Wolf (who bears a striking resemblance to Wile E Coyote) and Sam Sheepdog were two Warner Bros cartoon characters who would amiably line up to punch their time cards at sunrise and sunset each day with friendly greetings ("Morning Ralph", "Morning Sam").

But between punching on and off, they would play their respective roles as evil wolf trying to nab an innocent sheep and protective sheepdog with passion.

Ralph and Sam came to mind on Monday in federal Parliament as we saw a rare moment of accord between the major parties, which produced not just a compromise that would allow the Turnbull government's $6 billion "omnibus" savings bill to pass, but a much better bill containing more savings, more equity and the quiet abandonment of some dopey spending initiatives.

It was a good omen for the government, and for the Prime Minister, on the eve of the first anniversary of his coming to office, and after several months of predictions of dire unworkability of the Parliament voted in by Australians on July 2.

"This demonstrates that we are delivering on our economic plan," the Prime Minister enthused. "We are delivering on our commitment to bring the budget back into balance and that with goodwill and good faith, working across the aisle we can deliver results in this Parliament which the Australian people elected."

Before punching the question time time clock, the two sides of politics were almost gushy about each other.

"The government appreciates the constructive engagement with the opposition," government ministers said in statement. "Labor thanks the government for its engagement," Labor frontbenchers responded.

Of course, it didn't last long. Labor was quick out of the boxes to highlight other cuts it was not supporting. The government was unkind enough to find ways to mention Sam Dastyari.

But beyond the theatrics, the deal is an important lesson on what can be achieved by co-operation. The result was savings garnered from cutting welfare payments to higher-income earners, instead of those who are really struggling.

There was compromise on renewable energy funding. Business had research and development spending cut but all the major business groups were prepared to live with that, given the net result was the guaranteed passage of the savings through Parliament.
direction: supportive for government-backed equities and fiscal-conservative sectors

headline: Labor calls for Robert's head over China trip
intro: Labor leader Bill Shorten has called for Human Resources Minister Stuart Robert to resign over revelations he was at a meeting with a senior Chinese minister and representatives from a Liberal Party donor's company on a "private" trip to China to oversee a mining deal.
text:
Labor leader Bill Shorten has called for Human Resources Minister Stuart Robert to resign over revelations he was at a meeting with a senior Chinese minister and representatives from a Liberal Party donor's company on a "private" trip to China to oversee a mining deal.

Mr Shorten said it was "time for Malcolm Turnbull to show the leadership he promised and sack Stuart Robert," accusing Mr Turnbull of "outsourcing his responsibilities" to the public service by waiting for the findings of Department of Prime Minister and cabinet secretary Martin Parkinson's investigation into the matter.

Government ministers declined to strongly defend Mr Robert on Tuesday, simply saying he had their support and they were waiting for the findings of the investigation that Mr Turnbull commissioned on Monday.

The Australian Financial Review reported on Tuesday that Mr Robert also met a Chinese government minister along with representatives from the donor's company on his trip, which he says that he paid for, was "private" and conducted when he was on personal leave.

A Chinese government website says Mr Robert met China's vice-minister of land and resources, Wang Min, a day after he attended a signing ceremony for Liberal Party donor Paul Marks' Nimrod Resources with China Minmetals Corporation, on August 19, 2014. The website also said representatives of Nimrod Resources attended the same meeting with the minister.

Mr Robert has declared he holds shares in Evolution Gold, a company linked to Mr Marks. Mr Marks, who is a friend of Mr Robert, also donated $340,000 to the Liberal Party in 2014-15.

Labor say this constitutes a conflict of interest and a potential breach of the ministerial code of conduct, as the code says ministers should not act as consultants or advisers to businesses, whether they are paid or not when in office.

The Coalition tried to block Labor questions directed at Mr Robert in question time on Tuesday, but Mr Robert was forced to answer.

"I say to the house, I am confident I have not acted inappropriately and as the PM said yesterday, this matter has been referred to the highest public servant in the land, Dr Martin Parkinson for review. I will fully assist the secretary in his review," he said.

Education Minister Simon Birmingham said: "I don't know all of the circumstances but Martin Parkinson as secretary of the Department of Prime Minister and Cabinet, I have complete faith will get to the bottom of the matter".

Indigenous Affairs Minister Nigel Scullion said: "that is a matter for the Prime Minister and I understand he's provided that, quite rightly, to Martin Parkinson to make an investigation. This is a government that values probity and transparency and that's exactly what's happening".

Earlier, the opposition moved a motion to increase the pressure on Mr Robert to front up and answer questions about his trip.

"If we were in doubt about the wrong that's been done here, today we've had a fresh revelation that Mr Robert, while in Beijing, met with a Chinese minister the next day.

"Mr Robert needs to come into the Parliament and make a statement as to what he did," Labor Shadow Attorney-General Mark Dreyfus said.

Labor said the Prime Minister's own statement of ministerial standards has "a blanket ban on ministers providing assistance to companies in a private capacity".

"The Minister for Veterans' Affairs provided assistance in a private capacity to Nimrod Resources to seal a mining deal with a Chinese state-owned company during a trip to China," Mr Dreyfus said.
direction: bearish for Nimrod Resources and related mining shares

headline: Don't write off traditional media: PwC
intro: Free-to-air television and newspapers are set to see their share of the Australian advertising market decline, but don't write them off yet, says Megan Brownlow, because they have been among the best at embracing new business models.
text:
Free-to-air television and newspapers are set to see their share of the Australian advertising market decline, but don't write them off yet, says Megan Brownlow, because they have been among the best at embracing new business models.

The two mediums are set to see their share of Australia's advertising market slip to 18.5 per cent and 10 per cent of a $18.7 billion total respectively by 2020, according to PricewaterhouseCoopers' Australian Media and Entertainment Outlook 2016-20, edited by Ms Brownlow.

There is no doubt traditional media is facing challenges from all angles with the barriers to entry significantly lower than in years gone by. But don't go ringing to death knell just yet.

Advertising revenue for "linear" free-to-air is only set to fall by a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of negative 1 per cent to $3.6 billion by 2020, while for newspapers the slide is larger at 5 per cent CAGR.

However, these forecasts are missing an important ingredient in the future of media companies - digital advertising revenue.

"People tend to be dismissive of traditional media, but actually traditional media has been among the best at embracing new business models," Ms Brownlow told Fairfax Media.

Online is just another channel, albeit more competitive, for readers and viewers to consume media.

Internet advertising, according to PwC is set to grow from 39 per cent of a $14.9 billion ad market in 2015 to 51.4 per cent of the $18.7 billion market expected in 2020.

One of the big problems for media companies is that digital advertising is a lower-value transaction and the competition for dollars is much fiercer, and for newspapers particularly the "rivers of gold", or classifieds, that funded the sector have gone elsewhere.

Ms Brownlow said that over the period she expects broadcast and online video to converge into one pool, rather than advertisers having separate budgets for TV and streaming which will impact how media is bought.

"That some of the platforms, that are exchanges or are selling video, you can flip the metrics between digital metrics and TV metrics. TubeMogul, for example, if you're a buyer who is used to TV metrics, you can buy using TV metrics, but essentially what you are buying is online video," Ms Brownlow said.

"New currencies in the market like OzTam's online video metrics - these are the sorts of things, that once we get good data and good measurement tools that are, in a sense, platform agnostic, that's what will propel that view of TV and online video as just one bucket being video."

However, Ms Brownlow noted that online revenue won't necessarily offset declining traditional revenue, but it's important to take a slice of that market.

"It's a much more crowded market and most of that market is owned by YouTube and Facebook."

According to a report by Accenture, commissioned by US network ABC, multi-platform TV advertising has a halo effect on other forms of digital ads. The report concluded that multi-platform TV advertising improved return on investment for paid search, display and short-form video by an average of 10 per cent. While stand-alone digital advertising ROI without multi-platform TV dropped 18 per cent.

The gist of the study is that advertising is improved by quality long-form content.

However, that doesn't mean digital giants such as Facebook and Google aren't going keep coming for the media industry's lunch.

PwC technology, information, communications and entertainment industry leader David Wiadrowski said traditional media needs to branch out and create new revenue streams.

"If a consumer sees Fairfax or Channel Seven as a publisher of newspapers or a free-to-air channel, in the long-term they won't survive," he said.
direction: traditional media shares supported by digital adaptation

headline: Patrick, the wharfie that cried wolf
intro: Patrick's move to seek Fair Work Commission orders to force the waterfront union from its election-confected torpor illuminates only the misjudgment that saw the container port operator eschew a lock-out of workers back in May.
text:
Patrick's move to seek Fair Work Commission orders to force the waterfront union from its election-confected torpor illuminates only the misjudgment that saw the container port operator eschew a lock-out of workers back in May.

Asciano-owned Patrick and the Maritime Union of Australia have now been negotiating for more than 14 months over the replacement of the 2012 enterprise agreements that cover workers at the company's four container terminals.

Those discussions moved into entrenched deadlock through the back end of April as rolling strikes across the Patrick nation saw management trim juicy parts of the offer under debate and make very public commitments that what had been excised would stay excised.

To be clear here, the hub of this dispute is Patrick's Port Botany terminal and the union's requirement that its members there should be paid on a 35-hour roster while working only 32 hours. The union identifies this as an automation bonus that flows from the painful introduction of automation.

Patrick, on the other hand, sees this as gouging that aims to force the re-hiring of 60 workers of the more than 200 workers who lost their jobs through the automation ordeal. The company position is that this just will not happen.

In early May, to the further irritation of the union, Patrick took its reduced terms to a direct vote of its dockers. The union advised rejection. Not surprisingly, given the prerequisites of a safe and happy life on the waterfront, the workers got the message. The offer was rejected by 98.8 per cent of those who voted. To characterise that near unanimity as a disappointment to Patrick people would be an exercise in bitter understatement.

That said, Patrick went into that vote expecting to lose and fully expecting that the union would respond to its resounding shopfloor endorsement by initiating further and broader industrial action.

Unfortunately for Patrick, just four days before that necessarily provocative ballot was run and lost, Prime Minister Malcolm Turnbull visited the Governor General with his recommendation of a double dissolution.

As we have observed in the recent past, whether by orders, arrangement or telepathy, the union got the message and the industrial campaign appears to have ended for the duration of the election campaign.

The thing is though, before and after that ballot the trigger for an issue-defining lock-out was fully available under the law and publicly justifiable given accumulating cost and self-defeating logic of the strike campaign.

The object of a lock-out would be to announce a level of immediate and broad economic harm enough to see either a customer or government apply to the commission for orders to terminate the MUA bargaining period and force the negotiation into arbitration.

This could have been the Qantas story replayed. But Patrick demurred. It assessed that the time between the most recent industrial action (which had occurred in April) and the failed ballot meant that the commission would likely reject a lock-out application. This was not the position of legal counsel contacted by The Australian Financial Review at the time. And many still hold that this was a resolution opportunity missed.

Instead, as Thursday's application for commission orders makes plain, this dispute is now working to a pace defined by the union and the requirements of its political partners.

Patrick will arrive at the commission on Friday morning seeking to sustain a case that the MUA has failed the good faith bargaining obligations defined in the Fair Work Act.

Under the industrial law, good faith means attending and contributing to meetings. It means disclosing relevant information, offering genuine consideration of offers and refraining from capricious conduct. It also means responding to formal proposals in a timely manner.

Patrick contends that the union has failed this test, at the very least. It offered a preview of its case in an attachment to the application that will be heard on Friday morning and that Patrick has requested a decision upon within a week.

Patrick says the proposed agreement was sent to the union ahead of the ballot that opened on May 4. Patrick asserts that the union has known since May 12 that the offer taken to the ballot was final. The company identifies a confetti of communication between management and the union since. But still there has been no union response to the final offer.

Patrick asserts the union has failed to respond in a timely manner to an offer that has not earned the genuine consideration required by the law.

The union responds that it took Patrick a full month to deliver it with a form of the final offer that was acceptable. It characterises Patrick as playing election politics with process. "Patrick should immediately stop using the commission as a political plaything and return to the negotiating table," MUA national deputy secretary Will Tracey commented.

While we might object to the first point, the second is fair enough. Having avoided the shortcut to arbitration, sure it is beholden upon Patrick to resume discussions.

Instead, once again, the process will be delivered to adjudication by FWC vice-president Graeme Watson.

Of course, the last time Watson opined on this ludicrous situation was back in January when the sagacious vice-president decided to suspend the MUA's strike action against Patrick.

With foresight subsequently ignored by an FWC appellant panel, Watson determined that suspension would help resolve matters. Watson observed that Patrick appeared increasingly unresponsive to industrial action and that management would be left free to deal with the union issues at hand if it were able to end the constant bounce in and out of a "firefighting mode" forced by strikes.

Watson's rare but welcome temporary cancellation was immediately appealed by the union and the decision did not survive its assessment by a FWC full bench. In overturning Watson's decision, that panel could not identify any evidence of a hardening of Patrick's position or that further strike action would further polarise employer and union.

The perversity of a review that was received by many as being shaped by the commission's internal politics as much as it was by the industrial law has been fully amplified by subsequent events. As I see it, the real problem here is that winning this tactical battle pretty much does nothing to end a war that could get very heavy after the election.

I mean, let's presume that Patrick wins this round. So, the commission finds that the union has fallen short of all but one of the tests of good faith bargaining and VP Watson issues orders requiring a formal response to the final offer that both sides have been chewing over for better than six weeks. Then what?

Well, given Patrick has taken stuff off the table, the union will respond with a formal rejection and confirmation that everything it has offered is withdrawn and this whole thing goes back to square one. In other words, the talks start again. The alternative is the commission rejects Patrick's proposition. That means what? Well, the talks start again.

Either way, Patrick emerges with formal certainty on what is already presumed (that the final proposal is unacceptable) while getting no closer to the arbitrated outcome that appears to have been the default outcome the company has targeted for pretty much the past six months.

Right now, Patrick looks and sounds increasingly like the wharfie that cried wolf. Thursday's management rhetoric was numbingly familiar. Patrick complained of limited options in the face of an impasse that has lasted months, it reported the diligence of its exploration of levers to secure agreement and threatened that a lock-out remained a "live option should we confront further unwarranted industrial action".

"We have repeatedly said we would welcome government intervention or arbitration from the Fair Work Commission to help avoid such a damaging scenario and to resolve all outstanding issues," Patrick offered on Thursday. But this is a warning beginning to ring hollow and this is a dispute that now looks far more likely to be settled by Patrick's new owners, Qube and Brookfield. They expect to take the keys in August and they increasingly expect that this will be the first thing they have to deal with.
direction: downward pressure on Patrick shares due to prolonged industrial dispute and operational risks

headline: Unilever brews up sweeter deal on T2 acquisition
intro: It was never going to be the bargain of the century, but Unilever's acquisition of gourmet tea business T2 has cost the world's largest tea company less than originally expected.
text:
It was never going to be the bargain of the century, but Unilever's acquisition of gourmet tea business T2 has cost the world's largest tea company less than originally expected.

Unilever acquired T2 from co-founder Maryanne Shearer and her partner Bruce Crome in September 2013 for about $60 million and planned to transform the 40-store chain into a global business.

According to Unilever Australia's latest accounts, the owner of Lipton and Bushells booked gains of $31 million over the last two years - $1.9 million in 2015 and $29.1 million in 2014 - by reversing contingent considerations on the acquisition of T2.

A Unilever spokeswoman confirmed that the gains represented payments that would have been due to T2's former owners if the business had achieved certain levels of performance, but declined to comment further.

"As we are a publicly listed business we do not comment on local subsidiary results unless the subsidiary is listed in the local market," the spokeswoman said.

T2 now has about 65 stores in Australia, New Zealand, the UK and US and a thriving wholesale business selling unique brews such as Apple Crumble, Arctic Fire and Aussie Wattle Breakfast to restaurants, cafes, gourmet food stores and airlines. The first T2 UK store opened in April 2014 and the first New York store, in trendy SoHo, six months later.

Unilever's global president for refreshment, Kevin Havelock, sees scope for hundreds of T2 stores around the world.

While global sales of black tea and traditional teabags have been flat or in decline over the last few years, sales of specialty, green and herbal teas are rising between 10 and 30 per cent.

The value of the T2 acquisition was not disclosed at the time of the deal. According to the accounts, Unilever paid $58.6 million cash upfront and another $3.4 million the following year, while contingent considerations were valued at $31.8 million.

The cash consideration represented a multiple of 23 times earnings, based on T2's last reported profits of $2.48 million on sales of $57 million in 2013.

Since the deal, Unilever has booked a net gain of $2.4 million by selling T2 NZ to Unilever New Zealand in January 2015.

Unilever Australia's group net profit for 2015 fell 44 per cent to $47.9 million, mainly reflecting the $29 million one-off gain in 2014. Sales slipped 4.2 per cent to $1.56 billion in 2015 after rising 3.9 per cent to $1.63 billion in 2014.

Ms Shearer, who opened the first T2 store in the Melbourne suburb of Brunswick in 1996, stepped down from the T2 board in September 2015, Unilever records show.

She was replaced by new T2 chief executive Rachel Kelly.
direction: Unilever shares likely to rise on cost savings and expansion potential

headline: Entrepreneurs put ideas to test by pitching to 12-year-old schoolkids
intro: If you can explain it to a high school student you can explain it to anybody.Dylan Coyne, entrepreneur
text:
Start-ups Business hopefuls take on a very different audience.

Until Friday the latest pitch that start-up partners Annette McClelland and Michael Griffin had made was to the Telstra-backed accelerator Muru-D, which has nurtured many of Australia's entrepreneurial companies.

But Ms McClelland and Mr Griffin still hadn't raised the $500,000 capital they were looking for to bring their idea - a drone controller, which you can use with one hand - to fruition.

So the pair joined eight other hopeful start-ups at Sydney's Powerhouse Museum to pitch to a different audience - high school students.

The event, initiated by CSIRO's Data61 as part of the Spark Festival celebrating Australian start-up businesses, was a test of a popular trope in the entrepreneur community - you know your pitch is on target if a 12-year-old can understand it.

"If you can explain it to a high school student you can explain it to anybody," said Dylan Coyne, another young entrepreneur at the event who pitched his start-up Washable, an Uber-like service for car washing.

His service will allow people wanting part-time work cleaning cars to register with Washable and be assigned cars to wash at the owners' premises. The price will be as low as $35 for a small car and the cleaner keeps 80 per cent of the revenue.

Ms McClelland and Mr Griffin, who are working with the Hatchery+ start-up accelerator at the University of Technology Sydney, said their controller would work with all drones and make it far easier for a single drone operator to take pictures or video. One hand controls the drone while the other operates the camera.

The pair also said their controller made it easier to learn to fly a drone, with a 30 per cent reduction in training time. But what did the high school students say? "You know how you said it was easy to do with one hand. We don't know if you've been practising for hours," said one sceptical girl. "It would be good if somebody from the audience came up and had a turn just to show how easy it is."

A second student said their slides were too information heavy. And a third said she would like to hear more information about the target market for the drone controller. "Are you going to sell it to other companies or are you going to sell it directly."

Another pitch popular with students was the start-up business Elenation, which has developed wearable technology, similar to Fitbit, which measures the physical activity children do and rewards them with time on electronic games when they have done sufficient exercise.

"I can see my little brother really enjoying this with his friends," said one boy. But he thought it could be improved. He told Elenation's pitcher, Amee Atkins, that it needed a wider selection of electronic games as rewards.
direction: supportive for early-stage venture capital and tech start-ups
