headline: Coalition fuels Brexit sentiments: Shorten
intro: Labor leader Bill Shorten used his last major speech of the election campaign to argue the Coalition's policies would fuel the very discontent that had led to the rise of Donald Trump in the US and Britain's exit from the European Union.
text:
Labor leader Bill Shorten used his last major speech of the election campaign to argue the Coalition's policies would fuel the very discontent that had led to the rise of Donald Trump in the US and Britain's exit from the European Union.

After days of the Coalition using Brexit to its advantage by arguing it showed a need for economic and political stability, Mr Shorten turned the tables on Tuesday in a pre-election speech to the National Press Club.

He said the Coalition's cuts to health, education and welfare, while spending almost $50 billion on corporate tax cuts, would fuel the sense of inequality and alienation that was driving voters abroad to abandon the political class.

"The Liberals are asking Australians to reject the co-operative economic model and the social wage which has held our nation together for more than 30 years and delivered a quarter century of growth," he said. "And instead, they want Australians to embark upon a radical, expensive experiment and trickle-down economics.

"We know how this story goes. [Ronald] Reagan tried it, [Margaret] Thatcher tried it. A generation later, we got [Donald] Trump and we got Brexit.

"The gathering push of extreme right-wing populism around the globe is a warning to all of us not to leave people behind. Governments must include and must empower people. We must give every citizen a sense of being an active participant in transition, in control of what is happening to them, not a passive observer of change left behind on the scrap heap."

As voters prepare to go to the polls on Saturday, there is a growing sense that Labor cannot win, due in part to it stumbling over the budget in the critical final week.

Mr Shorten said the economic fallout from Brexit had been overstated and there was no cause for alarm.

"But its political and economic lesson cannot and should not be ignored," he said. "The Liberals invoke it for a call for stability, but they fundamentally misunderstand the source of the instability. It comes from a sense of inequality. From people feeling marginalised, forgotten, alienated, left behind by global change. It's a deep-seeded sense the political promises are wasted words. It comes from exactly the same sort of cynicism in policies that Mr Turnbull's offering Australians at this election. Tax cuts for the rich, nothing for the working and middle-class Australians, telling a generation of young Australians shut out of the housing market to get rich parents, pricing kids out of university, cutting funding from Medicare.

"It comes from a fundamental misunderstanding of both our world economy and our Australian economy."

In an appeal to voters, Mr Shorten said Labor had a comprehensive policy agenda that would enable it to "hit the ground running", whereas Mr Turnbull's agenda was painfully thin.

"You cannot dress timid stagnation up as stability and plead for people to stick with your mob for another time or for a while," he said.

He promised that, if elected, the first piece of legislation would be on same-sex marriage.

Mr Shorten continued his assault on the government over Medicare, insisting that, if re-elected, Mr Turnbull would dismantle the universal health care system "brick by brick".

He said Mr Turnbull was "lying to your face" by saying he would leave Medicare alone.

Key points

Coalition policies will fuel inequality and alienation.

Compares Turnbull to Reagan, Thatcher and Trump.
direction: bearish for domestic-focused shares and property trusts

text:
Comment&OpinionPhilip Baker

Getting Trumpled on cards

It goes to show that investors are in for a tricky time regardless of who wins the US election on November 8. p26

John Brogden

How 'fairness' hurts reform

For the last 30 years public policy reform was achievable if there were more winners than losers. Now there can be no losers at all. p39
direction: bearish for equities due to heightened political and policy uncertainty

headline: Life insurers line up for historic bond deal
intro: Government bonds, much like their 007 namesake James, have cheated death for nearly 40 years.
text:
Government bonds, much like their 007 namesake James, have cheated death for nearly 40 years.

So perhaps we should not be surprised that after the latest scare, the great bond rally has lived to fight another day.

And so it is that dealmakers are back out in front of investors. This week is set to be a big one in the local market as the Australian government prepares to launch its first-ever 30-year bond.

The 30-year deal has been years in the making, having initially been touted by then treasurer Joe Hockey. The pitch was all about extending Australia's debt maturity profile and also providing a much needed benchmark to price infrastructure debt.

The Australian Office of Financial Management, which manages the Treasury's bond sales, has six banks lined up to launch and run this week's historic deal as joint lead managers. Sales teams at ANZ Banking Group, Citi, Commonwealth Bank of Australia, Deutsche Bank, UBS and Westpac have all been primed and are ready to finally push the button.

While the AOFM keeps its cards close to its chest, and for good reason, traders reckon the transaction will launch on Tuesday and price on Wednesday. They're tipping a benchmark-sized transaction of at least $4 billion with demand likely to be strong and led by life insurers from around the world.

The most interesting element will be pricing. Remember there is no precedent transaction; the government has never done such a large "yield curve extension" which means there are likely to be a range of views in an untested part of the market.

By way of comparison, 30-year US Treasury bonds - at 2.45 per cent - trade at 75 basis points over their 10-year bonds which yield 1.70 per cent. German 30-year "bunds" - at 0.65 per cent - trade at that premium above their zero-yielding 10-year bunds.

Some believe the Aussie 30-year will be marketed at 20 basis points over our longest existing bond, the 2039s - which currently yield 2.90 per cent - to pay around 3.10 per cent.

If so, the 95 basis spread over our 2.15 per cent yielding 10 year bond would be cheap. But given the historic nature of this deal, the true clearing rate of Aussie 30-year debt will be known only once the deal is launched.
direction: supportive for government bonds and life insurers

headline: Woolworths shares surge on growth hope
intro: Woolworths needs to lift supermarket sales by at least 2 per cent this year to return to profit growth after posting a $1.2 billion loss in 2016 - its first as a listed company - and slashing its dividend.
text:
Woolworths needs to lift supermarket sales by at least 2 per cent this year to return to profit growth after posting a $1.2 billion loss in 2016 - its first as a listed company - and slashing its dividend.

Chief executive Brad Banducci gave Woolworths' long suffering shareholders a glimmer of hope that the worst may be over for Australia's largest retailer, saying same-store sales in Australian supermarkets had risen 0.3 per cent in the first eight weeks of 2017, the first growth in 15 months, despite intense competition from Coles and Aldi.

Woolworths shares, which have fallen more than 40 per cent in the past few years, rose as much as 7.5 per cent to their highest level since October, catching out investors who have short sold the stock.

"It may be too early to call a trend (but) we may have been through the worst as far as sales are concerned," said Deutsche Bank analyst Michael Simotas.

Mr Banducci, who took the helm from Grant O'Brien in February after a year running supermarkets, urged caution, saying volume growth would need to be sustained for Woolworths to return to profit growth this year.

"We still have work to do on rebuilding the group. We have an uphill challenge this year, but we have started with some momentum," Mr Banducci told The Australian Financial Review.

"I don't think it's a flash in the pan, but time will tell."

Woolworths plunged $1.2 billion into the red after slashing the value of its home improvement and general merchandise assets by $4 billion, reporting weaker earnings from supermarkets in Australia and New Zealand, Big W and hotels, and further losses in home improvement.

Excluding net impairment charges and restructuring costs of $2.6 billion, underlying net profit fell 64.4 per cent to $803.5 million and earnings before interest and tax from continuing operations - excluding hardware losses - fell 35.5 per cent to $2.56 billion, in line with Woolworths' revised guidance.

Woolworths slashed its final dividend by 54 per cent, from 72¢ to 33¢ a share, and will partially underwrite the dividend to refinance $700 million of debt and fund store refurbishments.

Ratings agencies Moody's and S&P said the weak results tested thresholds for the company's credit ratings, but stopped short of downgrades, citing positive same-store sales growth in the past few weeks and potential for further asset sales after the sale of home improvement.

Australian supermarket earnings plunged 40.8 per cent to $1.7 billion, reflecting more than $1 billion of investment into food prices, service and refurbishments in stores over the past 18 months.

Woolworths' world-beating food and petrol margins dropped from 7.3 per cent to 4.47 per cent, falling below those at Coles.

Woolworths has lost share to Coles and Aldi over the past two years after keeping its grocery prices too high, failing to refurbish stores and cutting store labour, leading to out of stocks.

Mr Banducci suggested margins were unlikely to go much lower, because he was "happy with where we sit on price".

Woolworths' largest shareholder, Perpetual Investments, said the early results of the $1 billion investment into price and service were encouraging.

"It's moving in the right direction and that's taken the market by surprise - the market was expecting negative comps for the whole of 2017," said Perpetual's head of equities, Paul Skamvougeras.

He also welcomed the sale of Masters, saying Woolworths had achieved a better than expected outcome.

Mr Banducci ruled a line under Woolworths' disastrous $3.3 billion seven-year foray into home improvement on Wednesday, selling Home Timber & Hardware to Metcash, Masters stores and sites to a consortium of retail and property investors, and Masters' inventory to auction house GA Australia for $1.5 billion, or less than $500 million after wind-up costs and lease compensation.

"But it's too early to tell if the worst is over - they still need to execute on the strategy," Mr Skamvougeras said.

"It's very pleasing to see some good news coming out of Woolies, the first for a while," said Merrill Lynch analyst David Errington. "The (first) eight weeks is terrific but we want to see this continuing."

UBS analyst Ben Gilbert also welcomed the growth in same-store food sales, but said the overall result was poor and there was a risk 2017 market forecasts would be cut, while the balance sheet and Woolworths' investment grade credit rating remained a concern.

"Unequivocally it's been a challenging year for the group - in this case the numbers speak for themselves," Mr Banducci said. "We are making progress but there's still a lot to do.

"We're hoping to get a much more sustainable virtuous loop in 2017 - that gives us the ability to cut prices and also get some earnings growth."

Mr Banducci has made it clear his focus is on supermarkets and has cleared the way for divestments or demergers of non-food businesses.

Woolworths reported earnings from liquor for the first time, fuelling shareholder hopes it will eventually be sold or demerged.

Sales rose 4.7 per cent to $7.6 billion while EBIT rose 3 per cent to $483.8 million. Liquor boss Martin Smith plans to open 33 stores this year, including 10 Dan Murphy's.

Big W lost $14.9 million before one-off costs, compared with a profit of $111.7 million previously, but same-store sales rose in the June quarter for the first time since September 2013.
direction: Woolworths shares rise

headline: Could a rising Star create Kiwi options?
intro: With The Star Entertainment Group throwing off bundles of cash from its flagship Sydney casino The Star, the gambling giant is fast catching up on arch rival Crown Resorts.
text:
With The Star Entertainment Group throwing off bundles of cash from its flagship Sydney casino The Star, the gambling giant is fast catching up on arch rival Crown Resorts.

The Star has a chunky pipeline of projects to progress, with the addition of a new tower in Sydney, Brisbane's Queen's Wharf and the expansion of the Jupiters complex on the Gold Coast.

But with its capital commitments on those projects remaining reasonably modest, well-placed sources said The Star may consider looking across the Tasman with a bid for SkyCity Entertainment Group.

The business case for a tie-up would have certainly crossed the mind of chief executive Matt Bekier.

The Star could boost its domestic footprint with SkyCity's Adelaide and Darwin facilities. But the big prize would be gaining exposure to SkyCity's Auckland and Queenstown casino cash cows which are raking in revenues and luring prized high rollers from Asia.

SkyCity's $2.5 billion market capitalisation is just over half that of The Star's value, but Bekier could conceivably strike a deal. The Star's net debt-to-EBITDA is approaching a modest 1-times and the market expects it could generate $2 billion in cash from its existing operations between 2017 and 2020.

Importantly The Star now has access to two deep-pocketed Hong Kong investors who have partnered with it on all three of its existing casinos.

Street Talk understands these investors may be open to tipping in additional funds to the developments.One further wildcard in the pack is Malaysian gaming giant Genting, The Star's second-largest shareholder, which has approval to up its stake in the ASX-listed operator. With Genting on board, a deal to swallow SkyCity would make a hugely powerful competitor to Crown.

And finally, there is expected to be one fewer research analyst covering Star with Bank of America's gaming analyst Mark Bryan among a handful of job cuts at the bank on Tuesday.

As Street Talk revealed, sources said BAML had let at least four members of its equity research team go, including Bryan, banks analyst Nicole Mehalski, equities strategist Malcolm Wood and contractors and chemicals analyst Brent Walsh.
direction: bullish for The Star Entertainment Group

headline: International retailers 'seek 1800 shops'
text:
Demand from international retailers could absorb the entire new supply of retail space coming on to the market in the major CBD strips and malls over the next five years, according to a new report by CBRE.

Its latest Pacific Retail Report forecasts 1.6 million square metres of retail space - about 1800 stores - could be required by international retailers over this period.

"If this all comes to pass, international brand expansion could absorb the supply pipeline over the next five years," said CBRE's head of research for Australia, Stephen McNabb.

CBRE tracks 450 international retailers globally and said 90 of them wanted to roll out stores in Australia, with a preference for prime CBD and regional malls in Sydney and Melbourne.

The real estate group said a growing appetite among Australians to shop, strong population growth, low international brand penetration compared with other countries and rising numbers of cashed-up Chinese visitors coming to Australia made the country attractive to international retailers.

"With all the incoming international retailers the field is getting pretty crowded, but with good operators," billionaire retail property investor John Gandel told The Australian Financial Review recently.

Despite international brands having expanded rapidly into the major capital cities in recent years, led by the likes of Zara, H&M, Uniqlo, Sephora and Topshop, their penetration of the Australian market sits at just 28 per cent compared with more than 45 per cent in China, Singapore and Hong Kong.

Their arrival has pushed up rents in prime locations and driven out some domestic retailers, but they have also helped stimulate billions of dollars of investment in shopping centres, among them the $660 million expansion of Vicinity Centres and Gandel Group's Chadstone mall in Melbourne, the biggest in the country, and numerous major mall projects by Scentre Group, AMP Capital, QIC and others.

"We haven't yet seen the peak of international entrants," Bryan Hynes, managing director of AMP Capital Shopping Centres, told The Australian Financial Review earlier this year.

Mr Hynes said another one or two "significant waves of retailers" wanted to get into the country, because they knew Australia was somewhere they could grow market share.

CBRE's Victorian head of retail leasing, Zelman Ainsworth, said brands looking to expand in Australia included Zadig & Voltaire, Kooples, Paul & Shark, Sandro and REISS.

"Global luxury groups like LVMH and Kering are looking to bring more of their brands over here while carmakers like Porsche, Mercedes and BMW all want to open their smaller retail stores in Sydney and Melbourne," Mr Ainsworth said.

CBRE expects luxury brands to dominate new arrivals in Australia and New Zealand over the next two to three years, but it says this will then slow with mid-range fashion and specialist clothing brands more prominent.

"These retailers will have a more wide-ranging impact than the luxury brands, as they tend to focus only on CBD or prime regional centre locations," Mr McNabb said.

A strong preference for international brands, food and beverage and retail-tainment from the younger demographic was supporting this change, as was an increase in Chinese tourists.

Chinese tourist arrivals have tripled in Australia and quadrupled in New Zealand over the past decade.

"Chinese tourists not only spend more per visit, but have a higher appetite for goods purchase to take home" Mr McNabb said.

The field is getting pretty crowded, but with good operators. John Gandel, billionaire retail property investor
direction: bullish for retail property trusts and REITs

headline: Westpac's tech initiative milestone
text:
Westpac Banking Corporation's tech chief has said home buyers will be the first to benefit as it progresses on the country's biggest banking technology initiative, signing a key contract with US giant Oracle.

The bank's chief information officer Dave Curran told The Australian Financial Review a dramatic overhaul of its internal technology systems - first revealed in 2014 - was tackling processes related to home loans first as it seeks to demonstrate its improved customer service capabilities.

Oracle will provide the platform to underpin Westpac's so-called Customer Service Hub, which will sit on top of the myriad older IT systems from across all of its divisions and brands including St. George, BankSA, Bank of Melbourne and RAMS and provide bankers with a single view of all customer interactions.

"We know buying a home is one of the most significant purchases a person will make in their lifetime and choosing to focus on this area first will ensure our customers looking to take this important step, are given a best-in-class service experience," Mr Curran said.

Westpac's approach to upgrading its tech capabilities represents a notably different approach to technology modernisation than the high-profile multibillion core systems upgrade Mr Curran oversaw in his time at Commonwealth Bank of Australia, but is just as important to Westpac.

Rather than starting at the bottom layer of technology, like at CBA, where the core systems were all upgraded so that they could then be built on top of, Westpac has started in the middle so that older systems can work in a more modern fashion.

Ultimately it is intended to allow Westpac to deal efficiently and more personally with customers across the growing range of tech platforms used for banking.

"We have chosen a middle-out customer hub solution as we did not want to restrict ourselves to either a bottom up core system replacement or a new bank build Jetstar-type model," Mr Curran said.

"This was new and distinguished the Westpac strategy from others. This was not without risk, as Westpac's distinctive multi-brand strategy required fresh thinking and a new approach."

Westpac selected Oracle in May, but only publicly confirmed the deal on Monday. The company will provide platforms for customer and product originations, loans and deposits, a single customer master, and customer relationship management capabilities.

Financial terms of the contract were not revealed, but Mr Curran said it was a multi-year deal and significant to both Westpac and Oracle.

In addition to the scale of platforms a large amount of additional integration work will be required. As part of Westpac's program the Oracle platforms must work with all customer channels, product ledgers and data and analytics systems.

Mr Curran said Westpac had already completed a so-called "steel thread" in its development environments - which is effectively a proof of concept that demonstrates that its ideas should work in reality.

Westpac has proven internally that it can successfully link its Customer Service Hub with its customer channels at the front of its technology architecture and the product systems at the back.

"The success of the steel thread gives us confidence in this middle-out approach and its ability to underpin the bank's strategy," Mr Curran said.

"Our biggest challenge now is bringing this to life for our customers in our production environments and over the next two to three years this will be focused on home ownership."
direction: Westpac shares likely to rise

intro: Earl Evans head of wealth management and board member Shaw and Partners
text:
Earl Evans head of wealth management and board member Shaw and Partners

First, business or economy?

Domestically, I go economy as it's tough to justify the cost of a business-class seat for such a short flight. Internationally, business or first - it beats swimming.

Most frequented destination

North America.

If I ran my own airline I would ...

Endeavour to have the planes running to a strict timetable and ensure they are on time every time. It seems to be a global phenomenon in the transport industry to always run late.

The worst place you've been lost

Marrakesh - both in the airport and in the city. The whole place is a maze, which was compounded by a language barrier making it difficult to get around.

Most memorable overseas dining

One would have to be Le Jules Verne, halfway up the Eiffel Tower. The food was unbelievable, cooked by Michelin-starred chef Alain Ducasse. An iconic venue in the world's most beautiful city.

In the suitcase

Everything. I am a chronic over-packer.

I have to have choice, which means that my suitcase always tips the scales regardless of my luggage allowance. Safe to say, I always get halfway through my trip and wonder why I lugged so much from A to B, and yet I never learn.

How do you make the most of a spare afternoon in a strange city?

I am a meticulous planner, which means that it's likely that I will have pre-arranged my "spare afternoon" before my departure from Sydney. I think the best way to see and really explore a city is by foot, so I'm likely to walk to monuments, museums or local food hot spots. I will always double-check my route with the concierge before setting off - a local's opinion is always valuable.

Travel tips

Upgrade everything wherever possible, and you can never read enough reviews on hotels and restaurants before going. Lonely Planet and TripAdvisor are a traveller's bibles.

What technology do you use?

My iPad Pro is my lifeline when I travel and of course my iPhone. My latest gadget is a Garmin GPS sports watch, which I use when tracking my distance, time and, importantly, my whereabouts as I prepare for a 52-kilometre open ocean paddle for charity across the "Channel of Bones" in Hawaii.

Best overseas purchase

Without a doubt the rare, vintage Bell and Ross watch that I unearthed at a tiny vintage shop in Paris - a complete fluke.

Best travelling tale

A few years ago, I sat next to Eric Bana on a plane from Sydney to Los Angeles. We talked about our passion for cars all the way across the Pacific, then I gave him some (no doubt invaluable) acting tips.

I think he appreciated it.

Best thing about travelling

The experience of travelling changes as you do more of it. Regardless, I love being out of the comfort zone of my daily routine. It's really special to witness the wonders of the world in food, culture and traditions on this great planet.

Top travel gripe

Customs. Customs, a million times over.

I can't imagine anyone can ever be happy about queuing to get across the border. It drives me nuts. In fact, I learnt the customs lesson the hard way. I was at JFK International Airport in New York five years ago after a long flight, and I was agitated by a very aggressive customs officer. Deciding to take action, I reported him to his superior for his attitude. Before I knew it, I was escorted straight to an interrogation room, questioned, patted down and searched for 90 minutes before being released. Welcome to America. Lesson: Don't complain - just suck it up.

lifeandleisure@afr.com.au
direction: bearish for travel and leisure stocks

headline: China learns the price of partnership
intro: Ownership without control has a strong oxymoronic flavour. This has done little to prevent the phenomenon from proliferating in corporate finance.
text:
Ownership without control has a strong oxymoronic flavour. This has done little to prevent the phenomenon from proliferating in corporate finance.

Google and Facebook offer investors shares that confer no possibility of influence on management. Owners of this "equity" can point to its past appreciation and hope for more of the same. China's Midea Group cannot legitimately avail itself of this excuse. It wants to buy a big chunk of German industrial-automation company Kuka - without, it insists, compromising Kuka's complete independence or its public listing in its home country.

A tie-up between the two companies could have logic. Midea may benefit from fuller automation of its production, which churns out €19 billion ($29.5 billion) of appliances a year. Kuka could benefit from a big partner in a market where it targets €1 billion in sales by 2020, implying 25 per cent compound annual growth.

And the two could collaborate on non-industrial robots, hybrids of the two groups' core products.

Midea wants more than 30 per cent of Kuka, which under German law requires it make a full tender offer. The offer is €115 a share in cash, a 36 per cent premium that values the target at 45 times 2015 earnings. If all the shareholders go for the cash, the 87 per cent of Kuka that Midea does not own will cost €3.6 billion. A just-short-of-majority stake will run to €1.5 billion.

Midea has the money. It has a market value of €19 billion and its debt burden is light relative to profits. All the same, a partnership does not require an equity stake and a few billion euros can buy a lot of robots. Perhaps Midea's leaders think Kuka's shares are a good buy, but it is an appliance company, not an investment fund. Another justification is that partial ownership creates alignment of interests. Midea has bought equity stakes in connection with other partnerships and is presumably pleased with the results.

For Midea, this notion may make some sense, given the modest sum. For Kuka, considering the size of the opportunity in robotics and importance of the Chinese market, a few billion euros of equity is modest if it is giving up autonomy. Unconflicted control of its strategy and intellectual property is Kuka's most precious asset. If Midea wants alignment, it should buy the whole company.
direction: bullish for Midea and Kuka shares

headline: Targets restricted by 'frustrating action' in takeover bids
text:
When a hostile takeover bid arrives on the doorstep, directors of the target company certainly earn their keep.

With the whole world watching, and with the clock ticking, they need to formulate a response, keep the market continuously informed, and keep the target's business running. They know that any false step is likely to result in the bidder hauling them before the Takeovers Panel and the court of public (or, at least, market) opinion.

They may have all sorts of other strategic opportunities before them, in various stages of development. There may be commercial and strategic proposals that they want to implement. They might think that the bid significantly undervalues the target, and that there are many better options available to create shareholder value. If it weren't for the bid, they'd act upon some or all of these other options. Indeed, to do otherwise would breach their fiduciary duties.

But once a takeover bid is announced, things change. In particular, the Takeovers Panel's "frustrating action" policy kicks in.

"Frustrating action" is a policy that can have a huge effect on target companies during a hostile bid. In some respects, the policy puts target companies into effective caretaker mode for the duration of the bid. During this period, significant transactions and initiatives - no matter how attractive or value-accretive - cannot be implemented by the target company without express approval from shareholders, if they might thwart the unwelcome bid by breaching the conditions that the bidder has elected to include.

The Takeovers Panel is now seeking comments on some proposed amendments to this policy. The aim of the amendments is to give target boards more certainty as to what they can and can't do when a takeover offer has been announced.

The purpose of the panel's policy is to constrain unreasonable spoiling actions by target boards, hence ensuring that target shareholders have the final say on whether a takeover bid succeeds. However, there are situations where target boards worry that their hands are excessively tied when seeking to manage the company through a bid. This issue has come to the fore as more and more bids have been accompanied by extensive and restrictive lists of conditions.

In these situations, targets have worried that they can't pursue a wide array of value-accretive actions - often for a lengthy period of time - and are relatively powerless to develop alternative strategies to the takeover bid. In practice, it is also very difficult to obtain shareholder approval for a potential alternative transaction - counter-parties are generally unwilling to agree to a transaction when the company subject to the bid must seek shareholder approval, effectively giving the company an option to withdraw.

Additionally, the fact that bidders' lengthy lists of conditions are almost certain to be breached at some point can give bidders an effective "free exit option" to walk away, if they decide later that they don't want to proceed. But, at the same time, the target remains shackled by the frustrating action policy.

To address these issues, the panel has proposed changes to its policy to emphasise that the frustrating action policy will have teeth only where the bid provides a "genuine opportunity" for shareholders to dispose of their shares. In other words, if there is lack of funding certainty, or the bid is demonstrably dead in the water, then target boards don't have to worry about the policy. Additionally, the policy won't apply where the bidder proposes overly restrictive conditions, or where another condition has been breached but the bidder has not confirmed within a reasonable time whether it will waive the breach (the "free exit option" conundrum).

While many of these principles are already mentioned in the policy, they are framed (in fairly soft language) as the types of things the panel would generally be minded to include in its considerations. They don't provide a clear course guide for target boards on when the policy applies and what the permissible courses of action available to them are. Hence target boards often have trouble getting comfortable that a proposed strategy does not fall foul of the policy, especially when an aggressive hostile bidder is breathing down their neck.

By proposing to put these matters in a more central position in the policy, and by framing them in a more definitive manner, boards should have considerably greater comfort when responding to opportunistic hostile bids. Fingers crossed the panel chooses to adopt the changes.

Simon Haddy is a partner at law firm Herbert Smith Freehills.
direction: bearish for target company shares

headline: Ainsworth cashes out $473m pokies stake
intro: Billionaire pokies king Len Ainsworth will donate "a large chunk" of a $473 million windfall to charity, after he sold his 53 per cent stake in the company he founded, Ainsworth Game Technology, to Austrian gaming company Novomatic.
text:
Billionaire pokies king Len Ainsworth will donate "a large chunk" of a $473 million windfall to charity, after he sold his 53 per cent stake in the company he founded, Ainsworth Game Technology, to Austrian gaming company Novomatic.

The 92-year-old gaming veteran will retain his role as chairman, with Novomatic saying it expects to keep its stake in the company at about 53 per cent and retain Ainsworth's ASX listing.

Mr Ainsworth said he expected to build on his previous philanthropic work by handing some of the money to health research, while also sharing part of the windfall with his family.

"I take the view I've been lucky in life and I should share my luck with my family," Mr Ainsworth said on Tuesday. "I think medicine and universities would be the target. If you can find a cure to something, that is a big prize in itself."

Mr Ainsworth has given away more than $30 million to medical research, including a donation to the redevelopment of Sydney's St Vincent's Private Hospital.

He said the move was partly inspired by his wish the company that bears his name will still be "alive and kicking" in 40 years.

"It gives all the staff a guarantee the company will be there for the future," he said. "I'm ambitious, of course, but I probably have 10 years rather than 40 years left on this planet."

The pokies entrepreneur's family wealth was estimated at $1.82 billion in the BRW 2015 Rich List.

In the 1990s he handed his family a lucrative stake in Aristocrat Leisure, which Mr Ainsworth founded, on discovering he had been diagnosed with prostate cancer.

The diagnosis famously proved false and Mr Ainsworth went on to create his second pokies empire, Ainsworth Game Technology, which competes with Aristocrat for pokie machine sales. .

Mr Ainsworth sold 172.1 million shares at $2.75 a share, netting the entrepreneur more than $470 million. The company's shares closed nearly 4 per cent higher at $2.12 on Tuesday.

Ainsworth Game Technology said it supported the deal and would convene an emergency meeting for shareholders to vote on the transaction.

Watermark Funds Management, a shareholder in Ainsworth, said it was suspicious of Novomatic's motives in holding a controlling stake without launching a takeover.

"It looks suspicious that Novomatic want to control Ainsworth but not own 100 per cent," Watermark investment analyst Joshua Ross said.

' Mr Ross said Novomatic would gain from access to the US but the prospect of a takeover from a third party was diminished.

The share sale was disclosed in Ainsworth's results, with its profit after tax in the first half of 2016 falling 4 per cent to $33.1 million, although stripping out currency gains the company boosted its net profit 7 per cent to $26.1 million.
direction: Ainsworth Game Technology shares

headline: Hutchinson to build air terminal
intro: Paul Little has appointed Hutchinson Builders for the construction of the VIP air terminal his development company is planning for Melbourne airport.
text:
Paul Little has appointed Hutchinson Builders for the construction of the VIP air terminal his development company is planning for Melbourne airport.

While work on the 19.5-hectare site cannot begin until the Little Group, which is also about to start a new ferry service in Port Phillip Bay, completes the regulatory processes needed to win so-called Major Development Plan approval, it is a welcome sign that construction, which successfully switched from resources work to housing, is now making its next transition to infrastructure.

For Hutchinson, which made half of its near $1.2 billion-worth of revenue last year from residential work, the contract, along with a separate win earlier this year of a multi-stage project with La Trobe University in Bendigo, shows that transition is happening.

"It's a turning point in the market," said Ben McArthur, one of Hutchinson's Victorian team leaders. "The resi stuff is starting to turn down and infrastructure projects like this - we're seeing more and more come across our desk. That stuff that's starting, whether it's private or government funded, is the university work or large infrastructure jobs like this."

Like many infrastructure projects, however, the so-called fixed base operator project is taking longer and will cost more than originally expected.

The cost of the development that will offer services to private jet clients such as hangarage, refuelling and pilot accommodation has doubled to $60 million since Little Group first talked about it publicly in 2014.

Work will begin by September this year in the best-case scenario, with operations beginning by the first quarter of 2018.
direction: bearish for Hutchinson Builders due to cost overruns and residential sector weakness

headline: St George claims top green award
intro: St George Bank, a division of financial conglomerate Westpac Group, has been awarded the first 6 Green Star award for the fitting out of a retail bank branch.
text:
St George Bank, a division of financial conglomerate Westpac Group, has been awarded the first 6 Green Star award for the fitting out of a retail bank branch.

The sustainability rating, which was undertaken with the Green Building Council of Australia, is intended to acknowledge building, material and design standards.

It is the only bank branch of 166 buildings with the rating. The others are offices or distribution centres.

Mirvac Group also recently received the award for Sirius, an office in Canberra.

Commonwealth Bank of Australia, the nation's biggest bank, has devised a strategy for attaining the standards but is yet to complete the building programme.

The St George Barangaroo branch incorporates building materials with reduced environmental impact and aims to reduce water and energy consumption. The award also encompasses operational practices it claims are environmentally efficient.

The bank says the measures can cut costs, boost productivity and help retain employees.

"Using resources efficiently helps create healthier environments for people to live and work," a council spokesman said.

The objective is to encourage sustainability by using designs and operations that are environmentally friendly into mainstream design, construction and operation.

St George retail general manager Ross Miller said: "Sustainability is something our people and customers really care about and contributes to better outcomes for the environment."

Institutional investors are increasingly under pressure to scrutinise listed companies on whether they support environmentally sustainable policies, such as reducing pollution and making buildings more sustainable. They routinely grill companies about their environmental policies on behalf of investors.

The Green Building Council of Australia was established about 13 years ago to develop a sustainable property industry and drive green building practices.

Westpac has previously been awarded the world's most sustainable bank in the 2015 Dow Jones Sustainability Index. The group's head office in Kent Street, Sydney, was awarded a 6 Green Star rating.

Council chief executive Romilly Madew said : "This sends a signal to the financial services sector that sustainability is a strategic business decision. It can cut costs, boost productivity [and] attract and retain employees."
direction: supportive for Westpac Group shares

headline: Banks sack few for misconduct
text:
Two out of the 150 cases reported by Commonwealth Bank of Australia staff to its internal whistleblower hotline in the past year led to bankers losing their jobs.

Nine resulted in formal warnings and 115 resulted in no formal discipline, according to figures provided by CBA to the parliamentary committee scrutinising bank conduct.

Hundreds of pages of responses to questions on notice were published by the House of Representatives Standing Committee on Economics on Thursday.

They provide details from each of the four big banks on the number of staff investigated and disciplined for breaches following the scandals that led to a political blowtorch being applied to the banking sector this year.

CBA said that of the complaints to its internal "SpeakUP Hotline" that resulted in no action, 55 of the matters involved allegations being disproved or not substantiated, 36 led to some other informal action being taken and 24 were referred somewhere else in the bank.

Westpac Banking Corp said in its responses to the committee that since October last year, its group investigations team had received and investigated 234 cases involving 286 people alleged to have breached the bank's code of conduct, including matters involving fraud, theft and bribery.

Of these cases, 63 led to termination of employment, 27 resulted in a resignation, 68 led to a warning or counselling, 47 matters were referred back to the business for training, and in 81 cases no further action was taken. But "of those dismissed, none were at the senior manager or executive level", Westpac said.

After National Australia Bank chief executive Andrew Thorburn came under pressure at the hearing during the first week of October to explain the conduct of 43 NAB wealth planners who had been sacked for wrongdoing, NAB has told the committee "no systemic issue warranting the dismissal of senior executives in respect of misconduct or serious breaches of the advisers was identified".

"As such, no senior executive at NAB was dismissed in direct relation to this issue," it said, adding that the advice business "has been restructured and the executive team has significantly changed since 2014".

ANZ Banking Group said in its responses that after refunding 390,000 accounts due to unclear fee disclosures, the bank "does not believe any current staff members are directly responsible for the misalignment" given the issue existed for a number of years.

ANZ also confirmed after 1.3 million of its OnePath customers were affected by various breaches, including unit pricing errors and not providing promised discounts, which led to approximately $4.5 million being paid to affected clients, the bank had "identified the need for enhanced capability in some areas and made some changes to management", but it did not point to any senior managers being dismissed.

The bank responses are likely to provide an impetus for the committee to push ahead with recommendations that could make senior bankers more accountable for the misconduct of their subordinates.

The CBA's whistleblower data comes a day after the bank received a historic first strike on its remuneration report from investors concerned the bonuses of senior CBA managers were too often paid out at the full rate and not reduced to reflect the bank's recent conduct scandals.
direction: Downward pressure on major bank shares

headline: Coca-Cola boss given share incentive
intro: Proxy advisers have given a tentative thumbs-up to Coca-Cola Amatil's plan to hand over at least $1.7 million in bonuses to managing director Alison Watkins if she manages to restore earnings per share growth to a modest 5 per cent.
text:
Proxy advisers have given a tentative thumbs-up to Coca-Cola Amatil's plan to hand over at least $1.7 million in bonuses to managing director Alison Watkins if she manages to restore earnings per share growth to a modest 5 per cent.

Coca-Cola Amatil wants the annual meeting in May to approve the issue of between 195,312 and 390,624 share rights worth $1.7 million to $3.5 million to Ms Watkins as part of the beverage company's 2016 to 2018 long-term incentive plan.

CCA plans to issue the share rights despite conceding in its latest annual report that the outcome of long-term incentive grants over the past five years had been "disappointing".

Since 2011, none of the share rights issued as part of long-term incentive plans vested because total shareholder returns and average earnings per share growth fell well short of targets.

Average earnings per share between 2012 and 2014 fell 10.3 per cent, compared with a 7 per cent EPS growth target, and total shareholder returns fell 13.2 per cent, ranking CCA in the bottom 20 per cent of its peers.

Ms Watkins, who took the helm from Terry Davis in March 2014, has vowed to restore earnings per share growth to mid-single digits over the next few years.

Ms Watkins will need to deliver average annual earnings per share growth of 5 per cent, total shareholder returns (TSR) of 8 per cent and outperform about 50 per cent of peer companies (relative TSR) to achieve 100 per cent of her target award of 65,104 share rights each year.

If she manages to lift earnings per share growth to more than 8 per cent, TSR to more than 12 per cent and beat 75 per cent of peers, she stands to receive 200 per cent of the target issue, or 130,208 shares a year.

While the performance thresholds are much lower than those during Mr Davis's 13-year regime, shareholders and proxy advisers said that, at first glance, they looked reasonable given structural challenges in the non-alcoholic beverages market.

"If you look at [EPS growth] in isolation and compare it with other companies that are growing quickly it does look low," said CGI Glass Lewis general manager Daniel Smith.

"But if you go back in time, CCA hasn't hit 5 per cent [EPS growth] in one year since 2010. We think that 5 to 8 per cent CAGR would be pretty decent growth given the maturity of the markets in which CCA operates," he said.

After a 33 per cent slump in profits during the previous two years, CCA's underlying net profit rose 4.8 per cent to $393.4 million in 2015 and earnings per share by 4.7 per cent.

Ms Watkins' total remuneration rose from $2.66 million to $4.5 million in 2015, boosted by $1.5 million in short-term bonuses, $694,518 in long-term incentives and a full 12 months' base pay of $2.26 million.

CCA is also seeking approval to increase the annual fee pool for non-executive directors by $500,000 to $2.8 million. The bottler has reached its fee cap after lifting the number of directors from eight to nine.

Long-serving non-executive directors Catherine Brenner and Tony Froggatt are seeking re-election, as is Virgin Australia chief John Borghetti, who joined the board last December.

Mr Borghetti is in line to eventually take the chairman's role from David Gonski, who is not expected to seek re-election when his current term expires.

Key points

Alison Watkins will need to find annual earnings per share growth of 5 per cent.

No incentive share rights have been vested since 2011.
direction: bearish for Coca-Cola Amatil shares

headline: Carnegie urges business to plan for the long term
intro: In a call to arms to get Australia's innovation engine firing, ANZ Banking Group's head of digital banking, Maile Carnegie, said companies across the economy need to adopt longer-term thinking and stop waiting for government to solve their problems.
text:
In a call to arms to get Australia's innovation engine firing, ANZ Banking Group's head of digital banking, Maile Carnegie, said companies across the economy need to adopt longer-term thinking and stop waiting for government to solve their problems.

Various policies set out in last year's innovation statement and the May budget had set Australia on the right path for lifting innovation, Ms Carnegie told the AFR Innovation Summit.

These included improving infrastructure by encouraging more research collaboration and providing access to risk capital; capability measures such as focusing on science and mathematics education; and fostering a culture of risk-taking.

"The program the government is working through is going after what is the very well understood formula [for improving innovation] - on that side of things I am incredibly optimistic," she said. "I actually think the bigger issue is making sure industry is also coming to the table."

Ms Carnegie, who before joining ANZ spent three years as managing director of Google in Australia, said she believed too many companies thought too much about the short term, waited for government to provide some magic bullet, and delayed innovative change until it was too late.

"If I look at the conversations we are having with many of our businesses, the short-term nature of delivering results is something we are going to have to tackle," she said. "I would love it if the national conversation moved from trying to find what the [innovation] engine needs to look like and actually starts to talk about why aren't we doing it."

Ms Carnegie is a board member of Innovation and Science Australia, chaired by Bill Ferris, and includes chief scientist Alan Finkel and technology leaders such as Paul Bassat, Scott Farquhar and Daniel Petre.

She said the board's most important work was to establish a strategic innovation plan for the next 15 years. The board is auditing the innovation landscape and may need to make choices about where Australia's comparative advantages lie before "recommending back to government where we need to make resource investments".

The definition of strategic innovation priorities by Singapore 20 years ago and the UK a decade ago had helped both countries climb the Global Innovation Index, she said.
direction: bullish for innovation-focused and technology shares

headline: Banks ease foreign bond investor fears
intro: The Australian banks have moved to reassure foreign bond investors they won't be getting a bill in the mail after interest rate payments went below zero on some of their $40 billion of outstanding negatively yielding bonds.
text:
The Australian banks have moved to reassure foreign bond investors they won't be getting a bill in the mail after interest rate payments went below zero on some of their $40 billion of outstanding negatively yielding bonds.

The decline in the European benchmark interest rates to well below zero has meant that certain lines of short-term floating bonds, totalling an estimated $10 billion, are now set to have negative coupons. If this happens, foreign investors will be paying the big banks to perform their $20 billion annual fund-raising tasks.

One investor said he was contacted as a courtesy after floating rate yields on more euro-denominated bonds dipped into negative territory to assure him that no investor would be required to pay the bank.

The negative rates on floating rate bonds is understood to impact about $10 billion of outstanding floating rate euro bonds issued mainly by ANZ Banking Group and Westpac.

The fall-out from The European Central Bank's new plan to purchase corporate bonds is acting to swell demand for corporate debt pushing down risk premiums and base interest rates to levels once considered an impossibility.

In recent weeks the floating benchmark rates in Europe have fallen so far into negative territory that even after investors charge their credit premium to lend to the Australian banks, the overall rate is still below zero.

While bonds have been negative for some time, the negative rate has typically been reflected in the price of the bond, rather than price.

For instance, an investor might pay $101 for a bond and only receive $100 upon maturity, therefore implying a negative 1 per cent return.

But now the phenomenon is occurring in floating rate bonds, where the actual coupon is a negative one. The equivalent of $10 billion of euro-denominated, Australian bank-issued, floating-rate bonds are referenced to the three-month Euribor rate, paying a predetermined spread above this rate. Swiss franc bonds, too, have been impacted.

For instance, a €600 million three-year Westpac New Zealand bond, maturing in March 2017 pays 40 basis points over the three-month Euribor rate. When that Euribor rate declines below 40 basis points, the coupon will turn negative.

An ANZ bond maturing in December 2016 that pays 47 basis points over the three-month Euribor rate, will also pay a negative rate if that interest rate declines below the credit spread.

In theory, the banks should be receiving a payment from investors. But it is understood the banks have reassured investors that the terms of the bond documents only allow them to pay interest, effectively capping the rate at zero - to the relief of investors that feared they would be writing cheques to the Australian banks.

It should also be pointed out that the funding is not entirely free for the Australian banks that have hedged their currency risk from these bond issues via derivatives known as cross-currency basis swaps.

These swaps are typically expensive for borrowers seeking to hedge euro currency risk, the costs between 40 and 70 basis points for funding between one and five years.

Still, negative interest rates have confounded the bond market with most systems set up to pay interest to investors, not to receive it.

As trillions of bonds move below zero on the back of aggressive central bank action, bond funds are grappling with the challenge of accepting a loss on their investment.

ANZ is understood to have the largest amount of outstanding bonds that pay a negative floating interest rate, at $6.2 billion accounting for most of the $9.3 billion of outstanding euro-denominated bonds that currently have negative yields.

ANZ also has $1.7 billion of fixed-rate Swiss franc bonds with negative interest rates. Westpac has around $10.2 billion of bonds with negative interest rate bonds of which $2.4 billion are floating rate securities.

Commonwealth Bank has about $11 billion of debt trading at negative interest rates, largely fixed-rate covered bonds issued in Swiss francs and euros.

National Australia Bank has about $8 billion of bonds with negative interest rates, largely in the form of fixed-rate euro bonds. The data was sourced from Thomson Reuters Eikon.

A NAB spokesman said it capped bond payments at zero.
direction: supportive for ANZ and Westpac bank shares

headline: Rear Window
intro: Magellan sell-down made Turnbull a poor man
text:
Magellan sell-down made Turnbull a poor man

To cope with the induced apathy of human history's most boring election campaign, we went digging through some of the Prime Minister's old investments. And we're glad we did! Here's one that might shake your faith in his superior economic management:

In September 2006, during Malcolm Turnbull's first term in Parliament, while cooling his heels as John Howard's Parliamentary Secretary for Water, the member for Wentworth's family investment vehicle Turnbull & Partners dumped its 20 per cent stake in Pengana Hedgefunds Group faster than the Murray-Darling basin could develop bad cholesterol.

Turnbull had set up Pengana in 2003, which was winding up when former UBS boss Chris Mackay and former Deutsche Banker Hamish Douglass swooped in with a pile of money from the likes of PBL's James Packer and Sussan's Naomi Milgrom. The Herald's headline: "Mates chip in $80m to rescue Turnbulls."

In that month, the Turnbulls waved goodbye to six million PGN shares at an average price of $1.56 per unit, netting themselves $9.4 million. Which sounds like decent loot, right?

Wrong! Check the ASX boards: barely 10 seconds after the Turnbulls walked, the stock took off like a space shuttle from Cape Canaveral and today, Magellan shares closed at $23.92, so the Crown Prince of Point Piper's six million shares would trade at $143.52 million, not hugely south of what BRW (very conservatively) reckons he's now worth.

Milgrom remains a major on Magellan's registry, while Packer got out 18 months ago. Former Citi banker Brett Paton has 2.8 million shares (circa $64 million) while UBS's former bank analyst Jeffrey Emmanuel speaks for 2 million ($46 million).

Why hasn't this become a major election issue? It goes to character Your Honour! See, while Labor tries to paint the PM as a silvertail, the Liberals could retaliate with incontrovertible evidence that the Turnbulls are substantially poorer than they could've been; that if not for an overcautious equities strategy (at the height of the sub-prime credit boom, when their neighbours were leveraging everything but their undarned socks), they'd be twice as rich, and no longer battling to meet their monthly mooring payments to the Foreshore Authority.

We tried this line on Turnbull's campaign chief Tony Nutt and research guru Mark Textor, but strangely they both seem to have changed their numbers. At the same time! What are the chances?
direction: bearish for Magellan Global Fund shares

text:
Contents

World	9

Leadership	12

Companies&Markets	13

Street Talk	14

Financial Services	16

Financial Times	19

Markets	20

Tables	23

Marketing&Media	29

Property	31

Letters/Rear Window	35

Features/Sports Biz	36-37

Editorial&Opinion	38

Adele Ferguson	40
direction: downward pressure on rate-sensitive sectors like banks and property trusts

headline: FIRB, doctors are kingmakers in the GenesisCare sale
intro: The fight for cancer and cardiac services provider GenesisCare won't be just about who has the biggest wallet - it will also be decided by who can win over the hearts and minds.
text:
The fight for cancer and cardiac services provider GenesisCare won't be just about who has the biggest wallet - it will also be decided by who can win over the hearts and minds.

The most important organs belong to the Foreign Investment Review Board, and the four bidders - Ping An, one of China's largest financial institutions; Macquarie Group-backed China Resources; and to a lesser extent private equity rivals Bain Capital and The Carlyle Group - are aware that this could be a major risk. But FIRB isn't the only group that needs winning over.

Bidders are also cognisant that the doctors who are selling a portion of their shares in GenesisCare (alongside KKR's stake) to fund its rapid expansion need to be considered in the sale process. These aren't ordinary shareholders, but highly paid, influential experts who may not want to work for one bidder or another.

Sorting out who is the favourite to win this race isn't easy.

Carlyle is certainly seen as having plenty of experience. It of course had success growing and floating Healthscope, and has a global healthcare team working on the deal alongside new Australian head David Bluff.

But perhaps more importantly it has had international success in GenesisCare's very specialised field, in that it owns radiation therapy assets around the world, including 9.9 per cent of Concord Medical, a radiotherapy player in China that is listed on the NYSE, and 8 per cent of Rede D'Or São Luiz, the largest hospital player in Brazil which operates 30 oncology/cancer care clinics.

Bain knows the asset well. Street Talk can reveal GenesisCare and Bain held talks last year which were reasonably well progressed, however, they couldn't agree on price.

But the team of China Resources and Macquarie is still seen as the ones to beat.

While China Resources has been dogged by controversy - its former chairman faced corruption allegations a year ago - it is now sorting out another, more palatable, controversy surrounding property developer Vanke. China Resources is opposing a coup that threatens Vanke's board.

Bids are due on July 25.
direction: GenesisCare shares face uncertainty and potential downside due to regulatory and stakeholder risks

headline: Mint forges deal with Singapore firm
intro: Mint Payments managing director Alex Teoh says a deal with Singapore's debit card network to roll out the company's mobile payment terminals is a "landmark deal" that should boost its chances of expanding into other Asian countries.
text:
Mint Payments managing director Alex Teoh says a deal with Singapore's debit card network to roll out the company's mobile payment terminals is a "landmark deal" that should boost its chances of expanding into other Asian countries.

Mr Teoh said it was one of the biggest deals for the mobile payments company, equivalent to its contract with Bank of New Zealand signed in 2013.

Under the five-year deal, Singapore's Network for Electronic Transfers (NETS), which is owned by Singapore's biggest banks, DBS Bank, Oversea-Chinese Banking Corporation and United Overseas Bank, gets a licence to distribute Mint's mobile payment terminals under the NETS brand and will be the "acquirer" or processor for Mint in Singapore.

The contract with NETS will also allow Mint to sell its terminals and online payments gateway to all financial institutions and merchants in Singapore.

News of the deal had leaked by last Wednesday and Mint shares jumped almost 20 per cent to 11¢ before the ASX queried the price rise and the stock was put into a trading halt from Thursday until Tuesday morning. The stock rose a further 11.5¢ on Tuesday before falling back a similar amount on Wednesday.

Last month, Mint announced its first deal in Asia with Asian Business Software Solutions, the Asian arm of MYOB. It signed a three-year deal to distribute Mint online invoice payments with ABSS software.

First listed in 2007, Mint's share price skyrocketed from just 2¢ to more than 40¢ in late 2013 when it announced its first big contracts with accounting software company MYOB and Bank of New Zealand.

During the following six months, however, it lost almost all the gains when there were no further deals forthcoming, and was queried by the ASX when its stock price halved to 7.9¢ in the two weeks to October 16, 2014.

After a trading halt, the company announced its CEO of six months, Robin Khuda, had been sacked and executive chairman Alex Teoh became CEO again.

NETS, established in 1985, is the biggest card payments network. It processed about $S23 billion ($22.6 billion) in transactions in 2015.
direction: bullish for Mint Payments shares

headline: Mayne Pharma shares slump on US pricing probe
text:
Reports that US prosecutors could soon charge generic drug makers on suspected price collusion have caused shares in Mayne Pharma to lose almost $400 million in market value.

The Adelaide-based company was swept up in a major sell-off of pharmaceutical firms sparked by a Bloomberg report that an antitrust investigation by the US Justice Department examining whether executives agreed to raise prices could deliver charges by the end of the year.

Overnight shares in US-listed drug giants named in the investigation, such as Mylan, Teva Pharmaceuticals and Lannett, all fell with the latter slumping 27 per cent.

Mayne Pharma shares fell 15.5 per cent to $1.44 on Friday, down from a 12-month high of $2.10 hit in August. The stock has returned to levels hit in June before the market got swept up in the promise of a transformative $888 million acquisition of generic drugs from Teva.

At the time of the deal Mayne disclosed that it was one of several generic companies to receive a subpoena from the authorities seeking information relating to the marketing, pricing and sales of select generic products.

The company said on Friday that it continued to co-operate with the investigation. "Mayne Pharma continues to believe these investigations will not have a material impact on its future earnings," the company said in a statement. "However, no assurance can be given as to the timing or outcome of the investigation."

Mayne said that in its case the investigation related to generic delayed-release doxycycline tablets known as Doryx, which can be used to treat a range of bacterial infections from acne to urinary tract infections, and potassium chloride powders, to treat low potassium levels.

The company began selling its doxycycline products in 2013 and potassium chloride powders last year, although it has since abandoned the latter product. A source close to the company said the combined revenue from those products in that whole time would be about $US11 million ($14.3 million).

The company had revenue of $267.3 million in 2015-16, which was up 89 per cent on the prior year. The US accounts for 87 per cent of sales.

UBS analyst Andrew Goodsall told clients that prices of immediate release doxycycline products increased materially prior to 2015, but Mayne does not manufacture this product. Similar patterns are not apparent in the delayed release market, he said.

"However, we expect that until there is clarity on any potential charges or outcome, the DOJ probe will overshadow the stock into the near future."

The investigation has been running for two years and has dragged over a dozen companies into its net.
direction: Mayne Pharma shares decline

headline: Reason for Coalition to be alarmed
intro: The Fairfax Ipsos poll has confirmed other published opinion polls in putting the major parties neck and neck on a two-party preferred basis. It shows the Coalition's primary vote as being four points lower than it was at the last election, with no hope in sight of a boost from a conservative minor party vote.
text:
The Fairfax Ipsos poll has confirmed other published opinion polls in putting the major parties neck and neck on a two-party preferred basis. It shows the Coalition's primary vote as being four points lower than it was at the last election, with no hope in sight of a boost from a conservative minor party vote.

The disappointment in Malcolm Turnbull, who has not been able to deliver on the hyper-expectations of voters, is there for all to see in both his own approval numbers and the party polling. But we now move into particularly uncharted waters for both sides of politics in terms of expectations.

The private polling for the major parties has tended to suggest, until now at least, that the public polls are overstating the deterioration in the government's standing and the improvement as a result in Labor's fortunes.

While the public polls put the parties at 50/50, the private polling suggested it was still around 52 to 48 in the government's favour.

All these figures become irrelevant, however, when we are working in this strange environment of unrealistic expectations about what a change of political leadership can do, and when - facing an election campaign both official and unofficial - of unprecedented length, voters' expectations about the results become a powerful factor in themselves.

The best thing going for Bill Shorten could well be that no one thinks he will actually win. The prospect of an "accidental" result in such circumstances is high: voters who think they can give the government a kick without a change is a factor that cannot be underestimated in an age when voters are not as connected to parties as they once were and when so many voters don't bother turning up, risking a fine if they don't.

We seem to be at the point now where the polls are telling us that, if their results were replicated on polling day, we would have at least a hung parliament or a Labor majority.

But whether it is in the political commentary or in the pragmatic assessments of markets and lobbyists, the presumption still seems to be that the government will be returned. Labor's policy positions are assessed in terms of how they may influence the government's positions, rather than how they might work if they were implemented.

The next threshold - of seriously considering that Labor may be in government in July - hasn't quite been reached. But if you were the Coalition, you might have reason to be alarmed that it is not that large a step to take as it embarks on the last couple of weeks of sittings of Parliament.
direction: uncertain political environment weighing on market confidence
