headline: Hopes for income tax cuts fade as wages growth slows
intro: New figures showing wage growth has eased to its slowest annual pace in almost two decades have further dimmed the prospects of a meaningful personal income tax cut at this year's federal budget.
text:
New figures showing wage growth has eased to its slowest annual pace in almost two decades have further dimmed the prospects of a meaningful personal income tax cut at this year's federal budget.

A week after Finance Minister Mathias Cormann downplayed the significance of bracket creep by pointing to the low wages growth of 2.3 per cent for the year to September, the December figures show it has slowed further to 2.2 per cent.

This was the slowest annual pace since the current index series was launched 18 years ago, as company cost-cutting, low inflation and last year's spike in unemployment keep a lid on demands for better pay.

Bracket creep is when a worker moves into a higher tax bracket as their income increases. For a year, the government has been citing it as a job killer and threat to growth as it prepares for a tax cut policy to take to the election.

But after it dumped plans to increase the rate and base of the GST and focus on smaller gains from changing superannuation tax concessions and trading away tax deductions, there is little revenue to pay for tax cuts, and the government has started downplaying expectations. Treasurer Scott Morrison said the cuts would be "modest" and Senator Cormann, echoing the long-stated views of economists, downplayed the urgency of bracket creep because of low wages growth.

The Australian Bureau of Statistics said on Wednesday that the seasonally adjusted wage price index rose a mere 0.5 per cent in the December quarter last year, to reach an annual rate of 2.2 per cent. This is the weakest rate of growth since the first full year of the current wage price index in 1998, and came in slightly below expectations.

The latest data means wages growth is only just staying ahead of the official consumer price index, with headline inflation last measured at 1.7 per cent and the trimmed mean, or core inflation, at 2.1 per cent. "Over the last year, private sector wages grew at a slower pace than the public sector: 2 per cent compared to 2.6 per cent, seasonally adjusted," the ABS said.

"In mining, electricity, gas, water and waste services; construction; and administrative and support services, wages grew over the last year at the lowest rate since the commencement of the series," it said. By state, wage growth was stronger in NSW and Victoria than in Western Australia, which has borne the brunt of the resources downturn. Wages there grew only 1.8 per cent year-on-year, compared with 2.1 per cent in NSW and 2.4 per cent in Victoria.

Growth rates also varied between employment sectors. Employees in financial and insurance services were best off, at 2.7 per cent, and those in administrative and support services fared the worst, with 1.4 per cent.
direction: downward pressure on consumer-discretionary and retail shares

headline: Foxtel has lower fee message for Disney, NBCUniversal
intro: Foxtel chief executive Richard Freudenstein has indicated that the cable and satellite TV monopoly will pay less in fees to Hollywood studios if they also offer their content direct to consumers.
text:
Foxtel chief executive Richard Freudenstein has indicated that the cable and satellite TV monopoly will pay less in fees to Hollywood studios if they also offer their content direct to consumers.

In an interview with Fairfax Media, Mr Freudenstein said Foxtel would have to make a "trade-off" with studios which supply it films and television programs but then also offer the same content direct to consumers via their own subscription video-on-demand (SVOD) services.

Moves by NBCUniversal and Disney to offer dedicated, cheap subscription video-on-demand services in Australia have added to the threat Foxtel's premium cable and satellite business already faces from generalist SVOD players led by Netflix.

But Mr Freudenstein predicted consumers will become confused by multiple offers and will stick with Foxtel as a one-stop shop for their video entertainment.

"I think you will find a lot of these people go over the top and try to go direct to the customer," said Mr Freudenstein. "I am very confident that the value we offer by aggregating a lot of these players is compelling and I worry a lot of these offers will confuse customers and make it too hard for them."

He added: "We work very closely with NBCUniversal and will continue to do so. Disney has no intention of taking any content away from Foxtel; they think Foxtel is a very key partner for them."

Mr Freudenstein added, however, that Foxtel wants to pay the appropriate amount for content, which could change if it is distributed elsewhere.

"It's a trade-off," he said. "When we look at our content partners, we look at what's the right amount to pay them based on the quality of their product and where they distribute it.

"We'll work out a way to comfortably live with whatever the market presents us."

As revealed by Fairfax, Disney, which has four dedicated channels with Disney content on Foxtel's cable and satellite service, is seeking a telecommunications partner to help it launch a subscription video-on-demand service direct to customers.

US network giant NBCUniversal will this week preview its own, reality-TV focused, streaming service called Hayu, which it will launch in March in Australia, the UK and Ireland, costing $5.99 a month and available on connected TVs, mobile, tablets and computers.

Foxtel's Arena channel is dominated by content from Foxtel's output deal with NBC for its Bravo channel. Much of that content, currently unique to Arena, will become available directly on Hayu from March.

Mr Freudenstein moved to shore up Foxtel's position in content last week by giving Moritz von Hauenschild, who returned to Foxtel in September as executive director of product and operations, responsibility for the long-term commercial deals Foxtel strikes with its channel partners. Mr von Hauenschild will focus in particular on ensuring Foxtel has comprehensive rights across all platforms, including Presto, Foxtel's subscription video-on-demand (SVOD) joint venture with Seven West Media, which has Mr von Hauenschild on its board.

Foxtel and Presto were dealt a blow last month when Stan, the SVOD service owned by Nine Entertainment and Fairfax Media, owner of The Australian Financial Review, signed a long-term exclusive licensing agreement with US cable giant CBS for its Showtime content, including the hit show Billions.

Mr Freudenstein said Foxtel was happy to let that deal pass it by on price grounds.

Foxtel is under pressure to control costs as it encounters rising competition.
direction: downward pressure on Foxtel shares

headline: AMP chief's job is on the line
intro: It is unfortunate for AMP chief executive Craig Meller that his revelation of escalating losses in AMP's life insurance business came on the same day Macquarie Group published strong half-year results.
text:
It is unfortunate for AMP chief executive Craig Meller that his revelation of escalating losses in AMP's life insurance business came on the same day Macquarie Group published strong half-year results.

Macquarie's results provided a text-book example of how to successfully run a portfolio of businesses in financial services. The AMP announcement showed the dangers of being overweight in one line of business when conditions change.

AMP is overweight in life insurance partly because of its 167-year history in that industry and partly because of its takeover of the Australian arm of AXA Asia Pacific in 2011.

The AXA bid meant AMP doubled down on life insurance just before an escalation in claims experience losses in every segment of the market including income protection, lump sum and group cover.

These negative trends have been exacerbated by higher policy lapse rates, which refers to people cancelling policies.

Yesterday's latest financial results from AMP and Macquarie reminded institutional investors of what can happen when a board and management get it right and what can happen when they get it wrong.

Macquarie chief executive Nicholas Moore cemented his reputation as a master at managing a range of businesses here and overseas.

In sharp contrast, AMP's Meller upset influential shareholders on the AMP register. There appears to be an intention to pressure the board to appoint a new CEO.

Meller certainly has a reputation for moving too slowly to deal with the problems in life insurance.

AMP's life insurance division, called wealth protection, was first hit with higher-than-expected losses in 2013. At that time Meller announced a "profit improvement plan" which included specific measures to improve claims handling. The company said it would invest in new claims analytics tools, build a new claims technology platform and assist customers to return to work sooner.

But three years later Meller has announced a plan to "reposition the wealth protection business in Australia as significantly less capital intensive with market-leading products and a transformed claims philosophy and process".

Meller says the second big initiative in three years to improve the claims payment philosophy and processing at AMP is because of a "step change" in the industry. He reckons this week's $1.7 billion writedown in the value of MLC Life by National Australia Bank is a further sign of industry-wide pressure on profit margins. He attributes higher experience losses to the increased public scrutiny of the industry following a series of investigative reports into CommInsure by Adele Ferguson from Fairfax Media.

Meller says the industry changed dramatically in about February or March this year. Claims experience losses started to escalate sharply.

"We all know the life insurance industry is on the nose at the present time," Meller told Chanticleer.

"The plain fact is that we have seen a spike in claims coming through the door and that appears to be an industry-wide issue."

Another view is that AMP was in negotiations with Munich Re to reinsure 50 per cent of $750 million in annual premium income of the AMP Life portfolio. Munich Re was the catalyst for AMP to revisit all its assumptions in its life business. That had a flow-on effect to the future profitability and the value of goodwill. It is possible that AMP did not have the balance sheet strength to deal with the issues in its life business.

Ironically, AMP is now talking about capital management because the $500 million received from Munich Re is in excess of its capital requirements.

The concern for shareholders is that life and income protection make up only 10 per cent of group profitability but have absorbed and will continue to absorb the bulk of management's time.

This takes the focus away from the other businesses, including AMP Capital and Australian wealth management. The new chairman of AMP, Catherine Brenner, will also be under pressure because she was chairman of AMP Life until recently.

But Meller says he takes responsibility for the company's financial performance.

"Let me be absolutely clear, I am chief executive of the group," he told Chanticleer. "I am absolutely accountable for the financial performance of the group. But I am also accountable for the actions the group has taken in order to deliver and improve on the situation."

AMP's latest woes came on the day Moore said profit at Macquarie this financial year would be as good as last year's record profit of $2.06 billion.

Not all of Macquarie's six businesses were performing at their optimum levels in the latest half but it is a tribute to the diversity in the portfolio that Macquarie was able to overcome a drop in performance fees at Macquarie Asset Management from $600 million to $200 million.

AMP's shareholders could well be experiencing déjà vu.

The company in 2016 appears to fit the following description given by its former chairman Peter Wilcox at the 2003 annual meeting: "AMP had too much of your money in the wrong business, at the wrong time, in the wrong place."

That comment was made after AMP's market capitalisation had declined by $14 billion in the space of two years. A series of initiatives including large asset sales in the United Kingdom and Australia turned the company around.

The situation at AMP today is not as dire as 2003 but it is definitely the case that AMP has too much of its capital tied up in life insurance, a business that barely makes any money, and not enough in funds management and financial planning.

TONY BOYD
direction: bearish for AMP shares

headline: Will unexpected turbulence threaten Qantas' stable trajectory?
text:
Leigh Clifford has never been afraid of a fight but the Qantas chairman can expect a less combative atmosphere than usual when he presides over his ninth annual meeting of the airline's shareholders later this week. While mobs of placard-carrying protesters have been common fixtures outside Qantas AGMs in recent years, there is not a lot to complain about at the moment.

Sixteen thousand staff have accepted wage freezes in return for bonuses, shareholders are receiving dividends for the first time in seven years, and the carrier has emerged from a $2 billion cost-cutting program leaner and more efficient than ever. Analysts expect the airline to deliver another $1 billion net profit this financial year.

This does not mean things are going to be smooth sailing for Qantas though. It never is for an airline.

Hong Kong-based Cathay Pacific, which is Asia's biggest international carrier, hit the panic button last week when it announced a "crucial review" of the company and slashed its earnings forecasts. Air New Zealand shares have also taken a hit after warning earnings will fall 50 per cent this year. These are just two examples of how tough it is in the international aviation market at the moment. Low fuel prices have encouraged airlines to add too much capacity on to popular routes, creating so much competitive tension that yields - or average ticket prices - are falling.

Qantas is not immune to this either but is holding up pretty well, helped by the fact that two-thirds of its earnings come from the domestic business. Investors will have a better idea at the end of the month how that is performing when it releases its quarterly traffic statistics. Qantas has warned revenues will be lower in the first half because of yield pressure and the domestic market looks patchy.

But the big question for shareholders at the AGM will be: What next for Qantas?

The airline has emerged victorious from a colourful five years during which chief executive Alan Joyce grounded the entire fleet to break a union deadlock, notched up a $2.8 billion loss, fought a bitter domestic capacity war with Virgin, and went to Canberra cap in hand seeking financial assistance. It is expected to make a $1 billion net profit this year for the second year in a row and is again spreading its wings with new routes being added in Asia, the region's fastest-growing market.

"Hats off to the guy for getting the company sorted out, he spent a lot of time restructuring and he has stripped out a lot of costs. He still, as he told me last night, has some way to go," Tim Clark, the president of Qantas's Dubai-based partner Emirates told The Australian Financial Review after spending two days with Joyce at a resort in New South Wales' Wolgan Valley last week.

Some see this as a natural exit point for Clifford, who was appointed chairman in 2007. Despite rumours in previous years he was preparing to step down, Clifford is not believed to be planning to announce his departure at this Friday's AGM in Sydney. This makes it highly unlikely Joyce would leave either. Typically, a new chairman is appointed who privately sounds out the sitting CEO about their intentions and then starts succession planning. Joyce, who turned 50 in June, points out he is relatively young for a chief executive and is not about to go anywhere.

Joyce's priority has been reducing debt and fixing the cost base. He has notched up $1.66 billion in permanent cost and revenue savings, with the remaining $400 million expected to be achieved in the 2017 financial year. Lower fuel prices have also helped, although Qantas is well-hedged. This is the last of the heavy lifting under the transformation program. So what next for Qantas strategically?

Investors say the big challenge for Qantas is finding growth in a stable domestic market, which accounts for two-thirds of its earnings. The rest comes from international which is also profitable but highly competitive. Analysts expect Qantas to make a $1 billion net profit this year, or underlying pretax earnings of $1.5 billion, according to Bloomberg. That is the same as last year. In 2018, the forecasts start to edge lower.

"Once the domestic market is back to sensible levels of maturity and the profit pool is restored, that market will only grow 2-3 per cent a year. It is a very mature market. It is very well served. That market will grow slowly," one observer notes.

What investors do not want is another repeat of the capacity war with Virgin Australia two years ago when Qantas used its 65 per cent market share as a line in the sand. A market which typically earns $700 million to $1 billion a year was suddenly reduced to zero. That is unlikely to happen again. The loyalty business remains a potential growth engine for Qantas, thanks to the value of the data having 11.4 million members brings. Qantas has announced a number of new ventures this year, including a tie-up with AirBnb, a new deal with Woolworths and a push into healthcare. There are more initiatives in the pipeline.

The focus is also on Qantas's international operations. Joyce was on the tarmac last week for a photo opportunity with a red Chinese "lion" as the airline announced it was resuming flights to Beijing after seven years. The new route, made possible through its alliance with China Eastern which swapped its slot at Beijing's international airport, means Asia now makes up 40 per cent of Qantas's international capacity. China will not be the silver bullet that results in a dramatic uplift in international revenue though. Competition for the 1.1 million Chinese tourists visiting Australia annually is fierce.

Yields on the trans-Pacific route are also under pressure and tourists are shying away from Europe due to terrorism fears, although this benefits Qantas with more Asian tourists visiting Australia. After years of disappointing results, Jetstar's joint ventures in Singapore, Vietnam and Japan are also profitable. More expansion is planned for Japan.

The arrival of the Boeing 787-9 Dreamliner aircraft in a year's time will also open the possibility of new routes such as Perth-London and Sydney-Chicago although not immediately.

After spending years exploring potential equity tie-ups with foreign airlines and launching a premium carrier out of Singapore or Kuala Lumpur, Qantas has settled on its network of powerful airline partners such as China Eastern and China Southern covering Asia, American Airlines for North America and Emirates, which code-shares to Europe.

The competition is stiff and declining yields on the trans-Pacific route worries some investors. On the upside, Qantas codeshares with other carriers on the most heavily-contested routes.

While nine out of the 13 analysts covering Qantas have a "buy" on the airline and an average 12-month target price of $4.02, the market is holding back until it has more evidence that the turnaround is sustainable.

Investors globally are wary of airlines which have a track record of extreme volatility. Qantas does look cheap though.

It is currently trading at around six times forward earnings, which compares to 20 times for Virgin, 17 times for Cathay Pacific and 14 for Singapore Airlines.

"Airlines are airlines. We know they are volatile but the vast majority of Qantas's earnings are less volatile than they used to be. The loyalty business speaks for itself, the domestic business there is an outbreak of pragmatism between Qantas and Virgin and they have reduced some of the volatility in their international business through their alliances," says one fund manager who bought Qantas shares this year.

Qantas shares closed at $3.24 on Friday. The stock fell below $1.00 in late 2013 when the carrier appeared to be on the verge of bankruptcy before soaring to more than $4.00 in April this year.

It has been unable to regain those highs since though, mainly due to subdued demand which is keeping a lid on domestic capacity growth.

But as long as Qantas does not get involved in another dog fight with Virgin or leave itself vulnerable if conditions suddenly deteriorate then investors will continue to stick by the airline.

michael.smith@fairfaxmedia.com.au
direction: Qantas shares supported by cost cuts and dividends despite sector headwinds

headline: Virgin Australia's half-year profit flying high
intro: Virgin Australia has posted an eight-fold rise in underlying profit before tax in the first half to $81.5 million, as a result of ongoing fare increases in the domestic market and better conditions in the international market.
text:
Virgin Australia has posted an eight-fold rise in underlying profit before tax in the first half to $81.5 million, as a result of ongoing fare increases in the domestic market and better conditions in the international market.

On a bottom-line basis, Virgin reported a $62.5 million profit in the first half, up from a $47.8 million loss in the same period the previous year. It did not reveal how much the lower fuel price had contributed to the result, but the carrier has previously flagged relatively lesser benefits from the oil price fall than rival Qantas.

Virgin will not unveil its detailed half-year financial figures until February 11, but it provided information on its December quarter results on Thursday because large shareholder Singapore Airlines is obliged to disclose its share of Virgin's earnings each quarter.

Virgin reported an underlying profit before tax of $73 million in the December quarter, up 32.2 per cent from the previous year, in what is typically the strongest quarter of the year for the airline.

Consensus estimates are for Virgin to report a full-year underlying profit before tax of $143.4 million, up from a $49 million underlying loss before tax the previous year. Qantas is expected to report an underlying profit before tax of $1.67 billion this financial year.

Virgin chief executive John Borghetti said his airline's performance in the December quarter was underpinned by strong unit revenue and yield growth, particularly in Virgin's domestic arm, as well as ongoing improvement in Tigerair Australia, strong momentum from the Velocity loyalty arm and strict cost discipline.

While the financial performance in the domestic market was positive because of fare increases, there were signs demand remained weak. Virgin cut its domestic capacity by 1.2 per cent during the period and filled 77.7 per cent of seats, down from 78.1 per cent the previous year.

Qantas, which trimmed mainline domestic capacity slightly in October and November but has not released December's figures, filled about 77.4 per cent of seats in that period.

Both carriers have been more rational with their capacity levels since 2014. Airfare prediction app Hopper's Consumer Airfare Index for Australia found in January the average domestic flight for leisure travellers had risen 2.9 per cent to $271 over the past year, despite the decline in oil prices. In other regions, such as the United States, domestic airfares have been falling.

Mr Borghetti said Virgin's international business had continued to improve, despite the financial impact of volcanic activity in Bali, which led the carrier to cancel about a third of its flights to that destination in November.

Virgin filled 82.7 per cent of international seats in the December quarter, up from 80.8 per cent the previous year, having flown 6 per cent fewer available seat kilometres because of the Bali issues.

Virgin will switch flights from Melbourne, Adelaide and Perth to Bali to low-cost carrier Tigerair from March to help improve financial results.
direction: Virgin Australia shares higher

headline: Sacked Rio executive ready for court battle
intro: had any evidence of the reasons for my termination of my employment given," said Mr Davies in the statement.
text:
had any evidence of the reasons for my termination of my employment given," said Mr Davies in the statement.

"Rio Tinto has made no effort to abide by due process or to respect my rights as an employee and it has given me no opportunity to answer any allegations."

Ms Valentine has made no comment.

Rio alerted fraud and securities agencies in Britain, US and Australia last week after emails from 2011 emerged showing Mr Davies asking Mr Walsh, who was then in charge of Rio's iron ore division, to approve a $US10.5 million ($14 million) payment to a political adviser in Guinea named Francois de Combret.

Mr Davies said in the emails that Mr de Combret had helped Rio secure its place as the operator of the Simandou iron ore project in Guinea, and the email trail shows that Mr Walsh sought further input from then CEO Tom Albanese.

Attempts by The Australian Financial Review to contact Mr Walsh in recent days have been unsuccessful, while Mr Albanese declined to comment when asked about the Guinea scandal on November 10.

External investigations into the matter are in their early stages. The Rio board said on Thursday that an internal investigation was sufficient to dismiss the two executives.

"The board concluded that the executives failed to maintain the standards expected of them under our global code of conduct "The Way We Work". In the circumstances the board terminated the contracts of both executives," the company said in a statement.

The Australian Federal Police said the matter had not yet been "formally referred" to it.

Rio said the dismissal decision "does not pre-judge the course of any external inquiries into this matter".

Rio said neither Mr Davies nor Ms Valentine will be eligible for any short-term incentive plan awards in 2016 and unvested incentive plan awards from previous years will also be cancelled.

Mr Davies is also a director of Rolls Royce in Britain, however at this stage his tenure there remains unchanged.

Rio CEO Jean-Sebastien Jacques is expected to be questioned about the Guinea saga when he visits Australia next week to give an investor briefing and a speech to the Melbourne Mining Club.

The company has appointed Bold Baatar to run the energy and minerals division that Mr Davies had previously fronted, while chief financial officer Chris Lynch will take on some extra duties in the legal affairs area left vacant by Ms Valentine.

Mr Baatar is vice-president of Rio's iron ore sales and marketing division and worked with Mr Jacques when he was in charge of the company's copper division.

A Mongolian citizen, Mr Baatar's appointment comes as Rio pushes ahead with development of a big mine expansion at the Oyu Tolgoi copper and gold mine in Mongolia.
direction: bearish for Rio Tinto shares

headline: What moved the market
intro: Iron ore
text:
Iron ore

Chinese iron ore and other commodity futures plunged on Wednesday as soaring bond yields renewed concerns about liquidity in the world's second-largest economy, driving speculators to liquidate bullish bets. Dalian iron ore slumped nearly 8 per cent to 556.0 yuan ($US81) a tonne, one of its largest percentage falls since futures launched three years ago. Overnight yuan borrowing costs in Shanghai surged to a two-month high on tight liquidity in the market after the central bank pulled funds from the financial system, which hurt commodity trading, analysts said.

Oil

Oil markets edged up in nervous trading ahead of a key OPEC meeting later in the day, with members of the producer cartel trying to thrash out an output cut to curb oversupply that has seen prices more than halve since 2014. Brent crude was trading at $US46.85 per barrel, up 0.9 per cent. Oil dropped nearly 4 per cent the previous session over disputes between Saudi Arabia, Iran and Iraq regarding details of the planned cut. Despite the disagreements, most analysts still expect some form of deal to emerge.

Housing approvals

The ABS data released on Wednesday showed total approvals fell 13 per cent in October, on a seasonally adjusted basis, with September's 8.7 per cent decline revised to a 9.6 per cent drop. Building approvals have now dropped in each of the past four months. But the very volatile high-rise approvals figure showed a monthly decline in October of about 40 per cent, to hit a two-year low. This data is "the clearest sign to date that Australia's high-rise driven dwelling construction boom is starting to turn down," Westpac economist Matthew Hassan says.

Aussie dollar

The dollar lost some momentum following a sharp correction in iron ore futures and the shockingly weak building approvals report, but the currency stabilised at US74.78¢ in late trade. For the month, the Aussie is down nearly 2 per cent, largely due to US dollar strength following the election of Donald Trump to US president. However, the currency has been holding up better than most of its peers, thanks to the rally in industrial metals prices over the month. Against most other currencies the Aussie has actually posted gains in November.
direction: bearish for iron ore and property/REIT sectors

headline: Banks cheer the end of US accounting 'abomination'
intro: New York | Jamie Dimon, chairman and chief executive of JPMorgan Chase, once denounced the 2007 mark-to-market rule as "one of the more ridiculous concepts that's ever been invented in accounting". Analysts mostly agreed, calling it a "fiction" and an "abomination".
text:
New York | Jamie Dimon, chairman and chief executive of JPMorgan Chase, once denounced the 2007 mark-to-market rule as "one of the more ridiculous concepts that's ever been invented in accounting". Analysts mostly agreed, calling it a "fiction" and an "abomination".

Now the US's accounting standard-setter has relented, dropping an eight-year-old decision that has routinely resulted in big - and confusing - swings in bank earnings from quarter to quarter. Under the rule, banks were allowed to use market prices when valuing their own debt, meaning they could book profits when their debt fell in value and losses when it rose.

When it was introduced, banks welcomed its flexibility, imagining it could be useful to smooth bumps in the performance of their main operating units.

But the financial crisis caused huge drops in the value of banks' bonds and exposed the rule's perversities. The closer a bank was seen to be to default, the cheaper its debt traded - and the greater the profit the bank could book.

Banks and investors lobbied for the rule's removal and on Tuesday the Financial Accounting Standards Board threw in the towel.

It said it would allow banks to move the gains and losses - known as debt valuation adjustments, or DVAs - from the income statement to the balance sheet, buried in a ragbag of non-operational items known as "other comprehensive income".

The move brings the US in line with the London-based International Accounting Standards Board, which changed its rules in July 2014. Banks in the US could in theory adopt the new rule in time for fourth-quarter earnings presentations beginning next week, but most are likely to wait for first-quarter results in April.

The change means that DVAs will still exist, but in a little-noticed corner of banks' financial statements.

Robert Willens, a tax and accounting expert in New York, said the FASB had been "shamed, almost" into an about-face."I don't think anyone expected that in practice the rule would have been so prominent, and elicited so much criticism." The original rule - Accounting Standard (FAS) 159, The Fair Value Option for Financial Assets and Financial Liabilities - was well-intended.

The FASB introduced it on the grounds that if banks were going to book their assets at market value, rather than cost, they should also book their liabilities at market value. Companies should be allowed to recognise gains when the value of their bonds fell below par, the FASB reasoned, on the assumption they would be able to buy them back at a discount. But right from the start, the rule proved to be "counter-intuitive", said Pri de Silva, a credit analyst at CreditSights, because it meant that banks booked income in bad times and expenses in good times.

Concerns among investors and analysts were magnified by violent swings in bond prices. In some turbulent periods, such as the flaring of the Greek debt crisis in mid-2010, DVAs accounted for as much as a fifth of the biggest banks' quarterly net income.
direction: US bank shares

headline: THE AUTOMATION MYTH
text:
Technology won't necessarily cut jobs, writes Workplace correspondent David Marin-Guzman.

If you were somehow looking forward to a jobless future, what with robots taking over the workplace, you may be waiting a long, long time.

Scientists, businesses and media pundits can't stop talking about the "unprecedented" pace of technological change and the risk that up to 40 per cent of jobs could be automated.

This week Elon Musk, himself at the cutting edge of developing driverless cars, added to the hype by predicting robots would replace so many jobs countries would be forced to implement a universal basic income.

For University of Melbourne professor Jeff Borland, the rise of the robots makes for "a sexy story". The problem is there is absolutely no evidence to support it.

The widely respected labour economist and self-confessed contrarian has caused a stir with his study Are Robots Taking Our Jobs?, which revealed automation's effect on the workplace was "vastly overstated".

The paper prompted inquiries from Department of Employment analysts and senior union leaders sceptical of the robot hype and has highlighted a long-standing divide between technologists and economists.

Borland, along with fellow Melbourne uni economist Michael Coelli, rigorously mapped out the shifts in demand over time for jobs with routine skills - the kind of work technology is replacing.

They discovered that the trend away from routinised jobs today was no different than was going on back in the 1970s and the 1980s, well before the introduction of computers into the workplace in the 1990s.

Moreover, the pace of job turnovers was no greater today than it was in the late 1960s.

Borland theorises that, just as has been the case with waves of automation since the industrial revolution, technology is creating jobs as quickly as it is eradicating them.

New digital technologies have increased demand for software designers, programmers and managers, while lower costs of production from automation have resulted in higher real incomes that in turn lead to more demand and more jobs.

But there is one difference. This time, big data and advances in computerisation mean technology is no longer replacing low-skilled blue-collar jobs. It's threatening cognitive-skilled white collar work - the bookkeeper, the lawyer, the doctor, the market analyst.

Some analysts argue these technological developments are happening so fast they could eradicate more jobs than they create.

Robotics expert and Queensland University of Technology professor Peter Corke is a case in point. He recalls how he used to make "sanguine statements" in lectures about how technology created jobs as much as it eradicated them. "I don't feel comfortable saying that now. I don't convince myself anymore."

But for Borland, technologists like Corke are confusing changes in the automation of certain types of jobs with changes in the total quantity of work.

"The rate of jobs being routinised may be changing, but that doesn't mean that technology in aggregate is having a larger disruptive effect on the labour market than it did previously."

He says the total amount of work available has actually remained stable since the mid-1960s, with the main variations due to recessions and changes in the business cycle.

If computers were causing major disruptions to work, Borland said you might expect to see jobs lasting for shorter periods. In fact, the opposite was happening.

Data from 1982 to 2013 showed there had been no increase in females working in jobs for less than a year. In fact, there had been a notable increase in females staying in jobs for 10 years or more.

Job tenure data for males showed similar trends, with no increase in the proportion of short-duration jobs.

Even the idea that technology's routinising of cognitive skills is a new development is open to question, Borland says.

"Automation of mid-skilled jobs may be something that's a little bit different, but it's not totally different.

"The weavers during the industrial revolution were seen as skilled workers, trained in the craft, earning higher than agricultural workers at the time when the cotton spinner replaced them."

It's not the first time pundits have sought to downplay the historical tendency of automation to create jobs and predicted their age's wave of innovation would be the one to wipe out work.

In 1930, the economist John Maynard Keynes famously predicted the 19th century had unleased such technological change that "our grandchildren" would work only three hours a day.

A 1983 report to the National Science Foundation entitled The Next Generation of Robots predicted "today's robotic developments ... will progressively reduce the labour used in industrial production, ultimately to something rather close to zero".

Borland notes humans have a tendency to exaggerate the current state of change for their own purposes. There's a natural bias to believe we live in significant times.

The visible rise of the digital economy over the past 20 years has no doubt contributed to that perception.

"You can't deny Amazon is changing the way people buy books, you can't deny Airbnb is changing the way we live - you can't deny new technologies are changing the way the economy operates.

"Clearly that's happening. But what I say is it's always been happening.

"In the start of the 20th century, when Ford and the big new US companies created huge mass production lines, that had a huge effect on the way goods were produced and economic activity happened.

"When you go back to the industrial revolution, when you introduced new ways of spinning cotton and new ways of weaving, that had a huge effect on the way economic activity was done.

"You can accept the technology is changing the way economic activity happens, but that does not follow that we're heading to a workless future."

That was not to say automation didn't raise policy issues. The maritime industry is one of the main industries most affected by automation in the last few years.

Steve Lee was one of 160 workers who lost their jobs last year through Patrick's push to fully automate its Port Botany terminal.

At the time, Lee had worked at the port for more than two decades and was ranked the second-best-performing crane operator on site.

He never expected a machine would replace him, leaving him to cope with a $1.26 million mortgage he'd signed up to the year before. After four months, Lee got rehired at the port as a contractor. But he said his experience shows automation can happen to anyone.

"It was a big reality check for me after 26 years to be told you don't have a job because the robots are coming in," he said.

"Especially when you don't give the company any reason to dismiss you."

Borland says policymakers still have to deal with adjustment problems around automation, including assisting those adversely affected and ensuring a flexible institutional environment that can take advantage of new technologies. But that has always been the issue with technology, he says.

Outside of the data, the problem of robots taking over our working futures is merely speculation.

"And I'd rather speculate based on the way history's been for the last 250 years than any other way." P
direction: bearish for automation-focused tech equities

headline: IMF says growth moving sideways
text:
The International Monetary Fund has trimmed its forecasts for Australia and the world for 2017, warning economic growth is moving "sideways".

The global economy is expected to grow 3.4 per cent next year, 0.1 percentage point less than in the IMF's previous world economic outlook six months ago, largely because of disappointing growth in the US and the shockwaves from Brexit.

Australian gross domestic product is expected to increase 2.7 per cent in 2017 after rising 2.9 per cent this year.

The 2017 GDP forecast for Australia has been cut 0.3 percentage points although growth this year has been revised up in light of the strong first half.

The lower growth forecasts for 2017 will give the Reserve Bank of Australia pause before deciding to raising rates any time soon. It held the cash rate steady at 1.5 per cent at its monthly meeting on Tuesday.

The IMF forecast for 2017 in Australia is now slightly below the RBA's 3 per cent in its August monetary policy statement. The federal budget's forecasts of growth of 2.5 per cent in 2016-17 and 3 per cent in 2017-18 are roughly consistent with the IMF.

While some are concerned that low interest rates will inflate an asset bubble, the IMF is much more worried about weak inflation and warned of a "deflation trap" if consumer prices start falling.

It endorsed "monetary policy loosening through asset purchases and, in some cases, negative deposit rates" to "help lift inflation expectations, and lower the real costs of borrowing for households and firms".

The IMF downplayed the slight downgrade to the Australia outlook, explaining in the commentary that accompanied its forecast that growth was "hovering" around 2.8 per cent.

It said Australian disposable income would be reduced by weak commodity prices but on the other hand Australian coal prices had increased 32 per cent since February 2016 in line with other energy and metal prices.

The biggest change in the forecasts was that expected growth in Britain in 2017 has been cut in half to just 1.1 per cent as the IMF remains convinced Brexit will hit confidence. US growth has been cut for this year but a recovery is expected to resume - albeit at a weaker pace in 2017. China is forecast to manage a smooth transition away from exports to domestic demand.

Treasurer Scott Morrison is due to attend the IMF's regular meeting in Washington next week where the outlook will be discussed.

Calling for fiscal policy, monetary policy and microeconomic reform aimed at boosting growth, the IMF warned of the political impact of sub-par growth. It said that "persistent stagnation, particularly in advanced economies, could further fuel populist calls for restrictions on trade and immigration".

"The Brexit vote and the ongoing US presidential election campaign have highlighted a fraying consensus about the benefits of cross-border economic integration," chief economist Maurice Obstfeld said.

The IMF has taken an axe to its Australian inflation forecasts catching up with similar moves by the RBA in recent months. It now expects consumer prices to rise just 1.3 per cent in 2016, about half what it expected six months ago, and 2.1 per cent in 2017, a slightly smaller cut.

Both the IMF and the RBA are thus now not expecting inflation to climb back inside its target range of 2 to 3 per cent a year until the end of the year.

In leaving official rates unchanged on Tuesday, RBA governor Phil Lowe conceded inflation remained quite low. "Given very subdued growth in labour costs and very low cost pressures elsewhere in the world, this is expected to remain the case for some time."

But he has previously said he is not an "inflation nutter" and will be careful about cutting interest rates to drive up inflation. The IMF has devoted a whole chapter of its report to the dangers of deflation or falling prices, which it now says affects 20 per cent of economies.

It warned a "deflationary cycle may emerge in which weak demand and deflation reinforce each other" and a "deflation trap" in which interest rates, already stuck at zero, cannot be cut to stimulate the economy.

It said that central banks should not back away from so-called unconventional policies such as quantitative easing. It said they should continue "monetary policy accommodation to help keep medium-term inflation expectations anchored - including a transparent commitment to more aggressive accommodation" if inflation stays below target for too long.
direction: downward pressure on rate-sensitive sectors like banks and property due to subdued growth and delayed rate hikes

headline: Treasurer's super plan for greater good
text:
Treasurer Scott Morrison is right to stick to his guns on the $500,000 non-concessional contributions cap. We need to fix our budget trajectory now, and in the absence of sufficient support for other measures (eg GST) it is unfortunately an easy fix until we get the budget back on track.

Arguing over whether or not it's retrospective is indulgent when we've got a very real and present budget trajectory issue to fix. Labor's proposed negative gearing changes are arguably retrospective as well, because they would change the demand dynamics of the market for those who've previously invested on one tax profile and are now having to sell into another.

It's not right to penalise a person on any excess over $500,000 that they might have put into super since 2007, but that aside, no one can seriously expect the taxpayer to subsidise investments over that sort of number. If you are in the impact zone for this change, that is more than $500,000 of cash that you do not need to make ends meet. In this budgetary environment, we cannot ask lower-income earners to subsidise an investment environment for the excess. Could anyone credibly call that "fair"?

Peter Carroll

Willoughby NSW

Coalition needs NXT

It is now clear the government can't achieve a super reform compromise with Labor or the Greens unless the resulting package is even more offensive to the Coalition's donors and volunteers than its own self-harming package.

Therefore it must now seek the required votes from the Senate crossbenchers. Several of these prefer the status quo but would agree to reasonable change if likely alternative changes were worse.

NXT will be the threshold group to win over. If the latter will agree to a package less severe on high earners than Labor or the Greens, the Coalition will be free to retreat without media criticism from its own self-harming package, and to produce something much more acceptable to its own supporters and MPs as well as to NXT and the other crossbenchers.

The package may then look more like this: a $1 million non-retrospective contribution cap and a $2 million balance threshold before 15 per cent tax applies to pensions. Since any restriction of super contributions just diverts funds into the unproductive housing bubble, moderate reforms will be beneficial for everyone.

Paul McCarthy

Ascot, WA

Time for accord

If Bill Shorten accepts that $500,000 is an appropriate lifetime cap for non-concessional contributions to superannuation - and it appears from what he said at the National Press Club, he does - why not support legislation that brings about such a cap?

If someone has been fortunate enough to have already contributed beyond this cap they are not required to reverse that contribution. They are therefore no more disadvantaged by the legislation than anyone who is yet to reach the cap. The legislation is no more retrospective for those who made non-concessional contributions over the past decade than it is for those who had planned to make these contributions over the next decade.

The government and the opposition are about as close to a unity ticket as you can get on super reform. How about both sides stop playing politics with the issue and get on with some sensible measures?

Richard Whan

Byron Bay, NSW
direction: bearish for property trusts and equity markets

headline: Start-up raises $6m to push childcare app
intro: A Melbourne-based tech start-up, which has been rapidly signing up childcare centres across Australia, Malaysia and the US to its app, has locked in a $6 million funding round from Sydney-based Airtree Ventures.
text:
A Melbourne-based tech start-up, which has been rapidly signing up childcare centres across Australia, Malaysia and the US to its app, has locked in a $6 million funding round from Sydney-based Airtree Ventures.

Xplor has created an online platform that allows parents to interact with their children in real time during the day, and the childcare centres and schools to manage their operations by accepting fees, processing payroll and monitoring real-time data such as internal finances and children's attendance and medical records.

The start-up's founder and chief executive, Mark Woodland, told The Australian Financial Review that the series A funding round was needed to hire enough people to meet the demand of new centres signing up to use Xplor, and then help it expand before the company goes public.

As well as earlier discussing US growth that included 112 childcare centres in Texas and more than 100,000 registered Australian users, Mr Woodland said Xplor had just finished setting up two separate Malaysian groups with 90 and 60 childcare centres under their control. It is also beginning to target schools in addition to early childcare centres.

"We have got a big presence in the US and Malaysia at the minute, so we are using the funding to finish that off so we can have a look at some other countries that have shown some interest," he said.

"Demand is really great because we are solving a common problem that they are all facing, where educators are unable to educate because they are doing so much administration."

While it is the app's ability to share photographs, videos and messages with children's parents throughout the day that fronts the company's marketing video, it is the back-office functionality that has made it an alluring investment opportunity for Airtree.

There is a growing range of apps available for schools to communicate with parents, such as ClassDojo, Kindyhub and RedCritter, but Mr Woodland said Xplor can potentially save a childcare centre the salaries of up to two administrators by automating payments and record keeping through integration with payments platforms (including PayPal) and accounting systems (including Xero).

He said most of the new apps simply dumped more work onto already busy teachers and carers by requiring them to provide updates during their work day. He said Xplor made it worthwhile for the teachers because its admin functions removed some of their non-teaching burdens.

"A lot of companies are doing communications apps, and I don't mean to have a crack at them, but they do it very half-arsed for the lack of a better word," Mr Woodland said.

"Schools or centres running these other apps will also have to run separate childcare management systems as well for fees, etc, whereas we are offering them one platform that does everything and it costs them nothing, so they are usually happy to sign on and give it a trial."

Xplor doesn't make its money from charging schools or childcare centres, rather a fee of $9.99 a month is borne by parents.

Airtree managing partner Craig Blair will take a seat on the Xplor board and said the fund had made one of its largest investments in Xplor because of the clearly demonstrable global market acceptance and potential for rapid and broad growth.

"This is on a trajectory to be a many hundreds of millions of dollars in value ... we are yet to see a business with this kind of short-term demand, so our challenge now is to deliver on that," Mr Blair said. "We love backing global companies where the product is clearly best practice globally and that is the case here. The product seems to be almost selling itself at the minute and the biggest problem it currently has is actually keeping up with the new customers that want to sign up ... which is a good problem."

One interesting discussion to be had in the Xplor boardroom will involve the question of what happens next. Mr Woodland said the company would be profitable before the end of the year and would be working towards a possible IPO next year.

Mr Blair, however, said the company would be best advised to focus on managing its rapid growth and securing reliable recurring revenue in the near term. "The honest answer is that it is too early [to plan for an IPO]," he said.

"This business still has enormous growth potential and, while we are huge fans of the ASX, that is mainly when a business is really ready to be understood by the market ... and this business is still years away from an IPO."
direction: bullish for Xplor and the tech sector

headline: The price of being a whistleblower
text:
Bill Dudley posed a couple of very good questions addressing a conference on reforming bank culture at the New York Federal Reserve.

"When people speak up to point out potential conduct issues, how are they treated?" asked the NY Fed president, who previously spent about 20 years at Goldman Sachs. "Are they held up as examples to emulate, or are they discouraged or even penalised?"

The fake-account scandal at Wells Fargo, the No. 2 bank by deposits in the US, showed that protections for whistleblowers can be almost laughably poor. Several employees have told the Financial Times they tried to alert higher-ups to cheating to hit sales targets and got nowhere. One even wrote an email in exasperation to John Stumpf, the former chairman and chief executive. (He said he had not seen it).

It is not just Wells. At Bank of America current and former employees say they have been thwarted in attempts to bring problems to light. Ruth Landaverde was a personal banker at a BofA branch in Los Angeles, until she left last year. When she called an internal hotline to complain that sales goals were set absurdly high and driving staff to sickness and despair, the person on the other end of the line tried to change her mind.

Word somehow got back to her manager. Ms Landaverde suspects it was because she was made to give her employee number. She had been assured the discussion would be confidential, she says.

"[My manager] came down harder on me as a result," she says. "It was like, 'Oh, you're telling on me'?"

BofA declined to comment on the case. There is a lot that needs to change, says Michael Winston. He is one of four former senior executives who have formed a group called Bank Whistleblowers United, pursuing a mission, they say, of restoring accountability to Wall Street.

Mr Winston, 65, was once pulling down $3 million a year. An expert in organisational strategy, employed by Lockheed Martin, McDonnell Douglas and Motorola, he was on the books of speaking agencies, addressing conferences around the world.

In 2006, at the peak of his earning power, he was hired by Countrywide, the aggressive mortgage originator based in California, which had designs on becoming the Goldman of the Pacific.

He knew something was up when he pulled into the carpark and saw the licence plate "FUND EM", he says. But after he got deeper, and started to flag concerns about reckless lending, he was marginalised, punished and ultimately dismissed.

He sued for wrongful termination, turned down a settlement and was awarded $3.8 million by a jury. He then saw that verdict overturned two years later by an appeals court, in secret, after 12 minutes deliberation, without any new evidence.

Now jobless, he shares a car with his son and says he worries - seriously - about starving to death.

If the Fed really wants to improve bank culture, he says, it needs to provide incentives for whistleblowers to expose fraud and corruption. The regulator must require banks to reward people for speaking out, while ensuring they are well protected "in reality, not just rhetorically". And it must mete out harsh and immediate punishment for any cases of retaliation.

"If we want people to come forward, like in the Wells Fargo situation, we need to respect them, not punish them," he says. "I know a lot of whistleblowers; nobody sees any protection. None."

Most big banks blew through analysts' forecasts for profit as they presented third-quarter earnings during the past week. But shares have outperformed the market only narrowly. Four of the biggest six are still trading below their theoretical break-up value. There are all sorts of reasons for such poor trading, including ultra-low interest rates, doubts over loan quality and the rise of smaller, nimbler competitors. But fear of litigation - caused by behaviour that could and should be stamped out - is probably among them too.

Wells says it has changed. Presenting results, the bank's new chief executive said he was "disturbed" by reports of retaliation against would-be whistleblowers. The bank says it is investigating every case, while coaching its 116,000 retail staff how to escalate concerns.

One small-business banker at BofA says he is "too scared" to use official channels to report wrongdoing - or to allow the Financial Times to use his first name. "I've heard stories," he says. "You complain and you end up losing your job."
direction: bearish for US banking sector

headline: Business attacks also attack growth
text:
The populist tide against big business is hitting businesses that are now themselves mostly on the slide. The big miners, the big banks and the big supermarkets are the pillars of the Australian sharemarket and the superannuation portfolios for most Australian workers. They account for most of the $20 billion paid by the nation's top dozen corporate taxpayers, along with the biggest workforces and much of the nation's corporate R&D. The big resources companies were the target of Labor's super-profits tax and now are being hit by the slump in iron ore, coal and oil prices. Labor wants a royal commission to break up the big banks in favour of the trade union-controlled superannuation funds, even as the banks are being forced to raise billions more capital. And the small business and farmer lobbies have convinced the Coalition to impose an anti-big business "effects test" aimed at the supermarkets, in part for daring to sell cheap milk to Australian families. Yet, as events this week showed, the golden age of the big supermarkets is over thanks to food price deflation and global competition from the likes of Aldi. Woolworths reported a $1.2 billion loss and hopes to grow its supermarket sales next year a mere 2 per cent. Coles' parent Wesfarmers reported its weakest profit since 2002. Big oil is doing better from a lower world oil price, but only this week Malcolm Turnbull told conservative populist radio talkback tormentor Alan Jones fluctuating petrol prices were "a real concern" he would raise personally with the competition watchdog.

Businesses rise and fall. Yet the populist tide reflects the complacency of a political system that refuses to recognise that Australia will now have to work harder to prevent a further slide in its prosperity, as Treasurer Scott Morrison warned this week. As Business Council of Australia president Catherine Livingstone asks, why attack the front line of the economy just as falling export prices and economic disruption are undermining the nation's prosperity? With the national income growing a sickly 2 per cent a year in nominal terms, wages can't grow must faster without further squeezing company profits and business investment in future economic growth.

Ideally, big business would be encouraged to kick-start Australian prosperity again. But the political system instead incites blame against business, an unproductive exercise other than for populists who seek to generate their own political capital from it. It is also shamelessly hypercritical. Big business that can find political sponsors will be favoured, not persecuted. After being on the brink of collapse a year ago, Bluescope Steel last week reported a big profit rise thanks to a NSW government payroll tax holiday, cost cutting and protectionist duties against Chinese imports in favour of its American plants. In South Australia, protectionist senator Nick Xenophon demands taxpayer handouts for the other Australian steelmaker, Arrium. Yet Arrium workers at the company's Whyalla steelworks this week voted down a 10 per cent pay cut proposed by administrator Korda Mentha to help save their jobs. Rather than become internationally competitive, they would prefer others subsidise them.

As big companies feel intense pressure to remain internationally competitive, a swarm of medium-size Australian companies are becoming local and even global growth stories. As we report today, they include generic-drug maker Mayne Pharma, born out of the Adelaide-based FH Faulding & Co. Then there's established global success story, blood plasma supplier CSL, along with Penfold's owner Treasury Wine Estates, consumer electronics and whitegoods retailer JB Hi-Fi, global food chain Domino's Pizzas, China health and food suppliers Blackmores and Bellamy's, and pet store chain Greencross, which this week reported double-digit revenue boost to $700 million. A pet-care boom is a sign of ongoing prosperity. Business that can avoid Australia's dysfunctional politics will be best placed to sustain it.

Ideally, big business would be encouraged to kick-start Australian prosperity again.
direction: bearish for big miners, banks, and supermarkets

headline: A sense of disbelief before politics once again wrong-foots everyone
text:
Even as US stock futures started to tumble with the dawning realisation that a potential victory by Donald Trump was not out of the question, there was still a sense of disbelief - just like there has been since the US presidential campaign began.

The one-liners continued to roll. It is rigged after all. Buy Boral and James Hardie they joked, that wall across Mexico is going to need a stack of building materials.

Not long after, though, smiles were well and truly gone with the growing realisation that Trump could actually win it.

Stocks were ditched and safe haven assets were snapped up.

In early afternoon trading the major S&P/ASX 200 Index slumped to 5050 after trading as high as 5314 earlier in the session, when votes in the US election were still being cast.

But later in the day when the Dow futures implied Wall Street would be down as much as 650 points on the open, the panic really set in.

In short, financial markets have got it wrong again.

First the UK election. Then the Brexit vote. Now the US election.

If any further evidence was needed of the disconnect that stands between Wall Street and Main Street, then surely the strong showing by Republican Donald Trump is it.

Right from the word go, markets have been quick off the draw to discount the possibility of a Trump victory despite the lesson from Brexit that opinion polls just can't be trusted.

When it comes to pricing political risk, financial markets seem to come up short.

All the time.

Maybe it was the "shame factor", as labelled by the Macquarie fixed income desk. No-one actually wants to say out loud that they back Trump given his colourful past, it's all too embarrassing.

The silence gives financial markets a false sense of security that he can't possibly win.

Whatever the reason, a wave of risk aversion was quick to sweep the local sharemarket on Wednesday once it looked like he would win.

Traders are pragmatic at best.

Maybe Wall Street types have an incentive to take an upbeat view of the world. To date, it hasn't been much of a career move to tell clients to sell stocks, although the rise and rise of shorting stocks as a way of making money is changing that.

In reality, despite the global financial crisis and it's aftermath, Wall Street has managed to carry on with business as usual thanks to all the taxpayer funded bailouts.

While those on Main Street were left to deal with the hangover of the deepest downturn since the Great Depression, it seemed that it was onwards and upwards for financial markets.

Making matters worse, no one seemed to be made accountable for the whole mess.

Then again, maybe Hillary Clinton should have known better.

It was Bill Clinton who used to say it was all about the economy and it's often said that if you understand the US consumer, you understand the US economy.

Consumption does account for about 70 per cent of US economic activity.

But despite all that the Federal Reserve has done, the latest economic report shows the annual growth rate in the US for the three months to September was still less than 3 per cent and it has struggled to be much more than 2 per cent for most of the past eight years.

Clinton's economic plan included spending on infrastructure and $US1.4 trillion in tax hikes over the next decade, mainly for the wealthiest 1 per cent of income earners, while redistributing income and wealth were also priorities.

But what was needed perhaps was more plans to generate economic growth that would create jobs and raise incomes to help the very poor.

For investors on Wednesday it was all, quite literally, breathtaking. Despite the poll showing, it was always going to be a close call.

The selling will continue but sharemarkets might not fall to the low levels they did earlier this year.

According to Capital Economics, global sharemarkets were worth around $US70 trillion heading into the election and even if they fall 10 per cent, they would still be above where they were at the start of the year when the price of oil was plummeting and fears about a slowdown in China caused a massive sell off.

The market capitalisation of global stockmarkets is now about $US8 trillion higher than back then.

Still, if investors like well-known bear Jeremy Grantham are right, there's not a lot for investors to look forward to.

The chief investment strategist at GMO, that has almost $US90 billion under management, has told his clients they face a long period of lacklustre returns regardless of who is in the White House.

Although he doesn't think the US sharemarket is in bubble territory, and so won't go "bang" any time soon, instead, "we are likely to limp into the setting sun with very low returns", he said.

"The consequences are dismal for investors," he added.

For investors on Wednesday it was all, quite literally, breathtaking.
direction: bearish for equities and risk assets

headline: Briefs
intro: Sunland lawsuit
text:
Sunland lawsuit

Sunland Group has claimed a partial victory in a $10 million damages lawsuit brought by accountant Marcus Lee, who blames the Gold Coast developer for the five years he was detained in Dubai over a property deal that turned sour. Managing director Sahbha Abedian said the Supreme Court of Queensland had struck out significant parts of the claim. But Mr Lee now has leave to re-plead his case. NICK LENAGHAN

Carlson Hotels sale

A unit of Chinese conglomerate HNA Group has agreed to buy Minneapolis-based Carlson Hotels, which owns brands including Radisson and Country Inns and Suites. HNA will acquire Carlson and its 51.3 per cent stake in Rezidor Hotel Group AB, Carlson's Brussels-based master licensee for Europe, the Middle East and Africa, and may begin a takeover offer for all of Rezidor, the companies said. Financial terms of the transaction weren't disclosed. Carlson's hotel group could fetch about $US2 billion ($2.6 billion) in a sale, people with knowledge of the matter said in March. In Australia, Carlson Rezidor operates Radisson Sydney, the Radisson Blu hotel near Circular Quay and the Radisson on Flagstaff Gardens, Melbourne. BLOOMBERG/LARRY SCHLESINGER

Negative gearing fear

A state-wide survey of the Real Estate Institute of Queensland's members and landlord clients found that 79 per cent of investors would abandon property as an investment strategy if Labor's negative gearing changes were brought in. REIQ chairman Rob Honeycombe said the findings of the survey sent to more than 14,000 members confirmed that changes to negative gearing would be disastrous for the Queensland property market. MATTHEW CRANSTON

Housing strength

House price growth picked up in April with a 1.5 per cent increase, after nearly flatlining in March, CoreLogic said. Growth in each of the five mainland capitals contributed to the overall rise that was already apparent over the first 28 days of the month, the data provider said on Thursday. CoreLogic will release its latest results on Monday. The pick-up comes from unexpectedly strong clearance rates. MICHAEL BLEBY

Ardent revision

Deutsche Bank has made minor revisions to its forecasts of Ardent Leisure's earnings. Full-year 2016 earnings are expected to be up 1 per cent to $60 million and full-year 2017 earnings up 2 per cent to $64.7 million. MATTHEW CRANSTON
direction: bearish for property investors and REITs

intro: Top storiesNews
text:
Top storiesNews

'Considerable errors' in oil royalties

Oil and gas companies may have incorrectly claimed billions in deductions for their operations on the North West Shelf. An audit office report report identifies $19.6 million in underpaid royalties - and possibly much more - over 18 months. p8

Companies & Markets

OneVue founder takes on big banks

The wealth management masterfunds of the big banks have "hidden behind basis point fees" for too long, reckons OneVue founder Connie McKeage, who has launched a fee-for-service competitor with major backers. p17

Technology

Robots threaten Aussie outsourcing

Australian outsourcing deals worth up to $4 billion are ripe for disruption in the next three years from a growing move towards robotic process automation and cloud-based options, a leading global consultancy has said. p22

Leadership

Shift to platform model vital

Australian corporates haven't moved swiftly enough to shift their business models to platforms, according to John Meacock, chief strategy officer for Australia and Asia for Deloitte. They are not innovating systematically, he said, and are focusing too much on product and staff-led innovation rather than business model innovation which will "shift the dial", he said. p38
direction: bearish for oil/gas and outsourcing sectors

headline: National authorities have failed to take globalisation seriously
intro: Domestic companies have a home-field advantage.
text:
Trade Regulators are turning into champions for local business and enemies of foreign enterprises. Populists didn't cause this, but they won't stop it either.

Evidence that globalisation is reversing continues to pile up: trade and international capital flows are sluggish and migration is increasingly being restricted. These trends emerged after the 2008 financial crisis, so they can't be blamed on a new populist backlash against globalisation. Rather, their source can be traced to national authorities' failure to take the logic of globalisation seriously.

In a year when Britain voted to "Brexit" from the European Union, and Republicans in the US chose Donald Trump as their presidential candidate, anti-globalisation populism does seem ubiquitous. But while it is tempting to see populism as a cause of global economic woes, the movement has had only limited political successes so far.

After all, the world economy is not sputtering because Poland and Hungary have populist right-wing governments. Left-wing populism, for its part, has even less to brag about: Fidel Castro is fading away in Cuba; Argentina is recovering from catastrophic mismanagement under the Nestor Kirchner and Cristina Fernandez de Kirchner presidencies; Venezuela has imploded under President Nicolas Maduro.

Globalisation is beleaguered partly because of decisions made by governments under the auspices of an open international order. But, more importantly, it is suffering as a result of judicial and quasi-judicial decisions to impose large financial penalties on foreign corporations.

In Europe, the European Commission has been leading the charge with ongoing antitrust proceedings against Microsoft and Google. Whether these cases are about US companies abusing competitive power, or about the EU influencing technology policy and promoting domestic alternatives to US companies, depends on one's loyalties.

Likewise, the US can and does take action against European companies. After the EU announced that it would require Apple to pay €13 billion ($19.6 billion) in back taxes, which it alleges were illegally reduced by the Irish government, the US fined Deutsche Bank, a German company, $US14 billion ($18.4 billion) to settle claims relating to its mortgage-backed securities business before the 2008 crash.

One could regard these various penalties as an effective response in a world where multinational corporations have become extremely skilled at reducing their conventional tax liabilities. The problem is that, unlike normal taxes, fines against companies are not predictively or uniformly levied, and must be haggled over and settled individually in each case. These discussions are often politicised and involve high-level government interventions.

What is the rationale for these fines? To be sure, Deutsche Bank and other European icons such as Volkswagen and British Petroleum should answer for, respectively, misdeeds in selling mortgage securities, systematically manipulating CO2-emissions tests, and polluting the Gulf of Mexico. But while foreign companies may act more aggressively to capture market share in new areas, they are not the only offenders, as demonstrated by the outrage against banks and US corporations after the GFC.

One difference is that it is easier for domestic companies to tell their own government why they are necessary, how many jobs they create, how many vendors and subcontractors they hire and so forth. Domestic companies have a home-field advantage when lobbying to escape or reduce penalties. In 2014, for example, Citigroup convinced the US government to halve a fine almost the size of Deutsche Bank's for the same mortgage abuse.

Thus, domestic corporate lobbying often leads to something akin to regulatory capture, whereby the government privileges national corporate interests. The public's ambient suspicion of corporations is then focused more directly on foreign companies.

US and EU judicial authorities are one source of this new nationalism. Another is the institutions responsible for international trade, especially when new technologies are in play.

Many of the fundamental technological breakthroughs over the past century have come from large government outlays for research and development that would be too expensive for any private firm. America's technological superiority since World War II is a result of its Cold War-era military-industrial mobilisation, which led to the development of satellite technology and what would become the internet.

Direct state support for high-tech companies often conflicts with international trade rules designed to encourage neutrality and a level playing field. For example, the World Trade Organisation just ruled that Airbus, a European company, has received state aid in violation of WTO rules. According to the WTO, Airbus's successful new Airbus A350 made it to market only because of the "direct and indirect effects" of long-term government support.

The Airbus controversy has been dragging on for decades, with Europeans countering that Boeing, a US company, is viable only because of its status as a US military supplier. This lobbying game is often a race to the bottom, and foreign companies are compelled to establish a local presence so that they can persuade politicians that they are just as beneficial to the economy as their native competitors.

Market economies cannot operate when their established rules are haphazardly enforced, which is what happens when national and international regulators turn into advocates for local enterprises and enemies of foreign businesses. This is not the result of a populist revolt; but populists in power certainly won't address it.

Harold James is professor of history and international affairs at Princeton University.

PROJECT SYNDICATE
direction: bearish for global equities and trade-exposed sectors

headline: 'Harsh' penalties for super breaches
text:
Hefty penalties have been built into new superannuation laws which for the first time limit all pension-phase accounts to $1.6 million, financial advisers say.

Penalties for breaches of the $1.6 million limit will apply immediately from next July.

The Tax Office will order the removal of excess amounts and calculate a notional earnings amount, with that earnings amount to be taxed at

15 per cent.

This and other details emerged late on Tuesday when the government released for consultation draft legislation to enact the most contentious elements of its super reforms.

"The legislation is very comprehensive," Dixon Advisory managing director Nerida Cole said. "Treasury have really tried to consider all angles, and the penalties for doing the wrong thing are harsh."

Ms Cole said there was a bit of wriggle room in that if the breach is less than $100,000 and adjusted within 60 days, penalties will not be incurred.

She said it would have been preferable to have a 12-month amnesty given there are only likely to be a few months between when the legislation gets through Federal Parliament and the July 2017 start date.

"The small buffer of $100,000 to recognise market movement is not sufficient," she said.

"We know businesses will need time to adjust systems, train staff and retirees will then need time to get and consider advice before acting."

In addition, death benefit income streams and reversionary pensions received after the death of a spouse will be included in the recipient's $1.6 million pension transfer limit.

"Fortunately the legislation will allow beneficiaries of these reversionary pensions up to six months after receipt of payment to adjust their financial arrangements," Ms Cole said.

The $1.6 million limit will apply from July 1 next year, the draft legislation says. "This cap is intended to limit the extent to which the tax-free benefits of retirement phase accounts can be used by high wealth individuals," it says.

"The value of superannuation interests that support superannuation income streams as at 30 June 2017, together with the commencement value of new superannuation income streams that start after that date, count towards an individual's cap."

In the case of a breach, the Australian Tax Office will direct an individual's super fund to reduce the balance by the excess amount. In extreme cases, the entire account balance can be deemed as no longer in retirement phase and therefore lose its tax-free status.
direction: bearish for superannuation funds and financial advice sector

headline: ASX see-saws higher after CBA update
intro: The Australian sharemarket ended a topsy-turvy trading day higher after investors embraced an otherwise "soft" quarterly update from Commonwealth Bank of Australia, while resources stocks struggled.
text:
The Australian sharemarket ended a topsy-turvy trading day higher after investors embraced an otherwise "soft" quarterly update from Commonwealth Bank of Australia, while resources stocks struggled.
