Investors began the week digesting Friday's disappointing US jobs and Sunday's weak Chinese import data, as well as the impact of oil supply worries due to bushfires in Canada, and the resulting fortunes of indices around the Asian region were mixed, with China on track to post its biggest daily loss in more than two months.

Despite falling to an intraday low of 5264, the S&P/ASX 200 recovered late in the trading session to close 0.5 per cent, or 29 points higher, at 5320.7, while the All Ordinaries ended 0.6 per cent, or 29 points, higher to 5387.8.

Perpetual's head of multi-asset investment strategy, Matthew Sherwood, said the intraday volatility in regional sharemarkets, which are near cyclical highs, was being driven by a mix of profit taking and swooping bargain hunters.

"That's really just part of the trend where markets have gone sideways in a lot of regions for a couple of weeks, lots of up and down. The market looks like they've hit their peak until they hit the next major leg up in earnings," he said.

Mr Sherwood said the market's strong rebound since mid-February - and helping the ASX post four straight weeks of gains - was underpinned by three factors: a US Federal Reserve reluctant to hike interest rates, a stimulatory China and supply declines in key commodity markets.

"When you look at all three drivers, what we can see is they're all temporary in their nature," he said.

Commonwealth Bank of Australia was the big ticket item in Monday's trade after releasing its third-quarter earnings update. The nation's biggest bank reported unaudited cash earnings were $2.3 billion but noted a rise in loan impairments and provisions and pressure on its margins.

UBS banking analyst Jonathan Mott remarked the bank had posted a "soft quarter". But investors embraced the update, and the stock proved the biggest lift in the index, adding 1.9 per cent to $75.78.

It was a strong session for the banks, with National Australia Bank rising 0.9 per cent to $28.45 and Westpac Banking Corporation up 1 per cent to $31.11.

ANZ Banking Group was the exception, falling 3 per cent to $24.38, the biggest drag on the index for the day, as it traded ex-dividend.

ANZ was joined on the laggers' board by Orica, which fell 12.3 per cent to $13.53, the day's worst performing stock, after the explosives maker posted a 33 per cent plunge in net profit, citing the "deterioration" in the global mining industry.

Miners also fell, despite starting the day higher, as steel and iron ore futures on the Chinese Dalian Commodities Exchange plummeted, reversing last month's speculation that China's economy was stabilising. BHP Billiton shed 0.3 per cent to $18.41, Rio Tinto shrunk 2.1 per cent to $46.74 and Fortescue Metals Group lost 2.3 per cent to $3.04. Materials was the only sector to post a loss for the day, down 0.6 per cent.

In company news, APN News and Media placed its shares in a trading halt ahead of an announcement to undertake a capital raising and demerger of its New Zealand business.

Media stocks enjoyed a strong day; Newscorp's Class B shares added 7.8 per cent, topping the leaderboard by percentage gain. Rival Fairfax Media added 5 per cent to 84¢ on speculation it would also spin off its New Zealand assets.

Meanwhile, Mirvac shares closed up 0.3 per cent to $1.95 despite Citi downgrading the stock from buy to neutral on concerns that foreign buyers may struggle to secure loans as banks including Westpac impose restrictions.

Among the blue chips, consumer staples posted a strong day with Wesfarmers gaining 1.4 per cent to $43.16 and Woolworths adding 1.5 per cent to $21.52. The sector was the day's strongest, up 1.7 per cent, followed by energy, which added 1.2 per cent.

Telstra shares also climbed, adding 0.5 per cent to $5.63.
direction: ASX 200 higher, resources lower

headline: Famed Beetaloo station could fetch $200m
text:
Beetaloo and Mungabroom, the Northern Territory cattle stations made famous by the late pioneering cattleman John Dunnicliff and backed by billionaire Brett Blundy, have been offered for sale with expectations the aggregation could fetch as much as $200 million.

Covering more than 1 million hectares on the western edge of the Barkly Tableland, the land is regarded as one of the best stations in the Territory due to Mr Dunnicliff's innovation and investment in watering points which significantly boosted the productivity and cattle carrying capacity.

The Dunnicliff family, which trades as the Barkly Pastoral Co, confirmed its decision to sell following Mr Dunnicliff's death earlier this year after a protracted battle with pancreatic cancer.

After his death, Mr Dunnicliff received an Order of Australia for "services to the livestock industry" with particular reference to the water development plan on Beetaloo.

The family said it felt the timing was right as the business moved from development phase to operational phase, but also as strong global investor demand continued to drive improved prices for cattle grazing land across Australia.

Ben Cameron, of Bentleys International Advisory, has been appointed to advise through a confidential off-market sale process.

An investment overview document that contains details on the properties and business assets will be made available to interested parties in early October.

"We are commencing preparation for the sale process and from our recent experience as advisers to some of the largest transactions in the Australian beef cattle industry we are confident that there will be significant interest," Mr Cameron said.

"Given the scale of the properties, size of the cattle herd and the extensive on-farm development Beetaloo is arguably one of the most improved large-scale cattle production businesses in Australia."

Beetaloo comprises 707,800 hectares, while Mungabroom covers 346,900 hectares.

The properties have been extensively developed since they were acquired in 2002, with 74 bores and 603 watering points covering more than 92 per cent of the total land area.

The aggregation is currently running about 85,000 head of Brahman and Brahman cross cattle. The properties have a carrying capacity over more than 100,000 cattle, which is unusually high.

Mr Cameron said Barkly Pastoral Co would seek interest in the properties from both Australian and overseas parties. Neither the family, nor Bentleys, commented on billionaire Brett Blundy's involvement in the sale process.

It is understood the retailer who purchased the neighbouring property Walhallow for $100 million from Macquarie Group's Paraway Pastoral last year, holds a debenture covering a substantial share of the Beetaloo business.

Mr Blundy has been growing his cattle station portfolio just as a number of notable Australian businesspeople such as Gina Rinehart, Andrew Forrest and Gerry Harvey have also been doing so.

Values for cattle stations have also started to rise especially with higher prices for beef owing to a shortage of supply in Australia.

A shortlist of interested parties for Beetaloo will be selected to proceed to due diligence in January next year.
direction: bullish for Australian agricultural land and pastoral sector

headline: SHARE PRICE & ACCUMULATION INDICESSOURCE: S&P INDEX SERVICES AUSTRALIA
intro: Share Price IndexAccumulation Index
direction: broad market consolidation with underperformance in energy and financials

headline: Think smart to stay in touch with customers
intro: Lift sales and reduce costs by getting a customer contact specialist to communicate with your clients, Jesse Matheson writes.
text:
Lift sales and reduce costs by getting a customer contact specialist to communicate with your clients, Jesse Matheson writes.

Getting and keeping in touch with customers is a challenge for any business, no matter how big or small.

If you manage an in-house contact centre, a key focus is reducing your costs. However, that can be overwhelming when you also have to manage day-to-day operations.

Outsourcing allows you to move the job of customer contact and communication off-site (either within Australia or overseas), enabling your team to focus on other important work.

However, if you think that using a business process outsourcer (BPO) for customer communication will mean you have less control or reduced results, think again.

TSA Group, an Australian-owned BPO, uses a hybrid of contact centres in Australia and the Philippines to help companies reduce costs, increase sales and drive customer advocacy.

Known as "SmartShoring", the process delivers a range of services from TSA Group's nine contact centres across Australia and two in the Philippines.

The services that can be outsourced to TSA Group include outbound and inbound phone-call campaigns, a range of digital methods (including email, SMS and online chat), and back-office processing services.

Because the work is split between the two countries, Australian business owners have more flexibility over their outsourced requirements than if they used a typical offshore-only approach, says Blaine Slater, TSA Group's head of business development.

"Our solutions teams based in Australia will meet with clients locally to discuss and map out all of the requirements," he says.

"Based on your strategic drivers, you will either give direction or we will help you work out where best to position your work."

TSA Group has been active in the outsourcing industry for almost two decades and moved into the Philippines over the past four years.

Since the move, it's been crucial to have Australians at every layer of its management structure in the Philippines, from centre manager to team leader.

The focus of this expat team is to develop local management and ensure that each program of work meets the desired competency level, delivered with an authentic Australian customer approach.

"This replicates how we manage our activity in Australia and is done by utilising the same Centre Management Operating Model across all of our sites," Slater says.

The Philippines has more than tripled its global share of the outsourcing market since 2004 . According to TSA Group, the Philippines now handles 12.3 per cent of the world's work in the industry. Other major incentives to use the Philippines as a contact centre location include labour costs, cultural compatibility and time zone.

"As Manila is in the same time zone as Perth, with access to a local talent pool of over 400,000 of college graduates each year, you can access quality staff," Slater says. No matter where you choose to outsource your work, he adds, you can be sure of "a consistent result and client experience".

Details: tsagroup.com.au
direction: bullish for TSA Group shares

headline: Infrastructure spend urged to kick-start growth
text:
The Turnbull government and superannuation funds are being challenged to ramp up investment in infrastructure projects to unlock a fresh wave of productivity-driven economic growth.

In a major report to be presented at a conference organised by Industry Super Australia next month in Parliament House, Canberra, the group has urged the Turnbull government to follow the NSW Baird government's example of engaging closely with the super sector to unlock potential projects.

The study, by economist Stephen Anthony, shows industry funds plan to invest at least $10 billion during the next five years in Australian airports, ports, roads and rail projects that will create 16,000 jobs.

"Our economy and overseas economies are misfiring because there is not enough capital expenditure, so we urgently need a pipeline of new projects," Mr Anthony told The Australian Financial Review. He said this requires super funds and governments "telling the story, so the community understands there's something here for them and that it's not just another word for privatisations."

The call to action comes amid something of a backlash against a global and domestic push by policymakers from the IMF to the Reserve Bank of Australia and Treasury for more infrastructure spending.

This week alone the Grattan Institute called for greater scrutiny of proposed spending plans, saying the 836 major transport projects in Australia since 2001 have cost $28 billion more than politicians promised they would.

In a similar vein, Deutsche Bank on Wednesday published an essay by its global equity strategists on "why you shouldn't invest in infrastructure", saying the private sector is likely to be in a position to fund no more than 20 per cent of the estimated $US1 trillion annual shortfall in required spending during the next 15 years.

Calls for fresh infrastructure spending are increasingly being heard among policymakers and politicians, who fear central bank stimulus is tapped out around the world and fuelling a dangerous inflation in asset prices, particularly for houses.

Mr Anthony, a former Treasury official who now works as Industry Super Australia's chief economist, argues the global economy is caught in a "vicious downward cycle" in which weak business investment is reducing trend economic growth rates, in turn, eating into further investment.

Super Australia's report calculates industry funds hold over $45 billion in unlisted infrastructure and property in Australia, and last year alone invested $2.8 billion.

In a crack at bank-run super funds, Mr Anthony says for-profit super funds and self-managed funds - which account for two-thirds of the $2 trillion pool of super savings - hold almost no unlisted infrastructure assets and only a small amount of listed assets.

With Infrastructure Australia putting the infrastructure shortfall around the nation about $80 billion, equivalent to about 7 per cent of the existing stock of infrastructure, industry funds are likely cover only about 12 per cent of the gap during the next decade.

Industry Super is hosting the November 21-22 conference with the Australian Council of Trade Unions and the Australian Industry Group, and is eager to challenge retail funds for what it says is a reluctance to invest in infrastructure.

The sector argues rather than investing in exotic financial products such a derivatives, it is putting money in the real economy.
direction: supportive for infrastructure and construction sector shares

headline: China's cement sector weighed down by itself
intro: In the three years to 2013, China consumed more concrete than the US used in the 20th century, according to the academic Vaclav Smil. China's economic growth has since slowed. Cement demand will never be as strong again. There remains, however, enough cement-making capacity in China to fill the peak appetite. If it is to earn positive economic returns, the industry has no choice but to shrink.
text:
In the three years to 2013, China consumed more concrete than the US used in the 20th century, according to the academic Vaclav Smil. China's economic growth has since slowed. Cement demand will never be as strong again. There remains, however, enough cement-making capacity in China to fill the peak appetite. If it is to earn positive economic returns, the industry has no choice but to shrink.

Chinese cement companies know this and believe that consolidation is the answer. The market may concur. Yesterday, shares in West China Cement rose nearly 6 per cent on news that a sufficient majority of option-holders had agreed to an acquisition by largest listed Chinese peer, Anhui Conch Cement. The deal should have the government's blessing, too. Last week, the State Council reiterated its desire for a more concentrated industry. It wants the top 10 players to control three-fifths of capacity by 2020. This is even less radical than it sounds. HSBC points out that the top 10 cement producers already controlled 54 per cent of the market as of the end of 2015. Nor will mergers achieve what the industry needs most: outright reduction in supply.

Consolidation has been showcased elsewhere. Last July, European giants Holcim and Lafarge merged to become the world's largest cement producer. Economies of scale did not spare the merged entity from having to announce a reduction in capex by November. Despite expected savings of $US1.6 billion ($2.2 billion) by 2017 and anticipated improved pricing power, the maiden set of results highlighted the need to conserve free cash flow. HeidelbergCement has been faring far better. It has, at last, digested its own overblown 2007 acquisition.

Enthusiasts for a cyclical China trade might point to a near-term pickup from stimulus-led demand. Last week, the government said it would spend nearly 7 per cent of gross domestic product on infrastructure projects over the next three years. Construction activity has improved as housing starts have rebounded. Fitch Ratings points out that clinker production capacity last year was 2 billion tonnes, compared with output of 1.3 billion. So, despite an increase in cement output of 13 per cent in the four months to April, prices over the same period actually dropped one-tenth. They look likely to keep sinking.
direction: bearish for Chinese cement sector

headline: Westfield's OneMarket defies Amazon
text:
Westfield's new retail tech spinout OneMarket is set to reshape the traditional shopping landscape, promising to connect bricks-and-mortar stores via a shared data network, enabling them to better compete with Amazon.

Freshly appointed chief executive of OneMarket, Don Kingsborough, told The Australian Financial Review the company had created a platform to connect retailers with shoppers when they were near stores, tapping into their search data to let them know when what they had been searching for was now in store, or could be bought for a cheaper price nearby.

"Retailers have grown up being isolated and they have never connected in a way in which they could share customer data," Mr Kingsborough said.

"They haven't had an operating system like Amazon where there's one identity like Prime and you can use that to buy all the things you want. That takes a network to do, so we've built a platform that connects all of these retailers and their isolated databases, so that a customer can search, buy and locate both online and in the physical world.

"We're taking the technology advantage and applying it to the physical world."

Rather than creating its own app, OneMarket is forming partnerships with global tech giants such as Facebook, Google and Apple, to enable the platform to communicate with shoppers and make purchases via existing networks such as Whatsapp, Facebook Messenger or text.

This component of the platform was at its heart, according to Mr Kingsborough, who said people were downloading fewer and fewer new apps.

"We're using the social apps already on your phone, which already have billions of users," he said. "For a network to be successful, you have to have scale and, secondly, you have to have customers that can use it seamlessly."

OneMarket was formed on the back of Westfield Labs and is based in San Francisco. Its chairman, Steven Lowy, told the Financial Review he had always intended to spin out the venture, which is set to list on the ASX next year with $200 million in cash, but the Westfield acquisition by Unibail-Rodamco had brought forward the timeline.

"It was always our intention to do two things: in the new year we were going to share with the investment community what our broader thoughts were for OneMarket, and we'd also made the conclusion that in time it would be the best solution for the business to be demerged from Westfield so that it could be what it is, a retail tech company, not a real estate company," he said.

"The investment thesis is vastly different and the investors are also vastly different. It's quite frankly earlier than we'd have done otherwise, but we were keen as part of the discussions to maintain the opportunity for Westfield shareholders to benefit from the hard work that's gone into getting us to this position today."

Westfield shareholders will own 90 per cent of OneMarket, but will have the option to sell out, with Mr Lowy asserting that the company has a very different risk profile to Westfield.

He said the company had been created from the outset with the "single objective" to help traditional retail survive the onslaught of companies such as Amazon.

How the listed company will do is going to be watched closely. Fund manager at SG Hiscock, Grant Berry, said the dedicated REIT funds were unlikely to own OneMarket, but that would not necessarily dampen the share price.

"Given lofty valuations for some tech stocks, who knows where it could potentially trade initially and in the medium term," he said.

OneMarket, formerly called Westfield Retail Solutions, has been externally focused for more than 18 months, since Mr Kingsborough, who is a former PayPal head of retail and founded retail gift card company Blackhawk, shifted from a non-executive director role with Westfield to running the tech division.

The company is remaining quiet for now on the financials of the business and how many retailers it has already locked in, but it is expected to provide an update to the market early in the new year.

Mr Kingsborough also said the business was in "test mode" and was intending to go live with a small group of retailers and shopping malls in the US and Britain at the end of March, before doing a wider rollout.

The platform will connect into retailers' existing technology systems via a set of application programming interfaces, so it can update in real time with what products are in stock and their prices across all the retailers.

Key pointsNew venture OneMarket aims to link shoppers with bricks-and-mortar retailers.

The spin-off business was formed on the back of Westfield Labs.
direction: bullish for Westfield and retail sector

headline: Renewing focus on fiscal policy
intro: The economy Loose monetary policy means the real effect of a potential credit downgrade may not be as great as the political one.
text:
The economy Loose monetary policy means the real effect of a potential credit downgrade may not be as great as the political one.

The Mid Year Economic and Financial Outlook is scheduled for December 19 and with it, no doubt, will come the usual rhetoric about the need to balance the budget to maintain the nation's AAA credit rating.

Fiscal policy is not the concern, however, of the institutional investors we speak to in Australia or overseas. Rather it is the impact of monetary policy and the risks associated with high household debt and house prices.

The heavy reliance on monetary policy to cushion the drag on the economy during the downturn in commodity prices and mining investment, has seen household debt rise to some of the highest levels among global peers and with it surging house prices in Sydney and Melbourne.

Yet a focus on fiscal stimulus could prove effective. Modelling commissioned by Citi from former Treasury and OECD economist Peter Downes, showed that a fiscal stimulus package of 1 per cent of gross domestic product could have obviated the need for up to 100 basis points of monetary policy easing.

At the global level there is a shift in the mindset of central bankers. Highly accommodative monetary policy is arguably becoming less popular as inflation troughs and as the relative cost of some ultra-accommodative regimes - such as operational issues and adverse effects on financial stability - are borne out.

And the budget outlook is still manageable. Despite low national income and budget revenue growth, some significant new expenditure commitments and a hostile Senate, the budget deficit as forecast in MYEFO is likely to remain similar to the last few years' deficit of 2 per cent to 2.5 per cent of GDP, with debt still well below global peers. Yes, there is still a structural gap between recurrent spending levels and revenue, so more needs to be done in that area, but not at the expense of productive investment.

The impact on the cost of funding for Australian banks and companies is often put forward as an argument for maintaining the AAA rating.

A one-notch sovereign downgrade would probably be mirrored with a one-notch downgrade by the domestic banks from AA- to A+. If this happens, Citi's credit analyst expects domestic banks' wholesale funding costs to increase by up to 15-20 basis points. Wholesale debt for the major banks, however, remains low and corporate bond spreads across the investment-grade credit spectrum are below the post-GFC average.

Evidence shows that other advanced economies that have experienced a one-notch downgrade (for example, Britain and the US) don't necessarily face higher sovereign borrowing costs or a widening in credit spreads. In June this year, a rating agency reduced its rating on Britain by two notches from AAA to AA. This was preceded by another agency cutting its rating on Britain from AA+ to AA. However, shortly after these ratings downgrades, yields on the benchmark 10-year gilt actually fell to a record low. The spread to US 10-year Treasuries also moved in favour of gilts.

A similar story unfolded in New Zealand in September 2011, when a ratings agency downgraded it from AA+ to AA. This was initially followed by slightly higher yields on the New Zealand 10-year government bond, but the yield rise was temporary and was shortly followed by sharp falls that completely reversed the initial gains.

There are two main reasons for the apparent contradiction of lower credit ratings and lower yields. First, much of the ratings change had already been priced in by the market and hence did not come as a surprise, which explains why yields didn't jump up immediately after the downgrades. Second, in both Britain and New Zealand the poor economic outlooks that triggered the downgrades were also responsible for the growing expectation of monetary easing by the central banks. In both cases, monetary policy easing dominated market expectations and relegated the credit downgrades to the back seats as drivers of yields.

Of course there is politics in losing the AAA rating. Australian history shows that governments who preside over ratings losses risk suffering the political stigma of being perceived as poor fiscal managers for some time afterwards. The community needs be better educated about the use of credit ratings and the benefits of public sector infrastructure spending.

More investment in worthwhile infrastructure projects by both the public and private sectors could help boost productivity growth and be complementary to business investment. Improving the pricing regime of infrastructure would help maximise the potential productivity benefits.

Paul Brennan is chief economist, and Josh Williamson a senior economist at Citi Australia.
direction: bearish for rate-sensitive sectors like banks and property due to high household debt and housing price risks

headline: Housing fix needs a 'feather' not shock
text:
Policies aimed at stopping galloping house prices from destabilising the economy must be applied "like a feather" to avoid a shock, which could halt apartment projects.

National Australia Bank chief executive Andrew Thorburn said such construction in outer Sydney and Melbourne was crucial as people continued to move to both cities.

As housing affordability continues to occupy the national conversation, he lamented the short-term nature of the policy debate, which he said had exacerbated a shortage of housing - the main driver of higher prices.

"You have to have a five to 10-year view on tax, city developments, [and] infrastructure," Mr Thorburn said in a briefing with reporters on Tuesday. "What we need is some long-term leadership - and that is very difficult to see happening in the current environment." The moderate new cap of 30 per cent of new lending being interest-only introduced by the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority was the latest measure to tighten lending but may have a limited impact because regulators "are trying to fight a fundamentally momentum-driven phenomena", he said.

Annual house price growth in Sydney of 18.9 per cent and 15.9 per cent in Melbourne had been largely driven by population growth as people moved for work, while housing supply was "not moving fast enough", he said.

Banks and regulators should be focused on the risks from large residential tower developments, but "we have to be careful we don't overreact".

Policy responses to housing needed to be applied "like a feather, because otherwise you could stop apartments being built," he said.

"And in the outer rings of Sydney and Melbourne, they are needed. Low rise, 30-in-a-block [developments] are needed in places like Hawthorn, and equivalent suburbs in Sydney."

Because interest rates and unemployment remain low and the economy is still growing, many borrowers still want to take on relatively large mortgages that they can afford. Potentially curbing their ability to get in to the market was "a very difficult challenge" that could create new problems if potential homeowners were turned down for loans and the market continued to rise, he said.

"If people are kept out of the market because of the rules, and they see prices continuing to go [up], and they say 'I can afford a 15 per cent deposit but if you make it 40 per cent, like they have done in New Zealand, I can't get in' - that is going to be interesting," Mr Thorburn said.

Responding to the speech delivered by APRA chairman Wayne Byres to The Australian Financial Review Banking & Wealth summit last week, Mr Thorburn said a mid-year consultation on the "risk weights" banks apply to mortgages "is not surprising to us, because it is not a U-turn or new story". APRA's message has been consistent, he said - banks needed to be unquestionably strong.

Wherever APRA landed on capital levels, Mr Thorburn said he expected banks would be able to respond by raising funds through dividend reinvestment plans and building more capital organically, as opposed to conducted large equity raisings. "Through bank DRP and potential underwrites [and] capital generation ... the banks have options to do things in an orderly way, so the economy can keeping adjusting through it and not have shocks."
direction: bearish for residential construction and property developers

headline: BHP calls end of the price rout
intro: BHP Billiton has committed to growing cash flow and has declared commodity prices have stopped falling, after slashing its dividend and posting its biggest loss since the miner was formed through a merger in 2001.
text:
BHP Billiton has committed to growing cash flow and has declared commodity prices have stopped falling, after slashing its dividend and posting its biggest loss since the miner was formed through a merger in 2001.

After announcing a $US6.38 billion ($8.27 billion) loss for the 2016 financial year, BHP chief executive Andrew Mackenzie said free cash flow would almost double to $US7 billion in fiscal 2017 if current commodity prices persisted.

He said his agenda of productivity improvements and cost-cutting would improve margins as prices slowly improved.

While he stopped short of saying the worst was over for the resources sector, Mr Mackenzie said commodity prices had stopped falling and would trade in line with recent levels.

"When we look to the medium term we expect prices to trade more or less in line ... There is a strong sense that prices have stopped falling as opposed to being in free fall," he said.

"That pause in the price fall means that we can now continue to drive down costs and open up a margin, which instead of only sharing with our customers we can now share with the investment in our company and with our investors.

"We have more or less halved our costs in unit terms over the last three to four years."

On an underlying basis, the $US1.21 billion profit was better than the $US1.09 billion analysts had expected. But total dividends for the year of US30¢ were below market consensus and dramatically lower than the $US1.24 paid to shareholders during fiscal 2015.

BHP's spending on new projects has been declining since its outlay topped $US21.7 billion in fiscal 2013, but it looks set to start creeping higher again, with the $US5.4 billion spend in fiscal 2017 to be followed by $US6.2 billion in the 2018 financial year.

A portion of that spending will likely be on the second phase of the Mad Dog oil project, which is expected to cost BHP $US2.5 billion and be approved before Christmas 2017.

The long awaited development of the Spence hypogene project in Chile should also be approved by the end of 2017.

Mr Mackenzie acknowledged the rising spend, but insisted he would keep a tight rein on capital commitments.

"As I look forward it is possible as margins open up and we see opportunities for growth that there may be a slight increase in capital but I wouldn't put it more strongly than that, and I think we can do pretty well at numbers equivalent to the average of the year just closed and the year ahead," he said.

"We are able to run this company with a lot less annual capital but still do many of the things we planned to do."

Aberdeen Asset Management is the second largest holder of BHP's London-listed shares, and senior investment manager Michelle Lopez said she wanted to see BHP make sensible decisions over its balance sheet and capital spending.

"We prefer internal investment if they have attractive projects that generate a sufficient return on capital over and above its cost of capital through the cycle. This should drive longer-term profits, dividends and ultimately the share price," she said.

"Sensible capital expenditure will likely manifest itself by BHP de-risking projects, including breaking down the way project planning and construction is carried out into more manageable iterative capital sums."

Tribeca Global Natural Resources Fund portfolio manager Ben Cleary said he was not expecting a dramatic increase in spending on growth, and he said plans for the struggling US onshore petroleum division remained unclear.

"Both BHP and Rio Tinto have underperformed most mid-caps year to date, their balance sheets are stretched, they can't sustain dividends and can't grow by acquisition at what is arguably the bottom of the cycle, confirming the poor record of asset allocation over the past 10 years," he said.

Morgans analyst Adrian Prendergast noted that, unlike recent years, the dividend payment was covered by free cash flow.

Shaw and Partners analyst Peter O'Connor said BHP was well placed operationally for fiscal 2017, and he said the company's debt and dividend situations should improve.

"Fiscal 2016 will very likely be seen as the earnings nadir," he said, noting that commodity prices were currently well above the average levels seen during the past year.

The underlying profit was well below last year's $US6.4 billion underlying result, which itself was the company's lowest profit since August 2004. Exceptional items tallied $US7.7 billion after tax.

Most of the exceptional items, $US6.1 billion after tax to be exact, were reported in BHP's half-year results in February. Among those, the carrying value of the US onshore petroleum assets was impaired by $US4.9 billion, while "global taxation matters" were behind a $US390 million charge.

The Samarco dam disaster was responsible for $US858 million of impairments in the first half, and that number grew in the second half as it became clear that BHP and joint venture partner Vale would need to dip into their own pockets to cover Samarco's costs.

Total charges related to Samarco over the year were $US2.1 billion after tax, and taxation charges ultimately rose to $US540 million.

BHP said it expected a technical report into the cause of the Samarco disaster would be published in coming weeks.

Commodity prices continued to work against the company in fiscal 2016, with average prices for its seven major commodities sliding year-on-year. Average iron ore prices declined 28 per cent year-on-year, while prices for copper and West Texas Intermediate crude oil were down 24 per cent and 36 per cent respectively.

Prices for benchmark Australian coking and thermal coals fell 22 per cent and 15 per cent respectively, compared with the average in fiscal 2015.

Despite iron ore prices sliding close to 70 per cent since the peak of the mining boom in February 2011, the bulk commodity continues to be the largest contributor to the earnings of BHP and its old rival Rio Tinto.
direction: BHP shares likely to stabilize or rise modestly

headline: Telstra boss Penn takes $1.6m pay cut
text:
Telstra chief executive Andy Penn will take home $5.21 million this year, a pay cut of almost $1.6 million as the telco giant's plunging share price hits CEO pay.

Mr Penn's is the latest in a string of CEO pay cuts this reporting season, including at Commonwealth Bank, Wesfarmers, AMP and CEOs including Seven West Media boss Tim Worner, former Brambles CEO Tom Gorman and Vocus CEO Geoff Horth joining the no-bonus club.

The departure of Australia Post CEO Ahmed Fahour has put the focus back on CEO pay, despite research by Egan Associates which shows the 25 new chief executives appointed to top 200 companies over the past 18 months received an average 25 per cent pay cut from their predecessors.

Mr Penn's statutory reported pay remained flat at $5.66 million compared to $5.64 million last year. However, the company also disclosed their executives' take-home pay, which revealed Mr Penn's pay pain.

The Telstra chief executive earned $2.3 million in fixed pay, a $1.49 million bonus and $1.04 million in shares, bringing his take-home pay to $5.21 million, which was disclosed in Friday's annual Telstra report.

The big decline was in Mr Penn's long-term incentive, which was worth $2.8 million last year based on a share price of $5.56, compared with the June 30 share price of $4.30 this year. Telstra finished flat at $3.67 on Friday.

Telstra is in a state of flux as it grapples with an annual $3 billion black hole created by the end of the rollout of the national broadband network and the loss of much of its wholesale business.

Telstra shares have been down nearly 30 per cent this year and hit a five-year low on August 17 after the company lifted full-year profit 1 per cent to $3.9 billion but said it would cut future dividends to fund network investment.

While Telstra left its 2016-17 dividend payout unchanged at 31¢, the company expects total dividends would fall to 22¢ in 2017-18. This includes ordinary and special dividends to be paid as a result of one-off payments from NBN.

The telco announced it will overhaul its remuneration plan next year to combine its short-term and long-term structure into one simplified version and extend the overall pay plan from four years to five years. Telstra said there is no change to the maximum opportunity its senior executives can earn.

"Our existing long-term incentive plan is complex and we believe a simpler remuneration model is in the interest of shareholders," Telstra said on Friday.

Other top-paid Telstra executives who also wore some pay pain included head of GE Global Enterprise Brendon Riley, whose reported pay fell from $3.57 million to $3.23 million.
direction: bearish for Telstra shares

headline: Telstra secures undersea cables on key Asian routes
text:
Telstra is combining three undersea cable systems to ensure its customers throughout Asia are not left stranded without reliable internet connections due to damage caused by natural disasters and ships.

Natural disasters, such as the 2011 earthquake and tsunami in Japan, and typhoons around Taiwan and the Philippines, can and have damaged undersea fibre-optic cables, which carry 97 per cent of the world's phone and internet traffic.

Telstra director of emerging markets Paul Abfalter told The Australian Financial Review the new "Always On" guarantee, which the telecommunications provider will announce on Monday at the Pacific Telecommunications Council conference in Hawaii, will allow its customers who need to use some of Asia's busiest routes, such as Hong Kong to Singapore, and Japan to Hong Kong, a cheaper alternative to paying for two systems, one as primary and the other as a back-up.

"If you're a Google, Netflix, Facebook, a carrier or a low-latency financial services provider, you need your service up 24/7," Mr Abfalter said.

Damage to undersea cables can have an impact on internet connections, upload and download speeds and latency that can affect transactions, high-frequency trading, real-time communications and video games among others.

For example, Australian internet users in December 2014 were hit with slow connections after damage to a cable between Perth and Singapore, which was thought to be caused by an earthquake or a ship's anchor. Damage to an undersea cable in the Arabian Sea in 2005 severed almost all communication in and out of Pakistan.

"We operate three different cable systems intra-Asia and we have the largest network, and the technology is getting to the point where we'll automatically be able to route customers [in the event of cable damage]," Mr Abfalter said.

"Initially we'll do that manually, it will only take four to eight hours, for the other operators it will take them weeks to get a ship out to fix the cable. In six to 12 months' time ... we'll be able to automatically route."

Demand for data in Asia is growing and undersea cables act as the arteries keeping the digital world connected. Telstra will be hoping that it can entice new customers and cement old ones with the guarantee, meaning they would not need to purchase from multiple vendors and potentially pay for unused capacity.

Telstra's $857 million acquisition of Pacnet in 2014 gave the telco a major boost in the Asia region. Two of the three systems it is combining to offer its new 'Always On' guarantee came from that acquisition, while the final system was a legacy network Telstra owned.

Telstra executive director of global sales Ellie Sweeney said the telco's undersea network accounted for up to 30 per cent of intra-regional capacity in the Asia Pacific.

"The strength and capacity of our network means customers can be assured that all of their subscribed bandwidth - whether it is 10GB (gigabyte) or 1TB (terabyte) - can be supported on the same route in the event of a single or multiple outages on the primary path," she said.
direction: bullish for Telstra shares

headline: Loy Yang EBA deal sealed
text:
AGL has successfully removed historic union restrictions on redundancies and outsourcing at its Loy Yang A power station after workers backed a new workplace agreement following two years of contentious bargaining.

Most of the power station and mine's employees avoided an expected Fair Work Commission arbitration on their conditions by voting up AGL's proposed deal in a secret ballot on Monday.

The Construction, Forestry, Mining and Energy Union, the Electrical Trades Union and AGL had been ordered into 21 days of conciliation before possible arbitration in June after the Victorian government intervened to stop AGL's proposed industrial action on grounds it would threaten the state economy.

The new four-year agreement grants workers 5 per cent annual pay increases but removes a long-standing no forced redundancies clause, restrictions on staff levels and the use of contractors.

AGL chief executive Andy Vesey said the vote was "a very good outcome" that would allow the company to manage its Loy Yang operations with a "modern" enterprise agreement.

"The new enterprise agreement allows for salary increases while providing operational flexibilities that are essential to the long-term sustainability of the site in the context of the ongoing transformation of the electricity market," he said.

CFMEU district secretary Geoff Dyke said the union agreed to the deal after recognising that, in past arbitrations, the commission had viewed no forced redundancies clauses as "social welfare" that "compelled a business to subsidise employment".

"We were denied the right to strike, we were denied the right to bargaining, so we took what we could salvage."
direction: AGL shares likely to rise on improved operational flexibility and cost control

headline: Investor splurge heaps pressure on banks, APRA
text:
Banks are almost certain to come under growing pressure to rein in lending after an explosion in investor borrowing confirms regulators are at risk of losing control of an overheating property market.

Experts have warned that unless a rapid re-accelleration in investor lending is countered, the Reserve Bank may also be forced to hike official interest rates sooner than it wants to, potentially undermining a broader economic expansion by crunching heavily indebted households and driving up the dollar.

Official figures published on Friday showed housing approvals rocketed to a record in January, with the value of money lent to investors surging 4.2 per cent in the month.

Over the year, lending to investors has now lurched up 27.5 per cent, just as the proportion of loans to first-time buyers fell to 13.4 per cent, which is close to the lowest rate in almost 13 years.

Evidence that investors are shaking off regulators' attempts to cool the market in late 2015 and early 2016 come amid disagreement within the government over whether to pare back the capital gains discount for property investors in the May budget.

Renewed alarm about the pace of investment lending came as new analysis showed that high rise developers are propping up prices by repurchasing apartments that might otherwise be sold on the open market at a lower price.

Apartments put on the open market within 12 to 18 months of completion are often being resold for up to 30 per cent less than the original sale price, a reduction including transaction costs and off the plan tax savings, valuers' analysis of sales in Melbourne, Sydney and Brisbane reveal.

Bill Evans, Westpac's chief economist, has repeatedly warned that regulators will need to adopt even more stringent so-called "macro prudential policies to slow housing in the second half" of this year.

Analysis by Westpac shows Friday's figures imply lending to investors is now "getting close" to the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority's 10 per cent speed-limit for annual growth in lending to the sector.

APRA bases its limit on figures for actual loans taken out - which reached an annual pace of 6.6 per cent in January. Friday's figures for the same month were for loan commitments.

"Lenders will either have to start reining in growth in investor loan approvals back to a sub 10 per cent annual pace in coming months or run the risk of sanctions from the regulator," Westpac said in a note to investors.

At the same time, with property prices surging more than 18 per cent in Sydney over the past year through January, there are growing signs of market manipulation by developers.

"Developers often do not want their high rise apartments to go on the open market until they are all sold," said Tony Kelly, managing director of valuation group Herron Todd White (Melbourne), which has been monitoring prices. "An apartment sold on the open market for a lower price could tarnish the brand."

The apartments were typically originally sold to off-the-plan buyers by commission earning property spruikers between 12 months and three years ago and are coming up for completion, which means buyers have to make final payments to complete the deal.

Many were sold to foreign buyers, particularly from mainland China, who often use them to offshore assets.

Ming Li, a director of Aussie Home Real Estate, claims up to 80 per cent of mainland buyers "would like to get their deposit back and walk away from the deal".

Dr Li said foreign buyers had been hit by a crackdown on local banks lending to overseas' borrowers, capital export restrictions by Chinese authorities and falling value of the apartments.

"Our clients are asking for settlements to be postponed for six months," Dr Li, whose company specialises in offshore and mainland buyers, said. "The developers have no choice but to agree."

Overseas' buyers are hoping for a relaxation of banking and capital tightening or the availability of alternative finance. Hong Kong and Singapore funds are offering interest rates of between 7 and 8 per cent. Some developers are providing vendor finance of between 6 and 7 per cent.

Citigroup, which has a high profile in Asia and is a popular source of funding, is limiting overseas mortgage lending to elite high-net-worth clients with minimum deposits or investments of $250,000 because of concerns about its capacity to handle the number of applications after the withdrawal of most other major Australian banks.

Local Chinese language websites are also reselling apartments with discounts of up to 10 per cent, which is typically what the original buyer paid for the deposit.

Described as 'nomination platforms', they enable the original purchaser to nominate a new buyer.

Nicholas Smedley, managing director of Steller, a property developer specialising in medium density apartments, said the problem appeared to be with high rise apartments.

The Herron Todd White analysis reveals a fall in apartment prices of between 10-20 per cent in the 18 months between when the apartment was purchased off the plan and resale. That could mean a $130,000 loss on a $650,000 apartment. Stamp duty, legal fees and agent's fees could add another 10 per cent in costs and losses.

"It is difficult to identity an overall average fall because the market is so diverse," says Robert Papaleo, national executive director of Charter Keck Cramer, a property advisory company. "But 30 per cent would be exceptional and reflect that off the plan contracts attract stamp duty savings (in some states)," he said.
direction: downward pressure on bank shares due to regulatory tightening and rate hike risks

headline: All the economy needs now is someone to spend
text:
Oh, dear. Just when everyone thought it couldn't get any worse on the wages front comes news that a raft of workers will now be taking a pay cut when they work on a Sunday.

Welcome to the 24/7 economy, where apparently it doesn't really matter what day you work, as long as you're working.

It all comes hot on the heels of the shared economy, that is also causing disruption through Uber and Airbnb.

In essence Sunday penalties were a good example of the trade-off between the cost of labour and having a job.

In the past they have simply reflected the underlying market forces of supply and demand.

Now these penalty rates are getting cut, however, it means those who currently receive them are going to be worse off.

Of course, in theory, more people are going to be employed now and more will start spending if it's cheaper for small business to keep their shops open for longer.

But will that really happen?

According to Fair Work Commissioner Iain Ross it will.

By cutting rates he says it means trading hours will be extended and shops can offer a wider range of services.

The exact opposite happens now because those rates make it just too expensive for many businesses to open on a Sunday.

There can be no doubt the local economy is becoming a tough environment for unskilled workers in general but this will also be seen as another win for business.

It all comes as the latest report on wages growth from the Australian Bureau of Statistics this week showed just how hard it is to get a pay rise.

Workers now are waiting almost twice as long as they used to for a pay rise, 21 months compared to 12 months previously, while those who are lucky enough to get one, have to put up with just a small amount when they do eventually get it.

It makes it hard to see who is going to do the extra spending.

As the governor of the Reserve Bank Philip Lowe also pointed out this week, there's more than enough households under the pump from a "sobering combination" of high debt levels and slow wages growth that will drag on spending.

It's a fair bet that group won't be opening their wallets on a Sunday on the back of a promise of longer trading hours and more to spend your money on.

And it's worth noting the youth unemployment rate is around 13.5 per cent, with around 300,000 people aged 15 to 24 without a job. It won't be them.

For many, Sunday is still a day for family and other domestic activities.

I work to live, I don't live for work, as someone once said.

The next mission for business will be to get those corporate tax cuts, but some argue over how effective they will be, too.

If the latest numbers on how much corporates are planning to invest in their business is any guide then the tax cuts can't come quick enough.

On Thursday the ABS reported that spending by companies on new buildings, equipment and machinery fell for a fourth straight quarter in the three months through December.

Bottom line is businesses have been on a spending strike since 2013.

But will a corporate tax cut spark a spending spree? Businesses are just as much a hostage to the economic cycle as households.

If households aren't spending why would business start?

Normally they spend and invest in tandem so you could argue that a cut in the personal tax rate is just as, if not more, important than a cut in the corporate tax rate.

Perpetual's Matthew Sherwood reckons that "investment is shrinking in its share of most modern economies, as the service sector is less capital intensive than manufacturing".

He thinks lower personal taxes "would encourage more consumer spending and this will absorb spare capacity faster and spark an investment response".

Maybe we are all lucky just to have a job.

In another 10 or 15 years there is enough research floating around that says almost 40 per cent of jobs will be at risk of being taken over by computers.

It's safe to assume those computers won't mind working on a weekend.
direction: bearish for unskilled workers and consumer spending, bullish for small business margins

headline: Self-regulatory model for the digital economy
text:
Data Governance Australia has published a draft code of conduct for collecting and sharing customer data that it hopes will become the centrepiece of a self-regulatory model, which staves off excessive government regulation of data standards in the new digital economy.

National Australia Bank, Westpac, IAG, Coles, Woolworths and Qantas have all been working on the code with Data Governance Australia, which is chaired by former Australian Competition and Consumer Commission chairman Graeme Samuel.

DGA, which was established in October to promote the responsible use of data, will be accepting submissions on the code for the next month.

The publication of the draft code on Wednesday comes after the Productivity Commission last month called for consumers to receive a "comprehensive right" to their data held by companies and government authorities. The commission recognised community confidence in data security and handling is essential in order for Australia to harness data to drive innovation and economic growth.

More companies are also seeking to share data with each other to gain better insights into their customers to better tailor products and marketing. The DGA code is designed to engender consumer trust and drive transparency by establishing standards for data-handling through principles governing the responsible collection, use, management and disclosure of data.

Mr Samuel recognised that many consumers remain sceptical about how data they generate is being used by corporate Australia but said regulation typically lags community expectations.

"The whole idea of the code is to have industry reflect on what community expectations are and to be able to react more quickly to community concerns and adjust behaviour accordingly," he said. "The use of data is changing extraordinarily. Every day of the week, massive changes are occurring. We are saying if you sign up to the code, people will trust you."

The code stipulates signatories must ensure that they do no harm to consumers by collecting or using data. They must also consider consumer "fairness" and only use the data for actual or anticipated legitimate business purposes. The code also sets out requirements for data transparency, accuracy, access, accountability and security.

A Code Authority has also been established to enforce it, chaired by consumer advocate Christopher Zinn.

The code allows for additional clauses to be added in the future as community or government expectations change, Mr Samuel said, and he called on the Department of Prime Minister and Cabinet - which is considering the government's response to the Productivity Commission's data report - to provide feedback on the code.

Ultimately, "it might be a less costly and more flexible way to deal with community issues relating to data," Mr Samuel said, compared to developing detailed legislation as recommended by the commission.

There are currently about 100 members of the DGA, which operates alongside the Australian Data Marketing Association. DGA plans on driving membership higher when the final document is finalised. The plan is for the code to be registered with the Australian Securities and Investments Commission. The banks have been calling for governance standards to be created to facilitate an "open banking" regime, a policy that the government committed to in the federal budget.

DGA board member Paul McCarney, co-founder and CEO of Data Republic, originally conceived the idea of a data industry code two years ago. "We are at the stage where huge productivity gains are about to be unlocked from people leveraging their data," he said. "As this continues, there could be increasing confusion about how people are treating data, and there needs to be an understanding about accountability to the market in how data is treated for customers."
direction: supportive for data-intensive sectors like banks and retailers

headline: No time for advisers to bury heads in the sand
text:
To be fair to financial advisers, they have few details to work with but it's clear they need to start thinking about their future now.

They have few details because the individual charged with overseeing new education and ethics standards for planners does not start in his role until September 18. But the basic requirements are known and financial advisers do not have much time to get ready for the new regime - or get out.

Sadly, those who bury their heads in the sand and make no decision about whether they will get with the new program or leave the industry could end up paying dearly.

The new requirements come after a series of financial planning scandals. Designed to turn advice into a profession by raising the education and ethical standards, they come into force from January 1, 2020 for existing advisers, by which point they will need to have signed up to a code of ethics.

Advisers who are already practising have until January 1, 2021 to pass a competency or registration exam and until January 2024 to obtain a relevant degree, or degree equivalent.

These dates might sound a long way off but they are not, particularly for advisers who own their practice and so will need to find a way to get out of their business if they decide the new regime is all too difficult.

Rod Bertino, director of Business Health, a business advisory practice based in Sydney, suspects that many will do just that.

"We've got a major education requirement looming. A lot of people will exit the industry," Bertino predicts.

The problem for business owners who decide it is all too difficult is that if they don't act soon, there will be a flood of similar businesses on the market at the same time. That does not bode well for the balance between supply and demand and hence for prices.

"This is a huge sleeper issue. There will be a significant price to pay for those who don't get ahead of the curve," Bertino says, noting that only about 12 per cent of advice businesses have effective succession strategies in place.

Worryingly for the other 88 per cent of businesses, Bertino questions whether buyer of last resort facilities, which provide a baseline valuation for practices, will be a reliable insurance policy.

Dante de Gori, chief executive of the Financial Planning Association, agrees time is of the essence.

"Planners have got to be thinking about it. They can't wait too much longer," he says.

Advisers need to understand that selling planning outfits is often a protracted affair, perhaps one that could take five years to complete. Principals who go the internal succession route need to first identify a suitably young, talented, entrepreneurial would-be buyer, who may not be sitting in the office, and groom them to take over the practice.

Given the long lead times, advisers may not have the luxury of waiting to see what the new education and ethical requirements will look like.

Deen Sanders was appointed to run the Financial Adviser Standards and Ethics Authority (FASEA) by Financial Services Minister Kelly O'Dwyer a little over two weeks ago and does not start for another couple of weeks. He has a barrage of issues to consider and decisions to make, such as what the competency test will look like, what it will test and how it will be structured, which degrees will be admissible and what will be acceptable as degree equivalent.

Practice owners may be thinking they will decide what to do when the exact requirements are known, but it may be too late.

At the very least they need to decide whether they will be prepared to up their skills or start laying the groundwork for the next owner.

As an incentive to mapping out the future, Bertino argues sound succession planning invariably leads to improvements in the bottom line.

He says that practices with effective succession planning in place earn on average 110 per cent more than practices that do not, partly because establishing a succession plan means establishing goals and knowing what successful outcomes look like.
direction: bearish for financial advice firms and related equities

headline: China's powerful jockey for best spots in the nation's inner circle
text:
China's five-yearly transfer of power is fast approaching and the first of many leadership lists have started to circulate.

A new Politburo Standing Committee, the Communist Party's top leadership group, will be announced at the National Party Congress due to be held in October or November.

The make-up of this group is the subject of intense speculation given China's opaque political system and the emergence of Chinese President Xi Jinping as the most powerful leader in decades.

Mr Xi is almost certain to maintain his positions at the head of the Party and the military but there is debate about whether Premier Li Keqiang will stay in his role. Some analysts predict he could be replaced by Wang Qishan, who has steered Mr Xi's vast anti-corruption campaign.

For this to happen, Mr Xi would need to disregard informal retirement rules to allow the 69-year-old Mr Wang to remain on the Standing Committee and take on the new position.

There also is great interest in whether Mr Xi will opt out of the custom to anoint a successor at the halfway mark of his decade-long tenure. The recent sacking and investigation of former Chongqing party chief and rising political star Sun Zhengcai has stoked speculation Mr Xi could fseek a third five-year term as leader.

"Xi Jinping is not seen as grooming any successor," says Willy Lam, adjunct professor at the Chinese University of Hong Kong's Centre for China Studies.

"He has strengthened control over the party and the military and it is hard to see any real or potential challenge to Xi Jinping."

If the new Standing Committee sticks to the unofficial rule of retirement at age 68, five positions among the seven-member group will become vacant, with only Mr Xi and Mr Li remaining. Among those vying for a spot is Wang Yang, 62, a vice-premier who was a contender in 2012 but missed out.

Mr Wang is regarded as a political reformer who favours a pro-market approach to running the economy. He has featured heavily in the state-owned media in recent months, a strong indicator of whether a candidate is in favour, and has recently been given some high-profile tasks. Last week, he was sent to Nepal amid a tense standoff between India and China over a border dispute.

Other potential leadership candidates include close advisers Li Zhanshu and Wang Huning, who have accompanied Mr Xi on most of his overseas trips. Mr Li is like a chief of staff to Mr Xi, while Mr Wang is head of the Communist Party's Central Policy Research Office. Mr Xi's top economic adviser Liu He and Shanghai Party Secretary Han Zheng, are also often mentioned in leadership speculation, the former because of his expertise in a year when China's economy is expected to slow and the latter because his current role is often one associated with promotion.

Perhaps of most interest will be whether the new team includes Guangdong Party Secretary Hu Chunhua, who along with Mr Sun was one of the younger leaders promoted to the 25-member Politburo in 2012.

His inclusion in the Standing Committee could signal Mr Xi is paving the way for a potential successor after all. Some analysts believe this unlikely because Mr Hu, or "Little Hu" as he is often called, is a protege of Mr Xi's predecessor, Hu Jintao, a rival powerbroker. Both worked in Tibet for long periods.

Still, the younger Mr Hu has one of China's most important provincial posts, heading Guangdong, which has a larger economy than Mexico or Indonesia. In a speech at the provincial Party Congress this year he made a show of declaring his political loyalty, mentioning Xi Jinping no fewer than 26 times.
direction: increased political uncertainty for China-related assets

headline: Glencore secures more copper in Queensland
text:
Glencore has secured the first offtake agreement from the revived Thalanga mine in central Queensland.

As the Swiss mining house competes with Yancoal in the sale of Rio Tinto's Hunter Valley thermal coal assets, it emerged as the winner in a competitive offtake process for the copper concentrate from Red River Resources' Thalanga mine.

Red River picked up the zinc, copper and lead mine from the administrators of Kagara in 2014 and is on track to return the shuttered mine to production in the fourth quarter of 2017 at a capital cost of just $17.2 million.

Managing director Mel Palancian said the emerging producer was pleased Glencore had emerged as its copper offtake partner after a long process that kicked off in late 2016.

"Glencore is a well-known, well-understood business partner and it is important that we have a counter-party that has some strength behind it," he said.

Glencore will purchase copper concentrate for three years from the Thalanga mine gate, which Mr Palancian said mitigated any risk associated with Glencore's warning last month it could no longer guarantee its copper processing facilities, which include a smelter in Mount Isa and refinery in Townsville, largely due to high energy costs.

"That is a state-wide issue that impacts everyone in Queensland and we are all doing our best to be competitive, but the agreement we have with Glencore is that they will take the concentrate at the mine gate," he said.

"What they choose to do with it from that point on is really up to them."

Red River has progressed to a short list of parties for Thalanga's lead and zinc offtake agreements.

Once Red River gets Thalanga into production it will be the first "pure" zinc producer on the ASX. Zinc last traded at about $US2500 a tonne, up from just over $US2000 a tonne this time last year but softer than a peak of almost $US3000 a tonne in February. "The fundamentals for zinc look very good - the stockpiles are dropping, the arbitrage between prices has opened up and now we are finally seeing a response in metal premiums with the zinc metal premium jumping significantly," Mr Palancian said.
direction: bullish for Red River Resources shares

headline: Consolidation tipped to grow
text:
There was intense merger and acquisition activity in the fleet and salary-packaging industry at the turn of the century, but after the global financial crisis things went quiet.

Now, with stable markets and access to funds, some of the big players are pursuing consolidation by looking to pick up smaller competitors.

Campbell Jones, CEO of inventory solutions at Cox Automotive Australia, said: "This will be an interesting area to watch in 2017 from both a fleet leasing and a dealer perspective."We see no major new entrants into either marketplace and only see the big getting bigger and growing their individual portfolios."

Simon Johnson, principal of corporate finance at Pitcher Partners, has been working with several clients to grow through acquisition. "What we are seeing in the last three years is larger groups looking for bolt-on acquisitions," he said.

"Some of these larger businesses are not growing potentially as much as they have in the past organically and, while capital is cheap to get, growth by acquisition is a good strategy."

Scale is another reason for existing players to look at acquisitions and Guy Steel, chief financial officer at WEX Australia, believes cost pressures will bring more consolidation activity.
direction: bullish for fleet leasing and salary-packaging sector

headline: Rio cuts jobs as energy prices hit
text:
Soaring electricity prices in coal power-rich Queensland and weak global aluminium prices have forced Rio Tinto's Boyne Smelters to cut production by 14 per cent and about 100 jobs.

The company confirmed the cuts in Gladstone on Thursday, saying it had been unable to secure a viable electricity deal from state government owned energy companies CS Energy and Stanwell Corporation.

Queensland's wholesale power prices have soared to an average of $96 a megawatt hour this year - second only to wind-heavy South Australia's $104/MWh - raising allegations of collusion among the three state-owned energy suppliers.

The cuts are worse than foreshadowed on January 19 when Rio said it would cut about 8 per cent or 45,000 tonnes, equating to about 30 jobs.

Boyne Smelters general manager Joe Rea said the cuts were "heart wrenching" and "a little soul destroying" after the workforce had toiled to improve productivity and safety achieved over the past year.

The 585,000 tonne a year plant is cutting production by 14 per cent and an equivalent cut from the 1000-strong workforce would take out more than 100 jobs.
direction: bearish for Rio Tinto shares

headline: Reinventure backs InDebted as disrupter
text:
Westpac Banking Corp's venture capital fund has invested in the debt collection platform InDebted in a deal that shows banks, telcos and utilities will seek to collect more bad debts by deploying technology across the archaic industry.

Reinventure Group has backed the $1 million seed fundraising round by InDebted, its 15th investment since the fund was established in early 2014. InDebted currently has three staff and plans to increase this to a dozen over the next 12 months. The Sydney-based start-up is already making revenue from its more than 1000 customers, mostly small businesses and some fintech lenders including SpotCap, whose managing director in Australia, Lachlan Heussler, is also an angel investor in the round.

InDebted has plans to expand its platform to service banks and other companies with large numbers of debtors, such as energy utilities or telecommunications firms. It believes its technology can help them reduce the size of internal collections units while developing a personalised approach to collections, making it more effective.

InDebted will compete against ASX-listed incumbent players Credit Corp and Collection House, which between them have nearly 20 per cent market share, according to IBIS, which reckons the Australian debt recovery industry earns $2 billion in annual revenue. Dun & Bradstreet, acquired two years ago by Archer Capital, is also in the space.

Yet the industry is highly fragmented, and the processes for collecting debts at many players are often paper-based and lack data analytics to tailor and improve approaches. As a result, it becomes uneconomic to chase many small debts; it is estimated around $3 billion of small bad debts are written off by SMEs each year. "There is a way to attack that," says Josh Foreman, InDebted's 28-year-old founder.

Some of the big debt-collection houses have minimum amounts for chasing of $1000. But the smallest debt that InDebted has collected has been $16; Mr Foreman said this was done profitably.

The websites of the incumbents are typically cumbersome and don't provide portals for materials to be submitted online. Collection methods involve cold calling from vast call centres, snail mail where the tone is often legalistic and aggressive, or SMS which asks people to call a 1300 number to progress the matter. This leads to many ducking and weaving to avoid payment - escalating collection costs.

In contrast, InDebted has developed application programming interfaces (APIs) to link to customer account systems and allow debts to be easily submitted. Sophisticated "skiptracing" techniques are used to locate customers abroad, various social media and messaging platforms are used, while flexible payments options are offered. Mr Foreman says customers are treated properly, which is appealing to the alternative finance sector, where "UX" (user experience) is so critical. This will also interest banks, given putting the customer's interests first has become a strategic priority given the political climate and focus on improving culture.

Reinventure general partner Kara Frederick said: "Debt collection has been relatively untouched by technology, much of it is manual and that, frankly, is a dumb approach. It is an inefficient industry; there is a high cost to getting paid. This means billions of dollars [globally] are left never being chased. Instead of using a baseball ball, InDebted uses a personalised, customer-centric approach. How you interface with a debtor has a meaningful impact on whether you get paid."

Unlike most of its competitors, the only fee InDebted charges is its contingency fee, which averages 20 per cent of the amount collected. There are no upfront fees, and no skiptracing fees. If a dispute escalates, InDebted is also able to provide the client with detailed data on when the debtor saw the communication. In the future, it plans to run machine learning algorithms over its systems to work out the optimal times to contact debtors.

The big four banks each have around 1000 staff in internal debt collection departments and in addition use dozens of external providers to assist with chasing bad debts. Mr Foreman said InDebted has also built a compliance engine that can manage training and qualify staff faster.
direction: bearish for incumbent debt collectors Credit Corp and Collection House

headline: ASX poised to resume slow march to 6000
text:
One hundred days after Donald Trump took charge of the White House, global markets retain a bullish bid as investors demonstrate a continuing willingness to focus on the positive.

The reality is that Mr Trump has yet to accomplish anything substantive in terms of policy and has failed to make much progress on implementing the pro-growth tax reform and infrastructure spending promises he made on the campaign trail.

For now, the focus has shifted from bets that the Trump administration would be able to bolster inflation to corporate earnings, which are providing investors with justification for stock valuations.

Over the weekend, the Nasdaq touched a record high, primarily on enthusiasm generated by the latest results from Alphabet and Amazon.com. Atlassian, the Aussie technology company, advanced 3 per cent, has seen its shares rise 43 per cent so far this year.

Both the Dow Jones and the S&P 500 are sitting near record highs, as are the FTSE 100 in London, Germany's DAX and the CAC 40 in France.

The S&P/ASX 200 starts the week at 5924 points, within striking distance of the 6000 mark. Good news from ANZ Banking Group, Woolworths, National Australia Bank and Qantas in the days ahead - all will reveal earnings numbers - will underpin a surge to record territory. Earnings helped bolster local shares on Friday.

ASX futures ended down 8 points over the weekend as Wall Street investors took a profit-taking breather in the wake of disappointing first-quarter economic growth data.

A policy meeting at the Reserve Bank tomorrow has the potential to subdue local bulls, though the central bank has little reason to disrupt markets. All bets are that the RBA will hold its key rate at 1.5 per cent. Last week's data showing that inflation is rising, however slowly, diminishing downside risks for rates.

One contrarian thought is that the RBA just might want to signal the door to a rate cut isn't entirely shut, in an effort to drive the Australian dollar lower, said Greg McKenna, chief market strategist at CFD and FX provider AxiTrader.

"My bet would be the RBA would like the Australian dollar lower to provide additional stimulus both now and in what could be an uncomfortable 2018 as the housing construction boom starts to unwind," Mr McKenna said.

The Australian dollar traded between US74.4¢ and US74.8¢ over the weekend, hovering near a three-month low.

NAB currency strategist Rodrigo Catril said the local currency has been "trading very heavy" and it is "vulnerable to the downside". "For now the AUD continues to find support around the US74.70/80¢ area, but from a technical perspective a break below that level could open the door for significant move lower."

On a positive note for the ASX, iron ore recouped 3.6 per cent on Friday, to $US68.80 a tonne, though activity was said to be light as Chinese traders and mills readied for a three-day break.

Many markets across Asia and Europe are closed today for the May Day holiday.

Among this week's higher-profiled US corporate results are Apple (Wednesday AEST), Facebook (Thursday AEST) and Berkshire Hathaway (Saturday AEST). Later in the week, there is a US central bank meeting with a statement on Thursday AEST and the release of April's government jobs report on Saturday AEST.

There was positive price news on copper, lead and nickel from London, And the price of oil edged higher on hope that OPEC might agree to extend production cuts long enough to reduce a global crude glut.

WEEK AHEAD MONDAY ■ CoreLogic April house price data ■ RBA Commodity Price Index ■ AiG performance of manufacturing index WEDNESDAY ■ RBA’s Rob Lowe speech on debt and housing ■ AiG performance of services index release TUESDAY ■ RBA May meeting ■ ANZ half-year results THURSDAY ■ March trade report ■ March new home sales ■ NAB halfyearresults FRIDAY ■ RBA quarterly statement on monetary policy ■ Fed chair Janet Yellen speech
direction: ASX 200 poised to rise toward 6000 on strong earnings

headline: RISK AND CAUTION
text:
Coming back from failure, learning from mistakes and sticking to their expertise are common themes among Australia's entrepreneurs, writes John Stensholt.

It's more than 40 years ago, but losing his business and just about all his assets is something Max Beck will never ever forget.

Beck, a Melbourne property developer and Financial Review Rich Lister, was on top of the world in booming Melbourne in the early 1970s and had targeted what was then the biggest operation in town.

"I thought I was Jesus Christ," Beck, aged 75, tells AFR Weekend. The biggest builder at that time was EA Watts and I thought, you know, I have to get there.
