My brother was my financial accountant and adviser. I'd never filed in a tax return. I really didn't know about cash flows. All I knew about was getting the jobs done and getting the jobs."

To keep winning those jobs and with a large workforce, Beck agreed to fixed-price contracts. But construction costs surged after Gough Whitlam became prime minister and Beck was almost wiped out.

"I had actually worked my guts out, and had the blocks of flats that I had owned. You know, I had accumulated a reasonable amount of wealth at that point. I lost the bloody lot, the whole lot has gone. I ended up with my house. So I was lucky to be 30 years of age, because you wouldn't want it to happen to you at our age now."

Beck eventually recovered and carved out a property career that has his wealth at $562 million. Yet his story is not unique among the wealthy elite.

Gerry Ryan, the owner of caravan manufacturer Jayco, can still remember how close he went to going under in the recession of the late 1980s. Frank Costa, head of the Costa fruit and vegetable empire, says he would never have been successful without good people around him, while billionaire cardboard-box maker and recycling magnate Anthony Pratt stubbornly - but successfully - refuses to invest in anything outside his core business.

But Beck does worry young entrepreneurs have lost sight of what it is like to work in a downturn and cope with adversity.

"Older entrepreneurs see more of the downside than the upside. So you need to get the rubber on the road and do the miles and have the experiences to be a bit more conservative with risk. But you won't be able to change that, I think. People will still get out there and make mistakes."

This week's Financial Review Rich List showed the amount of wealth held by the 200 richest Australians is more than ever before, and climbing rapidly.

For the first time, average wealth on the list tops $1 billion and the cut-off to make the elite level is a whopping $341 million.

From the outside, it looks like the list is surfing the ongoing housing boom or rising resources prices, and are making hay while the sun shines - and plenty of it.

But behind all that there is a lot of caution for the elder statesmen of the list.

For the perennially successful Rich Listers there are important themes that emerge. They plan, they stick to what they're good at, they learn from their mistakes, they see merit in training younger staff and managers, and they know their limits.

And it means they stay rich for a long time.

Flight Centre co-founder Geoff Harris says it is imperative that aspiring business people try to surround themselves with the best talent. "I think it's absolutely viable if there's young entrepreneurs out there starting up a business or whatever, the - the key thing is to get A graders in a key slot. So the key disciplines of your business, if you've got absolute A-grade people and no compromise there. If you've got B or C graders in those key areas of the business, you're going to fail."

Costa concurs, saying: "I used to say to my guys, 'Make sure we look after our best people, and make sure we look out always to recruit good people'.

"Because you can take all our facilities off us, take all our distribution centres, our coolrooms, our trucks, our computers, the lot. Take all the money off us. Leave me absolutely without a dollar. But give me the best team in this country in fresh produce, and you watch how quickly we will make dollars and put that equipment together."

For Pratt, who watched his father Richard struggle to survive the heady 1980s when his Visy empire expanded into financial services and insurance with dire financial consequences, strategy is all about growing in his industry - and virtually nothing else.

"I see the whole persistence thing to me is very important. I think I started the strategy we are on in 1991, and it took us more than a decade to get real momentum. That is the benefit of being in a private business as well."

Beck makes the observation that private money means less risk than those with managers who do not have as much of their wealth at stake, citing the example of Woolworths and its disastrous foray into the Masters hardware business as proof.

"One of the things that I find when I invest outside my expertise is I get really nervous if I don't understand that business."

Harris has moved on from Flight Centre, but in making investments in the successful Boost Juice chain and now the Fonda group of Mexican-themed restaurants, he has kept close to the lessons he learnt from his original travel retail days.

"I'm now in a restaurant chain and an online auction business [called Gavl] but there's commonalities in all of those. It's the right people with the right cultural fit, you feel comfortable with. Those partners have got to add value. You've got to have a bit of fun. It has got to be a growth industry. And I think it has probably got to be profitable as well, because turning around the dollar if it's something that isn't profitable is a lot of hard work."

Harris is still a big Flight Centre shareholder and has no intentions of selling his stock. But thinking long-term also means considering what the consequences are when it might be time to get out of business too, according to Costa.

"For young entrepreneurs starting up, it hasn't changed from 50 years [ago]. I think most young people start off, they pin their ears back in a new business. But you've got to spend equal time on the entry of the business, but also equal time on the exit if it doesn't work.

"So what's your thinking on your strategy for the entry into that new business, but also thinking through if the thing fails, how can I get out of it in a cost-efficient manner. So you're spending equal time on the exit, because most small businesses fail." P
direction: caution for property developers and construction firms

headline: Private equity gets creative on deals
text:
Private equity firms are looking at new ways to win over more Australian listed targets, while many are also considering longer investment horizons to attract a broader range of investors.

That is the view of law firm Minter Ellison, which is upbeat on future deal activity as the local industry looks to deploy an estimated $7 billion of capital, backed by conducive financing markets.

In a report to be released on Monday, titled Directions in Private Equity, Minter Ellison predicts the hot sectors to attracting investment will be disability, aged and home care services, healthcare, technology, agribusiness and financial services.

"Money is flowing and all the other macro factors are all lining up and that bodes well for activity," Minter Ellison partner Ricky Casali said. "I think [fiscal] 2018 is going to be busy and that will potentially carry into 2019."

But the numbers show a sluggish picture for this calendar year. Australian private equity activity amounted to $US10.9 billion ($14 billion) as at October 6, the lowest year-to-date total since 2014, according to Dealogic.

That follows the withdrawal of several private equity bids for Vocus Communications and Fairfax Media, publisher of The Australian Financial Review.

The largest divestments by private equity this year are TPG's $4 billion sale of Alinta Holdings and The Carlyle Group offloading its stake in Coates Hire. The largest acquisitions include South Australian land titles division, which involved Macquarie's infrastructure and real assets arm, and a joint bid by Pacific Equity Partners and Carlyle for iNova Pharmaceuticals.

Mr Casali said buyout firms were also increasingly looking to adopt new strategies or private merger and acquisition techniques to secure listed deals.

He referred to different iterations of break fees and the rollover equity structure adopted by Pacific Equity Partners in its takeover of Patties Foods. A similar structure that allows listed investors to share in future upside under new ownership is being used in a bid for Pepper Group by KKR & Co's credit arm.

"We have had lots of clients looking at things but they [public deals] are hard to execute so for every 10 you are going to execute on one or two. The hit rate is lower but the interest is there," he said.

Citigroup's local financial sponsors boss Dragi Ristevski said in areas where listed company valuations had softened buyer interest would increase. He is, however, mindful of challenges that still exist.

"Not surprisingly, the biggest impediment for public to private deals is bridging the valuation expectations of boards, shareholders and bidders," Mr Ristevski said.

"We've seen shareholders become more vocal and active in public to private transactions and the successful transactions will be those with the right strategy to engage with both boards and key shareholders on value."

Mr Ristevski also expects strong private equity deal activity in 2018. "Fund raising conditions are constructive and we've seen a material increase in participation from global institutional debt investors in our market, providing an additional source of liquidity to traditional Australian dollar-leveraged bank loans," he said.

Minter Ellison partner Nathan Cahill believes the local private equity and venture capital industry is becoming more sophisticated as it matures, new firms emerge and high-net-worth investors get more involved.

"Not only have you got new managers rising up ... it's also the sophistication of different managers has gone up," he said.

"What we are seeing now is a lot of funds where they are built to do a lot of early stage investments."
direction: bullish for private equity and target sectors

headline: Fresh start for BHP with 'Ks' at the helm
text:
It didn't take long for the wags to create a nickname for BHP Billiton's new top team, comprising the freshly-minted chairman Ken MacKenzie and chief executive Andrew Mackenzie - Big K and Little K.

As well as sharing a surname, the Canadian-born chairman and Scottish CEO will need to share a determination to fight back against the activist investors demanding change at the group, led by US hedge fund Elliott Management.

Former BHP chairman Don Argus says MacKenzie "obviously has a proven track record of adding value, and the succession process seems to have landed on a strong performer".

But the challenges will start almost straight away.

"He appears to have quite a few disgruntled shareholders exercising their views publicly, which is their right, so it seems that he and Andrew will need to spend time convincing them where the growth options are located," Argus tells AFR Weekend. "I suspect the so-called 'honeymoon' period will be short."

Still, MacKenzie and Mackenzie - and indeed the rest of BHP's board and management team - will be pleased, if not a touch surprised, that there will be a honeymoon at all.

After spending the first half of the week declaring BHP needed to appoint a "strong, experienced and visionary" chairman, Elliott welcomed MacKenzie's appointment on Friday as a "constructive step in bringing much-needed change to the direction of BHP".

A source said that, in a conference call with investors on Friday, Elliott's representatives said while the firm hadn't met MacKenzie he ticked a number of its boxes, not least because he is seen as a relative cleanskin - new to mining and, given he joined the board only in September, new to BHP.

Elliott also was encouraged that MacKenzie will go on a listening tour and meet shareholders in the coming months, before he formally takes the chairman's job in September. A source said Elliott was prepared to give MacKenzie time to listen - but it will want him to demonstrate a willingness to act from his first public statements.

Elliott's relatively warm welcome for MacKenzie has much to do with his sparkling reputation at Amcor, which he steered through the GFC and into an era of unprecedented success, lifting its market value from $4.5 billion to $12.5 billion during 10 years in charge.

It's a view supported by Ross Barker, the managing director of ASX-listed investment giant Australian Foundation Investment Company, a long-standing shareholder of both BHP and Amcor. "He (MacKenzie) managed a major transition of the company which extracted great value for shareholders," Barker tells AFR Weekend. "He brought discipline and focus on businesses that were able to provide a high return on investment through competitive advantage and productivity improvements. We are encouraged that he has been selected as the new chairman of BHP. His demonstrated skills will be important in ensuring the company keeps its focus on maximising shareholder value."

MacKenzie joined Amcor's American business in 1992, after a stint as a management consultant with Accenture. By 1997 he had moved to Australia and eventually ran the local flexible packaging division. He moved to Switzerland in 2001 to run Amcor Rentsch and Closures before returning to Australia in 2005 to take the top job.

It was a difficult time as Amcor had just been embroiled in a cartel with rival Visy Industries and was under pressure to improve its performance.

Chris Roberts, previously Amcor chairman and now chairman of Amcor spin-off Orora Group, hired MacKenzie at Amcor and describes him as "absolutely the best chief executive that I've ever worked with".

"He came into Amcor at a time when we were in disarray and really took it by the scruff of the neck," he tells AFR Weekend.

Graeme Liebelt, who chaired Amcor from December 2013, backed Roberts' view that MacKenzie's integrity was one of his finest qualities. But what distinguishes his legacy is that so much of what he put in place at Amcor still exists. "Ken is distinguished by very clear, strategic thinking, extremely strong discipline and putting in place repeatable processes," Liebelt says.

"Everyone was clear what was expected of them and what was needed to succeed."

Roberts said the "totally charming" MacKenzie's biggest attribute was that "he is relentless in the pursuit of the top talent in an organisation and he nurtures that talent".
direction: BHP shares face near-term pressure from activist investor demands

headline: China downplays OBOR strategic drivers
text:
Shanghai | China's ambitious foreign policy plan to overhaul global trade routes by spending billions of dollars on new infrastructure is an interesting case study in brand management.

After Xi Jinping, at the start of his presidency in 2013, outlined his plan for a new "Silk Road Economic Belt" and a "Maritime Silk Road" to improve China's links with Europe, Africa, Southeast Asia and the Middle East, it became known as "Yi Dai Yi Lu".

This directly translates as "One Belt, One Road" (OBOR). But two years on from the plan's unveiling, China's top decision-makers announced a name change. While the Mandarin version stayed the same, its official English translation was tweaked to the "Belt and Road Initiative".

The change attracted little attention at the time but is emblematic of China's struggle to overcome strategic concerns in the past four years and attract global support for OBOR.

Communist Party scholar Zhao Lei argued for the name change in 2015, insisting China needed to stress to its foreign partners OBOR was an "initiative" that would benefit countries around the world, not a "strategy" to serve only China's interests.

However, despite Beijing's best efforts, the OBOR label has stuck and widespread scepticism of the "initiative" remains.

That's why China is hosting 28 country leaders and dozens more ministers and government officials at a grand summit in Beijing on Sunday and Monday. It is hoped the summit, which will be attended by the heads of Russia, Indonesia, the Philippines, Italy, Greece, Spain and Malaysia, will bring credibility to the plan.

China has already signed contracts worth $US926 billion for OBOR projects with more than 60 countries. These include a port in Sri Lanka, a high-speed rail link between Singapore and Kunming, in China's southwest, and roads, rail and pipelines in Pakistan.

There are obvious economic drivers for OBOR. It allows China to boost activity in the country's less developed western regions and potentially export excess capacity in its steel and cement industries, while also increasing overall trade and tapping into new markets. But there are also obvious strategic drivers as China looks to expand its influence in the region.

The China Pakistan Economic Corridor, linking Kashgar in China's far west with Gwadar Port in Pakistan, is one of OBOR's more controversial projects. China has committed $US46 billion to the corridor and a Chinese company now controls the port.

A trade route through Pakistan offers China an alternative and shorter way to access the Indian Ocean, reducing its reliance on the Strait of Malacca. China has long been worried such reliance leaves it vulnerable to a blockade in times of conflict as most of its energy imports come from the Middle East.

There are also increasing reports of China's plans to base marines at Gwadar Port and service its navy ships, adding a stark military dimension to Beijing's economic plans.

Projects like these have made some governments wary about committing too heavily at this stage. Australia is among those adopting a more cautious approach. There had been a push for Canberra to link its $5 billion Northern Australia Infrastructure Facility to OBOR. Beijing was hopeful of an announcement when Premier Li Keqiang visited Australia in March.

However, the push was resisted and Trade Minister Steve Ciobo, who will attend the OBOR summit, confirmed this week the two will remain "separate". "They are both important initiatives," Mr Ciobo told The Australian Financial Review. "They're potentially complementary but they are separate."

Australia's wariness may stem from past experience. Canberra faced strong criticism from security analysts as well as then-US President Barack Obama when Darwin Port was sold to Chinese company Landbridge in 2015. While it is not officially listed as an OBOR project, Landbridge founder Ye Cheng said previously the Darwin Port lease met with China's foreign policy goals under OBOR. Since the acquisition, oversight of foreign takeovers in Australia has been tightened.

At the same time, there is some concern Australia's caution could hinder its involvement in a multi-trillion dollar infrastructure roll-out and damage relations with Beijing.

"China wants to promote, encourage, support and part-finance infrastructure projects throughout the region to accelerate economic growth and open up trading opportunities," said Former Victorian Premier John Brumby, who is a director of Chinese telecommunications firm Huawei in Australia and the president of the Australia China Business Council.

"I don't think it's any more complex than that."

Mr Brumby said there were opportunities for local companies in management, engineering or architecture across OBOR projects and "the language in Australia needs to be more supportive".

"There's still a good prospect for Australia to be a site for at least one if not two significant OBOR projects," he said.

Mr Ciobo said there were "clearly opportunities for Australia to be involved from financing through to construction", which is why he was attending the summit. "The more we can find out, the more detail that is revealed, the better informed we will be," he said.

China, for its part, has certainly been at pains to play down OBOR's strategic dimension. The name change was an effort to make the policy sound more benign; as part of a co-ordinated public relations strategy this week, the state-backed China Daily media organisation released a series of videos in which one of its American journalists explained OBOR to his daughter as a bedtime story. It's all about "moving stuff around".

Key points

The One Belt, One Road label has stuck and widespread scepticism remains.

A weekend summit hopes to bring credibility to the plan.
direction: bearish for infrastructure and construction stocks

headline: Media, mining and the sweet taste of honey are winners for Stokes
text:
He's had his controversies in 2017, but billionaire Kerry Stokes is doing what he does best: making money.

Just about all the stocks controlled by Stokes, a Financial Review Rich List perennial, are up strongly from the beginning of the year, even those in supposedly unfashionable industries.

Stokes has the bulk of his wealth in the listed Seven Group Holdings, which in turn owns about 35 per cent of Seven West Media.

SWM mainly comprises the Seven Network and West Australian newspaper assets, and has seen an often media-shy Stokes thrust into the spotlight as he was forced to deal with the fallout from chief executive Tim Worner's affair with Amber Harrison.

But despite its travails, both SWM and SGH have risen strongly in value on the ASX this year. SGH shares are up 31 per cent since January 1 and are worth 55 per cent more than this time a year ago. SWM shares have increased almost 19 per cent in the past month, to now be worth slightly more than at the start of the year.

SGH also owns the WesTrac Caterpillar dealership business that operates in Western Australia, NSW, the ACT and north-east China, one of many investments Stokes has with exposure to the mining sector.

Gold producer Saracen Mineral Holdings is another Stokes investment. Its shares have risen 38 per cent since January 1 and recent production numbers at the company's WA mines have been above analyst expectations.

Stokes also has been increasing his shareholding in BC Iron, now holding just under 28 per cent of the stock. BC Iron has a portfolio of iron ore assets in WA's Pilbara region and is a minnow, trading at about 1¢ per share. It has halved in recent months.

Oil and gas producer Beach Energy has been a better performer this year, rising about 9 per cent since January 1. The Adelaide-headquartered company has interests in more than 450 exploration and production tenements in Australia and New Zealand but has a core focus on the Cooper Basin.

Stokes' portfolio has diversification through his stakes in aged care property company Estia Health and Capilano Honey.

Estia shares have risen 15 per cent this year but are down 36 per cent in 12 months, while Capilano has risen 15 per cent in one month after falling earlier this year. Its shares are down 16 per cent since July last year.

He also has shares in minnow digital media company Newzulu and Goldfields Money, a former credit union based in Kalgoorlie.

Goldfield shares are up 11 per cent since the start of 2017.

Kerry StokesAge: 76Sector: Media, miningLives: Perth

KEY POINTS▪ Wealth mostly in Seven Group Holdings shares▪ SGH owns 35% of Seven West Media and WesTrac caterpillar dealerships▪ Portfolio also includes Capilano Honey and Estia Health
direction: bullish for Seven Group Holdings, Seven West Media, Saracen Mineral Holdings, and BC Iron

headline: Fix it quickly, Japan Post tells new Toll chief
text:
New Toll Group chief executive Michael Byrne is confident he can return the trucking company to profitability after being instructed by owners Japan Post to "fix it and hurry".

The $6.5 billion acquisition of Toll by Japan Post in 2015 has been a financial disaster for the Japanese group, which wrote off $4.9 billion on its Australian investment in fiscal 2017, forcing it into a $477 million net loss.

Japan Post executive Taneki Ono flew to Melbourne in July to explain the importance of turning around Toll to 150 senior executives from around the world at a leadership conference, including Japan's need to find homes for more than 100,000 people who remain displaced six years after the 2011 earthquake and tsunami, which triggered a meltdown of the Fukushima Daiichi nuclear power plant.

"We have a great moral obligation to help relieve those people of their suffering," Mr Byrne told The Australian Financial Review in his first interview since starting as CEO on January 1, when he turned up to the office while the rest of Australia celebrated New Year's Day. "That has now been explained deeply to the Toll management."

The Japanese government sold 20 per cent of its stake in Japan Post to the public in late 2015 after the Toll takeover, partially to raise money to rebuild cities and towns devastated by the tsunami. But plans to sell more shares to the public have been delayed by the poor performance of Japan Post's stock, which is stuck at its float price of ¥1400.

Mr Byrne won't disclose how much money Toll lost in 2017, saying only that it was "a lot". Toll posted an operating profit of $69 million in the 12 months to March but recorded an overall loss due to impairments and adjustments to its balance sheet.

The 52-year-old Mr Byrne was tapped by new Toll chairman John Mullen in mid-December after former chairman Ray Horsburgh and former CEO Brian Kruger were sent packing.

"I was in Europe in holidays and got asked to go to Japan for the day," Mr Byrne recalled. By the end of the day, after being interviewed by five of Japan Post's most senior people, he had the job. "They didn't muck around."

Mr Byrne and Mr Mullen already knew each other well. Mr Byrne, who ran family-owned trucking group Linfox between 2005 and 2014, was a client of ports and rail group Asciano, which Mr Mullen ran between 2011 and 2016.

"John's a lovely man, very charismatic, very statesmanlike," said Mr Byrne. "They call us yin and yang - I'm the operator and the blunt edge."

Mr Byrne said that even though the Japanese are "disappointed" with Toll's results and are now more engaged in developing its strategy, they remain "extremely supportive" of the Australian group, approving capital investments into the business.

"If you boil down probably 1000 pages of strategy, it's fix it and hurry and get back to what we're supposed to be doing - which is growing a world-class business."

Mr Byrne hasn't wasted time. Some 2000 jobs - about half of them overseas - including 1000 management jobs, are going, with the cuts expected to be completed at the end of August. The top management team is being restructured, with a new global chief financial officer, to be based in Melbourne, and a new global head of forwarding, to be based in Singapore, due to be announced this month.

Toll's Hong Kong office is being closed, with people moved to Singapore, where Toll has 2500 employees. Businesses that generate more than half of Toll's $8 billion in annual revenues will be run out of Singapore, where it is building an automated logistics hub known as "Toll City".

Mr Byrne wants to focus Toll's operations on Asia, Oceania and North America, and in Africa for the long term. But he plans to withdraw from countries in war zones as well as parts of Europe, and will avoid Central and South America.

Mr Byrne believes Toll's business "lost its way" by expanding into too many countries, noting that the 113 acquisitions made under former management over a dozen years were not well integrated, leading to complaints from customers. Instead of five divisions and 24 business units, Toll now has three divisions and 11 units.

And instead of a "world domination strategy", Mr Byrne plans to run the core business hard, focusing on improving IT systems and strengthening Toll's "human capital" with a graduate trainee program to make its 44,000-strong workforce more diverse. About half of this year's 15 graduates are women, and half have English as a second language. The program is expected to double in size next year and be more international, and Toll is also training 18 young people that Japan Post have sent to Australia.

The changes are already paying off - Toll had three months of consecutive year-on-year revenue growth for the first time in three years, in April, May and June.

Mr Byrne has met with 45 of Toll's top 50 customers, who have demanded better technology, including better data security. In March, Toll's board approved six new technology programs, with the company spending an additional $420 million during the next 2½ years on top of the $336 million it already spends annually on IT.

It plans to upgrade about 11,000 hand-held electronic PDAs (personal digital assistants), which use a variety of hardware and software, and create one global "MyToll" website where people can track goods and parcels.

Toll's chief information officer, Francoise Russo, goes to Japan every two months to swap technology ideas with Japan Post. "We need to learn from each other, we have particular skills in certain things - fleet and equipment and heavy assets - they have other skills, particularly in last-mile delivery and customer B2C." Mr Byrne makes about 10 trips annually to Japan, including attending four two-day board meetings every year.

Toll's 37,000 customers, which include big retailers like Woolworths and Coles as well as the Australian and Singaporean governments, have higher expectations than they did in the past, Mr Byrne says. "The days are long gone where supply chain was seen as a back door to the escape hatch where someone went if they weren't really good doing something else."

The imminent arrival of US retail giant Amazon in Australia is "a good thing", Mr Byrne added. "It's changed the conversation and it's made people aware that they need to adapt.

"We're in a semi-unique position - I have to help all my customers adapt and be agile to compete with a new competitor because we have such a big retail business ... and Amazon are in different parts of the world as our customer, so I also have to help them."

Toll, like others, is in talks with Amazon about providing services to the retail group in Australia but "no decisions have been made", Mr Byrne said.
direction: bearish for Toll Group shares

headline: BHP to review Minerals Council link
text:
BHP Billiton has effectively put the Minerals Council on 12 months' notice after a review of its membership of a mining world of industry peak councils that aimed to describe inconsistencies in approach to climate and energy policies, and assess if discordances demanded disaffiliation.

A review of 21 association memberships, which was announced in September by BHP chief external affairs officer Geoff Healy, has identified "material" differences between the company and three major peak councils on 10 critical climate and energy policies.

BHP's troublesome three are the Minerals Council of Australia, the US Chamber of Commerce and the World Coal Association and the company has confirmed a different approach will be taken to dealing with its disconcerts with each of them.

In a decision that will be greeted with mischievous delight by the anti-coal lobby, BHP has signalled it will leave the WCA by the end of March, barring a rear-guard defence by the global coal lobby.

BHP has signalled it is biased to sticking with the US Chamber of Commerce because the benefits of membership of what is a hugely influential organisation in a distant but critical political geography apparently demonstrably outweigh what is arguably the longest list of differences of view on a wide range of policy.

Again, BHP says it will approach the US Chamber to discuss the points of difference and it will make a decision on its membership by March 31 next year.

And, finally, BHP says it will retain its place at the MCA, but warns it will review its membership towards the back end of 2018. BHP has subtly advertised the nature of this threat by revealing its MCA membership fee last year was $1.86 million and that this represented 17 per cent of the organisation's total subscription revenue.

BHP has made plain its preference is the MCA should make a pragmatic retreat from the frontlines of both climate and energy policy.

"Industry associations are also an important forum for debate and we believe they are most effective when they allow discussion of a wide range of views. However, having a range of members can also make it difficult to form a consensus or 'industry' view, particularly if the topic has different impacts for different members," the BHP review advised.

"In climate and energy policy areas, where no broad industry consensus exists, associations should generally refrain from advocacy in those areas, with individual members being best placed to outline their views independently."

The MCA welcomed BHP decision to retain its membership of the organisation saying: "BHP has been a valued member of the Minerals Council for many years and we look forward to that relationship continuing into the future."

But it refused our invitation to comment on whether or not it might contemplate withdrawal from the frontline of the carbon or energy debates or, indeed, if it assessed that its present policy positions had the transparently forged consensus that BHP seemed to now require.

BHP's review confirms two "material differences" between the company's positions on climate and energy policy and those being advocated by the MCA. Both those disjunctions result from the collapse of certainty in the Australian energy market. The first fissure was opened by the MCA's occasional promotion of energy certainty over carbon emissions reduction and the second by its more consistent advocacy of government intervention in energy markets in support of quite specific technologies.

BHP assessed the MCA had "at times" proposed an approach to energy policy that put "reliability and affordability" ahead of emissions reduction and it had highlighted the costs associated with meeting Australia's international commitments "at the expense of addressing the importance of reducing emissions".

"We believe climate policy and energy policy are inextricably linked, and that energy reliability, energy affordability and emissions reduction should be considered on an integrated basis," BHP said.

The second bone of contention is far less faceted and subtle.

BHP believes that free and open markets generate the most efficient and productive investment decisions. It believes just as certainly that energy markets should be designed to be both fuel and technology neutral.

"Government intervention in resources and energy markets should only be in response to a demonstrated market failure and informed by cost-benefit analysis," BHP reminds us in its membership review.

But the debate over Australian energy policy has had the MCA surrender both neutrality and a preference for market-based outcomes through its advocacy of government funding for HELE (high-energy, low-emission) electricity generation technologies.

Further, in introducing the results of its review, BHP again appears to indicate that the MCA's approach to the Finkel review had proven a tipping point in the company's tolerance for points of difference between its policy positions and those of the lobbies it helps finance.

"BHP publicly supported (and participated in) the Independent Inquiry into the Future Security of the National Electricity Market (Finkel review)," BHP noted. "We publicly supported the 50 recommendations of the Finkel review and specifically endorsed the principles of the clean energy target."

Needless to say, the immediate past management of the MCA was a quietly hostile contributor to the Finkel process and offered a genuinely aggressive welcome to the CET proposal.

As we have previously noted, BHP had urged the MCA to take the opposite tack on Finkel, identifying the review as an opportunity to forge a political and industry consensus around a reform package. BHP's preference was to absorb the pain in the name of the greater gain.

From BHP's perspective, the report presented on Tuesday represents another important milestone in its long march to a new era of public affairs transparency. The miner will doubtless earn praise and reproach in unequal measure for its efforts. Some at the MCA argue that BHP finds itself in the thrall of the Green-Christian. Others have been acerbically critical of BHP's role in the ousting of former MCA boss Brendan Pearson.

But whatever its reception, the membership review lands a BHP orange-coated reminder that very little happens at the Global Australian without reflection and deep, diligent process. The Big Thinkers identify this reassessment, and the unique model it has designed to identify and defuse present and future conflicts between company and peak councils, as another critical moment in its mission to rehabilitate and retain its social licence.

In what was a Daniel-in-the-Lions'-Den moment, BHP's Healy took this message to directly to the MCA and its most powerful constituents in September. Healy announced that the collapse of trust in industry had reached a potentially dangerous "tipping point" both locally and globally.

"Though I believe that businesses are generally doing more than ever to satisfy their stakeholders, perception is truth," he told the MCA annual Minerals Week gathering in Canberra. "And many perceive business across the board as complacent, out of touch, and untrustworthy. The challenge is especially acute for our sector.

"We are not only facing a loss of trust, but the risk of losing basic support for our industry, even where we have operations that are acknowledged to operate sustainably and responsibly. And when we have made, and continue to make, a massive contribution to economic growth through taxes and royalties, employment, purchasing and investment.

"This is a serious problem, because trust, in our sector, is not a 'nice to have'. It is a prerequisite for everything we do, and aspire to do.

"We need to make the case - more confidently and more effectively than ever before - for the positive contribution that well-run and responsible resource companies make to society. And we need to make that case far more widely than ever before.

"But this is not just about communication. We need to eliminate the gap between what we say and what we do. We need to deliver better and fairer outcomes," he urged.

"Each of us in this room knows the essential role resources play in the world, the good we can deliver, the potential we can unlock. The difference we can make to the lives of employees, communities, suppliers, customers and shareholders all over the world.

"Our most valuable assets are not iron ore, coal, copper or petroleum, they are relationships of trust. And we do not have as many of those as we need," Healy told his MCA audience.
direction: bearish for BHP shares

headline: ACCC redraws lines in mobile spectrum battle
text:
Australian Competition and Consumer Commission chief Rod Sims has flagged the regulator may reconsider how it views mobile spectrum ownership ahead of the 5G auction, which the NBN fears may undermine its relevance.

The government auction, which is expected to be the first globally and attract interest from Telstra, Vodafone Hutchinson Australia, Optus, TPG and more, has prompted NBN chief Bill Morrow to call for protection.

Buying 5G, which is still in testing, will allow telcos that successfully bid for it to use their own networks to provide faster mobile coverage, potentially bypassing NBN and threatening its forecast revenue.

Speaking at the RadComms conference in Sydney on Wednesday, Mr Sims said the regulator would consider competition holistically, rather than individual spectrums, to ensure incumbents did not lock out new entrants, such as TPG.

"There is an imperative to prevent incumbents from purchasing spectrum for the purpose of preventing the new entrant from competing effectively in the downstream markets," Mr Sims said.

"In an unrestricted auction, could we expect to see Telstra buying 100 megahertz in the metropolitan areas and NBN buying in the outer metropolitan areas and regional centres? Where does that leave the others?"

Australia's auctions of 3.4 to 3.6 gigahertz bands are tipped to be the first 5G auctions globally. In Australia, Optus and NBN already have significant holdings in the 3.4 gigahertz band, while Telstra has smaller holdings.

"Our past approach to competition limits has been quite simple: we sought to prevent dominance of spectrum by any one licensee in particular bands. We are rethinking this approach," Mr Sims told the conference. "Instead, and this is a key point, we want to consider spectrum holdings holistically rather than in particular bands."

He specifically addressed the NBN concerns and made it clear he was against any kind of protection for the NBN.

"Mobile services are relatively lightly regulated and we expect that this is where capital will head. 5G is an incredibly expensive proposition, but offers real pay-offs for mobile network operators in the Australian environment," Mr Sims said. "Obviously, the threat in this context is to the NBN. We have clearly said that we do not support any extension of the Regional Broadband Scheme to the mobile operators. The way to counteract threats is not to impose regulation to protect the NBN."

Analysts have speculated that David Teoh's TPG will bid for 5G to bypass the NBN, which suggests it may need to raise capital from the equity markets. At its 2016 full-year results, TPG warned the heavy cost of delivering data over the NBN would crimp the telco's margins.
sentiment: mixed with a negative bias for NBN, positive for Telstra/Optus
direction: NBN shares face headwinds from potential revenue loss, while telcos benefit from clearer spectrum rules

headline: Fresh strategies for dealing with the disrupted political world
text:
The disrupted international political environment calls for a new approach to trading. So here are trading strategies retail investors may want to consider to sharpen their online trading in 2017.

Greg McKenna is the chief market strategist at AxiTrader. He says one idea is to take advantage of what he calls "Manic Mondays": take a contrarian view to trading, or a least buy on bad news from the weekend that has prompted stock values to tumble lower.

Often this is an overreaction and prices will come back over the week. "On a Monday, Asian and Australian trading is a great opportunity to take the opposite view from the investing universe after the weekend news," he says. "If the world is ending, then buy. If it's a celebration, time to sell."

Similarly, Stephen Innes, senior currency trader at OANDA Australia and Asia Pacific, says the simplest non-algorithmic, news-driven strategy is to trade based on US President Donald Trump's tweets. "Algorithmic traders don't open and close positions based on Trump's tweets as [the tweets] are way too sporadic to serve as an important trading strategy," he says.

This is an opportunity for retail investors and mum-and-dad traders. For instance, should Trump make announcements about his proposed infrastructure program, this can boost toll road companies' share prices.

Chief executive of Bell Direct Arnie Selvarajah says clients are wanting greater insights into corporate activity.

They are increasingly using its Directors Trading Insights tool, which highlights announcements about directors and senior management selling or buying stock in their company. "People are growing more sophisticated, such as keeping a close eye on directors trading in their companies," Selvarajah says. "That information was there before but it is being used much more regularly and in a more insightful manner."

Selvarajah also notes that clients are looking for better returns than those offered on cash deposits. "It makes sense then, for our clients to explore the fixed income sector, including exchange traded bonds," he says.

Ric Spooner, chief market analyst at CMC Markets Australia, says the long strangle is a technical strategy also designed for the Trump era. "This works if there's a big pick-up in volatility and option prices rise," Spooner says. "In a long strangle, you buy a call option at a higher strike price and a put option at a lower strike price. Traders win if markets go haywire, but they lose if volatility stays low or gets lower. It's a way of capturing any unexpected lift in volatility that may come with the new administration." Loss is limited to the premium paid.

The spread between the price of copper and gold is another Trump-related strategy. "Both are influenced by the US dollar and interest rates, but copper is an industrial metal, whereas gold has a big investment and risk component to its price," Spooner says. "After the US election, we saw a really big increase in the copper price based on expectations of increased infrastructure spending. It well and truly outperformed gold.

"But recently gold has started to hold its own and there has been trading around this, with those in the market testing the extremes of the range and [thus] expressing their view on the Trump presidency." When the price of copper rises the reverse strategy is an alternative: buy gold and sell copper.

Back home, Spooner says using contracts for difference (CFDs) to express a view about the major banks is an option for retail investors.

All four major banks are now quite similar domestic financial institutions. But overseas traders or institutions push the price of one bank stock up and push down the price of another as they sell one and buy the other, he says.

So for short-term traders, using CFDs to buy one bank stock and sell another bank stock simultaneously can be a way of taking advantage of short-term changes in their share prices.

The crack spread is Spooner's final strategy. It allows investors to trade the difference between the price of oil and the price of its more refined products.
direction: volatile with potential for short-term dips on political news

headline: Wesfarmers pulls off seamless transition
text:
Wesfarmers has pulled off a seamless leadership change, with shareholders expecting managing director-elect Rob Scott to drive acquisitions and asset sales to reinvigorate returns after taking the helm from Richard Goyder in November.

Mr Scott, who will be Wesfarmers' eighth chief executive in 103 years and the seventh promoted from within, has promised no major changes in strategy and has emphasised his commitment to Wesfarmers' conglomerate mode, its focus on capital discipline and delivering top-quartile shareholder returns.

Mr Scott takes the helm at a challenging time, with the Western Australian economy struggling, Coles losing its lead over Woolworths for the first time in seven years, Target bleeding cash and Bunnings working double time to turn around the Homebase hardware business it acquired in Britain last year.

"There are a lot of challenges around but there are also opportunities," the 47-year-old former Olympic rower said from his home town of Perth yesterday.

"One of the great things about Wesfarmers is the diversity ... when some businesses have some headwinds others do well - that's the benefit of our conglomerate model."

"As a conglomerate we recognise the importance of taking a long-term view and working through the cycle. We'll do everything in our power to avoid a short-term focus ... if that is at the expense of longer-term shareholder returns."

Investors welcomed his appointment and said Wesfarmers' succession planning had been exemplary.

"Wesfarmers have continued their tradition of promoting from within and this appointment shouldn't be any great surprise," said Wilson Asset Management chief investment officer Chris Stott. "Richard [Goyder] has done a phenomenal job for the company over a long period of time and goes out on a high."

However, investors believe Mr Scott may be more inclined than Mr Goyder to look for acquisitions and consider recycling assets such as Bunnings, Target, Kmart and Officeworks through divestments and initial public offers, pointing to his 10-year stint in investment banking and corporate finance before he rejoined Wesfarmers in 2004.

"His experience suggests he could be more deal-oriented if he worked in investment banking for a while," said Antares Capital portfolio manager John Guadagnuolo.

"The portfolio is looking a bit soggy in terms of growth - Coles has slowed given Woolworths recovery and the grocery industry is mature," he said. "Bunnings has done a terrific job and the UK is a potential platform for growth but that's a few years down the track. From a risk-adjusted rate-of- return perspective, moving away from the Australian retail sector is not a bad idea."

The potential sale of Wesfarmers' coal operations, which have been on the market for several months and are estimated to be worth about $2.4 billion, could free up surplus capital for acquisitions.

"They have more opportunities to invest organically," Mr Stott said, pointing to the rollout of Bunnings in Britain and Ireland and the need to return Target to profitability.

Mr Scott said Wesfarmers was evaluating options for the sale of coal - "if we get an attractive price we'd consider it very seriously."

"One of the great parts about our model is that we can be both opportunistic and selective in terms of where we allocate capital - that applies as well to how we monetise businesses and release value from the capital that has been invested," he said.

He played down suggestions that Wesfarmers would allocate more capital outside the retail sector, which now generates 80 per cent of group earnings, to rebalance the portfolio.

"My approach to capital allocation is very much driven by where the opportunities are to deliver satisfactory returns on our capital."

Mr Scott, who has been overseeing Wesfarmers' industrials businesses for the past 18 months, will become deputy chief executive immediately and will join the board as managing director after the annual meeting in November.

The succession announcement followed widespread speculation late last year that Mr Scott had been tapped to succeed Mr Goyder.

Chairman Michael Chaney revealed that two other strong internal candidates, finance director Terry Bowen and former Bunnings chief John Gillam, who resigned in December, had told the board late last year they were not interested in the role.

Mr Chaney defended the internal appointment, saying the board did consider whether there was anyone better outside and came to the view it would do better appointing from within.

Mr Chaney paid tribute to Mr Goyder, who joined Wesfarmers in 1993 and succeeded Mr Chaney as managing director in July 2005.

During Mr Goyder's reign Wesfarmers' market value has risen from $15 billion to almost $50 billion, buoyed by the $20 billion acquisition of the Coles Group in 2007. The acquisition doubled the size of the company but diluted return on equity.

Mr Goyder said his decision to retire at the end of this year had not been easy but he was "thrilled" with Mr Scott's appointment.
direction: bullish for Wesfarmers shares

headline: Glencore knocks on AEC door
text:
Glencore has called in the Australian Electoral Commission to run future employee ballots at a selection of its Australian coal mines in response to claims that the coal union had exerted excessive influence over a recent vote at the strikebound Oak North coal mine in Queensland.

"Glencore has been advised in confidence that prior to casting their votes on the proposed new enterprise agreement our employees were instructed by the CFMEU to take a screenshot of their votes to demonstrate they had voted 'no'," the company said in the statement on Wednesday.

"We are deeply concerned by this and have engaged the Australian Electoral Commission to oversee subsequent rounds of voting at our Oaky North coal mine," Glencore said.

While Glencore's statement refers specifically to Oaky North, where a new ballot is expected to be run and lost within days, we understand that the company has received similar complaints about ballots run at a handful of the Hunter Valley mines also gripped by industrial action over disputed enterprise agreements.

The changed election governance will see ballots conducted in a secure room and mine employees forced to surrender mobile phones and other digital devices before voting.

The global miner's allegations of union overreach arrive in the immediate wake of Wednesday's publication by the Brisbane Courier Mail of examples of excessive and outrageous behaviour by some miners populating a union-managed picket of the Oaky North mine.

The Courier reported that one picketer was facing charges and four others faced police investigation on the basis of reports passed on by mine security. The evidence provided includes video and audio recordings of threats of violence against contract workers who daily cross the picket line to go to work, and against their families.

The Courier report offered a list of disgusting examples of intimidation that included the threat to rape the children of one contract worker. Another is threatened with assault by a crowbar.

Glencore has confirmed recordings made by the security team at Oaky North had been passed to Queensland police. It also confirmed that that Courier's report on the content of those recording is accurate.

That tension are running very high at Oaky North is no surprise. The workplace law encourages robust negotiation between employer and union over enterprise agreements. But this sort of stuff is simply unacceptable and it cannot be tolerated by the union or the community it represents.

The roots of this contest go back 2015 when the existing employment agreement at Oaky North expired. The dawdling progress to a new agreement saw industrial action triggered in May. Glencore then locked out union workers for a week in June. When industrial action continued, Glencore locked the gates again in late July.

CFMEU members have not been allowed back on the premises since. Some 185 CFMEU members have now not worked, or been paid by Glencore, for 86 days and the routine of weeklong extensions to the lockout seems to continue rolling on with the union leadership due their next notification on Tuesday.

As ever, the union is delivering financial support to its striking brotherhood. But it must be an increasingly expensive business even given the wealth contained in the National Assistance Fund. That might perhaps explain why Wednesday bought a $180,000 donation to the Oaky North strikers by the CFMEU's construction arm.

The National CFMEU convention generated $150,000 of that, while the ETU handed over $20,000 and a further $10,000 was raised courtesy of a $100 donation from each delegate.

But all the signs are that this dispute will eat a whole lot more cash yet.

There have so far been 23 meetings between company and union to negotiate settlement of a new deal, the most recent just last week. But, if anything, the sides are now further apart, and more entrenched, than they were when talks began two years ago.

In part that's because Oaky North now sits the front line of what the CFMEU celebrates as a national and international campaign against "Grubcore". Whether by plan or pure happenstance, there are agreements open across pretty much the sweep of Glencore's coal fleet in Australia with industrial action now rolling through the Hunter Valley.

The seeds of the national elements of this campaign are essentially familiar.

The union is seeking to recover lost control over the way Glencore runs its mines by curtailing the use of contract workers. The union wants to remove standing provisions that allow unfettered discretion over the use of contract and casual workers. It would also like to constrain Glencore's ability to reshape shifts and to return to the days when the company communicated with the workforce through a union representative.

Glencore will not budge on the contractors' clauses. It argues they are bog standard across all the agreements of its major competitors in coal and any change at all would limit its capacity to competitively flex its operations through the notoriously fickle coal cycle.

It stands similarly firm on the other initiatives that aim to diminish management prerogative. In Queensland and NSW, Glencore's position is represented by the union as intransigence that reveals an ambition to somehow casualise the workforce.

More likely than compromise, it seems to me that Glencore's next step will be to exercise other rights available under the industrial law. One option, for example, would be to seek termination of the standing agreement, a situation that would see wages and conditions set by the much more frugal black coal industry award.

But the front-line hostility in NSW is nowhere near the savage intensity on display in Queensland, where the union is allowing striking workers to reinforce a long legacy of militancy by the state's branch of the CFMEU Mining & Energy division.

The tactics on display here will be familiar to anyone who followed fierce and costly 18 months of industrial action that hit the state's biggest coal miner, the BHP Mitsubishi Alliance, from 2012.

The Queensland branch is run now, as it was then, by district president Stephen Smyth. He is a powerful fellow. The work he has done on illuminating the frailties that have seen "black lung" re-emerge in Australian coal mining is truly admirable. But he runs a branch that has consistently indulged excessive behaviour. His recent appearance on the front line at Oaky North did nothing to contain swirling emotions.

Evidence presented to the Fair Work Commission in September indicates his picket-line aggression was naked and, even if only briefly, intemperate. That evidence was presented in a case that aimed to test whether or not Glencore had unjustly issued disciplinary notices against to 26 Oaky North workers who were accused of breaching company codes of conduct in their behaviour on the picket line and in commentary offered on social media.

The lapses described by the Courier only affirm the need for Smyth and the union to recover firm control of his people.

One of the backdrops to the whole "Grubcore" contest is the search for a replacement for the CFMEU Mining & Energy Division's general secretary of legendarily long standing, Andrew Vickers.

Vickers retired last month and a two-horse contest is now under way to elect his successor.

One of the candidates is the Queensland district vice-president Chris Brodsky. He happens to be the hombre running the day to day of the Oaky North contest. But even though he comes from Vickers' part of the world, Brodsky has not earned the retiring giant's backing. Indeed, the way we read the advice Vickers offered the members, he has earned something damagingly less than support.

In late September, Vickers offered a stinging Facebook endorsement of Brodsky's opponent, Grahame Kelly, who is the extant secretary of the union's powerful Northern and NSW District.

"The role of GS [general secretary] is getting more difficult by the day," Vickers wrote in a Facebook post in late September.

"The union is under the microscope by regulators and governments. If you want to act in the best interest of the Union, support and vote for Grahame. If you want to support short-term populist jingoism, support somebody else," Vickers announced.

"I don't even vote," he finished. "Be smart."

Ouch.
direction: bearish for Glencore shares

headline: IAG puts Asian expansion plans on ice
text:
ASX-listed insurer IAG will stop investing in its underperforming Asian arm, but chief executive Peter Harmer says there are no plans yet to sell its core operations in the region.

Mr Harmer said IAG would focus on its Australia and New Zealand markets after a fruitless 18-month search for investment opportunities in Thailand, India and Malaysia, where IAG earnings fell by more than 50 per cent in the year to June 30.

"It is unlikely we will make further investments in Asia in the short term," he told AFR Weekend after the insurer's annual general meeting yesterday. "We are going to wait and see how that landscape settles, and then we may or may not. I don't want you to go away from this thinking 'oh it's only a matter of time' [before we invest again]. If it doesn't play out the right way, we may not."

IAG full-year results in August revealed deficiencies in its Asian businesses.

The region's consolidated gross written premium for 2017 dropped by 5.2 per cent. On a country basis, Thailand, Malaysia, Vietnam and Indonesia all reported drops in gross written premium, with India being the only country to improve, hitting $553 million, up from $447 million in 2016.

The Asia arm makes up 3 per cent of the group's $11.8 billion total GWP. When Mr Harmer took the reins in 2015, IAG was predicting that 10 per cent of its total revenue would come out of its Asia businesses by 2016.

Morningstar analysts have been critical of IAG hanging on to its Asian assets, suggesting instead it should act immediately and sell them off.

"In our view, IAG should sell the Asian businesses and use the proceeds to concentrate on more profitable markets in Australia and New Zealand. The sooner the firm bites the bullet and moves on from Asia, the better," said Morningstar analyst David Ellis.

Mr Harmer said he "understood" where Morningstar was coming from, but it was too soon to determine if it would start to sell them off. "Shareholders have been quite patient. We've got $850 million invested in those Asian businesses and even if you take our corporate office costs out it still doesn't meet our hurdle rate," he said.

"The danger is to go too far ahead. My view is we need to be continually vigilant in portfolio management. We've exited businesses in Australia, for example Swann Insurance and NSW Worker's Compensation, so we will take hard decisions if it's in the shareholders' interest. But I don't want to predict what that would be today in Asia."

IAG is increasingly looking at the individual territories, and seeing them as separate businesses.

"They all have their own characteristics and trade in markets that is unique in their own way. In Asia our core markets are Thailand, Malaysia and India. We've been in exit negotiations with our partner in China, but it's a long process. Vietnam is a small business," said Mr Harmer.

IAG has previously pitched its growth plans firmly in Asia, despite a mixed record in the region.

In 2014, the company took a $60 million writedown on its investment in China motor insurer Bohai. But this did not deter Mr Harmer from saying on his appointment in October 2015 that China would be instrumental to his growth strategy.

But before he officially took over the reins, IAG was forced to kill its Chinese expansion plans after shareholder backlash.

Key pointsCEO says the group will now focus on its Australia and New Zealand markets.

It is "too soon" to determine if it will start to sell off its Asian businesses.
direction: bearish for IAG shares

headline: Chanticleer’s Chook roast
text:
Keeping an open mind about technology is crucial to future success.

There is no better way to respond to an overused four-letter acronym than to create your own, as Kai Riemer did at the first BOSS Emerging Leaders networking event for 2017. In front of about 220 people in the Ivy Ballroom in Sydney, the technology professor from the University of Sydney Business School said it was time to move on from the term “VUCA”.

This military acronym, which stands for Volatility, Uncertainty, Complexity and Ambiguity, proved to be a useful starting point for a sometimes fiery panel discussion about the impact of technology disruption.

Riemer was joined on the panel by the “dame of drones” (and BOSS Young Executive alumna) Dr Catherine Ball and The Smith Family chief executive Lisa O’Brien.

The 90-minute panel discussion covered many topics, including the skills needed by leaders of the future and how to bust through the bureaucracies holding back some of our largest companies.

Riemer reminded the BOSS audience that the term VUCA has sometimes been used to reinforce the orthodox thinking about technology that pervades Silicon Valley. He says we should not succumb to a blind belief in technology but keep an open mind.

He was particularly scathing about those who celebrate algorithms and machine learning as silver-bullet solutions for higher productivity. He said the core problem with the use of algorithms in business is they can lock an organisation into legacy structures and outdated ways of thinking.

For example, an algorithm used in human resources could be relied on to accurately identify candidates that had been successful in the past. But hiring more of the same would probably compromise diversity and be detrimental to an organisation trying to respond to change.

Riemer’s perspective on technology appears to challenge the thinking that underpins the current wave of “director tourism”. This involves boards of directors from leading Australian companies making annual pilgrimages to Silicon Valley to absorb the wisdom of executives from big US tech fi rms.

Chook Roast believes it would be far more stimulating if boards followed the lead of REA Group, which held its board immersion in China in late May. After all, China is the most mobile-connected nation in the world and the future of business is all about mobile.

So how did Riemer encourage the emerging leaders at the BOSS event to think outside the square? He summed up his approach to technology disruption with the acronym SLAC, which stands for Sense-making, Learning, Agility and Creativity.

During the Emerging Leaders panel  discussion, Riemer said it was crucial to ask questions to make sense of change . “It’s important to keep learning new skills and question your own assumptions . Creativity is the only way to approach the future.”

Riemer says the world has become so complex it is not possible to fully comprehend and break down problems. That demands an agile approach.

Of course, agile thinking is the new black in business. But in Chook Roast’s experience, few have been able to define what it really means.

Riemer believes agile is, first and foremost, a set of values. “Agile is about trust in self-organising teams and not micro-management.

“It does not compromise on quality or time of delivery, but remains flexible regarding the project’s scope. Rather than planning exactly what to deliver, it is about iterating towards a good solution. It’s a good approach when the exact outcome is unknown or unknowable.”

The most recent high-profile implementation of agile in Australia is at ANZ Banking Group, which wants to use it to improve customer service. ANZ chief executive Shayne Elliott said it was also about breaking down bureaucratic walls.

“The use of agile will mean a much less hierarchical ANZ, one built around small, collaborative, self-directed teams focused on delivering continuous improvement in the customer experience,” he said in early May.

During the panel discussion there was a focus on the issue of bureaucracies in business.

But instead of looking at why bureaucratic processes suffocate innovation, the panelists talked about how to cut through red tape.

Riemer said bureaucracies are useful in stable environments with a focus on managing compliance and risk. But when faced with disruption there is a tendency for managers to fall back on rules to avoid taking risks.

“I think it is important to balance bureaucracies with spaces in which employees can engage in open sense-making without the fear of retribution,” Riemer says.

“The problem with bureaucracies is that they crowd out trust and trust is the basis of agility. By learning to follow the rules we literally forget how to trust each other.”
direction: cautious for technology sector

headline: Tracking shoppers: GPT turns pings into purchases
text:
At Rouse Hill shopping centre in Sydney's north-west, there is plenty of "pinging" going on as shoppers pass through the busy mall.

That's the connection their phones are making with the Wi-Fi in the mall where landlord GPT Group has already laid in fibre-optic cable and is pursuing a $250 million redevelopment.

It is all part of the $9.5 billion property trust's effort to harness the power of data analytics - big data - to boost productivity in their malls.

"If we can influence a shopper in how they behave and get them to visit the asset and spend more. For us that is nirvana," said GPT's head of retail Vanessa Orth.

"Then it is about driving productivity, helping our retailers convert sales. If we have productive retailers, they are making money and we ultimately make money."

The embrace of big data has sent GPT on a sharp learning curve in the past two years.

Analysing those "pings" to the W-Fi ultimately allows GPT to measure how effective the landlord's electronic direct mail marketing has been, which is sent to consumers who have signed up to a database.

"The beauty of Wi-Fi is that we can then track whether any of them return to the asset and whether any return to the store," Ms Orth said.

"How powerful has that content been to drive behaviour?

"For us it's about how can we influence behaviour with content, really curating content to make it relevant for that person."

That level of analysis is the holy grail of any marketing effort, allowing the retail landlord to determine its effectiveness down to an individual level and modifying the message according to market segments.

It's just one of a slate of strategies GPT employs.

Another focus is "lease line conversion"- the proportion of passersby who cross the threshold in to individual outlets.

Again, Wi-Fi data is used to monitor the amount of foot traffic passing a retailer and how many then stop to shop inside.

"If it's longer than four minutes, they're in to buy," Ms Orth said.

Wi-Fi is not the only arrow in GPT's quiver. Credit card data, provided by analytics firm Quantium, allows GPT and its retailers to analyse a mall's trade area and work out which neighbourhoods are spending and how they are spending.

"It's all about market share and owning your market," Ms Orth said. "We've just never had that data before."

GPT is also dipping deeper, beyond the information it gathers itself and into a "data lake" managed by consultancy EY, which draws on larger pools of analysis. Further ahead it may be able to finesse its reach to individual consumers, beaming customised messages as they pass through the mall.
direction: GPT Group shares likely to rise

headline: Landmark Airtasker, CBA deal
text:
Commonwealth Bank of Australia has signed a landmark deal with online jobs marketplace Airtasker that they hope will change the way customers prove the veracity of their identity in the online sharing economy.

The companies have kicked off a pilot program that lets CBA customers prove their identity on the online jobs market to help establish a more reliable reputation. Engendering trust is crucial for platforms, individuals and businesses in the online realm where strangers hook up to complete jobs and pay for jobs.

After connecting through an application programming interface (API), CBA will verify Airtasker customer names and dates of birth against bank account information to let them add a CBA "badge" to their Airtasker profile.

In the future, CBA could vouch for its customers by publishing the longevity of the banking relationship and, should customers approve, the strength of their balance sheet.

Airtasker CEO Tim Fung said building trust was the most important thing for businesses operating online.

"Airtasker's role is trying to create that fabric of social trust and, by doing that, unlocking e-commerce potential."

But Mr Fung said for most businesses, the cost of establishing trust could be very high.

Airtasker wants to extend the CBA deal into other areas where reputation can be digitised and shared, including licences and university qualifications.

"It is in our interest to help build trust with other users on our platform, because that will help them know you and work with you, and that is going to create more transactions, and more revenue," Mr Fung said.

Pete Steel, CBA executive general manager of digital, said it would look to open its API to other members of the sharing economy who had the necessary high-level security protocols to help CBA's customers establish their credentials. "You could imagine a world where it could be valuable for a small business or consumer to say 'I have banked with you for 10 years', or 'I am a 5-star customer'. We have to be very careful about privacy. But ... that is a direction we could go in the future: to bring our view about a customer and help them exert their reputation into the sharing economy."

Australia's digital economy could be turbo-charged if the government led cross-industry collaboration to develop digital identity solutions that could be used to streamline online activity, Mr Fung said. "The government should be focusing on this like a piece of infrastructure. The connections between people are inefficient because we all spend this time vetting each other, over and again.

"Even things like 100-point ID checks: you do it with one company, and then the next and next - all separately. Why don't the companies talk to each other, so we don't have to do it again and again? You would create massive efficiencies in how we work with other people."
direction: CBA shares likely to rise

headline: LABOR SENATOR: 'I'M INCREDIBLY INSECURE'
text:
Labor's Sam Dastyari is hoping to launch a comeback by being open about his personal shortfalls, writes Aaron Patrick, but can he resist his natural instincts?

Lunch with a banker fell through so I gave Sam Dastyari a try.

On an hour's notice, one of Australian politics' great self promoters agreed to meet at Fratelli Fresh on Bridge Street in central Sydney. The 34-year-old senator turned up solo 20 minutes late.

"I'm devastated we're not doing this with kebabs," he said, and launched into a cred-establishing anecdote about being asked to open a friend's fast-food outlet around the corner. "You can put that in. You're going to ruin my reputation."

Dastyari was part way through a publicity campaign for his redemptive memoir, One Halal of a Story, which included an Australian Story episode and enough media coverage to make a regular author blush.

In my mind, Dastyari personifies the light and the dark of the Labor Party: an aspirational movement that champions enlightened social advancement, its execution tarnished by cynical street politics.

The son of Iranian immigrants fleeing an oppressive political regime, Dastyari raced through the NSW Labor apparatus on sheer chutzpah and superman-like energy. The Right faction rewarded him with a Senate seat in 2013. Exploiting the latitude of the Senate committee system, he turned himself into one of the most visible Labor politicians in Canberra, a remarkable achievement for a rookie backbencher.

His downfall took place last year after The Australian Financial Review drew attention to a 10-month-old disclosure that he had received about $1670 from a Chinese businessman. He also asked pro-Chinese questions of Australian public servants after a Chinese donor covered a $5000 legal bill.

For a Labor activist who emerged from the NSW division of the party when it was controlled by the corrupt now-prison-dwelling Eddie Obeid ("an incredibly charming, charismatic person," Dastyari says), the behaviour was a depressing example of the venality that has undermined the party's moral authority.

After resigning as Labor's consumer affairs spokesman rather than be sacked, Dastyari is seeking what he calls a "second look" from voters, and presumably the media. Which is why he agreed to lunch, even though I asked at his failed mea-culpa press conference last year if he was a Chinese agent and then reported he had sent me a text saying "screw you".

Dastyari, who disappeared from public view, admitted seeking a doctor's diagnosis for depression. For therapy, he started writing an autobiographical Rolling Stone article that morphed into One Halal of a Story.

"I went through a very deep, dark period," he volunteered. "What I need to learn to do is slow down and think before I act, be a bit calmer, but I don't want to lose my sense of adventure."

The restaurant had assigned us a wall-side table where we could be seen by the whole room. We requested and were given a larger, more discreet seating. I ordered Fratelli's signature entree, soft cheese wrapped in a zucchini flower. "This is amazing," Dastyari told the Italian waitress.

Carolina had been in Australia a few months. I asked if she recognised my guest. "He's a senator?" she tentatively answered.

"You're famous!" I said. "Politician famous - that doesn't count as famous," he replied, the self deprecation sounding rote.

"I reckon you've got a higher profile than 95 per cent of the shadow ministry," I said.

"I'm not sure that's right, and not all of that is good, let's be clear," he said.

"People say you're a show pony."

"Yeah, yeah, and also I'm controversial. I'm polarising."

In an age where politics is fought minute-to-minute and keyboard-to-keyboard, Dastyari is a digital gladiator. A self-made video about Sydney house prices has 1 million views, a huge number for a political ad dressed up as entertainment.

Other stunts like having his book halal certified, or calling praying on a Friday at Sydney's Lakemba mosque the most Australian thing you could do, also drew huge interest online, much of it negative.

I asked if the abuse ever hurt. "Not really, because I bait it," he said. "I know that is going to get the Right-wing trolls to go crazy. I think that's who I am."

A curious mixture of indifference to criticism and personal insecurity, Dastyari said he was concerned by the opinions of only two people: wife Helen Dastyari and Labor leader Bill Shorten.

"Is that because Bill has the power to promote you?" I ask.

"That's because I feel a sense of guilt about putting Bill through all that stuff last year. You know Bill - he's a working-class boy. He was so angry."

I burst out laughing. Blue-collar Bill is one of the great tropes of the MBA's life. The Labor leader's mother was a legal academic who sent him to Melbourne's most expensive Catholic school.

Dastyari's parents are known and respected within Sydney's small Iranian community for their intelligence and grace. Their only and adored son chose to marry into another community - the Labor Party - and wed the daughter of Peter Barron, a Labor fixture and press secretary to Prime Minister Bob Hawke.

I met Helen at a dinner organised by Dastyari's publisher a month earlier. In conversation, Helen lit up.

At other times, she seemed to observe her husband's garrulous networking with silent resignation - at one point he was surrounded by six fascinated women - as though sitting through a slapstick performance for the umpteenth time.

Was I being too harsh, I asked.

"No not at all," he said. "Helen's view has always been that private Sam and public Sam are much more different people."

"Don't tell me there's a quiet Sam?" I asked. "Is there a vulnerable Sam?"

"She thinks there is a lot of bravado in the Dastyari performance," he said. "I'm an incredibly insecure person. I think a lot of people in politics are incredibly insecure - the fact that you need that level of public adulation."

Dastyari eats pork and drinks alcohol. For lunch, he ordered a T-bone steak with potatoes and a light beer. He polished off the meat and let me pick a few butter-smeared potatoes off his plate. He ordered a second $12 beer (the profit margins must be great), which I took as a sign he was pretending to enjoy my company. I appreciated the effort.

Both our phones kept ringing, which was a little annoying because it tripped my iPhone's recorder. Helpfully, Dastyari showed me that by switching to airplane mode, calls wouldn't stop the recording.
