(When I returned from the bathroom he was typing away on an iPad, which he put away as soon as he saw me.)

He wanted to talk about his plan to give Australia Post a banking licence, a step he believes could drive down bank fees and save failing country post offices. Attacking the banking industry has been good for his career. Dastyari proposed a royal commission into banks a year before it become Labor Party policy.

"This is part of your jihad against the banks?" I ask, tabloid headlines in my eyes.

"I'm careful with the word jihad as a non-practising Muslim," he said. (Dastyari was particularly pleased with his response to this question. I know because he rang the next day to discuss what I was going to write.)

Instead of underplaying his ethnicity to the broader community - which other MP would celebrate the word halal? - Dastyari is marketing his immigrant heritage. His own life is his political message.

That message is resolutely liberal, in the classical sense of the word. By framing the Australian national character through its acceptance of immigrants like himself - a tolerance he knows is being strained by conflict between the mostly Christian West and Islamic extremists from Asia and the Middle East - Dastyari is the anti-One Nation. Perhaps he's the antidote to anti-Mulism Australian sentiment.

"While it might specifically refer to food prepared in keeping with Islamic traditions, the word 'halal' has come to mean much more than that," he writes in One Halal of a Story. "In the language of far-Right politicians and social-media trolls, 'halal' is code for 'Muslim'.

"So just as rejecting halal has become a way for bigots to 'nudge nudge wink wink' their values to each other, tucking into a halal snack pack speaks to the embrace of a more open and pluralistic Australia. That, and a raging hangover."

Dastyari's mission is to make politics interesting, which is why he was so open during our lunch. With cynicism towards mainstream politicians on the rise, competition for the swinging voter is intense. Labor's future supporters don't watch television news or buy newspapers. Dastyari is reaching out to voters with whom Shorten or Malcolm Turnbull struggle to communicate. The danger, of course, is that Dastyari won't be taken seriously by the establishment that will determine how far his political career progresses: the leaders of his own party, and others who can throw up crash barriers in front of the Dastyari bus.

He may be a media tart. But Dastyari isn't stupid. His parliamentary mentor is Chris Bowen, the shadow treasurer and a likely candidate for Labor leader if Shorten fails.

Maybe it was the alcohol, or the gravital force of his downfall, but the end-of-lunch conversation drifted back to Dastyari's self-destruction. "This book is about that press conference, right?" I said, accusingly.

"Well, read it. The book's about a lot of things."

"Aren't you asking voters for redemption, for a second chance?"

"Redemption and second chance are very strong language. What I am asking for is a second look. What I want to do is this: we make politics boring; we make politics stale. This is an incredibly fun, interesting, exciting business we are in, and it is also absurd and I try to capture a lot of that absurdity."

I tried to tease out what job he wants in a Labor government. I got nowhere. "You know what I really want to do in life?" he said. "Sydney talkback radio."

When her shift finished, Carolina the waitress came over and shook hands. She seemed genuinely pleased to have served us. After I settled the bill, Dastyari quipped "Do they take RMB?"

Always the joker. W

FRATELLI FRESH,

Bridge Street, Sydney

1 large Pellegrino water, $9.50

4 Vixen beer, $48

2 zucchini flowers, $10

1 chicken scaloppine, $29

1 Bistecca steak, $32

Total: $128.50

This is an incredibly fun, interesting, exciting business we are in, and it is also absurd.
direction: no clear market direction implied

headline: Cyber raids lift interest in Israeli firm
text:
Israeli cyber security company Votiro Cybersec has closed a $11.2 million capital raising from Australian investors, before an imminent expansion down under and an IPO on the Australian Securities Exchange.

The company's pre-IPO round, which it said was oversubscribed, saw cornerstone investor Daniel Sekers' Divergent Group return, in addition to Redfield Asset Management and a range of undisclosed high-net-worth backers and small funds.

Votiro chief executive Itay Glick told The Australian Financial Review that interest was boosted by the number of high-profile attacks in recent weeks, such as WannaCry and Petya, which he believed would have been prevented with Votiro's technology.

The company has patented a process known as "content disarm and reconstruction", which involves breaking down files into objects, neutralising any malicious code and reconstructing the file into a safe to use and fully functional file. This lets the company prevent unknown threats, known as zero-day exploits.

"In the WannaCry example, you could have an email sent to you and when you open the attachment it installs the malware and starts propagating in the network," Mr Glick said. "With Votiro, we'd neutralise the attachment before it goes to the user. When the user opens the email, they'd either see a blank document as the attachment or as it was without the malware."

Experts have said WannaCry was not spread by emails, rather by targeted attacks on organisations using older versions of Windows, but many attacks are propagated by phishing emails.

Votiro's capital-raising plans were first revealed by The Australian Financial Review in March and the company still intends to list on the ASX at the end of 2017. The cyber security firm launched in 2010 and its founders, Mr Glick and Aviv Grafi, both previously served in the 8200 cyber-warfare unit of the Israeli defence force.

In May, the business was named as one of Gartner's "cool vendors" of 2017.

Mr Glick said the company was in the process of choosing between Melbourne and Sydney for its new headquarters, and a decision was expected in the coming days.

"Australia is investing heavily in being a global player in cyber security, and regionally it sits well with the Asia-Pacific as our fastest-growing region," he said. "There is some heavy competition between the states and we're looking at how they can best assist the business. It could be an incentive plan, tax credit, or if they are able to make introductions for us to the right people. They're very aggressive on the incentives and we're trying to get the best for the business."

In the first half of the year, the company claimed to have grown sales more than 600 per cent, but it would not disclose sales or revenue figures. It also now has 1.5 million users from governments, defence forces, financial institutions, critical infrastructure, hospitals and stock exchanges.

When asked why so many zero-day exploits still occur when technology such as Votiro's exists, Mr Glick said the challenge was to convince companies they needed to upgrade their current cyber defences.

"Organisations are used to using what they already have and they don't know to invest in something new unless someone tells them they're extremely exposed," he said.
direction: bullish for Votiro Cybersec and the broader cyber security sector

headline: FAMILY MONEY
text:
A paper trail will keep the peace, writes Bina Brown.

They may have raised you, provided opportunities and selflessly supported you during your youth, but when it comes to helping elderly parents it may pay to make it a gift with strings attached.

With the entry cost of aged care accommodation anywhere between $300,000 and $1 million, it is not uncommon for successful kids to stump up all or some extra cash just to ensure their parents are in a nicer place than they could afford alone.

But before you start transferring money and signing contracts, make sure you look at the bigger picture.

Like most situations involving government support, money and family, it can get complicated and be detrimental to your and their financial situation.

If the quoted market price of a room in an aged care facility is $550,000 but a loved one only has $250,000 in the bank plus the age pension to survive on, then the kids should be the avenue of last resort when it comes to making a lump sum payment towards the room. So says Aged Care Steps technical director Lara Hansen. That's because it will impact on their pension entitlement and could skew any inheritance.

There are three cost components of aged care: the accommodation cost which is set by the facility in the form of a refundable accommodation deposit (RAD); the basic daily care fee which is set at 85 per cent of the age pension; and a means-tested care fee.

Rather than pay the RAD as a lump sum, it can be paid as an equivalent daily interest charge known as the daily accommodation payment (DAP). The rate is set by the government and is currently 5.76 per cent.

Residents also have the option to pay a combination of the RAD and DAP or to pay a part RAD and then deduct the DAP from this. As an example of the latter and using the same $550,000 example, a resident could pay $250,000 RAD and use this to finance the interest payments of $47.47 a day. This would obviously mean less deposit would be returned to the estate.

Also watch out for facilities that charge additional daily fees of around $20 for residents who pay the RAD upfront, rather than in instalments.

Further, if the facility offers rooms with extra or additional services, it can charge a further $20-$120 a day for better quality furnishings, a choice of meals, newspapers or wine with dinner. For many elderly people these can make all the difference.

These are the three options generally considered by adult children, and how you need to navigate each situation:

■ Kids pay the RAD

Paying the full RAD on someone else's behalf might look like the simplest solution to ensuring a good room in a better facility. But regardless of who pays the RAD, it becomes the resident's asset for aged care fee purposes and generally gets repaid to the estate when the resident dies.

If the intention is for the person who paid the money to get it back, this has to be documented. Otherwise, says estate law specialist with DDCS Lawyers Rebecca Tetlow, the RAD could end up being distributed to a number of beneficiaries in the deceased resident's will. "It might mean the parent updating their will to account for the fact a child has paid the extra money or having a document loan agreement so there is an obligation on the resident's estate to pay the money back after death," she explains.

Complications may arise if the parent has impaired decision-making capacity and the child intending to make the RAD payment is the enduring power of attorney. This is because whoever has power of attorney cannot enter into a conflict transaction and so needs to tread carefully.

"It may be that they are signing a document as the lender and on behalf of their parent as the borrower, so are at risk of being on both sides of the transaction," Tetlow says.

Solutions depend on whether there are other attorneys and whether other siblings are willing to entering into an agreement with the one lending the money that the money will be repaid. Attorneys in this position should obtain specific legal advice.

Aged Care Steps' Hansen says even if there is a loan agreement with the kids, using the funds to pay the RAD can have unintended financial consequences on their parents' ongoing care costs.

Payment of a RAD doesn't count towards a resident's assets for age pension purposes but it does get counted as an asset when it comes to calculating their means-tested care fee. Unfortunately, there is no loan offset available for funds borrowed to pay the RAD, which means 100 per cent of the paid RAD is assessable.

"It may mean that the person paying the RAD may also need to help out on an ongoing basis because there is a higher means-tested care fee," Hansen adds.

So using the earlier example, if the kids were to pay the full RAD of $550,000, the means-tested care fees would increase by about $10,000 a year. If the kids pay for the higher monthly bill, they would need to decide whether they are doing it with no obligation or want the funds to be repaid, in which case they would need to look at including this in a loan agreement, says Hansen.

■ Kids pay the extra expenses

Facilities with extra service status don't necessarily offer a higher level of care for residents, and can have a significant effect if someone is low on cash.

For an extra charge of anywhere between $20 and $120 a day, the facility provides additional hotel-type services and a higher standard of accommodation, food and services than the average provided by aged care homes which do not have extra service status.

Affinity Aged Care Financial Services adviser Donald Swanborough says most new facilities are predominantly "extra" or "additional services" which, for someone on the pension alone, may potentially put it out of reach unless family helps. Affinity provides specialist financial advice tailored to aged care

"The issue for that family is how do the paying members get reimbursed for their outlay when mum or dad passes?" says Swanborough.

Tetlow says that if whoever is covering the extra expenses expects to be repaid at some point, it is important to be clear about it with other family members.

"It may be that the funds won't be available until after death. If the money spent has benefited the resident and it is properly accounted for, then the executor would assess it and may reimburse the person - but this shouldn't be automatically assumed. Communication is the key here. These arrangements are always improved if everyone in the family is clear on who is paying for what and what is intended in the end," says Tetlow.

■ Granny flat rights

Promises of "don't worry mum, you won't have to go to a home - we will look after you" aren't always easily met but one possibility is to make use of the "granny flat right" provisions within the Social Security Act.

It is a way of transferring the parents' home or other funds to the children, purchasing a home in the children's name or paying for renovation costs to the children's existing home in return for the right to be provided with lifetime accommodation without falling foul of Centrelink's gifting rules. No "granny flat" has to be actually built but there does have to be a designated room or area that allows for the parent's exclusive occupancy.

There also has to be an agreement in place which shows they have security of tenure and details any other arrangements like contributing to the cost of running the house etc.

Swanborough says that if parents transfer cash to the kids to create a "granny flat right", the amount that is allowed that is not subject to gifting rules is based on a Centrelink formula involving the couple's pension and their life expectancy.

For example, an 80-year-old couple could put aside $336,948 to give to the kids which is exempt from Centrelink's gifting rules.

Hansen says families have to be clear about the purpose of the arrangement for the money to be exempt from Centrelink gifting rules if the parents subsequently needed to go into aged care.

"If you have an Aged Care Assessment Team assessment for residential aged care then a granny flat right may not be the best solution.

It says to Centrelink that as a move to aged care could have been expected at the time, that any transfer of property or funds to create the right will most likely be treated as a gift rather than a genuine living arrangement for the longer term," says Hansen.

"If there is a move to aged care within five years of the right being created, gifting rules may apply, resulting in the loss of the age pension and potentially not having enough funds available to pay for aged care fees," she says.

When it comes to estate planning, any property or funds transferred to the child in exchange for the right no longer forms part of the parent's estate, as once the parent vacates the right ceases to exist. But gifting rules may apply if the parent vacates the right and instead lives permanently somewhere else.

This means that upon the death of the parent, any property or money handed over to the child will not be distributed in accordance with their will, as it is no longer an estate asset, says Hansen.

Bina Brown is a director of aged care placement company Third Age Matters.
direction: cautious for family wealth and aged care sector

headline: Explorers seem attractive as oil gushes higher
text:
Despite the best efforts of US shale oil drillers, Opec remains the main source of crude supply for the world. More than once in the past it has promised to curtail supply to support prices, most recently last November. This time the promises have begun to bite. A supply squeeze from key Opec producers is having an impact on inventories. As supply tightens, a more optimistic view on oil is taking hold.

Brent crude has rallied strongly, up more than a quarter since late June to $US58, hitting new highs for the year. One way to measure the change in sentiment is by observing the spread between near-term and longer-term oil prices on its futures curve. A higher price for future delivery should encourage traders to buy oil, store it and sell forward to lock in the price difference. Recently, this spread has reversed as oil prices for near-term delivery have risen faster than those in the months ahead.

That suggests oil supply is tighter and that inventories will have to cover the shortfall. This drawdown of oil stocks has already begun. The global overhang of supply that has plagued the crude market for years has fallen a third, from a peak of 600 million barrels above the five-year inventory average, believes Energy Aspects. That sounds great news for the companies that produce the black gold.

So far this quarter, that has been the case. Shares of exploration and production companies listed in the UK have burst back into life. Former leading lights in the sector such as Africa specialist Tullow Oil and North Sea driller Premier Oil have jumped as much as 30 per cent since the end of June.

Part of the reason is that both carry substantial amounts of net debt. Tullow has borrowings well over three times its operating profits (adjusted for depreciation). At Premier it is four times. With leverage like this, their earnings sensitivity to higher oil revenues is acute. Share price gains at the big integrated groups have trailed these companies. Even fellow explorer Cairn Energy, which has sufficient cash on hand to fund its drill programme, has also lagged behind.

Portfolio managers, most of whom will have relatively little oil weighting among their holdings, may remain unimpressed. Most E&Ps are still too small to have much impact on portfolio performance. That does not take away from the opportunity, though. These oil explorers should go higher still.
direction: bullish for oil exploration and production shares

headline: IAG ups guidance on unexpected claims performance
text:
Despite taking a hit from Tropical Cyclone Debbie, Insurance Australia Group has raised its margin guidance after compulsory third-party liability, professional risks and workers' compensation claims were lower than the insurer had expected.

The company behind brands such as NRMA and CGU said its reported margin guidance for full-year 2017 will increase from a range of 10.5-12.5 per cent to a range of 13.5-15.5 per cent.

This is a turnaround from April, when the group's insurance margin guidance was trimmed from a range of 12.5-14.5 per cent to a more modest range of 10.5-12.5 per cent, after an estimated $140 million in claims from Tropical Cyclone Debbie and heavy claims from a northern Sydney hailstorm in late February. IAG's previous margin guidance assumed reserve releases - or reductions in the amount of money it holds against claims - of 2 per cent of net earned premium (NEP), but this has now been raised to 5 per cent of NEP, equivalent to $425 million.

"We assume the bulk of these releases relate to wage inflation for NSW CTP. While this does not relate to current accident year trends, we believe it signals IAG is also now more confident on the stabilisation of trends in NSW," UBA analyst James Coghill said.

This comes after QBE issued a shock earnings downgrade that prompted investors to wipe $1.9 billion off the insurer's market value last week.

The beleaguered global insurer was also issued with a "please explain" notice from the sharemarket regulator over the timing of its downgrade, with questions being raised over whether it should have let the market know earlier about "significant" issues in its emerging markets business.

The third of Australia's largest listed general insurers, Suncorp has also been under some pressure.

A recent investor note from Morgan Stanley said that where IAG offers the "purest" general insurance exposure, Suncorp's is diluted by its bank and its "underperforming" life business.

"Suncorp has four times the CTP exposure where back book margins and reserve releases are likely to come under pressure," it said.

UBS tipped more releases to come due to long-tail classes, such as CTP and worker's compensation claims, benefiting from the spate of low inflation. "While we typically view abnormal releases (those above normalised releases of 1 per cent NEP) as one off in nature, with low inflation likely to persist near term we see higher reserve release continuing to benefit FY18 and FY19, albeit at a moderating rate," it said. At the close of the market on Wednesday IAG shares were 6.41 per cent higher, up 42¢ to $6.97.

The company, which is due to release its full-year results on August 23, said other assumptions relating to margin guidance remain unchanged for FY17, including net losses for the year from natural disasters of $850 million.

In June, IAG boosted its protection against major disasters by purchasing additional reinsurance cover worth $1 billion.

The insurer already holds gross protection of $7 billion, which is due for renewal on January 1.
direction: IAG shares likely to rise

headline: Banks forced to scrap some sales bonuses
text:
Banks will be forced to make wholesale changes to the way they reward staff members under a series of recommendations that look to scrap bonus payments to retail bank employees that rely on hitting sales targets.

They will also be asked to stop paying some incentives to mortgage brokers under the recommendations which seek to do away with soft dollar payments, such as travel and hospitality, and volume payments on top of upfront and trailing commissions. The recommendations will also crack down on campaign offers, in which a bank ramps up bonus terms for a limited period to take market share.

The Sedgwick Retail Banking Remuneration Review, which has been conducted by former Australian Public Service Commissioner Stephen Sedgwick for the Australian Bankers Association, will be released on Wednesday and will contain 21 recommendations. The review aims to bridge the emerging trust gap between the industry and its customers by breaking the nexus between targets for selling products and incentive payments.

The centrepiece of the reforms is the scaling back of bonus schemes where hitting sales targets is the main driver of behaviour. The review will recommend sales targets for a range of products sold by staff - including credit cards, personal loans and insurance - contribute a maximum weighting of 50 per cent in any incentive payment and must fall to 33 per cent or less by 2020.

The review recommends that any incentive or variable reward payment be constructed with a range of inputs that better reflect the responsibilities and consumer expectations flowing from the role while improving consumer outcomes. It will also recommend that volume-based triggers such as accelerators, modifiers and gateways in which staff become eligible for higher incentive rates are also dropped immediately. The devices were identified by Mr Sedgwick in his first issues paper and are effectively triggers for payment increases when targets are reached. Sedgwick has recommended they be replaced by devices that lead to better customer outcomes.

The recommendations will pose considerable cultural and procedural challenges for the banks with the reforms consigning both formal and informal sales leaderboards to the junk heap. Performance management systems which typically rely heavily on sales metrics will also need to be restructured to include much greater emphasis on staff behaviour and customer outcomes.

While some bank employees will be largely unaffected by the changes, a large cohort of sales-oriented staff members will find the new regime challenging and are expected to drift away from the banking system entirely.

The aim of the report is to introduce reward systems that are "product neutral" and therefore reduce the chances of systems that hurt customers.

The report contains a set of highly specific recommendations for payments to the mortgage broking industry but in what may be a surprise for the industry it stops short of recommending the same guidelines it has laid out for retail banking staff.

Mortgage brokers have grown to be an essential channel for banks with brokers now accounting for 54 per cent of all mortgage flow. Brokers are primarily rewarded on the volume of loans that they sell customers.

The review recommends that the banks work in conjunction with their commercial partners to introduce a new focus on governance comparable with the improvements they make to their own staff incentive programs.

It does, however, get prescriptive on some matters for mortgage brokers and recommends putting an end to soft dollar payments, cutting out the third layer of incentives such as volume-based payments which are paid in addition to upfront and trailing commissions, and finally, stopping the practice of ramping up incentives payable to brokers during campaigns.

Although the review does not find enough evidence to support an outright ban on all product-based payments in retail banking it does conclude that many of the banks' current practices carry enough risks that they must be changed.

Recommendations are expected to be implemented by the banks as soon as practically possible with any transitional arrangements to be completed by 2020.

The banks are also expected to include details of the new incentive arrangements in their annual reports to shareholders and update changes.

The big four banks are expected to support the recommendations with NAB and Commonwealth Bank pledging to implement the report's final recommendations following the release of an issues paper in January.

The review was commissioned by the Australian Bankers Association as part of a six-point plan announced in April 2016 to address the trust gap between customers and the banks following a series of scandals in wealth management and life insurance.

Paying the price

Key recommendations from the Sedgwick Retail Banking Remuneration Review

■ Banks remove variable reward payments and campaign related incentives that are directly linked to sales or the achievement of sales targets

■ Variable reward payments no longer include any accelerators related to financial measures

■ Banks cease providing mortgage brokers with volume based incentives that are additional to upfront and trail commissions

SOURCE: FINANCIAL REVIEW
direction: downward pressure on major bank shares

headline: Ramsay under fire over CEO succession plan
text:
Ramsay Health Care chairman Michael Siddle has come under fire over the company's failure to name a successor to chief executive Chris Rex, who said he would step down this year.

Mr Rex flagged his retirement from the nation's largest private hospital operator, after 22 years with the company, on Thursday while delivering an upgrade to full-year guidance, and now expects net profit growth of 12 per cent to 14 per cent for 2016-17, up from 10 per cent to 12 per cent previously stated.

Despite the upbeat outlook, several shareholders were unimpressed that Mr Rex's heir was not named on the same day as his retirement, as Wesfarmers did earlier in February with the succession of managing director, Richard Goyder, and his replacement, Rob Scott.

Ramsay shares fell $2.31, or 3.2 per cent, to $69.43, its lowest close for two weeks.

"My concern is the apparent lack of succession planning, whether there is something going on behind the scenes, I don't know," Greencape Capital portfolio manager David Pace said.

"It's the board's role to ensure that the continuity and succession plan protects shareholders. The lesson learned from Brambles, when the successor is an external appointment, is that there is risk around discontinuity in strategy and earnings guidance." Watermark Funds Management analyst Nick Cameron also criticised Mr Siddle.

"As a shareholder we are not happy with the way the board has handled this news," he said. "This is an ASX50 company with a reputation of being one of the best-managed companies in Australia. We thought they would have delivered a more detailed succession plan to the market to coincide with Mr Rex's announced retirement. It's disappointing because this has overshadowed what was a strong operational result, highlighting Ramsay's superior platform versus its competitors."

Mr Cameron was hopeful investors will be provided with greater access to other top managers in the business.

Ramsay has a history of passing the baton internally, with late founder Paul Ramsay passing to Pat Grier, and Mr Grier passing to Mr Rex.

Sources suggested possible internal candidates include group chief operating officer Craig McNally, who has been at Ramsay for over 25 years, and Danny Sims, who runs the Australian operations and is also responsible for hospitals in Indonesia and Malaysia. US-born Mr Sims has been at Ramsay since 2003.

Mr Siddle said succession planning is well under way but declined to confirm if there was a shortlist of candidates. He said it could be months before a new head is named.

"Frankly, we are not ready (to announce a new CEO)," he said. "Like any board we are always looking at succession planning. We are looking internally and externally. We have engaged a recruitment firm and been talking to them for some time in anticipation of this."

Mr Siddle added that while prior CEOs have been internal, "it's in shareholders' interest" to conduct an external search.

Mr Rex, who turns 58 in June, said he has many things he would like to do upon his retirement, and has many interests like academics, travel and fitness. "Over the past few months I decided this is the right time. I've got millions of things I would like to do before I die and I want to get cracking on them," he said.

"On the other side, from a work point of view, it has been a fantastic 22 years, nine as CEO, and I want to leave while I'm energetic and give someone the opportunity to take the company forward for the next exciting stage of growth."

Gruelling travelling requirements for the CEO given Ramsay's operations now span six countries played a role in his decision.

Following analysts' questions, Mr Rex confirmed he did plan on selling some shares, given much of his personal wealth was tied up in the company; he owns an $84 million stake.

Under Mr Rex's tenure the company's value has grown from a market value of $1.8 billion to nearly $14.5 billion, making it one of the largest healthcare companies in the world operating 220 hospitals and facilities.

He said Ramsay had a very robust start to the year, with revenue up 3.5 per cent to $4.3 billion in the six months to December, while net profit climbed 13.8 per cent to $255.9 million.

Australian hospitals (and pharmacy) revenue grew 8.8 per cent $2.4 billion. Australian earnings before interest and tax climbed 15 per cent $348.1 million, driven by strong admissions growth.

Mr Rex said the Australian pharmacy strategy announced in August is continuing to add retail stores, with Ramsay looking to triple its doors to 60 retail pharmacies at the end of June. Its UK operations had strong admissions growth but was negatively impacted by an ongoing shortage of nursing staff and the need to use agency staff.
direction: Ramsay Health Care shares likely to face further downward pressure

headline: Insolvency timing raised over collapse of EON Sports Radio
text:
The liquidator of EON Sports Radio is investigating if the collapsed digital radio business was trading while insolvent earlier this year.

EON was placed into liquidation by administrators Cor Cordis last week, after going off the air in May. The business had been struggling since late last year with staff often going without pay and entitlements.

While it had live A-League and National Basketball League rights, as well as having Australian radio rights to Talk sports’ English Premier League coverage, EON had struggled to attract sponsors.

Cor Cordis, appointed in June as administrator, stated in a recent creditors report that EON ‘‘may have been insolvent since April 2017’’ after funds pumped into the business over the previous two months had been exhausted. There are also questions being asked about EON’s connection to the struggling G1X digital racing media business and one-time high-flying education group Acquire Learning, now in administration.

EON was launched last year by Acquire Learning founder John Wall and music business identity Glenn Wheatley, who had formed the brand EON in the early 1980s with a traditional Melbourne FM radio station. Mr Wall had big plans for EON, saying at the time of its launch: ‘‘What we want to be is the Fox Sports for digital radio.’’

But documents prepared by Cor Cordis show EON lost $1.9 million since last July and posted negative revenue results in both December and January. Barry Wight of Cor Cordis said he was hopeful of selling some EON assets. ‘‘We are in discussions with a number of interested parties, and remain hopeful that a transaction can be achieved shortly,’’ Mr Wight said.

EON had been established almost a year after G1X was founded in a blaze of publicity in October 2015.Wall, a horseracing enthusiast, had ploughed several million dollars into the venture via Acquire, which counted former AFL chief executive Andrew Demetriou as chairman of its advisory board.

It is understood more than $1 million was spent on G1X’s marketing campaign that included billboards for its team of media identities. A television studio was also built in Acquire’s offices,while G1X sponsored races such as the Perth Cup and others in Melbourne and Sydney.

Several million dollars was also spent on the business acquiring a financial services licence and agreements with horse-racing authorities to launch G1X change in February, which Wall promoted as the first stock exchange for shares in horses anywhere in the world.

G1X change does not appear to be operating and there are now questions being raised about the relationship between G1X and Acquire, with documents showing Acquire is owed about $10.85 million in loans by G1X.

Acquire Learning was placed into voluntary administration in May. Cor Cordis’ Wight and Daniel Jura to witch are administrators on both EON Sports Radio and Acquire Learning, and are investigating Acquire and $25 million in loans to Acquire shareholders and G1X.

Mr Wall stepped down as a company director and owner of EON in March 2017 and sold his holding – held via an G1X company – to an EON employee and provided some funding as part of the deal, though documents showing the change of ownership were not lodged with the corporate regulator.

He had previously owned EON via G1X. Before then, G1X had been owned by Acquire but it sold G1X to a unit trust owned by Mr Wall in July last year.

Acquire had been a high-flying company in the education sector, making big profits from sharing the fee that the federal government paid learning providers to educate students, who were mostly taking out loans through VET FEE HELP scheme. But it struck trouble last year when the government pulled the VET FEE funding, leading to Acquire sacking more than 100 staff and downsizing its operations. It entered administration in April and was hit with a $4.5 million fine in May after being found to have taken advantage of vulnerable students.

Acquire’s financial issues are understood in turn to have caused funding problems at G1X. The Australian Financial Review has been told a string of former G1X employees have not been paid wages or superannuation entitlements and have now left the business.

G1X is still operating and is understood to be staffed by a handful of Acquire call centre employees, who sporadically update its website but send regular emails to a large mailing list promoting bookmakers.

The business is understood to be receiving tens of thousands of dollars a month from its bookmaker affiliate program.

Mr Wall did not return phone calls or answer emails.
direction: bearish for media and digital radio sector

headline: The RBA's economics problem: making the subject sexy again
text:
Go and ask a typical teenager what they think of economics and it's a fair bet they won't even have to utter a word and you'll know the answer.

The blank look on their face will say it all. If they do answer, maybe get ready for: It's so boring: It's useless. You can't use any of it. It's a scam.

Dismal science indeed. It's so bad even the Reserve Bank of Australia thinks it's an issue and more needs to be done to engage young people with the subject.

"In essence, economics has an image problem," said the RBA's Jacqui Dwyer, the head of the information department, in a speech to an education conference last weekend.

For sure economics can have competing paradigms and rival themes and forecasting can be a tough business, but good public policy needs the analytical skills you get by studying economics.

Yet no one is.

Dwyer's speech contained some alarming statistics that show a large decline over the past 25 years in the number of year 12 students taking economics as a subject.

There has also been a substantial drop in the number of schools that even bother to offer economics as a subject.

Dywer and the RBA worry that if students continue to sidestep traditional economics in favour of "new and arguably less-demanding subjects", such as business studies, then you're not going to get a good outcome when it comes to the big issues like long-term economic growth, jobs, climate change, income distribution, property prices and how to avoid another financial crisis.

Since 1992 the number of students doing economics in year 12 has fallen 70 per cent, according to Dwyer.

"While most of this decline occurred in the early 1990s, there has been a continued downward drift. This national pattern reflects very similar trends across the Australian states," she said.

The drop-off has been exacerbated by gender trends.

In 1992, about 50 per cent of students who were studying economics in their final year at school were female, whereas now just over a third of female students are taking it.

So where did it all go wrong?

The arrival of "business studies" in 1991 has had a huge impact and is a major reason for the decline in economics.

Business studies is now the fourth most popular subject, and is arguably a little easier to do, but even Dwyer notes there's more to it than that.

Back in the 1980s and early 1990s, there was a huge amount of national debate about financial deregulation that saw the Australian dollar floated and foreign banks open offices here. Then there was the labour market deregulation and tariff reform that were all hot topics of the day, she said.

But those days are gone. Now there's more pure politics at play and the state of the economy gets left to one side.

In addition, if schools don't offer the subject, it becomes less "core".

And then it gets back to the image thing.

"The bank's liaison has found that too few students understand what economics is and how it might be relevant to them," says Dwyer.

"Too few educators are equipped to teach it, and a growing share of high school teachers are delivering economics courses without having studied the subject." Then it just feeds on itself with not enough careers advisers, or educators, who can tell how it does relate to day-to-day living.

And then before you know it, Dwyer says, "little relevant Australian economic content and teaching resources are available".

A common complaint about economics these days is the large amount of mathematics involved, when the really interesting part is the insights into human behaviour and what makes them decide on a certain course of action.

One way to make economics more interesting is the website that uses Seinfeld episodes to explain certain concepts.

For example, one episode was devoted to how long Jerry, Elaine and George had to wait for a table at a Chinese restaurant.

They opted to stay and wait despite getting hungry and with plenty of other restaurants nearby.

That should help students understand opportunity cost.
direction: neutral to bearish for rate-sensitive sectors

headline: Buying into consumer diversity pays dividends
text:
Businesses that don't make allowances for customer diversity risk alienating a key part of the changing market, according to new research that finds customers from diverse backgrounds are more likely to feel unfairly treated.

One in three customers from diverse backgrounds walked away from a transaction in the past year because they felt disrespected, compared with one in five overall, according to a Deloitte report that surveyed 1244 individuals.

Those of diversity were defined as having an Indigenous background (3 per cent of population), being lesbian, gay or bisexual (11 per cent), disabled (18 per cent) or those who practise a noticeable faith. Diverse persons represent a larger part of the Australian population today thanthan at any time.

Notably, 59 per cent of respondents believed diverse customers were not always treated equitably and fairly in the marketplace.

Australian Human Rights Commission president Gillian Triggs lauded the research, which may be the first of its kind to explore consumer, rather than employee, diversity.

"We developed the thesis that maybe if we knew a little bit more about the diversity of the customer and responded to that in a business way, we would have good business outcomes and achieve some of the objectives of fairness, justice and human rights," she said. Ms Triggs explained that of the 22,000 inquiries and complaints she received in the past year, 28 per cent alleged discrimination on the basis of age, race, disability, or sexual orientation.

Deloitte partnered with Qantas, QBE, SBS and Westpac, large organisations that have ventured to cater for people of diversity. For example, Westpac developed video conferencing for deaf and hard-of-hearing customers, and Qantas offered a maternity leave pause in their frequent flyer program.

Qantas CEO Alan Joyce cited the company's long history of successfully adapting to the changing market. "If you don't reinvent yourself in the marketplace, you have no right to exist," he said. In addition to engaging customers of diversity, Mr Joyce pointed out that Australians are more socially aware and socially active today than ever. He said 80 per cent of all Australians will select brands that demonstrate a social conscience.

A recent Accenture study also found millennials (aged 18-35) were loyal to brands that were socially aware and supported worthwhile causes. As millennials continue to make up a larger portion of the market, understanding and accommodating people of diversity may be crucial to winning the support of consumers.

SBS chief executive Michael Ebeid offered advertising space to those who feature diversity in their marketing. He said that in many cases accommodating persons of diversity is "such a simple fix".

St George bank general manager Cathy Yuncken said the study was unique because it enabled organisations to view customer experience through a "different lens".

The panel of business owners from Qantas, SBS, St George, QBE and Westpac agreed the problem was more sophisticated than just adjusting their products to gain a competitive advantage. This is because of the broad knock-on effect of encouraging equality and diversity in their marketing.
direction: supportive for consumer-facing companies prioritizing inclusive customer service

headline: Pushpay shares jump on $33m placement
text:
Pushpay, an ASX-listed fintech seeking to convert United States church goers to donating via their mobile phones, has raised $US25 million ($33 million) in an oversubscribed placement as it targets a doubling of revenue next year.

Fifteen new institutional investors in Australia and New Zealand, where Pushpay is also listed, backed the private placement at $NZ1.51 per share, 26 per cent below the price when it raised $54 million ahead of its ASX float last October. The latest fundraising was managed and underwritten by Deutsche Craigs and Ord Minnett and initially reported by Street Talk.

When the Seattle-based company resumed trading on the ASX on Thursday, shares rose 6.9 per cent to $1.70.

Pushpay, which made a net loss of $US25 million for the year to March 31, is also planning to list in the US within the next three years as it plans to expand in North America, where it makes 97 per cent of its revenue and three-quarters of its 400 staff are based. It said it is targeting revenue of at least US$70 million in FY2018, double the level of this year, as existing customers put more of donations through the platform.

"People are used to using Amazon or Facebook in their normal life, but when they come to church, they get Windows 95 - they are so far behind the times," Pushpay co-founder and chief executive Chris Heaslip said. "Eighty-seven per cent of US church donations are made through cheques. We are driving conversions from using cheques to digital giving."

Pushpay, founded in mid 2011, makes one-third of its revenue by levying a software fee - which can range from $100 to $14,000 a month depending on the size of the church - while two-thirds comes from fees for processing credit card payments, around 1 per cent of the value of each donation.

In other action in the listed fintech sector on Thursday, Afterpay Touch Group, the new ASX-listed company formed from the merger of Afterpay and Touchcorp, said the deal had been completed after the schemes of arrangement received overwhelming shareholder support.

The new company has a market capitalisation of $660 million. Afterpay provides a deferred payment product for retailers, while Touch provides a payments platform used by telcos and retailers.

Executive chairman Anthony Eisen, a co-founder of Afterpay, said the deal "would boost the growth and earnings potential of the combined business". Afterpay Touch Group said on Thursday that Afterpay's underlying quarterly sales were up 87 per cent for the quarter ending June 30 to $271 million.
direction: bullish for Pushpay shares

headline: Asset sale pitch ramps up ahead of WA budget
text:
Dealmakers want to see a slice of the action when Western Australia's new Labor government hands down its maiden budget in four weeks, and are leaving no stone unturned in their quest to shake a few assets loose.

While a constant stream of investment bankers has been in and out of WA's Department of Treasury over the past 12 months, it is understood infrastructure fund managers have joined the procession in the lead-up to the September 7 budget.

With big infrastructure assets hard to come by, the big funds have been pitching their investment credentials with case studies showing what they have been able to do with privatised ports, electricity networks, toll roads and airports in Australia and globally.

Their pitch also leans on New South Wales' experience, where the much talked about "asset recycling" program saw mature infrastructure assets sold off to fund new projects. In turn, it has created plenty of jobs and helped turn around the state's economy.

And they also point to assets in Victoria and South Australia, too.

While WA's Treasury and even the politicians are said to be keen to hear the ideas, "privatisation" remains a dirty word among voters and the government is understandably hesitant to enact any plans only months after a successful election pitch to retain the likes of Western Power.

But the question is whether a looming $40 billion-odd debt pile, soft payroll tax, GST receipts and iron ore prices are enough to ease its stance.

The dealmakers hope so - and have come up with all sorts of potential sale ideas and structures, which could see assets and their associated debt shifted from the state's balance sheet and into private hands.

Of course, there is a lot of self-interest in the pitching. Privatisations have helped infrastructure managers such as Hastings Funds Management, IFM Investors and QIC Ltd fatten portfolios, increase their own annual fees and increase the chances of big performance payouts. Bankers reckon they have been pitching hard for the past two years, first to get former premier Colin Barnett to get the ball rolling with Utah Point, Fremantle Port and Western Power - none of which were sold - and now to convince the new government that it needs to move.

While Western Power is off the agenda, it is understood bankers have honed in on WA's state-owned power provider Horizon Power, which services regional and remote areas in the state's north-west.
direction: bearish for WA government-linked assets and utilities

headline: New $350m fund float hopes for a happy new year
text:
The dash for captive capital is showing no signs of slowing down as the 2018 pipeline of fund floats builds.

The latest to come to market is London- and Brisbane-based Gryphon Capital Investments with the float process expected to get under way in early 2018.

The credit fund is planning a $350 million listing of a trust that will target a 5 per cent per annum income, paid monthly to investors. The $1.8 billion secured credit investor specialises in global asset-backed securities.

The float would be the second credit fund to raise money via a listed structure in recent months. Earlier this year, Australian corporate loan fund Metrics Credit Partners raised over $500 million on the stock exchange. The Gryphon Capital Income Trust will target a slightly higher income of 3.5 percentage points over the Reserve Bank cash rate compared to Metric's target of 3.25 per cent.

These products are capitalising on retail investors' appetite for reliable income particularly in the absence of a retail corporate bond market and are seen as complementary to a hybrid portfolio because the securities in the fund rank higher in the capital structure.

Also waiting in the wings is Nikko Asset Management which has targeted an April 2018 listing of its equity income fund. The fund could raise up to $500 million.

Gryphon is being advised by Seed Partnerships, which has blazed a trail in listed investment offerings over the past 18 months. It is understood the syndicate will comprise Morgans, Bell Potter, Ord Minnett and Shaw and Partners with additional banks and brokers likely to join in the coming weeks.

And it seems new, more shareholder-friendly structures have become standard in the market after the VGI Partners listing earlier this year. Gryphon will follow suit and pay the costs of the IPO and all ongoing expenses. The firm will charge wholesale management fees of 0.75 per cent and is not expected to issue options.
direction: supportive for listed credit funds and income-focused REITs/trusts

headline: Growth-induced complacency
text:
Compared with past performances, it's been a remarkable achievement: 26 years of hardly interrupted economic growth, by some counts overtaking the Netherlands as the economy with the longest sustained expansion phase. It's a victory for good policymaking and Australian business enterprise.

The inflexible, protected and over-regulated pre-1980s Australian economy was prone to commodity-fuelled booms and busts. A flexible exchange rate and the discipline of foreign competition has provided the flexibility to absorb external shocks. Under Whitlam, the early 1970s produced "stagflation", the end of "full employment" and the jobless rate hit 5 per cent for the first time since the 1930s Depression. Trade union bastardry amid an OPEC oil price boom led to the recession and double-digit jobless rate of the early 1980s. Then the asset price boom generated by financial deregulation and a corporate borrowing binge turned into Paul Keating's "recession we had to have" a decade later. But the early 1990s broke Australia's inflation stick. With a reformed economy, Australia rode out the 1997 east Asian Crisis, the early 2000s tech wreck and notably, the global financial crisis. In the two decades following this recession, per capita incomes rose by two thirds in real terms.

But much of our most recent good performance has come from the luck of our China boom. Australian households used too much of this boom, and its aftermath, to borrow to record highs against over-inflated house prices. And Australian governments have borrowed too much to finance spending they just can't stop. Since iron ore prices peaked at $US180 a tonne in late 2011, Australia has basically been in an income recession. Our productivity growth has stalled, reflecting the lack of genuine economic reform for a decade and a half. And our sustainable rate of economic growth appears to have fallen. Now we are highly exposed to any new bad shock. Worst of all, Australia has become complacent about our remarkable modern prosperity and unable to commit to the economic growth required to secure it.

FWC is a useless relic

One day before figures showing the weakest economic growth since the global financial crisis, the Fair Work Commission jacked up Australia's minimum wage floor by 3.3 per cent or by $22 a week. Just as rising energy costs and digital competition are undermining established business models across the economy, the FWC has raised the already-high minimum price employers must pay Australians to work to $18.29 per hour or $694.90 per week. The competitive pressure explains why wages growth and inflation have ground to a virtual halt. The mining boom ratcheted up Australia's cost structure. Now that the boom has ended, it needs to fall, either through rising productivity or lower wages.

Trying to defy these forces, the FWC willfully denies the obvious consequence: a higher wage floor will mean less demand for labour, which will particularly hit the least-skilled. Labour market specialist Roger Wilkins from the University of Melbourne's HILDA longitudinal survey says he is stunned by the decision. But the FWC determines that it previously has been "over cautious" about the job-suppressing effects of a higher minimum wage. Research from the UK had "fortified" its view, backed by various contortions to explain away Australia's weak productivity performance and the extraordinary claim that the labour market looks to be in "a reasonably healthy state" even with a jobless rate stuck not much below 6 per cent. In the end, it hangs its permanent increase in the wage floor on a temporary jump in corporate profits caused by a spike in the iron ore price that already has mostly reversed.

The result is a reminder of how useless the FWC has become. It a relic of the past, exhumed by the previous Labor government to reinforce the power and patronage system of the labour movement. It has delivered a remorseful payback for its previous half-sensible but out-of-character decision to modestly cut job-destroying Sunday penalty rates.
direction: bearish for highly-leveraged households and property sector

headline: Speculators face pressure from high debt
text:
Some 1.9 million investment properties could come under selling pressure as speculators with multiple properties or those nearing retirement are forced to sell, Citi Research says.

Citi says the traditional response of investors to ride out a downturn was now less viable because of high debt levels, particularly among investors aged in their 50s and 60s.

The analysis of Digital Finance Analytics data revealed a surprisingly high level of property speculation, with around 12 per cent of residential investors owning six or more properties, a risk factor also highlighted by the Reserve Bank on Friday.

Taku Ekanayake, 29, has bought six investment properties in the past 30 months around Australia, primarily the suburbs of Brisbane, but has not been able to afford a home in Sydney.

The properties are tenanted and his mortgages are variable interest only.

"At the moment rental income is covering mortgage repayments. Interest rate increases are inevitable from current record lows. It is a concern to the general public because wage growth is flat. But in my case I can sit it out," Mr Ekanayake said.

"I will have to negatively gear the properties if there is an interest rate increase. I would also have to reassess the use of interest only variable rates," he said.

Options include reassessing the portfolio, switching to fixed rates, or increasing negative gearing, he said. Negative gearing is used used when the gross income generated by the investments is less than the cost of owning and managing the investment, including depreciation and interest.

He said another concern for investors is increase in supply of apartments and units around inner Melbourne and Brisbane that could put pressure on rents and make it tougher to find tenants.

"I have strong cash flows," said Mr Ekanayake, who was born in Tokyo. "If I sold my $2 million portfolio I could afford to buy a home in Sydney. But I am young and would prefer to keep investing in property and renting close to Sydney's central business district."

During the past nine months he has switched from IT consultancy to mortgage broking and set up his own company, Kin Financial.

Three of his mortgages are with the Commonwealth Bank of Australia and the remainder are spread across Westpac, ANZ and National Australia Bank.

The Citi report says a fall in property prices would force more owners to sell, as their "belief in ongoing capital gains evaporates". It describes mortgage debt as "the most important economic and social issue of our time". In the past 14 years household debt to household income has increased from 140 per cent to 194 per cent.

Westpac, with about 50 per cent, and Commonwealth Bank of Australia, with around 40 per cent, are the most exposed to interest-only loans, the report states. Citi also warns lenders could face increased legal actions from borrowers who claim they were mis-sold loans if an economic downturn forces distressed portfolio sales and financial losses.
direction: downward pressure on residential property and highly leveraged investors

headline: NAB boss on front foot as capital rules loom
text:
The big four bank chiefs and their treasury departments will be waiting by the phone late on Tuesday afternoon in anticipation of the prudential regulator's download of new capital requirements.

Street Talk understands the banks will get a heads-up late on Tuesday ahead of the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority making a formal announcement well before the ASX starts trading on Wednesday morning.

While the timetable has moved around in the past fortnight, bank chiefs are confident APRA won't push the timing out any further.

National Australia Bank chief executive Andrew Thorburn has already been on the front foot over the past week, meeting with fund managers and, among other things, talking through the proposed changes.

He is said to have signalled to several investors that while the headline changes were likely to be significant, if regulators allowed a two- to three-year transition period NAB would likely meet the more stringent requirements through organic capital generation rather than a bumper equity issue. He was pressing the flesh with fundies after postponing meetings following the bank's interim results in May.

That came as he also dealt with the unexpected bank levy announced by the Turnbull government. [Remember Thorburn is also chairman of the Australian Bankers Association].

Back on capital though, equity capital markets bankers will also be on stand-by this week to assess the quantum of any APRA changes and hope that the prospect of the big four kicking off tens of billions in equity raisings remains alive.

There are two facets to the announcement.

The first is APRA's interpretation of capital levels that ensure the nation's financial institutions are "unquestionably strong" in terms of their common equity tier one (CET1) capital levels and other measures including liquidity. A new CET1 target is the most likely outcome following David Murray's Financial System Inquiry which recommended a new threshold be put in place.

The other closely watched item of business is whether APRA raises risk weightings on some loan products which would essentially see banks have to hold more capital against their lending books. There are also other levers including the additional capital buffer required for "domestic systemically important banks".

Analysts at Morgan Stanley estimate the average major bank CET1 ratios will come in at 10.2 per cent in financial year 2017 and 10.4 per cent the following year.

CLSA banking analyst Brian Johnson believes the big four may be required to raise their CET1 to a minimum 10.5 per cent, and that risk weightings on investor-owned property could rise significantly. If that scenario plays out, the big four would need to raise about $30 billion between them.
direction: downward pressure on bank shares due to capital raise risks

headline: Why a $10million social advertiser ditched Snapchat
text:
Kylie Jenner notwithstanding, the platform is expensive.

A start-up with a $10 million annual spend on social media marketing has gone from Snapchat-first to all but deserting the platform in the last nine months, claiming Facebook and Instagram drive more sales and have more attentive local representatives.

HiSmile, a teeth-whitening subscription commerce business, has a $20 million revenue run rate and is forecasting $40 million by January 2018. Between 50 per cent and 55 per cent of turnover is spent on social media marketing, and most of that on so-called "influencers" with large or loyal followerships of their social media accounts.

Snapchat was the platform HiSmile's founders Nik Mirkovic, 22, and Alex Tomic, 23, focused on.

As late as September 2016, one of their 22 employees was dedicated solely to curating the video "snaps" uploaded to HiSmile's account.

However, that employee has subsequently been redeployed to the team curating HiSmile's accounts with Facebook and its subsidiary Instagram, where the 2014-founded start-up has 800,000 "likes" and 550,000 followers respectively.

"Snapchat is still in the mix, obviously our target market of 15-to-25 year olds is the same as its core user base, but try as we might we just haven't got the engagement there that we get with our posts on Facebook and Insta," Mr Tomic said.

HiSmile's success on those platforms has been with multiple posts daily of "content aimed at making people smile and share", often cute animal pictures pulled from other accounts, which Mr Tomic said it paid to "boost" using Facebook's targeting tools. HiSmile's feed only has an occasional appearance of the product - a mouthpiece with an LED light, filled with a sodium bicarbonate gel - almost always in a smartphone photo or video where it is being used by an influencer.

"You've got to broadcast and sell a lifestyle before you get down to selling your product, that's how your brand gets longevity," Mr Mirkovic said.

However this approach of a producing a high volume of posts in the hope a few go viral, and eventually convert into subscriptions, did not work for a company on Snapchat, Mr Tomic said: "It's like a closed loop."

There is no way to "boost" content on Snapchat, a spokesperson from its headquarters on California's Venice Beach confirmed.

"We're not a broadcast medium, we're about small networks sharing things with each other which they would not on a platform where they are quote-unquote 'friends' with their high school teacher," the spokesperson said, alluding to Facebook.

The conundrum for advertisers is that where Facebook allows targeting and provides some analytics for the relatively small investment of a post boost, Snapchat won't enable targeting and analytics until a businesses buys a display advertising package. Under this arrangement, understood to start at $US10,000 ($13,000) a month in the US, either the advertiser's video-with-sound "Snap Ads" appear between user-generated snaps, or its sponsored "lenses" are made available to users to incorporate into selfies.

"They might work really well, but the problem is it's a big investment to create a suitable ad or make a lens people will want to play with, where we already know that Facebook and Insta are working - their algorithms reward sharing, it's simple," Mr Tomic said.

HiSmile's direct Snapchat spend has to date been restricted to branded "geofilters" it buys to cover the times and locations it hosts a pop-up store. A one-day, Australia-wide geofilter reaching all 4 million active Snapchat users costs $100,000, Mr Tomic said. However, HiSmile has reserved that kind of spend for Kylie Jenner.

Last October, HiSmile inked an endorsement deal with the 19-year-old reality TV star for posts across her social media accounts,including an Instagram with 90 million followers.

"You buy a Snapchat geofilter and it just goes to everyone in that area, which is great for McDonald's but not for us when we're so focused on that 15-to-25 niche," Mr Mirkovic said.

"Our five Instagram posts with Kylie have all had over 1 million views and 100,000 comments and you see the sales uplift immediately after each one." HiSmile would not disclose its financial arrangement with Ms Jenner. But it has likely paid $200,000 for each Instagram post alone, estimated Tina Zafiropoulos, a Sydney-based "influencer manager" for the likes of Deliveroo.

"This sort of spend on an influencer is made out to be outrageous, but think about it - on top of the reach, the brand becomes associated with Kylie Jenner," Ms Zafiropoulos said. "This kind of recognition simply cannot be achieved with a series of billboards or TV commercials that cost a similar amount."

Ms Jenner has uploaded "snaps" for HiSmile too, but her Snapchat followership is not publicly known - none are, to the HiSmile founders' frustration. But that is the point, according to the Snapchat spokesperson.

"In our filing [for last month's $33 billion IPO on the New York Stock Exchange] we described ourselves as a camera company, not a social media company," the spokesperson said.

"That whole popularity contest vibe of other social media platforms is not what we are about."

This attitude may explain why HiSmile's founders have not, despite reaching out to Snapchat's Sydney office which opened last June, so far secured as much as a phone number.

"Facebook just gave us a local rep who has a handful of accounts, we're up there with Nike or Boost Juice in their world and they're asking us to trial new advertiser products," Mr Mirkovic said. "With Snapchat it's like they're trying to be cool ... worried about associating with advertisers."

However the Snapchat spokesperson said the Sydney office was "a small team working through a big list" that had household-name, mass-market advertisers at the top.

The spokesperson pointed to research on 12 Snapchat ad campaigns, conducted by its data provider Oracle, which showed all but one had delivered offline sales growth.
direction: bearish for Snap Inc. shares

headline: UBER'S LONDON PILE-UP
subhead: Technology Uber has become a whipping boy for problems with the gig economy and fears over competition
text:
Technology Uber has become a whipping boy for problems with the gig economy and fears over competition, writes James Bloodworth.

On September 22, Uber, the app-based ride-hailing company, hit a major roadblock in Britain. Transport for London, which regulates the taxi cab industry, refused to renew the licence that allowed Uber to operate in the metropolitan area. In a statement, TfL said Uber demonstrates "a lack of corporate responsibility in relation to a number of issues which have potential public safety and security implications". These included the company's approach to reporting serious criminal offences, its use of Greyball technology that could, in theory, prevent regulators from gaining access to the Uber app, and the company's method of acquiring drivers' medical papers and criminal-record checks.

Until London's regulatory pushback, Uber had thrived in Britain. The interests of the public, as consumers, were understood to be intrinsically bound together with those of this Silicon Valley disrupter in a struggle against restrictive business practices. Uber's media cheerleaders have portrayed the company as a liberator in a battle of innovators in opposition to vested interests, tech-savvy creatives against stolid bureaucrats, the future versus the past.

Yet the past now appears to be exacting its revenge. Or, to be more precise, the future may not look as laissez faire as Uber's champions would have us believe.

For about three months this year, I drove an Uber taxi in London (as research for a book about the company). My entry into this world of casual employment was greeted by reams of pseudo-emancipatory rhetoric about my "autonomy", "being my own boss", and how I would be turning my car into a "money machine".

Of course, the Uber driver soon discovers just how flimsy are the foundations on which these euphemisms rest. During my "onboarding" session, a brief classroom training for new drivers, an Uber employee informed me that as a driver I could not "pick and choose" which jobs I accepted. At the end of a typical week, I took home only a little more than the minimum wage of £7.50 ($12.70) an hour, and that was before deducting the inevitable expenses or loss of earnings that would result from illness or time off. I also learned that Uber would closely monitor my customer satisfaction rating. If it went too low, Uber would temporarily ban me from using the app.

None of these conditions would be particularly unusual in regular salaried employment. But Uber purports to simply facilitate the rider's relationship with the taxi driver who provides the transportation. As our onboarding instructor put it, "Uber is a technology app, it's not a private-hire company". This is how the company attempts to distinguish itself from the private-hire companies whose so-called minicabs make up more than 75 per cent of licensed taxis in London. But all taxis and taxi operators that are not black cabs - which are subject to more stringent licensing and regulation - need a private-hire operator's licence to work in London, including Uber. And Uber had one, until September 22.

Yet Uber is, in some respects, an improvement on what existed before. Many of my fellow Uber drivers had previously had a torrid time working for traditional minicab firms. All but one of those I met were first-generation migrants to Britain, and most had - initially, at least - seen Uber as a welcome opportunity. Some had indeed joined Uber precisely to escape traditional minicab companies' penurious rates of pay and tyrannical human controllers, who assign rides to drivers and are often notorious for their favouritism. If nothing else, Uber's algorithm was not going to prevent you from earning enough to eat because it didn't like your face.

One unfortunate consequence of TfL's ruling is that it will penalise a great many drivers from immigrant backgrounds who have not only been underpaid and discriminated against by private-hire companies, but have also been de facto excluded from London's tight-knit black-cab industry. Uber's own data suggests that around a third of its drivers in London come from neighbourhoods with unemployment rates of more than 10 per cent. Out on the road, the Uber driver already sits at the bottom of the pecking order. Black-cab drivers occasionally curse you and call you "a scab". The Licensed Taxi Drivers Association, the trade body representing the capital's black-cab drivers, has 11,000 members and considerable lobbying power with the mayor of London, Sadiq Khan, and TfL, whereas Uber drivers are represented mainly by the much smaller Independent Workers Union of Great Britain.

The trying experiences of drivers in the less regulated minicab industry point to a contradiction in the TfL decision: it's not as though standards were exemplary before Uber arrived. Many cab companies, mostly small, independent operators, ran for years with similar employment practices to Uber. As James Farrar, head of the private-hire drivers' branch of the IWGB union, told me when I spoke to him a few months ago, "This was a rotten trade before Uber ever came along and we mustn't lose sight of that ... We need to clean up the whole trade." With its innovative app and greater capacity to invest, Uber is simply better at doing what many traditional firms did. And by taking human controllers out of the game, Uber has arguably created more equality of opportunity among drivers.

That Uber has become a whipping boy for the wider "gig economy" may in part explain the mayor's support for TfL's decision to withhold the company's licence, thereby making an example of the company. This is less a case of the regulator stepping up - it is, after all, TfL that carries out the safety checks and issues licences to Uber drivers - and more an instance of it caving in to the powerful black-cab lobby. Last year, the Licensed Taxi Drivers Association launched an inflammatory poster campaign implying that passengers were at risk of being raped by an Uber driver. It is hard to get away from the feeling that Uber has also been singled out thanks to the scaremongering directed at its predominantly immigrant drivers.

If anything has undone Uber, it is the hubris of its claim to be a vehicle for freedom and prosperity - when, in fact, it only made more visible the precariousness of casual employment in Britain's poorly regulated labour market. For that, it has been punished.R

THE NEW YORK REVIEW OF BOOKS

Out on the road, the Uber driver sits at the bottom of the pecking order.
direction: bearish for Uber and ride-hailing sector

headline: Beach Energy's $1.6b gas market game-changer
text:
Beach Energy has charged into the major league in the east coast gas sector, inking a $1.59 billion deal to buy Origin Energy's conventional oil and gas business in a move that also advances the energy ambitions of 23 per cent shareholder Seven Group Holdings.

The deal, which will more than double the mid-cap oil and gas player's production and treble reserves, trumped Origin's alternative plan to float Lattice Energy, which some investors had always seen as unlikely.

Origin will use the proceeds to pay down another chunk of debt, which is now expected to be under $7 billion by mid-2018, from over $12 billion in 2015.

As reported first on Street Talk, Beach also beat a bid from Sydney-based private equity player Questus Energy for Lattice, which owns fields in Western Australia and New Zealand, as well as in the eastern states.

While the price was described as "pretty ritzy" by one analyst, the transaction is backed by gas sales contracts between Origin and Lattice that lock in gas price increases for at least the next three years.

The contracts allow the deal to be largely debt funded, limiting the equity raising needed by Beach to $301 million. That compares to earlier estimates that Beach, which has a market value around $1.5 billion, could need to sell $700 million-plus of new shares to buy Lattice.

Seven Group chief executive Ryan Stokes, a Beach director, described Lattice as "a really strong fit" for Beach with its range of assets that diversifies its production and reserves.

It also reinforces Seven's strategy of expanding in energy as it rebalances its business to de-emphasise its traditional powerbase of media and building out petroleum and industrial services. "Seeing Beach get bigger is a key focus for us," Mr Stokes said, adding that backing the Beach bid is an effective way for Seven to participate in the evolving east coast gas market "in a meaningful way".

Beach chief executive Matt Kay described the acquisition as "transformational" for Beach, enhancing its growth opportunities and adding offshore and overseas fields to a portfolio currently limited to central Australia's Cooper Basin.

As Beach was the front-runner to buy Lattice, the deal was largely expected. But Dermot Woods, at Westoz Funds Management, said it was "good to have the certainty out there", and to have indications of the gas prices in the supply contracts between Origin and Lattice.

While both parties declined to reveal the price of the contracts, Mr Kay said it was higher than Beach's average realised gas price in 2016-17 of $6.10 a gigajoule. The tariff would increase in three annual step-ups, linked to the inflation rate, then move to "market prices".

"One can assume they are going to have a rising gas price for the next four or five years," Mr Woods said. "Name another commodity company you can say that about."

Mr Stokes said Origin's keenness to get security of supply and certainty over gas prices worked in favour of the funding structure.

"That enables you to use leverage because you take the commodity risk out of it," he said.

Beach will take on up to $1.58 billion of new debt, from ANZ, CBA and Credit Suisse, to help fund the deal and provide working capital, while some existing debt will be refinanced.

Gearing will jump towards 35 per cent, but should be under 25 per cent by mid-2019 and Beach could be net cash by mid-2021, Mr Kay said.

The shares will be sold at 75¢ apiece, a 9.1 per cent discount to Wednesday's close. Beach stock was halted from trading for the institutional part of the 3-for-14 rights offer.

Origin, whose shares edged up 5¢ to $7.50, initially proposed a float of Lattice in its December restructuring to simplify its business and lift shareholder returns. But market watchers immediately suggested a trade sale was more likely.

Chief executive Frank Calabria said the sale "represents the best overall value" to Origin shareholders but insisted the IPO remained "a very credible alternative" to the end.

The deal largely brings to an end Origin's asset sale program, with Mr Calabria saying the only remaining one being "actively considered" is the smart metering business Acumen.

JPMorgan analyst Mark Busuttil described the deal as "broadly positive" for Origin, which he calculates should be able to repay $1 billion-$1.1 billion of debt. But some analysts saw it as expensive for Beach, with SHA Energy Consulting's Scott Ashton saying it was "enough to at least discourage an IPO".

Andrew Mitchell at Ophir Asset Management said the price "looks acceptable" for both companies.

"Origin now has certainty and an attractive price, while the key deal metrics are accretive for Beach," Mr Mitchell said.
