Seven has committed to taking up its full allocation of stock and will also sub-underwrite the offer, meaning its stake in Beach could rise to about 26 per cent. Beach is being advised by Credit Suisse and Luminis Partners, while Macquarie and UBS are advising Origin.

Origin retains its 37.5 per cent stake in the APLNG project as well as major undeveloped assets such as the Ironbark coal seam gas field in Queensland.
direction: bullish for Beach Energy and Seven Group Holdings

headline: Golden Mile follows two rewarding paths
subhead: A bet both ways ADVERTISING FEATURE
text:
This newly listed explorer/developer is dividing its focus between an old faithful - gold - and a new kid on the block - cobalt.

 Tim Putt is excited - he's at Diggers and Dealers and back in the West Australian goldfields after spending 10 years around the globe, with a homegrown exploration story to tell at the annual mining forum at Kalgoorlie.

Putt is the CEO and executive director of Golden Mile Resources (GMR).

The minerals exploration and development company, which listed on the ASX in June, is dividing its attention among its two cobalt and four gold projects in Western Australia.

Putt sees it as "a bet both ways".

With the global push towards renewables and the need for battery storage, lithium has moved into the spotlight, but Putt says the focus should also be on cobalt.

"There's actually twice as much cobalt in a lithium battery as there is lithium," he says.

Things have changed since 2014 when he first became involved in the Quicksilver project , located 340 kilometres south-east of Perth in the Southwest Mineral Field.

"When that project was first pegged and had the initial work done on it, the industry wasn't really interested in cobalt," he says.

"And then, lo and behold, six months ago cobalt became all the rage."

Now a number of companies are popping up with cobalt or lithium projects, but a lot will be shaken out as "who's real and who's not" starts to become clearer to the market, he says.

"We've just completed the first phase of drilling on our Quicksilver cobalt project and it's progressing nicely.

"Geologically we're seeing all the things we'd hoped and expected.

"It was the project that we had the greatest understanding of and it also showed some very high grades of cobalt - close to 1 per cent in the near-surface environment."

The effort is now centred on systematic drilling to establish the grades and consistency of subsurface nickel-cobalt mineralisation, and its depth.

"But our feeling is that Quicksilver's going to move quickly to a resource, with estimates of tonnage and grade allowing the viability of further development to be assessed.

"We would hope to have that by the end of the year, and from there the project should develop at an accelerated pace."

Putt says Quicksilver ticks a lot of boxes that many other resource projects don't, including proximity to infrastructure, with a railhead only five kilometres away giving direct access to the port at Albany, as well as power and water through the region.

But he is quick to point out that Golden Mile Resources has more than one arrow in its quiver.

"We've certainly acquired a good suite of projects that cover a wide range of different exploration stages, everything from greenfields projects to near-term resources," he says.

"As a business we need to be testing and advancing these projects, and be more than a one-trick pony, as it were.

"It's important to be moving projects into the 'pipeline' and potential production."

GMR's other cobalt project is Minara, near Glencore's world-class Murrin Murrin nickel-cobalt project, in the Northeastern Goldfields.

GMR has recently obtained a full database of the historical drilling data for the project, which Putt says was focused on finding nickel but shows anomalous cobalt intercepts.

"We're seeing nice thick intercepts of mineralisation, which is what you want with these deposits from a development point of view."

Putt cites previous drilling showing results that include intercepts of 28 metres at close to 0.7 per cent nickel and 0.1 per cent cobalt.

"It's still early days at Minara but the tonnes can add up quite quickly when you've got these thick intercepts."

And despite a packed field of cobalt projects, Putt says both Quicksilver and Minara should rate highly.

After languishing below $US30,000 a tonne for most of 2015 and 2016, the market price of cobalt recently spiked above $US60,000 a tonne and is currently not far below that.

Putt says the projects don't depend on the spike being sustained.

Feasibility studies on comparable projects, with similar or even lower grades than GMR, indicate that these projects are potentially profitable at prices in the region of $US20,000-$US25,000 a tonne, he says.

It's not all about cobalt though.

GMR has four gold projects, all in the Northeastern Goldfields, with Ironstone Well the most advanced.

The gold-focused projects are mostly around Leonora, an area that - while under-explored in Putt's view - has produced some outstanding gold deposits, including the high-grade Gwalia mine.

Putt says he and most of his technical team started their careers in gold in Western Australia in the 1980s and 1990s and are now coming full circle.

"It's great to be working in our own backyard again.

"Western Australia, one of the best exploration jurisdictions in the world, is a great address.

"It's still 'elephant country' and there are more elephants to be found."
direction: bullish for Golden Mile Resources (GMR) and the broader mining/exploration sector

headline: Fortescue could move into net cash territory
text:
The days of Andrew Forrest's bold debt-fuelled strategy for Fortescue Metals Group might be over, with the company's new chief financial officer, Elizabeth Gaines, signalling she is willing to move the company into a net cash position as it seeks to balance investment and shareholder returns.

In her first interview since stepping into the role in early February, Ms Gaines told The Australian Financial Review she doesn't plan to make any "sudden changes" to the mining company's strategy, which has had it take advantage of higher-than-expected iron ore prices to dramatically reduce debt. The former Helloworld chief executive has been on the Fortescue board as a non-executive director, and a member of its audit and risk management committee and finance committee, since 2013.

"I think that unique position of coming from the board and being part of that strategy means coming into the role I don't think it is about making any sudden changes because we have been on a very deliberate path for some time now," Ms Gaines said. "We have certainly always said we want to repay debt so we can get the gearing down and that is certainly part of the strategy but it is broader than that as well.

"It tends to be a number of different limbs to that overall strategy around debt management, investing in our core business for the long term, looking at low-cost growth opportunities and then returns to shareholders."

Ms Gaines joins Fortescue's executive team in the wake of a significant improvement in its financial health during the past four years.

It has achieved a long-pursued 40 per cent gross gearing target and last week repaid another $US1 billion of debt, knocking its gross debt to $US4.2 billion, a far cry from its peak of $US12.7 billion in 2013.

But Ms Gaines' time in the chief financial officer role will not come without its own challenges, with Fortescue the subject of close market scrutiny as it considers how to spend its growing pile of cash. The $19 billion company had $US1.2 billion cash on hand at the half- year, within its target "liquidity balance" of between $US1 billion to $US1.5 billion.

Ms Gaines says Fortescue does not have a new gearing target but debt reduction will remain a priority, to the extent the company could find itself in a net cash position, where it has more cash than borrowings.

Analysts have tipped the company could enter a net cash phase as early as next financial year if the strength in iron ore prices continues.

"I don't think we are adverse to that concept but there other priorities for cash as well as we move forward so again it is a balance amongst the various parts of our overall strategies," she said.

Fortescue has flagged a $US1 billion to $US1.5 billion spend, predominantly in 2018 and 2019, to replace its Firetail mine, is spending $US556 million on eight new ships and up to $US200 million on a tug haven at Port Hedland port.

The company will also spend $US40 million this year on exploration in iron ore and as an early stage diversification effort in copper-gold in South Australia, NSW and Ecuador.

"I think when we are approaching growth opportunities it is more about low cost but opportunities that have the potential to deliver significant value," Ms Gaines said, as opposed to any large-scale acquisitions.

The iron ore price slid to $US79.36 per tonne on Monday, its lowest level since early January after reaching as high as $US94.86 per tonne on February 21 but remains above the $US40 per tonne mark, which it sank to in late 2015.

Ms Gaines said investment decisions were made after rigorous tests at a range of iron ore price scenarios but the volatility commodity prices inflict on mining businesses is not something particularly challenging or unique to the industry.

"I think I haven't yet known a business that isn't subject to some sort of volatility, whatever that might be," she said.
direction: Fortescue Metals Group shares likely to rise

headline: Domino's grabs German slice
text:
Domino's Pizza Enterprises will spend up to $63 million to buy Germany's Hallo Pizza chain, taking its store numbers in Europe's biggest economy to more than 300.

Domino's German joint venture, in which it has a two-thirds stake, will pay €32 million ($48 million) for the chain, with an additional €20 million to €30 millon to be spent on converting the Hallo outlets to Domino's stores.

The total cost to Domino's in Australia will be between $52.6 million and $63 million.

Hallo Pizza has 170 stores, meaning Domino's Pizza Deutschland will go from 209 stores to between 300 and 340 stores, depending on how the brand conversion process goes.

"The acquisition of Hallo Pizza strengthens our leading market position and assists Domino's to accelerate towards achieving its target of operating 1000 stores in Germany," chief executive Don Meij said.

Domino's said the deal, to be funded through cashflow and new debt facilities, will make a "small positive contribution" to earnings in 2017-18.

The deal should be completed by early next year, although it may take up to three years to convert some stores.

Domino's shares rose 3.5 per cent to $49.35.
direction: Domino's shares rise

headline: Rising stocks feed best week of 2017
text:
The Australian sharemarket closed not far off a two-year high on Friday, holding onto most of this week's outsized gains.

The S&P/ASX 200 index shed 0.5 per cent in choppy trade as some investors took profits on the last day of the quarter. But the benchmark index still had a weekly gain of 1.9 per cent, the most this year. It ended a solid month in which the ASX 200 rose 2.7 per cent to 5864.9, while over the quarter the index posted a gain of 3.5 per cent.

Feeding this week's rally was an $8 billion bonanza in dividends distributed to shareholders over the past five sessions, with a further $7.2 billion to be distributed next week.

"The banks continue to lead the market higher and [are] helped by a recovery in resources with commodity prices bouncing backs [from recent selloffs] across the board," said Bell Potter senior stockbroker Richard Coppleson.

This week's rally had caught "bearish" institutional investors off guard, forcing them to jump on board when the market first pushed to two-year highs on Tuesday.

"With valuations high in some stocks and others looking ordinary, it looks to me that some [institutional investors] are buying more banks so that they 'participate' in the rally and 'park the cash' somewhere that looks like it's going up ... and that's liquid enough so they can exit quickly to buy resources or other stocks later on."

On Friday, the big four banks gave up some of their gains. Falling between 0.5 and 1.4 per cent on Friday, they still rose between 1.5 and 3.9 per cent over the five trading sessions, with NAB the top performer.

The materials sector was the only one to post overall declines over the week, though Rio Tinto added 0.6 per cent. BHP and Fortescue were down 0.9 and 0.5 per cent respectively over the five sessions.

Fairfax Media shed 2.8 per cent on Friday after news that private equity interest in the group has declined. It still closed up 0.5 per cent for the week.

 What moved the market

Lending restrictions

The banking watchdog announced limits on interest-only residential loans to reinforce sound mortgage lending practices in a hot property market, pointing to "an environment of heightened risks". New policies announced yesterday included limiting the flow of new interest-only lending to 30 per cent of total new residential mortgage lending, from 40 per cent.

Cyclone wreckage

Insurers face serious costs from tropical cyclone Debbie but the impact will not be as bad as the bill that followed Yasi in 2011, credit ratings agency Standard and Poor's says. "While the gross claims of the disaster will be material, net claims are likely to be manageable and below category five cyclone Yasi," S&P said. Meanwhile, CBA says it is evident that Debbie will have a dampening effect on Q1 and Q2 economic growth..

Chinese PMI

Activity in China's manufacturing sector expanded at the fastest pace in five years, adding to evidence that the world's second-largest economy is gaining momentum early in the year. The closely followed official Purchasing Managers' Index (PMI) rose to 51.8 in March, compared with the previous month's 51.6, ad topping economist expectations of 51.7.

Credit growth

Private sector credit came in below market expectations for the second consecutive month in February, but investor lending remained brisk. Total credit rose just 0.3 per cent in February, while housing-related credit ticked up to 0.6 per cent, broadly in line with the average run-rate of the past year. Investor lending once again rose 0.6 per cent, while owner-occupier credits grew at 0.5 per cent.

 Stock watch

Quintis

The appointment of a new interim CEO did little to stem the downward slide in Quintis, the target of activist short-sellers, which fell a further 4.6 per cent on Friday to $1.04. The new head of the sandalwood grower is Julius Matthys, a former long-time senior executive at BHP Billiton, who is replacing former managing director and major shareholder Frank Wilson, who quit earlier this week to partner an unnamed overseas bidder seeking to take over the company. Shares in Quintis, formerly known as TFS, have slumped 26.5 per cent over the last two weeks after activist investor group Glaucus issued a negative report on the company, suggesting the stock is worth zero.
direction: bullish for banks and resources

headline: How to catch a millennial
text:
The chief executives of our largest companies are betting on social media to secure Generation Y as staff and customers.

How do the established behemoths of the ASX attract young Australians as customers and staff? It’s a question that challenges three leading chief executives of the banking, fast-moving consumer goods and premium retail organisations who met to discuss the issue in the offices of Facebook in Sydney for a special BOSS roundtable. For Alison Watkins of Coca-Cola Amatil, Shayne Elliott from the ANZ Banking Group and John Dixon of David Jones some searching questions are: How does a CEO overseeing thousands of people stay in touch with the smartphone-connected younger generations? What sort of staff should you employ to ensure your business is implementing the technologies that will connect with them? Do the changes in digital technology mean rethinking traditional employment strategies? Should advertising campaigns change and put more emphasis on social media? What are the appropriate boundaries for the use of personal data? Before answering these questions, Stephen Scheeler (pictured right), managing director of Facebook in Australia and New Zealand, provided some context to the mobile revolution. He says the penetration of smartphones in Australia is about 84 per cent but among those aged under 24 it is 90 per cent.

Scheeler says the most profound change to have occurred over the past decade is in the consumer experience. “The biggest single disconnect and difference between one generation and the next is what they expect from brands that they love, from services that they get and from the media that they consume,” he says. “Over the next three, four, five years I think the shift is going to start to become absolutely profound. It is going to loom so massively in every business in Australia, every enterprise is going to have a huge mobile challenge and I don’t think many are prepared to deal with it today.” Scheeler says that even Facebook, which has a 32-year-old CEO in Mark Zuckerberg, struggles to keep up with the pace of change driven by youth.

ENCOURAGE COLLABORATION

Alison Watkins says she is fortunate to have a young son who is a gaming YouTuber. “I went along to this amazing gaming convention called BlizzCon in LA just a couple of weeks back with my son,” she says. “It was just to really understand how the gaming industry is changing the way young consumers are entertaining themselves and how do we as a company engage with that. “You can easily be sitting there thinking you get it, but you’re so out of touch.

Watkins says the need to be in touch with younger Australians has affected her thinking about the Coca-Cola Amatil workforce. “We have a lot of really good young people in our business,” she says. “What I think we need to do really well is listening and empowering them. So I don’t think we’ve got that right yet. Judgment and experience, I like to think, still counts for something. So it’s how do you find that balance?” Coca-Cola Amatil has just launched Facebook at Work to encourage collaboration at work among young staff members. Watkins is impressed by the approach taken by Shayne Elliott at ANZ, who appointed former Google Australia managing director Maile Carnegie as group executive of digital banking. “Maile’s job in running digital is how to create that great customer experience. Nobody wants a mortgage. People want to buy a home or a stable business or whatever but nobody wants a loan. So how do you engage that customer experience and make that interesting and not a commodity?” Elliott says.

STRATEGIC THINKING

Elliott admits that virtually all the big financial decisions taken in people’s lives are done face-to-face and not online. That explains why the bank is opening branches with no tellers and no money. Instead the people in the branch are there to advise on home loans, insurance and other products. Elliott says the explosion in the use of smartphones has influenced the bank’s strategic thinking. It was the first and only one of the big four banks to offer Apple Pay and its goMoney app is used by one million customers. The Apple Pay decision was an obvious one for Elliott for two reasons. He says iPhones are owned by affluent Australians and two-thirds of ANZ customers have iPhones. “We should listen to our customers and it forces us to innovate, it forces us to think of new ways to service those customers,” he says. Elliott says ANZ’s new chief information officer, Gerard Florian, is a big data and cloud-computing specialist who will use digital technology to speed up everything about the ANZ customer experience.

DIGITAL SPACE

 Dixon is shocked that Australia has one of the highest levels of smartphone penetration, one of the most advanced online banking systems and yet retailers are significantly lagging. “I think it’s pretty obvious to everybody that if you’ve got that high level of smartphone penetration, you’ve got people living their lives through mobile technology in such an increasing way, that it’s going to happen in retail,” he says. “I think retail’s just got a lot of catching up to do.” One topic that is at the top of mind for all three CEOs and Scheeler at Facebook is privacy. Elliott says he won’t allow ANZ to use analysis of personal data to push products to customers unless there is a benefit to them. “There’s a caution in our industry about how we use data to make sure we’re doing it for a good purpose,” he says. “We know there’s a group of people out there who love that and there’s a whole bunch of people who find it creepy and don’t like targeted things.”

SPECIAL OFFERS

Watkins says Coca-Cola Amatil tries things with consumers in pilot studies to discover if consumers find them useful. For example, she is not sure if some consumers will like a new technology that the company may use to allow Coke vending machines to communicate and interact with someone passing by with a smartphone. The idea is to target special offers but it is only in its infancy. Dixon has already entered a partnership with Facebook. “I think our partnership with Facebook is very important in terms of directly reaching customers, hearing back directly from them, what they like, what they don’t like, what they want more of, what they want less of,” he says. “I think also it is part of how we bring together all the various forms of data into the business.” Scheeler says Facebook is very cautious about the use of users’ data. “The heart and soul of Facebook is respect for our users’ data and the experiences that they have with us,” he says. “So we are very cautious about the use of that data, how we allow it to be used either by ourselves or third parties."
direction: pressure on traditional banking and retail valuations

headline: Mackenzie joins battle over Western values
text:
Exclusive | The BHP chief says business must speak up for our liberal system, writes Matthew Stevens.

Andrew Mackenzie has "respectfully" assessed that China's "ever-strong leader" model is a point of fragility in the long-term sustainability of an evolving economic revolution that validates its single-party state and increasingly secures the prosperity of the global economy.

Having spent four years manufacturing BHP's "dream" asset portfolio, and developing the executive "dream team" that now manages them, the global miner's chief executive feels now honour-bound to join the "stramash" over the rise of small politics and the corrosion of Western values that sit at the core of our liberal prosperity.

"The international world order is under a lot of threat right now," BHP's chief executive warned in a sweeping pre-Christmas interview with AFR Weekend.

"China has a degree of self confidence, some of which is good and, you know, good for us. The drive they have to build their economy, the dynamism and the belt and road initiative, fantastic.

"But they also have a belief in a way of being, with a single party and an ever-strong leader, that I am not as convinced of for the long term.

"I think Western values - competition, debate, dissipation of powers, separations of powers - ultimately are far better. I think the West has proven that. That is, of course, the system that gave rise to us. And having given rise to us, I think it is our duty to speak up for it."

Mackenzie identifies this responsibility to represent Western liberal values through both a commercial and personal prism.

"To me it is so right for us to stick up for that for the long term, because if we are going to sustain the kind of growth [China is driving], then I think you need it. The growth will be more substantial, you will solve a lot more problems through technology, the world will be a better place and BHP will thrive.

"So it is a long-term shareholder issue, which is why Think Big is right in the crosshairs of looking after the long-term shareholder value of this company."

Think Big is, of course, the $10 million-plus return to brand marketing that BHP launched in May 2016. Mackenzie said Think Big was about "reminding people that this society flourishes when businesses behave in the right way, when they create jobs, when they distribute wealth, when they compete feverishly for ideas and technology and markets.

"It is the life blood," he continued. "And once a country like China gets through the middle income, it needs that, or it will go the wrong way.

"Right now in the West, and I don't mind it to be honest, there is a lot of debate that might look like kind of small politics. People get very worked up about what is going on here about dual citizenship, but hey-ho, look at what is going on in Germany, look at Brexit, look at what is happening in New Zealand. It is everywhere.

"And it is because there is maybe a greater battle of ideas than we have ever had. Which I feel partly stimulated by.

"That makes it easy for somebody who believes in authority and strength to say, 'what a mess, we should stick with this strong man model', if I could put it that way, and that democracy is a bit of an inefficient way of dong things. I completely challenge that.

"But while there is that political stramash - there is a good Scottish word - it is appropriate for companies like us, in a relatively humble way, just to stick up for the international world order that, without it, we would never have existed."

Just for the record there, stramash means a tumult or brawl.

"I think it is good that we can welcome all the great things that are happening in Asia but I also continue to advocate for these things. But in the past until recently, you could have expected that leadership to come form the United States ... I don't need to finish that sentence. They have a certain momentum away from worrying about the world being liberal - no different from when Britain lost its empire and the economic clout, in a relative sense, to have the same level of soft power. We should get real about that.

"China is growing. And it has to be welcomed rather than contained. But it is important for companies and for the leaders of Western values, and a country that has a lot to benefit from all of this, for Australia, to say, we will stick up for them [Western values]. Occasionally that does happen here, which is good. So not all politics is small."

So who should be leading this assertion of Western liberalism?

"I would like to think it is a combination of political leaders who acknowledge the centrist way - and it doesn't matter whether it is centre-left of centre-right - and multinational businesses who behave responsibly and free of hypocrisy who can speak up for these things."

Now, back at the BHP coal front, Mackenzie sits more comfortable with the shape of things.

He accepts the observation that 2017 has been a pivotal year for the business and that the year ahead will see further purification of a portfolio that he reckons to be "already near damn perfect".

"There is a sense now that finally I can get on with doing what I really wanted to do, which is show that, with the ore bodies that we have and an agenda of safety and productivity, brilliant capital allocation and retaining and winning the social licence, that you can really unlock the potential that has been in construction for decades by my forebears at BHP."

The year past saw BHP pass a typically diverse collection of milestones, some long mapped out, others that arrived a surprise. There was boardroom succession that saw the chairman's baton pass from Jac Nasser to Ken MacKenzie and there was the decision to make final retreat from the $US35 billion or so "misfire" on US shale oil.

There was the return to opportunism with acquisition of an offshore oil discovery called Trion from Mexico's state-owned oil company for a work program that could eventually top $US1.2 billion. And there was the almost-final assemblage of the capital allocation framework that will be used to filter all future decisions on how best to spend the wealth that BHP creates.

Shadowing all of that progress and more was BHP's unexpected emergence on the radar of opportunity of the activist fund Elliott Associates. Having spent months of private discussion with BHP, Paul Singer's fund went public in April with calls for the collapse of the miner's dual listing, the listing of its US petroleum business on the New York Exchange and the embrace of a deeply constraining set of capital allocation priorities.

Given the insights BHP had offered its new shareholder through months of engagement, the Elliott pitch was cannily timed to ensure that progress on everything from portfolio management to board renewal would be seen through its lens of activism. That is the name of that game, I suppose.

But, it must be said, Mackenzie seems genuinely sanguine about that whole thing. "You know, the longer I am in this job [he is coming up to year five] the more I have learned to appreciate criticism. It sharpens you up, it makes you a better leader. And it forces us to explain ourselves better and in doing that make better decisions.

So was Elliott good or bad for BHP?

"Criticism and challenge is always good for you," he said. "And, you know, it is part of the power of the Western model. I go all the way back to some of my roots, the Scottish enlightenment, even someone like JS Mills, who I claim for Scotland because his dad was Scottish, you know, the more you have debate and the more that that debate can happen freely, the more you will arrive at better ideas.

"If you see Elliott in that light, that there is challenge of ideas, it is fine. But you have to be a little cautious because their approach to life is relatively - they would not thank me for this - it has a short-term feeling.

"They showed up talking about the DLC [dual listing]. I have said openly that when I became CEO one of my pitches was to think about the value of unification. But at that time I concluded that, relative to doing things like South32, that it was potentially very distracting for, at best, low value and probably, under most circumstances, a negative cost benefit.

"When I initially engaged with them, I thought that maybe they had found a way of cracking this nut. I was genuinely disappointed when they hadn't."

Two lessons of the Elliott experience will resound. New chairman Ken MacKenzie is determined that BHP will take more account of its shareholders. And CEO Mackenzie is determined that BHP will hold less secrets.

"We had to become more open about our plans for shale because a lack of openness was being interpreted as reluctance to deal with it or obfuscation. And it wasn't that at all. When you have a lot of people who are working for you and then they suddenly hear 'not wanted on voyage', that creates its own risks. On balance, though, it was very liberating.

"I am naturally inclined to transparency. Not to the point where I would do something commercially stupid. I am employed to look after the assets of BHP. But I think the world is moving in that way. Clearly, there are things that have to remain private, but maybe in some of these commercial things, we have to manage them more transparently. And everything that we do about our social licence, it has to be about transparency and openness.

"So the sooner the BHP modus operandi embraces that, and finds a way to make sure that it is a more positive thing for the company and the power leadership, the better."

The final exit from shale looms the next seminal moment in the refinement of BHP. Mackenzie agrees with the idea it will be a mini-South32 moment. It will release human and financial capital. It will end distraction and complication. And it will allow BHP to focus on oil investment more appropriate to its business model.

"The great advantage of shale is that you could invest into a rising price," Mackenzie said.

"The problem with that is, of course, that if we see that, so does everybody else. Your supply chain becomes more taut and expensive so that they are the ones that really benefit from a rising price, not the operator. Generally we are in a world where you like to have the boot on the other foot."

The commercial corollary of the decision to leave shale was the move 18 months ago to direct more capital at offshore oil exploration. It has worked, with material oil discoveries on the US side of the Gulf of Mexico and a major gas find near enough to existing LNG facilities in Trinidad. The fact this investment could be made counter-cyclically announces one of the rich attractions of offshore drilling over shale.

"That was a counter-cyclical investment because we were able to see rig rates dropping by 75-80 per cent. We said 'we have the prospects now let's get on with drilling them at a fraction of the cost that it would have taken to drill them when the price was higher.' In some cases, we are confirming areas we don't want to be in but in other cases we are making discoveries that will generate a healthier competition for [BHP] capital in the future."

So, what are the other milestones for the year ahead? Mackenzie ticks of a list of priorities, from further demonstrating capital and cost discipline and revealing the potential of productivity to drive growth. He nominates the sustained ramp-up of copper production at Escondida, a successful end to the Olympic Dam upgrade and the rapid demonstration of the potential of BHP's Mexican plays as things to look for.

But Mackenzie says he will enter the New Year with a now practised "chronic unease".

"We use a phrase in our safety discussions that there needs to be more chronic unease around our sites. Something from left field can hit you at any time and you need to keep wondering, do you have the right people and the right processes around you to make sure you can handle that while still carrying on with out long-term goals? We have learned to do that well through some of the challenges we have faced."
direction: bearish for BHP due to geopolitical and governance risks

headline: A political inquiry misses the point
text:
Welcome to rule by the Senate menagerie of Labor and an eccentric cross-bench. Combine them with renegade lower house Nationals, driven into madness by fear of One Nation's weekend performance in Queensland, and we remain set for a possible grab-bag inquiry into Australia's huge financial sector that will suck more life out of an already distracted political system.

Even with the Greens politicking over their support, just three Nationals set to vote with the opposition leaves Malcolm Turnbull with a potential choice of either a humiliating U-turn, or a major loss on the floor of Parliament on Nationals senator Barry O'Sullivan's private members bill. Like the proverbial camel that was meant to be a horse, a bank inquiry idea triggered by specific complaints rather than systemic failure has now morphed into something else. Labor wants to look at bank regulators, the Nationals want insurance - including that related to mental health - as well as farmers, and small business. The Greens had wanted to include executive pay and vertical integration in banking as the price of support. These are all complex areas in their own right. Creating a high-quality inquiry that produces new insights and recommendations for reforms is far less likely than a cynical exercise in political grandstanding.

The panic among the Nationals has undone a year's work by the government in trying to head off Labor pressure for an inquiry. There is the intrusive Bank Executive Accountability Regime, and a last resort victims tribunal now proposed by Treasurer Scott Morrison. They all have flaws, but they were at least speedy, direct and practical compared with a $200 million, years-long banking system royal commission that avoids the fundamental systemic question at hand: whether the cult of the great Australian dream has blown the mother of all housing bubbles that is bound to end in tears.

The House of Representatives economics committee already calls the bank chiefs to Canberra for an annual grilling. A supposedly pro-enterprise Coalition slapped a $6.5 billion tax on the big banks just because they had the money and nobody liked them. Now rebel Nationals are engineering an inquiry more wacky than one the government might have conceded earlier, when they could have written workable terms of reference.

Instead, this ramshackle inquisition will come for a government that has more serious economic issues pressing on it - sub-par growth, weak productivity growth and signs of a weakening in budget discipline in order to buy middle class votes. It would distract a banking sector already set for upheaval by customer-driven technology, and which could leave it with rules unsuited to a new era. The life insurance industry faces a threat to its sustainability from permanent disability claims related to mental health which threaten to be the "bad back" of the 21st century, but which backers of the inquiry want expanded.

Then there is super. If Nationals want to look for systemic flaws, they could start with one of the most egregious. That is, default super fund memberships agreed in industrial deals being directed into industry funds controlled by unions: a financial system overlap with the industrial relations system that's completely out of place.

The splits over banking are just one small fissure in the splintering of centrist parties of both left and right under pressure from the politics of entitlement and identity, most recently as One Nation harvested blue-collar discontent in Queensland on Saturday. Those things are no longer negated by rising growth, opportunity, and prosperity. And that's hard to reverse when the political system has geared itself to handing out the remaining shares, rather than responding by creating new growth. Selling growth-inducing business tax cuts instead, an idea that Labor's Bill Shorten and Chris Bowen once supported, hardly cuts it politically when there are unpopular bankers to bash. It's a sign of political failure.

The panic among the Nationals has undone a year's work by the government.
direction: bearish for the financial sector

headline: MARCO POLO
text:
Our experts look at a global investment.

Fresnillo

While opinions remain split on the long-term direction of precious metals prices, those looking for high-quality exposures globally could do well to consider UK-listed Fresnillo. The company is one of the largest producers, with operations and exploration tenements well located in the rich Mexican gold and silver belt. The company's flagship namesake mine has been in operation for almost 500 years and is one of six deposits it operates.

The 2016 full-year result was positive, followed by strong performance in the first half of 2017. The company reported a higher profit in the six months to June 30 on improved revenues. Net operating cash flow came in lower, but the balance sheet remains in good shape. Further, the dividend was upped for the half.

The company posted an 87 per cent increase in profit, to US$310.1 million. Adjusted revenue underpinned the result, contributing an 11.5 per cent increase to US$1.1 billion, compared to the same result in 2016. Adjusted revenue for the half was positively impacted by higher silver production and also received a boost from higher realised silver prices.

Fresnillo reported an 11.2 per cent increase in silver production compared to the same half for 2016, to 28 million ounces.

Driving the result was the arrival of first silver from the first phase of the San Julian mine, of three million ounces.

On 2017 guidance, the company remains on track to achieve an annual production record of 58 million-61 million ounces for silver, and 870,000-900,000 ounces of gold. Operations delivered a record 50.3 million ounces of silver for 2016 and a record 935,513 ounces of gold.

Adjusted production costs saw a 14.4 per cent increase on the 2016 first half, to US$43.3 million.

On International Financial Reporting Standards, the company reported an improvement in its cost per tonne charge of 12.4 per cent on the same half in 2016, to US$1.46 per tonne. Fresnillo continues to work hard to maintain a control over its cost base, and improve its operating margins.

The company's balance sheet also remains in good shape with a net debt position of US$113.5 million at the end of the half year. Cash or its equivalent rose by US$27.1 million from a year earlier, to US$912 million at June 30, 2017.

The interim payout was US10.6¢ per share compared to US8.6¢ per share for same half in 2016, a rise of 23.3 per cent year-on-year.

Fresnillo is not cheap on the face of it, trading on more than 25 times earnings, but the stock certainly has tremendous leverage to rising silver prices.

We see precious metals generally doing well when inflation, which has been absent for many years, finally starts to tick up after more than a decade of money printing globally.

If we are right, Fresnillo should perform. It is the company's ground exposure that delivers the long-life silver producing capability and considerable blue-sky potential from exploration, and with these characteristics, long-term shareholder value.

Interests associated with Fat Prophets declare a holding in Fresnillo. For a free trial to Fat Prophets' daily market commentary please call 1300 881 177 or email info@fatprophets.com.au.
direction: bullish for Fresnillo shares

headline: Investors look for upgrades
text:
As earnings season begins this week, investors are largely expecting many upgrades thanks to a turnaround in commodity prices

But this will be largely confined to resources companies. For industrials, excluding financials, investors are facing a continued downgrade cycle, meaning selecting specific stocks may be a better strategy than hitching on to a trend.

While many analysts are sceptical about an improving economic outlook for Australia, pointing to a slowing housing-linked sector and lagging consumer sentiment, Morgan Stanley says companies with earnings certainty are likely to receive buying support.

"Put simply, if companies show a first-half earnings split that compares favourably to prior years, we'd expect their stocks to be rewarded for added certainty," said James Bales, equity analyst at Morgan Stanley.

Companies such as Baby Bunting, which are positioned for a strong first-half result, are better placed than those that have guidance for the second half of the year, such as Automotive Holdings Group.

While the US dollar has roared higher following the US election in November, the firm expects an even further drop in the Australian dollar, to US65¢ by December.

The team points to slowing global trade volumes and risks to China's growth story as the biggest weights on the Aussie.

"These forecasts imply similar double-digit declines against other major currencies," Mr Blake said. "The magnitude of such a move is too big to ignore."

As such, Morgan Stanley sees foreign earners and tourism-exposed stocks as the beneficiaries, such as Corporate Travel Management. While generally a weaker currency implies people would travel less, Morgan Stanley points to Corporate Travel Management's alignment to the business sector, which is unlikely to change the location or necessity of their trips.

Small caps may also begin to see some industry consolidation. A low-growth environment and the opportunity to build larger companies, either organically or via acquisition, could bring about a material re-rating.

Morgan Stanley points to Mantra Group as a possible acquirer, given the hotel group was adding rooms and increasing market share in 2016.

A highly fragmented hotel market could pose consolidation opportunities.

That said, regulatory risk will weigh on small-cap companies, says the firm. Aged care, radiology, car dealerships and China retailing have all suffered abrupt changes in regulation, which has placed downward pressure on earnings.
direction: bearish for industrials and the Australian dollar, bullish for resources exporters

headline: Finkel fires Coalition coal battle
text:
Liberal Party conservatives are demanding Malcolm Turnbull allow so-called clean coal to qualify as a low emissions fuel and be eligible for incentives if he wants party room support to implement a Clean Energy Target.

The moves put hopes of bipartisan support for the Finkel review and an end to the climate wars in doubt because the Labor Party, which is prepared to drop its support for a more robust emissions intensity scheme and negotiate a CET, will not back the coal demand.

After former prime minister Tony Abbott hit out on Monday, labelling the CET advocated by Chief Scientist Alan Finkel as a "magic pudding" and a "tax on coal", opposition energy spokesman Mark Butler demanded the Prime Minister rein in the rebels.

"If Malcolm Turnbull doesn't pull his party room into line, pretty quickly, he's going to completely lose control of this process before it even begins," Mr Butler said.

In an interview with The Australian Financial Review, Dr Finkel said a CET placed no prohibition on coal but during the preparation of his report, the modelling showed there was little investment appetite for coal-fired power because the economics did not stack up.

"In our model, we allowed for the possibility but none of them came in, purely on economic grounds," he said.

Dr Finkel also defended his work from attacks by conservatives, including Senator Eric Abetz, who accused him of using dodgy modelling to back his claim that a CET was the best option in terms of lower power prices.

It would have slightly more effect than an emissions intensity scheme in lowering prices and would make power about $90 a year per household cheaper than the current approach of doing nothing.

"I'm as confident as I can be but I do realise that models are as good as the assumptions on which they are based," he said.

"We tried to be extremely open. We tried to be as open as we can, as thoughtful as we can."

Dr Finkel said, for example, that the business as usual scenario was based on currently legislated policies and "sensible" interest rates "and we did not assume we would be saved by commercially unproven technology". These include geothermal technology and carbon capture and storage, which other studies have claimed will be producing up to 40 per cent of power by 2050. In a report to government on Friday, Dr Finkel recommended a CET be adopted after 2020 to ensure a stable transition towards cleaner energy sources while Australia still met its Paris agreement of reducing emissions by 26 per cent to 28 per cent on 2005 levels by 2030.

A CET with bipartisan support would restore investor certainty to a volatile and dysfunctional energy market, resulting in lower prices. The most expensive option in terms of power prices would be to do nothing, Dr Finkel said.

A CET would stipulate that a set percentage of power each year be generated using low emissions sources.

At a minimum these would include renewable energy, gas and coal using carbon capture and storage technology, if it can be developed, and for which certificates, similar to the Renewable Energy Target certificates, would be issued.

Existing generators would see out their natural lives and coal use would only fall slightly under a CET.

The Greens oppose the target because it does not cut emissions hard enough while Mr Abbott and other conservatives, including Craig Kelly, one of the most outspoken critics of change, say it is too harsh.

They will be pushing for a higher threshold to enable certificates for so-called clean coal, or High Energy, Low Emissions (HELE) power stations, which are already being built elsewhere. Dr Finkel did not recommend a baseline but modelled a scenario in which a low emissions source would be defined as producing a maximum 600kg (0.6 tonnes) of pollution per megawatt hour of energy. HELE has a 0.7 baseline.

The 0.6 baseline would enable Australia to meet its Paris commitments and zero emissions by 2070.

Asked if increasing the baseline to include HELE coal would jeopardise the Paris commitment, Dr Finkel said he could not be sure because the scenario was not modelled. "There will be ramifications of going higher (than 0.6), there will be ramifications of going lower," he said.

"There will be ramifications on cost (of power) and stability and confidence in reaching the threshold."

Dr Finkel said under a CET "there are no prohibitions just incentives".

"I see the CET as the incentive to bring new generation to the market. The CET does not actually, didn't think about the merits of any generation technology above the threshold."

Mr Abbott, who tore down Malcolm Turnbull's leadership in 2009 over climate change policy, said "my anxiety" centred around claims the low emissions sources would be rewarded with incentives while high emissions sources - coal - would not be punished.

"It's going to be a magic pudding," Mr Abbott said. "If you are rewarding one type of energy then that money has got to come from somewhere.

"It's effectively a tax on coal."

While business, industry and manufacturing are overwhelmingly in favour of a CET, the Minerals Council of Australia is fighting to include so-called clean coal.

Writing in today's The Australian Financial Review, MCA chief executive Brendan Pearson criticises the Finkel review for ignoring nuclear energy and not modelling HELE to qualify as "clean energy".

"It is high enough to include open cycle gas generation and just low enough to exclude new coal generation. The reality is that new super-efficient coal generation has played a fundamental role in reducing global CO2 emissions over recent decades," Mr Pearson says.
direction: bearish for coal-fired power generators and utilities

headline: One bad shot doesn't change the rules of the game
text:
Australia must be proactive and patient while America finds its misplaced leadership again.

Two weeks on a US State Department-sponsored lecture tour around Australia last month reminded me what a civil, civilised country looks like. Aussies have a lot to feel good about: more than a quarter-century of unbroken economic growth, a remarkably harmonious society considering that one in four citizens is an immigrant, and an array of exotic recreational drugs from flat whites to footy.

Even Aussie politics is a source of envy: if only America's leading scandal were about whether legislators can be dual nationals with unsavoury places like New Zealand.

But compared with my last tour three years ago, the Australians I met - at least in the policy elite - were clearly more unsettled. They were worried about erosion of the liberal international order, more anxious than before about the implications of China's rise, and of course deeply perplexed by the recent turn of events in Washington. All of this is forcing Australians to re-think their role in the Asia-Pacific region and the world.

Australia was a founder and champion of the global order established after World War II - the institutions, rules, and norms that have underpinned broad security and prosperity for more than seven decades. But since the terrorist events at the turn of the millennium and the global financial crisis that followed soon after, the liberal order has been under stress. It has been challenged from without, by non-state actors bent on disruption and by new powers demanding more voice in shaping the rules; and from within, by citizens unhappy that the order is no longer delivering the kind of broadly shared benefits that it used to.

Meanwhile, Australians have been famously ambivalent about their relationship with China. Concerns about Beijing's growing military capabilities and maritime assertiveness have been balanced by the gravitational pull of China's rapid economic growth and its boost to Australian resource exports. But the balance has shifted in recent years as China's economy has slowed, as two-way investment has made the Australia-China economic relationship more complex, and as Beijing has begun to push out its economic power in an effort to create a Sino-centric order. Just at a time when US leadership is most needed, Australians look on with alarm at political developments in the United States and wonder whether they can count on us.

Will Washington drag Australia into conflict - a trade war if not a hot one? Will the United States continue to play a counterbalancing role to China in the Asia-Pacific, or will it withdraw from regional leadership, as the decision to withdraw from the Trans-Pacific Partnership suggested? These and many other questions about Donald Trump's America are causing understandable anxiety Down Under.

In a speech at a CSIS-organised conference in Sydney at the start of my trip, former prime minister Paul Keating portrayed a United States in decline and questioned Washington's continued ability to lead the global order. Keating memorably said: "Having pawned the crown, [Washington] can't redeem it at its full value." He asserted that China would inevitably resume its historical role at the centre of an Asian order.

I'm not so sure. A lot has happened in the 200 years since China lost its place at the centre of Asian affairs: the United States rose to global prominence, strong regional powers like Australia and Japan emerged, a widely accepted set of liberal institutions and rules was established, and deepening economic integration produced a real, if embryonic, sense of regional identity. This is no longer Tang Dynasty Asia. China has little soft-power attraction for countries of the region, and any one of a plethora of internal challenges - from massive debt to a rapidly ageing population to catastrophic environmental degradation - could derail its progress.

By contrast, the United States is more resilient than Mr Keating suggests. It remains a geographically and resource-blessed country with unparalleled military capabilities, innovative prowess, and soft power. Moreover, America's national interests will almost certainly draw it back into active participation - indeed leadership - in Asia-Pacific affairs. We cannot afford to pull back from a region where half the world's economy and middle-class population reside, and where some of the world's most challenging problems - from North Korea to pandemic disease - arise.

To reassure my Aussie friends while on my recent tour, I used a metaphor from one of the few sports that unites us: golf. The US may have just shanked the ball deep into the woods, but we're still a strong player and will eventually get back on the green. Just as a bad shot doesn't change the rules of golf, erratic leadership and misguided policies can't change a country's national interests, and ours clearly lie in the Asia-Pacific.

I would urge our strong partners Down Under to keep hitting the ball straight and true. Specifically, Australia can do three things. First, work even harder to defend the liberal international order. Among other things, this means joining forces with Japan, Singapore, and others to complete a high-standard TPP-11 agreement that preserves the market-opening and rule-making gains in the original deal - and keeps a seat warm for the United States to return some day. It also means ensuring that other efforts to promote economic integration across the Asia-Pacific - whether trade agreements or infrastructure investments - conform to the highest possible standards.

Second, Australians should not be shy about reminding their American partners of what both have to lose if the liberal order is undermined by protectionism or withdrawal. This may require more tense phone calls between leaders in Canberra and Washington. But that's what friends are for.

Finally, be patient. Washington is likely to spend the next few years hacking around in the woods looking for its ball. But we'll be back; we have to be.
direction: cautious outlook for rate-sensitive sectors due to geopolitical uncertainty

headline: Top fund managers back new platform
text:
More than 25 big-name fund managers, including Magellan, Vanguard and Macquarie Group, have thrown their weight behind a new managed fund trading platform that its founder says may be a challenger to established bank-run platforms and the ASX's mFunds.

The platform, uXchange, allows members to manage a portfolio of Australian and global managed funds and was launched last week by fintech MA Operator. It has more than 80 managed funds on its investment menu and 14 Australian Financial Services licensees from non-bank aligned financial planning groups have started using the service.

Shannon Bernasconi, co-founder and executive director of MA Operator, said uXchange charges no ongoing fee to its users, instead charging per transaction, capped at $50 a trade.

"But uXchange also provides a distribution channel for fund managers that wasn't there before," Ms Bernasconi said. "It's a concentrated distribution channel for high net worth and self-managed superannuation fund investors, through an independent channel that isn't then influencing their own products and services like a platform would.

"The reaction [from fund managers] has been that this is a great mechanism for them to start connecting to a market [of non-aligned licensees] that was quite hard to get to before because is was so fragmented."

Fund managers can appear on the exchange fee free for the first year. Ms Bernasconi said that managers might then pay a one-off fee of $1000.

"If as a community we wanted to do some kind of marketing, something for the purpose of growth, then we could leverage that $1000," she said.

"It was not my intention to make money from fund managers. It's a channel for the people, mostly the adviser and the client, but the fund managers get to benefit from this distribution channel."

According to uXchange's approved product list at June 16, global fund manager Dimensional had 21 funds on the platform, while Vanguard was close behind with 18. Magellan's global fund appears, along with some of its other of its funds.

Eight of Pinnacle Investment Management's funds from managers including Hyperion, Plato and Spheria have also joined the platform, which has Equity Trustees as its responsible entity.

Pinnacle director of distribution Adrian Whittingham said the financial services industry needed more creative ideas. "What is attractive about uXchange or mFund, or other product providers, is that the market needs innovation," he said.

"If you make it challenging for people to invest, they will choose passive or do it yourself. So at the heart of this, it is about accessibility."

Ms Bernasconi predicted that uXchange would compete directly with bank-owned platforms and mFunds, which she said had disappointed from an accessibility perspective.

"MFunds came out of a need, like any innovation, for those investors that are moving off platform to have access to managed funds," she said. "The concept was right, the need was there, it's just the implementation of it has fallen short of the expectations."

"From personal experience, when you do place an order through mFund, you do have to go specifically to the broker's actual screens, so a person at the adviser end has to physically do something, whereas they're used to a world where that just happens."

The mFund service was launched in March 2014 to satisfy investor appetite for an alternative to shares.

Data from the most recent ASX funds monthly update shows mFund now offers access to 173 funds from 55 fund managers, with funds under management at the end of May standing at about $347 million, an increase of 148 per cent over 12 months.

In February, the Australian Securities and Investments Commission allowed products requiring long-form product disclosure statements - such as hedge funds and derivative products - to sit on the ASX-operated exchange, which allows retail investors to invest in unlisted managed funds.

Ms Bernasconi said uXchange "crowd sources" any funds its adds via adviser or client requests. "We don't determine the list, which is what a bank-based platform does …"
direction: bearish for bank-run platforms and ASX mFunds

headline: LETTERS
text:
Bite the bullet and increase the GST

Yet again we have the federal government trying to provide a Band-Aid solution to Australia's deficit problem. Instead of dealing with the problem by increasing the GST and expanding the coverage of the GST, the government is considering increasing the Medicare Levy by some 0.5 per cent, which would raise a paltry $4 billion. Departing NSW premier Mike Baird has just said that the one thing he regretted was not achieving federal tax reform.

The Liberal/ National Party Coalition is destined for election defeat. They rattle on about the size of the deficit and fear of losing our AAA credit rating. The best way to achieve that is to increase the GST and not change superannuation rules, which again raises peanuts and cost them sizeable votes at the last election.

Come on Prime Minister, show some real backbone and raise the GST.

Tony Mitchell

St Ives, NSW

The high price of

Mike Baird's legacy

While your coverage looked at Mike Baird's economic record, it missed the numerous things that have upset many people. Selling off poles and wires (really, just an off-the-book loan with a creative way of finding some collateral), Miller's Point evictions, selling of government heritage buildings, chopping down trees, initiating WestConnex and the forced council amalgamations. Not to mention the greyhound ban backflip.

If Mr Baird improved the NSW economy, it came at far too high a cost.

We live in world, not just an economy, and he made a Faustian bargain. We can only wonder if he has put enough people off-side who would have otherwise voted for him and/or the Liberal Party, and whether his legacy will be remembered at the next election.

John August

North Ryde, NSW

Death benefits

Debra Cleveland's article "Crucial to rethink your super legacy" (January 21-22) reveals how retired individuals with large super balances and associated reversionary pensions will have their legacy plans torpedoed after the implementation of the $1.6 million transfer balance cap.

After July 1, 2017, reversionary pensions to surviving spouses and dependents will cease to operate as effectively as originally intended and many binding death benefit nominations will have to be redrafted.

But there is a window of opportunity for old gents hell bent on denying the tax man and maximising their super benefits. That option is to depart this world before July I, 2017. The financial benefits of a premature demise are significant. Homicide rates in prestige suburbs are likely to increase. Food-tasters could be run off their feet. Euthanasia counsellors might see a surge in demand for their services. Investors should increase exposure to funeral parlours. At wakes, tears will be replaced by smiles.

So many unintended consequences.

John Cahill

Mosman, NSW

IR reform vital

Graeme Watson's resignation from the Fair Work Commission provides an opportunity to take a look not only at the Commission but the industrial relations system overall ("Fair Work dysfunction needs fixing", January 24). The system is overly technical, cumbersome, and based on overseeing and umpiring rather than relying mainly on workplace agreements. It is adversarial in nature, expensive, and not conducive to shortcuts that may be in the interest of all parties at the workplace. And unions still play a far bigger role than needed and wanted by many employees. In the interest of our economy the government cannot ignore this vital area any longer.

Michael Schilling

Millswood, SA
direction: negative for government-linked assets and utilities

headline: Brisbane closes still look good
text:
Metro Property Development boss Luke Hartman says "doom and gloom" talk about investors not being able to settle their Brisbane off-the-plan apartment purchases is not playing out on the ground.

Metro, founded in 2010 by Mr Hartman, Devine Limited founder David Devine and Ken Woodley, has 35 per cent of the Brisbane apartment market in addition to development projects in Sydney, Melbourne and Adelaide.

"Our performance on settlements has been very good," Mr Hartman told The Australian Financial Review.

"We finished four Brisbane towers covering 950 units over the last four months and 95 per cent of these have settled.

"Quality developments are settling. But they are taking longer than historic averages due mainly to the processing of [mortgage] applications."

But Mr Hartman said he was not seeing any major problems or signs of the market collapsing.

"A lot of the forecasters are not on the ground and so they are not experiencing the market first hand," he said.

The Reserve Bank, the major banks and analysts have all voiced their concerns about an oversupply of apartments on the East Coast, particularly in Brisbane and Melbourne, with Morgan Stanley warning of a 100,000 national surplus of apartments by 2018.

The Australian Bureau of Statistics reported Brisbane unit prices fell 0.6 per cent over the September quarter, the first quarterly decline in two years, while CoreLogic said they fell 0.6 per cent over the December quarter with no growth over 2016.

Recently, AMP Capital chief economist Shane Oliver warned apartment prices in Sydney, Melbourne and Brisbane could fall 15-20 per cent in the next two years because of over-building.

Despite these concerns and a softening in prices, Mr Hartman said there was huge demand for rental accommodation in Brisbane and very low vacancy rates across the towers Metro has developed and built.

He said tougher funding conditions and rising construction costs would keep the market in check and ensure an oversupply of apartments did not happen.
direction: bearish for Brisbane apartment developers and REITs

headline: Total tax impost puts levy in stark contrast
text:
Treasurer Scott Morrison must be laughing all the way to the bank! Ken Henry is one of the most economically literate of the bankers yet the arguments he puts against the bank levy are weak. The most damning criticism seems to be that it reminds him of some bank taxes in 1982.

APRA figures show the big four banks pay tax of $12 billion and are due for a $2 billion to $3 billion tax cut under the government's plan. That puts the $1.6 billion levy for the big four plus Macquarie in context.

The more important questions relate to the best ways of taxing the economic rents generated in a concentrated banking industry. Henry discussed taxing rent in the abstract in his tax review. It would be fascinating to know what he really thinks is the "best" way to tax the exorbitant rents in the modern banking system. But we will probably never know given his obligations as chairman of the board to maximise returns to National Australia Bank shareholders.

David Richardson

The Australia Institute

Manuka, ACT
direction: bearish for major bank shares

headline: Private equity won't eat the banks' mortgage lunch
text:
No one likes to see private equity turn up on their patch, so it's hardly surprising that local bankers are far from thrilled to see that US private equity giants KKR and Blackstone have now established a foothold in the country's $1.7 trillion mortgage market.

What's more, bankers complain, their arrival comes at a particularly inopportune time. Not only are the banks' credit committees imposing tougher rules on property loans, but the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA) has put the brakes on their lending operations big-time, forcing them to tighten their debt-servicing tests, and limit how many interest-only and investor loans they can make.

That's already meant that many home buyers, property investors and developers are now bypassing the banks and looking for alternative sources of funding from non-bank lenders. Buoyant housing markets in Sydney and Melbourne mean that property developers can still earn good margins on the right developments, even if they have to pay more for the funding.

And that means business conditions are extremely good for non-bank lenders such as Pepper (which was acquired by KKR for $682 million in September) and La Trobe Financial (in which Blackstone snapped up an 80 per cent stake this week).

But while the big US private equity firms obviously sniff an enticing opportunity, some analysts believe bankers shouldn't worry that private equity is about to eat their lunch. They argue KKR and Blackstone are unlikely to cause anything like the same damage wreaked by mortgage brokers such as Aussie Home Loans founder John Symonds when they broke the banks' stranglehold on the home loan market in the 1990s.

That's because private equity firms aren't interested in becoming long-term players in the Australian property market. Their business model is to buy into a business, improve its profitability, and then to sell it within three to five years.

What's more, it's clear that APRA and the Reserve Bank are keeping a close watch on developments in the Sydney and Melbourne property markets.

Of course, the Reserve Bank knows that when mortgage rates go down, individuals can take on more debt to buy a house, because lower interest rates mean that their repayments are lower. And this pushes up the overall level of housing prices, as well as household debt to income levels.

This wouldn't be a problem if interest rates were guaranteed to stay extremely low. The risk is that if economic activity picks up, and pushes inflation higher, the Reserve Bank will lift interest rates. And this could create difficulties for some over-leveraged borrowers.

As Reserve Bank governor Philip Lowe explained in a speech last month, "the concern has been that as the household sector takes on ever-more debt relative to its income, the risk of medium-term problems increases".

One way to guard against this risk is to ensure the banks maintain strong lending standards. And, as Lowe noted, the prudential measures introduced by APRA "have worked in this direction".

"Growth in lending to investors has slowed, fewer loans are being made with very high loan-to-valuation ratios, debt-servicing tests have been tightened and fewer interest-only loans are being made."

But the Reserve Bank is also very aware that while the country's banks have shouldered the brunt of the burden in helping to prevent the Sydney and Melbourne property markets from becoming dangerously frothy, non-bank lenders have been picking up the business their larger rivals have been forced to turn down.

The minutes from the Reserve Bank's December board meeting note that "housing credit growth had eased a little over the second half of 2017, as growth in lending to owner-occupiers had slowed somewhat and growth in lending to investors had stabilised at a lower level than in the first half of the year."

This slowdown in housing credit growth "had been accounted for by the major banks, which had been more affected by the need to restrain interest-only lending to comply with the supervisory measures announced earlier in the year".

In contrast, "growth in housing lending by non-authorised deposit-taking institutions (non-ADIs) had picked up, although these institutions' share of overall housing lending remained small".

According to analysts, the implications are clear. Non-bank home lenders will be allowed to enjoy a regulation-light existence so long as they remain fringe players in the housing market. But if they ever manage to capture a significant share of the mortgage market, they can also expect to see some shackles imposed on their lending activities.

In addition, "members noted that these lenders charged higher interest rates on average than ADIs, which is likely to reflect both the borrower risk profile and higher funding costs of non-ADIs."
sentiment: mixed with a negative bias for major banks, positive for non-bank lenders
direction: pressure on bank mortgage margins, growth for non-bank lenders

headline: RBA, GDP set to fuel bullish sentiment
text:
Despite missing expectations, the latest US jobs data encouraged investors to bet on still higher equity valuations, setting the stage for a higher local open to start a data-heavy week.

ASX futures were up 24 points over the weekend. The Australian dollar traded 0.3 per cent higher.

The advance was modest on Wall Street, but enough to keep all three major US benchmarks hovering near record highs. The tech-tilted Nasdaq finished at a record closing high after its best week this year. Wall Street is closed on Monday for the Labor Day holiday.

The latest slew of US data bolstered market optimism.

The US economy added 156,000 jobs in August, less than the consensus forecast for 180,000 yet within reach of the 175,000 trend this past year. On the positive side, the manufacturing sector added 36,000 jobs last month, the most in four years.

"This sudden strength in manufacturing may reflect a better tone in exports, further reinforcing the point that the global economy has quietly taken a turn for the better, despite all the non-stop headline drama elsewhere," said Bank of Montreal chief economist Doug Porter.

He's got a point. The latest PMI data from across the globe pointed to improved manufacturing in the eurozone, Britain, Brazil, Japan and China.

Growth in China's manufacturing sector unexpectedly accelerated last month. We'll get data this week on China's services sector as well as trade.

Capital Economics is bearish on the outlook for China, because of "the impact of tighter monetary conditions", according to global economist Nikita Shah. In contrast, Mr Porter said he saw China on track to post a higher pace of growth this year for the first time since 2010.

And of course Australia's manufacturing sector is a bright light domestically. Last week's capital expenditure report showed capex rose for a second quarter in a row; "a lasting recovery in business spending might finally be starting to emerge", wrote Besa Deda, St George Banking Group's chief economist.

For global investors, in sync with the data, the focus remains on what the US central bank is going to do this month and later in the year. Policymakers are poised to approve the start of paring back the Fed's massive balance sheet and there's still a window open for a third rate hike this year.

"The [August jobs] data will embolden those who fear the lowflation demon and are calling for a delay in further rate hikes and an extremely measured reduction in the balance sheet," wrote Mohamed A. El-Erian in a comment for Bloomberg. "But it will also fuel concerns about a looser-for-longer policy stance fuelling excessive financial risk-taking by the private sector and increasing the threat of future financial instability."

This week there are policy meetings at the Bank of Canada, the European Central Bank and the Riksbank.

Locally investors will be watching the Reserve Bank's policy decision on Tuesday; no change is expected.

NAB director of economics David de Garis said the RBA was expected "to recognise that the near-term growth momentum of the economy continues to improve".

RBA governor Philip Lowe, head of economic analysis Alex Heath and deputy governor Guy Debelle will each have a chance to offer insight on the economy as the week unfolds; Dr Lowe will get two kicks at the can - on Tuesday evening and Thursday.

Accompanying the official chatter, the highlight data point will be second-quarter GDP on Wednesday.

"NAB looks for the Australian economy's growth rate to have stepped up in the June quarter to 0.6 per cent from 0.3 per cent in the March quarter," said Mr de Garis. "Annual growth that is likely virtually steady at 1.6 per cent after 1.7 per cent to Q1. Pre-GDP partials on Monday and Tuesday will indicate whether that estimate remains on track or not."

WEEK AHEAD

MONDAY ■ 2Q company profits and business inventories ■ Aug ANZ job ads

TUESDAY ■ RBA meeting, governor Lowe speaks ■ 2Q current account balance

WEDNESDAY ■ 2Q GDP ■ Fed's beige book ■ Bank of Canada meeting

THURSDAY ■ Jul retail sales and trade balance ■ China foreign reserves

FRIDAY ■ RBA's Lowe and Debelle speak ■ China Aug trade balance

SOURCE: WESTPAC, NAB
direction: bullish for ASX futures and global equities

headline: Gas buyer argues for a Narrabri pipeline via Newcastle
text:
A group of NSW manufacturers has stepped up its efforts to ensure gas from Santos' $2 billion Narrabri project is kept within the state to supply struggling local buyers rather than sucked north for export through Gladstone.

Hunter Gas Pipeline, which is leading the group, has told the NSW Department of Planning that Santos' assurances that the gas would feed the NSW domestic market can't be relied on given it failed in its previous claims that its GLNG venture in Queensland wouldn't draw gas from the domestic market.

Hunter Gas argues gas from the proposed Narrabri project should be sent in an already-approved $500 million pipeline proposed by Jemena that would take it to Newcastle, rather than in the new Western Slopes line planned by APA Group in co-ordination with Santos.

Santos said it had "no plans" to send any of the gas from Narrabri via Jemena's proposed pipeline through the Hunter Valley.

A spokesman said the company had "always been clear" that gas from the project would be made available to the NSW domestic market.

But in a formal submission to the planning department on the Narrabri project, Hunter Gas managing director Garbis Simonian argues the Narrabri proposals as they currently stand with the Western Slopes pipeline component "do not meet the public interest test".

The Western Slopes line would take gas from Narrabri and feed it into the Sydney-Moomba line, so open up broader options on markets for the gas, including Queensland, in theory. Santos says it would be "the most efficient and timely way to bring much-needed gas to the NSW domestic market".

Mr Simonian also urged the government to apply "use it or lose it" conditions on the Narrabri development project, citing the "urgent need for gas for NSW homes and businesses, together with the need for gas to underpin the electricity grid in times of high demand". Some 300,000 jobs in NSW were in gas-reliant manufacturing and could be at risk if supplies run short, he said.

Santos's controversial and long-delayed Narrabri project could supply up to half of the state's gas needs.

Hunter Gas signed an initial accord in early May with Jemena for the Chinese-controlled company to build and run the proposed $500 million pipeline from Narrabri to Newcastle, which already has development approval from the government.

The Jemena line is supported by other industrial gas users, including Mr Simonian's Weston Aluminium.

A line to Newcastle would also solve a gas supply problem at the Colongra power plant on the Central Coast, Mr Simonian said.

EIS submissions close on Monday.
direction: bearish for Santos and pipeline developers

headline: Changing times for eager Aussie start-ups
text:
The Australian start-up ecosystem seems to have a new-found confidence that it can now go toe-to-toe with the rest of the world, and there's a new breed of globally ambitious founders popping up everywhere. But this doesn't mean that it's getting easier to scale a company here.

The rate at which companies are getting to initial scale in Australia is still growing disproportionately slower than the growth of start-ups overall.
