We tend to spend a lot of time talking about how we can get more founders to start companies - but if we're serious about building the ecosystem we need to focus more on ways to help later stage start-ups as well.

Getting from $1 million to $10 million in annualised revenues is one of the most agonising phases that any software company can go through. It is a slow and painful journey of discovery and a lot never make it through - they either burn out or hit a wall.

The struggle is real. The good news? Once you do hit $10 million, your destiny will be in your own hands.

There's no guarantees that you'll go on to become a unicorn - but at this point you'll be very hard to kill off and there will usually be at least some options for funding or a path to profitability. So why is scaling still so challenging in Australia? And how can you maximise your chances of making it through the wilderness?

The usual reason people give for why Australian companies have difficulty scaling is the scarcity of local capital. But today the situation is changing quickly. More than $1 billion in new VC funds have been raised in the past three years, and several sizeable funds ($200 million-plus) have emerged, which have the capital and the risk appetite to support founders through this scale stage and beyond.

We've also seen international investors showing they are willing to make the jump to Australia. However even with great unit economics, you're unlikely to be at cash flow break even at this point, so growth usually means increasing your burn substantially.

If you're a software business think carefully about payment terms and pricing. Many founders never ask for upfront annual payments or grossly underprice their products.

It's also worth considering using venture debt to extend your runway during this phase.

A lot of Australia's biggest global success stories have come from companies selling self-service SaaS with high-volume, high-velocity sales models. But it now seems that many Aussie founders (and some VCs) see this as the only sales model worth having. Unfortunately, self-service just doesn't work for everyone.

Without a first mover advantage or some sort of inherent network effect (think Slack or Atlassian), it's pretty difficult to build long-term competitive differentiation if you're selling self- service software at low price points. If you're selling to enterprises then you will usually need at least some specialist salespeople.

Of course, building a sales team comes with unique challenges. If your customers are overseas, you'll need to start building an international presence faster. This may mean adopting the "Israeli" model - building a local R & D team but moving the go-to-market team overseas as soon as you have initial signs of product/market fit.

It also means you're going to need to hire a sales team - and eventually a VP sales. In my experience this is one of the hardest hires to get right.

When you start to scale, new functional roles emerge like marketing, sales, support and product management. I've seen many founders get to $1 million in revenue with ease but then hit the wall at $3-5 million - and often it's because they haven't hired strong enough VPs to support them in these areas. But hiring great marketing, sales, support and product VPs in Australia is still tough. The great CEOs I've worked with overseas often spend 20 per cent to 30 per cent of their time recruiting for VPs. As an Aussie founder, you'll need to look harder and further for great talent so expect to spend more than 30 per cent of your time recruiting. And don't be afraid to look outside Australia.

To get to real scale, you'll likely need to attract later-stage investors at some point. This means being very careful about the investor terms you accept early on. Unfortunately, there seem to be a lot of crappy terms floating around in Australian seed-stage term sheets - and some can do a lot of damage to a company when it tries to scale.

Things like giant super pro rata rights (a right to invest a very large portion of the next round), full ratchet anti-dilution provisions, tranched investments and valuation ratchets that are tied to long range forecasts. These sorts of terms can cause big problems for follow-on investors since it can impact the future value of their own stake, and may even scare them off entirely.

James Cameron is a partner at AirTree Ventures.
direction: supportive for late-stage tech start-ups and venture capital

headline: Do economists shape politics?
text:
Not so long ago economists appeared to rule the world. The globalisation reaching into every home was christened the brainchild of neoliberal economics. But when things began to go wrong, starting in 2007, economists took a beating. Now, with the threat of deglobalisation hanging over us, economists stand on the sidelines, feeling ignored.

This recent turn of events might leave us wondering: do economists have the power and influence required to affect political and policy outcomes, or is it politics that determines which strains of economics are cherry-picked and ultimately championed?

It's not just an abstract question. The answer tells us the extent to which economists are to blame for the policy failures of the globalisation era, and the extent to which they can help shape policy responses to come.

John Maynard Keynes and Milton Friedman, perhaps the two most influential economists of the 20th century, had different views on the matter. For Keynes, economists were in the driver's seat. "Practical men who believe themselves to be quite exempt from any intellectual influence, are usually the slaves of some defunct economist," he scoffed. Were Keynes alive today, he would no doubt argue that the global financial crisis, Brexit and the election of Donald Trump are all a result of a failed free-market economic agenda. Economists would be squarely in the dock.
direction: ambiguous impact on specific sectors

headline: China releases first of Crown staffers
text:
China released 10 employees of casino operator Crown Resorts on Wednesday, the first of 16 who were detained in October and sentenced in June for gambling crimes.

Four employees, including Australian national Jerry Xuan, emerged from a Shanghai detention facility accompanied by family members and security officials, according to a Reuters reporter at the scene. They left immediately in cars without speaking to media.

Another six were released from a second facility in the city, said a man who identified himself as a lawyer for the families and who declined to give his name.

"Everything went OK," a family member of one of the employees told Reuters on condition of anonymity.

Nineteen employees were detained as Crown was trying to attract high-spending Chinese to its casinos outside of China, where gambling is illegal except in the territory of Macau. A court jailed 16 of the employees including three Australians for nine to 10 months, backdated to their October detention.

The incident prompted Crown to shift focus to its home market. It had been a shareholder of Macau-focused Melco Resorts & Entertainment but sold its remaining stake for $1.16 billion in May.

The Department of Foreign Affairs confirmed that two Australians were among the Crown Resorts employees released. "Two Australians were released on 12 July at the conclusion of their sentence," the statement on Wednesday said.

"Due to our privacy obligations, we will not provide further comment on the individuals."

The Australian government has provided consular assistance to three Australian Crown employees since they were detained in Shanghai in October 2016.

A total of 19 current and former Crown Resorts staff, including three Australians, pleaded guilty to charges of illegal promotion of gambling on the Chinese mainland at a hearing in China in June.

Of the 19, 16 were fined and sentenced to a jail term.

Three defendants, who had been released on bail last November, were not fined or sentenced to prison.

Of those fined and jailed, 11 received a sentence of nine months, and five received a sentence of 10 months, with time in detention taken into account in all cases.

Crown's head of international VIP gambling, Australia's Jason O'Connor, was sentenced to 10 months in jail and fined $390,000.

Australian-Chinese dual nationals, Mr Xuan and Jenny Pan, received sentences of nine months' imprisonment and were fined $78,000 and $39,000 respectively. The marketing of casinos and organising overseas gambling trips for 10 or more people are illegal on mainland China. Chinese authorities have been cracking down on gambling as part of a fight against corruption.

Australian-Chinese dual nationals, Mr Xuan and Jenny Pan, received sentences of nine months' imprisonment and were fined $78,000 and $39,000 respectively. The marketing of casinos and organising overseas gambling trips for 10 or more people are illegal on mainland China. Chinese authorities have been cracking down on gambling as part of a fight against corruption. Crown Resorts is controlled by billionaire James Packer.

REUTERS, AAP
direction: Crown Resorts shares likely to rise as legal uncertainty resolves and focus returns to core domestic operations

headline: China's PMI nears best in four years
text:
Shanghai | Chinese manufacturers are more optimistic about the future than at any point in almost two years, even as the pick-up in the world's second-biggest economy continued to favour larger state-owned firms.

Helped by a jump in exports, the Caixin General Manufacturing PMI rose to 51.7 points in February, although it is still below levels seen at the end of last year. The recovery in manufacturing conditions, which began in late 2015, has coincided with unprecedented credit expansion by Beijing aimed at keeping growth rates steady.

Most economist believe the rate of credit growth, which topped 15 per cent last year, is unsustainable and Caixin warned the rebound in manufacturing may not last.

"It is premature to jump to the conclusion that the recovery is entrenched," said Zhong Zhengsheng, the director of macro-economic analysis at Caixin. He said the trend for this year would be clearer by the second quarter.

Economists are looking towards the National People's Congress which begins in Beijing on Sunday for signals on the year ahead.

While the growth target is expected to be set at about 6.5 per cent, there will be more focus on money supply (M2) and any indication the government is looking to tighten credit growth.

The Caixin PMI, which focuses more on private sector firms, was better than economists had expected and close to its highest level in four years.

The government's official PMI, also released on Wednesday, came in at a stronger-than-expected 51.6 points.

But its sub-indices showed larger state-owned firms were benefiting most from the uptick in the economy.

The large enterprise PMI was 53.3 points in February, up 0.6 percentage points from the previous month, while the small enterprise PMI was flat at 46.4 points. A reading below 50 points indicates a sector in contraction.

"The PMIs suggest growth remains reasonably strong by recent standards. But this largely comes on the back of stronger external demand which we don't think will be sustained," said Julian Evans-Pritchard from Capital Economics. He said growth from China's major trading partners would likely fall away during the year.

"Meanwhile, we think that domestic demand growth in China, which appears to have plateaued recently, will slow in the coming quarters as a tighter monetary and fiscal stance continues to weigh on credit growth and infrastructure investment," Mr Evans-Pritchard said.

China's central bank raised a key lending rate for the first time in six years in early February, signalling it would take stronger measures to contain credit. While the People's Bank of China only raised the seven-day repurchase rate by 10 basis points to 2.35 per cent, it was viewed as a signal that interest rates were on the way up.

The looser credit environment in China over the last year has boosted demand for commodities as China's housing market picked up.

This has delivered higher than expected demand for iron ore and coking coal and helped Australia report stronger than expected annual economic growth of 2.4 per cent on Wednesday.
direction: cautious outlook for Chinese manufacturing and credit-sensitive sectors

headline: Energy bullying and indecision
text:
Malcolm Turnbull is entering dangerous territory as he scraps with power major AGL over the future of its Liddell power plant in the NSW Hunter Valley.

AGL plans to close the coal-fired power station when it reaches the end of its economical life in 2022. The Prime Minister is seeking to strong-arm its chief executive, Andy Vesey, into keeping the plant open, or selling it to someone who will.

What will Mr Turnbull do if the AGL board confirms the company will indeed close Liddell but invest in other generation that will help stabilise the grid? Will he double down on Energy Minister Josh Frydenberg's attacks on "the big energy companies who are making big profits off the back of Australian families"?

A clear and present crisis calls for decisive action. But the political bickering continues to undermine business investment confidence, as industry players told The Australian Financial Review's Australian Energy Future round table yesterday. As CEO of Alinta Energy Jeff Dimery told the round table: "It's a bit like playing a game of football with invisible goalposts … we don't know what it is we're actually shooting for."

Moreover, the Prime Minister is setting a dangerous precedent of government bullying of individual companies, with responsibilities to shareholders, into non-commercial decisions to enact its policy priorities. That will raise the risk of taxpayers paying above the odds to either buy or facilitate the purchase of a plant like Liddell, making the government reliant on its success. That's even when energy regulators at a separate forum yesterday made clear there were other solutions to the post-2022 shortfall in reliable energy other than extending the life of an old coal-fired power station.

To diffuse the immediate pressure, Mr Vesey has asked the government for 90 days to prepare a plan to replace the electricity generation that will be lost when Liddell closes, which it was planning to do in any case. However, the problem is not AGL's intransigence but a decade of failed energy and climate policies from both sides of politics.
direction: bearish for energy sector shares

headline: Gas better than Liddell, says Labor
text:
Labor has slammed government plans to extend the life of the Liddell power station as inadequate and is stepping up a campaign to use gas as a source of reliable baseload power while the energy market transitions towards cleaner sources.

With Prime Minister Malcolm Turnbull and Energy Minister Josh Frydenberg scheduled to meet AGL boss Andy Vesey on Monday to try to convince him to sell Liddell to a willing operator, Mr Shorten said prolonging the life of a clapped-out power plant was not substitute for energy policy.

"If something can be done, which is commercial, Labor is not going to immediately rule that out," Mr Shorten said of the Liddell option. "But ... if Mr Turnbull's energy strategy is to keep a power plant, which is 50 years old, running for 60 years, I hope he's got a little bit more than just that plan.

"This is just another example of poorly thought out, ideological government intervention making things worse, not better, for the people they are supposed to be serving."

For over a week now, Mr Shorten has been demanding Mr Turnbull pull the trigger the government gave itself and force gas exporters to return a proportion back to the domestic market. "Malcolm Turnbull should be working to address the energy crisis right now - a fair dinkum clean energy target, pull the trigger on gas export controls, prepare a strategic reserve for the shortfall this summer," Mr Shorten said.

Mr Turnbull wants to discuss options to prolong the life of Liddell for at least five years beyond its scheduled 2022 closure after the Australian Energy Market Operator warned this week the closure would cause a 1000 megawatt deficiency in baseload power supply in NSW and Victoria. AEMO suggests keeping open old coal-fired plans until renewable storage and other technology became sufficiently advanced was preferable to building new coal-fired power plants.

It questioned the market viability of the latter option and said there was no time to build one anyway before Liddell closes.

A Labor source said the Opposition, while not opposed to Liddell, believed developing gas-fired generation to guarantee baseload supply, using gas seconded from exporters, was a more viable option.

The split does not bode well for the much-hoped-for bipartisanship from business, industry and consumers who are hurting from high energy costs.

Mr Shorten said any blackouts and price increases this summer would be on Mr Turnbull's head. Mr Turnbull returned fire, calling his opponent "blackout Bill".

On Monday, Mr Vesey is expected to reject overtures to sell Liddell, and instead argue a clean energy plant, possibly including gas back-up, that AGL has planned for the site will replace the lost generation capacity.

Delta Electricity is the favourite to take over Liddell should AGL agree to sell but it was not optimistic. "The simple answer is it ain't for sale and we ain't buying," Delta's company secretary Steve Gurney told AFR Weekend.

He said if it were for sale Delta would have to conduct a full due diligence process and consider all factors including the state of the market and the plant, and the rehabilitation liabilities for the eventual closure of the site.

The NSW government continued to fuel speculation that it could either take over Liddell or build a so-called clean-coal fired plant.

"Renewables may be the way of the future but we must continue to invest in traditional sources to ensure our energy supply," said state Treasurer Dominic Perrottet. 

Key points

Labor has lashed the government's Liddell power station plan.

Bill Shorten says prolonging the life of a clapped-out power plant is a bad idea.
direction: bearish for AGL and incumbent coal generators

headline: One-man brand
text:
The world loves this quiet achiever’s red carpet and bridal couture gowns.

Steven Khalil is known for his glamorous gowns, but he grew up in far from glamorous circumstances – raised with three siblings near Campbelltown on the outskirts of Sydney by a single Lebanese mother. Money was tight and his dream to create bridal couture wasn’t taken too seriously.

Despite his family’s doubts Khalil persisted, studying fashion at TAFE in Bankstown. “I wanted to learn how to design and construct beautiful gowns and I needed to make it work to prove that doing what you love is the most important thing,” he says.

Now aged 39, he’s proven that and more. With 643,200 Instagram followers, Khalil’s business is global. Evening gowns cost about $9000 and bridal couture up to $35,000 – women regularly fly in to his Paddington atelier and boutique from San Francisco, New York, London, Dubai, Saudi Arabia and Hong Kong to create the wedding dress of their dreams. Celebrity wearers of his gowns include Princess Maria-Olympia of Greece, Jennifer Lopez and Kylie Jenner.

After a successful trunk show in London in 2016, his gowns are being stocked by the Wedding Club of Knightsbridge and South Kensington. His 2017 runway show at this year’s Mercedes-Benz Fashion Week Australia was a standout. He’s now building on his success by releasing a costume jewellery line and developing his own bridal range of French and Italian fabrics that are hand-beaded in India.

Ready-to-wear bridal veils and evening bags are coming.

An Australian-made eau de parfum costing $190 will be released in spring. “It’s about creating a signature scent and for me that’s the next step. I want to follow in [Chanel’s] footsteps.” His rationale is simple: there are girls who can’t spend money on evening and wedding dresses – perfume and jewellery make the brand accessible. “It’s affordable luxury.”

Later this year Khalil will take a trunk show to Milan.

Having shown twice in New York, it is his dream to break into the European market. He is considering a runway show in Paris featuring day to evening wear as well as bridal.

Khalil now employs 20 seamstresses, is the sole owner of his company and has financed everything himself. The boy from Campbelltown has done well but he acknowledges it’s only a matter of time before he seeks a like-minded investor to grow his business. ●
direction: bullish for Steven Khalil's business

headline: How to help house price losers
text:
An Australian's house is his or her tax haven, and much else besides that. But supply hasn't kept up with demand, which is causing serious flow-on effects. 

Australia might have escaped the worst of the global financial crisis and the subsequent weak recovery, but it has not been completely unscathed.

The period of falling commodity prices and mining investment has increased the risk of bitter and potentially growth-sapping intergenerational division.

Low interest rates have helped the economy negotiate the end of the mining investment boom, but at the cost of fuelling a risky housing price boom in Sydney and Melbourne.

The real estate boom is finally topping out, but the factors behind the low interest rates and high demand for real estate - strong population growth, low inflation, slow wages growth and high household debt - could be around for quite a while.

At the same time, efforts to expand the effective supply of land in the major cities continue to run up against the resistance of property owners and other interests.

Economist Judith Yates argues in a new report on the housing market published by the Committee of Economic Development of Australia (CEDA) that the pressures on housing affordability are likely to continue for years.

If the scenario of the last decade's residential property boom is repeated, prices will fall back in real terms by enough to burn the late entrants and perhaps a developer or two, but most of the cycle's real capital gains will be held.

That would be a good outcome for the majority of investors and their banks, and for Australia's macroeconomic managers who worry about the impact of a property price slump on household spending and even on financial system stability. But it would mean that real median property prices will have trebled in Sydney and Melbourne since 1990.

Even with the greater borrowing capacity afforded by low interest rates, home ownership is out of the reach of a substantial proportion of younger households.

It is often argued that increases in real estate wealth are largely illusory, but the difference between winning and losing in the real estate market is very real. The increase in property prices has changed the distribution of wealth, with Baby Boomers the primary winners and generations X and Y the biggest losers.

This has contributed to continuing strong decline in home ownership of Australians in the 25-34 and 35-44 age groups.

There are two overlapping housing affordability problems.

One is the change in the distribution of wealth and the ongoing benefits that go with it. An Australian's owner-occupied home is his or her tax haven. Housing ownership also increases access to credit, and often provides the collateral for people starting a business.

The other problem is that the combination of strong demand and constrained supply also may have limited the access of low-income families to the Sydney and Melbourne labour markets.

Yates warns that low-income families are being pushed to city fringes or beyond, where they face long and expensive commutes or are restricted to the outer regions' limited employment opportunities, reinforcing their socioeconomic disadvantage.

They will join a slower-growing workforce whose members, if we believe the warnings about a secular slowdown in productivity growth, are likely to be faced with higher tax and health insurance burdens to support the ageing population.

Among the recommendations included in the CEDA report are tax and welfare reforms to encourage downsizing, reduction in local government planning restrictions, and more effective investment in urban transport to increase the effective supply of land.

At the same time, the report recommends reform of tenancy laws to meet the needs of long-term renters, and less concessional capital gains tax and the replacement of stamp duties with land tax.

Many politicians will agree in principle with all of those recommendations, but wonder how they might achieve the changes in practice.

Certainly none will be more difficult than persuading residents to accept an expansion of high- and medium-rise accommodation. However, every year Australians flock to Europe to admire the medium-density lifestyles of cities like Paris and Barcelona.

Perhaps the key to changing attitudes here is to show that Australia's federal and state governments can also orchestrate highly attractive medium- and high-rise developments.

The spread of European-style trams in Sydney could be an important part of that development.

But there may have to be a major cultural change: close government oversight of design and development may be necessary if we are to shake off the image of the 1960s and '70s ugly "walk-ups" .
direction: bearish for residential property and real estate developers

headline: Deutsche's Aussie staff query Asia bigwigs on bonuses
text:
Deutsche Bank's local staff got their chance to pepper regional management with questions on compensation on Monday.

Street Talk understands Deutsche's Asia Pacific chief Werner Steinmueller and regional corporate and investment bank boss James McMurdo led a whole-of-bank town hall.

They provided an update on the region and Deutsche's stronger capital position post its bumper €8 billion raising in March this year.

But the questions that were top of mind for employees centred around compensation - namely bonuses - returning next year. Steinmueller has assured staff that this year's freeze was a one-off and payments will resume as normal in 2018.

Also the pair were here to send off outgoing Australia chief Michael Ormaechea, who parts ways with Deutsche next week.

His departure coincides with the start date of his successor, former Goldman Sachs partner Anthony Miller, on July 3.

Ormaechea told staff of his intentions to retire in January after 22 years with Deutsche.

A farewell dinner was said to have been held at Cafe Sydney on Monday evening.

Miller, who has big shoes to fill, has spent time in Australia and Asia and parted ways with Goldman at the end of 2016.

He most recently led Goldman's Asia Pacific investment banking solutions arm from Hong Kong.

Miller was promoted to that role from Sydney where he was in charge of Goldman's financing group for Australia and New Zealand.

He joined Goldman in 2001 and had several roles with the firm, including head of credit capital markets.
direction: supportive for Deutsche Bank shares

text:
Inside

Venture capital Hostplus ramps up investments p16

Market minds Bond yield rally won't last p20

Matthew Stevens Santos' coal seam gas plan rebuffed p28
direction: bond yields and rate-sensitive sectors face near-term headwinds

headline: SA's solar-battery game-changer
text:
A $1 billion solar-battery farm to be built by Lyon Group and Downer in South Australia's Riverland will be the world's largest and ready to help prevent blackouts in the state's fragile power grid by next summer.

Lyon Group, a Brisbane-based partnership headed by David Green and backed by Mitsubishi of Japan and US hedge fund Magnetar Capital, has secured land and will start construction in June to be operational in December.

The project includes a 330 megawatt solar farm costing $700 million and a 100MW battery with four hours of storage - or 400MWh capacity. Grid connection negotiations are well advanced on privately owned scrubland land near the town of Morgan.

A battery farm of that size would have been big enough to prevent the partial SA blackout on 8 February - although not the epic blackout that crippled the state on 28 September last year.

The Riverland project isn't dependent on the tender for grid-scale batteries to fortify SA's fragile power grid announced by the Weatherill government two weeks ago - but the outcome of that tender could influence the final configuration of the Riverland project.

Tesla, software billionaire Mike Cannon-Brookes, AGL Energy and ZEN Energy are eyeing the government tender and a similar one in Victoria, but Lyon is set to be the first to put batteries on the ground in the race to build a world-scale storage project in Australia.

Mr Green told reporters in Adelaide private equity firm Blackstone was among those backing the project via Magnetar. The solar power it generates will qualify for renewable energy target subsidies - currently worth $84 per MWh - on top of the wholesale market price.

"We have the capital. That's already secured," he said. Mr Green said big players like Blackstone-backed funds didn't "come in behind something that's ill-considered". He said "full performance warranties up to 20 years" could be offered for the project, which will use lithium ion batteries.

The project looms as a game-changer in the roiling debate over how to fix SA's energy security woes and fortify the wider national grid as large eastern states follow SA's lead and install more wind and solar energy, triggering the retirement of old, coal-fired power stations such as Victoria's 1600MW Hazelwood plant, which shut down its last turbines on Wednesday.

It is in addition to a previously announced $350-400 million Kingfisher solar-battery farm project at Roxby Downs, a pivotal point in the SA power grid given its proximity to BHP Billiton's giant Olympic Dam copper-gold mine and Oz Minerals' Prominent Hill mine. The Kingfisher project, with 120MW of solar panels and 100MW/200MWh of batteries, is due to be operational in June 2018.

Both mining projects suffered prolonged outages after SA's statewide blackout on 28 September, which sparked a national debate on energy security.

Lyon Group is being partnered by US-based battery partner AES Energy Storage, which participated in California's emergency roll-out of large-scale battery storage last year along with Tesla and Greensmith, the supplier to South Australian battery company ZEN Energy. Transport, engineering and maintenance services firm Downer is earmarked to be the contractor for the Riverland project.

Key points

A private consortium is building a giant solar-battery farm in South Australia.

Funding is already secured, and construction will commence in June.
direction: bullish for renewable energy and battery storage stocks

headline: TOUGH DECISION ON NBN FOR MR BROADBAND
text:
As the national rollout gathers pace, the NBN is becoming a major headache for Prime Minister Malcolm Turnbull, write Max Mason and Paul Smith.

Mister Broadband took the old adage the best defence is a good offence to a new level this week, sheeting home all blame for the on-the-nose National Broadband Network to the former Labor government. But it's going to take more than finger pointing to fix the problems.

Just when it should be turning into a shiny new political asset as the national rollout gathers pace, the NBN is becoming a major headache for Prime Minister Malcolm Turnbull with a big spike in the numbers of consumers complaining about sluggish speeds and connection problems.

Despite the initial big talk about delivering the Coalition's fibre-to-the-node based broadband network faster and cheaper than Labor's "gold plated" fibre-to-the-premise version, the PM is embroiled in a PR nightmare.

The NBN is taking longer to deliver and costing more than promised and looking sub-standard to boot.

Yet the current problems can be fixed. As long as the focus can be shifted away from the technology debate that has dogged the rollout since the beginning and toward the loosening of commercial constraints.

NBN CEO Bill Morrow and other telecommunications industry commentators are increasingly focused on demonstrating that the consumer complaints dragging the NBN back into the mire reflect problems not with the technology chosen but, rather, with an overly ambitious business plan.

Recent interviews and comments made on the public record appear to be early moves in a chess game aimed at persuading the government to reduce the commercial return expectations on NBN, either by bringing it on to the budget bottom line as an infrastructure investment, or by significantly increasing its own investment.

Neither is an attractive course of action for the government. But if there is no change, the PR nightmare could get a whole lot worse.

Right now, NBN is ready to connect in 6.4 million premises. Just over 3 million already have active connections. Fast forward to the first half of 2019, when the next Federal election is expected to be held, and the NBN will, theoretically, be nearly complete.

Some 11.9 million premises are forecast to be ready for service by the end of 2019-20 and 8.1 million are expected to be using the network by then. The big question is: how many will be happy with it?

Morrow has acknowledged 15 per cent of users are seriously dissatisfied with the service. Complaints related to NBN services surged 159.3 per cent to 27,195 in 2016-17, according to the Telecommunications Industry Ombudsman annual report.

Media coverage such as this week ABC's Four Corners program, which inspired Australian jealousy by highlighting New Zealanders with vastly faster services. The program may have missed the mark with its Kiwi comparison in terms of the actual issue slowing Australian broadband speeds, but it further fanned the flames. If the NBN is embarrassing government now, with just 3 million active connections, it looks like getting a whole lot worse when 8 million premises are online.

This week Turnbull finally acknowledged what many in government, industry and NBN itself have known for a long time - the project was in trouble from the start when the business was set up as a profit-making entity. It's this that's putting the squeeze on the network's current performance, not the copper or lack of fibre.

There is no argument that fibre is a better and faster technology but, in the majority of cases, the network being rolled out under the Coalition's multi-technology mix can easily cater to the current needs of most Australians.

The problem lies with the fact that in order to make its commercial return NBN is trying to charge retail service providers (RSPs) more than they are willing to pay for capacity. Therefore RSPs are selling NBN packages that cannot deliver the promised speeds.

This controversial connectivity virtual circuit (CVC) charge levied by NBN on the RSPs and could be removed if there was less commercial demand on the network.

NBN has a target average revenue per user of $52 that it needs to achieve to pay for the build out. This includes the payments it makes to Telstra to pay for the use of Telstra's ducts, rack space in exchanges and access to pits. Those charges equate to about $15 per user.

If it didn't have these payments to Telstra, NBN could slash its revenue target by just under 29 per cent to $37 per end user, lower or drop the CVC, and encourage retailers to buy more capacity.

NBN is often compared with its New Zealand counterpart Chorus, which is rolling out a largely fibre-to-the-premise network. Unlike NBN, however, Chorus does not have to pay to use existing infrastructure because it already owns it. Formerly New Zealand Telecom, the company split its wholesale and retail operations in order to win the contract to build out the country's fibre network.

As part of that split, the new wholesale business - Chorus - kept the existing infrastructure.

In Australia, Telstra was against a similar split and the then Labor government didn't believe it could force the incumbent telco to do so. Instead, the new monopoly NBN was established, set up from scratch with no infrastructure or existing business expertise of its own.

This is an important factor in understanding why the NBN is costing so much to roll out and why it is charging so much for wholesale access to recover those costs.

Presently, the NBN's corporate plan aims for a rate of return of between 3.2 per cent and 3.7 per cent which is enough to cover costs, such as debt repayments, and to 2040.

To stay on the government's balance sheet as a cash for asset transfer, the NBN's rate of return need only be at least the long-run inflation rate, which is currently 2.5 per cent. If it fell below that rate, it would be counted as a grant rather than equity and add $29 billion to the deficit.

The NBN equity value is $29 billion while the government has lent another $20 billion to NBN at cheaper rates than it could get in a commercial environment.

In the immediate term Turnbull says this is comfortably achievable, and the government is clearly not keen to backtrack, but behind the scenes it wouldn't surprise if it was working out how to shuffle the pack without losing face.

There don't seem to be many other viable solutions to this increasingly problematic situation.

"Either the govt changes the economic constraints so NBN Co collect less money in total; or consumers get used to paying more for the bandwidth they need to do the job," Former NBN director and founder of internet service provider Internode Simon Hackett wrote on Twitter this week.

"No third answer. Network has capacity, but economic model is busted. Frog has now been boiled."

There are those that believe Turnbull should stand firm against the RSPs complaints, not write down the government's investment, and wait for the massive churn event and NBN pricing tweaks to cycle through. Earlier this week, Morrow hinted at new pricing offers, which could offer the 50 Megabit per second download over 20 Mbps upload plan at the same price as the 25/5Mbps plan.

However, a write-down would certainly go a long way to helping deal with the issues pounding NBN.

While it would leave RSPs nowhere to hide if they fail to deliver on speed expectations, it would also be an acknowledgement that CVC charges are hitting an industry that is already doing it tough.

For the NBN is a massive churn event - every user in Australia needs to choose which provider they will go with.

An unprecedented battle for market share is under way where companies are concurrently fighting for new customers and battling to hold on to the ones they have.

Costs related to marketing, truck rollouts, new modems, new deals are all hitting RSPs, with experts estimating the cost of acquisition for one user can be between $200 to $300.

No doubt the government would prefer consumers have a change of heart in terms of their willingness to pay more than $60 per month for high-speed internet, but that doesn't seem likely.

In which case, removing the commercial shackles from the NBN would solve the service issues in the short term, kicking the FTTN versus FTTP debate further down the road ... where Turnbull always intended it to be.

Quote Quote Quote Quote

An unprecedented battle for market share is under way

The big question is: how many will be happy with it?
direction: bearish for NBN Co and telecommunications sector

headline: China, US can reach regional compromise
text:
A picture of a much more benign strategic environment for Australia - despite the uncertainty following the election of Donald Trump and tensions in the region - has emerged from the Crawford Australian Leadership Forum.

Eminent panellists from China, the US, Singapore and Australia at the forum at the Australian National University on Tuesday painted a picture of a China that was keen to find an accommodation with its neighbours in the South China Sea, a deal between North Korea and the US on nuclear weapons being close, and a relatively benign and pragmatic view of US policy by China.

Perhaps the most interesting perspective on regional affairs came from former long-serving Singaporean foreign minister George Yeo, who urged Australians to learn from the way south-east Asia has dealt with China over hundreds of years.

Mr Yeo urged Australia to consider its strategy and business investments in Asia through the prism of south-east Asia rather than China alone, and gave an insight into the way China's smaller neighbours had dealt with the behemoth back to the Ming Dynasty.

"South-east Asian countries have a long history of the waxing and waning of China's dynasties and they know that when China is organised ... it is just not worth the fight.

"No one in south-east Asia wants China as an enemy," he said, though along with a number of speakers he had pointed out that China, through its long history, has no history of being a coloniser.

"China is going to become bigger and stronger," he said. "It's unstoppable. It is the only major country in the world today which can exercise a national will. When they are right, they are awesome. When they are wrong, they are also awesome. "

He noted that this capacity to actually deliver on national goals meant it had a "credibility and continuity" which was now manifesting itself in its capacity to move markets on the back of policy announcements.

"You can be sure that when they announce something in a five-year plan that at the end of the five-year plan, something close to it happens.

"So you read their five-year plans carefully. How many governments in the world can match that? And this is one fifth of humanity.

"If a country deals with China they know at least it is an unequal contest, that if they are unhappy with you they can put pressure on using multiple acupuncture points [including tourism and buying iron ore]."

He noted as an example that mainland Chinese tourism in Taiwan has recently dropped by two thirds. The lesson from this was that south-east Asian neighbours were always seeking to diversify because no one wants to be wholly dependent on China.

He pleaded with the US to not try to force countries in the region to choose between the US and China.

"Under pressure many countries would decide to deal with China because that is where their bread is buttered. Do we want Americans in the Western Pacific? Of course. So my appeal to my American friends is 'don't force anybody to choose' and if you don't you will find that everyone will want to be your friend."

Professor Shen Dingli from Fudan University in Shanghai, candidly admitted that "we know many [of our regional partners] do not like us particularly because of our behaviour in the South China Seas area". But he said China would "try to finesse these challenges by being cooperative".

This might involve, for example, agreements on fishing activities, and economic development through the so-called Belt and Road Initiative (BRI).

However, the secretary of Australia's Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade, Frances Adamson, reflected wariness about the BRI when she told the forum there were concerns about the transparency of tendering in the scheme.

"While we are ... supportive in principle of more infrastructure in this region and beyond, we are concerned about as you would expect us to be about transparency, about rules, about open contracts and governance arrangements," she said.

"Because we've seen, including in the South Pacific ... where big infrastructure projects have brought unsustainable debt burdens and ultimately proven more of a minus than a plus," she said.

She also said "China's actions in the South China Sea have rightly given rise to concerns" among its neighbours.

"What we would be looking for in the period ahead is reassurance from China, not only by its actions but by its words," she said.
direction: supportive for Australian equities and risk assets

headline: Craveable Brands gives up float plan
text:
Investors remain nervous about buying private equity-backed floats, with fast-food franchisor Craveable Brands the latest high-profile company to withdraw plans to list on the ASX.

As revealed by Street Talk yesterday afternoon, Craveable, which is the franchisor of about 570 restaurants across its Red Rooster, Oporto and Chicken Treat brands, withdrew its float plans just days before the owner of the business, private equity firm Archer Capital, was scheduled to work out a final price for the float.

But a shock profit downgrade from Retail Food Group, and a bearish report on Domino's Pizza Enterprises, saw both stocks fall sharply this week and further dent sentiment towards Craveable.

"The decision was taken due to current conditions in the local equity market, including the broader sentiment weighing down the overall retail sector," Craveable said in a statement.

It is understood there has not be a decision on whether to pursue a trade sale or alternative exit strategy for the business.

Archer Capital had been seeking to raise about $250 million at $2.09 to $3.07 a share.

Craveable's brokers Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley told fund managers during last week's initial public offering roadshow that the float would come to market at 11.5-times to 12.5-times profit, on a 2018 financial year P/E basis. But fund managers said the deal was too expensive.

"Vendor expectations on exit multiples remain too high," John Grace, portfolio manager at Ausbil Investment Management, told AFR Weekend.

"Recent disappointing performances from some of the private-equity backed listings have led to increased caution from investors."

A string of private-equity backed floats have disappointed investors in recent years, including services group Spotless, hospital operator Healthscope and, most infamously, retail chain Dick Smith, which was also backed by Archer.

In May, tap and appliances group Zip Industries, backed by Quadrant Private Equity, pulled its $500 million deal, while a month earlier Accolade Wines, which is 80 per cent owned by CHAMP Private Equity, decided against a $1 billion public float.

"Private equity initial public offerings are uninvestible at the moment because of the sheer amount of earnings manipulation occurring before the float," one portfolio manager said. "Multiples aren't the issue. Weak boards and short-term decision making mean businesses are dressed up for sale but then implode a few years after listing."

Other high-profile floats to be pulled include Wesfarmers' proposed spin-off of Officeworks and Retail Apparel Group, which opted for a $320 million trade sale in May.

The next test of the IPO market is likely to be Melbourne-based investment solutions and superannuation advice company NetWealth Group.

Craveable's 11.5-times to 12.5-times earnings multiple range equated to an 8.6 per cent to 9.3 per cent dividend yield, which it hoped would draw in the retail investor market alongside the defensive nature of the portfolio.

The price range was also at a slight discount to the already listed fast food company Collins Foods, which makes most of its money from KFC franchises in Queensland and NSW.

Collins Foods is trading at 13.2-times forecast profit.

Ironically, the last week also saw one of the better performed floats of the year hit the boards, with Brett Kelly's accounting firm Kelly + Partners enjoying a strong first few days on the ASX.

The company listed at a $1 issue price and popped to $1.36 on its first day, before closing on Friday at $1.42
direction: bearish for retail food stocks and private equity-backed floats

headline: Coal union auditor red-flags accounts
text:
The qualification of its latest financial accounts by new auditors has delivered the Queensland coal union to the creeping quagmire of community scepticism over the exuberance with which Australian officialdom spends other people's money.

The June 30, 2016, accounts for

two financial entities of the Queensland district of the CFMEU mining and energy division were published just before Christmas carrying distinctly different qualification by the union's auditors, Brisbane-based SRJWalkerWayland.

The auditor raised formal concern over credit card spending booked against business expenses in the union's main financial accounts and, separately, expressed discomfort over the accuracy of previous valuations of balance sheet reserves in what appears to be the coal union's property business.

In signing off on the union's core operating accounts on December 4, the auditor reported: "We were unable to obtain sufficient appropriate evidence to verify that the credit card expenses ($721,116) that have been included as business expenses in the union's annual financial accounts have been incurred solely for business purposes."

For the record, the same accounts announce that the ceiling on its Commonwealth Bank business cards and the auto pay facility is $750,000. That account is secured by the union's liquid assets. And, to be quite clear, there is liquidity enough to make any bank comfortable with that threshold. At June 30 the union had $13.4 million either in bank accounts or in short-term deposits.

On December 20 SRJWalker

Wayland signed off on the accounts of a lesser-known financial entity of the coal union. It has a very long name and a longer history. It is called the Construction, Forestry, Mining and Energy, Industrial Union of Employees, Queensland Mining, Energy and Ports District. It grew out of a north Queensland mining union that disappeared with the great consolidation of the 1990s and it now appears to be an entity owned in equal part by the construction and coal mining wings of the Queensland branch of the CFMEU. Among other things, it owns union property and in July SRJWalkerWayland won the mandate to audit the business.

The result is that a property portfolio previously valued at $8.36 million has been written down to $4.78 million, with the auditor expressing plain discomfort with the underpinnings of the 2015 valuation. But the concerns did not stop with property valuations past. The SRJWalkerWayland qualification reads:

"We were appointed as auditors of the Construction, Foresty, Mining and Energy, Industrial Union of Employees, Queensland, Mining, Energy and Ports District on July 8, 2016. We were unable to obtain sufficient appropriate evidence to verify the accuracy of the opening balances of the capital reserve ($200,750), mortuary benefit reserve ($2,571,231), revaluation reserves ($9,951,330) and property plant and equipment ($8,365,254).

On December 5, just 24 hours after the Queensland coal union received the first of its two qualified audits, state secretary Tim Whyte penned his name to numbers. But an otherwise pretty frank introduction to accounts that were published a few weeks later carried no acknowledgement of the auditor's qualification.

Whyte reported that the union had made a $2.7 million loss in financial year 2016. He explained that this loss reflected declining membership, lower interest rates, rising legal costs flowing from "the level of industrial activity in serving our membership", the cost of the Queensland branch's admirable black lung campaign and various unspecified legal settlements that totalled $525,000.

Coal union membership in Queensland slipped by 462 to 6369 through the year to June last year. Fee revenue fell by a proportionate $693,297 to $9,654,602. In total, the union's revenues fell by $1 million to $11.4 million in 2015-16 while expenses rose by $1.39 million to $15.2 million.

But, on some of the cost fronts at least, Whyte was unnecessarily remorseful. Mainstream legal expenses fell by $400,000 over 2015-16.

And, while the new war on black lung would seem to be at the heart of "campaign administration costs" that rose from $24,000 to $386,000 over the year, it represented only a third of the blowout in the union's "other expenses" number. It rose by $1 million to top out at $2.6 million.

Outside of the black lung campaign, the big contributors to the debilitating spending increases included a $211,947 write-off of an unspecified collection of loans and a near 70 per cent increase in spending on travel, accommodation and car hire. In 2014-15, union officials spent $455,000 on various forms of travel. That surged to $765,000.

As you might expect, SRJWalker

Wayland rejected our invitation to confirm whether or not this pocket of the union P&L is the father of December's qualification. Indeed, the coal union's newly-robust auditor did not respond to any of the questions we emailed them on Monday.

But the union's response to the auditor's December discomfort has been rapid. It has called a second set of independent accountants to run a forensic review of the credit card spending and has offered advice that qualification was triggered by a small number of credit card users that accounted for spending much, much less than the $721,116 cited by the auditor.

This urgency is appropriate on three levels, one obvious and two not so.

The yuletide kerfuffle over the way politicians have been spending our money stands as a firm reminder of a growing community intolerance of unearned and inappropriate indulgences.

The less obvious pressures, though, are likely the more important for the CFMEU leadership.

As wealthy and powerful as it is, the Queensland coal union is not the home of solidarity it once was. Times are seriously tough in the coal industry with workers currently swapping pay for job security and outsourcing is creeping through mines in Queensland and NSW.

The Queensland union's state leadership is viewed by some as excessively peripatetic and increasingly distant from the coal face. Those simmering tensions have been fanned over recent times by a succession of mismanaged disputes and negotiations, the most recent being a long, long strike at an Anglo American coal mine that ended with a picket line collapsing after the company won the right to make selected strikers redundant.

That an independent auditor has announced uncertainty over credit card spending could well amplify the distance between some of the union's local lodges and its executive.

And then, finally, there is the Fair Work Commission. Unions have to lodge their accounts with the FWC. Accounts that arrive with qualification would routinely earn particular review by the commission's compliance unit. Under certain circumstances that review could become a formal inquiry and the commission has considerable powers to gather evidence.

So far the qualified Queensland coal union accounts have not been lodged with the commission. Self-evidently, a rapid-fire, thorough and independent reassessment of the union's credit card spending could well mitigate the Fair Work risk.

The really odd thing about this credit card issue is that the auditor could be left unable to determine whether or not all of last year's spending was business related. You see the Queensland district of the coal union has a very stringent set of rules and requirements on using and accounting for credit card spending. It was more recently spelled out in the union's 2016 "Policies" document.

After noting all the usual stuff about getting and keeping valid tax receipts, the union offers very specific advice on the need to justify personal spending on cards. "Private usage of cards is only permitted in infrequent and exceptional circumstances and where personal expenditure is incurred in conjunction with union related expenditure e.g. room service or mini bar included in a hotel bill, the personal expenditure component is to be deducted from a claim sheet or deducted from wages within the normal billing period for the card. Personal use of the Corporate Card will be monitored by the district secretary/district president and office manager and personal use may be restricted or stopped if overuse applies."

Given the required rules of accountability were being lived by Queensland district officials, it is hard to see how an auditor might be doubtful of credit card spending in amounts substantial enough to warrant the damaging qualification that was painted to the union's latest accounts.
direction: bearish for the Queensland coal union and its associated property assets

headline: Deregulate if you really want to innovate
text:
Australia's workplace red tape is more likely to achieve mediocrity than cutting-edge change.

The surge of speeches and articles on the coming invasion of artificial intelligence and robotics has been matched by predictions that Australia is destined to be a tech laggard rather than a frontier.

If we are to avoid that fate, and capture the benefits of productivity-boosting technology and innovation, then we are going to have to get the regulatory settings right.

Productivity growth, or lack of it, explains the contrast between innovation and stagnation. Productivity also has a direct causal link to the greater spread of income and opportunity across society. Globally, we can see the difference between economies where firms and governments make full use of the latest technological and managerial innovations, and those that lag and are failing. The gap between the two is growing and the speed of change is accelerating.

The extent to which innovation is encouraged and embraced is a product of business and societal culture. Employees who acquire new jobs created by technology are engaged and empowered. The mining truck driver who now monitors production activities as a remote drone pilot, or the stevedore who now operates the port container terminal from a control room, can enthusiastically demonstrate the benefits for the enterprise and for themselves. Well-paid jobs in technologically advanced businesses are the product of those who invest, the ideas of innovators and the workplace culture that embraces them. Our national prosperity is dependent on the extent to which Australian businesses and their workforces successfully combine those elements.

Whilst not the drivers, governments and regulators play a critical role in encouraging best practice. Britain has embraced a landmark study on the productivity benefits of greater employee engagement. Its employment agency, ACAS runs courses in how to build greater employee engagement within enterprises. The recent Taylor review of modern work practices concluded that "the best way to achieve better work is not national regulation, but responsible corporate governance, good management and strong employment relations within the organisation".

In Australia, the Fair Work Ombudsman has online training courses for employees and employers. However, the overwhelming orientation of this material is compliance with our complex web of employment laws including minimum award and statutory entitlements, unfair dismissal, anti-discrimination, adverse action and anti-bullying rules.

Employers that have not managed to escape the Fair Work bargaining vortex must also contend with a maze of artificial technical requirements, pre-bargaining obligations, inconsistently applied approval rules, code compliance requirements and adversarial dynamics. The legal framework imposes a culture that discourages innovation and distracts management from addressing high-trust, productive, employee relations. A system that imposes such a complex and onerous compliance regime is destined to achieve mediocrity at best. Debates on the shape of regulation for new forms of work, such as work platforms in the gig economy, have begun. Jim Stanford, of the Australia Institute, and Andrew Stewart, of Flinders University, have even argued that regulators should be creative and ambitious to extend minimum employment obligations.

We can learn from contrasting regulatory approaches in the US. The app Rover matches would-be "pet sitters" with "pet owners". Nine thousand would-be sitters are registered in New York alone. Because historical regulations preclude anyone without a kennel licence from earning money to care for a pet, Rover has been warned that users of the platform are in violation of those rules and are liable for fines of $1000. In contrast, Colorado has addressed the issue by allowing people to care for up to three dogs without being subject to the regulations and licensing requirements that apply to kennels.

The benefits of innovation are likely to accrue to nimble societies that are prepared to rethink the purpose and impact of regulation and quickly recalibrate.

Victor Hugo famously said that it is possible to resist an invasion of armies, but not an invasion of ideas. The evidence is clear that ideas and innovations are coming our way in a veritable tsunami. Successful businesses are building the cultural elements to harness the potential. Those that do not are likely to be casualties. Equally, the regulatory mindset needs to change. If governments fail this task, so, too, will Australia.
direction: bearish for regulated sectors, bullish for tech innovators

headline: Coleman lauds liability and open data
text:
Liberal MP David Coleman, the chairman of the parliamentary committee examining the banks, says the government should reject objections by three of the big four banks to a new disclosure regime that would increase scrutiny of senior executive accountability.

During hearings last month in Canberra, Commonwealth Bank, National Australia Bank and Westpac objected to proposals to force banks to describe specific consequences for executives when misdemeanours occur in their divisions, while ANZ has agreed to the moves.

Writing in Monday's Australian Financial Review, Mr Coleman said the committee's recommendation "will place intense, ongoing pressure on senior executives to fix these issues, which is exactly what is required". The measures would "focus the minds of CEO-reporting executives very intently on avoiding licence condition breaches".

Ahead of his appearance at The Australian Financial Review Banking & Wealth Summit on Wednesday, Mr Coleman reiterated the committee's call to establish "a clear, permanent function" within the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission to provide "a constant and granular focus on competition" in the banking sector. "We want the ACCC to be required to establish a permanent standing force to investigate systemic competition issues in banking," he writes.

It is understood the committee's second report on the banks following the March hearings will be released in the next fortnight. Treasurer Scott Morrison is also expected to respond soon to the committee's recommendations.

The committee wants banks to open their unique customer data assets to customers to allow them to share that data with competitors to shop around for a better deal. The Productivity Commission delivered to the government last week its final report on data availability and use, which makes similar recommendations. Mr Coleman said it was "very important that banks do not control the process through which consumer data is opened up".

"The government can't allow the banks to control the open data rules - they are much too conflicted," he writes.
direction: bearish for the big four banks

headline: Technology won't make us redundant
text:
One of the nation's top bureaucrats has issued a call to arms on the opportunities thrown up by "unprecedented technological change" and urged citizens not to give in to "dystopian fear-mongering about the future".

Martin Parkinson, the former Treasury secretary who now heads the Department of Prime Minister and Cabinet, argues that done right, the technological revolution will drive the next wave of jobs, productivity growth and living standards.

Conceding that there will be losers - workers made "technologically redundant" or stuck in low-wage growth sectors - Dr Parkinson says that managing and harnessing the accelerating change is a fundamental challenge for leadership across government and the economy.

"Make no mistake: no fundamental change to the economy and society of the sort we envisage is easy or uncontested," Dr Parkinson said in a speech to the Sydney Institute yesterday.

However, there will be "massive opportunities" given that positive technology shocks lift national income, and tend to eliminate tasks but not whole jobs.

"So long as workers can adapt to the new technology, they will be better off," he said, while noting that there is no evidence that technology is reducing the number of available jobs or increasing casualisation.

Dr Parkinson pushes back against the notion that technology is a driver of growing inequality, but warned it may make things worse if it creates a divide between those who can and can't harness it.

"We're also yet to see if the mega tech firms like Amazon and Alibaba, Apple and Google, will become the 'robber barons' of the last century - although I'd like to think that modern regulatory institutions significantly reduce this risk.

"But again, I caution against dystopian predictions that technology will make us redundant or society more unequal or monopolistic."

Dr Parkinson said that over the next 25 years, technology may be the single biggest driver of jobs, productivity and living standards, and that to thrive every sector of the economy will need to stay vigilant.

"As each industry faces its own 'Kodak moment', none can rest on past success," he said.

"The same goes for government and the public service. Rather than seeking to slow the adoption of technological changes, we need to disperse it faster."

Dr Parkinson argues that governments have a major role in taking advantage of technology "with access to infrastructure like high-speed internet and data and cyber security capabilities".

On data, governments should be doing more to liberate the information it holds to spur productivity and economic growth.

"Governments hold voluminous amounts of data that we need to use better to support smarter and more targeted policy, services and regulations.

"Yet as long as citizens are more willing to share personal information with Facebook than with government, we have our work cut out to earn trust in how effectively we use the data we hold."

He concludes part of the challenge is ensuring people aren't left behind by technology, by creating an education system that "helps workers use technology rather than be replaced by it".

"Perhaps this means more continuous, life-long learning. Probably it means 'being more human', with more abstract, creative thinking and inter-personal contact, less knowledge and rote learning that robots can do.

"But it certainly means a high quality education system available to all."

He concludes by drawing a comparison to the distorted, fractious social media environment that has contributed to Trump and Brexit with newspapers in the US in the 1870s, which were "party political and full of rhetoric".

As technology cut the cost of printing, competition rose and newspapers became more responsive to what readers wanted rather than their owners.

Key pointsFormer Treasury secretary says change will drive the next wave of jobs.

Leadership faces a fundamental challenge to harness the revolution.
direction: bullish for technology sector

headline: Mitchell musters $70 million Kimberley cattle stations deal
text:
Media man Harold Mitchell has sold his burgeoning cattle station empire in Australia's Kimberley region in Western Australia for more than $70 million.

The sale, which includes APN Outdoor chairman Doug Flynn's stake as well, is understood to have been acquired by a wealthy Chinese businessman.

In 2015 Mr Mitchell appointed KPMG and ANZ to sell Yougawalla Pastoral Company, which includes three main cattle stations spanning more than 850,000 hectares and controls about 45,000 head of cattle.

Mr Mitchell declined to comment on the deal, which is believed to have already received approval from the Foreign Investment Review Board. The Pastoral Lands Board of Western Australia is still to approve the transaction.

Yougawalla Pastoral also leases the 161,000-hectare Louisa Downs, the 141,000ha Bohemia Downs, the 80,000ha Carranya Station and the 182,000ha Lamboo Station, but none of these properties are part of the deal with the foreign buyer.

Mr Mitchell's timing couldn't be better, buying when prices were in the doldrums and selling at what appears to be back at the top of the market.

He started buying during the financial crisis picking up the main Yougawalla station for about $10 million. In 2011 he snapped up the 274,000ha Bulka Station adjoining Yougawalla for about $9 million.

Then in 2013 when the live export cattle trade had been smashed and the drought and prices were squeezing the cattle industry, Mr Mitchell shelled out $4.3 million for another massive cattle station - the 177,100ha Margaret River Station with 6000 head of cattle.

Mr Mitchell, who played a low-key but important role in negotiations with Indonesia on the live export trade, decided to sell after splitting with his wife of 50 years.

But the timing is right with pastoral property values rising sharply in the last few years.
direction: bullish for agricultural and pastoral assets

headline: Innovation in leadership at law firms
text:
The nation's largest law firms have escalated the task of provoking change and embracing technology in a profession steeped in tradition to top-tier management — and not all are leaving it to the lawyers.

Among six new partners announced by Gilbert + Tobin this week are its chief operating officer, former McKinsey & Company principal Sam Nickless, who is among prominent personalities driving innovation across the profession.

"The importance of innovation means that the role of COO is more than just managing the 'back office' — it increasingly is client-facing and engaging with our legal teams on their services," Mr Nickless said.

"The evolution of legal services will include skills from a range of specialties — technology, data analytics, project management, process redesign — coming together with lawyers to deliver for clients."

Mr Nickless, whose journey to G+T came via stints leading strategy at National Australia Bank, Aristocrat Leisure and GPT Group, takes the title from July 1, despite having never practiced law.

"When I was at university I hoped I'd one day be a partner at a top law firm, I guess I got here the long way around," he said.

Among strategies spearheaded by Mr Nickless in almost two-and-a-half years at G+T is the firm's investment in startup LegalVision, which provides web-based fixed-fee services in a corporate structure.

But Mr Nickless warns the "popular notion is of robot lawyers answering complex questions with a nuanced answer" can lead to disappointment.

"The nearer-term impact will be the technology automating some of the grunt work tasks that are low value-added but need to get done. The long-term change will be profound," he said.

Rival national firm Clayton Utz similarly has a partner in charge of change, but theirs is a lawyer — and innovation is his sole focus.

Brad Vann was made director of innovation in July 2016 after more than three decades at the firm as an infrastructure projects lawyer. To colleagues, he was the clear choice: a "thinker" with a ferocious curiosity.

"I have a head full of ideas but for us now the key focus will be on execution," he said.

This week Mr Vann returned from Chicago, where he met with data science consultants lexpredict and e-discovery software company kCura. The firm has signed up for forensics software from the latter.

"Successful firms have always been innovative," he said.

"But there is such a dramatic change approaching that you have to formalise your engagement with it.

"I'm still a lawyer. You're trying to apply everything you've read into your day-to-day practice."

Critical to its strategy is engaging staff at all levels and across all services to contribute to ideas generation and testing; a common theme across firms.

MinterEllison was among the first to move a partner into the innovation space full time, elevating employment expert Andrew Cunningham to the role of innovation and networks leader in November 2014.

The firm is upsetting the industry apple cart in other ways as it moves into big four consulting territory under the unorthodox leadership of former accountant and PwC chief Tony Harrington.

Mr Harrington said Mr Cunningham was doing "some wonderful things" in the innovation space. Last year it launched its contract lawyer service MinterEllison Flex and a taskflow management system for clients.

Other firms have elevated the role to executive level, handed to non-lawyers with business backgrounds.

King & Wood Mallesons executive director of innovation Michelle Mahoney has driven the firm's strategy for two years, including securing the first Australian licence to use the AI tool Kira.

"The next big thing, I think, will be about big data and using those insights to really change the game," she said.

"We strongly encourage ideas from across the firm and the innovation group helps to bring those ideas to life."

Herbert Smith Freehills chief operating officer Nicole Bamforth, who previously spent more than a decade at global management consulting firm AT Kearney, said the firm had deliberately decided against appointing a director of innovation.

"We wanted it to be everyone's responsibility," she said.

"We really want it to be a fundamental part of how we think, how we work, how we do things."

The innovation task falls within Ms Bamforth's domain and the firm has hired two innovation facilitators in the past six months — based in the UK and Australia — to encourage collaboration and ideas testing.

Almost 1500 staff used a Vision Lab tool to submit 419 ideas and cast 57,000 votes last year.

"All the research tells us firms that are more innovative are more successful. They're more profitable, people are more engaged.

"For us it's not just about playing with technology, it is about having that culture and combining that with the technology."

Baker & McKenzie partner Astrid Raetze, who is on the global firm's innovation committee, said being a good lawyer was "no longer enough".

"Digitisation, artificial intelligence and machine learning are all very real and the legal industry is right in the middle of the changes they will bring.

"These are uncertain times for our global clients, in an increasingly complex world, in an industry facing disruption. So there's an opportunity — to either own the space or fall victim to it."
direction: supportive for law firm equities and legal tech

headline: Barana books landmark Readers Digest Building
text:
Greg Shand's Barana Group has bought the landmark Reader's Digest Building in Sydney's Surry Hills for $52.673 million.

On a prominent island site, the building at 26-32 Waterloo Road will soon be fully occupied by Woolworths' subsidiary Endeavour Drinks Group on an eight-year lease.

Barana bought the property from Argus Property Partners which had acquired it in 2014 for $30 million. The sale price reflects a yield of below 6 per cent and will deliver a strong return for Argus investors.

"This is an astute purchase for Barana given the building's location, strong tenancy profile and future potential," said CBRE's Scott Gray-Spencer, who brokered the deal.

"It also represents a great recycling of capital by Argus, who have added considerable value to the property by extending Endeavour Drinks Group's lease over the entire building."

The four-storey building has a total lettable area of 7055 square metres. The land area is 3965sq m, encompassing the entire block bounded by Cooper Street, Waterloo Street, Adelaide Street and Adelaide Place.

"There has been an increasing trend for renovation and residential conversions in Surry Hills over the past decade, with the market having also benefited from tremendous growth in office rents over the past three years," Mr Gray-Spencer said.

"... The property's character feel and its location close to the new light rail station on Devonshire Street should mean that the trend in office rent growth for this property will continue."

The property will be added to Barana's $450 million portfolio of offices, hotels, retail assets and apartment developments in Australia.

Among them is the Novotel Hotel at St Kilda beach in Melbourne, where Barana has explored the property's potential for residential development.

The Barana portfolio includes commercial property in London and Switzerland. Its main activities are syndication, asset management and property development.

The Surry Hills deal is the first in what is expected to be a flurry of deals in Sydney in coming weeks, after more than $1 billion of stock poured into the market over the past month.

This week alone, two more towers hit the market. One is the prime positioned 299 Elizabeth Street on the edge of Hyde Park, which was acquired just three years ago by a Chinese group as a potential residential conversion play.

The other is 160 Sussex Street, a renovated 15-storey B-grade commercial tower held by Burcher Property Group.
direction: bullish for Barana Group and the commercial property sector

headline: What moved the market
text:
Copper market abuse

Red Kite Management, the world's largest metals hedge fund, is suing British bank Barclays for alleged market abuse in the copper market that it claims cost the firm at least $US850 million between 2010 and 2013. Red Kite alleges that Barclays allowed staff to share confidential information about its positions with the bank's proprietary traders on the floor of the London Metal Exchange.

The greenback

The US dollar rose on Friday, bolstered by increased optimism about the prospects for US tax reform. President Donald Trump's drive to overhaul the tax code cleared a critical hurdle on Friday when the Senate approved a budget blueprint for the 2018 fiscal year that will pave the way for Republicans to pursue a tax-cut package without Democratic support.

Japan elections

The US dollar rose 0.6 per cent on the day to 113.2 yen, having risen to as high as 113.315 yen, its strongest level since October 6 ahead of elections in Japan on Sunday. A typhoon is expected to bring more rain to an already sodden Japan, potentially affecting election turnout. Polls show the ruling Liberal Democratic Party and its coalition partner are on track to take about 300 of the 465 seats up for grabs.

Crown

Crown shares dropped 5.7 per cent over the week. Whistleblower allegations that Crown casino rigged pokie machines and let gamblers avoid money-laundering laws in Melbourne are being "evaluated" by the Australian Federal Police. The AFP confirmed on Friday that independent MP Andrew Wilkie had referred the case he outlined using parliamentary privilege earlier this week.
direction: Crown shares likely to face further downward pressure

headline: Fears report will stymie class action reform
text:
Leading class action lawyers are looking to the federal government to rescue hopes for meaningful reform after giving the thumbs down to a "pro-claims" Victorian report.

They say proposals by the Victorian Law Reform Commission to allow contingency fees would only accelerate the rapid growth in claims, and are dismayed at the commission's "do-nothing" stance on issues such as litigation funding.

The VLRC said litigation funding should be left to the federal government due to a parallel inquiry by the Australian Law Reform Commission (ALRC).
