In a discussion paper released two weeks ago, the ALRC also argued law firms should be allowed to compete with funders via contingency fees, but said funders should be licensed.

Federal Attorney-General Christian Porter has signalled his support for licensing litigation funders. He noted the consensus on the issue, saying "most people view it as a sensible approach".

There has been a tenfold increase in shareholder class actions payouts over the past decade (from $127 million in 2007 to $1.5 billion in 2017), and the cost of directors' and officers' insurance has risen by as much as 400 per cent in the past year.

The ALRC report noted that since 2002, 66 shareholder class actions - 20 in the past two years - have been filed in the Federal Court. All have settled before trial, with an average payout of $50 million. It costs around $10 million to bring a claim and a similar sum to defend one.

Plaintiff law firm Slater and Gordon said the VLRC report was "a strong endorsement of the importance of class actions" with "pragmatic suggestions that will strengthen the regime".

However Stuart Clark, of Clayton Utz, a former president of the Law Council of Australia, said it was "very disappointing". "This was an opportunity to craft a sensible regulatory regime that could have been pressed federally by the Victorian government," Mr Clark said.

"We must now hope that the ALRC takes up the challenge and recommends that the federal government acts and acts quickly."

Litigation funder IMF Bentham also counselled against Victoria going it alone: "Without a national approach, greater complexity will arise with respect to multiplicity of and competing class actions."

Mr Clark said the Victorian report's recommendation that the regulation of litigation funders be referred to the Council of Australian Governments (COAG) was "perhaps the worst possible outcome".

"COAG has all too often been the graveyard of good proposals for reform," Mr Clark said. The VLRC proposals to place limits on the availability of adverse costs orders and remove the power to order security for costs in non-funded matters would "lead to significant damage to Australian business".

"There won't be a need for funders. There will be plenty of firms happy to run a 'test case' or 'public interest matter' on a no-win no-fee basis with handsome hourly rates and an uplift - and no downside if they lose a few."

Jason Betts, a Herbert Smith Freehills partner who is helping AMP to defend five class actions, said significant sections of the VLRC report were "pro-class actions".

"There seems to be a growing assumption that we need more class actions in this country. I can't see the basis for this."

With the two law reform commissions looking at different issues, he said any "cherry-picking" would create knock-on problems.

"Introducing contingency fees for example could aggravate copycat claims ... we need holistic reforms."

Mr Betts said the purpose of a class action was to "have a single finding on common questions - not multiple class action promoters jostling for poll position on who gets the right to sue".

John Emmerig, a partner at Jones Day who is on several class actions committees, said contingency fees and the "world's most lenient" class action procedures would be "a toxic combination for the legal system".

While he laments the absence of shareholder class action rulings in Australian courts, Mr Emmerig warns that a United States Supreme Court ruling in the late 1980s made it even easier to prove shareholder claims.

"When added to the existing US contingency fee system, it led to an explosion of claims. The US legal system is still dealing with the aftermath, despite attempts at reforms which have had limited success."

Mr Emmerig said "genuine claims should be permissible".

"It's the speculative and ill-founded claims that nevertheless harm the target company simply because they are threatened or launched when they shouldn't.

"That is the problem that needs to be expunged from the system."

The Victorian Attorney-General, Martin Pakula, said he was considering the commission's report and how it might interact with the ALRC inquiry. Submissions to the ALRC inquiry close on July 30, with the report due on December 21.
direction: bearish for listed companies and their insurers

headline: THE ESSENTIAL MRS MERKEL
text:
In the 2017 election campaign, everything is focused on Angela Merkel. You can't have a more successful embodiment of power than that. And that really is, in a way, the issue, writes Laura Tingle.

The political insider strides across his Berlin office to a pile of newspapers.

"Actually, you have come on the perfect day for me to point out what the problem is," he says, brandishing one of Germany's major newspapers.

The paper features a striking black and white portrait on its front page of Heiner Geissler, a former secretary-general of the Christian Democratic Union, the party of German Chancellor Angela Merkel, who died last week.

Geissler, our insider observes, was the man who got Helmut Kohl into Merkel's job in 1982 and shaped his chancellorship until the two fell out in 1989. He was the one who ensured the Chancellor never lost touch with the party's base or core values.

"He was the embodiment of the CDU. He was very mindful of the needs of the party, versus the needs of the government," he says.

"He was a centre of vitality for Kohl in forcing him to think differently about issues."

By contrast, he says, Merkel "dominates unlike anyone since Konrad Adenauer in the 1950s and 1960s".

The CDU, as a result, is sometimes called these days the "club to elect the Chancellor".

"I recall five or six years ago a CDU official saying "we can never bring down a Chancellor on a matter of content as long as she delivers a majority. So everyone is focused on her. You can't have a more successful embodiment of power than that."

And that really is, in a way, the issue of this German election campaign.

Yes, there is the collapse of the first serious competitive challenge to Merkel's position as Chancellor by the former president of the European Parliament, Martin Schulz, who heads the Social Democrats (SPD). The other major party at the centre of German politics is now polling just 20 per cent of the vote.

Yes, there is the rise of the anti-immigration (and anti-Europe) party the Alternative for Deutschland (AfD) which has seen its poll standing rise to 12 per cent last week, meaning it now has the third largest following of any party in Germany and is assured of a place in the Bundestag - the first far-right party in 70 years to achieve this.

But the success of the CDU - and the shape of the government it will be able to form after Sunday's election - rests very much on which of two different views of Merkel prevail.

The Chancellor of the last 12 years is seen outside Germany as the new "Leader of the Free World". Other Western countries are now looking to Germany, and Merkel, to take more of a leadership role at their head in international affairs, and in shaping the contentious debate about Europe itself. (Even countries who don't like the idea of German leadership - like Poland and Hungary - naturally assume it will come from the continent's biggest economy.)

In terms of the election campaign, the CDU's pitch is all about Merkel representing security - both security in a national security sense, and in the sense of security of German society.

"For a Germany in which we live well and happily" is the party's splendidly anodyne slogan.

(The SPD's slogan, on the other hand, has a certain ring to it in Australia: "Es ist Zeit" - "It is time" - though based on the polls it apparently isn't)

Yet the alternative domestic view is of Merkel as the ultimate representative of a pragmatic centre that leads from behind and refuses to discuss the issues that matter to voters, whether they be the contentious issue of immigration or issues like housing and childcare.

Merkel and the CDU have recovered polling figures from a slump after her 2015 decision to allow 1 million asylum seekers into the country. But the issue has not gone away in voters' minds.

The immediate question is whether a dangerous complacency - built on the expectation that Merkel is going to win easily - and/or voter resentment at the failure at the political centre to talk about issues that matter, will splinter German politics.

The polls suggest that whatever the result is on Sunday, German politics will have to reset after the election to address the disgruntlement voters are clearly feeling, and to take into account the very different make-up of the parliament

There is little enthusiasm for another :grand coalition" between the CDU and the SPD. But, polling at around 37 per cent of the vote, Merkel will need at least two minor parties to form government, with the expectation that this would the free-market Liberals on the right and the Greens on the left.

And the observation is widely made that, whatever the AfD's vote is, its presence has already changed the nature of the German political debate: the refusal of the major parties to talk about immigration, for example, is largely seen as trying to deprive the AfD of oxygen.

Much of the government's re-election strategy rests in Merkel's political appeal as a leader who will stand up to the rough men of the world in difficult times: Donald Trump in the United States, Vladimir Putin in Russia and Recep Tayyip Erdogan in Turkey. But particularly Erdogan.

Germany led a European deal with Erdogan which essentially pays Turkey to keep millions of refugees there, rather than let them move into Europe, and there is a smouldering resentment in Germany about being held over a barrel.

Erdogan has also bought into the German election, telling the more than 1 million Turks who have lived in Germany since the 1950s and 1960s to boycott the major political parties.

Psychologist Stephan Grünewald's market research institute, the Reingold Institute in Cologne, conducted focus groups on the German electorate and talked about his findings with the German magazine Der Spiegel this month.

"In the in-depth interviews, all people wanted to talk about was the refugee crisis, refugee crisis, refugee crisis," he said. "Despite being so elegantly left out of the campaign, it is still a sore spot that hasn't been treated by politicians.

"The crisis two years ago plunged voters into a dilemma for which they still haven't found a clear response. Do I open the door, or do I close it? On one hand, they want to be part of the welcoming culture, but they are also afraid of being overwhelmed by foreigners and of no longer being able to recognise their own country. As a result, they want policymakers to develop a plan, to establish a compromise position. But they haven't, and now voters feel abandoned.

" Voters are disoriented, full of uncertainties. They describe Germany either as an ailing, run-down country or as a secure island of affluence in a sea of risk. It's all very fragile and leads to emotional outbursts. I have never before seen so much anger and hatred among test subjects."

But he did not believe this would lead to a huge increase in political radicalisation.

"Not yet, because in reaction to the perceived hardening of the fronts, voters are also taking a step back. They argue that we cannot afford to slip into polarisation because we are surrounded by three brutes: Trump, Erdogan and Putin. The anger is being expressed in shadowy digital worlds, but in the analog world, they keep a tighter rein on themselves.

"She is seen as the person who can tame the brutes. The Chancellor is seen as the only one we can depend on, so we have to have a good relationship to her."

A CDU strategist agrees. Campaigning 101, he says, means you talk about your strong suit, rather than the vulnerabilities. In this case, that means talking about the economy, about Merkel herself and Trump, Putin and Erdogan as "showcases for what comes next and who will defend the Western narrative".

"You reframe the debate: instead of a refugee crisis, you offer stability and security. Yes, 2015 was a one-off incident, a humanitarian imperative, but now we have procedures and mechanisms in place so that it won't happen again."

Yet the whole pitch about Merkel is about a leader who, ironically, has few strong views of her own.

A large part of the dynamic in this election campaign flows from the way she has stolen the policies of her competitors to the left to dominate the centre of politics.

She used the Fukushima disaster, for example, to announce an about turn on nuclear power and in favour of renewables.

Just a few months ago she suddenly announced that there should be a free parliamentary vote on same-sex marriage - which was looming as an issue the SPD and others would campaign on - and brought the debate on within a week (but voted against it herself).

With the SPD - which has been in a "Grand Coalition" government with Merkel's CDU and sister Bavarian party the CSU - floundering to find a point of policy difference, Merkel's enlargement of the centre has been an act of political genius and the beginning of a major problem.

It has led to an unprecedented fracturing of the vote among minor parties in Germany.

"The genius of Merkel is that she has no ideological convictions," says our newspaper-flourishing insider.

"She's a problem solver. She doesn't see things as political problems but as technical or diplomatic problems."

But this only made her more vulnerable after one million people crossed the borders into Germany in 2015. It wasn't just about xenophobia, he says, it was a "complete bankruptcy of state power".

Stephen Grünewald says voters believe she will win by a wide margin, "but it is still little more than a half-hearted expression of loyalty".

"The tried-and-true will once again be put on probation."

Sylke Tempel, from the German Council on Foreign Relations, also talks about the Merkel pragmatism.

The CDU, she says, was never the tough neo-liberal party equivalent of conservative parties in other parts of the world so it has been easier for Merkel to push it to the left.

"She is the master of the understatement," Dr Tempel says. "So you have to listen closely to see what she really wants."

There was perhaps no better example of that than her comments in May after the G7 and NATO summits and Trump's signals of a US withdrawal from the affairs of the world.

"Up to a certain extent," she told a CDU gathering in a beer tent in Munich, "we are now on our own."

The Chancellor's political style is definitely "less is more". She doesn't talk all the time. She doesn't make grand or aggressive statements. She often waits until everyone else has had their say before intervening.

She is one of just two G20 leaders who doesn't have a twitter account.

Merkel faces the challenges of shaping European policy and dealing with the three "brutes" in a term in which the election result seems almost certain to involve a more difficult political juggling act at home, and at a time when most people expect this will be her last term. Once again, her position is so dominant within her own party that there is no clear successor, making the jostling for position another major distraction.

Defence Minister Ursula von der Leyen is mostly mentioned as a successor, but not with great enthusiasm. Some critics say she has been "struggling" in the job. Interior Minister Thomas de Maizière is another option, but is seen more as a bureaucrat.

Josef Janning from the European Council on Foreign Relations reflects the way Germans speak of Merkel when he asserts that the Chancellor "will make it very clear, in order to not be a lame duck, that she will serve a full term".

Merkel's authority is such that there is a sense that if she says something like this, it will be so.
direction: uncertainty for German equities and European markets

headline: Two deals underpin Perth's recovery
text:
Perth's office leasing is on the mend, with AFD Power Engineering pre-leasing the whole of level 16 at 200 St Georges Terrace in the CBD.

The company signed a three-year lease for the 1084sq m space to accommodate their operations, ahead of the departure of the incumbent lessee, QBE. QBE gave up that level to renew an "extend and blend" lease at the same building to over 3500 square metres. The A-grade lease was struck at gross annual rent of about $625 a square metre with a market level incentive.

CBRE director Lachlan Lewis negotiated the lease.

Two in Perth

Also in Perth, architectural facade supply and installation company ABS Façade has relocated to an 947sq m office/warehouse with a yard in Herdsman Business Park on a two-year lease.

Savills Lachlan Webster negotiated the two-year lease with options at 34 Walters Drive, Osborne Park, at a gross annual rent of $105 a square metre for a private landlord.

"After more than 12 months on the market, the office is now leased for the full asking price, showing improving signs in Perth's industrial market, which is firmly in recovery mode," Mr Webster said.

True blue deal

True Blue Timber has secured a five-year lease over a 2580sq m industrial property at 9 Walker Place, Wetherill Park in Sydney's west, from landlord Fairfield City Council.

The company has agreed to a gross annual rent $340,000.

LJ Hooker's Marcel Elias acted for the council.

Wes-side story

Also in Sydney's west, WesTrucks has leased a 4205sq m industrial facility at 324 Woodpark Road from Goodman Group, paying a gross annual rent of $131 a square metre over five years.

Knight Frank's John Swanson brokered the deal.

CBD core

Financial services business Cuscal has taken out a four-year lease for a 516sq m whole office floor at Level 1, 89 York in the Sydney CBD, for four years.

The business will pay a gross annual rent of about $800 to $900 a square metre for the property, which it will use for its business.

Pymble package

In Sydney's north, Shirley Consulting Engineers will move into a newly leased 368sq m commercial space on the ground floor of 19-23 Bridge Street, Pymble, after agreeing to a four-year lease.

JLL's Francesco Princi negotiated the four-year lease at a gross annual rent of $345 a square metre.

Power play

Romak Hardware Distributors will move into a newly leased 3041sq m two-level office/warehouse at Goodman Group's Power Park in Dandenong in Melbourne.

Romak struck a five-year deal at a net annual rent of $90 a square metre for 2/24 Columbia Court.

The property has hardstand and parking.

Savills' Kosta Filinis and Alexander Andrews brokered the deal.

Footscray forward

Government-funded training provider Odyssey House Victoria has agreed terms on a five-year deal with options over a a 788sq m office at Level 1, 202-208 Nicholson Street in Melbourne's Footscray.

Fitzroys's facilitated the struck at an annual rent of $200,000 per annum.

Scoresby scores

Pellicano has signed up new tenant Solberg International on a 10-year lease over the 570sq m office/warehouse at 42 Jellico Drive, Scoresby in Melbourne.

The property comprises a 311sq m clear span warehouse and 259sq m of flexible office/showroom space, with nine car spaces.

Colliers International's Sam Hibbins closed the deal on the unusually long lease at a gross annual rent of $131 square metre.

Popular CBD

Market and user experience research expert GfK Australia has agreed terms over a 117sq m office at part of Level 6, 50 Queen Street in the Melbourne CBD.

The business relocated from East Melbourne and will pay a gross annual rent of $61,308 over five years.

JLL's Alexandra Harper and Edward Robinson marketed the property for a private owner.

GfK was represented by CBRE's Jon Barkla.

Melegant move

Property developer and investor Melegant Group has struck a three-year lease over 280sq m of office space at part of Level 3, 990 Whitehorse Road, Box Hill in Melbourne.

The deal at the former ATO building will cost the company a net annual rent of $98,250.

JLL's Tim Sugar negotiated the deal for landlord Trendwell Investments Pty Ltd.

Dental deal

A Melbourne CBD dentist has opened a second practice at the mixed-use project at 804 Heidelberg Road in Alphington in Melbourne after signing a five-year lease with options at a net annual rent of $42,500.

The 127sq m ground-floor commercial property, unit 3, is at the base of a 14-unit apartment building, Editor Apartments.

Gray Johnson's Rory White has negotiated a three-month rent-free period for the new tenant.

Submissions to stan@fairfaxmedia.com.au
direction: supportive for Perth and Sydney commercial property sectors

headline: Kay & Burton director spends $8.66m on office site
text:
Veteran Toorak estate agent Ross Savas has emerged as the buyer of a two-storey office building in Richmond on the city fringe which sold under the hammer last month for $8.66 million.

Mr Savas, joint managing director at Kay & Burton alongside Mike Gibson, has sold some of Melbourne's most expensive homes for high-profile clients like Shannon Bennett, Paul Little and John Gandel in a career spanning more than 23 years.

A caveat placed over the title shows he was the winning bidder for 11-15 Albert Street, which stands on a 750 square metre corner site just two kilometres from the Melbourne central business district.

The deal was struck on a yield of just 1.7 per cent, reflecting the commercial development potential on the site. Richmond and other inner-city suburbs such as Cremorne have become office development hotspots as vacancies tighten in the CBD.

The price paid by Mr Savas - who could not be reached for comment - smashed the reserve by $700,000, delivering a handsome payday to local owners Irving Birch, Marian Goldberg and Sandra Reisner. The property last sold for $470,000 in 1985.

According to the selling agents, Teska Carson's Matthew Feld and Michael Ludski, the property attracted a high level of interest across a broad range of potential buyers and was purchased by a local developer.

"The property had something for everyone including a solid fully leased building on a very good site in arguably the most coveted city fringe market," Mr Feld said.

"It was an attractive investment as is, but the generous land area, commercial zoning and virtually three-street access provided significant development potential too."

The site is home to a solid brick commercial building of 1132 square metres which is split into three tenancies currently let on short-term leases to separate tenants at a combined $146,676 per annum net.

It offers 31 metres frontage to Albert Street, as well as frontages to Sanders Place and a rear laneway, facilitating any future development.

Mr Ludski said competition between four bidders had resulted in a very strong land rate of $11.500 a sq m.

"These sort of properties so close to the CBD are becoming increasingly hard to find especially in the inner-eastern Richmond/Cremorne area and especially those that offer existing income along with excellent development upside," he said.
direction: bullish for commercial property and developers

headline: You can't build trust by talking about job creation
text:
Until the next election, we are destined to hear a lot more about "job creation". This is a shame because the narrative of job creation is deeply flawed.

Sound economic policy or business investment can, and often does, lead to employment and wages growth. But the blanket equation of increased retained earnings equalling immediate job creation is an oversimplification.

Moreover, it is clearly not working. Trust in business is at a dramatic and historic low. Instead of merely swimming against the tide, corporates must sidestep the job creationists and respond to the facts before them.

The first promise of capitalism is not employment but liberty. Liberty is the glue that has bound capitalist liberal democracies so well over the post-war period and kept at bay the class-based struggles that bedevilled so many before it. Consciously or otherwise, job creationists are undermining the sense of liberty that individuals have within their economic lives. They have taken what was a means of national progress and turned it into an end, and they are doing it at precisely the wrong time, with the public's level of trust in the system at a low point.

Of course removing the term "job creation" from the corporate-public relations lexicon will leave a lot of blank space. So what should fill the space and become the dominant discourse? Aspiration and co-operation are the two things that business needs most from individuals and society, and what individuals and society most need from it. We need to position ourselves as enablers of individual aspiration by declaring our purpose and inviting the willing and able to help us achieve it.

While we may assume that all businesses operate for a purpose, we are yet to find any whose genuine purpose is the creation of jobs.

Research by EY's Beacon Institute revealed a big trend that proposes "a human-centred, socially engaged conception of purpose that seeks to create value for a broad set of stakeholders". The research revealed businesses that flourish in today's turbulent global economy are those that embrace a humanistic, multi-stakeholder purpose. They fully align all that they do to pursue their purpose.

The opportunity to declare a purpose has, for the most part, been mislaid by business. Corporations have learnt to fear being penalised for their failure to appease. The resulting overuse of communication specialists has led to a deluge of shallow, often competing aims.

The public is entitled to receive information that is factual, transparent, and can be readily verified.

Businesses should be explicit in their communication. Australian workplaces are stacked with smart, highly educated, independent minds who can provide this clarity. Remedying the trust deficit involves establishing your business' clear adherence to the rule of law rather than obscuring your purpose in the hope that you'll avoid scrutiny.

Robust leadership is essential here. As the Edelman Trust Barometer has clearly pointed out, when the public is disinclined to trust a CEO it harms the standing of that business. We know that the community adjudicates a leader's characteristics as representative of an organisation's. The public's trust in a particular business is, therefore, predicated on the trust status of the leader.

Leadership entails being responsive to public opinion and not fearful of it. Trusted leaders ensure the alignment of purpose throughout the business by setting strategy and objectives for the organisation to adhere to. Finally, leadership requires the courage to admit failure and face the consequences of errors made.

The public are not looking for an excuse to tear down private enterprise, they are looking for an excuse to believe in it again. Dispensing with the rhetoric of job creationism and focusing on purpose, authenticity and leadership is one path by which a redefining of the rights and obligations of business can be achieved.

Adam Carrel is a partner and Trent van Veen is presiding partner at EY.
direction: bearish for corporate equities and business investment

headline: Livingstone defends move to promote Comyn to CEO
text:
Commonwealth Bank promoted the head of the scandal-ridden retail bank to chief executive because no external candidate would have been immune from a regulatory scandal, chairman Catherine Livingstone says.

At the royal commission hearing, counsel assisting Rowena Orr, QC, challenged the CBA board's decision to promote Matt Comyn to the chief executive position, given he was overseeing the retail division at a time it was hit by the money-laundering scandal, misselling of consumer credit insurance and irresponsible mortgage lending.

Ms Orr asked: "I want to ask you about the sort of message that you think it sent to others within CBA and to the broader community to promote the person who was in charge of the division in which all of those issues occurred?"

After Ms Orr repeated her question twice, Ms Livingstone admitted the board considered "for weeks" the message of promoting Mr Comyn would send to the wider community. "The easy answer for us would have been to appoint an external person," she said. "To find an external person globally at that level who has not been involved in some regulatory event is almost impossible.

"And I don't mean that as a joke. So the message that it actually sent inside the organisation is one that these issues are unacceptable and we're determined to fix them. That was the message it sent."

She said Mr Comyn's appointment was met positively both internally and externally. "The internal response was overwhelmingly positive because of the respect the organisation has for Matt [Comyn], his transparency, his personal ethics and his character, and his ability to admit mistakes.

"Externally, if I speak to investors, if I speak to people who have seen Matt now since he has become CEO, again, there is almost universal praise for Matt."

In January, CBA named Mr Comyn as its new chief executive, replacing Ian Narev effective April this year.

It was a surprise appointment because many had expected Ms Livingstone to look for external candidates to replace Mr Narev.

In contrast, AMP brought in an external candidate - Credit Suisse's Francesco De Ferrari - to lead the company after CEO Craig Meller and chairman Catherine Brenner resigned following the revelations from the royal commission.

Ms Livingstone said Mr Comyn took responsibility for what had happened in the bank's retail division.

"The first thing he did was apologise. Apologised to me, apologised to the board for what had happened, and his failings in that," she said.
direction: downward pressure on CBA shares

headline: ANZ to face grilling on NT overdrafts
text:
ANZ will be questioned about the formal and informal overdrafts it issued to Indigenous customers this week, as the Hayne royal commission begins its examination of banks' and insurers' interactions with Indigenous people.

In her opening submission on Monday, counsel assisting the royal commission, Rowena Orr, QC, said one area of focus will be the high dishonour and overdraft fees banks charged.

The case study will focus on Groote Island in the Northern Territory where its residents commonly use ANZ's Access Basic account - a fee-free transaction account for people on certain government benefits.

Ms Orr said ANZ offered two formal overdraft facilities - ANZ Assured and ANZ Personal Overdrafts - as well as informal overdrafts across a variety of retail transaction accounts.

Informal overdrafts allow customers to go into a negative balance to meet direct debit and payment needs, but it may come with a fee or interest charge.

ANZ's Queensland and Northern Territory retail branch general manager Tony Tapsall will give evidence.

Ms Orr said the royal commission will not be considering the issue of consumer leases because it falls outside the scope of the terms of reference.

As flagged by The Australian Financial Review, the commission will also focus on funeral insurers, some of their "predatory sales practices" and the provision of "inappropriate policies" to Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islanders, Ms Orr said.

One of the case studies will be the Aboriginal Community Benefit Fund, which now operates under the banners of ACBF Funeral Plans and the Aboriginal Community Funeral Plan.

Indigenous customer Tracey Walsh will appear as a witness to give evidence about her dealings with ACBF before and following signing up to its funeral insurance.

One of ACBF's directors, Bryn Jones, will also give evidence.

Another case study will be Select AFSL, which trades under the name Let's Insure. Let's Insure's products are issued by St Andrew's Life Insurance, which is part of the Bank of Queensland.

Ms Orr said the commission will hear evidence from a customer who will share her experience of being sold funeral insurance policies which "she did not understand and could not afford".

She said ASIC's research shows that Aboriginal and Torres Strait Island customers of funeral insurers are much younger, with 50 per cent being under the age of 20, and a higher proportion of customers have had policies cancelled for not paying the premium, compared with non-Indigenous customers.

Ms Orr said Aboriginal and Torres Strait Islanders are twice as likely to be financially excluded than the rest of Australia's population.

Holding a transaction account, a credit card and general insurance are often used as three indicators of financial exclusion.

She said common obstacles to accessing affordable financial products included language barriers, remoteness, financial literacy, lack of identity documents and different family structures.

Monday's royal commission hearing in Darwin's Supreme Court went largely uninterrupted by onlookers and politicians.

This was in stark contrast to the hearing in Brisbane where Queensland MP Bob Katter turned up with a group of farmers to hold press conferences and demand immediate answers from Commissioner Kenneth Hayne.
direction: bearish for ANZ shares

headline: Improving housing affordability levels 'a win, but it's not success'
text:
Sydney homeowners pay more than half of their earnings to meet mortgage payments, even after falling prices inthe NSW capital triggered an improvement in affordability in the first quarter.

Mortgage repayments in Sydney slipped to 56.5 per cent of earnings in the three months to March, the third quarter of decline since June's peak of59.7 per cent, the Housing Industry Association's latest Affordability Report shows.

The report, which shows Melbourne mortgage repayments held near-steady at 48.1 per cent of earnings and Hobart worsened to 31 per cent from 30 per cent, shows how a number of different factors - such as housing construction, population change and economic activity - influence housing affordability across Australia's regional markets. But it also lays bare the entrenched severity of housing costs in the country's two largest cities that not even a short-term improvement can resolve.

"It's a win, but it's not success," said HIA principal economist Tim Reardon. "Sydney and Melbourne are still significantly less affordable than over the last 20 years."

Sydney dwelling values fell 1.7 per cent in the March quarter, CoreLogic figures show. The higher the score on the index, which combines dwelling prices, mortgage interest rates and wage developments in a single measure, the more affordable the housing in a city. Sydney and Melbourne's latest readings sit below their 20-year averages, along with Hobart, where prices are surging ahead of incomes.

Population growth and employment growth were driving dwelling prices higher in Melbourne and holding back any significant improvement in affordability, Mr Reardon said.

"That is driven by very large house price growth, which has continued out of cycle to all other jurisdictions, due to strong employment and strong population growth," he said.

Every other city was below its two-decade average and the reasons, of course, differ. In Brisbane, where mortgage repayments average 32.5 per cent of earnings, the slew of new apartments reaching completion and lowering prices is improving the overall affordability picture.

Perth takes the accolade of the country's most affordable city - with the highest index reading - because its own housing affordability was hammered during the heady years of the resources boom. Mortgage repayments account for just 27.3 per cent of earnings on average.

Adelaide's weak population growth - and net loss of young people interstate - is keeping housing demand at a level that the construction of new dwellings can match. Mortgage repayments in the SA capital were little changed in March from the previous quarter at 32.5 per cent, the HIA said.

Canberra also remains more affordable than over its 20-year average, and with mortgage repayments holding steady at 34.3 per cent, it reflected the country as a whole, Mr Reardon said.

"There's an increasing density of housing which is being absorbed through strong population growth," he said.

The lobby group for volume house builders said the figures showed that land and construction costs were still too high and planning processes too slow, and policy reforms were needed to speed up new dwelling creation.
direction: bearish for Sydney/Melbourne residential property and REITs

headline: Economists cast doubt over RBA's 'utopia'
text:
If the RBA's updated forecasts for inflation prove correct, this will be the fifth straight year consumer price growth has come in below the bottom of the bank's target range.

It will also be the third straight year for the bank's preferred underlying inflation measure also being below the target. The RBA's preferred measure of underlying inflation reached 2 per cent over the year to June.

The annual number is now expected to be 1.75 per cent by the time December rolls around, before heading back to 2 per cent by June 2019 and 2.25 by the end of next year.

After such a long period of subdued price growth, achieving escape velocity above 2 per cent core inflation on a sustainable basis - which is what the RBA needs to see firm evidence of before lifting rates - likely requires everything to go just right.

Luckily, everything going just right is exactly what governor Phil Lowe and his team are forecasting.

The bank factored into its forecasts market pricing pointing to rates lifting only "around the end of next year", reflecting bonds traders' subdued expectations that inflation will push meaningfully above the bottom of the range until well after then.

The economy will grow by 3.25 per cent in this year and the next - well above estimates of trend growth of 2.75 per cent. The March quarter numbers suggested annual GDP growth of just above 3 per cent.

This solid growth will help drive unemployment lower and eventually to 5 per cent by the end of 2020.

But financial sector economists are not convinced.

The RBA's latest outlook "paints a pretty perfect picture of the future," Capital Economics' Paul Dales writes. "If you were to ask any economist what they would like to happen in an ideal world they would draw something similar."

The headline from TD Securities' Annette Beacher was even more blunt: "RBA: utopia by 2020".

The main bone of contention between the RBA's number crunchers and their private sector peers is around the outlook for consumption. "The outlook for household consumption growth continues to represent a significant uncertainty for the forecasts, in large part due to uncertainty around household income growth," the bank said in Friday's statement.

Nobody is sure how quickly spare capacity will be diminished in the labour market, and even less sure around how far that needs to play out before we get meaningful wages growth - especially in the context of labour markets such as in the US where the jobless rate has dropped as far as 3.8 per cent in recent months without any clear signs of much firmer wages.

Then there's falling house prices and the impact that could have on confidence and spending.

"From our perspective, weak wages growth, a slowdown in employment growth, and potential negative wealth effects loom as more significant risks to these [RBA's] forecasts than the bank appears to be prepared to accept, at least in the statement," Westpac chief economist Bill Evans says.

JP Morgan's Sally Auld says her much lower forecast around consumption growth "probably accounts for most of the difference" between her more subdued outlook for overall economic growth.

She sees consumption expanding at a pace of 2.4 per cent in 2018 and 2 per cent in 2019, versus the RBA's previous assumption of 3 per cent growth over the next few years.
direction: bearish for rate-sensitive sectors like banks and REITs due to delayed rate hike expectations

headline: Blue Sky's Oaktree rescue deal approved by shareholders
text:
Shareholders of beleaguered asset manager Blue Sky Alternative Investments approved the conversion of Oaktree Capital Management's loan to equity, completing a rescue deal that outgoing chairman John Kain said would represent "strengthened foundations".

Blue Sky also named Andrew Day, the former boss of Hastings Funds Management, as chairman to succeed Mr Kain. The Oaktree conversion was sealed with more than 93 per cent of votes cast in favour at an annual meeting in Brisbane on Monday.

Brisbane-based Blue Sky was the target of the now defunct activist short seller Glaucus in March, setting off a chain of events that led to the departure of the firm's former managing director, and the unravelling of the group's growth, which was fuelled by seeding new funds in unlisted markets.

Glaucus accused Blue Sky of inflating the valuations of the underlying assets it managed, and overstating its fee-earning assets under management, which Blue Sky rejects.

It eventually admitted to relying on undrawn credit to achieve its assets under management figure, the composition of which was not previously disclosed.

Mr Kain said Blue Sky never deviated from its prospectus disclosures with regard to measuring fee-earning assets under management, and that it was best to judge Blue Sky's valuations by its ability to sell those assets at an improved value. His re-election as a director incurred a protest vote of 26 per cent.

"This year, our reputation has suffered dearly. We have taken the steps to rectify this and are confident that today marks the turning point, from which our reputation can begin to recover and we can focus on the future, rather than the past," he said.

After a question from a shareholder, Mr Kain admitted the company should have done a better job in dealing with short sellers targeting the company. Short sellers now account for 3 per cent to 5 per cent of the company's register.

"We were aware that there would be some short activity but we didn't anticipate this," he said. "This had happened to us in late 2016. And this is the second type of its activity in Australia. Corporate Travel Management have also had the benefits of our playbook. Most boardrooms would have a strategy about how to deal with this now." (Corporate Travel is facing a short attack from hedge fund VGI Partners).

Mr Kain admitted the short-selling campaign was a "bloody well organised and co-ordinated attack". "We still don't know everything that went on. Maybe we should have. It's almost unprecedented in this country," he said.

He took aim at Glaucus, saying it had been less than honest in launching its attack away from the reach of Australian authorities.

"Our [antagonists] hid from our laws. Then when things progressed they liquidated their business and then went away," he said. (The former Glaucus principals announced they would go their separate ways in May to establish two new short-selling firms).

Following a review of portfolio valuations for the June quarter, versus the previous quarter, 31 per cent of assets were valued downward by more than 10 per cent and 19 per cent were valued upward by more than 10 per cent.

Blue Sky's $50 million convertible loan from Los Angeles-based fund manager Oaktree was deemed as "not fair" but reasonable by Grant Thornton, the independent expert, in October. Byron Beath from Oaktree will also assume a board seat.

With shareholders venting their anger at the performance of the company, Mr Kain was bullish about the Oaktree partnership. He emphasised it was a private equity transaction in response to a question about whether the larger manager could act as a "potential vulture".

No profit guidance for 2018-19 was issued.
direction: bearish for Blue Sky shares

headline: FlexiGroup hit on CEO change
text:
FlexiGroup's stock was hit hard on Monday in response to a changeover in chief executive from Symon-Brewis-Weston to Prospa's Rebecca James.

In an ASX announcement, FlexiGroup said Mr Brewis-Weston would step down on September 3, after just three years in the top job, while Ms James would take over in mid-October.

Ms James is online lender Prospa's chief marketing and enterprise officer, and had a stint at ME Bank as marketing boss.

FlexiGroup's statement said chief financial officer Ross Aucutt will be interim CEO in the intervening period before Ms James' start.

Investors pummeled FlexiGroup's stock after the announcement, which saw the shares finish 13.6 per cent lower at $1.97. The slump came despite chairman Andrew Abercrombie reiterating the company's profit guidance.

"Rebecca James is an extremely capable executive with significant financial services experience," Mr Abercrombie said in the statement. "The board has been particularly impressed by her ability to create and commercialise innovative digital solutions in financial services."

The financing company's interim results in February were marred by the write-down of goodwill associated with the closure of the 24-year-old Flexirent brand.
direction: bearish for FlexiGroup shares

headline: ACCC criminal case alleges broad cartel
text:
A healthcare equipment company at the centre of a landmark criminal cartel case allegedly sought to create a price-fixing deal covering more than 40 businesses across the country, court documents reveal.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission also alleges the Mildura-based family business Country Care Group engaged in criminal cartel conduct when participating in NSW Health tenders by getting competitors to either bid lower or drop out of the tender process all together.

The case is the first time the competition watchdog has used the criminal cartel provisions under the Competition and Consumer Act against an Australian corporation.

Charge sheets against Country Care managing director Robert Hogan show he is facing 48 counts of aiding, abetting, counselling or procuring cartel offences between May 2014 and April 2016, while former business development manager Cameron Harrison is facing 44 charges.

According to the ACCC, in 2014, Mr Hogan reached an understanding with Marrickville-based business Patient Handling that, in the event HealthShare NSW requested bids for equipment, Patient Handling would not bid while Country Care would.

The ACCC alleges Mr Hogan gave effect to the cartel deal on November 7, 2014, the closing date for a state-wide, standing-offer contract to purchase and distribute clinical furniture items, including wheelchairs, shower furniture, and trolleys.

In 2015, Mr Hogan allegedly reached another cartel agreement with Melbourne-based Human Care Australia that both parties would bid in a NSW health tender "but they would do so on the basis that one of those bids would be more likely to be successful than the other".

Mr Hogan and Mr Harrison allegedly gave effect to the deal between May 8 and May 12, when tenders closed for a contract on beds and mattresses across NSW.

Patient Handling declined to comment on the allegations and Human Care Australia did not respond to questions before publication.

An ACCC spokesperson said "no further charges are pending against other parties as a result of this investigation".

The next year, on or about April 28, 2016 in Mildura, the ACCC alleged Country Care, Mr Hogan and Mr Harrison sought to enter a cartel agreement with 42 healthcare equipment companies.

The companies extended across NSW, Victoria, South Australia, West Australia and Queensland.

The ACCC claimed the proposed cartel deal aimed at or had the effect of "fixing, controlling or maintaining, of the price for goods that were supplied, or were likely to be supplied" by both Country Care and the businesses.

In a statement last month, Mr Hogan said the company was committed to continuing its operations and had built a "strong legal team" to assist it. "It remains business as usual for Country Care and I am personally totally focused on working with our strong leadership team to ensure we continue to deliver for our customers," he said.

A company spokesman noted that of the allegations, 43 relate to a single agreement. According to Country Care's website, its equipment features in more than 80 "showrooms" and warehouses across Australia including Patient Handling and some of the 42 companies alleged to be part of the price-fixing agreement.

The ACCC's case was adjourned this week to the Melbourne Magistrate Court on June 6.

An individual found guilty of criminal cartel conduct faces up to 10 years jail and the company faces fines of up to 10 per cent of the turnover or three times the profit gained.

Key points

Country Care managing director Robert Hogan is facing 48 charges.

Ex-business development manager Cameron Harrison is facing 44 charges.
direction: bearish for Country Care Group and the healthcare equipment sector

headline: The best and worst deals of the year
subhead: Investment | Some buyers took big risks that have paid off.
text:
Investment | Some buyers took big risks that have paid off.

As the Reserve Bank of Australia closely monitors the commercial real estate sector - with a fresh warning about valuations - The Australian Financial Review has looked at some of the key commercial real estate deals of 2017 that surprised market participants and gave some insight into how far the market could go this year.

These six deals - potentially both the best and worst deals of the year depending on what side of the fence you are looking from - were mainly in Sydney where there has been extraordinary growth based on rent forecasts. Most of the deals were office related but with big questions over the health of physical retailing, a few shopping centre deals had to be included.

Toy story

The first big deal of the year to reprice the market was the purchase of the ASX headquarters in Sydney by Hong Kong-based Early Light International Group, backed by billionaire toy maker Francis Choi.

The investor paid $335 million for 20 Bridge Street on a 4.5 per cent fully leased yield and a passing yield of 3.9 per cent. At 20,329 square metres, the rate paid equated to $16,478 per sq m. At the time it seemed like the agents, Savills' Simon Fenn, Ian Hetherington and Ben Azar, had cracked the big one at the top of the market, but it was just the beginning.

Mr Choi went on to buy private equity group Blackstone's 1 Castlereagh Street tower in Sydney for about $220 million later that year at a higher price per sq m. It's hard to work out how much leverage the investor has taken on - the Financial Review suspects very little - to buy the properties, but that is a key determinant in how far the market can run.

The local real estate investment trusts learnt their lesson from the financial crisis and have all reduced gearing into the low 20 per cent range from 40 per cent. The RBA warns on this issue most of all: "If these higher commercial valuations are not sustained, say because of a marked increase in global long-term interest rates, highly leveraged investors close to the maximum LVR covenants on bank debt could become vulnerable to breaching loan covenants."

Nexus Dexus

The so-called "Westfield" of office towers in Australia - Dexus - made one of its biggest calls in several years by buying a 25 per cent stake in the MLC Centre at 19 Martin Place for $361.3 million, with its wholesale fund taking the other quarter share for the same price. As first reported by AFR Property, the deal included a fully underwritten $500 million institutional placement.

It was a big call and one that met some criticism from analysts at UBS: "While the price for the MLC Centre was sharper than we anticipated, we were more surprised Dexus was the buyer. Dexus has previously shown more price discipline which may now be in question."

Other landlords thought the price was too much, others thought it was good buying. The price per square metre is difficult to work out because of a retail component but it's clear that since the MLC deal, negotiated by JLL's Rob Sewell and Simon Rooney and Savills led by Ian Hetherington, there has been much sharper pricing.

Dexus chief executive Darren Steinberg is at the nexus of deal making in Sydney and will no doubt be vindicated when the MLC stakes are shown to have higher valuations at Dexus' next financial results.

Sale of the Centuria

In what could be seen as the best and the worst deal of the year, property funds manager Centuria tripled its money after selling Sydney landmark Swire House to Lendlease's investment arm in a $270.05 million deal.

Centuria purchased the tower at 10 Spring Street in 2013 for $91.64 million, but Lendlease, which was looking to amalgamate properties on Spring Street for a super development site, had to beat the competition. It did, but whether the price paid is worthwhile may not be seen for many years. Any development there will also crucially depend on inner-city planning laws that are still to be introduced.

Pitted

The record in Sydney is said to have been 3 Spring Street, a small building which sold for $44 million back in the late 1980s (after the equities market crashed) on a capitalisation rate of 3.5 per cent. However, that record is getting a few nudges and the sale of 130 Pitt Street, Sydney, in December 2017 saw just how close those records are to being broken.

The 10,893 square metre building sold for $229 million to PA Realty, a joint venture between Mitsubishi Estate Co and CLSA Real Estate, on an initial yield of 3.7 per cent.

The deal reflected pricing of about $21,000 per square metre - well and truly above the deal struck for 20 Bridge Street with toy maker Mr Choi. Ben Azar and Ian Hetherington of Savills Australia and Josh Cullen and Rick Butler of Inc RE negotiated the Pitt Street deal, which shows that the cash flowing in from Asia could well continue into the 2018 year and potentially help see records broken.

Gully of the leeches

In the local indigenous language, the Brisbane suburb of Indooroopilly means "gully of the leeches". And for some, the purchase of a 50 per cent stake in and management rights of the Indooroopilly Shopping Centre by AMP Capital could be a very draining experience. Many property players say the management of the centre has been poor - there have been some big arguments with tenants and as a local who grew up travelling through the centre (and just returned from a spot of Christmas shopping there), I can say it has become a very ordinary centre from what was once a great Westfield-managed operation.

AMP struck its deal on a yield of 4.25 per cent through JLL's Simon Rooney in what is the country's biggest ever single-asset retail property deal. Scentre, which operates Westfields in Australia, was understood to be more than $100 million softer on pricing. Just how much time and money will be leached from AMP to turn that asset around is something that is going to be watched very, very closely, especially given the growing pressures from online retailing. At least AMP can take management control of the centre from here on and benefit from a strong trading catchment area.

For the RBA there are still concerns over retail assets: "Rents have been flat and price growth has lagged that of other commercial property segments. Liaison with industry suggests that strong competition in the retail sector, particularly from online and new entrants, is compressing retailers' margins, constraining their ability to accommodate rent increases. In liaison, banks have also expressed concern over the outlook for the retail sector due to this increased competition."

Note: We left the Westfield takeover out as there is not any Australian property in that takeover deal.

Garden variety

There is bound to be a big Chinese development deal in there somewhere. The board of ASX-listed Phileo Australia were overjoyed when major Chinese developer Country Garden agreed to pay a record $400 million for its 363-hectare land parcel on Black Forest Road in Wyndham Vale in Melbourne's outer west.

Phileo paid just $14.5 million for the site back in 2004 when it was farmland, before its recent rezoning to a future residential estate (with the capacity for around 5000 dwellings) exponentially increasing its value.

The deal equates to around $1.1 million a hectare - in line with what other sites in Melbourne's growth corridors have sold for recently - but none have been nearly as big, nor will take as long to develop - meaning it's a major punt by Country Garden on the land market remaining buoyant (as it currently is) for the next 15 to 20 years.

Some insiders claim Country Garden offered as much as $100 million more than the next highest bidder, and there has been some suggestion - denied by Country Garden - that it sought to renegotiate the terms of the sale.

Country Garden Australia managing director Guotao Hu expressed excitement at the opportunity to deliver a major housing project, but even more excited would surely have been Phileo managing director Rudy Koh and the board, who hold over 60 per cent of the stock in the media-shy company and will make a motza from the deal. The board has already been splashing the cash about, awarding director Alfred Sung a whopping bonus of $5 million in November for "long-standing involvement" and "excellent past performance".
direction: bearish for commercial real estate and REITs

headline: CS bonus pot could shrink
text:
Local bonus day rolls around this week for JPMorgan, Goldman Sachs and Citigroup.

Over at Swiss-based firm Credit Suisse, Street Talk understands bonus day is slated for mid-February.

Expectations are that the bonus kitty will be slightly smaller for Australian staff than this time last year, after a bumper fee year for the bank in 2016.

Credit Suisse finished last year ranked sixth in equity capital markets but outside the top 10 for announced mergers and acquisitions, on Dealogic numbers. The M&A league tables were, however, skewed by the record acquisition of Westfield Corporation by European giant Unibail-Rodamco, which is away off from completion.

For investment banking revenue, Credit Suisse finished 2017 in 10th spot, trailing many of its peers and well behind the top three. Those ranked in the first three spots were Macquarie Group, UBS and JPMorgan respectively.

Last year was marred for Credit Suisse locally by the shock exit of investment banking chief Michael Stock. It is unclear what entitlements, or guaranteed payments, Stock may have received on the way out.

Morgan Stanley got the bonus ball rolling last week when local staff were informed of their payments, if they are to receive one. The outcome was said to be in line or slightly up from last year for the majority of employees.
direction: bearish for Credit Suisse shares

headline: Ex-LinkedIn boss backs CV disrupter
text:
Former LinkedIn Australia boss Cliff Rosenberg used to run a business based on the written CV, now he's backed a start-up that can replace it.

Mr Rosenberg has led a $1.6 million raise for myInterview, which offers a video job interview platform, which can use artificial intelligence to shortlist candidates.

Founded in 2014 by Sydney-based Guy Abelsohn and Tel Aviv-based Benjamin Gillman, myInterview has processed 100,000 interviews to date for clients including Catch Group, which this year used it to vet 1000 applications for 40 customer-facing jobs.

Several Catch staff who would be working directly with the new hires watched all the videos and shortlisted candidates themselves, although myInterview's artificial intelligence helped flag those with the most retail-friendly energy.

Catch's talent-acquisition manager, Ben Cartland, said the 40 hires were made in 15 days. In the two-minute videos submitted by job applicants, they are asked questions that the founders claim have been developed with behavioral psychologists.

Although some employers used video interviews as their sole means of shortlisting candidates, Mr Rosenberg said they were best used as an "augmentation" of the traditional CV. "This balances the unconscious bias that typically occurs with written resumes. By adding in an AI layer, the platform also helps recruiters select the best applicants in a timely and cost-effective manner," said Mr Rosenberg, whose other private equity investments include fintech market darling Afterpay. Mr Gillman said the fundraising, co-led by UK-based seed investor Entree Capital, was aimed to grow myInterview to 500,000 interviews processed in 2018-19, including entry to new verticals such as policy qualification for insurance companies.
direction: bullish for myInterview and related tech/startup sector

headline: Big banks, AMP facing $6b bill
text:
Leading banking analyst Brett Le Mesurier has put an estimate of at least $6 billion on consumer refunds, reviews and litigation for the big four banks and AMP.

It comes as investors speculate that National Australia Bank will release some updated figures for its compensation scheme ahead of a grilling in the House Economics Committee on Friday.

The other big banks took some of their medicine last week before their appearance in Parliament, where they apologised profusely for any number of things.

Le Mesurier's $6 billion remediation figure is calculated on a pre-tax basis, and includes the cost of implementing the programs as well as forecasts for 2019.

It is worth noting that the $6 billion-plus compensation bill is by no means definitive. It has been calculated as an explosion of litigation erupts, the corporate regulator gets tougher and more shoddy practices come to light. Since July 2011 the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) has secured $1.83 billion in remediation for customers.

The Commonwealth Bank has been embroiled in the most scandals in recent years and it is therefore little surprise it has the highest remediation payout at $2.3 billion. This includes a settlement with Austrac in relation to the money-laundering scandal, where it was alleged CBA had "serious and systemic non compliance" and its controls and procedures were not effective, which in some cases enabled customers to create fake names, enabling organised crime syndicates and terrorists to launder money.

CBA issued a statement to shareholders last week that showed it had spent $580 million in pre-tax improving the processes and administration of its advice business as well as determining the amounts to be refunded to customers. Only $270 million of that money had been spent remediating customers.

The amount relates to the periods 2012 to 2018. CBA bought these advice businesses nearly 20 years ago, which begs the question what was happening in the first decade of ownership and why it took so long to start fixing things up. It is also worth noting that its Colonial Financial Planning business was subject to a two-year enforceable undertaking from ASIC in late 2011 due to rampant misconduct.

Separately, Slater and Gordon last week lodged a "Get Your Super Back" class action against CBA and Colonial First State in the Federal Court, with the law firm estimating it could exceed $100 million. The case alleges trustees, who are supposed to act in the best interests of members, invested members' savings with CBA and received uncompetitive bank interest rates. CBA denies the allegations and is defending the claim.

This is one of a number of class actions expected to be launched in the coming months. Other law firms are looking at misconduct in relation to credit cards, life insurance definitions and irresponsible lending.

Le Mesurier of Shaw and Partners ranks ANZ Bank second highest in remediation costs at $1.2 billion pre-tax, with over $200 million spent on customer remediation in 2017 and the first half of 2018. The majority of the remediation relates to banking products.

In the second half of 2018 ANZ allocated a further $300 million to customer remediation, again mostly related to banking products, and $230 million was spent setting up and running the scheme.

AMP ranked a close third at $1.17 billion. AMP's remediation and associated costs in the first half of 2018 of more than $500 million pre-tax might seem like a large number but when it is put into the context that it has the largest funds under advice it starts to look inadequate, particularly given what has come out of the royal commission.

Some of the sensational revelations that poured out of the royal commission triggered five separate class actions as AMP admitted it deliberately charged consumers fees for no services on a systematic basis. It then misled the corporate regulator, in the process letting down shareholders and decimating the share price.

Westpac's remediation bill ranks fourth highest at an estimated $1 billion pre-tax. Unlike CBA, the majority of its $1 billion is likely to find its way into customer pockets.

NAB's bill is likely to become clearer this week. Given the recent announcements from ANZ, Westpac and CBA, an after tax figure of more than $200 million, is unlikely to surprise the market. The total cost may well be larger than this.

For instance, on July 26 NAB's super fund trustee NULIS agreed to refund 305,000 superannuation customers an average of $220 after failing to explain to customers they could switch off a financial planning fee. This amounts to $67 million and then there is interest on top of that.

Since then ASIC has taken legal action against NULIS Nominees and MLC Nominees, alleging it misled super customers and wrongly took $100 million in fees.

There is also a lot of interest in the sale of ANZ's OnePath Pensions and Investments business to IOOF after the boss of IOOF gave testimony at the royal commission that raised a series of issues and misconduct. The upshot was questions, including at a parliamentary hearing on Friday by shadow financial services minister Clare O'Neil, as to whether it was in the best interests of ANZ's OnePath members to be offloaded to a company that had serious regulatory issues.

After the parliamentary hearing, ANZ executive Alexis George said the transaction with IOOF was scheduled for completion at the end of the first quarter of next year. The final report into the Hayne commission is due for release on February 1, which is expected to include findings on IOOF.

She said ANZ and the trustees separately have been having a lot of discussions with IOOF, including the independence of the board, governance and controls it has in place. She said IOOF was being asked for "certain" commitments on governance and policies.

She also admitted that some of the royal commission revelations were a surprise.

During the royal commission it became known the Australian Prudential Regulatory Authority had written to IOOF at various times outlining serious issues such as "difficulty in obtaining accurate and current information in relation to issues such as ... culture, identification of responsible persons and the information flow and relationship between the board and management and the Fairfax Media matter".

That "Fairfax Media matter" related to serious misconduct including insider trading, allegations of front-running, misrepresentation of performance figures and the boss of the research division getting staff to cheat on his behalf on compliance exams.

In mid-2017 an internal memo stated: "Since December 2015, APRA has identified a number of instances where IOOF has failed to adequately identify conflicts of interest and either avoid or appropriately deal with them."

The lack of regard for governance and processes was highlighted when a board meeting held the week before IOOF boss Chris Kelaher gave testimony resulted in the production of handwritten minutes on scraps of paper. When asked by council assisting Michael Hodge QC, if Kelaher understood what was on the bits of paper, he retorted that he wasn't a handwriting expert.

If ANZ and the trustees determine it is in the best interests of ANZ members that the sale proceeds, it will leave them open to litigation and reputational damage if the royal commission makes damning findings or something blows up in the future.

The duty of trustees to act in members' best interests has opened up a huge can of worms for financial institutions that own retail funds. Entrenched underperformance in many cases and the conflict between acting in shareholders' best interests and members best interests is problematic.

Opposition Leader Bill Shorten raised the issue last week when he said Labor would consider forcing for-profit funds to outsource the trusteeship to an independent organisation and give APRA the power to sack trustees of funds that regularly underperform.

It is steeped in politics but given super is compulsory and conflicts have been on full display during the royal commission, it is an issue that needs to be addressed. In the meantime, there will be a lot of remediation to bandage up the wound.
direction: bearish for big banks and AMP

headline: Austar 'coal bursts' leave 200 jobs in limbo
text:
More than 200 coal mining jobs in NSW remain in limbo as Yancoal seeks clarity from government regulators over the future of the Austar mine near Cessnock.

Safety regulators have barred Yancoal from underground mining at Austar since mid-May following an underground "coal burst", a phenomenon where geological pressures cause a dynamic explosion of rock.

That incident came four years after geological pressure at Austar sparked a similar burst that killed two men who were working in the underground mine.

Yancoal is keen to resume mining at Austar and has asked the NSW Industrial Relations Commission whether there are legal grounds for a review of the prohibition notice that was put in place by safety regulators on May 18.

With an answer to that inquiry, and clarity from the regulator, not expected any time soon, Yancoal has started redeploying the 205 people who work at Austar to maintenance and expansion projects at the company's other Hunter Valley mines like Moolarben.

But such redeployment is not a permanent solution, and Yancoal is keen for clarity over the future of Austar sooner rather than later.

"Until all legal avenues have been exhausted and we can re-enter the mine to move the longwall, we are unable to continue proposed longwall activity," Yancoal chief Reinhold Schmidt said.

Austar is not the only coal mine under close watch by NSW mine safety regulators at the moment; several "coal bursts" have occurred at South32's Appin mine in the Illawarra region in recent years, with the mine suffering a fresh prohibition notice in May.

The issues at Austar are more of a problem for Yancoal's lenders than for the company itself; Yancoal transferred ownership of Austar and some other assets to a new company called Watagan Mining in March 2016, with Watagan owned by three Yancoal lenders.

Yancoal effectively works as a mining and marketing services contractor at Austar.

Coal prices have been surprisingly strong in 2018, with the federal government's top commodity forecaster predicting the Australian coal sector exported more than $60 billion of the black stuff in the year to June 30.

The combined value of coking and thermal coal exports in fiscal 2018 is believed to have exceeded the value of iron ore exports in the year, ending iron ore's long reign as Australia's most lucrative commodity export.

Prices for premium thermal coal from Newcastle have been near six-year highs in recent months, and the commodity was fetching $US116 per tonne in recent days, having surged close to 20 per cent since March.

Prices for premium hard coking coal from Queensland have also been stronger than expected, and were fetching $US195 per tonne in recent days.

Yancoal shares last traded at 14¢.
direction: bearish for Yancoal and the coal sector

headline: Sydney hail costliest event of stormy year
text:
A catastrophic hailstorm in Sydney on Thursday is set to be the most expensive weather event of the year, with major insurers projecting costs in the hundreds of millions.

As AFR Weekend went to press on Friday evening, the Insurance Council of Australia reported the industry had received 25,000 claims worth $125 million. The figure was expected to rise.

IAG and Suncorp both confirmed the storm, which the Insurance Council of Australia declared a catastrophe, would cost more in claims than any event of the financial year so far.

Insurance giant IAG paused trading on Friday morning to announce the storms that belted parts of Sydney and the NSW central coast with golf ball-sized hailstones would cost the insurer $169 million. That figure took into account its quota share agreements with reinsurers, meaning actual claims from IAG customers would be higher.

In an ASX announcement just hours after the storm, IAG revealed it had already received 6500 claims and expected many more in the coming days. The majority were motor vehicle claims.

The hailstorm will bring the total cost to IAG of natural peril claims to between $410 million and $430 million before tax, post-quota share for the 2019 financial year. The severe rain in NSW, Queensland and Victoria in early December was the second most expensive, costing the insurer $70 million.

Suncorp confirmed the storm would be the most expensive single event of the year, costing the insurer up to $250 million. That would be more than three times the cost of the second most expensive event, the rain in early December, which cost $80 million.

As of Friday afternoon QBE had not released projected losses.

IAG and Suncorp shares plummeted on the news. IAG closed down 5.04 per cent at $6.60 a share on Friday, while Suncorp closed down 4.02 per cent at $12.40 a share. QBE, which is a more diversified business, closed down 1.03 per cent at $9.65 a share.

IAG Australia division chief executive Mark Milliner said: "Our priority is to help customers affected by the hailstorm as soon as possible. Extra employees have been allocated to the claims and repair management teams and our online claim lodgment facility is assisting the rapid assessment of claims."

IAG said it was setting up "specialist hail-repair units" across Sydney to assess and repair hail-damaged vehicles. The insurer said these units would be in place "as soon as possible".

Hailstorms are among the most expensive weather events for insurers. The costs of this particular storm are expected to blow out further as it occurred in a densely populated area just days before Christmas, when supply of repairers is lower than usual, meaning repair prices are higher.

Before the hailstorm, total insured losses for the year from catastrophic events sat at $260 million. ICA spokesman Campbell Fuller said this figure would rise sharply as the final insured damage bill for the hailstorm became clear in the coming weeks.

Before the hailstorm, flash floods in Hobart in May had been the most costly catastrophe of the year, causing $100 million in insured losses.

Early year bushfires in Victoria and New South Wales, floods in North Queensland, and Cyclone Marcus, which battered the Northern Territory, rounded out the 2018 catalogue of catastrophes.

However, nothing reached the heights of the 1999 Eastern Sydney hailstorm, Australia's most expensive natural disaster, which caused insured losses of $1.7 billion.

Mr Fuller said the hailstorms were "unlikely" to affect motor and home insurance premiums, saying 2018 had so far not been a bad year for extreme weather. "Insurers anticipate losses from summer storms and most will have made provisions," he said.

The ICA declares an event a catastrophe only under certain circumstances, and it is not necessary linked to the cost.

The heavy rain in early December was not classed as a catastrophe.

Declaring an event a catastrophe allows insurers to prioritise claims and work with governments and emergency services.

Key pointsBy Friday night claims worth $125 million had been filed with insurers.

The hailstorm damage is "unlikely" to affect motor and home premiums.
direction: downward pressure on IAG and Suncorp shares

headline: Majority of home owners worry about prices
text:
About 60 per cent of home owners who have acquired residential property in the past three years are concerned they will owe more than the value of their homes as house prices continue to slide, a survey by ME Bank says.

Nearly 50 per cent of those who have jumped into the market during the boom also regretted the prices paid, while falling house prices made them feel less wealthy, and this may affect their spending.

The cutback on spending as a result of the housing downturn could also hurt the economy, UBS has said. The bank predicted a 10 per cent fall in house prices could cut 2 per cent from household spending, although that might take another six months or more to bite.

Unlike the 1500 home buyers who ME surveyed, ME head of home loans Andrew Bartolo was more sanguine about house prices.

"The Australian property market has seen seven price declines-recover cycles in Sydney since 1984 and all have seen prices recover, most within four years," he said.

But other analysts and forecasters like BIS Oxford Economics take the view the housing downturn this time will last longer, even up to 15 years against a regular "seven-year" lull, particularly if negative gearing is removed. Unlike previous cycles, all three major cities - Sydney, Melbourne and Brisbane - have participated in the recent boom. As the housing market is notoriously driven by "animal spirits" rather than economic fundamentals, a University of Technology research paper says, the downturn will be longer and harder, BIS predicts.

In an end-of-year BIS Oxford Economics update last week, managing director Robert Mellor anticipated house prices to be down at least 12 per cent from peak to trough, especially in Sydney. So far, Sydney has chalked up a 9.5 per cent drop from peak prices last year.

But if prices fall past 15 per cent, it would be particularly difficult for housing markets to "get out" of the slump quickly leading to a long, sustained downturn, Mr Mellor warned.
direction: bearish for residential property and consumer discretionary sectors

headline: Arena REIT a takeover target: Macquarie
text:
Childcare landlord Arena REIT is shaping as a potential takeover target, softened up by recent weaker sentiment in the sector.

Analysts at Macquarie - who have an outperform on the stock - have nominated powerful funds manager Charter Hall as a likely suitor in the event of any corporate action emerging around the $600 million property trust.

In a $205 million cash deal, Charter Hall recently took over the listed Folkestone funds management platform, which operates a childcare centre property trust, the only listed peer for Arena. A merger of the two trusts has long been mooted in the sector.

"Takeover risk remains a potential source of upside for Arena REIT," the Macquarie analysts, Darren Leung, Rob Freeman and Stuart McLean, wrote in a client note this week.

"With the recent change in ownership of management of peer company Folkestone Education Trust, we believe takeover risk has increased for Arena REIT.

"With Charter Hall's unlisted capital partners and our view of the business looking to gain scale in the childcare market, we believe the number of takeover options for Arena REIT has increased."

Adding to speculation will be the recent weakness in Arena's stock price, which closed at $2.20 on Tuesday, well off a recent high in September of $2.44 and the 12-month high of $2.50 last November.
