The childcare sector has weathered negative sentiment in recent weeks after Australia's biggest listed childcare operator, G8 Education, which operates 512 childcare centres, revealed a 22 per cent slide in first-half profits In August as it battled against oversupply.

While that may have weighed on Arena's stock - making it an opportunistic target - the property trust itself is well positioned in the sector, with a robust portfolio of well-leased centres, according to Macquarie.

"Childcare trading conditions subdued but expected to improve in the next 12-24 months. Fixed escalators and quality portfolio should shield Arena REIT," they wrote.

Led by David Harrison, Charter Hall has an extensive network of equity sources globally which invest in the f unlisted and listed funds it manages.

"We've articulated to the market previously that we've got a very strong interest in the education sector," Mr Harrison told The Australian Financial Review in August during the Folkestone takeover.

"We see this as a vertical expansion of our focus on education from universities through to early learning."

According to Macquarie, an unlisted capital bid could provide the highest upside for a takeover of the Arena trust.

That is because unlisted capital has the lowest return hurdles and could provide the highest takeover price, partly by increasing the leverage in Arena.

"If Arena was run as an externally managed vehicle and levered at 40 per cent, this could result in a takeover price of $2.53 per share," the Macquarie analysts wrote.

A takeover bid could also emerge from the private equity players, at a takeover price of $2.48 per share cash, on Macquarie's estimate.
direction: Arena REIT shares to rise on takeover speculation

headline: Alcohol price shake-up in NT alarms sector
text:
Large wine companies and big liquor retailers are concerned about the Northern Territory government's plans to introduce a minimum floor price for alcohol from July 1 amid increasing speculation that Western Australia may do likewise.

They say it is a piecemeal approach to policymaking that will punish responsible drinkers.

The move, in practical terms, would lift the price tag on even the cheapest bottle of wine to about $10 because it is based on a formula calculated by how many standard drinks it contains.

The minimum floor price of $1.30 per unit would also hit cask wine sales hard. They make up between 30 and 35 per cent of total wine sales in Australia. The Northern Territory government's plans come after a wide-ranging review of alcohol harm last year, and wine will be hit harder than beer because of the higher levels of alcohol per package.

Angus McPherson, the managing director of Australasia for Treasury Wine Estates, said his company didn't sell any cask wine in Australia but such an ad hoc approach to policy would be damaging to the entire $5 billion wine industry. Moves to reduce alcohol harm needed a national, collaborative approach using a planned and consistent strategy.

"Our concerns are that an ad hoc approach will cause significant damage to the industry. If the states want to go down a minimum floor pricing path then a national approach needs to be taken to deal with their concerns."

Treasury Wines is Australia's biggest wine company and has heavily re-positioned its portfolio to the premium and luxury end of the market, with brands including Penfolds, Wolf Blass and Wynns, and has delivered booming profits with exports to China up sharply.

Fergus Taylor, the executive director of Alcohol Beverages Australia, said alcohol abuse was a serious issue which needed to be tackled, but the Northern Territory minimum floor price was not the right mechanism. "The people who are the problem drinkers, they are the least responsive to things like price intervention," Mr Taylor said. "People who don't have a lot of money but do the right thing will be punished."

Alcohol Beverages Australia is the over-arching organisation which represents large liquor retailers and alcohol producers. Its backers include Coles Liquor and Woolworths' Endeavour Drinks Group, which operates the Dan Murphy's and BWS chain, beer maker Lion, Asahi, Coca-Cola Amatil, Accolade Wines and Treasury Wine Estates.

Wine would be hit much harder than beer because of the higher level of alcohol. The WA government has also been looking at the possibility of implementing a minimum floor price for alcohol.

Mr Taylor said there were high-quality wines at lower price points. "There are $7 bottles of wine winning awards," he said. "When you try and solve social problems at the demand end it's very problematic". Coles won an industry award for a $5 private label shiraz in 2016.

The NT government announced its floor pricing plans in late February after an alcohol review by former NT Supreme Court chief justice Trevor Riley who found the NT had one of the highest per capita rates of alcohol consumption in the world. His report found that 44 per cent of people drank alcohol at a risky level at least once a month.

Wines of Western Australia chief executive Larry Jorgensen said the industry organisation wanted a collaborative approach with the Western Australian government on the best way to reduce harmful alcohol consumption, while minimising unnecessary negative impacts on the broader community, he said.

Treasury Wine's Mr McPherson said separate reforms to the rebates under the Wine Equalisation Tax had been heavily supported by Treasury and would help to reduce excess supply.

But minimum floor prices implemented in an ad hoc fashion could arrest momentum just as the industry recovered from over-supply of grapes, particularly in the low-end commercial wine sector.

Key points

The NT's plans come after a wide-ranging review of alcohol harm last year.

The minimum floor price of $1.30 per unit will also hit cask wine sales hard.
direction: bearish for wine and liquor stocks

headline: Apartment owners unite for St Kilda Road sale
text:
A group of 25 owners of apartments and offices have joined together to sell their building in Melbourne's St Kilda Road precinct.

The property at 545 St Kilda Road sits on a land holding of 2865 square metres, creating a potential development site that could fetch as much as $30 million.

The existing mixed-use strata-owned complex includes residential units and offices, whose owners have appointed JLL's Alex McColl and Michael Godfrey to broker the property.

The building itself was constructed in the 1930s. It is close to The Alfred hospital and public transport, including Prahran station and the new Anzac train station, which is being developed.

The 25-unit complex has dual access to St Kilda Road and Alfred Lane to the rear, and is near Fawkner Park and Albert Park Lake.

"This offering represents a very rare scenario. It is almost unheard of to see a strata property of this scale offered in one line in such a prime location," Mr McColl said.

"There aren't many opportunities to acquire a site of this calibre that has strong potential to suit a variety of uses, including commercial, residential and medical."

In the past five years, 10 office buildings have been withdrawn for conversion to residential - a total of 96,350 square metres - within the St Kilda Road precinct.

"Locally, we have seen increased instances of owners coming together to consolidate their properties to take advantage of the demand for larger commercial-zoned sites in Melbourne's CBD and city fringe," Mr Godfrey said.

There has been plenty of action in St Kilda Road's commercial market in recent months.

Last month, prominent developer and investor John Beville put the commercial office building at 509 St Kilda Road on the market with expectations of $160 million or more.

Abacus, partnered by Singapore's Wing Tai, snapped up 464 St Kilda Road for $95.38 million in April.

In other recent deals on the strip, Singapore's Tong Eng Group bought a tower from Myer Family Investments for $77 million, and Rockworth Capital Partners took over 390 St Kilda Road from Fort Street Real Estate Capital for $98 million.
direction: bullish for Melbourne CBD property developers and strata owners

headline: ASIC's admission will reverberate
text:
The sirens finally - finally - appear to be wailing for Australia's tough cop on the corporate beat.

The chairman of the Australian Securities and Investment Commission, James Shipton, revealed to the royal commission on Friday morning that he has told his squad of crime fighters they need a good reason not to launch legal action when a financial institution reports a breach.

And when was this sweeping show of strength made? Well, two or three weeks ago.

The bewilderment of senior counsel Rowena Orr was obvious, and infectious.

She had taken Shipton to the example of Commonwealth Bank's mis-selling of credit card insurance. This is the case the royal commission examined back in August, where CBA admitted selling income protection insurance for credit cards to people who didn't have jobs, and could thus never claim on the insurance.

CBA itself notified ASIC of a problem with the insurance product in May 2015. And just a month before the royal commission pored over the matter in August this year, the bank announced refunds to customers.

But Orr said it wasn't until October 22 "nearly 2½ years after the notification by CBA and months after the matter was examined in the public hearings in the royal commission" that the ASIC enforcement team accepted a referral to investigate the matter.

Not take action, mind you - just start an investigation.

Shipton admitted this was a mistake, and said ASIC's failure to quickly investigate NAB's introducer program, discussed late on Thursday by Orr, was also a mistake.

But he insisted things are changing.

"You should have confidence that it's different today, because we now have issued guidance that would mean that a decision like the one in CCI ... would have a very different starting point," Shipton told Orr.

"The starting point today would be to ask the question and turn our minds to why not litigate this demonstrable breach. That is the clear guidance today that stands today."

"And that's as of a few weeks ago?" Orr asked.

"That is of two or three weeks ago, yes."

This was breathtaking evidence. All year, the royal commission storm's been raging. All year, Shipton and his team have been talking tough. All year, the community's hunger for ASIC to get tougher has been blindingly obvious.

And yet it's taken all year for the chairman to finally come up with this sort of decree.

Orr, backed up by Commissioner Ken Hayne, who grew noticeably frustrated with what he suggested was Shipton's inability to answer Orr's questions, then went to ASIC's enforcement policies, which have clearly leaned more towards tools such as enforceable undertakings and community benefit payments, where a naughty firm makes a payment to a organisation rather than a fine.

Orr took Shipton to an academic study where a institution suggested these payments were "more palatable" than fines.

Shipton again insisted ASIC was changing and it is "minded to use the enforceable undertaking tool less and court enforcement action more".

But there is clearly more work to be done on ASIC's processes and systems around enforcement.

Orr challenged Shipton over why ASIC seeks an indication from a naughty entity before it slaps it with an infringement notice.

"The parking inspector doesn't seek an indication from the person he's giving a parking fine to as to whether they will accept and pay it. He just does it. Why don't you just do that?"

Shipton explained that an unwillingness to accept an infringement notice would see ASIC head to court. But he argued that ASIC is trying to use its resources as efficiently as possible; getting an entity to accept an infringement notice avoids a bunch of procedures that "not only takes up the time of our enforcement teams ... [and] then has to go to an internal delegate, and so there is a whole flow of procedure and resource usage".

Clearly a rethink of these processes and systems is needed. The same goes for ASIC's budget and resources, too.

But for goodness sake, do it faster.

j.thomson@faifaxmedia.com.au
direction: bearish for banks and financial services sector

headline: Board leaders face angry new world
text:
Fallout A shift in public opinion has made for an uneasy AGM season.

The goalposts have radically shifted for directors in a matter of a few weeks.

What's most notable about AMP's defiant submission to the Hayne royal commission on Friday and APRA's scathing report this week into the Commonwealth Bank is that both boards believe they did nothing wrong.

Both boards have failed to recognise the drastic shift in public opinion which is turning into genuine anger from institutional investors, demonstrated by the 43 per cent protest vote against QBE on Thursday, or the public shaming of AMP chairman Catherine Brenner who was forced to resign on Monday.

AMP has moved to avoid a bloodbath at its annual meeting next Thursday with the hasty appointment of former CBA chief executive David Murray on Friday.

Leading directors - used to being re-elected with 95 per cent support - now face one of the toughest AGM seasons on record.

"The banking royal commission has done for bank boards what Jack the Ripper did for blind dates," former Transfield chairman and BCA president Tony Shepherd warned this week, while another ASX-listed chairman told AFR Weekend they were reviewing their board roles as the job had become "just too dangerous".

We have heard the cry of wolf from directors before but the extent of the seismic shift was nicely demonstrated by Australian head of proxy firm ISS, Vas Kolesnikoff, who last year was facing complaints to the Australian Securities and Investments Commission, with directors seeking to have him removed because of his overly aggressive AGM recommendations.

Before next week's AMP AGM, Kolesnikoff advised investors to wait for the Hayne royal commission's findings on AMP. He wanted to see how the board responded on executive pay before voting against the three directors facing re-election. But the mob was already baying for blood, demanding Brenner's resignation in what some directors have described as a modern-day corporate witch-hunt.

Credible voices like veteran investor Peter Morgan are calling for the rest of the AMP board to be purged, warning all "boards are massively overrated".

The signs of an investor revolt began last year after an overhaul of the CBA board following the AUSTRAC allegations and Grant King being forced to stand down from the BHP board.

Then during last year's AGM season, we witnessed the first big votes against individual directors. Goodman chairman Ian Ferrier received a 22 per cent protest vote. Brambles directors Stephen Jones and Brian Long received 25 and 18 per cent against votes. CBA's Andrew Mohl and Gerry Harvey both faced a 15 per cent protest vote.

Director of Lend Lease Colin Carter, who has conducted hundreds of board reviews, previously told AFR Weekend "boards cannot get rid of a director if they don't think they are any good ... it is the only job in the world where your colleagues and superiors can't sack you".

He admits the world has changed. "When Treasurer Scott Morrison says directors have to be held accountable it is clear that directors have become the whipping boy for the country. Things have gone awry and the avalanche of public disillusionment has hit them," Carter told the AFR Weekend on Friday.
direction: downward pressure on bank and listed company shares

headline: Retailers hope for 'big bang' finish
text:
Retailers that have held off discounting to protect margins are counting on a "big bang" finish to the Christmas shopping season to help meet sales and earnings targets.

Shares in most discretionary retailers have fallen sharply in the December quarter in expectation that Christmas 2018 will fall short of expectations and sales growth will not be strong enough to deliver margin growth, leading to a round of earnings downgrades in February.

However, Citigroup's head of research Craig Woolford is still forecasting a "decent" Christmas, while the Australian Retailers Association is sticking to its forecast for a 2.9 per cent increase in Christmas spending, despite a slow start in early December.

"I think we are going to get there but it will come with a big bang at the end," ARA executive director Russell Zimmerman said on Wednesday. "I'm told by most retailers [sales] are up but they're not up as much as they'd like."

Due to the timing of Christmas this year - it's the first time in six years Christmas falls on a Tuesday - many consumers will leave their non-food shopping until Saturday and Sunday and their fresh food shopping until Monday.

The ARA expects consumers to splurge almost $15 billion over the next five days.

"I really believe it's going to happen with a big bang in the last few days," Mr Zimmerman said.

Mr Woolford agreed, saying: "Saturday, Sunday and Monday are going to be enormous trading days given the way people have planned their time off and Christmas shopping - this weekend will be bigger than last weekend."

Christmas is make or break time for many retailers and can account for more than a third of annual earnings for discretionary retailers and twice average monthly earnings for food retailers, according to Citigroup.

Mr Woolford said foot traffic in shopping centres was better than this time last year, when traffic fell almost 20 per cent in the week before Christmas, and most of the factors required for a good Christmas were in place.

Sales have been stable, enabling retailers to manage inventory and staffing and wages have improved slightly, while pre-Christmas discounting has not been as widespread as it was in 2017.

However, Mr Woolford is worried post-Christmas clearance sales could fizzle this year because many bargain-hunters snapped up deals during Black Friday and Cyber Monday promotions, which pulled sales forward into November.

"Boxing Day could end up being quite good but the appeal of the promotions could peter out for shoppers quite quickly," he said.

In Sydney's Pitt Street Mall on Wednesday, Port Macquarie resident Jill Follington, who comes to Sydney every year for Christmas shopping, planned to spend more than $1200 on presents, almost exclusively in stores rather than online.

Kim Luscombe, from Mulgoa near Penrith, was shopping with her three daughters and mother Robyn Doyle. Ms Luscombe had already bought about 50 Christmas presents, spending "a couple" of thousand dollars, half in stores and half online. That's well above the average $664 Australians spend during Christmas, according to deals site Picodi.

Popular products this year, according to retail software management company vend, include environmentally sustainable and reusable products (up more than 1000 per cent since 2017), computers and accessories (up 300 per cent), vintage vinyl and records (up 260 per cent), active wear (up 230 per cent) and drones and virtual reality products (up 78 per cent).

These trends auger well for retailers such as JB Hi-Fi, whose shares have fallen 12 per cent this quarter, and Rebel Sport owner Super Retail Group, whose shares are down 19 per cent.

Key pointsThe ARA expects consumers to splurge almost $15 billion over the next five days.

Foot traffic in shopping centres has been better than at this time last year.
direction: discretionary retailers

headline: Premier invokes Musk spirit
text:
South Australian Premier Jay Weatherill promised a $2 billion infrastructure spending program that includes part-funding a new $700 million deepwater port to help miners and grain farmers export from the Spencer Gulf region, as he invoked the spirit of entrepreneurs Elon Musk and Sanjeev Gupta in his re-election push.

The Labor Party has been in power in South Australia since 2002 and Mr Weatherill is seeking to extend that to an extraordinary 20-year stretch if he wins the March 17 election.

He said the big investments by Tesla boss Elon Musk and British billionaire Sanjeev Gupta in the state were symbolic of people "anticipating the future" rather than looking backward as he argued he was best positioned to continue a transition of the economy into industries of the future.

"They send a powerful message to the world," he said of Mr Musk's big storage battery which has been built near Jamestown in the state's mid-north and the bold $800 million virtual battery plant connecting up to 50,000 houses in Adelaide with free solar panels and storage batteries, and Mr Gupta's bold plan to revive the former Arrium business and the Whyalla steelworks.

Labor held its official campaign launch on Sunday at the Adelaide Convention Centre, the same venue where Mr Musk outlined his ambitious plan to land a spacecraft on Mars by 2022.

Earlier on Sunday, SA Liberal leader Steven Marshall ran though his plans for the first 100 days if he wins the election. These include a $45 million plan to scrap payroll tax for all small businesses with a payroll of $1.5 million or less, and a $100 million home battery subsidy scheme.

Mr Marshall said it was time to "hit the reset button" after a dysfunctional Labor government. Nick Xenophon, who left the Senate at a federal level late last year to head the new SA Best party, is a thorn in the side of both major parties and experts are predicting he will hold the balance of power in the 47-seat lower house in SA.

Mr Xenophon is being heavily criticised by both major parties in advertising campaigns, and so far has 35 SA Best candidates lined up to run in different lower house seats. Labor advertisements are questioning the unusual viewpoints in the past of some of the SA Best candidates.

But it was everyday infrastructure spending that was in the spotlight at the Labor launch, with Mr Weatherill also outlining plans to remove seven level crossings in suburban Adelaide to reduce traffic snarls, and extending the city's tram network.

The infrastructure spending would create 1700 construction jobs annually for each of the next four years.

But specific details for the deepsea bulk commodity port at one of several potential sites in the Spencer Gulf, the large body of water between the Yorke Peninsula and Eyre Peninsula, weren't released.

Large industrial towns such as Whyalla and Port Pirie are on the coastline of the Spencer Gulf.

Mr Weatherill said the new port would be partly funded by the state government, but it would be seeking private investment. He declined to nominate a preferred site and acknowledged there had been several proposals over the past few years by private operators for a new Spencer Gulf port. "We will invest to make sure that one or a number of ports get up," he told reporters after the launch.

ASX-listed iron ore hopeful Iron Road has been among those pushing for a deepsea port to allow commodities to be shipped from its proposed iron ore project on the Eyre Peninsula, while Mr Gupta's GFG Alliance has also proposed doing more with the Whyalla port which it acquired as part of the broader acquisition of the former Arrium business last year.

Another private consortium, which includes businessman and Adelaide Crows chairman Rob Chapman, has proposed a $100 million port at Lucky Bay on the Eyre Peninsula.
direction: bullish for SA infrastructure, mining, and energy stocks

headline: Too much solar puts power grid at risk
text:
The record uptake of subsidised rooftop solar power into homes and businesses in some areas is beginning to undermine the power grid and is forcing policymakers to consider cutting off flows of solar power into the grid.

The grid hasn't kept pace with the explosion of solar homeowners wanting to export surplus energy back into the grid, setting up a two-way flow the centralised networks weren't designed for and reducing power quality.

Poor power quality might mean that lights flicker or dim when an energy-hungry appliance like a pool pump is switched on. Surplus wind and solar power can also turn wholesale electricity prices negative, forcing high cost generators out of the market.

Solar rooftop installations hit another record in August, with 142 megawatts of small-scale residential and commercial systems coming on line, data from Sunwiz, a consultancy, shows. That lifted the total for the year to date to 974 MW, which is 49 per cent more than the same stage last year and not far shy of the full year record of just under 1100 MW in 2017.

Commercial and industrial systems larger than 100 kW are also booming among shopping centre owners Vicinity Centres and Stockland Group, manufacturers Liberty OneSteel, Primo Smallgoods, Hunter Douglas window coverings, egg farmers Pace Farms and Brisbane Airport.

Australian Energy Market Operator chief executive Audrey Zibelman said curtailment - which already applies to wind power in South Australia about six per cent of the time - could also be required for rooftop solar as a last resort "in some instances".

Capacity in small-scale rooftop solar now totals about 7500 MW and commercial and industrial systems lift the total "distributed energy resources" to nearly 10,000 MW - just over one sixth of system capacity.

AEMO already curtails wind farm output in SA when surplus generation threatens system stability and says wind has been curtailed for about 6 per cent of the time over the past 13 months. It says it curtails wind in SA to 1295 MW if there is insufficient spinning generation - which usually means gas turbines - online in the state.

Trevor St Baker, who owns Vales Point coal power station in NSW, says wind curtailment should go further, so that "dispatchable" - or firm - generation, such as coal and gas, is always at least 50 per cent of supply. "The extent of the problem is quite serious for existing market participants and new investors," he says.

Ms Zibelman said AEMO is working to avoid this by better integrating distributed energy resources - including solar, batteries and electric vehicles "behind the meter" in homes and businesses - into the grid. It will run trials to get ahead of regulatory changes and is developing a register which will be in place next year to enhance visibility of distributed energy.

"As with any form of generation where technical limits of the networks are exceeded, including both transmission and distribution, action is taken by the network operators and AEMO to address any risk. This may include modifying the control systems of the generators (including residential solar), changes to network configurations or equipment, or in some instances curtailing the generation source," she said.

Energy Networks Australia chief Andrew Dillon said the issue of power flowing back to feeders is already happening in areas with high solar penetration, such as South Australia and Queensland, where solar penetration exceeds 33 per cent and 30 per cent.

As more renewables come on stream, on a "benign" day like Boxing Day, all of South Australia could be supplied by solar, Mr Dillon said. "At one level that's good news but on another level it creates significant technical challenges" for electrical engineers as regards how you maintain the stability of the system with zero synchronous generators."

Experts say the practice should be a last resort for solar homes and businesses and there are better ways to manage the problem. "If there are investment flows into particular parts of existing grids that are not strong there will be some curtailment issues. It's about how we line up enabling investment in strengthening the grid with the new investment in generation that's happening," Mr Dillon said.

ENA is working with AEMO on programs to better manage distributed energy. Frank Tudor, former chief executive of Horizon Power, a Western Australian-owned utility, said earlier this year that if solar and other distributed resources are well managed, they can be a solution rather than a problem and help to stabilise the system rather than disrupt it - a "cleverer way to deal with it" rather than rushing to curtail solar households.

This is already happening in trials and small commercial deployments around the country. Vicinity Centres is trialling Power Ledger's blockchain technology to get better data on how it is using energy and eventually to share power with neighbours, upstart retailers such as Powershop and software companies Reposit Power, Evergen and Greensync have deployed "demand response" service which pay customers to curtail their own usage, send power from their solar panels, batteries and smart appliances back to the grid at times of high demand, and Queensland's state-owned Energex uses demand response to tap customers' pool pumps and hot water systems.

At one level that's good news but on another level it creates significant technical challenges. Andrew Dillon, Energy Networks Australia CEO
direction: bearish for traditional energy generators and grid infrastructure stocks

headline: Chanticleer's Chook Roast
text:
Investorswant transparency fromfundmanagers about what they take a stand on.

One of the first cabs off the rank duringOctober andNovember’s season of annual meetingswas that of Australian Foundation Investment Company (AFIC), the 90-year-old grandfather of Australia’s burgeoning listed investment company scene.

Wandering into themeeting inMelbourne was enough to make your correspondent feel decidedly young. Themedian agemust have been pushing 80, and as proceedings stretched into a second hour, two attendees decided a short nap was in order. But a couple of sharper souls braved the slightly clubby atmosphere to pose some tricky questions to the board.

The first camewhen the meeting voted on the appointment of Ross Barker as a director.

Barker was previously the chief executive of AFIC, and hismove to the board table struck at least one investor as beyond the pale.

“It is, in fact, a fairlywell-known governance issue nowthat a CEO should not remain on the board looking over the shoulder of his successor,” the questioner said.

No doubt about that.The days of CEOs moving straight into directorships are all but gone, and the questioner was quick to point out that AFIC would probably be dubious if a similar transitionwas to occur at a major Australian company.

Outgoing chairmanTerry Campbell said he understood the point, but referred to the company’s long and proud history, saying: “I felt that the corporate knowledge that Ross represents was very important.”

Not surprisingly, Barker’s appointment received the overwhelming support of the meeting, a space in which investors tend to followthe recommendations of their directors.

But the vote had raised a good question: is it a case for some moneymanagers of do as I say, and not as I do ?

This question was amplified following a discussion about the royal commission, in which several AFIC boardmembers expressed  their support for cultural change in the banks, and a shift away fromthe bonus-driven attitude ofmany big financial institutions. An investor raised a hand.Given the board’s clear distaste and concern for bonuses, had AFIC used its shareholdings in the banks to vote against their remuneration reports?

Campbell explained that discussion between investors such as AFIC and company chairmen, and the heads of board remuneration committees, had increased markedly in recent years. As such, much of AFIC’s work on pressing points about remuneration or other governance matters wasmore effectively done behind the scenes.

He also intimated that AFIC was not afraid to use a bit of stick and carrot; it may tell a company it wasn’t happywith an aspect of pay or corporate governance, andwarn that itmay vote against the following year’s remuneration report if things didn’t improve.

THE NUCLEAR OPTION It’s hardly a declaration of war, but it is typical of theway many institutional investors approach board discussions. In the clubby world of corporate Australia, going public with concerns is largely seen as the nuclear option.

But as the royal commission forces amuch higher level of scrutiny on executives of boards andmanagement, it would not be a surprise to see this trickle down to investors.

If you rail against bonuses – or a particular director, or an accounting treatment or some other corporate governance issue – surely you shouldbe able todemonstratehowthiswas reflected in theway you voted your shares.

Institutional investors in theUS are required to disclose howthey vote in relation to individual companies, but Australian investors seem a longway behind, despite some shifts in industry standards and legislative change.

Michael Chandler, director of corporate governance at the local office of advisory firmMorrowSodali, says the pressure on funds to bemore transparent with their voting intentions is rising, led in no small part by superannuation funds.These giants are increasingly bringing their corporate governance functions back in-house and beefing them up such that they can use their clout to affect change.

A good example is AustralianSuper’s policy of voting against director re-elections on ASX 200 companieswith single-gender boards, unless the company can demonstrate its commitment to gender diversity in the future.

But the best example is surely provided by Local Government Super.Three years ago the fund set up awebsite that hosts a searchable directory that displays not just its voting record, but its voting intentions.

Will themarket demand this level of transparency fromother investors? Perhaps not, but unless institutions are prepared to plant their flag on big issues – such as bank bonuses – theymight find themselves forced to showtheir hand on every single matter.
direction: bearish for AFIC due to governance concerns

headline: Construction company Pindan pledges it will pay creditors
text:
Funds management and construction group Pindan has confirmed it is behind in payments to subcontractors and other creditors, but says it is not about to become the latest victim of an industry downturn in Western Australia. Pindan director David Pringle said the company had a temporary cash flow issue and was relying on asset sales to pay creditors.

The latest issues for Pindan come less than 18 months after it raised $72 million from a 40 per cent sale to Singapore-listed Oxley Holdings. Pindan's funds management division is overseeing 18 property developments throughout Australia which it says have a total value of $1.3 billion.

Mr Pringle, who co-founded the company in 1977, said Pindan was not in financial difficulty, had a strong balance sheet and healthy order book.

"We just have a cash flow issue with swapping banks and some settlements of properties. We've sold some properties and that has taken a little bit longer [than expected to settle], and that has messed up our situation.

"They are all unconditional, so it is all set to go. We will be receiving about $15 million, and then all our creditors will be brought up to date."

One contractor said he was owed more than $80,000 for work carried out last year and was aware of other small businesses alarmed by the non-payment. The contractor, who did not want to be named, said his situation was becoming desperate after a string of collapses affecting even long-standing construction companies.

The appointment of administrators at 65-year-old Cooper & Oxley last month came after subcontractors trying to recover tools and equipment from building sites broke through fences and clashed with guards.

The WA government is reviewing the security of payment to subcontractors. It has signalled it will introduce new industry-wide laws.

The review will consider a demerit point system for registered builders who do not pay debts to subcontractors and suppliers in a timely manner and whether statutory trust arrangements should be introduced to protect money owed to subcontractors.

Mr Pringle said it was no secret that the property and construction markets in WA had been through a tough period. "We see the market as starting to turn. It is not going to take off, but it will improve over the rest of this calendar year," he said.

Mr Pringle said Pindan was over the fallout from its joint venture with John Holland to build the Eastern Goldfields Regional Prison, now owned by AMP Capital, under a fixed-price contract with the WA government.

"It was very difficult ... but all those subcontractors have been paid."
direction: bearish for Pindan and construction sector

headline: Australia's high company tax rate is wage theft on a grand scale
text:
Taxation Too many of the arguments against the tax cuts are misleading, hurting Australian businesses, jobs and workers.

 The purpose of the government's 10-year Enterprise Tax Plan plan is clear: To make sure Australian corporate tax rates are globally competitive so businesses in Australia and from overseas are incentivised to increase investment in Australia. Experience shows that increased investment increases jobs and incomes. This is fact - not conjecture as some contend - with the ABS reporting that historically 55 per cent of growth in GDP has gone to wages and salaries.

The recent reductions in US corporate tax rates has, along with other adjustments, supercharged the US economy and increased the risks for Australia. Almost 56 per cent of Australia's $5.8 trillion capital stock has been funded by foreign capital. The US is the largest source of foreign domestic investment in Australia, accounting for about a quarter. If our tax rates are uncompetitive that flow of foreign capital will reduce.

Furthermore, there is an increased likelihood that domestic capital previously funding investment in Australia will be increasingly invested overseas. For example, 23 per cent of superannuation fund assets are already allocated in international equites and investments. This could easily be expanded.

Many of Australia's largest businesses and biggest employers have spoken out in support of this plan, as have many economists, agencies and other commentators. While the risk of uncompetitive tax rates and the benefits of the Enterprise Tax Plan seem clear, some are opposed to it. But to oppose policies that create economic growth, jobs and ultimately income growth is wages theft on a grand scale.

Treasury estimates that the plan will add 1 per cent, or $18 billion, to GDP each year. As has been the case in the past, 55 per cent, or nearly $10 billion each year, could flow to increased wages and salaries - a huge and recurring benefit to all Australians.

Robust debate on the merits of any major policy should be welcomed. Opposition based on misunderstanding and misrepresentation should be challenged.

The reduction in tax revenue as a result of the implementation of this plan over 10 years is estimated to be $65 billion. This has led to claims that business would not be paying its fair share of tax, there will be less money to fund public services, it will add to the deficit and it is a gift to big business.

There are facts that can be brought to bear on these criticisms.

Australia's reliance on corporate tax is estimated to be $84 billion this year, and is the third highest in OECD countries as a share of total tax revenue. Australia has one of the strongest tax compliance regimes and the level of compliance is high. Some seem to delight in the discovery that not all companies pay tax: but all they have discovered is that not all companies generate a taxable profit in a given period.

Some might mistakenly compare the $65 billion reduction over the 10-year period of implementation with annual corporate tax payments. This is, of course, erroneous. The $65 billion should be compared with the estimate for corporate tax payments over the same 10-year period.

The Parliamentary Budget Office estimates corporate tax payments will be $1.1 trillion in the period. The planned corporate tax reduction should be compared with the net benefit of an $18 billion annual increase in GDP, or $180 billion over the 10 years (in today's dollars).

The payoff from the investment in the Enterprise Tax Plan is overwhelmingly positive. Importantly, the $65 billion corporate tax reduction doesn't disappear from the economy. Some $30 billion of the $65 billion is now committed with the first phase of cuts for businesses with revenue of less than $50 million. Treasury's MYEFO indicated that the budget is on track with a forecast surplus within three years, driven substantially by higher company tax collections. The 10-year implementation period sees much of the cost fall within a period of forecast surplus. So the income growth from the implementation of the plan and the taxes on it are an important contributor to the return to surplus.

We have clearly struggled to find a path back to sustainable surplus by controlling expenditure, so we must back policies that will help our economy grow out of deficits and reduce our country's debt.

Perhaps the most disappointing aspect of some arguments against the plan is that big business will benefit. The Business Council doesn't support the plan because big business will benefit, we support the plan because all will benefit. Businesses big and small depend on each other. It is estimated about half of small and medium business revenue comes from other businesses. Big business needs the flexibility, innovation, competitiveness and responsiveness that smaller businesses bring.

If big businesses prosper, so will all other businesses and - given that business employs about 80 per cent of all employees - so will all Australians.

Those that oppose the company tax cut need to explain whether they intend to increase taxes for businesses already benefiting under the plan. They also need to explain why their dislike for big businesses justifies depriving the many thousands of small and medium enterprises employing millions of Australians the benefit they will get from implementing the plan. They forget that for many in small business, their profit is their wages and that a reduction in tax is an increase in their wages.

I expect that the debate will continue for a while yet. My hope is that the debate will increasingly be informed by the evidence, that the shallowness of opposition to it will become clear and that at the end of the day we can support the only plan on the table to produce a growing economy and more prosperous community.

Grant King is president of the Business Council of Australia.
direction: bullish for Australian equities and foreign-invested sectors

headline: UK bid to join TPP may help counter China
text:
London | Australia would welcome the United Kingdom joining the Trans-Pacific Partnership because it would help extend British influence in the Asia-Pacific, Malcolm Turnbull said.

With Australia encouraging the UK to become more invested in the region once it exits the European Union and help act as a counterweight to China, Mr Turnbull also welcomed an announcement by the British government on Thursday to establish new embassies in the region.

UK Foreign Secretary Boris Johnson announced nine new embassies across the world including four in the Caribbean, two in Africa and three in the Asia-Pacific countries of Vanuatu, Samoa and Tonga

"An increased global footprint will also ensure that Britain and its allies are able to counter the malign influences of countries who seek to undermine the UK," Mr Johnson said.

"The UK will also use its world-class soft power to continue to win hearts and minds for the global good.

"After we leave the EU, global Britain will remain outward facing, open for business and a champion of the rules-based international order."

Mr Turnbull, who will hold talks on Saturday with British Prime Minster Theresa May, has been in London this week for the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting (CHOGM).

Mr Turnbull has spent a lot of time on the sidelines focused on pushing back at the use of infrastructure, loans and other soft diplomacy by China to spread its influence in the region. Vanuatu, where the UK will establish an embassy, is especially sensitive following reports last week of preliminary discussions between China and Vanuatu of China using a port it funded there as a military base one day.

Malcolm Turnbull received an assurance in London from Vanuatu Prime Minister Charlot Salwai that this would never happen. He also met the leaders of Fiji and the Solomon Islands and assured the latter that Australia would build an undersea internet cable connecting the Solomon Islands and Papua New Guinea. This squeezes out Chinese state-owned telco Huawei.

Tensions between Australia and China are at a high and Mr Turnbull did not deny an ABC report on Thursday about a tense stand-off between three Australian warships and the Chinese navy earlier this month in international waters in the South China Sea.

The ABC confirmed HMAS Anzac, HMAS Toowoomba and HMAS Success were challenged by the Chinese navy en route to Vietnam for a goodwill visit.

Mr Turnbull said Australia would not be intimidated. "All I can say is that Australia asserts and practises its right of freedom of navigation throughout the world's oceans, including of course the South China Sea," he said.

Mr Turnbull told reporters the United Kingdom would also benefit from joining the 11-member TPP, a regional free trade pact, once it had left the EU and needed new trading partners. "We've had very preliminary discussions and the UK have shown real interest," he said.

"You can see the attraction of it to the UK. If they were to join the TPP they would be entering into a high-quality trade agreement with 11 other countries in one hit."

Mr Turnbull said the UK would have to negotiate its entry but "nonetheless it's impressive to see the strong interest".

Australia is also keen to negotiate a bilateral free trade agreement with the UK but cannot begin formal talks until Brexit is complete. Mr Turnbull and Mrs May will discuss the matter when they meet on Saturday.

Trade was also on the agenda on Friday when Mr Turnbull caught up with Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi.

Federal Labor supports the push back against Chinese encroachment but believes the government has itself to blame for ignoring Pacific Island nations and cutting aid, leaving them vulnerable to overtures from Beijing.
direction: bearish for China-exposed exporters and miners

headline: Coalition may dump tax cuts
text:
The Turnbull government is splintering over its all-or-nothing approach to company tax cuts and may compromise by excluding the nation's largest businesses, including banks.

While the intention is to secure the passage of the full remainder of the tax cut package, and Finance Minister Mathias Cormann believes he can still win over One Nation, there are competing views within cabinet that the government should take whatever the Senate offers and then shelve the rest of the policy.

This includes securing a tax cut for companies with annual turnovers capped at $500 million, which independent senator Derryn Hinch is prepared to support This would exclude banks, miners, major retailers and other big employers.

"You live with what the Senate decides, that's it, drop it," said one minister. People have had their say and we're done."

Informal talks are going at senior levels within government following Saturday's shock result in the Longman byelection.

Although the 3.7 percentage point two-party swing towards Labor was within the average 3.8 point byelection swing away from a government, the surge to One Nation and the low 29.6 per cent LNP primary vote has rattled the Coalition.

It has sparked an internal discussion about the need for changes to the company tax cuts and other policies, and fuelled fears as to whether Malcolm Turnbull can resonate in Queensland where the Coalition holds eight seats by a margin of less than 5 per cent.

"Most of the anxiety up here is what Malcolm can do to reconnect," one Queenslander said.

The merger of the Queensland Liberal and National parties, which robbed the Nationals of their separate identity, was also being blamed by some for the rise of One Nation.

The government has already legislated a business tax cut for firms with turnovers capped at $50 million. The rest of the legislation aims to cut the rate from 30 per cent to 25 per cent for all businesses by 2026-27.

A month ago, the government postponed its company tax cuts push until after the super Saturday byelections because it was unable to secure the votes of two One Nation and two Centre Alliance senators.

After super Saturday, One Nation ruled out supporting the remainder of the package and South Australian independent Tim Storer also slammed the door on the tax cuts.

"The super Saturday results are an indirect vindication of my stand on the Turnbull government's tax cut program," he said.

"Voters in SA, Queensland, Tasmania and Western Australia all made it clear that they believe the government should focus on providing essential services and building the jobs and industries of tomorrow, rather than on company tax cuts, which the evidence shows have little impact on wages and growth.

Another option being canvassed by some, should the Senate stymie the remainder of the package, is to fast track the fall from the current rate of 27.5 per cent to 25 per cent for businesses with turnovers under $50 million. The rate is scheduled to drop to 25 per cent in 2026-27 and Labor will legislate to stop this fall if elected. Bringing forward the drop will put pressure on Labor at the next election over small and medium business.

As business groups urged the government to hold its nerve and fight for the full tax cut package, so too did Labor, which would be robbed of its most potent attack line if the government abandoned tax cuts for the biggest businesses, including the banks.

"If Malcolm Turnbull drops his key economic policy, the Australian people are entitled to ask what is the point of Malcolm Turnbull?," said shadow treasurer Chris Bowen.

"What is the point of the Turnbull administration? They have spent the better part of three years telling us the corporate tax cut was absolutely vital. If it goes, Malcolm Turnbull has to go and Scott Morrison has to go with him."

Former prime minister Tony Abbott advocated dropping the tax cuts for political reasons.

"I certainly accept the economic case for company tax cuts but there are no votes in company tax cuts because what the public wants is money in their pockets directly, not indirectly," he told radio 2GB.

Mr Abbott also called for a slashing of the immigration rate and took a shot at Mr Turnbull for saying the byelections were, in part, a test between him and Bill Shorten.

"Don't set yourself up to fail, don't set tests for yourself which are going to be very hard to pass," he said.

Australian Chamber of Commerce and Industry chief executive James Pearson said there was no evidence company tax cuts were behind the super Saturday results and called for calm in the Coalition.

Senator Cormann said he intended to put the legislation to a Senate vote when Parliament resumed in the middle of August.

He said the government was "absolutely committed to this plan" when asked if the Coalition would take it to the next election.

"If we keep our taxes in Australia high, we lock in a competitive advantage for businesses overseas to take business, jobs and investment away from us," he said.

Mr Morrison said "we are seeking to have the legislation passed because we believe that is what is in the best interests of a stronger economy".
direction: bearish for banks and large-cap equities

headline: Fears Beijing tightening HK link as mega-bridge opens
text:
Hong Kong | After years of delays and cost blowouts, the world's longest cross-sea link - uniting mainland China with former British colony Hong Kong - will open on Tuesday at curiously short notice.

President Xi Jinping is tipped to be heading south to personally cut the ribbon on the 55-kilometre network comprising bridges, an undersea tunnel and two artificial islands. Together, the structures will put major cities in southern China less than an hour's drive away from Hong Kong and the gambling hub of Macau.

The mega-bridge opening comes weeks after the inauguration of a new high-speed bullet train from Hong Kong to China. All this activity comes at a time when many in the special administrative region are feeling jittery about Hong Kong's closer ties to Beijing.

The bridge is part of China's strategy to link the former British and Portuguese colonies to the thriving cities in the country's south, linking up a collective economy larger than Australia's while creating a technology hub to rival Silicon Valley.

But China's decision to open the Hong Kong-Zhuhai-Macau bridge with less than a week's notice appeared to catch the Hong Kong government by surprise. Local bus companies charged with ferrying commuters across the new border crossings complained they would not be ready in time.

Hong Kong's transport secretary Frank Chan Fan, who was quoted saying he did not know when the bridge would open, said on Friday traffic could start using the link on Wednesday, the day after the official opening. He rejected suggestions the project, which traverses the mouth of the Pearl River delta, had been rushed.

"This is mega-scale infrastructure. During the last trial we did drills for three days. It has proven to be effective," Mr Chan said. The massive bridge snakes its way past the fishing villages of Hong Kong's Lantau Island into the South China Sea. A taskforce of more than 100 police will be deployed at the port and link roads on the Hong Kong side of the bridge, while bus "captains" have been recruited to ferry commuters across the border via shuttle buses.

Despite the government's assurances, one source confirmed Beijing had decided to push ahead with the opening without consulting Hong Kong officials. The huge piece of infrastructure was still not showing up on Google maps last week.

Tight control of the opening is seen by some as tactic by Beijing to show the former British colony who is boss.

The project is viewed suspiciously by many Hong Kong residents as a white elephant aimed mainly at allowing China to prematurely extend its influence into the former British colony it took back in 1997 on the proviso it would remain independent for 50 years. The cost of the entire project is unclear, but the Hong Kong government is estimated to have spent $US15 billion ($21 billion) on its share.

The new high-speed bullet train linking Hong Kong to mainland China upset pro-democracy activists because it involves Chinese immigration officers being stationed on Hong Kong soil.

However, the Hong Kong government maintains the bridge will help foster an economic boom in the region by enabling goods from factories in southern China to be more quickly transported to ports. The network would also boost tourism and make it easier for commuters to cross the border, it says.

The bridge is important for many Hong Kong-based Australian companies, who are advising local authorities on the so-called "Greater Bay" project - an infrastructure plan to transform the region into a technology hub. "The opening of the bridge and the high-speed rail mark a major milestone in the development of the Greater Bay Area," Jacinta Reddan, chief executive of the Australian Chamber of Commerce in Hong Kong, told The Australian Financial Review.
direction: bearish for Hong Kong-listed equities and regional property sectors

headline: Call for tougher penalties to stop trade secret theft
text:
Business is urging the Turnbull government go further with the protection of trade secrets and intellectual property after foreign interference laws passed by Parliament last week made the theft of trade secrets a crime for the first time in Australia.

While much of the focus with the government's package is on stopping foreign influence and meddling in Australian politics, it also created an offence punishable by up to 15 years in jail if a person dishonestly receives, obtains, takes, copies or duplicates, sells, buys or discloses commercial information on behalf of a foreign principal.

The laws echo the US approach, where there have been six convictions for economic espionage between 1996 and 2016. Five of the cases involved offenders selling or passing on information to Chinese companies, including the theft of microchip blueprints from Silicon Valley and a former Boeing engineer giving aviation technology to the Chinese.

The theft of intellectual property has also emerged as a flashpoint in Donald Trump's trade fight with Beijing, with the US President imposing $US50 billion of tariffs to stop "unfair transfers of American technology and intellectual property" to China. A report released by the White House last month accused China of engaging in state-sponsored intellectual property theft, estimating the cost at between $US180 billion and $US540 billion annually. Attorney-General Christian Porter said foreign intelligence services were targeting intellectual property and trade secrets held by the private sector and universities, and data from the financial sector.

"The Espionage and Foreign Interference Bill contains critical reforms targeting economic espionage, which is the state-sponsored theft of trade secrets," Mr Porter said.

Australian Industry Group chief executive Innes Willox said the new laws were a start to protect businesses from foreign threats but would also like tougher laws aimed at stopping the domestic theft of commercial information.

"Theft of trade secrets is anathema to industry and there have been a number of recent examples of industrial espionage by foreign entities in the defence sector and, anecdotally in other industries, where contract negotiations have been compromised," he said.

"Often, though, these offences solely originate from offshore where the perpetrator will be out of reach of our laws. But where such trade secret theft has local involvement, we welcome the strong deterrent now in place through the new legislation.

"More could be done generally to protect trade secrets and our current laws don't reflect the hard line taken by the United States and many of our other trading partners."

Clayton Utz partner John Collins, who chairs the Law Council of Australia's intellectual property committee, said while other countries had introduced statutory protections around trade secrets, he did not believe Australia was lagging behind.

He said the Australian approach had been to regard theft of trade secrets as a commercial matter than a criminal one, with victims able to apply for injunctions from equity courts such as the NSW Supreme Court or Federal Court to stop someone using stolen information.

Mr Collins acknowledged the lack of penalties for perpetrators was something that might be addressed in the future.

Key pointsAustralian Industry Group fears the domestic theft of commercial information.

It is urging a hardline stance taken by countries such as the US.
direction: increased regulatory costs and litigation risks for tech and IP-heavy firms

headline: ALP to make big polluters pay
text:
The nation's biggest polluting industries face emissions restrictions under a federal Labor government with a plan to make them offset excess emissions by buying credits or some other measure.

The baseline and credit scheme will most likely apply to the 350 biggest polluters, from manufacturing to LNG production, while the transport sector will also have to do its bit to reach an economy-wide emissions reduction target of 45 per cent on 2005 levels by 2030. Agriculture is likely to have land-clearing restrictions reimposed. While the policy avoids a price on carbon, opposition climate change and energy spokesman Mark Butler said there would be "an emissions trading-type scheme" for the manufacturing and LNG sectors.

News More reports p4-5

Matthew Stevens p33, Tony Wood p39
direction: bearish for manufacturing, LNG, and agriculture sectors

headline: Decade-low slowdown in housing hits McGrath first-quarter earnings
text:
ASX-listed real estate agency McGrath has revealed an earnings loss for the first quarter of 2019, as weakening housing markets and slower residential sales rock the sector.

Even with the boost of the seasonally buoyant spring selling season, which in the past five-year boom had started as early as August, McGrath made a loss in earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation (EBITDA) of $1.9 million for the quarter to September, as indicated in an update to the ASX yesterday.

In a signal to the market of worsening conditions to come, the company expects to only break even for the full 2019 financial year.

The culmination of slower sales rates, as evidenced by weaker auction clearance rates across the eastern seaboard (the final auction clearance rate for last weekend in Sydney will likely be confirmed at a decade low of 37 per cent) and rising stock levels due to fewer and unwilling buyers, has led to fewer settled sales and weaker residential trading.

"Over the financial year to date, we have seen a noticeable slowdown in the market, with a correction of residential property values experienced across the entire real estate sector," McGrath chief executive Geoff Lucas said.

"In addition, the tightened lending environment is impacting current transaction volumes. However, we believe this will ultimately strengthen the stability of our property market."

"While McGrath has also been affected by this downturn, we are encouraged by our number of new listings performing better than the market, reflecting the strength of our brand, our agents' commitment to customer service and our progress in continuing to attract and retain some of the best real estate talent in the country."

Outside of auctions, in the generally larger private treaty sales pool, the days on market for listings have also risen for McGrath evidencing slower sales in all areas. Total days on market have risen to 54 from 47 a year ago.

Overall in Sydney, days on market has risen to 70 days for both houses and units compared with 46 and 58 days a year ago respectively, September data from Domain shows.

In Melbourne, days on market for houses and units are 53 and 79 in August, compared to 42 and 82 at the same time last year.

Due to a lower turnover in sales in both auctions and private treaty sales, unsold homes are piling up despite a lower number of new listings, McGrath said.

The number of settled home sales in Sydney, Melbourne and Brisbane in the year to September has fallen 18.5 per cent, 15.8 per cent and 11.0 per cent respectively.

McGrath said it had however out-performed the market over the quarter in certain aspects.

In NSW and Queensland, where McGrath mainly operates in, it didn't suffer as big a slowdown in new listings compared to state averages.

In Queensland and NSW, its new listings were down 5.8 per cent and up 1.9 per cent respectively while overall new listings for the two states were down 6.9 per cent and flat respectively.

The company expects its results to be better in the December quarter where most of the spring bumper sales occur.

McGrath's share price fell 1¢ to 32.5¢ at the close of Tuesday.

The company's share price has fallen heavily from its listing price of $2.10 when it went public just less than three years ago.

A $35 million write-down in goodwill for company-owned agencies in the 2018 financial year followed a series of trading downgrades over the past three years and internal chaos within the McGrath management and board.
direction: bearish for McGrath and broader property/REIT sector

headline: Dow's modelling under scrutiny
text:
The man who would introduce Andrew Forrest to the east coast gas business will go head-to-head with Dow Chemicals in challenging the proposition that a massive new $5 billion west-to-east gas pipeline makes more economic sense than "virtual" links proposed by the likes of Australian Industrial Energy.

AIE founder and chief executive James Baulderstone will contest Dow's modelling at The Australian Financial Review National Energy Summit on Thursday and, along the way, declare that the official forecaster has got the east coast market outlook wrong, that a supply crisis is upon us and that importing liquid natural gas is the safety net that NSW will soon urgently need.

AIE is currently working to acquire commercial and industrial gas customers enough to support a plan to park a $250 million liquid natural gas import facility at Port Kembla.

But on Wednesday, Dow visited the AFR summit to refloat the game-changing potential of linking east coast energy customers to the gas wealth of the distant north-west of Australia.

To the understandable consternation of Baulderstone and others that need a gas price of better than $10 a gigajoule to secure their LNG import projects, Dow reckoned its massive pipeline can get gas to the east at a cost of $6/GJ.

Mind you, that number relies on being able to acquire WA gas at $3.25/GJ. It also relies, ambitiously enough, on transportation costs of a spare $1.25/GJ and on the owners of the $5 billion pipeline being happy with a capital recovery of just $1.50/GJ.

The Dow proposal would arrive with a capacity of 600 petajoules. That is pretty much equivalent to the whole east coast gas demand. And that, we are assured, explains the modest transportation and capital cost numbers.

The other reason the transport costs look low is that Dow has modelled the journey to Moomba. Getting that gas to Sydney would add another $1.50 or so to the transport cost.

Baulderstone will arrive at Thursday's summit sessions forewarned and forearmed. He will suggest that Dow has been wildly bullish on the cost of WA gas and of moving it eastwards. The next generation of Woodside gas in Western Australia will cost $6.77/GJ to extract and AIE has estimated it would cost $7/GJ to pipe gas all the way to Sydney.

In other words, Baulderstone will announced the Dow pricing model as illusory and disturbingly so. As we have said here often enough, Australian gas customers, from commercial to domestic, need to get used to the idea of paying for gas. The days of $6/GJ are gone. It costs more than that to get it out of the ground. And costs are only going to rise as the second and third generation coal seams are introduced to the system.

Baulderstone will also reveal, for the first time, just how competitive the transport costs of his "virtual pipeline" are. It will cost just 59¢/GJ to ship gas from WA to Port Kembla and just 66¢/GJ to source the stuff from Singapore. And accessing the pricing arbitrage made available by the US arrival into the seaborne LNG trade will cost just $1.32/GJ.

The way Baulderstone sees it, this transport advantage leaves AIE free to source gas from a broader base of supplier that sell their products on different pricing indices. To appreciate what that means all you need to know is that US gas is presently changing hands at about $US3.50/BTU ($5.00) and through most of this year that price has been ranging much closer to $3/BTU.

The electricity market is deep into a transition from which it cannot and should not retreat but managing the evolution in train requires leadership and collaboration from everyone, including governments.

Pretty much anyone who was anyone that presented to the Energy Summit - from Origin Energy boss Frank Calabria to the Australian Energy Market Operator chief executive Audrey Zibelman, from Energy Security Board chair Kerry Schott to the pioneer of climate change mitigation Professor Ross Garnaut - expressed some level of frustration at the failure of governments to coordinate and cooperate on a national energy policy.

Australia is in the midst of a transition unique in the developed world, Zibleman told the conference. No one is responding to the age of energy disruption with the same reaching zeal as Australia.

The great days of centralised base load generation are behind us. The immediate challenge is to manage a transition to the new era of intermittency without undermining the security or affordability of supply. And the challenge ahead is to replace the coal-burning cornerstones of the national grid with an appropriate blend of intermittent and firming generation and the quantum of storage that makes the most efficient use of the massive roll-out of renewable generation both at an industrial and domestic level.

Instead of a couple of dozen huge power plants, the Australian market will be connected to myriad generation options that stretch from domestic rooftops through industrial-sized solar and wind plants to flexible gas fired plants and, for the next 15 years or so, a decreasing number of coal fired monsters. The geographic footprint of this system will be unprecedented. So too will be the variation in the volume that this new family of generators will deliver to the system.

Against this backdrop, it is simply unacceptable that the federal government has so evidently given up on the idea that a national energy policy that welds the twin ambitions of security and emissions reduction.

"As you know only too well, there have been interventions upon interventions in this market over a ling period of time," Australia's new Energy Minister, Angus Taylor told the conference. "Everyone thinks the answer is easy, bipartisanship. But it is hard to see how that can happen when we [the government] have a 26 per cent emissions reduction target and the other side has 45 per cent. There is no room for bipartisanship."

That Taylor was gifted with his portfolio after investing quality time in undermining his own government's progress on delivering us the National Energy Guarantee says it all, really.

Scott Morrison told Taylor to see his new role through the twin prisms of power prices and supply security. And he sure has taken that direction to heart. In opening our national Energy Summit, Taylor welcomed the lower retail prices that arrived last July as a government success story and signalled an expectation that they would fall further in next year's annual re-set.

He also fulfilled the promise to resuscitate the reliability mechanism that would have arrived with the NEG whose arrival he worked to circumvent. And, with one minor lapse, the minister made it through his speech without blackguarding electricity retailers.

Fair enough. Energy ministers have to claim victories where they can these days.

But no one imagines that system affordability, security and reliability can be manufactured without some level of alignment and commitment on emissions. The NEG was nothing like a perfect plan. But it did approach the holistic policy Australia needed and, just as importantly, it was one around which a level of consensus had emerged everywhere but inside the government.

Instead of a policy, we have a government that needs to duck-shove issues of security and price to the states, that is waving the "big stick" at the gentailers and the gas producers alike while telling the industry that it should stop waiting for policy certainty.

"The government has been clear that the industry needs to stop waiting for policy certainty and just get on with the job of providing more affordable and reliable energy," Origin's Calabria observed.

"We've been getting on with it for the past decade. Not just in the absence of policy certainty, but in the absence of any policy consistency," he said after earlier observing that the industry had wasted a full year on the failed NEG mission.

"But as I stand here today, we are no closer to having a coordinated view of the way forward on energy and climate policy," Calabria said.

"We were so close on the National Energy Guarantee. It was an incredible effort by the Energy Security board to get broad-based consensus for such a wicked problem," he observed before succumbing to the sort of relentless optimism that keeps the wheels of business turning. "The goodwill and intent of industry and government gives me optimism we can get there again."

Let's hope so. But it might take a change of government to get there.
direction: bearish for AIE and LNG importers

headline: Company tax cuts to help retirees
text:
Tax and superannuation experts have rejected Pauline Hanson's claim that self-funded retirees will be worse off if the company tax rate for big businesses is dropped.

SMSF Association chief executive John Maroney said the One Nation senator's analysis was "not valid" because a lower rate would boost after-tax profits, which would flow through as higher dividends for all investors, including retirees.

"A lower company tax rate would result in higher earnings which would either then be distributed in higher dividends or used to increase the value of companies over time," he said.

"If retirees were getting a refund for franking credits in the pension phase, yes, that refund would be lower but they would have received the benefit of the higher earnings already."

Last week in Parliament, Senator Hanson said she would not support the government's Enterprise Tax Plan because "a lower tax rate equals lower franking credits and less money in the hands of self-funded retirees and those saving for the future".

University of NSW economics professor Richard Holden said that assertion was untrue. "Senator Hanson is raising a reasonable thing to think about, which is what about self-funded retirees? I just don't think her logic is correct," he said. Senator Hanson's comments relate to Australia's system of dividend imputation, which means shareholders get credits for tax paid at the company level.

While ever the corporate rate is set at 30 per cent, investors receive dividends franked at 30 per cent. If the rate drops to 25 per cent, credits will be franked at 25 per cent.

Let's say a company has $100 in pre-tax profit and decides to pay all of it out as a dividend. At a tax rate of 30 per cent, the investor receives $70 plus a $30 franking credit; at 25 per cent, the figures are $85 and $25.

If the investor is a super fund on a 15 per cent tax rate, the end result is $70 in dividends and a $15 refund under the first scenario, and $75 in dividends and a $10 refund under the second.

Where a lower company tax rate will make a difference is in the fact that companies will have more money to reinvest in growth, KPMG tax partner Grant Wardell-Johnson said. "That will manifest itself in longer-term capital gain for the domestic shareholders of Australian companies," he said.

"There is going to be less tax paid, more cash in the company, more investment and then greater dividends in the future."

But the really big benefits would come from Australia maintaining its attractiveness as a destination for foreign investment, he added.

PwC tax partner Paul Abbey said whether or not Senator Hanson was right depended on how companies responded. "If you look at listed companies, reducing the corporate tax rate means more money in the company's hands," he said.

"The question is, with that money do they maintain their payout ratio as a proportion of their profit, or do they maintain payout ratio as a gross number? If they maintain it as a gross number and attach a lower franking credit to it, shareholders are obviously worse off.

But if the payout ratio is 40 per cent of profit, for example, and after-tax profits are higher because of the lower tax rate, then the payout will go up.

"The franking credit is lower but the gross payout compensates for the lower credit and theoretically you end up in the same position," he said.

Senator Hanson told the Senate last Wednesday that comparing the US tax rate with that of Australia was "ridiculous when the new system in America restricts deductions but in Australia deductions are unlimited".

In any case, "foreign-owned multinationals don't consider corporate income tax rates in the investment decisions because they don't expect to pay any tax in Australia", she said.

"It is just too easy to contrive a paper loss or a non-taxable income. Lowering business income tax rates will not make Australia an attractive destination for investment unless government delivers globally competitive electricity and gas prices."

Key points

Pauline Hanson says a tax cut would leave less money in hands of SMSF holders.

Comparing the US tax rate with that of Australia is "ridiculous", she says.
direction: bullish for equities and dividend-paying shares

headline: Record labour shortages point way to focus on wage growth
text:
Labour shortages are the highest they have been since the global financial crisis, raising expectations of future wage growth, according to the latest National Australia Bank business survey.

The survey, which is seen as a leading indicator of the unemployment rate, revealed a sharp increase in labour unavailability as a constraint on output at the end of last year, rising from about 45 per cent in the June quarter to above 55 per cent.

About 17 per cent of those firms reported that labour unavailability was a "significant" constraint on output in the December quarter.

Both figures were the highest share of firms reporting labour difficulties since September 2008, before the global financial crisis, and marked a trend in the survey since mid-2015.

The NAB survey said lack of suitable labour was now a bigger constraint on firms' output than demand.

Macquarie Wealth Management economist Justin Fabo warned the NAB business survey's data on labour shortages should be ignored "at your peril. This indicator has consistently been the best leading indicator of Australia's unemployment rate for nearly three decades."

The rise in firms finding labour more difficult to get correlated with a sharp fall late last year in the percentage of workers not expecting to lose their jobs this year. "The [Reserve] Bank follows the NAB business surveys very closely and it will like what it sees.

"Alongside the strong global growth backdrop, the business surveys will give the bank more confidence in its own forecasts for growth to pick up domestically."

Following the survey's release on Thursday, the Reserve Bank forecast unemployment to fall further than its estimates three months ago. But the bank said it was still unclear whether the hiring surge would translate into wage pressures.

Despite the greater difficulties finding labour, the NAB survey found wage cost pressures would not grow much in the near term and had fallen back to previous lows of below 2 per cent.

However, there were signs of life for wage increases under enterprise agreements.

The survey forecast enterprise agreements would see average wage growth of 2.6 per cent over the next year, higher than 2.25 per cent reported in the first half of the year.

NAB chief economist Alan Oster said while the survey had some mixed signals the recovering economy and tightening labour market meant it was "more likely than not" to lift inflation pressures.

"The survey's measure of both near-term and longer-term [one year ahead] hiring intentions picked up, while firms are reporting increased difficulty finding suitable labour, which tends to have a high correlation with the unemployment rate and could foreshadow some upward momentum in wages growth."
direction: bearish for rate-sensitive sectors like banks and REITs due to rising wage and rate expectations

headline: Myer shares rise as CEO's plan comes into focus
text:
