Myer shares soared more than 37 per cent on Thursday - catching out short sellers betting on the retailer's demise - as investors reassessed the potential of new chief executive John King's turnaround plan.

Myer is the eighth most shorted stock on the market, with 11.3 per cent of its shares in the hands of short sellers who expected the retailer to continue to struggle or even collapse under the weight of $2.7 billion in lease liabilities.

However, sentiment towards the stock appears to have abruptly changed since Mr King unveiled his turnaround strategy on Wednesday and announced new deals with bankers, who have agreed to relax lending covenants and extend banking facilities.

Short sellers were forced to cover positions as Myer stock rose 15.5¢ to 57¢, a seven-month high, in heavy turnover of 50 million shares.

"It's been caught in this vacuum of lack of information and people put it in the too hard basket - now they're more positively inclined," said Simon Conn, a portfolio manager at Investors Mutual, which is Myer's second largest shareholder with a 9.8 per cent stake. "There's a greater understanding of Mr King's plans to turn around the business - as more people meet [him] they understand he's a very solid retailer with a strong background in department store retailing and an understanding of what needs to be done."

Mr Conn said there were encouraging signs in the July-half, with gross margins rising at the fastest rate for years and same-store sales in bricks and mortar stores falling at a slower rate. Other investors said the rebound was bigger than expected given the level of short-selling and wondered if a potential bidder was assembling a stake. Analysts cautiously endorsed elements of Mr King's strategy, including using exclusive third-party and private-label brands, better customer service and a stronger online offer to lure customers, saying it was similar to the plans of other bricks and mortar stores under threat from Amazon and structural changes in retailing.

However, analysts questioned Mr King's plan to wind back discounting, saying he would struggle to wean shoppers off price-based promotions, and said growth in gross margins from higher private label sales, reduced discounting and better sourcing may not be enough to offset operating deleverage from falling topline sales.

Analysts also said that while Myer's new lending agreements and relaxed debt covenants had given the department store chain more breathing room, debt repayments and covenants would constrain Mr King's ability to spend on stores and cut prices.

However, Mr King is putting his money where his mouth is, buying another 100,000 shares on-market on Thursday for $43,500, taking his stake to 150,000 shares and 2.4 million performance rights.

Citigroup analyst Bryan Raymond downgraded his share price target to 36¢ from 43¢ and cut his net profit forecast by 8.6 per cent to just $22.1 million - 32 per cent below Myer's underlying net profit in 2018. Mr Raymond said he was cautious about any retailer trying to unwind promotional activity given how accustomed consumers were to buying on promotion. "The degree of difficulty is very high when attempting to execute a strategy predicated on reduced promotional intensity, exclusivity and customer experience, without sufficient balance sheet flexibility to execute it," he said.

Deutsche Bank analyst Michael Simotas was also sceptical, cutting his earnings forecasts by 16 per cent and his share price target from 38¢ to 36¢.

JP Morgan analyst Shaun Cousins was more optimistic, increasing his share price target from 37¢ to 43¢, lifting his recommendation from underweight to neutral, and saying Mr King had announced a "credible strategy".
direction: Myer shares rise

headline: US tax changes, China policy boost for some
text:
The new year has started on a bullish note with increasing optimism around synchronised global growth and, after a year of political impasse, some reform is occurring in the US with the "Tax Cuts and Jobs Act".

The major changes here include lowering the company tax rate from 35 per cent to 21 per cent, allowing the full upfront deductibility of capital expenditure while winding back some deductions, taxing overseas profits at a minimum rate and limiting the deductibility of interest beyond 30 per cent of income. This will benefit a lot of US corporates through lower tax rates, but will also be a big benefit to overseas holders of US equities and Australian companies making profits and paying tax in the US. There are a raft of Australian companies that stand to benefit here, although the multitude of rule changes makes for relative winners and losers.

There are also second order effects from the tax changes through a potential acceleration in US growth. Changes to improve the deductibility of capital expenditure as well as the potential for an infrastructure stimulus package could further bolster US growth at a time when it and global growth are already accelerating. We are also seeing clear signs of an economic upswing in Europe and Japan with emerging markets beginning to join the party as well. This augurs well for companies with an exposure to a stronger global demand, the US economy and higher infrastructure spending.

China's policy shifts are also having an important impact as a combination of supply side reform and stronger environmental policies have limited the supply of many industrial commodities and seen prices rise. This adjustment to China's supply is allowing prices to move up in tradeable goods which should help to bolster inflation, but will also provide scope for a lift in manufacturing investment in developed economies. A good example of this is in steel where capacity cuts have seen China's steel exports fall considerably and has supported higher global steel prices. Combined with a strengthening demand profile this has created one of the most positive environments in decades for US steel companies.

Three stocks that we like that will benefit from these tax changes and growth trends are Aristocrat Leisure, Ansell and BlueScope Steel.

Aristocrat Leisure will benefit from the lower US taxes as well as from increasing consumption expenditure as more money is spent on gaming. This will be captured through the company's improving share in its traditional slot machine market as well as its recent moves to expand into the fast growing online and social gaming segments.

Ansell is well positioned to capture improving global growth and a stronger industrial cycle through its offering of protective clothing and equipment. The ability to pass through input price pressures from the current year will also help margins.

BlueScope Steel is well positioned with improving steel spreads in the Asian region as well as stronger steel prices in the US which will benefit its North Star operation. Its exposure to prefabricated building won't hurt either with a stronger global investment cycle.

Sean Fenton is a portfolio manager at Tribeca Investment Partners.

We are also seeing clear signs of an economic upswing in Europe and Japan.
direction: bullish for Australian companies with US earnings exposure and global growth beneficiaries

headline: NSW says real estate agents can't self-regulate
text:
The NSW government and the Real Estate Institute of New South Wales are trading more blows as they disagree on how real estate agents need to be governed against a backdrop of trust account frauds.

Last week the NSW government convicted a Sydney real estate agent, CLB Property Group licensee-in-charge Christopher Black, for a $120,000 trust account fraud, and recently convicted another Sydney agent, Zacharis Gregory Imisides, for defrauding $425,000. Byron Bay-based luxury agent Nicolette van Wijngaarden has also been charged with one of Australia's largest trust account frauds, of $3.69 million. REINSW blamed the frauds on the government, saying the new laws on trust account audits are too "passive" and has called for a parliamentary inquiry. Agents are required to conduct an independent audit but only auditors need to lodge those that show irregularities.

NSW hit back saying the new law cuts red tape while governing the industry. Last week, the government said it issued 47 infringement notices in a recent crackdown on agents in Sydney's eastern suburbs. It also called REINSW's inquiry demand "baseless" and said the institute was incapable of interpreting law and regulating its agents.

"Real estate agents are among the least trusted professions in the state and every month we can see why, with regular cases of fraud, underquoting and other compliance issues," Minister for Better Regulation Matt Kean said.

"There are many good and honest real estate agents, but the bad apples are ruining it for everyone else.

"We want a strong real estate sector, but that won't happen if we put agents in charge of regulating themselves.

"If the public doesn't trust the industry to comply with the law, how can it trust the industry to enforce it?

The real tension, however, stems from two longstanding issues. The government will not endorse the industry's pursuit of self-regulation, which it claims is REINSW's guise for "revenue raising", while the institute says the government stymies industry governance by allowing agents to qualify on cheap and easy professional courses.

"REINSW makes about $1.5 million a year selling CPD courses and it has campaigned to force real estate agents to do more CPD and to stop Registered Training Organisations - their competition - from providing mandatory courses," Mr Kean said.

He is also concerned with the poor quality of training by the REINSW.

But allowing people to qualify as real estate agents on a "one-week course" is not good governance, the institute's chief executive officer, Tim McKibbin, argues.
direction: bearish for NSW real estate sector and REITs

headline: A quick GST fix at the expense of real reform
text:
As Reserve Bank governor Philip Lowe suggests, Australians don't have the appetite for serious tax reform. Malcolm Turnbull and Scott Morrison have thrown $9 billion over a decade at fixing a system for carving up the $70 billion or so GST money each year among the states that has been blown up by Western Australia's mining boom bonanza. At its worst, WA received only 30¢ out of every dollar of GST money it would get under a straight per capita allocation. That has been an absurd distortion even by the measure of Australia's particularly over-wrought system of federal fiscal equalisation.

The Productivity Commission review has provided a political fix for Mr Turnbull and Mr Morrison, by dealing with the glaring outlier problem of WA. But they should have used the cue to kick start a new wave of reform of federal and state taxes and financial relations to help stoke the productivity growth needed to sustain the prosperity everyone says they want. Mr Morrison didn't even attach any reform strings to his financial overcompensation of the states, such as Infrastructure Australia's proposal to pay states for better managing their infrastructure planning.

Fiscal equalisation with the Australian federation has been going on since before the Commonwealth Grants Commission was set up in 1933 amid the WA's Depression era threats to secede from the federation. The modern version pools and shares out GST to lift government services in the poorest states up to the level of the strongest state, on a ridiculously precise and complex statistics-driven formula that lags behind changes in the real economy. It was never designed to live with volatility, like the fabulous spike in iron ore export revenues into thinly populated WA which made it the fiscally strongest under the system.

Now the federal government is spending $9 billion on top-up payments, much of it to WA, so that no state ever gets back less than 75 per cent of its GST. The very obvious trick is to give WA a bigger and more reasonable share of the $70 billion GST pie without taking away from the other states. Easy, get federal taxpayers to pay for increasing the size of the pie.

While politically appealing, this short-term fix also further entrenches the worst features of the system. HFE is a well-intentioned, socialist attempt to equalise the fiscal capacity of state governments to deliver particular services. But like socialism, it not only takes too little account of effort, it actually becomes a disincentive to the efforts of others. What happens when one state takes controversial policy reforms now to boost economic growth and its tax base in the future? Answer: much of the reform's fiscal dividend gets redistributed to the other states.

The PC's solution to this reform disincentive would be to equalise the states according their average fiscal capacity. Mr Morrison's solution to the WA outlier problem is to equalise to the second-strongest state, which will be either NSW or Victoria. It gets technical, but the practical effect of the PC recommendation would be to remove policy reform disincentives from the two biggest states covering 70 per cent of the federation's population. But it would have generated a bit more political heat and required a fair bit more over-compensation from federal taxpayers. Mr Morrison argues that the states don't have the stomach for serious tax reform. But NSW's Coalition Treasurer Dominic Perrottet is seeking political cover for reform, just as previous NSW premier Mike Baird was pushing federal-state financial reform before Mr Turnbull swept it off the table in late 2015 and early 2016.

With a one-seat majority and byelections looming in multiple states, it's obvious why Mr Turnbull and Mr Morrison are taking the cautious option. After the political disaster of the 2014 Abbott-Hockey budget, they are rebuilding the Coalition's standing bit by bit. But the uncomfortable truth is that all this incremental policy reform won't deliver the prosperity voters want. And it will leave Australia weaker when the fundamental geopolitical power shifts should make us focused on becoming stronger.

Incremental policy reform won't deliver the prosperity that voters want.
direction: negative for productivity-focused reforms and fiscal efficiency

headline: 'Misled' on energy, Abbott claims
text:
Tony Abbott's own words have been used against him by Energy Minister Josh Frydenberg as he sought to quell an internal revolt against the National Energy Guarantee being led by the former prime minister.

Mr Abbott and his followers believe Australia's energy policy should have no emissions reduction target, and during Tuesday's weekly meeting of Coalition MPs and senators, Mr Abbott claimed he was "misled by bureaucrats" when he committed Australia at the Paris climate talks to reducing all emissions 26-28 per cent from 2005 levels by 2030.

Tasmanian Liberal senator and Abbott ally Eric Abetz told the meeting the target was only ever aspirational.

In response, Mr Frydenberg read to the party room Mr Abbott's comments in 2015 in which he committed Australia to the Paris targets. "There's a definite commitment to 26 per cent, but we believe under the policies that we've got, with the circumstances that we think will apply, that we can go to 28 per cent," Mr Frydenberg quoted Mr Abbott as saying three years ago.

Mr Abbott, who wants to build coal-fired power stations, raised recent concerns by Tomago Aluminium chief Matt Howell about the unreliability of renewable energy and its battery back-up capacity.

Mr Frydenberg told the party room: "I'm glad you raised that, Tony." He said that before the meeting he had spoken to Mr Howell, as well as BHP's Mike Henry and BlueScope Steel's Paul O'Malley, all of whom had wanted the message passed on that they wanted the NEG implemented.

Seen as the last chance to end 15 years of policy uncertainty that has led to high rices and unreliable power, the NEG would require retailers to meet reliability and emissions reduction targets. The government wants the emissions target for the NEG at 26 per cent, which Labor says is too low and which the Coalition rebels say is too high and should not be there at all.

Liberal MP Trent Zimmerman spoke in favour of the NEG.

Others who spoke against it included Liberals Craig Kelly and Ian Macdonald, who asked why the Coalition was bothering with any reduction policy at all.

Prime Minister Malcolm Turnbull said Australia must meet its Paris commitments set by Mr Abbott in 2015 and "do our bit".

Mr Kelly wants a "hockey stick" approach to reductions in which there would be no emissions reduction at all until the final year and then, assuming the technology exists, it would all be done at once. This would require the reduction of 900 million tonnes of carbon pollution in a single year.

A draft design of the NEG released last week made it easier to phase in reductions over the years by allowing for a 50,000-megawatts exemption for small retailers each year and allowing bigger retailers who miss the reduction target one year to make up a proportion of it the next year.

Labor wants an emissions reduction target of 45 per cent on 2005 levels by 2050.
direction: bearish for renewable energy and utilities, supportive for coal and heavy industry

headline: ASX falls as trade tariff deadline looms
text:
The Australian sharemarket has fallen on the first day of the new fiscal year, falling on mild losses made by index heavyweights across the sectors.

The S&P/ASX 200 index closed 16.8 points, or 0.3 per cent, lower at 6177.8 on Monday, despite strong overnight indications that the index would start the week higher.

ASX futures were up 23 points before the open after an overnight advance of the three US benchmark indices and a rally in oil and gold prices.

The sharemarket traded fairly cautiously on Monday ahead of a string of economic data this week and the July 6 deadline when the US is set to impose import tariffs on Chinese goods.

Australian shares opened higher on Monday but fell throughout the day, led lower by the banks and resource stocks with most heavyweights only making mild gains or losses.

ANZ led the losses, falling 1.5 per cent to $27.83, Westpac closed 0.4 per cent lower at $29.18, while Commonwealth Bank fell 0.2 per cent to $72.70.

BHP Billiton also fell, down 0.9 per cent to $33.60.

Sigma Healthcare shares fell 40.1 per cent to 48.5¢ on Monday, hitting seven-year lows after the pharmacy operator announced it had lost a supply contract with Chemist Warehouse and cut its earning forecast.

The company lowered is underlying EBIT form $90 million to $75 million for 2018-19 and halved its underlying EBIT for the 2019-20 year.

Automotive Holdings shares fell 8.8 per cent to $2.60 after the company announced that a unit of China's HNA Group terminated the purchase of AHG's refrigerated logistics business due to "liquidity problems." The company announced in November it would sell the business for $280 million.

Village Roadshow announced the sale of the Wet'n'Wild Sydney water park to Spanish international entertainment operator Parques Reunidos for $40 million.

The transaction is expected to be competed in the first quarter of the 2019 financial year and will be used to reduce Village Roadshow's debt levels. The company's shares closed 1.7 per cent lower at $2.27.
direction: downward pressure on ASX 200, banks, and resource stocks

headline: AFR's new China partner
text:
For six decades The Australian Financial Review has promoted the business voice of economic openness as the surest route to national prosperity and security. We are now pleased to partner with a comparable voice in Australia's major trading partner - Caixin, China's foremost independent business and finance news outlet. Under a content-sharing partnership, Financial Review readers and subscribers will get access to a selection of Caixin articles on Chinese business, and financial and economic developments.

The media partnership comes amid rising tensions between China the United States that have been mirrored in the Australia-China relationship. Unlike what many had hoped, China's economic development has not led to greater political liberalisation. Instead, Xi Jinping has become more of an entrenched strongman leader. And Beijing has flouted international rules, such as its militarisation of the South China Sea. In response, Australia has introduced laws against foreign interference in our domestic affairs, and rejected a number of proposed large-scale Chinese investments in Australian telecommunications, electricity and, perhaps soon, gas grids.

Donald Trump's trade war on China has caught Beijing on the back foot. Australia would worry if the US-China trade war escalated, the new federal Treasury secretary, Philip Gaetjens told a forum in which the Financial Review partnership with Caixin was launched yesterday. But Australia also wants China to conform more to international rules and norms. At yesterday's forum, some suggested the Trump trade attacks could have the silver lining of challenging internal opposition to China's economic liberalisation. As Caixin's dynamic founder and publisher Hu Shuli says, rather than retreat, China's best response to trade threats is to "fling open its doors even wider".
direction: bearish for China-exposed sectors and foreign investment flows

headline: Swan bows out, proud of his legacy
text:
Being treasurer in difficult economic times is never a career enhancer, but being treasurer at an inflection point in the economic debate can be a career ender.

Wayne Swan survived both, albeit without ever really getting much public love for his efforts, and joins that rare band of politicians who get to leave politics at a time of his own choosing, after announcing on the weekend that he will not contest the next federal election.

Swan's legacy is framed by the global financial crisis, and by his ambitions to be a treasurer who oversaw a new wave of tax reform, an ambition that would be consumed by the debate about a new mining tax.

When Swan became treasurer in 2007, he was following in the footsteps of two treasurers - Paul Keating and Peter Costello - who were known as much for the lethal quality of their political presence as their policy records. There were big shoes to fill. But Swan often seemed to choke on the weight of expectation that he could take on a similar attack role.

A renowned work ethic and determination to deliver good policy outcomes only seemed to make it harder for him to be able to assert his message at a political level.

And, of course, he became treasurer at a king tide in both the global economy and the economic debate.

Swan became treasurer in late 2007, just months before the risks of the financial meltdown that led to the global financial crisis became clear. And he became treasurer just as the long-term tide was starting to turn on the global debate on the role of budgetary policy.

The Coalition had ridden the resources boom - which delivered unprecedented rivers of revenue to the federal budget - through the noughties with little discipline on spending.

In the last five years of the Howard government, the growth in government spending in real terms had been less than 3.5 per cent just once, and had peaked at 4.6 per cent.

Swan came into the portfolio determined to change things. But the GFC intervened.

It is without doubt that the first intervention by the Rudd government to maintain economic confidence helped stave off recession - a feat unmatched in the Western world - even though its second, massive intervention remains a matter of political controversy.

The legacy of those interventions was that Swan spent the rest of his time in the treasury portfolio not only trying to nurse a stubbornly wobbly recovery but persistent attacks on Labor's budgetary management.

After spending surged by an eye-watering 12.7 per cent in 2008-09, it was cut to real growth of 4.2 per cent in 2009-10.

Spending surged again in 2011-12 to 4.8 per cent but fell 3.2 per cent in 2012-13.

Labor worked hard to lock in its expensive legacy policies - such as the National Disability Insurance Scheme - in its later years, at the cost to spending in the years beyond which it would be in office.

Laura Tingle is The Australian Financial Review's political editor.
direction: neutral to slightly bearish for rate-sensitive sectors

headline: Blue chips confronting complexity
text:
If the next round of royal commission hearings that start on Monday go to the script of past hearings, then at some stage a bedraggled executive will be asked to explain why the bank's systems failed to pick up some bit of egregious behaviour or another.

"These are complex issues," the bank executive will likely tell Commissioner Ken Hayne. "And these systems are necessarily complex too."

That big companies with big systems are inherently complex is no surprise. But just how complex these blue chip groups can become was hammered home last week by Telstra, which revealed it would reduce the number of products it offered in its consumer and small business divisions from 1800 to just 20.

The reduction is perhaps not as dramatic as it sounds. Telstra chief executive Andy Penn explained that the company's antiquated systems meant that every different combination of product options had to be individually hard-coded. About 1400 of the 1800 products Telstra will get rid off are no longer offered.

Still, reducing your range from 400 options to 20 is still a serious cut. The complexity of the bank systems have been similarly highlighted by NAB chief executive Andrew Thorburn, who plans to reduce the bank's product range by half over the next three years.

Internal systems across the banking sector are notoriously complex, as the royal commission has repeatedly found. The chief risk officer at Commonwealth Bank, David Cohen, finished the previous round of the royal commission hearings, which examined small business lending, by admitting it could take up to five years to get the bank's risk systems in place.

Revelations about bloated systems and processes within big companies raise an obvious question: Why do such well-resourced companies, with such apparently smart executives, allow these levels of complexity to take hold?

Part of the answer is that complexity can pay. In commoditised industries where customers like to compare product offerings across a range of providers, complexity - typically dressed up as a special offerings or customisation - can make comparisons difficult. This was memorably described by the creator of the Dilbert cartoon, Scott Adams, as "confusopoly". He defined it as "a group of companies with similar products who intentionally confuse customers instead of competing on price".

I had a personal example of this recently when I rang Foxtel to ask why I was paying double what new customers were being offered for what looked like the same service. "Ah sir, but your plan is different," I was told.

And it was - but in such a minor way that I was left feeling like I'd had the wool pulled over my eyes as well as being over-charged.

For more evidence that complexity pays, it's also worth that Penn said the simplification of Telstra's product range would wipe out $500 million in recurring revenue. The royal commission and the Laker report into the culture inside the Commonwealth Bank also suggest big companies can use complexity as a bit of a crutch.

The Laker report mentions the complexity of its systems and processes no less than 30 times; complexity appears to have both reassured executives (if it is complex, it must be smart) and provided an excuse not to tackle certain issues.

Investors should welcome the fact that the increased scrutiny on Australian business - be it through the transparency offered be industry disrupters, or through events such as the royal commission - is forcing executives and boards to confront complexity.

Clearly, big companies have big challenges. But where complexity exists, business leaders need to ask who it serves.

Because as Telstra and NAB shows, complexity in a product range doesn't serve customers.

And as the royal commission has shown time and time again, complexity in systems and processes doesn't serve executives or directors.
direction: bearish for banks and Telstra

headline: Decision to keep CommSec is paying off with profit
text:
Commonwealth Bank of Australia's retention of the nation's largest online broker, CommSec, is paying off as the group shrugged off impairments to post a solid lift in annual profit.

Both CBA and rival National Australia Bank have committed to online broking, while ANZ Banking Group sealed a transaction last year that would see customers of ANZ Share Investing, formerly E*TRADE Australia, transition to a CMC platform.

Westpac Banking Corp hasn't commented on the future of its online broking operations, suggesting it remains committed to the business.

CommSec, short for Commonwealth Securities, posted a 13 per cent lift in profit to $114.2 million for the 12 months ended June 30, accounts lodged with the corporate regulator showed. Revenue was underpinned by brokerage revenue and commission and rose to $232.5 million in the period, from $211.4 million a year earlier.

Higher management fees and transaction services costs pushed expenses higher though. They hit $78.5 million, up from $76.1 million in the 2017 fiscal year.

But the 2018 results followed a slip in profit in the prior year as the bottom line was hit by impairments. In the year ended June 30, impairment losses were reined in to $7.7 million reflecting its investment in IWL, from $8 million in the prior period but didn't take the gloss off the headline earnings result.

CBA acquired ASX-listed brokerage IWL in 2007 just ahead of the fallout from the global financial crisis.

The accounts lodged with the Australian Securities & Investments Commission by CommSec do not include revenues from the branded margin lending business or the CommSec cash management account.

CBA chief executive Matt Comyn has a strong knowledge of the CommSec unit after he held a number of senior leadership roles there including managing director from 2006 to 2010.

CommSec is now run by Richard Burns, who took over in recent months as executive general manager of the unit from Paul Rayson. Mr Burns' LinkedIn profile lists him as the general manager of customers at soon-to-be demerged CBA mortgage broking arm Aussie Home Loans. He was a manager of customer experience in CBA's equities and margin lending division and had a stint at CommSec as strategy boss.

CBA's broader profit results handed down in August highlighted the growing use of mobile phones for trades and noted that CommSec had more than 225,000 active mobile users.

ANZ's decision to step back from online broking followed that of Macquarie Group and HSBC, which both outsource the function but retain their front end branding to customers.

CBA's decision to keep its dominant online broking arm, comes after the bank retreated from institutional equities and formed an alliance with Goldman Sachs three years ago. CBA is also withdrawing from many of its wealth businesses that have raised the ire of regulators and the Hayne royal commission.

CBA's funds management arms, including the global asset management, planning businesses and mortgage broking are being demerged into a separate entity to be called CFS Group.
direction: Bullish for CBA shares

headline: McGrath writes off all goodwill
text:
ASX-listed real estate agency McGrath is on the road to recovery after writing off all goodwill, booking a $59 million full-year impairment expense and a net loss of $63 million.

The damage to goodwill was related to company-owned agencies, which accounted for $57 million of the full impairment, which was previously flagged in a guidance update.

After a tumultuous year that resulted in the appointment of a new board and chief executive, the group, which floated in 2015, also booked a 23 per cent fall in revenue to $99 million. In comparison, it made $129 million in 2016-17 and $121 million in 2015-16.

With expenses and goodwill impairment now under control, the embattled company that has struggled to turn a profit since last year said it will turn its focus to stabilising topline revenue in 2019 by focusing on increasing sales of well-located properties and improving agent productivity. It warned, however, it would face headwinds in challenging residential conditions, including housing finance.

Cleared auctions in Sydney and Melbourne have fallen year on year, while house prices have also dropped in both cities.

Full-year fees from property management, McGrath's largest source of EBITDA at 39 per cent of the business, also dipped as the number of properties managed in company-owned offices fell to 7215 from 7463 last year. There were fewer referrals during the year due to the departure of agents.

Including franchise properties under management, the group's rent roll was steady at about 33,000 compared to 32,000 in 2017. Taking out impairment charges, the company made a small full-year earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation (EBITDA) of $1 million.

"With the new board and senior leadership team settled in, the last quarter of 2018 saw the beginning of a period of stabilisation, and we are in the midst of a business turnaround. We are already seeing good progress in culture and agent recruitment, and critically we are seeing some strong sales results within our key markets," chief executive Geoff Lucas said.

"While the current property market conditions are challenging, we believe this will continue to generate some very real opportunities as more agencies and salespeople will be seeking to join larger, well capitalised, quality branded groups like McGrath. Company-owned agent numbers returned to growth over the fourth quarter of 2018 and that trend has continued into 2019.

"Our strategic plan has, as its centrepiece, the goal of growing McGrath's franchised and company offices across Australia over the next five years."

The group's project marketing business had previously slowed down due to tighter regulatory conditions, such as the clampdown on both local and foreign investors through financing restrictions, but the company said there could be new marketing activity in growth markets in western Sydney such as Rouse Hill. However, it said it wasn't planning on a big push on growth in that area yet.

While its alliance with new shareholder, developer Aqualand, didn't guarantee McGrath instant project marketing deals, Mr Lucas said access to developers could generate new leads. The group will also beef up its technology to provide agents with more analyses across their sales. Agent resignations have also stopped, with the total number of agents at 560 agents.

It has zero debt on its balance sheet.
direction: downward pressure on McGrath shares due to revenue decline and challenging market conditions

headline: China to free jailed former Rio executive
text:
Shanghai/Sydney | Jailed former Rio Tinto executive Stern Hu will be a free man this week as China prepares to release the Australian citizen from a Shanghai prison where he has served a nine-year sentence for bribery and industrial espionage.

His release comes as two other former Rio Tinto employees consider taking legal action against the mining giant over the circumstances that led to their lengthy jail terms in China. It is believed they will ask Mr Hu to be part of any legal case but have not yet made contact with him.

Mr Hu is due to be released on Wednesday, according to Chinese court documents, although sources told The Australian Financial Review his release date could be shifted by a day or two to avoid media attention when he leaves Shanghai. Mr Hu is expected to briefly visit his parents, who live in China, before returning to Australia.

Peter Humphrey, a British corporate investigator who spent nine months with Mr Hu in Qingpu Prison on the outskirts of Shanghai, said the former Rio employee "kept his head down in prison".

"He avoided any outbursts or trouble. He worked diligently as a librarian, translator for the officers and a cell group leader," Mr Humphrey told the Financial Review.

"He never really expressed anger over his imprisonment but he was certainly dejected. He has lost a huge chunk of his life."

Mr Humphrey also said Mr Hu, who was suffering from heart problems and whose hair turned white in captivity, struggled to get adequate medical treatment. But he had regular family visits while in prison.

His release will be a reminder of a low-point in the Australia-China relationship when he was detained in July 2009. It comes at a difficult time for the federal government, which wants to avoid further offending China after parliament last week passed foreign interference laws and assesses whether Chinese telco Huawei can bid for sensitive 5G network contracts.

It has also emerged that two of Mr Hu's former colleagues, who have since been released from prison, are considering legal action against Rio Tinto.

Robbie Ge Minqiang and Jeff Liu Caikui, who were also jailed for bribe-taking, are understood to be in Shanghai. Another, Wang Yong, is due for release in 2021. Mr Liu declined to comment on whether he was considering legal action or how he was treated by Rio Tinto when contacted by the Financial Review on Sunday. "It is a sensitive time for me," he said.

"I have not seen Stern for the last nine years so I don't have any information about him. I don't know the exact timing [of his release]." The Financial Review could not reach Mr Ge for comment.

According to a source who is in contact with the former Rio employees, they are waiting for Mr Hu to be released from prison so they can "work out a plan to take action against Rio".

This could potentially involve looking at whether they take legal action against Rio, although it is unclear in what jurisdiction that would be.

"They will need to do a lot of thinking and advice-taking," said the source.

Rio Tinto declined to comment on Mr Hu's release or any potential legal action. It is understood Rio is not aware of any potential action by the two former employees. Mr Hu's lawyer at the time, Shi Keqiang, said he has no knowledge of any case, but Yang Bailin - another lawyer who defended the fourth employee, Mr Wang - said he had "heard something" but did not have any detail.

Mr Hu was detained at the height of the so-called "iron ore wars" which marked a low point for Sino-Australia relations. His arrest was criticised as payback for Rio's withdrawal from a joint venture with Chinese state-owned company Chinalco. There were suggestions at the time that miners were colluding to keep the price of iron ore artificially high.

Rio sacked Mr Hu and the three other employees for their "deplorable" bribe-taking and sought to rebuild bridges with China, opening a new office in Shanghai.

Two sources said there were negotiations underway with Chinese authorities to allow Mr Hu to visit his elderly parents in northeastern China before he left for Australia. It is believed his wife, Zhu Xiaoli, also an Australian citizen, has returned to Australia. He also has two grown children.

"Stern's plan was to go to Tianjin to see his parents and then go to Australia and live a quiet life," a source close to the situation said.

"When someone is in this situation, it's generally not safe to stay in China. It's better that Stern is out of the country while the dust settles," the source said.

Mr Hu was convicted during a three-day trial in Shanghai in 2010. He reportedly pleaded guilty to the charges believing he would be deported immediately after sentencing. Amid calls for leniency, former prime minister Tony Abbott raised his case during a 2014 meeting with Chinese President Xi Jinping. Mr Hu has had his jail sentence cut twice since being convicted.

Mr Humphrey said he expected Mr Hu to be transferred to a temporary cell block ahead of his release this week.

Mr Humphrey and his wife, Yu Yingzeng, were jailed on charges of "illegally acquiring personal information" in August 2014 after being hired by GlaxoSmithKline as consultants.

Key pointsStern Hu was detained in 2009 and later jailed for a offences including bribery.

His release may presage legal action against Rio by other former employees.
direction: bearish for Rio Tinto due to legal and reputational risks

headline: Countering the fake news of unfair tax cuts
text:
There has been no shortage of cant, nonsense and misdirection about the government's budget tax plan and how "unfair" it is. Yet detailed analysis by Deloitte Access Economics, published exclusively in The Australian Financial Review yesterday, shows that to be nonsense. In fact, under the Coalition's new plans, four out of five taxpayers will be better off, and the wealthiest 20 per cent will carry even more of the income tax burden, now sitting just below a stonking 80 per cent.

Under the Coalition's plan, the top quartile of Australian taxpayers pay more tax than under Labor, which instead uses the stealth taxation of bracket creep to pay down budget deficits. Labor targets its tax reductions at swinging voters in the middle of the income distribution rather than seek to counter bracket creep that will soon be pushing minimum wage earners up into the middle class 32.5 per cent tax bracket.

As Deloitte Access Economics' Chris Richardson says, high-income earners still get the biggest cash reduction in their taxes. But rather than proof of a regressive tax system, it is actually confirmation of its progressiveness because they shoulder so much of the tax burden to begin with. It's just maths, not morality. But this doesn't stop Bill Shorten from peddling the fake news that Malcolm Turnbull is dishing out personal income tax cuts for the rich and company tax cuts for multinationals, at the expense of the poor and workers, as if government spending isn't stuck at a record high plateau, the poor don't pay net tax to begin with and workers end up shouldering the ultimate incidence of company tax through lower investment, productivity and wages.

Mr Shorten will seek to turn the five byelections that will likely take place in July into a referendum on the fairness of the tax cuts. So, as with the Mediscare campaign in 2016, he will again traipse around to marginal seats and wilfully mislead Australians on how the Turnbull government is going to dud them. And Greens leader Richard Di Natale accused the 'wonks' at Deloitte of producing a report that simply suits their own corporate interests!

This goes directly to the central problem with Australian politics: that it has become consumed with carving up the pie instead of getting economic growth back to over 3 per cent to deliver an economy that can give Australians a pay rise while also funding Labor's spending monuments. Australia's prosperity relies on incentivising people to work, save, invest and hire. Yet the whole argument has now become bogged down by the sort of redistribution agenda last seen in Australia prior to the Hawke-Keating reforms when, as the late Singaporean prime minister Lee Kuan Yew said, Australia was in danger of becoming the "poor white trash of Asia".

Politics has become consumed with carving up the pie.
direction: supportive for equity markets and growth-oriented sectors

headline: Canberra's sloganfest kills policy
text:
Rhetoric | Both sides of politics are dragging the country down by mindlessly abusing and demeaning each other. We should be better than that.

Divisive public discourse and the dumbing down of policy debates into abusive slogans are dragging our nation backwards. They are not unique to Australia. It's as if we are methodically working our way through the Donald Trump playbook. Every week the US President tweets hatred: "Shady James Comey", "Cryin' Chuck Schumer", and his old favourite, "Crooked Hillary Clinton." Really, is this truly making America great again?

Malcolm Turnbull, who attacked his predecessor, Tony Abbott, for his obsession with three-word slogans, has economised, dropping out one word to describe the Opposition Leader as "Shifty Shorten", "Electricity Bill" and "Blackout Bill".

Labor, too, has travelled the low road, attacking the Prime Minister for being wealthy, showing no respect even for the office of the Prime Minister by describing its occupant not as "Prime Minister Turnbull" or "Mr Turnbull", but just "Turnbull". Yet the Prime Minister's own side coined his most derogatory description, "Mr Harbourside Mansion". Does this denigration matter? You bet it does. These are the nation's leaders and leadership aspirants. They set an appalling example to the people they seek to lead. They promote rancour, not harmony. They hope to profit politically from a divided Australia.

Labor denigrates "the big end of town" as if only the small end of town is worthy. Try googling Bob Hawke and "the big end of town". You won't find him making any such references, telling me as a new staffer in the mid-1980s never to use such divisive language in any of his speeches.

Across the parliamentary aisle, government ministers shriek "class envy" and "class warfare" whenever Labor announces a policy to close down tax shelters utilised by the better-off. No matter if closing down shelters is for the purpose of repairing the budget. They are sacrosanct in the modern era when conservative politicians seek to appeal to the most basic instincts of their key supporters. Broadening the tax base to lower the rates is just so, well ... old school.

News Corp legitimately criticises the Twittersphere's sloganeering as small-minded tribalism substituting for rational debate, yet posts on its front pages cartoon caricatures disparaging progressive politicians as clowns and crooks.

Just last Saturday, in its flagship The Weekend Australian, News Corp ran a piece from a regular columnist - a former union official no less - inviting Australian Labor Party members to discover "their inner commie". Hmm, ALP members and supporters are, in the minds of the Murdoch media, communists. And that's not small-minded tribalism?

The spectacle of politicians demeaning each other - aided, abetted and applauded by the mainstream media - is not the mark of a robust democracy but of a declining civilisation. In America, Trump needs only 26 per cent of eligible voters to support him in a total turnout of 60 per cent on polling day 2020 and he will likely be re-elected.

The question for Australia is whether we want to hold hands with the United States as it journeys down memory lane to the 1950s or whether we are energetic and courageous enough to create our own future.

That future will be built upon recognising the legitimacy of everyday people going about their daily lives without feeling the need to claim membership of an oppressed minority in order to gain political attention. Our future will rely on mutual respect between business, workers and those who claim to represent them. How often do you hear our leaders say the public is disgusted with the parties' parliamentary question time schoolyard antics, only to go back in the next day and double down on that same behaviour? It's as if slagging off at each other to the delighted whelps of the backbench is an addiction they just cannot kick.

My call to lift the standard of political behaviour will be seized upon by some as criticism of Bill Shorten. They miss the point. It's a plea to both sides of politics to return to the robust but basically civil behaviour that characterised the parliamentary exchanges of Hawke, Peacock, Howard, Crean, Beazley - and yes, even Keating, who overwhelmingly deployed humour to make his political case.

Having promised a "kinder, gentler polity" back in 2010, Abbott proceeded to rip the place apart, but it's no excuse for the 45th Parliament continuing in the tradition of abusive hyper-partisanship. As a nation we are much better than that.

Craig Emerson is managing director of Craig Emerson Economics and adjunct professor at Victoria University's College of Business.
direction: negative for market stability and policy certainty

headline: Witness pair gives bank a big break
text:
The two central witnesses in Westpac Banking Corporation's interest rate-fixing case were able to withstand intense questioning from the regulator's barristers with their credibility mostly intact, providing the bank crucial legal cover.

Traders Colin Roden and Sophie Johnston gave reliable testimony, Federal Court judge Jonathan Beach said in his judgment, despite attempts by lawyers for the Australian Securities and Investments Commission to portray them as misleading.

The finding is important to Westpac, which took a risk by backing the integrity of its employees in a high-profile case against a well-resourced opponent that could afford three Queen's counsel - Philip Crutchfield, Peter Collinson and Michael Borsky.

One of the big advantages for Westpac was that Mr Roden, who has since retired, said he didn't remember any of the specific trades.

Mr Roden's lack of memory about events almost eight years earlier made it harder for ASIC's barristers to pin him down about comments in recorded telephone calls that ASIC said were proof he was manipulating an interest rate benchmark. Mr Roden persuaded the judge that some of his apparently incriminating comments were humour or banter. Justice Beach even expressed some empathy for Mr Roden for emphasising in his innocence stronger terms in a "reflexive stance to the length and combative nature of the cross-examination".

"In my opinion, for the most part Mr Roden gave responsive answers," Justice Beach wrote in Thursday's judgment. "He also made concessions where called for."

Another trader, Sophie Johnston, featured in phone calls with Mr Roden that were important parts of the evidence. ASIC's lawyers said Ms Johnston appeared overly prepared in her evidence, a criticism that didn't work with the judge. "The fact that she was well prepared in giving her evidence should be a reason to compliment rather than to condemn," he wrote.

ASIC's lawyers complained that Ms Johnston qualified more than 100 of her answers with the word "potentially" or the expression "not necessarily".

"As to Ms Johnston being criticised for being unduly cautious and careful in her evidence ... I agree with Westpac that this is not a balanced criticism."

Justice Beach wasn't entirely convinced by the two traders' evidence. He found that, despite the bank's denials, they attempted to influence the bank bill swap rate, a benchmark of the daily lending price between Australian banks.

If the judge thought the witnesses were lying, the consequences for Westpac could have been worse. Westpac was represented by a senior counsel from Sydney, Matthew Darke. "We're pleased overall that he [the judge] found the witnesses overall reliable and truthful," a bank spokesman said.

Still, Westpac acknowledges that it has changed how its traders operate. "These are events from 2010," the spokesman said. "We've done a lot of training since then."
direction: Westpac shares likely to rise

headline: Morrison tests China's resolve with Pacific build-up
text:
The government's relationship with China faces a stiff test, with Scott Morrison to announce today a major foreign policy pivot towards the South-West Pacific, and Treasurer Josh Frydenberg deciding to block the $13 billion takeover of the APA Group by Hong Kong's CK Group.

With Foreign Minister Marise Payne in Beijing to meet her Chinese counterpart and thaw relations, the Prime Minister will commit to a $2 billion regional infrastructure fund, $1 billion in boosted export assistance, increased security, sporting and cultural ties, and five new embassies as it escalates its foreign policy focus on a region in which China is seeking to spread its influence.

Mr Morrison, who is scheduled to meet China's leaders at next week's East Asia Summit in Singapore and the Asia-Pacific Economic and Co-operation Summit in Port Moresby, will announce the regional "step up" in a speech in Townsville. He will describe the South-West Pacific as "where Australia can make the biggest difference in world affairs".

"My government is returning the Pacific to where it should be - front and centre of Australia's strategic outlook, foreign policy and personal connections, including at the highest levels of government," he will say.

"While we have natural advantages in terms of history, proximity and shared values, Australia cannot take its influence in the South-West Pacific for granted. And too often we have." The government rejected that either the step up in the South-West Pacific or the APA Group decision were motivated by China.

Although CKI group is a Hong Kong company, there were concerns about Beijing's reach into Hong Kong. The APA bid was scrutinised on national security grounds by the government's newly-created Critical Infrastructure Committee and, as The Australian Financial Review revealed in September, there were national security concerns about the bid.

Mr Frydenberg rejected the bid yesterday on the grounds it would result in an "undue concentration of foreign ownership by a single company group in our most significant gas transmission business" and he stressed the decision was "not an adverse reflection on CK Group or the individual companies".

"My preliminary view reflects the size and significance of APA Group," he said. Senator Payne is expected to explain the decision to the Chinese today.

The Morrison government is working on rebuilding the relationship with Beijing which deteriorated under Malcolm Turnbull but Mr Morrison also acknowledged last week Australia must navigate a "testing" and higher degree of strategic competition between the United States and China.

Today's announcements in Townsville will build on last week's confirmation by Mr Morrison that Australia will upgrade the Lombrum naval base on Manus Island, Papua New Guinea, and will involve a reprioritisation of the aid budget.

Mr Morrison will pledge $2 billion in aid money and long-term loans to establish an Australian Infrastructure Financing Facility for the Pacific (AIFFP) which will be used for high-priority infrastructure projects in the region in telecommunications, transport and water. This fund, similar to one Labor has also promised, will be in addition to a trilateral initiative involving Australia, Japan and the United States which will act as a rival to China's Belt and Road Initiative.

Australia, the US and Japan are expected to announce next week the first wave of Indo-Pacific projects to be supported under this program as part of US Vice-President Mike Pence's attendance at the APEC summit.

Mr Morrison will also announce new diplomatic missions in Palau, the Marshall Islands, French Polynesia, Niue and the Cook Islands.

He will boost by $1 billion the budget of Efic, Australia's export financing agency to encourage Australian businesses to invest more in the region, especially in infrastructure.

In a snub to the ABC, Mr Morrison has been negotiating with commercial networks about broadcasting Australian content into the regions. The Abbott government shut the ABC's Australia Network, which broadcast into the region, sparking criticism it was killing off an important tool for soft diplomacy. Mr Morrison is reversing that but not with the ABC.

"I've been speaking to Free-TV Australia and the commercial TV networks about how we get more of our Australian content into the region. Our Pacific family switching on to the same stories, news dramas and sports we are watching at home," he will say.

Mr Morrison will also announce expanded sporting links as well as increased ties between military and police. This will include annual meetings of defence, police and border security officials. "It's part of a larger vision of Australia as a force for good in the Pacific, working with others to ensure our region is secure, stable and sovereign," Mr Morrison will say.

"We seek co-operation with others - New Zealand, the United States, Japan, China, France and the UK - to ensure our engagement supports common goals.

Australia has already stepped up its relationship with Vanuatu, following reports Beijing had held preliminary discussions with Vanuatu about building up its military presence.

And Australia stepped in to fund an undersea internet cable between the two nations, squeezing out a rival bid by Chinese telco Huawei.
direction: bearish for APA Group and broader market sentiment

headline: Property market is gripped by FOOP
text:
After a few months of their house sitting forlornly on the market, my neighbourhood friends have just pulled down the "For Sale" sign.

The planned auction was cancelled weeks ago due to lack of competitive bidders. Since then, buyer interest has been virtually non-existent - certainly at anything like the price the would-be vendors had been hoping for and were told they would attract when first talking to an agent earlier this year.

Downsizing, they now gloomily accept, will have to wait, possibly many years. This has become a common Sydney and Melbourne story, another example of the dramatic ups and downs in the Australian property market.

Instead of dinner party conversations lamenting high house prices impeding the ability of anyone's children to buy in, the bigger concern now is how much further prices will fall. OK, that might sound absurdly indulgent given the dramatic increase in house prices over the previous five years. Compared to a 70 per cent plus rise in Sydney and close to 60 per cent in Melbourne, what does a single-digit decline over the past year really matter?

But more economists and industry players are now predicting much bigger declines to come, certainly in Australia's biggest two cities. Auction clearance rates are now in the mid-30 per cent range. The last time that happened was exactly a decade ago in late 2008 as the global financial crisis hit confidence and the economy.

This decade's version is due to a potent combination of factors. It includes regulators moving to cool investor purchases, banks tightening lending standards in response to revelations and potential recommendations from the royal commission, governments making it harder and more expensive for foreign (Chinese) investors to bid while a glut of new apartments has come on to the market.

Then there's Labor's plan to ban negative gearing for investment properties and half the capital gains taxes concession. Given the state of politics, that no longer looks like a theoretical or distant risk to prices.

The result is that Fear Of Missing Out has been replaced by Fear of Over Paying - when it looks as if the market will only become cheaper in the months and probably the years ahead.

Yet the abrupt change in sentiment is happening when the Australian economy is still thriving, unemployment is falling and the government is insisting better times are ahead for wages growth.

So what happens now? Alert readers may recall my credibility on this issue is threadbare given my strong advice to my 20-something daughter to rush to buy a modest apartment at an eye-watering price at the very peak of the market midway through last year.

My about-to-be son-in-law has kept a dignified silence on this so far but my daughter is blunter about why they will never again take notice of my recommendations - except perhaps to do the opposite.

For obvious reasons, I have since restrained myself from offering any property suggestions to my sons.

I am, however, reassured to know I am far from alone in my inability to pick what would happen in the property market in 2018.

Just one economist surveyed by The Australian Financial Review in mid-last year predicted prices would fall - and his estimate of a 4 per cent fall in Sydney is already overtaken. Other economists believed prices would increase in Sydney by as much as 9 per cent. It's another example of how market excess eventually produces an inevitable but somehow unexpected overreaction - creating new unintended consequences that will take too much time to recognise, let alone correct.

The slowdown in prices in Sydney and Melbourne is still not likely to translate into the more alarmist predictions of 40 per cent market falls - but what would I know? Rather more credentialled types like AMP Capital's Shane Oliver, for example, predict the market will fall 20 per cent peak to trough. That's probably the good news.

The bad news is that property has been a major driver of economic growth and that a big reduction in development activity will inevitably spread more broadly.

Similarly, the obverse wealth effect when households hear the average house is dropping $1000 a week in value will increasingly dent consumer confidence and willingness to spend.

That's the backdrop to the Coalition's enthusiasm for warning of the terrible risks to prices and the entire property market due to Labor's policy of banning negative gearing for other than new properties and halving the 50 per cent capital gains taxes concession.

The Coalition attempted to make this Labor policy an election campaign issue in 2016 but with stratospheric property prices and great concern about housing affordability, this didn't have much political impact. Many voters thought the result would be beneficial in cooling an apparently out-of-control market.

The property outlook these days is obviously very different. A report commissioned by Master Builders Australia says the ALP policy would mean a big hit to the economy due to a massive fall in housing construction activity over the next five years, worsening the current downturn.

That sounds suspiciously like another unintended consequence looming. It makes Labor's policy more high risk - politically as well as economically.

But Chris Bowen, Labor's treasury spokesman, insists the slowdown will just allow it to "be implemented more smoothly" because so many investors have left the market.

"I mean the property market is slowing now but it will quicken at some point and investors will roar back into the market, but we are getting the policy settings right now to ensure that first-home buyers are on a more level playing field," he said on ABC radio.

Try selling that idea to a Sydney or Melbourne homeowner in a rapidly falling market. Feels more like catching a knife.
direction: bearish for residential property and related sectors

headline: ICD Property lands in SkyCity carpark
text:
Melbourne-based ICD Property, the Chinese-backed developer of one of Melbourne's largest apartment towers, has expanded across the Tasman, acquiring an Auckland car park for $40 million from SkyCity Entertainment Group.

The 427-bay Federal Street carpark is near to SkyCity's Auckland casino and had been held on the books at $22 million.

"As our first international acquisition this site presents an exciting step in ICD's growth," said ICD's deputy managing director, Matt Khoo.

"Auckland drew our attention because it has strong market fundamentals, strong economic growth and in recent years has become a truly global city. We're looking forward to the opportunities this site presents from an investment and development perspective."

Colliers International handled the process.

Four years ago, ICD, led by managing director Michael Mai, won backing from giant Chinese property developer Sino-Ocean Land Holdings to develop a 63-level, $300 million high-rise tower on A'Beckett Street in the Melbourne CBD.

In Sydney, ICD is part of a joint venture with the City Tattersalls Club to develop a boutique 100-room hotel and 267-apartment tower at the club's three interconnected buildings on Pitt Street.

For SkyCity, the divestment of the Federal Street car park in Auckland follows a broader strategy of moving to an 'asset-lighter' strategy by divesting non-core assets and businesses.

The proceeds of the sale would be used, initially, to pay down debt and then fund future growth opportunities, the casino operator said.
direction: ICD Property shares face pressure from high leverage and expansion risks, while SkyCity benefits from debt reduction

headline: ACCC calls for ban on insurance commissions
text:
The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has recommended banning commissions paid to general insurance brokers in northern Australia, as part of a sweeping set of reforms to the insurance market in that part of the country.

The competition watchdog found conflicts of interest in the sector were "common and significant" in areas around and above the Tropic of Capricorn, which runs through Rockhampton in Queensland, through Alice Springs to Carnavon on the west coast.

The ACCC also recommended exempting insurance products from stamp duty, making products more comparable and applying unfair contract terms protections to insurance products.

It also recommended the establishment of a national insurance comparison website, and called for more transparency in insurance contracts.

In the interim report of its Northern Australian Insurance Inquiry, published on Tuesday, the ACCC found people in the north had seen their insurance premiums rocket 130 per cent in real terms over the past 10 years, compared with 50 per cent in the rest of the country.

It blamed extreme weather and the cost of servicing the area for these high premiums, as well as a move towards setting premiums according to individual addresses rather than pooling the risk across regions.

It found that insurers in these regions, rather than competing for market share, were setting premiums too high to deter consumers from taking out cover. It found insurers were often operating at a loss in these areas.

"This is the most comprehensive analysis ever undertaken of the home, contents and strata insurance markets in northern Australia," ACCC deputy chairman Delia Rickard said.

"The anxiety caused by often unaffordable insurance premiums, and the inability of some people to even insure their homes, is causing real distress for people living in northern Western Australia, northern Queensland and the Northern Territory, and has the potential to limit economic development in the region."

Ms Rickard said the proposed reforms did not need to be restricted to northern Australia, but could apply nationally.

Removing stamp duty would require the agreement of state and territory governments. The ACCC said if this could not be agreed on, stamp duty should be based on the sum insured rather than the premium, suggesting revenue collected should go towards improving insurance affordability and funding mitigation work.

The inquiry began in October 2017, and will run for three years.
direction: bearish for general insurance brokers and insurers

headline: Three reasons why Latitude wants more than the banks
text:
Consumer credit business Latitude Financial Group's owners are expected to ask equities investors to pay a "mid-teens" multiple for shares at the company's $2 billion-odd initial public offering.

Formal marketing for the slated IPO is due to get under way when reports from Latitude's five-strong lead manager syndicate hit the printers today, which means they will be on fund managers' desks in no time.

As always, the biggest talking point will be Latitude's valuation and how the multiples stack up against similar businesses in Australia and offshore.

Latitude is expected to be compared to the major banks, which are trading at 12 to 13 times forward profit. It is similar in nature to the banks - Latitude offers personal loans, credit cards and point of sale finance - albeit at the pointier end of the market, and becoming more so as the big four shrink towards their core products.

Latitude's lead managers are expected to argue the company should be valued at a bigger multiple than the big four. The three-pronged pitch is easy enough to understand; Latitude offers more earnings growth than the banks, has a lower cost-to-income ratio and a return on equity of more than 20 per cent.

It means we could see valuation ranges in the reports stretch into the high teens. Analysts will no doubt have a basket of growth-type stocks to support such numbers.

Fund managers will spend the next couple of weeks picking through the thesis. They're booked for meetings with the lead managers' analysts starting later this week, and expect to be asked to start thinking about taking a cornerstone stake in the float next month.

They know Latitude is not without risks.

ASIC's new-found focus on lenders and their repayment rules, product disclosure and interest rates hangs over the company, as it does other players in the financial services landscape. Fundies are tipping a comprehensive risk section in Latitude's prospectus, particularly after smaller float Prospa's IPO was pulled in June right as it was due to hit the ASX boards.

There is also expected to be significant interest in US-listed Synchrony Financial, an American consumer finance company that shares an ex-GE past with Latitude, which is due to report its June quarter numbers later this week.

Synchrony Financial shares are down 13 per cent this year to be trading at 9.5-times next 12 month earnings per share, according to S&P Capital IQ, partly on fears over a large contract it has with Walmart.

Latitude has similarly large contracts with some of its clients, including Harvey Norman, which means there will be plenty of focus on the quality of the company's earnings in coming weeks.

Deutsche Bank, Goldman Sachs and UBS are overseeing Latitude's marketing as global co-ordinators, while Macquarie Capital and Morgan Stanley are also lead managers.
direction: bullish for Latitude Financial Group shares

headline: Premier attacks gold miners over royalty fight
text:
West Australian Premier Mark McGowan has assured iron ore miners he will take a hands-off approach to the industry despite the state's unprecedented debt problems.

Mr McGowan said iron ore miners were paying their way in resource-rich WA and ruled out tampering with state agreements as a way to raise revenue.

However, Mr McGowan savaged gold miners for their successful campaign against royalty increases proposed in last year's state budget.

The gold industry campaigned strongly against two proposals to raise royalties - one that would have raised $392 million over the forward estimates and a compromise proposal that was expected to raise $332 million.

"Gold miners are making a fortune out of the state and we tried to scoop back a little bit for the public, and the Liberals and the Nationals opposed it," he said.

"Gold miners on average were making $500 an ounce, that's minimum, making that profit, and we tried to get $14 net back an ounce for taxpayers and they rejected it.

"The gold miners know they could afford it. They just knew that they had some patsies that they could get to stop it, so that if they didn't have to pay it, they wouldn't. They had the Liberals and Nationals in their pocket."

In the lead-up to last year's state election, iron ore miners BHP Billiton and Rio Tinto saw off a WA Nationals push to impose a tax rise that would have cost them about $3 billion a year.

Former WA Nationals leader Brendon Grylls campaigned for the lease rental fee on their operations to rise from 25¢ a tonne to $5.

Mr McGowan said there were two significant differences between the iron ore and gold industries in indicating he would not revisit the rent issue or a proposal to convince the Pilbara giants to buy out the fees in a lump sum.

"One is iron ore is already paying a royalty, which is 10 per cent of mine head value ... they are already doing that, whereas gold is not, gold is paying about 4 per cent of mine head value," he said. "The second thing is, it is tied up in State Agreement Acts and they are contracts. Ripping up contracts unilaterally is a fraught thing to do."

Mr McGowan said ripping up state agreements in the iron ore sector would introduce significant sovereign risk and have negative consequences for investment in Australia.
direction: bearish for gold mining shares

headline: Aveo hit in earnings guidance fail
text:
Retirement operator Aveo has taken a hammering on the stockmarket after it declined to confirm earnings guidance at its annual shareholder meeting yesterday.

Its shares had fallen 10.4 per cent, down 19¢ to $1.69 at the end of trading on Wednesday.

The retirement giant, which is 22.7 per cent owned by Malaysia's Mulpha, has shed nearly a quarter of its value over the past two years in the wake of damaging allegations about its business practices and treatment of residents.

"Due to current market conditions and uncertainty around future sales levels, management will not be confirming financial year 2019 earnings per share guidance today," chief executive Geoff Grady said.

"Negativity around residential markets will, however, both constrain the number of sales able to be written and the time to affect settlement timeframes. This has been particularly noticeable in Sydney."

Mr Grady noted there would be a "significant skew of profits" to the second half due to the timing of new retirement development unit deliveries and the settlement of non-retirement lots.

Aveo is a targeting full-year distribution based on a payout range of 40 per cent to 60 per cent of underlying profit.

At its August results, the retirement village owner and operator gave guidance for its 2019 earnings 20.4¢ per share. Its shares jumped on its announcement that it had launched a strategic review to boost its share price, including bringing in capital partners.

On Wednesday, Mr Grady said Aveo and adviser Bank of America Merrill Lynch had inquiries from a number of parties interested in transactions "either as a whole or for a partnership at the asset level".
direction: bearish for Aveo shares and retirement sector

headline: Singapore, Bristol show what's possible
text:
Inspiration 'Problem-solving' cities are leading the way.

While the creation of a truly smart city in Australia may appear to be a complex and long-range task, proponents are finding inspiration in successful projects established in other parts of the world.

Participants in a recent round table hosted by The Australian Financial Review and the Australian Computer Society on the topic pointed to initiatives underway everywhere from Britain to Singapore as evidence that progress was not only possible but led to demonstrable benefits for the urban centres involved.

General manager workplace experiences at Mirvac, Paul Edwards, says a good example of what is possible can be seen in the British city of Bristol.

There, the council is undertaking a multi-phase smart city project designed to improve everything from traffic flows and rubbish collection to air quality and health services.

"They've branded themselves the 'problem-solving city' and they've done that through setting up all the network underneath that allows [data] to be gathered in a consistent way," he says. "But, far more importantly, they've got the platform that goes across all of that, so they can look at the data from anywhere."

Edwards says Bristol City Council has successfully built a model where industry, local government and the university work hand-in-hand with the start-up community. If a project requires research then the university leads, it and if it's an industrial project then industry leads.

"I like that model because they've recognised that ... it's a long journey, and you've got to [work together] to solve problems," he says.

Smart city projects are also rapidly gaining steam in the Asia-Pacific region. Associate Dean (Research, Strategy and Management) at UTS, Michael Blumenstein, says Singapore is a solid example of what can be achieved with thorough planning.

"What's interesting there is they are going for the big problems and looking at things like transport," he says. "They are designing smart, autonomous electric trams that will be running around the city in the form of platoons."

The trams will connect and disconnect on the basis of changes in passenger demand across the city.

However, Blumenstein says a truly smart city requires not just selected high-tech projects but an overall vision for what it wants to become.

"I suspect that there are lots of cities trying to use showpieces like that to articulate their maturity in the smart city space, but none - to my knowledge - has all the pieces together," he says.

Edwards says cities with a strategy of becoming "smart" are taking a phased approach to implementations. In this way the success of early activities can add value to other initiatives that follow.

"The first phase is doing the really simple things like putting sensors on bins to make rubbish collection more efficient," he says. "That's easy and lots of councils are doing it all over the world. It's not that smart, but it's an important first step."

Edwards says a second step is often looking at the lighting in public spaces and adding sensors and controllers to make its operation more efficient. This, in turn, leads to the deployment of a range of other sensors that can monitor everything from traffic flows to air quality and the movement of crowds.

With multiple sensor networks in place, the next phase is looking at how the different data streams being collected can be combined and add value in more complex ways. This could involve, for example, traffic management systems that alter traffic flows in times of poor air quality.

"One of the good measures of maturity [in smart cities] is data sharing and the policies wrapped around that," says Jack Archer, chief executive of the Regional Australia Institute. "A lot of people say that the oil of the digital economy is data [so] you have to have policy wrapped around that.

"You can't just say we're collecting data from all these sensors and we'll make it available willy-nilly.

"You have to know when it's too risky to share some data, when it's too personal [or] when it's inappropriate for national security."

It's a long journey, and you've got to [work together] to solve problems. Paul Edwards, Mirvac
direction: supportive for property developers and tech infrastructure stocks

headline: BHP must justify its 'unusual' exposure to oil: chairman
text:
BHP chairman Ken MacKenzie has vowed that investors will soon be treated to a "sizeable amount" of shareholder returns, and says BHP must continue to justify its "unusual" exposure to conventional oil assets.

Speaking at an Australian Shareholders Association event on Wednesday evening, Mr MacKenzie conceded it was "a bit unusual" for a mining company to also own oil assets in Australia, the US and Mexico.

With BHP close to selling its unconventional oil and gas assets in US shale, some shareholders like Elliott Management have pondered whether the time might be right for BHP to divest or demerge its entire petroleum division.

Mr MacKenzie said an exit from conventional oil and gas was not on the cards, but said BHP must continue to justify its involvement in the sector.

"We acknowledge that it is a bit unusual for a mining company to also have oil and gas assets ... the board and management both agree that we have to continue to justify our participation in the petroleum industry," he said.

"We need to continue to demonstrate that we are as good as anybody else at operating these assets and if we are, we get to continue to play there and, if we are not, it sort of becomes self fulfilling."

Mr MacKenzie said he was satisfied BHP compared well against its peers as an operator of conventional oil assets.
