"We are good at it, we have strong operating capability that benchmarks very well against our peers," he said.

"We think we are the logical owner for the petroleum business, we are the ones that are best positioned to extract the value from that business."

BHP entered the oil and gas industry with an investment in Victoria's Bass Strait oil fields in the 1960s and Mr MacKenzie said BHP would "do it all over again" if faced with the same investment decision today.

"If you look at the history of the company since the 1960s this has been an attractive place for our shareholders to be invested in. We believe it will continue to be an attractive place going forward," he said.

The comments come a week before BHP publishes full-year production data and six weeks before BHP reports its full-year financial results.

With commodity prices holding strong over the past year, BHP is expected to treat shareholders to strong returns next month, further bolstered by the proceeds from the shale sale which are tipped to top $US10 billion.

Mr MacKenzie said the structure of BHP's capital allocation framework ensured shareholders were headed for a handsome pay day.

"If you understand how the framework works, there is a sizeable amount of money that is going to come back to shareholders," he said.

"We can do that either through dividends or through share buybacks or through a combination of both those things, quite frankly at this point in time we are a bit agnostic about which way we might return cash to shareholders."

Mr MacKenzie said the composition of the shareholder returns would depend on the value of BHP's share price, the discount applied to its London shares and its franking credit balance.

Mr MacKenzie's appearance at the ASA event is part of a broader "listening tour" he is undertaking of BHP shareholders ahead of the July and August reporting seasons.
direction: downward pressure on BHP shares

text:
BHP makes glacial

progress on

gender parity pledge

With great fanfare in October 2016, BHP chief executive Andrew Mackenzie announced the miner's "aspirational goal" for 50 per cent of his employees to, by 2025, be women. He might as well have laid out a roadmap for replacing his ore volumes with Play-Doh.

Don't get us wrong, a target is admirable and necessary, and BHP already exceeds it in the company's professional functions (back in 2016 the fairer sex accounted for 77 per cent of "clerical" roles, so those booking limousines and fetching lattes for the dudes). But to make even a qualified promise that the sun-starved workforce of its Peruvian zinc mine (according to the latest World Bank data, only 9.1 per cent of workers in Peru's industrials are female; and that's principally in above-ground manufacturing!) will soon be teeming with sheilas is utterly laughable. The prospects of parity in the Pilbara are equally, regrettably ludicrous. We repeat our desire to be proven wrong.

BHP's FY18 annual report lobbed on September 18 disclosed the Big Australian's smallish female workforce as now 21.7 per cent of its 27,476-strong workforce. Funny then, that the same document referred to BHP's "more than 62,000 employees" (a number clearly padded lavishly by contractors).

Anyhow, BHP's percentage of women in its workforce has merely crawled north from 20.1 per cent the year before. That's an annual growth rate of 1.6 per cent. In the dying months of his tenure, the CEO is hardly bearing down on this particular KPI. At its current rate of growth, it will take until 2035, another 18 years, for BHP to reach Mackenzie's grand vision of 50/50.

Which is hardly his problem, the Scotsman now aping Westpac's Gail "Cleopatra" Kelly, grandly saving the world by hanging dreamy millstones around a successor's neck.

The nearly catatonic and brilliantly silent CEO of Australia's oldest bank, Brian Hartzer, has not only stuck with Cleopatra's ambitious promise that 50 per cent of Westpac's "leaders" would be women by 2017, he's even pretended to meet it by changing the definition of what a leader is. And Naples is now the cleanest city in the world because trash that smells bad isn't counted as rotting garbage.

Westpac claimed in October 2017 that 50 per cent of its leaders were women. Yet it reported, compulsorily to the Commonwealth, that only 39.31 per cent of its leaders are women, up from 38.6 per cent the previous year.

Busted sanctioning this really grubby case study in insincerity (funded by nearly $100 billion of shareholder equity), Hartzer's entitled indignation revealed so much about why this newspaper, and this column included, was wrong to defend the financial services industry from Kenneth Hayne's gimlet eye. We're sorry. If only more of them were.

Acting AGL chief Brett Redman has some fans

The proudest shareholders at the AGL Energy annual meeting at the Melbourne Recital Centre had to be Alan and Jeanette Redman, parents of Brett, the acting CEO since Andy Vesey suddenly got his marching orders last month.

The beaming couple travelled from their home in Cobbitty in the NSW Southern Highlands. Pere Redman - coincidentally a longstanding AGL shareholder - wisely declined to comment on the circumstances of the baton change when approached by a reporter at this paper. But he almost forgot himself when another shareholder - habitual Financial Review letter writer Kaz Kazim of Randwick, NSW - declared himself far from satisfied with the company's explanation for the dramatic events. To which Redman hinted there might be more to it than meets the eye.

Well, we're sure that's the case. The company was sticking to the official line: chairman Graeme Hunt insisted adamantly that it was merely a routine succession and nothing to do with Vesey's strained "government relations".

Some boards rue the day they made GTT Ventures their fundraiser

We know they do things differently out west, but even our Perth informants reckon this is unusual.

In February, boutique corporate advisory GTT Ventures led the float of tiny exploration company Accelerate Resources. Fast forward seven months, and GTT - which got a stack of cheap IPO shares and is still on a $10,000 a month retainer for corporate advisory services - is trying to oust a member of Accelerate's board!

The current board isn't having a bar of it, recommending a vote against their own adviser, which they reckon wants to change the strategy and "gain access to the company's cash asset". GTT's Charles Thomas says the firm is just trying to protect its investment through a seat on the board (Accelerate's share price has been falling since March).

But it's not even the only time GTT has pulled a stunt like this. It successfully outed the board of Baraka Energy & Resources in August, after having raised money for the company only in April.

It's a risky strategy for anyone providing professional services. Least of all those servicing the small-cap end, where fundraisers and advisers are often paid in stock. But Thomas says the firm had mandates from many other listed companies who are "very happy" with GTT.

All three of GTT's founders are former Bell Potter operatives, and one of them, Rocco Tassone, is a director of the MMA Fight Store (mixed martial arts, for the uninitiated). Tassone is also well known in Perth for having been dragged into the 2013 insider trading trial of one-time colleague Rob Catena. During one memorable courtroom exchange, where he was appearing as a witness, he said stockbrokers instigated market rumours and passed them on to clients. "It is our job to convince people to buy and sell," he said. That's always worth keeping in mind. . .
direction: bearish for BHP shares

headline: Kogan to push into whitegoods
text:
Competition between online retailer Kogan.com and giants JB Hi-Fi and Harvey Norman is set to intensify, with Kogan announcing plans to push into whitegoods and kitchen appliances.

Kogan.com founder and chief executive Ruslan Kogan said on Monday the sector was "ripe for the picking". His company has signed supply and logistics agreements with unnamed players and should be selling fridges, washing machines, dryers, dishwashers, ovens and cooktops by the end of the calendar year.

Mr Kogan said the existing players in the market - which is dominated by Harvey Norman, JB Hi-Fi and JB's subsidiary chain The Good Guys - had been able to enjoy inflated tension because the logistics challenge made it tough to compete.

"We would have loved to have been in this industry 12 years ago," Mr Kogan told The Australian Financial Review on Monday.

"These are bulkiest items that require a larger logistics footprint, which we've been building out - building it out over the last 12 years but much more aggressively in the last few months.

"The logistics side of things that meant this is an insulated industry. It has been categories that have been protected from online retail."

The plan had an immediate effect on the sector. JB H-Fi shares fell 3.7 per cent in a broadly stronger market, while Harvey Norman stock fell 2.8 per cent. Kogan.com stock soared 6.6 per cent to $9.76.

The strong margins on offer has also meant suppliers have been hesitant to back a new entrant, Mr Kogan said, adding this was "not through lack of trying and asking".

The products will be sold under the Kogan.com brand. Mr Kogan said he had secured supply agreements with "leading players in this space" and would use its existing third-party logistics providers to get the goods into the market.

Kogan.com will release more details about its whitegoods push later in the year but Mr Kogan said the group's offering would bring a "new level of efficiency" to the market.

"Any time you've got a market with limited competition, you know that Aussies are paying too much."

The whitegoods push might also allow Kogan.com to push into other parts of the home building and renovations market over time.

Kogan.com's shares have recovered strongly from a dip in April following the release of its numbers for the March quarter. Mr Kogan said there were "many other categories that lack competition" due to difficulties around bulky goods logistics.

While revenue jumped 46.1 per cent on the prior year, shareholders pushed the stock as low as $7.40 on April 24 as concerns about the company's growth rate grew.

However, the stock has surged since and closed at $9.12 last Friday. It hit an all-time high of $9.85 in March.

Mr Kogan rejoined the Financial Review Rich List last month with a fortune of $395 million.

Key pointsKogan should be selling large whitegoods products by the end of the calendar year.

JB H-Fi and Harvey Norman shares fell and Kogan.com stock soared 6.6 per cent.
sentiment: mixed with a negative bias for incumbents
direction: bearish for JB Hi-Fi and Harvey Norman, bullish for Kogan

headline: Fidelity sold Corporate Travel after gains
text:
Fund manager Fidelity International sold out of short-seller target Corporate Travel Management before an activist campaign by VGI Partners, partly because of the positive share price performance by the high-growth stock until the activist hedge fund struck.

"Following a period of strong performance and based on our research of the company, our investment team took the decision to reduce our holdings in Corporate Travel. As a result, we no longer hold Corporate Travel in any of our pooled funds," Fidelity International said.

Portfolio manager Kate Howitt said a report that showed Fidelity owned Corporate Travel was incorrect. The investment firm was cited in an appendix that included a table of Corporate Travel's major shareholders sourced from Bloomberg data and dated October 26.

Corporate Travel is currently fighting off an attack from VGI, which has taken a short position in Corporate Travel and increased that position since it began its campaign.

On Thursday, Corporate Travel hit back at what it described as VGI Partners' "mischievous and misleading claims", extensively citing the findings of accounting firm EY, which it hired last week to assess so-called "red flags" raised by the hedge fund.

Broadly, Ms Howitt said that she has noted a growing trend of short-selling in the Australian market. "Definitely it's more of a thing for the market because long-short is becoming more of a thing for the market," she said.

Fidelity is predominantly long-only but Ms Howitt notes that the fund giant does operate some long-short funds. "We think that shorting is a natural component of an efficient market," she said.

Ms Howitt said that short-selling reports putting "breadcrumbs" together were some of the most interesting parts of the short-selling process.

"Does it lay a trail through the forest or doesn't it?" Or put another way "these short seller reports will always contain smoke and the question is whether they also contain fire".

Some businesses are more vulnerable to allegations from short sellers than others, the fund manager said. The type of customer that a company has, for example, can help to determine how much of an effect a short-selling attack can have on a business.

The portfolio manager also pointed to another change she has observed in the Australian investment landscape - increased engagement by companies with investors on remuneration.

"That's the beautiful thing about the two-strikes legislation that came in. Boards now come and engage with us a lot more than they used to.

"They want to hear what we think a lot more than they used to. So that's been a wonderful piece of policy and regulation and I think has improved the quality of all listed Australian corporates."

Ms Howitt said that Fidelity's governance specialist is currently in Australia for six weeks for the annual general meeting season.

"It's a really interesting time," she said.

"There's more consensus around the view that the typical long-term incentive [plan] with a total shareholder return and earnings per share hurdle is not really delivering the right outcomes either from community perceptions or outcomes to executives and therefore we need to change.

"There's some degree of agreement around that. There's much less agreement around 'therefore what do we do differently?"'

There are various options being explored on that topic, the manager said.

"I think we will see a lot of movement and some experimentation on that."

Key points

Fidelity said a report that showed it owned Corporate Travel was incorrect.

Corporate Travel is currently fighting off an attack from VGI Partners.
direction: downward pressure on Corporate Travel Management shares

headline: Senex to kick off Atlas gas sale talks
text:
Senex Energy is set to kick off serious discussions with potential gas customers within weeks as it fast-tracks its $200 million-plus Atlas gas project in Queensland with the backing of its US private equity shareholder EIG Global Energy Partners.

Managing director Ian Davies said Senex had fielded tentative interest from domestic gas customers for over 150 terajoules a day of demand during the state government tender process for Project Atlas, which it won last September against some much bigger rivals.

The oil and gas junior, whose US backer is a founder of Santos suitor Harbour Energy, will now go back to those interested parties for further talks, with the aim of sealing gas sales this year.

Senex won the fiercely contested tender with a commitment to spend more than $200 million in developing gas resources near Miles to start production in 2019. Under the terms of the auction, the gas must be sold into the east coast market, rather than exported as LNG. At the same time, the company is developing its Western Surat project which is supplying gas to Santos' GLNG export plant in Gladstone.

One of the losing bidders in the Queensland government tender, Incitec Pivot, is hunting for affordable gas to keep its Gibson Island fertiliser plant in Queensland running when an existing contract expires at the end of September. But Mr Davies said Senex couldn't supply gas soon enough to meet Incitec's needs, although it would continue to hold discussions with Incitec.

In a quarterly report on Friday, Senex said it expects to secure a petroleum lease for Project Atlas in mid-2018 and is working on planning, land access, cultural heritage agreements and environmental impact assessments in preparation.

It lodged a submission for environmental approval for the project, which involves about 100 wells to produce more than 30 terajoules a day, to the Queensland government last quarter.

Senex is thought to still need a deal to secure gas transportation and processing infrastructure.

The junior is at the same time anticipating winning approvals for the next phase of the Western Surat project, potentially involving 40-50 additional wells, around mid-year.

Mr Davies said that the 30 wells in the second phase of Western Surat were delivered "in line with our commitments on cost control and schedule", but pointed to some "initial operational challenges" that Senex is working to address. The wells are producing about 2 TJ a day of gas.

The company, which has about $82 million of cash, is meanwhile looking to agree a funding deal early this year that would support both projects. That would involve commercial bank debt and potentially funding from EIG, with no new equity needed, Mr Davies said.

Shares in the company were flat at 37¢ at 2:26pm AEDT.

Meanwhile, relations with EIG have been tightened at board level, with the US firm's managing director in Australia, Andy Zhmurovsky, starting as a non-executive director on January 1.

A director representing Senex's former long-standing significant shareholder The Sentient Group has resigned. Sentient sold its 13.5 per cent stake in Senex in December.

Senex is thought to still need a deal to secure gas transportation and processing infrastructure.
direction: bullish for Senex Energy shares

headline: Yazaki cops record $46m fine for cartel behaviour
text:
A Japanese car parts supplier has copped a record fine for colluding with a competitor when supplying wire harnesses to Australian Toyota manufacturers.

On Wednesday, the appeal judges of the Full Federal Court fined Yazaki $46 million for cartel behaviour, which represents 10 per cent of the company's turnover in Australia and the highest penalty ever handed down under the federal competition and consumer legislation.

This follows a 2015 court finding that Yazaki had co-ordinated with its competitor, Sumitomo, when providing quotes to Toyota for the supply of wire harnesses used in the manufacture of Toyota Camry. Wire harnesses distribute power and electrical signals to other components in a car.

This is a big win for the competition regulator, which has been pursuing Yazaki since 2012. The penalty is also a significant jump from the $9.5 million penalty initially awarded by Justice Anthony Besanko last year.

Before this case, the largest fine for a cartel was handed down over a decade ago, when the late billionaire Richard Pratt's Visy Group was fined $36 million.

The appeal judges said Justice Besanko was wrong to place such great weight on the fact certain decisions were made in Japan.

"One starts with the real world reality that the firms in question, Yazaki and [Sumitomo], took the trouble to form a worldwide cartel to protect their respective businesses in discrete geographic markets by agreeing upon and fixing prices in tenders to share out each of those markets by reference to incumbency," the court said.

"This was done notwithstanding a process set up by [Toyota] ... that was supposed to be open and competitive for the purchasing of goods in a world-wide tender, divided up by specific country locations."

In blunt terms, [Toyota] was seeking honest competitive tenders for geographic locations all around the world (including Australia)."

"In response, Yazaki and [Sumitomo] surreptitiously rigged the bids for that tender in relation to each geographic location (including Australia)."

The appeal judges said only a "very sizeable penalty" is likely to act as deterrent for future breaches of law by Yazaki, and also deter similarly-sized global companies contemplating similar conduct.

Australian Competition and Consumer Commission chairman Rod Sims said this case means Australia has come a long way towards aligning our penalties with penalties overseas.

Earlier this year, the OECD found the average penalties for cartel were more than 10 times bigger than Australia's in other OCED countries.

"Cartel conduct is illegal because it not only cheats consumers and other businesses, it also restricts healthy economic growth," Mr Sims said on Wednesday.

"For this reason, it is of considerable importance that penalties imposed by the courts are large enough to act as a sufficient deterrent to prevent companies and their employees contravening Australia's competition laws."
direction: bearish for Yazaki shares

headline: Banks tap debt markets for $9b in just 10 days
text:
Australia's big four banks have raised $9 billion in the first 10 days of the year, taking advantage of the most favourable conditions in capital markets since the financial crisis.

The frantic pace of issuance exceeds the $5.25 billion raised by the major banks in January 2017 as they lock in lower funding costs and further reduce the need to compete aggressively for deposit funding.

On Tuesday, ANZ Banking Group issued $3 billion of three-year and five-year debt in the Australian market, paying a rate of 3.13 per cent for the five-year tranche. The margin ANZ paid for the five-year of 77 basis points over the bank rate is a 30 per cent decline from the 111 basis points paid by the Commonwealth Bank when it sold five-year bonds to Australian investors in January 2017.

The ANZ raising kicked off activity in the Australian bond market for 2018 and offshore deals from National Australia Bank, Westpac and the Commonwealth Bank.

National Australia Bank raised $US2.5 billion of three-year and five-year bonds in the US, which according to credit analysts represented cheaper or equivalent funding compared to pricing in the Australian bond market.

In the first week of 2018, Westpac raised €1.75 billion ($2.7 billion) of long-term debt via a covered bond issue while the Commonwealth Bank raised $US1.25 billion of 30-year junior ranking debt that shored up its regulatory capital requirements.

The CBA raising took advantage of several favourable factors such as low ultra-long-term interest rates fuelled by demand from life insurance companies and a decline in credit spreads for long-term junior ranking debt.

The favourable state of the capital markets should help ease net interest margin pressures for the Australian banks that had until now been focused on raising more expensive long-term debt to comply with Net Stable Funding Ratio requirements, which came into effect this month. The NSFR forms part of the so-called Basel III global banking reforms and requires banks to better match their funding terms with that of the loans they extend.

While the banks source most of their funding via deposits from households and corporations, they are reliant on the capital markets to plug the so-called funding gap.

Favourable conditions in capital markets typically reduce the need for the major banks to chase deposits by offering attractive rates to savers.

Australia's banks are typically active in the first month of the year, when international bond investors are keen to put their money to work. The major banks raised about $100 billion in domestic and international bond markets in 2017 but are expected to reduce their activity in 2018.

The banks' annual funding requirements are a function of maturities that need to be refinanced, but also the gap in loan growth relative to deposits. With Australian Prudential Regulatory Authority data showing major bank loan growth slowing to 5 per cent as limits on investor lending have an impact, their need for additional wholesale funding is expected to decline.

The raising also comes as global bond markets finally respond to the improved global economic growth outlook and strong sentiment as equity markets hit new highs.

Yesterday, the US 10-year bond rate rose above the key 2.5 per cent level for the first time since March 2017. It came as reduced bond purchases by the Bank of Japan prompted traders to price in the prospect that global central banks will speed up the "normalisation" process of winding back their accommodative policies.

Australian banks, however, typically hedge the interest rate component of their wholesale funding and are more concerned about the credit spread they pay above the risk-free rate, rather than outright borrowing rates.

Key pointsAustralia's big four banks

are taking advantage of favourable capital markets.

The raisings come as bond markets respond to strong global sentiment.
direction: Banks benefit from lower funding costs and reduced deposit competition

headline: Top earners have to wait for taxcuts
text:
The government has confirmed high-income earners will receive tax relief in Tuesday's budget but they will have to take a back seat to those on lower incomes whose taxes will be reduced from July 1 this year, starting by as little as $5 a week.

While the tax cuts will initially be targeted at low-and-middle-income earners as defined by earning less than $87,000, Finance Minister Mathias Cormann effectively confirmed that over the medium term - several years - people on higher incomes, including those above the $180,000 top tax threshold, would also be targeted.

"We will be prioritising low and middle income earners in the first instance, but there will be a phased approach moving forward," he told Sky News on Sunday.

"We do recognise that high income earners are carrying a very significant tax burden in our economy today. We do need to ensure that all Australians have the right incentive and are encouraged to work harder and make an even better contribution to the Australian economy."

Treasurer Scott Morrison used the traditional pre-budget interview with Channel Nine to hose down expectations over the initial size of the tax cuts, which will be rolled out over a decade.

"I'm not going to pretend that these are going to be mammoth tax cuts, or anything like that. That wouldn't be responsible," he said.

"They will be what is affordable, they will be real and they will be within what the budget can afford."

He indicated they could be worth as little as $5 a week, which was the size of a Howard government tax cut in 2003 which then minister Amanda Vanstone ridiculed as enough for "a sandwich and a milkshake if you are lucky".

Mr Morrison said that comment resonated back then because a $5-a-week tax cut was relatively small to other tax cuts being frequently handed out by the Howard government.

This time, however, anyone who levelled such a criticism would be economically irresponsible. "It's been a long time since there has been some real relief in this area. And, if they were to make that [sandwich and milkshake] observation then it would be very hard to say that the government is being irresponsible at the same time. You can't have it both ways," he said.

The Australian Financial Review understands the tax cuts could also be more substantial than just adjustments to the various rates and income thresholds.

"We're not just tinkering around this time," said a source, in reference to the decision two years ago to lift from $80,000 to $87,000 the threshold at which the 37 per cent tax rate kicks in.

Thanks to a revenue surge and lower-than-expected spending, the budget will start paying for the tax cuts immediately while at the same time compensate for the decision to scrap plans to lift the Medicare rebate by 0.5 percentage points, a decision which cost the budget $8 billion over the four-year forward estimates.

The government is also committed to returning the budget to balance in 2019-20 and to surplus a year later in 2020-21. There remains strong speculation the balance - which was forecast to be a $2.5 billion deficit in the last budget update - could become a small surplus on budget day although senior sources cautioned against this, saying there wasn't that much extra money to go around.

Over the 10 years, the tax cuts will ramp up as the government hits its cap of tax revenue being no more than 23.9 per cent of the economy, which was last forecast to be reached in 2022-23.

Senator Cormann accepted voters, especially those on higher incomes, would have to be patient in waiting for their tax cut but the timetable would be legislated, just as the government is seeking to legislate its 10-year plan to cut company taxes.

"We think it is important to set out the direction that the government wants to go in. That is what we did with our business tax cuts," he said.

"People are able to make judgements now based on what they expect the future tax policy settings will be."

The Coalition plans to use the income tax cut trajectory as a basis for seeking re-election. This budget will be the last before the federal election is called either later this year or early next year. The budget will also spend billions on infrastructure and there will be a large package to help baby boomers age in their own homes, rather than nursing homes. Ths will include funding to clear a waiting list of more than 100,000 people seeking assistance for in-home care.

As well as the revenue surge, there will be other money-making measures to pay for increased spending including an announcement on Sunday that the government plans to raise $3.6 billion over four years by clamping down on illegal tobacco sales, or chop-chop, which has proliferated in the wake of sharp increases to tobacco excise over recent years. As well as better-than-expected revenue from company taxes, last year's record creation of 415,000 jobs has caused a surge in income tax and less spending on unemployment benefits. "We now have the lowest level of welfare dependence of people of working age than at any time in the last 25 years," Mr Morrison said.

Going into the election, Labor will have at its disposal up to $220 billion more than the Coalition over 10 years thanks to its tax hike plans, which include raising the top marginal tax rate to 47 per cent, curbing negative gearing and capital gains tax exemptions, and repealing some or all of the company tax cuts.

Shadow treasurer Chris Bowen confirmed Labor would match the tax cuts in Tuesday's budget, spend more on health and education, and have stronger surpluses.

"Because we've engaged in that difficult reform process, which the government has single-handedly refused to do, we can actually have budget repair and return to surplus and engage in tax reform which benefits low-and middle-income earners as well."
direction: cautious for rate-sensitive sectors due to fiscal drag and limited immediate stimulus

headline: Amazon bargains for grocery subscribers
text:
Amazon is poised to take a bigger bite of Australia's $3 billion online food and grocery market early next year by launching Subscribe & Save, a discounted subscription service for regularly bought goods such as pet food, toilet paper and washing powder.

Amazon Australia country manager Rocco Braeuniger says it is one of Amazon's most popular services overseas and the online behemoth is keen to introduce it in Australia to augment its food and grocery range, launched in October. Consumer goods experts say subscription-based services threaten to undermine the "one-stop shop" model of supermarkets by siphoning off volumes, especially in bulky goods such as nappies, pet food and soft drinks, and giving customers fewer reasons to shop in store.

Companies p15
direction: bearish for Australian supermarket shares

headline: Letters
text:
Trade war could be good for Australia

There is no real threat from the US China trade war.

Has anyone noticed that the long-term picture on global tariff levels actually looks a lot like the longer-term interest rate trends - very much to the downside to quite historically negligible levels.

This, of course, makes perfect sense. Low interest rates are a function of low inflation, which is a function of higher competition, which is a function of global trade, which is a function of lower tariffs.

As with interest rates, there is plenty of room for tariffs to rise without any undue impact on global economic growth. There is room for some error.

Certainly, trade will change direction to some degree, but the businesses conducting these imports and exports, are going to continue to import and export. With the US, or China, or with someone else!

The idea that businesses will stop, on the basis of tariffs, is a completely absurd.

Australia is likely to be a major beneficiary. It is important for good relations to be maintained at a time when Chinese businesses will be earnestly considering parties outside of the US with which to do business. This will be in all areas of trade, and not just where tariffs are being applied.

We should all be excited about the future.

Clifford Bennett

Main Beach, Qld

Big 4 eroded by

consulting fees

John Harvey's letter (August 9) regarding the AFR review of Richard Brooks' Bean Counters (August 8) perhaps misses the point of the book, and the article.

We can agree that the role of auditors is incredibly important in a properly functioning market. But this has been, and continues to be, eroded by the lure of management consulting fees. There can be little to no independence by a firm when the majority of that firm's revenue consists of consulting fees, perhaps harking back to the days prior to the collapse of the "Big 5".

Perhaps Mr Harvey could consider the role of the Big 4 today, with their heavily qualified audit and advisory reports, and for recent examples: their role in the CPA Advice saga, their role in the collapse of Carillion in the UK, the scandal that threatens to end KPMG in South Africa, PwC's ban from auditing in India, or all four's role in the Panama Papers and other transfer pricing schemes.

Richard Brooks' book points out that there is a concentration of power that is actively undoing the pioneering and fearless work of the accountants that lend their names to the Big 4, and creating another class of "too big to fail" entities that fall outside any meaningful regulation.

Michael Kinder

Sydney, NSW

Farmers must heed

climate change

It seems neither motherhood nor farmers can be criticised. Common to all the contributions on this page regarding farmers is an absence of any consideration of climate change. By this, I mean the anthropic contribution which, to deniers, is all that really matters. Climate changes have been a factor over geological time, but the recent two centuries have seen a vast rise in carbon dioxide emissions due to the burning of fossil fuels. Aaron Patrick's "NSW farmers don't deserve $1b in drought aid" (August 3) may have seemed a tad insensitive to some, but the science is in — and we had better believe it.

Ian Nowak

Subiaco, WA

How about them Apples?

Reports on Apple becoming the first listed company to reach a $US1 trillion market valuation should be looked at in perspective: when compared with other valuations in the economic world it may suddenly appear to be a minnow.

The collective government programs of quantitative easing put in place around the world after the global financial crisis were valued at more than 10 Apples.

The amount of funds deposited in world investment companies is valued at around 40 Apples.

The annual world gross domestic product tree grows around 80 Apples a year.

World debt is a gobsmacking 240 "bad Apples".

But gambling or investment - or whatever you call the world financial derivatives markets - is nominally valued at around 1200 Apples!

Wayne Grant

Phillip, ACT

Tantrum unbecoming

So senator David Leyonhjelm says he won't support legislation that he personally supports as an act of revenge on Malcolm Turnbull. Sounds like Pauline Hanson at her most childish.

Keith Parsons

Newcastle, NSW

The negative in NEG

For Baby Boomers who grew up with cheap power, the political class' inability to come up with a viable energy policy, and the reality not only of an unreliable electricity grid but significantly higher energy costs is now sinking in. For them, NEG may represent No Electricity Grandpa. For the following generations struggling with energy bills and flat wages growth, NEG likely means No Electoral Gratification for those in power who are unwilling, or unable, to deliver it.

Peter Bedwell

Haberfield, NSW
direction: bullish for Australian exporters and miners

headline: Preparing for future of work starts now
text:
Widespread fears about the future of work usually focus on too many jobs being replaced by robotics and advances in artificial intelligence and machine learning. Yet the evidence of any such trend under way in developed economies is non-existent.

Australia's fall in the unemployment rate to 5 per cent is no aberration. If anything, Australia's lagging despite the impressive rate of job creation. It's another example that even intense community concerns may not reflect reality - at least not yet.

So in the US, the natural home of high-tech changes, the unemployment rate was down to 3.7 per cent in September, the lowest in half a century. In Britain, it's 4 per cent and in Germany, just 3.4 per cent. Even the Italian unemployment rate is back under 10 per cent while in the straggler economy, Spain, it is under 15 per cent - terrible but much improved compared with the 27 per cent rate of 2013. Sure, that could all suddenly change.

But for now it translates into complaints more focused on the lack of real wages growth - as well as the loss of relatively well-paid manufacturing jobs superseded by the uncertainty of the gig economy and unreliable hours and pay. Such concerns have created vast armies of outraged voters during the past several years, a resentment that helped propel Donald Trump into the White House.

In Australia, Bill Shorten is also successfully selling the argument life is unfair and things must change. Add in revelations of bad behaviour by banks fuelling broader resentment of the level of executive salaries overall. It's a message clearly resonating with the public as the price of some necessities - though not overall inflation - increases more than pay packets. The ACTU prosecutes the unfairness of the current system with even more vehement denunciations.

But if the diagnosis of a problem is wrong, how does that help with finding a solution?

By coincidence, Karen Chester, deputy chairwoman of the Productivity Commission, and Jennifer Westacott, chief executive of the Business Council of Australia, tried to reach beyond the political cacophony in two separate speeches on Wednesday. They were examining a different notion of the "future of work" and how to improve that future for more Australians.

Chester, as befits a productivity commissioner, emphasises the sort of facts that receive relatively little attention even if she agrees the nature of work is indeed changing.

As the recent Productivity Commission report concluded, income inequality has only modestly increased in Australia, largely due to a progressive tax and transfer system and the mobility of most people over time. "Here it's clear that perceptions do not match reality," Chester told a CEDA lunch in Brisbane.

"For over the last three decades, Australia has seen strong income growth across the distribution. In contrast, US income growth has been miserly, except at the very top."

Nor are accusations of increasing casualisation of the workforce accurate. "In fact, the percentage of casual workers has not changed significantly since 2001," she said. "And the 'gig' economy, whilst still in its infancy, has not translated yet to any increase in the percentage of independent contractors."

Rather than the dominant spectre of underemployment, for example, Chester notes average hours worked per person are about 14 per cent higher now than in the early 1980s. Certainly, there has been an increase in the percentage of people working part-time - but more usually due to their preference. She also points out jobs that have disappeared have often been unpleasant, physically tiring, dangerous or tedious - while new jobs created are more likely to be creative or caring (think designers and advertisers, nurses and childcarers).

"Since the 1980s, there has been a fall in the proportion of jobs that are routine (from 50 per cent to 37 per cent); while non-routine jobs have increased (from 42 per cent to 53 per cent)," she says.

But where she believes the Australian system is in need of radical change is in the approach to education, skills and training. This is vital for a workforce needing to rely on lifelong learning to keep up with changing demands of any job as well as moving between occupations and industries.

Warning signs Australia is heading in the wrong direction are obvious enough. Deteriorating school results in subjects that matter for future work - such as maths and reading - are lamented but never fixed. Chester describes the vocational education system as a "mess" and argues VET and universities are struggling to deliver relevant qualifications. "Retraining is inconvenient and expensive," she says. "And the approach of education institutions remains outdated and outmoded - still emphasising a one-career-for-life approach."

Beyond the disappointment of abandoned corporate tax cuts, Jennifer Westcott shares this critical view of massive flaws in education and training, citing employers finding their businesses highly affected by lack of literacy and numeracy.

"These were once people who could get away with poor literacy skills," she told the National Press Club. "But now where everything is online and everything assumes a level of literacy and digital capability, they are excluded from society."

Nor are prospects usually much improved by post-school education and training. VET continues to be a second-class system, according to Westacott, while TAFE remains undervalued. "Students continue to make poor choices and undertake degrees that do not always get them into the jobs they want," she says. "The system remains clunky and extremely difficult for people in the mid part of their career to reskill and retrain."

The future of work should be about fixing present mistakes - not repeating them. No evidence yet of that either.
direction: bearish for banks and consumer discretionary

headline: Charter Hall in Healthscope mix as management goes global
text:
As Healthscope's board does its best to deflect attention from suitor BGH Capital and partner AustralianSuper, its management team has been busy pitching the company's proposed hospital property trust to potential investors at home and abroad.

And from what we hear, the pitch has netted more than a couple of interested parties. Leading the local contenders is Charter Hall Group, which has made its name investing into assets with long tenancies and has been expanding into social infrastructure.

Healthscope's property trust is expected to fit both bills. The company is looking for an investor to take a 49 per cent stake in a vehicle that owns about $1 billion worth of hospital properties, which are leased back to operator Healthscope. The leases are long term in nature and receive rental payments worth $80 million to $90 million in the first year.

Offshore, the strong contenders are expected to include Canada's NorthWest Healthcare Properties Real Estate Investment Trust, which took a 10 per cent stake in Healthscope earlier this year, as well as property players that have previously taken a close look at Healthscope's portfolio including Ventas, Medical Properties Trust and a range of Asia-based investors.

It is understood all the aforementioned names took meetings with Healthscope management including chief financial officer Michael Sammells recently, as the company's representatives kicked off the property pitch with a global roadshow.

Tyre-kickers said they were told an information memorandum was due out in the first half of November, and Healthscope and adviser UBS would likely be calling for indicative offers in early December.

While it is early days, Healthscope management were said to have returned from the roadshow confident the property spin-off could release the sort of capital that would make the transaction value-accretive for shareholders.

Already expectations are rising that the process will rustle up a healthy premium to the current book value of the Healthscope assets as investors bank on the value of 20-year leases.

Indeed, the bidding could put a value of as much as $1.8 billion on the Healthscope property book, at an initial cap rate of 5 per cent. The portfolio has about a $1 billion book value.

It comes as Healthscope's board prepares to front shareholders at the annual general meeting on Wednesday. Much of the attention is expected to be on the BGH/AustralianSuper proposal - which was refreshed last week and was contingent on the spin-off not happening - although the company will no doubt be keen to remind shareholders about its plans for the property trust.
direction: Healthscope shares and property trusts likely to rise on strong investor interest and valuation support

headline: NAB delivers on $1.5b transformation
text:
The ambitious three-year transformation program at National Australia Bank is not only tracking well, it clearly poses no threat to the bank's status as the highest yielding of the big four.

When the program was launched by chief executive Andrew Thorburn there was understandable scepticism given that it asked shareholders to trust that management will deliver a high-performing bank in three years.

Corporate Australia has witnessed many failed transformation programs in the past 20 years, many of which revolved around the implementation of new technology. Probably the highest-profile transformation program on foot now is the four-year program at Telstra under CEO Andy Penn.

At NAB, Thorburn is boosting investment in the business by $1.5 billion over three years and cutting costs of $1 billion. Also, he is sacking 6000 people and bringing in 1500 with different skills. He wants to get the cost-to-income ratio down to an industry-leading 35 per cent.

Based on the full-year results released on Thursday, the transformation is on track. About 1900 employees left the bank in the full year and costs were reduced by $320 million. The bank, which spends about $1 billion a year on its business, lifted spending by about $500 million in 2018.

Thorburn has followed through on his promise to streamline management. About 94 per cent of staff are only seven management layers away from Thorburn. He says it will be 100 per cent very soon.

The 2018 result was clouded by conduct charges and restructuring costs associated with the transformation.

The $320 million in charges for remediation of customers in 2018 are part and parcel of the fallout from the Hayne royal commission.

Another sign of the damage from the Hayne inquiry is the bank's net promoter score, which is negative 16 per cent. That is better than two of its peers, which have NPS scores of minus 21 and minus 18.

But Thorburn says the day-to-day interaction with customers is much better judging from the NPS score of plus 50 in branches and plus 60 in contact centres.

When you strip out the remediation and restructuring charges, the bank's underlying profit was reasonably good with increased revenue, higher market share in key lending segments and good credit quality.

Thorburn says the bank is targeting a broadly flat expense growth for 2019 and 2020, excluding remediation costs. The critical element of any bank result for most investors is the dividend and, in this respect, NAB has delivered despite being forced to lift its payout ratio to more than 90 per cent.

NAB has been paying the same 99¢-a-share dividend every six months for nine successive halves and the September 2018 half-year was no exception.

The stock is now yielding more than 11 per cent after taking into account its 100 per cent franking credits.

At worst, NAB will be able to maintain its dividend at $1.98 a share through the three-year transformation period. The only caveat that it must lift its core equity tier-one capital to 10.5 per cent by January 2020.

It should be able to do that through the use of the dividend reinvestment plan while maintaining the dividend at 99¢ each half- year.

BHP shows resources

in sweet spot

BHP's plan to return $US10.4 billion ($14.7 billion) through a buyback and a special dividend is an emphatically positive signal that the resources sector is in a good place at the moment.

Sensible disposal strategies, strong commodity prices and an end to crazy expansion in projects which didn't make a return on investment are behind BHP's capital return largesse.

Its capital return plan was one of many positive developments in the resources sector this week.

Gina Rinehart's Hancock Prospecting lifted profit 28 per cent to $1.75 billion in the year to June and the Glencore's Hunter Valley coal mining operations are firing on all cylinders judging from a briefing for analysts on Wednesday.

Rinehart, who is using the bulk of her profits to pay down debt, has been one of the beneficiaries of consistently strong demand from Asia for iron ore.

Glencore's upbeat disclosures about the performance of its Hail Creek thermal coal mine will be read by some as an indictment of Rio Tinto, which sold an 82 per cent interest in the Hail Creek joint venture for $1.55 billion in March this year.

It is a tribute to the mining skills of Glencore's global head of coal assets, Peter Freyberg, that following a redesign the Hail Creek mine will deliver cost savings of $110 million a year. It will operate with 427 fewer employees while increasing production levels.

Coal is now Australia's single largest export earner according to the latest analysis of the September trade data by Annette Beacher, chief Asia Pacific macro strategist at TD Securities. She says the number-two export earner after coal is tourism and third is iron ore, based on annualised quarterly data.

Beacher says the most notable aspect of the latest $3 billion trade surplus for September was the surge in LNG exports, which have tripled in the last three years to be approaching $50 billion. LNG will be the number-one export by 2020.

Beacher says that working out of an office in Singapore gives her a different perspective on the Australian economy to those economists in Australia. She can look through the noise about falling housing prices and the threat posed by the US-China trade wars.

She says the revival of the fortunes of Australia's commodity producers is unequivocally positive for the economy which is heading towards GDP forecast upgrades on the back of higher export income.

"Infrastructure spending and accelerating LNG exports are supporting growth, such that the economy is less dependent on the fortunes of iron ore, house prices and consumer spending than it used to be," Beacher says.

"Having said that, an iron ore price above $US65 a tonne is positive for exports and budget revenues. As economic growth is increasingly broad-based, another bout of iron ore price weakness - for whatever reason - is unlikely to materially dent the outlook for Australia, nor concern the RBA."

She says Australia might well be in the fortunate position of having twin surpluses - a trade surplus and a fiscal surplus.

Beacher is less concerned about a slowing Chinese economy than other commentators. She says Chinese industrial production has slowed from 20 per cent per annum in 2009 to 6 per cent per annum now and yet China's imports of commodities remain high and are rising.

The $14.7 billion BHP capital management program is being funded by the sale of the company's onshore gas assets in the United States. This disposal can be partly sheeted home to the pressure brought to bear by activist investor Elliott.

That is not to say that Elliott is happy with the buyback and special dividend. It is still focused on forcing BHP to end its dual-listed company structure. It won't admit defeat even though BHP is not budging.

Elliott alleges the dual-listed structure destroys franking credits. It bases this claim on analysis by hired consultant FTI Consulting.

Most institutional shareholders interviewed by Chanticleer said they were happy with the capital management program.

That is not to say there was no criticism. John Abernethy, chief investment officer at Clime Asset Management, which has $900 million under management, says the buyback and special dividend should not have been approved by the Australian Taxation Office.

He says the ATO has rubber stamped a buyback scheme that allows high-tax-paying shareholders to minimise capital gains tax through the use of the capital losses.

Analysis of the BHP off-market buyback by Evans and Partners suggests the after-tax proceeds for someone on zero tax will be $39 a share and $21.47 for someone on the highest marginal tax rate. A super fund paying 15 per cent tax would have after tax proceeds of $33.18 per share.

Geoff Wilson, chairman of Wilson Asset Management, says BHP is smart to get franking credits off its balance sheet and into the hands of shareholders ahead of the next election which could see the Labor Party in power.

Labor plans to put an end to the payment of franking credits in cash to Australians on zero tax rates.

Wilson would not be surprised if other companies moved quickly to get franking credits to shareholders.

He says the opposition does not realise the extent of the impact of its planned changes to the franking credit system.

TONY BOYD

Disclosure: The author's self managed super fund owns shares in BHP.
direction: NAB shares likely to rise

headline: Coalition support crumbles
text:
A week of internal warfare has left the Coalition reeling, with its support in freefall and Malcolm Turnbull's personal ratings plummeting to levels not seen since the peak of the dual citizenship crisis.

The latest Fairfax Media/Ipsos poll shows the Coalition primary vote has nosedived 6 percentage points to 33 per cent in a month and now trails Labor by 55 per cent to 45 per cent on a two-party-preferred basis.

That represents a 4-point swing to Labor since the last poll a month ago when it led by 51 per cent to 49 per cent, and a 5-point swing since the July 2016 federal election. That would result in up to 21 seats being lost if replicated on polling day.

As cabinet held a crisis meeting in Canberra last night ahead of this week's parliamentary sitting, the poll showed the catalyst for all the trouble - the National Energy Guarantee - is popular with voters.

The poll of 1200 voters, taken from Thursday to Saturday night last week, shows 54 per cent back the NEG while just 22 per cent are opposed and 24 per cent don't know. The NEG is being attacked by climate sceptics in the Coalition, led by Tony Abbott, who are opposed to it having an emissions reduction target. The government will try to soften opposition by agreeing to regulate the target rather than legislate it. But the backflip has failed to appease the critics and has upset others who feel it will be too easy for a future Labor government to ramp up.

The poll finds 56 per cent believe the government's efforts on climate change are "too little", 28 per cent believe they are "about right" and 13 per cent say they are "too much".

The other policy set to be dumped this week - the company tax cuts - also remain relatively popular, with 47 per cent in favour of cutting the rate for all companies over 10 years, and 44 per cent against.

But the infighting in the Coalition has moved beyond the NEG. Ministers and MPs spent the weekend on the phones to each other. Peter Dutton is entertaining the prospect of a challenge and nobody could predict with any clarity how events would unfold this week.

"Turnbull has lost his authority and can't stay as leader but we can't go to Dutton," summarised one minister of the general view.

The poll shows the infighting has destroyed the hard-won gains of the past eight months during which the Coalition, in all the published polls, had pulled within striking distance of Labor. Mr Turnbull's signature strength, his personal ratings, have also taken a battering. His approval rating fell 9 points to 46 per cent and his disapproval rating rose 10 points to 48 per cent. It is the first time since the end of last year, when the Coalition was in the midst of the citizenship crisis, that Mr Turnbull's disapproval rating has been higher than his approval rating.

Over the same period, Labor leader Bill Shorten's approval rating has increased 3 points to 41 per cent and his disapproval rating has fallen 2 points to 52 per cent.

Mr Turnbull's support as preferred prime minister has dropped 9 points in a month to 48 per cent while Mr Shorten's has risen 6 points to 36 per cent.

Senior minister Christopher Pyne, who usually dismisses trouble, acknowledged the seriousness of the situation last night. "There are some people who don't support the current leader and that is quite obvious," he said. "We have the right policy mix and if we are united we will win the next election."

Mr Turnbull attempted to take control of the energy debate Sunday by confirming a range of measures additional to the NEG that will directly address price concerns among MPs. This includes threatening power companies with regulation to stop them gouging consumers, forcing retailers to offer customers the option of low-cost, default price contracts and effectively underwriting the construction of a new generator of "firm" electricity by agreeing to be a buyer of last resort of its power.

But when he defended the backflip on the NEG's emissions reduction target of 26 per cent on 2005 levels by 2030, it sparked a social media war with Mr Abbott who, as prime minister, committed Australia at the Paris climate summit to reduce its emissions by 26 per cent to 28 per cent by 2030.

"The target in the NEG is by 2030 to reduce emissions by 26 per cent from 2005 standards. We will meet this in a canter. As Tony Abbott said in 2015, it is a strong and responsible target, environmentally and economically responsible," Mr Turnbull said on social media.

In response, Mr Abbott attacked him on Twitter: "Emissions targets that made sense three years ago when all countries were supposed to be in Paris and we didn't need policy change and wouldn't face economic dislocation do not make sense now. @TurnbullMalcolm take note," he tweeted.

When asked about the numbers being marshalled against him, Mr Turnbull maintained his attention was on policy.

"I'm focused on getting energy prices down and I'll leave you to all of the speculation," he said.

The NEG would mandate power retailers to source energy which meets reliability and emissions reduction targets. The latter is a 26 per cent reduction in 2005 levels by 2030.

This sparked the revolt, with rebels believing there should either be no target or that it should not be legislated because that was a surrender of sovereignty to Paris.

To soften this, the government has agreed to regulate it but that has only worsened the situation.

Labor on Sunday announced that if elected, it would also introduce the default contracts for retailers which was an ACCC recommendation.

Treasurer Scott Morrison accused it of cheating.

"At school, if you try to pass off others' work as your own, if you try and copy off the person sitting next to you in class during a test, you get busted and the rest of the class knows you're a cheater. That is what Bill Shorten and Labor are doing today, dishonestly trying to represent the government's work on energy as their own," he said.
direction: political instability weighs on market confidence

headline: Childcare centre deal-making set to fire up as cycle turns positive
text:
Commonwealth Bank of Australia expects the level of buying and selling among the nation's 7600 childcare centre operators to speed up over the next 12 months as multi-centre operators look to expand, with the low point in the cycle having passed.

Sachin Kumar, the national director of healthcare in CBA's Business and Private Banking division, said margins were being compressed and almost half of the industry players analysed in a comprehensive report by the bank suffered falling profits in the previous six months.

"If you look at the operating environment there is margin compression," Mr Kumar said.

Average occupancy rates across the country were running at 75 per cent, which was marginally lower than in 2017, and many players are still concerned about the number of new entrants into the sector, which has been through a significant oversupply as developers built too many centres.

Mr Kumar said it was multi-centre operators who had the biggest appetite for expansion now, with more capital at their disposal to use at a time of pressure on margins.

"The intention is to continue to be in that growth mindset," Mr Kumar said.

CBA has compiled its first Early Learning Insights Report into the childcare sector in conjunction with the Australian Childcare Alliance, with a candid assessment of the industry conditions across 266 centres. Mr Kumar didn't want to comment on whether banks had imposed tougher criteria on potential borrowers wanting to buy more childcare centres.

It comes as the share price of Australia's largest ASX-listed childcare centre operator, G8 Education, has jumped 48 per cent since hitting a low point of $1.91 on October 26 as investors bet that an industry bottom has already passed and that conditions should slowly improve. Shares of smaller rival Think Childcare have also made gains, up 36 per cent since November 7.

G8 chief executive Gary Carroll said on November 15 that G8 had 32 centres in the "underperforming" category across its network of 500 centres and they were making $3.5 million in combined losses at an earnings before interest and tax level, but that overall occupancy rates were starting to improve.

The oversupply had stemmed largely from over-zealous developers and property players building too many childcare centres without closely monitoring specific catchment areas and the needs of local communities. G8 shares were at $4.55 a year ago and on Friday closed at $2.80.

The CBA report found that one in three childcare centre operators still had a pessimistic outlook on the sector. Average sector-wide occupancy rates had fallen from 77 per cent in 2017 to 75 per cent this year. There were some high performers however, with 37 per cent reporting occupancy levels above 85 per cent, but there had also been a worsening of those with occupancy levels below 70 per cent. There were 36 per cent in that unenviable position of a sub-70 per cent occupancy rate, compared with 28per cent a year earlier.

Mr Carroll said in a November 15 market update that G8 expects childcare occupancy rates across its business to be at 75 per cent by the end of calendar 2018. It has a target of 81 per cent in 2022.

Mr Kumar said it was single-site operators who tended to be more confident now about the outlook, with word-of-mouth a powerful marketing tool in a local catchment area.

"If you are embedded in the business you're probably closer to the community," he said.

The CBA report found it was the multi-centre operators that were on the front foot with growth plans for the next 12 to 24 months. The number of respondents who signalled they were likely to sell their centres was 9 per cent, down from 12 per cent a year ago.
direction: bearish for childcare sector operators

headline: Brickworks slides as housing hits 'pothole'
text:
Brickworks shares suffered the biggest drop in more than a year after the building materials maker said credit curbs were causing customers to delay and cancel plans to build houses.

Shares in the company that operates brands including Austral Bricks, Bristile Roofing and Auswest Timbers were trading down 4.9 per cent on Thursday after it warned of "patchy sales" due to delays and cancellations, despite having a strong east coast order book.

Brickworks shares fell 92¢, or 5.4 per cent, to close at $16.17.

"All of a sudden we've got this pothole in the demand, which has come from nowhere," chief executive Lindsay Partridge told The Australian Financial Review.

"What we are seeing - and I think it caught everyone a little bit by surprise - is the credit squeeze that's been induced, there's no real reason for it, we've got low inflation, low unemployment, we've got good immigration, and also we've got this credit squeeze."

Regulator-driven restrictions on lending to home buyers - reinforced by the banking royal commission - have hit investors and engineered a slowdown in the east coast housing markets, but they are also feeling their way deeper into the construction market.

Last month, Queensland-centred home builder Tamawood said slower lending decisions had delayed commencements and triggered a 4.5 per cent fall in net profit.

The news took the gloss off the company's results, in which earnings before interest and tax for the year to June 30 rose across each of its building products, property and investments divisions - the last of which includes a 42.7 per cent stake in diversified investment group Washington H. Soul Pattinson and Co.

"It was an excellent trading period," Mr Partridge said. "It was one of the rare times when all parts of the business performed well. Investments are up strongly, our property was up a little bit on what was already a strong year and building products bounced back and was the highest of this cycle."

Brickworks' net after-profit tax fell 5.8 per cent to $175.4 million as revenue fell - reflecting a $77.4 million land-sale boost the previous year - and also reflecting the impact of items including $7.1 million after-tax restructuring and commissioning costs within Building Products and $39.2 million in net cost relating to WHSP-related impairments and tax expenses.

The company declared a 36¢ dividend, up from 34¢ a year earlier.

Tighter lending rules had hit the house-building industry suddenly because they caught up a number of people who were already well advanced in the process, Mr Partridge said.

"So the reason why it hit so fast, was that people who were in that pipeline - they might have been six or nine months in the pipeline - got to the point where their house was approved, they go to the builder to sign the final contract, they give the final go-ahead, and they've got to go back to the bank to check and then the bank says no," he said.

"That's where it has really put an instant drop-off in business."

The credit-induced slowdown meant the housing market could ill-afford the further headwinds that would come from Labor's plan to limit negative gearing tax benefits to new home purchases, he said.

"They don't need to do it because the investors already have dropped away," Mr Partridge said.

"The international people have already dropped away and the first home buyers are up 70 per cent in NSW and 40 per cent in Victoria. So it's all heading in the right direction."

Any change to negative gearing would cause a shortage in rental housing stock because investors would withdraw from the market, he said.

Mr Partridge also said his company was being "gouged" by power prices that were up about $1 million extra per month than they were last year and would rise to the same extent next year.

"During the years 2018 and 2019, our total gas and electricity bill will climb from $50 million to $70 million - so basically $1 million per month extra."
direction: Brickworks shares and residential construction sector likely to decline

headline: We are in a new Cold War against the West's values
subhead: Strategy | Washington believes that a world of great power contests has returned. That will demand new thinking in Australia.
text:
Two years ago, Russian Prime Minister Dimitri Medvedev announced to European security officials that the world was entering a new cold war. The message has taken some time to trickle through. A decade ago, Australia's 2009 defence white paper also warned of China's rising military power and political influence, but the idea was still unfathomable to most people. Now the recently released unclassified summary of the US National Defence Strategy, and the US National Security Strategy from which it stems, has removed any doubt: we are back in a sharply competitive global era.

The Cold War comparison seems improbable. Unlike the old USSR, the economies of China and the US are deeply enmeshed. And war has happily been a distant spectre for many decades - few among the West's younger generations wake up worrying about how to prevent it.

But for China and Russia, the revisionist powers that the defence strategy calls out, a win in this new contest means the ability to dominate the regions of Asia or Europe, and to frame global politics. Just as in the original Cold War, it's a win for the United States if major war is prevented.

US Defence Secretary Jim Mattis, a man not prone to hyperbole, twice announced the stakes of the contest to a Washington audience last Friday: "Simply, we must be the best if the values that grew out of the Enlightenment are to survive."

The strategic direction drafted by America's foreign and defence policy professionals is remarkably clear. The defence strategy's maxim of being "strategically predictable for allies and operationally unpredictable for adversaries" could be read as a jog to the Trump administration in which the strategy has been formulated. And, of course, the strength of the strategy is reliant on the ability of Congress to fund it, and its commitment to sustain it.

Two aspects of the strategy are critical for Australia. The first is its emphasis on a potentially expanded system of American alliances in the Indo-Pacific. Secretary Mattis called for "new partners, maybe non-traditional partners". The most likely new entrant is India, which the National Security Strategy indicated would become a "stronger strategic and defence partner" of the United States. Washington has been drawing India closer since the second Bush administration. The country was given "major defence partner status" under the Obama administration, and the export of military drones was authorised last year. The Trump administration could well go further and consider the sale of F35 Joint Strike Fighters to India.

While the long-mooted Quadrilateral alliance structure between Australia, India, Japan and the United States -- as evidenced in The Australian Financial Review's exclusive interview with Japanese Prime Minister Shinzo Abe -- still has some political road to travel, expect an intensification of the sub-alliance security web between the Quadrilateral countries. Secretary Mattis is this week in Jakarta to boost strategic efforts with Indonesia. Further foreign military sales are likely for Jakarta, as well as increased counter-terrorism co-operation. These initiatives will need to be closely co-ordinated with Australian officials - who were surprised by Apache attack helicopter sales to Jakarta in 2012 - and have worked intimately with Indonesia's counter-terrorism agencies.

The second critical aspect for Australia will be the somewhat cryptic references to "expanding the competitive space".

This is shorthand for ways in which the US might use economic sanctions against security transgressions. For much of the past decade - at least until the Obama administration was trying to sell the strategic value of the TPP - US officials worked to keep economic and security issues with China in separate lanes.

Now, as Australia's foreign policy white paper accurately portended, we can expect spikier geo-economic statecraft.

President Trump has consistently threatened a tougher stance on trade with China, and last month said "I have been soft on China because the only thing more important to me than trade is war". The year-long pause in any economic confrontation with China is more a reflection of the caution required in a mutually dependent international economy, and North Korean negotiations, than a lack of domestic support for Trump's plans. Washington has just launched what could be the first surgical strikes in a trade war with China, lifting tariffs on imported solar panels and washing machines.

Australia has grown unaccustomed to thinking on the grand strategic scale, where competition and conflict must be considered across a range of intertwined domains. Our recent defence and foreign policy white papers, as well as public debates on foreign influence operations, have been an important corrective in this regard. But Australia still needs to augment its own national security staff, potentially with a national security council, before it can look at the long-term competitive strategies the US is now openly discussing.

US national security leaders have watched their allies in the Indo-Pacific pursue a mix of strategic options to hedge against a rising China and a distracted United States. This defence strategy acknowledges the decline in power relativities, particularly in its understated acceptance that the United States can now fight a major war in only one theatre, rather than two. That fact is important to Australian defence planning.

Which strategic theatre the US should prioritise - Europe or Asia - is still not settled. But regardless this defence strategy will seek allies willing to more closely co-ordinate investment in defence capabilities. More importantly though, it will need its allies to accept the strategy's core political premise - that the very foundations of Western civilisation are under assault.

James Brown is a senior fellow and adjunct associate professor of the United States Studies Centre at the University of Sydney. He is also the president of RSL NSW, Australia's largest veterans' charity.
direction: bearish for risk assets and equities

headline: Qantas threatens to reduce US flights
text:
Qantas is warning it may have to cut back or potentially cancel its A380 service between Sydney and Dallas and reduce code-sharing arrangements if the Trump administration fails to approve a "joint business" proposal with American Airlines.

Qantas and American Airlines have filed a new application with the US Department of Transport seeking approval to form a joint business that the airlines argue will better serve customers flying between North America and Australia and New Zealand, a statement yesterday said.

The 238-page application comes after the US authorities rejected a plan to allow the two airlines to co-ordinate on things such as prices and flight schedules. The airlines agreed to file a new application but argued the 2016 decision did not take into account, among other things, intense competition on the Trans-Pacific route.

Qantas says a tie-up with American Airlines would generate up to 180,000 new trips between the US and Australia and New Zealand every year. The airline argues it would unlock $US300 million annually in consumer benefits including $US221 million, from expanding its code-sharing deal, which would open up more destinations.

A further $89 million in value would be achieved by delivering a wider range of fare classes across both networks, including lower fares and discounts.

"The joint business will also give American and Qantas the opportunity to launch additional routes between the US and Australia and New Zealand, including new flights to city pairs currently not served by either carrier," Qantas said in a statement.

"An expanded relationship will encourage significant improvements in the overall customer experience, including more improved frequent-flyer benefits and investments in lounges, baggage systems and other infrastructure designed to better serve the carriers' joint customers."

The fresh application came with an ominous warning. "Critically, if the joint business is not approved, American and Qantas will have no choice but to further reduce code sharing on their networks. This will jeopardise the number of services and routes each carrier flies between the US and Australia and New Zealand," Qantas said. "For example, Qantas may be forced to reduce the frequency of, downgauge or potentially cancel its A380 service between Sydney and Dallas/Fort Worth, and American may further reduce its services between Los Angeles and Sydney and Auckland."

When its initial application was blocked, American Airlines downgraded its service to Australia and New Zealand. The airlines no longer code share on flights between the US and Sydney. Qantas said a joint business is often necessary to allow more efficient operations because "virtually all countries prohibit foreign carriers from providing service between two points within their country".

The airline argues revenue-pooling, rather than code share, maximises incentives for carriers to open up their networks to the joint business partner. This unlocks hundreds or thousands of connecting flight options not economically feasible while code sharing.

The application cites a study of international airline co-operation spanning 17 years that showed revenue pooling cut the lowest average fares for connecting services by 8 per cent.
direction: Qantas shares likely to decline

headline: Wall St surges as Fed rates near neutral
text:
Washington | Donald Trump's constant browbeating of Jerome Powell may just be working, with the Federal Reserve chairman conceding for the first time that US interest rates are now near their so-called neutral level, suggesting he may slow the pace of normalisation in 2019.

Stocks leapt the most in eight months as growth stocks surged, and the US dollar fell after Mr Powell said the Fed's benchmark rate is "just below" estimates of where it neither adds to nor detracts from growth. The Dow closed 618 points higher, paced by Caterpillar and Boeing. Amazon was 6.1 per cent higher, Netflix rose 6 per cent; Apple added 3.9 per cent.

In a speech that focused on financial stability risks, Mr Powell started by saying there was a "great deal" to like about the economic outlook, but warned that "we know that things often turn out to be quite different from even the most careful forecasts".

The remarks are his first after weeks of turmoil on financial markets, which some investors have blamed on a speech the Fed chairman gave in early October when he declared the central bank "a long way" from getting to neutral - spurring speculation of additional hikes. While he did nothing to dissuade expectations of another rise in December, Mr Powell's revised position on the level of rates indicates they may not rise much above the current 2 per cent to 2.25 range.

"Assuming the Fed hikes in December, the market is currently pricing essentially one hike in 2019 and currently the median dot for 2019 is suggesting the Fed is looking to hike 3 times next year," NAB FX strategist Rodrigo Catril said in a morning note. "Based on recent Fed rhetoric and distribution of dots, we think there is a material chance that the median dot in 2019 goes down to two hikes, so on this basis current market pricing looks aggressive."

After a surge in growth, much of it driven by the Trump administration's corporate tax cuts, there are growing signs of a slowdown, including in the US housing sector and fallout from the President's widening trade war.
