The Atlanta Fed's GDPNow indicator sees growth in the current quarter at a 2.5 per cent annual pace; compared with government estimates of a 3.5 per cent annual pace in the September quarter and 4.2 per cent in the June quarter.

Adding to the central bank's challenge have been attacks from Mr Trump, who this week slammed his Fed appointee as a greater risk to the US economy than China. "So far, I'm not even a little bit happy with my selection of Jay. Not even a little bit," Mr Trump said of Mr Powell in an interview with the Washington Post.

Mr Powell appeared to push back against that criticism, saying the central bank was taking seriously its job of balancing risks across the economy.

"Our gradual pace of racing interest rates has been an exercise in balancing risks. We know that moving too fast would risk shortening the expansion.

"We also know that moving too slowly - keeping interest rates too low for too long - could risk other distortions in the form of higher inflation or destabilising financial imbalances.

"Our path of gradual increases has been designed to balance these two risks, both of which we must take seriously."

The Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 618 points, or 2.5 per cent, with 29 of its 30 components rising. The S&P 500 was up 2.3 per cent, the Nasdaq advanced 3 per cent. All three major benchmarks accelerated into the close.

Futures markets suggested that rather than three rates hikes in 2019, there may only be two. The US 10-year Treasury note was little changed at 3.06 per cent in late trade. The dollar spot index was down 0.6 per cent.

Mr Powell urged people to distinguish between market volatility and events that threaten financial stability.

"Large, sustained declines in equity prices can put downward pressure on spending and confidence. From the financial stability perspective, however, today we do not see dangerous excesses in the stock market."

Responding to a question after the speech, Mr Powell warned that expansions inevitably come to an end.

"Business cycles don't last forever, unless you're Australia, where they're in year 27 - which feels like forever," he quipped.

Key pointsJerome Powell said there was a "great deal" to like about the outlook.

After a surge in growth

there are growing signs

of a slowdown.
direction: bullish for US equities and growth stocks

headline: 'Triage before a bit of surgery' for troubled Fletcher Building
text:
Investors hoping for a quick turnaround in the fortunes of Australasian construction and building products group Fletcher Building will need to show much more patience, says one fund manager who described a big $710 million-plus capital raising as "triage before a bit of surgery".

Fletcher began trading again on Friday on the New Zealand and Australian stock exchanges after a three-day halt while a 1-for-4.46 entitlement offer was undertaken for institutions, with retail shareholders getting their turn from April 23.

The Australian dollar price in the offer has been set at $4.51 for local retail shareholders, a 22 per cent discount to the price at which Fletcher was trading on the ASX on Friday afternoon. The stock lost 3 per cent and was sitting at $5.82 as it returned to trading. It was trading at $8 a year ago.

Harbour Asset Management portfolio manager Shane Solly said while new Fletcher chief executive Ross Taylor was making the right moves in cleaning up the mess, there were substantial operational problems still needing to be ironed out.

"I think investors need to be pretty patient," he said. The capital raising helped repair the balance sheet in the short term, while selling the $1 billion-plus Formica laminates business in the United States was logical after years of being a drag.

"It's triage before a bit of surgery," Mr Solly said. United States-based noteholders owed $NZ1.1 billion had been vigorously agitating for serious balance sheet repair, with some of them having been burnt in the collapse of the United Kingdom's second largest construction company, Carillion, a few months ago.

Fletcher generates about 30 per cent of its revenues from Australian businesses including plumbing and bathroom supplies chain Tradelink, Iplex and Stramit, but Mr Taylor signalled they weren't for sale.

There had been speculation last week that Bunnings owner Wesfarmers may have been eyeing a weakened Fletcher, but they haven't been buying any shares.

It was the New Zealand commercial construction arm which sent Fletcher spiralling, with the company rocked by big write-downs of almost $NZ1 billion over the past two years stemming from construction cost blowouts on 14 commercial projects in New Zealand.

The cost blowouts claimed the scalp of chairman Sir Ralph Norris. Sir Ralph, a former Commonwealth Bank of Australia chief executive from 2005 to 2011, will step down as chairman by the annual general meeting in October.

On April 17, Fletcher announced it was raising $NZ750 million ($710 million) in the deeply discounted rights issue and it would also sell its Formica and Roof Tile Group businesses to shrink its focus to just New Zealand and Australia.

Mr Taylor, who took over as chief executive of Fletcher in November, 2017, has been undertaking a strategic review with the final results due in June. But the review was sufficiently advanced to enable the announcement of the proposed sell-offs along with the capital raising.

Mr Taylor is a former UGL chief executive in Australia. Harbour's Mr Solly said he was the right type of chief executive as Fletcher looked to restore some credibility because he had strong operational experience rather than being focused on mergers and acquisitions.

"He's the right type of person. Get the sleeves rolled up, get the hard hat on," Mr Solly said.

The US-based Formica laminates business was acquired for $900 million 11 years ago, just before the global financial crisis. Fletcher bought it from private equity firms Cerberus Capital Management and Oaktree. It was aiming to build a global champion in kitchen laminates but it was ultimately a serious drag on investment returns. Experts say it is likely that mid-market private equity funds will be among the buyers this time around.
direction: bearish for Fletcher Building shares

headline: Macquarie's Freeman shifts to Paraway CFO
text:
Macquarie Group's investor relations manager James Freeman is shifting roles within the financial services giant after agreeing to become finance chief of agricultural firm Paraway Pastoral Company.

The former Deutsche Bank financial services research analyst joined Macquarie about three years ago, initially in the funds management group before moving to investor relations.

Paraway - which operates sheep and cattle businesses and has a combined land holding of more than 4.4 million hectares - is a wholly owned operating entity of the Macquarie Pastoral Fund. The fund is managed by Macquarie Agricultural Funds Management, which sits within the group's infrastructure and real assets division.

Mr Freeman's new role adds to changes in Macquarie's investor relations unit over the past year, with former boss Karen Khadi shifting to become a chief financial officer within the asset management unit. She was replaced late last year by Sam Dobson, who was previously Macquarie's local head of transport research.

Paraway's website notes it has acquired more than 30 properties and aggregated those into 23 pastoral businesses. The company can run more than 200,000 cattle and 240,000 sheep across its portfolio, in addition to dryland and irrigated cropping.

Investors in Macquarie Group will receive a first-quarter trading update at its AGM on Thursday.
direction: neutral to slightly bearish for Macquarie Group

headline: Dynamic duo launches first project
text:
In an industry dominated by men and big egos, Sonya Miller and Olivia Christie are looking to carve out their own niche in property development with a more collaborative approach.

After long careers in construction, development and project management - with senior roles at companies including FKP Property Group, Charter Hall, Multiplex, Grocon and VicUrban - the duo has launched its first standalone development project under the banner of Dynamic Property Group (DPG).

The project is a boutique $25 million apartment complex with 14 designer residences overlooking Elsternwick Park lake in leafy Elwood, in Melbourne's inner south.

The duo snapped up the $6 million site - comprising two neighbouring houses on St Kilda Street - in late 2016 in an off-market deal and secured a permit last year from the City of Port Phillip without having to argue a case at a planning tribunal.

"We chatted with the neighbours and came up with a scheme that didn't push the boundaries of what was possible on the site," said Ms Miller.

"One of our points of difference is our focus on taking a collaborative approach."

The other is the experience both have gained working on a variety of commercial and residential projects for some of the country's biggest listed and private property development companies.

Before establishing DPG, they set up project and development management consultancy Armitage Jones, winning management of projects such as Charter Hall's $600 million Wesley Place development in the heart of the Melbourne CBD, the $100 million Balmoral Quay Marina townhouse development in Port Phillip and Melbourne restaurateur Chris Lucas' Chin Chin Sydney restaurant in The Griffiths Teas building in Surry Hills.

"We started off working on construction sites so it's given us a great grounding," Ms Christie said. "And working on these prized projects, we've had to prove ourselves, time and time again."

Designed by architects DKO, their three-level Elwood Park development features 14 two- and three-bedroom apartments targeted at local downsizers and families. Prices start at $1.25 million for a 95-square-metre apartment.

Penthouses include rooftop terraces while the ground-floor apartments have their own private garden terraces. The project also incorporates hanging vertical gardens on the facade, the use of natural materials like timber, and floor-to-ceiling glazing to allow plenty of natural light.

Ms Christie said both were particularly passionate about the evolving wellness side of the residential property industry.

While off-the-plan sales have just begun at Elwood Park, the pair are already hunting for further opportunities in the inner suburbs.
direction: supportive for boutique property developers and real estate sector

headline: Disruptor disrupted: Auditor warns on BuyMyplace future
text:
The high cost of online Google advertising is burning through the cash reserves of real estate disruptors, making it harder for them turn a profit.

Grant Thornton, the auditor of ASX-listed BuyMyplace (BMP), has highlighted the DIY selling platform's precarious financial position after it reported a $2.8 million loss for the six months to December 2017.

Its December half-year results show BMP spent $870,000 on advertising and marketing, a 68 per cent increase on the prior period, and more than half of its total revenue for the period of $1.5 million.

In its independent auditors review report, Grant Thornton noted the company's latest net loss, up from $1.7 million in the prior period, indicated that "a material uncertainty exists that may cast significant doubt on the company's ability to continue as a going concern".

BMP, founded in 2007 and listed in 2016, helps home sellers sell their homes without an estate agent for as little as $700, one of dozens of companies that have grabbed a small but growing market niche as vendors look for ways to reduce the cost of selling their homes.

BMP has sold more than 5000 homes and achieved average savings of $20,500.

However, as with all other disruptors including British giant Purplebricks - which is growing rapidly in Australia - BMP (which has a market cap of just $9 million) has had to invest heavily in Google Ads and Google Keywords (popular search terms) to promote its services to consumers.

BMP CEO Colin Keating told The Australian Financial Review it was "not unusual" for micro cap companies at BMP's stage of growth to have such concerns called out by its auditors and it remained highly viable.

But, he said, since taking over as CEO in November last year, a significant review of the business had seen BMP "address the high cost of Google advertising" with the company making "significant reductions in optimising how we use both with Google Adwords and Google Keywords".

"It's difficult to go head-to-head with Purplebricks with its obvious deep pockets," Mr Keating said.

Colin Sacks, which runs another online platform, forsalebyowner.com.au, said it had scaled back its Google Adwords campaigns after the costs had spiralled as high as $9 per click and was now more focused on promotion through social media.

"We decided some time ago to scale back on our Google Adwords spending because of the escalating costs over the last few years for search terms relevant to our business. Real estate is a highly competitive sector on the internet, and that is also reflected in the cost per click of many search terms relevant to that industry," Mr Sacks said.

"Google Adwords was a fantastic marketing tool for our business [when it was about $2 a click] ... but when it reached $9, it no longer became a sustainable form of advertising for us," he said.

Mr Sacks said the great thing about online social platforms is the ability to "choose the demographics of who gets shown the advert, which results in the advertising spend being more specific to the audience you are trying to reach".
direction: bearish for BuyMyplace shares

headline: TAFE NSW out of touch, annoys business with uninformed strategy
text:
Skills education in NSW is in crisis, with business complaining of difficulty in working with TAFE, enrolments falling and an independent report arguing a massive reorganisation several years ago has returned the organisation to a discredited, centralised structure against the recommendations of three earlier government reports.

The sudden and unexpected resignation last week of the head of TAFE NSW, Jon Black, on an unrelated matter has drawn attention to the $1.8 billion TAFE NSW enterprise.

Businesspeople employing apprentices in NSW say it is impossible to obtain official documents showing the attendance records of students, and TAFE NSW will not supply employers with apprentices' training schedules.

On at least one occasion, TAFE NSW declined to supply an apprentice's results to the employer "on grounds of privacy". Attendance records and enrolment have been a recurring problem with NSW TAFE. In 2016-17 it had to spend $10 million to deal with failures in its student administration system, simply to establish the integrity of the data.

According to the NSW auditor-general, that was despite spending $40 million three years ago on a student administration and learning system which is now so unreliable it will be replaced with a new system at a cost of $89 million.

Last year TAFE NSW reported a deficit of $51 million compared to a surplus of $65 million the previous year. This came mainly because of a decline in student revenue.

While the issue of student revenue has been clouded by the problems in TAFE NSW enrolment data, figures for government-funded students show a fall in enrolments of 11.8 per cent in the past 12 months, and a drop of 16.8 per cent since the peak in 2012.

In July, a report for the LH Martin Institute, a centre for education governance at Melbourne University, said the "OneTAFE" restructure in 2016, introduced three months after Mr Black was appointed, amalgamated 10 autonomous TAFEs into one organisation and centralised teaching and curriculum development.

This went against the recommendations of a report by management consultant Brian Scott in the late 1980s which had paved the way for a widely acclaimed "dispersed institute" model. Authors of the report, adjunct professor and former director of TAFE Western Sydney Institute, Robin Shreeve, and Joanna Palser, described the 2016 reforms as a "sudden reversal" of the previous strategy, which had been intended to reduce public expenditure and increase efficiency.

A subsequent report by the former secretary of Prime Minister and Cabinet, Michael Keating, and another by the former managing director of Deutsche Bank, Kerry Schott, had recommended devolution of responsibility away from TAFE head office, especially in relation to negotiating teachers' employment conditions.

The 2016 restructure was meant to be financed by "fee for service" training programs sold to business and governments. However, the LH Martin report said there had been "no real analysis of how big this market was and whether TAFE had the capability and capacity to service it".

Former teachers at TAFE NSW are angry about changes at the organisation and are using a lobby group, TAFE Community Alliance (TCA), to argue for reform.

Spokesperson for TCA, and a former deputy director-general of the NSW department education and training, Jozefa Sobski said by entering the contestable-training market TAFE NSW had trimmed down its core activities - which meant it was creating courses that no longer responded to local needs.

The staff hiring policy had shifted away from employing people with industry skills and giving them teaching qualifications.

This meant the teaching workforce was less relevant to business and employers. Ms Sobski described Mr Black's OneTAFE model as a "techno fantasy" which contradicted the TAFE Act, which said the organisation's objective was to provide services relevant to the needs of industry, business, students and other client groups.

"His goals were ambitions but he failed to understand the core mission of the organisation.

"My question for the NSW government is: how is TAFE NSW fulfilling its mission," Ms Sobski said.

By contrast to the decline in student enrolments of 11.8 per cent in NSW last year, enrolments in Victoria went up by 8.9 per cent and in South Australia they declined by 2 per cent.

Apart from the problems unique to TAFE NSW, the sector nationally has sustained a 30.1 per cent fall in enrolments since 2012, with blame being pinned on a crisis over shonky private operators and a debacle in student fee assistance combined with a federal policy which supercharged university enrolments at the expense of vocational education.

The LH Martin report said a national review was needed for the entire sector.
direction: bearish for TAFE NSW

headline: Schwartz checks out at IGA site
text:
Developer turned publisher Morry Schwartz was among the winners at a portfolio auction in Melbourne, with the $21.55 million sale of one of the city's best-located supermarkets.

Six of the seven properties were knocked down under the hammer at the CBRE-run auction, with multiple bidders paying nearly $48 million in total for the swag of properties.

The IGA supermarket at 620 Collins Street stands opposite Southern Cross Station, one of the city's busiest intersections for pedestrians. It sold on a yield of 4.13 per cent.

The supermarket is at the base of the 28-storey Liberty Tower, developed almost 20 years ago by Mr Schwartz, the publisher of The Monthly and Saturday Paper, and his brother Danny.

The brothers had held onto the large retail site since the tower was completed in 2002.

"It's market price. It's the price that I expected. It was very, very close to its valuation price," Mr Schwartz told The Australian Financial Review.

Other properties sold in the CBRE auction included a free-standing Officeworks in Hobart, which changed hands for $11,725,000 on a 6.81 per cent yield, a 7-Eleven service station in North Melbourne, which sold for $4 million on a 4.57 per cent yield, and another 7-Eleven service station at Kallangur in Queensland, which sold for $5.27 million on a 5.88 per cent yield.

An early learning centre in Albury sold for $2.65 million on a 7.1 per cent yield while a pharmacy in Melbourne's east sold for $2.57 million on a 6.1 per cent yield.

CBRE's Mark Wizel said the portfolio auction process was attracting offshore investors and gave prospective purchasers the opportunity to bid on a number of properties at the one event, where they could be confident they were buying at "true market value".

A year ago the Schwartz family sold a prime Melbourne CBD portfolio for close to $100 million.
direction: bullish for retail property and REITs

headline: Tax-loss selling another risk for the ASX dogs
text:
Blue-chip names such as Telstra, AMP, Perpetual and QBE will come under scrutiny over the final trading week of the financial year as investors consider dumping the stocks to crystallise tax losses.

TMS Capital portfolio manager Ben Clark said that because the market has performed strongly this year - the S&P/ASX200 Index has gained 9.8 per cent so far in 2017-18 - investors will have been looking for ways to reduce their overall tax bill due to gains made elsewhere.

The four worst performers in the top 200 so far this financial year, and therefore perceived to be some of the most vulnerable to so-called tax-loss selling, are Telstra, down 36.7 per cent, childcare operator G8 Education, down 35.5 per cent, wealth giant AMP, down 30.3 per cent and Priceline parent Australian Pharmaceutical Industries, down 29.7 per cent.

The top 10 laggards also include financial services giants like Perpetual, Magellan and QBE, private hospital operator Ramsay Health Care and veterinary chain Greencross.

"Companies that are more vulnerable are the ones that have performed badly," Mr Clark said. "What we normally see is that these kind of stocks probably have another leg lower."

Professional investors are aware of the tax-loss selling phenomenon and will be keeping a keen eye on the worst-performing stocks to assess if they should pick up a bargain, he said.

Indeed, the ASX has climbed sharply over the past week and is showing a gain of 2.2 per cent so far.

The gains for the ASX were made as other markets have put in a less than stellar performance and this has led to speculation that this year's tax loss selling is mainly complete and cashed-up investors are looking for attractive market entry points. "It could be one factor - every other market is lacklustre," said AMP Capital head of investments Shane Oliver. "Even on Monday we were pretty resilient to the trade wars story. A rebound from tax loss selling could be an explanation," he added in a reference to the escalating tensions between the US and China that have pressured other stock markets.

Banks have performed particularly well over the past week, having fallen sharply in the past couple of months.

"It's quite possible that investors had decided to sell their banks," Mr Oliver said. "They had come down in price and investors might have decided to lock in the losses to offset gains made elsewhere as the market is broadly higher over the year. You could argue that the banks were oversold. They then started to turn and investors may have thought that they had better get back in."

Richard Coppleson at Bell Potter agreed that tax-loss selling appears to have been a feature of the market this year, pointing to companies such as telecommunications reseller Amaysim and wealth technology provider IRESS as potential examples.

He noted that institutional investors can start selling for tax purposes as early as the middle of May while individual investors are more likely to sell out of a stock to crystallise their tax position in the early weeks of June.

"It's a funny phenomenon that financial market economists would likely recognise as a market inefficiency," Mr Oliver at AMP Capital commented.

It's also one that remains stubbornly persistent. "Normally investors such as Warren Buffett would recognise patterns such as this but, for some reason, this pattern tends to persist," he said.

Another reason that selling of poorer performers is seen near the end of the financial year is that fund managers may start to clear out their worst stocks from portfolios, according to Mr Clark.

"In July and August fund managers will be sitting down with their investors and if you owned a share on June 30 you will be talking about it," he said.
direction: downward pressure on underperforming blue-chip stocks due to tax-loss selling

headline: Billy Bragg seeks harmony on Brexit
text:
People's choice The singer-songwriter looks back on days out with Albo and Boris

His fans call him the people's poet, and English singer-songwriter Billy Bragg has a fittingly people-centric solution to the Brexit malaise: a citizens' assembly.

Ahead of shows in Australia next year, where the left-wing activist will play early albums such as Workers Playtime on the guitar with which they were recorded, and will likely catch up with old friend Anthony Albanese, Bragg told AFR Weekend that a citizens' assembly was the least divisive way of guiding what Britain does next.

"Get the politicians out of it, broadcast the whole thing live on the internet, and let's have a bit of deliberation about Brexit," he tells AFR Weekend, sitting at home in Dorset, but with an accent still firmly in Barking, Essex, where he was born in 1957.

Even before another, less likely, old acquaintance - Boris Johnson - threw Parliament into chaos with his attempts to prorogue it and force a no-deal Brexit, some on Bragg's Remain side of the debate had been calling for a citizens' assembly: a randomly chosen group of voters who hear independent expert evidence on a matter of national importance and make recommendations to the government, which usually become the subject of a referendum.

Bragg points out that Ireland used an assembly in 2016 to sort out the otherwise intractable issue of legalising abortion. "No one seems to want to deal with nuance at the moment, but if anything's going to save us from Brexit, it's highlighting the practical realities for [Ireland's] Good Friday Agreement, for the economic arrangements we've built up over 40 years with our European neighbours," he says.

Many on Bragg's side of the Brexit debate want to go straight to a second referendum, but the singer-songwriter behind A New England says that won't heal division like a citizens' assembly could. "I want to get out of Brexit but I don't want us to keep coming back to it every five years. To put this to bed we need 15 per cent of Leavers to come vote with us, so let's discuss the palatable compromises we're willing to make to bring them on board," he says.

"It may be the result of a citizens' assembly is a second Brexit referendum, but at least it's come from a place of consensus rather than division."

Bragg has a unique insight into the British Prime Minister. In 2000, at the behest of the BBC, he invited Johnson - then editor of The Spectator - to visit the Glastonbury music festival for the first time. In footage now immortalised on YouTube, the unlikely pair get henna tattoos, encounter nudists and perform in the poetry tent (Johnson recites The Iliad in ancient Greek), as the future prime minister declares the event a "capitalist extravaganza".

Bragg has never spoken to Johnson since, but says that day gave him the measure of the man. "We had a right old day ... [but] I got the sense from him walking around Glastonbury that he didn't have any perception of other people's needs, he was just in the moment wherever he was," Bragg says.

"Love them or hate them, at least [former prime ministers] Thatcher and Blair had plans. Beyond whatever keeps him at No. 10, Boris has no central principle driving him forward - he's more akin to Trump."

Bragg had a less fraught political accomplice at December's Fairgrounds Festival south of Sydney, where Albanese showed him around and interviewed him on stage.

Bragg has known Albanese since the days of Red Wedge, the musicians' collective that campaigned against Margaret Thatcher ahead of the 1987 British election, and which also had ties to the Australian Labor Party.

"Albo really knows his stuff, politics and music, so that was a lot of fun," Bragg says. The singer was pleased when Albanese quoted one of his recent song titles, Not Everything That Counts Can Be Counted, in his first speech as Labor leader this year.

However, he demurs when asked if Albanese might have to shed some principles if he's to be elected.

"I have respect for anyone who's trying to make sense of the world the way it is. Show me a politician who's doing more good things than bad things, and that's good enough for me," he says.

And would the man who so eloquently outlined his vision for a kinder, gentler world in 1985 chart-topper Between the Wars ever stand for office himself? "I've spent 35 years becoming a beloved entertainer. If I wanted everyone to hate me, I'd go and be a traffic warden or something," he says.

Billy Bragg will play shows focused on his first six albums (1983-96) on an Australian tour beginning in April.
direction: UK political uncertainty weighs on market sentiment

headline: Impact investor set to go beyond ESG
text:
It's often the little questions that can tell an investor the most about a company.

When fund manager Tim King found himself in front of the management team of an ASX-listed healthcare company recently, he asked why it had chosen not to reveal its carbon emissions.

King was told not to worry. The emissions were small, and clearly not a big focus for the executives.

But King was taken aback. "You wouldn't say the same thing about the balance sheet or the P&L," he tells Chanticleer.

As the chief investment officer and co-founder of new impact investing firm Melior Investment Management, the answers to these sorts of questions matter more to King than most.

The firm, which King has started with chief executive Lucy Steed, officially opens its doors on Monday. The pair believes Melior is the first dedicated equities-focused impact manager in Australia.

King and Steed have been backed by private equity veterans Anthony Kerwick and Rob Koczkar, who worked together at Pacific Equity Partners before founding Adamantem Capital.

Koczkar is passionate about the impact investing sector, having helped steer Australia's largest social impact investment: the creation of childcare group Goodstart Early Learning, which was born from the ashes of ABC Learning in 2010.

Koczkar has watched the debate around environment, social and governance (ESG) investment principles develop over the past 15 years. He believes the sector is at a tipping point, where the widespread adoption of and support for ESG investing is evolving into momentum behind the style of impact investing that Melior will pursue.

"They [Steed and King] had a shared passion for the power of investment capital to shape behaviours in the corporate sector."

King, a former head of company and ESG research for Colonial First State Global Asset Management's Australian Equities Core Fund, and Steed, the former head of managed accounts and managed funds at BT Financial Group, have spent 18 moths developing a "rigorous and repeatable" framework that is based on the United Nation's 17 sustainable development goals.

There's no doubt these principles can be pretty high level and somewhat broad - themes such as "no poverty", "zero hunger" and "life below water" don't fit neatly into investment criteria.

So Melior has developed investment themes to turn these high level ideas into action. These range from the unsurprising (such as climate action, renewable energy, water and health) through to the unique (such as inclusive finance, affordable housing and responsible consumption).

The focus for Melior (Latin for "better") is on companies that are actually doing good and having a positive impact - rather than just minimising harm.

Melior's mandate allows it to invest in the ASX All Ordinaries and the main Kiwi market index, the NZX 50.

King and Steed say Melior's investment universe within that cohort is about 200 companies. But the pair say that had the firm started seven years ago, it would have been only about 78 companies.

Rio Tinto, which has disposed of its coal assets in the past 18 months, is a good example of a company that has made the transition from the naughty list to the nice list.

Refreshingly, Steed and King take a pragmatic view of their investment universe. "No company is black or white in this listed investment space," Steed says.

So while there are sectors the firm won't touch - gambling and thermal coal are good examples - the scoring system they have developed will take into account the fact that companies are not necessarily easy to classify.

Mining, for example, isn't off the Melior radar. Given Melior supports themes such as the electrification of vehicles, it wouldn't be practical to rule out miners who produce materials that will be crucial to this transformation.

"We would consider investing in a nickel, or copper or lithium mining company, as examples, provided that they extract those materials in a responsible way," King says, adding this would mean close consideration of issues like safety, governance and mine rehabilitation.

The scorecard will also attempt to take a company's future intentions into account. For example, it wouldn't be possible for Melior to invest in South32 at present given its thermal coal assets in South Africa. But as the miner gets closer to acting on its stated intention to dispose of this business, its score will improve.

The caveat is that a company cannot earn more than 5 per cent of its revenue from one of Melior's no-go areas, such as thermal coal.

Importantly, King will not buy ESG research off the shelf, a common and potentially problematic strategy used by many fund managers. Often the providence of the ESG ratings can be somewhat vague; one prominent rating firm is understood to use algorithms that measure online mentions of ESG issues as its key tool for rating companies.

Not surprisingly, King sees developing Melior's own ESG research as central to its product offering. But he also argues off-the-shelf ESG research too often provides "a smorgasbord of issues, but those issues are not always material".

King and Steed have conducted 170 company visits as they have developed their ESG and financial analysis.

King says companies have been, in the main, responsive on ESG issues - in part because there are economic gains to be made too.

He gives the example of the real estate investment trust sector, where a sort of arms race has broken out as companies try to outdo each other on the plans to go carbon neutral or even carbon positive. Such moves are positive for the environment, but also lower costs.

Still, King says he's astonished when he hears some directors and executives say they've never been asked the sort of questions Melior wants answered.

He provides the example of a healthcare company which, unlike many of its peers, didn't disclose its animal testing regime. When King asked why, the company clammed up.

"That's when we start worrying as to what is actually going on behind the scenes," King says.

He argues that big-ticket probes into corporate misconduct - think the banking royal commission, the royal commission into aged care and federal parliament's inquiry into franchising - are, at their core, ESG issues.

Melior hopes its approach will help it spot the next set of potential regulatory, reputation or legal risks well before they reach the royal commission stage.

"We can drive better returns because we can avoid a material set of risks that most people aren't considering," King says.

On the topic of banking, King is blunt about where Melior would be putting its money.

"In terms of the big four, at this point of time - and I stress things can change - Westpac is the only bank that makes the grade from our perspective."

He argues the bank is further through the remediation and restructuring process than its peers, and it earns extra points for being one of the few Australian companies committed to a science-based emissions target.

The recent decision taken by Westpac's New Zealand subsidiary to not ask potential hires what they currently earn in a bid to avoid gender bias is another good initiative, King says.

Importantly, Melior will talk publicly about its investment decisions, including by regularly reporting on the progress and impact of their stock picks and by making their voting intentions clear.

Koczkar says the firm wants to take a role in educating companies and other investors. "That's got to be a public conversation," he says.

Steed says Melior has been in talks with super funds, universities, and financial adviser groups as it rattles the tin for potential investors. High net worth individuals are also increasingly interested in impact investing, spurred on in part by their Millennial kids and grandkids.

"We are finding a lot of their offspring are putting on the pressure," Steed says.

And while it's been a tough period for small fund managers, with several shutting their doors, Koczkar insists Melior's team is committed for the long term.

"You don't get people like Lucy and Tim leave so-called real jobs without a sense that its both going to be financial viable and change the outcomes for our community in a really meaningful way," he says.

"We've got plenty of runway. We're not going anywhere."
direction: supportive for ESG and impact-focused equities

headline: Lau tells Australia it must resolve China spat
text:
Hong Kong logistics tycoon John Lau says Canberra must resolve its political differences with China for the sake of economic growth, dismissing fears that the growing diplomatic row between the two countries will hurt Australian imports.

"Sitting in Hong Kong, I really can't understand why there is this dichotomy in Australia. Economically there is a close-knit relationship. One day this dichotomy has to be resolved. It is up to the political parties to work this through," Mr Lau told The Australian Financial Review on Tuesday.

"I don't expect the Australian government will be in the same boat as the Philippines government. I think your government has enough experience [to deal with the relationship]. Most businesses in Australia by now know the future lies in this fast development of China. It is a no-brainer."

Mr Lau is the founding chairman of Australia-listed e-commerce venture eCargo, which helps exporters sell products into China's booming e-commerce market. His powerful shareholders include retailing identities Rupert Myer and Solomon Lew. Hong Kong and Australia-listed eCargo acquired a 45 per cent stake in Jessica's Suitcase, the online firm owned by Jessica Rudd, the daughter of former prime minister Kevin Rudd, in January. It will hold its annual general meeting in Melbourne on Wednesday.

Ms Rudd, who sits on the company's board, said she was not worried that the political tensions would spill over into Australia's trade relationship with China. "Politicians will talk and the business people will boo," she said.

Ms Rudd's father has been a critic of the Coalition's approach to the China relationship, accusing Prime Minister Malcolm Turnbull of offending China.

Ms Rudd said politics was not impacting on demand for Australian products in China. "What we continue to see is that our customers in China are very keen to have an Australian lifestyle. If Australian business has a China question, we have a China answer."

Mr Lau said eCargo was profitable after three years of transformation and acquisitions as it seeks to tap the opportunity from China's middle-class consumers for Australian dairy, seafood, wine and other products.

Mr Lau is the founder of the international freight forwarding, supply chain and logistics services groups CS Logistics Holdings, based in Hong Kong. He said his investment in the Australian market highlighted his confidence in Chinese demand for Australia's intellectual property, management expertise and brands despite recent blips in the diplomatic relationship.

eCargo's new chief executive Eva Zhang said Treasury Wine Estates, which last month complained the political situation was slowing down the time it took to clear imports at Chinese ports, said eCargo had sold more than 4000 bottles of the company's wine since late last year. "Over the last six months we have transformed the business," she said.

Mr Lau said Australia did not have the same economic muscle to go down the same path as US President Donald Trump and make economic threats. "I believe politicians will be sensible and businesses in Australia will have enough influence to resolve this spat."

He said eCargo was now cash-flow positive and he was confident the company would build on its acquisitions to serve as a "bridge between Australia and China".

While China's booming online retail market is expected to slow, Mr Lau said there was still huge growth potential.

US technology giant Google has announced it is investing $US550 million ($747 million) in Chinese e-commerce giant JD.com.
direction: bearish for exporters and trade-sensitive shares

headline: China omits key US caveat in truce spin
text:
Shanghai | China's propaganda machine went into overdrive on Monday, selling the message that President Xi Jinping had negotiated an apparent permanent truce in the country's trade war with the United States.

The country's main sharemarket rallied almost 3 per cent after state-run newspapers carried front-page images of Mr Xi and US President Donald Trump shaking hands after emerging from dinner in Buenos Aires with a deal that would reset the commercial relationship between the world's two biggest economies.

But there was no mention of the fact Mr Trump's ceasefire was conditional on Washington and Beijing resolving their differences over the more sensitive issues of intellectual property theft and technology transfer within 90 days.

While investors and foreign business executives in China welcomed the deal as a step in the right direction, privately many were sceptical about the long-term prospects of Mr Xi and Mr Trump turning the positive rhetoric into a permanent ceasefire. The deal announced after Sunday's dinner summit was light on detail and analysts noted China did not officially acknowledge the truce was temporary.

"The result is better than the market expected, but the huge divide remaining continues to suggest a bumpy ride ahead," Citi economist Li-Gang Liu said.

"Comparing the official statements, we find that the US stresses more China's structural reform issues on forced technology transfer, IP protection, non-tariff barriers, cyber intrusions and cyber theft, services and agriculture."

Ken Jarrett, president of the American Chamber of Commerce in Shanghai, said the outcome of the talks at the G20 gathering was encouraging, but peace would not be permanent unless Beijing addressed the structural issues that made it hard for US companies to compete in China.

"For that to happen, the Chinese government must address the long-standing concerns of the American business community regarding China's trade and investment policies: market access restrictions, weak intellectual property rights protection, unfair industrial policies, the slow pace of economic reforms and lack of regulatory transparency," Mr Jarrett said.

"A genuine response to those concerns, with measurable goals, is what our members want to see and we hope the discussions in Buenos Aires represent a step in that direction."

The Shanghai Composite Index rose 2.5 per cent after the two leaders both claimed victory in efforts to stave off a trade war that was wiping billions of dollars off global markets. China's biggest sharemarket has lost 22 per cent of its value so far this year. The Chinese currency strengthened against the US dollar on Monday.

Andy Rothman, investment strategist at Matthews Asia, said the result of the Buenos Aires dinner was positive, but Mr Xi would have to improve market access for foreign companies while Mr Trump would have to abandon his "misguided" focus on the bilateral trade deficit to make it work.

"I think the odds of after-dinner success are pretty good," Mr Rothman said.

"Given the very positive spin that Trump put on the meeting - calling it 'amazing and productive' - and his apparent desire to avoid a trade war which would damage the US economy and equity markets, as well as his re-election prospects, I expect him to instruct his team to focus on achieving a pragmatic deal.

"Xi will also want to avoid a trade war which could cost Chinese jobs, and he must also understand that the vast majority of concessions sought by the US, such as better protection for intellectual property, are critical to the future success of his own economy."

Mr Trump tweeted on Monday (AEDT) that China had agreed to reduce and remove tariffs on imports of cars from the United States. However, there was no confirmation on this agreement from China on Monday.

While China said it had agreed to import more US products, it did not give further details or acknowledge Washington's statement it had committed to negotiations on forced technology transfer, intellectual property protection, non-tariff barriers and cyber theft.

Meanwhile, the China Caixin manufacturing PMI for November rose to 50.2 in November from 50.1 a month earlier, which was better than expected and higher than last week's official manufacturing data, which fell to a two-year low.

Key pointsChina's main sharemarket rallied almost 3 per cent on the news of the trade deal.

Many are sceptical about turning the positive rhetoric into a permanent ceasefire.
direction: cautious for China equities and rate-sensitive sectors

headline: Taiwan investors buy Inala Plaza for $56 million
text:
The Raptis Group, founded by the late seafood king George Raptis, has sold the Inala Plaza Shopping Centre in Brisbane's south for more than $56 million to the Wen family's Chin Hong Investment Corporation.

Mr Raptis, who died in 2017, paid $32 million for the Woolworths and Aldi-anchored neighbourhood centre in 2013, when he purchased it from the 360 Capital Retail Fund.

The off-market deal with the Taiwanese investors was struck on a yield of 6.5 to 7 per cent. The sale of the Inala Plaza was brokered by CBRE's Joe Tynan and Michael Hedger, who were unable to comment.

The mall incorporates a speciality grocer and 60 speciality tenants including BWS, The Reject Shop and Lowes.

It stands on a three-hectare site at 156 Inala Avenue, on a major intersection that connects the Centenary Motorway and Beaudesert Road and is integrated with the adjoining Inala Civic Centre.

The mall's tenancy mix is focused on convenience-based retailing options with a mix of food and beverage, medical and service retailers.

Its sale comes amid growing scrutiny of retail property transactions after Vicinity Centres devalued its neighbourhood malls by 5.7 per cent recently.

Neighbourhood centres, which are typically anchored by a supermarket and other convenience-based retailers, are considered better insulated against the growth of online retailing but the Vicinity downgrade has raised questions about that thesis. Recent convenience retail property deals include Coles selling a supermarket near Geelong to local investors for $23.5 million. This week, SCA Property Group, which owns a $3.15 billion portfolio of Coles and Woolworths-anchored malls, reported a solid interim result with earnings up 17.5 per cent. For Chin Hong, the acquisition of Inala Plaza adds to its burgeoning Australian commercial property investments portfolio.

This includes the DFO Jindalee centre in Brisbane's western suburbs that it bought for $46.6 million in 2016, and the Burpengary Plaza in Brisbane's north that has undergone a $35 million redevelopment.

Chin Hong was drawn into a local government political controversy when it was revealed the company donated more than $60,000 to the election campaign of currently suspended Logan mayor Luke Smith in 2016.

At the time Chin Hong was seeking approval to develop one of its malls in Springwood.

Last year, Chin Hong Investment Corporation manager Jenshuo Wen rejected any link between the donations and any future plans the company may have for its Springwood shopping centre.
direction: bearish for retail property trusts

headline: Letters
text:
Rational coal approach would be win-win

Congratulations to Robert Milner (February 22) for pointing out the naivety of the anti-coal lobbyists' obstruction to our coal mining industry, in total disregard to the fact that this will not affect world coal usage at all.

A more rational approach would be for our governments to encourage coal, but divert most of the royalties and taxes towards the development and installation of renewable technologies. We could have jobs, improve pollution from Asia burning our cleaner coal, and speed up Australia's greening with more renewable energy.

John Dyson

Shepparton, Vic

Adani jobs push a short-term solution

Re: "Labor candidates spruik Adani jobs" (February 20). The anxiety of candidates in regional Queensland leading to a strong jobs focus in campaigning is understandable. However, they should temper their enthusiasm with a degree of realism. As they well know, mining jobs in the future are unlikely to be found in coal, especially not in mining thermal coal in the Galilee Basin. At a time when our trading partners are more sensitive to air pollution from coal-fired power stations, we learn an inconvenient fact about coal from Adani's proposed Carmichael mine: It is not as good as Adani would have us believe as regards pollution when burnt.

Politicians and unionists with workers' true interests at heart will refrain from pushing the "jobs" barrow too hard and instead explore the "Just Transition" avenue as well.

John Gare

Kew East, Vic

Don't kick watchdogs,

boost their funding

The latest brickbat hurled at our hapless corporate regulators has come from the IMF ("Australian regulators must lift their game", February 23-24).

Even so, amid all the furore over the royal commission, it seems the politicians got off lightly.

There has been much criticism about the reluctance of ASIC, ACCC and APRA to litigate, but little has been said about those responsible for their niggardly funding. Both major parties have maintained effective, if subtle, control over regulators by letting it be known that a "light touch" is required.

Over the years, we have read about how the latest court loss by ASIC or ACCC will blow a hole in their budget, with the inevitable result they will have to think twice about taking on large corporations. Is it too much to hope that governments will, from now on, see the replenishment of regulators' resources as part of their duty?

Gale Treadgold

Burradoo, NSW

Aid cut exploited

During Julie Bishop's watch as foreign minister, we saw China's steady encroachment on the South Pacific, with nary a thing done by our government to counteract it. In fact quite the opposite occurred - Bishop oversaw the biggest-ever reduction in Australia's foreign aid spend, creating the vacuum that China has been so adroit at exploiting.

Greg Poropat

Albion, Qld
direction: bearish for coal miners and related infrastructure stocks

headline: WOULD YOU PAY $8M FOR THIS HOUSE?
text:
Property It's known as the Shanghai of the South, and prices in Chatswood on Sydney's lower north shore reflect its status as a magnet for Chinese cash, writes Aaron Patrick.

There is a seven-bedroom orange-brick house for sale in Chatswood, with plush brown carpeting, fake marble floors and grated doors. Outside, a few stunted trees cling to a lawn so small it takes less time to mow than to wheel a lawnmower to and from the double garage and fire up.

Local real estate agent Mendy Lau is trying to sell the Nicholson Street property, which is opposite one of Chatswood's few generous parks, for a Hong Kong-based owner for $8.38 million - a price that places the outer-suburban-like house among the trophy homes that overlook the harbour from Sydney's lower north shore.

As property owners in Sydney, Melbourne, Brisbane and Adelaide celebrate what looks like the end of a two-year price dip, the Chatswood house is a pricey cog in one of the most important and least-understood forces driving the $6.6 trillion Australian home market: Chinese cash.

Through their purchasing power, strong family ties and specific tastes, Chinese citizens and Australians of Chinese descent have created microeconomies where the conventional rules of property valuations don't seem to apply.

Nonetheless, Chinese demand is such a big swing factor that Asian-dominated markets like Chatswood may be a canary-like indicator for property overall.

Ten kilometres and six train stops north of central Sydney, Chatswood is the most desirable suburb for many families from China. About 34 per cent of the suburb's residents are of Chinese heritage, and 26 per cent were born in mainland China or Hong Kong, according to the 2016 census.

Chatswood is a brand for buyers of Chinese descent. Houses in the adjacent suburbs of Artarmon, Roseville and Willoughby sell for approximately 15 to 20 per cent less than similar properties in Chatswood, according to Tim Holgate, the sales director of Belle Property Lane Cove.

From the pork dumplings at the New Shanghai restaurant in Chatswood Chase to the raw fish heads on sale at the Mandarin shopping centre, central Chatswood looks, feels and sounds a little like downtown Shanghai. Many older migrants, uncomfortable driving, insist on living within walking distance of shops.

"I think most ethnic groups, they live together," says Tina Lee, the owner of the One Agency real estate firm in the suburb. "Most Asian migrants, they like to live in the area for the low maintenance. When they go downstairs there are cafes, shops, supermarkets. You just name it."

This Saturday, Holgate plans to auction 3 Bowen Street on the west side of the Pacific Highway, which places the property in Chatswood West. The renovated, semi-detached house is a 15-minute walk from Chatswood's main shopping centres, which puts it outside the Chinese buying zone.

"If that was 300 metres from Chatswood Chase it could go for $2.5 million," Holgate says. "Our guidance is 1.8."

At the start of the decade, the average asking price in the suburb was nudging towards $1 million, according to SQM Research. Last January, it hit $2.27 million, making Chatswood one of the most expensive non-beach or harbourside suburbs in Australia's most expensive city. (To obtain consistent data, SQM relies on sellers' target prices instead of reported sales.)

Then, in the Year of the Dog, the Shanghai of the South turned on a penny - downwards. The precise trigger remains unclear. The banking regulator had gradually been tightening lending controls since 2014, a policy that led the broader Sydney market to start spluttering in the first half of 2017, and turn down in the second.

The macro-prudential credit crimp counteracted the stimulus of falling interest rates, but applied only to Australian loans. Foreigners, especially wealthy Chinese who could extract their money from China, were often paying in cash.

On July 1, 2017, the NSW government had imposed an extra 8 per cent tax on non-resident foreign buyers, who aren't allowed to buy second-hand homes anyway. In theory, the tax on a $2 million Chatswood off-the-plan apartment for a buyer from China was an incredible $255,490.

In practice, though, many buyers of Chinese heritage are Australian citizens living locally, or in Singapore or Hong Kong , according to one of the suburb's most prolific agents, who asked not to be identified.

Around the same time as the stamp duty hike, estate agents said they picked up talk that the Chinese government had started enforcing capital controls more rigidly. The claims were plausible but hard to verify.

Demand evaporated. By the end of 2018 the average asking price in the suburb was $1.69 million - a drop of 26 per cent in 11 months, SQM monthly data found, and a far greater fall than the 9.9 per cent drop across Sydney over the full year.

The turnaround, as elsewhere, was as abrupt as the decline. When the Liberal Party won the federal election on May 18, the political threat to property investment disappeared. That month, Chatswood houses bounced up to $2.5 million. Since then, sales prices have averaged $2.42 million.

"More than 50 per cent of the people coming through our open houses are Chinese," says Brent Courtney, who works the suburb for McGrath, referring to Australian-Chinese buyers and foreigners. "I'm finding prices are very strong. Probably a 10 per cent gain off the bottom from late last year."

Six of the seven houses and apartments listed for auction last Saturday sold, according to the Domain real estate site, on a day the city-wide clearance rate was 77 per cent. This weekend, another seven are up.

As a sign of foreign demand, at a time when Australians are fiscally wary, Chatswood's revival is contributing to a wave of excitment that has swept Sydney's north shore and may be influencing the eastern suburbs and inner west - an urban spread valued about the same as New Zealand's entire housing market.

Sydney-wide house and apartment prices are up 5 per cent since the end of June, according to CoreLogic.

The reality, though, is more complicated than the headlines suggest.

In Chatswood, the new demand doesn't appear to have been matched by a surge in supply, agents say. In a season which typically provides agents more than one-third of their annual sales, there were 120 houses and apartments for sale on the Domain website at the end of September. A year earlier there were 187.

"It's not like a normal spring," Courtney says. "We could be 30 to 40 per cent down in the level of houses [sold]."

And while Chinese buyers have returned, agents report fewer than during the boom. "One in two, or two in three, are Chinese buyers," Courtney says.

"Up to mid-2017 it would have been 90 per cent of buyers were Chinese."

A number theories are posited to explain what's going on. Some are circular. One agent says Chatswood residents who want to move from apartments to houses - a common trade known as upgrading - are wary of being caught without somewhere to live because of the lack of stock.

Other factors include low interest rates, which make property a more attractive investment, and unrest in Hong Kong driving capital to Australia and elsewhere.

Chinese tastes are specific, agents and buyers say. Abutting a cemetery is bad luck. T-intersections are out, because of car lights. Pools and lawns aren't drawcards, because they require maintenance and can be dangerous for young children.

Number fours are unpopular too, because four sounds like death in Chinese.

"A lot of these Chinese have come from apartment living in China," says Henny Stier, an Indonesian-Chinese who co-owns OH Property Group, a buyers' agent in Chatswood.

"They consider that to be troublesome. They like to pave everything."

Chatswood illustrates what happens when a property market becomes dominated by a single, wealthy ethnic group, experts say.

"The problem is the prices have gotten so crazy that [there are] no other buyers, unless you are able to pay $4 million for a rather unattractive house," Stier says. "The average buyer no longer sees value at that price point. It is a niche pool of buyers who must have that walk to shops."

On Wednesday, 60-year-old Mei Zhang checked out 3 Darling Street, along with a dozen or so other people, all but two of them Asian. The three-bedroom, single-storey brick house is conventionally attractive in the classic 1950s Chatswood style: polished wood floors, leadlight windows and high ceilings.

For around $3.3 million, though, it isn't amazing. The kitchen is separated from the living area by a wall, and a couple of small rooms have been tacked on the rear with little care about their appearance.

The Asian owners had moved out in 2014, to Kirribilli, selling agent Isabella Yan of Shead says. The house was left empty in case they came to regret sharing harbour views with the prime minister.

Zhang, who is an Australian citizen, lives three suburbs north in Killara. Worried about her knees weakening, she would love the convenience of Chatswood's shops, restaurants and public transport.

Standing on the porch, she gestures across the road at Beauchamp Park, where children play in the sunshine. "I would like to live near a park. But I think I prefer more modern [architecture]," she says, slapping the brick wall.P

Disclosure: Unfortunately for the author, he owns a house on the wrong side of the Willoughby-Chatswood boundary.
direction: supportive for Sydney residential property prices

headline: Central banks tune in to climate change
text:
The past week has been Kristalina Georgieva's "debut" party: as the newly installed managing director of the IMF, she has presided over her inaugural annual meeting of the fund which concludes on Sunday (Monday AEDT).

As she moved around Washington, investors should have noted one intriguing detail: the first public panel she chose to participate in did not focus on the economic outlook or IMF reform programs. Instead, she headlined the event "Can Central Banks Fight Climate Change?", where she not only called on central bankers to act, but announced the IMF "is gearing up very rapidly to integrate climate risks into our surveillance work". A cynic might be tempted to dismiss this as a public relations stunt. After all, central bankers never used to think their mandate covered environmental issues. And the IMF's staff sometimes joke the institution should be called, "It's Mostly Fiscal", given its past policy stance.

But Dr Georgieva's move is actually a sign of a subtle but striking paradigm shift. Since 2017, 46 central banks and regulators have joined the Network for Greening the Financial System, launched by Mark Carney, Bank of England governor, with counterparts in France and China among others.

This group has kept a low(ish) profile, partly because the US Federal Reserve has remained aloof. But, last month, the New York state financial regulator joined. I would bet the Fed will soon become at least an "observer" too. As its ranks swell, the NGFS is catalysing a debate among central banks about what to do next.

There are already two broad areas of agreement, Dr Georgieva says. One is that central banks will increasingly need to factor climate change considerations into their economic forecasts and monetary policy. "Already weather shocks are a source of volatility," Philip Lane, chief economist of the European Central Bank, told the IMF gathering.

Second, regulators are determined to ensure the private sector is preparing for climate change shocks. This includes the direct impact from environmental changes (say, if rising sea levels affect mortgages in coastal areas), and indirect shocks (if, for example, climate policy changes create "stranded" or worthless assets on investor balance sheets.) "Today there are at least $US12 trillion ($17.5 trillion) of high carbon-emitting assets that could be stranded assets," Dr Georgieva said. "How do you stress test [banks] for [these] risks?"

But there is also debate over three more questions, where there is less agreement. Should a framework for reporting climate change risks become mandatory for banks, insurance companies and asset managers? Should central banks try to change financial flows and investor behaviour by introducing tiered capital charges for assets, depending on whether they are "green" or "brown"? And should central banks use their own balance sheets to promote green policies by refusing to hold brown assets and investing in green ones instead?

I doubt whether these three ideas will deliver tangible policy measures soon, though. One practical impediment is continued disagreement about how to define what is green or brown: many economic activities lie on an "olive" spectrum since the environmental impact is mixed and changing.

Meanwhile, central bankers are deeply unsure whether they have any mandate to do more than simply react to climate change issues by modelling the financial stability risks.

"Central banks do have to deal with the risks of climate but they cannot substitute for climate policy [from politicians]," Sabine Mauderer, a board member of the Bundesbank, told the IMF event. And Signe Krogstrup, assistant governor of the Danish central bank, said on another panel: "There are calls for central banks to do more, but there is a big grey zone where central banks are not sure what fits in the mandate."

While it could take years for these issues to play out, investors need to understand the debate is moving. And history shows changes in the policy paradigm often begin with seemingly outlandish concepts emerging at conferences - and only become mainstream years later, amid a crisis.

It would pay to keep a close eye on what Dr Georgieva does next with this debate. Better still, watch her predecessor Christine Lagarde, as she starts her new role as president of the European Central Bank. Dr Carney might have launched the NGFS; but the ECB could soon become ground zero for testing these novel ideas.

Gillian Tett is chair of the editorial board and editor-at-large, US of the Financial Times. She writes weekly columns, covering a range of economic, financial, political and social issues.
direction: pressure on rate-sensitive sectors due to potential future regulatory tightening

headline: Results a portent of grim times to come
text:
Commonwealth Bank boss Matt Comyn provided investors with precious little cause for cheer when he released the banking giant's full-year results.

But beyond disappointment over stubbornly high compliance costs and its failure to indulge shareholders with a special dividend, there's a more profound cause for concern: the Sydney-based bank is already showing some scars from the souring economic backdrop and will inevitably feel even worse pain as the US-China trade conflict escalates.

In its latest profit results, CBA revealed its problem loans have climbed to $7.8 billion, due to a combination of weak wages growth (which have caused borrowers to fall behind on personal loan repayments) and the worsening outlook for the property and retail trade sectors. Six months ago, the bank's problem loans - it calls them "troublesome and impaired assets" - stood at $6.7 billion.

CBA conceded some households are clearly struggling to keep up repayments on their personal loans, with the proportion of personal loans where no interest has been paid for at least 90 days rising to 1.56 per cent, from 1.44 per cent a year ago. "Personal loan arrears remained elevated due to pockets of stress in western Sydney and Melbourne," the bank noted.

The savage correction in the residential property market has also ravaged household balance sheets.

Although only 0.68 per cent of the bank's home loans are more than three months behind in interest repayments, there has been an increase in accounts that are in negative equity - where the value of the house is less than the mortgage.

As CBA pointed out, "based on June 2019 valuations, approximately 3.5 per cent of Australian home loan accounts and 4.5 per cent of balances are in negative equity".

CBA is now nursing $2.1 billion of "troublesome and impaired" consumer loans, up from $1.8 billion only six months earlier.

Although these problem loans represent a very tiny fraction of the bank's massive consumer lending book, the growing signs of strain in household balance sheets are a worrying development at a time when robust jobs growth has pushed the unemployment rate to 5.2 per cent, and borrowing rates have fallen to all-time lows. But it's the clear signs of stress in some parts of CBA's business lending book that will be of greater concern for investors.

The construction industry is clearly experiencing major problems, with CBA noting that troublesome and impaired loans spiked to $579 million in June, up from $419 million earlier. Problem loans now account for 7.1 per cent of CBA's total $8.2 billion exposure to the construction sector, compared with only 5.1 per cent six months ago.

As CBA notes, the credit outlook for the construction industry "remains cautious, despite a positive growth outlook largely from government-supported infrastructure projects".

Not surprisingly, retail and wholesale traders are also finding it difficult to keep up with their loan payments, with CBA's problem loans from this sector rising to $636 million, compared with $478 million six months earlier.

As a result, problem loans now account for 3.2 per cent of CBA's total exposure to the retail and wholesale trading sector.

As the bank noted, "the retail trade sector remains weak, challenged by low wage growth, falling house prices, continued subdued consumer sentiment and online disruptors".

And CBA sees little likelihood of any quick improvement in the sector's fortunes.

"Retail trading conditions, particularly in the discretionary retail sectors are expected to continue to be challenged by higher competition and downward pressure on prices and profitability, notwithstanding recent fiscal and monetary stimulus," it said.
direction: downward pressure on Commonwealth Bank shares

headline: Energy retailers on the rise
text:
The turbulent east coast electricity and gas retail market is poised for further disruption following the announcement by the UK's fast-growing OVO Group to enter the sector.

The market is still digesting the looming entry of Shell and has been struggling with the partial re-regulation of prices.

OVO, which sealed a £500 million ($911 million) deal last week to buy rival SSE's household energy retail business in the UK, has lodged applications for an electricity and a gas retailing licence. It intends to sell power and gas to households in NSW, the ACT, South Australia and Queensland.

Since launching in the UK 10 years ago, Bristol-based OVO has signed up 1.5 million household customers and will become the biggest retailer after BG once its deal with SSE is finalised, adding about 5.8 million accounts.

The Financial Times noted that the company, founded by former City trader Stephen Fitzpatrick, had gained market share in the UK at the expense of rivals, often swallowing them up along the way. OVO, 20 per cent owned by Japan's Mitsubishi, has invested heavily over the past three years in scalable operating platforms, smart data capabilities and connected home services, Mr Fitzpatrick said last week. He said that would ensure it was well-positioned to grow and take advantage of new opportunities in the changing market. OVO has also entered Spain and France, and will add Germany and Italy next year.

In the UK, it describes itself as the first mainstream energy company with no coal or nuclear in its fuel mix.

"The company ethos and approach has always been to make a complex market simpler for customers, whilst providing them with excellent service and access to more renewable energy," OVO said in its application to the Australian Energy Market Operator for an Australian electricity retailing licence.

Morgan Stanley analyst Rob Koh has been advising clients of a shake-up in Australia's electricity retailing market, saying the traditional "big 3" - AGL Energy, Origin and EnergyAustralia - could become the "big 5/6" with the addition of Shell and OVO.
direction: bearish for incumbent energy retailers

headline: Drug trade chaos as dark web sites shut down
text:
Illegal drug sales in online markets have been seriously disrupted since three of the largest dark web marketplaces were taken down in the past few weeks, say experts.

"Right now [online drug markets] are in disarray," said Eileen Ormsby, author of two books about the dark web. "Everybody is running around and not really sure what's going on."

The annual independent Global Drug Survey has shown a growing portion of drug users are buying from the dark web - a section of the internet accessible only by using software that maintains a user's anonymity.

"Australia is the highest per-capita user of the dark-web markets, mainly because of our geographic isolation," Ms Ormsby said.

Last month the world's largest dark-web platform, Dream Market, was abandoned by its vendors and their customers after site administrators said it would move operations to another dark web site. The suspicious circumstances surrounding the announcement created concerns the site had been compromised by law enforcement agencies and was being used to collect information on its users.

Then in the first week of May, two other sites on the dark web were taken down by European law enforcement authorities: Silkkitie and the world's second largest platform, Wall Street Market, which had 1.15 million buyers and 5400 vendors.

Ms Ormsby, a former commercial lawyer, said that while law enforcement are not openly behind the Dream Market announcement, there is doubt among buyers and sellers.

"It's not unknown for law enforcement to run markets as honeypots after they take them down for a little while, just to get some information about people that are using them."

The recent reprisals by law enforcement had created more concerns after operators of Deepdotweb, a "clearweb" site, had also been arrested, Ms Ormsby said.

Deepdotweb was hosted on the open internet and published news about the dark web. The site was seized by authorities because it contained links to and advertising for dark web sites.

Associate professor James Martin, criminology discipline convenor at Swinburne University, said that, as with any legal market, trust was critical to the online drug trade.

Dr Martin has been researching the online drug trade since 2012 and, along with collaborators, has recently finished interviewing a series of dark-web dealers. Not only have buyers and sellers been on edge about sites under the control of authorities, Dr Martin said there was a constant threat of marketplace sites closing down in an "exit scam", where site administrators disappear with all payments held on behalf of sellers.

The online trade in drugs and other blackmarket goods first rose to prominence with the original dark web marketplace, Silk Road. That site was taken down after its founder, American Ross Ulbricht (also known as Dread Pirate Roberts), was arrested in 2013. Since then the online trade has continued to grow, and Australian consumers have been among the world's most devout. "If you look at the Global Drug Survey since Silk Road started, every year more and more people respond that they're buying their drugs on the dark web so it has become a much more popular way to buy drugs," Ms Ormsby said.

The fear that websites may be under the control of authorities stems from 2017 when a joint operation by European and US law enforcement took down two dark web marketplaces, AlphaBay and Hansa.

AlphaBay, which was the largest market at the time, was said to have facilitated trade in more than $US 1 billion of heroin, cocaine, ecstasy and other illicit products. Then after authorities took the site down in June 2017, users flocked to Hansa.

What users didn't know was that Hansa had been covertly taken over by authorities and for a month law enforcement ran the site and collected intelligence on its users.

But while recent events may have caused a significant disruption and delivered a blow to the trust that underpins these markets, both Dr Martin and Ms Ormsby said, inevitably, there will be another market to which buyers and sellers will be able to turn.
direction: negative for dark-web-adjacent tech and cybersecurity shares

headline: Lendlease wins $14b UK project
text:
London | Lendlease has bagged an £8 billion ($14 billion) urban redevelopment contract in London, almost twice the size of any of its previous British contracts and one of the largest urban regeneration projects in Europe.

The company has put its foot to the floor in Britain over the past 18 months, building up a hefty pipeline of work totalling over $33 billion in end-development value and becoming one of the country's largest developers.

Last month it was chosen as the partner in the £1.5 billion Birmingham Smithfield project, adding to a £2.4 billion project to build and then redevelop the Commonwealth Games athletes' village there, alongside four London projects in Silvertown, Tottenham, Euston and Elephant & Castle worth a combined £11.5 billion.

The latest project, Thamesmead Waterfront, will be a joint venture with London housing charity the Peabody Estate to build 11,500 homes and overhaul a town centre on a 101-hectare riverfront site in south-east London over 30 years. Details of the joint venture will be finalised by mid-2019.

The development, which includes 92,900 square metres of new cultural, community and commercial spaces, includes 2.5 kilometres of undeveloped river frontage, and abuts Abbey Wood, which is on the soon-to-be-opened Crossrail line connecting Heathrow to central London, the Docklands banking district and the south-east.

Peabody is a well-known Victorian-era social housing charity, founded by a wealthy American philanthropist, that now runs large housing estates all over London. The group provides over 55,000 homes and services to more than 111,000 residents and 8000 care and support customers.

Peabody's development partners in the Thamesmead project include two London councils, the Greater London Authority, and Transport for London, which runs the Tube network and is weighing up a new Docklands Light Rail connection to the site.

The charity's chief executive Brendan Sarsfield described the development as "a real game-changer for this part of London", while Lendlease Europe's CEO Dan Labbad called the site "one of London's largest areas of opportunity to develop new housing and to support business and employment growth".

Lendlease said last August that projects in its two European markets - Britain and Italy - now accounted for more than half its global development pipeline, having added almost $22 billion to that pipeline in 2017-18.

This proportion looks set to increase when results are next reported, despite the region's economic slowdown and Brexit uncertainty.

"Britain, and in particular London, remain among the world's most resilient investment destinations," Lendlease CEO Steve McCann said.

"Britain's long-term viability remains highly attractive to a company like Lendlease where in the past 18 months Lendlease has secured or has preferred status in major urbanisation projects of more than $33 billion in end development value - a timely reminder that Britain is very much open for business."
direction: Bullish for Lendlease shares

headline: Private health cover spins towards death spiral
text:
Insurance Not enough young people want to cross-subsidise the care of older private health customers. That adds up to big trouble.

The future of the private health insurance industry looks bleak. Premiums are rising and people are dropping their cover, especially the young and the healthy. Those who are left are more likely to need health care, which drives insurance costs up further. About 100,000 fewer Australians have private hospital cover today than a year ago.

Private health insurance in Australia faces a reckoning. Labor has promised that if it wins the May 18 federal election it will order a comprehensive Productivity Commission review of the private health sector. The Coalition should match that promise.
