Rising costs per person have placed private health insurers under increasing pressure. Premiums have risen faster than inflation, and more people are downgrading their cover by opting for products with deductions and exclusions.

The age divide is ominous. The number of people covered by private health insurance has declined in every age group up to age 60. This drop is biggest among 20-39-year-olds. Meanwhile, the number of people with private health insurance is rising for every age group over 60, particularly among 70-79-year-olds. There are almost 100,000 fewer 20-39-year-olds and 360,000 more people over 60 insured now than five years ago.

Australia's "community rating" rules require insurers to charge all people the same premium regardless of age. It's a vicious cycle. The young and healthy (low users of health services) cross-subsidise the old and sick (high users of health services), which makes health insurance more attractive to the elderly and less attractive to the young.

As the cross-subsidy from young to old increases, private health insurance becomes increasingly poor value for younger Australians. Their rational response is to downgrade their cover or opt out completely. This worsens the insurers' risk profile, because a greater proportion of high-cost users remain in the pool, placing further pressure on premiums.

The incentives to encourage young people to take out private health insurance are losing their effect.

Lifetime Health Cover, introduced by the Howard government in 2000, modified the community rating rules by allowing funds to charge higher premiums for people joining after age 30 (the penalty disappears after 10 years of continuous private health insurance cover). This boosted membership in the short term, but now the declining trend has resumed.

Funds are finding it increasingly difficult to entice financially savvy Millennials and Generation Zers to buy their product. Self-insurance is becoming a more attractive option for young people who seem less concerned about future costs.

From April 1 this year, insurers have been able to offer 18-29-year-olds a discount on hospital insurance premiums of up to 2 per cent a year, up to a maximum of 10 per cent, phased out by age 41. But it is unlikely to prove a big enough discount to encourage significant numbers of young people to take out health insurance, especially given that many do not have stable employment.

In response to rising premiums, consumers of all ages are downgrading their cover. In 1997 about two-thirds of private health insurance policies were top cover, with no deductions, excesses or exclusions. That proportion is now around one in eight. This has meant a massive transfer in who bears the risk of the costs of hospital admissions - from the private health insurers to the insured person.

Too often the product definitions have been opaque, or hidden in the small print, so that what a patient thinks the excess will be, and what the actual excess is, are quite different things. Surgeons often impose booking fees and other charges not known to the private insurer, nor covered by them.

The government's response to the fine print debacle, and the resultant increased public cynicism about private health insurance, has been to standardise product definitions into four broad categories: gold, silver, bronze and basic. But the goal of making things simpler for customers has been undermined by the creation of "plus" categories (eg, "Basic plus") which provide more than the category minimum. These plus products differ from insurer to insurer, recreating the product proliferation that standardisation was meant to eliminate.

The shift in risk has not been accompanied by any public education campaign to improve health literacy and help consumers make informed health insurance choices.

So consumer dissatisfaction has increased yet again.

The private health insurance industry faces a vicious cycle and the time is now right to step back and address industry fundamentals. A Productivity Commission review - or some other form of industry deep dive - is now essential. A do nothing, say nothing policy is clearly not the answer.

Stephen Duckett is director of the health program at the Grattan Institute.
direction: bearish for private health insurers

headline: Parents resist efforts to lift literacy, maths: expert
text:
A former education minister says it's hard for schools to lift literacy and numeracy levels because parents push back, saying their children will "get stressed".

Adrian Piccoli, who was education minister in NSW for six years, said every time he tried to set minimum standards for maths and literacy parents "went crazy" and said the pressure would get to their kids.

"I think you do have to have expectations on students. They can't coast through school and then expect to do well at uni or in a trade. You have to tell them they have to meet a standard."

Professor Piccoli was responding to a Productivity Commission report that said the school system was letting poorer students down by not equipping them with basic maths and reading skills.

This showed up in Program for International Student Assessment (PISA) scores, which have been falling since 2000 across all levels of Australian society, leading to a higher failure rate for disadvantaged students at university.

On Tuesday the CSIRO warned that productivity gains in the economy were at risk from falling PISA results.

It said good cognitive skills had a big effect on GDP and future well being. But the steady decline in Australia's PISA maths scores was undermining cognitive skill development.

Professor Piccoli, who runs the Gonski Institute for Education at the University of NSW, said people had to be capable of living in society and that meant knowing how to fill in a form, add up a grocery bill or go on to further study.

He said universities were part of the problem because many did not set prerequisite levels for maths. As a result students didn't think they needed to study maths at school.

But even if governments intervened to set higher standards, there was still a problem with parents. "People say to me why can't education in Australia be more like Singapore? The problem is parents here are not ready to do what parents in Singapore do for their kids."

Research fellow at the Centre for Independent Studies, Blaise Joseph, said Australia was not alone in having an achievement gap between disadvantaged and more-well-off children.

But the findings of the Productivity Commission highlighted the need for state governments to focus on literacy and numeracy because "ultimately they run the schools".

"Student results come back to how well literacy and numeracy were taught in the early years at school. Every subject they study is dependent on them.

"Numeracy results have been falling is schools, so then the unis have to invest time and money in bridging courses. Even if kids don't go on to uni what they learn at school is very important."

But Mr Joseph said the answer was not necessarily to throw more money at the schools. Increased funding had been matched by a decline in PISA scores since 2003.

Some educators said the problem with the Productivity Commission report was it assumed everyone had to go to university. John Hattie, who is chairman of the Australian Institute for Teaching and School Leadership, said all universities in Australia were basically the same, which did not offer a lot of choice to students.

It would be better if school leavers were offered some middle ground, similar to community colleges in the Unites States.
direction: negative for education sector and broader productivity-linked equities

headline: PropertyGuru to take on new markets - if it can school them
text:
The company backed by some of the world's biggest private equity investors and speculated to be one of Australia's biggest technology listings of the year, PropertyGuru, is eyeing new markets for its real estate classifieds business.

PropertyGuru chief executive Hari Krishnan told The Australian Financial Review that the south-east Asia property classifieds and services business has a front-row seat to monitor real estate development in the region, and is looking at opportunities to enter new markets, as it did in Vietnam.

But it's only going to leap into markets where it can become the number one player, he said.

"We're not interested in planting flags. We're in five markets, there's a big addressable market in just these five," Mr Krishnan said.

"Instead of just spreading and proliferating, we're much more interested in going deep, bringing these digital solutions; we're not just a marketplace. I think it's much more about can you get to that [market leading] position, monetise, exercise pricing power, build a strong financial profile in that market. We're seeing that already in Singapore, we're seeing that very much so in Vietnam, and increasingly so in the other markets."

PropertyGuru operates real estate classifieds sites as well as other property related businesses, including B2B software FastKey, which it licenses to property developers to help them manage the move into digital, from distributing sales material, document generation and bookings.

It has also developed augmented reality product PropertyGuru Lens, rolling it out only in Singapore so far, where consumers can use the company's app and via a phone's camera, point towards apartments and other property to see what is available in that building.

Mr Krishnan said PropertyGuru's Asia Property Awards, where developers from around the region submit their projects to win awards, also give the business a unique insight into markets where it has not launched yet.

It has operations in Singapore, Thailand, Indonesia and Vietnam where it is the market leader, and is also in Malaysia where it is "a close No.2", according to Mr Krishnan.

The Singapore-based PropertyGuru counts private equity heavyweights TPG Capital, KKR and Square Peg - including co-founder Paul Bassat, who sits on the board - as investors.

The firm is understood to be looking at listing on the Australian Securities Exchange, with investment banks Credit Suisse and UBS tapped to manage the float, as revealed by the Financial Review's Street Talk in June.

Mr Krishnan would not be drawn on speculation of an initial public offering. If it were to list on the ASX, it would be one of the biggest technology floats of 2019. KKR tipped in $S200 million ($213.7 million) last October. PropertyGuru used the funds to acquire the remaining stake in Vietnam property classifieds site Batdongsan.

Mr Krishnan declined to comment on what PropertyGuru's valuation was as a result of the transaction. It has raised about $S436 million over its life.

"We are looking at potential strategic or financing options. No decisions have been made yet. But yes, there are a lot of exciting opportunities in our part of the world," he said.
direction: bullish for PropertyGuru shares

headline: Rocket blasts off with vitamin start-up
text:
European investor Rocket Internet, which helped build The Iconic in online fashion retailing and HelloFresh in home meal deliveries, has turned its sights on Australia's $5 billion vitamin industry.

Rocket Internet has injected extra funds into Australian start-up Vitable after robust early success in a new model of selling personalised packs of vitamin pills direct to busy consumers.

Vitable co-founder Larah Loutati said the company, which began operations in March, had "cut out the middle man" with its e-commerce model and was also tapping into the increasing focus on health and well-being.

Vitable uses an algorithm built by nutritional experts to tailor personalised sachets of vitamins and supplements for customers. They are delivered direct to households by Australia Post, in a market dominated by big brands like Blackmores and Swisse.

She said Vitable was pitched at the premium end of the market, and by stripping out the retail margins was able to keep costs down.

"In terms of price we're on par or under where Blackmores and Swisse would generally be," she said.

The company's vitamins are made under contract by fully accredited manufacturers in Australia, and the orders are packed at a pharmacy in New South Wales.

Ms Loutati founded the business with Ilyas Anane. Mr Anane said across the $5 billion vitamin market, about 75 per cent of consumers bought their products "off the shelf" from retailers like Chemist Warehouse without having tailored advice for their own particular needs.

Rocket Internet provided some seed funding originally, and then injected further funds as it gained momentum. Sales had been tripling each month, albeit off a low base.

Ms Loutati declined to specify the size of the stake which Rocket Internet had obtained.

But having Rocket Internet's funds and expertise was invaluable because they weren't afraid of expanding businesses quickly.

"It's definitely the speed at which they operate. We really like the way they move fast," she said.

Rocket Internet was a strong backer of The Iconic, which has grown to become one of the best known online fashion retailers in Australia. The Iconic is now part of the Global Fashion Group, which listed on the Frankfurt Stock Exchange in June in a $1.8 billion float.

Global Fashion Group also owns online retailers Zalora, Dafiti and Lamoda.

The Iconic's revenues climbed by 38 per cent to $371 million in the year ended December, and revenues have risen fivefold since 2014.

Rocket earlier this year sold out of global home meal kits group HelloFresh, which in Australia is in a fierce battle with rival Marley Spoon. Supermarket giant Woolworths in June took a stake in Marley Spoon.

Ms Loutati worked at HelloFresh in Australia, and for three years from the end of 2015 was the Head of Product Development and Consumer Experience for that firm.

She said the main customer base for Vitable was largely between the 25 to 45 age group. When the business was in the planning phase, the co-founders thought it would be the tech-savvy Millennials who might form the core customer base.

"Actually the customer segment is a little older than we thought," she said.

The Vitable expansion comes as big global players increasingly look to personalised nutrition as a growth industry. The health science arm of global food giant Nestle in late August acquired vitamin subscription service Persona in the United States.

Vitable wants to build its brand locally to give it extra clout in the eyes of Asian consumers before expanding into Asia.

"Expanding to Asia Pacific is definitely in our planning," she said.
direction: bullish for Vitable and the broader health/well-being sector

headline: Australian-French navy patrols on the horizon
text:
France's navy chief wants Australian and French warships to conduct joint patrols, including escort duties for the massive Charles De Gaulle aircraft carrier, as part of deepening co-operation between the two militaries.

In an interview with The Australian Financial Review, Admiral Christophe Prazuck emphasised the need for navies like those of Australia and France to assert the importance of freedom of navigation and the international law of the sea, which is under strain from China.

Admiral Prazuck is in Sydney for the Pacific Maritime Expo, as part of a large French delegation of military personnel, diplomats and 55 businesses.

President Emmanuel Macron has declared France's intention to remain a key player in the Indo-Pacific because of its territories in the Pacific and Indian oceans. Less well-known is that Australia shares its longest border - the maritime boundary - with France.

Admiral Prazuck said Australia's 2016 selection of a French-designed submarine to replace the Collins class had been a "game changer" for relations and both countries had resolved to co-operate more broadly militarily.

He said French warships and research vessels were the most frequent foreign navy visitors to Australian ports, with 12 arrivals a year.

"Since the signing of this contract our links have been tighter and tighter with the Australian navy, not only on submarines but every aspect of maritime warfare," he said.

Admiral Prazuck revealed this co-operation had gone as far as co-ordinating the deployment of frigates in the region to ensure Australian and French warships were not patrolling the same waters, or if they did end up in the same place, they would carry out exercises together.

He also spoke of greater interoperability between the two navies, particularly in the areas of anti-submarine warfare and amphibious landings. Next year Australian, Japanese and US troops and ships will join French ones for joint exercises off New Caledonia.

"Another area where I think we could go further is the escort of capital ships, like when our aircraft carrier comes to the Indian Ocean," Admiral Prazuck said, adding he had discussed it with Australian navy chief Mike Noonan. "Or I would like French frigates escorting Australian amphibious ships.

"When you are escorting a capital ship, it is very demanding but a very useful exercise. You learn a lot."

France has one aircraft carrier, Charles De Gaulle, which is the only nuclear-powered carrier in service outside the US navy.

Admiral Prazuck also raised the prospect of Australian and French sailors serving on each other's submarines to help train crews ahead of the eventual arrival of the next-generation boats.

"All doors are wide open to host our Australian friends and officers on our submarines. It is certainly possible we do that," he said.

Admiral Prazuck said a strong navy was crucial to France, which has the world's second biggest exclusive economic zone behind the US, while Australia is in third place.

France has recently stepped up its deployments in the heavily contested South China Sea, which Admiral Prazuck said was to illustrate the importance on preserving open seas.

"We are concerned each time the law of the sea is undermined or threatened," he said. "Each country has its own political agenda and its own constraints and it's own objectives. The main thing is to share the same message and defence of the law of the sea."
direction: bullish for defence stocks

headline: Labor slams LIC loophole on commissions
text:
The federal opposition has slammed a loophole created by the Coalition government that allows financial advisers to sell listed fund investments to clients and be paid lucrative commissions in return.

Shadow financial services minister Stephen Jones hit out at the "conflicted remuneration" after AFR Weekend revealed some independent advisers blew the whistle on peers for aggressively pushing listed investment companies (LICs) and listed investment trusts (LITs).

The share price collapses of Dixon Advisory's US property fund and Melbourne hedge fund L1 Capital's LIC, both listed on the Australian Securities Exchange, have triggered calls for regulators to crack down on advisers and brokers selling LICs and LITs.

Shadow minister for financial services Stephen Jones said mum and dad investors were being exposed to "highly risky investments" that were opaque and difficult to value.

"It's time to end the conflicted remuneration model that is motivating advisers to push clients in these products," Mr Jones said.

"It's hard to believe that without conflicted commissions the advisers would be recommending these risky investments."

An LIC is an ASX-listed company set up to invest in a portfolio of securities, managed by an investment manager.

The LIC market has rapidly doubled to $45 billion since 2014, when the Abbott government watered down Labor's Future of Financial Advice (FOFA) laws to allow advisers and brokers to resume receiving commissions for selling listed securities.

FOFA was intended to end conflicted remuneration for financial advisers, but some advisers are receiving commissions of up to 3 per cent to sell LICs and LITs to mum and dad investors.

LICs have become more exotic, in recent years, moving beyond traditional listed shares to include hedge funds, private equity, direct loans and junk bonds.

Financial advisers warned a wave of new high-yielding debt LICs were about to hit the market as investors chase returns in a low interest rate environment.

Chris Brycki, chief executive of online investment adviser Stockspot and a former UBS portfolio manager, said he was "baffled" that advisers with a fiduciary responsibility could recommend LICs and LITs over lower cost index funds or exchange traded funds (ETFs).

"Index ETFs are more liquid, transparent, tax efficient, consistently trade at NAV [net asset value] because of their open-ended structure and have better performance," Mr Brycki said.

"In my opinion, conflicted remuneration from LICs is the primary reason they've remained popular with brokers and advisers, at the expense of better-performing ETFs.

"LICs pay commissions, ETFs don't. Sadly this means most brokers and advisers promote the former rather than the latter despite compelling evidence they're worse for the end client."

The number of LICs and LITs doubled to 114 over the past five years. More than half of LICs were trading at a discount of greater than 10 per cent of their pre-tax net tangible asset value as of May 31, according to an analysis of Morningstar data.

Wilson Asset Management founder Geoff Wilson, who manages six LICs worth $3.2 billion and spearheaded a retiree campaign against Labor's election plan to end the refundability of franking credits, rejected the criticisms of LICs.

If the LICs traded at a discount for a period, the board could do buybacks or return capital, he said. "Discounts and premiums change over time and the disconnect provides the buying or selling opportunity for investors.

"WAM is trading at a 20 per cent premium."

Key pointsLabor says mum and dad investors are being exposed to highly risky investments.

It says advisers recommend them because of conflicted commissions.
direction: bearish for LICs and LITs

headline: Throwing money won't change culture
text:
With respect, your claim in "Culture to cost big three banks $500m" (July 12) that increased amounts of regulatory capital will "force [NAB, Westpac and ANZ] to address major flaws in their culture" ignores the key drivers of organisational culture. Requiring the banks to increase the amount of capital they hold by less than a quarter of a percentage point is not going to change the behaviours of the people within these organisations.

It is to be hoped the banks realise this, though the self-assessments of NAB and Westpac, completed in November 2018 after the royal commission's release of its September 2018 interim report, suggest otherwise.

To illustrate my point, the word "misconduct" appears only five times in the NAB's 62-page self-assessment, and 10 times in Westpac's 126-page self-assessment. That compares to the 406 times it appears in the royal commission's 375-page interim report.

At its heart, cultural change is about changing beliefs about the way things should be done. That is because beliefs drive values, and shared values drive organisational culture.

If the banks do not tackle what their people believe about how the business of banking should be conducted, the behaviours that led to the misconduct uncovered by the royal commission will not change.

John Hindmarsh

Alphington, Vic

Taxpayers will soon be hit by NBN disaster

Chanticleer has done taxpayers a huge favour with his erudite summary "Mobile risk to NBN plan", (July 11), which highlighted how woefully Australians have been served by our political class when it comes to wasting our money.

That Communications Minister Paul Fletcher has so promptly said "never" to the potential for Telstra or any of the telco retailers to acquire any of the already past-its-use-by-date NBN, and with the bureaucratic support of ACCC chairman Rod Sims, provides clear evidence of such concerns.

Reading Chanticleer's commentary underpins the belief of myself and most thinking Australians that it is only a matter of time before the federal government - of whatever colour - passes the hat around in the vain hope that somebody will take the NBN off its hands, and a huge write-off of value will be charged to the country's debt profile.

Peter B Alexander

South Yarra, Vic

Economic malaise akin to Japanese disease

Looking at the property market over the past 25 years - particularly in Sydney and Melbourne - it strikes me that the economic naivety of our politicians has driven the cost of housing beyond long-term stability. Policies such as negative gearing and pump priming the economy every time there is a slowdown, followed by a housing-driven recovery, has leveraged up the costs and debt attached beyond a point of recovery. The latest efforts to stabilise prices will cause the same issues to persist.

The people of Australia carry so much debt that collectively they cannot make the economy tick. Normal consumer behaviour cannot be recommenced. The overall lowering of living standards will hinder the economy for a decade or more.

High underemployment both in the under 30s and over 50s, plus high debt, means we have hit a tipping point that may cause long-term stagnation.

The very low rates of return in safe- haven cash and fixed interest may lead to cashed-up investors pumping even more funds into residential property seeking a higher yield income return.

The replication in Australia of the Japanese economic disease that commenced 25 years ago appears to be now almost certain.

Robert Dawson

Waverton, NSW

Cost of Whitehaven dividend not counted

The story "Whitehaven boss hints at hefty dividends for shareholders" (July 12) missed the ecological dividends.

Whitehaven took the heart out of critically endangered white box woodlands in the Leard State Forest near Narrabri. Its 'offsets' are not white box. Less than 1 per cent of these rare woodlands had survived since 1788. The forest, full of nesting sites, is vital habitat for more than 396 species of native plants and animals, 34 of which face extinction.

To boot, our children will pick up Whitehaven's carbon dividend from the coal burnt, in terms of global heating and, for example, the loss of food productivity of the Murray-Darling Basin.

Bob Brown

Cygnet, Tas

Don't blame states

Re "State renewable targets put pressure on coal-fired power", (July 13). With the energy policy vacuum federally, why criticise just Victoria and Queensland for going it alone? At least some governments recognise their responsibility for dealing with rising emissions.

Beverley McIntyre

Camberwell, Vic

ATO Uber-wrong

The Australian Taxation Office recently confirmed its interpretation that the fringe benefits tax exemptions for specific cases of employee taxi travel do not extend to similar trips in rideshare vehicles (such as Uber) or other vehicles for hire.

If the ATO cannot see its way clear to changing this view, it is important that the government sponsors legislative amendments to ensure that trips in rideshare vehicles give rise to the same FBT exemptions as taxi trips.

There can be no policy basis for the present discriminatory treatment given that rideshare is now legal and regulated in all states and territories.

John Burge

Curtin, ACT
direction: bearish for NAB, Westpac, and ANZ

headline: Rear Window
text:
Josh Frydenberg's brave appointment of Graeme Samuel

One unexpected takeout from Josh Frydenberg's royal commission response was the appointment of former competition tsar (turned airline lobbyist) Graeme Samuel to conduct a review of APRA.

Early press described Samuel as "highly regarded". Which he is, in some circles. Not quite so at Raheen. Memories are long in the Pratt family, which has never forgiven Samuel for vigorously pursuing Dick Pratt for cartel behaviour. A civil case brought by the ACCC succeeded in 2007, with Visy fined $36 million. But then, the ACCC began a criminal prosecution against Pratt, which was dropped in April 2009. The Pratt patriarch died the day after.

Samuel is still persona non-grata in some circles over it. Particularly in parts of Melbourne's Jewish community, of which Frydenberg is both a proud and treasured member.

Last week's donations dump showed Anthony Pratt, Australia's richest man, and Dick's son, once again giving generously to the Liberal Party. And sure, Samuel was appointed by Peter Dutton to review Australia's Medical Research Institutes in 2014, but this is rather more of a big deal.

The latest preferment from Frydenberg and the Liberals seems ill-conceived, at least if self-interest is the relevant measure. After all, money kinda matters in election years.

John King boosts Myer's bankruptcy expertise

The third remaking of post-TPG Myer continues under British retailer John King. And in the established tradition of business leaders surrounding themselves with people they know, King has wasted no time clearing out predecessor Richard Umbers' management team and restacking the executive floor with his former team at failed UK retailer House of Fraser.

Chief merchandising officer Allan Winstanley spent nine years at HOF alongside King before joining US department store Sears in 2017 as head of apparel. Winstanley joined Myer in July and Sears filed for bankruptcy in October. In between, HOF entered administration.

As if the turnaround team wasn't collapse-credentialled enough, Myer is about to hire King's successor as House of Fraser CEO, Nigel Oddy, as its new chief operating officer. No kidding. Commenting on his HOF appointment in 2014, Oddy revealed that "we have already agreed locations to expand the brand in China, with three stores due to open over the next two years, and plan to open further new stores in the Middle East." That never happened (shock, horror) but it sure does remind us of Bernie Brookes' Myer Dubai ("there aren't many new sites where we can build Myer stores, so we are forced to look overseas"), which also, mercifully, never happened. Like Winstanley, Oddy left House of Fraser in 2017.

Needless to say, House of Fraser was a laggard online, too reliant on concessions and with an oversize store footprint. Sound familiar?

And now the band's reforming - though for what will surely be their farewell tour; three experts in a dying format, fresh from lavish flops, and to do what? Tell us with some authority what an iceberg looks like up close? Myer chairman Garry Hounsell's right to cite their relevant experience all right, but it's experience at failure. These boys are playing him off a break. Admittedly, that is one of the few easy assignments left in retail.

A slew of retailers has issued profit warnings since a soft shopping Christmas: Kathmandu, PAS Group, Wesfarmers for Kmart and The Reject Shop. Myer, whose reporting half ends on January 31, obstinately refused to do the same despite leaked internal numbers forcing it to confirm a 4.8 per cent fall in first-quarter sales. But luckily Hounsell used to run accounting firm Arthur Andersen (also now defunct), so he's real good with numbers.

ANZ swears Shayne Elliott isn't trying to distract you

Coincidence, not conspiracy, had ANZ chief Shayne Elliott spruiking copies of the Big Issue below the bank's Docklands headquarters on Monday.

At least, that's what his spinners insist. Seems the date's been in Elliott's diary for six months, long before he knew when the government would pass on Kenneth Hayne's report to the public. And despite the eyebrows raised by having him raising funds for the homeless on this of all days, could you imagine the headlines if anyone had caught him cancelling?

It must have been a relatively pleasant distraction from the agony of waiting on the report. Bankers across the country spent the day on tenterhooks. Apart from those at the nation's largest bank, who courtesy of ASIC had a fresh "fees for no service" crisis to deal with.

Elliott refused to answer the questions of the journalists who rushed to the photo-opp, but he did sell all 60 copies he'd been provided (up from the 55 he sold last year). At $9 a pop, that's $540 raised, which, for Elliott, must be the of the least productive use of his time since he did this last year. Though all for a good cause.

BHP's Andrew Mackenzie and Telstra's Andy Penn will take their turns selling copies this week as part of the charity's annual event.

In other matters Royal Commission, Elliott wasn't alone in failing to factor it into his forward planing. APRA deputy chair Helen Rowell, who oversees the superannuation sector for the regulator, is currently on annual leave. We hear an overseas trip was organised months before the date of the report's release was known. There'll be six super-specific changes for her to get across when she gets back ...

L1 Capital spruiks Boral as Boral downgrades, again

Haven't Boral shareholders suffered enough? For that matter, haven't unitholders in L1 Capital's Long Short Fund, which only last Thursday justified its large Boral investment in its December quarterly investor update.

By Monday, Boral had its own ASX announcement: its third earnings downgrade since April 2018. That prompted even more shareholders to jump ship, Boral sinking 7.9 per cent by the close.

Astoundingly, it's not the first time L1 Capital has made a point of justifying its Boral investment only to have the company issue a downgrade! Last October, Mark Landau and Rafi Lamm highlighted Boral chief Mike Kane's "solid results" and "upbeat commentary" about the fourth quarter result, only to have Boral issue a trading downgrade within the week. You'd think Kane was purposefully trying to rebuff their confidence in him! If it happens one more time other hedge funds will start shorting the moment the L1 Capital's letter lands.

Thursday's quarterly letter to unitholders stated that Boral is "badly misunderstood by many investors and its shares are exceptionally good value today". Given they're even better value this week, we don't expect L1 Capital to have joined Monday's stampede out of the stock.

Last April, L1 raised a bumper $1.3 billion for its inaugural LIC, demand for its initial $600 million offering running hot after years of prodigious returns in its unlisted funds. This was controversial. Many fund managers avoid stewarding too much money in the one fund for fear of their investment strategies overwhelming the market in small stocks (and thus affecting returns). The quarterly update took aim at this idea, pointing out that the key drags on performance in the L1 Capital Long Short fund are large stocks, which could easily handle its interest. "The money raised in the LIC was not the cause of the weak performance," the fund managers argue.

We hope that's some comfort to investors who poured money in at the float. Alas, on current performance, ex-avocado grower Neil Delroy is still down $1.6 million on his initial investment, while Paul Ainsworth (son of pokies baron Len) is $1.33 million out of pocket.
direction: bearish for Myer and Liberal-linked entities

headline: New ministry mixes stability with an eye to re-election
text:
Scott Morrison's new cabinet demonstrates both continuity and strategic change. In keeping the same personnel in the headline jobs - Treasury, Finance, Health, Education, Home Affairs and the Foreign Ministry - the Prime Minister has ensured the very stability at the top of government that he campaigned on. It is deliberately unchanged compared with the instability and fluidity on the Treasury benches during low-level civil war that raged after Tony Abbott was deposed in 2015 until Malcolm Turnbull met the same fate in 2018. Mr Morrison also clearly moved early to shore up policy and political weaknesses with an eye to re-election in three years' time.

Mr Morrison took advantage of a backbench revolt over energy to seize the top job. To satisfy his backers he then separated the jobs of energy minister and climate change. Angus Taylor was colloquially called the "minister for lower power prices" and given a big stick to wield on the energy gentailers. The Australian Financial Review cautioned against this split - given the inextricably linked nature of the climate and energy. They have now been brought back together and Mr Taylor is the Minister for Energy and Emissions Reduction. This is an area in which the Coalition will need a much more credible policy framework settled well before the 2022 election. Mr Morrison made much of the Snowy 2.0 pumped hydro scheme during the election campaign. Yet the current plan for forced divestment of energy companies and taxpayer-funded emissions reduction are not things a Liberal government should be doing. Hard graft to build a political and business consensus over a framework to give some level of certainty to investors that creates an implied carbon price is needed. There is already a shadow carbon price operating in the corporate economy - companies pricing in policy changes they expect in the future. It's time for the government to take this out of the front line of adversarial politics. It also needs to get rid of the Renewable Energy Target and other schemes and agencies through which the federal government tries to pick climate winners and has destabilised the National Energy Market.

The government has a lot of catching up to do on industrial relations. Mr Morrison has given industrial relations to Attorney-General Christian Porter to help crack down on workplace lawlessness. This is in keeping with the Abbott model of attempting to use the law to smash the unions. The underwhelming Michaela Cash has been moved across to be Minister for Employment. The Coalition has wasted the past six years, too terrified of the unions invoking the ghost of WorkChoices. In this term it must confront the entire cosy industrial relations club: the unions, employers groups, and the gravy train of consultants, advisers and lawyers to turn the Coalition into the party of higher wages. It can do this by driving higher-performing, co-operative workplaces, where businesses - and employees - are freed from the early 20th century strictures of the awards system to compete in an open labour market. Failure to do this will mean the Coalition will struggle to get productivity up and increase pay packets.

The Prime Minister has unusually made himself Minister for the Public Service, as well as appointing his close ally (and former Canberra flatmate) Stuart Robert to head up the $18 billion National Disability Insurance Scheme, as well as being Minister for Government Services. In doing so, Mr Morrison is taking on board NSW and NZ centre-right governments' focus on improving the consumer-facing services that governments provide. Paul Fletcher's appointment as Communications Minister brings with it a pressure to write down the woeful NBN, which was never worth the money the government spent on it.

And finally the appointment of senator Jane Hume as Assistant Minister for Superannuation, Financial Services, Financial Services and Financial Technology is a positive move for a principles-based review into retirement incomes that Treasurer Josh Frydenberg announced in AFR Weekend last Saturday.

The Morrison ministry is missing some familiar faces from the past decade but it finally gives Mr Morrison the clear air to move from personalities so deeply involved in almost bringing the Coalition down.
direction: bearish for energy sector and utilities

headline: ASX advances on solid US jobs growth
text:
Australian shares rebounded on Monday from a loss late last week, following a strong session on Wall Street on Friday as stocks rose on the back of a solid US employment report.

The S&P/ASX 200 Index rose 40.1 points, or 0.7 per cent, to 6221.4 while the broader All Ordinaries closed the session 40.2 points, or 0.6 per cent, higher at 6310.8.

The US Labor Department reported on Friday that non-farm payrolls rose by 196,000 in March, ahead of the expected 177,000 increase.

"There was a massive sigh of relief ... as Friday's jobs report set aside recessionary fears," SPI Asset Management head of trading and market strategy Stephen Innes. "Global equity indices closed on a high note on the back of the report. While the solid data print does walk back some domestic recessionary concerns, it doesn't alleviate potential adverse knock-on effects from a possible worsening economic climate in China, Europe or even Brexit fallout."

The major resource stocks led the market on Monday as oil prices edged to a five-month high, and US and Chinese officials signalled solid progress in trade negotiations.

BHP Group led the market, climbing 1.7 per cent to $39.89, Fortescue Metals Group rose 2.8 per cent to $8, Rio Tinto advanced 1.6 per cent to $101.48 and South32 closed 1.6 per cent higher at $3.88.

Woodside Petroleum closed at $34.56, up 1.4 per cent, Santos rose 2.3 per cent to $6.82, Origin Energy advanced 2.1 per cent to $7.25 and WorleyParsons climbed 3 per cent to $14.64.

The major banks were the biggest drag on the market although their declines were only muted. NAB fell 0.4 per cent to $24.70, Westpac closed 0.7 per cent lower at $25.88, ANZ declined 0.6 per cent to $25.80 and Commonwealth Bank slid 0.3 per cent to $70.67.

Lynas Corp rose slightly after Malaysia Prime Minister Mahathir Mohamad said on Friday Lynas would be able to continue processing its rare earths in the country, if it agreed to first decontaminate raw material before sending it there.
direction: bullish for resource stocks and the ASX 200

headline: Shorten reaches out to miners
text:
Bill Shorten has moved to build bridges with business just days from the election with a significant concession on industrial relations and confirmation he will hold a national economic summit within weeks, if elected.

With both Mr Shorten and Scott Morrison scheduled to make their final major speeches of the campaign today, the Labor leader used a business address in Perth to assure the corporate community not to fear a Labor government.

"I don't look out at this crowd and see a room full of class enemies," he said. "I think this country works best when we work together."

Mr Shorten said that, if elected, he would consider changes to the Fair Work Act relating to greenfields agreements - union and employer brokered workplace agreements for a new major project, such as a mine or gasfield.

Instead of workplace agreements being renegotiated every three or four years, Mr Shorten said he was open to agreements that would allow unions and mine owners to negotiate an agreement that would last the life of the project, giving both workers and investors long-term certainty.

"We want to look at the ability for companies to negotiate with unions for extended greenfields agreements, project life, you can go to the global investors who will back it," he said. "They'll be good-paying jobs. You get the certainty of the arrangement, the union gets the certainty of the arrangement, the workforce get the certainty of the arrangement.

"If you want to deliver these outcomes you have to vote Labor because this current government doesn't have an industrial relations policy."

The suggestion was welcomed by gas giant Woodside as "an important proposal for pragmatic change to the current system".

"It would provide certainty for both employers and the workforce, and help protect Australia's competitiveness so that Australia's trade-exposed industries as well as nation-building infrastructure projects bring forward another wave of jobs and investment," said chief executive Peter Coleman.

CFMEU national construction secretary Dave Noonan said the union wanted to see the detail before adopting a position.

Mr Shorten also announced that the business summit he has long flagged if elected would be much larger than originally anticipated and be held in Perth in early June. He would invite large and small business, unions, state governments, farmers and other key sectors to discuss productivity, industrial relations, wages, and the economy.

Mr Shorten said it would not be an accord, but it was essential to get people together in the same room.

"In my experience you are far better off getting people with all sorts of different views in the same room," he said.

"All that is wrong with Australia at the moment is that you've got people in different rooms yelling at each other.

"That's what people hate when they see it on the nightly news.

"'Don't come to us with all your problems, come to us with your answers'. This is the sort of prime minister I'll be."

Mr Morrison will address the National Press Club in Canberra today and reinforce his case against a switch to Labor.

"My message is this: now is not the time to turn back. Now is the time to get on and keep on with the work of building our economy by backing in the choices Australians are wanting to make every day, and to enable them to plan for their future with confidence," he will say.

"Labor is proposing a big taxing, big spending agenda, once again at a time when Australians can least afford the bill that they will be forced to pay, not just over the next three years, but at least the next decade."

Mr Shorten has eschewed the Press Club tradition of the final week of the campaign and will return to the Blacktown Civic Centre where Gough Whitlam gave his final It's Time speech in 1972. Mr Shorten will argue Australia cannot afford not to change.

He will argue that, like in 1972, this election will be a generational decision for voters and "the door stands ajar".

Climate change will feature heavily in his speech and he will again warn against the "chaos" of a Coalition government reliant on Clive Palmer and Pauline Hanson.
direction: bullish for mining and resources sector

headline: 'No further tightening': market spared
text:
The royal commission into banking has already jolted the property market with a self-imposed crackdown on lending by the big banks, but its final report won't spark a further credit crunch, economists say.

There was no directive for banks to further tighten their lending practices in Kenneth Hayne's final report into the finance sector on Monday, with the desired outcome on lending practices by banks having already been met.

"I consider that the steps ... taken by banks to strengthen their home lending practices and to reduce their reliance on the HEM - are being taken with a view to improving compliance with the responsible lending provisions of the NCCP Act," Mr Hayne wrote in his report.

Since the royal commission began in March 2018, the lending practices of Australia's biggest banks have been under the spotlight, beginning with ANZ which was dragged through the coals for not verifying the expenses of those applying for home loans.

The bad publicity for the big banks during the royal commission kicked them into action last year, as they began to crack down on borrowers' living expenses in assessing their ability to repay loans, including a forensic look at discretionary spending on things like UberEats or online "layby" shopping.

AMP Capital chief economist Shane Oliver said the final report showed the royal commission was "not pointing to any further tightening".

"The royal commission has reinforced the lending standards APRA has already put through the system and that tightening started back in 2017 and gathered pace into the early part of last year," Dr Oliver said. "The anecdotes of it being harder to get a loan, that was happening around March, so the tightening and the negative impact on the property market because of credit tightening has already been evident and there aren't recommendations to push that further.

"The focus going forward will be making sure these regulations are maintained," he added.

In the final report, Hayne acknowledged that a number of banks had already altered their lending procedures by further inquiring about a borrowers' financial situation and by taking steps to verify each situation as well as reducing their reliance on the HEM, the household expenditure measure.

Property prices in Sydney peaked in mid-2017 and have been on a downward trajectory since, but they have fallen almost 10 per cent in Sydney and more than 8 per cent in Melbourne since the royal commission began.

"The effect of the royal commission on property will last for years, but not forever," Dr Oliver said.

"You go through periods where lending standards become lax and then there's some sort of blow up," Dr Oliver said.

"There will come a point down the track where it's concluded maybe the tighter lending has all gone too far, however I don't think we will quickly return to an investor-driven boom."

Economist Terry Rawnsley of SGS Economics agreed there wouldn't be further "belt tightening" as the big banks had already pre-emptively made changes to their lending practices in the lead-up to the final report.

"I think they've already tightened and over-tightened," Mr Rawnsley said. "We've seen in the last year that the housing market has softened, and that's been driven by some policy changes like APRA's crackdown on investor lending and state governments' stamp duty surcharges for foreign buyers."

Key pointsThe Hayne commission's report won't spark a credit crunch, economists say.

AMP's Shane Oliver said the royal commission did not point to further tightening.
direction: supportive for big bank shares

headline: PNG seeks $1.5 billion in loans from Australia, dropping China debt plan
text:
Papua New Guinea is seeking $1.5 billion in loans from Australia to help fund government spending programs, weeks after floating the idea of China refinancing its entire national debt.

Commerce Minister Wera Mori said the bid was raised with Treasurer Josh Frydenberg at a meeting yesterday.

"I would like to see it [the loan] done by Australia alone, but if not Australia could take the lead," he told The Australian Financial Review on the sidelines of a conference in Sydney.

"Australia has always been our friend, where else can we go?"

Mr Frydenberg declined to comment but confirmed he had met with PNG's Treasurer Sam Basil.

In recent years Australia has balked at providing budget support to PNG since ending the practice in June 2000 and concentrating on direct funding of aid programs.

Since then Canberra has typically worked through multilateral organisations such as the World Bank or the International Monetary Fund when helping to finance budget support programs.

Such a reluctance to intervene directly may have eased in recent years as China looms larger as a strategic competitor in the Pacific and the Morrison government seeks to refocus on the region as part of its "Pacific step-up".

PNG last requested financing support from Australia in 2017, when the former government of Peter O'Neill sought to have Australia's entire $558 million annual aid program delivered in the form of budget support.

Canberra rejected the idea, with former minister for international development Concetta Fierravanti-Wells warning that "aid was not charity".

Corruption has been one of the major concerns in providing direct budget assistance, but Mr Mori said the new government of James Marape was "stepping up the fight" in this area.

"We have demonstrated this by holding a commission of inquiry into the UBS loan and we are strengthening ICAC [the Independent Commission Against Corruption]," he said.

Earlier, Mr Mori told the PNG Investment Conference that new loans were needed to shore up the budget and stabilise the economy.

"The new Marape/Steven government is looking for some type of assistance from the Australian government to stabilise the PNG economy and for budget support," he said.

On the sidelines of the conference, Mr Mori indicated fresh funds would not be sought from China for budget support.

"I think we have too much exposure to China ... that's just my personal view," he said.

Earlier in the month a press release from Mr Marape's office indicated PNG was seeking $11.8 billion from China to restructure its national debt.

But just a day later the Prime Minister sought to hose down any loan package from China, saying the statement was put out without his knowledge.

Mr Marape said PNG was seeking assistance from China along with others such as the World Bank and "non-traditional partners".

The PNG economy has faltered this year amid foreign exchange shortages and a lack of new resource projects, leading to government revenue falling well behind budget targets.

Stephen Howes, a PNG specialist at the Australian National University, said PNG's economic recovery in 2018 had stalled this year and the country was suffering boom and bust cycles.

"To be sure, future-year prospects are better, with some important infrastructure projects under way and the Papua LNG agreement signed, though now under review," he wrote on August 8.
direction: negative for Australian banks and fiscal outlook

headline: Shadow banks post four-fold growth
text:
Shadow banks are growing market share among owner-occupiers at four times the rate of their mainstream banking rivals by aggressively pitching for borrowers rejected by nervous deposit-taking lenders with offers of lower rates, easier terms and cash incentives.

Lenders such as Resimac Group, the newly named amalgam of shadow banks Homeloans and Resimac, is restructuring its loan book to accommodate more sub-prime borrowers with patchy credit rates unlikely to get a loan from a mainstream competitor.

For example, it is simplifying the way it reviews borrowers with existing defaults and bankruptcies ranging from listed but discharged bankrupts through to those with more than one default on their credit bureau report.

It has also launched a "summer campaign" for the new Resimac Group brand with cuts of up to 30 basis points across prime and low-doc products and waiving the $120 annual fee for a decade, a saving of $1200.

Other shadow lenders, such as Peppermoney, are offering discounts to alternative documentation borrowers, who typically have credit scores between prime and sub-prime borrowers, and waiving establishment, title protection and legal fees worth about $1800.

Shadow, or non-authorised deposit-taking institutions, are typically offering principal and interest, owner-occupied loans at 52 basis points less than the major banks, or about 3.77 per cent for a $1 million loan at 80 per cent loan to value ratio, according to Canstar, which monitors rates and fees.

Major banks include CBA, Westpac Group, ANZ and NAB.

A comparable principal and interest investment loan is about 38 basis points lower, or a headline rate of 4.12 per cent. Shadow bank headline rates are also about 30 basis points lower than non-major ADIs, which includes community banks, co-operatives and mutuals.

Shadow bank owner-occupier lending in the two years to October 2018 increased from $64 billion to $102 billion, a rise of nearly 60 per cent, according to Digital Finance Analytics, an independent consultancy.

During the same period ADIs have grown market share by about 14 per cent, according to the consultancy. Mortgage borrowers are two-and-a-half times more likely to have a home loan approved by a regulation-lite shadow bank than a big four competitor, its analysis also reveals.

The gap between the sectors has widened since February when the banking royal commission put the spotlight on authorised deposit-taking institution (ADI) lending practices, particularly for the big four lenders.

Shadow banks have been gaining market share since early 2016 when macro-prudential controls imposed by regulators targeted lending standards in a successful bid to ease pressure during the property boom.

Their total share is close to 9.5 per cent and growing strongly as borrowers switch from mainstream lenders in a bid to get a better deal, or more competitive introductory rates. In late 2016 the market share was just over 7 per cent.

Shadow banking executives say the big four have been overzealous in lifting their lending standards, which has sent a flood of new business their way, but they have hit back at suggestions they are extending easy money to risky borrowers.
direction: bearish for major banks

headline: Building on energy market transition
text:
Soaring power prices have prompted Australian businesses to look for innovative solutions, and one retailer is helping them by opening up access to the wholesale energy market.

Melbourne-based Flow Power has developed a model aimed at providing commercial and industrial users with the same tools that large retailers employ to purchase energy.

These could take the form of large-scale corporate renewables power purchase agreements (PPAs), access to the spot market, use of derivative contracts, and even pricing that allows customers to take advantage of flexible operations.

‘‘What we do is materially very different to what the incumbent players do,’’ says CEO Matthew van der Linden.

‘‘We are very much about delivering results that actually enable customers to unlock value from their energy.’’ While prices constantly change in the wholesale market, they typically stay below the average, which was 8.4c/kWh last calendar year, more than 60 per cent of the time.

New technologies such as renewable energy, are creating opportunities to generate energy at a lower operational cost.

Despite retailers investing heavily in renewables’ capacity in recent years to meet their regulatory obligations, few users have benefited from the falling cost of generation.

One of Flow Power’s key tools to overcome this situation is through corporate renewable PPAs.

Under this arrangement, it contracts power from large-scale wind farms or solar farms and then signs up back-toback long-term contracts with customers for a part of that capacity.

‘‘For a business, it is relatively simple – it’s a significantly reduced cost for their energy by accessing the lower cost renewables,’’ says van der Linden.

‘‘They also achieve sustainability goals and support investment in the new generation.’’ Out of the 76 corporate renewable PPAs publicly announced in Australia, Flow Power has been directly involved in a third.

Some of its major customers include industrial giant Molycop, Sydney Opera House and Olam Orchards.

In October alone, it signed up agreements to move Sydney and Newcastle to 100 per cent renewable energy.

Flow Power employs proprietary energy management technology to help customers manage usage in line with the market.

Its kWatch platform includes a controller and a portal that helps operators spot those times so they can immediately adjust their energy needs.

This could be achieved, for example, by shutting down a crusher at amine or an irrigator’s pumps over short periods of time to minimise exposure to high price peaks.

Alternatively, they can shift to localised power sources such as generators or battery storage, which may be cheaper during such periods. This helps businesses alleviate pressure on the system during peak times.

This is called demand response and customers regularly save up to 30 per cent on their energy costs, with some customers achieving up to 60 per cent savings, the company says.

Flow Power has evolved its offering in the past few years towards thinking holistically about energy – from where it is sourced to how it is used, van der Linden says.

As a result, it has boosted resources in engineering, research and development, behindthe- meter services and account management to ensure it offers a holistic service to energy users.

‘‘We believe technology is crucial for the future so we are dedicating resources to research and development,’’ van der Linden says.

‘‘We are backing that up with a serious engineering department that we have grown in the past 12 months.’’ The retailer now installs a kWatch Controller for every customer signing up to provide live per second usage data, which they say helps businesses manage how they use power.

Flow Power’s growth plans have been supported after one of Canada’s biggest pension funds, OPTrust, acquired majority control in the business last year.

OPTrust has helped Flow Power take on the market in away it couldn’t have previously, van der Linden says, also assisting significantly in securing off-take agreements with projects.

The retailer expects to continue expanding its offerings as it looks to gain share in amarket transitioning to a low-carbon future where renewables are central.

‘‘We are not after just the big players, we really do want to make the corporate PPA model available for everybody,’’ he says.

‘‘Our goal is to continue to put the customer at the centre of the energy market where they take control of their energy needs.’’
direction: bullish for renewable energy and energy efficiency sectors

headline: Bruised Boeing replaces top executive of MAX 737 division
text:
New York/London | Boeing has replaced the head of its commercial aeroplanes division, marking the most significant leadership change after two deadly 737 MAX crashes.

The jet maker said on Wednesday (AEDT) that Stan Deal would succeed Kevin McAllister, president and chief executive of Boeing Commercial Airplanes since 2016, effective immediately.

Mr Deal was previously president and chief executive of Boeing's global services unit. He will be replaced in that position by Ted Colbert, previously the company's chief information officer.

Shares in Boeing rose 1.8 per cent in New York on Tuesday.

"We're grateful to Kevin for his dedicated and tireless service to Boeing, its customers and its communities during a challenging time, and for his commitment to support this transition," said Dennis Muilenburg, Boeing chief executive, who was recently stripped of his chairmanship.

Mr McAllister was poached by Mr Muilenburg in 2016 from General Electric, where he was head of its aviation business. He was a rare outside hire for Boeing and was put in charge of its commercial aircraft business with a mission to shake up and modernise production, as well as generate greater revenue form after sales service.

However, he was also the executive responsible for resolving the problems with the 737.

His unexpected departure comes just the day before Boeing was widely expected to announce in third-quarter results another multibillion-dollar hit to profits as a result of the global grounding of its 737 MAX aircraft.

The 737 MAX 8 has been banned from flying by aviation authorities around the world after two fatal crashes within five months left hundreds dead.

Last July, Boeing announced a $US4.9 billion ($7.2 billion) after-tax charge related to the grounding in its first estimate of the cost of compensating airlines for schedule disruptions and delays in aircraft deliveries.

The departure also comes as Boeing faces spiralling criticism over its disclosure of information relating to the automated software system that has been blamed for the two crashes.

A series of text messages sent in 2016 by two Boeing technical pilots — who prepare training simulators and manuals — was leaked last Friday. These appeared to suggest that Boeing had long been aware of problems with the aircraft's anti-stall system, known as the manoeuvring characteristics augmentation system, or MCAS.

However, Boeing has argued that these texts had been handed over to the US Department of Justice after the first crash in October 2017 and were not about problems with the software, but with the simulator.

Nevertheless, the head of the Federal Aviation Administration last week publicly reprimanded Boeing over its failure to disclose the texts to the regulator until recently.

The strained relationship between Boeing and the FAA has raised concerns that the timetable for re-certifying the 737 MAX could now be delayed into next year.

S&P this week revised its outlook on Boeing from stable to negative, but retained its A credit rating. It cited the texts and concern that the aircraft's return to service could be delayed. It also said the events of recent days could "have a lasting impact on the company's reputation, competitive position, profitability, and - ultimately - its financial strength".

Boeing this week put out a progress report of all the steps it was taking to address the problems of the 737. It said all the upgrades to MCAS had been completed and handed to the FAA.
direction: bearish for Boeing shares

headline: Credible Labs founder joins Young Rich List
text:
Former Brisbane boy Stephen Dash landed in Silicon Valley in September 2012 with one suitcase and one contact in San Francisco.

Mr Dash, 35, rocketed on to the Financial Review Young Rich List yesterday with plans to grow his consumer finance marketplace Credible Labs into the next REA Group for Fox Corp chief executive Lachlan Murdoch.

"We've come a considerable way since then, a lot of hard work from a great team I have around me," said Mr Dash, who went to school at Brisbane's Marist College in Ashgrove.

Fox will pay $US265 million ($390 million) for 67 per cent of the equity in Credible Labs, and will also stump up $US75 million to fund the company's operations over the next two years. The deal values Credible at $585 million.

Mr Dash holds 43.5 per cent of the company, where he will remain CEO, but exchange 33 per cent of Credible's equity into units in a newly created Fox subsidiary. His 33 per cent holding in the new Fox subsidiary is worth $193.1 million and he will sell the balance remaining for $55.25 million, taking the total value of the deal for him to nearly $265 million.

The transaction, if approved by shareholders, is essentially a buyout with the founder and chief executive keeping skin in the game.

"This is the next phase of our growth where we're partnering with someone who can bring the two most important things we need, distribution and capital," Mr Dash said.

"It's a unique opportunity that we've got ourselves in with this particular partnership - they've done it before ... some good examples here in Australia, REA is a good example. There's a lot of similarities in the potential."

The junior Murdoch made News Corp's original investment in real estate classifieds business REA in 2000, paying $2.25 million in cash and $8 million in contra advertising for a 44 per cent stake. News Corp now owns 61.6 per cent and the business has a market capitalisation of $12.8 billion.

Mr Dash spent nearly five years at investment bank JPMorgan in the mid-2000s before starting a business, Quick Charge Media, that installed mobile charging stations at airports. He admitted this was a "great learning experience, not a great outcome" and exited in 2012.

Over 2011 and 2012, Mr Dash spent nearly two years as an investment director at Mark Carnegie's private equity, venture capital and advisory firm at the same time as Afterpay founder Nick Molnar.

Credible is a financial technology company founded in 2012. It operates a marketplace platform for student, personal and now mortgage loans. It listed on the ASX in July 2017 at $1.21. On Friday the share price closed at $2.06.

Fox made its initial approach on May 29. Fox's offer is $2.21 per CHESS Depositary Interests, or CDI, 31 per cent above the closing price of $1.69 the day before its approach.

A CDI is a financial product that allows beneficial ownership of foreign financial products. Credible's "common stock" is incorporated in the US as a private company.

Shareholders were mixed in their view of the deal on Monday. Billionaire chairman of Thorney Group Alex Waislitz, who holds 4 per cent of the company, said it was a great endorsement of the business model.

"We think that if Credible keeps delivering it could easily reach a billion-dollar valuation in one to two years' time, which would make today's offer price look like a bargain."

However, Mr Dash emphasised he was taking the maximum amount of cash out that he is allowed because he thinks the offer is so attractive.

"I think it's a very attractive valuation given where our fundamentals are. It's 10 times revenue and our comps trade at half that, around 4.8 times revenue. Lending Tree is the big one in the US. I think it's a very attractive price and I'm selling a really meaningful stake as part of the transaction."

One of Credible's earliest financial backers, Dean Dorrell from Carthona Capital, has known Mr Dash since they worked together at M.H. Carnegie, and has a holding of just under 5 per cent.

"Less than six months ago I was telling anyone that would listen how undervalued the company was and that it would trade at multiples of the share price then," he said.

"In terms of returns, across all rounds, the IRR will be mid-60s per cent - which we are particularly happy about considering we first invested in early 2015."

Mr Dash and Fox chief technology officer Paul Cheesman both noted Credible would soon have needed to raise more capital, diluting shareholders, as the company looks to continue its expansion, particularly the push into the mortgage market, which is in its infancy.

Mr Dash said the deal with Fox ticked all the boxes in terms of providing capital - an extra $US75 million - and getting a major distribution platform in Fox, which has a host of sports, business, national and local news television and digital media assets across the US. The deal is the second major transaction since Fox completed the $US71.3 billion sale of its film and entertainment assets to Disney in March.

"If Stephen and the board were to continue on their own they'd need to raise money in the next six to 12 months," Mr Cheesman said.

"Not only are we paying a premium on the price, but we're also putting an additional $US75 million into the company so that they don't have to raise in the future. That helps secure the business."

He said looking at the work Mr Murdoch and News Corp did with REA in Australia, an acquisition of a marketplace platform to take advantage of Fox's scale is a natural extension.

"The logic was pretty simple. We were looking to leverage our distribution and reach and high levels of engagement we've got in the US through all of our digital properties, beyond just advertising and subscriptions - those are our two core revenue streams we've got today," Mr Cheesman said.

"Digital growth is a major focus for the new business. We feel strongly that this is a category that will help. If we're in the shoes of these marketplaces, whether it's Credible or others, distribution is the key name in the game. You can either buy that or strategic partner to get that. That's the big thing we bring to the table."

WITH PAUL SMITH
direction: bullish for Credible Labs and founder Stephen Dash

headline: Vanguard returns to its actively managed roots
text:
The $7.9 trillion investment giant Vanguard is preparing to offer a suite of actively managed funds in a bid to win a greater share of the Australian market, where the majority of assets are still actively managed.

Vanguard Australia would make available three competitively priced active funds, two of which would be managed on behalf of Vanguard by other well-known global managers.

"There is momentum around passive investing in Australia but it is interesting in the market that there is still a significant portion of assets that continue to be actively managed - at around 80 per cent," Vanguard Australia's managing director Frank Kolimago told The Australian Financial Review.

The three funds include an emerging market equity fund managed by Boston-based Wellington and a global growth equities fund managed by well-regarded Scottish firm Baillie Gifford. A third fixed-income and credit fund would be managed by Vanguard's bond portfolio managers.

He said the firm could not disclose the fees at this point but said "they're going to be low in relative and absolute terms".

While Vanguard is best known for its pioneering index tracking funds that have forced down fees and sparked upheaval in the asset management sector, Mr Kolimago said Vanguard's active funds tended to outperform their peers with the key advantage being "the fee differential".

"We want to pick firms that have the right people, philosophy and process and we want to be able to enable them to do their best work through low fee structures,"

The firm has about $165 billion of assets under management in Australia, of which $16 billion was managed in the form of exchange-traded funds.

Vanguard actually has its roots in active management and before the firm's founder, Jack Bogle, created the world's first index fund in the late 1960s, it offered 11 actively managed mutual funds.

"In those humble early days, the prospects were not great. Indexing has become this incredible force but we manage over a trillion dollars of active strategies," Mr Kolimago said.

Mr Kolimago joined the firm in 1996 around the time Vanguard broadened into non-US markets.

He has held the position of Australian head for just over a year after moving from the United States, where he oversaw one of the firm's more successful new initiatives - the expansion into financial advice via its 'Personal Advisor Service'.

Vanguard first revealed its intention to offer financial advice services in an interview with Financial Review in 2015, and they have grown from a standing start four years ago to over $US130 billion of assets under advice.

Mr Kolimago said the firm came to realise that there was a real demand for advice given the complexity of managing personal finances near or at retirement.

But he said personal interaction remained at the heart of providing financial advice.

"There is a need for an empathic person that can relate to a couple that may not have spoken to each other about what they want in retirement."

But he said the firm was surprised by the appreciation and ability "to embrace technology" by those seeking advice.

Vanguard has no branches but does have three large service centres in Pennsylvania, Arizona and North Carolina allowing clients to talk to advisers over a video link.

"It was amazing how frequently they would accept that technology. We had a 92-year-old woman enrol for advice over a video.

"What we are learning is where there might be a parallel in Australia is that technology can engage and deliver the service in a more scalable cost-efficient manner."

Vanguard, like many other fund managers, is conscious of the huge pool of capital that is shifting into the retirement phase as Western populations age.

But, he said, far less capital was being pulled out of financial assets by retirees to be spent on goods and services than they and others had assumed.

"In both the United States and a place like Australia we are just not seeing that draw-down activity at the levels we anticipated.

"People tend to be living more frugally than what we had anticipated."

Mr Kolimago said that in the aftermath of the royal commission there has been an increase in demand for Vanguard's low-cost products, particularly from independent advisers.

"Increasingly advisers see the merits of using the building-block strategies that Vanguard can offer - the broadly diversified easy-to-assemble parts of the portfolio that may have a passive orientation."
sentiment: mixed with a positive bias for the asset management sector
direction: increased competition and fee pressure on active managers

headline: McCormack in charge as PM goes on holiday
text:
Prime Minister Scott Morrison is on leave this week, taking a short end-of-year overseas holiday with his family.

Nationals leader Michael McCormack is acting PM while Mr Morrison is away on the planned leave.

The trip was not announced and Mr McCormack's office referred questions to a spokesman for the Prime Minister.

It is Mr Morrison's first holiday since taking his family to Fiji after his May federal election win.

Yesterday's mid-year economic and fiscal outlook report was the final set-piece political event of the year for Mr Morrison. He will be back to work early in the New Year, in preparation for travel to India and Japan for official bilateral visits.

Previous leaders regularly took leave after Parliament rose for the Christmas break, with John Howard and his family often holidaying north of Sydney.

TOM McILROY
direction: no significant market impact

headline: Wagners battles slump as activists plan new attacks
text:
Wagners is coming under renewed attack from activists who don't want the Queensland construction group to make money from building infrastructure for Adani's Carmichael coal mine after its share price plunged amid fears the company will lose a key cement contract.

Building infrastructure such as roads and airports for the Carmichael mine in Queensland could support Wagners' earnings if building materials group Boral, which has a contract to buy cement from Wagners until the end of 2021, is successful in forcing the construction company to drop its cement prices.

Wagners, which reported a first-half net profit of $6.9 million, said last week that Boral had claimed it could buy cement at significantly cheaper prices elsewhere in south-east Queensland. Wagners said it had challenged the allegedly cheaper prices and that it would stop supplying Boral with cement for up to six months at a cost of $20 million while the dispute was resolved.

Wagners shares, which floated at $2.71 in December 2017 and hit all-time highs of $4.89 in October, slumped after Wagners revealed the dispute, closing at a new record low of $2.19 on Friday.

The agreement with Boral, Wagners' biggest cement customer, accounts for one-third of Wagners' earnings and about 40 per cent of its cement volumes. Morgans analysts said the dispute was "critical" for Wagners to resolve, noting that cement is the company's most profitable product and that the price cutting - which Morgans attributed to Cement Australia - reflected a slowdown in construction activity in Queensland.

FNZC analysts said Wagners could lose the Boral contract permanently if the pricing dispute was not sorted out.

Boral competes with Wagners in making cement but after the building group bought the Queensland group's construction materials business in 2011, it agreed to keep buying cement from Wagners.

A supply agreement signed between the two companies requires Wagners to provide cement to Boral until December 2021, with Wagners having an option to extend the agreement for another 10 years. The price paid for cement is adjusted in line with inflation, with a price review every three years. The most recent price review was in July 2017.

Wagners could offset any loss in income from Boral with contracts with Adani if it succeeds in building an airport or roads for the Carmichael mine but has come under fresh attack from Galilee Blockade activists, who have been telephoning Wagners' executives to warn the company's operations will be disrupted if it starts any work for the Indian conglomerate.

Wagners said it was in discussion with Adani, which has not received any government funding to build the Carmichael mine, on different proposals and would provide more information "if and when work is awarded or starts".

Adani said it was doing as much work as possible under its existing approval conditions, including roadworks, signage and cattle grid bypasses and had engaged a number of contractors.
direction: Wagners shares decline

headline: Six tails wagging Coalition dog
text:
Climate change Every seat counts, but to watch the PM talk about climate change is to get the impression that some count more than others.
