The idea that Scott Morrison's election policies should be decided according to what he thinks is popular in electorates such as Wentworth in Sydney and Kooyong in Melbourne is like Donald Trump running for president by appealing to voters in Manhattan and San Francisco.

Yet when it comes to the Coalition's climate change policies that's what is happening. It's not quite as bad as Labor's position on the Adani coal mine because while the Coalition has only one message on climate change, Bill Shorten has two equal and opposite messages on Adani according to who he's talking to at the time.

As a result of byelections and redistributions the Coalition holds 73 out of the 151 seats in the House of Representatives. Labor notionally has 72 seats. The Coalition's problem at the election in a fortnight's time is it needs not just to hold seats, but win seats to get to a majority of 76.

Obviously, every seat counts. But to watch Scott Morrison talk about climate change is to get the impression that some seats count more than others.

The Coalition's climate change policies and the way it talks about the issue are determined by the belief that voters in six Liberal heartland seats across the country, want "real action" on climate change.

These six seats - Warringah and Wentworth in NSW, Goldstein and Higgins and Kooyong in Victoria, and Curtin in Western Australia - are the tail wagging the Coalition's climate change dog.

When it comes to climate change it's as if the concerns about ever-higher electricity prices from voters living in the nation's other 145 federal seats don't count.

The point has often been made about the disproportionate influence on politics that 520,000 Tasmanians have with their 12 senators compared with NSW, with a population of 7.9 million people, that also has 12 senators.

But that doesn't compare with the power wielded by a single swinging voter in Wentworth who goes on the nightly ABC News and announces climate change will decide her vote. A preoccupation with these six seats means that for example the Coalition can't even discuss the benefits of Australia withdrawing from the Paris Climate Agreement, or the advantages of adopting nuclear power, let alone have any Coalition MP repeat what the the country's chief scientist has admitted, which is that nothing Australia does to reduce its emissions of carbon dioxide will have any noticeable impact on the planet's climate.

There's good evidence that nationwide climate change is nowhere near as big an issue as it might, or it is pretended to be in those six seats. A few days ago an Essential Research poll revealed that when voters were asked about the importance of specific campaign issues on how they would vote, climate change ranked behind, in order, healthcare, national security and terrorism, management of the economy, jobs, education, and tax. Further, climate change only narrowly beat immigration and then housing as issues of importance. Fifty per cent of Greens voters said climate change was an important issue to them, for Labor voters that figure was 31 per cent, and for Coalition voters 16 per cent.

Despite results like this, somehow the effect of climate change policy on the Coalition is that it continues to make and unmake Liberal leaders.

Amid all the talk of the need for "real action" it's forgotten that the Paris Climate Agreement to which Tony Abbott committed Australia to when he was PM, and which was implemented by Malcolm Turnbull as PM, requires Australia on a per-capita basis to reduce its emissions more deeply than the United States, Japan, the European Union, or China. As Abbott announced in a press release at the time of Australia's commitment to Paris in August 2015, "Our [Australia's] emissions intensity and emissions per person will fall further than other developed economies."

Clearly, to voters in some of the country's most affluent electorates, Australia making the heaviest reductions to its carbon dioxide emission in the developed world doesn't qualify as "real action" on climate change.

Eventually, it might dawn upon the Coalition that when it comes to satisfying rich voters in wealthy electorates who say they want "real action" on climate change, more action will never be enough.

John Roskam is executive director of the Institute of Public Affairs.
direction: bearish for climate-sensitive sectors and utilities

headline: What moved the market
text:
LNG exports

China may have become the dominant buyer of Australian LNG after surpassing Japan for the third consecutive month in June. China has outstripped Japan's market share on only seven occasions but an ambitious plan by Beijing to increase LNG import capacity fourfold over the next two decades suggests it will continue to take market share away from Japan. Just a few years ago, Japan was buying nearly 80 per cent of Australia's LNG, however, a sharp decline in June means it is now buying just over 30 per cent.

Copper

Copper prices firmed slightly on Friday ahead a meeting between US and Chinese negotiators in Shanghai this week. Copper rose 0.2 per cent to $US5977 a tonne on Friday. The talks between Beijing and Washington are the first in-person talks since both countries struck a truce at the G20 Summit last month. "... But the probability of a comprehensive deal between the two countries remains slim as neither side seems eager to agree," said London Capital Group senior market analyst Ipek Ozkardeskaya.

US dollar

The US dollar strengthened on Friday on the back of a solid US GDP print, ahead of a widely expected rate cut from the Federal Open Market Committee on Thursday morning (AEST). "The messaging in the post-meeting statement and the press conference will be key ..." NAB economist Tapas Strickland noted. "Given the better-than-expected GDP numbers on Friday, those in the FOMC who are framing in terms of an 'insurance cut' may find a greater voice and there may also be a dissenter or two."

US Fed

Morgan Stanley believes the Federal Open Market Committee will be aggressive in its rate decision on Thursday (AEST) forecasting a 0.5 percentage point cut. "The FOMC will cut the federal funds rate target range by 50 basis points, to 1.75-2 per cent, with neutral policy guidance and continued assurances that it will act as appropriate to sustain the expansion," said economist Ellen Zentner. "Meanwhile, it may acknowledge higher market-based inflation compensation but, if so, will stress that it remains low."
direction: pressure on rate-sensitive sectors and uncertainty for exporters

headline: Super conflicts will grow with funds' clout
text:
Bill Shorten deserves praise for delivering an unequivocal statement on how the union movement needs to respect the best interests of super fund members.

But the growing size and power of industry funds means there are a multitude of questions funds will need to answer about how they act in the best interests of members.

Shorten, who for more than a decade sat as a trustee of the super fund that eventually became AustralianSuper, provided what should be the final word on suggestions union-backed industry funds should press companies on very specific industrial-relations issues, such as BHP's decision to withdraw from a minor shipping contract that made 80 ship workers redundant.

"Let me be very clear here. The superannuation legislation is black and white. Trustees have to act in the best interest of their members. They've got to hang up the hat of whatever organisation they might work for or sponsor them at the door of the meeting," Shorten said on Tuesday.

The best interest test is still malleable enough to cover lobbying on all sorts of issues, including climate change, diversity and corporate governance - provided they can be tied back to a company's financial performance.

It could even extend to some industrial-relations issues. You could imagine a super fund would express displeasure at the reputational damage if a company had widespread underpayment issues. But as Shorten says, the super laws really give no leeway - campaigning on anything other than issues that affect the best financial interest of fund members just isn't on. However, even when viewing the best-interest tests through a financial lens, it is clear there are other conflicts that will emerge as super funds get bigger and more powerful.

Take AustralianSuper's recent takeover bids for hospital operator Healthscope and education group Navitas, both of which were done in a consortium with private equity firm BGH Capital.

The Healthscope bid is all but dead and the Navitas bid is all but over the line. But these deals are just the start. AustralianSuper trustee Heather Ridout said at The Australian Financial Review Business Summit this month that the fund would pursue more of these deals as it put its enormous pool of funds to work.

These take-private bids contain a number of potential best-interest conflicts.

The Navitas and Healthscope bids used the same initial tactic: AustralianSuper, as an existing and substantial shareholder of both companies, declared it would support the BGH consortium's offer to the exclusion of other bids.

Many would see this as potential best-interest conflict: by supporting its own bid to the exclusion of others, AusSuper risked denying members access to a higher bid. The counter argument is that AusSuper obviously believes it can extract higher returns from taking the business private, and therefore members' interests are inherently in focus.

But notably, BGH's revised and successful offer for Navitas saw AustralianSuper change its position such that it could support a superior bid, which suggests it was at least sensitive to this point.

And of course, fund members who only have exposure to Australian shares, and do not have exposure to private equity in their AusSuper account, could miss out under the AusSuper exclusive bid arrangement.

Under the "balanced" option offered by AusSuper - home to 90 per cent of members - the portfolio has a 22 per cent exposure to Australian equities, and a 4 per cent exposure to private equity.

But a member in the "stable" option - the portfolio with the lowest risk, designed for members close to or in retirement - have zero exposure to private equity and so will not be able to enjoy any Navitas upside.

In this way, AusSuper could be seen as prioritising the best interests of one category of members over another in these take-private deals.

Of course, given so many of its members are in the recommended balanced option - indeed, any member that has moved out of this option has made a deliberate choice - then AusSuper arguably can do little but to prioritise the best interests of the vast majority of members. To do otherwise would probably be impractical.

AustralianSuper has said it will review the BGH bids in due course. It will be fascinating to see how it approaches the task of managing these conflicts and discussing them with members, given its intention to pursue such deals.

But there are wider and, perhaps, more philosophical questions for a super sector still in its first decades of existence: How does a super fund think about a fund member's changing best interests as they go through their working life? And how should the best-interest test be applied as the super sector becomes more complex and potentially customised?
direction: increased regulatory scrutiny and potential reputational risks for super funds and their portfolio companies

headline: letters
text:
Vale Tim Fischer

Tim Fischer was a colourful political figure and he will be missed by many.

His deadly illness was likely contributed to by his service in South Vietnam when he was exposed to the defoliant Agent Orange, which was sprayed on local villages by the US air force. This evil act not only affected thousands of Vietnamese but similarly former ADF personnel and their partners.

Adrian Jackson

Middle Park, Vic

Frydenberg wrong on dividend levels

Treasurer Josh Frydenberg's criticism of the increased level of rate of dividends in the past 12 months compared with the previous four years misses a key cause.

The surge reflects understandable concern at the impact of Labor's proposed changes to imputation credits, causing many listed companies to bring forward and pay in 2018-19 franked dividends that would otherwise have not been paid until the current 2019-20 year.

So having falsely blamed last year's dividend surge on boards' reluctance to re-invest, Frydenberg will presumably claim, equally falsely, that his recent utterances are responsible for the decline in dividend payments in the current year.

George McGregor

Malvern, Vic

Shining a light on WA gas discovery

The onshore West Erregulla-2 gas discovery 300km north of Perth is superb news for shareholders, customers and LNG prices ("'Staggering' WA find raises cheap LNG hope", August 29). For a while.

The life of a field depends on size and exploitation but is typically 15 to 30 years, extended by fracking before being abandoned.

In comparison solar projects such as the Sun Cable, NT project (August 5) are diamonds - they are forever. The gas discoveries are good news for Australia's energy self-sufficiency but our future is with our competitive advantage in renewables.

Malcolm Cameron

Camberwell, Vic

Life insurance reform long overdue

The Senate is due to debate long-overdue reforms to life insurance in superannuation in September when Parliament returns.

But lobbyists are using heavy-handed tactics to block changes ("'Book of death' bid to keep default life insurance", August 12).

ASIC, the Productivity Commission, and parliamentary inquiries have all urged reforms, but the insurance lobby is using shock and awe tactics to pressure senators.

Treasurer Josh Frydenberg should be applauded for standing up to 'the life insurance mafia'.

Cameron Sinclair, senior policy adviser

Super Consumers Australia
direction: bearish for dividend-paying shares and REITs

headline: PM pledges $1bn SME growth fund
text:
Scott Morrison will today pledge to help create 250,000 new small businesses over the next five years, and will also unveil an equity plan to help existing small and medium businesses expand.

Eager to keep the election campaign focused on the economy, the Prime Minister will announce the establishment of an Australian Business Growth Fund.

It will involve the government partnering with the banks and providing small and medium business owners with equity needed for expansion while not forcing them to give up control of their business, as can occur now.

Based on similar funds operating in the United Kingdom and Canada, the fund will be seeded with $100 million in government money.

"Many small and family businesses can only get equity funding by giving up control of their business," Mr Morrison said.

"The fund will deliver patient capital to these businesses so they can grow without having to give up control.

"As the fund matures, it is anticipated to grow to $1 billion and back 30-50 businesses each year with annual turnovers between $2 million to $50 million.

"We know small business is the engine room of Australia and we pledge to back them every step of the way."

The government has already been in consultation with the banks and other institutions about providing the necessary financial support "to bring it to life".

In echoes of his pledge at the start of the year to create 1.25 million jobs over the next five years if elected, Mr Morrison will set a new target today of an average 50,000 new small businesses a year for the next five years, bettering by 20,000 the number which were created over the previous same period.

The target will be a gross amount, not net. About 10,000 small businesses fail each year.

The definition of a small business is one with an annual turnover less than $10 million or one with fewer than 20 employees.

While Labor leader Bill Shorten spends the week focused on increasing wages, Mr Morrison, who will be campaigning in South Australia, will tie his pledge to his claim the economy will be stronger under the Coalition and the conditions ripe for the creation of more businesses.

"My government will create 250,000 new small and family businesses across the country over the next five years through our plan for a stronger economy, while Labor wants to tax those small businesses to make them even smaller," he said.

On small business, there is a fair degree of bipartisanship but the government sees it as a core constituency.

Labor and the government both back the new company tax regime, which will reduce the tax rate to 25 per cent for businesses with turnovers under $50 million.

In the federal budget earlier this month, the government also announced an extension of the Instant Asset Write-off Scheme in which the amount that could be written off increased from $25,000 to $30,000.

Also, the eligibility criterion was expanded from businesses with less than $10 million in annual revenue to those with less than $50 million.

And late last year, amid fears of a credit crunch in the wake of the banking royal commission, the government announced a $2 billion fund to provide loans for the small business sector.

The Australian Business Securitisation Fund will begin on July 1 and provide funding to small banks and non-bank lenders to lend to small and family businesses.

All these policies have bipartisan support.

Today's announcement of an Australian Business Growth Fund is designed to grow existing businesses rather than create new ones.

Treasurer Josh Frydenberg said yesterday there have also been discussions with the the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority (APRA), which would regulate the new growth fund.

"This is for those businesses like a local brewery or restaurant that wants to expand interstate or even overseas, or maybe a family owned construction company wanting to grow to so they can meet demand."

When Mr Morrison made his jobs pledge earlier this year it was widely derided because he was pledging no real increase in jobs growth over the next five years as compared to the previous five years.

The pledge to create 250,000 new small businesses exceeds the 230,000 which were created in the previous five years.

With just under four weeks until Australia goes to the polls, Labor leader Bill Shorten has shifted his focus this week to wages.

On Monday, he promised, that if elected, he would push the Fair Work Commission to grant a real increase to the minimum wage on July 1, meaning it would have to be above inflation.

Today, he will announce a significant increase to the minimum wage that must be paid to overseas workers n temporary visas to stop what Labor says is the abuse of such visas to undercut wages and freeze out locals.

Labor also reaffirmed over the Easter weekend its pledge to reverse cuts made to Sunday and public holiday penalty rates.
direction: supportive for small-cap equities and banking sector

headline: Not the right time to cash in our recession insurance
text:
Fiscal policy Money is cheap and there is a case for stimulus spending. But acting now will just make the next downturn harder.

The Reserve Bank has been encouraging the government to break open the piggy bank to help the economy move faster. And there's certainly a good case for that.

But there's at least as good a case for the Feds to hold off on extra stimulus.

The bank's view is well known, but here's a recap:

Australia has slowed: Unemployment is up, wage gains are at risk of backpedalling, and growth is well below trend. Inflation is stubborn: Inflation is harder to get moving than it used to be - the RBA has cut its view of 'full employment' to an unemployment rate of 4.5 per cent, down from the earlier 5 per cent. So the task is big: To get unemployment down to 4.5 per cent means creating 200,000 more jobs. Yet the Reserve Bank is almost out of ammo: To get an extra 200,000 jobs, you would need to cut the RBA's cash rate by 3 percentage points. But today's cash rate is just 0.75 per cent.Whereas the Feds have more ammo: The federal budget is back near balance. If we need to, Australia can use fiscal firepower in a way that few other nations can. The fall in interest rates adds to the case for action: The biggest economic story of 2019 isn't the global trade wars, it's the further collapse in global borrowing costs. The case for governments to fund long-lived public works is stronger when borrowing costs are lower. That's a persuasive case. If we "do more" - if the Reserve keeps cutting and heads into quantitative easing, and if the Feds boost infrastructure (perhaps via the states) and even bring forward extra tax cuts - then more jobs would be created and wage growth would lift.

But the Feds don't look as if they'll be coming to the party. That's why Deloitte Access Economics forecasts Australia's economy will be muddling through the next year or so rather than breaking the long-running cycle of low wage gains. The broad consensus is that they are not coming to the party for political reasons - getting back in the black is seen as an electoral winner. But there are also good economic arguments why they should hold the purse strings tightly shut, at least for now.

So let's consider the "no" case:

It's an insurance issue. The RBA is right that more needs to be done to get unemployment down and to get wage and price inflation up. But if Australia does that, then both the RBA and the Feds will have spent a lot of their 'rainy day fund'. The more "recession insurance" we cash in now, the less we'd have in any crisis. For the RBA, due to the technical constraints on what quantitative easing can achieve, and for the Feds, it's due to the political constraints on stimulus. That's a trade-off. If there's no crisis over the next five years or so, then the RBA will have been right, and we should be busting a gut on stimulus now. But if there is a crisis, there's a good case that going harder now could cost Australia rather more at that time; the RBA's good intentions will have worsened the next recession.

That's the debate we think Australia should have - where this nation sits on the trade-off between helping 200,000 people extra into jobs and boosting wages for those already employed, versus keeping more of a strategic reserve of stimulus in case it's needed later.

There's no right answer, but we're not all that unhappy with how policy has been unfolding. And that's all the more true given the rising dangers in the global geopolitical landscape. After all, the greater the risk, the more valuable an insurance policy becomes.

The bottom line is whether Australia's future average rate of unemployment would be lower under the "do more now" school of thought championed by the Reserve Bank, or the "rainy day fund" approach of hanging on to some firepower in case it is needed later.

We think that both sides have a point, and neither has a monopoly on the truth. If you believe one side of this debate is completely right and the other is completely wrong, then you aren't doing justice to a fiendishly complicated problem.

If something bad happens and a recession does head our way, then we should absolutely sacrifice the surplus in a heartbeat. It's a means to an end: a healthy starting point for the budget can and should make a material difference to Australia's ability to limit the size of any recession that threatens us.

Chris Richardson and Stephen Smith are partners of Deloitte Access Economics.
direction: cautious for rate-sensitive sectors

headline: Fund leader warns of 'cashing out'
text:
The chairman of Australia's largest super fund said the economy was likely to be soft for a couple of years but warned her 2.2 million members against directing the fund to move their savings to cash, as trade tensions weigh on the global market.

AustralianSuper's Heather Ridout said she had seen an unprecedented level of political interference in the global market.

"Generally, I worry that world politics has intruded in the investment landscape over the last 12 months - more than it ever has in my experience," Ms Ridout told the Australia-Israel Chamber of Commerce business lunch on Friday.

She said the economy could be "pretty soft" and the United States' market was late in the cycle after a bull run, and these factors would be reflected in members' returns.

AustralianSuper expects returns of about 7 per cent this financial year, compared to double-digit returns in previous years. "I think it is a more cautious world, it is a more volatile world," she said.

However, Ms Ridout warned members against changing asset allocations to rely too heavily on cash.

"Be very careful about taking your money out and putting it in cash," she said. "A lot of people did in the last quarter last year and they've missed the recovery from that volatility."

She said the market volatility last year was caused by trade tensions and other factors, rather than fundamental economic factors.

The warning came as large, wealthy families have sold off assets. On Thursday, billionaire James Packer revealed he sold a 20 per cent stake in Crown Resorts to Hong Kong billionaire Lawrence Ho.

AustralianSuper chief executive Ian Silk said the super fund's investing model would change significantly.

Twelve years ago, all its capital was handled by external managers; now, only 60 per cent to 63 per cent is managed by external parties and the rest is managed in-house.

"The next change is going to be a much greater allocation offshore and much greater direct investment," he said. This would change AustralianSuper's operating model.

"We were previously an organisation that focused on managers; increasingly, we're going to be an organisation that focuses on securities," he said. "And many of those decisions to purchase these securities are going to be made by people that work in our office."

AustralianSuper shocked the market last year when it used its major shareholding in private hospital operator Healthscope to make a joint takeover bid alongside the $2.3 billion private equity firm BGH.

However, Ms Ridout said being an active manager of assets did not mean the super fund was necessarily going to take an activist stance. So far it had stopped in investing only one asset: tobacco.

"I can't think of one good company that wouldn't want AustralianSuper in their shareholding, because we're long-term investors - we're into long-term value creation. We're not there trying to affect the board," she said.

"Divestment is the absolute last resort for AustralianSuper. So we're patient capital."
direction: cautious for equities, warning against defensive cash shifts

headline: Regal Funds wades into water assets, prepares fund
text:
Australia's most watched hedge fund is making a move into uncharted waters.

Street Talk understands Sydney-based Regal Funds Management has taken a stake in water rights and farmland assets manager Kilter Rural as part of a wider joint venture.

Regal and Kilter are expected to launch a new fund, called The Australian Farmlands Fund (AFF), which has already secured $20 million in seed funding and is slated to go to potential third party investors with a maiden capital raising next month.

AFF has been set up to purchase farmland and water assets with access to irrigation.

The fund is expected to target farmland assets that are under-utilised or mismanaged to add value, or farmland that is capital-constrained and seeking a partner to add value.

It is understood Regal bought an equity stake in Kilter as part of the arrangement.

Regal CEO Brendan O'Connor and the firm's independent director, Ian Gibson, will join Kilter's board, to help oversee Regal's investment.

It's a step sideways for Regal - one of the country's best known long/short equities investors, whose flagship Atlantic Absolute Return Fund has averaged 31.7 per cent a year since inception in 2004, according to the firm's website - although it would argue its experience managing alternative assets holds it in good stead.

And it is also one that will shine the spotlight on Kilter, which manages more than $200 million of water, farmland and ecosystem assets, including on behalf of Vic Super.
direction: bullish for Regal Funds and Kilter Rural shares

headline: Westpac crowns new king of advice
text:
When Westpac Banking Corp withdrew from financial advice at a cost to shareholders of up to $300 million it crowned a new king of independent financial advice in Australia, a former Westpac employee, Glenn Calder.

Calder, who started his financial advisory career at AMP and left Westpac four years ago, is well known in the elite world of truly independent financial advice because of his long-term commitment to ethical conduct and the unique business model at his firm, Viridian Advisory.

Every financial adviser working for Viridian must own shares in the company and a third of these shares must be held in perpetuity as a sign of their long-term financial commitment to acting legally, ethically and morally. An adviser who does not act in this way will be forced to leave the firm and the ethical shares will not be repaid for 12 months after their departure.

Another unique aspect of the Viridian business model is that it offers its financial advisory clients shares in the unlisted public company. Clients who opt to take up this option must seek separate independent financial advice.

In recent years the Viridian shares have yielded up to 10 per cent and provided capital gains of 10 to 12 per cent a year to shareholders, based on a valuation methodology using a multiple of future earnings. This year the shares are yielding 6 per cent.

Viridian does not pay bonuses or commissions and it will be under no obligation to recommend financial products sold by Westpac's former funds management arm, BT Investment Management.

Nor will Veridian be under any obligation to use Westpac's investment administration platform, Panorama. But Calder believes the platform administration market will ultimately have only three or four platforms.

This is because of the competitive pressure to keep upgrading the platform technology aimed directly at independent financial advisers. It says a lot about the lack of technological innnovation inside large institutions that two start-ups, Netwealth and Hub24, have been winning the lion's share of net flows to platforms after Panorama.

The Westpac decision to pull out of financial advice marks a strategic turning point in an industry that, for 20 years, was convinced it was a good idea to cross-sell in-house financial products to customers.

Customers at the big four banks were viewed as warm leads for selling insurance, mutual funds, trusts and financial advice. But the failure to closely monitor what was being sold and who it was being sold to led to the Hayne royal commission.

The Hayne inquiry uncovered what many had said was a problem for years. The culture inside wealth management was very different to the culture inside banking. Apart from anything else, the banks and AMP and IOOF failed to invest in the processes necessary to control the billing of customers.

There are broader consequences for the financial services sector and consumers from the decision by the major banks to pull out of financial advice. No longer can the clients of financial advisory firms rely on the safety blanket called moral suasion.

One stark way to look at this transformation in the risks and the economics of the financial advice industry is to compare the capital backing Viridian with the equity capital underpinning Westpac.

Viridian, which has been adviser to many BT Financial Advice customers over the past 14 months as part of a pilot program, has issued share capital of $90 million. This is more than 700 times smaller than the $64.5 billion in shareholders equity on the Westpac balance sheet.

On first blush, these numbers should make clients of BT Financial Advice nervous. They are being forced to give up the option of making a claim on the shareholders of Westpac should they be given advice that later warrants compensation or remediation.

About 10,000 customers are being pushed out of the protective bubble offered by Westpac's BT Financial Advice and its access to the bank's capital. Thanks to the Hayne royal commission Westpac has exercised its moral responsibility to use its shareholders' funds to cover perceived or real financial mistakes.

For example, last year Westpac decided to remove grandfathered payments to salaried BT Financial advisers. This benefited more than 140,000 BT Financial Advice customers.

Westpac never explicitly said its equity would be made available to compensate and remediate customers who suffered losses because of bad or non-existent advice. But that is what happened in the remorseless anti-banking climate fuelled by the Hayne inquiry. The final amount of compensation paid to customers by the big four banks and AMP and IOOF will not be known for several years. But at this stage the estimate is about $6 billion to cover compensation for poor advice and other breaches of their legal obligations to customers in superannuation funds and holding insurance policies.

Calder says Veridian has professional indemnity insurance, which is in place to cover the consequences of advisers not acting in the best interests of clients. But he says the "sentinel" shares held by each adviser are a constant reminder of the fiduciary duty of each adviser to act in the interests of clients and the industry.

The financial consequences for Veridian of being chosen by BT Financial Group's CEO, Brad Cooper, to be the privileged buyer of part of the BT Financial Advice business are quite staggering.

At the moment the firm has about $2.5 billion under advice and employs 40 financial planners. It is possible that by the close of the transaction with Westpac it will have up to $17 billion in funds under advice, employ about 130 advisers and have 400 staff.

Calder says Westpac CEO Brian Hartzer was right when he said the number of advisers aligned or working for the big four banks would continue to decline. They have fallen from 40 per cent in 2015 to 23 per cent of the total industry.

Calder says Viridian has put in place a number of strategies to ensure clients are given independent advice. The chief investment officer is solely responsible for choosing the financial products for customers.

There is no pressure on the advisers to recommend products manufactured by another arm of the business as happens at other firms.

One of the conundrums exposed by the Westpac decision to pull out of advice was the cost of compliance. Westpac said it lost $53 million in its advice business last year before $137 million in remediation costs. It lost $38 million in 2017.

Hartzer said the cost of compliance for advice offered by salaried staff made the business uneconomic. Calder, however, says the compliance costs can be borne by his firm and he can still make a profit.

TONY BOYD
direction: supportive for independent advisory firms and Viridian Advisory

headline: Complexity helps to justify fees
text:
Warren Buffett is a genius investor but he also has a second, less talked about super power. He explains complex financial concepts in terms anyone can understand. Many investors would welcome his focus on simplicity when it comes to running their self-managed superannuation funds.

Why are investing and SMSFs so complicated? Are they by their very nature complex or do we in the finance industry increase the complexity, deliberately or not? Do investors willingly gravitate towards complexity?

Buffett puts huge effort into his letters to shareholders and he respects his reader. This is a rare gift indeed. This also helps to explain the runaway success of books like Scott Pape's The Barefoot Investor.

Consumers crave financial knowledge in language and concepts they can relate to. Deep down, we all want to Marie Kondo our finances.

The biggest problem, I believe, lies in language and communication. My industry is littered with insider jargon and impenetrable terminology. We shroud important concepts in acronyms and Greek letters. It's not a DIY super fund, it's an SMSF. It's not a low-cost fund traded on the ASX, it's an ETF (exchange traded fund). Your investments don't outperform the index, they generate alpha.

As inside members of the finance club, we speak in shorthand gobbledegook to each other which seems perfectly normal. But when we communicate with consumers, we're baffled when they don't understand us. Ironically, studies have shown that using jargon makes you untrustworthy. This problem is us, not you.

People in finance are good at finance but we are terrible at explaining it in plain English to consumers. We assume you know what we know. We assume you love our slides with impenetrable graphs. We assume you devour our research reports, most of which would fail a Year 12 English exam for readability.

Another issue is that investing and SMSFs really are complicated. SMSFs sit at the intersection of several complex laws. They're a trust structure and so are subject to trust law dating back to the Crusades. As a superannuation vehicle, they are subject to detailed super legislation. They derive their tax concessions from the tax laws, and investing in your SMSF is subject to the Corporations Act. Each is highly complex, let alone when they overlap and interact. So if the rules feel complex, it's because they are.

Investing is also complex. It presents itself as a science with formula and rules, cause and effect relationships. Learn them all and you should have it mastered. But when the proverbial hits the fan, it doesn't behave anything like a science. So you also have to understand the psychology of investing (behavioural finance).

Last but not least, my industry has a vested interest in complexity. Think of The Wizard of Oz. As long as the curtain is drawn to consumers and the financial players can hide behind complexity, consumers can believe financial gurus have the secrets.

We bombard you with choice, too much choice. How do you filter it all and make informed decisions? Surely the most complex products have to be the best ones or they wouldn't cost so much?

Sadly, this 60 Minutes quote from former Goldman Sachs employee Greg Smith is all too true: "The quickest way to make money on Wall Street is to take the most sophisticated product and try to sell it to the least sophisticated client." Complexity helps justify fees.

As consumers, we don't like boring, we're time poor, we like action and we want to somehow access the industry's investing secrets - the best investments, the perfect asset allocation. The real secret is no one knows this in advance, only in the rear-view mirror.

The finance industry hopes you don't ever pull that curtain open.SI

Tim Mackay is a financial adviser at Quantum Financial.
direction: pressure on high-fee fund managers and financial services firms

headline: INVESTING LESSONS FROM THE DECADE
text:
What's ahead With equity and bond markets expensive, diversification will be difficult, writes William McInnes.

The curtain on the 2010s will be drawn at the end of this year, closing a decade dominated by major geopolitical headlines. The European debt crisis, Brexit, the election of US President Donald Trump, an iron ore boom and record low interest rates have been the story of the past 10 years, as the market has struggled to recover from the global financial crisis.

This is not just a history lesson, though - the last decade also holds clues as to what investors can expect for the next 10 years, where they might find the best returns or a safe haven in troubled times.

Diversification could prove to be the biggest challenge in the next decade, however, with equity and bond markets looking either fully valued or overpriced.

"You need a more comprehensive strategy in your portfolio to cope with an environment of lower returns and higher risks," says Perpetual head of investment strategy Matt Sherwood.

"Government bonds are more overpriced than equities and a key question is can fully valued equity markets be diversified into even more expensive government bond markets?"

The 2010s were dominated by outsized returns across almost every asset class, as shown in the graphic on the next page illustrating how an investment of $10,000 10 years ago would have grown.

But returns on Wall Street were particularly strong. "There's no doubt in the past decade the success story has been US equities," says Sherwood.

"They've been supported by fairly large buybacks, a large growth sector and continually supportive monetary and fiscal policy. All these factors have enabled the corporate sector to extract reasonable earnings growth from what has been the slowest economic recovery in history."

At an index level, US shares have also outperformed due to the high concentration of large-cap growth stocks.

"In the S&P 500, its large-cap stocks are dominated by technology firms," says Sherwood.

"If you go back 30, 40 or 50 years, it was dominated by industrial giants. The US market is the largest evolving creature on this planet. They fundamentally admire entrepreneurs and when you do this for 400 years, it delivers the results in the index evolution."

In the past 10 years, as shown in the graphic, US shares have returned 16.8 per cent annually, well above the returns on any other assets and more than double the return from Australian shares.

"What that shows is that despite having favourably high dividend yields, Australia has significantly underperformed," says Sherwood.

"We have very few major growth stocks in the index and instead our market is more exposed to highly mature industries which are dominated by oligopolies. Mature industries don't tend to generate very large earnings growth."

While Australia's sharemarket has paid out high dividends to shareholders, it has come at the expense of lower total returns.

"The payout ratio for an Australian listed company is about 77 per cent and that's more than double the US, double Japan and double emerging markets," says Sherwood.

"I think it's capped our growth. Our mature industry structure just can't generate strong enough earnings growth."

The underperformance of Australian equities has been matched by the outperformance of domestic bonds, which have had substantially higher returns than international fixed income.

"Many markets have actually hit their lows from a cash rate and bond yield perspective well into this cycle," says Vanguard Australia head of investment strategy Aidan Geysen. "Australia is at the lowest point we've been for both 10-year bond yields and the cash rate, more than 10 years on from a financial crisis. This has resulted in very strong returns from bonds."

Stimulus measures introduced by various governments in the past decade have boosted asset classes, but the policy tap is starting to run dry.

"The quantitative easing and stimulus packages unleashed on the back of the global financial crisis certainly boosted returns across a number of assets," says Geysen.

"Clearly the kind of unconventional policy tools used by central banks during the financial crisis were not just used during the period of recession."

A lack of monetary policy stimulus could weigh on some asset classes in the next 10 years, with the strong returns from property likely to reverse.

These have been historically strong, outperforming domestic shares over the past decade. "Australian property has benefited from a structural decline in interest rates," Sherwood says.

"Australia until recently couldn't build enough housing to accommodate the growing population. That's supported real estate prices, but how much further can you push on that piece of string?"

He suggests the returns on property are unlikely to continue, with credit growth tightening in the past few years.

"Australian households are up to their eyeballs in debt," he says. "This massive structural upswing we saw in leverage is now running out of puff. The state of household balance sheets is going to be a massive issue."

So what else lies ahead for the next decade? Unfortunately for investors, weaker growth and higher risk.

Market strategists say inflation is likely to remain below target for several years across the globe, with monetary policy remaining loose to boost economic growth.

"Where that gets you is a pretty bleak place for fixed income and bond returns," says JPMorgan Asset Management global strategist, multi-asset solutions, Patrik Schöwitz.

"Bond yields are low, interest rates are low around the world and we assume there will be a recovery at some point to higher interest rates and bond yields but it will take longer."

This creates an ideal environment for equities at least. Bond yields are close to record lows, monetary policy is extremely accommodating and the global growth picture appears to be getting slightly brighter with a phase one trade deal agreed and Brexit closer to completion after the election of a Conservative government in the UK.

"Just in a relative sense, risky assets look better, not because they look good but because safe assets look so bad," says Schöwitz.

JPMorgan forecasts equity returns across the globe to average around 6.5 per cent over the next decade while Australian equities are forecast to average 5.8 per cent.

"That's not great in a historic context but it sure is a lot better than what you're going to get from fixed income," says Schöwitz.

The strong returns across other asset classes from the last decade are also unlikely to continue into the 2020s.

"Some of it was from the catch-up from the financial crisis, some of it reflects the very low risk-free rates that we currently have with central bank policy rates and bond yields where they are," says Vanguard's Geysen.

"All these things mean we've had outsized returns for this period - some of this is possibly borrowing from the future. Equity valuations are extended and bond yields are at all-time lows."

Investors are unlikely to be content with accepting weaker returns, however, with some strategists suggesting alternative assets could be the ideal place to look for a stronger return on investment.

"You can go into income-generating investments in the alternates space and even real estate doesn't look crazy in that context because it actually generates a yield," says JPMorgan's Schöwitz.

"With real estate and infrastructure, if you're going to earn 5 to 6 per cent extra income every year for a number of years, that's going to offset a lot of that pain that will come at some point when the cycle ends."

Sherwood says investors need to find other ways to diversify their return from equities instead of fixed income.

"Equities are fully valued and bonds are extremely expensive owing to central bank policy," he says. "While that combination has served investors well in the last decade, it's going to handicap their portfolio in the next decade.

"Alternate assets are also historically expensive given the decline we've seen in government bonds.

"What we need to find is assets that have a reverse correlation to equity markets in stress periods or even just find assets which have no correlation with equities in stress periods."

The message is investors need to temper their expectations in the next 10 years if they're hoping their portfolios will be able to deliver similar returns to the past decade with the same amount of risk.

The graphic demonstrates the importance of asset allocation in matching your investment goals, particularly as the market enters what could prove to be a difficult decade of investing.

"I would argue that the asset classes point to the importance of asset allocation. Given where valuations are, this will culminate in low rates of return and higher risk moving forward," says Sherwood.

"Does this matter? For a young investor probably not, as they are investing for the multiple decades and can ride out the vagaries of the cycle.

"However, for a person approaching retirement, the sequence of returns matters a great deal as they don't have the time to recover losses, particularly if they are close to drawing down on their super."SI
direction: cautious outlook for equities and bonds due to overvaluation

headline: No longer a barbarian, says PE king Gray
text:
BGH Capital's Ben Gray says private equity funds are no longer the "barbarians at the gate" seeking to slash and burn companies for a quick buck.

These private funds would increasingly work with industry superannuation funds to own and grow major companies, said Mr Gray, one of the heavyweights of Australia's PE industry.

Mr Gray took control of listed education provider Navitas on Friday and said the major super funds had no choice but to hunt and buy more recession-proof companies to improve their returns in a low-rate world.

"The barbarians at the gate kind of theme is just completely ancient history," Mr Gray told The Australian Financial Review in a rare interview at 101 Collins Street in Melbourne. "There are some players particularly in the US that still portray that image but they are the minority in the industry.

"The private equity sector today is trillions of dollars of assets, trillions and trillions of dollars, so it is a massive, massive sector. It is the alternative to the public market today and is not a niche sector anymore."

Private equity buyouts shot up by 10 per cent to $582 billion last year, capping the strongest five-year stretch in the industry's history.

Estimates of private equity's so-called "dry-powder", that is, capital that has been raised but yet to be deployed, ranges from between $US500 billion and $US1.5 trillion ($715 billion and $2.14 trillion) globally. Once the use of debt was accounted for, private equity could finance more than $US5 trillion of acquisitions.

Local venture capital and private equity funds also had a bumper year in 2018, and the value of assets under management hit an all-time high of $30 billion.

Major super funds such as AustralianSuper are increasingly working with PE firms like BGH to identify companies to buy, such as the takeover of Navitas after failing in their bid to buy the listed Healthscope late last year.

AustralianSuper's chief investment officer Mark Delaney said on Thursday the $165 billion fund would invest in more private markets offshore to maintain strong returns.

Mr Gray said "super funds like AustralianSuper have no option but to diversify into more private assets as have some of the leading pension funds around the world.

Mr Gray refused to specify which sectors he was looking at but said the key was finding recession-proof companies.

He put both Navitas and Healthscope in that category while admitting infrastructure assets were "pretty fully priced at the moment". He said the "whole world today is flush with capital" and very low interest rates made finding quality assets difficult.

"Most people in our industry today would expect some sort of economic softness in the next couple of years, so people are generally steering clear of businesses that are going to be significantly impacted by a period of economic softness," Mr Gray said.

"Part of it is about the fact that people are focusing more on businesses that should grow through a period of economic softness and we are certainly doing that."

BGH appointed a new CEO for Navitas, Scott Jones - the son of founder Rod Jones, who also backed BGH's bid - on Friday.

Mr Jones said he was pleased the new owners - which include the British Columbia Investment Management Corporation, Canada Pension Plan Investment Board and Ontario Teachers' Pension Plan Board - were all long-term investors.

"Being a private company allows us to do this clearly out of the public eye and make these diligent longer-term investments to sustain that growth over a period of time," Mr Jones told the Financial Review.

Mark Wiseman, the US chairman of Blackrock Alternative Investors, said Blackrock was investing heavily to take advantage of the global shift of investors into private assets.

"As a firm, we are investing in both organically and inorganically developing our capabilities," Mr Wiseman said.

Key pointsBen Gray says major super funds had to hunt and buy recession-proof companies.

PE buyouts shot up by 10pc last year, capping a record five-year period.
direction: supportive for private equity firms and industry superannuation funds

headline: APLNG payout fuels Origin dividend hopes
text:
Origin Energy reaped a bigger-than-expected $943 million payout from its Australia Pacific LNG venture in Queensland last financial year, fuelling expectations of a faster paydown of debt and an increase in dividends.

Higher export and domestic gas prices and lower capital spending at APLNG drove the bumper cash payout to 35 per cent owner Origin for 2018-19, easily beating Origin's guidance of $850 million.

It represents a 160 per cent hike on the APLNG payout to Origin in 2017-18.

The market welcomed the news, with Indian Pacific Funds Management's Preston Hamersley voicing confidence Origin will increase its dividend payout despite "tough" conditions for the core energy markets business.

The electricity and gas supplier resumed dividend payments in February after a three-year hiatus when it focused on paying down debt, which ballooned to about $12 billion in 2015.

"The full-year APLNG cash dividend is around $40 million higher than expected ... which should see Origin comfortably move into their target range" for gearing, Mr Hamersley said.

"They are now poised to return to a more generous payout ratio, having flagged to the market they will announce a new dividend policy at the 2019 financial year result."

Both Standard & Poor's and Moody's upgraded their credit rating for Origin in April.

However, the market reaction was tempered by more subdued results from the energy markets business, where chief executive Frank Calabria has pointed to several challenges, including the partial re-regulation of household power prices, intense retail competition, and declining wholesale electricity prices.

Electricity sales volumes slipped 3 per cent in the full year due to customer losses and lower consumption, including a 6 per cent decline in the fourth quarter. Gas volumes also slid 3 per cent, with less gas going into power generation, partly offset by higher business sales.

RBC Capital Markets analyst Ben Wilson said the figures were weaker than he had anticipated but noted Origin hadn't changed its guidance for the division's full-year gross earnings of $1.5 billion-$1.6 billion. The full-year profit report is due on August 22.

Origin did, however, flag a $160 million-$180 million increase in a charge for remediation costs at its former Osborne gasworks site in South Australia.

JPMorgan's Mark Busuttil said the strong cashflows reported from APLNG could be overshadowed by earnings downgrades as a result and is also anticipating weaker guidance on energy markets earnings for 2019-2020.

Shares in Origin rose 1 per cent to $7.94.

Origin's share of revenues at APLNG was $2.79 billion, up 36 per cent on a 28 per cent increase in export LNG prices and a 12 per cent rise in domestic gas prices. ConocoPhillips and China's Sinopec own stakes in APLNG.

The average realised price of LNG rose to $US10.12 per million British thermal units, although prices softened in the June quarter. Origin's average price for domestic gas increased to $5.04 per gigajoule for the full year.

APLNG's sales into the domestic market slipped 5 per cent to 73.2 petajoules.

As per the deal between Queensland LNG exporters and the federal government, APLNG offered any gas available beyond contracted deliveries to the local market.

Mr Calabria said APLNG "continues its strong operational and financial performance" and highlighted that the venture had signed several new sales contracts with domestic manufacturers. Those included one with Incitec Pivot that helped defer the closure of the Gibson Island fertiliser plant.

Origin said it is advancing preparations to drill two horizontal appraisal wells at its shale gas acreage in the Beetaloo Basin in the Northern Territory, where drilling programs were interrupted by the moratorium on fracking, which was lifted last year.

Origin paid a 10¢ a share dividend for the first half and has flagged a similar payout for the second half.

Adjusted net debt was about $6 billion at the end of December, which Mr Calabria said in February was approaching the target, and is set to fall further with completion of the $231 million sale of its Ironbark gas project to APLNG, expected in August. Mr Hamersley said he expected a staged increase in the dividend payout given "tough" conditions in energy markets and "still elevated" debt, but noted a 65 per cent payout ratio by 2020-21 would see Origin paying about 42¢ per share.
direction: Origin Energy shares likely to rise on higher dividend expectations

headline: Advertisers, are you paying attention?
text:
Advertisers take note: if you've been paying attention, you will realise that the attention economy is well and truly here. It will be the new currency of advertising impact, so it's time to understand it a little better.

In Cannes last month, I talked publicly for the first time about working on Dentsu Aegis Network's global Attention Economy initiative. The initiative will see our research outputs being applied to advertising planning and buying tools, based on an objective value of attention across platforms, channels and formats.

Backed by a cross-section of global broadcasters, social media and video-sharing platforms, the 15-month project collected and analysed data using our Intelligent Research System. For this type of research, our custom-built data collection app passively collects facial footage in a natural setting, collects viewing environment variables, intercepts ad load to replace with test ad, and redirects the participant to a virtual store to make their shopping choices.

If you have followed my work over the past couple of years, you will be pleased to hear that the usual suspects again stood up to be counted as factors in ad effectiveness - pixels, time in view, and coverage. And a new suspect emerged - sound. Although the prevalence of sound for online ads is so low, we need more evidence before making any bold claims on its contribution to effectiveness.

This project took a bends-inducing deep dive into the layers of human attention; deeper than we've gone before. Generally, I'm not happy unless we're challenging what we've done before. As a future-facing researcher, the idea of breaking new ground, rather than simple replication and extension, is why I get up in the morning. Well, that and the cat wants to be fed, every day!

Let's step back a bit. Psychologists define attention as information taking possession of the mind. Marketers have adopted the same definition; you just need to replace 'information' with 'ad content'. The idea is that a person executes significant focus leading to cognitive processing and eventual behavioural outcomes. Translating this theory to marketing speak, only when consumer attention is held, and an ad is actively watched, can it be valuable. Plenty of people who commercialise attention, whether collected via technology or even recall, reinforce this thinking.

That story, however, started in the 1960s with the 'Father of Advertising', David Ogilvy. It comes from a time when there were only three TV channels to consider, and holding attention and persuading people to buy was altogether more possible. A time when ads were so fresh, and brand innovations so new, that viewers would actually want to watch ads. Those days have well and truly gone.

Human attention to advertising is complicated and seemingly unique to every individual. This, we expect, is in part due to the nature of the individual's ability to process at the time of the ad. Something that can be heavily impacted by environmental context, as well as the highly individual nature of consumer behaviour. Have they had previous exposure to the brand, to the category, to advertising? All of these variables make a difference.

So, it's not surprising that in our data we have discovered that 'eyes nearby' offers more value than we first gave it credit for, in fact almost as much value as 'eyes on ad'. We found that the greatest uplift in a sales impact actually happens from eyes simply being near to the ad (the definition of 'near to' varies between TV screens and mobile screens), while 'eyes directly on ad' attention provided an incremental uplift only. Sure, this might be different for new brands in a new category, but the likelihood that we haven't seen or heard of a brand, let alone a new category, is low. This is what Robert Health talks about regarding his low attention processing work.

While many advertisers believe that 'eyes on ad' is the only thing that matters for processing purposes, it seems the most valuable gift is left on the table if the role of presence (even in passive viewing) is not recognised. There is more work to do here and we have a PhD student taking on this topic (contact us if you want to be involved).

But it seems for now, you actually don't need to bring a gift, just your presence is enough.

Karen Nelson-Field is a professor at University of Adelaide.
direction: bullish for Dentsu Aegis Network and digital advertising platforms

headline: Financial advisers get two years more to upgrade
text:
Financial advisers will be given up to an extra two years to upgrade their professional standards and qualifications under a concession announced by the Morrison government.

Existing financial planners will have until January 2022 to pass a financial advice exam and until January 2026 to graduate from a financial advice university or TAFE degree accredited by Financial Adviser Standards and Ethics Authority, Assistant Minister for Financial Services Jane Hume said.

Labor shadow minister for financial services Stephen Jones slammed the delay, arguing the reforms were being pushed back just months after the Hayne royal commission criticised a lack of professional standards as a major concern. "The government are being negligent and dragging their feet on urgent reform," Mr Jones said.

Financial advisers have been lobbying members of Parliament to water down the reforms.

The executive director of the Association of Independently Owned Financial Professionals, Peter Johnston, thanked Senator Hume for moderating former Liberal minister Kelly O'Dwyer's timetable, but he said the government should give further leeway to advisers specialising in life insurance and income protection. "It's a very good start, but we don't think it goes far enough," Mr Johnston said on Sunday. "Risk insurance advisers should not need a four-year degree. They should need a 12-month refresher diploma in risk insurance because they're only dealing in that area." The reforms emanated from the 2009 collapse of Storm Financial that cost consumers $3 billion and the subsequent Future of Financial Advice laws.

Senator Hume said the government was committed to restoring confidence in the financial system, including building trust in financial advice as a "true profession".

"While making these changes to raise education standards in the industry, we also need to balance the impact of these reforms against maintaining the ongoing availability, quality and affordability of advice," she said on Friday. "Therefore, the government intends to legislate to provide additional time for existing advisers to meet new qualification and examination requirements set by FASEA."

The scandal-plagued financial advice sector has come under severe financial and regulatory pressure due to FOFA, the banking royal commission, a government-pledged ban on grandfathered commissions and new qualification requirements.

Mr Johnston said 16 financial advisers had committed suicide.

The valuation price of financial advice practices had fallen from about 3-3.5 times gross revenue to about 1-1.5 times, which had affected the retirement plans of financial advisers who were in their 50s and would struggle to go back to study for four years, he said.

AMP will pay advisers a multiple of 2.5 times the earnings from their clients to buy their businesses, down from 4 times earnings under an earlier scheme. Independent financial advisers are planning to challenge in the High Court the government's move to ban up to $2 billion in annual commissions.

The government in February agreed to the Hayne royal commission recommendation to end grandfathering of conflicted remuneration from January 2021, arguing trailing commissions "can entrench clients in older products even when newer, better and more affordable products are available on the market". Planners have seized on section 51(31) of the constitution, which states that the Commonwealth has the power to make laws with respect to "the acquisition of property on just terms". They argue the federal government can take a person's property, such as varying payments derived from their contractual arrangements with product manufacturers, but they must be fairly compensated.
direction: bearish for financial advice sector

headline: ASX hit as economic warning bells ring
text:
The ringing of a number of economic warning bells pushed the Australian sharemarket lower on Wednesday, with concerns global growth was slowing faster than expected, leading investors to sell.

The first major sign the trade war was beginning to weigh on the US emerged on Tuesday, with the ISM US manufacturing purchasing managers' index entering contraction territory, falling below 50 to 49.1 points in August, the lowest since 2016.

"To see it tilt into contraction territory signals that the US might not be able to maintain its "exception" as a lone outlier of growth while the rest of the world is struggling through what amounts to a manufacturing recession," said JPMorgan Private Bank head of investment strategy for Asia, Alex Wolf.

Shares on Wall Street responded by selling off. The Dow Jones Industrial Average dropped 1.1 per cent to 26118, the Nasdaq Composite slid 1.1 per cent to 7874.2 and the S&P 500 fell 0.7 per cent to 2906.3.

"If the latest ISM number points to further weakness in the US, then it could mean the last remaining engine of trade and export growth could cease serving as an offset to a slowing China and slowing Europe," Mr Wolf said. "If the manufacturing and capex cycle slows further in the US, in line with the rest of world, then Asian growth will likely remain under some pressure."

Australian shares fell heavily at the open but rallied through the session, despite the economy recording the weakest annual GDP growth print since the global financial crisis. The S&P/ASX 200 Index fell 20.4 points, or 0.3 per cent, to 6553, recovering from its 1 per cent sell-off at the open.

The contraction in US manufacturing pushed commodity prices lower, with concern demand for oil and metal would be sapped.

BHP Group dragged the market with a 0.3 per cent loss to close at $36.32, South32 finished 0.7 per cent lower at $8.00, Woodside Petroleum lost 1.1 per cent to close at $31.19 and Origin Energy dropped 1.3 per cent to $7.38.

Australia's GDP growth for the second quarter printed in-line with expectations, doing little to move the market.

The annual growth rate for the quarter was the worst since September 2009.

"These are challenging times. The Australian economy is navigating a period of cyclical weakness, centred on construction, particularly housing after a tightening of lending standards," said Westpac senior economist Andrew Hanlan. "The global economy is slowing and downside risks have intensified as the global trade and technology war escalates, denting business confidence and business investment plans."

The major banks, which had already started the session weaker, were unchanged on the figure but rallied later in the session. Commonwealth Bank dipped 0.1 per cent to $78.69, Westpac traded 0.3 per cent lower to $28.25, ANZ declined 0.4 per cent to $26.58 and NAB fell 1¢ to $27.32.

CSR has dropped 6.6 per cent to $3.71 after it was downgraded by JPMorgan to underweight from neutral. The broker also cut its share price, citing a dip in residential construction.

A flight to safer havens benefited gold miners on Wednesday as gold prices rose 1.2 per cent to $US1547.11 ($2280) an ounce. Saracen Minerals Holdings firmed 2.1 per cent to $3.83, Northern Star Resources rose 3.1 per cent to $12.18, Evolution Mining advanced 3.1 per cent to $5.37 and Newcrest Mining added 2.5 per cent to finish at $37.96.

The economic storm clouds also pushed investors back into the bond market, with the yield on the Australian 10-year note falling for the first time in four sessions. While demand for bonds fell through the session, the slight fall in bond yields still buoyed real estate investment trust and infrastructure stocks slightly.

Dexus shares rose 0.8 per cent to $12.78, Goodman Group advanced 0.2 per cent to $14.21, Cromwell Property Group climbed 0.8 per cent to $1.25 and Sydney Airport firmed 0.1 per cent to $8.43. Metcash also firmed after it was upgraded to buy at Goldman Sachs. It closed 3.5 per cent higher at $2.93.
direction: bearish for the ASX and rate-sensitive sectors

headline: Inside the revived ANZ push in Asia
text:
Banking New life is being breathed into disrupted ambitions in the region, writes James Eyers.

Hong Kong | It's Thursday night, and two of ANZ Bank's group executives, Mark Whelan and Farhan Faruqui, are hosting a group of local business journalists to a banquet at the China Club, on the top floor of the Old Bank of China building in the city's Central district.

In the old-world room, with bookcases filled with dusty histories of China and walls adorned with Communist Party motifs, the ANZ bankers are upbeat about the future, despite a taxing decade where the bank has under-delivered in Asia, making investors sceptical about its ability to succeed here.

The message from institutional boss Whelan and international head Faruqui to the chroniclers of banking in the region: after three years shrinking the institutional division back to a leaner group of staff and more profitable clients, ANZ is ready to expand again. But it's a story that jars with the popular narrative of ANZ's Asian retreat.

Soon after taking over as CEO from Mike Smith, who had a penchant for Asia and dream of selling credit cards and deposit accounts to the Asian masses, Shayne Elliott made waves at the start of 2016 when he announced ANZ's exit from its subscale retail operations, including those purchased by Smith from Royal Bank of Scotland in 2009.

Five years after that deal, the banking world had been turned on its head: the cost of capital had soared after the global financial crisis, regulation was more onerous and margins had been crunched. Elliott also decided to jettison lending to small Asian businesses and exit ANZ's minority equity holdings in various Asian banks.

But amid all the stories of cutting and running, a key part of Elliott's strategy was mostly overlooked: ANZ was slimming down in order to refocus its efforts banking the big-end-of-town across the region.

The message Whelan and Faruqui delivered this week was also consistent with ANZ's original intention when it pushed into Asian institutional banking in 2007: it would operate as a connector of Australian and New Zealand companies seeking to expand into the region, and for regional and global companies wanting exposure to the antipodes.

Over two days in Hong Kong this week, AFR Weekend spent an hour with eight of ANZ's most senior bankers to understand the bank's ambitions across 15 countries in the bustling region. Two themes emerged: the bank has built an impressive dossier of customers it wants to be in business with; and it's confident the rise of China will continue to create opportunity, despite a growing number of doomsayers and the trade war with the United States.

ANZ is banker to Chinese e-commerce giants Alibaba, Tencent, Baidu and JD.com and also works with US tech giants including Amazon, Apple and Oracle. It has regional mandates from multinationals including Nestle, Unilever and Toyota. In India, it banks Tata and Reliance Industries. In South Korea, Samsung, LG and Hyundai are on its books. It counts all of China's major food groups, banks and oil companies as customers.

"If you think of who are the biggest multinationals in Asia, we pretty much deal with all of them," says Faruqui, who joined ANZ five years ago after a 23-year career at Citi.

ANZ's long-term presence in the region has allowed it to develop many of these relationships from the ground floor, especially the major Chinese technology companies. For example, ANZ was one of just five banks to form the first international loan syndicate for Alibaba almost 10 years ago.

"That was a time when they wanted us there, they needed us around the table, and we came through," says Faruqui. Ditto for Tencent, which he says ANZ lent to "well before most banks discovered who Tencent was".

A deep understanding of how the forces of technology are reshaping all industries is central to ANZ's offering and is helping coverage of other core sectors: resources and energy; food, beverages and agribusiness; and financial institutions.

"We were early entrants into the technology space in China in particular," Faruqui says. "We felt if you don't understand technology and what is happening in the technology firms, it is very hard to calibrate what is happening in the rest of the industries, because technology has become such an integral part of everything."

ANZ's institutional bank reported $1.7 billion of profit in 2017-18, just over one-quarter of the whole group's. Revenue was $4.2 billion. But Faruqui says returns are most important, and confirms these are improving and heading towards the low double digits, a vast improvement on three years ago.

"We want to make sure we can manage revenue and returns simultaneously," he says. "We don't want our capital to be invested in this region at a return that is going to be a huge drag for our investors in Australia, so we set ourselves a higher bar.

"We are growing quite impressively, and that's largely due to the focus that we've got. We are focused on the customers we have, and we think there is a lot of runway to penetrate their wallet."

He insists ANZ is gaining market share on its two biggest competitors, HSBC and Standard Chartered, after both tried to capitalise on ANZ's retail retreat, including by telling some of the media assembled in the China Club on Thursday night that ANZ was not committed to the region.

But over the past two years, Faruqui reckons ANZ has been growing at 1.5 times the system average for high-quality customers. If HSBC and Standard Chartered are growing at 3 per cent to 4 per cent, "we are definitely growing at higher than that", he adds. "I don't feel we are at a disadvantage in terms of competing with them."

So trade with Asia remains a distinguishing factor between ANZ and the other major Australian banks, which are shrinking to sameness. Faruqui says while ANZ's institutional banking market share for purely Australia companies is around the same as the other majors, if an Australian client is doing any business in Asia, ANZ's market share rises to 1.5 times the level of the next biggest Australian bank, and when banking international companies coming into Australia, ANZ's share is 2.8 times the next biggest.

Institutional investors recognise that connecting companies in the region is a competitive advantage for ANZ, but they remain sceptical about its ability to win against the bigger local players in commoditised products such as payments. ANZ understands it needs to rebuild trust with investors scarred by its poor performance in Asia over the years and must deliver a consistent strategy and better returns.

"I don't think investors will pay a premium for ANZ for what they are doing," says Investors Mutual portfolio manager Hugh Giddy. "But the strategy of targeting a smaller group of clients could be warranted, particularly if they have some competitive advantage, such as trade finance for Asian companies dealing with Australia and New Zealand. If they don't have a competitive advantage, I imagine their business would be lacking scale, and they would not be very successful with relatively commoditised products like transaction banking."

Other obstacles to its reinvigorated strategy could be the macroeconomic headwinds facing China, and the fallout from the trade war between China and the United States.

On both these issues, Faruqui is relatively sanguine. Recent China manufacturing PMI numbers point to a turnaround, and he is confident trade tensions will be resolved. If lower value production moves out of China to other south-east Asian markets, "we are also there, so we will capture it on that side if we don't capture it in China".

Another risk is deteriorating credit quality. At the end of last year, ANZ spent four months stress-testing its entire Asian portfolio right down to the individual company level to test for the impact of a deep recession and financial crisis. Faruqui says the results were pleasing. "A very large percentage passed with flying colours because of the quality of the portfolio we have. That gives us confidence."

The writer travelled to Hong Kong as a guest of ANZ.
direction: Bullish for ANZ shares

headline: BHP's carbon hard line
text:
BHP will push customers around the world to reduce their greenhouse gas emissions as part of a $US400 million ($569.8 million) spend on new technologies and strategies to reduce its carbon footprint.

Chief executive Andrew Mackenzie speaking in London last night said that BHP would set a public goal for its "scope 3" emissions, which include the greenhouse gases created by the steel mills and power stations that burn BHP's coal around the world.

This represents a landmark departure from the traditional attitude of Australian resource exporters to the emissions generated by their customers in other countries.

The shift came as Mr Mackenzie lauded governments in the UK and Europe for their climate policies, conspicuously omitting the Australian government and saying climate policy direction was needed from governments "now more than ever".

BHP's Australian iron ore and coking coal divisions make it the world's biggest supplier of steel-making ingredients, and the company's scope 3 emissions were almost 40 times larger than the 16.5 million tonnes of carbon emissions directly generated by its operations in fiscal 2018.

BHP's scope 3 emissions are further exacerbated when customers burn the oil and gas produced by BHP in Australia and the United States.

Warning the risks from continued reliance on fossil fuels "could be existential", Mr Mackenzie said BHP's customers should be able to enjoy its products with less impact on the environment.

"We must take a product stewardship role for all emissions across our value chain, and commit to work with the shippers, the processors and users of our products to reduce scope 3 emissions," he said in a speech in London.

"We will also set public goals to address scope 3 emissions."
