The vow comes barely a week after BHP challenged shipbuilders to make a "step change" towards lower pollution, saying it would favour providers that can deliver reduced carbon, sulphur and nitrous oxide emissions in a tender for the right to carry about 10 per cent of its iron ore exports to China.

The company has also invested in efforts to establish carbon capture and storage in the Chinese steel sector over the past three years. BHP will fund more of those projects in the next five years, vowing to spend $US400 million on a "climate investment program".

"This program will scale up low emissions technologies that decarbonise our operations. It will drive investment in nature-based solutions and encourage further collective action on scope 3 emissions," said Mr Mackenzie.

"We hope that greater commercial success of these investments will breed even greater ambition."

Mr Mackenzie's remuneration is already influenced by the greenhouse gas emissions directly produced by BHP's operations, dubbed scope 1 and scope 2 emissions, and he said the company would strengthen the link between emissions targets and executive pay.

It is believed the link will be strengthened by increasing the weighting of BHP's emissions performance when calculating pay starting in fiscal 2021.

The company would also set a new "medium term" target for its scope 1 and scope 2 emissions, adding to the existing target of ensuring such emissions remained at 2017 levels by 2022.

BHP is currently behind the pace required on that 2022 target, with emissions in fiscal 2018 rising 1 per cent above 2017 levels on the back of increased production at BHP's Chilean copper mines and the commissioning of a new desalination plant to supply those mines with water.

Mr Mackenzie said there was no silver bullet that would decarbonise the global economy. Instead, society should adopt an "all of the above" attitude.

"Renewables, nuclear, hydrogen, long-term storage of electricity, coal and gas with carbon capture and storage [CCS], negative emissions technologies like reforestation and biomass with CCS, and other approaches will all contribute to lower carbon outcomes," he said.

Mr Mackenzie said a carbon pricing scheme alone would not be enough to fight the "emerging crisis" of global warming.

"We cannot just back the horse with the best PR campaign," he said, urging governments to provide "technology-neutral regulation and leadership".

Writing on the same topic in London's Financial Times on Tuesday, Mr Mackenzie said those calling for widespread divestment of fossil fuel producers were misguided.

"I believe that those who simplistically call for divestment from all resource companies are fundamentally wrong. The mining industry must play a leading role in this," he said.

BHP's stance over scope 3 emissions separates it from rival miner Rio Tinto, which argued in May that it could not set targets for scope 3 emissions because the ultimate deployment of technologies to reduce such emissions "lies within the control of our customers, not Rio Tinto".

Despite taking that stance, Rio chairman Simon Thompson later indicated his company would work with customers on efforts to reduce emissions.

"There is no question we have to find more collaborative ways of working together looking at the whole supply chain, it isn't just the mining part, it's the shipping part and then of course it is the steel manufacturing and the end user of the steel," he said on May 9.

VicSuper was among the 6 per cent of Rio shareholders that voted in favour of an unsuccessful shareholder motion in May to compel Rio to set targets for its scope 3 emissions.

VicSuper is a member of the Australian Council of Superannuation Investors (ACSI), and ACSI chief executive Louise Davidson said Australian companies needed to do more.

"Although the number of companies reporting their climate risk exposure is increasing, a majority still only provide limited information. We expect companies to make substantive improvements in their management of climate risk and welcome efforts to transition to a low carbon world," she said.

The Australasian Centre for Corporate Responsibility has moved resolutions over climate policy at BHP and Rio's annual shareholder meetings in recent years, and spokesman Dan Gocher said setting a goal for scope 3 emissions was "a landmark shift from corporate Australia".
direction: bearish for BHP shares

headline: Contrarian Schroders sticks to guns
text:
Monday fundie Patience has its virtues investing in resources.

It's kick off for the August reporting season, and Schroders is expecting miners to produce robust earnings, after Rio Tinto last week posted its strongest revenue and profits since 2014.

Schroders' head of Australian equities Martin Conlon and deputy head of Australian equities Andrew Fleming were early buyers of mining stocks a few years ago - an experience they describe as "painful" - but their patience has been rewarded as commodity prices rise and serves as a reminder contrarian strategies can still deliver.

"At current iron ore prices, it would be astonishing if the results from the BHPs and the Rio Tintos aren't good," Conlon says. "They will be good. Most of the materials sector will produce reasonable results."

Rio Tinto kicked off the reporting season for the miners on August 1 by posting its best revenue and profit result since 2014 and a surprise $US1 billion special dividend. Iron ore prices have surged this year after a tailings dam tragedy in Brazil disrupted supply, sending the bulk commodity to more than $US120 a tonne in July.

The fund managers bought in when the iron ore price was moving down to $US30 a tonne and sector earnings were weakening. The companies they regarded as good long-term investments "were getting crushed", says Conlon. But that's "generally when we are increasing our positions in stocks". While he admits being too early a few years ago in building the position - "much to our pain" - Conlon aims to avoid short-term investment decisions. The fund holds stocks for eight years on average.

"We look at investing as a long-term exercise where the value of a company is about what it earns over a long period, not where returns are going in the next half," Conlon says. The Schroders Australian equities team manages $12.7 billion.

The large resources companies are also favourably positioned to survive the test of time, the fund managers believe. With BHP and Rio Tinto, "we think that they are as guaranteed as any company in the stockmarket to be around in the next 30 to 40 years," Conlon says.

"When we took a big position in resources a few years back that was very contrarian at the time," Fleming says. Commodity prices were trading below their average on a long-run basis, and valuations were sinking as investors factored in the prospect of weaker earnings.

Fast-forward to today and this year's share price performance for Rio and the broader sector is making the managers think about how much upside lies ahead.

"Obviously [the gains] make it tougher in the sense that the businesses are clearly more aggressively valued than they were a few years ago," Conlon notes. "Three or four years ago they were astoundingly good value. Now they are probably reasonable value," he says. But "no one is expecting $US120 a tonne iron ore to be a sustainable number".

Solid results from the mining sector are boosting overall market earnings at a time when, as Conlon says, "the free market parts of the economy are doing it pretty tough".

Banks, for example, are on track to produce little to marginally positive earnings growth this year, Fleming says. And "that's on record-low credit losses. It's very hard to see how growth accelerates from here with rates being so low. It's difficult to see how that stimulates a lot of demand.

"It's also very difficult to see how revenue growth can be anything more than low single-digit and if credit losses go up, then profits have to fall," Fleming says.

For the banks, the big question is whether they can cut costs sufficiently to support profits. "The banks have got big cost-cutting plans but they are plans, they haven't been realised. The big swing factor will be credit losses."

Operating risk charges are also likely to be a factor for the banking sector, he says. "If you look around the world what's been seen in Australia from misconduct is a fraction of what's been seen elsewhere," Fleming warns.

Conlon adds that the banks "are subject to long cycles".

"They have had a good long one but now we appear to have hit the limits there. It doesn't matter how cheap you make money, you still need borrowers." Still, central banks are hard at work making money as cheap as they can - with the Federal Reserve lowering the Fed funds rate by 25 basis points on Thursday, the first reduction since the global financial crisis. In Conlon's view, altering interest rates only serves to "push wealth around".

"Unfortunately we are trapped in an environment where central bankers are desperately trying to control the dials on the global economy in the belief that they can engineer a better outcome than the economy would on its own," he says. "The world's not growing much and we are clearly relying a lot on financialisation, which to us is pretty disconnected with the real economy. If you look at the past 20 to 30 years, it's been about interest rates."

Interest rate cuts, or the expectation of lower rates, increases the attractiveness of a range of assets - from shares to bonds to property - sending investors on a hunt for the best returns. "People have called it the bubble in everything," Conlon says.

While lower interest rates have pushed assets up indiscriminately, stocks appear to have been a key beneficiary of the hunt for yield.

"Investors in some ways are being dragged kicking and screaming into stocks. The income of every other asset has disappeared," Conlon says. "The stockmarket still has some income on an earnings yield - of 5 per cent if you're lucky."

Equity investors are facing a tough choice: taking on valuation risk or taking on earnings risk.

"At the moment, it's objective that we are paying the highest multiples we ever have on those companies that are perceived as growth. That means the future needs to be much rosier again for the returns to be good," Conlon says.

Earnings risk is real, warns Fleming. "Unfortunately that's one option. The other is valuation risk. Hardly any companies are sitting in the middle. If you don't have earnings risk - you are typically trading on the highest multiples you ever have."

The Schroders team produced a return of 10.4 per cent during 2018-19, outperforming the median long-only manager on a return of 8 per cent but underperforming the index, which produced a return of 11.9 per cent.

Assets rising broadly and in unison can present challenges for investors who prefer to make money by taking a contrarian view. Conlon calls it "as painful a time as nearly anyone can remember". The fund managers say they have seen some of their contrarian peers capitulate and head into stocks they normally would run from.

"You can see it happening in the market, a lot of people who are traditionally contrarian and value start justifying buying a few stocks that they wouldn't normally because it's very painful. So it's slow, and the pressure is there. The thumbscrews keep getting tightened," Conlon says.

But they are standing firm.
direction: bullish for Australian mining and resources sector

headline: Jobs worries increase as GDP disappoints
text:
A growing number of Australians are worried about losing their jobs following soft economic growth numbers, as a slide in consumer sentiment matches the loss in business confidence and conditions.

Consumer confidence has now fallen to its lowest level since September 2017, according to the latest Westpac Melbourne Institute.

The Unemployment Expectations Index recorded an 8.9 per cent jump, indicating more consumers expect unemployment to rise in the year ahead.

The index for the overall economic outlook for the next 12 months fell 6.9 per cent, while the index on housing sentiment sank to its lowest on record and the percentage of people who thought the best place for savings was in real estate slipped to just 9 per cent - the worst level since 1974.

Some economists are arguing the negative sentiment might be overdone, with last week's weaker economic growth figure of 2.3 per cent in the year to December weighing heavily on people's spending intentions.

Commonwealth Bank of Australia economist Gareth Aird asked, "Are we talking ourselves into a downturn?" and said that the Australian consumer needed a "circuit breaker".

NAB Business conditions and confidence survey for February released on Tuesday showed that profitability had dipped and trading conditions had softened but that measure of employment was still well above average.

"Indicators of labour demand will be important in assessing if the labour market is at a turning point," the NAB survey said. The NAB's survey measure of employment is still well above average and implies ongoing employment growth of 19,000 per month.

However, the latest Westpac MI Surveys suggests that although unemployment expectations are back near their long-run average - rather than to outright weak levels - the jump still points to a material deterioration from the strong labour market conditions that prevailed throughout 2018. Westpac senior economist Matthew Hassan said any optimism seen in February's consumer confidence numbers had been "short-lived" and that there still appeared to be uncertainty around consumer confidence.

"The main development over the last month was the December-quarter national accounts update that showed Australia's economic growth slowing to a 1 per cent annual pace over the second half of 2018, widely described as a 'per capita recession' in media coverage. The survey detail indicates that this had a significant negative impact on confidence."

ANZ economist David Plank allayed concerns yesterday by suggesting GDP numbers were not the best indicator for monetary policy and that the Reserve Bank of Australia would keep interest rates on hold this year.

"We expect the RBA to be on hold in 2019 and 2020. GDP has been a poor guide to RBA action. We see the unemployment rate tracking sideways. That, plus more stimulus in the budget, will keep the RBA on hold."

CommSec's Craig James said the situation on consumer sentiment was a "case of being alert and not alarmed".

But there few positives. More home buyers believe that now is a good time to look for a bargain and buy a home with the index that charts this response now at a 5-year high, up 30 per cent from the mid-2017 low. With house prices falling in Sydney by 10.4 per cent in the last year there have been concerns about the so-called wealth effect where falling house prices start to crimp spending.

The latest index, however, showed that consumer attitudes towards major household purchases had steadied with the "time to buy a major household item" sub-index down just 0.6 per cent.
direction: bearish for domestic-focused shares and property trusts

headline: Global interest in innovative solution
text: The risks, complexity and costs of cyber-attacks are growing exponentially, as the cybersecurity industry struggles to keep up with digital criminals intent on perpetrating data theft, business disruption, fraud and ransom on a global scale.Business is squeezed between tighter rules on individual privacy, on the one hand, and the rising cost of security solutions on the other.Businesses rely on network to manage and share data and every network is prone to cyber threat. Aswe adopt advanced technologies such as Cloud and IoT, cyber threats are also becoming sophisticated.To monitor and control such sophisticated threat we need to move from traditional security measures and apply advanced threat detection and monitoring techniques. AUnified threat management (UTM) solution uses intelligent defence, adaptive analytics and integrated controls to detect and demolish the attacks in real-time.The industry needs innovation and Australia’s only next-gen firewall company Red Piranha provides just that with its Crystal Eye product a Unified Threat Management (UTM) solution.Recently awarded the Consensus Innovation Award, following in the footsteps of Atlassian and WiseTech Global, Crystal Eye is leading the way with its list of features, with judges saying Red Piranha’s business model was comprehensive and well designed for their target market.Not only the world’s most powerful UTM, Crystal Eye also features the industry’s first inbuilt gateway application whitelisting as well as email scanning, data loss prevention and several other innovative features, that collectively provide exceptional security orchestration, automation and response. All of this on one platform with only one set of policies and logs to adjust, a critical factor in reducing the response time to threats, responding to incidents, and creating consistent security policies that are easy to apply across the network.Industry analyst Gartner predicts worldwide spending on information security products and serviceswill hit $US124 million in 2019. The cost of so-called ‘‘mega breaches,’’ can run as high as $US350 million with the average price of a data breach being $US3.6 million, according to a joint report from IBM and the Ponemon Institute.The Office of the Australian Information Commissioner said 215 breaches of privacy were reported under the Notifiable Data Breaches Scheme in the March quarter 2019. Of these, 61 per cent were due to malicious or criminal attacks, 35 per cent to human error, and 4 per cent to system errors. In the year to March, breaches totalled just under 1000.But cybersecurity expert Adam Bennett, CEO of Red Piranha, says anecdotal evidence suggests the actual number of breaches is probably far higher.‘‘We believe it only captures a fraction of what’s happening,’’ he says.Small and medium enterprises are particularly at risk because few cybersecurity products have been developed to meet their needs at an affordable price, says Bennett.‘‘What these enterprises need is a cost-effective way of deliveringmultiple controls that address business risk, and real business issues around cyber-security as well as automating compliance and governance.’’ The consequences of having data compromised have grown enormously. Twenty years ago, if an enterprise was hacked, their home page might have been defaced. Now entire business systems are being affected, operations are having to shut down, and vital data is being lost.At the same time, computer networks are growing faster than we can keep up with, and this increases the available attack surface, Bennett says.‘‘The threat landscape increases along with it, and it’s almost impossible for IT teams to defend against it.’’ Building a cybersecurity system is often compared to making a house secure, starting with doors, windows, locks and a fence and progressing to bars, alarms and CCTV.‘‘In information security, we call these things ‘controls’,’’ says Bennett.‘‘Often you can’t see them or touch them – they’re technical or policy responses, and it’s the layering up of these controls which allows you to get overall security.’’ Security is no longer amatter of protection, but also one of detection and response. For example, if a company puts up a brand-new firewall and no one is watching it a hacker could breach the firewall, but no one would be the wiser.‘‘We address that ‘defence in depth’ problem by deployment in the infrastructure where we put what’s called aUnified Threat Management platform, to cover these areas of protection as well as detection and response,’’ Bennett says.‘‘TheUTMapproach gives you a single pane of glass and allows you to reduce management burden and integration points ... [as opposed to] buying from multiple vendors, where the technology might not integrate well.’’ Bennett says the company aims to ‘‘bring toplevel, defence-grade cybersecurity to everyone’’.Red Piranha has established partnerships with Managed Service Providers so they can provide its products to SMEs and enterprises as part of the bundle of IT services.The company with its global partners in the Open Information and Security Foundation, one of themost significant international threatsharing consortiums, together process more than 14 million ‘‘indicators of compromise’’ a day.‘‘If one of our partners’ clients gets hacked in France, within hours our clients are protected against it,’’ he says.‘‘That threat data is processed and fed back in and used to update our platform up to four times a day, so our clients are protected up to the minute against the evolving threats.’’ It’s not one thing that gives you security; it’s all of them collectively that provide peace of mind.
direction: bullish for Red Piranha shares

headline: Citadel under siege as shares take hammering
text:
The Citadel Group is a company under siege. Just two weeks ago, it was worth $330 million but now, its market cap is barely more than $200 million.

Rising 300 per cent during its first four years of trading, the company could have been considered a burgeoning tech darling and was touted as a takeover target last year.

But a 40 per cent sell-off just over a week ago on the back of 46 per cent reduction in its projected full-year gross profit margin has left many investors and analysts nervous.

The downgrade only compounded woes from earlier in the year, when a disappointing half-year result in February saw the company fall more than 20 per cent over a week.

The 5.5 per cent increase in revenue and 5.4 per cent increase in net profit after tax for the half were both well below expectations but at the time, chief executive Daniel Stanley said he was confident of the outlook for the remainder of the fiscal year.

He may not be so confident now.

Citadel provides integration and managed service solutions, protecting information and data as well as providing content management systems to a range of clients.

The company derives most of its revenue from long-term managed services contracts and high-quality strategic advisory services.

A large amount of that comes from its contracts with state and federal government departments, which have been spending sparingly over the past few months.

Adding to the reduced government spending, the group is in the midst of a revenue model shift, pivoting to becoming a software and services company and that rotation is coming at a cost.

But for all its troubles, the company still has its fans willing to grit their teeth in the short term with a long-term reward in mind. Bell Potter analyst Chris Savage is still confident the company can enjoy strong growth in the next 12 months despite cutting his valuation on the company.

While he reduced his share price expectation on Citadel from $9.50 to $7, his forecast still implies growth of more than 60 per cent in the next year.

"In short, the update is disappointing but we do not believe the company is broken and will recover," he noted.

He also suggested some of the first-half losses could have been driven by a "general lack of decision making in federal government departments" ahead of the federal election.

"In theory, decisions will start being made again after the election and project extensions, for instance, will resume again," he said.

RBC Capital Markets analyst Gary Sherriff also retained his outperform recommendation on the stock but reduced his price target heavily. He suggested margins would remain flat as the business transitioned, but acknowledged some upside from the election result.

"Perhaps the only positive for Citadel near term is the Coalition being more supportive towards Defence and Home Affairs spend, relative to Labor," he said. "We remain cautious near term but can see a pathway to 2019-20 growth."

Other analysts are less confident, saying the most recent result showed how vulnerable the business was to any form of downturn.

Wilsons analyst Shane Storey said the pre-election dormancy in Canberra was only "partly to blame" and suggested the business's results were too exposed to downside risks. "The update revealed how dependent results are on the variable upside within projects and how exposed the fourth quarter is to that high-margin element," he noted.

Evans & Partners also reduced its recommendation on the stock to "neutral", saying the company had provided very little information for such a significant change in its outlook.

"Citadel's market release was very light on detail and colour, so we don't have significant new incremental information to add," said analyst Paul Mason.

"Given the magnitude of the negative earnings trajectory being reported, a lack of conference call or detail on the split between deferrals and reduced spend generally, we don't have a basis to maintain a positive view."

Citadel's share price has firmed in the week since its sell-off, rising a little over 5 per cent. The ASX has given tech stocks a rough ride in the past week, with the information technology sector the worst performer on the market. On Tuesday, Technology One demonstrated just how stretched valuations on the local tech sector were, falling heavily despite a strong result.

The sector is attracting interest from overseas investors who are keen to chase the growth the burgeoning ASX technology names provide but that buying is pushing valuations to stretching point. Citadel is an example of how impatient investors can be when short-term results don't pass muster.

The company takes its name from the most fortified tower within a medieval castle, the last line of defence where people retreated if a city or castle was ever breached. It sounds like a good place for the company to be right now as the market bears begin to enter the fray.
direction: bearish for Citadel Group shares

headline: Advisers question Coles governance
text:
Coles has defended chairman James Graham after proxy advisers questioned his independence and said the retailer's board structure failed to meet governance guidelines.

Coles considers Mr Graham, who is up for re-election at the annual meeting next week, to be an independent director.

However, proxy advisers at CGI Glass Lewis said the veteran investment banker was "affiliated" because he was chairman and founder of Gresham Partners. Gresham is 50 per cent owned by Wesfarmers and was financial adviser to the group on the demerger of Coles last November. Gresham has also provided advisory services to Coles.

"We have classified (non-executive director) Graham as affiliated as we hold the threshold for an independent classification high," CGI Glass Lewis said in a report to investors before Coles' annual meeting, its first since the demerger.

"Without an independent chairman, but with a majority of independent directors, the board structure does not meet ACSI [Australian Council of Superannuation Investors] guidelines," the report said.

However, Coles said that although Mr Graham was chairman of Gresham, he was not involved in providing advice to Wesfarmers on the demerger and the services had not continued since the demerger. Coles has no ownership interest in Gresham.

"The board has concluded that Mr Graham's relationship with Gresham Partners does not interfere with or compromise his ability to exercise objective or independent judgment in relation to Coles," a Coles spokesman said.

"The board further considers that Mr Graham's extensive experience as a director enables him to bring a deep understanding of Coles' operations and the industry and Mr Graham's knowledge is invaluable to the board," he said.

CGI Glass Lewis refrained from recommending that shareholders vote against Mr Graham or any of the affiliated directors based on independence concerns "at this time", and said it would continue to monitor the issue.

The report is likely to reignite shareholder concerns raised at the time of Mr Graham's appointment by Wesfarmers last year.

"It's vital to Coles' future success they have a truly independent chairman to ensure capital decisions are in the best interest of all shareholders, not just Wesfarmers, and someone with no previous connection would have been preferable," DNR Capital fund manager Chris Tynan said at the time.

Wesfarmers chief executive Rob Scott was forced to defend the appointment, saying Mr Graham knew Coles well and would give shareholders comfort "given his experience and awareness of the Coles business".

Wesfarmers retained a 15 per cent stake in Coles after the demerger and appointed David Cheesewright as its representative on the Coles board.

CGI Glass Lewis also questioned millions of dollars in "transition" awards paid to Coles chief executive Steven Cain, chief financial officer Leah Weckert and chief operating officer Greg Davis, saying it was sceptical of significant awards that were not aligned with company performance.

Coles agreed to pay Mr Cain $3.9 million in cash ($1.5 million is due at the end of December) to compensate him for incentives forfeited when he left Metcash last year.

It also paid Ms Weckert $709,560 and Mr Davis $630,720 in incentives to support the company's operational and strategic goals during and after the demerger.

CGI Glass Lewis said the awards might have been warranted to expedite Mr Cain's appointment and retain Ms Weckert and Mr Davis, but it would have preferred to see them paid in equity.
direction: downward pressure on Coles shares due to governance concerns

headline: Indonesia posts surprise trade surplus on eve of election
text:
Jakarta | Indonesia posted a surprise trade surplus for a second straight month in March, supporting the central bank's outlook of a narrowing current account deficit, but economists warn a trade deficit could return after Wednesday's presidential elections.

Indonesia had a surplus of $US540 million ($753 million) in March, the largest since June 2018 and compared with a forecast of $US180 million deficit in a Reuters poll. This followed February's surprise $US330 million surplus.

Last year's record $US8.5 billion trade deficit has been used by opposition candidate Prabowo Subianto to attack President Joko Widodo, saying the incumbent has managed the economy badly. Mr Joko said efforts by his administration to reverse the deficit, including raising tariffs to halt imports and relaxing rules to support exports, have been successful in narrowing the trade gap in the first quarter.

Indonesians will vote on Wednesday to decide who gets to lead south-east Asia's largest economy for the next five years.

A risk of trade deficit returning after this week's elections is "highly likely", said Bhima Yudhistira Adhinegara, an economist with the Jakarta-based think tank Institute for Development of Economics and Finance (INDEF).

He argues that imports of building materials used in Mr Joko's infrastructure push, which have been paused since last year, could resume after the elections.

"If the candidate number 2 wins, he has pledged to cut staple prices within his first 100 days, so he would likely have to import food too," Mr Adhinegara said, referring to Mr Prabowo.

Wisnu Wardana, an economist with Bank Danamon, said investment-related imports appeared to have been halted in the first quarter and that the sustainability of trade surplus would depend on whether investment picks up post elections.

Imports fell 6.76 per cent from a year earlier to $US13.49 billion in March, marking the third consecutive month of contraction, led by declining shipments of raw materials. The poll had forecast a 3.76 per cent decline.

Meanwhile, exports dropped sharply, at a 10.01 per cent on-year rate, to $US14.03 billion, although not as much as the 11.82 per cent fall expected in the poll.

Analysts say an improvement in trade and current account deficits is a determining factor for Bank Indonesia to start loosening monetary policy, now that inflation is stable and the US Federal Reserve will probably not raise rates further this year.

BI last year increased rates by a total of 175 basis points in response to a weak rupiah and capital outflows that were partly prompted by rising US interest rates and large trade and current account deficits.

The latest data suggested 2019's current account gap would narrow to 2.78 per cent of GDP from 2.98 per cent last year, Bank Mandiri's economist Andry Asmoro said.

BI may also wait and see what kind of economic changes the elections bring before cutting interest rates, said INDEF's Mr Adhinegara. The central bank's next policy meeting is scheduled for the end of next week.
direction: bearish for Indonesian equities and currency due to post-election trade deficit risks

headline: Rear Window
text:
Rio Tinto's JS Jacques buys second Sydney home

Permit us a footnote to the ongoing institutional weirdness of Rio Tinto.

In yesterday's column we juxtaposed the curious inconsistency of the board's continuing indulgence of its chief executive Jean-Sebastian Jacques and its ongoing stand-off with Jacques' predecessor, Sam Walsh. The latter received a benign email in 2011, hasn't been accused of any wrongdoing but is being denied payment of $29.6 million of earned remuneration. The former told a meeting room full of staff to "fit in or f--- off" in 2016 but trousered £4.289 million ($7.77 million) last year, including 70 per cent of his maximum short-term bonus - for financial performance almost entirely the work of Walsh and his finance director Chris Lynch.

Another uninstructive juxtaposition is Rio's 2016 sacking of its chief counsel over the payment of a consultant notable for his closeness to the Guinean President with this week's appointment of a new chief counsel whose current employer is in the headlines over the payment of a consultant (Michael Cohen) notable for his closeness to the US President. Go figure.

As is Rio's brag that it's "the only major mining company with a fossil-fuel-free portfolio [and so] well-positioned to contribute to a low-carbon future" while its continuing operations cause more carbon emissions than any other Australian miner. That's from Rio's own filings with the Commonwealth's Clean Energy Regulator, taking into account both its direct pollution (Scope 1) and that of its required electricity (Scope 2). This reporting regime excludes Scope 3 emissions, happily the only environmental output Rio has ever disavowed their responsibility for.

Then there's Rio's pledge last month of "greater transparency and accountability [to] build trust and credibility" while Jacques runs a $151.3 billion, London-headquartered public company from Sydney and the company refuses to disclose that very fact. And now Jacques and his wife Muriel Demarcus are really putting their roots down nowhere near Rio's businesses, customers or major investors.

Following the couple's $3 million acquisition of their Walsh Bay residence on Sydney Harbour in October 2017 (proving their immigration status, given Australia's property investment restrictions on foreign non-residents), and as first noted by Jonathan Chancellor's Property Observer this week, Demarcus has just paid another $3 million for a "weekender" on Sydney's northern beaches. The Avalon Beach "bush cottage" has its own deepwater jetty, pontoon and working boathouse.

It would be a very long voyage - by sea or otherwise - to the closest Rio Tinto stakeholder. Riverview Road, Avalon, is 3685 kilometres from Rio's operations centre in Perth, 4052 kilometre from its Yandicoogina mine in the Pilbara, 8385 kilometres from the iron ore terminal at Qingdao port, 8934 kilometres from Sinosteel's head office in Beijing and 16,966 kilometres from BlackRock's portfolio managers in London's Drapers Gardens. Yet three kilometres farther still, on St James' Square, Rio's non-executive directors assemble to deliberate gravely … on Walsh's conduct! That and a little greenwashing. Dearie, dearie me...

Embattled fund manager Neil Woodford keeps an eye on ... something

It's the biggest financial scandal going down in the UK. And unfortunately for the investors caught in the middle, it's a long way from over.

Those who've trusted their money to hot-shot stock picker Neil Woodford won't get it back anytime soon, the embattled British fund manager (with the oversight of his trustees at the ASX-listed Link Group) having flagged his intentions to keep gating redemptions at his investment empire.

The announcement was made overnight on Tuesday through Woodford's preferred forum: a YouTube video. Watching it, we couldn't help getting distracted by what could be glimpsed over Woodford's shoulder.

There are three computer screens. A Bloomberg interface usually sits on two, while an (unfortunately unreadable) Excel spreadsheet sits on the third. But one of the screens with Bloomberg sometimes shifted to display scenes from what appears to be a security camera feed.

In one shoot, a lime-green couch can be glimpsed. In another, a man sitting at a desk, his chair draped in a high-vis vest.

What do you reckon Woodford's surveilling? His own employees? His luxury personal equestrian centre? Or maybe it's a feed to his trustees at Link, currently being investigated by Britain's Financial Conduct Authority for having allowed Woodford to get around liquidity requirements by listing some of his illiquid assets at the notoriously low-regulation stockmarket in Guernsey.

In one of the shots sits a mug with the word "Crikey" on it. That mug, in case you wondered, is for sale at coolthings.com.au. "A great gift for an office colleague who's prone to the odd exclamation of bewilderment," reads the blurb. All things considered this seems entirely appropriate.

Blockbuster fortnight at Trans Tasman Business Circle

The normally somewhat dull Trans Tasman Business circle - a lunch club for Kiwi-Australians and those with business interests across the ditch - suddenly got sexy.

Westpac's equity-lite CEO Brian Hartzer is due there at the end of July to talk about "working for growth". Which could be hard over the ditch. What's growing fastest there are the calls for a royal commission. Central to the discontent with the banking sector was the light punishment given to ex-ANZ country chief David Hisco, who departed last month over what ANZ believes are nine years of expense "mischaracterisation" (Hisco disagrees with ANZ's assessment.) His exit was initially put down to "extended sick leave", and while he forfeited stock, he did also leave with a year's pay. ANZ is New Zealand's largest bank and is ultimately overseen by another of the country's favourite sons in Shayne Elliott.

And if that isn't enough to excite a room full of Kiwis, there's the Bledisloe Cup Women's Luncheon a week later in Perth, starring, would you believe it, another Kiwi in Rugby Australia chief executive Raelene Castle. New Zealand has only won the thing 47 times, but hope springs eternal. As eternal as the hope that no one will mention Israel Folau and whether gays are going to hell.

And a week after that, it'll be former NSW premier Nick Greiner's turn. He's giving an evening address titled "Stories I Can Now Share". Sounds intriguing.

If Australia's corporate lunch types start sounding Kiwi by mid-August, that'll be why.
direction: bearish for Rio Tinto shares

headline: Rear Window
text:
Peter Costello leads Mitch Fifield send-off

Profound mediocrity has its rewards. Former communications minister Mitch Fifield will take his ease on New York's East River in a fortnight, having resigned from the Senate on August 16 and his replacement, former Corangamite MP Sarah Henderson, awaiting only the Victorian Parliament's ratification of her preselection to take his place on the red leather. Profound mediocrity has...

Having plotted against prime minister Tony Abbott in 2015 and vaulting into cabinet, Fifield then doublecrossed Malcolm Turnbull last August, lining up behind Peter Dutton. Little wonder Scott Morrison induced him outta there. The man's estrangement from loyalty aside, 15 years as a complete ministerial passenger secured his absence from the Liberal pantheon.

On Friday, Fifield gathered those who still remember him for lunch at Melbourne institution, Bamboo House. It was also a chance for Future Fund and Nine chairman Peter Costello to gaze upon his protégés: with Fifield, Speaker Tony Smith, state opposition leader Michael O'Brien and GRACosway managing director Richard King, all alumni of the Treasurer's office. A motley legacy - like with Costello's 11 budgets, a couple didn't quite sing. Former Liberal state director (and now Asahi executive) Julian Sheezel rounded out the table.

Fifield's residence on Beekman Place adjoins that of Australia's Consul General, Alastair Walton, whose term expires in July. A Goldman Sachs vulgarian appointed senselessly by Turnbull, ScoMo is no fan. Tick tock...

These people. And in New York! It bears contemplation by any prime minister's confidant (or irritant): imagine where you might get sent if you were half good.

Justice Mark Dreyfus and the Isaacs by-election

Speculation is now positively feverish that Labor's shadow attorney-general Mark Dreyfus will announce his departure from federal parliament within weeks, causing a byelection in his Melbourne seat of Isaacs.

Though Victoria's Court of Appeal president Chris Maxwell won't reach his mandatory retirement age of 70 until January 2023, he's said to be ready for early retirement. Dreyfus recently met Premier Dan Andrews, presumably to discuss the role - certainly not to talk level crossings in Edithvale, given the local member actually lives in Malvern.

Such an appointment would undermine the judiciary's independence and damage the court, but wouldn't its loss be our gain?! As a young QC in 2005, Dreyfus sought the same gig but was overlooked for Maxwell by Steve Bracks' attorney-general Rob Hulls. That left the pompous, unexceptional silk to pursue jurisprudential glory via Canberra, deposing sitting MP Ann Corcoran in 2007 before spending an entire seven months in 2013 as the nation's first law officer. He now serves on the opposition's House tactics team as its unwitting devil's advocate.

Before Bill Shorten's shock defeat at May's federal poll, Dreyfus was set to return as Commonwealth attorney-general, then join the High Court on the occasion of a vacancy (likely the retirement of Justice Geoffrey Nettle in December 2020), despite his ceaseless bellyaching about the Administrative Appeals Tribunal being "full of Liberal mates" and his unironic pledge to "restore a transparent merits-based policy to judicial appointments". Dreyfus' pungent ambition is only casually tethered to his sufferability; indeed, the two may be inversely proportionate.

Dreyfus also threatened to resign in 2016 if Kimberley Kitching was selected to fill Stephen Conroy's vacancy in the Senate. She was, but cruelly, he didn't.

Dreyfus wants the ALP's unsuccessful Higgins candidate (and another barrister) Fiona McLeod to replace him in Isaacs, presumably so its parliamentary representative can continue admiring it from afar. Suffice it to say the Right will have something to say about that.

With a 6.4 per cent margin, and a profile too suburban for the Greens, the by-election promises to be uneventful, even though the government will control its timing.

Xavier College Class of '84 draws a crowd

On Saturday night in Melbourne, a motley collection of former schoolboys toasted 35 years since their graduation. Of course, this being a reunion of Melbourne's pricey Xavier College, it was no ordinary assembly.

The rollcall at Carlton's Clyde Hotel included leading lights of the law, such Victorian Court of Appeal Judge Richard Naill, top criminal barrister Dermot Dann QC, and Daniel Crennan QC, formerly of the Victorian Bar but now ensconced as ASIC's "chief prosecutor". There were your high-profile journalists, included The Australian's national affairs editor Simon Benson, and the same newspaper's industrial relations reporter Ewin Hannan. And your political types, including Andrews government minister Luke Donnellan and the Institute of Public Affairs' John Roskam. Everyone, we were assured, got along very well.

Alas, that year's most recognisable Old Xaverian gave it a miss. Hopefully Bill Shorten can make the 40th.

ISignThis founder is used to scrutiny

The ASX and ASIC may have indefinitely suspended it from trading. But the ISignThis CEO and founder isn't worried.

"The company welcomes any opportunity to address regulator queries, which are a normal part of operating in regulated markets and industries, and is fully co-operating with regulators," John Karantzis said last week, rather implausibly, about the highly unusual forced and immediate suspension brought about by two agencies at once.

But then again, Karantzis has had plenty of success holding his own. The very formation of ISignThis was mired in legal contestation, which Karantzis came out of very well back in 2015.

At the time, Karantzis, through his company Southern Ocean, was a consultant to sugar refinery Sugar Australia, when his services were abruptly terminated by a new CEO, who wanted to bring things in-house. Later, looking into it, Sugar Australia refused to give Karantzis any of his owed termination payments, alleging a coterie of sins, the most pertinent to our interests being that Karantzis' concurrent work on ISignThis meant he couldn't be fulfilling his contractual obligations to work for Sugar Australia.

Neither this nor much of Sugar Australia's other allegations convinced Victorian County Court Judge Maree Kennedy, who threw most of Sugar Australia's case out and awarded Karantzis total damages of $265,000 (minus a phone bill of $164,160 he had to pay back).

We don't imagine Karantzis is too disappointed in retrospect about losing his $302,208 a year gig with Sugar Australia. Even after all the recent scrutiny of ISignThis, including an Ownership Matters report last month questioning the company's finances and ownership, the company is worth $1.1 billion.
direction: bearish for political stability and related corporate governance perceptions

headline: Campbell Soup Co re-tests Arnott's bids; KKR not done yet
text:
The fat lady is finally warming up her vocal cords at US consumer goods giant Campbell Soup Company.

Two months after Campbell Soup took final bids for its international arm, which includes Arnott's biscuits and Campbell's soup sold in Australia, the company and its advisers are understood to be working with two potential acquirers.

Street Talk understands Campbell's advisers, Goldman Sachs and Evercore, have asked trade player Mondelez International and buyout giant KKR to reconfirm bids this week. Neither has been able to hit the $3 billion-plus asking price and terms in the past few attempts - and may well not again - but it is understood Campbell Soup is hopeful that at least one of the bidders can sufficiently improve its existing offer to provide a pathway to an agreed deal.

The most interesting thing is that KKR - the $US150 billion asset manager - is back in the auction, after it looked like it was very much a one-horse race for Mondelez International.

Lending sources told this column KKR had been back reviewing elements of its offer, including the financing structure, to see whether it may be able to give some ground and win Campbell's signature. Lenders reckon there is some new-found confidence in the KKR camp.

Of course the door has been left ajar by Mondelez, which has had its team working around the clock to deal with potential antitrust issues associated with its offer. It is understood Mondelez's team is confident they have come up with a proposal that would be attractive to Campbell, thought to include some divestments in Australia, although it remains to be seen whether it will be enough.

If Mondelez were to swallow the Campbell International portfolio in full, it would emerge with about 70 per cent of the Australian biscuit market. Arnott's has about 61 per cent of the biscuit market, with brands such as Tim Tams, Shapes, Iced Vovo's and Tiny Teddy. Mondelez has about 8 per cent, mainly through the Oreo, Ritz and Captains Table brands, many of which are former Nabisco brands, which was formerly owned by KKR.

One concession may be to sell enough assets to reduce its market share to about 50 per cent and create a strong No.2 player with about 20 per cent share, to appease ACCC concerns. Campbell is likely to want Mondelez to carry the risk associated with such a proposal.

Australia is the biggest part of the Campbell International portfolio.

The unit recorded $US1.05 billion ($1.43 billion) revenue and $US202 million in earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation in the 2018 financial year from the sale of snacks, soup, stock and juices in Australia, New Zealand, Asia Pacific and EMEA, according to confidential financial information given to prospective acquirers earlier in the process.

It comes after Campbell Soup Company took bids for the international portfolio on March 20.
direction: upward pressure on Campbell Soup shares due to renewed competitive bidding and potential deal premium

headline: Industry insurance fund paid out $44m to union
text:
Union income insurance fund Protect has paid out more than $44 million in profit share to the Electrical Trades Union Victoria over two years, raising questions over the fund's transparency.

Protect, founded by the ETU Victoria and the National Electrical Contractors Association, revealed it distributed $60 million in "surplus funds" to its two founders for the first time in its submission to a Senate inquiry into the Morrison government's bill to regulate worker benefit funds.

The payments to the ETU Victoria, covering 2017 and 2018, are more than double the $16.9 million that was previously reported based on the union's 2017 financial reports and raises questions over the fund's transparency.

According to Protect's submission, it distributed $12.8 million to the ETU in 2016-17 and an additional $31.5 million following a "very strong investment year" in 2017-18.

NECA received $14.8 million over those same years, made up of $4.3 million in 2017 and $10.5 million in 2018.

The Protect board decided to distribute the amounts in response to the government's original regulation bill introduced in 2017, which unions argue will prevent them using surplus funds for training or benefits such as ambulance or funeral cover.

"With the introduction of the Bill and faced with the threat of eliminating the ability of a trust to distribute surpluses to its sponsors/beneficiaries, extraordinary distributions of current and prior year income were made as have been disclosed in the accounts of the ETU and NECA," Protect's submission said.

The amounts were far in excess of surplus distributions in previous years, which have averaged $630,000 for the ETU and $210,000 to NECA over the past two decades.

Protect's 2017-18 annual report made no mention of the amounts and the fund has previously refused requests to identify the total amount distributed to its sponsors.

The ETU Victoria has also not identified the amount distributed, despite issuing frequent statements on the matter, while NECA has refused to respond to any questions about its share.

Industrial Relations Minister Christian Porter said the latest revelation was "just astounding".

"Where is the accounting of expenditure of the $60 million to explain to members what their hard earned money is actually being spent on?" he said.

"It highlights, yet again, the absolute lack of accounting regulation and transparency to funds which hold workers' money and reinforces the need for the legislation, currently before Parliament, to be passed into law."

The ETU Victoria was not available for comment but it has previously said it puts its share into a special purpose trust used for ambulance cover, funeral benefits, domestic violence and training.

The money cannot be spent on industrial or political matters and the union has signed a facility arrangement with Protect to guarantee any shortfalls at the fund. Protect's submission said "to our knowledge" NECA directs its share of the surplus funds to its Victorian Education Fund, which invests in training.
direction: negative for insurance sector reputation and regulatory outlook

headline: Bank chairman sets stage and standards for future
text:
Phil Chronican hasn't wasted his opportunity.

As the chairman-elect of NAB and its acting chief executive, Chronican straddles the two most powerful roles in the bank in a way unlikely to be repeated any time soon.

He's used this power both to set a platform for his incoming chief executive Ross McEwan and, by whacking executive salaries, to show investors the level of accountability they should expect under his chairmanship.

The biggest example of Chronican's deck clearing is National Australia Bank's final dividend, which fell 16 per cent to 83¢, as full-year cash profit in 2019 fell 10.6 per cent to $5.1 billion.

Having cut the interim dividend by the identical amount, NAB's decision to cut again isn't exactly a surprise.

But it is a reflection of a challenging environment - surely the choice phrase of the bank annual profit reporting season - marked by falling interest rates (NAB has $88 billion in deposits of savings and transactional deposits at or near interest-rate floors) and falling returns. NAB saw a 7.7 per cent fall in consumer banking profits and a 2.4 per cent in profits from its key business and private banking division.

Chronican told this column yesterday the dividend policy had been set with an eye to the future.

NAB wants to be in a position where the dividend is at a level where the bank can generate surplus capital alongside paying strong returns; this hasn't happened in this half, but Chronican says if there were no one-off items in the first half of the 2020 year, the benefits of the policy should start flowing through to NAB's capital position.

Chronican has also ensured McEwan can hit the ground running by pushing ahead with the three-year, $1.5 billion plan developed by departed chief executive Andrew Thorburn to cut costs and simplify the bank. This is showing promising signs.

Spending on technology has helped reduce the number of IT applications by 11 per cent, with 19 per cent of applications shifted to lower-cost cloud environments. Revenue per business banker is up 20 per cent. Over-the-counter branch transactions are down 30 per cent, and call centre volumes are down 17 per cent.

Expenses rose 0.4 per cent during the 2019 financial year, a figure Chronican will quite reasonably claim meets the "broadly flat" forecast the bank had given. Given Commonwealth Bank saw expenses rise 2.6 per cent in its 2019 financial year, and Westpac's grew 3 per cent, this is a reasonable outcome; ANZ was the winner in the cost-cutting category last year, with expenses down 3.2 per cent.

NAB says $480 million of cost cuts in 2019 helped to absorb soaring compliance and customer remediation costs. The bank has a staggering 950 people in its customer remediation team.

"The last thing I wanted was for the organisation to lose momentum, so I've been keen that we continue to drive the transformation agenda," Chronican says. "Also it was important that we dealt as far as possible with the issues of the past."

And despite the "challenging" conditions, there is a little light at the end of the revenue tunnel too for NAB.

Although cash earnings were down in three of the four divisions - business and private, consumer and wealth, and corporate and intuitional banking, with New Zealand banking seeing cash earnings rise 5.1 per cent - there was underlying profit growth everywhere except consumer and banking.

Chronican is clear that consumer banking isn't suddenly going to get easier. He points to the experience in the United States and Europe, where plunging interest rates have weighed heavily on retail banking profits.

"Consumer banking will be tough because the very low interest-rate environment is simply damaging to consumer banking business models. Those low cost deposits aren't worth what they were."

But the overseas experience shows the worm will eventually turn. After a few rate rises in the US, the banks there roared back into profitability.

"We've got to have an eye to the future here - these businesses will return to profitably," Chronican says.

"We just need to make sure we're positioned with a good proposition with a good share of customer."

Naturally, Chronican doesn't want to see another rate rise. His message to Reserve Bank governor Phil Lowe is that there is some better activity coming through from the housing market, with mortgage applications jumping 18 per cent in recent weeks.

But he remains concerned about business lending - not surprisingly, given NAB's focus on this area - which remains stubbornly low.

"We need to understand in more depth why businesses aren't investing. That really has to be the source of the next cycle of economic growth," Chronican says.

"Further interest rate cuts aren't going to be the solution to the problem - we need more than that."

The standards Chronican set during his short period as both CEO and chairman is reflected in the bonus outcomes for NAB's executive team.

After outgoing chairman Ken Henry was rocked by an 88 per cent vote against NAB's remuneration report at the bank's annual meeting late last December, Chronican has clearly listened.

There were no short-term bonuses handed out in 2019 - a maximum of $14.4 million was up for grabs - and no increases to fixed remuneration.

Thorburn, who resigned in the firestorm that engulfed NAB after the release of the royal commission's final report in February, already had forfeited up to $21 million in deferred remuneration.

"We wanted to send a very clear message that we had listened," Chronican says. "This has obviously been a year where the shareholders have had to put up with a lot."

One interesting question flowing from this decision is whether McEwan might find it hard to attract top talent when he inevitably begins to tweak his senior leadership team once he comes on board on December 2.

Chronican says he won't predict what changes McEwan might make when he arrives. But he says an important selling point for any potential executives the new NAB chief goes after will be that the bank has worked hard to ensure the issues of the past don't drag on.

However, he also doesn't shy away from a principle that appears to have been forgotten by many boards and management teams when it comes to pay: "There's upside and downside there for executives."
direction: downward pressure on NAB shares

headline: Nickel surge puts the spotlight on Poseidon
text:
Andrew Forrest-backed Poseidon Nickel is in the frame to accelerate mine re-opening plans in Western Australia as prices surge to their highest levels in years.

The company is looking to bring the mothballed Black Swan operations, which include the high-grade Silver Swan mine, back into production 50 years on this month from the Poseidon nickel boom.

There was speculation a restart, currently on track for some time in the next eight months, could be bought forward after nickel prices reached a five-year high on Monday.

The spike in the London benchmark three-month price to $18,470 a tonne came with a cloud hanging over supplies out of Indonesia and the future of the Chinese-owned Ramu nickel plant in Papua New Guinea after a big waste spill.

ASX-listed nickel stocks rose sharply on Monday, with Western Areas Limited up more than 15 per cent to $2.86, Independence Group up almost 9 per cent to $5.92, Poseidon up 13 per cent to 4.3¢ and junior explorer St George Mining up almost 18 per cent to 16.5¢ after announcing a new nickel sulphide discovery at its flagship Mt Alexander project.

Indonesia has flagged banning nickel exports from December, raising concerns about a supply deficit.

Indonesia's Energy and Mineral resources Minister Ignasius Jonan announced the island nation would bring forward a ban on exporting ore by two years in a move that would cut supply to China's nickel pig iron ore.

The ban is consistent with Indonesia's push to force nickel miners there into downstream processing and could result in significant reduction in global supply in the medium term.

Independence managing director Peter Bradford said the current nickel price dynamic had been building for some time, with demand outstripping supply over the past three years.

He said this had led to a drawdown in inventory to levels where any supply surprise could lead to immediate and sharp price movement.

"Going forward there is the risk of increased tightness with continued demand for nickel into stainless steel and increasing demand for nickel into electric vehicle batteries.

"Under-exploration and under-development in new nickel production, largely as a result of sustained low prices over the last few years, means that supply-side response will be slow."

Mr Bradford said higher prices were needed to encourage exploration and new development.

He predicted nickel supply from Indonesia would grow over time and that was a positive for the industry overall.

Shaw & Partners analyst Peter O'Connor said he did not expect any shutdown of the Ramu operations to cause a multi-quarter supply disruption.

However, he said the situation in Indonesia had the potential to cause a significant squeeze in supply in the short to medium term.

Mr O'Connor said another key factor for nickel stocks was emerging demand from battery makers looking to secure supply from WA's nickel sulphide producers.

Both Western Areas and Independence appear likely to receive higher premiums for their nickel concentrate as they negotiate fresh off-take agreements.

Mr O'Connor said sustained strength in the nickel price would encourage Poseidon and others with mothballed assets to return to production as had been the case when the iron ore price spiked at the start of 2019.
direction: bullish for nickel miners and Poseidon Nickel

headline: Wynn bid for Crown not dead
text:
Las Vegas casinos giant Wynn Resorts' sudden retreat from a $10 billion buyout of James Packer's Crown Resorts may only be temporary while it grapples with the fallout from a regulatory probe in the United States which threatens its ownership of a new $US2.6 billion Boston casino resort.

The abrupt reversal triggered a 9.1 per cent slump in Crown shares yesterday to $12.77 after Wynn "terminated" talks with Australia's biggest casino group.

But it only gave up about half of the near 20 per cent gain Crown made on Tuesday after the implied offer of $14.75 per share by Wynn in a cash and scrip deal was revealed publicly.

Wynn's own shares fell on the Nasdaq in the US by 3.9 per cent on Tuesday night Australian time, with US analysts largely lukewarm on the pricing and benefits of the Crown deal to Wynn, which runs casinos in the US and Macau.

Wynn chief executive Matt Maddox is under pressure as the Massachussetts Gaming Commission scrutinises what he knew about a string of allegations over cover-ups and spying on employees by Wynn to protect founder and former chief executive Steve Wynn from sexual misconduct allegations.

Mr Wynn, who no longer holds any shares in Wynn Resorts, denies any wrongdoing.

There are also suggestions that other potential suitors may have a close look at Crown now that 46.1 per cent shareholder Mr Packer has effectively declared that Crown is for sale at the right price by engaging with a suitor over several weeks.

A sell-out by Mr Packer would hasten an exit from public life by the billionaire who has been battling mental health issues.

There had been weeks of talks behind the scenes by Wynn and Crown, including a lower-priced initial proposal which remained secret, before the latest proposal at $14.75 became public in a Crown announcement. This was prompted by revelations of serious talks between the two companies in The Australian Financial Review's Street Talk column.

Macquarie Group gaming analysts said despite termination, Crown would continue to trade at a premium to Macquarie's fair value price of $12.20 per share. There was also the possibility another suitor might come to the table. It titled its research note, "Takeover abandoned ... for now?".

"Overall, we think that for a transaction to stack up, it would need to be supported by revenue synergies with increased VIP volumes being re-directed from Asia into Australia," Macquarie said. It pointed out that Galaxy Entertainment has a large VIP business in Macau, while Genting "have significant Asian gaming operations".

Wynn's $US2.6 billion ($3.6 billion) Encore Boston Harbor complex is due to open in June, but the Massachusetts Gaming Commission is yet to rule on whether Wynn should retain a licence to operate it.

The regulator has been questioning Wynn executives, including chief executive Mr Maddox.

In addition, a 200-page report dated March 15 by the commission's investigators argues management knew of employees' sexual misconduct allegations against Mr Wynn but failed to follow company policy on how to handle them.

"Their efforts at secrecy made it exceedingly difficult, if not impossible, for gaming regulators to detect potentially derogatory information through typical regulatory means," investigators said in the report.

Even though Mr Wynn and individuals identified in the report with responsibility for the corporate failures have been replaced, the investigation was scathing.

"Those changes at the company do not, however, erase the fact that the corporate failures revealed in this investigation are significant, repetitive and reflective of the company's historical governance practices," they wrote.

"Inaction and failures on the part of the identified former executives at this publicly traded company, which was led by its founder at the time, appear to have contributed to a culture where employees were reluctant to report allegations against Mr Wynn to management."

Crown made a brief statement to the ASX yesterday, which acknowledged the termination statement by Wynn in the US . "Crown notes that Wynn has announced that it has terminated all discussions with Crown concerning any transaction," the statement said.

Mr Packer's own stake in Crown is worth $4.6 billion at the implied takeover proposal price of $14.75.

Credit Suisse gaming analysts said it was unlikely that Australian casino rival Star Entertainment Group would put forward a counter proposal to merge with Crown, although they estimated the synergies would be worth between $1 and $1.50 per Crown share.

They also concluded that "the industrial logic to the (Wynn) deal is not obvious from a Wynn perspective", given there were no material synergies, the takeover price wasn't "deep value" and it appeared dilutive on an earnings per share basis to Wynn.

But overall, Credit Suisse concluded that even though it was at a mature stage, "Australia is an attractive gaming market which has shown modest growth for 20 years".

Wynn shut down the $10 billion takeover offer for Crown, blaming the "premature" disclosure of what the Las Vegas gambling giant described as preliminary negotiations.

Only hours after confirming via a US Securities and Exchange Commission report that it was talking to Melbourne-based Crown, Wynn issued a terse statement that all bets were off.

"Following the premature disclosure of preliminary discussions, Wynn Resorts has terminated all discussions with Crown Resorts concerning any transaction," Wynn said.

Crown runs casinos in Melbourne and Perth and is eyeing 2021 for the opening of its flagship $2.2 billion property at Barangaroo in Sydney.

While the deal has been put on ice, US-based gambling analysts began assessing its strength, led by Morgan Stanley's Thomas Allen, who said it would make strategic sense for Wynn.

Even though Wynn has traditionally been a builder of properties rather than a buyer, Crown's portfolio would fit with the Las Vegas company's focus on owning only a "handful of luxury assets in the best gaming markets in the world", Mr Allen said.

In Morgan Stanley's view, Australia is a strong market with gambling spend per head running at about $US1000 ($1404) versus $US470 in the US, as well as the significant Chinese customer base. About 75 per cent of Wynn's revenue in 2018 was in Macau, while the company's most recent management disclosure indicated that 54 per cent of its 2017 Vegas table game revenue was from international players, with Chinese likely to be the largest proportion.

"This could create an attractive cross-selling opportunity," Mr Allen said.

However, he warned that a potential drawback is Australia's distance from Macau, Vegas and Boston, which could weigh on the company's historically lean management.

"Regulatory approval could be another potential hurdle, with Australian regulators in the past taking a long time to approve foreign ownership," he wrote in a research note.
direction: downward pressure on Crown and Wynn shares

headline: ABS becomes a Gruen project
text:
Respected public servant and economist David Gruen has been named as the new boss of the Australian Bureau of Statistics (ABS).

Treasurer Josh Frydenberg said Dr Gruen, currently deputy secretary at the Department of the Prime Minister and Cabinet, would succeed outgoing Australian Statistician David Kalisch next month.

The five-year appointment means Dr Gruen will oversee the August 2021 national census.

Before his appointment to the Prime Minister's department, there was speculation he would be a candidate to lead the ABS in 2014.

A 13-year veteran of the Reserve Bank of Australia, including in its economics analysis and economics research departments, Dr Gruen is a graduate of Cambridge and the Australian National University in Canberra.

He was a visiting lecturer in the economics department and the Woodrow Wilson School at Princeton University in the 1990s and currently leads Prime Minister and Cabinet's economics group, including the specialist behavioural economics unit.

Mr Frydenberg and Assistant Treasurer Michael Sukkar announced the appointment yesterday, describing Dr Gruen as a highly experienced public service leader.

"He has a demonstrated track record of leadership in public policy and outstanding economic and international credentials," they said in a statement.

"The skills and experience Dr Gruen will bring to the position will ensure the ABS continues to be responsive to community, business and government needs for data, statistics and insights.

"The government wishes to thank the outgoing Australian Statistician Mr David Kalisch for his leadership over the past five years. Mr Kalisch made a significant contribution during his time as the Australian Statistician."

Dr Gruen is married to Jenny Wilkinson, the independent Parliamentary Budget Officer.

He is the son of Fred Gruen, the noted Austrian-born economist and one of the "Dunera boys" who fled to Australia to escape the Nazis.

His brother, economist Nicholas Gruen, led the first cost-benefit analysis of the national population snapshot this year, finding it delivers a sixfold benefit to the economy and is "critical" to the fairness, efficiency and integrity of the nation's economic and social infrastructure.

Appointed in 2014, Mr Kalisch oversaw the troubled 2016 census, which experienced a 40-hour online outage after a series of denial of service attacks and high-profile technology failures.

Testing for the next census is under way, with question selection being considered by the Morrison government.

Nearly three-quarters of households are expected to complete the next national statistical snapshot online.

In 2017, Dr Gruen told an economics conference strong jobs policies to help workers left behind by technology were needed to avoid the rise of populist politicians with anti-trade policies like US President Donald Trump in Australia.
direction: neutral to slightly bearish for rate-sensitive sectors

headline: Appen wants to be a tech company
text:
Innovation The data cruncher is powered by a crowd of human workers but it hopes to boost them with artificial intelligence, writes Yolanda Redrup.

Appen may be widely considered one of the hottest tech stocks on the ASX, but really, its journey to becoming a tech company is only just beginning.

That might be a surprising idea to those who have watched the language and search data services provider's impressive rise, from listing on the ASX with an issue price of price of 50¢ in January 2015, to trading above $14 today, giving it a valuation of $1.5 billion.

But that is how chief executive Mark Brayan thinks about the company he has run for the last 3½ years.

As Appen's share price gained 53 per cent in 2018, landing it sixth for performance among the top 200 companies, many market watchers began grouping it with local tech favourites like WiseTech Global, Afterpay Touch, and Xero. But unlike these businesses, Brayan says that Appen has a way to go before it really takes advantage of artificial intelligence and automation to ensure its processes are as efficient as they could be.

At its most basic, Appen's main service is crunching data that big companies then use to underpin their own clever technology. At Amazon or Microsoft, for example, that data might be used to train their voice assistants Alexa or Cortana. Online retailers use Appen's crunched data to deliver the results their customers are searching for.

It all sounds sufficiently "tech" until you realise that Appen's secret weapon is a million-strong "crowd" of remote workers who do the data crunching.

"We have humans involved in our business because we're working with human-annotated data, but we're investing in tech to have the tech augment the preparation and delivery of that data," Brayan says. "The first era as a listed company has been about scale and growth and jumping on that opportunity that's there, but the second era will be much more tech-forward in the way we do things."

A typical task for Appen and its crowd of humans might be sorting through a data dump of 1 million images of streets, with a request from a company to go through and tag every street sign in the photos. This would then be used to train the algorithms that will one day help driverless cars to function.

Members of Appen's crowd, who work from their own homes, cafes or co-working spaces around the globe, go through and tag each of the images.

But, within the next three or so years, Brayan wants to have created a system in which humans can look at the first 10,000 of 1 million images, and then a machine learning-based computer program would tag the rest of the street signs. This will speed up processes dramatically, allowing Appen to offer a cheaper price for its services, and free up the time of its crowd so the company can take on more projects.

Brayan believes it will also be the catalyst for investors to price the business like one of its tech peers, which generally trade on higher price-earnings multiples.

"The longer-term scenario is that we become more of a tech business. The tech elements will go to growth and we'll be able to deliver higher volumes of data, improve margins, stay ahead of competitors and win more share from them through this investment," Brayan says.

"When that will flow through to value, I don't know. The market will have to see margin improvements ... but in the next three years I think we'll have a different-looking business and a different sense of value."

Brayan may be looking at the prospect of investors being willing to pay more for its earnings, but Appen is already trading on a forward price-earnings multiple of 31 times, compared to 14 times for the S&P/ASX200. The high price already has some investors wondering when the right time to sell out could be.

AusBil Investment Management portfolio manager Mason Willoughby-Thomas bought into Appen in the lead-up to its 2015 initial public offering and has held on to a small parcel of shares since then, but says it is getting quite expensive.

"I liked the story from the IPO days. It was one of those companies that was tough to get your head around because it had some ideas and concepts you had to understand to know where the company was going," he tells AFR Weekend.

"We were one of the few institutional investors who took up the stock at the time, so it's been a pretty good investment for me over the last four years ... There haven't been that many that have done this well.

"The only issue to consider is the valuation and at around the $14-$15 it is pushing it a bit."

The company's origins trace back to 1996 in the spare room of Dr Julie and Chris Vonwiller's Gordon home in Sydney's upper north shore. Julie had been a linguist at The University of Sydney and left to start her own automated speech-recognition start-up.

Chris, an engineer with Telstra who had been the managing director of the telco's multimedia division and the first chairman of Telstra Bigpond, joined Appen full-time in 1999.

The company won tech companies like Nuance, Philips, Siemens and Toshiba as clients in its early years and then in 2009 private equity came knocking.

Jeremy Samuel's Anacacia Capital bought a 57 per cent stake in the business for an undisclosed sum and shortly afterwards, in April 2010, Chris Vonwiller stepped aside as chief executive and the company appointed Bill Pulver to the top job. Pulver had previously been the president of online ratings agency NetRatings, and would go on to become the boss of the Australian Rugby Union - a position he held until mid-last year.

"We saw in Appen ... the need for data for speech recognition for major tech companies and later content relevance for search and social media," Samuel says.

A major milestone came in 2011 when Appen acquired US company Butler Hill, which had been focused on the search relevance market. With it came Appen's first mega tech client, Microsoft. Pulver says the business started growing "in an incredible way" after the deal.

"For me, [joining Appen] was a real jump outside my comfort zone, but it was a really interesting space," he says. "We've tapped into a wonderful vein of opportunity with these smart people who want to work from home and it's been a society change that's aligned well with our clients and what we do."

Pulver, who remains on the Appen board, is also an investor in the company and in 2018 made about $9.8 million from the sale of 800,495 shares. His remaining stake of just under 1 per cent is worth about $14 million.

In late 2014 Anacacia decided to float Appen, which allowed it to complete a staged sell-down. It still holds some shares today through its Wattle public equity fund.

Appen also counts institutional investors such as Vinva Investment Management, Vanguard and Manulife Financial Corp on its register. The Vonwillers remain the largest shareholders with 10.4 per cent, according to Bloomberg.

"Floating a sub-$50 million business is not easy to gain interest from larger institutions. Early brokers like Bell Potter and Taylor Collison supported the stock with research and introductions to various institutions. Later we introduced more of the bulge bracket investment banks," Samuel says.

"We methodically crossed our stock to improve the quality of the share register, enabling new shareholders to access the growth."
