Samuel says Appen's biggest risk is still its reliance on very large, powerful customers: "If Facebook or Google or Amazon changed their strategy or their sourcing policies, it could have a significant impact on Appen and its competitors.

"[But] the market appears to be expanding rapidly. It's a credit to the board and management team that they continued to produce results and meet or exceed expectations. It's a true Australian success story."

That is a key challenge for Brayan, a seasoned executive who previously led listed companies Integrated Research and Concept Systems, as well as resources technology provider MST Global.

For Brayan, running a business is like an endurance sport. Luckily for him, it's one of the things he's always been good at.

The lifelong Sydneysider reluctantly admits that he showed signs of being a good leader in high school, partaking in Scouts and also being made a school prefect, but beyond that he says he didn't stand out from the crowd.

"I wasn't academic or a sporting high achiever ... but I was good at endurance events like running. The path for me [to becoming a CEO] was me saying that 'This is what I want in my career' and then chipping away at it," he says.

"The metaphor I like is based on a Belgian cyclist from the '40s and '50s called Briek Schotte and he rode a particular race 20 times in a row, which is extraordinary. He got asked how he trained to do that and he said you have to ride until you don't know which village you're from and you have to keep going because if you don't, you'll never get home."

Cycling is Brayan's sport of choice, believing that to run a business you need not just a sharp mind, but a strong body. It's not unusual for Appen's US staff to receive a flurry of emails from Brayan on their Friday afternoon, while he's having his Saturday morning coffee after a 230-kilometre ride.

Since he took the top job in July 2015 Appen shares have climbed from about 70¢ to $15.59 on Friday (when they added another 5 per cent in a good day for tech stocks). They hit an all-time high of $15.74 last year, before being caught up in the tech sell-off.

While the leap of more than 2000 per cent is substantial, Brayan says he doesn't pay much attention to the share price and he's more focused on delivering sustainable growth - something investors have come to expect, with the company issuing regular earnings upgrades.

Appen has forecast earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation for the full year of between $62 million and $65 million.

The vast majority of the company's revenue is generated in the US, but it has recently started pushing more aggressively into China, which Brayan believes will soon overtake the US as the biggest market for AI in the world.

While Appen's footprint in the region is currently small, building up its presence is one of Brayan's key priorities for 2019. However, he is cautious that the market has a unique regulatory market and way of doing business that makes it different to expanding in other markets - a challenge that investors are also wary of.

"There are some local providers in China that are the incumbents, but if they do gain traction there it could be a significant upside," Willoughby-Thomas says.

"The big opportunity moving into Asia is being able to sign up someone like Alibaba, Baidu or Tencent."

Brayan is focused on keeping it simple. "People understand what we do and that's important. You have to have a plan and simply and clearly communicate it," he says. "When I've tried to over-complicate things, that's when it hasn't worked."
direction: Bullish for Appen and ASX tech stocks

headline: IR class action costs ruling appealed
text:
A landmark decision that requires litigation funders to pay costs in class actions for underpayments could drive up funder returns by millions of dollars and reduce workers' share of their entitlements.

UK-based funder Augusta Ventures has appealed a precedent ruling that it must pay security of costs up-front for its multi-million dollar class action against BHP and labour hire firms over the alleged underpayment of casual miners at Mount Arthur coal mine.

In court documents backing Augusta, Canberra law firm Adero argued the requirement would likely drive Augusta Venture's share up by $5.7 million or 38 per cent of all likely damages, imposing a "substantial injustice" on group members.

The documents reveal that Augusta Ventures is entitled to 300 per cent of the legal costs in the action or 25 per cent of the settlement, whichever is greater, and takes priority over group members.

Augusta Ventures and Adero have been behind an explosion of class actions in the industrial relations space in recent years, as the Fair Work Act's no-costs jurisdiction reduces the risk profile for mass litigation.

In its appeal, Augusta Ventures revealed that it had deployed $41 million into class actions in Australia since 2014 and currently funds two thirds of all employment-related actions.

However, last month, Justice Michael Lee threatened to chill those class actions when he held funders should have to pay costs if they lose.

The judge held there was no compelling argument that "this [Fair Work Act costs] protection should be somehow extended to non-party funders who are using these claims to their perceived commercial advantage".

It means Augusta Ventures must pay $3.1 million in security of costs in its BHP case and Adero is expecting employers in all seven of its underpayment class actions to seek their own security.

Augusta managing director Neill Brennan said in an affidavit lodged in the appeal that the funder would seek to pay the security of costs through an insurance policy.

According to Mr Markham, the scheme would involve an up-front payment of $1.3 million and a success premium on winning, valued between $250,000 and $1.12 million depending on how long the case takes.

"Where the costs of the [insurance] policy are passed on to group members it would likely equal an impost of $1.9 million", he said, out of likely damages between $16.5 million and $22 million.

Under Augusta's 300 per cent return option, Mr Markham said "the likely impost on group members would be $5.7 million in total which I estimate to be about 38 per cent of the likely recovery of group member entitlements".

He said the amount did not include the success premium that would be payable if the firm won.

Australian Industry Group chief executive Innes Willox said the appeal documents show class action laws need to be reformed.

"Under the [litigation funding arrangement] the plaintiffs could receive nothing from any sum awarded at the conclusion of the class action," he said. "For example, if Augusta Ventures' outlay is $3 million and the sum awarded is $8 million, Augusta would be entitled to the whole amount leaving nothing for the plaintiffs."

He said the government should regulate funders as financial products and impose reasonable limits on returns.

However, Mr Markham said that Mr Willox was not taking into account the court's role in approving any return arrangements as reasonable.

"The comments by Mr Willox grossly misrepresents the role of court approval within class actions, and are at complete odds with the current status of the common fund applications that were made before Justice Lee in June 2019."

He said Augusta Ventures had indicated in the BHP case it would seek to defer the issue of commission until settlement approval.
direction: bearish for litigation funders and class action plaintiffs

headline: IOOF misses APRA deadline
text:
An independent reviewer has found that embattled wealth group IOOF failed to set up a dedicated monitoring body for superannuation trustees by its March deadline.

The $2.3 billion company revealed on Friday that it had failed to meet four of the 76 goals agreed between IOOF and the prudential regulator.

The action plan between the company and regulator was put in place to make sure IOOF acted in the best interest of superannuation members. It came as the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority in December also sought to disqualify five IOOF executives from sitting on super fund boards.

APRA made a preliminary finding that IOOF breached its licence conditions because of the failures. The firm said it will not dispute APRA's notice and it is confident it will meet the new deadline of the end of June.

IOOF shares closed 2.5 per cent lower on Friday at $6.46, above a 12-month low of $4.20 hit in December.

This finding raises more questions about whether the company's billion-dollar purchase of ANZ's OnePath pension and investment business will go ahead. ANZ agreed to sell its pension and investment business and four aligned dealer groups to IOOF for $975 million. The sale of the aligned dealer groups is completed, but the main part of the deal has been delayed and may not go ahead. An ANZ spokesman said on Friday the bank "remain[s] in contact with IOOF".

On the same day in December as APRA revealed its court action against IOOF and its executive, it issued a show cause notice to announce its intention to direct IOOF to comply with its registrable superannuation entity licence conditions and impose conditions on super fund trustees' licensees. That forced chief executive Christopher Kelaher to leave and chairman George Venardos to step down to make room for board member, Allan Griffiths.

Mr Griffiths said on Friday: "We have been actively ... addressing the governance issues raised by APRA ... [and] will continue working constructively to complete the remaining initiatives."
direction: IOOF shares likely to face further downward pressure

headline: Big super flexes its muscles
text:
Industry superannuation funds will use their massive clout as investors to drive a post-Hayne transformation of corporate culture, "refashioning" business to focus on the long term.

As the Business Council of Australia complains about big business "bashing", industry super tsar Greg Combet said the fall of AMP should serve as a warning to the rest of corporate Australia about the consequences of a focus on short-term profit over customers and community.

The former Labor minister who now chairs both Industry Super Australia and IFM Investors, which is owned by industry funds, flagged a potentially painful transition for some companies, especially banks and other financial services businesses but also in energy, mining and the rest of the economy.

"Banks and other financial services companies in particular need to think very carefully about how to refashion themselves to rebuild trust," he said.

Mr Combet said investors, emboldened by the findings of the royal commission, were preparing to exercise their voting rights and influence with governments and the public to force a focus on environmental, social and governance matters in the interests of long-term value creation.

At the same time, Treasurer Josh Frydenberg wrote to Australian Prudential Regulation Authority chairman Wayne Byres on Sunday expressing alarm about union demands in February that industry super funds use their leverage as investors to pressure BHP to save the jobs of seafarers.

The Treasurer said this was a "dangerous development" and warned that superannuation was "not a plaything for union bosses nor a platform for pushing their industrial relations agenda".

Mr Frydenberg sought the "urgent advice" of Mr Byres on this "aggressive union behaviour" and whether the regulator had adequate powers to ensure trustees were acting in the best interests of members.

The super system is projected to be worth $4.2 trillion in today's dollars by 2030. Rice Warner predicts that industry funds will control the largest stake at about 36 per cent of the market. IFM, which is owned by industry funds and manages large amounts of their money, is one of the nation's largest equities investors.

Mr Combet said super funds wanted to partner with government and business but there was an implicit threat that super funds will have to take more money offshore if local companies looked like they will underperform over the long term.

"I think from an Australian national interest point of view, obviously it's desirable to maximise how much capital can be deployed into the domestic economy," Mr Combet said.

"But to maximise investment in the domestic economy for the good of society some things have to be tackled as a consequence of the royal commission."

Mr Combet said businesses that used conflicted remuneration and vertical integration to squeeze consumers for short-term profits would be first in the firing line.

"You'll see more focus on ESG issues but also over time an interest in business models, in particular in the financial services sector," Mr Combet said. "AMP's model has destroyed value. That is not in the interests of shareholders and not in the interests of super fund members whose funds have invested in it."

Mr Combet noted Glencore's about-face on coal production under pressure from investor action group Climate Change 100+ as an illustration of the power of collective action by investors.

In a move it describes as unprecedented globally, IFM has determined the carbon footprint of its infrastructure portfolios and will this year publish emissions reduction targets for its Australian assets. The belief is that if listed companies are required to act on climate change risks, so too should unlisted infrastructure businesses.

"The energy sector is an example of where long-term thinking is needed," Mr Combet said. "We have to start making a significant transition from old coal-fired power plants to renewable energy generation and distribution."

Mr Combet shrugged off concerns that industry super funds were susceptible to activist agendas, including campaigns by unions. "What I'm talking about has nothing to do with activism," he said.

"We want to work with Aussie business to get it on a long-term sustainable basis, not hostage to the short-term share price or six-monthly profit announcements. We, as major investors, want Aussie business to succeed."

The Australian Council of Trade Unions has urged 30 industry funds to use their leverage as shareholders to force BHP to guarantee the jobs of up to 80 seafarers. ACTU national president Michelle O'Neil urged funds to set up meetings with BHP.

Ms O'Neil is an alternate board member on the biggest industry fund, AustralianSuper. The ACTU and Australian Industry Group are shareholders of non-profit AustralianSuper, which at $140 billion is the nation's largest super fund.

AustralianSuper has $33 billion invested in the Australian stock market and is believed to be the largest single block of active capital controlled by a single entity in the market.

Asked about the BHP issue, Australian Industry Group chief executive Innes Willox, who sits on the board of AustralianSuper, told the Your Money program that industry funds were singularly focused on their legal obligation to deliver for members.

"There was an attempt by the ACTU to revive an idea they've kicked around for a while which is that industry super and super more generally should use their investment might to influence industrial relations outcomes," he said. "That is not going to happen. It is totally against the remit of industry super funds. I'll put a line through it; we will not allow that to happen."

Mr Combet said: "Every decision has to be seen through a member's interest lens. This is one of the important things about the joint representation structure on the industry funds between business and unions.

"When they come together there is a single focus and obligation, which is in the interests of the members. I was on the AustralianSuper board for a long period of time and have a very close association with a lot of the funds. That's how they work; that's why they are successful."

The Treasurer said Labor Leader Bill Shorten should not allow militant unions to pressure super funds to use their leverage over listed companies and their boards.

"It's time Bill Shorten distanced himself from his union masters and condemned this aggressive union behaviour," Mr Frydenberg said in a statement.

"His continued silence on these issues confirms what we already know that Labor is on the side of the unions, not the superannuation members."

The Treasurer said that actions of unions showed "complete disregard for the law and the responsibilities of trustees to discharge their duties in the best interests of members".

"Superannuation is the product of the hard work of all members and represents their nest egg in retirement. It must be protected."
direction: bearish for banks and financial services

headline: House price surge sparks rush to build
text:
Property prices rising at an annualised rate of 20 per cent in Sydney and Melbourne have spurred developers to start bidding for housing project sites again but also have raised serious concerns about housing affordability.

Melbourne home prices grew 2.3 per cent in just 31 days - the fastest rate in 10 years - while Sydney was up 1.7 per cent for the month, a clear indicator that the Reserve Bank's three interest rate cuts have motivated buyers and jacked up auction clearance rates.

"Is this really the way you want to restimulate the economy again by ramping up asset prices?" economist Stephen Koukoulas said.

"Yes, there will be a positive wealth effect - there is a strong linkage of house prices to people spending more - but this could also see the affordability problem re-emerge and make it an issue by the next election," he said.

Developers are starting to read the tealeaves on the future of housing supply by bidding on sites for new apartment and housing projects.

This could lead to the residential construction sector bottoming out earlier than expected. The sector's collapse is expected to have wiped out 1 percentage point of GDP growth by the time it recovers next year.

The chief executive of one of the largest developers in Sydney, Aqualand's John Carfi, said vendors of key development sites were confident enough to bring their properties to market because developers were making increasingly competitive bids.

"There's been a lack of stock in the market - suddenly the stock arrives and people are out there rushing, trying to snap it up," Mr Carfi said.

"It's not developers saying 'bring it on'. It's owners and vendors bringing stuff to the market, because they think the market is back on again, and they're going to get the price expectation they had before the market softened - which is probably similar to what is happening in the housing market."

The Reserve Bank is watching closely for early signs of another housing market bubble. It has suggested there are as yet no concerns because housing credit growth is running at only 3.1 per cent - the lowest since records began in 1977.

Credit in housing for investors has also contracted for the first time, leading to banks slashing borrowing rates for investors, having ramped them up.

RBA governor Philip Lowe has acknowledged "further signs of a turnaround in established housing markets", but that "new dwelling activity has weakened and growth in housing credit remains low".

The central bank has been worried the residential construction downturn could be worse than expected. This would have serious implications for economic growth as construction contributes about 6 per cent of GDP.

The RBA was concerned for smaller contractors, saying the outlook for them was "not so great in the period ahead", which could lead to job losses, softer wage growth, lower spending and weak inflation - all drivers for further rate cuts. The bank is trying to balance this with the surge in home prices, which is known to help boost spending in the economy and, in turn, grow jobs and inflation.

"Lower interest rates do push up asset prices and higher asset prices are supposed to encourage more investment and there's a wealth effect and people spend a bit more," Dr Lowe said this week. "So, that is part of the transmission mechanism. [Are house prices] a problem? I don't think so."

Stuart Penklis, head of residential for listed developer Mirvac, said there had been noticeable improvement in consumer sentiment in the past two months.

This was encouraging the developer to do more projects, he said.

"We came out this week and announced that we have acquired a site in Milperra - the Western Sydney University site - and we've purchased another site at Milperra which is the former Riverland golf course site, so we are starting to restock predominantly in the master-planned community medium-density space. That's where we are seeing some opportunities."

With unit prices in Sydney on track to grow 16.8 per cent annually, and 20 per cent in Melbourne, real estate agents are noticing the pick up in demand from developers.

Savills director of residential site sales Neil Cooke said there had been a surge in interest from developers looking for medium- to high-density apartment sites in Sydney.

"There has definitely been an uptick in site enquiries - probably about 30 to 50 per cent more enquiries than what we had earlier in the year, which tells me there's definitely been some renewed enthusiasm in the market," he said. "Developers have their eye on the next couple of years being a lot more positive now than where they were in the last 12 to 18 months."

However, even with this activity, Commonwealth Bank economists forecast an undersupply of apartments as soon next year.

"We expect dwelling prices to continue to lift from here," senior economist Kristina Clifton said. "Yesterday's building approvals data showed approvals are now running at or below underlying demand. An undersupply of dwellings would also add to upward pressure on dwelling prices."

This would lead to the affordability issue recurring.

"If prices continue at this [20 per cent annualised] pace for the next three months and then slow down to something more sustainable like a 5 per cent pace then affordability will have deteriorated quite a lot going into next year, because wages are only growing at, say, 2.3 per cent," AMP Capital's chief economist Shane Oliver said.

"If prices continue to go up like this, then it's inevitable that we'll start to see much stronger credit growth, but it'll take a while for that to come through.

So I think any intervention by the regulators [in terms of tighter lending conditions] is least six months away".
direction: residential construction sector

headline: Lowe accuses banks of fee gouging
text:
Reserve Bank of Australia governor Philip Lowe has singled out Commonwealth Bank for its slow rollout of the new, real-time payments system and accused the big four banks of gouging fees on international money transfers.

While the take-up rate of the new payments system is quicker than most other countries, Dr Lowe said CBA was creating a drag on the system's potential. Speaking in Sydney yesterday, Dr Lowe said that "in nearly every case" the banks charged higher fees for international money transfers than digital money transfer operators (MTOs). For the major banks, the average mark-up over the wholesale exchange rate is around 5.5 per cent, versus about 1 per cent for the digital MTOS.

"For many people, the costs here are still too high," he said. News p10
direction: bearish for major banks

headline: Breville cooks up a sous vide storm
text:
Australian consumers are more inclined to burn their beef on a barbie than cook it in a pot of boiling water, but the growing international popularity of sous vide cooking has prompted Breville Group to make its first acquisition in two years.

The appliance maker has bought a Seattle-based food and technology company, ChefSteps, which makes Joule, the world's smallest sous vide immersion circulator controlled by a smartphone app.

Chefsteps, which was founded by two chefs, one of whom worked with celebrity chef Heston Blumenthal at The Fat Duck, also provides about 1 million followers with content such as sous vide cooking lessons and recipes.

The acquisition complements Breville's existing range of commercial sous vide appliances sold through its Polyscience Culinary division.

The value of the acquisition, which included an upfront cash payment and an earn-out agreement, was not disclosed and it was not reported to the Australian Securities Exchange, suggesting the initial outlay was modest.

Breville chief executive Jim Clayton said the acquisition would not have a material impact on earnings in 2020 "but hopefully in 2021 or 2022 and so forth it will start to have some impact".

"It's a really solid consumer offering in sous vide which complements our commercial offering," Mr Clayton told The Australian Financial Review.

Sous vide, which means "under vacuum", has been a popular cooking method for professional chefs for decades but has started gaining traction with consumers because it enables them to cook meat, vegetables and even desserts to perfect "doneness".

Food is cooked in a sealed plastic bag in a water bath at a precise temperature and time. It can also be kept heated without overcooking while cooks prepare other elements of a meal.

Mr Clayton said the Joule, which is the size of a paper towel holder, was the most accessible and well-designed of four or five products in the North American market, where sous vide cooking has now become mainstream.

Breville was attracted to ChefSteps not only because of the Joule device but ChefSteps' community of 1 million engaged members looking for solutions in the kitchen, he said.
direction: Breville shares likely to rise

headline: Three banks in breach of liquidity rules: APRA
text:
Macquarie Group is among three banks that may be required to hold more funding and liquidity after breaching the prudential regulator's liquidity requirements.

The under-fire Australian Prudential Regulation Authority, which was the subject of a scathing 146-page capability report this month, said HSBC Australia, Rabobank Australia and Macquarie Bank were breaching mandatory liquidity standards because of the structure of their intra-company arrangements, which meant funding could be withdrawn in times of financial stress.

APRA, which began examining the issue last November, said the banks will be required to restate their past funding and liquidity ratios and change some parts of their agreements.

In a statement to the ASX, Macquarie Group said restating its accounts to comply with APRA's regulations would not impact the overall financial or capital position of the consolidated group, though would likely show a "historical non-compliance".

Importantly, the regulator, which will continue to talk to the banks in coming weeks, said it was considering "a range of further options, including the imposition of higher funding and liquidity requirements".

"Macquarie Bank, Rabobank Australia and HSBC Australia are financially sound, with strong liquidity and funding positions in the current stable environment," APRA deputy chairman John Lonsdale said. "However, to ensure they would be able to withstand a scenario of financial stress, group funding agreements for Australian banks must be watertight, so they can be relied on when they would be most needed."

APRA first introduced the liquidity standard in 2014.

Banking analysts and investors said the breach appeared to be technical and wasn't likely to change their view of the stock unless APRA issued fines.

That is considered unlikely, despite the Graeme Samuel review recommendations including that APRA review the adequacy of its penalties.

In a statement to the exchange, Macquarie said in the past year, loans to Macquarie Bank represented between 10 per cent and 15 per cent of the bank's total funding.

The group added it was working with the regulator but stressed it hadn't been aware of APRA's view of the clause until recently.

"Had Macquarie Group and Macquarie Bank been aware of APRA's interpretation of the material adverse change clause, they would have removed the clause earlier to ensure the funding counted toward Macquarie Bank's liquidity coverage ratio," the statement said. Macquarie said it had removed the problematic MAC clause.

Specifically, under the previous master loan agreement, repayment of intra-group funding could be accelerated and fall short of the 30-day liquidity coverage ratio [LCR] horizon, the bank said.

Macquarie shares fell earlier in the day, but closed up 43¢ to $129.31. In early May, Macquarie shares reached highs of more than $136 a share. The company isn't among the ASX 200's top performers, having notched up a year-to-date return of 19 per cent, a performance which ranks it 99th.

HSBC declined to give details on how it had breached APRA's requirements.

Rabobank Australia chief executive Peter Knoblanche said the breach related to a "type of clause contained within the funding agreement with our parent which APRA has recently clarified". He said the bank was working with the regulator.
direction: bearish for Macquarie Group, HSBC Australia, and Rabobank Australia

headline: Homicide squad called to Pilbara lithium mine
text:
West Australian police have sent homicide squad detectives to a mine site to investigate what is being called a suspicious death.

The investigation was launched after the death a man in his 40s at the Pilbara Minerals lithium mine about 6.30am on Monday.

It is understood a 30-year-old man has been arrested at the mine about 130 kilometres from Port Hedland in WA's rugged Pilbara region.

There were unconfirmed reports of a fight at the site before workers were due to start their morning shift.

Pilbara Minerals issued a statement saying it regretted to advise there had been at fatality at its Pilgangoora site as a result of an incident that was unrelated to mining operations.

It said it was cooperating with the investigation and could not provide any further details.

Mining operations have been temporarily suspended.

Pilbara Minerals said it was doing everything it could to support its workers at "this difficult time".

"I am extremely shocked and saddened by what has occurred today," managing director Ken Brinsden said. "At this difficult time our focus is on ensuring that our people are cared for and supported, while we continue to support the police investigation that is currently under way.

"On behalf of Pilbara Minerals, I would like to express our sincere condolences to the affected people and their families."

The company went into a trading halt but has requested it is lifted when the stockmarket reopens on Tuesday.

Operations at Pilgangoora have come to a virtual standstill in recent weeks as the lithium sector grapples with soft prices caused by sharp growing pains in the market for the battery-making commodity.

The Pilgangoora is close to the Albemarle-Mineral Resources' Wodgina lithium mine put into care and maintenance on November 1.

The indefinite shutdown at Wodgina was announced on the day US battery metals giant Albemarle finalised a deal to hand Chris Ellison-led Mineral Resources $US1.3 billion ($1.9 billion) for a controlling stake.

Mineral Resources said at the time it would retain about 30 of 270 workers at Wodgina and look to redeploy about 135 others.

Pilbara Minerals has parted ways with about 40 workers at Pilgangoora mine after scaling back operations.
direction: Pilbara Minerals shares likely to decline

headline: UK-Australia FTA could include visa-free travel
text:
Australian workers could enjoy visa-free travel to the UK under a post-Brexit free trade agreement, with Canberra and London eager to sign a deal before Christmas 2020.

Boris Johnson's landslide election win has renewed momentum for a trade deal with Australia. Formal negotiations are set to begin soon after the UK leaves the European Union, which is expected in January.

Already two-way trade is valued at $27 billion, with the UK the second-largest source of foreign investment in Australia, at $574.8 billion in 2018.

Mr Johnson has long advocated for trans-Tasman-style free movement between Britain and Australia post-Brexit, calling for "creative thinking" among Anglosphere countries.

"These are nations that are very similar in many ways - we share very, very similar interests and a uniquely shared set of values," Mr Johnson said during the Conservative Party's leadership race this year.

He first promoted the idea of an Australia-Britain common travel area as mayor of London, but the proposal has been met with hesitation by some in Australia - amid concerns about the number of British citizens who could take it up.

British High Commissioner to Australia Vicki Treadell said business links and immigration controls would be part of any deal.

"We want easy business to be done between our two countries," she said on Monday.

"Part of that is actually how the movement of people takes place between our two countries.

"We want to open up sectors in both directions to do even more trade and attract even more investment."

Ms Treadell said Britain wanted to maintain and consolidate its position in Australia and to see more Australian investment into the UK.

She highlighted investment by Australian firms including Lendlease as among success stories. Agriculture, financial and professional services, and other investment are all likely to be part of any deal.

The potential pact has been under development for two years and negotiating teams would take talks to a formal setting after the expected January 31 Brexit departure.

"I think the ambition on both sides is to do it as quickly as possible. A year is not unrealistic because there is political will on both sides," Ms Treadell said.

Treasurer Josh Frydenberg on Monday described the free trade agreement as a big priority for both governments.

He was in contact with his UK counterpart, Chancellor of the Exchequer Sajid Javid, after Friday's election result.

Mr Frydenberg lashed outgoing UK Labor leader Jeremy Corbyn.

"It was fantastic to see Boris Johnson and his team get up," he said.

"Corbyn was an extremist. He had some pretty abhorrent views and I think some members of the Labour Party were ill advised to get close to such a person.

"I was happy to see him defeated and defeated pretty substantially."

Labor's trade spokeswoman Madeleine King said a fair and open trade agreement would be welcomed by the Opposition.

"But, as with all trade agreements, it is important that an Australia-UK FTA is in the best interests of Australian workers and Australian business," she said.

"Labor believes that diversifying and growing Australian export markets across the world is vital for the economy, for jobs, and the prosperity of all Australians."

UK International Trade Secretary Liz Truss told The Australian Financial Review in September that liberalising services was a top priority, with making it easier for British citizens and Australians to work in each other's countries.
direction: supportive for Australian equities and the AUD

headline: HEALTH INSURANCE WOES: DEATH SPIRAL OR HICCUP?
text:
Conundrum Dire predictions on the future of the industry are contradicted by record share prices for major players, writes James Fernyhough.

You would think the Grattan Institute's alarming declaration earlier this month that the private health insurance (PHI) industry is in a "death spiral" would be ringing in the ears of investors.

But the opposite appears to be true. Medibank and NIB, Australia's two largest ASX-listed PHIs, are enjoying record share prices. And according to industry research house IBISWorld, the industry is on track to see revenues and profits grow over the next five years. So what is going on?

The statistics are clear: Australians are abandoning private health insurance in alarming numbers. The Australian Prudential Regulation Authority's latest figures show the total percentage of people with health insurance at the end of 2018 fell by 1 percentage point year-on-year to 44.6 per cent, its lowest level since 2006. That was a continuation of a four-year trend.

Young people are ditching their cover at much higher rates, with an almost 7 percentage point drop in the proportion of 20 to 29-year-olds with private hospital cover.

Almost every other age cohort under 65 also saw a drop in coverage rates in 2018.

People over the age of 65, meanwhile, are increasing their cover, with the percentage of 90 to 94-year-olds leaping almost 9 percentage points. With fewer low-risk young people cross-subsidising older high-risk people, premiums are bound to increase.

The exodus has been widely linked to the fact premium increases have far outpaced wage growth. Since 2011, Grattan said health insurance premiums had increased by 30 per cent, while wages increased by just 8 per cent.

The Australian Financial Review spoke to a number of people under 35 who had ditched, or were considering ditching, their health insurance. All of them complained of rocketing premiums.

Kristofor Lawson, a 32-year-old self-employed man working in the media, said he was considering dropping his cover because of what he called "insane price rises".

"I feel like health insurance is always rising well above inflation and there's no clear reason or explanation why this should be the case. I also hate the idea that I'm propping up other generations when I'm less likely to require things like hospital coverage," he said.

He said when he first took insurance out nine years ago, he was paying $120 a month. Now he's paying almost double that.

"Every year the price rises make health insurance just that bit more unaffordable," he said. "I do believe public health insurance is an important part of our economy. But I just want there to be more clarity into why prices are rising so dramatically. The government should really be capping the price rises. After all they're strongly encouraging people to move to PHI - yet when prices keep going up you have to wonder why bother?"

Matthew Bourke, a 31-year-old man from Footscray, Melbourne, cancelled his policy with Medibank last week after he read calls by NIB managing director Mark Fitzgibbon to abolish Medicare and make private health insurance compulsory - albeit with the government paying the premiums of people who could not afford them.

"Feelings that PHI was a rip-off had been brewing for a while but a PHI leader publicly undermining Medicare was the straw that broke the camel's back and motivated me to pick up the phone and cancel," Mr Bourke said, adding he was "not going back any time soon".

He said he took CHOICE's test to see if he'd save or lose money on health insurance, and learnt he would be better off financially without it.

"I hardly used the private health system and felt the coverage largely duplicated services available on Medicare. The extras package was a glorified coupon book."

The outraged responses to Mr Fitzgibbon's proposal on social media and in the comment sections only further confirmed the public's frustration with the PHI industry.

And yet despite all this, the major PHIs look to be in reasonable financial shape.

In an industry report published in June, IBISWorld had this to say: "Premiums are forecast to rise and investment returns are projected to recover over the next five years, driving industry revenue growth. Industry revenue is forecast to grow at an annualised 3.8 per cent over the five years through 2023-24, to reach $30.5 billion. Profitability is also projected to increase over the period, despite benefit payments rising, as operators reduce management expenses."

The outlook for Medibank and NIB, the nation's second and fourth biggest PHIs respectively, and the two largest ASX-listed health funds, is in line with IBISWorld's prediction. In his most recent assessment following the May federal election result, JPMorgan analyst Siddharth Parameswaran upgraded Medibank to neutral, with a share price target of $3.05.

His assessment of Medibank's prospects was mixed, but hardly reflective of a death spiral. "[F]rom FY18 onwards, industry growth has slowed and Medibank has been losing significant share," he wrote. "Claims inflation in the industry had declined, but premium rate increases, which initially lagged the reducing claims trends, are now following and are likely to claw back some of the improvement in margins we saw earlier."

Mr Parameswaran said there had also been "increasing political pressure to continue with very low rate capped increases into the future - with some politicians focusing on RoEs being too high (particularly on the Labor side)".

Labor had gone to the election with a proposal to cap annual premium increases at 2 per cent for two years. That would have been a massive restriction on PHIs, given premiums this year have increased on average 3.25 per cent, the lowest increase in 18 years.

Labor's shock loss changed the outlook dramatically.

"In light of the Coalition victory in the recent federal election, we think the risks on the downside are now less pronounced than before (although there is still some downward pressure on margins)," Mr Parameswaran said.

At the time of the May election Medibank's share price was $2.88. Since then it has rocketed past JPMorgan's target, closing on Friday at $3.62.

Citi analyst Nigel Pittaway was similarly mixed in his analysis of NIB in a note on July 18, predicting cost cutting and "benign" claims inflation would allow NIB to beat its own profit guidance. But he added: "Nonetheless we wonder if the relief rally has gone too far in light of industry operating conditions, which still remain difficult."

Mr Pittaway said falling industry participation, ongoing affordability pressures and political pushes for premiums to stay low - the government has the power to reject proposed premium increases - would keep premium increases well below 3 per cent for the next two years.

Health economist Stephen Duckett, co-author of the Grattan Institute report, said the public's perception that premium increases were simply a result of cynical profiteering was wrong.

"The vast bulk of the premium increases can be explained by benefit payment increases," he said.

Dr Duckett's paper was a diagnosis of the problem rather than a solution to it. His assessment was that the current system of universal public health coverage through Medicare, with subsidised private health insurance, was "muddled" about the role of private insurance. Was it is intended to be a non-competing complement to Medicare, or a genuine comprehensive alternative?

While Dr Duckett insisted he had not taken a position on what the solution should be, two potential solutions emerged. One was to stop subsidising private health insurance, so that it becomes a complement to, rather than a competitor of, Medicare. The second was to continue subsiding private health insurance so that it is a genuine and equitable private sector-led alternative to a 100 per cent taxpayer-funded, government-run insurance program.

Mr Fitzgibbon's proposal to scrap Medicare and introduce mandatory private insurance with government support for those who can't afford to pay the premiums takes the second route, and according to Dr Henry Cutler, director of Macquarie University Centre for the Health Economy, it is not as radical as it sounds.

"Some people may baulk at the idea of allowing private health insurers to fund all healthcare, but this represents the 'European' way of funding healthcare. But these healthcare systems are no better, nor more efficient, than the Australian healthcare system so it will be an almost impossible task for a government to sell this idea to the Australian public," he said.

"Having private health insurers fund all medical care would not address the impending funding crisis. For that we just need to invest in prevention and keep people out of hospital, which is what private health insurers should be focused upon," Dr Cutler said.

Dr Duckett said the alternative - withdrawing government subsidies to PHIs - would also be politically difficult to pull off, particularly with regard to the 30 per cent premium rebate.

"In part, the rebate has been baked into family budgets, and so people have made their decisions based on this $9 billion subsidy to this industry," he said.

Which makes the prospect of profound reform of a "muddled" system unlikely. So where does that leave the health funds?

In an unenviable position, according to Dr Cutler. "If the private health insurance sector can't turn the decline in membership around, or stem the increase in benefits paid, they will need to continue increasing prices to maintain their revenue and margins. That will not go down well with members," he said.
direction: bearish for private health insurance sector

headline: Flight control
text:
The “wow factor” is a term that often arises when describing a certain home. Usually, the property embraces one or more design elements that are simply out-of-the-ordinary, using scale, material, form – ora combination of – to produce something in between jaw-dropping and eye-popping.

Paradoxically, though, wow factor has lessto do with architects wanting to flex somekind of out-there creativity.

Rather, wow is often what you get when logic, pragmatism and solid architectural design principles are applied first and foremost.

Internationally recognised Sydney architect Peter Stutchbury made this plain when articulating his thoughts about one of his early projects, Wedge House, perched atop a rocky outcrop at Whale Beach on Sydney’s northern beaches. The property, which has now hit the market, was built for the current owners between 1998 and 2000.

“Architecture can be explained logically beyond the creative,” Stutchbury has said about Wedge House. “The experimental thought can be integrated progressively.”

Borrowing a line for a moment, it could sound like: “Build it and the wow will come.” But build it soundly to conditions. In this instance, a tricky sloping block, offering views of the ocean.

For Stutchbury, this demanded an irregular design to accommodate an irregular site.

What he came up with was a structure comprised of two intersecting wedges. One, constructed from concrete, is firmly planted into the earth while the other – made from more lightweight materials, like timber and glass – appears to soar above it, bird-like. It’s an analogy that fits.

Again, these are contrasting forces at play, much like the creativity and logic of the architect.

One wing of the building – housing the main bedroom, office, laundry and outdoor shower – implies nurture and protection; literally, a sense of being nestled and grounded. Look around. Lush garden surrounds.

The other wing – a light-filled, open-plan living, dining and kitchen – suggests vulnerability and flightiness.

A pointy beak-like timber deck, cantilevered over the kids’ quarters below, connects the eye to sky and water, as does the kitchen, a glassbox projecting from a timber batten wall. Freedom awaits.

Lending the house a little more wow is the tilting, seemingly twisting plane of the roof,a design that recalls the corrugated skillionroof of the Sydney Olympic Park Archery Centre Stutchbury designed for the 2000 Games. It’sa habit of designers: letting one project inform another.

LJ Hooker principal David Edwards fullygets that the wow factor comes from architectural challenge.

“Architects are inspired by topography,” says Edwards.

“It gives them the opportunity to express themselves creatively as opposed to a building on a level block of land.”

Stutchbury’s design also successfully captures the archetypal beach house and coastal lifestyle: the use of light materials like timber and corrugated iron but also something that feelsin tune with the elements. One minute openand exposed, the next snug and sheltered.

It’s also the right response, too, when the house is only 10 minutes’ walk from the beach and sits on a private site with views out to sea and the horizon. Multiple areas of the homelead directly the block’s lush surrounds.

As Stutchbury explains: “Wedge House is an intuitive response to the issue of seaside living on a specific site.”
direction: supportive for high-end residential property and architecture sector

headline: Action shifting to East Melbourne
text:
Private developer Brendan Sullivan has put a seven-level office block in East Melbourne, a city fringe suburb, on the market with expectations of $40 million or more.

At 122-130 Wellington Parade, the building has about 4000 square metres of office space and is fully occupied by both office tenants and ground floor retail tenancies. A three-bedroom residence with an expansive outdoor terrace occupies the rooftop.

Just a block from the Pullman on the Park hotel and the Fitzroy Gardens, the building overlooks the Melbourne Cricket Ground and Jolimont precinct and is a short walk from the CBD.

The East Melbourne market is attracting increasing attention from developers and institutional investors.

In one of the biggest hotel deals last year, Asian investment firm iProsperity bought the five-star Pullman on the Park from Brookfield for a final selling price of as much as $200 million.

Developers including Mirvac, which has sold out The Eastbourne apartment development nearby, and Cbus Property, which has fully sold an upmarket residential project in a joint venture with Brookfield, have directed investment into the neighbourhood.

Title documents show the Wellington Parade property is held by Melbourne investor and developer Brendan Sullivan, who has a history in recycling and reworking investments in the CBD as well the exposure in the suburbs.

Built in the 1960s, the Wellington Parade building has had several major refurbishments. East Melbourne has an office vacancy rate of 3.1 per cent.

CBRE's Josh Rutman, Mark Wizel, Lewis Tong and Kiran Pillai have been appointed to handle the building.

"It's arguably an opportunity for the next cycle, but, given the property's prime location, vistas and strong holding income, we're expecting the campaign to generate strong interest from land bankers and developers," Mr Rutman said.

Mr Wizel said the East Melbourne precinct was registering significant rental growth as office supply remained constrained.

"East Melbourne has benefited from the flow-on effect of record low vacancies in the Melbourne CBD, with absorption and effective rent growth figures for the precinct among the strongest in the country," he said.

In the central city itself, private investor appetite drove down average yields on CBD buildings priced below $25 million to a new low of 3.6 per cent in 2018, according to CBRE figures.
direction: bullish for East Melbourne office and property sectors

headline: Investors urged to play tough amid new recession fears
text:
Fund managers are sceptical that a recession is around the corner in Australia but, should one arrive, investors would do well to remember there are a few key rules to protect portfolios in times of turmoil.

Recession fears were back in focus on Thursday after a former Australian prime minister flagged up to a one-in-three chance that there will be a contraction in Australia next year after 28 years of continuous growth.

Kevin Rudd opined that Australia's policymakers are complacent about the risk of recession as storm clouds gather. That includes a US recession, the potential inability of central banks to further stimulate economic growth and growing geopolitical risks.

"There's a lot of complacency after 28 years of growth," said Hugh Dive at Atlas Funds Management, who is unconvinced that a recession is set to hit Australia, at least in the near term.

He pointed out that the employment picture remains robust and the Reserve Bank of Australia still has room to move to support the economy by lowering interest rates.

However, should recession - generally defined by two consecutive quarters of economic contraction - arrive, he said "what you want to own are the companies that have been through tough times in the past".

"Companies that have solid dividends, and produce the goods that consumers need day in and day out" are desirable, and firms that investors should consider in tough times, he said.

Supermarkets Coles and Woolworths are examples of the kinds of companies that sell goods which consumers will continue to buy when recession hits, the fund manager said.

"Everyone's share price will go down but the more robust companies will survive," he said.

"What you don't want are companies with high levels of debt. You don't want concept stocks and you don't want those companies that need capital markets to keep going."

Mr Dive pointed out that the driver of any recession-related sell-off would be key. In the technology sell-off at the start of the century, the companies that were hit the hardest were the tech companies. "Other companies were dragged down but they recovered."

Emma Fisher, portfolio manager at Airlie Funds Management, was equally unconvinced a recession is near. "It's not our base case," she said, with the caveat: "we can't rule it out."

She has been monitoring the corporate mood during the August reporting season, which wraps up this week.

"What we have seen this reporting season is companies calling out weakness in the domestic economy."

Ms Fisher also holds the view that strong balance sheets are key in any recession and that investors should also look to own the dominant players in a sector.

"It can be a chance to increase market share," she said. "There can be a pretty big disparity between the number one and number two player in the same industry."

Companies with structural growth stories that don't rely on the economic cycle are also useful to own if and when recession hits, she said, giving blood products group CSL as an example.

"The businesses that you need to worry about in a recession are those that are reliant on debt and equity," she added.

Ben Clark, portfolio manager at TMS Capital, agreed there was only a slim likelihood of a domestic recession in the near term.

"I think that a recession is highly unlikely in the next year or two. Mainly because we have the cheapest interest rates in history," he said.

The Australian cash rate stands at a record low 1 per cent after the Reserve Bank of Australia cut rates in July to a fresh record low.

At the same time, he acknowledged that recession is a possibility. "If you were thinking recession and where you would want to be, you would want to be out of domestic cyclicals. There are more factors that are outside their control that would affect profits."

Debt levels are "always an issue".

Mr Clark pointed out that it is hard, if not impossible, to predict recessions as they are often the result of an economic shock.

"The biggest chance of a recession would be an offshore shock," he said.

Ms Fisher said "history tells us that recessions are mostly led by the US".

In this regard, there could be reason to worry. The US yield curve has inverted again this week and historically has been a signal pre-empting a recession.

The economic news has been downbeat from Europe as well. Data out mid-August showed that a slump in exports sent the German economy into reverse in the second quarter, with gross domestic product down 0.1 per cent quarter-on-quarter.

On an adjusted basis, the country's annual growth rate slowed to 0.4 per cent in the second quarter from 0.9 per cent in the first quarter.
direction: defensive rotation into consumer staples (Coles, Woolworths) amid broader market caution

headline: Airports tap Finkelstein in clash against airlines
text:
Airports have stepped up pressure on the federal government to ignore airlines' demands for regulation by bringing in former Federal Court Justice Ray Finkelstein to bat on their behalf.

Mr Finkelstein, who was president of the Australian Competition Tribunal between 2008 and 2011, has told the federal government it should stick with the current "light-handed" system as it prepares to release a final report on airport regulation from the Productivity Commission.

Calls from airlines for access to commercial arbitration during disputes were not necessary nor appropriate, Mr Finkelstein said in a memo to the federal government.

"If adopted, a rational airline will refer any controversial issue to arbitration because an award will rarely, if ever, result in a more onerous obligation than would be the product of a negotiated outcome," he said, adding that airlines would typically try arbitration "simply for the chance of doing better".

"The inefficiency of such a scheme goes without saying," he said.

Mr Finkelstein was paid by airports to review the commission's draft decision.

The government is due to release the commission's final report by October 22. It is not expected to differ significantly from the commission's draft report, which was released in February and rejected airlines' calls for access to independent arbitration to resolve disputes, saying it could have a "chilling effect" on investment.

Airlines have been lobbying the federal government to intervene - Qantas chief executive Alan Joyce and Virgin CEO Paul Scurrah jointly called for reform in September.

The airlines claim airports charge excessive fees and reap "super profits," and that consumers bear the brunt of high airport charges.

Airports say they do not set the prices charged by shops and car rental companies operating out of airports, whereas airlines have control over the prices they charge for food and drinks on flights.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission, which monitors how much money airports make but does not have the authority to regulate them, also wants airlines to have access to arbitration.

But Mr Finkelstein said government regulation was necessary only in instances of "market failure" and that the commission had concluded there was no misuse of market power by airports and that any benefit from arbitration was outweighed by the risks and costs of an arbitral regime.
direction: bullish for airport shares

headline: Risks in trading Trump's trade war
text:
After consulting numerous Chinese and US intelligence, defence and political experts, I believe the probabilities have shifted materially from the benign modal case in which China and the US resolve a trade deal towards much more polarised, or bi-modal, outcomes involving a deal or no deal with each contingency appearing equally likely. This could elevate volatility, which will beget investment opportunities.

The changing probabilities have emerged as Donald Trump seeks to straddle two conflicting schools of thought on how to manage the challenge posed by the rise of a totalitarian, one-party (increasingly, one-person) political system that seeks to dominate its domestic and foreign counterparties to secure and sustain its aspirational primacy.

Last year the central case was that a transactional, market-sensitive and myopic Trump - who is desperate to impress America's corporate oligarchs - would strive to secure a (pyrrhic?) trade victory heading into the 2020 election. It was assumed that this deal would be struck irrespective of whether it addressed the growing concerns China hawks like former Trump adviser Steve Bannon have regarding the Middle Kingdom's "predatory" behaviour.

By predatory, Bannon means the systematic program China has been implementing for decades to slingshot its economic and military development to the point where it is unassailable. This, of course, involves appropriating foreign technology, intellectual property and military expertise to enable China's ascendancy while the state unfairly subsidises local enterprise to obtain market share from offshore competitors playing by the rules of a different game (ie, fair and free trade).

The 2018 interpretation of Trump's mercenary character traits reflected the prevailing wisdom in the economics community that getting a deal done was more important to Trump than the substance of that deal. Whenever Trump deviated from the path espoused by this "Goldman Sachs consensus", he has been punished by markets. The hawks want Trump to resist his more impatient and hedonistic instincts to prevail in the longer-term strategic game. More precisely, they want him to weather immediate financial storms to preserve America's hegemony, which is predicated on the rules-based, liberal-democratic order where everyone competes on equal terms without extensive state subsidies and/or interference.

This is Bannon's eloquently articulated doctrine, which for the first time is gaining bipartisan currency throughout America. According to the hawks, China is engaged in a multi-decade mission to wrest economic, military and geo-political supremacy away from the US and its allies. The intensity of this mission has accelerated under President Xi Jinping, who is the most powerful and ideological Chinese leader since Mao, and in 2018 removed all term limits to establishing a perpetual legacy.

Xi's ideology asserts that China has always required an existential challenge, if not outright kinetic conflict, to galvanise its people to unite as one to overcome perceived threats to its existence where Xi himself, the Chinese state, the Chinese Communist Party and the Chinese people are increasingly fused into a single, indivisible entity.

The endgame is a utopian nirvana (socialism) that is never actually obtained. This objective is, however, the unifying ambition that binds the Chinese people together in support of maintaining the primacy of the Chinese Communist Party, which is a vehicle to preserve the power and control of several key Chinese dynasties, the current apex of which is represented by Xi.

Wittingly or unwittingly, Trump is the first modern leader to push back on China's quest in a credible and strategically thoughtful fashion. As one analyst remarked, it may just be the random coincidence of madness and strategy in Washington.

Hawks like Bannon think appeasing China has propagated Western decline as financial market elites accept sub-optimal deals that transfer enormous wealth to them personally while ceding wealth creation opportunities to China over the long run. And they believe that the West must act promptly in the finite window it has left to excise this cancer from the global economy.

Bannon's project, which has gained unprecedented traction among Democrat and Republican leaders during Trump's term, advocates "decoupling" the Western and Chinese economies as the least-worst option given China's irreversible course. This means tariffs are positive because they work to reverse China's competitive cost advantages and encourage rivals to develop substitutable products. This is why we are hearing more and more about shifting essential supply chains out of China into alternative domains.

A trade deal could still be struck by Trump if markets go into free-fall and the Goldman Sachs consensus prevails and/or Xi realises he has made a mistake by precipitating decoupling, which will deny China the incredible prosperity that it has extracted from the capitalist system that is the essential source of its power and influence.

Notwithstanding the apparent loss of face, the option still exists for Xi to sign up to some sort of comprehensive deal, but then gradually back out of it over time and/or stealthily cheat, which is arguably the optimal choice right now.

I still, therefore, attach a 50 per cent probability to a deal being done, one way or another.

There is an equal probability that no deal is done either because China miscalculates and refuses to sign up to one because Xi welcomes a nationally unifying crisis, or because Trump becomes enamoured with the political benefits of being seen to use decoupling to save the US economy from being disintermediated by its most important geopolitical rival.

The probability of this second scenario has increased since Joe Biden, a China dove, entered the presidential race and claimed that China "was not bad" and "not competition for us". This has emboldened Trump to wedge Biden on America's China choice, which Trump thinks will play out in his favour at the 2020 election.SI

The author is a portfolio manager with Coolabah Capital Investments, which invests in fixed-income securities including those discussed by this column.
direction: increased volatility and uncertainty for risk assets

headline: From here to eternity
text:
Can residential property be a collector’s item? It’s not such a strange idea when you consider the rise of boutique developments designed to outshine existing stock and become iconic in their own right.

Located a five-minute drive from the beach and only 13 kilometres from the CBD, Avenueis the latest project from developer Skelton and is poised to deliver a dose of designer living toa quiet pocket of exclusive Brighton East.

Skelton managing director Michael Skelton describes Avenue as a “passion-led project”. While he expects it to be popular with locals and those looking for an “outdoors lifestyle and access to schools”, he is also hoping to attract buyers with an appetite for high-end design.

“With Avenue, I’m not looking for speculators or people who don’t intend to hold this property for a substantial time,” he says.

“The detail and thought placed into Avenue positions it toward the traits of a collector. Timing-wise, a purchaser missing out on Avenue can’t simply wait one to two years when the broader market has shifted and purchase something similar, because opportunitieslike these are exceptionally rare.”

A collection of nine, three-storey townhomes to be launched in July, Avenue is the visionof architects ADDARC, a Melbourne firm challenging traditional design.

Architect Rohan Appel has used familiar forms and materials in a contemporary way with refined detailing in a bid to ensure Avenue sits comfortably within its surrounds. “The material choices of brickwork, charred timber and zinc cladding are enhanced by the changing daylight across their surfaces,” says Appel.

The main bedroom is the hero in the design, with its cathedral ceilings pitching up to 4.4 metres and the full-height glazing flooding the space with an abundance of dappled light.

In the kitchen ADDARC have used full-height timber grain cupboards to provide storage space and a sense of warmth, as well as a stone wall splashback, and induction cooking.

Another standout feature is the option to personalise the basement configuration. Buyers can choose a large, multi-purpose room fora cinema or gym plus a two-car garage, ora three-car garage with a workshop or wine cellar, says Skelton.

Outside, award-winning landscape architect Jack Merlo has created a strong connection between the built form and the landscaped environment.

Starting with the main pedestrian pathway, where a green arbour enriches the arrival,and continuing through each townhome, the landscaping will include evergreen screening plants, flowering shrubs and climbers that bring seasonal change, and blooms year round.
direction: bullish for boutique residential property developers and high-end real estate

headline: Rear Window
text:
Blue Sky's new risk boss a chip off the old block

It might be a profoundly unwise place to park your money, but for fodder, Blue Sky Alternative Investments never, ever disappoints.

On Thursday, the Enron of Eagle Street announced that its interim chief risk officer since August, Andrew Werro, was staying in the role permanently.

Werro's biography is largely comprehensive: he's been a consultant on wealth remediation to HSBC and spent a decade at Macquarie Group, including as chief financial officer of its retail bank. He was even communications director at APN News and Media (now Here, There & Everywhere). But oddly, he was also CFO and company secretary for "a listed regional TV company".

So why not name it? Which one? Prime? Southern Cross? Nope. Turns out it was Media Corporation Australia, which in 2003 (while Werro was its CFO and company secretary), was delisted by the ASX for failing to pay its listing fees! It was wound up in 2006 after the company conducted a 2004 buyback it never paid the shareholders for.

Yep, this is the guy the post-Rob Shand Blue Sky, now tortuously rehabilitating, has put in charge of risk management. Pure, solid gold.

Meanwhile, Blue Sky's former chief investment officer Alex McNab has set up his own shop, VSB Capital. You would have to be a thrill seeker to give him so much as a sunburnt coin but, hey, this is Queensland we're talking about. He'll probably raise hatfuls!

Only two weeks back, the investment heads of Blue Sky's water fund Michael Blakeney and Nick Waters quit, with group CEO Kim Morison describing their departure as "disappointing."

He — like his shareholders — must be familiar with the feeling.

PINCHMe blames its own out-of-date prospectus for downgrade

In its three months on the ASX, PINCHMe has shed 68.8 per cent of its value. So when it announced a few days ago that FY18 revenues would be 13.5 per cent lower than projected, its already-savaged share price was immovable.

The free sample review business had an unusual reason for the downgrade. "When we set out prospectus forecast early in the calendar year, we were not anticipating a prolonged IPO listing process," its announcement states. This process, it seems, was a "significant distraction for management and took material time away from growing the business".

Such an excuse would be plausible if PINCHMe had listed sometime in April. But PINCHMe's prospectus is dated September 13, and sets out a timeline for listing in mid-October (which is exactly what happened). Now, by PINCHMe's own admission, the forecasts in that prospectus were by September already several months out of date, having been set out "early in the calendar year" and seemingly not revised before being sent out months later to investors!

PINCHMe is helmed by Jeremy Reid, of Everest Financial fame. His colourful early history in Australian public markets culminated in a two-year ASIC ban in 2013. But this wasn't an issue for PINCHMe's wealthy early investors, who include carsales.com co-founder Walter Piscotta, Josh Liberman's LJCB Holdings, Paul Little, Kerry and Ryan Stokes, and David Droga. Fingers crossed they're still happy to back the start-up: it'll need all the support it can get.

PINCHMe's downgrade included notice that its cash-on-hand at December 31 was $2.7 million, after it raised $8 million from the offer. Even at the time of the prospectus, it had forecast a net loss of $3.6 million for the full year. Within 12 months of listing, it was always going to need to raise more capital (or debt). After the past few weeks, how easy do you reckon that will be?

Shorten should retire the working-class hero schtick

Is there anything more nauseating than a member of Australia's elite proclaiming themselves lacking in influence?

Take Bill Shorten's latest bout of mythmaking. "When you're on the Labor side, the working people's side, you don't own the big banks, you don't own the big private health insurers and you don't own the big media, so that's sort of a fact of life," he told the ABC last week, in comments reported amid revelations he'd refused an invitation to meet Rupert Murdoch in the United States.

We can respect Shorten breaking the tradition that sees Australian political leaders of both stripes genuflecting to the Sun King (it hardly worked out well for Kevin Rudd anyway). But the idea of Shorten as some kind of downtrodden outsider is rather rich (pun intended).

This working-class hero went to leafy Kew's Xavier College! His first marriage was to the daughter of a multimillionaire Liberal politician he met while both studied for their MBAs.

His second (current) marriage was to the daughter of the then-governor-general, who he met while he was a minister and she did PR for the resources industry. He's wined and dined with billionaires. But even before that, since university - or perhaps earlier, when his parents scrimped and saved to send Bill and his investment banker of a twin brother to the Jesuits - Shorten has been on the path to power. And he's already had (and mixed with) his share of it.

As for not owning big stakes in corporate Australia, Shorten spent the nine years to 2007 (when he was elected to Parliament) as a director of AustralianSuper. You'd be hard-pressed to find a more powerful investor, nor one more keen to make its influence felt. It literally owns large stakes in Australia's biggest companies, including the big four banks. It's a private equity investor in private health insurance. True, we can't accuse Ian Silk or his moustache of calling the shots at "big media" like News Corp or Nine. But the fund he runs does own some media in super-funded and increasingly popular news site The New Daily.

Labor's come a long way since the 1930s. Many of its political representatives are now more comfortable in the boardroom than on the shop floor. And Shorten, the nation's presumptive prime minister, has less claim to the working-class script than most of them. He could pay the nation the compliment of not overestimating its stupidity with such breathtaking bull.
direction: Blue Sky Alternative Investments shares likely to decline

text:
Kevin Rudd: man of letters

It's customary, in December, to reflect upon what we've most cherished about this year. In that spirit, have we ever told you how much we've enjoyed Kevin Rudd's prodigious letter-writing campaign?

The Asia Society president had another missive in The Guardian on Wednesday. In it, he objected to an exceedingly brief reference by David Marr to his government having "collapsed". "Did my leadership collapse? No. It was torn down," Rudd thundered, reminding readers he'd been overthrown by a "premeditated coup". As if anyone could forget.

It was the sixth letter Rudd has had published by a major Australian newspaper this month alone, defending policies as diverse as the ill-fated Carbon Pollution Reduction Scheme, the 2008 apology to indigenous Australians and his advocacy for a larger Australian population.

Unsurprisingly for a man infamous for his work ethic, completing a PhD still leaves Rudd plenty of time for other interests. So prominent is his letter writing that it has even prompted knowing commentary from other readers, like "Sarah of Watsonia" in the Herald Sun (November 26), who sent in a text remarking that "I've had many texts unpublished, but I've found the solution ... sign off as Kevin Rudd, New York."

But some of Rudd's most glorious letters aren't in the Australian press at all. Take November's 193-word rebuke in The Independent to a claim by one John Doherty that given Australia's costly experience rolling out the national broadband network, voters in the United Kingdom should be wary of Jeremy Corbyn's plan to roll out blanket fibre internet.

"Any comparison to Australia's experience is facile in the extreme," wrote Rudd, after a detailed examination of the differences in approach. And who is John Doherty? Just some poor sod from Stratford-upon-Avon who dared pen a 79-word letter to The Independent, published a few days earlier.

It's clearly a sore point for Rudd, who spent much of February railing at this newspaper on the very same topic (including through a feisty 585-word response to a piece written by your male columnist). Still, in days of yore, it's doubtful an armchair commentator in rural Britain could have provoked a former prime minister of Australia to furious rebuttal. Thomas Friedman was right: the world really is flat.

More Afterpay advisers in the money

Further to the gay abandon with which Afterpay Touch chief executive Anthony Eisen has been spraying around "employee" share options to the company's consultants and other hangers-on, we should note that Clinton administration Treasury Secretary Larry Summers isn't the only US Advisory Board member to have 100,000 of those bad boys firmly in his skyrocket.

But while Summers' are underwater (exercisable at $31.90, so worthless while Afterpay's share price hovers at $29.57), his co-panellists Matt Kaness and Uli Becker are both comfortably in the money.

Kaness, a former chief strategy officer at Urban Outfitters, was granted his in September last year, exercisable at $16.96. He converted and sold 33,336 of them on October 16, collaring (based on that day's close) $1.13 million for the marvellous price of $565,378. And the top rate of US capital gains tax is now 20 per cent. Kaness' remaining options vest progressively to September 2020. With Afterpay having recruited Urban Outfitters as a marquee merchant, Kaness was worth every paper dollar fired in his general direction through Eisen's scrip firehose.

Becker, Reebok's former CEO, got himself a strike price of $19.80 upon his grant on March 22 but the first third of his options don't vest until February 2020.

The options' allocation date for both Becker and Kaness reveals precisely how legitimate this advisory board truly is. It was announced on October 9, the day after the company's former boss David Hancock resigned from the board. Eisen had bought Summers' endorsement on September 25 (the same day Afterpay received its interim audit ordered by AUSTRAC).

He then browsed through his contacts and simply prevailed upon two advisers he already had on his magical share drip feed. Just another Eisen lesson in deluxe window dressing. Boy can that man dance.

Farallon gets better of Whitehaven shareholders

When it comes to block trades in Australian-listed companies, it's almost permanently a vendor's market!

San Francisco hedge fund Farallon Capital Management was, until November 14, Whitehaven Coal's largest shareholder, having inherited nearly 10 per cent of the miner in 2013 as a creditor of Nathan Tinkler and having built the long-term stake to 146 million shares or 14.2 per cent of the register.

Last month it sold 95 million of those shares at $3.22 each through UBS, including 9.2 million of them to Whitehaven director (and Farallon representative) Ray Zage.

Farallon's remaining 51 million shares are escrowed for 60 days. Not that anyone's counting down the days to throw bad money after worse - especially Zage, we bet.

Three weeks later, on December 5, Whitehaven cut its production and profit guidance, sending the stock down to $2.63; it's settled at $2.65, 17 per cent below Farallon's price.

Of particular amusement was the downgrade's rationale, being: "challenges being experienced sourcing skilled operators for Maules Creek, Whitehaven's largest operation" and "impacts arising from dust events related to severe and ongoing drought conditions".

Is the company seriously implying there's been an abrupt exodus from the Gunnedah Basin of working-age adults with heavy vehicle permits?

Given the region's alternative career offerings - cooking meth at grandma's (while she's licking stamps for board at Bupa's maltreatment home) or repacking Qantas headsets in Tamworth Correctional Centre - there should be a queue the length of Narrabri High Street for gigs pulling $150,000 to drive a dump truck. Perhaps there are just no licensed locals left who'd pass the piss test?
direction: neutral to bearish for financial markets

headline: CROWN, HO ROLL DICE
text:
Gambling Both sides in a $1.8 billion casino deal face a very Sydney dilemma, writes Neil Chenoweth.

Politicians hate investigations of all kinds, so when the NSW Independent Liquor and Gaming Authority last Thursday announced an inquiry into Lawrence Ho's buy into Crown Resorts, the first question was why. Was the government pushed, or did it jump?
