headline: BIG MOVE BACK HOME
text:
Pandemic Up to a fifth of Australia's highly skilled expats have returned this year, writes Sally Patten.

Shane Masters lived offshore for 13 years and had tried twice before to move back to Australia before finally succeeding this year, prompted, in part, by the spread of the coronavirus.

"I've got a great gig. I'm happy," says Masters, who manages the South Australian VC Fund, a $50 million venture capital fund backed by the state government.

Masters is one of thousands of Australian professionals who have returned this year, either permanently or temporarily, against the backdrop of COVID-19. Indeed about one in five of Australia's highly skilled expat community has come home in 2020, according to preliminary findings of a survey by Advance, a platform that connects Australians around the world.

Of those, 19 per cent are looking for a new job, 18 per cent are working remotely for their offshore employer, 15 per cent are building their own business and 10 per cent have transferred to the Australian office of their current employer. Nearly a third have found a new job locally.

Thirty per cent of returned expats are here to stay, 21 per cent intend to leave as soon as the international border opens, and 33 per cent would like to move back offshore at a later date, the survey found. "Children and ageing parents changes the equation of living overseas. Expats have always thought they were 24 hours away by plane. Now they know they are not," says Yasmin Allen, chairman of Advance and a director of Cochlear, ASX and Santos.

Allen says the stream of highly skilled expats returning to Australia is great for the local economy. When expats are hired into senior roles, they create jobs further down the organisation, they have the skills and experience to help Australian companies expand and they understand Australia culturally.

"A lot of people who go overseas are entrepreneurial. That's exactly what we want in Australia. They are risk-takers and companies have to take some risks so they can grow," she says.

"And if they are Australian, they understand the culture. So you get the best of their overseas experience and ideas and ways of doing business, and they can bring that back in the Australian context.

"They have a broad knowledge. That is exactly what we want."

Allen may well have been talking about Jacyln Lee-Joe, who was the chief marketing officer of streaming giant Netflix, based in Los Angeles, until she found herself in Sydney on a holiday with her family when the shutters came down over the international border. Unlike Masters, Lee-Joe is in the "looking for a job" category, although she is consulting in the meantime.

Despite the skills and experience that many expats and former expats have built during their offshore careers, landing senior roles back home can be far from easy. In a separate survey by Advance, 83 per cent of human resources respondents said they were reluctant or cautious about recommending returning Australians for local roles. About 35 per cent said that recruiting Australians was more trouble than it was worth. Half of returning Australians who had found a new job had done so through a professional or personal connection.

James Low is a director in the customer services hub at Westpac, working on a contract. Low, who has a bachelor of engineering and an MBA, lived in London for 15 years and had heard the horror stories about expats finding it difficult to find jobs in Australia. About two years ago, while he was working at Lloyds Bank, Low started meeting people in the local financial services industry when he was back in Australia on holidays. It was thanks to those contacts that he was offered the contract position at Westpac, which he started in January and where he is responsible for digitising and simplifying the bank's mortgage broker business.

Low, originally from Perth, says he is looking for a permanent role. "Relationships are definitely a lot more important here. It seems easier to be tapped on the shoulder if you have a good network, which makes it harder for expats who have been away for an extended period of time. In the larger UK market, it is not as easy for everyone to know each other so it's actually less about who you know, and jobs are advertised," Low says.

"Recruiters [in Australia] don't necessarily value the breadth that is gained overseas. That type of experience helps with leadership and judgment, which is really important in a world that is changing." There are also fewer questions from recruiters that relate to an executive's adaptability and resilience, he adds.

Job difficulties aside, Low is happy and relieved to be back in Australia.

He and his wife had been planning to return before their oldest child started school and came back late last year, before COVID-19 hit.

"I definitely feel sympathy for those that can't return at the moment," Low says. "Right now with COVID, we have got to think of ourselves as lucky. Career-wise it does have an impact, but right now just being back is a blessing. With COVID as a backdrop, we are glad we are home with family."

The first time Masters tried to return to live in Australia was in 2010, five years after he left. He had been working at global law firm Linklaters in London and Stockholm, and landed a job at Baker & Mackenzie in Sydney. From there, Masters went to RMWilliams Agricultural Holdings, but when that didn't work out, he headed back offshore to complete an executive MBA.

Masters tried again to return to Australia after his EMBA at French school HEC and armed with experience in California working for AGR Partners, a fund that takes minority stakes in food and agribusiness companies.

"I came and tried to interview for jobs. I didn't have much of a network or community in Australia. It was great in the US, great in Europe but I was neglecting the fact that you have got to have people here who will be your advocate," Masters says.

So last year he embarked on a five-year plan with the goal of moving back to his home state of South Australia. The idea was to save enough money and expand his network so he could set up a business - while making useful contacts to support his role at French company Unigrains, where he was investing in agribusinesses. One thing led to another, as they say, and earlier this year Masters was offered a position at asset manager Artesian, responsible for the management of the South Australian VC Fund. He was about to be transferred by Unigrains to the US but decided to head south instead.

"I was concerned that COVID would wipe out economies. I thought: 'There is going to be a massive potential impact. So that is going to potentially put Australia back into recession and then if I want to actually get back in the next three years that could be really, really difficult. So it is either now, or I may never get another opportunity to get a role back in South Australia'," says Masters.

After his departure from the CEO role at ailing department store chain Myer, Richard Umbers joined German discount retailer Kaufland, based in Stuttgart. His role was to support the group's entry into Australia, but in January, Kauflandabandoned its plans to launch in Australia.Umbers returned to Australia in March and has been living at Redhill on the Mornington Peninsula, south of Melbourne, ever since. "It wasn't a bad decision to come back to Australia," he says.

"Necessarily the frame of reference you've got now is changing. I can imagine that there are many Australians now who are stranded overseas who, given their circumstances overseas, perhaps they're even losing their jobs.

"Given the circumstances, they would be very keen to return and so the sooner we do get those open borders and for people be able to move, I think it will be good for Australia, " Umbers says, although he is mindful of opening up in such a way that would trigger more lockdowns.

He is currently a consultant to a major Australian retailer, although he declines to say which one. When restrictions on movement ease, Umbers plans to move to a recently purchased apartment in Melbourne and would eventually like to move back in to executive life. Originally from the UK, Umbers says that while he was in Germany, he remained in touch with Australia.

"I've always kept very connected. I consider myself a global Australian and I have wide connections. Particularly in a business like large format retail, it's very international business. And so I keep track of the markets and all the countries and indeed supply chains up into Asia," Umbers says.

Masters finishes his discussion with AFR BOSS with a piece of advice for Australian companies, which currently face severe obstacles in the form of a closed international border when it comes to expanding in overseas markets. There are thousands of Australians living offshore who could act as a bridge to new markets.

"I think there's this huge opportunity for Australian companies to connect with expats. Those people who are going to be advocates, or are able to help in this period of not being able to travel and get face to face.

"There's a lot people out there who I think are willing soldiers for whatever it is you want to sell or buy or grow."AFR
direction: supportive for domestic venture capital and skilled labor sectors

headline: $A could rally even if RBA cuts rates, starts QE
text:
The Reserve Bank may cut interest rates and even introduce a large-scale bond buying programme today, but this historic step into unconventional policy might prove a disappointment if the aim is to weaken the currency.

"If the Australian dollar ends higher on a day when the RBA has announced a suite of additional measures, it will be hard to see them breaking open the champagne," said Ray Attrill, head of foreign exchange strategy at National Australia Bank.

One of the central bank's key motivations for introducing purchases of longer-term government bonds is widely thought to be to try to lower the value of the Australian dollar.

While the RBA has not yet explicitly said it will buy more bonds, Reserve Bank officials have been talking for the last month about the pros and cons of bond buying in the same breath as the desirability of a weaker currency to aid the economic recovery.

Markets have listened and the Australian dollar is already almost 2.5 per cent below where it was a month ago, sitting at US70.09¢. The 10-year yields on Australian government bonds fell about 8 per cent in October to trade at 0.81 per cent on Monday.

The magnitude of the fall in the currency is signalling to Mr Attrill that a widely expected 0.15 percentage point cut in three key RBA policy rates, to 0.1 per cent, is fully priced into markets.

And the declines at the longer end of the yield curve have been made in anticipation of RBA bond buying, he said, believing the RBA will need to announce somewhere in the region of $140 billion in new purchases to justify that drop in yield.

As such, the markets would need a dovish surprise to push the Australian dollar lower. But Mr Attrill is not expecting an aggressive bond programme and said the expansion of the RBA's balance sheet, while historic, "is probably going to look relatively timid" compared with global peers.

Morgan Stanley currency strategist David Adams agreed that the "risk skew is towards a hawkish surprise, not a dovish one".

"We see scope for the RBA to underdeliver on asset purchases relative to market expectations," he said. "An open-ended purchase program with limited operational detail probably fails to reach the market's bar."

Sean Callow, senior currency strategist at Westpac, believes there's the potential for a "knee-jerk rally" if the RBA announces it will buy more bonds "but doesn't put an exciting total on it like $100 billion".

He is expecting any rally in the Australian dollar to be brief.

Still, Westpac is retaining its forecasts of US75¢ by the end of this calendar year and US80¢ by the end of 2021. And NAB is also sticking with its US80¢ forecast by the end of next year.

Mr Attrill said NAB's forecast was more closely aligned to the bank's view on likely weakness in the US dollar than to drivers of the Australian dollar.

"In our view, no matter who wins the US election, the US dollar is going down," he said. "Whatever anyone else does in terms of QE, the Federal Reserve is going to match it," he said.

The Australian dollar also tends to be heavily influenced by perceptions of economic growth and the level of risk in global markets.

As such, "there are no guarantees the Australian dollar will not appreciate even in the face of quantitative easing," said Wilsons strategist David Cassidy.

"The RBA is not conducting its monetary policy in a vacuum - its balance sheet is tiny in a global context - and our expectation of a progressive recovery in the local and global economy should ultimately place some upward pressure on the Australian dollar."

Wilsons is retaining its US75¢ 12-month target for the Australian dollar against the US dollar.
direction: AUD weakness and potential disappointment for rate-cut expectations

headline: Black box thinking may work better than lockdowns
text:
Coronavirus suppression Is tracking and tracing alone more effective? We need further evidence before we shut down communities and entire economies.

As Victoria turns to another hard lockdown in its attempts to flatten the COVID-19 curve, there has been an understandable focus on the costs being incurred during these enforced shutdowns - perhaps $1 billion a week in Victoria's case. What we haven't seen yet is a thorough evidence-based analysis of the effectiveness of lockdowns and what their contribution is to saving lives and getting the economy running again.

Do lockdowns in any form actually work? Are hard lockdowns producing better health, economic and societal outcomes than softer lockdowns or no lockdowns? If you compare Sweden (no lockdown) with Britain (a hard three-month lockdown), it seems Sweden had a better outcome economically and in terms of fatalities.

Or are governments better off channelling some of the billions of dollars that are being spent on lockdowns to other seemingly effective strategies such as testing, tracing and isolation?

The history of lockdowns of this sort is all quite recent. China used a draconian lockdown model in Wuhan and elsewhere to slow the virus' spread and to try to stop second and third waves. While there have been further cases, China does seem to have had some success.

What's unclear is whether this success was based on the lockdown or on more effective testing and tracing as Chinese officials fine-tuned their response. Nor is it clear if a democratic society can convince people to accept such drastic restrictions.

New Zealand also had a hard and long lockdown, but achieved a similar outcome to Queensland, which until this week had much softer and shorter restrictions by comparison. Both, however, seemed effective at testing and tracing.

Elsewhere in the world, the evidence on lockdown effectiveness to date seems inconclusive. Robert VerBruggen, a journalist at US publication National Review, looked at many studies and feedback on lockdowns and found that some seemed to work and others didn't.

While there is some correlation between imposing some sort of lockdown (soft or hard, wide or narrow) and flattening the curve, there is no proof of causation: that is, it is unclear whether it was the lockdown that worked, or the success in slowing the virus' spread was based more on the testing, tracing and isolation regimes.

For example, did NZ and Queensland produce similar outcomes because they had similarly successful testing, tracing and isolation programs alongside differing degrees of lockdown?

We know that most epidemiologists predict second, third and possibly fourth waves of infection unless an effective vaccine is developed. This is when lockdowns will have to start all over again.

Can these measures be justified, compared with the alternative of investing much more in widespread testing, efficient tracing and isolating those infected - learning to live with the virus while flattening the curve? Had Victoria done this in the first wave, a second hard lockdown may not have been needed, even if lockdowns did help.

It would be more prudent to direct tens of millions of dollars towards a large-scale testing program (test everyone in Melbourne every two weeks), tracing contacts (using thousands of trained contact tracers) and effective isolation (hotels or hospitals for the infected, and home for their contacts). These resources will then also be available for the next wave of the virus.

We could also borrow a strategy used in the animal world and implement a more localised zonal lockdown system, as former veterinarian Matt Makin has advocated. This is the main strategy employed now in Britain and Europe.

Health officials cannot yet rely on a vaccine, and seem to be turning to what is now becoming conventional wisdom - lockdowns - despite the apparent lack of clear evidence of their success.

Once, bloodletting was conventional medical wisdom. For about 1700 years, this technique was used by respected doctors to treat all types of disease. There was no evidence it worked, but it was accepted as the best course of treatment.

In his book Black Box Thinking, author Matthew Syed highlights the contrast between the airline industry and the medical field in their willingness to embrace evidence-based science. When something goes wrong in the air, there is a heavy reliance on data as well as independent investigators and regulators to ensure it doesn't happen again. The data is obtained from the almost indestructible black box recorders fitted to all commercial aircraft.

Syed writes: "If ever there is an air accident, the first thing that happens is the data from these flight recorders is released publicly so the reason for the accident can be established and procedures can be examined and if necessary changed immediately so the same air crash never happens again.

"In 2013, there were 36.4 million commercial flights worldwide carrying more than 3 billion passengers and only 204 people died in air accidents - a rate of one accident per 2.4 million flights. In 2014, that number fell even lower, with one accident for every 8.3 million flights."

Syed compares this approach with healthcare to cite a very different system for reviewing strategies and, in some cases, errors : "In 2013, the prestigious Journal of Patient Safety published a study which estimated that around 400,000 patients a year die in American hospitals alone through avoidable harm . . . 400,000 patients each year is the equivalent of two jumbo jets falling out of the sky every 24 hours every day of the year."

What may work with COVID-19 is government transparency, and bringing the community on board with workable strategies backed by scientific evidence and data - effectively, black box thinking.

The key suppression actions are now well known - social distancing, cleanliness, isolation if sick, and the wearing of masks, especially indoors. This needs to be accompanied by widespread testing and accurate contact tracing, regardless of cost, and then isolation of infected people and significant contacts.

Business and other affected organisations must have a say in decisions on lockdowns and border closures. At the moment it seems our leaders get all their advice from health officials, with no input from communities or business. Clearly, we have to balance the enormous cost of lockdowns with their efficacy. Do they work? We don't think so. If there is evidence they do, let's see that evidence and understand it.

Graham Turner is chief executive of Flight Centre.
direction: bearish for economy-sensitive sectors and equities

headline: State's borderline cruelty
text:
Queensland's refusal to let a Canberra woman bury her father has inflamed the dispute over arbitrary border closures, escalating a push from government and business for the adoption of a nationally consistent system of coronavirus hotspots, rather than blanket bans.

It has also further poisoned relations between Scott Morrison and Queensland Premier Annastacia Palaszczuk, who accused the Prime Minister of bullying and intimidating her after he rang to plead the case of the woman, named Sarah Caisip.

In doing so, Ms Palaszczuk, who is expected to keep her borders closed to NSW and the ACT until at least the October 31 state election, absolved herself of any responsibility for the funeral ban by revealing she had delegated powers over borders to her Chief Health Office, Jeanette Young.

"It was not my decision," the Premier said.

The fresh explosion coincided with the launch of a new campaign by Qantas which involved writing to selected state and federal MPs in Queensland, Western Australia, Tasmania and South Australia, urging them to open up to each other and other low-risk jurisdictions such as the ACT and NSW, by adopting a nationally consistent set of border protocols.

"We have a situation where there are large numbers of states and territories that have had zero cases and they're not even open to each other," said Qantas chief executive Alan Joyce.

"Western Australia, South Australia, Northern Territory, Queensland. Tasmania, we've got closure there still with very low cases, no cases, and it's been like that for a while and we don't have any determination of when the borders will open."

The airline and tourism industry are also frustrated with Dr Young, who they believe is overly cautious .

One industry source said that at a Tourism and Events Economic Recovery Group meeting a week ago, convened by the Queensland government and attended by dozens of industry representatives, Dr Young expressed alarm at the numbers of people flying from Sydney to Ballina for a holiday in Byron Bay.

Even though the tourists were still in NSW, Dr Young noted they were "too close for comfort", a comment which gave those in the room little hope that Queensland would be open any time soon.

Mr Morrison said that it made no sense to not let Ms Caisip attend her father's funeral given she came from Canberra, which had not had a coronavirus infection for 62 days and had not had a case of community transmission for 145 days.

She had tried to visit her father before he died but approval took 20 days.

Mr Morrison said the focus should have been on the family, not him or Ms Palaszczuk. He said Australia should not lose its humanity.

"This was just one day I hoped that something different could be done," he said.

"Today just hurt. Sarah doesn't get today back, she never gets it back."

Queensland designates Canberra a hotspot because it has porous borders with NSW. Under a hotspot model Mr Morrison wants the states to adopt by Christmas, the ACT would be a no-risk jurisdiction.

After much lobbying, Ms Caisip was allowed only to view her father's body but had to be dressed in full protective clothing and escorted from her hotel quarantine by the police. Ms Caisip had travelled to Brisbane to see her gravely ill father but was in mandatory quarantine when he died.

Dr Young made no apology for her decision not to allow Ms Caisip to attend the funeral, saying she did not want to establish a precedent for compassionate cases.

Similarly, the Palaszczuk government is allowing just one of four siblings in NSW to visit their dying father in Queensland.

"I am extremely risk averse, and I'm not going to ever do anything that puts the Queensland population at risk," she said.

She defended, however, the entry granted to the AFL hierarchy and movie star Tom Hanks because they brought money to the state. They, too, had to quarantine, she said.

"I have given class exemptions to people in the sporting industry for a whole range of codes because it is important that we start that work, but they all go into quarantine," she said.

"I have given exemptions for people in entertainment and film because that is bringing a lot of money into this state and, can I say, we need every single dollar in our state.

"We need to make sure that our economy is going ahead as much as it can, as long as it is safe."

Mr Morrison said that was a double standard that should annoy even Queenslanders.

Qantas has vowed to fight to open the borders where safe to do so and expects other industry players to join the Qantas campaign. It has also asked its 20,000 stood-down workers to sign a petition.

Yesterday, as six states and territories recorded no new cases of coronavirus and NSW recorded seven, Qantas urged the states to develop nationally consistent protocols for border closures that were "balanced and proportionate with defined thresholds informed by medical advice for the safe reopening of internal borders, excluding hotspot areas as determined by health authorities".

"Arbitrary border restrictions are having a profound economic and social cost to communities, business and supply chains in Queensland," says one letter sent to the member for Cairns, Michael Healy.

He rejected the appeal outright.
direction: bearish for airlines and domestic travel stocks

headline: Perth-based GenusPlus taps two brokers for $150m IPO
text:
One of the country's biggest powerline and power substation construction and maintenance businesses is poised to hit the ASX boards, after bulking up with a slew of acquisitions in the past two years.

Street Talk understands Perth-based GenusPlus - which counts the likes of BHP, Rio Tinto, Telstra and Optus as clients - has tapped Ashanti Capital and Bell Potter for a $150 million float, expected in the first quarter of 2021.

Sources said while the deal was still a few months away, the company was tipped to raise somewhere between $40 million and $50 million for its initial public offering.

Officially formed in 2017, it is understood GenusPlus has grown its revenue from nothing to around $300 million in three years. The company helps its clients build and maintain their powerline infrastructure, substations and transmission towers.

Prior to forming GenusPlus, the David Riches-founded business was known as Powerlines Plus, and offered powerline construction and maintenance services in remote parts of the country.

GenusPlus was created three years ago when Powerlines Plus acquired Complete Cabling and also established another division named Proton Power, which provides engineering services for high-voltage power cables.

The company has made five more acquisitions since, including national electrical contractor EC&M late last year.

Riches still runs the business alongside his brother Matt and is supported by a board that includes ex-Macmahon and Ausdrill chief financial officer Jose Martins and former chief executive of Southern Cross Electrical Engineers and AusGroup Simon High.

GenusPlus' operations are focused on the West Australian market, however, it is understood while listing preparations are ticking over in the background it is also plotting its expansion into New South Wales and Victoria.
direction: bullish for GenusPlus shares

headline: Schools good with tech says OECD, but not so the teachers
text:
Australian schools and students have high-quality digital technology but teachers are not utilising it and in some cases schools are not encouraging teachers to learn how to use it, according to the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development.

Data from the 2018 Program for International Student Assessment (PISA) shows only 36 per cent of teachers are given incentives to use digital devices in schools.

That was well below the OECD average and below countries such as Jordan, Malaysia and Portugal.

Top of the list for integrating digital devices into classes were Taiwan, Lithuania and Slovenia.

Although the survey was done before the shift to online owing to COVID-19, the Centre for Independent Studies says the findings should be read as a warning since they reveal underlying poor attitudes towards technology in the classroom.

Research fellow, education program at the CIS, Glenn Fahey, said Australia has plenty of resources for online learning but it does not have the right leverage.

"Online teaching is patchy, delayed and inconsistent. It seems to me teachers and schools are reluctant to make this transition in a productive way. Teachers are unlikely to have formal training in digital teaching and they're unlikely to get professional development in it.

"Anecdotally, unions have a real aversion to education innovation. They don't want things to change, they're slow to the party."

Among other findings, less than 60 per cent of teachers said they had sufficient time to prepare lessons integrating digital devices, which was also well below the OECD average and only 72 per cent of principals said they had resources for teachers to learn how to use digital equipment.

"Australia has one of the lowest levels of offering incentives to teachers to incorporate online into teaching. That's things like allowing online assessment, using software to enhance marking. Or making professional development geared to what is innovative and new, not just ticking boxes to meet the hours."

Unexpectedly, the absence of incentives for teachers to use digital devices was worst in schools that are classed as heavily disadvantaged or remote. Only about 20 per cent of teachers in disadvantaged schools said they were encouraged to use digital technology in classes.

Mr Fahey said this was despite the redistribution of education money to less well-off schools since the Gonski One and Gonski Two reports. He said it was hard to gauge how money was being spent on technology in many schools but the disadvantage gap was still very wide.

"We are probably not as clever as we thought in providing solutions to those gap problems," he said.

Although Australian students had above average access to computers at home, above average bandwidth and high-powered computers, they were well below average in having "access to a quiet place to study" at home.

During the COVID-19 shutdown schools had ignored the importance of giving students somewhere to work.

Schools everywhere, except the Northern Territory, are open only for children of essential workers.

It was wrong to keep kids out of school when schools provided vacant places for students to study quietly, even if classes were online, Mr Fahey said.
direction: bearish for education technology and edtech-related shares

headline: Lendlease first to strike CFMEU deal
text:
Lendlease has become the first tier-one builder to reach a deal with the Construction, Forestry, Maritime, Mining and Energy Union on its controversial NSW agreement but has achieved concessions on pay rises and fixed days off.

The top-tier builder lodged its in-principle agreement with the building industry watchdog yesterday, but it reduced the CFMEU's proposed base rate increases from 5 per cent a year to 3 per cent, for an overall rise of 3.5 per cent including allowances and redundancy contributions.

Lendlease also agreed to a more relaxed fixed rostered-days-off calendar that will require sites to be shut down once a month rather than every second Monday as per the union's original proposal. The amended calendar would not apply to existing projects, which operate under full flexibility, or to new projects that operate on a five-day work week.

The announcement of a deal would typically see a domino effect among top-tier builders but this year it comes as a breakout group of non-union builders has emerged due to claims the union's proposals are unsustainable.

A Lendlease spokesman confirmed "we've reached in-principle agreement on our proposed NSW and ACT enterprise agreement with representatives of our workforce and the CFMEU, which is now before the Australian Building and Construction Commission for 2016 building code assessment".

"Construction workers will continue to play a vital role as our economy recovers from COVID-19," he said.

"The proposed agreement strikes the right balance between flexibility and health and wellbeing benefits for our workforce, and productivity. This is important for an industry historically working on average a six-day week."

It is understood that part of the reasoning for the lower increase in base rates is because Lendlease already pays above the union's rates.

The ABCC must now ensure the agreement is compliant with the building code, which prohibits agreement restrictions on managerial prerogative, before it is put to a vote of employees.

The Australian Financial Review reported yesterday that the CFMEU was distributing a list to subcontractors last week that claimed deals with Lendlease and eight other builders after an 18-month industrial stand-off.

However, Lendlease did not comment at the time and at least three of the builders on the list denied they had reached an agreement. Multiplex and John Holland also did not deny the union's claims.

The CFMEU declined to comment on the Lendlease deal.

However, sources said its NSW secretary Darren Greenfield had given other major builders until Christmas to officially sign on to the agreement.

NSW Treasurer Dominic Perrottet last week branded the CFMEU's 5 per cent pay rises and nine-day fortnight as originating in "la la land" and warned the claims could risk projects and jobs during the economic recovery.

The deal comes as five second-tier builders have successfully negotiated non-union agreements with their workforce over the past two months in what the industry says is an unprecedented response to the unsustainability of the CFMEU's agreement.

Lendlease is now competing against newly non-union builder Richard Crookes in a shortlist for the new Powerhouse Museum project in Parramatta.

About 260 subcontractors have agreed to the CFMEU's deal but with its 5 per cent pay rises and original RDO calendar with a nine-day fortnight.

Key pointsLendlease has lodged its CFMEU agreement with the industry watchdog ABCC.

Sources say the union has given other major builders until Christmas to sign on.
direction: Lendlease shares and the construction sector

headline: Portfolio shows resilience as virus tests its strength
text:
Ingenia Community's mixed business model is buoying the developer and landlord, with income from its government-subsidised renters holding up through the pandemic and bookings booming for its holiday parks, which closed for three months but are now poised to tap the growing domestic tourism market.

Investors responded to the news yesterday, pushing Ingenia's shares 14¢, or 3 per cent, higher to $4.81 after the company reported 325 settlements for the year to June, down just 3 per cent on its record year in 2019 and said rental income from its market of downsizing Baby Boomers proved resilient.

The company, which also claimed $4.4 million in the federal government's JobKeeper payments primarily to hang on to 300-plus staff after its tourism parks shut in March, said bookings for the next year were now up 24.7 per cent on a year ago.

Chief executive Simon Owen told investors the company had not recorded any cases of COVID-19 across its operations or locations and that the pandemic showed the "quality and resilience" of the company's portfolio.

"Ingenia is firmly focused at the seniors end of the affordable housing market, which is the deepest part of the market, with few competitors or alternatives," Mr Owen said. "Our holiday business is strongly leveraged to a recovery in domestic tourism. Caravan sales are very strong and the two most popular regions in Australia for caravanning and camping are the NSW North Coast and the NSW South Coast, where Ingenia has market-leading positions."

A greater number of sites from a year earlier, rising property values and development margins pushed the company's pre-tax profit up almost 17 per cent to $27.8 million and net after-tax profit 7.2 per cent higher to $31.4 million.

Ingenia declared a final 4.4¢ dividend - less than 5.8¢ of a year earlier -bringing its full-year total to 10¢, down from the 11.2¢ of 2019.

Ingenia, which Mr Owen said was seeking to use the "once in a decade opportunity" of the pandemic to lift its market share "and slingshot past the competition", yesterday also said it had acquired, with US partner Sun Communities, its third and largest-to-date asset, a development site at Morisset on the NSW Central Coast from Winarch Capital for $30 million.

While the closure of state borders could affect Ingenia's traditional peak holiday flow of tourists from Victoria and NSW into Queensland, the company was taking a more localised approach to marketing to encourage people to travel within their state, Mr Owen said. "Even if the borders are shut into next year, we're going to have a really strong Christmas."
direction: Ingenia Community shares higher

headline: BlueScope to review NZ arm after $200m hit
text:
Steelmaker BlueScope says demand for its products in Australia in the June half has been about the same as the December half despite economic fallout from the COVID-19 pandemic, while its China operations have rebounded faster than expected after shutdowns.

But chief executive Mark Vassella warned there is still major uncertainty across the main markets in which it operates, particularly in the United States where its North Star mill in Ohio has been running at 90 per cent capacity as demand weakens from car makers.

Mr Vassella also said BlueScope had begun a strategic review of its New Zealand operations and would take a $200 million impairment on the business as it assessed whether it was worthwhile continuing with the New Zealand arm at a time when carbon and energy policy changes are making it harder to justify.

BlueScope on Friday revealed that it expected full-year earnings before interest and tax would be about $560 million, less than half of the $1.35 billion delivered in 2018-19.

"There is currently a high level of uncertainty in BlueScope's key markets at the start of FY2021 due to the pandemic and weaker steel spreads," Mr Vassella said.

BlueScope said it expected that it would produce underlying earnings before interest and tax of about $260 million for the June half. Goldman Sachs analyst Owen Birrell has a "buy" rating on the stock and a 12-month price target of $14.66. He expects Australian steel volumes to continue to be solid. BlueScope shares are currently trading at around $11.20.

Mr Birrell said the BlueScope update confirmed "channel checks" by the stockbroking house out in the field that the Australian construction market "remained robust". That had been helped along by the federal government's Home Builder stimulus program, government infrastructure spending and rebuilds after the bushfires.

Mr Vassella said overall, business conditions had been better than expected compared with some of the dire predictions of a few months ago. But uncertainty prevailed because of the effects on economies from COVID-19.

BlueScope runs four steel plants in China and is part-way through a $1 billion upgrade of its North Star steel plant in Ohio in the United States.

The steelmaker on March 19 pulled its profit guidance, joining dozens of other ASX-listed companies in scrapping guidance as the coronavirus pandemic up-ended even the most carefully managed companies.

US car making giants Ford, General Motors and Fiat Chrysler have all backed off on production levels because of sinking demand as COVID-19 decimates the economy. Mr Vassella said from mid-March to mid-May shutdowns by automakers in the US had put downward pressure on margins, but the average utilisation capacity rate across the June half had been above 90 per cent.

The North Star mill is a big supplier to US automotive companies and whitegoods manufacturers. BlueScope in August 2019 gave the green light to the $1 billion expansion of North Star, which had been running at full capacity.

Prior to COVID-19, North Star was the star performer for BlueScope over the past few years. The expanded North Star mill will have an extra 850,000 tonnes of capacity when the upgrade is finished by 2023.

Mr Vassella said the company had net cash of about $100 million as of June 30.

Mr Vassella said the countrywide shutdowns imposed by the New Zealand government in March and April had put a dent in the New Zealand and Pacific Steel operations, which had already been suffering ongoing cost pressures.
direction: downward pressure on BlueScope shares

headline: Now for start of on-shoring era: Liveris
text:
Washington | Andrew Liveris, the former Dow Chemical chief who helped write manufacturing policy for Barack Obama and Donald Trump - and is now doing the same for Prime Minister Scott Morrison - believes the post-coronavirus economy needs a historic overhaul.

"Australia drank the free-trade juice and decided that off-shoring was OK. Well, that era is gone," says Liveris, who's been tapped to work with Nev Power's advisory commission into restoring local manufacturing.

"We've got to now realise we've got to really look at on-shoring key capabilities," he says.

Liveris says Australia should never have accepted the hollowing-out of its manufacturing base - part of what is known as "Dutch disease" - to the extent it has.

While he insists he's not seeking to create a system of "picking winners" in the mould of national corporate champions or conglomerates common in places such as China, Japan and South Korea, Liveris says there is a need to focus on competitive advantage in some sectors.

"I don't think you tilt as far as we've tilted, in which 20 per cent is domestic and 80 per cent imported," he says of Australia's reliance on foreign manufacturers.

"You need some balance in there that lessens the reliance [on foreign suppliers], and safeguards your basics and essentials."

The remarks by the former long-serving chief executive officer of one of the world's largest manufacturing companies encapsulates a new boldness among some corporate leaders to question the free-trade-at-all costs, anti-subsidy orthodoxy that has dominated economic policy-making in recent decades.

Liveris acknowledges he sees the crisis as an opportunity to aggressively tackle weaknesses caused by Australia's dependence on global supply chains.

The comments also mesh with Prime Minister Morrison's announcement this week that he wants to better safeguard Australia's "economic sovereignty" by spurring local manufacturing in industries such as medical equipment.

"We've spent many decades being a resource commodity exporter and more lately a service industry based on tourism, education and some digital," says Liveris.

In the meantime, manufacturing has contracted to 6 per cent of the economy from, around 40 per cent in the 1970s.

That model of Australia being "willing to export commodities and import finished goods is old and broken", says Liveris.

According to the Darwin-born engineer, Australia's inability to compete in scalable industrial sectors was laid bare by 1990s trade liberalisation, globalisation and domestic deregulation. "The most recent fatality of that was the automotive industry," he says.

By contrast, smaller economies such as Singapore, Taiwan, South Korea and Hong Kong have succeeded because "they worked hard on the research side", linking universities to foreign direct investment.

"That strategy is something Australia virtually ignored for a lot of reasons, not least being we're a rich country and can live off the annuity of our farm, hotel, quarry mindset," says Liveris.

That's meant Australia has over recent decades missed out on a broad swath of innovations from what World Economic Forum founder Klaus Schwab has dubbed "Industry 4.0", which includes the digital economy, big data analytics, artificial intelligence, robotics, drones and other advanced technologies.

"We have done a decent job in the medical area," says Liveris.

"But support for R&D has basically disappeared whereas other countries have massive credits to encourage innovation and scalability.

"We really need to put our focus on more of those things and put suggestions in place. And then we've got to find sectors that matter."

At the top of his list is health, followed by defence and cyber. Next comes energy, which must include "transitioning, using natural gas, away from coal to renewables", followed by water and food.

"These are all vital sectors that a country needs to have for its citizens," Liveris says.

His taskforce includes Australian Industry Group chief executive Innes Willox, Manufacturing Australia CEO Ben Eade, Australian Manufacturing Workers' Union national secretary Paul Bastian and Advanced Manufacturing Growth Centre managing director Jens Goennemann.

Liveris pushes back on suggestions he is seeking to turn back the clock.

"You don't want to erect tariff barriers and subsidise sectors ... or worse still, not pay your people the right wages," he says.

"So we've got to go up the food chain. This is not making shoes and assembling watches. It's about the high end, the stuff that really matters to the nation.

"Why don't we become the technology provider to the world on ways to have health and hygiene in your food supply chain? Why aren't we the number one precision agriculture country in the world?"

"I don't want to waste a crisis. We have a call to action, we have a need. My training tells me jump, get in and define it as fast as we can.

"And keep the 80:20 rule in place. You're not going to solve everything."
sentiment: mixed with a positive bias for domestic manufacturing
direction: bullish for local industrial and manufacturing stocks

headline: ASX up 1pc as miners' gains offset bank falls
text:
Australia's iron ore and gold miners have driven sharemarket gains for a second consecutive session, offsetting falls among the big banks.

The S&P/ASX 200 Index gained 55.7 points, or 1 per cent, to close at 5460.5 yesterday, despite a 1.7 per cent fall for the shares that mark up the market-heavy S&P/ASX 200 Banks index.

After another solid gain, the ASX 200 benchmark is now within 215 points of the post-trough high set at the end of April. The benchmark has now bounced within a range of about 220 points over May as investors grapple with significant uncertainties that further threaten the sharemarket recovery and the continued loosening of containment measures across the developed world.

Westpac led the declines among Australia's major banks, dropping 2.3 per cent to finish at $14.91. The remainder of the big four all closed between 1 per cent to 2 per cent lower.

"The economic data is awful, and the macro-outlook is potentially bleak," said AXA Investment Management chief investment officer Chris Iggo. "However, the worst of the shock is behind us. Policy has stabilised things and the slow emergence from lockdown is underway, with activity starting to pick-up.

"There remain uncertainties and investors need to be patient in building a medium term strategy. Focus on the policy outlook and how that might continue to support markets.

The efficacy of the country's fiscal policy was supported by the Bureau of Statistics' (ABS) fortnightly survey of households released yesterday. The survey suggested there were labour market improvements in early April.

"This is very encouraging given the size of the hit to the labour market, although the ABS cautioned that the increases were not statistically significant," National Australia Bank's economists said.

Investor sentiment was also supported by the rollback of containment measures in Australia and other countries around the world. Yesterday's sharemarket gains followed a weekend that had many taking advantage of the latest round of reduced restrictions.

"It remains the case that we need to see a successful interaction between opening up economies and managing the virus and then progress on a more permanent solution to that threat," Mr Iggo said. "We aren't there yet. As a result, we should expect continued volatility in equity averages and risk metrics because it takes a lot of other good news to trump the bad macro."

The gold miners were among the best performers yesterday as demand for the precious metal held because of the outlook for money supply and uncertain economic conditions.

Gold futures passed $US1770 per ounce yesterday, after having last traded for $US1700 just last week. Saracen Minerals posted the biggest gain among the major producers in the sector, rising 11.3 per cent to close at $5.62. Australia's largest gold miner, Newcrest Mining, added 6.7 per cent to close at $32.26.

Australia's iron ore producers are also benefiting from a commodity price tailwind. Prices for the base metal traded as high as $93 a tonne yesterday as demand from steelmakers in China further recovers after lockdown measures imposed earlier in the year.

The earnings outlook boost for the industry was good for Fortescue Metals Group and Rio Tinto, whose shares climbed 5.8 per cent each to finish at $13.28 and $90.30 respectively; BHP Group shares jumped 4.5 per cent to finish yesterday's trading at $33.10.

The reduction of containment measures also boosted crude oil prices, adding to the resource-driven sharemarket rise. Among Australia's oil and gas producers Santos added 5.9 per cent to close at $4.87, Oil Search shares advanced 5.6 per cent to $3 and Beach Petroleum rose 4.2 per cent to $1.49.

Rural services group Elders was among the top performers, with the firm's shares hitting a 10-year high on the back of its half-year results.

Elders reported a 53 per cent jump in underlying earnings before interest and tax for the six months ending March 31, lifting its shares by 9.9 per cent to $10.34.
direction: miners up, banks down

headline: What price for living with a virus we can't kill?
text:
This week the Crossroads Hotel, one of the first Sydney watering holes on the highway inbound from virus-hit Melbourne, transformed from local pub to national metaphor. Which way is Australia heading as it manages a global pandemic which has infected 14 million people and killed almost 600,000? Up in Queensland, the winter holiday amusement park attractions are busy. Down in Melbourne, intensive-care beds are being prepared for the worst. Virus hotspots there range from the tower block homes of the lowest-paid to posh city law offices.

The Sydney pub is now said to be a symbol of a successful NSW strategy of suppression: containing and managing a COVID-19 spot outbreak by impressively tracking and tracing thousands of potentially infected people around Sydney, even if the virus is never totally eradicated. But Melbourne's much harder initial lockdown - and the threats by the besieged Andrews government to lock down even harder in coming days - has instead become a symbol of the futility of a strategy of elimination, so easily undone by blunders in handling

high-risk people such as quarantined international arrivals. That allowed the virus to infect Melbourne's suburbs and eventually, via the Hume Highway, the Crossroads Hotel. This weekend, the rising numbers in Victoria now threaten to turn the state's failed hardline approach into an overall loss of control of the pandemic there. Yet just days ago state government health officials were still out there talking about an elimination plan.

The difference between suppression and elimination should be semantic. Elimination is a dividend of a successful suppression strategy based on quarantine, social distancing, personal hygiene, and tracking and testing. With 490 active cases in NSW, 2000 in Victoria, but just handfuls in the other states and territories, elimination is effectively what has been achieved around much of Australia. The problem starts if politicians feel compelled to tell voters that they have eliminated the virus for good, when in fact all they have is a suppression strategy that has worked so far - or at least has not been found out yet.

There is really only good suppression, and suppression done badly. The latter results from blunders that allow this elusive and fast-moving virus, like water, to find its way through careless gaps. Good suppression means social discipline at times, and not a few restrictions. It will seem irksome, and the anxiety it creates might suppress some economic activity as well. But better a rapid and agile response to inevitable outbreaks, and conditioning the public to expect them, than promising virus elimination that can only really be achieved with a vaccine. It is the same approach being taken across Europe, with the continent reopening for the summer holidays even as new flare-ups are found and dealt with. Eliminating by social control rather than a vaccine would effectively mean scorched earth, eliminating vast swaths of the economy too, and destroying health and wellbeing through economic depression - an entirely perverse result.

With 19 per cent of Australia's GDP locked down in Melbourne, the city is going to drag back on national recovery, as it is. NSW has largely kept the state open, at the cost of closing the Victorian border, limiting numbers in pubs and restaurants, and banning dancing at weddings. But had Victoria been as quick as NSW - which learned hard early lessons from mishandling the cruise ship Ruby Princess - then it too would be putting out scattered fires and not trying to halt mass outbreaks with a stop-start-stop-again lockdown of an entire metropolis.

A virus that defies eradication means this emergency could go on for a long time, perhaps years. There will be tension between protecting health and the freedoms of an opened economy. But will Australians be asked to pay an extra price to maintain that freedom? It's clear that data and personal tracking and tracing are the keys to managing outbreaks. Pub and restaurant patrons already have to give contact details. And it was genomic testing that tracked patient zero in the Crossroads Hotel cluster back to Melbourne. Will it be that some intrusion and lost privacy is the price of keeping Australians free of the virus, and free to work, learn and play as well?
direction: bearish for domestic consumer and hospitality sectors

headline: PwC partners shop their talents to rivals
text:
Exclusive | Eleven partners from PwC, who generated an estimated $44 million in annual fees, have approached rivals about taking them on as a group hire as they seek better work and pay beyond the big four firm. The outfits approached include rival consulting giants KPMG, Accenture and Deloitte.

PwC, the country's biggest professional services firm by revenue, recently restructured its consulting division, which is believed to be a factor behind the move. The approaches occurred as the COVID-19 pandemic hit the big firms' profits and led to a shift in client spending to technology consulting services over other "discretionary" forms of consulting.

Accounting & Consulting p35
direction: bearish for PwC, neutral for rivals

headline: AMP, CBA, Westpac face fresh threat
text:
Australian mid-tier law firm Piper Alderman is preparing to launch class action lawsuits against financial institutions including AMP, Commonwealth Bank and Westpac for payment of conflicted remuneration to financial advisers.

It is understood a number of litigation suits against financial services providers are imminent relating to alleged contraventions of the laws brought under the Rudd-Gillard Labor government's Future of Financial Advice (FoFA) reforms in 2012.

The controversial reforms banned the payment of investment commissions, introduced a duty for financial advisers to act in the best interests of clients and added a raft of disclosure and administrative requirements.

In a statement, Piper Alderman confirmed it is planning to bring a "series of class actions against a number of major financial services institutions, including AMP, CBA and Westpac" relating to potential breaches of FoFA and evidence uncovered by the Hayne royal commission.

The actions are "designed to compensate customers of three institutions who were charged commissions in contravention of the law", the statement said. They will be open to consumers who purchased financial products on the advice of a financial planner authorised by licensees including AMP Financial Planning, AMP's Hillross and Charter businesses, and Commonwealth Financial Planning.

It will also be open to former customers of defunct licensees such as Westpac's Securitor and Magnitude subsidiaries and CBA's troubled Financial Wisdom brand, all of which were closed as part of the big banks' partial exit from the wealth management industry.

Count Financial, which CBA sold to ASX-listed wealth and accounting group CountPlus for $2.5 million after buying it for $373 million from Rich Lister Barry Lambert in 2011, is also on the hook.

The actions will be bankrolled by London-headquartered Woodsford Litigation Funding, which defended the role of third-party funders in facilitating access to justice for aggrieved consumers.

"In this era of increased focus on, and regulatory reform of, litigation funding in Australia, it bears remembering that class actions of this nature come about because of the serious misconduct of banks and other large corporations, including the misconduct that was exposed by the royal commission," said Woodsford chief investment officer Charlie Morris. "Litigation funding simply makes it financially possible for actions of this nature to be advanced and for the victims of that misconduct to be duly compensated."

The comments follow a proposal by the Morrison government to re-regulate the business of litigation funding, requiring financiers of class actions to hold a financial services licence.

AMP, which is subject to at least three active class actions and has been threatened with others, has been critical of the litigation funding industry.

"[Litigation funders] have created an escalating cost of doing business in Australia and it is reflected in professional indemnity insurance, it will result in job losses and higher costs passed on to consumers," AMP chief executive Francesco De Ferrari told a parliamentary hearing in July. "This is something I really worry about."
direction: bearish for AMP, CBA, and Westpac

headline: Homemaker centres outperforming malls
text:
Aventus Group boss Darren Holland called large-format retail centres "the affordable bedrock of Australian retail" after the homemaker centre landlord defied the challenging retail environment affecting traditional malls owners, with upgraded earnings guidance and a strong operating performance.

Boosted by lower debt costs that offset a slight easing in like-for-like income growth, Mr Holland said Aventus was on track to hit the top end of its full-year earnings guidance of 3 to 4 per cent funds from operations per security growth, or 19 to 19.2¢.

While some retail property funds have delivered low or even negative returns, the Brett Blundy-backed owner of 20 homemaker centres worth $2.2 billion delivered a 44 per cent total return in the 12 months to December.

Mr Holland, who founded Aventus with retail billionaire Mr Blundy (the trust's biggest security holder with 29 per cent), predicted the group would again outperform peers after reporting a 3.1 per cent rise in net operating income for the six months to December.

"Not all retail is created equal," Mr Holland told The Australian Financial Review, repeating a comment he made last year. Mr Holland said the more affordable rents in large format retail centres - Aventus rents average $317 per square metre compared with $793 in neighbourhood centres and $967 in sub-regional centres - were a key differentiating feature, which kept occupancy costs sustainable.

He said Aventus had no exposure to the recent spate of retail brand collapses, highlighting comments made this week by JB Hi-Fi boss Richard Murray, who said its large format stores outperformed traditional shopping centres. "All the retail store closures have been in shopping centres. Our centres have no department stores or discount department stores and we have less than 2 per cent exposure to the riskier categories of fashion and apparel."

Asked if the tougher retail environment had contributed to a drop in net operating income growth from 3.5 per cent reported in August to 3.1 per cent reported on Tuesday, Mr Holland said it had not.

"Growth of 3.5 per cent was a one-off. We've returned to the medium to long term average, which is [still] sector-leading," he said.

In addition, Aventus achieved higher rents on renewals and new leases - known as "positive leasing spreads" in the industry - compared with both GPT and SCA Property Group whose retail leasing spreads were negative. Occupancy across its portfolio, which includes Hills Super Centre and Marsden Park Home in Sydney, touched 99 per cent.

Despite the upgraded earnings guidance, Aventus shares ended the day up less than 1 per cent at $2.89 - in line with the market. However, analysts were upbeat, with Macquarie Securities calling it a "good outcome in a difficult retail market, particularly in light of supply additions from [rival] Home Consortium."

Jefferies analysts Andrew Dodds and Sholto Maconochie noted operating metrics had improved "across the board" and Aventus' balance sheet was in "better shape" then six months ago.
direction: Aventus Group shares and large-format retail property trusts

headline: Bargaining 'taking longer than it should': Labor
text:
Australia's bargaining system has stalled and needs to get moving again, Labor says. Opposition industrial relations spokesman Tony Burke said bargaining "is much harder at the moment and taking much longer than it should".

"We've had situations like the Kmart agreement, where you had massive support from the workforce. You had one particular commissioner knock the decision out. Eventually, the full bench ruled it back in," he told ABC's Insiders program. He said policies "that get bargaining moving again are going to be really important".

"I don't think that anyone says every rule that's there at the moment should remain unchanged," he said.

ACTU secretary Sally McManus said Australia's bargaining system was restrictive and based on a model developed nearly three decades ago when the economy was very different.

"A lot of the restrictions placed on working people create inefficiencies or just lock large numbers of people out of accessing collective bargaining," she told The Australian Financial Review.

"The pandemic has highlighted that sectoral and industry approaches, working alongside a capacity to deal with workplace specific issues, are helping to protect jobs and provides us with an example of how we could approach improving the bargaining system."

On the topic of whether Labor supported ACTU's push for a $30 a week increase to minimum wage, Mr Burke agreed a pay rise was needed but he did not say just how much it should be.

Mr Burke said Labor would attempt to disallow the Morrison government's decision to fast-track cuts to pay and conditions in enterprise agreements, warning it is a "really dangerous change".

NATASHA BODDY, WITH AAP
direction: downward pressure on wage-cost sensitive sectors and equities

headline: 'Tiger team' ready to export ventilators
text:
A dramatic expansion of ventilator and medical protective equipment production in Australia has built new industries that are set to outlast COVID-19, with enough stock now in place to begin exports.

Industry Minister Karen Andrews said the "extraordinary" growth in local manufacturing capability had seen targets for life-saving ventilators met around the country. Domestic production of face masks and medical gowns had also massively increased.

Australia could now begin helping other countries boost their ventilator stocks to fight coronavirus.

"We are getting requests from some other nations" about whether they can buy some of our supply," Ms Andrews told The Australian Financial Review.

"We've got an export market for ventilators here in Australia off the back of good old Aussie ingenuity."

A qualified mechanical engineer, Ms Andrews assembled a powerful "tiger team" of business and public service bosses, scientists and senior Defence officials to break down manufacturing and supply blockages as federal and state governments were faced with a looming health crisis.

Dulux Paint chairman and chief executive Patrick Houlihan, infrastructure specialist and former Army brigadier Darren Norman, former Health Department boss Glenys Beauchamp and Chief Defence Scientist Tanya Monro were among senior players called together to form the emergency response team.

"I needed to get the people I knew could deliver for me on track," Ms Andrews said.

"I was told increased procurement from overseas was needed and there was no way Australia would be able to produce any more than 37 million surgical face masks."

As a result of the push from federal and state governments, personal protective equipment (PPE) manufacturer Med-Con is on track to make at least 50 million face masks every six months. The firm's production lines have been expanded with the help of Defence Force personnel.

CSIRO boss Larry Marshall has worked with researchers and manufacturers to produce filter materials for face masks which can be made from Australian wool.

Ventilator manufacturer ResMed said this month it was ready to supply ventilators through several more waves of COVID-19.

The federal government has ordered 5500 ventilators from the company, which also has a $US32 million ($50 million) contract to supply 2550 units to the US Federal Emergency Management Agency.

Ms Andrews said while a reduction in demand around the world would be welcomed, the expanded manufacturing in Australia was ready and in place.

"It has clearly demonstrated that Australia's strengths lie in ingenuity, innovation and being able to deliver against the odds," she said.

Victorian linen manufacturer Clets Australia has been contracted to make intensive care medical gowns, using ultrasonic welding equipment to stop the spread of germs.

The new supply capacity could help state and territory health authorities deal with any second wave of cases.

Yesterday, NSW Premier Gladys Berejiklian said the state had teamed up with universities and manufacturers to produce new ventilators, including two prototypes in early production.
direction: bullish for Australian industrial and manufacturing stocks

headline: It's a virus stand-off at 10 paces in Geneva
text:
London | Australia and the European Union have steered a resolution through the World Health Assembly that will set up an inquiry into the origins of COVID-19. But in the immediate aftermath, the Coalition government's victory looks pyrrhic.

It was designed as a black eye for China, which is accused of covering up the pandemic in its early stages, blindsiding the West. And also as a kick up the backside for the World Health Organisation (WHO), which has been castigated for failing to call out the Chinese for this.

Instead, it looks more like bloody noses all round.

There's no doubt that China has lost a lot of reputational kudos during the coronavirus pandemic. Its response has veered between truculent defence and shrill offence, both deepening old rifts and opening up new ones.

But once again, Beijing has shown it can play the multilateral game better than the lead-footed Trump administration. US President Donald Trump's only register is disruption and disputation, whereas Chinese President Xi Jinping at least seems able to see which way the wind is blowing.

At the World Health Assembly (WHA) in Geneva this week, Xi got a Chinese candidate into a leadership position, further securing Beijing's place in senior UN positions.

He also promised a sack of cash to the WHO, kept Taiwan in the deep freeze, and said China would make any Chinese-developed vaccine available to the developing world - unlike the US, which is resisting any altruistic impulse on sharing vaccines.

Beijing realised the resolution was going to carry, and is making the best of it: a collaborative feint, then try to kick the inquiry into the long grass or bury it in bureaucracy. It's likely to succeed: since the investigation will be carried out by WHO scientists not green berets, its ability to get to the bottom of the virus depends on Chinese co-operation, which gives China leverage.

Trump, meanwhile, has less to crow about. The resolution also calls for more funding for the WHO, and confirms the organisation's centrality to global health policy.

He's left only with his nuclear option: to pull out of the WHO. This would be an act of self-harm, diplomatically and epidemiologically, rather than a triumph.

Even America's most loyal allies aren't going to go all the way over the trenches with him on this one.

Which brings us to Australia. Prime Minister Scott Morrison has sought to carve out a role in which he tries to bring a sense of discipline and constructive purpose in the wake of acts of Trumpian disruption.

You can almost picture Morrison turning to Trump and saying: "You were only supposed to blow the bloody doors off."

Where Trump offered threat and vitriol, Australia sponsored a proactive resolution and built an alliance behind it.

But the risk of being collateral damage in the trans-Pacific firefight is always high, and this time the Morrison government has arguably been burnt.

Also caught in the crossfire is the hapless WHO itself - a political pawn in Trump's effort to deflect blame from himself and shore up his re-election bid.

Trump hearkened back to the WHO's more hectoring director-general during the SARS crisis, Gro Harlem Brundtland. But that was 20 years ago, China's clout and resources have grown immeasurably since then, and it's hard to picture any WHO boss having the wherewithal these days to bully Beijing effectively.

That means a likely loss of non-hypothecated US funding in a month's time. This will be painful in the short term, but many experts suggest the most egregious gaps will be filled by China, other Western countries, and large private donors, who get to be the good guys.

Trump has blown the bloody doors off all right. But add this to the recent move by the European Union and Australia to bypass him at the World Trade Organisation, and you wonder what he really has to show for it.

Despite the immense economic and political significance of the US, he may find that the multilateral car can stutter off down the road without him.
direction: bearish for risk assets and exporters due to geopolitical friction

headline: IMF forecasts a softer hit to economy
text:
Australia's economic slump this year will be shallower than the International Monetary Fund expected just three months ago, but tax reform and the elimination of wasteful spending may well be needed to ensure record debt levels are serviceable.

The IMF has also forecast a bounce-back in the price of base metals and food prices this year and next year because of supply chain disruptions and recovering demand, which could bode well for Australia's mining and agricultural exports.

The local economy is now expected to contract 4.2 per cent this calendar year, less severe than the 6.7 per cent fall it tipped in April and the 4.5 per cent hit tipped in June. Next year the economy is expected to grow 3 per cent.

Gross domestic product is forecast to contract 4.4 per cent globally this year, down from June's forecast of 4.9 per cent, while the economy will recover 5.2 per cent next year, slightly less than the 5.4 per cent forecast in June.

"We are projecting a somewhat less severe though still deep recession in 2020, relative to our June forecast," the IMF said. Concerns about the serviceability of debt levels were raised by the Washington DC-based organisation.

"Sovereign debt levels are set to increase significantly even as downgrades to potential output imply a smaller tax base that makes it harder to service the debt," the IMF warned.

"To ensure that debt remains on a sustainable path over the medium term governments may need to increase the progressivity of their taxes and ensure that corporations pay their fair share of taxes while eliminating wasteful spending.

"Although adopting new revenue measures during the crisis will be difficult, governments may need to consider raising progressive taxes on more affluent individuals and those relatively less affected by the crisis (including increasing tax rates on higher income brackets, high-end property, capital gains and wealth) as well as changes to corporate taxation that ensure firms pay taxes commensurate with profitability."

The IMF said tax and spending measures should "privilege initiatives that can help lift potential output".

The IMF's forecasts were compiled before the Morrison government's October 6 budget in which tax relief to individuals and business was the main feature.

In good news for Australia's mining and agricultural sector, the IMF said food prices were projected to rise by 0.4 per cent in 2020 and then increase 4.3 per cent in the year thereafter on tighter supply conditions, especially in the meat sector.

Its annual base metal price index is projected to increase by 0.8 per cent in 2020 and by a further 3 per cent in 2021.

"The possibility of a second wave of COVID-19, the sustainability of strong China demand, and tensions between China and the United States are the major risks to metal prices falling."

Unsurprisingly, the COVID-19 pandemic led to a sharp reduction in coal consumption but the outlook is balanced.

"Australian prices have been depressed by China's apparent tightening of import restrictions and by Japan's intention to phase out inefficient coal-fired power plants by 2030," the IMF said.

"The pandemic may have dented coal consumption but, probably, only temporarily.

"Countries that have recently, or not yet, seen per capita coal consumption peak (including China, India and Indonesia) account for the lion's share of global coal consumption, which will therefore take years to decline in the absence of significant policy actions.

"Econometric analysis suggest that a full coal phase out will occur long after low-carbon energy sources start to gain importance in the energy mix."
direction: bullish for mining and agricultural export shares

headline: Vodafone's revenue dived before TPG merger
text:
Vodafone Hutchison Australia's revenue plummeted 12 per cent in the six months before the merger with TPG Telecom in late June, as the COVID-19 fallout and existing problems caused the telco to lose mobile customers.

In the merged company's first set of half-year results, TPG Telecom reported a six-month revenue of $1.5 million, down 11 per cent on the previous period. Earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation fell 9 per cent to $531 million.

The company now known as TPG Telecom is actually a rebadged Vodafone Hutchison Australia, so its results apply almost exclusively to the old Vodafone. The old TPG was merged into the new company at the end of June, so Friday's results include just four days of that company's results.

On its own, Vodafone Hutchison Australia never turned a profit, so Friday's results marked a welcome change: a maiden profit of $83 million. But that figure was an artificial feature of the merger. On its own, Vodafone made a net loss of $117 million.

Vodafone had been rapidly losing market share in the mobile segment before the merger, a feature of its dwindling mobile spectrum capacity. This trend continued, with postpaid mobile customer numbers falling 2 per cent to 3.354 million and prepaid mobile customers falling 10 per cent to 1.818 million.

The situation was made worse by an 80 per cent fall in roaming revenue as a result of the COVID-19-related ban on international travel. The travel ban also resulted in a 30 per cent decrease in prepaid connections and 20 per cent decrease in postpaid connections, in part thanks to fewer international students travelling to Australia.

But together, TPG and Vodafone gained market share in NBN resale, taking total NBN customers past the 2 million mark. This could prove very important, as chief executive Iñaki Berroeta said with the company's new, much-expanded spectrum holdings, it would start marketing 4G or 5G fixed wireless products to customers on lower speed NBN plans.

All three mobile network operators Telstra, Optus and TPG are now openly attempting to win customers off the NBN with 5G home broadband products transmitted over the airwaves rather than along fibre-optic cable that runs underground.

Mr Berroeta said the first half of calendar 2020 was an "unprecedented and complex period", with four separate issues buffeting the telco.

"We simultaneously supported our customers to help keep them connected through COVID, moderated the financial impacts of the pandemic on our own business, completed the merger and commenced our 5G rollout after an 18-month delay due to the vendor restrictions," he said.

"While our results reflect a negative impact from COVID on the mobile sector, they also demonstrate the relative resilience of the industry and our capacity to continue to deliver the essential services which our customers rely on."
direction: downward pressure on TPG Telecom shares

headline: Wing and a prayer
text:
New York's Winged Foot course has a reputation for humbling champions, writes Richard Allen.

One hundred years ago, when American golf course designer A.W. Tillinghast was asked by keen golfers of the New York Athletic Club to produce for them "a man-sized course" on Long Island, he was more than happy to comply. He moved 7200 tonnes of rock and removed 7800 trees to meet their brief, creating a course with several long and tight par fours and greens with false fronts guarded by deep and unforgiving bunkers. Tillinghast said that shots to the greens would need to be played with "rifle-like accuracy".

Winged Foot Golf Club, with its gabled stone clubhouse - 35 minutes' drive from Manhattan just outside the town of Mamaroneck - remains one of America's most iconic and difficult championship courses, particularly when the United States Golf Association sets it up for a US Open, letting the rough grow, pinching in the fairways and speeding up the greens.

Despite the fact it's been 14 years since Australian Geoff Ogilvy won the last

US Open to be held at Winged Foot, the players in this year's tournament, to be held from September 17 to 20 (rescheduled from June 18-21), will be very aware of the course's reputation.

They will know of the dramas of 1974 when American Hale Irwin won the event with an underwhelming score of seven over par. After the first round - when not one player was under par and the greatest of them all, Jack Nicklaus, had putted off the final green - Sandy Tatum, chairman of the USGA's competition committee, answered the critics with: "We are not trying to humiliate the best players in the world; we're trying to identify them."

The press dubbed the event "Massacre at Winged Foot". Many said the set-up was a complete overreaction to what had happened the previous year, when Johnny Miller shot 63 in the final round at Oakmont to win his first US Open.

Winged Foot has hosted five previous

US Opens, and none has been straightforward for the winner. In 1929, the great American amateur Bobby Jones won after limping over the line to get into a playoff against Al Espinosa. In 1959, Billy Casper won by a shot after one-putting 31 of the 72 greens, and, in 1984, Fuzzy Zoeller beat Australian Greg Norman in a lopsided playoff after Norman made several improbable pars during the final round.
