In 2006, Ogilvy triumphed in one of the wildest finishes in the event's history. Neither of the three runners-up parred the final hole, while two of them - Colin Montgomerie and Phil Mickelson - recorded inexplicable double-bogeys. Montgomerie's six was simply careless, while Mickelson blazed away off the tee, hit a marquee and couldn't recover. Never was David Feherty's observation about him more apt: "Watching Phil Mickelson play golf is like watching a drunk chase a balloon near the edge of a cliff."

Despite the recent exploits of world No. 2 Spaniard Jon Rahm at the BMW Championship - when he holed a 20-metre putt on the first playoff hole to beat Dustin Johnson - expect an American to triumph at the US Open. Although non-Americans have won nine of the past 20, Americans have won each of the past five. Today they occupy eight of the top 10 spots in the world rankings. World No. 1 Johnson is in imperious form, winning the recent Northern Trust Open by a mind-boggling 11 shots.

As the stars of players like Mickelson, Tiger Woods and the younger Jordan Spieth start to wane, the American college system produces a seemingly endless supply of young players to take their place. Collin Morikawa, the 23-year-old who won last month's USA PGA Championship at Harding Park, is a case in point.

One minute he is playing for the University of California, Berkeley, the next he's winning a major golf tournament.

Notably, eight of the top 15 players in the world are Americans aged 30 or younger: Justin Thomas, Morikawa, Brooks Koepka, Bryson DeChambeau, Patrick Reed,

Xander Shauffele, Patrick Cantlay and Daniel Berger.

There will, of course, be lots of interest in Tiger Woods - world No. 17 - and his quest to overtake Jack Nicklaus' record of 18 majors. Woods, 44, currently sits on 15 majors and knows that time is running out. In fact, only five golfers in the history of the game have won majors at an age greater than Woods will be at the US Open: Julius Boros, Jerry Barber, Old Tom Morris, Nicklaus and Irwin. One of them, Old Tom, won the 1867 Open Championship in Scotland in a field of 14.

No amount of ab crunches, bicep curls and dead lifts will keep Woods competing with the young guns of the US Tour, who today outdrive him by 30 or 40 metres.

Golf today, more than ever, is a young man's game. As much as Woods might remind himself that Sam Snead won the Greater Greensborough Open at the age of 52, he also knows that Snead's simple syrupy swing ensured his longevity. By contrast, Woods' swing has led to eight knee and back operations.

The reality is that Woods has won one major of the past 32 (last year's US Masters). "It's getting tighter and harder to win events," he said at Harding Park. His career earnings of $US120 million will make his current predicament easier to digest.L&L
direction: bearish for golf-related equities due to pandemic uncertainty

headline: 'I think the market is going to go nuts'
text:
Sydney's auction clearance rate rose to 74.8 per cent at the weekend, the best result in a month, while property agents in Melbourne expect the market to "go nuts" after the ban on private home inspections is lifted at midnight.

Craigieburn selling agent and auctioneer Trish Orrico of Ray White said buyers in Melbourne would return in large numbers now that open home restrictions were eased.

"I have dozens of buyers dying to get out there to inspect the houses," Ms Orrico said. "We've had 26 people booked for September 14 when we thought restrictions will be eased, and now we've had them on standby. I think the market is going to go nuts.

"Our customers need to sell their properties and now we're able to do private inspections again, we'll be able to assist them. It's fantastic news."

Ray White Group managing director Dan White was confident volumes and transactions would rebound solidly in Victoria from Monday.

"There are a lot of properties that are ready to go, so I think stock numbers will rise strongly. I think Melbourne would be able to catch up with Sydney's strong performance now that private open homes can go ahead. Both have similar drivers and there's a lot of pent-up demand," he said.

The Real Estate Institute of Victoria said the relaxing of restrictions would lead to a slow and gradual return to real estate transactions in Melbourne.

"It is encouraging to see the plight of many Victorians who were unable to make decisions about their own property has been reconsidered by the government," president Leah Calnan said.

Cressida Wall, the executive director for Victoria of the Property Council of Australia, said the reopening of the Melbourne real estate market would deliver vital economic stimulus that the state desperately needs .

"Bringing forward the reopening of Melbourne's real estate market is a practical step that will create jobs, drive investment and get Victoria's economy moving again," Ms Wall said.

A total of 815 auctions took place in Sydney at the weekend, up 20 per cent from the previous week's 679 and the largest volume recorded since April, CoreLogic results showed. Of the 630 reported results, 471 were cleared.

Sydney-based selling agent Thomas McGlynn of BresicWhitney said listings had been rising steadily over the past few weeks.

"It's looking like we'll be on track to have a better September this year compared to last year," Mr McGlynn said. "People are bidding on properties after seeing it for the first time, and that's something you only see when there's a bit of confidence in the market."

Low activity persisted in Melbourne with just 60 homes going under the hammer, up from last week when only 11 homes were taken to auction. Of the 49 collected results, only 28.6 per cent were successful, as a large chunk (63 per cent) were taken out of the market.

Across the smaller capitals, Canberra continued to record strong results, with a 84.9 per cent success rate based on the reported auctions.

More than three out of four (77.3 per cent) homes in Adelaide sold, while Brisbane cleared 50 per cent.
direction: bullish for residential property and real estate sector

headline: Tech giants win concessions in code
text:
Facebook and Google will be permitted to count the monetary worth of online readers they deliver news websites in calculations to determine how much money they pay media organisations for the value of journalism on their platforms, under compromises reached by the federal government.

The final digital platform reforms to be unveiled today by Treasurer Josh Frydenberg will leave Facebook and Google facing a new independent umpire if they fail to strike commercial deals with news publishers.

In an added concession to the digital giants, Facebook's Instagram and Google's YouTube will both be excluded from the new digital platform rules governing their interaction with public interest news organisations.

Public broadcasters the ABC and SBS will be included, in a move that could bolster their revenue and be popular with Labor and the Greens.

Mr Frydenberg and Communications Minister Paul Fletcher privately briefed about 20 Coalition backbenchers on the digital platform competition draft legislation yesterday, after intense lobbying from the technology giants and local media companies on an earlier draft code.

Sources said Mr Frydenberg told colleagues the two digital giants had huge market power and the threat of the final arbitration system would hopefully force Facebook and Google to the negotiating table with news organisations.

Facebook and Google would also be required to give news websites 14 days' notice - down from 28 days - before major changes to search algorithms to allow time and an understanding of how the changes could affect their rankings and appearance on the platforms.

Minor day-to-day algorithm tweaks made automatically by platform machines would not be bound by the disclosure rule, a source said.

Under the mandatory bargaining code, if the parties fail to strike a financial deal, an auction system similar to schemes used by American baseball teams in player pay disputes would be overseen by the Australian Communications and Media Authority.

Organisations engaged in public interest journalism and the two American digital giants would submit their best financial offer.

Independent arbitrators, selected from a panel of 12 mediators chosen by ACMA, would make binding final decisions by selecting one of the two monetary bids.

The umpire would generally not choose an alternative amount, such as a figure between the two bids, in an attempt to encourage the parties to the centre and avoid lobbing unrealistic high-ball and low-ball bids.

The arbitrator would retain some discretion if both parties made totally unrealistic bids, a source said.

Under a new concession to the powerful tech giants, a "two-way value of exchange" would recognise the monetary value they deliver news outlets through online reader traffic in any calculation.

One media executive said they were not surprised by the compromise of including the two-way value exchange.

"That's what [Google and Facebook have] been consistently arguing all along. If that's a compromise that gets the thing closer to fruition, it's just a reflection of the way the conversation was going to go anyway."

Another media executive said there was already a value exchange between news publishers and the tech giants on search engine optimisation and the like, meaning publishers remained confident that the sums Google and Facebook would have to pay would be significant.

"If we thought there was value for referral traffic we would pay for it," one executive said.

A single arbitrator would typically make the decision, but the parties could request that a panel of three arbitrators oversee the final binding decision.

Facebook and Google have previously criticised the reforms as "unworkable" and have been pressing for a compromise to a more commercial solution.

In future, the treasurer of the day will have the discretionary power to add and remove digital platforms.

Among some Liberal backbenchers, there is unease about the government giving new regulatory powers to ACMA to make world-leading interventions in such a major, fast-changing and innovative sector of the economy.

Renegade backbencher Craig Kelly asked Mr Frydenberg why Twitter was not also being included.

The legislation is expected to be referred to a Senate committee before a final vote in Parliament around February or March next year.

Key pointsThe value of online readers will be recognised in payment calculations.

An independent mediator will rule if a deal with news publishers can't be struck.
direction: bearish for Facebook and Google shares

headline: Bank short-sellers now going long as early forecasts prove too dire
text:
One of the biggest banking bears going around used to be Perpetual portfolio manager Anthony Aboud, who runs the long/short equity hedge fund called Perpetual Pure Equity Alpha. Aboud (aka "Spud") was also very bearish on the housing market.

While he will not disclose whether he was short-selling bank stocks, he would certainly have made some good coin had he managed to do so before COVID-19.

Despite Australia's extraordinarily resilient performance, the four major banks are all still trading miles below their late-February peaks.

ANZ hit $27.24 at that time, and is now at $18.54. After rising to $27.40 in February, NAB is languishing at $18.76. Westpac was at $25.81, and is now down at $18.12.

Since 2015 this column has argued the four major banks should trade about one to 1.5 times book value as their returns on equity converge to their cost of equity. At that juncture, they were all trading at two to three times book value.

ANZ is now at 0.86 times, NAB 0.99, and Westpac 0.97. In contrast, CBA appears much more expensive at 1.67 times book.

In their latest results, the major banks forecast that national house prices would fall by more than 10 per cent (CBA projected a 12 per cent loss), while they expected the unemployment rate to finish this year at 9 per cent. These numbers have driven their default and loss assumptions, and hence their provisioning for bad debts.

And they have proven far too pessimistic.

Consistent with our March 2020 forecasts, Australian house prices have only fallen 2.8 per cent, while the jobless rate is at 6.8 per cent (we projected it would settle between 6 per cent and 7 per cent).

Our March central case involved the housing market rebounding after a six-month hiatus following which national home values would appreciate by 10 to 20 per cent over the coming years.

This is playing out to script, with dwelling prices rising in six of the eight capital cities in September and across all non-metro regional markets, according to CoreLogic's data. Significantly, it appears as if the Sydney market may now be turning, with prices having flat-lined since September 22.

The bottom line is that with the government running one of the most aggressive fiscal stimulus programs in the world, and the Reserve Bank of Australia appropriately seeking to do everything possible to boost employment during this once-in-100 years shock, there is the prospect of the banks undertaking write-backs of excessively pessimistic provisioning estimates.

Enter the diminutive Aboud, who was a sneaky rugby union half-back to this rampaging, barrel-chested hooker in a stellar under-13s team. (The side was undefeated the next year with the addition of TDM's energetic Hamish Corlett behind the scrum and a staunch Tom Cowan at full-back.)

"I have been negatively positioned in the banks for a long time," Aboud says. "My main concern has been the net interest margin pressure coming from low interest rates and a flattening yield curve, competition from non-banks as open banking is implemented, and back-to-front book re-pricing."

While noting that "over the last year NAB, WBC and ANZ have fallen by 42 per cent on average", Aboud reveals he has now "neutralised and gone long" those three stocks. (He will not be drawn on whether neutralised means short-covering!)

"While the NIM pressures are still present, we feel that this is well reflected in the analysts' forecasts and the banks' prices."

Aboud highlights that with the introduction of a new accounting standard called AASB 9 (or IFRS 9), banks have to provision for future expected losses upfront rather than the historical practice of waiting for defaults and losses to materialise.

"ANZ, WBC and NAB had to make this call in late April and early May when they reported their results," he says. "Suffice to say that was close to the peak of despair and hence their assumptions about 2021 unemployment and house prices were predictably extremely dire.

"Even more interesting was the fact that all analysts apart from Coolabah's team thought these banks were being overly optimistic. Yet if unemployment is not as bad as what was thought in April/May - flash to Bill Evans' recent forecast changes - and house prices actually increase rather than decrease, then there will be provision write-backs in 2021 and beyond.

"If we see better-than-expected economic conditions in 2021 relative to April expectations, this will create a tailwind for earnings, reduce the prospect of risk-weighted asset inflation (as a function of deteriorating credit quality), and increase the chance of a proper fully-franked dividend around the 6 per cent level.

"Throw in medium-term cost-cutting opportunities through digitisation, and those three banks are starting to look like good value."

(We also own these three stocks in one institutional portfolio.)

The federal budget provided further support for housing in the form of 10,000 new first home buyer loan guarantees. And while the budget forecast only very modest population growth, it has not accounted for the fact there has been a huge influx in Australians (about 484,000) returning from overseas.

Over the last three decades, Canberra has massively underestimated population growth, and we project that net overseas migration will surprise on the high side in the years ahead as Prime Minister Scott Morrison and Treasurer Josh Frydenberg wage a war to attract top global talent and capital.

Under Frydenberg, the budget maintained Morrison's form of significantly under-promising and over-delivering (a dynamic first explained by this column in 2017), with some absurdly cautious assumptions around, for example, iron ore prices. There is every possibility that Frydenberg beats his budget forecasts by a long margin.

And although a fickle Standard & Poor's put Australia's AAA credit rating on outlook for a one-third probability of a downgrade over the next few years, the ratings agency likewise assumed house prices would slump by 10 per cent. S&P will be forced to significantly upgrade its housing outlook, which would further reduce the probability of a sovereign rating downgrade. The fact is Australia has outperformed every AAA credit on earth during COVID-19.

In a recent report on the world's top 100-rated banks, S&P stated that "one of the few systems where we don't expect a weakening in the major banks' risk-adjusted capital ratios in 2020 and beyond is Australia, because its institutions retain in our view sizeable headroom in their earnings to absorb the likely increase in credit losses and contraction in interest spreads and fee income".

November will see the emergence of greater symbiosis between fiscal and monetary policy, and another Team Australia moment if the RBA strives to continue to support employment and inflation. Here I was surprised to read former RBA governor Ian Macfarlane's remarks that the RBA's actions during the COVID-19 crisis "haven't done any good".

The RBA's own quant research comprehensively demonstrates that the diverse package of measures it unleashed in March have materially reduced unemployment, mitigated deflation and contributed to superior economic growth than would have been the case had it done nothing. It emphatically concludes that without these actions "economic outcomes would be more varied and adverse".

The claim that the RBA has exhausted all its options is also nonsense. Under the cerebral leadership of governor Philip Lowe and deputy governor Guy Debelle, the RBA has slashed its cash rate to a record low, created an immensely impactful $200 billion direct lending facility for banks that has protected households and businesses from more expensive funding markets, crushed the cost of government borrowing down to 0.25 per cent for three-year money by launching a novel bond-purchasing program, and buttressed the liquidity of the Commonwealth and state government bond markets.

According to S&P, the latter initiative has directly enhanced state government credit ratings, and reduced their borrowing costs, given that liquidity is a key rating criterion. Working with APRA, the RBA has also signalled that it will likely adjust its $223 billion Committed Liquidity Facility to both reduce taxpayers' contingent liability risks and put downward pressure on federal and state borrowing costs.

In a recent speech, Debelle disclosed that the RBA has a rich range of future options available to it to help maximise employment growth and national prosperity at a time when Australia has confronted the fastest contraction in growth since the Great Depression.

And if there was any remaining doubt, simply look at the housing market. Without the RBA's interventions, household wealth would be a helluva lot lower.SI

Christopher Joye is a portfolio manager with Coolabah Capital which invests in fixed-income securities.
direction: major bank shares

headline: Village all set for buyout as bids start low
text:
One of Village Roadshow's largest independent investors has dismissed the latest private equity bid as "clearly inadequate", as the theme park and cinema company prepares to open its books for two of the country's largest private equity funds.

Yesterday morning, BGH Capital, headed by Robin Bishop, Ben Gray and Simon Harle, lobbed an indicative $4 a share offer for Village Roadshow, which is 42 per cent owned by brothers Robert and John Kirby and former chief executive Graham Burke.

Jostling among the three men, who are divided on the best strategy for the business, has prompted management and board changes in the past year. Most of the trio's shares are jointly held. BGH's bid is slightly higher than PEP's $3.90 a share offer received in December, and is similar in structure, in that it offers both cash and scrip.

After a hastily convened board meeting yesterday morning, sources told The Australian Financial Review that Village is preparing to give both private equity firms access to a data room and will run parallel due diligence processes of between four and six weeks.

Mittleman Brothers' Christopher Mittleman, whose fund owns more than 5 per cent of Village Roadshow, said a buyout "should not occur at less than $5.42 per share", which represents 9 times earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation.

Mr Mittleman said both bids roughly valued the firm at 7 times EBITDA, and noted in the six years before the Dreamworld tragedy, Village traded at an average 8.6 times EBITDA - without a premium for control.

He added that Village Roadshow operates Sea World, which trades at 10 times EBITDA, while in the movie theatre space Cineplex in Canada was just bought out by Cineworld for 10 times EBITDA.

A data room is not yet ready and Village is still putting together all the information needed to open up for the buyout firms.

Last week, The Australian Financial Review's Street Talk column revealed UBS, which is advising the company, tried to create an auction last July for the group and approached four private equity firms. Shannon Wolfers and David Brown are leading the deal for PEP. Following PEP's bid in December, Village shareholders had been pushing the company to start a process to potentially get a higher offer.

Various break-up scenarios done by a number of investment banks have suggested Village Roadshow could be worth between $4 and $5 per share.

Associates of PEP took out a call option on a 19 per cent stake in Village Roadshow via a purchase from the company's largest shareholder, Village Roadshow Corporation.

Village Roadshow Corporation owns 34 per cent of the ASX-listed Village Roadshow and is owned equally by the Kirby brothers and Mr Burke.

The three each own additional shares for a combined controlling 42 per cent voting stake.

John Kirby has been warring against his brother Robert and Mr Burke for the past 18 months. He has been unable to use his shareholding because it is held in the private vehicle and he has been outvoted by both men.

The family boardroom feud spilled out into the public domain in early 2019. John Kirby and other major shareholders have advocated for the sale of assets to focus on the Australian theme parks and cinema businesses, a reduction in large corporate overheads and an independent chief executive and chairman.

In February, Mr Burke announced he would cede the role of CEO at the end of 2019. But, Village Roadshow in June announced Robert Kirby's son, Clark Kirby, would replace Mr Burke. Robert also announced he would relinquish control of his chairman role, and stripped John of his deputy title.

Clark began as Village Roadshow CEO on January 1. The Kirby brothers and Mr Burke will not be part of the board deliberations on the deal.

Former Foxtel CEO Peter Tonagh is heading a committee as the lead independent non-executive director.

Village shares were trading at $4 late in the session yesterday.

Key pointsBGH offer indicative $4 a share, while PEP bids $3.90 in both cash and scrip.

Village Roadshow to set up data room access for both private equity funds.
direction: Village Roadshow shares face downward pressure due to low bid valuations and investor opposition
