headline: Juukan blast blows up Rio's brand: ex-boss
text:
The former Rio Tinto chief who urged the mining industry to embrace native title 25 years ago says reputational damage from destroying a culturally significant site at Juukan Gorge in Western Australia far exceeds the value of iron ore there.

But Leon Davis, who was Rio chief executive between 1997 and 2000, said he suspected a misunderstanding was behind the blast rather than greed.

Rio has attracted international criticism, including from former prime minister Kevin Rudd, after it detonated explosives in the gorge on May 24 and destroyed 46,000-year-old rock shelters of significance to the Puutu Kunti Kurrama and Pinikura (PKKP) people.

Mr Davis said he felt great compassion for both the PKKP people and Rio in the wake of the incident.

"This is extremely important for their worldwide business because Australia is not the only place where it is very important that Rio Tinto function well with indigenous people," he said.

Mr Davis said the iron ore in Juukan Gorge was "absolutely not" as valuable as the damage done to Rio's brand in the wake of the incident.

"I can't help but feel that the problem is due to misunderstanding, certainly not ill will, and it must be very disappointing for everybody concerned that this has occurred," he said.

Rio says it thought it had secured the consent of the PKKP people as well as the legal right to conduct the blasting, only to learn the traditional landowners wanted to preserve the caves after explosives were installed and it was deemed unsafe to remove them.

The PKKP people have rejected the suggestion Rio did not know they were opposed to blasting, and investors like UniSuper said they had met with Rio to stress the importance of such sites.

"We await findings of a company investigation," UniSuper said on Twitter yesterday.

Norges Bank declined to comment on the incident and its investment in Rio, but added: "We share information with our Council of Ethics when it comes to potential ethical breaches."

Mr Davis was managing director of Rio's antecedent CRA in 1995 when he publicly endorsed the High Court's 1992 Mabo decision and the legislative response to the landmark decision, the 1993 Native Title Act.

The stance put CRA at odds with most of the mining industry at the time, and Mr Davis later served as chief executive of Rio Tinto between 1997 and 2000 after it merged with CRA.

"I think that Rio Tinto still has the same culture as when I left; working with Indigenous people is very important to them," he said yesterday.

"This is certainly not the place that they would want to be. Somehow as a society we need to ensure that these sorts of things are avoided."

Aside from running Rio, Mr Davis also served as Westpac chairman between 2000 and 2007.

Mr Rudd accused Rio of "corporate arrogance" earlier this week and used Twitter to ask why Prime Minister Scott Morrison had not condemned the destruction of the shelters.

West Australian Minister for Indigenous Affairs Ben Wyatt has vowed to reform the state's legislation regarding Indigenous heritage, suggesting he would create a mechanism for review of approvals when new information about the value of a site comes to hand after an approval for development has been issued.

Norges Bank only returned to Rio's register last year after the miner exited Indonesia's environmentally contentious Grasberg copper mine.
direction: bearish for Rio Tinto shares

headline: Priceline owner API to expand beauty unit
text:
Australian Pharmaceutical Industries is ramping up plans to expand its beauty business after a jump in demand for products and services such as injectable cosmetics and facial peels as consumers emerge from lockdown.

Chief executive Richard Vincent said demand for cosmetic injectable treatments at Clear Skincare clinics soared 61 per cent and bookings rose 25 per cent when clinics resumed trading after lockdown, while sales at Priceline pharmacies rose 10 per cent when stores reopened, fuelled by demand for beauty products.

While many consumers are still working from home, Mr Vincent said, people wanted to look good on video calls and were spending more on skincare but less on make-up such as colour cosmetics.

"There's no doubt with Zoom calls and Teams and other video-based tools people are looking at themselves on the screen," Mr Vincent said. "If anything, the desire for affordable skincare has increased as a result of COVID."

API's underlying net profit for the 12 months ending August fell 46 per cent to $30.65 million, in line with guidance, and the group swung to a $7.9 million loss after writing down the value of the Soul Pattinson Chemist brand and biting the bullet on loss-making Priceline stores.

Earnings fell in all divisions as the coronavirus pandemic led to enforced store and clinic closures and reduced foot traffic, especially in CBD stores and large shopping centres, and less demand for cold and flu treatments.

However, Mr Vincent said the group was well positioned to take advantage of increased demand for health and beauty products, and expected earnings to rebound in 2021, barring another major COVID-19 outbreak and forced store closures.

"We have seen both of our retail businesses rebound strongly in states that have emerged from COVID-19," he said.

Clear Skincare, which was acquired by API in 2018, was hit hard by the pandemic, with earnings falling 11 per cent and sales by almost 12 per cent. All 60-odd Clear Skincare clinics were closed for up to three months during the national lockdown and about 14 stores in Melbourne have been closed for three months.

API opened 15 clinics and an online store during the year and another four stores since August, lifting the network to 71. Mr Vincent sees scope for at least 100 clinics across Australia.

Priceline earnings fell 14 per cent as non-pharmacy stores were forced to close and foot traffic in CBD stores and shopping centres went backwards, dragging retail sales down 5.2 per cent.

While Priceline's online sales doubled, demand for high-margin products such as colour cosmetics fell, while social distancing led to reduced demand for cold and flu treatments and analgesics.

API announced late last week it would permanently close 14 company-owned Priceline non-pharmacy stores because of the "long-term impacts of COVID-19 on foot traffic in CBDs and shopping centres relative to ongoing rents".

But the group plans to open between 15 and 20 new Priceline pharmacies a year and Mr Vincent said there was strong interest in the banner from independent pharmacists seeking a point of difference from the Chemist Warehouse chain.

API relaunched Priceline's Sister Club loyalty program and plans to make more personalised offers to encourage existing customers to spend more. "That's where we see the growth in Priceline," he said.

In pharmacy distribution, revenues rose 4 per cent, or 6.1 per cent excluding the negative impact of Hepatitis C sales, but gross profits slipped 1.8 per cent, due mainly to PBS reforms.

Consumer brands profits fell 24 per cent and sales dropped 18 per cent after delays in sourcing raw materials from China and India, and reduced demand for over-the-counter drugs.

API has been in discussions with the federal government about pharmacies and pharmacy wholesalers playing a role in distributing a vaccine for COVID-19.

"If and when a vaccine becomes available, a co-ordinated, whole of healthcare approach involving GPs, nurses, hospitals, aged care homes and pharmacists will serve Australia well again," Mr Vincent said.

API trimmed its final dividend by 2¢ to 2¢ a share, payable on December 15.
direction: bullish for API shares

headline: Flurry of deal interest pushes ASX up 0.5pc
text:
A rush of deal-making lifted the S&P/ASX 200 index 0.5 per cent yesterday in an exciting session that puts Link Administration in play. The benchmark index closed up 29.7 points at 6131.9.

Link soared 25 per cent to $4.99 upon a takeover bid from Pacific Equity Partners and The Carlyle Group at $5.20 a share. The owner of the PEXA platform is an ex-PEP investment.

Link's 9.65 per cent shareholder, Perpetual, appears to support the approach unless a better offer comes along.

Bravura Solutions advanced almost 9 per cent to $3.68. Bravura agreed to buy UK pensions administration technology company Delta Financial Systems for up to £23 million ($41.5 million) in a deal which it said would be accretive for earnings in financial 2021.

Funded from cash, Bravura said Delta is forecast to achieve revenue growth in the range of 20 per cent to 30 per cent with margins comparable to its own wealth management business.

Buy now, pay later business Zip Co rallied more than 5 per cent to $7.99.

Zip appeared to move on quarterly updates from Sezzle and Splitit last week that sparked a strong performance for their share prices. Broker Citi opened a "catalyst watch" on Zip on the belief the company could deliver better than expected first-quarter results.

The broker's analysis of user metrics points to a solid first quarter, especially for Quadpay in the US.

Fibre infrastructure company OptiComm rallied 9 per cent to $6.50 in dramatic fashion. The takeover target received a proposal from Aware Super on Monday at $6.50 a share (including a fully franked special dividend of 10¢ a share). It already had a cash and stock offer from Uniti at $5.85 a share.

OptiComm successfully applied for the scheme meeting, scheduled for today, to be postponed to a date yet to be determined.

Commonwealth Bank of Australia rose 1.3 per cent to $68.61. Loan repayment deferrals fell last month to 129,000 from 174,000 in August and 210,000 in June, and total loan balances on these deferrals now stand at $42 billion, down from $59 billion in August and $67 billion in June.

"We are conscious that many of our customers still require our ongoing support, particularly in regions most affected by COVID-19, such as Victoria," chief executive Matt Comyn said. Westpac added 1.87 per cent to $18.55, National Australia Bank 1.6 per cent to $18.99 and ANZ Banking Group 2 per cent to $18.98.

Crown Resorts fell 2 per cent to $8.80 after Crown Melbourne received a show cause notice from the Victorian Commission for Gambling and Liquor Regulation related to Crown Melbourne's compliance with junket protocols.

Crown must show why disciplinary action should not be taken in relation to an alleged non-compliance.

Lithium producer Pilbara Minerals has warned prices remain weak even as it lifted production 80 per cent in the September quarter. The company produced 62,404 tonnes of spodumene concentrate, up from 34,484 tonnes in the June quarter; Pilbara Minerals shares were flat at 37.5¢.

Sydney Airport rose 1 per cent to $6.08. Morgan Stanley raised its recommendation to "overweight" from "equal weight" on a belief that green shoots will appear in routes in the next year and lift valuations back to historical levels. The broker also lowered its rating on Auckland International Airport to "equal weight" from "overweight". Auckland's ASX-traded shares fell 1.6 per cent to $6.88.

All Ords winner Chalice Gold Mines surged 17 per cent to $3.51. The explorer made a bullish presentation to the Diggers and Dealers mining conference outlining its Julimar nickel, copper and palladium project in Western Australia, and Pyramid Hill gold project in Victoria.

OM Holdings agreed to commercial terms with Element 25 to sell 100 per cent of the manganese ore produced by Element 25 from the first stage of the Butcherbird Project development to OM. It added 1.5 per cent to 33¢.

The People's Bank of China fixed the midpoint for the yuan at its strongest against the US dollar since April 2019. The yuan was fixed at 6.7126 against the US dollar, a 1 per cent fall from Friday.

Broker Citi opened a 'catalyst watch' on Zip on the belief it could deliver better than expected first-quarter results.
direction: bullish for ASX 200, Link Administration, Bravura Solutions, Zip Co, and OptiComm

headline: What we learnt: beware pandemic cost-cutting boasts
text:
Andrew Bassat, the chief executive of online classifieds giant SEEK, is wary of companies that brag about cost-cutting during the COVID-19 crisis.

"I don't get it," he says simply.

Bassat, having reported a 51 per cent fall in underlying profit for 2019 to $90 million, and having sent his shares down almost 10 per cent by warning 2021 profit would come in at just $20 million, it would have made sense to get staff costs down by $10 million.

But SEEK has not cut jobs or pay. And Bassat says the letters of gratitude he has received from staff will be worth "hundreds of millions in future goodwill".

In fact, Bassat says he isn't too worried about this year's profit and is mainly focused on where the business will sit in 2022 and 2023.

It would be easy to pull back on investment in staff, technology and the suite of early-stage ventures SEEK has on its books, or to take a hard line with customers by enforcing contracts despite COVID-19.

But the experience of the GFC looms large. SEEK invested heavily through the period and while it saw its share price tumble 75 per cent between February 2007 and September 2009, it reaped the benefits over the next decade. The stock closed at $19.58 yesterday.

"We've been tested before. Our profit this year doesn't matter. Broadly, we're much more concerned about what the business looks like in 2022 and '23," Bassat says.

I've long been fascinated by Bassat's focus on the long term, but there's no doubt its risks and rewards become much more stark in a crisis like this.

The reflex of investors and commentators is to applaud management for pulling on levers such as expenses and capital expenditure to manage through a crisis, but Bassat's attitude is a reminder that such behaviour comes at a cost.

What is the point of eking out a slightly better profit in the middle of a crisis if it stunts your growth for the next three to five years?

On the road to a new normal: Like a driver stuck in the morning commute, Transurban chief executive Scott Charlton is learning to roll at multiple speeds.

His Queensland office is about 80 per cent full, his Sydney office is 50 per cent full and his Melbourne HQ is, of course, deserted. But Charlton is taking solace from the fact local and global trends suggest road traffic bounces back strongly, much faster than active mobility (walking and cycling) and public transport.

"As you come out of government restrictions the recovery, particularly on the road, happens quite quickly," he says.

Like Bassat, Charlton kept on all his staff through the pandemic, and is now hunting opportunities to help governments that will need to get infrastructure projects going to stimulate their economies.

NSW, where Transurban has already lodged an unsolicited proposal, and North America, are the key targets.

Computershare says no (to cuts): Computershare boss Stuart Irving did something likely to be rare this earnings season - he provided guidance for the 2021 financial year, forecasting underlying earnings to rise 10 per cent.

But like SEEK and Transurban, his short-term focus has been on getting through the crisis with an eye to the future.

Not only did he retain all jobs, but Irving set up a hardship fund for staff. It's an investment, he says, will pay handsome returns.

"Don't take this as any change to corporate ambition or motivation, but there is more to life than shaping short-term EPS," he said.

Magellan's bonus restraint: Hamish Douglass' funds management giant, Magellan, enjoyed another ripper year, but there's a focus on the long-term here, too.

While the average employee expense for the 128 staff in its funds management rose 15 per cent to $576,000 in fiscal 2020, bonus payments were cut, and Douglass waved his bonus entirely.
direction: downward pressure on SEEK shares

headline: 'Get me an offer': Nervous sellers ready for quick deals say agents
text:
Pent-up demand is driving a spike in residential property demand, with many nervous sellers ready to do a deal before auction rather than risk being caught in another COVID-19 shutdown, according to mortgage brokers.

In Melbourne, where some restrictions on house inspections were lifted this week, scheduled auctions will almost double to about 80 from the previous week. However, that is still close to record lows for peak season spring sales.

Buyers agent Cate Bakos said sellers were still nervous. "Sellers are saying get me a good offer and I'll sell ... It's hot at the moment, but the question is whether it's being driven by long-term demand or undersupply."

Buyer interest remains buoyant beyond inner suburban areas, particularly regional centres with good transport links to big city central business districts.

David Morrell, director of buyers' agency Morrell & Koren, which specialises in top-end properties in Melbourne's leafy inner suburbs, said "a lot of old stock" that had not sold was being put back on the market amid "lethargic market conditions".

"A lot of damage has been done to confidence. People are just not doing anything," he said.

Rich Harvey, a buyer's agent, said a limited supply of luxury properties was pushing prices up in Sydney.

"Without being able to travel overseas, prestige home buyers have looked to feather their own nest and create the best possible environment at home," he said.

Investment bank Morgan Stanley believes that continuing mortgage holidays and government support were likely to keep housing stress "relatively contained" during the spring selling season.

AMP's head of investment strategy, Shane Oliver, has revised down its top-to-bottom forecast for price falls to between 5 per cent and 10 per cent, down from a projected decline 10 to 15 per cent. Prices have already fallen about 3 per cent.

Oliver predicted capital city prices are likely to rise again by about mid-next year as record low interest rates and government support continue to prop up the market.

"The outlook into next year is messy with a wide variety of positives and negatives," Mr Oliver said.

Big negatives included high unemployment, distressed sales, falling rents and high vacancy rates, particularly for high-rise apartments, he said.

Across the capital cities, scheduled auctions this weekend are set to be lower except in Perth and Melbourne, which are at extremely low volumes, according to CoreLogic, which monitors housing activity.

"Nationally, demand is slightly more subdued than in the months leading up to the pandemic," said Eliza Owen of CoreLogic.
direction: residential property prices and sales volume

headline: Team Australia fires a blank on workplace reform
text:
In what looks to be a backroom deal between Industrial Relations Minister Christian Porter and mining and energy union boss Tony Maher, the Morrison government proposes to break up the militant CFMEU by varying the law that prohibits union demergers from being unwound after five years. Allowing for the obvious differences, this is like the mafia doing a deal with the FBI to rub out the black sheep "family" whose mayhem threatens to trigger a crackdown on the entire organised crime business. Amid the civil war that has engulfed the national CFMEU, its mining and manufacturing divisions want to escape the appalling behaviour of John Setka's law-breaking Victorian construction division and its influence in other states.

Splitting up would allow the mining and manufacturing unions to get back, in relative peace, to the core business of extracting the rents generated by Australia's archaic industrial relations system that grants trade unions monopoly rights to represent workers across swaths of the workforce. And it may leave Mr Setka's law-breaking construction union more exposed to being properly outlawed. Hence Labor and some other unions may even back the government's demerger ambush to isolate the construction division and its militant maritime union allies and protect other union's institutional privileges.

But this welcome development merely attacks the disgrace of one of the last bastions of outright thuggery, intimidation and habitual law-breaking that the labour movement, to its shame, has continued to harbour. The leadership of the labour movement has degenerated since Bob Hawke deregistered the Builders Labourers Federation in the 1980s. Now it claims faux class warfare victory, delivered by Pauline Hanson in the Senate, over the government's Ensuring Integrity Bill that was designed to insist that legally privileged trade unions follow proper governance in the wake of the royal commission into trade union corruption.

So, the welcome government-union move against the CFMEU's construction division distracts from the Coalition's failure to even discuss any substantial change to workplace regulation. Mr Porter's measures seek to deal with the complete mess of the Fair Work Commission's handling of "casual" work by including a sop to the ACTU over so-called "insecure" work. They may remove some of the more ridiculous legalistic impediments to enterprise bargaining but without returning to the productivity-enhancing system envisaged by Paul Keating after the early 1990s recession. They extend to life-of-project greenfield agreements of the sort Bill Shorten took to last year's election. And they might modestly simplify some of the byzantine penalty rate structure that has fuelled so-called "wage theft". But it all amounts to lowest-common-denominator tinkering with the outdated industrial relations machinery, instead of recognising that the whole conflict-based workplace framework that came out of the 1890s is not fit for the 21st-century economy of knowledge-based jobs, flexible working from home, and private contractor tradies.

The politics are obvious: Scott Morrison doesn't want to frighten his Quiet Australians or give Labor and the ACTU the opportunity to repeat the 2007 anti-WorkChoices campaign at the next election. This even rules out the "enterprise contract" idea proposed by the Productivity Commission, which would give small and medium businesses the flexibility to strike individual agreements with freely consenting employees, and bypass the costly and complex IR club of lawyers, union and commission officials. There is nothing "right wing" or ideological about calling for a more flexible system of workplace regulation to allow high-performing Australian businesses and their employees to sustain high-paid Australian jobs by competing in a post-COVID-19 information-age global economy. The Team Australia response to COVID-19 has assembled massive Keynesian demand-side firepower to cushion the blow to the economy. But the political system refuses to even consider the sort of supply-side workplace flexibilities needed to revive economic growth, pay down debt and restore prosperity. If the government is serious about jobs and growth, it needs to do more than fire blanks at Australia's industrial relations rigidities.
direction: bearish for unionized sectors and construction stocks

headline: Interest Rate Securities TRADING DATA FOR THE WEEK ENDING FRIDAY, MAY 1, 2020
direction: downward pressure on corporate bond prices and bank hybrid securities

headline: Soft power goes up in smoke
text:
Global perspective A unique connection to nature is at the heart of our international appeal. Foot-dragging on climate change is squandering it.

As we mark Australia Day amid the bushfire crisis, few can dispute that our global image has taken a hit. The Lucky Country, a label that has stuck since Donald Horne's 1964 tome, is looking decidedly less lucky.

Where once Australia existed in the popular imagination as a place of pristine natural beauty, we are now viewed as the developed nation most ravaged by climate change.

Images have been broadcast across the globe of this season's conflagrations turning shimmering blue skies blood red, world-class beaches into evacuation zones, and eucalyptus forests into killing fields for one billion native animals.

The outpouring of sympathy and international solidarity reflects the fact that, in the eyes of the world, this disaster strikes at the heart of the Australian way of life.

If Australia is now seen in a different, darker light, it is because of the damage done not just to the environment but also to our reputation as global citizens. The international media has made a link between the bushfires and our climate policy. The cat is out of the bag.

Our rancour, partisanship and short-termism on the climate crisis present growing sinkholes for our international reputation. The cost to Australia's soft power is just beginning to materialise.

The fact is our visibility as a nation is far larger than our 1.3 per cent contribution to global greenhouse emissions. The American Dream is powered by the innovation and dynamism of Silicon Valley, the Chinese Dream by export-led growth lifting millions out of poverty. But Australians can boast of a unique relationship between their quality of life and nature. The environment goes to the heart of our global identity,

self-confidence and appeal.

Our soft power helps fuel the success of our tourism industry, our agricultural exports, our foreign policy and even our demographic destiny - as we seek to attract the world's best and brightest to immigrate to our shores.

Without it, we are a small, isolated country of 25 million people. With it, we are a middle power with near superpower levels of global stardom.

The hope of many around the world is that better climate policy will arise as a phoenix from the ashes of this disaster. The government's determination to stay its course and defend the coal industry in the face of global efforts to cut greenhouse emissions suggests otherwise.

The greatest self-deception has come in allowing ourselves to think of Australia as a spectator when in fact we are a central player in the world's most pressing crisis.

We frequently hear the argument that actions from individual countries such as ours will, on their own, make little difference to global warming.

This tactic can prevail for some time but is not strategically sustainable. Firestorms such as those experienced in the summer of 2020 will not be the last. More frequent and severe climate shocks mean we can almost certainly expect losses to the economy will not be transitory but close to permanent.

If all countries that individually produced less than 2 per cent of global emissions said they were too small to do anything, a third of the world's greenhouse emissions would go unchecked. That is why we have global agreements.

Something different has to start somewhere. The economist Ross Garnaut has compellingly laid out Australia's potential to be an economic superpower of the future post-carbon world. We can start by taking small but meaningful steps. Restoring international trust in Australia begins by taking our existing targets seriously.

This should not be too difficult, given that our international commitments are not so terribly ambitious.

But if a country like ours, with all its compelling reasons for immediate action on climate change, is not seen to be honouring its commitments to the international community, how can we expect larger emitters such as China and India to do so?

The logic of middle-power diplomacy is that it is in our self-interest to set an example of upholding a global

rules-based order.

This means implementing the Paris Agreement in good faith. Watering down our pledges using carry-over credits from the Kyoto Protocol has no credibility internationally, and threatens to render any new agreement on the implementation of the global climate agreement practically useless. Over time, this should also include honouring our pledge as a member of the G20 to phase out inefficient fossil fuel subsidies.

Without a renewed initiative on climate change our broader diplomatic efforts will suffer too. In our near abroad, China's strategic overtures may find greater traction among Pacific island neighbours facing rising sea levels.

Further afield, we are engaged in trade negotiations with the European Union, a bloc which accounts for a quarter of global GDP and has pledged to reach net zero emissions by 2050 or earlier. President Emmanuel Macron of France has signalled he will push Australia to make its emissions targets enforceable as a condition for approving the trade deal.

Australia is never the dominant force on anything in the world. But we matter.

As far as the world is concerned, we are now ground zero in the war on climate change.

The hour is late, and we've been dreadfully slow out of the starting gate. But the sooner we come to grips with shifting global opinion, and the challenges ahead, the better we will be able to act to secure our future.

Never let a good crisis go to waste.

Herve Lemahieu is director of the Asian power and diplomacy program at the Lowy Institute.
direction: bearish for Australian equities and tourism-related sectors

headline: Scheme would kill investment, experts warn
text:
The Morrison government's plan to back a gas-fired power plant in NSW is unnecessary "overkill" that would discourage private investment in the grid, energy experts say.

Grattan Institute's energy program director Tony Wood said the federal government's plan to build the plant if business did not step up following the closure of the Liddell coal-fired power station confirmed the worst fears about the federal takeover of Snowy Hydro.

"You now have a government that is not only overseeing a market, but it now has its own team in the field," Mr Wood told The Australian Financial Review.

"The private sector would be asking why they would build a new gas-fired power plant in the Hunter when they are going to get drowned by Snowy 2.0 when it comes online?"

Mr Wood said the latest report from the Australian Energy Market Operator said NSW needed only a further 300 megawatts of new capacity over the next decade.

"We don't need 1000 megawatts. It's overkill. We already have a few things which could fill that gap, but the market doesn't need 1000 megawatts. This is unnecessary intervention," he said.

Mr Wood said the proposed government intervention was also "second guessing" and undermining its own proposed retailer reliability obligation policy which was supposed to force the private sector to cover any supply gaps.

Australian Energy Council chief executive Sarah McNamara said the federal government's threat to go it alone on a gas power station could deter the very investments the government was attempting to encourage.

Ms McNamara said Energy Minister Angus Taylor was already proposing the federal government help underwrite new power generation ahead of yesterday's announcement.

"There are no material reliability concerns that would warrant this kind of interventionist approach and there are already market mechanisms in place to address any shortfall identified," she said.

"The sector is struggling to make final investment decisions in an environment of ongoing policy uncertainty. For more than a decade we have been warning of the dampening effect state and federal government interventions have on investor confidence."

Mr Morrison and Mr Taylor want a gas-fired recovery from the coronavirus pandemic and the key to that is ensuring gas and electricity prices stay low.

Mr Taylor cited the Liddell taskforce review which showed prices could rise 30 per cent over two years unless there was adequate dispatchable capacity to replace AGL's coal plant, which is closing in 2023. The Liddell review has yet to be publicly released.

"Over the last decade, the private sector has not built a single new reliable power plant in NSW," he said.

"The government expects industry to step up and deliver the new dispatchable capacity required to ensure a reliable and affordable energy system. If the industry steps up, we'll step back."

Australian Industry Group chief executive Innes Willox predicted the government's trigger to intervene in the power generation - a deadline of April 21 to replace Liddell's capacity - "will likely never have to be pulled".

"In the latest energy projections, neither NSW or any other region is expected to breach the reliability standard, based on announced supply and expected demand," he said.

"We should be cautious not to demand more reliability than we are willing to pay for."
direction: bearish for energy sector and private investment

headline: Labor to deny tax cuts for top earners
text:
Federal Labor is likely to go to the next election promising to scale back the stage three income tax cuts, rather than revoke them, to avoid a Coalition campaign accusing it of denying further tax relief to the lower paid.

Opposition Leader Anthony Albanese is suggesting Labor will amend the cuts to exclude those on the highest incomes, saying $80 billion of the $130 billion the cuts are worth were unwarranted because they would go to "high-end" earners.

The cuts, passed by Parliament after last year's election, are scheduled to begin on July 1, 2024.

From that day, income between $45,000 and $200,000 would be taxed at 30 per cent and incomes above $200,000 would be taxed at 45 per cent. The cuts were budgeted to cost $130 billion between implementation and the end of the decade.

Labor has never supported the stage three cuts but is conscious that should it win the next election, due either late next year or early 2022, it would need Senate approval to either revoke them or make changes.

Mr Albanese is now suggesting doing the latter. This would stand a better chance of passing the Senate, enable Labor to fund its own election promises, deliver tax relief to low- and middle-income earners, and still return tens of billions to the budget bottom line.

"The stage three tax cuts are $130 billion," Mr Albanese said.

"Our concern is that about $80 billion of that is really at the high end.

"If we are going to pay back the debt but we are to decrease our revenue and continue to have to spend ... then you have to have a path back to pay back that debt."

On Sunday, Treasurer Josh Frydenberg said the stage two tax cuts were brought forward by two years in last week's federal budget because the government wanted "the biggest bang for our buck".

"Those lower- and middle-income earners, they will get the best and biggest boost from these initiatives," he said.

Mr Albanese said that was an admission the tax cuts to those on the highest incomes would not deliver bang for buck in terms of stimulus.

"It seems to me that the Treasurer has conceded that you don't get bang for your buck at the high end because it tends to be saved rather than spent," he said.

"And we actually do have to have a path as well, a pathway back to paying back this trillion-dollar debt that the government has created."

Modelling by the Australia Institute, which opposes the tax cuts, estimated that 35 per cent, or about $46 billion, would go to those earning more than $180,000, and 26 per cent, or $34 billion, would go to those earning more than $200,000.

Labor could amend the cuts to apply the 30 per cent rate to income up to $180,000 and still save $80 billion. No decision has yet been made.

The government said it was hypocritical that Labor was opposed to a tax cut for those on high incomes, given it announced a childcare policy last week that would extend subsidies to families earning up to $530,000, up from the present $365,000.

It will also scrap altogether the annual $10,560 cap on subsidies that applies to families with a combined income of more than $180,000.

Prime Minister Scott Morrison said Mr Albanese wanted to deny people tax cuts that were already legislated.

"These tax cuts are designed to ensure that 94 per cent of Australians won't pay more than 30¢ in the dollar in tax," he said.

"They had that argument at the last election and the Australian people made their decision on it. So what you can always know from Labor, when you hear that they want to spend more, always know that they want to tax you more. That's how it works with Labor."

Shadow treasurer Jim Chalmers will use his budget reply today at the National Press Club to criticise last week's budget as woefully inadequate.

Mr Chalmers will take issue with the four-year goal to return unemployment to below 6 per cent, saying it should have been more ambitious.

"If Labor was in government now, our fiscal strategy would be anchored around a more ambitious approach to employment. To chart a path back to full employment, in a way that starts to tackle insecure work and chronic underemployment," he will say.

"This approach doesn't have to mean more debt. It means debt that's better deployed to kick-start the recovery and create more jobs. To boost demand now, and put in place policies that lift the speed limit on growth, to make it stronger, more inclusive and more sustainable."
direction: downward pressure on high-income consumer discretionary and luxury retail stocks

headline: China to lift Wuhan lockdown in April
text:
Shanghai | Beijing will early next month lift a two-month lockdown on Wuhan, the Chinese city at the centre of the coronavirus outbreak, in a significant turning point in authorities' battle against the pandemic.

Hubei, where more than 50 million people have been quarantined off from the rest of the country since late January, announced it would lift traffic controls on people travelling in or out of the province from midnight yesterday.

Travel restrictions for the capital Wuhan would be removed two weeks later, on April 8.

The announcement is the strongest signal so far in what is now a global drama that the Chinese government believes it has the outbreak, which started in Wuhan, under control. The city of 11 million people was put in an unprecedented lockdown on January 23, stranding hundreds of Australians and thousands of other foreigners who were not allowed to leave.

Many locals were unable to return home to their relatives during that period as hospitals became overwhelmed. More than 3000 people, mainly inside Hubei, died during the outbreak, as residents were strictly confined to their apartments for almost two months.

Next month's easing of restriction, however, will apply only to travellers awarded a "green code" on a mobile phone application designed to track the movements and health status of China's population.

Authorities said business operations would resume, but gave no timetable. Hubei province is one of China's key manufacturing hubs. Wuhan reported five consecutive days of zero new infections on Monday. The city reported one new infection yesterday, a doctor at a hospital, local media reported.

Yesterday's development came as China reported a jump in new coronavirus cases from travellers returning to the country from overseas, a trend which is dashing hopes that the country where the pandemic started had the health crisis completely under control.

Health authorities said 74 of the 78 new cases reported in data collected in the 24 hours until midnight on Monday were imported. This was almost double the number of new infections reported the previous day and an ominous shift from last week's positive data which showed zero domestic infections for three days in a row. China accounts for more than 80,000 of the almost 400,000 cases of COVID-19 worldwide, according to data compiled by Johns Hopkins University in the US.

While the increase in new cases being brought back into the country from Chinese citizens living overseas is modest so far, the trend is worrying for Australia, which has been watching China's recovery closely.

"The epidemic control situation in our country is still serious because the confirmed cases are rising sharply overseas, and the risk of imported cases in China is high, which greatly worries me," Professor Li Lanjuan, an epidemiologist leading a team of medical experts in Beijing, told local media.

Chinese health officials this week reported the first case of a patient believed to have contracted COVID-19 from someone who had returned from overseas, triggering concerns that it was too late to contain the uptick in imported cases. There is also growing concern in China about warnings some carriers have no symptoms and were unknowingly transmitting the virus.

China has stepped up measures to quarantine citizens and expatriates returning to the country, many fleeing Europe and the United States. From Monday, all arriving international flights to Beijing were being diverted to 12 other Chinese cities where passengers were tested and processed before being allowed to return to the capital.

Passengers arriving in other cities have to be tested before being quarantined for 14 days in a government-designated hotel or at home.

The removal of travel restrictions for Wuhan will be sold by China as a milestone in its battle to contain the virus. Officials in the city were criticised for ignoring warnings from local doctors about a "SARS-like virus" in late December, before raising the alarm in late January. During that time hundreds of thousands of people left the city and travelled throughout China and the world for the annual Chinese New Year holiday.

Hong Kong also further tightened its borders this week by banning non-residents and transit passengers. The move, endorsed by Beijing, was seen as another way to stem the flow of international arrivals into China by using Hong Kong as a gateway.

The total number of imported cases into China is now more than 400, with the biggest clusters in international gateways such as Beijing and Shanghai. With more than half of China's factories and businesses now believed to be fully operational, local governments are weighing up the need to contain health risks with a directive from Beijing to get the world's second-largest economy back up and running.
direction: bullish for Chinese equities and global risk assets

headline: ASX hits record on rate cut expectations
text:
The local sharemarket closed at a record yesterday on expectations of an earlier rate cut from the Reserve Bank of Australia following soft employment numbers.

The S&P/ASX 200 Index closed 17.9 points, or 0.25 per cent, higher at 7162.5, topping the record close set on Wednesday.

The latest labour force report shows January's jobless rate climbed to 5.3 per cent from December's revised 5.1 per cent.

The result was worse than the 5.2 per cent economists had forecast.

The market had been pricing in an October cash rate cut but that was brought forward to September.

"After unexpectedly falling to 5.1 per cent in December, unemployment rebounded to 5.3 per cent ... returning to the 5.2-5.3 per cent range prevailing since April 2019," National Australia Bank economists said.

"Although the bushfires appear to have had a smaller impact on the economy than first thought ... the coronavirus is a new economic shock to activity with a travel ban restricting Chinese tourists and students.

"NAB forecasts that unemployment will slowly edge up to 5.6 per cent by end-2020 and the impact of the virus raises the risk that this occurs sooner than expected."

The prospects of further monetary easing put pressure on the Aussie dollar, which fell to US66.3¢.

Merchant payments provider Tyro closed 2.1 per cent lower on its half-year report card.

While the growth company reported a $19 million loss, it booked top-line growth of 28.4 per cent — slightly above analysts' average expectations — with total transaction values for the six months rising to $11.1 billion. The shares closed at $4.26, up 29.1 per cent since their ASX debut in December.

WiseTech shares continued falling following its results, closing down another 11.78 per cent at $18.88.

Shares in fund manager Perpetual Investments jumped despite a 20 per cent fall in pre-tax profit and lower average funds under management, down 5 per cent half-on-half and 11 per cent year-on-year.

Shares in the company closed 11.15 per cent higher at $47.27

Coca Cola Amatil provided 2020 earnings guidance along with its 2019 full-year results. It expects mid-single digit earnings per share growth.

After reporting net profit after tax of $374.4 million for calendar 2019, up 34.2 per cent on the prior year, Coca Cola shares ended the day 8.55 per cent higher at $13.07.

Qantas released its results along with a warning of a hit to earnings because of the Covid-19 virus. Its shares closed up 5.87 per cent, however, after the company reported first-half underlying profit before tax of $771 million.

The airline expected the second-half impact of Coronavirus to be between $100 million and $150 million.

Santos shares slid 1.1 per cent to $8.08 after the company reported a 1 per cent fall in underlying profit for 2019 and reduced its dividend to US5¢ per share.

The oil price has risen by close to 10 per cent over the past week, benefiting a number of oil and gas shares on expectations of further slowing in the spread of Covid-19.

Also in resources, coal miner Whitehaven's shares dropped 5.51 per cent to $2.23 after it reported a 91 per cent fall in first-half profits off the back of lower coal prices.

"More subdued pricing, in combination with a number of transient production challenges and higher unit costs, has given rise to a more testing first half," chief executive Paul Flynn said.

The Charter Hall REIT securities slid 0.93 per cent to $13.90 despite the company lifting full-year operating earnings guidance to 2.3 per cent, from 2.2 per cent. A distribution of 14.52¢ a unit will be paid on February 28.

Boral told investors it expects 2020 earnings to be lower than 2019, with net profit after tax in the range of $320 million to $340 million.

Its shares closed effectively flat at $4.83.

Among the other companies to release earnings results was Medibank, which closed 2 per cent lower at $2.92. Origin shares rose 1.79 per cent to $7.97.

NAB forecasts that unemployment will slowly edge up to 5.6 per cent by end-2020.
direction: bullish for the ASX 200 and rate-sensitive sectors

headline: The humble servo is best placed to beat pandemic
text:
With long leases to national tenants and exposure to rising domestic travel, service stations are expected to outperform all other asset classes during the pandemic and in the subsequent recovery, according to a survey carried out by national valuation firm m3property.

Service station values were forecast to hold steady with yields tightening, a relatively better performance than other forms of commercial property.

Medical-use properties, industrial assets, self-storage facilities and residential subdivisions would also come through the crisis with their values relatively intact. But asset classes battling high vacancy rates such as hotels and malls could experience valuation declines of up to 25 per cent and will take the longest to recover, the survey of 43 of the firm's top valuers found.

"While prime, well-located assets are likely to perform better across the sectors, secondary assets and assets subject to high vacancy, short weighted average lease expiry, poor location or requiring significant capital expenditure will experience a greater decrease in value with investors taking a conservative approach to risk," m3property national research director Jennifer Williams said.

Among the hardest-hit asset classes will be secondary hotels - properties outside major urban centres or regional tourism hubs - with expectations that values could fall by between 10 and 25 per cent and a recovery could to take up to 18 months.

The outlook is better for hotels in prime CBD and regional locations, despite very low capital city occupancy rates and more than 13,000 rooms under construction.

James Ruben, NSW director at m3property, said the hotel sector was "quite stratified and segmented" with the larger hotel players having access to the capital resources to get through the pandemic and come out stronger on the other side.

"Naturally the whole market is going to offer discounts to get people in to their properties. The better products will fill up more quicker and will be better placed to compete for market share in the recovery," he said.

For retail property, m3property valuers said neighbourhood centres, which were usually anchored by a major supermarket, would be less impacted than the larger regional and sub-regional malls, which have a higher proportion of discretionary retailers.

This divergence has already been highlighted in the poor performance of major mall funds like those managed by AMP Capital and the recent 11 per cent devaluation of the Vicinity Centres portfolio.

On a brighter note, service stations, which usually include a convenience retail outlet, were the only asset class out of 12 that valuers thought would see tighter yields.

"With the recommencement of intrastate travel and borders starting to reopen petrol stations are well placed to benefit from increased domestic travel while international borders remain closed," m3property said.

Prime industrial properties were expected to experience minimal change in values or yields but the value of secondary industrial assets was expected to fall as occupiers gravitated to bigger and more modern facilities.

In offices, m3property valuers anticipated that prime assets with strong income security were likely to maintain their values.

"Short-term, Brisbane and Melbourne yields could soften by slightly more than other markets with prime assets in Melbourne currently reflecting a 10 to 20 basis point softening in value," NSW managing director Andrew Duguid said.
direction: service station REITs and property trusts

headline: Westpac's reporting troubles rise
text:
AUSTRAC is expected to widen its case against Westpac after the besieged bank said it found 365,000 "incomplete or inaccurate" reports made to the financial crimes regulator relating to large fund transfers out of Australia.

In addition, the bank has found 175,000 transactions that were not reported to AUSTRAC at all, about twice as many as initially disclosed in May.

Westpac disclosed the new figures to the market as it responded to a "notice to produce" from the regulator, which is preparing to file an expanded statement of claim in the Federal Court by the middle of August.

It is the second time the bank's possible transgressions have widened significantly, after AUSTRAC said in June that the number of customers who may have been involved in potential child exploitation was 23 times larger than identified in its original bombshell claim last November.

Westpac told the stock exchange yesterday the incomplete "threshold transaction reports" (TTRs), which require AUSTRAC to be notified when more than $10,000 is sent in or out of the country, were found after further investigations and suggested the ultimate number of botched reports might still go higher.

But the judge hearing the case, which will go to trial next year if it is not settled, wants AUSTRAC to finalise its claim against the bank as soon as possible, given the substance of the failings are known.

Westpac said it has been notified by AUSTRAC that it may amend its statement of claim to include allegations arising from the additional TTR issues.

Each of the new unreported transactions, or incomplete reports, could amount to an additional, separate breach of the strict anti-money-laundering law. However, the bank said this was not necessarily the case.

"A significant proportion of the potential reporting issues relate to a range of complex scenarios where the legislation requires Westpac to exercise judgment on how multiple transactions may be aggregated and whether a threshold transaction has actually occurred," Westpac said in a one-page release.

"Westpac continues to engage with AUSTRAC in relation to these TTR issues, and notes that the numbers above may change."

The issues relating to TTRs are separate to the problems identified by AUSTRAC relating to "international funds transfer instructions" (IFTI) not being properly filed when Westpac facilitated payments on behalf of clients of some of its correspondent banks.

Westpac first reported to the market it had more issues with its TTRs - in addition to the IFTI and risk-management failings set out by AUSTRAC in its initial statement of claim - in its interim results on May 4.

It said then it had self-reported TTR issues to AUSTRAC relating to "an estimated 60,000 to 90,000 TTRs" that had not been reported to AUSTRAC.

That was higher than the late reporting of 17,870 TTRs that were the subject of the original AUSTRAC claim. It also flagged issues with incomplete or inaccurate information without specifying in May the number of reports involved.

AUSTRAC then requested further information from Westpac in June on the issues relating to its TTR reporting.

Counsel for AUSTRAC, Wendy Harris, QC, said at a directions hearing in June the regulator had flagged its intention to pursue Westpac over new breaches of section 43 of the act, which requires an institution to report threshold transactions or those over $10,000 to AUSTRAC within 10 days and in the format specified.

A revised statement of claim now being prepared will reveal next month how many of the additional problematic TTFs will be included in the litigation.

Westpac and AUSTRAC have been trying to settle the case but have not agreed on the appropriate penalty.

Westpac has admitted to most of the contraventions alleged by AUSTRAC, including the failure to file 23 million IFTIs. But has defended itself against the broader allegation that it does not have an adequate program to mitigate and manage serious financial crime.

Federal Court judge Jonathan Beach has called on AUSTRAC to finalise its claims against Westpac so the matter could be brought to court. "I do think it's time to close this pleading for want of a better word ... We do need to guillotine it at some stage," Justice Beach said in June.

AUSTRAC is due to file an amended statement of claim by August 14, with the next case management meeting scheduled for September 18.
direction: Westpac shares likely to decline due to escalating regulatory penalties and legal uncertainty

headline: ASIC ticks off ASX over a second glitch
text:
The corporate regulator has criticised the Australian Securities Exchange for a second day, raising further concerns with the performance of the main infrastructure and warning the market operator its licence could be hit with "further actions". The ASX's comeback from the longest trading outage in four years on Monday did not go as smoothly as hoped yesterday and was impeded by delays, eventually resolved, with the ageing CHESS system of clearing and settlement late in the session.

The market rose to a nine-month high yesterday after a vaccine breakthrough by US drug manufacturer Moderna buoyed hopes for a faster-than-expected economic recovery. The S&P/ASX 200 Index climbed 13.9 points, or 0.2 per cent, to 6498.

Companies p15, Markets p22
direction: ASX infrastructure and settlement-related stocks face headwinds

headline: Hrdlicka as Virgin CEO sparks fears
text:
Jayne Hrdlicka's appointment to the top job at Virgin Australia has raised doubts over its future, with unions concerned it means the relaunched airline will become a lower-cost carrier, as chief executive Paul Scurrah exits.

Ms Hrdlicka, a former Bain & Company consultant and ex-Jetstar boss, will take the reins of Virgin 2.0 from early next month.

The announcement yesterday ended months of speculation over what role she would play in the company after US private equity giant Bain Capital won a hard-fought bidding war for the nation's No.2 airline in late June.

After Bain's managing director, Mike Murphy, declared in June "we're behind him [Scurrah]" and called fears Ms Hrdlicka would take over "unfounded", the change in leadership has raised fears among workers that the new private equity owner will renege on its vision for a mid-market Virgin.

"All of that has been thrown into disarray," Transport Workers Union, national secretary Michael Kaine said. "The Australian community has been misled." The union suspended talks with Bain over the leadership change on Wednesday.

A source said Bain had forced Mr Scurrah out over conflicts regarding the size of Virgin's international network and the number of higher-end passenger offerings, such as lounges, it would retain.

Mr Scurrah was said to be pushing to keep the airline's options open to launch a low-cost offshoot similar to the now-defunct Tigerair Australia in the future.

"Having seen the company through COVID-19, voluntary administration, the sale to Bain Capital and the redesign of the business, I will be stepping down as CEO and managing director," Mr Scurrah said in a statement.

However, he declined to elaborate on the reasons for his exit.

If Virgin 2.0 were to become a low-cost carrier, it would thrust the airline into direct competition with Ms Hrdlicka's former employer Jetstar, starving parent company Qantas for competitors at the upper end of the domestic market.

Among the promises the private equity firm made when securing the airline, was that Virgin would have 5000 to 6000 employees and operate 60 to 70 planes at full capacity. Seven unions, led by the TWU, wrote to Bain asking the firm to clarify these commitments as news emerged Mr Scurrah was preparing to leave the airline.

In response, Mr Murphy yesterday recommitted to employing 6000 at full tilt - something Virgin's management team formally outlined in August - but did not put a firm number on the fleet.

"The size of the Virgin Australia fleet will be determined by customer demand and - critically - the number of routes open to normal travel," Mr Murphy said in a letter to union representatives.

"This change in leadership does not signal a change in strategy or a change in stance with respect to trade unions. Our people are the foundation of this airline, and ensuring their jobs are sustainable, good jobs for the future is our highest priority."

The unions had also asked for a commitment to retain the Virgin Australia Regional Airlines business, the international arm and airport lounges.

Mr Murphy's letter - titled "toughest year survival" - referred to these businesses only in an attached strategy document disclosed to the ASX in August. That announcement said Virgin was reviewing different operating models for VARA, would focus on short-haul international with fewer long-haul services and maintain a network of airport lounges.

Ms Hrdlicka, who had been previously floated as a possible non-executive director or even chairwoman of Virgin 2.0's board, did not respond to a request for an interview.

Still, she said in a statement: "I am delighted to be joining Virgin Australia at this important time.

"I appreciate Virgin Australia's unique culture, and I want to protect and build on it. And I am determined that Virgin Australia reinvigorates its strong brand and its passion for customer service while embracing the diversity, talent and strength of its people."

Key backers of the relaunched Virgin - Richard Branson's Virgin Group and the Queensland state government - appeared unperturbed by the sudden change in chief executives.

A spokeswoman for Queensland Treasurer Cameron Dick would not comment on Mr Scurrah's sacking, despite the Palaszczuk government stumping up a $200 million package to keep Virgin's headquarters in Brisbane.

Mr Scurrah also has close links to the Labor government.

"Staffing matters and appointments at Virgin are a matter for the company," the spokeswoman said.

Meanwhile, the Virgin Group is still pushing for a stake in the relaunched carrier. Bain has offered the British conglomerate 5 per cent shareholding, but Virgin Group is seeking 10 per cent.

Virgin Australia fell into voluntary administration under the strain of the COVID-19 pandemic in April, appointing Deloitte as its corporate undertakers.

Virgin's lead administration Vaughan Strawbridge said Bain had reaffirmed to him they would not reposition the airline as a low-cost operator.

"Virgin Australia will be a 'hybrid' airline, offering great value to customers," he said.
direction: bearish for Virgin Australia shares

text:
Cardno

Full year	2020	2019

Revenue ($m)	 978.3	 936.9

Net profit ($m)	 56.6	 -44.5

Final dividend	nil	nil
direction: Cardno shares likely to rise on return to profitability

headline: JobKeeper details: Workers to keep two weeks' leave
text:
Workers in distressed businesses which qualify for the JobKeeper payment cannot be forced to take all their annual leave but must be left with two weeks' worth.

Unveiling details of the $130 billion emergency wage subsidy ahead of its passage through Parliament yesterday, Industrial Relations Minister Christian Porter also defended companies such as Qantas Group that are using the $1500 payments to subsidise holiday pay.

Unions argue the $1500 should be paid on top of holiday pay, rather than used to pay down existing liabilities.

But Mr Porter said the payment was designed to help distressed businesses pay workers, whether they were on leave or at work.

"The $1500 subsidises the business to pay that person while they're on leave during a period where there's no demand or very little demand for that business. No different from subsidising the wage," Mr Porter said.

"And when someone goes on leave, they have to be paid their normal wage.

"The employers have no business. They're not doing very well at the moment, right? We're in the middle of an economic challenge like we've never seen before.

"Whether or not they're paying their employee in a business with next to zero work to be on leave or doing something else for a couple of hours a week, does it really matter that much if it saves the employee's job and helps the employer keep their business afloat?" Mr Porter said.

The legislation for the JobKeeper package will pass both Houses of Parliament today during a specially convened one-day skeleton sitting.

Labor and the ACTU failed to secure any changes to the legislation, including extending it to casuals who have worked for more than one employer over the past 12 months, or changing the methodology by which it will be enabled.

Under the legislation, an employee can change the days a person works and also request the worker wash off annual leave.

The worker is entitled to keep two weeks of leave. Both these changes - the days worked and the washing off of leave - would have to be by agreement between workers and bosses.

"An employer can't make them do anything with respect to leave and days worked. But it would be impossible to actually agree to have a direction from an employer which left you with less than two weeks' leave," Mr Porter said.

The legislation also changes the Fair Work Act to enable employers to give a "JobKeeper-enabling Stand Down". This allows them to cut a worker's hours so they only work enough to earn $1500 per fortnight under their usual wage or salary.

"You can only make that alteration if the pandemic has meant that there is no work for that person to do compared to what they would usually do or a limitation in the work that they would do compared to what they would usually do," Mr Porter said.

The third key change will enable an employer to alter an employee's duties and location of work so long as the changes were safe and reasonable and within the skill set of the employee.
direction: pressure on corporate profitability and cash flow for affected sectors

headline: Pandemic doesn't hold back Centuria fund
text:
ASX-listed Centuria Industrial REIT has kicked off the new financial year with what it says is the single largest industrial property deal to take place in Australia after it acquired a Telstra data centre in Melbourne's Clayton and two smaller industrial properties, which will tip the fund's total portfolio value over $2 billion.

The Telstra deal, worth $416.7 million, includes a 30-year lease-back arrangement with the telco retaining ownership of its IT and telecommunications equipment. As a result, Telstra will become the fund's largest tenant.

The fund is also undertaking due diligence on another $45 million of assets, with sales expected to settle in the first half of this financial year.

The announcement coincided with the fund's full-year results, which recorded a lift in the valuation of its industrial assets by 1.3 per cent despite the COVID-19 pandemic wreaking havoc on other areas of the property market.

"If anything, this pandemic has strengthened the importance of industrial," Centuria Industrial REIT fund manager Jesse Curtis said.

"With everyone at home, home deliveries and that kind of e-commerce activity is actually increasing so we think industrial will probably become more important and more desirable and will remain an essential service as part of the ongoing operations down there."

Mr Curtis added that the fund benefited from 52 per cent of its tenancy base belonging to production, packaging and distribution of consumer staples and pharmaceutical sectors.

While funds from operations were in line with a revised guidance at 18.9 cents per unit, Mr Curtis warned that given the "ongoing impacts of the COVID-19 pandemic" the FFO guidance for 2021 was expected to fall to 17.4 cents - a decrease of 7.9 per cent.

The fund recorded a statutory net profit of $75.3 million, lower than the $88.8 million last year.

Mr Curtis said the REIT had made $1 million in provisions for rent relief as a result of the pandemic, but emphasised the portfolio had demonstrated resilience despite COVID-19, with 99 per cent of tenants continuing to operate through the pandemic. On average, 97 per cent of rent was collected between April and June.

During the financial year the fund increased occupancy levels across its portfolio from 95 per cent to 98 per cent and a weighted average lease expiry of 7.2 years up from 4.3 years in FY2019.

Following the sale of its data centre in Melbourne, Telstra said it had now raised $1.5 billion through the sale of property assets since 2018.

Mr Curtis said the deals announced today showed the scale at which the industrial REIT could play.
direction: bullish for Centuria Industrial REIT and industrial property sector
