headline: Change of station
text:
From a reclaimed 'cinema of sin' to eateries repurposed from old ticket counters, there is much to explore in 36 hours in a revitalised King's Cross, writes Mark Vanhoenacker.

When I was young I liked to read about cities I never dreamt I'd visit. And that's how I fell in love with the name of the London train station and neighbourhood known as King's Cross. But when I first moved to England, in 1996, grace wasn't so easy to find amid the widespread social and economic challenges that had come to define this corner of north London.

Today, much (but by no means all) of the neighbourhood has been transformed by swank restaurants and shops, expensively repurposed industrial structures and the glittering glass offices in which the gears of a decent chunk of London's booming knowledge economy turn ever faster. Welcome, in these Brexit-tossed times, to King's Cross and to one of London's most fascinating stories of adversity and renewal.

FRIDAY

2.30pm | Urban fervour

For long-time residents, the transformation of King's Cross has meant opportunities but also challenges, including gentrification, crowds and controversies over facial recognition cameras. You're safe from all three at Calthorpe Community Garden. This verdant hideout - the product of community activism, on a site once earmarked for offices - offers secluded benches, a swing and the chance to wander past garden plots lovingly tended by some of London's friendliest strangers. Enjoy a cheese and pickle toastie at the homey cafe.

4pm | A tale of two stations

Only a "chilly Londoner", wrote E.M. Forster, would fail to find character in London's great railway terminals. To Margaret, in Howards End, King's Cross station "had always suggested infinity" - and to think she never saw the new concourse, opened in 2012, which added soaring space and much-needed light to a hub that's busier than many airports. Keep an eye out for the sign for Platform 9¾, where Harry Potter caught the express to Hogwarts. Then cross the street to the spellbinding St Pancras International station, a hub for commuter and national services as well as high-speed Eurostar trains to continental Europe. The battle to preserve and restore this Victorian Gothic masterpiece took decades, and many Londoners would agree that it's now the jewel in the crown of railway terminals that encircle the capital. Welcome just-disembarked Parisians with a tune on the public pianos - one's a signed gift from Elton John. Then snag a selfie with the statue of poet John Betjeman (whose advocacy helped save the station) or with the gold-leafed, 5.5-metre-wide clock. After the previous clock was dropped by a crane in the 1960s, a train guard gathered the shards in a wheelbarrow and took them home to his farm. Dent - the clockmakers responsible for Big Ben - and another firm relied on these fragments to create the replica you see today.

5pm | Happy hours

The old ticket office of St Pancras station was the very image of Victorian splendour. Now it's home to the Booking Office, one of my favourite bars. Here, just yards from gleaming Eurostar trains, fete Anglo-continental harmonies with a lavender vesper: an entente cordiale that includes Chase vodka and gin from Herefordshire, French vermouth, and bee pollen from Lille's Sébastopol Market. Then stick your landing at the German Gymnasium, one of Britain's first purpose-built gyms when it opened in the 1860s; today it's a buzzing restaurant. The parallel bars are long gone, although grabbing a prime-time reservation here might qualify as an Olympic event. Award yourself the schupfnudeln (hand-rolled potato noodles, celeriac, mushroom, truffles and butter sauce).

7.30pm | "Only last night I found myself lost ..." My musical introduction to King's Cross came via the song with the same name by the Pet Shop Boys, the British dance-pop duo. The tough neighbourhood in which the group's lead singer, Neil Tennant, found a haunting metaphor for Thatcherite Britain was also home to a thriving club scene. Today, concerts at

St Pancras Old Church showcase mostly emerging artists as well as the likes of Phoebe Bridgers, Sam Smith and Sinead O'Connor. Or see who's playing at the Water Rats, the pub that in a former incarnation hosted Bob Dylan's first British concert, in 1962; the Pogues' first concert, in 1982; and the first London show of Oasis, in 1994.

SATURDAY

10am | Shop 'til you ...

In the mid-19th century a set of buildings was constructed to allow coal to be lowered from just-arrived trains and then packed for distribution across London. Today, Coal Drops Yard (renovated by Heatherwick Studio, the King's Cross-based firm that also designed the Vessel at Manhattan's Hudson Yards) is home to a shopping venue that's the epitome of post-industrial chic. Start at Wolf & Badger, a high-end stockist of independent artisans, where you can find vegan lip balm by Hertfordshire-based Evolve Beauty and English-spun Merino woolwear by Peregrine, founded in 1796. Move on to Tom Dixon's interior design shop, where the star attractions are the geometrically swirling Spring pendant lamps. Finally, in a metropolis in which homelessness is rising, don't miss Boutique by Shelter. Purchases of donated designer and vintage items - including the

occasional Hermès handbag and Burberry suit - benefit Shelter, a prominent homelessness charity.

11am | A mad day out

It's hard to believe that St Pancras Old Church was once a riverside country chapel, distinguished by its origins (the church claims to have been "a site of Christian worship since the fourth century"); its well-stocked graveyard (the scene of body-snatching in Charles Dickens' A Tale of Two Cities), and its distance from a much smaller London ("walk not there too late", as one 16th-century chronicler summarised the church's rural, thief-ridden environs).

The customisable tours are highly recommended. Ask your guide about the shape of British phone boxes, disinterments on a scale that would make Stephen King blanch and potential visits from a

motley gallery of ghosts, including Mary Shelley, John Lennon and the son of Benjamin Franklin.

1pm | Digging in

Of all the alterations that the Industrial Revolution inflicted on Britain's landscape, few are as pleasing as the Regent's Canal, cut in the early 19th century to link the Grand Union Canal at Paddington to the River Thames at Limehouse. Stroll along the towpaths, overgrown with sycamore, ash and willow, then stop at Word on the Water, a bookshop on a canal boat, where you can warm your hands over the wood stove and commune with Star, an elderly beagle-collie who's spent her whole long

life on boats.

3pm | Royal London

Kings Place is home to one of London's best-curated selections of concerts, talks and exhibitions. Start your explorations at Pangolin London, a sculpture-focused gallery that has an art trail around the complex. If you pinwheel backward into the icy canal while admiring the works, as I nearly did, the Rotunda restaurant offers hot chocolate and warm blankets on its waterside deck.

6.30pm | Sweet and bitter

Chef and cookbook author Yotam Ottolenghi is a fan of walks along the Regent's Canal; his kids play in the fountains of Granary Square and the most popular of his London restaurants is in nearby Islington. In other words, he's the perfect Londoner to take yours truly on a food tour of the new King's Cross. For a quick bite, he recommended the tasty, too-big-to-finish sandwiches from Bodega Rita's. For a proper meal he chose Barrafina, an upscale tapas restaurant, where he ordered his go-to dishes: arroz negro with cuttlefish and Iberian pork, the classic tortilla and a green salad. I won't forget how joyfully he scraped out the last of the arroz negro: "When it caramelises it goes sweet and bitter," he explained. "It's a different world when you hit the bottom of the pan."

SUNDAY

10am | Somers time

Squeezed between St Pancras and Euston stations is the quiet, mostly residential neighbourhood of Somers Town. Start with a walk through the Ossulston Estate. Completed not long before World War II, in the heart of what had once been known as the Somers Town slum, this homage to Viennese-style modernism has been described as one of London's most architecturally important housing projects. Then stop outside 15 Phoenix Road, the former home of Henry Croft, the orphan, street sweeper and rat catcher behind the "Pearly Kings and Queens" - a uniquely London working-class tradition, still going strong, that involves charitable fundraising, inherited titles and elaborately pearl-bedecked garb. Finally, rest your feet at the Somers Town Coffee House - a pub, despite the name, that feels as local as anything could in this cosmopolitan corner of town.

Noon | Mother tongue

If written culture has a centre of gravity, it's lurking somewhere in the stacks of the British Library. The collection - the world's second largest - contains more than

170 million items. In terms of shelf space, it grows by eight kilometres a year. Behind-the-scenes tours (book online) finish in the Treasures Gallery, home to the fourth-century Codex Sinaiticus, the earliest surviving Bible to contain the complete New Testament; handwritten first drafts of Beatles lyrics; a ninth-century Chinese version of the Diamond Sutra, a Buddhist text, described as the "world's earliest dated, printed book", and Jane Austen's desk. The library's delightful shop has something for everyone on your list, including Literary London jigsaw puzzles and ingenious stools and side tables made of recycled paper.

2pm | "Fear contended with desire ..."

Around Caledonian Road are numerous monuments to an older King's Cross. Start outside the Scala, once London's legendary "cinema of sin". A filmmaker called it "a country club for criminals and lunatics"; today, the Scala houses a nightclub. Next up is the non-profit Housmans Bookshop. Named for Laurence Housman - peace activist, suffragist and younger brother of poet A.E. Housman - this haven for the anti-establishment opened in King's Cross in 1959. Sections include "Trotsky", "Gramsci" and "Anarchist Key Thinkers". There's a fine selection of books about King's Cross and London, too. Finish at Drink, Shop & Do, an art, music, food and learning venue set up after the 2007-08 financial crisis to encourage creativity and community. The neon "Adult & Erotica" sign is original, while the classes - recently, on how to bling your beret, paint watercolour portraits of dogs or sculpt a clay version of performer Tina Turner - are all the proof you need that King's Cross, like London, is never the same place twice.L&L

THE NEW YORK TIMES

Mark Vanhoenacker is a columnist for the Financial Times and the author of Skyfaring and How to Land a Plane.

●iWhere to stay

St Pancras Renaissance

Splurge at this aptly named establishment, first opened as the Midland Grand Hotel in 1873. It reopened in 2011 after a comprehensive renovation and is one of the grandest hotels in London. Rates from £529 ($1040).

Standard Hotel

Another monumental renovation, right across Euston Road, is home to Europe's first Standard. It took years and many millions to turn this brutalist former municipal structure into the luxury hotel, which opened last summer - even though, as one employee told me with a smile, "it already looked like a Standard". King's Terrace rooms, some with balconies featuring barely concealed outdoor bathtubs, from £450.
direction: bearish for UK equities due to Brexit uncertainty

headline: Industry Insight
text:
This content is produced by The Australian Financial Review in commercial partnership with the Australian Institute of Company Directors.

The decisions made by boards have a profound impact across society and the economy.

Nearly all of Australia's most important institutions - our largest companies and charities, our hospitals and emergency services, our sporting clubs and arts companies - are governed by a board of directors or a similar body.

Being a director is not a straightforward job. Boards often need to make decisions with imperfect information in response to complex situations. Directors need foresight to understand how actions now might play out well into the future, making the trade-off between risk and reward.

They need compassion for the organisation's stakeholders and a strong moral compass. They need to think independently and have the fortitude to question other board members when they feel something is not quite right. And all of this with a legal regime that imposes heavy personal liability on directors for missteps.

Take the past seven months. Directors have been tested as their organisations confronted the many challenges of COVID-19. Boards needed to oversee the roll-out of crisis management plans and ensure the health and safety of staff and customers. As organisations rapidly shifted to remote working, they will have discussed how to mitigate risks like the potential for cybersecurity breaches.

With the pandemic disrupting operations and organisations seeing precipitous declines in revenue, some boards will have had difficult meetings on whether to continue trading and many boards will have worked with management to realign strategy and adapt business models - work that is still ongoing for many organisations.

The community quite rightly expects a lot of directors. Directors are leaders. The welfare and livelihoods of many depend on them making good decisions.

As we have seen with the recent high-profile issues at Rio Tinto and AMP Capital, shareholders, staff and the community at large feel let down when organisational leaders fail to meet expectations. Without the requirement to obtain a licence or qualification to sit on a board, it is incumbent on directors, and aspiring directors, themselves to ensure they have the right skills, knowledge and mindset to successfully steward an organisation.

They need to understand their role, as distinct from management, and their duties and obligations under the law. They also need to develop strategic acumen and a sense of the levers they can pull to shape an organisation's culture for the better.

For those who are considering stepping into the boardroom, education is essential. Just as no one would fly on a plane with an unqualified pilot, why should the staff, customers or other stakeholders trust a director with no training? But professional development for directors is not a one-off, set-and-forget exercise. Very experienced directors do make mistakes and they are more inclined to do so if they fall behind.

As technology rapidly changes, as new risks emerge and as regulation evolves, directors must stay across the latest developments.

Just as organisations invest in continual professional development for staff, ongoing professional development is an investment in being a better and more effective director.

The collective success of our organisations and institutions is born out of the personal commitment of directors to ensure they are up to the job.

For directors, the satisfaction of creating the right environment for people to succeed and achieve an organisation's mission makes that ongoing effort worthwhile.

For more on the AICD's director education programs, visit aicd.com.au/education.

Directors are leaders. The welfare and livelihoods of many depend on them making good decisions.
direction: increased scrutiny and liability risks for corporate boards

headline: Lending laws face limbo as Senate tails off
text:
Key crossbenchers remain unconvinced by the Morrison government's plan to scrap responsible lending laws, while moves to streamline environmental approvals are also set to remain in limbo during the parliament's final two sitting weeks of the year.

Treasurer Josh Frydenberg will introduce legislation to push ahead with changes to responsible lending laws, which he argues is necessary for the post-coronavirus economic recovery.

Submissions to Treasury on the draft legislation closed 10 days ago but banking victims have pleaded with the government to abandon the change.

The government will probably need the support of three of the five Senate crossbenchers to support relaxing rules for the banks as Labor and the Greens are poised to oppose the overhaul.

The responsible lending laws were introduced following the global financial crisis but the government wants to shift back from "lender beware" to the traditional "borrower beware".

Independent senator Rex Patrick said he was taking advice from the Australian Bankers Association but was yet to be convinced. "I'm not sure if it is in anyone's interest to have loans made to people who cannot afford them," he said.

Fellow crossbencher Stirling Griff, who will meet consumer groups tomorrow over the changes, is also unsure. "I can see some merit in their argument but there are a lot of concerns," he said.

"You can't expect people to protect themselves and that's the reason the laws were put in in the first place."

One Nation leader Pauline Hanson and independent senator Jacqui Lambie are also yet to declare their hand on the responsible lending laws.

However, Senator Hanson will push for a clampdown on overseas litigation funders, demanding Mr Frydenberg regulate the industry and cap costs on a sliding scale. Labor has put forward a motion to disallow litigation funding regulations in the Corporations Act, which needs to be resolved by Thursday, but Senator Hanson is proposing her amendments as an alternative.

"Current laws are weak and have seen litigants lose as much as half of their court-awarded payouts to lawyers and overseas funders who not only gouge victims but avoid paying tax here in Australia," Senator Hanson said. "Litigants deserve to walk away with at least 70 per cent of their payouts, not half."

The government's hopes of gaining support for another measure it says will boost the economy, streamlining environmental approvals, has also been dealt a blow.

Changes to the Environmental Protection and Biodiversity Act to devolve approval powers to the states, as recommended by businessman Graeme Samuel's review, have been rejected by Labor, Greens and crossbench senators.

The three groupings all issued dissenting reports saying the bill should not be passed.

Senator Griff said the legislation still needed significant work and, along with senators Patrick and Lambie, he would not let it pass this side of Christmas.

Another piece of legislation the government wants to introduce in the Parliament's final sitting weeks, the merger of the Family and the Federal Circuit courts, is also running into opposition. The Family Court's first chief justice, Elizabeth Evatt, warned the merger would be counterproductive.

"The increasing number of cases in which issues of family violence and child abuse are raised has led to an even greater need today for family law jurisdiction to be vested exclusively in specialised judges who can give their full attention to the needs of family law clients without being diverted to exercise other unrelated jurisdictions," she said.
direction: bearish for banks

headline: Drummond upbeat despite outbreak
text:
Like the rest of us, Craig Drummond is watching the numbers out of the Sydney COVID-19 outbreak closely.

And having lived through Melbourne's second wave, the chief executive of private health insurance giant Medibank was relieved to see a fall in new cases in Sydney from 30 on Sunday to 15 yesterday. "You'd take those numbers any time, especially early in an outbreak," he says.

Like many of the chief executives surveyed by The Australian Financial Review in our annual Chanticleer CEO poll, Drummond never held the view the pandemic was over, despite Australia's world-class handling of the virus. Sydney is a reminder outbreaks will occur and communities need to be ready.

"There's an inevitability about some of this and I think it's about how you respond. I think one of the issues is what are the learnings from this," he says.

Masks and social distancing should be one lesson, Drummond says, after being struck during a business trip to Sydney in recent weeks by the "stark contrast between the Victorian community and the NSW community".

As soon as he got off the plane he was shocked by the lack of masks in Sydney and the lower prevalence of social distancing compared to Melbourne.

Clearly, Victoria's battles with the virus continue to inform both public health settings and community attitudes, but Drummond wonders if the Sydney outbreak should be a catalyst for change in attitude in other parts of the country.

"It's a stark reminder for all of us that we need to continue to be very vigilant. And I think in parts of the community that vigilance has probably slackened."

While business leaders have tended to rail against the type of rapid border closures that we've seen in recent days, Drummond is more understanding - what happened in Melbourne shows why premiers want to protect their citizens and economies from an outbreak.

That said, he would like to see a greater degree of flexibility, particularly for those compassionate cases involving very ill people.

Where his greatest sympathies lie is with the small business owners hit hardest by the Sydney cluster, many of which will be coming into their most important annual trading period. The empty shops around Melbourne's suburbs and particularly its CBD are reminders of the SMEs that won't make it back when the pandemic passes.

But for all of that, the outbreak hasn't robbed Drummond of his optimism about the year ahead.

He says the government's efforts to stimulate the economy have been powerful and argues corporate Australia is in a strong position from a balance sheet perspective.

More than this, he says the clear message from the federal government is that it will prop up the economy if fresh pandemic problems emerge.

"I'd be very hopeful that we get momentum back in the early part of the next year," he says. "I think consumers and businesses should feel optimistic - not buoyant, but optimistic - that the government has got our back, and they can look to grow."

Australia's top CEOs overwhelmingly told the Chanticleer CEO poll that while they were cautiously optimistic about the outlook for COVID-19, vigilance is still required as we await the rollout of the vaccine.

"A vaccine will mark the beginning of the end for the pandemic, but the end will last longer than any of us want," said Incitec Pivot boss Jeanne Johns in a comment that neatly sums up the sentiment.

"The pandemic won't end until the vaccine is available internationally, which will take time given the supply and logistical challenges to make it available globally."

CEOs also made it clear that even when the vaccine has been deployed, the pandemic will leave permanent changes to how we live and work.

"We need an effective vaccine before we can see an open society again," Tabcorp chief David Attenborough said.

"Even then, COVID will continue to shape business in terms of the way consumers choose to interact with business and how confident they are in doing so, ongoing economic uncertainty and how we make our workplaces more productive."

Medibank, which yesterday announced a 3.25 per cent increase in its health premiums for 2021, shut its Sydney office on Friday and has closed its Brookvale store in the city's northern beaches region.

The group plans to reopen its Melbourne office in the first week of February, with Drummond saying his team will give itself a little more time to get staff comfortable with logistics such as public transport arrangements and lifts. "We'll just give people even a bit more flexibility. But we do believe that people should come back to the office."

British deal underscores

key to City Chic's rise

Christmas has come a few days early for Phil Ryan.

The chief executive of women's plus-size fashion retailer City Chic Collective was palpably excited yesterday as he announced the ASX group had acquired the e-commerce and wholesale businesses of British plus-size chain Evans from collapsed retail giant Arcadia Group.

While buying a business out of administration brings no shortage of risks, Ryan is confident he is buying the best bits - that is, not the bricks and mortar retail outlets - of a global leader in what City Chic sees as a $50 billion curvy apparel market.

Ryan says he has studied Evans closely for more than a decade and even worked with Arcadia on supply of product. Having talked up City Chic's ability to move offshore, Ryan says it is "time for us to take the next step in this region".

"We looked up to Evans and I have really followed its journey," Ryan gushes. "I've been across this brand forever. You might say I've done 15 years of due diligence as a fan."

City Chic will pay £23.1 million ($41 million) in cash for the Evans brand and intellectual property, its customer base and its inventory. The e-commerce and wholesale business generated £26 million of sales in the 12 months to the end of August, or about 43 per cent of the total sales of the business.

While the struggles of the broader British retail market have weighed on sales in recent years - and the struggles of Arcadia have hurt in the past 12 months - Ryan says the combination of the strength of the Evans brand and the growth trajectory in the online side of the business will make the deal earnings accretive in the first full year under City Chic Collective's ownership.

But Ryan is looking well beyond the first year. "Really it's not about that. It's about the ability to take this brand and grow it by leveraging our product mix over time."

For all Arcadia's problems - Sir Philip Green's empire, which most prominently included the Topshop chain, collapsed on November 30 - Ryan says it did a good job shifting Evans' focus from bricks and mortar to digital. There are just 30 standalone stores and 70 concession outlets left; these will trade until the end of March.

As part of the e-commerce strategy, Arcadia also built an online marketplace where it would sell its own brands and others.

"That gives us the opportunity to introduce our brands quickly and seamlessly as customers are used to having other brands on the site."

Ryan, who has been talking with Arcadia since the start of the year and started due diligence well before the group was placed in administration, says the marketplace is likely to be wound back over time to brands only from the broader City Chic stable.

"I think you need to be a vertical retailer these days to have any penetration and profitability."

And he cautions that it will take time for City Chic to understand exactly what the Evans customer wants.

"I've watched this woman from afar, but I haven't had the privilege of talking to her," Ryan says. "I need her to tell me what's going to sell so I can home in on the range and edit for her."

But the way Ryan talks about his customer base is striking and goes directly to City Chic's tight focus on the plus-size sector.

It's a single mindedness that investors love; even with all the risks associated with a deal in an offshore market, and in buying a business out of administration, the stock surged almost 12 per cent yesterday morning to $3.55.

It's now up 32 per cent since the start of the year, and 342 per cent since the market hit the bottom in late March.
direction: downward pressure on Medibank shares

headline: Morgan Stanley does splits
text:
Wall Street giant Morgan Stanley has fired up two back-up trading sites to ensure its securities and trading units can operate as normal in coming weeks.

It is understood Morgan Stanley has opened a small second trading desk inside Sydney's Chifley Tower, albeit on a different floor and accessible by a different lift well to its main trading desk.

It has also sent some sales and trading staff to work from its disaster recovery site in Homebush, about 15km west of the CBD, not far from where rival Bank of America has its bunker.

The bank has also activated coronavirus contingency plans across its retail, investment banking, operations and other units, which include having some staff work from home or at alternate sites, and others in the office.

Morgan Stanley is one of Australia's three biggest equities traders, with substantial institutional and retail businesses. The sites are expected to be used to process trades for both parts. It is also a large participant in fixed interest and other markets.

It follows similar moves by Morgan Stanley offshore and mirrors rivals in Australia including Bank of America, JPMorgan, Goldman Sachs, UBS and Macquarie Capital. Credit Suisse is expected to implement a "split teams" policy for its local team as early as Monday.

The moves are designed to ensure investment banks can continue to advise clients and process trades should the coronavirus pandemic worsen.

While M&A and capital markets deal activity has slowed considerably, volatile market conditions have seen trading volumes pick up. The bigger the value of trades, the higher the commission for trading desks - and Australian equities desks have had some of their busiest ever days this week.

The majors' Australian investment banking teams have escaped evacuations and shut office scenarios, for now.

As Street Talk revealed on Thursday, mid-tier stockbroker Bell Potter cancelled client meetings and sent some staff home after one of its senior retail brokers was believed to have contracted coronavirus. Its institutional equities desk was taking orders as usual.
direction: bearish for financials and equities

headline: Victoria's bitter $12b hit from new lockdown
text:
Melbourne's revived lockdown and border closure is set to cost Victoria's economy as much as $2 billion a week, delay business investment and snuff out the recovery in the jobs market.

As Victorian Premier Daniel Andrews ordered 5.1 million Melburnians to stay home for six weeks, meaning a $12 billion hit, economists said it would force the Morrison government to recast its planned July 23 economic statement, which will include details on the next phase of the JobKeeper wage subsidy scheme.

The lockdown comes as Reserve Bank of Australia governor Philip Lowe warned of heightened uncertainty on the economic recovery.

"The outlook, including the nature and speed of the expected recovery, remains highly uncertain and the pandemic is likely to have long-lasting effects on the economy," Dr Lowe said.

Treasurer Josh Frydenberg pledged the government would continue to provide support to Victoria and had already injected $10 billion into the state's economy from JobKeeper payments and the business cash flow boost over the past three months.

"The situation in Victoria is serious and we are at a critical point. We must all work together to contain the outbreak of new COVID-19 cases," Mr Frydenberg told The Australian Financial Review.

"With Victoria comprising around a quarter of the Australian economy, what happens in Victoria matters to the rest of the nation and will impact on the speed and trajectory of our economic recovery."

Australian Chamber of Commerce and Industry chief executive James Pearson said employers and employees across Melbourne would have to continue relying on government support for some time.

"This may well mean extending JobKeeper for not just the hardest hit industries right across the country, but extending it geographically for those imposed by more restrictions," he said.

Despite calls, the government will not retain free childcare - which ends this week nationally - for parents in lockdown areas.

However, Education Minister Dan Tehan will allow centres to waive the gap fee paid by parents if they withdraw their child for COVID-19 reasons, saving families money while retaining their place. The government will continue to pay the childcare subsidy to the centre.

With Melbourne making up about 20 per cent of the nation's population, Commonwealth Bank economist Gareth Aird said if Victoria's gross state product was 10 per cent lower because of the shutdown, gross domestic product would be 2.25 per cent lower over that period.

"Given the shutdown is expected to last six weeks it could be expected to shave 1 per cent off Q3 20 GDP. It's a significant hit," he said.

Mr Frydenberg said in May the economy lost $4 billion for every week of coronavirus restrictions. That $4 billion hit was "equivalent to what around 4 million Australians on the median wage would earn in a week".

With Victoria's economy making up just under 25 per cent of the national economy, full lockdown restrictions would wipe as much as $1 billion off the state's economy a week.

But other estimates put the damage higher. Victoria's GDP in the March quarter was $121 billion placing the state's weekly economic output at $10 billion.

During the worst week of the April shutdown, bank data showed spending from credit cards was down as much as 40 per cent compared with the same week last year.

Applying that magnitude to Victoria's $5.75 billion in consumption per week equates to a $2.3 billion hit to state consumption per week.

NAB chief economist Alan Oster said an estimate of such magnitude was probably on the "very high" side but that there would still be a substantial hit to the economy.

"Short answer is no one knows. It depends on how long it lasts and what closures it triggers. Obviously it's not positive."

IFM Investors chief economist Alex Joiner said the estimate of the $1 billion to $2.5 billion cost per week to the Victorian economy was "worthwhile".

"This is definitely going to have to impact the Treasurer's forecasts that are being prepared for July 23 economic statement," Dr Joiner said.

ANZ economist Cherelle Murphy said such estimates were "fair" but that the big economic danger from Victoria's outbreak and tighter restrictions was the jobs market.
direction: bearish for Australian equities and rate-sensitive sectors

headline: Virus has SE Asian economies gasping
text:
Kuala Lumpur | South-east Asian nations are scrambling to offset the impact of the coronavirus as the health crisis begins to threaten financial stability in the region.

The Singaporean government has unveiled a stimulus package, Indonesia has moved aggressively to prop up its currency after spooked investors sold off government bonds, and in Thailand, the central bank is expected to cut rates after the government trimmed its growth forecast for the tourism-dependent economy.

Across the region, the travel sector faces a massive drop in demand due to travel bans in and out of China. The Malaysian hotel industry, for example, says in the tourism hotspots of Malacca, Ipoh, Port Dickson and Penang, hotel occupancy rates could fall as much as 50 per cent.

Authorities are acting to shore up local businesses and reassure investors, while the country's finance minister has flagged possible stimulus measures.

The health crisis is particularly poorly timed for Malaysia, where the travel industry was hoping 30 million tourists would respond this year to a big promotional campaign and inject 100 billion ringgit ($36 billion) into the economy. The nation's increasing reliance on tourism means the coronavirus will likely deal a heavier blow than SARS in 2003, which shaved 0.4 percentage points off third-quarter growth.

Malaysian Chinese make up about one quarter of Malaysia's population and, as the nation has moved up the development ladder, travel between the two countries has increased. Last year China was the biggest market for Malaysian tourism after Singapore and Indonesia.

Singapore's 2020 budget, to be handed down in two weeks, will contain at least 30 economic support measures. These will range from interventions to ensure the financial system doesn't seize up due to reduced liquidity to financial support for companies supplying materials and equipment needed to combat the virus.

Singapore barely escaped a recession last year after the US-China trade war exacerbated structural weaknesses. Lee Hsien Loong's government, which is expected go to the polls this year, was already tipped to unveil a big-spending budget on February 18.

Citi economists, who have cut their forecast for GDP growth in Singapore this year to 1.3 per cent from 1.8 per cent, believe the government will follow up with other support measures to reduce the virus's impact on the economy.

"These initial interventions aim to boost confidence, but they are unlikely to be sufficient to curtail a sharp downturn in [the first quarter]", the economists wrote in a research note yesterday.

In Indonesia, the central bank intervened in foreign exchange and bond markets yesterday to prop up the national currency, the rupiah.

Bank Indonesia bought around 1.5 trillion rupiah ($165 million) worth of bonds on the secondary market in morning trading, and was expected to continue buying until the market closed, Nanang Hendarsah, executive director of BI's Monetary Management Department told The Australian Financial Review.

Indonesia's view on banning Chinese travellers has changed dramatically in just a few days. Last week one government minister said it would cause too much of a diplomatic rift to refuse entry to all China nationals. But as of yesterday, no travellers who have spent more than 14 days in China are allowed into Indonesia. From tomorrow, all flights between the two countries will be banned indefinitely.

The ban is more bad news for Bali, already reeling from the cancellation of all Chinese package tours. Some businesses on the holiday island have already closed their doors, reports the Bali outpost of the Association of the Indonesian Tour and Travels Agencies (ASITA).

ASITA estimates the island lost at least $US8 million ($12 million) in one week after 20,000 to 25,000 Chinese tourists expected to holiday there during the Lunar New Year failed to show.

Chinese tourists account for about 30 per cent of the Bali tourist market.

Key pointsIn two weeks Singapore's budget will bring in at least 30 new support measures.

Thailand, Malaysia and Indonesia have been hit by tourism cancellations.
direction: bearish for SE Asian travel, tourism, and hospitality stocks

headline: Milk of public support not won by farmer hypocrisy
text:
Probably the most hypocritical complaint from the dairy industry and its political allies is that Australia is running out of milk.

Terry Richardson, the president of industry group Australian Dairy Farmers, declared a year ago: "We are not far off from being a net importer of milk fat." Last week, the message was amplified by Agriculture Minister David Littleproud, who predicted an imminent "tipping point" for Aussie milk.

"There won't be enough dairy farmers in this country to continue to supply fresh milk for Australians right around the country," the Nationals deputy leader said on Wednesday.

What both men left out of their Chicken Little-like warnings was that, eight months ago, one industry in particular demanded the government ratify trade treaties with Indonesia and Hong Kong so it could reverse a decline in its share of global exports: dairy.

The latest attempt to reverse two decades of industry deregulation is a demand by Australian Dairy Farmers that Woolworths, Coles and Aldi increase the price of store-label drinking milk by 36 per cent to $1.50 a litre. It also wants unspecified price rises for cheese, cream, butter and other dairy products.

The campaign, which has received considerable support from Littleproud, is an example of the atavistic policy instincts of the agricultural lobby: if in doubt, seek protection.

The government and dairy lobby cite the loss of 500 dairy farms in the 2018-19 financial year to warn that Australia's milk self-sufficiency is under threat.

In reality, the decline in individually owned farms is not recent, nor has it hurt productivity. Before deregulation in 2000, dairy was the most heavily subsidised corner of agriculture. After it was forced to compete like any other industry, the best farmers thrived.

The number of farms more than halved. As production shifted from Queensland and NSW to more suitable land in Victoria and Tasmania, the value of milk produced by each farm more than tripled, according to the Australian Bureau of Agricultural and Resource Economics. As less land and labour was used, profits rose too.

Australia's 1.4 million cows became the fourth-largest global dairy resource after New Zealand, the European Union and the United States. Today, 35 per cent of dairy production is sold overseas, generating about $3 billion a year.

The change wasn't easy, and some farmers didn't have the skills to make it. Many small farms that have been in the family for two or three generations and had no debt continue to live comfortably, milking 200 to 300 cows a day themselves, or with a single employee.

Others, who scaled up to 1000 or more cows, found themselves running mini-corporations. They usually needed at least 10 staff and had to learn about rostering, industrial relations, health and safety regulations, and payment systems.

In some cases, diseconomies of scale emerged. While large herds stand for hours on concrete waiting to be milked, they aren't producing milk. Moving 1000 cows to a fresh pasture takes a lot longer than a herd one-fifth the size.

To reduce the energy cows expend not producing milk, some farmers split their herds in two. That doubles the complexity of their rosters. "Scale can be a good thing," says Brian Crockart, a dairy consultant in northern Victoria. "But some make that change and they are not good at employing people, or managing staff, and they make no more money than in the past, and they have more headaches."

Overall, though, the industry appears to be in robust shape. The average farm's cash income will increase 36 per cent to $165,000 this financial year, according to ABARES, a government forecaster.

Littleproud and others complain cheap supermarket milk has "devalued" the dairy industry. In an economic sense, the claim appears unsupported.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission, questioning industry executives under oath and sequestering company documents, told the Senate five months ago it didn't find any evidence "that farm-gate price movements were directly influenced by prevailing retail or domestic wholesale prices".

There have been 10 dairy reviews in 10 years. At the behest of a far-right populist, Pauline Hanson, the Senate is conducting an 11th, which is considering the industry's price-fixing request. Woolworths says it won't jack prices up. Coles' position is unclear.

Believing Woolworths, Coles and Aldi are responsible for their troubles may make struggling dairy farmers feel better about their plight.

Maybe, though, it is time the industry, and its political representatives, ended its obsession with making Australians pay more for milk and adopt a philosophy that has helped other industries thrive: never blame the customer.
direction: bearish for dairy sector and supermarket shares

headline: The insider trading fix to keep start-ups local
text:
Sometimes the most obscure things make a difference.

In its recent Issues Paper on FinTech and RegTech, the Senate standing committee sought questions by last Friday on whether programmable trading by start-up founders could keep companies onshore. Specifically:

"The committee is interested in measures that encourage successful local company founders to list their companies in Australia rather than overseas. To this end, the committee is interested in feedback on the potential for a scheme similar to the Rule 10b5-1 trading plans available in the United States to be offered in Australia."

Interestingly, the committee suggests this change is among a handful that could make start-up founders list on the Australian Securities Exchange, rather than in places with more balanced (aka well-thought-through) insider trading laws:

"Rule 10b5-1 plans benefit both companies and their insiders by offering clarity and certainty on how insiders can plan and structure securities transactions to avoid incurring liability. Atlassian's founders were able to undertake stock transactions utilising this scheme in early 2020 through their US company listing."

Changes to Australian insider trading laws to enable Rule 10b5-1 trading plans have been an obvious need for almost 20 years. This and a raft of other initiatives to take the hard edges off our insider trading regime have been on the table since the 2001 report of the Companies and Securities Advisory Committee into the issues. Adoption of a rule similar to the US was literally the second recommendation in that report. And not a single reform has been made since.

The US Rule 10b5-1 plan insider trading exemption is delightfully simple: you are not guilty of insider trading if you program trading in shares or other securities at a time when you do not have any inside information. And you don't mess with the plan (come hell or high water).

So, for example, if an executive puts in place a program to sell 5 per cent of the shares they hold in their company on the first trading day of each month for six months, it does not matter if they later come into possession of MNPI (material non-public information).

Under current Australian regulatory settings, that does not work because the offence of insider trading is triggered at any point at which there is a "dealing" in securities. Dealing takes place both at the time an instruction to trade is given and at the point a trade occurs.

If you contract to sell the shares in your company today but then become aware of MNPI before settlement and do not tell the buyer, you are technically guilty of insider trading. Even if the sale contract says categorically that you have no obligation to update. If you sign up to participate in a company's dividend reinvestment plan and have kept that in place for years but get MNPI a few days before the dividend is paid to you in scrip, you offend.

This hits executives hardest because they get MNPI all the time. And that is where a Rule 10b5-1 plan works best. US executives can put in place a program - say in a "trading window" - and then set and forget. They may tear their hair out when they get positive news and have programmed a series of sales, but at least they can do it. Australian executives spend their whole lives confined to short windows and cannot average their position through progressive trading.

It also adds to volatility. For example, large shareholders coming out of escrow post-IPO could program progressive sales rather than sell immediately in one line.

We do not think that the Australian Securities and Investments Commission is bloody-minded enough to take anyone to task for programmed trading under a set-and-forget plan. But the simple fact is that clients don't do it because we cannot rule out prosecution for them. There is also the reputational risk that goes with any spectre of wrongdoing of this kind.

The killer here is uncertainty - and the frustration it generates. Focusing the assessment on a single point in time won't diminish market integrity, makes eminent sense and would better align the local regime with its global peers.

Reform to permit programmed trading is overdue. Governments and regulators could do worse than reopen the Corporations and Securities Advisory Committee's 2001 report and consider the other recommendations that deserve more than two decades of neglect.

The authors are partners of King & Wood Mallesons.
direction: supportive for ASX-listed start-ups and tech sector

headline: Home gym trend pumps Rebel owner Super Retail
text:
People who are barred from travelling overseas and steering clear of eating out at restaurants in the COVID-19 pandemic have shifted some of their household spending to sprucing up their cars and making sure they stay fit.

Super Retail Group, which owns retail chains Supercheap Auto, Rebel sporting goods and clothing, camping and outdoor activities chain BCF and the Macpac outdoorwear brand, has continued to generate booming sales, which are up 25 per cent for the first 17 weeks of the 2020-21 financial year.

Shares in Super Retail jumped by 4.8 per cent to $11.69 by 4pm AEST. The stock was trading at below $4 in March in the first phase of a big sharemarket sell-off, when the pandemic fears were at their worst.

Profit margins are also fatter by about 200 basis points as the company backs off on promotional activity, mainly in the Rebel chain. This means that the jump in revenue is flowing through to the bottom line because customers are flocking without the extra enticement of discounted price tags.

Online sales across the businesses have soared by 132 per cent as the structural shift to e-commerce gathers pace amid restrictions on bricks and mortar retail, which were in place in Melbourne and Auckland for much of the period. Melbourne is slowly emerging from an extended lockdown, but many experts predict there will be wariness for some time on the prospect of a physical visit to the shops.

But Super Retail chief executive Anthony Heraghty cautioned that, because of continued economic uncertainty, the stellar start should not necessarily be extrapolated to the full financial year.

The pandemic has triggered major shifts in consumer behaviour. Many shoppers have opted to make a Click and Collect purchase, where they do the transaction online and then head to the store or a depot to pick the goods up. Click and Collect comprises 44 per cent of Super Retail's online sales.

Supercheap Auto total sales growth for the financial year to date is 22 per cent, and 21 per cent on a like-for-like basis, which strips out any store openings or closures.

Rebel total sales growth was up 16 per cent, and the same on a like-for-like measure. A rise in the number of people working from home has resulted in a spike in leisurewear sales at the chain. Due to gym closures during the pandemic, people have set up their own circuit training and exercise area in their home or in the garage.

The BCF chain had a 63 per cent jump in total sales in the first 17 weeks as shoppers stocked up for intra-state camping trips and outdoor activities once the first phase of lockdowns in states outside of Victoria ended.

Stockbroking house UBS last month estimated that Australians spent a combined $63 billion annually on international travel and domestic travel before the pandemic hit.

Macpac was the black sheep of the main brands. Its sales growth slipped by 2 per cent for the first 17 weeks.

The strong growth at Supercheap Auto is also mirrored at rival auto parts chains such as Autobarn, owned by ASX-listed Bapcor.

On October 12, Bapcor chief executive Darryl Abotomey said booming sales in the September quarter stemmed from people wanting to "dress up" their vehicles because they could not fly anywhere.

Many consumers had cash in their pockets because they were unable to travel overseas or interstate, while a spike in used car sales had also prompted those new owners to want to upgrade those vehicles with better technology and comfort.
direction: Bullish for Super Retail Group (ASX: SRG)

headline: China steel fury behind barley threat
text:
Sydney/Shanghai | Australian barley growers are set to become collateral damage in a broader dispute with China over the government's aggressive use of anti-dumping measures against its steel and aluminium producers.

Confidential documents seen by The Australian Financial Review indicate that China will claim a lack of co-operation from barley growers to justify imposing a 73.6 per cent duty on Australian imports.

The documents also allege the $10 billion rescue plan for the Murray-Darling River Basin system, dating back to 2007, had provided irrigation subsidies to barley growers.

Beijing is set to impose the duties despite its own beer producers warning the levies would hurt the industry, push up prices and "increase trade uncertainty".

Trade experts said the action was less about local market conditions and more about Beijing punishing Australia for levying duties of between 15 per cent and 102 per cent on Chinese makers of steel and aluminium.

"China is really angry about Australia's long-term position and the way it calculates duties against Chinese companies," said Dr Zhou Weihuan, a senior lecturer in trade law at the University of NSW.

China's Foreign Ministry yesterday denied the anti-dumping probe was politically motivated, saying it was a "normal trade dispute investigation".

But Scott Morrison indicated earlier yesterday that China's threat to impose import duties on barley could be linked to previous Australian trade actions.

"We have had anti-dumping inquiries in relation to Chinese products to Australia. Not all those decisions were well received," the Prime Minister said.

The Productivity Commission has regularly chastised the federal government for imposing anti-dumping duties on Chinese steel and aluminium, saying again last month there was "no convincing justifications for these measures".

"Australia is one of the most prolific users of anti-dumping measures in the world and continues to impose an array of anti-dumping measures," the report said.

In its "essential facts" report seen by the Financial Review, China claims large Australian players CBH Grain, GrainCorp, Glencore, Cargill and ADM had not provided an "accurate and full report" on the production costs of barley.

It said four smaller producers did not provide any information.

It, therefore, said all barley imports from Australia should be slugged with a duty of 73.6 per cent. This is substantially more than the 56 per cent duty originally sought by Chinese industry groups.

Since the dispute began in November 2018, China has increasingly looked to import barley from Canada.

Australia has been given 11 days to respond before China issues its final report on May 19.
direction: bearish for Australian barley growers and agri-sector shares

headline: AMP investors still bullish on break-up despite $5b offer
text:
AMP shares skyrocketed 20 per cent on Friday following confirmation of US investor Ares Management's offer to acquire the troubled wealth manager outright, but institutional shareholders maintain the 171-year-old company won't survive in its current form.

The Australian Financial Review's Street Talk column on Thursday revealed the California-based fund manager was in advanced talks to buy AMP and had commenced due diligence on a potential $5 billion transaction.

AMP's confirmation of the report sent its languishing share price soaring 20.7 per cent to $1.54 by 2.30pm in Friday's trade.

Both AMP and Ares declined to comment but a statement to the Australian Securities Exchange said an "indicative, non-binding, conditional proposal ... to acquire 100 per cent of the shares in AMP" had been received. It added that takeover talks are in a "very preliminary stage".

AMP claimed it had also received "significant interest" from other parties and that its hopes of executing chief executive Francesco De Ferrari's multi-year transformation plan were still alive.

The offer came in response to a "portfolio review" initiated by AMP chairman Debra Hazelton in September after the shock departure of chairman David Murray over the company's handling of sexual harassment allegations against senior executives, revealed by the Financial Review.

The review is being run by investment banks Credit Suisse and Goldman Sachs and may recommend a change in AMP's mix of assets, with a potential break-up of the company a possibility.

It is understood that Ares' offer is for AMP in its entirety, including the problematic financial advice division tarred by the Hayne royal commission and its under-performing superannuation funds.

Market sources said the US bidder was concerned about "value loss and leakage" were the sprawling wealth management group to be broken up.

But existing institutional shareholders in AMP were sceptical that the prospective buyer would or should keep the company intact.

"I would be astonished if a 'whole of company' transaction was Ares' intention," said Simon Mawhinney, chief investment officer of AMP's second-largest shareholder, Allan Gray.

"Do they really want to own a bank?"

Mr Mawhinney, whose fund owns about 7 per cent of the wealth management giant, said AMP faced only two likely scenarios, whether the Ares bid was successful or not. It would either be sold off "piecemeal", with different assets going to different buyers, or bought whole and then dismembered subsequently.

The third option of "AMP as we know it continuing" would only become feasible if no compelling bids were made, meaning it is now unlikely to occur, the investment manager speculated.

Fellow AMP shareholder Hamish Carlisle of Merlon Capital Partners stood by his previous contention that a break-up of AMP is in investors' best interests, with a specific focus on safeguarding the company's most lucrative assets

Though Ares, a $252 billion investment house, would almost double its global assets under management if it subsumed the whole of AMP into its operations, Mr Carlisle suggested it was likely only really interested in the $58 billion managed by AMP's Capital's unlisted infrastructure and property funds, which offer the widest margins within the AMP empire.

The AMP Capital division was until recently led by Boe Pahari, the executive at the centre of the Financial Review's investigation of sexual harassment claims, which sparked a staff and shareholder backlash. He has since been demoted to his previous role as head of infrastructure equity.

In August, Mr De Ferrari said the business was central to the company's future and turnaround efforts, outlining a bold if uncertain ambition to "build the best global private markets platform in the world".

Mr Carlisle believes the best outcome would be for the attractive private markets operations of AMP Capital to be spun off and listed on the stock exchange as a separate business, with a separate board and corporate governance.

"It would then be in a better position to attract staff and clients and be better valued by the market," he said.

But Merlon is not opposed to the idea of an interested party purchasing AMP as a whole - as long as it is at the right price.

The reported offer price from Ares, officially unconfirmed by either party but reported to be in the realm of $5 billion, is not the right price in his opinion.

AMP's valuation is complicated by the surplus capital the company has on hand, which Merlon estimates is worth about $1.5 billion if you include the so-called board buffer. Other sources have put the figure variously between $500 million and $2 billion.

"Ares can apply AMP's surplus capital against whatever they pay," Mr Carlisle said. "So if they spend $5 billion they are really spending $3.5 billion — and $3.5 billion for the actual operating assets of AMP seems low".

Equity analyst Harry Dudley of Watermark Funds Management agreed the reported offer price of $5 billion was too low.

"[Even] if we go all the way to $6 billion, we would see a price of around $1.75 per share," Mr Dudley explained.

"This would mean just about anyone who bought shares prior to 2020 would be selling at a loss. Unless you bought in at the capital raise in 2019, you'd be getting a premium of 15¢ share — not a great return for the wild ride you've had to endure."

He described Ares as a logical acquirer of the company, suggesting it could keep AMP Capital - which complements its own business model as a manager of property, credit and alternative investments - while restructuring the wealth business within its private equity arm.

He touted Macquarie as a potential acquirer of a restructured AMP wealth division or its growing bank.

But he warned that Ares' ability to turn around AMP's wealth business, given the liabilities stemming from the royal commission and multiple class actions afoot, was far from certain.

"The banks have effectively given up on rehabilitating their wealth arms," Mr Dudley pointed out.

Financial services M&A expert Steve Prendeville, of Forte Asset Solutions, agreed Macquarie was still in the mix to purchase AMP Bank or even its North wealth management platform.
direction: AMP shares rise

headline: ASX falls 2.5pc in worst day in three weeks
text:
The Australian sharemarket suffered its worst day in three weeks yesterday despite New Zealand joining the growing list of countries to announce plans to ease containment measures after a slowdown in the growth of new COVID-19 cases.

The S&P/ASX 200 Index closed 134.5 points, or 2.5 per cent, lower at 5353, leaving the benchmark 25.3 per cent below its February record high but 17.8 per cent above the low set on March 23.

The massive fall in activity around the world meant oil prices opened the week sharply lower while investors warned shares will remain under pressure until there is more certainty on when lockdowns will end.

"The key thing really will be when we get some element of clarity on when we come out of lockouts," said Hugh Dive, Atlas Funds Management's chief investment officer.

"We are seeing a bit of a flattening out of the curve but until we know [when containment measures will be lifted], it's very difficult difficult for a lot of companies to give guidance and also to make plans."

While New Zealand Prime Minister Jacinda Ardern announced plans to roll back containment measures from next week, the unknown that remains for any nation winding back restrictions is whether new cases will begin to rise again at a rate that could slow or reverse progress toward normalisation.

The important question for investors is how does COVID-19 change the long-term earnings trajectory for a company, said Ophir Asset Management's head of research, Luke McMillan.

"First, can it make it through, and second will there be permanently altered buying behaviour of customers and how meaningful will this be," Mr McMillan said. "This second question is what investors also need to be considering."

Mr McMillan's comments came as reports emerged that Virgin Australia's management was expected to call in administrators.

As revealed by The Australian Financial Review's Street Talk, the airline's board was scheduled to meet last night to discuss Virgin's future and the leadership team is expected to conclude the appointment of voluntary administrators is the only option. The company's shares remained suspended from trade yesterday.

Australian-listed energy companies were the main drag across the exchange as investors responded to oil price falls. Suppressed demand for crude oil because of global containment measures has led to the diminishing of oil storage capacity, with the problem more concentrated in the US.

US crude dropped to prices not seen for more than two decades in early trade, with West Texas Intermediate (WTI) futures dropping below $US16 a barrel as concerns about the supply glut mounted, eroding gains made after the deal to cut production among producers oil.

European oil prices have also fallen but less than in the US. Last week, Brent crude prices fell about 11 per cent, while WTI lost close to twice that amount.

Among Australia's oil and gas producers, Origin Energy slid 5.4 per cent to $4.89, Woodside Petroleum lost 4.4 per cent to finish the day at $20.15, Santos fell 3.7 per cent to close at $4.13, and Beach Energy dropped 3.6 per cent to $1.34.

Caltex also fell sharply after Montreal-based Alimentation Couche-Tard said it was walking away from its proposed $8.8 billion acquisition of the petroleum products company. Caltex shares dropped 7.8 per cent to close at $21.72.

Metcash shares entered a trading halt after the company said it would seek to raise $330 million from institutional investors at $2.80 a share. Its shares last closed at $3.04.

Shopping Centres Australasia Property Group announced plans to raise as much as $50 million, offering existing security owners the ability to add to their holdings for $2.16 a unit. Its units closed 0.9 per cent higher at $2.26.

Also in real estate, National Storage REIT told investors its revenue has proven resilient, with the self-storage business and property owner telling investors it expected earnings per security of between 8.5¢ and 9¢ this financial year. It ended the day 3 per cent lower at $3.03.
direction: bearish for the ASX 200 and rate-sensitive sectors

headline: HomeCo seeks $300m for mall REIT
text:
Home Consortium has hit the road to raise $300 million of fresh equity for its Daily Needs REIT spin-off that will hold mainly metropolitan malls anchored by supermarkets and large-format retailers.

The new fund, to be listed on the ASX and expected to start trading on November 23, will be seeded with 17 malls worth $844 million.

All have high exposure to the non-discretionary retail sector that has proved resilient to the growth of online shopping and the pandemic.

With an offer price of $1.33 per unit and 484 million units on issue, the real estate investment trust will have market capitalisation at initial public offering of $644 million.

The forecast distribution yield this financial year is 5.5 per cent.

A spokesman for HomeCo said marketing had kicked off following "very positive soundings with both institutional and retail investors last week".

Its imminent float comes as institutional investors reweigh their retail property exposures to non-discretionary assets such as neighbourhood centres and sub-regional centres, which are anchored by supermarkets and have little exposure to discretionary spending.

Existing HomeCo security holders, including the Financial Review Rich List families behind Chemist Warehouse and Spotlight Group, will emerge with about a 27 per cent stake through an in-specie distribution. About 46 per cent will be held by new investors and 27 per cent by HomeCo, which will also manage the REIT.

"The proposed HomeCo Daily Needs REIT is consistent with HomeCo's stated own, develop and manage strategy and establishes the platform for HomeCo to unlock additional value and growth through further capital recycling and ongoing management fee streams from assets under management," said HomeCo chief executive David Di Pilla last month.

HomeCo also plans to establish and manage an unlisted wholesale HealthCo fund to hold about $150 million of aged-care facilities and other key health tenants, including medicare and childcare centres.

Post-IPO, HomeCo will retain about $500 million of assets on its balance sheet as part of a "capital-light model".

In anticipation of the Daily Needs REIT float, HomeCo has beefed up the seed portfolio. In July, it acquired three supermarket-anchored shopping centres from Woolworths for $127.8 million. Last month it acquired $220 million of assets in western Sydney, including the Glenmore Park Town Centre near Penrith and the Gregory Hills Town Centre. It will buy another mall before the IPO.

The Daily Needs REIT portfolio will trade on an initial yield of 5.9 per cent, with 98 per cent occupancy, a weighted average lease expiry of 8.4 years and gearing at 27 per cent. Over 80 per cent of the assets will be on the east coast, with more than half of the portfolio in Sydney and Melbourne.

HomeCo also announced the third independent director of the Daily Needs REIT would be Stephanie Lai, a director of ASX-listed telco Superloop.
direction: bullish for HomeCo Daily Needs REIT and non-discretionary retail property sector

text:
This content is produced by The Australian Financial Review in commercial partnership with Accenture.

In a year defined by bushfires, floods, a pandemic, economic crisis and strained international relations, it is little wonder Australia's defence and security is top of mind for governments, business leaders and citizens.

The recent budget announcement pledged new investment in cyber security to keep Australians safe and secure online and further investments for law enforcement and intelligence agencies to keep us safe from foreign and domestic threats - all on top of the existing investments of billions for new capabilities already announced.

Prime Minister Scott Morrison has warned Australia needs to ready itself for a post-pandemic world that is "more dangerous and disorderly", where the threats are often invisible.

In the race to rapidly build and equip our defence force for this new reality, the need for innovation and collaboration across the whole defence ecosystem has never been stronger.

Defence organisations, including Australian Defence Industry enterprises, need to embrace digital transformation, which is accelerating faster than ever. The transformation must go a step further.

Defence forces don't just need more technology; they need technology that reflects experiential knowledge.

In the private sector, we are seeing organisations build new products, services and experiences that connect deep human and business insights with the possibilities of technology to define and deliver new realities - experiences that can make lives easier, healthier, safer, productive and rewarding.

Similarly, the defence sector is facing a new class of organisational issues that are complex and involve people and technology.

Designing from an understanding of human experience will be critical in creating effective solutions that deliver on the promise of new technologies.

Adapting to human+: For most of us, technology is now a fundamental part of our working lives, particularly in a world where remote working is becoming more important.

We have become human+ workers: our own skills and capabilities augmented by technology.

This growing collaboration between humans and technology creates new opportunities, but also presents new challenges.

Like many of today's enterprises, defence organisations are still optimised for the workforce of the past, leaving a disconnect that stretches across the organisation.

To thrive, defence leaders must rethink the way they hire and train, adopting approaches that are better suited to the adaptability of the human+ workforce.

That includes seeking out the untapped talent within their existing workforces, reskilling and better matching current employees with new roles and opportunities.

It requires a human+ mindset from the start - where every new technology, every new role and every new process is designed with the human experience in mind.

From the outside in: Outside of defence, businesses can transform how they innovate by focusing on maturing digital technologies, scientific advancements and emerging DARQ technologies (distributed ledger technology, artificial intelligence, extended reality and quantum computing).

These technologies could inject skills and innovative thought into defence organisations where, as in any industry, the ability to facilitate continuous innovation is vital.

The question of how to adapt the best of private sector technology for defence, remains.

It is a vital question, if defence organisations are to keep up with other nations on the battlefield and at home.

The ability to innovate at speed is justifiably offset by heightened legal, moral and security concerns.

We need ways to bring "thinking from the outside" and adopt and adapt for defence needs.

The responsibility of investing in the future of Australia's defence and national security is immense.

Designing from an understanding of human experience is critical in creating effective capabilities that will meet the challenges of an uncertain future and keep this country safe.

All the better that we invest in growing sovereign Australian capabilities as part of this. AFR

The defence sector is facing a new class of organisational issues that are complex and involve people and technology.
direction: bullish for defence and security sector

headline: Recovery could still go wrong
text:
The Morrison government can't quite contain its delight at confirmation the economy is rebounding far more quickly than anticipated just a few months ago.

The 3.3 per cent jump in GDP growth over the September quarter - the biggest quarterly increase in GDP since 1976 - is another boost for all the optimists touting the resilience of the Australian economy.

That result was despite the second-largest state - responsible for about one quarter of the national economy - being closed for business and further contracting over that period. Victoria's reopening since will at least boost the December quarter figures.

Roll on the opening of all domestic borders this month and a likely vaccine available by next year. Business and consumer confidence are coming back, along with economic recovery. Recession over, technically speaking.

What could possibly go wrong? Plenty, of course.

Reserve Bank governor Philip Lowe is not the only one pointing out the economic road ahead is still bumpy, with very "uneven" results across different types of retail, construction and housing sectors, for example.

Just as the September GDP figures were being released, Lowe reminded a parliamentary committee that he sees the closed shops every time he steps outside the RBA office in Sydney's CBD.

But he says in an era of record low interest rates, such "unevenness" in the economy is a matter for government rather than the RBA.

Treasurer Josh Frydenberg would proudly agree with the dominant role of fiscal policy in steering the Australian economy through the uncharted waters of 2020. He appreciates the demand for continued government support for an economy still smaller and unemployment higher than this time last year. Jobs, jobs, jobs.

But as reflected in his October budget, Frydenberg wants to use government stimulus to accelerate private investment and spending rather than continuing to supercharge public debt levels indefinitely.

"Government is not the solution," he says. "Government can be a catalyst for the solution and the solution lies in the private sector taking those decisions to hire new people, to innovate, to grow, to expand.

"We'll look for targeted support where it's appropriate, but as for our macro supports, like JobKeeper, they are tapering down."

The adequacy of that approach will be tested well beyond 2020. But it also relies on other urgent financial reforms the Treasurer considers crucial for private sector investment and growth, particularly among small and medium-sized businesses.

As well as changes to insolvency laws, his preferred measures include getting rid of the responsible lending laws introduced after the global financial crisis. According to the Treasurer, this signals "the most significant reforms to Australia's credit framework in a decade" in order to increase the flow of credit. He wants this ASAP to boost the economy when it's most needed - right now.

The fundamental change is shifting the onus from "lender beware" back to "borrower beware" in terms of accountability for ability to repay loans. These stricter rules had been enforced by the Australian Securities and Investments Commission - hardly the Treasurer's favourite regulator.

Frydenberg wants mainstream lending to revert to supervision under less prescriptive lending standards applied by the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority.

But while change was backed by the banks and the RBA, it is meeting opposition from consumer groups and academics claiming it is dangerous as well as rushed. The government may not have sufficient crossbench support in the Senate next week, meaning the bill would be referred to a committee which would - at best - delay it for several months.

The bigger question is will the change really accelerate the flow of credit - and, if so, at what cost?

The government has already put ASIC back in its (much smaller) box after the regulator's flawed decision to take Westpac to court over how the bank assessed the creditworthiness of mortgage borrowers.

It not only lost twice in Federal Court, it infuriated the government and the RBA by pursuing a case that would have made it legally riskier for banks to lend and required more onerous assessments about households' capacity to borrow. A mid-year decision to not appeal to the High Court came too late to avoid more damage to ASIC's standing in Canberra.

Yet the government wants to go further in overturning responsible lending laws altogether. That's even though bank chief executives suggest anaemic demand for business credit won't necessarily improve as demand reflects what is happening in the economy more broadly.

So the change is more about speeding up delays and difficulties in the banks' processing of applications to remove obstacles. Delays may be partly due to lack of sufficient investment in technology. But under current laws, for example, even long-standing customers with good records have to go through laborious, intensive checks about any new debt facilities.

ANZ's Shayne Elliott says overturning the laws would not mean more borrowers getting through the approval process because the bank's appetite for risk hasn't changed.

"It's about operational efficiency rather than unleashing any sort of loan growth," he said as he delivered the bank's results a month ago.

"At the margin there might be some customers who get approved but what we think will happen if the process gets faster and less invasive."

This is supposed to make it easier and quicker for small businesses to borrow. Although responsible lending laws don't formally cover business lending, most small business owners borrow on personal accounts using their homes as security.

Frydenberg entertains no doubts. RIP, the ghosts of the Hayne royal commission.
direction: cautious outlook for rate-sensitive sectors due to uneven recovery and policy uncertainty

headline: Payroll data shows job losses slowing
text:
The rate of jobs being lost in the COVID-19-hit economy is slowing, while earlier estimates of job losses have also been revised down, indicating the Morrison government's JobKeeper scheme is working.

The latest payroll data from the Australian Bureau of Statistics and Australian Tax Office show that over the seven weeks from mid-March to early May, total jobs fell by 7.3 per cent.

Based on the 13 million employed in March, that 7.3 per cent fall equates to 950,000 jobs lost in the seven weeks.

ABS head of labour statistics Bjorn Jarvis said the week-to-week changes are now much smaller than they were early in the pandemic. "The latest data shows a further slowing in the fall in COVID-19 job losses between mid-April and early May."

The decrease in the number of jobs in the week ending May 2 was 1.1 per cent. This is down from the 1.5 per cent fall in the week to April 18, and well down from the 5.5 per cent in the week to April 4.

Total wages paid have also increased because of JobKeeper. As employers paid their workers on the promise of being reimbursed by the government, the total wages bill paid increased by 0.9 per cent in the week to May 2, compared with an increase of 0.5 per cent in the previous week.

The ABS also lowered earlier estimates of job losses, saying total job losses as of the week ending April 18 have been revised down from 7.5 per cent to 7.1 per cent.

That would lower the earlier estimate of 975,000 jobs being lost in five weeks down to 923,000 and would partially account for the surprisingly lower than forecast unemployment rate for April, when 600,000 jobs were lost.

The new payroll figures showing a slower rate of job losses and a rise in wages had economists declare the JobKeeper program a success.
