"The success that policy has had in stemming a greater decline in jobs and wages is to be celebrated and helps explain the relative strength in household spending and consumer sentiment," ANZ economist Catherine Birch said.

She warned that talk of reining in the JobKeeper program - which now covers 6.3 million workers - could be seriously detrimental.

"There is a risk that the withdrawal of JobKeeper could see under-utilisation continue to rise over a longer period and be counterproductive to improving economic and social outcomes," she said.

Citi senior economist Josh Williamson also said the JobKeeper program was clearly at the centre of a turnaround.
direction: supportive for Australian equities and rate-sensitive sectors

headline: Adamantem, Bruce Dixon plan to clean up, again
text:
There's a touch of the band coming back together as Adamantem Capital pursues Spotless' dirty laundry.

Street Talk understands Adamantem founders Anthony Kerwick and Rob Koczkar have reeled in well-known executive Bruce Dixon to help put together a turnaround plan for Spotless' now up-for-sale laundries, which will form a key part of the private equity firm's expected bid.

It's a coup for Adamantem and would make them one of the lead contenders for the laundries auction.

Dixon is known as a shrewd operator and ran Spotless for a few years last decade - funnily enough, when it was under the stewardship of Koczkar and Kerwick's then employer, Pacific Equity Partners.

It was a hugely profitable few years for the various shareholders involved, with Spotless relisted on the ASX and later scooped up by Downer Group.

Dixon has since hopped into the pubs game and is in bed with another private equity firm, KKR, at Australian Venue Co, which includes what was Dixon Hospitality Group.

You would have to think Dixon and Adamantem's founders would've established a strong rapport as part of their last Spotless play, and would have some ideas about how to dress up the ailing laundries unit.

Not that it will be a cakewalk for the Adamantem boys. Others eyeing some more work in the laundries industry include Euronext-listed giant Elis, Anchorage Capital Partners and US-based global laundry business Alsco.

Its a sales process organised after Spotless - controlled by Downer Group - mandated Sydney-based boutique VCA Partners last year to oversee an auction for the laundries business.
direction: bullish for Spotless shares

headline: Cameron backs ASX hopeful Nature One Dairy
text:
Jan Cameron, the Tasmanian entrepreneur caught in the corporate regulator's crosshairs, has wasted no time backing a fresh infant formula player, putting her money on ASX hopeful Nature One Dairy.

However, the timing of the IPO slated for the third quarter is in question after Nature One fired its stockbroker, EL&C Baillieu.

Ms Cameron was an early backer of Bellamy's Australia, snapped up late last year by China's Mengnui Dairy in a $1.5 billion deal, which made her about $300 million.

She was the largest shareholder via her Black Prince Private Foundation entity, which has come under scrutiny from the corporate regulator.

On Friday, the Australian Securities and Investments Commission (ASIC) pressed criminal charges against Ms Cameron, who could face a five-year jail term and more than $40,000 in fines, after it alleged she failed to disclose her links to Black Prince.

The Kathmandu chain founder has invested about $10 million taking a 19.9 per cent stake in Nature One, which not only has its own brand of infant formula but also manufactures for Nuchev (under the Oli6 brand), Bright Dairy and Coles home brand at its processing facility in Carrum Downs, Victoria.

Ms Cameron told The Australian Financial Review she would have a board seat, although it was likely it would not be taken by herself.

She said she was "not actively looking" for a new infant formula play but was attracted to Nature One since its formula is made from 100 per cent Australian ingredients from dairy farms in Gippsland, Victoria.

"I'm not aware of any other product in the market that has that," she said. "That was a strong factor in me buying into the company.

"The plant is state of the art. I was also very impressed by [CEO] Nick Dimopoulos and the degree of detail he went into, in the facility and the product. Australian baby milk formula is not going to lose its popularity."

Singapore-based businessman Mr Dimopoulos founded the company in 2015 with his wife, Masie Ng-Dimopoulos, who is COO.

The pair will end up controlling about 50 per cent of the company after the float, which could command a $100 million-plus market capitalisation after raising a further $30 million via the planned IPO.

But the timing of the offer is likely to be pushed out given Nature One said it now had "no current relationship" with boutique stockbroking firm EL&C Baillieu, which acted as co-lead manager on the pre-IPO raise, along with advisory firm Sanston Securities Australia.

The outgoing head of corporate finance at the Melbourne stockbroker, Stephen Macaw, has been entangled in an elaborate sting that has caused huge embarrassment and the loss of his job.

"Nature One Dairy will go to market in the near future to consider and identify a lead manager for the IPO," a company spokesman said.

Nature One garnered a further $5 million of pre-IPO funds from investors such as Acorn Capital and SG Hiscock.

Mr Dimopoulos said the board was not yet formed and he could remain as both CEO and chairman given his expertise and relationships in the sector.

"I'm fluent in Mandarin," he said. "I'm not Kevin Rudd, but I read and write and speak. I recently sealed a deal with Bright Dairy. We are working with a number of Chinese dairies."

Ms Cameron said she was reasonably confident that Nature One would get the needed final tick of approval from China's State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR), which would allow it to directly export infant formula to China.
direction: bearish for Nature One due to regulatory scrutiny and IPO timing uncertainty

headline: Trump's 'buy the dip' tweet is tone deaf to investors' real-life scepticism
text:
Donald Trump's tweeting of the presidential version of "buy the dip" yet again confirms the only poll he cares about is the strength of Wall Street indexes.

The President's observation that the US sharemarket was "starting to look very good to me" looks tone deaf against investor wariness about whether it's a good idea to be holding richly valued stocks at a time when China's sagging growth outlook and supply chain disruptions point to a dip in first and possibly second-quarter earnings estimates.

But the bond market is having none of the Tweeter-in-Chief's bluster.

The slide in US bond yields, the flattening of the yield curve and the pricing in of two interest rate cuts - plus a little bit more - by the end of the year is a repudiation by the largest financial market in the world of the President's sanguine view of the coronavirus outbreak.

As coronavirus impacts have spread from Wuhan to the C-suites of some of the world's largest companies, equities investors have had forced upon them a reality check that has been widely held thinking in bond and currency markets for weeks.

The cascading impact of the coronavirus outbreak prompted Goldman Sachs to cut its forecast for first-quarter US growth to 1.2 per cent from 1.4 per cent.

Having hand-on-heart promised to pay more attention to macro factors after the global financial crisis, many managers of stock portfolios have been caught out chasing momentum.

Momentum in a narrow group of tech stocks last week propelled the S&P 500 Index's prospective price-earnings multiple to 19 times, its highest since 2002. Nine of the 11 sectors traded on forward multiples higher than their 20-year averages.

Multiple expansion has been fuelled by prices running well ahead of earnings growth.

The S&P 500 Index's forward PE was 16.2 times at this time last year, but has expanded thanks to a 21 per cent rise in the market against a modest 4.1 per cent increase in forward earnings per share estimates, according to FactSet.

However, the fourth-quarter results from tech behemoth Apple have proved a watershed moment for investors. Having warned about supply chain disruption, it is only now the challenges facing multinational companies in a world of just-in-time inventory management have hit home.

While stocks have been forced into a sharp repricing of a less bullish short-term outlook, bond markets have taken a more circumspect view for weeks.

The slide in the US 30-year bond yield to a record low 1.84 per cent was the headline news from Monday's trading, but across the curve investors and traders pushed yields close to record or multi-year lows.

The pricing of US rate cut expectations shows there will be growing calls for the Federal Reserve to act should the outbreak worsen - or land on US shores.

It's foolish to think rate cuts can fix broken supply chains or bolster consumer demand in affected areas, but the market's Pavlovian response to any crisis is more liquidity.

The Fed has dismissed the idea it suffers from a "hall of mirrors" problem. The idea is the central bank is forced into cutting interest rates even if it doesn't want to because it doesn't want to spark a sell-off triggered by disappointing market expectations.

But with two cuts priced in this year, coupled with a tweeting president who has previously ridiculed Fed chair Jerome Powell for not easing policy more aggressively, the Fed will be under pressure to cut further the longer the outbreak is uncontained and global growth threatened.

The strength of the US dollar could provide an out for the Fed. The strong currency raises the risk of importing disinflation at a time when commodity prices have headed sharply lower.

Getting inflation back to 2 per cent gets harder as global growth slows and oil prices falter.

While more rate cuts may go some way to soothing sentiment, the reality is that coordinated action by global health authorities in helping the sick on Main Street is going to be far more beneficial to the global growth trajectory and jittery markets than another hit of cheap and easy money for Wall Street.
direction: bearish for equities, particularly richly valued tech stocks

headline: Genworth cancels earnings guidance
text:
Genworth, Australia's biggest lenders' mortgage insurer, has cancelled its guidance as it faces the likelihood that large numbers of low income mortgage customers will default on their repayments.

In a statement to the ASX, Genworth said uncertainty over the long-term impact of the COVID-19 crisis, including the evolving government and regulatory interventions, made it impossible to provide an accurate earnings forecast.

"Given the challenges in forecasting these impacts, Genworth considers it is no longer appropriate to maintain its guidance for FY2020 and so is withdrawing its FY2020 guidance from the market," the company said.

Genworth insures banks and other lenders against the risk that customers with loan to value ratio loans of more than 80 per cent will default on their repayments. The majority of its coverage is for first-time buyers.

By far its biggest customer is the Commonwealth Bank of Australia, which accounts for more than 50 per cent of its business.

CBA announced last week that it would defer loan repayments by up to six months, a measure likely to delay potential delinquencies.

Two weeks into the crisis, and many companies, including major airlines and retailers and thousands of small businesses, are already laying off or standing down workers. Medical experts warn a return to normality can only come once a vaccine for COVID-19 is developed and widely distributed, which could take 18 months or more.

That suggests large numbers of people could be without an adequate income for two years or more.

Genworth's recently appointed chief executive officer Pauline Blight-Johnston said: "Genworth is committed to safeguarding the interests of its people, customers, shareholders and other stakeholders and we are also mindful of our social responsibility to do our part to support the community.

"We are working closely with our customers and continue to maintain our standards of service to support them and their borrowers."

Prior to the crisis, Genworth had been on a sharp upward trajectory, thanks to a healthy recovery in the housing market. In February, it reported full-year statutory net profit after tax of $120.1 million, up from $75.7 million in 2018.
direction: bearish for Genworth and mortgage insurers

headline: The women shattering the C-suite ceiling in tech
text:
Leadership An emerging group is not afraid to do things differently.

When Xero chief customer officer Rachael Powell was growing up, the job she's doing today didn't even exist.

It was impossible for Powell, who started out wanting to be a journalist like her grandma who worked for German newspaper Der Spiegel until she died at 88, to envision the job she'd find herself doing a few decades later.

This, she says, will be the reality for many women and men poised to embark on their careers today and for employers it means needing to look beyond a job description.

"For me, building a team is about understanding the skills I need, but also the aptitudes that are needed, so I spent a lot of time focusing on bringing the right people together," Powell says. "The way I interview people is more of a conversation than an interrogation. I assess through tone and inference what someone is going to be highly engaged with.

"There's a lot of ambiguity when it comes to new roles, so you need people with good problem-solving skills, that are fantastic at stakeholder management and are exceptional communicators. These are the skills that are important for any company looking to grow fast."

As Xero's chief customer officer (and formerly also its chief people officer), Powell is responsible for ensuring Xero customers, no matter where they are, have a "consistent and outrageous experience".

Powell is in charge of shaping a customer's exposure to Xero from when they first hear of the brand, through to their interaction with the company via its website, trying the product for the first time and the sales experience.

She's one of an increasing number of women pushing into the senior leadership ranks at the country's biggest listed tech companies.

At Xero, 42 per cent of the leadership team are women, as are 42 per cent of employees overall.

The new female faces are a welcome addition to what for a long time has been a predominantly white, male cohort of senior leaders in the country's tech firms.

Earlier this year Cassandra Williams was appointed as chief enterprise risk officer at Afterpay, having previously worked at Commonwealth Bank, while at Xero Kirsty Godfrey-Billy went from chief accounting officer to chief financial officer when former CFO Sankar Narayan resigned to lead SiteMinder in 2018.

At logistics software company WiseTech, Gail Williamson has also climbed the corporate ranks, shifting from head of investor relations and corporate affairs to chief growth officer in 2017.

Williamson, like Powell, is in a role that did not exist 10 years ago. As more new roles are created, she is hopeful that more businesses will embrace the need for greater diversity in their organisations - and not just from a gender perspective.

"You have to have culture, gender, socio-economic and life experience diversity," she says.

"Embracing diversity is the same as embracing innovation, neither is optional if we want better outcomes. When organisations embrace diversity, encourage creative abrasion and, importantly, can match those with engineered processes and semi-autonomous execution, growth can be exponential..

"Building teams stacked with diversity of cultures, experiences, gender, education and thinking styles, that are also given the freedom to be creative along with technology architectures to drive data and transparency, can generate stronger engagement, delivery and organisational agility."

Unlike other chief growth officers at other firms, her job, she says, is not just fancy name for a marketing or sales role.

"Some chief growth officer roles are centred on sales, but here the role ... involves accelerating growth and geographic expansion by aligning resources, looking at the tech pipeline, distribution channels, communications and acting as a soundboard between areas," Williamson says.

"Looking across functions and geographies lets your team have a vision on things that can be globally scalable, semi-automated and have local configurations that let you operate in many markets."

Williamson, who grew up in Belfast, Northern Ireland, before coming to Australia for high school, says she has always had equal weighting between her left and right brain - enjoying art, but also mathematics. She, too, does not think linearly about who would make a good fit for a role.

Having dabbled in film production and design, as well as spending most of her career in investor relations (spanning a range of sectors including financial services and healthcare) before taking on the chief growth officer role at WiseTech, she knows the value of cross-industry experience.

"There's a fear of experimenting, but having cross-industry experience is an enormously beneficial asset and it sets you up well to work in an innovative space," she says.

"I used to hire people to work in funds management while at Henderson in London who had zoology degrees.

"I've had periods in my career where I've concentrated on immersing myself on an area like actuarial work and balanced it with a period of deep creative work and working in tech ... it's offered an extraordinary mix of being able to execute those different sides of your personalities."

While women like Williamson and newly appointed Telstra director Bridget Loudon have been busy shattering the glass ceiling within the tech sector, opening up the path for others to follow in their footsteps, there are still very few female CEOs of listed tech companies - big or small - in Australia, or globally.

Of the companies included in the ASX's All Tech Index, Alcidion chief executive Kate Quirke is the only female. When you look beyond tech to the ASX top 200, the figures are only slightly better, with 6 per cent of companies in the top 200 led by women.

At the board level there has been progress. Now there's only one company in the ASX200 with no female directors, down from 50 five years ago. But there are still more Michaels and Peters on boards than women - particularly at Westpac - and e-commerce company MyDeal is slated to come to market without any female board members.

Afterpay chief enterprise risk officer Cassandra Williams has been on boards before, but says she wouldn't do it again until she's no longer an executive.

"For me it's all about Afterpay [at the moment]. This is a great role and there's a lot for me to learn. In the long term, though, I can see myself on boards. It's a really great step, especially with the experience I've got," she says.

"It's that thing that will happen when I'm ready to not be operating and running a function."

Williams, who joined Afterpay in March as the world switched overnight to remote working, is a lawyer by training - a profession she chose in part due to her family, but also thanks to the '80s television show LA Law.

After university she got her first job as an article clerk at a three-partner firm in Hobart, before moving to Perth and then London to practise, and taking on roles with the London Stock Exchange and the UK Financial Services Authority.

After leading the UK FSA, she joined Investec, before being lured back to Australia and joining the Commonwealth Bank, where she was most recently chief compliance officer.

Williams says her time at CBA was an amazing experience and gave her a chance to "get into the weeds" of retail banking, but ultimately she was lured to Afterpay by the opportunity to be involved in a smaller, growing business, where she would be stretched and pushed to learn new things.

"It does feel different. Things move at a different speed, it's much faster," she says. "The decision making is speedier and people work really well together ... What I'm doing is also so intrinsically built into what the organisation is trying to do.

"I'm responsible for governance, operational risk and compliance. It's very much about engagement at a leadership team level, working with my direct reports or steering initiatives that we'll be working on, writing a policy or developing new frameworks.

"Every day is completely varied and that's exciting. There's nothing I'd change about it."

Williams took on the job at Afterpay during COVID-19 and was consequently thrust into a new role while remote working with a team she had never met.

The experience for her was not unusual, though, having worked from home 19 years earlier when her daughter was a baby.

"I already knew you could be super-productive," she says.

"At that time, in 2000, working from home was not a thing, so I was pretty much the only person I knew who did it.

"For the first few years, I didn't have a work phone or blackberry. I had a laptop and the ability to remote access, but there was no WiFi. Access to the internet was by dial-up and that could take a while - long enough to make a coffee and put on the washing. I would bring files home to work on, and I would be productive enough to get everything done, plus pick up my daughter early from nursery. So I actually continued to work from home until I moved to Australia in 2010 - moving to one day a week when I took on a management role."

Williams, Williamson and Powell were all optimistic that diversity in the technology sector would continue to improve and that visibility was key to encouraging the next generation of women to consider careers in tech.

Programs like Startmate's women-only fellowship are now creating pathways for women in traditional careers to transition into roles in high-growth, early stage tech companies. The most recent application round for the fellowship had more than 600 applicants for 100 positions, with the expectation that at least 50 per cent of these would come out the other side of the eight week program with jobs in tech start-ups.

Xero chief financial officer Kirsty Godfrey-Billy says Xero is the only company she would have left her partnership role at PwC for.

"I think [more diversity] will happen. There's a number of females coming through university and I have a number of women in my team, but as you go up seniority levels, there's far fewer," she says.

"It's not that we don't have the talent ... women are fantastic leaders and so it's beneficial for all ASX companies to have a fair share of women in the C-suite, not just in the roles that are usually the 'women' roles. But leading finance, product, and being CEOs. It's important to have that diversity because we do offer different leadership aspects and thinking."

Godfrey-Billy had believed PwC would be her final workplace, but in 2016 Xero co-founder Rod Drury offered her the chance to join the fast-growing cloud accounting software company.

"One of my key pieces of advice to people is to just take opportunities. I've taken risks," she says.

"I saw the opportunity and the value I could create. I haven't looked back since moving to Xero. Something on reflection that women are very good at is wearing masks to make ourselves fit the environment we're in. Every day I was putting on a different mask depending on the meeting or the situation.

"At Xero, they approached me, so I decided they got me. I purposefully went into the role with no masks at all and ensuring that I turned up really authentically in every situation and that's been quite transformative."

For Godfrey-Billy, being her true self in the workplace and having confidence in her abilities did not come naturally.

Previously she had been in job interviews where she felt like she should hide the fact that she is a mother, but at Xero she felt empowered to be herself and embrace her leadership style which is centred on emotional intelligence and collaboration.

"It comes back to self-confidence in what you bring to the table. I'm not a typical CFO. I'm not mid-50s, I'm not male, I have a child, but that's cool. I am who I am," she says.

"But what you need to be now is very different from the cardigan-wearing guy of 30 years ago. You need emotional intelligence, you need to relate to people, to communicate and care about your clients or business if you're in a bigger corporate. It's more about relationships and providing a service, not just delivering a finished product."
direction: supportive for Xero (XRO)

headline: LNG industry unfazed by US-China deal
text:
LNG industry chiefs in Australia say they can withstand the extra competition expected to flow from the $US52.4 billion ($76 billion) US-China trade deal on energy.

But they acknowledge the boost the industry in Australia received from the trade tensions between the two superpowers before the deal was signed looks set to fade.

Sherry Duhe, chief financial officer at Australia's biggest LNG exporter Woodside Petroleum, said the company had never factored in any benefit from the China-US trade dispute, although the uncertainty around US LNG tariffs had played in its favour.

The apparent cooling of tensions underlined the need to be internationally competitive, she said.

Woodside's proposed Scarborough and Browse projects in Western Australia, expected to cost a combined $46 billion, stacked up well and Australia had a geographic advantage to access Asia-Pacific customers, she said.

"In the case of Scarborough, Pluto and Browse we have the 'dream team' of contractors, the pricing is still competitive, we're brownfield, and those are the things that regardless of what happens with pricing and tariffs mean we will be competitive and can be the preferred supplier," Ms Duhe said.

"So even if there is a full agreement between the US and China, we are still neutral around that, but it remains to be seen how it will work out."

Spot LNG prices in north Asia plunged 21 per cent overnight in thin trading, which NAB head of commodity research Lachlan Shaw put down to several large supply tenders hitting the weak market, combined with the China deal. The "JKM" futures price dropped to $US4.18 per million British thermal units, from $US5.295 on Thursday.

Santos chief executive Kevin Gallagher said the company, whose LNG output is underpinned by long-term contracts, wouldn't be affected during the term of the $US200 billion two-year phase one deal, of which the energy component will include crude oil, refined products and coal as well as LNG.

When it came to new LNG projects, he said brownfield investments such as Santos' Barossa gas project off Australia's northern coast that will supply the existing Darwin LNG plant were "very competitive" and located close to Asia, attracting strong interest from customers across the region.

"Australia has a long track record of safe and reliable LNG supply to Asia, strong joint venture partnerships with Asian companies and secure, low-cost shipping routes to Asia that will stand Australia's LNG industry in good stead for the future," Mr Gallagher said.

The US will need to take a 20 per cent share of China's $US270 billion oil and gas imports to reach the $US52.4 billion target for 2020 and 2021, said Bernstein Research analyst Neil Beveridge.

He said increased crude oil and LPG imports would be the only meaningful way to reach it, given it was "harder to move the needle" with LNG, where new export projects in the US would take 4-5 years to build and where most of China's imports are under long-term contracts.

Australia, which last year overtook Qatar to become the world's largest LNG exporter, is China's largest LNG supplier, meeting 38 per cent of its imports in December, according to consultancy EnergyQuest.

About 70 per cent of China's LNG demand over the next two years is already contracted, leaving little scope for LNG to meet much of the extra Chinese-US energy trade in that time.

Neither the 5 per cent tariff on US crude oil nor the 25 per cent tariff on US LNG is to be reduced or removed by China under the phase one deal, noted Wood Mackenzie Asia-Pacific's Gavin Thompson. That would make it "challenging" for China to massively increase imports of oil and LNG from the US while tariffs remain in place, he said.

Key pointsAustralia accounted for 38 per cent of China's LNG imports in December.

Woodside and Santos touted Australia's proximity to the Asia-Pacific market.
direction: downward pressure on LNG exporters and energy sector shares

headline: Brits tough it out for a Brexit deal
text:
Continental drift The EU started with all the cards, but didn't bank on British willingness to walk away - or to see the long-term rewards of Brexit.

We have our stereotypical visions of the British: they're either posh lords and ladies living in grand country houses with all the foppish elegance of Downton Abbey. Or they're an exploited,

poverty-stricken proletariat of barely literate lager louts.

In reality, the average Brit is middle class, polite, invariably understated and has a welcome love of Australia.

But that's not all. Behind that British gentility are a people as tough and ruthless as any I've seen. They're as tough as the Vietnamese and Koreans - the toughest people I've come across in Asia. They have a quiet but intense pride in their country, its history and its values. They don't express patriotic sentiments in the laconic way we do in Australia or with the fervour of Americans. But as history shows, good luck to you if you want to attack the Poms. They'll take setback after setback but they won't give up.

That's a lesson the European Union has learnt over the past 4½ years of Brexit negotiations with the UK.

The EU began the negotiations in a strong position. After all, there are 450 million EU citizens and just 67 million Brits. The EU judged the UK needed it more than the EU needed Britain.

Brussels had a plan. Britain could leave the political decision-making structures of the European Union but remain locked into the social and economic system - in particular, the single market and the customs union. That would ensure it could never steal a competitive march on the EU.

So how did it go?

The Brits got off to a bad start. Once Theresa May had lost her overall majority in Parliament, the EU was able to play merry with the British in the negotiations. The UK looked weak and lost. In particular, the British establishment, which was appalled by the decision to leave the EU, fought back vigorously to try to overturn the referendum result. Deadlock with Brussels in the negotiations helped them, and no doubt the EU was happy to assist.

By the middle of 2019, the claims of the Brexiteers that they could negotiate a free trade agreement with the EU, recapture sovereignty over British waters and escape from the regulatory structures of Brussels were looking forlorn.

But here's the point. Never underestimate the determination of the British public. That is, the ordinary punters - not the grandees of politics, the public service, the judiciary, journalism and academia.

When Boris Johnson asked them to elect his government to end the deadlock and "to get Brexit done", they gave him an overwhelming majority. Within weeks of the election, he had concluded the terms of Britain's withdrawal from the EU.

But that was just phase one of the negotiations. Phase two was to try to get free trade with the EU for British goods, make sure the UK took control of its own waters, and ensure that it was no longer beholden to the decision-making institutions of the EU.

This was a tough call.

The British negotiating team had to adopt a position that was brave; very brave. They had to be prepared to walk away from the negotiations altogether on the ground that a bad deal was worse than no deal.

Just understand that if it had come to that, it would've been a significant setback for a British economy already reeling from the horrors of COVID-19.

So the British government had to convince the EU that it would rather suffer all of that pain than put up with the agreement the EU was demanding. In particular, the EU wanted Britain to accept indefinitely regulations on the environment, social policy and business.

Not just existing regulations, by the way. It demanded it could make new regulations and if Britain, which would have no say in the making of those regulations, did not accept them, then the EU could unilaterally impose tariffs on whatever British exports it chose.

The EU judged that Britain would rather accept this unpalatable agreement than end up in the economic wilderness.

For months the British government tried to persuade Brussels that this would never be acceptable. The British would choose the economic wilderness rather than become a colony of the EU. It was as simple as that.

The EU was convinced that if it held out for long enough, the British would finally cave in. The reverse turned out to be the case.

Finally, all 27 members of the EU decided it was better to have an agreement with Britain than not, and backed down on some of their core demands.

There is a moral to this story. Whether you agree with Brexit or think it is fundamentally mistaken, Boris Johnson's government has delivered almost all that it wanted. Johnson did so by using the old negotiating technique: be prepared to walk away altogether from a negotiation, otherwise don't enter into it in the first place.

And there's something else Britain has gained. The British government judges that there will be huge changes in technology over the next 20 years and that Britain could be a leader in artificial intelligence, fintech, biotechnology, electric vehicles and so on. As these industries develop, the Brits think the EU will want to regulate them. The British government won't do that. It aspires to foster and encourage entrepreneurship, and that will give the UK a great advantage over the EU.

So when all is said and done, the Brexiteers have got what they wanted. We'll see how it goes from here.

Alexander Downer was foreign minister from 1996 to 2007, and is a former high commissioner to the UK.
direction: bearish for UK equities and trade-linked sectors

headline: MARKET MASTERS IN THE BARGAIN BIN
text:
Equities There's an opportunity for investors who believe star fund managers can close the discounts on listed investment companies and trusts, writes Jonathan Shapiro.

They are the most venerated and feared names in funds management. Hamish Douglass, Chris Mackay, Phil King, Alex Waislitz and Rob Luciano have directors shaking, brokers bending over backwards and the heads of the world's largest companies on speed dial.

But in the world of listed funds, reputation doesn't count for much.

In fact, the ASX-listed funds they command are all plagued by the same problem as their less-illustrious peers - large discounts to asset value. That is, new investors will only buy into their listed investment companies and trusts (LICs and LITs) at a price that is well below the value of the investments they're overseeing.

Their reputations depend on closing that gap and they need to convince you, the individual investor, that they're worth backing in order to narrow those problematic discounts.

"The hunter has become the hunted," says Affluence Funds Management's Daryl Wilson in reference to one of his favoured positions in VG1 and VG8 - two listed investment companies managed by Rob Luciano's VGI Partners.

"At about the time VG1 floated, the manager, VGI Partners, was quite vocal about cleaning up the LIC sector and being an aggressor to mop up LICs trading at unsustainable discounts," he adds. But the discounts on the VGI funds have widened to levels Luciano says is "not acceptable". Wilson says "the time for more substantial action to close the discount must be close at hand".

"It is not palatable for VGI, which has built a reputation as a brutal short seller, to have LICs trading at 15 per cent to 25 per cent discounts for too long. Both LICs are trading below their IPO prices."

Wilson may be right. Luciano held a webinar targeted at retail investors on Wednesday, and said marketing efforts this week had helped narrow the gap.

VGI, he said, hoped to emulate Wilson Asset Management founder and king of LICS Geoff Wilson, whose funds command large premiums.

But Luciano is not alone in his predicament. Magellan's Hamish Douglass, Australia's most successful investor, is trying to come up with solutions to narrow discounts on his listed funds.

Global legends also trade for a cut price. Wall Street star Bill Ackman, who jetted into Australia in 2014 to market his Amsterdam-listed closed-end fund Pershing Square Holdings, hasn't been able to close the 30 per cent discount on his fund, despite an incredible recovery from disastrous investments and numerous measures aimed at narrowing the discount. It was a similar story for Phil King's Regal and L1 Capital's Raphael Lamm and Mark Landau, whose rocky listings were a turning point for the industry.

"In the absolute returns space, the Regal strategy, RF1, has shot the lights out post March-April, and is the top-performing strategy in the peer group," says Independent Investment Research's Rodney Lay.

In fact, Regal is the one fund that has succeeded in restoring its unit price to a premium after trading at a discount as large as 27 per cent, and RF1's 40 per cent gain makes it the top-performing LIC this year.

But what are these discounts, what causes them and why are they such a problem for these star managers?

■ The dreaded discount

Unlike an open-ended managed fund in which units are created or cancelled based on the value of the fund's assets, investors in an LIC or LIT subscribe to an issue of units at a point in time, via an initial public offering.

The investor can only exit by selling to another buyer on the exchange through a share trade. That means the price is a function of supply and demand and therefore can be below or above the value of the fund's assets at any given time, resulting in discounts and premiums.

The discounts make it expensive for existing investors to sell out of their holdings while premiums make it expensive for new investors to add to their holdings.

So what are LICs and LITs good for? The main benefit for investors is that they can allocate to a fund manager on the ASX.

For fund managers, a listed fund is highly advantageous because the funds raised remain in the company and are considered "permanent". They don't have to worry about redemptions, which they say allows them to take longer-term bets.

But the reality is that capital can come under threat if the fund manager fails to deal with the discount.

Activist investors can emerge on the register and force action that results in the discount being closed - such as forcing the wind-up of the fund so that the full value of the assets can be realised. That can be humiliating and costly to the manager.

■ The perfect storm

The discounts grew particularly large after what proved to be a perfect storm for the listed fund sector in 2020.

They were already under fire and the subject of a government review around conflicted remuneration to brokers when the March market sell-off sent anxious investors scrambling for the exit while buyers stood aside.

The result was a rapid decline in unit prices and a sharp widening in unit price discounts relative to the assets within the fund that had themselves plunged as the pandemic spooked markets.

If sentiment was weak heading into the crisis it hit rock bottom in the aftermath, as investors were left with loss-making positions that were hard to exit in investment vehicles that had been derided in the media as built on conflicted advice.

"LICs are persona non grata to me now," said one financial adviser who was asked about the sector. "We only played a couple the last few years but are still paying for it where we did.

"They really are a problematic structure with precious little redeeming features for the investor."

But it was about then that patient, savvy investors were snapping up what they believed were incredible bargains. With funds trading at sharp discounts to their net assets, buyers were effectively buying - in Warren Buffett parlance - a dollar for 75¢.

Among them were Brisbane-based listed fund specialist Affluence Funds Management, which was buying up listed funds for about 75 per cent of the value of their assets, helping it to a 21 per cent return above the broader share market benchmark.

"While a lot of the easy gains have been made, there are still a range of LICs trading at prices that excite us," Affluence's Wilson tells The Australian Financial Review.

In fact, nimble self-directed investors are on an equal footing and can potentially take advantage of large discounts. They could also benefit from the actions of activist investors who pressure funds to act in order to close wide discounts.

"Continuing pressure from investors, including activists, will help deal with some of the laggards," says Wilson.

All this makes the listed fund sector a fascinating battleground where high-powered hedge fund managers find themselves answerable to plucky activists, financial advisers and mum and dad investors.

■ Cleaning up the 'shit show'

While fund managers have come under fire for raising funds via an LIC, they do have an interest in narrowing the prevailing discount. But what is the best way?

Like a bad hangover, there's no truly effective cure. In September, analyst Rodney Lay of IIR penned a note: "Three ways to clean up this munted shit show".

The strong title expressed his frustration at the way the listed fund vehicle had been set up by managers in their favour, and not that of investors.

These include "appallingly long-dated" management agreements, boards that aren't truly independent, vested interests in not winding up or converting funds where appropriate, poor management of discounts and management styles that did not capitalise on the "single biggest advantage" of closed-end funds - that is, the capital is captive.

Lay points out that in the case of open-ended funds, managers live and die by the sword - that is, if they perform badly the money flows out of the door and if they do well, it rushes in.

But closed-end managers tend to raise more money when demand creates a premium but fail to scale back funds when demand is weak, resulting in discounts.

"Investors in open-ended funds have a greater balance of power versus close-ended funds - they can redeem in the context of poor performance."

What riled Lay is a "sense of entitlement" among managers that "exploited the captive capital for their own economic advantage" and at the expense of long-suffering investors, who have been left with persistent and deep discounts.

Lay says fund managers should do one of three things to deal with the discounts: convert to an active exchange traded fund, which allows investors to redeem or invest at the net asset value; hold periodic continuation votes in which investors can choose to wind up the funds; or set up mechanisms to manage discounts, such as buybacks tied to discounts.

There is activity underway. Several funds are advanced in winding up or converting into structures that will allow investors to realise the full asset value, or merging with other funds.

Meanwhile, activists are circling underperforming funds such as Tribeca's Natural Resources LIC - which had a soured investment in its credit fund and Templeton's value fund.

Affluence's Wilson says he has several positions where he expects corporate action to occur for at least another year "providing a steady stream of short-term trading opportunities".

"There are a range of activist investors who hold positions in multiple LICs, which will help to nudge boards along in several instances."

IIR's Lay agrees that shareholders should mobilise for their own interest.

"IIR would encourage shareholders/unitholders in vehicles with persistent deep discounts and relative underperformance to get active. Pressure boards - apparently they are there to act in your interest."

■ Time for a comeback?

While Lay is critical of the structures and intentions of managers, he says the funds that came to market in the previous two years have actually managed their portfolios quite well, even if the unit performance has bombed.

"While this cohort represents only 19 per cent of the total listed management investment (LMI) sector by number, we note that the top three LMIs and six of the top 10 LMIs by returns belong to this cohort. We also note five of the 10 lowest-volatility LMIs also belong to this cohort."

Affluence's Wilson favours two managers of the recent vintage - VGI Partners and L1 Capital's LSF.

Back in early 2018, L1 Capital's $1 billion IPO was the largest ever LIC. But it made a horrible start and while the managers have delivered strong returns since, the unit price has lagged.

That troubled float is said to be the reason equity managers have cancelled or downsized later raisings.

But Wilson says the fund is "positioned for economic recovery, continued stimulus and a vaccine".

"If they're right, LSF could have a very good year. If they're wrong, and performance suffers again, a wind-up or conversion to an unlisted fund could be on the horizon."

Another favourite of Wilson's is the Thorney Opportunities Fund, which is one of two LICs run by billionaire Waislitz.

"Short-term performance has not been great but long-term results have been.

It's trading at an unsustainable discount of almost 30 per cent, which must be of great embarrassment to the manager and will need to be rectified."

So for individual investors who believe these star fund managers can rediscover or retain their touch, and have both the power and the will to close those discounts, there's an opportunity.

For the broader sector, their ability to do so is the first necessary step in regaining the trust of a deeply suspicious market.SI
direction: downward pressure on LIC/LIT valuations

headline: US-Iran crisis raises fears of global sell-off
text:
Global equity markets could be rocked by a savage correction if tensions between the United States and Iran escalate and a jump in the oil price is sustained.

Investors were caught off-guard on Friday, after an airstrike ordered by US President Donald Trump killed Major General Qassem Soleimani, the leader of the foreign wing of Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps.

Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei threatened "severe retaliation" in response to the killing, sparking a sell-off of risk assets, with Iran and the US now pushed to the brink of all-out war.

The local sharemarket ended Friday's session higher, but is set to trade cautiously this week. While ASX futures are down just 4 points, or 0.1 per cent, ahead of Monday's session, losses could be substantially greater if Iran retaliates.

Shares on Wall Street were hit on Friday, ending a shortened week of trading with a loss.

The Dow Jones Industrial Average lost 233.92 points, or 0.8 per cent, to 28634.88, the Nasdaq Composite fell 71.42 points, or 0.8 per cent, to 9020.77 and the S&P 500 slid 23.00 points, or 0.7 per cent, to 3234.85.

Global equity markets could be hit further in the coming weeks, with December's strong equity market rally across the globe amid easing geopolitical tensions leaving the market exposed to a substantial pullback.

"The big rally in risk assets in December certainly looked like a play on the 2020 story - benign conditions, a trade truce and more money printing in G3 economies," said ING head of commodities strategy Warren Patterson.

"Were events in the Middle East to escalate severely, overweight positioning in risk assets could easily trigger a 7 to 10 per cent correction in global equity markets."

The MSCI World Index has risen 3.1 per cent since the start of December, pushed higher by news that the US and China agreed to a "phase one" trade deal. This gave markets temporary relief from the uncertainty of a trade war, which has dominated investor sentiment since early 2018.

"The easing of the strain between China and US had calmed the market into year end, but the flare-up in the Middle East highlights the risk that politics will play over the course of 2020," said JPMorgan Asset Management global market strategist Kerry Craig.

"The market impact will be dictated by the extent the souring relationship impacts the oil price and whether or not there was already a political premium in the oil price."

Crude prices are set to be one of the key indicators of sentiment over US-Iran tensions, with the Islamic Republic responsible for 4 per cent of the world's total oil production.

Brent crude rose 3.5 per cent to $US68.60 a barrel on Friday, settling at its highest level since September, while West Texas Intermediate firmed 3.1 per cent to $US63.05 a barrel, its highest since April.

"I think from a positioning perspective the move higher for crude can sustain," said Tribeca Investment Partners Asia chief executive and portfolio manager Ben Cleary.

He said the oil market was fundamentally balanced after recent cuts by the so-called "OPEC+" nations, which includes members of the Organisation of the Petroleum Exporting Countries plus 10 other oil-producing countries.

"The key for 2020 will be US production, with consensus expectations for a small year-on-year production increase," Mr Cleary said. "But it's possible that it's negative given the spate of production issues witnessed in the fourth quarter of 2019."

He added consumption from India and China remained strong, with more chance of an increase in demand than a decrease. "I think most things look fairly good for oil for the next three to six months," he said.

Any sustained increase in the price of oil could have ramifications for a number of central banks, with inflationary pressures from higher fuel prices likely to be a point of interest for policymakers.

"Historically, the economic damage from these events comes when a surge in energy prices creates rising inflation pressures and rate hikes by central banks," said JPMorgan's Mr Craig.

However, the price spike could be contained as the global oil market has plenty of spare capacity. Prices have already remained relatively resilient in the face of escalations in the tensions between the US and Iran.

In 2018, President Trump pulled out of a nuclear accord created by the Obama government, which relieved sanctions on Iran in exchange for giving up its nuclear weapons program.

The two countries came to a flashpoint in June last year, with Washington accusing Iran of attacking two oil tankers in the Persian Gulf, a move denied by Tehran.

Later that month, Iran's Revolutionary Guard shot down a US drone after it allegedly entered Iranian airspace.

Oil markets have so far proved relatively resilient to the rising tensions, even with sanctions introduced against the Islamic Republic by the US hampering its ability to sell its oil.

"Any significant and sustained disruption [to global supply] would likely mean the end of the OPEC+ deal to cut production by 1.7 million barrels per day, with members wanting to ensure adequate supply for the market," said ING's Mr Patterson.

"There is also the potential for emergency releases from government stocks."

ING also noted Mr Trump's desire to keep oil prices low, particularly ahead of November's presidential election, saying they expected Brent crude to trend back towards $US60 a barrel through the first quarter.

A drone attack on Saudi Arabia's energy infrastructure in mid-September took 6 per cent of the globe's crude supply offline and sent oil prices soaring.

Prices recovered within two weeks, as producers worked quickly to bring additional supply online.

"More often than not, the knee-jerk reaction in markets has reversed over the course of the coming months," Mr Craig said.

"As there is scope for other oil-producing nations to at least offset some of the decrease in supply from Iran or Iraq, as well as the fact that the US is less reliant on oil imports, the market impact may be more narrow than in the past."

The spike in oil prices and weakness in equities at the end of last week saw an increase in investor appetite for safe-haven assets.

Gold surged 1.5 per cent to $US1552.38 an ounce on Friday, its biggest spike since late August.

The yield on the Australian 10-year note dropped 9 basis points to 1.25 per cent on Friday, while the US 10-year Treasury yield fell 9 basis points to 1.79 per cent.

The sudden return of volatility across global markets following a month of relatively solid returns has showcased just how vulnerable markets are, particularly with global equities trading around record highs.
direction: bearish for global equities and risk assets

headline: BHP restarts ore tests five years after fatal dam burst
text:
BHP and Brazilian miner Vale look set to bring more supply into the booming iron ore market, with the gradual restart of the infamous Samarco operation underway.

A tragic dam collapse at the Brazilian operation in November 2015 killed at least 19 people and destroyed nearby villages, prompting BHP and Vale to halt production and focus instead on reparations, compensation and rehabilitation of the environment.

That five-year hiatus came to an end over the past week when engineers began testing aspects of the iron ore filtration and production systems at Samarco's operations in the state of Minas Gerais.

"Samarco has begun commissioning and testing the new filtration plant and one concentrator at the Germano complex in Minas Gerais, Brazil in preparation to restart operations," said BHP spokeswoman. "BHP expects Samarco to restart operations gradually in the coming weeks after all equipment tests have been completed to meet safety requirements for people and the environment."

The schedule suggest Samarco's iron ore products will be back into the market in the first quarter of 2021, by which time Chinese iron ore stockpiles are tipped to fall to a four-and-a-half year low. While the restart has long been planned for late 2020, the timing is convenient for BHP, which is coming under intense pressure from China over surging iron ore prices.

A slump in November iron ore exports from Port Hedland, where BHP and Fortescue ship their Australian iron ore, sparked heated questions from Chinese authorities.

The type of iron ore BHP sells from Port Hedland was fetching $US155.07 per tonne on Tuesday evening.

Samarco traditionally sold different iron ore products, such as manufactured pellets and concentrates that are rich in iron. BHP has not stated the exact volumes that Samarco will produce under its revised operating model, but it appears very likely to be much less than the 29 million tonnes produced in fiscal 2015.

Approval documents published last year suggested Samarco would produce about eight million tonnes per year in the first few years after restart.

Volumes would ramp up over a six-year period, at which time an investment decision would be needed to take production to 15 million tonnes per year.

The revived Samarco will adopt a different waste disposal method to the old method, which required large volumes of mud to be stored in big dams like the one that failed in November 2015.

Known as "dry stacking", the new method was the subject of a meeting of the world's biggest mining bosses in Melbourne in November 2018.

"Samarco will restart operations using a new system of tailings disposal incorporating a confined pit and tailings filtering system for dry stacking," said the BHP spokeswoman.

BHP and Vale created a foundation called Renova to conduct the reparations with the local community, industry and environment, and Renova has paid out more than $2.6 billion of remediation and compensation to date.

"The extensive work undertaken by the Renova Foundation to remediate and compensate for the damages of the failure of Samarco's Fundão dam in 2015 continues, and BHP remains fully committed to supporting the Foundation in its work," said the spokeswoman.
direction: downward pressure on iron ore prices and miner shares

headline: Easter publishing and the cyber attack on Nine
text:
■ Today's Easter bumper edition is on the news stand until Monday. Regular daily print publishing resumes on Tuesday, April 6. Online publishing continues throughout Easter at afr.com and on our iPad and iPhone editions.

Nine, publisher of The Australian Financial Review, was hit by a damaging cyber attack at its Sydney headquarters last weekend. It is suspected to be a ransomware attack.

This has affected the publication of the newspaper and, as a result, some financial markets data, including the sharemarket tables, are not printed in the bumper edition. They are available at afr.com.

We are working to return regular features to readers as soon as possible.

We apologise for the disruption.
direction: bearish for Nine shares

headline: Cohen returns as Bain partner
text:
Partners on the move

■ Jane Cohen, 47, will return to Bain as a partner from this month. She will focus on the firm's consumer strategy practice. Her most recent role was as a partner at KPMG, where she spent two years as part of the firm's global strategy group. She has also held senior roles at Australia Post and Pacific Brands. Prior to this she spent 12 years at Bain where she rose to the position of principal. She is based in Melbourne.

■ Tim Goodhew, 33, has joined Deloitte as a partner. He previously spent more than 11 years at EY, where his most recent role was as a director in the firm's consulting division. Mr Goodhew has also held roles as an actuarial analyst at Suncorp and Zurich Financial Services Australia. He is based in Sydney.

■ Christoph Schweizer has been elected the next global CEO of the Boston Consulting Group. Mr Schweizer, 48, will take up the role in October. He has worked at BCG for 23 years and has been the head of the firm's operations in central and eastern Europe and the Middle East since 2017. He is based in Germany.

Emerging experts

■ Lemuel Khoo, 27, has joined Protiviti as a manager in the firm's technology consulting team. He has previously worked as an analyst at ANZ and Qantas, and as a consultant at EY. He is based in Melbourne.

■ Grace Geddes has joined Protiviti as a senior consultant in the firm's technology consulting team. The 25-year-old previously worked as a senior consultant at EY, where she advised financial services clients on technology risk. She is based in Melbourne.

■ Kaitlyn Su, 29. has also joined Protiviti as a senior consultant in the firm's risk and compliance team. Ms Su previously worked at AMP as a consultant, and at Deloitte as an analyst, where she worked on remediation and compliance. She is based in Sydney.
direction: neutral to slightly bearish for professional services stocks

headline: A 'high bar' to opening bubble
text:
Queenstown | Scott Morrison and Jacinda Ardern say they will not do anything to jeopardise the freedoms and safety Australia and New Zealand enjoy by prematurely expanding the trans-Tasman travel bubble.

Mr Morrison played up the economic benefits being shared by the two countries with quarantine-free travel, while also acknowledging the need to bring Pacific island nation workers into the bubble to fill skills gaps.

"With our borders largely closed, effectively closed, it is the Australian and New Zealand economy which is sustaining each other," he said.

"While the rest of the world is closed off to us at this time, I think it will increase our appreciation of the economic ties that we have between Australia and New Zealand."

Mr Morrison on Sunday night told an event attended by Australian and New Zealand business leaders in Queenstown that bringing Vanuatu, the Solomon Islands, Fiji and Tonga into the bubble was a real possibility.

Yesterday he said bringing workers into the bubble, with appropriate quarantine, was an early step on that pathway.

"But you've got to do that safely," he said.

New Zealand last month granted a handful of border exemptions to critical skifields workers following lobbying from the tourism industry, which said they were necessary to enable skifields to open at 100 per cent capacity this season.

Australian farmers have been calling for more Pacific island workers to be allowed into Australia, citing worker shortages across the country.

Addressing the media with Mr Morrison after the annual Australia-New Zealand Leaders Meeting, Ms Ardern said options were being explored to grow the bubble, but the bar to adding countries would be set "very high".

"Both Australia and New Zealand share an enthusiasm for, where it is safe to do so, exploring additional options for expanding the trans-Tasman bubble," she said.

"We both acknowledge we want to maintain the freedoms we have; that means maintaining a very high bar that between ourselves we have set.

"We don't want to jeopardise the freedom we have within our own countries to move about freely and to protect the health of our citizens."

Concerns over growing the bubble are not just about COVID-19 entering the two countries.

Vaccination programs among Pacific island nations remain in their very early stages and the local health systems are ill equipped to deal with a major outbreak of the virus.

"We are very mindful that across the Pacific COVID has had and will continue to have the potential to cause terrible devastation to communities," Mr Morrison said. "We are incredibly aware of the health risks."

Plans to include Singapore and Taiwan in the bubble were shelved earlier this year following virus outbreaks in those two countries.
direction: supportive for tourism and agriculture sectors

headline: Reliance passes test, one day at a time
text:
While Australia endlessly debates what living with the delta variant of COVID-19 might look like, Heath Sharp is getting on with it.

The chief executive of plumbing supplies group Reliance Worldwide operates 15 manufacturing plants, 24 distribution hubs and five R&D centres around the world.

Key locations include Britain, where Prime Minister Boris Johnson has effectively decided to reopen a heavily vaccinated economy despite delta raging, and global HQ in Atlanta, Georgia, where governor Brian Kemp is trying to prevent private businesses from putting in place COVID-19 safety measures.

But Sharp says things feel, well, pretty normal.

Yes, safety protocols to protect Reliance's people remain in place: temperature checks at the factory gate, masks inside facilities,and social distancing rules. In Atlanta, Reliance has been incentivising staff to get vaccinated, including bringing in medical providers to make it easier to get a jab on the job.

In Britain, Sharp has kept the safety COVID-19 protocols in place despite Johnson's moves to open up. "And frankly, that was really well received by our people," he says.

One of the biggest challenges in a delta world is staffing and Reliance has been over-rostering workers in Britain to ensure its factories can keep output up. "Running the factories becomes a case of wondering every day how many people will be quarantining."

But for all that, Sharp says the delta wave has probably been a net positive for Reliance, as it's helped prolong the strong demand that started when the pandemic struck in early 2020 and shows little signs of slowing down.

"People are viewing their home as their castle," he explains. Renovating or rebuilding to accommodate working or learning from home, or even just to make life a little more comfortable, continues apace. That helped Reliance deliver a 63 per cent increase in underlying profit to $211.9 million for the 12 months ended June 30, with sales up 15 per cent to $1.3 billion.

It's a particularly impressive result given the supply chain issues and raw material rises that hit the business as the global economy boomed upon reopening. Those pressures, combined with the challenges of managing through COVID-19, made 2021 a year of perpetual problem solving.

"It was a great year from a results point of view, but it was delivered day-by-day."

Sharp admits he's been waiting for the COVID-19 demand spike to dissipate, but it hasn't yet. And so now he's wondering if the sector is seeing structural change, where the "home is my castle" theme resets the level of underlying demand in the market, and makes Reliance a sort of permanent COVID-19 winner.

Lockdowns like those in place in Australia will create hurdles, and Sharp says while Reliance hasn't seen a disruption to sales so far, there may be a hit as restrictions on the construction sector bite.

COVID-19 history says any loss of demand will be temporary and Reliance will bounce back when lockdowns end. "We've been tested, without question," Sharp says of the last 12 months, during which the group's shares are up 75 per cent.

"We've learnt a lot about ourselves, and our markets have been tested, too - and proven resilient."

WAM Microcap

soars to record year

It shouldn't be a surprise that the best year in the history of Wilson Asset Management's WAM Microcap listed invested company was built on stocks that have been, at one time or another, considered some of the worst stocks on the ASX.

Microcap investing is all about finding unloved gems that can rally spectacularly, and often these can be found in the market's cast-offs.

"We see a lot of the beaten-up companies that used to be in the small- and mid-cap space that have been forgotten about," lead portfolio manager Oscar Oberg says.

Such as Temple & Webster, an IPO dud that spent years in the wilderness before catching fire during last year's COVID-19 lockdown. It rose 38 per cent in the 2021 financial year.

Or Seven West Media, which started the year at 10¢ and ended it at 35¢, as the broader media sector rose on the reopening trade.

Or Virtus Health, which rose 120 per cent across 2021, having fallen badly out of favour in the depths of lockdown.

These stocks and a bevy of others, including Capitol Heath, Atomos and marketing business Enero Group (infamously known as Photon Group) helped WAM Microcap deliver a 53.2 per cent return for the 2021 year, exactly 20 per cent above its benchmark, the ASX Small Ordinaries Accumulation Index.

Oberg says while the LIC has a bottom-up focus, it was able to play the year's two main themes well: first, the boom in e-commerce and retail amid 2020's lockdowns, and then the rotation to cyclical stocks as the reopening trade came through in late 2020 and into 2021.

That tilt to cyclicals remains in place.

"We are definitely more weighted to a reopening of borders," he says. "We think governments are going to be under pressure to open up, once we get that 70 per cent or 80 per cent vaccination rate."

Oberg is hoping a couple of beaten-up stocks will provide a perfect way to ride that trade.

Dreamworld might be the thing most investors think about when you look at Ardent Leisure, but Oberg is most focused on the group's US bowling alley and amusement centre business Main Event, in which it sold a 24.2 per cent stake to private equity firm Redbird Capital Partners in June last year.

He says the US reopening should bode well for cash flow from Ardent, but should Redbird decide to exercise its option to buy another 27 per cent of Main Event, it will need to pay much more than the market expects. Ardent stock has more than doubled in the last 12 months.

WAM Microcap is also betting on Kiwi group Tourism Holdings, which owns a big fleet of campervans, and Oberg likes agricultural services group Ridley Corp as a potential takeover target.

While he concedes the market looks expensive, he says that's not necessarily true for individual stocks and there is no shortage of unloved gems to be found. "You only need a handful of them to start turning."

Prospa's record quarter underlines SME resilience

There is no question that it's small- and medium-sized businesses that are hit hardest by the long lockdowns we are living with in Victoria and NSW. But the full-year results from SME lender Prospa suggests these businesses at least entered lockdown after one of the best periods the Australian economy has seen for a decade.

Prospa's loan originations across the 2021 financial year rose 8.1 per cent to $483.7 million, but it was the June quarter when things really took off, with record originations of $183 million.

And chief executive Greg Moshal and chief financial officer Beau Bortoli say that despite the lockdowns across the country at the start of the new financial year, business is holding up well, with demand strong in July.

Moshal says it's important to remember that despite the lockdown narrative dominating the nation's consciousness, the economy is running at multiple speeds. So while cafes in the CBDs of Melbourne and Sydney remain shut, Prospa's lending to the hospitality sector hasn't fallen off a cliff - it's just moved into the suburbs.

The pair also point out that lending to tradies remains particularly strong - the renovation boom that has been running for the best part of 18 months still has plenty of room to run, apparently.

Prospa, which uses a proprietary credit engine for its lending decisions, at least has a bit more lockdown data to work with this time around. The performance of various sectors during last year's lockdowns is informing lending decisions this time around, to help the lender sort companies with temporary problems from those with more structural ones.

But there's also a degree of self-selection among the SME community. Moshal says it's the businesses still performing well that are driving credit demand; the strugglers are generally steering clear of taking on extra debt.

Prospa shares jumped 12 per cent yesterday and are now 47 per cent stronger over the last 12 months.
direction: Reliance Worldwide shares likely to rise
