headline: Banks to fuel next stage of dividend spree
text:
The resources sector will deliver windfall dividends for shareholders this year, but investors who doubt the sustainability of commodity prices are already thinking about the next source of income growth and concluding banks and industrial stocks offer the greater opportunity.

Rethinking how investors access income was discussed at Morningstar's investment conference yesterday, with fund managers encouraging investors to look outside conventional assets such as bonds and instead to the stocks leading the recovery from COVID-19.

"Income heavy lifting is no longer being done by bonds [because] central banks have anchored interest rates, which has changed the opportunity set for those requiring income, and they're going to have to consider equities," Michael Maughan, portfolio manager at Tyndall Asset Management, said.

"The dividend outlook is really strong and we expect that to make up a big part of total returns going forward given we're back to pre-COVID market levels," he said.

Forecasts have S&P/ASX 200 dividends surging 22 per cent in the 2021 financial year. While materials stocks account for more than half of those forecasts, thanks to a 43 per cent increase, financials, industrials and real estate investment trusts (REITs) are also all growing by more than 20 per cent, according to Mr Maughan.

And while the country's big miners, particularly iron ore stocks, will drive the dividend growth in the short term, questions linger about the sector's ability to maintain high payouts once commodity prices begin to decline.

"Commodity prices, particularly iron ore, have been extremely strong so the cashflow-generating ability of BHP, Rio Tinto and Fortescue are fantastic. So the strength of their balance sheets will enable them to pay generous dividends," another portfolio manager at Tyndall, Malcolm Whitten, said.

"The dilemma, though, is the trade-off between our long-term assumption and intrinsic valuation of those major resource names because we don't think commodity prices can sustain these levels longer-term. So for that reason, we're positioned close to market weight to collect that dividend and see what happens after that."

Instead, Mr Maughan and Mr Whitten, who together manage Tyndall's Australian share income fund, are looking outside the resources sector for future growth.

Mr Maughan pointed out that the S&P/ASX 200, excluding materials stocks, is expected to grow dividends by 7 per cent in 2022 and replicate that figure in 2023, with a significant part of that rise coming from Australia's major banks.

"In the next two years, banks and industrial stocks still have a normalisation to come through in their payout ratio and profits," Mr Maughan said.

The country's biggest lenders cut dividends last year on the back of cautious provisions undertaken at the beginning of the pandemic and limits put on payout ratios by the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority. The banks also strategically built up reserves in case of a spike in defaults.

This vigilance has set the banks up to rebound strongly, mirroring the performance of the economy, which is expected to be reflected in consistent dividend payouts.

"The likelihood and instances of bad debt has been low, and the provisions will likely unwind, so reported earnings will increase and as confidence returns, dividend payout ratios will rise," Mr Whitten said.

"We're also at a point now where the banks are flush with capital and we expect those capital returns from the banks to start soon, particularly ANZ and Commonwealth Bank, because they are sitting on a lot of excess capital," Mr Maughan added.

The return of strong dividends from the major banks comes at a time when the search for income is becoming increasingly difficult, while also growing in importance.

"The supply of income opportunities has been reduced and equities are now the best source of income available today," Mr Maughan said.

"At the same time, the need for capital growth is going to increase as people are continuing to live longer, and now we've got the risk of inflation which has reared its head again."

The significance of having reliable income was twofold, Mr Maughan said. "It gives money to live off from consistent cash flow, but more importantly, it gives capital to live off in capital drawdown years.

"That means you don't have to sell shares at the bottom and lock in that capital loss and this can make a huge difference to long-term returns."
direction: bullish for banks and industrial stocks

headline: Amatil leaves bourse after 51 years
text:
Coca-Cola European Partners says Peter West will take the helm of Coca-Cola Amatil's Australian, Pacific and Indonesia businesses following its successful $9.8 billion takeover.

Mr West is managing director of Amatil's Australian business and was widely expected to take the reins from group managing director Alison Watkins when the takeover was completed.

In a statement issued last night, Coke Europe said Mr West would become vice-president and general manager for a newly created Australia, Pacific and Indonesia (API) business unit and would join Coke Europe's executive leadership team.

The statement did not mention Ms Watkins' future, but an Amatil spokesman confirmed she could step down on May 10. Chief financial officer Greg Barnes will also be leaving the business.

After 51 years on the bourse, Amatil shares traded for the last time yesterday after Australia's largest non-alcoholic beverage bottler was swallowed up by Coke Europe.

The Supreme Court of New South Wales approved the London-based bottler's $9.8 billion takeover on Tuesday after Amatil shareholders overwhelmingly voted in favour of the $13.50 a share offer last Friday.

More than 17 million Amatil shares changed hands and the stock, which went ex dividend on Tuesday, closed at $13.30, reflecting the value of Coke Europe's all-or-nothing offer to Amatil's independent shareholders.

Independent shareholders will receive $13.32 a share cash on May 10, when the scheme of arrangement is implemented, and will receive Amatil's fully franked 18¢ a share final dividend on April 30.

Coke Europe, which said on Tuesday it would change its name to Coca-Cola Europacific Partners, launched a new note issue overnight to fund the acquisition.

Moody's Investors Service assigned a Baa1 senior unsecured rating to the proposed notes.

"This is a significant moment for Coca-Cola Amatil and Coca-Cola European Partners, and our new name reflects what will be a broader and more balanced geographic footprint, doubling our consumer reach," said CCEP chief executive Damian Gammell.

"As one company, we can go further together, driving growth and scaling faster," said Mr Gammell, who hopes to not only convince Australian and New Zealand consumers to drink more Coke, bottled water and juice, but to accelerate growth by investing more heavily in Indonesia and eventually expanding into new Asian markets.

Amatil shareholders will not be able to participate in the future growth unless they are prepared to buy shares in Coca-Cola Europacific, which is listed on Euronext Amsterdam, the New York Stock Exchange, the London Stock Exchange and Spanish stock exchanges.

Coke Europacific has no current plans to list in Australia, though Amatil will account for about 21 per cent of the group's revenues of more than €13.5 billion ($21 billion), and 23 per cent of earnings of €2.3 billion.

The takeover of the company, through a deal engineered by Amatil's major shareholder and franchisor, The Coca-Cola Co, will no doubt come as a disappointment to legendary Coca-Cola Amatil chief Dean Wills.

As chief executive and chairman, Mr Wills changed Amatil's future more than 30 years ago by selling out of tobacco, poultry, snack foods and packaging to hitch its wagon to the word's largest soft drink company.
direction: Amatil shares to trade at takeover premium

headline: Equities are the only sensible foundation for private pensions
text:
"Never make forecasts, especially about the future." Nobody knows who said this first. The point, however, is that we have to make forecasts, or at least guesses, about the uncertain future.

Of nothing is this truer than long-term investments for delivering security in old age. The problem - inescapable, but fundamental - is that the sensible way to do this is to invest in risky assets.

The risks cannot be eliminated. Someone must bear them. The question is, who this should be, and how it should be done?

The tragedy of British pension arrangements is that the attempt to force safety on private arrangements is forcing them into collapse. Worse, it has allowed the older generation of pensioners to extract all the risk-bearing capacity of private sponsors for their benefit, so moving the younger generation into permanent insecurity.

This is a scandal.

A question, however, is how risky investment in "risky" assets actually is? The evidence on this is encouraging.

The work of Elroy Dimson, Paul Marsh and Mike Staunton in successive Credit Suisse Global Investment yearbooks shows that over the past 120 years, diversified portfolios of equities in the corporate sectors of high-income market economies have done staggeringly well.

The geometric mean annual real return in these economies has been 5.4 per cent. In the US, the mean return has been 6.6 per cent. In the UK, it has been 5.4 per cent.

A pound invested and reinvested in the UK market in 1900 would have become £572 by 2020, after adjusting for inflation.

A dollar invested in the US market would have become $US2291, on the same basis. But a pound invested in UK government bonds would have become £10.40, and a dollar invested in US bonds $US12.50.

The only guarantee the bond market gives is one of poverty.

Economists call this gigantic divergence an "equity risk premium". The word "risk" reflects a quasi-religious belief that this divergence reflects rational adjustment to risk. Properly risk-adjusted, expected returns were the same. But attempts to justify this belief mostly seem far-fetched.

One justification is that wars or revolutions can ruin equities. This is true. But they also destroy bonds. Moreover, the massive long-term outperformance of equities in the major markets occurred despite two world wars, communist revolutions, the Depression, hyper-inflations, the great inflation of the 1970s, and the global financial crisis of the 2000s.

Of course, a global thermonuclear war or catastrophic climate change would be worse. But bonds would not bring safety. Nothing would. Global disaster is not insurable.

People such as Nobel laureate Robert Shiller and British economist Andrew Smithers argue that while sharemarkets do oscillate, their returns are mean-reverting in the long run.

In hindsight, at least, such mean-reversion has been striking, notably in the crucial case of the US. This supports the view that holding a widely diversified portfolio of equities is far and away the best long-term investment strategy in our uncertain world. If one is as clever and skilled as Warren Buffett, the returns from attending to signals of under- and over-valuation have been staggering.

Whether one can rely on this for the future is debated. Moreover, there is an obvious problem: that of the volatility noted above.

The variability of annual equity returns has been somewhat higher than that of bonds. Far more important, sharemarkets can deliver negative returns, from peaks, over long periods.

Currently, the cyclically adjusted earnings yield on the S&P 500 is only 2.6 per cent. This is at crash-warning levels by historical standards. Should a prolonged bear market occur, equity valuations might collapse. The valuations of the mid-1960s did not return for 30 years.

The big threat then is that, as John Maynard Keynes allegedly said, "the market can remain irrational longer than you can stay solvent". In effect, there is a shortage of speculators able to prevent equities from remaining durably mispriced. The bankruptcy constraint binds markets.

So equities may offer a free lunch. But you must be able to wait for the meal. This creates an opportunity and a danger.

The opportunity is for an investor with a sufficiently long time horizon to make extraordinary returns. In a well-ordered world, multigeneration pension funds ought to be such investors. The danger is that such funds (and still more so individuals investing on their own) will exhaust their room for manoeuvre before their investments pay off.

Fears of a huge bear market, not unrealistic at current valuations, might force them into the bond or cash traps.
direction: bullish for equities and equity-focused private pension funds

headline: Letters
text:
Unreasonable rant

on ScoMo's speech

According to Rear Window, Scott Morrison's address to The Sydney Institute's annual dinner on Monday was unendurable and incoherent. ("Scott Morrison takes credit for a uniquely Australian blunder", December 16.)

This is just a rant. The Prime Minister's speech was neither unendurable nor incoherent. And it didn't go for 39 minutes - an error of some 25 per cent. Readers of The Australian Financial Review can check all this on Sky News, YouTube or Facebook.

By the way, if Rear Window wishes to appropriate the term "Can you bear it?" - which appears each week in my Media Watch Dog blog - it should note that the term ends with a question mark. Not an exclamation mark!

Gerard Henderson

The Sydney Institute

Astounding defence

of China on trade

Truthfully I had to do a double-take that the article "It's China that plays by the rules (December 15) wasn't the latest concoction from the meretricious Global Times news outlet.

I suppose the author might be extended due credit if we set aside China's secular intellectual property violations, coercive trade tactics and usurious tariffs levied on Australian exports on trumped-up grounds of product dumping. Then and only then.

Shang-Jin Wei's data might support the notion that China is not an "off-the-chart" WTO violator but evidence to hand suggests that is precisely what they are when viewed through the lens of Australia's recent trade experiences. It's an affront to suggest otherwise.

Ben Griffiths

Bronte, NSW

Not too late for a

price on carbon

This paper advocates putting a price on carbon. So does the OECD, under Secretary-General Mathias Cormann. And so does the Business Council of Australia, as do countless Australian business leaders, among them Andrew Liveris - most recently in the article "The classical liberal solution to climate change is a carbon price" (December 15).

It is still not too late. All we need is the blessing of Barnaby Joyce. The rules of combat in the Climate Wars have deterred Anthony Albanese from moving in this direction but Barnaby, with the backing of his carbon farmers, could surely waive this objection.

Or is it going to be left to voters in the federal election to spare Australia the indignity of being forced to price carbon at source - to avoid paying the same price or more to trading partners under their dreaded "carbon border adjustment mechanisms".

John Gare

Kew East, Vic

Speed trumps accuracy

for COVID-19 tests

Delays between PCR testing and state entry means COVID-19 could circulate unchecked for four days. Rapid antigen tests (RATs) could reduce this risk. RATs provide on-the-spot results, accuracy of about 70 per cent at a cost of about $10 per test. A PCR test is 10 times the cost, has more than 90 per cent accuracy but gives the horse a chance to bolt.

Why choose accuracy (PCR) over speed (RAT)? Three ASX-listed companies control about 80 per cent of the pathology market and in FY21 they made off like bandits with the money thanks to PCR tests. Revenue up about 30 per cent and profits more than double last year. Some PCR tests should be able to be replaced with RATs. Don't wait for governments to make the rules.

Help close the gap by taking a RAT at border crossings. Spend $10 for your fellow citizens. Don't be a rat, get a RAT.

Moira Daw

Kirribilli, NSW
direction: bearish for Australian exporters and trade-sensitive sectors

headline: It's a sorry sight when Fed chiefs turn traders
text:
For just about any investor who's not all in on stocks, hating on the Federal Reserve is a way of life.

The infinite money printing and the moral hazard attitude of the world's most powerful central bank is the easiest thing for them to blame for the party they've not been enjoying.

But last week, it must be said, they had a particularly legitimate gripe. The presidents of the Dallas and Boston Federal Reserves were partying harder than most, and agreed they may have been overdoing it.

While these public officials had repeatedly filed notices on their trading activity each year, the brazen hyperactivity on display was extraordinary.

The Dallas Fed president Robert Kaplan averaged about 25 instances of million-dollar-plus stock trades a year, usually entering and exiting stocks from Alibaba to credit ETFs within a 12-month period.

Meanwhile, the Boston Fed's Eric Rosengren bought into several real estate funds. This was all allowed under the rules, but wasn't a great look.

So last week, they decided to halt their trading on ethical grounds, and shut their trading accounts. By the end of the month, they will convert their holdings into passive funds.

Naturally, conspiracy theorists think there's a convenience aspect to the timing. The market wobbles since the announcement are not helping their cause.

For such high-powered public servants who play a role in setting the cost of money, and the ultimate direction of the sharemarket, it's hard not to be irked. It's fair to say the American public expected better.

So, what about Australia? Well, it doesn't look like it's going on here.

The two highest ranking Reserve Bank of Australia officials, governor Phil Lowe and deputy Guy Debelle, are required to provide periodic material personal interest disclosures. There's no hot trading tips contained within their filings.

They do both own a house in Randwick (Debelle has a mortgage) and the only vestige of privilege is their respective complimentary membership to the Qantas chairman's lounge, which has been pretty useless of late.

The lure of day trading does not appear to be strong in Martin Place. One Reserve Bank staffer privately said they wouldn't consider fixing the home loan rate on the mortgage, amid concerns about how that might be perceived.

More often than not, it's the perception to maintain the full faith of the public or the paying punter that makes conflict management so important.

Consider the former head of the Swiss central bank chief, Philipp Hildebrand. He resigned in 2012 after his wife made more than $500,000 on currency trades that benefited from the bank's decision to intervene.

Hildebrand was adamant and said he had no idea of his wife's fortuitous positioning, until after the act. He presented emails to prove as such but he fell on his sword after he could not provide "irrefutable evidence".

That's a cautionary tale that conflicts need to be proactively managed and anticipated as extensively as possible if you're in a such a high position of public trust. These important institutions depend on it, now more than ever in an age where social media gives oxygen to even the most unsubstantiated of claims.

Beyond the high towers of central banking, attitudes toward conflicts between individuals and organisations can vary significantly.

US investment banks, for instance, are notoriously restrictive about share trading and any form of inducements. Yet research analysts at local stockbroking firms are free to trade the stocks they cover, even though their calls almost certainly influence the share price.

The big four accounting firms, which some believe don't appear to manage the conflicts between their audit and consulting functions, are tough on staff owning shares. In some cases, even exchange-traded funds are ruled out.

Then there's the management consulting firm McKinsey's secretive endowment fund, which the firm insisted was run at arm's length.

Within the asset management profession too, there's a wide spectrum of policies about owning stocks in personal accounts.

Often, they centre on ensuring alignment with clients, but there are countless opportunities for conflicts, real and perceived. And true alignment always appears elusive.

Individuals in the industry, too, have diverse views on what a conflict is and whether it should be managed.

Quite often when the issue comes up, it's accompanied by a wink, and the phrase "no conflict, no interest" as if this clever little twist on words alone excuses a whole raft of questionable actions.

What is certain is that conflicts exist anywhere and everywhere in finance in a variety of forms. Sometimes they're petty, and sometimes they're lucrative. They are often impossible to detect, and so moral fibre and a sense of duty is the only safeguard.
direction: short-term volatility and bearish pressure on equities

headline: Rear Window
text:
John Karantzis has everything under control

It turns out we were wrong to say iSignthis chief executive John Karantzis has been slapped with a $5 million tax bill and banned from leaving the country. It was actually $10 million! Shortfall interest ($861,641) and administrative penalties of $4 million will do that ...

Last October, the Australian Taxation Office began an audit of Karantzis' personal income tax affairs for the six years to June 30, 2020. On November 25, the ATO politely asked Karantzis to provide 14 days' notice of his intention to leave Australia. On November 28, without telling the ATO, Karantzis applied to the Department of Home Affairs to depart for Cyprus. We now also know that on December 4, Karantzis transferred his Hawthorn home - his primary asset in Australia - into his wife's name! Is it any wonder the ATO slapped a Departure Prohibition Order (DPO) on him on December 18?

True to form, Karantzis is now suing the Tax Commissioner in the Federal Court over his amended tax assessments for 2015, 2016 and 2017, and the case filings are absolute dynamite.

In the ATO's statement of reasons, there is an entire section titled "Your attempt to depart Australia during our review of your tax affairs without notice to the Commissioner". It comes right after the one called "Your steps to conceal your true tax position and those of your associates". Karantzis' supporters might want to skip the part where he allegedly doctored his bank statements.

Comically, Karantzis claimed zero income in the three tax returns now in dispute, despite his salary (FY15: $200,773; FY16: $219,000; FY17: $228,125) being publicly disclosed in the iSignthis annual report lodged with the ASX! And he did not declare any interest in the British Virgin Islands entities that held/hold his shares in iSignthis, despite these entities - and his interest in them - being recorded in iSignthis disclosures to the ASX and in his mortgage application with the Commonwealth Bank. As we've said before, this guy is the real deal.

Karantzis is now challenging the 2015 assessment on the basis he is merely "one of many shareholders" in iSignthis Ltd (BVI). Overtaxed, he says, for an entity he forgot to tell the taxman about! But if that's true, why didn't he produce the documentation eight months ago?

Karantzis' solicitors at HWL Ebsworth wrote to the ATO on May 6 seeking permission for him to leave the country for 92 days on iSignthis business. They assured the tax office he had transferred ownership of his house to Mrs Karantzis "to make it simpler for her to administer the day-to-day running of the household". What, every time she called Telstra or AGL they asked to see her name on the title deed? This motive would sound no less plausible being warbled by a galah instead of written by a lawyer billing $800 an hour.

Also because "his wife was seeking greater control and autonomy in their marriage after their relationship was put under significant strain from [iSignthis] being suspended from trading on the ASX, the defamatory articles published by the Financial Review, and the COVID-19 lockdowns". His solicitors did not, funnily enough, identify any marital strain caused by her husband's transformation into Australia's biggest serial litigant this side of Clive Palmer.

His wife had wasted no time seizing her autonomy, selling the house in February for $3.6 million - something HWL Ebsworth omitted to mention.

In pleading for his intercontinental liberation, HWL Ebsworth furnished the commissioner with the compelling reasons Karantzis has to return to Australia as promised, such as that iSignthis is on the cusp of being granted a banking licence by the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority, and that he's the nominated responsible manager. Yep, APRA's gonna give the green light to this one any day now. Karantzis will be the first person in the history of Australian banking to be operating under an ADI (authorised deposit-taking institution) and a DPO at the same time!

Or that Karantzis is CEO of the National Stock Exchange of Australia, which "requires [his] ongoing focus and presence ... The transformation of the business includes the appointment of Mr Graeme Samuel, AC, to the position of chairman."

We presume Samuel learnt all about Karantzis' DPO in the extensive due diligence he performed before accepting a non-executive directorship of the NSX, but more than three months later, he's still not its chairman. As for Karantzis leading the NSX transformation, the NSX told the ASX on June 2 that Karantzis intended to step down.

Another reason we can be positive Karantzis would rush back from Nicosia is that he's "engaged in discussions with directors of the New Zealand Stock Exchange to explore potential mutual business interests and opportunities". Again, this was three months ago, so the two parties must be very close to announcing their historic merger.

Just wait until the ATO gets around to issuing Karantzis' amended assessment for 2018, the year in which his 446 million iSignthis shares (then worth $67 million) were transferred from iSignthis BVI to Select All BVI and 137 million more (worth $25 million) ended up in the hands of directors, family and associates. If Karantzis is considered a flight risk now, what would he be after those capital gains tax events?

Corporate Travel clips the ticket on UK's private jet

In a world where corporate travel has more or less ground to a halt, Corporate Travel Management has had an excellent pandemic.

Alone of Australia's major travel stocks, its share price is up threefold since March last year. It's already flagging a return to profit by the end of this year, and maintains plenty of cash on its balance sheet.

Helping things along has been what chief Jamie Pherous describes as "an enormous amount" of business for the British government, for whom it manages repatriation flights and quarantine arrangements. Along with other services, such as private jet rentals.

This month, Britain's i newspaper revealed the Brisbane-based travel company was last year contracted by the UK government to provide a Union Jack-emblazoned Airbus A321 for use by Boris Johnson and his ministers and officials.

This "duly awarded" contract could cost the British taxpayer no less than £75 million ($141.2 million) over the next five years (depending on usage), while buying the same plane straight from Airbus would cost £79 million over its entire lifetime.

Corporate Travel Management has no aircraft leasing division, and is itself leasing the jet from Titan Airways. Why would the British government need Corporate Travel to clip the ticket on a contract with a British charter airline?

Beats us! Though shareholders must greatly admire Corporate Travel's ability to milk the British taxpayer.

In September, Pherous told this newspaper that a source "close" to Johnson had called the company asking it to "solve" their repatriation problem. The quote has been widely cited in the British press.

And no wonder: Corporate Travel's myriad UK contracts seem to have flowed seamlessly ever since.
direction: iSignthis shares likely to decline due to legal and reputational risks

headline: Zip buys two overseas buy now, pay later companies
text:
Zip Co will invest more than $160 million to take full ownership of two buy now, pay later companies in which it has previously owned minority stakes as it seeks to expand its global presence in the sector.

The $3.9 billion Sydney-based consumer finance fintech said it would pay about €89 million ($140 million) for the remaining shares in European operator Twisto Payments and about $US16 million ($21 million) to buy out Spotii, which operates in the Middle East.

Those transactions were valued at $180 million on an enterprise value basis, Zip said, and were intended to establish a presence in the large European market and fast-growing Middle Eastern market.

The Twisto acquisition came after exercising a call option to buy the outstanding stake, to be paid in the form of shares, cash or a combination that can be determined by Zip at the time of the acquisition. It is scheduled for completion in the fourth quarter of the calendar year.

Zip can also determine whether it pays for the remaining stake in United Arab Emirates-based Spotii in the form of shares, cash or a combination. Acquisition of the start-up is scheduled to occur in the third quarter of the year.

The market initially cheered the deals as Zip's share price rose as much as 4 per cent, but the gains were pared as the company ended the session 0.85 per cent higher at $7.10.

So far this year, Zip has outperformed rival buy now, pay later operators Afterpay and Sezzle. Zip has gained 36 per cent in 2021. That compares to a 21 per cent fall in Afterpay, which has a far larger market capitalisation of $27 billion, and a 26 per cent gain for smaller rival Sezzle.

All three companies have stated their ambitions to list in the United States.

The transactions, which Zip flagged in December, add to its investment in US buy now, pay later operator Quadpay, which has increased its annual transactions by more than 200 per cent since the consummation of that deal.

Zip said the Twisto and Spotii acquisitions followed a similar approach to Quadpay, in which it makes a low-risk minority investment.

It can then monitor things based on the cultural fit, management alignment, the business plan and the suitability for integration.

It said Twisto and Spotii would benefit from the synergies of being part of a truly global payments organisation.
direction: Zip Co shares higher

text:
Comment&OpinionKenneth Rogoff

Shock for COP hopefuls

Unless energy security can be married to climate literacy, then the politics of a cleaner planet will continue to be very difficult. p46

John Kehoe

Buy now, pay later battle

A heavyweight fight is under way over buy now, pay later rules. It is drawing in Afterpay, the RBA, the Commonwealth Bank, senators and lobbyists. p47
direction: bearish for BNPL and banking stocks

headline: Blackstone's big deal changes the game
text:
Phil Pearce, the chief executive of ESR Australia, describes the $3.8 billion purchase of Blackstone's 45 industrial and logistics properties by ESR and GIC as "transformational".

It's an overworked term. But in this case correct.

The deal will transform traditional thinking about commercial real estate. In the age of e-commerce, global real estate investors will pay a higher multiple - or in real estate terms, a lower yield - for warehouses than they will pay for office towers or shopping centres.

The deal will transform the balance sheets, and funds management income, of a host of real estate investment trusts with logistics properties.

It will transform ESR and its partner, the Singapore sovereign wealth fund GIC - already two global logistics heavyweights - into the third-largest owners and managers of the warehouses that support e-commerce in Australia.

And paradoxically, it will start investors thinking again about the relatively higher returns on other sectors, such as offices and non-discretionary retail.

Last Saturday evening, after 12 weeks of work capped by an intense 36 hours with the lawyers, ESR and GIC touched out another Singapore sovereign wealth fund, Temasek subsidiary Mapletree, to emerge with one of the prizes of Australian real estate.

GIC, which, according to Real Capital Analytics, was the largest foreign buyer of Australian commercial real estate last year, bought 80 per cent of the portfolio, and ESR took 20 per cent along with the management.

The bankers - ANZ, Mitsubishi UFJ, Standard Chartered and United Overseas Bank - stumped up more than $2 billion, at an all-in cost of under 2 per cent, to make it happen.

The prize is 1.4 million square metres of warehouse space, of which 53 per cent is in Melbourne, leased for an average of more than six years to heavyweights such as Woolworths, Lineage Logistics and Toll.

Equally importantly, the facilities sit on 360 hectares of land. Pearce calls it an "income-producing land bank in cracking locations" which, according to the agents, JLL and Eastdil Secured, would support another $2 billion of development.

The immediate focus is the yield. Divide the income, $150 million, by the price and the yield is 3.9 per cent. Wrong, says Pearce. Account for the purchase of the management, and the yield is 4.5 per cent.

Either figure is low. Today's graph, from JLL, shows just how much prime industrial yields have compressed, to a point now lower than the yields on offices or retail.

The compression, and the commensurate rise in price, is set to continue. Already one investment in Sydney's Eastern Creek has all but sold on a yield of 3.6 per cent.

There is no magic to this. In the era of e-commerce, distribution centres are critical. Logistics tenants will bid up the rents and real estate investors will bid up values

JLL's head of research in Australia, Andrew Ballantyne, estimates that logistics and industrial assets will deliver real (after inflation) rental growth of 100-130 basis points a year.

That might be conservative. Researchers are now modelling the relationship between the growth in online retailing and the need for more logistics space.

Blackstone's senior managing director in Australia, Chris Tynan, says rental growth on logistics properties accelerated in the US, Britain and Canada once online sales exceeded 13 per cent of total retail spending - a figure Australia is fast approaching in the wake of the pandemic.

Tony Iuliano, JLL's head of capital markets, industrial and logistics, Australia, who negotiated the sale with Stuart Crow and Adrian Rowse, points to the weight of investment money chasing logistics property.

The Blackstone offering attracted more than $45 billion worth of institutional capital, and in one deal took out most of the $4.5 billion average annual turnover in prime logistics real estate.

"I have been telling the world for 20 years that sheds are the way to go, and finally they have woken up," says Iuliano.

The Charter Hall Group is the second-largest investor and manager of logistics facilities in Australia after Goodman Group. Chief executive David Harrison says demand is far outstripping supply, and that cap rates will keep falling, to under 4 per cent for premium assets and 4.5-5 per cent for secondary properties.

UBS real estate analyst Grant McCasker says the deal will reset prime logistics cap rates with a boost to average prime property valuations of about 15 per cent. All the REITs with logistics exposure benefit, with Goodman Group and Charter Hall the biggest beneficiaries.

As logistics yields fall, the cash returns on retail and office sectors could start to look relatively more attractive, despite logistics' stronger rental growth outlook.

JLL's director of retail investments in Australia, Nick Willis, says quality metropolitan retail property, underpinned by major tenants, is now trading at a 200 basis point spread to logistics, and investors are seeing the value arbitrage.

The transaction is particularly transformative for ESR, already the largest logistics group in Asia Pacific with about $US30 billion ($39 billion) in assets under management, and a Hong Kong listing backed by heavyweights such as Oxford Properties.

Pearce, its Australian chief executive, targeted the Blackstone portfolio from the time he arrived in 2017, buying the development and management expertise of Commercial & Industrial Property and then the assets and funds management operation of PropertyLink to establish an integrated logistics business with the assets held largely off balance sheet by leading institutions.

He was Goodman Group's respected inaugural managing director for Greater China, building a $5 billion operation and taking a seat on the Goodman board.

Tynan says Blackstone still has more than $10 billion worth of debt and equity positions in Australia and is still buying logistics properties, though with a focus on assets which are close to mass markets and will support last-mile daily delivery.

"I am not calling the top of the logistics market," Tynan says.

Robert Harley is a former property editor of The Australian Financial Review. He is at rob@rharley.com.au
direction: bullish for logistics REITs and industrial property sectors

headline: Television fight over ratings crown ignores shift to digital
text:
Australia's TV industry is making the seismic shift to digital, but as the official broadcast ratings year came to an end at the weekend, the focus was largely on traditional TV audiences rather than those watching via digital means, nor was it on "total TV", a term the industry uses to talk about traditional broadcast and broadcast video-on-demand combined.

Indeed, as the official ratings year ended, Seven West Media claimed the ratings crown for the total people metric across traditional broadcast.

"It's important because there's winners and there's losers, and in 2021, we won the year," Seven West Media chief executive James Warburton says.

Seven did just beat rival 9Network with the total people metric, with an audience share of 38.2 per cent for the survey year excluding Easter and the Olympics, with the 9Network on 38 per cent. Network 10 was well behind with a share of 23.8 per cent.

"It's fantastic to be back to the No.1 position. Total people is crucially important because it means more people have watched your network than any other," Mr Warburton said.

The 9Network and ViacomCBS-owned Network 10 don't agree.

"There's only one metric that matters and that is the metric that matters to advertisers. Every campaign that is bought in television is bought within those three demographics that we focus on - 16- to 39-year-olds, 25- to 54-year-olds, and grocery shoppers with children," Nine chief sales officer Michael Stephenson said.

"That's where our businesses are focused. That's how we produce, create or curate content. Everything is focused on delivering in those demographics. And once again, of course, we are the undisputed leader."

The 9Network won those demographics with 37.9 per cent, 36.3 per cent and 39.2 per cent respectively.

Ten chief sales officer Rod Prosser agrees it is the advertising demographics that matter as "it's the money that matters". "We've got a really potent audience in the demos, and we're really pleased with that," he said.

"But certainly, it is the demographics that matter, 60 per cent of the [advertising] revenue is spent against 25-54."

While it may seem a game to claim the title of Australia's most-watched network, free-to-air television advertising is worth $2.5 billion.

As Warburton told investors in June: "Ratings lead to revenue and revenue share, and a reminder that every share point is worth $21 million to the bottom line."

While the TV networks grapple over who won what, those with the advertising dollars suggest the transition to digital is causing teething problems.

"We're going through this transition at the moment where we've almost got these parallel universes of linear TV, which are bought and appraised in a completely different way, to what you might call catch-up TV or streaming," said Chris Walton, the managing director of independent media agency Nunn Media, which works with clients to plan and buy advertising space,

He suggests the TV networks are failing to come to grips with shifting audiences, still promising huge TV audiences on traditional broadcast TV.

While the TV networks' advertising inventory is in high demand, Walton suggests it is because they are having to provide what is called "make goods" to advertising partners.

That is, the networks have to deliver more ads for a brand after their first run was not as watched by the audiences predicted, meaning the TV network has not delivered on what was sold to the advertiser. "The village expectations need to be reset because there's so many shows under-delivering all the time that they're forever catching up with make-goods," Mr Walton said.

He said he wants the networks to better manage expectations and improve relationships with their advertising clients.

So what ratings crown should the networks be focused on?

According to PwC Australia's latest Entertainment and Media Outlook, the broadcast video-on-demand advertising market hit $229 million in 2020, and in a strong market is projected to reach just under $1 billion by 2025.

Linear TV revenue, on the other hand, declined and while it is still larger than its growing digital sister BVOD, it faces audience fragmentation in the years ahead, and shrinking revenue.

If it is the money that matters, a TV ratings crown needs to include the growing audiences on digital, as anything else is just not the full story.
direction: bearish for traditional TV broadcasters like Seven West Media due to digital shift risks
pressure on rate-sensitive sectors like banks and property trusts

headline: Low productivity remains black hole in recovery
text:
At the end of 2021, it's impossible not to be impressed with Australia's rebound from the frozen pandemic economy of 2020. Even with the delta-driven reprise of lockdowns in NSW and Victoria this year and closed borders in other states, the economy has still grown at about its long-term average of 3 per cent and the Reserve Bank forecasts a bounceback to 5.5 per cent in 2022.

Unemployment did rise back above 5 per cent last month, but the RBA still expects joblessness to fall to sustained levels "with a four in front of it" by the new year. That's when Treasurer Josh Frydenberg says he will start looking at budget repair, though, with an election due by May, few will be holding their breath.

Mr Frydenberg is already assured of a long-awaited catch-up in business investment when he announces the mid-year budget update tomorrow. Total new investment will be up 16 per cent this year and next, with non-mining investment up 8.5 per cent rather than just 1.5 per cent as feared at the May budget. That's the best performance in a dismal investment decade: $20 billion to be spent by business.

Business expects a wave of revenge spending from the estimated $230 billion savings pile that households have accumulated from support payments and enforced stays at home.

Australia's post-pandemic boom is welcome, but it has been bought at a heavy price of spending income now that is yet to be generated, merely borrowed from the future. The mid-year economic and fiscal outlook is expected to reveal that the deficit bottom line is tens of billions of dollars better off over four years than forecast back in the May budget, as the economy rapidly improves.

But even Deloitte Access Economics' upbeat outlook would leave $239 billion of deficits over the next four years, from a near-balanced budget in 2019. Long-term debt approaches a trillion dollars. And there is, so far, no announced plan to get back to balance, let alone to surplus. Growing the economy is the main way out. And that requires higher productivity: that delivers more goods and services for every unit of labour and capital. But productivity is now the economy's other big black hole.

Productivity was not mentioned at all in the Treasurer's budget speech in May. And even if Mr Frydenberg raises it tomorrow - as he should - then there is nothing standing behind it. If Australian productivity repeated its performance during the 1990s - 1.8 per cent growth annually in the five years to 2000 - the economy would in a decade hence be 10 per cent bigger, suggests former Productivity Commission chairman Gary Banks. Australia would quickly outgrow its debt.

Instead, the Productivity Commission this week confirmed that the past decade was the worst for productivity growth since records began.

The May budget included an underlying assumption that annual productivity growth would return to the 30-year average of 1.5 per cent. The longer it takes for productivity to recover to those levels, the lower the growth and the higher the debt. But it would now take another 1990s-style revolution to restore productivity from the drop down to labour productivity growth of 1.1 per cent last calendar year, and zero as recently as 2018-19. Total factor productivity, which includes capital, slumped to just 0.2 per cent last year. Where is a decade of rising productivity growth going to come from?

Australia has more anti-productivity policies than ones that will cut costs and encourage innovation - critical bottlenecks in industrial relations, taxation, energy and infrastructure. The business capex rebound will give workers better tools to produce more. But turning this into an ongoing productivity rebound will require serious policy choices to be made on the tax system and workplace reform, to begin with.

With no sign of that, the next government will need to tackle locked-in extra spending on programs such as for aged care and disability care, which of course now are seriously blowing out in cost. The bottom line is that Australia has used its China boom windfall of the past decade to pay for more and more social policy monuments that will become more and more unaffordable.
direction: cautious outlook for growth stocks and highly leveraged sectors due to productivity concerns and future borrowing costs

headline: CBA quizzed on investment spending surge
text:
Commonwealth Bank of Australia has been forced to boost investment spending to chase growth amid a flurry of digital competitors, raising questions about whether it is dishing out too much given ultra-low interest rates are crunching revenue.

With CBA's expense growth over the full-year rising more quickly than its income, several analysts reacted to its impressive 19.8 per cent jump in cash profit to $8.65 billion by questioning whether spending on digital projects had got ahead of itself.

The Reserve Bank's extraordinary policy settings crimped CBA's interest earnings by $500 million over the year.

But CBA chief executive Matt Comyn defended the surging investment, which leapt 26 per cent to $1.8 billion, saying it was necessary to remain competitive in an economy where more services are being delivered online as COVID-19 accelerates digital transformation across the economy.

"We think that [spending] is critical, particularly for the medium- and long-term performance of the bank," he said.

Slides in CBA's investor pack pointed to "reimagined products and services", including a variety of start-up investments and partnerships to distinguish its offering and several new brands, which are being developed in-house.

These include Unloan, a new digital home loan origination app that will be launched later this year.

With one in three Australians calling CBA their main financial institution, the bank reported double-digit net profit growth in its retail and business banking divisions, which also benefited from a steep reduction in loan impairments. This allowed it to issue a big dividend and share buyback while also lifting investment.

But banking analysts expressed concern about "negative jaws" over the full year (expenses growing faster than revenue).

CBA's operating income was up 1.7 per cent to $24.2 billion, while its operating expenses grew by 3.3 per cent to $11.4 billion, including additional customer remediation costs of $114 million. These were the numbers banking analysts jumped on during the conference call.

JPMorgan's Andrew Triggs criticised the underlying cost growth as being "stubbornly high", while Macquarie's Victor German questioned the level of investing, which is much higher than its rival banks.

Credit Suisse's Jarrod Martin asked Mr Comyn: "Revenue is pretty challenged from a margin perspective, so the simple question here is, should we expect, in the near term, that expense growth will outpace revenue growth?"

"I recognise it is a simple question. Unfortunately, I am not going to give you a simple answer," Mr Comyn replied, after analysts from Goldman Sachs and Morgan Stanley also homed in on expense growth, asking whether it was sustainable.

"We are comfortable to be investing more than peers, so long as we are investing that well," Mr Comyn said.

"We need to continue to pursue opportunities for organic growth in our franchise. We think we are well positioned and have strong competitive advantages, and we want to both persist and enhance those."

CBA said it had to spend more on staff, especially in the highly competitive technology sector, and it was recruiting more than 600 engineers. With hard border closures making it harder to attract skilled IT workers to Australia, CBA said it had established an office in India "to leverage the global talent pool".

The higher spending came in the face of a revenue squeeze, which has largely been inflicted by the Reserve Bank.

CBA's net interest margin, the difference between what it receives in interest payments from borrowers and pays out to depositors, was down 0.04 per cent to 2.03 per cent for the year.

This was dragged lower by the impact of the record low cash rate, which is sitting at 10 basis points, higher holdings of liquid assets, and competition in home loans, which is leading to higher levels of discounting.

Mr Comyn said low official rates presented "a very challenging scenario for banks all around the world" and bank investors should prepare for this to continue.

"We have tried to manage the margin as best we can ... but we are going to feel continuing pressure on the net interest market for the foreseeable future, and certainly until we start to see a rising interest rate cycle," he said.

CBA chief financial officer Alan Docherty applauded the Reserve Bank of New Zealand's indications that it would become the first economy to lift rates from record lows, describing this as a "welcome sign of progress".

But in Australia, he said margin pressures would remain and these would be exacerbated by more mortgage customers preferring lower-margin, fixed-rate home loans and more depositors switching into term products that paid higher rates.

"We know that low interest rates will continue to feed through to lower returns for our deposits and equity hedges," Mr Docherty said.

"We expect price competition will remain intense across our key lending portfolios and on the other side of balance sheet, we are likely to see our competitors bid up pricing to attract deposits."

While the RBA's Term Funding Facility had reduced wholesale funding costs, overall "there are more headwinds than tailwinds, and we will need to continue to focus on disciplined margin management to offset those pressures", he said.

Of its $1.8 billion in investment, CBA reduced the amount of risk and compliance spending to 46 per cent of that total, down from 52 per cent a year earlier, while spending on productivity and growth initiatives rose to 32 per cent, from 21 per cent.

Projects included migrating more computing to the cloud, with 43 per cent of the bank's total compute now generated from external facilities.

It also continued to invest to simplify core platforms "to support greater resilience and more rapid development of change we can roll out to customers", Mr Comyn said.

With Square bidding for Afterpay last week - which observers predict could be a precursor to a more aggressive move against the banks - CBA laid out its response plans, saying it would offer two new payments terminals, including a mobile tap-and-pay reader that looks similar to Square's offering.

As neobanks and non-bank payment providers such as Afterpay and Zip add budgeting features to their apps, CBA also pointed to numerous customers using its digital tools, including 830,000 customers engaging with its app's cash-flow tools, 450,000 users of its bill predictor, and 1.4 million claims started with a "benefits finder" service.

As for Unloan, which will launch later this year as a standalone, digital-only, direct-to-customer brand, Mr Comyn said: "We think it is going to be at least as good as anything that is on the market."

Unloan is responding to new fintech mortgage lenders such as Athena Home Loans and Tic:Toc.

Key to CBA's digital strategy is making a concerted effort to link its 800,000 business customers with its 11 million retail customers to help drive sales for the business customers.

This includes introducing a new buy now, pay later card called StepPay, which will launch later this month and has 80,000 customers pre-registered, while CBA said more than 1000 merchants had signed up for the Little Birdie retail rewards offering which will be integrated into the CBA banking app.
direction: short-term pressure on CBA shares due to margin compression and high capex

headline: ACCC to let Woolworths swallow PFD
text:
Australian Competition and Consumer Commission chairman Rod Sims says he will keep a closer eye on the $120 billion food sector after clearing the way for Woolworths to take control of PFD Food Services despite concerns about the effect on competition.

Mr Sims said the ACCC feared the deal - which will increase Woolworths' 33 per cent share of the total food and grocery market by between 1 and 3 per cent - would have a "very negative" impact on suppliers but could not come up with enough evidence it would substantially lessen competition in the $18 billion food distribution sector.

"We're not saying there won't be some harm to suppliers," Mr Sims told The Australian Financial Review yesterday.

"But the response [from market players] was so mixed we could not convince ourselves there would be a substantial lessening of competition.

"We're recognising the reality that when one of the big two retailer players gets into the wholesale market, very likely things will change," he said. "To oppose the merger you'd have to be clear about how it's going to change and have enough evidence to support that view.

"It's something we'll keep an eye on," he said. "Our concerns about the food and grocery code have been there for some time."

The ACCC has pushed for changes to the food and grocery code of conduct. It wants the code to be mandatory and to contain penalties for breaches. "We'll certainly watch and if we get evidence we could go back to the government and see if we can get a strong code rather than a weak one," Mr Sims said.

The ACCC's decision disappointed independent food service distributors and independent retailers, who said the commission had "got it wrong".

In February five organisations - the Australian Convenience and Petroleum Marketers Association, the Council of Small Business Organisations Australia, the Master Grocers Association, the Australasian Association of Convenience Stores and Independent Food Distributors Australia - urged the competition regulator to block the deal.

Their primary concerns were that the acquisition would reduce choice for consumers and some suppliers, particularly smaller suppliers, and increase costs for suppliers if PFD's trading terms were harmonised with those of Woolworths.

Due to the dominance of Woolworths and Coles, food service is an important alternative channel for food manufacturers and distributors and margins for suppliers tend to be higher than those in the supermarket channel.

"While we acknowledge the ACCC's efforts in investigating this proposed acquisition, we are incredibly disappointed by the decision to allow Woolworths to acquire PFD, one of the most significant players in the food distribution services sector," said Richard Hinson, chairman of Independent Food Distributors.

"This will ultimately cost hundreds of jobs in the food distribution sectors, many of which will be in regional Australia."

"It's a bad decision and it's going to be a significant challenge moving forward," said NAFDA Foodservice chief executive Brad Lee.

He said the ACCC did not want another "black eye" after losing several recent cases in court.

PFD purchases food products from suppliers and distributes them to businesses such as restaurants, cafes, hotels and clubs, petrol and convenience stores and institutions such as hospitals.

It has annual revenue of about $2.1 billion and speaks for about 15 per cent of the wholesale food services sector. The acquisition will make Woolworths the second-largest player in the sector after Bidfood.
direction: Woolworths shares likely to rise

headline: Power giants seek plan to avert chaos
text:
Australia's major electricity generators have backed a call by AGL Energy boss Graeme Hunt for a national plan to smooth the phase-out of coal-fired power, warning of the fallout for consumers and jobs should the energy transition fall into chaos.

Some industry leaders fear sudden failures of ageing coal-fired generators would trigger price shocks and blackouts amid the rise of cheap renewable power, which threatens to make coal power uneconomic and drive early shutdowns.

The unprecedented degree of co-operation required for such a plan would likely require authorisation by the competition regulator or explicit regulatory sanction. It would also be politically fraught, given the past reluctance of the federal and Queensland governments to acknowledge any need to plan for early coal power exits.

Mr Hunt, who runs the country's biggest coal power generator, said on Thursday a plan is needed that goes beyond the reforms proposed for the National Electricity Market to give certainty to industry, investors, consumers and others about the pathway towards the eventual shutdown of plants.

"It all plays back to this question about how do we get an orderly transition from our current make-up of 70 per cent coal-fired generation into something which is coming [to] allow us getting to carbon-neutral," he said.

"You need that kind of framework that will allow some form of orderly transition from where we are to where we want to be."

Alinta Energy CEO Jeff Dimery threw his weight behind the push.

"We broadly agree with Graeme's comments," Mr Dimery told AFR Weekend.

"Having witnessed the material impacts of significant generator exits on consumers, employees and other stakeholders, I can confidently say that having long-term transparency and planning will significantly reduce risk for those stakeholders, and the grid."

The debate comes amid escalating worries about potential breakdowns of ageing generators that could lead to what Origin Energy CEO Frank Calabria has described as a "messy" transition to low-carbon energy.

While the advance notice that needs to be given by coal plant owners of shutdowns has been lengthened since the relatively sudden shutdown of Hazelwood in Victoria in 2017 triggered price spikes, the industry has been rattled by the political battering suffered by AGL when it advised of plans to close its Liddell generator.

AGL gave notice of at least five years that it would close Liddell when it reached the age of 50 years in 2022, only to face extraordinary pressure from the Turnbull government to reverse its decision or to sell the plant to someone who would keep it open. AGL partially relented in 2019, agreeing to keep three units running through the 2022-23 summer.

Industry sources say clarity on the timing of closures is essential for owners to manage capital spending in their plants' final years.

The uncertainty around the continuing viability of the Yallourn generator was behind its owner EnergyAustralia's deal with the Victorian government in March that guaranteed its operation until 2028. But that deal has also unsettled rivals, given its secret terms and suspicions it may distort competition.

EnergyAustralia's head of markets Ross Edwards agreed on Friday there needs to be a mechanism that "provides confidence and ensures there is always adequate capacity in the system to meet consumer demand".

Mr Edwards said the existing market frameworks "need to be enhanced" so that flexible, dispatchable capacity is in the system as ageing coal plants retire.

He suggested, however, that any additional measures to smooth the phase-out of coal power should be covered as part of the Energy Security Board's process to reform the rules of the NEM.

"Ultimately the outcome everyone wants is an orderly transition to a lower emissions grid, one that ensures reliability and that can be delivered at lowest cost for customers," said Sarah McNamara, the head of the Australian Energy Council, which represents major suppliers.

"With the dramatic shifts under way in the electricity sector, it is critical that any reforms avoid disorderly closure of existing plant."

She said any specific framework "would need to be carefully considered alongside existing requirements governing coal plant closures, as well as other reform proposals."

Industry sources agree that getting consensus between federal and state governments, generators, employer groups and others for a phase-out of coal looks nigh on impossible, despite some economies such as Germany having done so.

Government and regulator backing would be critical to avoid accusations of cartel conduct.

"In the hypothetical scenario of energy generators co-ordinating when they close their coal plants, the companies involved could come to the ACCC for authorisation," said Rod Sims, chairman of the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission.

"That would likely be a complicated process of weighing up the costs and benefits of the co-ordinated conduct."

Mr Sims noted governments could also legislate a solution that would protect the companies involved from competition laws.

While setting a glide path for emissions reductions towards a set target would give industry similar goalposts to deliver on, that option appears equally problematic politically.

"We're in a world of next-best options," one source said.

With that in mind, the Energy Security Board, the top energy policy adviser to federal and state governments, has been developing workaround options to at least go some way towards increasing certainty about the future of coal plants.

The ESB's draft recommendations leaked to the media late last month include a strategic reserve of generation to ensure adequate supply, and "enhancements to existing generator exit mechanisms" to give more certainty on the future availability of capacity.

They also suggest payments for dispatchable generators such as coal and gas plants for having capacity available, rather than just for the power they produce.
direction: bearish for coal generators and utilities due to transition uncertainty

headline: Pay link to metrics is the way to go
text:
At Charter Hall, the staff aren't paid bonuses only for improving the property owner and fund manager's financial performance.

Part of their bonus is dependent on helping the company meet its environmental, social and governance goals.

In fact, employees aren't even eligible to earn a bonus if they don't adhere to the company's values and complete its risk and compliance training, regardless of how well they have performed in other metrics.

"You cannot get a bonus if you haven't lined up behaviourally," says Charter Hall chief experience officer Natalie Devlin.

Beyond that, executives have to meet key performance indicators for ESG requirements that support the company's aims and are specific to the individual's role.

For instance, building managers might have a metric on improving the energy efficiency of their buildings to help Charter Hall meet its objective of net zero emissions by 2030.

In the governance metric, other staff will be required to meet objectives around modern slavery and human rights in the company's supply chain.

The company also supports the community by giving 1 per cent of its space, 1 per cent of its time and 1 per cent of the profits from its funds as an investment.

This year, it is supporting vulnerable young Australians, whose plight has been made worse by the COVID-19 pandemic.

"In our community investment, we've got targets that people meet around volunteering and ownership by people within certain roles," Devlin says.

Every executive has at least one ESG metric in the KPIs, and it is proving effective in driving behavioural change, she says.

"I feel like that's the most important lever - if you don't measure it, it doesn't get done. That's a general HR philosophy - people will focus on what they are being pointed to.

"So we always use our scorecard every year to focus on what our business priorities are," Devlin says.

Charter Hall is one of an increasing number of Australian companies building ESG measures into their remuneration structure as a result of increasing focus on ESG performance from regulators and investors.

Research by global governance firm Guerdon Associates shows that in Australia about 30 per cent of executive pay in the ASX-300 companies is linked to ESG performance, significantly more than the global average of 20 per cent for advanced economies.

"It's really taken off like a rocket," says Michael Robinson, an Australian-based director of Guerdon. "We were quite surprised by how advanced Australia was relative to the rest of the world."

Among the ASX-100 companies in Australia, the best-performing sector for ESG-linked bonuses is utilities. All companies in that sector adopt ESG Plus metrics in their incentive structures, the Guerdon research shows.

Next is the financial sector, with 94 per cent of ASX-100 companies adopting ESG Plus metrics in their bonus structures, followed by industrials on 92 per cent, and real estate on 90 per cent.

The communication services, healthcare and tech sectors lag, with an adoption rate of only 50 per cent.

All up, 82 per cent of ASX-100 companies have adopted ESG Plus metrics in their incentives, compared with just 67 per cent globally. AFR
direction: Charter Hall shares supported by strong ESG governance and sustainable growth narrative
