headline: ASX climbs 0.2pc as resources stocks soar
text:
Australian shares edged higher on the penultimate day of earnings season, with the market lifted by a bumper result from Fortescue Metals Group and a strong lead from Wall Street following the Jackson Hole Symposium.

The S&P/ASX 200 added 16.2 points, or 0.2 per cent, to finish yesterday's session at 7504.5. The broader All Ordinaries Index rose 28.5 points, or 0.4 per cent, to 7788.6.

Global markets rallied following Federal Reserve chairman Jerome Powell maintaining ambiguity over when it will taper its bond purchases this year.

"The dovish undertones from Powell's speech was music to equity markets' ears - quantitative easing tapering is coming soon, but the punch bowl will remain stimulated with no rate hikes within sight," said National Australia Bank economists.

This optimism reached the Australian market with the benchmark opening 0.5 per cent higher before losing some steam later in the session.

Miners and energy rallied with rising commodity prices.

Fortescue surged 6.6 per cent to $21.32 after reporting a record profit of $US10.3 billion and a $2.11 final dividend.

Iron ore miners rose in tandem as the price of the steel-making commodity climbed 3 per cent to $US157.55 a tonne. BHP advanced 2.5 per cent to $45.83; Rio Tinto jumped 3.1 per cent to $133.12; Mineral Resources added 2.7 per cent to $53.53; and Champion Iron firmed 2.8 per cent to $5.84.

Other miners to post solid gains included Pilbara Minerals, up 6.3 per cent to $2.20; South32 up 6.2 per cent to $3.08; Alumina 5.7 per cent to $1.77; Orocobre added 5.4 per cent to $8.76; Chalice Mining 4.4 per cent to $6.87; OZ Minerals 3.7 per cent to $23.27; and Lynas 3.7 per cent to $6.66.

A strong rise in oil prices supported the energy sector: Santos rose 2.4 per cent to $6.11; Viva Energy climbed 1 per cent to $2.11; Oil Search added 1.1 per cent to $3.75; and Worley Parsons jumped 3.6 per cent to $10.89.

Funeral parlour and cemetery operator InvoCare led the market, soaring 8.7 per cent to $12.15 on news that it had returned to profit, with statutory profit after tax of $44 million for the six months to June 30, a turnaround from the $18 million loss reported in the prior corresponding period.

Retailer Temple & Webster announced a record year of revenue and profit, and announced it continues to experience strong tailwinds due to the adoption of online shopping. The stock surged 10.6 per cent to $14.34.

Adore Beauty advanced 3.3 per cent to $4.97 on news it has delivered a maiden $845,000 net profit for the year following its $1.3 million loss in 2020.

Altium was the session's biggest laggard, plunging 14.3 per cent to $29.85 after posting an unaudited result for the financial year that included a 246.4 per cent rise in profit and a 6.4 per cent lift in revenue.

The software company blamed a shortage of auditors for its results delay.

Nuix dived 10.8 per cent to $2.56 after the embattled tech company revealed it swung to a loss in the 2021 financial year. The stock had been trading 10 per cent higher earlier in the session.

"Investors took favourable pricing in the morning session to bail out while the going was good, from there it was a landslide, and with a third of Nuix's shares coming out of escrow next Monday, they could be heading towards even more pain," said the CEO of Openmarkets Group, Ivan Tchourilov.

Healthcare provider Healius fell 7.4 per cent to $4.67 despite reporting a 179 per cent rise in net profit and telling the market it had experienced a "positive start" to the 2022 financial year.

The big four banks lost ground; Commonwealth Bank tumbled 1.1 per cent to $100.42, NAB dropped 0.4 per cent to $27.54, Westpac dipped 0.7 per cent to $25.80 and ANZ fell 1.1 per cent to $28.

Travel stocks also endured a tough session as NSW recorded 1290 new COVID-19 cases. Flight Centre dropped 1.4 per cent to $16.65; Qantas declined 2.1 per cent to $5.04; Corporate Travel Management fell 2.8 per cent to $22.74; Webjet tumbled 0.5 per cent to $5.50; and Sydney Airport dipped 0.6 per cent to $7.85.
direction: bullish for resources and miners

headline: New airline Bonza to launch in early 2022
text:
A US investment firm with over $US3 billion ($4.1 billion) in managed assets will bankroll the latest challenge to Australia's aviation market, an independent low-cost carrier called Bonza, which aims to take off early next year.

Founded by former Virgin Blue executive Tim Jordan, the new airline will fly Boeing 737-8 jets leased from linchpin investor 777 Partners. It will forgo the lucrative "golden triangle" routes between Sydney, Brisbane and Melbourne for underserved leisure locations in hopes of stimulating new demand.

Mr Jordan said this was a more sustainable business model than taking on established players like Qantas, Virgin Australia and Regional Express on their home turf.

"Those markets are very competitive already. We are about doing something different," he said. "In Kazakhstan, domestic aviation has grown 37 per cent in the first half of 2021 versus 2019. It is one of the fastest growing markets in the world because they are stimulating new demand that didn't exist prior."

It represents the second major challenge to the traditional airline duopoly since Virgin threatened to collapse at the start of the pandemic. Regional airline Rex's launched domestic capital city flights earlier in 2021.

Mr Jordan, who has 30 years of industry experience as a consultant and an airline executive, would not say if he saw Rex as Bonza's main rival given the former's regional focus.

He said Bonza would not be a "me too" airline, and would keep costs down by omitting creature comforts favoured by business travellers such as a frequent flyer scheme or airport lounges. Mr Jordan and 777 Partners targeted Australia as it is the only significant air travel market without an independent low-cost airline - Qantas owns Jetstar - and the pair have scheduled Bonza's take-off for early 2022.

"A lot of today's issues should be in the rearview mirror by then. It is a great time to start an airline; there is pent-up demand, and we can also leverage good opportunities in the aviation market like cheap jets," Mr Jordan said.

777 Partners is an experienced aviation investor, having poured cash into Canadian low-cost carrier Flair and a regional airline alliance in the Asia-Pacific called Value Alliance.

777 managing partner Josh Wander said Australia was a "huge potential" in the aviation market.

"There is a huge opportunity to both do good and do well by democratising air travel through lower costs," Mr Wander said. "We want to increase consumer choice and make travel more affordable and more accessible for all Australians."
direction: bearish for Qantas and Virgin Australia

headline: Stashed trillions to trigger spree
text:
London | Consumers around the world have stockpiled an extra $US5.4 trillion ($7 trillion) of savings since the coronavirus pandemic began and are becoming increasingly confident about the economic outlook, paving the way for a strong rebound in spending as businesses reopen.

Households around the globe accumulated the excess - defined as the additional savings compared with the 2019 spending pattern and equating to more than 6 per cent of global gross domestic product - by the end of the first quarter of this year, according to estimates by credit rating agency Moody's.

And booming global consumer confidence suggests shoppers will be willing to spend again as soon as shops, bars and restaurants reopen when restrictions to control the spread of the virus are eased. In the first quarter of this year, the Conference Board global consumer confidence index hit its highest level since records began in 2005, with significant uplifts in all regions of the world.

Mark Zandi, chief economist at Moody's Analytics, said: "The combination of an unleashing of significant pent-up demand and overflowing excess saving will drive a surge in consumer spending across the globe as countries approach herd immunity and open up."

If consumers spend about a third of their excess savings, they would boost global output by just over 2 percentage points both this year and next, Moody's estimated.

Despite the global economy last year suffering its largest fall in output in modern history, household incomes have largely been protected by unprecedented government stimulus schemes in most advanced economies. Consumers also reduced spending in the face of high uncertainty about jobs and incomes, and because many service businesses were shut or restricted.

As a result, in 2020 household saving rates in many advanced economies reached their highest levels this century, according to OECD data.

Mr Zandi said excess saving was highest in developed economies, in particular North America and Europe where lockdowns have been widely implemented and government spending has been high.

In the US alone, households have piled up more than $US2 trillion in extra savings, Moody's estimated. That is before the giant transfers from US President Joe Biden's $US1.9 trillion stimulus program kick in. Together, they are enough to potentially fuel an "extended consumption splurge", said Krishna Guha, economist at the investment banking adviser Evercore ISI.

Silvia Ardagna, economist at Barclays, expects "a fairly rapid acceleration in household spending" in the US and "to lesser extent" in the UK.

A number of Middle Eastern countries where government support has been generous also have significant excess savings, while in Asia, accumulated excess savings were lower than in other regions as the pandemic has been contained and the impact on household behaviour was less pronounced.

In South America and eastern Europe, savings were lower as a result of the heavy hit from the pandemic and less government support.

However, the impact of the pandemic has been highly unequal and savings have been largely accumulated by richer households in all regions.

The Morning Consult consumer confidence index showed overall steady improvements between January and April across 15 large economies, but a larger share of lower-income households reported their financial conditions had worsened compared with a year ago.

More than a third of richer households in many countries, including Australia, said now was a good time to make big purchases, but that was not the case for poorer households, data from Morning Consult showed.

Jan Hatzius, economist at Goldman Sachs, estimated that nearly two-thirds of US excess savings were held by the richest 40 per cent of the population and suggested this could hold back the scale of the boost because "high-income households will hold [rather than spend] the bulk of excess savings".

Adam Slater, lead economist at Oxford Economics, said: "If excess savings are mostly held by wealthier households and these are treated as a wealth increase rather than an income addition, we would expect a much lower level of [additional] spending."
direction: Bullish for consumer discretionary and retail sectors

headline: Miners tumble in bruising day for blue chips
text:
The weakening price of iron ore triggered a rapid sell-off in BHP, Rio Tinto and Fortescue Metals Group yesterday, dragging Australian shares to their longest run of daily losses since October.

The trio of global miners are each among the world's top four iron ore producers and the combined declines wiped tens of billions of dollars in market value from the three.

BHP was hit hardest, falling 6.4 per cent to $44.67, its lowest share price since April. The stock is down 15.4 per cent this week after unveiling bumper profits and a plan to consolidate its sharemarket listing in Australia.

Fortescue shed 6.2 per cent to $20.13, its worst day since January, while Rio Tinto dropped 5.7 per cent to $107.17, its lowest price since December.

The materials sector, which is dominated by the mining giants, closed down 3.7 per cent, its worst showing since May last year and by far the poorest performer among the sharemarket sectors yesterday.

Materials companies have fallen 10.6 per cent since a record high earlier this month, pushing the sector into a correction, which is defined by a drop of a 10th from a peak.

The action wiped 37.5 points from the S&P/ASX 200, dragging the benchmark down 0.5 per cent to 7464.6 for its fourth consecutive daily decline. This marks the first time since October the benchmark has fallen in four sessions on the trot. The broader S&P All Ordinaries benchmark fell by the same margin, shedding 35 points to 7735.3.

The drop for Australia's global miners follows a plunge in the price of iron ore. The commodity fell 4.6 per cent to below $US160 per tonne after trading near its record at $US220 a tonne a month ago. Global demand for steel, driven by China and helped along by construction and housing booms around the world, has driven the price higher this year despite many analysts anticipating an eventual fall below $US100 a tonne.

The recent decline has accelerated on concerns about steel output restrictions in China, according to Vivek Dhar, mining and energy commodities analyst for CBA. "Prices have now declined 31 per cent from July 15 to August 18, signalling just how quickly fortunes have turned for the steel-making ingredient," he said.

"Steel mills in China are tolerating lower grade ores with higher impurities as their objective is now cost minimisation over maximising productivity," Mr Dhar said.

Healthcare shares led the market with a 2 per cent gain yesterday powered by a 3.2 per cent advance for CSL to $302.83, after the company reported a 13 per cent jump in net profit on Wednesday to $3.3 billion.

Redbubble, which operates an e-commerce platform, was another standout and the best performer on yesterday. Shares in the Melbourne-based tech group jumped by nearly a fifth to $3.64, after growing profits to $52.7 million in the financial year to June up from about $5 million in the prior period.

Losses for blue chips this week have knocked the market from a peak reached on Friday and come in the middle of corporate earnings season for the financial year that ended in June.

Investors were primed for rocketing profits following a robust first-half earnings season earlier in the year and companies have largely delivered.

But the high expectations entering the reporting season have pushed up stocks to record highs, creating a fragile market that has edged lower in the past week with some of the year's best performing sectors, such as financials, starting to slip. The market also digested mixed economic news as the prolonged COVID-19 lockdown in Sydney weighs on growth and has stoked concerns the country will be plunged into a double-dip recession.

The jobless rate for July fell to 4.6 per cent, the lowest level since 2008. But the decline reflected the NSW lockdown, which has placed more people beyond the employment statistics, artificially skewing the figures.

The government anticipates the Australian economy will contract by 2 per cent in the September quarter, according to comments from Treasurer Josh Frydenberg.
direction: bearish for miners and materials sector

headline: Lithium boom 2.0 drives $4b mine merger
text:
This year's lithium price boom will be more durable than the last and can withstand an acceleration of supply, according to the two Australian listed companies that struck a $4 billion battery minerals merger yesterday.

The combination of Orocobre and Galaxy Resources will create the world's fifth-biggest lithium producer by market value and one that is more diversified by product and geography, with ambitions of securing a place in the ASX100.

Brisbane-based Orocobre and Perth-based Galaxy produce lithium in Argentina and Western Australia respectively and were the pioneers of the locally-listed lithium sector, which is split between those that extract the commodity from South American groundwater and those that extract it from hard Australian rock.

Both companies rely on a single asset for their revenue despite years of work to develop new projects.

The merged entity will not only enjoy the security of having two revenue generating assets that produce different lithium products in separate jurisdictions, but it will also boast a long list of growth projects, none of which are in Australia.

Demand for lithium is increasingly being driven by makers of modern batteries for use in a range of devices, but particularly electric vehicles.

The addition of new supply is a sensitive topic in the lithium industry after a surge in Australian mine output in 2017 and 2018 far exceeded global demand and killed the four-year lithium boom that had raged since 2014.

A severe price downturn after 2018 pushed both Galaxy and Orocobre into the red in fiscal 2020, but their fortunes have rebounded recently with prices for the lithium carbonate that Orocobre produces in Argentina almost doubling over the past nine months, while Galaxy has also seen better prices for the lithium-rich spodumene concentrate it sells from WA.

Orocobre boss Martin Perez De Solay will lead the combined entity if the merger is approved by shareholders, and vowed to grow production by "accelerating" growth projects.

"Based on today's forecasts it is full speed ahead," he said of those growth projects in Argentina and Canada.

Big companies like BHP have predicted that rapid lithium supply growth will put a cap on prices and that view has convinced the mining giant to focus on other "future facing" commodities such as copper and nickel.

But Galaxy chief executive Simon Hay, who previously worked for BHP, said the surge in lithium prices over the past nine months was more sustainable than the boom that occurred between 2014 and 2018.

"This time around the demand has a lot more legs, it is a lot more durable," he told The Australian Financial Review.

"Previously, China spoke for three quarters of demand and growth. Now we see Europe is outpacing China in growth and in gross numbers of tonnage of lithium, and North America is just emerging, so you will have three major growth fronts, not just one. So that is why we are really confident in the demand projections".

Mr Hay - who will be president of international business for the merged entity - said there was no intention to push the market into oversupply again.

"A lot of the producers, and Orocobre and Galaxy are in this club, are definitely rational. We have both taken tonnes offline when we needed to and most of the majors are doing the same, so I think that helps in stabilising the lithium industry," he said.

Electric vehicle sales were weak through 2019 and early 2020, but have roared back to life with extremely strong sales in Europe and China over the past seven months.

"The difference this time is the strength of the demand," said Mr Perez De Solay.

Investors embraced the notion that a merger would better equip the companies to survive and deliver on their growth ambitions, with Orocobre shares rising more than 5 per cent and Galaxy shares up more than 6 per cent.

"Our initial view of the proposed merger is positive, with the transaction, if completed, delivering significant operating, marketing and financial synergies," Canaccord Genuity analyst Reg Spencer said.

"Moreover, the combined entity will have materially increased liquidity and improved access to capital, which we see as a major enabler to delivering accelerated growth plans."

Galaxy shareholders will get 0.569 Orocobre shares for every Galaxy share they own under the all-scrip offer, which has been endorsed by the boards of both companies.

Galaxy's Mt Cattlin mine in WA will reach the end of its working life in about five years and the merged entity will cease operating on Australian soil at that time, although Mr Perez De Solay pledged to retain a Perth office to support the merged company's headquarters in Buenos Aires.

It long-term future is focused on the Americas rather than Australia, with Orocobre's Olaroz lithium operation in Argentina soon to be joined by the $US153 million ($187 million) Sal de Vida lithium project that Galaxy has under construction in Argentina.

Both companies expressed a desire to accelerate Sal de Vida, suggesting something more akin to the bigger, $US474 million version of the project that was envisaged in 2018 could yet be fast-tracked.

The companies also expressed a desire to accelerate studies into the $US244 million James Bay lithium project in Canada, particularly over where to locate a downstream processing infrastructure.

Producing lithium in Canada and Argentina means the merged entity will be more focused on North American customers, suggesting a pivot from the current situation where Chinese customers buy 100 per cent of Galaxy's product and more than half of Orocobre's product.

Like rare earths producer Lynas, Orocobre has strong links to Japanese lenders and corporate partners such as Toyoto Tsusho, and those bonds will only be enhanced once Orocobre's majority owned lithium hydroxide processing plant is completed in Japan's Fukushima province next year.

That plant will turn the lithium carbonate from Orocobre's Argentinian asset into lithium hydroxide, which is increasingly desired by battery manufacturers because it offers greater energy storage than lithium carbonate.

Mr Spencer said the big multinational lithium producers, such as US company Albemarle, tended to attract a premium from investors and that may become available to the entity that emerges from a combination of Orocobre and Galaxy.

"We think the enlarged scale of the combined entity could attract significantly higher valuation multiples, noting larger cap peers trade on 15 to 20 times EBITDA [earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation]," he said.

The merger continues a period of rapid consolidation in the local sector, with IGO Limited buying a stake in Australia's best hard rock lithium mine at Greenbushes as well as a downstream processing hub south of Perth.

Wesfarmers announced in February that it would go ahead with development of a new lithium mine and processing hub in WA, while incumbent exporter Pilbara Minerals took ownership of a neighbouring mine that was previously owned by Altura Mining.

Pilbara Minerals closed almost 2 per cent higher yesterday.
direction: bullish for lithium miners and battery materials sector

headline: Innovation hub builds on robust Parramatta market
text:
Parramatta's construction boom took another step forward yesterday with a topping-out ceremony for the new $300 million Engineering Innovation Hub building - a partnership between the Charter Hall Group and Western Sydney University.

As foundation tenant, Western Sydney University will be leasing up to 14,000 square metres of the 29,000sq m development, which is on track for completion during the third quarter of this year.

Carmel Hourigan, Charter Hall Office CEO, said the project is being strongly marketed to other prospective tenants.

Ms Hourigan is confident it will lease well even though Parramatta office vacancy rates have doubled to 6.4 per cent in the past 12 months.

Despite the rising vacancy rate, Parramatta remains the strongest office market in NSW, with demand coming from high-quality tenants, including NAB, QBE, Westpac and the Commonwealth Bank.

Vanessa Rader, head of research at Ray White, said in the six months to December 31, Parramatta's net absorption rate increased to 28,673sq m.

"There's good demand for quality assets," Ms Rader said, adding that the increase in office vacancy rates is entirely due to new stock coming onto the market, not lack of demand.

Ross Grove, Western Sydney regional director for the Property Council of Australia, said Parramatta has more office space leased than it did before the pandemic "and we have more people taking up office space than leaving".

"Parramatta still has the lowest vacancy rate of any commercial centre in NSW and sits well below the nationwide average, which saw a rise from 9.6 per cent to 11.7 per cent," he said.

"For all that is going on, Parramatta is still in a strong position." Ms Hourigan said the building will feature state-of-the-art commercial and education facilities within a smart building design.

Features included smartphone lift-call capability, touchless smart bathroom amenities, cyber security integration and electric vehicle charging facilities.

Western Sydney University chancellor Barney Glover said the Engineering Innovation Hub will house a collaborative engineering program in partnership with the University of NSW.

Courses in architecture, industrial design and entrepreneurship will also be offered.
direction: bullish for Charter Hall Group and NSW office REITs

headline: Westpac sells life arm to TAL in $900m deal
text:
Westpac has announced it will sell its Australian life insurance business to TAL Dai-ichi Life Australia in a deal worth $900 million to the bank.

Under a 20-year deal, Westpac will cease manufacturing life insurance but continue to distribute products from TAL to clients. Westpac shares rose 1 per cent at the open in line with rival banks.

The bank will book a $1.3 billion accounting loss while the transaction will add about 12 basis points to its common equity tier 1 (CET1) ratio. The deal is expected to be completed in the second half of next year.

The transaction was conducted at a multiple of 0.96 times embedded value and includes ongoing payments to Westpac. Westpac will provide TAL with a combination of provisions, warranties and indemnities.

Westpac's group chief executive specialist businesses and group strategy, Jason Yetton, said life insurance was an important product for the bank's customers and he was happy to partner with TAL in this way.

"TAL already offers insurance products to more than 4.5 million Australians and is well placed to help Westpac's customers protect the people they love", Mr Yetton said in a statement.

"This transaction is another step in simplifying the bank while continuing to help customers with their life insurance needs by partnering with TAL."

TAL group CEO Brett Clark said the purchase showed Dai-ichi Life's commitment to the Australian life insurance market and would provide a pipeline for growth in the years ahead.

"Westpac is Australia's first bank and oldest company, and we are delighted to be partnering with one of Australia's largest and most significant companies. Both TAL and Westpac share a strong belief in the value of life insurance in the community," Mr Clark said in a statement.

The transaction is the latest in a string of asset sales from the bank that included its general insurance arm to Allianz for $725 million, its NZ life business to Fidelity Life for $373 million and its vehicle finance business to Cerberus Capital for an undisclosed amount.

Evans and Partners analyst Matthew Wilson described the 0.96 embedded value as soft but understandable given the market conditions.

"This is the last cab off the rank," Mr Wilson said in a flash note to clients.

"Only one more asset to sell - BT/Panorama which has been delayed until FY22 [financial year 2022] due to required investment spend to separate the asset."

The sale of BT's Panorama has been further complicated by a week-long outage that has enraged financial planners and their clients. The service was finally restored on Sunday with a spokesman sheeting the issues back to the user interface.
direction: Westpac shares and banking sector

headline: Australia 'can't avoid methane deal effects'
text:
The Biden administration's global initiative to cut methane emissions by 30 per cent by the end of the decade will affect Australian industry despite Prime Minister Scott Morrison refusing sign onto the pledge, say investor groups and analysts.

But they caution the initiative, endorsed by more than 100 other countries, will wash through the Australian economy inefficiently, meaning industry will be forced to act, but less consistently and productively than might have been possible via a nationally adopted approach.

Australia is the world's fifth-largest methane emitter not to sign up to the agreement, according to 2020 data from Climate Watch. The rebel pack is led by China, Russia, India and Iran.

Emma Herd, partner of climate change and sustainability at EY, rejected the idea that Australia could gain a competitive advantage by opting out of the pledge, telling The Australian Financial Review the global initiative would shape Australian industry through trading relationships, international standards and access to capital.

"Demand dynamics ... are complex. Global commitments like these will impact Australian business as much as our own domestic targets.

"We know that the actions our trading partners are taking to decarbonise will flow through to economic and financial implications for Australian industry," she said.

Agriculture is by far the largest emitter of methane in Australia, according to Department of Industry data, followed by the fugitive emissions from coal mining, land clearing, food waste and sewage, and the oil and gas sectors.

The challenge for agriculture is that it is one of the sectors most vulnerable to climate change yet it faces difficulty in reducing emissions which mostly come from grazing cattle and sheep.

Grattan institute's Tony Wood said that "materially cutting those emissions is simply not currently possible".

The Biden administration yesterday made its move to immediately propose methane reduction regulations targeted at the US oil and gas industry via the Environmental Protection Agency.

A Woodside Petroleum spokeswoman said the major LNG exporter backed global action on methane but would not be drawn on whether the Morrison government should have signed the pledge.

Morné Engelbrecht, acting chief executive officer of Beach Energy, a major domestic gas producer that is also poised to start LNG exports, said the company "fully accepts" that reducing emissions, including methane, is a priority but wouldn't be drawn on whether he supported the government's move.

The Investor Group on Climate Change, which represents members with $2 trillion under management, says if agriculture, coal and oil and gas companies fail to cut methane emissions they may still face investor pressure and risk forcing up the cost of borrowing.
direction: bearish for agriculture and coal sectors
pressure on rate-sensitive sectors like banks and property due to rising rate expectations

headline: Mall owners eye return of cabin-fever consumers
text:
The strength of the malls was the surprise of the property results.

Scentre Group, the owner and manager of Westfield Living Centres, is so confident of a post-lockdown bounce in visitors, sales and income that chief executive Peter Allen has confirmed guidance for the current half - provided the lockdowns are "substantially eased" in time for the November, December and January sales.

The outlook, coupled with the fact that the value of its centres didn't fall but actually rose a tad, sent Scentre securities up 6 per cent on the day of the results, and another 2 per cent yesterday.

Jarden analyst Lou Pirenc calls Scentre the "top opening trade in the sector", upgrading his target price to $3.45 - compared with yesterday's close of $2.78.

Pirenc warns that a reopening in late October "could still prove optimistic" but nevertheless sees potential for a "significant earnings recovery" in Scentre's 2022 (calendar) results; for increased confidence in the underlying valuation, currently $3.64 a security, not counting the very substantial value of the Westfield management; and for a rerating similar to global mall peers.

Several other analysts also upgraded Scentre, though less enthusiastically than Pirenc.

However, Macquarie Research maintained its underperform rating in part due to the $4.1 billion of subordinated hybrid notes issued last year at the onset of COVID-19, to ensure liquidity. The hybrids are not counted for bank or bond covenants, but Macquarie Research does include them when estimating Scentre's real gearing at around 40 per cent.

Long-term investors will hope Pirenc is right. Remember Scentre was worth more than $5 a security in 2016, dropped under $1.50 in the panic, and, as recently as 2019, paid a distribution of 22.6¢ a security, not the 14¢ to which it has committed this year.

Like all mall stocks, Scentre has been rerated in the face of the long-term challenge of online retail - a challenge which has further to go if the penetration in the UK and USA is a guide.

Online was having an impact before the pandemic; it was boosted by the lockdowns; and it will continue to erode bricks-and-mortar retailing long after any post-lockdown bounce turns into a new normality.

All mall owners are rising to that omnichannel retail challenge.

Vicinity Centres plans mixed-use development on its sites; GPT Group is increasing its exposure to logistics real estate and developing excess land around key malls; Stockland foreshadows an increased weighting to its logistics, life science and technology pipeline.

Scentre's Allen, a veteran Westfield executive, aims to do what online retail cannot; give cabin-fevered consumers a place to go.

For 20 million Australians and New Zealanders living within easy distance of a Westfield, Allen wants his "living centres" to become their "third place" after home and work. Already near half of Scentre's space is devoted to uses that cannot be bought online.

Today's graphs, from Scentre, show the bounce that followed the lockdowns over the past year. Retail sales in the June half were actually higher than those in pre-pandemic 2019, excluding COVID-crippled operations such as cinemas and travel agents.

All the listed mall operations reported improved performance in the June half, though all bar Scentre withheld guidance.

GPT reported a "robust recovery" in retail sales. More than 100 per cent of billings were collected, helping to make up for last year's rent deferrals. More leasing deals were done than in any half since 2013, delivering lower vacancy, fewer holdovers, and reduced leasing spreads but still with the all-important fixed rental increases.

US shopping mall giant Simon Property Group also enjoyed an increase in shopper traffic, sales, and leasing activity in its first and second quarters - enough to boost full-year guidance and justify the doubling of the stock price since last October.

At the moment the mall managers in NSW and Victoria have to negotiate the reintroduced code of conduct, which mandates the waiving and deferral of rent for small to medium businesses in proportion to the retailer's lost income.

Australian Retailers Association chief executive Paul Zahra says the measure has "saved the majority of small-business owners".

Mall owners and managers are not happy with the code - not with its fairness nor the way its constrains negotiations with bigger business. But they are looking to October and stressing the importance - to state and federal governments - of relaxing restrictions as soon as possible.

Zahra says the bounce following the lifting of restrictions will vary by sector with many small businesses so hard hit that they may not reopen at all.

His other caveat is CBD retailing, dependent on international tourists, foreign students and flexing office workers, will take "months or years to recover". Simon Fonteyn, the managing director of the largest online provider of leasing data in Australia, LeaseInfo, says that if lockdowns are lifted in time for the November to January trading - which accounts for 60 to 70 per cent of many annual turnovers - retailers should be OK.

"But that is a big if," he warns. "It depends on how the restrictions are lifted and how comfortable people are in shopping out.

"If not, it will be a lot harder for retailers to get through the next six months ... I think a lot of people are considering their options."

Nevertheless, GPT director Bob Johnston expects a "recovery in retail sales following the lifting of restrictions" based on robust jobs growth, house price appreciation, record low interest rates and high levels of household savings.

In the medium term, much is expected from what Latitude chief executive Ahmed Fahour calls "revenge spending". In the longer term, it might be signs of stabilisation after years of erosion by e-commerce that are really encouraging. Of course the malls were not the only theme of reporting season. The strength of industrial and logistics was confirmed as was the power of successful funds management, in Charter Hall Group's surge.

Robert Harley is a former property editor of The Australian Financial Review and is at rob@rharley.co.au. The Harley family holds Scentre securities.
direction: bullish for Scentre Group and retail property sector

headline: CSR boss says home building timelines blowing out by up to a year
text:
The chief executive of building products group CSR Ltd says the time it takes for new houses to be constructed is getting longer because of disruptions in supply chains, labour shortages and cost pressures, as she also foreshadows continued price increases across many products.

Julie Coates says construction timelines are continuing to lengthen and are now six to 12 months longer for the construction of a new home, once the application process and building on site are combined.

The company is in a good position to keep capitalising on robust orders for its products, which include PGH bricks, Monier roofing, Gyprock plasterboard and Bradford insulation, and has been implementing price increases amid strong demand from customers.

"We will continue to do that where we need to," Ms Coates said, adding that the idea was to try to recover rising input costs after absorbing as much as possible internally. Noting that the group did not lift prices in 2020, she said: "There was an element of catch-up."

CSR lifted its first-half dividend payout by 59 per cent to 13.5¢ per share after producing a strong net profit after tax for the six months ended September 30, which was up 167 per cent to $157 million. It was bumped up by a $70 million one-off gain through carry-forward tax losses stemming from the 2019 sale of the Viridian glass business.

The core business made strong headway despite the disruptions from lockdowns in Sydney and Melbourne. Net profit after tax before one-offs was up 30 per cent to $86.6 million. CSR shares gained 3.8 per cent to $6.23 in late trading on the ASX yesterday.

CSR's building products division lifted earnings before interest and tax by 25 per cent to $121 million, spurred by robust demand in the detached housing market after a flood of applications from owners using the federal government's HomeBuilder scheme.

But the apartment market slowed. Ms Coates said the broader building industry was in solid shape despite the temporary shutdowns of construction. "The industry as a whole is performing pretty well," she said.

But a shortage of tradespeople was causing headaches across the industry, particularly in bricklaying. The absence of international migration had been a big handbrake. "That's a very important source of labour," she said.

CSR also has a 25 per cent stake in the Tomago aluminium smelter in NSW, which accounts for about 12 per cent of the state's electricity consumption.

The aluminium division of CSR also notched up improvements as prices increased and it limited the downside through hedging positions. The EBIT from aluminium rose to $18.3 million from $6.2 million a year earlier. The business is 92 per cent hedged for the rest of 2021-22.

Ms Coates said CSR would keep unlocking extra value from its property portfolio. She expects EBIT from property to reach $34 million in 2021-22, which includes the proceeds of the next stage of the Horsley Park project in western Sydney.

She said the pandemic added complexity to the operations, but the company was able to navigate through it.

Ms Coates has been chief executive for two years. She replaced Rob Sindel, who had run the company since 2011. Mr Sindel is now the lead independent director in the boardroom at rival Boral.

CSR shares have risen from $5.23 in early July. The stock was sitting above $6 in mid-June after a previous climb from $4.63 in early November 2020.

Elsewhere in the construction materials sector, cement group Adbri announced it had acquired Queensland-based Zanows' Concrete & Quarries for $58 million.

It operates two concrete plants, a sand and gravel quarry, and a hard rock quarry, and supplies the south-east Queensland market.
direction: CSR shares higher
bond prices fall as yields rise across the curve

headline: Wild swings no deterrent for young crypto investors
text:
The wild swings in cryptocurrency markets might be too severe for most investors, but research from trading platform eToro suggests Millennial and Gen Z market participants are unfazed.

Its survey of 6000 investors globally (in which 500 Australians participated) found 38 per cent of respondents aged 18 to 34 invested in crypto assets, compared with 21 per cent of respondents aged 35 to 44, 10 per cent of those aged 45 to 54 and just 2 per cent of the over-55s.

An additional 35 per cent in the Millennial/Generation Z demographic intend to invest in crypto over the next 12 months. By contrast, 22 per cent of investors aged 35 to 44 said the same, while 10 per cent of those aged 45 to 54 and 4 per cent of those 55 and older intend to deploy funds to the volatile asset class.

Almost half (44 per cent) of the age cohort believe bitcoin will be the best investment opportunity over the last quarter of 2021.

The finding is particularly interesting given the survey was conducted in the last two weeks of September when bitcoin's price took a dive, falling from $US47,879 on the 17th to $41,587 on the 30th (with, naturally, some violent ups and downs in between).

Similarly, 22 per cent said ether - the digital token linked to the ethereum blockchain - would provide the best returns of any asset class over the period. Ether's price fell from $US3280 in mid-August to $US2838 in mid-September just before the research was conducted. It then spiked again to reach $4263 per coin early last month and has risen bumpily since.

Thirteen per cent of investors in the 18 to 34 category anticipate meme currency dogecoin will be the best investment looking forward (compared with 1 per cent of over-55s). In late September when the survey was conducted, it was trading at levels 70 per cent lower than its May 2021 peak. But, you'd have to imagine at least a portion of the dogecoin bulls were selecting it in jest - the coin was started as an inside joke.

Granted, the research is based on a small sample size. But it provides at least some insight into the bullishness of young investors on the crypto market - and its two biggest assets by market capitalisation in particular.

eToro Australian managing director Robert Francis said the finding lined up with trading activity on the platform, which suggests Millennial users have enduring demand for crypto assets.

"Not only are they taking the steps to educate themselves about the crypto industry as a whole, but they are also doing their due diligence by researching the individual crypto assets they want to invest in, keeping up to date with the news and diversifying their portfolios with a range of other asset classes in order to hedge against risks such as inflation," Mr Francis said.

"We are excited to see these young investors flourish and really start to shift crypto beyond a store of value by taking advantage of the endless opportunities that these emerging assets present."

Commonwealth Bank has announced a landmark move to add cryptocurrency trading functionality to its banking app - a move reported to be motivated at least partly by demand from younger customers - and a flurry of crypto-themed equity funds have listed on local sharemarkets.
direction: bullish for crypto assets and related equities

headline: Property boom is turning Greens' birthplace Liberal
text:
They are few, they are poorly educated, they are unhealthy and parochial.

But Tasmanians are, finally, catching up to the people they refer to as "mainlanders" in a way that is reshaping the politics of an island celebrated as the birthplace of the Australian Greens: through their own property boom.

"Tasmania arguably has [the] strongest property market in country," says the state's most famous economist, Saul Eslake. "It costs more to buy in Hobart than in Adelaide, Brisbane or Perth. It costs more to rent in Hobart than Melbourne."

Robust growth under a reasonably competent seven-year-old Liberal government, underpinned by generous federal subsidies and an export boom, has lower unemployment close to the national average, a 40-year aberration.

Tasmanian workers have increased their economic output at double the rate of other Australians over five years, a sign one of the weakest states is catching up to the rest of the nation.

The political consequences should become clear this Saturday, when a state election is held. Unlike Victoria, where Labor dominates parliament and agencies of the state, Tasmania may be about to, for the first time in its history, elect a Liberal government for the third time in a row.

There have been few polls, but analysts and commentators expect Premier Peter Gutwein to emerge with the most seats and probably retain government. On Friday morning, the tattooed former financial adviser told voters they risked an unstable Labor government reliant on Greens support if they didn't vote Liberal.

"We know that minority governments don't work," he said.

The day before, veteran Examiner journalist Sean Ford predicted the Liberal Party would win 13 of the 25 seats in the House of Assembly, the same number it held just before the election. Labor would win nine, the Greens two and an independent - not the one whose defection from the Liberal Party triggered the election - would be re-elected, he said.

Hobart psephologist Kevin Bonham said few polls made the election too uncertain to call. "The range of results is a considerably increased majority [for the Liberal Party] to losing a couple of seats at the worst," he said on Friday.

The campaign has been shaped by the state's new economic health. Higher property prices have created homelessness, stressed younger voters and switched rental homes into short-term tourist accommodation.

It has also improved the prosperity of the majority of Tasmanians who own their homes, and the many who have built holiday houses ubiquitously and modestly known as "shacks".

Last year, a three-storey, five-bedroom house in the southern Hobart suburb of Sandy Bay sold for $4.82 million, the year's record, according the Hobart Mercury.

Voters probably regard Gutwein's decision in March to isolate his island from the continent as his greatest achievement. It was almost universally praised, even by the tourism owners hardest hit, and remains the foundation of his re-election pitch.

In response, Labor leader Rebecca White has tried to shift the focus from an eliminated COVID-19 to Tasmania's many remaining challenges.

She frequently complains about the healthcare system, which struggles to look after an ageing, overweight and sick population that smokes and drinks more than other Australians.

The effect of the claims - with no apparent evidence - that the government may sell TAFE colleges or Hydro Tasmaniam, are unpredictable, Bonham said. The opposition even misspelt "privatisation" on its release.
direction: bullish for Tasmanian property sector and Liberal-aligned assets

headline: Former Oil Search boss Botten says UBS 'greedy' on fees
text:
Former Oil Search managing director Peter Botten has branded UBS "greedy" for the fees it charged on a deal in Papua New Guinea and said he was uncomfortable with the investment bank being on both sides of the transaction.

In much-anticipated evidence to the royal commission into the so-called "UBS loan scandal", Mr Botten singled out the investment bank for criticism.

"Unfortunately, investment banks sometimes are particularly greedy. And, unfortunately, in this case I think that might be an appropriate characterisation," he said.

The criticism is certain to sting UBS, which has refused to co-operate with the inquiry, given Mr Botten was their client at the time. The bank has always maintained the fees it charged the PNG government to borrow $1.2 billion to purchase a 10.1 per cent stake in Oil Search were appropriate.

Documents tendered to the commission show that Paddy Jilek, a senior adviser to UBS at the time, was one of the main bankers on the deal. The other UBS banker named in the tendered emails was Mitch Turner, who remains at UBS as head of Australasian Energy, according to his LinkedIn profile.

At the time Guy Fowler was the head of investment banking at UBS, while Matthew Grounds was the country head. Both men are now at Barrenjoey Capital Partners.

Mr Botten said Oil Search had retained Goldman Sachs to conduct a $1.2 billion capital raising in 2014, with the proceeds to be used to purchase a 22 per cent interest in the Elk Antelope gas fields. He said that once the PNG government decided it would take up the entire share issue, UBS took over the mandate from Goldmans, along with its role advising the state.

Asked by counsel assisting, James Renwick, SC, if he was "content" with the arrangement, Mr Botten replied: "I would not use the word content ... It was the reality of the day."

Mr Botten resigned as head of Oil Search last year and earlier this year became chairman of AGL Energy.

In earlier evidence yesterday by former PNG treasurer Don Polye, UBS was accused of having a conflict of interest given it advised the PNG government, providing of the loan and conducted the capital raising for Oil Search.

"UBS preyed on a poor country like PNG," he said. "The people that devised this loan are the enemies of PNG."

The investment bank is estimated to have made more than $100 million in fees and interest from the deal.

The inquiry heard on Monday that PNG lost $US432 million ($576 million) from the transaction - equivalent to almost 70 per cent of the country's annual health budget. The Pacific nation was forced to sell its 10.1 per cent stake in Oil Search when the stock declined and it did not have the funds to put extra capital into the loan.

The former PNG prime minister, Peter O'Neill, who championed the deal, falsely claimed in 2017 that PNG had made a $39 million profit on the deal.

Mr Botten said he did not have direct knowledge of the fees UBS charged PNG, but he was aware of the fees the investment bank was proposing to charge Oil Search for a similar deal if the placement to the PNG government fell over.

The inquiry has heard that at least three senior cabinet ministers opposed the deal and that it was unconstitutional, as Mr O'Neill did not seek parliamentary approval for the "highly speculative" transaction.
direction: bearish for UBS shares

headline: Cut fees, sell malls: AMP rescue plan
text:
AMP chair Debra Hazelton has struck a bold plan to retain control over a $5 billion property fund, one of the crown jewels of the AMP Capital platform, and stave off its takeover by rival Dexus by cutting management fees by 75 per cent and selling off $1.8 billion in assets.

The 22-page plan was delivered to AMP Capital Funds Management's chair Ming Long last month as AMP itself raced to lock in a deal with New York's Ares Management to take a controlling stake in AMP Capital's $60 billion private markets business.

Though the deadline to finalise that transaction passed with no deal struck, the two parties are still in talks with Ares interested in taking out 100 per cent of AMP Capital's property and infrastructure business.

More pressing for AMP is an April 27 deadline when the 30 or more institutional investors in the AMP Capital Diversified Property Fund will vote on a proposal to merge with Dexus' $10 billion wholesale fund, a plan which has already garnered support from the target fund's independent board.

The heart of the Hazelton rescue plan, obtained by The Australian Financial Review, proposes a drastic cut to the management fees AMP Capital levies on the ADPF fund from 60 basis points to 15 for the next two years, "which is effectively cost recovery", it said.

After that, fees would rise to 30 basis points, still half the current rate.

"We believe that this restructured and highly competitive fee regime demonstrates the commitment of AMP Capital to deliver a compelling and cost-effective investment proposition for unitholders in ADPF," Ms Hazelton wrote in the proposal.

The second major plank in the Hazelton rescue plan is a $1.8 billion sell-down in assets from the ADPF, to help generate funds to answer around $2.2 billion in redemption requests.

The bulk of the divestment, around $1.5 billion worth, is focused on the fund's exit from shopping malls, a move which would shift the portfolio weighting more heavily in favour of office and industrial assets.

Included in the sell-down are stakes in some of the country's best-known shopping destinations: Indooroopilly in Brisbane, Garden City Booragoon in Perth; and in Sydney, Westfield Warringah Mall and the Macquarie Centre.

AMP underwrites the high level of risk in executing a large volume of major mall deals in a market which has turned against retail property investment, by stepping in as a buyer itself. AMP would take out ADPF's stakes in Garden City and Indooroopilly while investing $90 million into the fund, for a total of $840 million.

Another key element of the divestment plan is an offer from a local fund manager to buy the two quarter stakes in Warringah Mall, held by ADPF and its sister fund, the AMP Capital Shopping Centre Fund, for around $800 million. That deal would then generate cash for the malls fund to buy ADPF's stake in the Macquarie Centre.

"The divestment strategy outlined provides adequate liquidity to fund the $2.188 million of outstanding redemption while significantly decreasing its overall retail exposure and increasing exposure to the office and logistics sectors, improving the quality and positioning of the portfolio to enable the fund to raise new equity," Ms Hazelton wrote.

When the plan did not win the board's backing, AMP went directly to ADPF investors, in the hope the hefty fees cut and underwritten mall sales will secure their loyalty at next week's showdown with Dexus.
direction: AMP shares and property sector under pressure

headline: Officeworks sees light in solar robots
text:
Officeworks is planning to install solar panels and batteries at a distribution centre in Melbourne to power a fleet of robots and help achieve ambitious sustainability targets.

Officeworks managing director Sarah Hunter said the batteries, to be installed at the Truganina centralised fulfilment centre (CFC) in conjunction with Officeworks' robotics partner Korber, would be a first in Australia.

"Korber is keen to partner with us to try solar and batteries on the roof of the CFC to see if we can create an environment where the AMRs [autonomous robots] are charging on fully renewable energy, so there's no need for generators," Ms Hunter told The Australian Financial Review as retailer launched its 2021 sustainability report.

"That facility will be phenomenal from a sustainability perspective and the first of its kind in Australia," Ms Hunter said.

Officeworks also plans to install solar panels on at least 20 stores this year, taking the number of stores powered by renewable energy to almost 30, while switching lighting to LED (light-emitting diode) and using materials such as light reflective paint so stores and distribution centres absorb less heat and use less energy.

The investment, part-funded by Officeworks' landlords, is in line with the group's strategy to achieve 100 per cent renewable energy by 2025 and net zero carbon emissions by 2030.

It is also targeting zero waste to landfill by repairing, recycling or reusing about 17,000 tonnes of material by 2025, ensuring all packaging is recyclable or reusable and, with Greening Australia, planting 2 million trees to replace those used in the production of paper products.

"We recycled over 91 per cent of operational waste last year, and more than 60 stores now pay less than $1 a month in waste, so there is a really tangible financial benefit, it's not just about being better for the people and the planet," Ms Hunter said.

Officeworks' sales soared during the pandemic as consumers forced to work and learn from home snapped up desks, office chairs, printers, monitors, computers and laptops.

Sales rose 8.7 per cent to $3 billion in 2021, after soaring 20 per cent in 2020, and earnings rose 7.6 per cent to $212 million following 14 per cent growth in 2020.

Rather than making Officeworks' sustainability targets more difficult to achieve, the pandemic, which struck soon after the bushfires, had heightened consumer awareness of climate change and the fragility of the planet, Ms Hunter said.

"Our customers are even more committed to making a positive difference than they were 12 to 24 months ago," Ms Hunter said.

"If anything, it's building momentum and people are particularly looking for large corporations to make a positive difference.

"In a time when you don't necessarily have a lot of control, particularly given what's been happening with the global pandemic, and you're spending a lot more time in your home, you are very conscious of the choices you make."
direction: Officeworks shares likely to benefit from cost savings and ESG appeal

headline: Slattery hoovers up senior execs, big move pending
text:
Macquarie's MIRA may be hogging all the attention in telco infrastructure, but don't forget about one of the local sector's pioneers.

Bevan Slattery, best known for selling fibre optic group PIPE Networks to David Teoh's TPG and founding data centre developer NEXTDC years before MIRA emerged as a player, appears to be gearing up for a big move of his own.

Slattery has beefed up his family office, Capital[b], recruiting a bunch of tech sector and financial services types in what is a clear sign he's rolling up his sleeves and keen to get back in on the action.

He's hired former Credit Suisse Australia head of financial sponsors banker and QIC private equity dealmaker Evan Kilpatrick as Capital[b] chief investment officer, and global telco executive Jim Clarke to run SUB.CO, a dark fibre investment. And Slattery has also recruited the services of Kate Jones, another Queenslander who served as a minister in the state's government and ran a bunch of portfolios including innovation and state development.

All up, it is understood Slattery has more than doubled Capital[b]'s team in recent months.

So the smart money is on Slattery making a big play, and the big question is where he and his hefty team plan to strike. His traditional hunting ground is in the technology sector, and specifically cloud, data and infrastructure. They're all pretty hot areas, thanks to buyers such as MIRA snapping up assets left, right and centre.

Slattery's existing investments include Oman Australia Cable, sonar radar group Fiber Sense and ASX-listed software companies intelliHR and Pointerra.
direction: bullish for technology and data infrastructure stocks
