headline: AusSuper trims equity holdings as risks rise
text:
The chief investment officer of AustralianSuper has warned investors the bumper returns of the past decade are unlikely to be sustained, with the $250 billion giant trimming its positions in equity markets as it braces for interest rates to move higher.

Mark Delaney has played down fears that the country's biggest super funds will continue to take ASX-listed companies private after AusSuper's involvement in this year's $24 billion takeover of Sydney Airport, instead talking up plans to double the size of his London office as part of a push into offshore private capital markets.

Mr Delaney, who was working remotely from Coffs Harbour, said superannuation members have enjoyed remarkable returns since the global financial crisis, with AusSuper's balanced strategy delivering 10 straight years of returns above 10 per cent per annum. But he warned that a period that has seen asset values propelled higher by falling interest rates was likely coming to an end and rising rates could end the late-stage bull market.

"You're not going to have another 10 years like that," Mr Delaney said. "It always ends when the central banks take the punchbowl away. It looks to me as if they're starting that process, hoping they just need to take a couple of cups out, rather than the whole bowl. But time will tell."

He said AusSuper's equity team has started to reduce its risk exposure as it waits to see how the pandemic unfolds and whether high inflation can be contained.

"We've been bullish on stocks since COVID. We're trimming those positions now. We're not rushing to the other side of the boat, we're just acknowledging that the world has become a bit more difficult for investing than what it was 12 months ago - prices are higher, interest rates are rising and inflation is more of a problem.

"Prices will normalise - prices are expensive now - and then we could take advantage of better prices."

Mr Delaney said the record level of deal-making seen this year was another sign of a late-stage bull market. AusSuper has been central to this trend, with the acquisition of Sydney Airport Corporation sparking concerns that cashed-up pension funds could target more publicly listed companies, potentially depriving public market investors of opportunities.

The chief executives of several of Australia's biggest ASX property companies warned earlier this month they expected their firms could become takeover targets for super funds if they did not perform.

While Mr Delaney said it was no bad thing that public companies feel under pressure to perform, he played down suggestions AusSuper would ramp up take-private deals in the wake of the Sydney Airport transaction.

He stressed AustralianSuper was not equipped to run operating businesses without a partner and said the deals tended to be expensive; the time to buy listed property companies, for example, was probably 18 months ago.

"I think the opportunity set [for take-private deals] is narrow, these deals are hard to do and there's lots of great companies in Australia we can allocate capital to," Mr Delaney said, pointing out that of the $24 billion deployed by AusSuper this year, $2 billion went into capital raisings by ASX companies, including CSL's blockbuster raising last week.

"The main game for us really is to provide capital to capital markets in Australia and let them get on with the task.

"That's a much easier way to make money."

Where AustralianSuper does want to expand is offshore. Mr Delaney said he expects that over time the proportion of the fund's assets invested offshore will likely rise from 50 per cent to above 60 per cent, and he wants to expand AusSuper's London office from 43 people to about 100, and build up the newly established New York base.

Mr Delaney said a local presence was particularly vital in private markets, where local knowledge was vital. He gave the example of a property deal in Britain the fund is working on, led by an AusSuper executive who previously worked for the Queen's property management company, The Crown Estate.

"You can't do that sort of thing with a telephone call from Australia. You've got to know the people and how it works. I don't think it's viable for us to build a large global private markets program internationally and do it ourselves unless we have people on the ground."

Mr Delaney also expressed disappointment about the federal government's crackdown on proxy advisors, which appears likely to force a restructure of the Australian Council of Superannuation Investors, an industry-fund backed proxy advisor of which AusSuper is a prominent member.

Mr Delaney, who helped establish ACSI, said it provided valuable checks and balances on issues such as corporate governance and rejected suggestions that proxy firms were trying to dictate how companies should be run.

"As always, people who do things which aren't always popular come in for a bit of stick, and I think they've come in for a bit of stick," he said. "The company's job is to run themselves, the advisors are just saying, 'this is what the shareholders expect'. So it's a bit about shooting the messenger, I think."

ACSI is particularly concerned with environmental, social and governance matters, which are sharply in focus amid what Mr Delaney said was the biggest and fastest energy transition in history.

Although AusSuper has a policy of pursuing net zero emission by 2050 across its portfolio, Mr Delaney said it was not the role of the fund to intervene on individual energy company projects, as some climate activists have called for.

"Companies are the allocators of capital - I call them the custodians of our members' capital. We've hired these people, so as long as they've got a sensible plan for managing it, that's fine."
direction: bearish for equities and rate-sensitive sectors

headline: CBA opens doors to Dutch pension capital
text:
Commonwealth Bank hopes its new European headquarters will link large corporate customers to sustainable investing heavyweights at the heart of powerful shifts across capital markets that have pressured the financing needs of Australian blue chips.

Australia's largest lender opened the doors to its new Amsterdam office in May, switching its European command post from London in light of Brexit.

The new base doubles as an access point to Dutch pension giants that ave emerged as strident champions of environmental, social and governance investing, which increasingly factors in issues including climate change and board diversity in allocating debt and equity capital.

"Europe is one of the leaders in terms of pension money. It is also going to lead the world in sustainability and climate-related activity," said Andrew Hinchliff, group executive, institutional banking and markets for CBA, in an interview with The Australian Financial Review.

"Our Amsterdam office will be the hub of accessing that intellectual capital and pools of money to bring back here."

The Dutch pension industry is the largest in Europe and one of the biggest in the world, and is among the most progressive in guiding portfolios by ESG criteria. The country's courts have also come into play, requiring Shell, the oil giant, to hasten its carbon emission cutting efforts in June.

Mr Hinchliff said the prominence of sustainability in capital markets today was a risk but also presented an opportunity as Australian businesses pressed into growth areas such as mining metals such as lithium and copper that underpinned electric batteries.

The bank was working with Australian companies "to help them navigate this new operating environment and get access to the huge wall of willing green capital that is looking to invest in projects and businesses that will drive the world's transition", Mr Hinchliff said. "The pace of change has been swift over the last year, and capital is absolutely differentiating between those who are moving and those who are lagging."

The comments come within a week of Prime Minister Scott Morrison warning that Australian companies face financing pressures because of climate change.

Mr Morrison said "financiers are already making decisions regardless of governments", and that he wanted "to make sure that Australian companies can get loans", in remarks following the publication of a report from a UN body that revealed the quickening pace of global warming.

Mr Morrison has declined to commit to a net zero emissions target by 2050, an approach out of step with close allies, including the US and the UK.

Australian businesses and governments face the prospect of a frosty reception from large fund managers when raising capital, which may include a valuation discount for deals that fall foul of asset managers' ESG policies.

By connecting big Australian businesses with leaders in sustainable investing, CBA also hopes to consolidate its role in debt capital markets after jumping up the leaderboard in a busy stretch of bond issuance and lending through the pandemic.

CBA ranked first in Australian debt financing last financial year, up from fourth in the previous year, after increasing the amount raised by two-thirds to $28.3 billion.

The lending and bond issuance have been helped by a global wave of mergers and acquisitions, with multibillion-dollar takeover bids for the likes of Sydney Airport and Crown Resorts.

The activity helped Mr Hinchliff's institutional banking and markets unit increase net profit before tax by a third to $1.2 billion for the 2020-21 financial year. The contribution helped to power $8.6 billion in net profit across the bank for the period, up almost a fifth from the previous year, allowing the bank to return $10 billion to shareholders in dividends and buybacks.

"There is an M&A frenzy going on," Mr Hinchliff said. "The amount of money, both equity and debt, and the amount of investors representing that equity and debt is greater than I've ever seen it."
direction: bullish for CBA shares

headline: PAG completes $300m takeover of Unispace
text:
Brothers Charles and Gareth Hales have sold out of Unispace, the office design firm they founded 11 years ago, after completing the $300-million sale of the company to PAG Asia Capital.

The former majority-shareholder Hales brothers - sons of Bruce Hales, head of the secretive fundamentalist Plymouth Brethren Christian Church - will stay in advisory roles, however, as Unispace sets out to boost its Asia revenue to $100 million by 2024.

Since 2018, the company has targeted customers in Singapore, Hong Kong and Malaysia. Last year it established a team in India. Company filings show Unispace reported revenue of $128 million last year, though they do not show how much of that came from Asian operations.

"This move will realise our growth potential through new markets and by leveraging PAG's extensive global networks, specifically in key Asian growth markets," Unispace chief executive Steve Quirk said.

The deal - first reported by The Australian Financial Review's Street Talk column in December - came after a $400 million planned acquisition by CPE Capital fell through a year earlier.

Asia-based PAG is an increasingly active player in Australia, having acquired the 200-franchise Cheesecake Shop network in 2017 and last year agreeing to fund regional airline REX's expansion into east-coast capital city services. It also manages $US40 billion ($51.6 billion) in assets for more than 150 institutional investors.

"Our investment will facilitate Unispace's plans for further global expansion and build on an already successful business," said PAG chairman and chief executive Weijian Shan.

"We believe our extensive experience in real estate and property management investments will bring tremendous value to support the growth of Unispace."

PAG was part of a TPG Capital-led consortium that in 2015 acquired Cushman & Wakefield for $US2 billion, giving it a stake in the commercial real estate agency that now stands at just over 21 per cent through its vehicle Pacific Alliance Investment Management. Unispace is counting on the relationship with Cushman & Wakefield to help it expand.

Japan, China, Philippines and Korea are markets the company is targeting. It is also looking at Germany and Canada and would consider acquisitions.
direction: bullish for PAG and Unispace

headline: NSW Ports monopoly to last 50 years: Sims
text:
NSW Ports could have a monopoly for moving containers in the state for 50 years after the Federal Court threw out the competition watchdog's attempts to stop compensation being paid to Port Botany if the Port of Newcastle develops a rival container terminal, ACCC chairman Rod Sims has said.

"It's a real setback for competition; it's a setback for productivity; it's a setback for the NSW economy," Mr Sims told The Australian Financial Review.

He said NSW Ports, owned by AustralianSuper and IFM, could have an "effective monopoly" for moving containers in the state for 50 years after the Federal Court dismissed proceedings brought by the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission.

"What chance have we got of improving productivity when this is the attitude to competition and growth in the Australian economy?" he said.

The ACCC took the NSW Ports consortium, which operates Port Botany and Port Kembla, to court in 2018, alleging deeds in the ports' sale agreement were "anti-competitive and illegal".

The ports were sold by the NSW government to the investment groups in a 50-year deed for $5.1 billion in 2013.

The deeds were designed to protect the funds against the development of a potential competing container terminal at the Port of Newcastle.

This obliges the NSW government to pay AustralianSuper and IFM and their consortium partner Tawreed Investments a $100 fee on every container moved in and out through Newcastle once the port grew big enough to handle 30,000 20-foot equivalent units annually.

The Port of Newcastle was privatised in 2014 and its owners, the Infrastructure Fund and China Merchants Port Holdings, must reimburse the NSW government for any compensation paid to the consortium.

Container traffic at the Port of Newcastle has not yet exceeded the specified cap, so no payments have been made by the NSW government.

The ACCC had asked the Federal Court for an injunction stopping AustralianSuper and IFM from seeking compensation.

Port Botany is the only port in NSW with dedicated container terminal facilities but the Port of Newcastle has been trying to develop similar facilities to diversify away from coal exports, which account for most of the goods moved through its terminal.

Mr Sims said: "This judgment provides an enormous hurdle for the Port of Newcastle to compete with Port Botany and Port Kembla, because of financial consequences arising from the deeds. Less competition usually results in higher charges for businesses and consumers."

The ACCC argued the deeds had an anti-competitive purpose and effect but the Federal Court dismissed its claims.

The court's judgment has been made available only to the parties' lawyers.

The ACCC said it would consider the judgment and whether it could appeal.

The sale of Port Botany and Port Kembla in May 2013 was led by former NSW premier Mike Baird, who was treasurer at the time of the transaction.

So-called "port commitment deeds" were entered into as part of the privatisation.

The Port of Newcastle said it was disappointed by the decision. AustralianSuper and IFM declined to comment.

A spokesman for NSW Treasurer Dominic Perrottet said the state government would not comment on the decision until it had considered the judgment in full.
direction: bearish for NSW Ports and its owners

headline: Citi prepares retreat from Australian consumer banking
text:
Global banking giant Citi is set to farewell its Australian credit cards and mortgages business as part of a wider consumer bank sale.

As Street Talk revealed yesterday, Citi's head for Australia, Marc Luet, has warned big customers of an imminent retreat from consumer banking in Australia, telling them Citi would push ahead with plans to sell the business.

An announcement was expected as early as last night, with confirmation Citi plans to offload the business to a new owner.

Citi's investment bankers have started talking to potential acquirers as part of a sale process under way.

At Street Talk reported in February, Citi's top local banker, Tony Osmond, is in involved in the search, and has been shopping the group's domestic credit-card business to banks and other potential acquirers. The portfolio is expected to attract interest from incumbent Australian banks and non-bank lenders, who could absorb Citi's assets into their existing loanbooks.

Citi's consumer banking retreat includes all its consumer-focused operations including credit cards, loans, retail banking, wealth management for high-net-worth individuals and mortgages.

The Australian sale plans come as Citi reviews its retail banking operations in the region. The group has about 20 consumer banks globally, a dozen in the Asia-Pacific region including Australia, South Korea and Thailand.

Should Citi find a buyer for its Australian consumer operations, it would leave the Wall Street bank focused on institutional banking and capital markets businesses, which include investment banking, capital markets and advisory, markets and securities trading, commercial banking and treasury services.

The retreat comes after Citi spent decades trying to build its Australian consumer bank. The group arguably had the most success in credit cards, where it is the fifth biggest player in Australia trailing only the big four banks. Data group IBISWorld reckons it has a 12.6 per cent market share, having built the business since it obtained a full banking licence in Australia in 1985.

Citi is one of 10 foreign-owned subsidiary banks in Australia, alongside the likes of HSBC, ING Bank, Arab Bank and Rabobank.

It had $11.5 billion in total residents' loans and finance leases in Australia, according to APRA data.
direction: negative for Citi, positive for incumbent Australian banks and non-bank lenders

headline: Dan Murphy's customers go for the best
text:
Drinkers with spare cash because they cannot travel overseas are increasingly buying higher-priced premium products, but the profit margins from Endeavour Group's fast-growing online liquor operations still lag those generated when people come into stores and choose products off the shelf.

Chief executive Steve Donohue said the shift to premiumisation was also happening in the group's hotels when they could trade outside of lockdowns.

With the introduction of order and pay at tables inside pubs, more people were ordering higher-priced cocktails.

"They want to treat themselves," he said.

Lockdowns in Sydney and Melbourne caused revenue at Endeavour Group's hotels business to drop 10 per cent in the three months ended October 3 compared with a year earlier, when Victoria was also grappling with an extended lockdown. The hotels are down 40 per cent on two years ago.

But the group's retail arm, led by the Dan Murphy's and BWS chains, only suffered a 0.2 per cent drop compared with a year ago as drinkers shifted further toward online ordering.

Endeavour operates 251 Dan Murphy's liquor retailing outlets and 1401 BWS outlets, and 336 hotels and clubs, with 12,402 poker machines across 295 of those hotels. The company was demerged from supermarket giant Woolworths and listed as a separate entity on the ASX in late June.

Mr Donohue said retail sales rose 21 per cent compared with two years ago. Online sales hit $305 million in the 14 weeks from June 28 to October 3, which represented 11.5 per cent of total sales. This was a record for e-commerce inside the business. In the same period a year earlier, online sales revenue was $227 million.

About 40 per cent of the group's hotels were shut in the 14-week period because of lockdowns.

The retail component of the Endeavour business includes bottle shops and drive-through operations attached to hotels. Retail sales edged 0.2 per cent lower to $2.65 billion compared with a year ago. Hotel revenue dropped 9.9 per cent to $282 million. Overall, sales in the 14 weeks ended October 3 were down 1.2 per cent to $2.94 billion.

Mr Donohue said retail customers were also flocking to the group's on-demand delivery services when ordering online, with speed of delivery increasingly important. But the profit margins from the online operations were still behind those of traditional retail stores inside the group.

Mr Donohue said industry-wide staff shortages would be a constraint in the phased reopening of hotels in Victoria and NSW, while he also said the global supply chain disruptions affecting many industries was resulting in shortages of some imported drinks.

Some staff had chosen to leave the industry. Finding chefs and supervisory workers was difficult in some areas. "A lot of them require certification. They're sort of skilled roles," he said.

Endeavour has been making strong inroads in its loyalty programs in the Dan Murphy's business, with the "My Dans" program growing to 5.9 million members, compared with 4.8 million a year ago.
direction: supportive for Endeavour Group shares

headline: Coal will be paid to firm up the grid
text:
Energy Minister Angus Taylor is increasingly confident he has won the backing of state and territory counterparts for reforms that could keep coal and gas plants alive to ensure reliable electricity supply and boost investment in firming power.

Mr Taylor and his fellow ministers agreed to release today a long-anticipated Energy Security Board report that argues the explosion in low-cost renewable energy production must be matched with "sufficient investment in reliable generation".

As revealed by The Australian Financial Review late last month, the board's main recommendations are that the National Electricity Market set up a capacity mechanism to ensure dispatchable energy capacity remains viable and available across the system.

"Taking action now is critical and we need a co-ordinated approach to market design to keep the lights on and costs down," Mr Taylor will say when he releases the report, adding that the commitment of all energy ministers to designing the market after 2025 "recognises the importance of working together."

Kerry Schott, chairman of the board, the top energy policy advisory for governments, will say today that the mechanism was required to bring forward the "right mix of firm, flexible and variable resources when needed. That capacity might come from pumped hydro, batteries, wind, gas or coal, with those most able to be fast and flexible most likely to be relied upon and most likely to receive revenue.

"Participants need sufficient incentives and confidence to invest in new capacity. Jurisdictions need assurance that participants will meet the power system's physical needs at all times and if that assurance is not there, governments will intervene (as they have done) to provide missing gas peakers and pumped hydro as thermal coal exits."

The release of the board's recommendations looks set to add fuel to the debate about whether such a radical reform of the National Electricity Market is needed, and whether the beefed-up measures to ensure reliability will end up costing customers more.

Some developers of renewable energy projects are worried that they will work to delay the shutdown of ageing coal-fired power plants and reduce opportunities for the development of new wind and solar farms.

Large energy users have voiced worries that the move to a "capacity" market represents "gold-plating" of electricity reliability that will come at a steep cost for consumers.

At the heart of the concerns is the recommendation for an introduction of a "capacity mechanism", which would reward power producers for having capacity available to generate power, not just for power they produce.

While the mechanism is described in the board's report as technology-neutral, it could prop up the economics of coal power plants, which have been hit by a collapse in wholesale electricity prices due to the flood of cheap wind and solar power on to the grid.

The board also argues the capacity mechanism would bring a benefit to consumers of around $1.3 billion.

An open letter in The Australian Financial Review yesterday, signed by senior executives from companies including Tesla Energy and Enphase Energy, and lobby groups such as The Australia Institute, said the new reliability mechanism would "prop up unprofitable coal generators, increasing costs and delaying the clean energy transition".

But big energy suppliers, led by Origin Energy and EnergyAustralia, have backed the idea, saying it would avoid capacity shortfalls and customer price surges.

"While the transition to renewable energy takes place, there needs to be a mechanism that provides confidence and ensures there is always adequate capacity in the system to meet consumer demand," said Ross Edwards, head of markets at EnergyAustralia.

"A capacity market complements renewable investment that's being driven through other mechanisms.

"Once a capacity market is in place and customers have assurances that their lights will stay on, we can and should push for even more clean and cheap wind and solar power."

The board's report notes, using Australian Energy Market Operator data, that half of the National Electricity Market's coal power fleet could retire by as early as 2030. During the same period more than 29 gigawatts of new renewable capacity will enter, putting extra pressure on existing thermal generators to maintain the grid and generate power at peak times.

The report's authors - which include the heads of AEMO, the Australian Energy Regulator and the Australian Energy Markets Commission - argue that capacity mechanisms are established features of energy markets. They also noted that Texas - which suffered a major power crisis in February as severe storms disrupted energy systems - does not have one.

Power producers say coal power generators provide vital services to the grid that they are not paid for - such as inertia - and that a new market structure is needed to provide incentives for investment in "dispatchable" capacity such as pumped hydro or gas power, needed to fill in the gaps between weather-dependent renewable power.

The ESB's paper to ministers recognises the "significant stakeholder concerns" over the capacity market proposal and commits to working with stakeholders to develop the detailed design of the system for ministers to agree on in mid-2023.

It suggests such a market could be implemented by introducing a "physical retailer reliability obligation", which would require retailers and other liable parties to commit in advance to contracting reliable power supply to cover their load.

The board also recommends reforms in the way that congestion in the power grid is managed, other than just building more transmission lines to link up distant renewable projects such as wind and solar farms.

Proponents say larger transmission systems would avoid the need for coal and gas firming capacity as shortfalls caused by weather systems could be offset by drawing energy from a more widely dispersed grid.

Dr Schott will point out that Australia was "already well progressed down this path", with plans to develop priority transmission projects identified by AEMO.

"Nevertheless, even if it were physically possible to build enough transmission to connect all new generation projects located in increasingly remote locations, the costs to consumers would be unjustifiably high," she will say. "A new efficient grid network will have some congestion. But the level of congestion needs to be managed.

"The benefits of new renewable generation diminish as the energy generated from new solar or wind farms either goes to waste or displaces existing renewable energy."

The board's proposals on transmission would "help ensure that the generation that is being built can be operated successfully rather than building new projects that can't connect to the grid and/or force those already there to be constrained", she said.

"The reforms favour long-term operators over short-term speculators."

Federal, state and territory energy ministers agreed last Friday to release the board's post-2025 report this week and are slated to meet again in September to agree on reforms for consideration by national cabinet in October.
direction: bullish for coal and gas generators

headline: Human services group APM puts $1b-plus raising to investors
text:
Human services group APM will seek to raise more than $1 billion for its initial public offering, in a deal that would value the group at north of $3 billion or "mid-20s" times forecast profit.

It is understood APM"s management and brokers will kick off meetings with potential cornerstone backers today, asking them to think about APM as an Australian services success story with an evolving global footprint.

It is expected to tell funds it is on track to record $1.3 billion revenue in the 2022 financial year, which would be 30 per cent higher than last year, while last year's $1 billion revenue came on the back of 38 per cent growth on the two years prior.

In terms of earnings, APM expects to make about $300 million at the EBITDA line in FY22, and more than $155 million in net profit after tax and amortisation.

The company plans to offer up a combined 30 per cent stake as part of the float. Founder Megan Wynne would retain about 35 per cent, private equity backer Madison Dearborn Partners would hold another 30 per cent and management the rest.

Wynne and management would be expected to sign a long-dated escrow arrangement, formally tying them to the company for at least the next two years.

A $1 billion-plus raising for a 30 per cent stake would imply a $3 billion-plus market value. In terms of profit, APM is expected to shoot for a "mid-20s" multiple on a price-to-earnings basis.

There's few, if any, obvious listed comparable companies. Fund managers will likely use the S&P/ASX200 Industrials index as a starting point, which is trading at about 22-times profit, as a starting point, and then look at top-line and earnings growth.

Similar businesses from a global growth-oriented services perspective include IDP Education, IPH Ltd and ALS.

APM is expected to tell funds it is operating across 10 countries, with about 7500 people on its team. The group supported more than 1 million clients in the past 12 months, offering an array of human services; supporting people with disabilities and/or mental health needs, assisting veterans and helping people emerge from the criminal justice system. A large portion of its revenue is understood to stem from government contracts.

It is expected to pitch itself as a company that walks the talk; more than half of its senior leadership team is female, for example.

A float - and a $3 billion-plus listing - would cap a remarkable rise for Wynne, who started at APM in 1994 when it had only a handful of employees.

Goldman Sachs is leading the float. UBS is a joint bookrunner, while Credit Suisse and Bank of America are joint lead managers. Gilbert + Tobin is doing the legal work.
direction: bullish for APM shares

headline: What to do when currency moves hit your portfolio
text:
In the darkest hours of last year's virus crisis, the Australian dollar collapsed to US55¢ against the US dollar as it was dumped in the rush to secure the safety of the default global currency. Once the panic was over, the Australian dollar began a revaluation journey that has seen it rise back to US78¢.

Over that same period of revaluation, overseas sharemarkets staged a spectacular recovery, led by the US. Many Australian investors were left wondering why the returns from their international shares failed to keep up. The answer lies in what is referred to as "the currency effect" because of the rising Australian dollar.

There's no question the diversification offered by investing in international assets provides enormous benefits to a portfolio, as well as opportunities that are simply not available in the Australian market. However, on top of the challenge of finding attractive investments, there's the added obstacle that fluctuations in the currency can have a sizeable impact on returns.

Just as with shares, a currency can be cheap or expensive. When the Australian dollar is cheap, it takes more to buy the equivalent amount of a foreign currency, and vice versa. It's a matter for debate as to what level the Australian dollar needs to fall to in order to be considered "cheap", but over the past 20 years anywhere below US65¢ is a decent rule of thumb, and about US88¢ it's getting expensive.

The problem comes when cheap Australian dollars are used to buy an international asset and then the dollar rises strongly, as happened last year.

For instance, buying $US20,000 worth of a US stock when the Australian dollar is at US65¢ will cost $30,769 (20,000/0.65). If the share price rises 10 per cent over the next year and the currency remains unchanged, the investment will be worth $33,846 (22,000/0.65).

However, if instead the Australian dollar had appreciated by 20 per cent, to US78¢, the investment would be worth only $28,205 (22,000/0.78). The currency effect has more than wiped out the benefits of the higher share price.

The way to overcome this potential problem is to "hedge" the currency, which eliminates the impact of currency fluctuations. A hedge acts like a form of insurance, so the return will reflect only the change in value of the underlying investment.

As an example, VanEck has an exchange traded fund listed in Australia that is based on the MSCI World Quality Index ("quality" is a filter that targets higher growth), which comes in a hedged and unhedged version.

From the bottoming of global sharemarkets in March 2020 to the end of April this year, the hedged version (ASX:QHAL) went up by more than 73 per cent. The unhedged version of the same portfolio went up by only 32 per cent. The difference was entirely because of the impact of the rising Australian dollar. Indeed, any Australian-bought, unhedged international investment will have underperformed a hedged version by the same amount over that same period.

The opposite can happen too. In 2011, taking advantage of the Australian dollar trading at $US1.10 to buy unhedged international investments provided a multi-year tailwind as the currency gradually fell back towards its long-term trading range.

There are three solutions to address the currency effect on a portfolio. First, if the Australian dollar appears cheap, consider buying international investments in a hedged version.

Second, there are ETFs that can act as a kind of insurance overlay for a whole portfolio. For example, the BetaShares Strong Australian Dollar Fund (ASX:AUDS) is designed to increase by more than 2 per cent if the Australian dollar rises against the US dollar by 1 per cent. Its Strong US Dollar Fund does the opposite.

Third, do nothing. There is research that argues the long-term effects of currency fluctuations are negligible because they tend to gyrate around the average. So for those times when the Aussie dollar appears cheap, simply offset that against the times when it appears expensive.SI

James Weir is a director of Steward Wealth.
direction: headwinds for international equity returns due to AUD strength

headline: Investors need right climate rules
text:
Risks on road to net zero Billions in clean energy investment are being held back for lack of clear green reporting benchmarks. The Coalition can't help investors because it is too caught up in its own politics.

An underappreciated development from the Glasgow climate conference was the steps taken by our independent financial regulators towards providing more guidance on climate-related risks to our financial system and our economy.

This is a really welcome and important move towards unlocking more investment, jobs and opportunities from cleaner and cheaper energy.

Investors have been leading the way in ensuring there is greater transparency and accountability about these risks, but there's only so much they can do when the existing reporting framework is insufficient, inconsistent and inadequate.

Labor understands these frustrations and we want to see clearer guidance from regulators and government about what companies should be reporting.

Like the Reserve Bank and prudential regulator APRA, we'd like to see disclosures that are more usable, credible and comparable.

This would set a baseline around the world that we can measure ourselves against and would help investors make informed decisions.

We know climate change poses a risk to the entire financial system, and countries such as New Zealand and the Britain have already embraced comprehensive reporting systems.

More and more companies are committing to net zero emissions and have established targets and plans to get them there. Employers and regulators are leading the way where Scott Morrison, Barnaby Joyce and Josh Frydenberg have not, and cannot, because of internal political divisions in the Coalition parties.

Labor will take responsibility and provide leadership here where it has been missing.

However, a lack of consistent and transparent information is only part of the story.

The Liberals' and Nationals' 22 "policies in eight years have created serious and damaging uncertainty for investors. Then, when investors desperately needed a plan, in the

lead-up to Glasgow, all they got from the Prime Minister was a pamphlet.

The headline in The Australian Financial Review said it all: "'Whack-weary' CEOs lament paralysing effect of Morrison carbon plan".

Policy uncertainty pushes up the price of finance. That pushes up the price of new projects and technology, stifles innovation, puts upward pressure on energy costs and means investors increasingly need to look offshore.

We know the market is there for renewable energy investments in Australia, we see it all around the world. But we've been bucking the global trend - losing another race we should be winning.

What our nation desperately needs is political leadership, policy stability and clear and ambitious targets and policies that create certainty for investors to follow.

Economic modelling for the Investor Group on Climate Change suggests Australia would create $63 billion in fresh investment opportunities over the next five years by strengthening climate targets and policies in line with reaching net zero emissions by mid-century. And stronger 2030 policies can unlock $131 billion investment in clean industries and new jobs by the end of the decade.

In a recent survey, 70 per cent of Australian investors, managing more than $1 trillion in total, highlighted policy uncertainty as a key barrier to investment, up from 40 per cent last year.

This is a devastating vote of no confidence in Morrison and Frydenberg and the wasted decade of missed opportunities the Coalition has presided over.

As the Business Council of Australia modelling has demonstrated, taking meaningful action on cleaner and cheaper energy will not cost jobs and economic activity - it will create jobs and opportunities right around Australia, and especially in the regions.

Super funds recognise this and are investing in the transition to a more sustainable, low-carbon global economy - but nowhere near as much as they'd like to, and nowhere near enough here at home.

With Australia's superannuation assets set to grow from $3.3 trillion today to about $34 trillion by 2060, super should be central to the recovery.

But that won't happen without political leadership and the policies these investors are telling us they need to help support more jobs and opportunities in more parts of the country.

Just last month the Reserve Bank warned that international investors are increasingly adjusting their portfolios in response to climate risks and that Australia risks facing increasingly higher costs in relation to emissions-intensive activities.

So we have no more time to waste.

Sunlight isn't only good for generating cleaner and cheaper energy. Shining the light of transparency and creating more certainty for investors is also key to ensuring our economy is stronger and more productive after COVID-19 than it was before.

Jim Chalmers is Labor's federal shadow treasurer.
direction: bullish for clean energy and green investment sectors

headline: Markets live afr.com Breaking news and analysis for what's moving the market, online daily and in The Australian Financial Review
direction: downward pressure on rate-sensitive sectors like banks and REITs

headline: Credit Corp says demand for loans bouncing back
text:
People are tapping quick loans again after COVID-19 uncertainty and early superannuation withdrawals suppressed borrowing desire, financial services outfit Credit Corp says.

The lending book for Sydney-based Credit Corp, whose products include CarStart Finance and Wallet Wizard - which loans between $100 and $5000 - was pummelled when the pandemic broke out.

Lending fell from $230 million in December 2019 to $153 million by September last year. But that had "substantially recovered over the December quarter" to $168 million, Credit Corp said yesterday.

Application volumes exceeded the same period a year earlier and delinquencies remained at record lows, Credit Corp said.

"People seem a bit more confident about borrowing now than they potentially were in the past," Credit Corp chief executive Thomas Beregi told The Australian Financial Review.

Another factor squeezing borrowing was the government allowing early release of superannuation during the pandemic.

"We did notice that in both May and July, the two waves of superannuation [releases], that we had a lot of people pre-paying their loans," he said.

"Obviously the further we get from that superannuation withdrawal we can see people coming back and drawing down [loans] again.

He was speaking as Credit Corp outlined a 9.6 per cent lift in statutory half-year profits to $42.3 million. Earnings per share fell 10.7 per cent to 62.8¢ after the company issued $155 million in new stock last year. Dividends were flat at 36¢ a share.

The consumer loan division's earnings fell 30 per cent to $6.7 million as COVID-19 bit. Credit Corp's mainstay Australia-New Zealand debt collection business posted a 7 per cent earnings rise to $27.5 million, while the newer US debt chasing division more than doubled earnings to $8 million.

Morgans analyst Scott Murdoch in a note said he expected the market would take this North American result "positively given the potential of the US long-term".

Shares in Credit Corp jumped $1.80 to $31.26 by early afternoon trade as the company upgraded earnings forecasts from only two months ago.

Full-year profits should now range between $85 million and $90 million versus earlier estimates of between $70 million and $85 million, Credit Corp said.

Mr Beregi said another feature of COVID-19 - financiers offloading less blocks of debt - was easing. Agencies such as Credit Corp purchase debt ledgers at discount rates from institutions such as banks and try to reap a profit by collecting more money.

Mr Beregi said two large credit issuers in Australia had briefly stopped offloading ledgers.

"They subsequently recommenced but at lower volumes due to some of that forbearance and those relief programs they've had in place," he said. "But as those run off, we're seeing volumes start to recover."

Credit Corp finished last calendar year acquiring $160 million in ledgers from struggling rival Brisbane-based Collection House.
direction: Credit Corp shares higher

headline: Rental risk as Victoria investors sell
text:
Victoria's recent stamp duty hikes, higher land taxes and newly legislated minimum standards in market rental housing are prompting landlords to sell out and put the supply of rental stock at risk, agents warn.

Last month's state budget property tax increases, along with new laws around renting that came into effect at the end of March, had prompted some landlords to give up, said Michael Love, the head of Melbourne's northern suburbs-based Michael Love & Co agency.

"They're turning around and saying 'Is real estate the vehicle I want to be investing in for a return?'," said Mr Love, whose company manages 6000 rental properties.

"They're investing to get a return where they're taxed heavily, there are additional costs and holding costs which have never been there."

Anthony Webb, the head of eastern suburbs-based real estate agency PhilipWebb, agreed.

"It's the vibe of the legislation is just making the pendulum swing that much towards the tenants," said Mr Webb, whose business also has a rent roll of about 6000 homes.

"It's this combination of things making landlords go - it's too hard and we're going to sell."

It's likely some landlords will sell out in the face of higher costs. A regulatory impact statement accompanying the new legislation said it was likely that as a result of introduction of minimum standards, 9 per cent of rental providers would increase rent, 4 per cent would sell the property and 4 per cent would not acquire future rental properties.

But low interest rates, and a buoyant housing market, are also prompting many residential property investors to come back into a market they had previously departed.

Housing values surged to record highs across all capitals except Perth and Darwin, with more growth expected in coming months as strong demand from buyers outpaces the falling volume of listings, CoreLogic figures this week showed.

Dwelling prices across the combined capitals surged 2.3 per cent in May - the second fastest growth rate since the 1980s.

The latest official home loan numbers last month showed investor lending, rose in March at its fastest pace in almost two decades.

New data showing home loan commitments to investor buyers jumped 12.7 per cent from February, the fastest increase since July 2003, to a seasonally adjusted monthly total of $7.8 billion.

They were "enormous" numbers and challenged the picture of a market solely driven by owner-occupiers.

But real estate agents said that landlords selling out would lead to a net loss of rental stock that would hurt tenants.
direction: bearish for Victorian residential property and REITs

headline: Hunt accuses ABC co-host of left-wing bias on live TV
text:
A political advertising expert says government-issued messages have been hijacked by both sides of politics, after Health Minister Greg Hunt got into an argument with an ABC presenter over a Liberal Party post featuring a government vaccine announcement.

"Every side of government has been doing this for a long time," said Australian National University lecturer Andrew Hughes. "Probably because of the pandemic we are noticing it more."

News Breakfast co-host Michael Rowland complained to Mr Hunt yesterday about a social media post that featured the Liberal Party logo with news that the federal government had secured access to more doses of Pfizer's COVID-19 vaccine.

Mr Hunt responded by accusing the journalist of political bias, leading both men into an on-air tiff.

"I know this is an issue for you," Mr Hunt said. "In many ways you identify with the left, you do this a lot and I respect that."

"No minister, no no no, I find that offensive," Rowland replied. "I'm asking you; I'm exercised about what is right and wrong."

"Oh come on Michael," Mr Hunt said. "There is nobody who is watching who doesn't identify you with the left, and you should be open about that. I'm open about my origins."

Other leaders have sought political credit for government policy during the pandemic, including South Australia's Liberal Premier Steven Marshall, who on Tuesday posted a state government ad on his Facebook page boasting of 1 million COVID-19 tests.

Government advertising stepped up significantly when Kevin Rudd was in power from 2007 to 2010, according to Dr Hughes, who said Mr Hunt would have escaped criticism if he had left out the Liberal Party's name and symbol.

"To use the brand logo makes it really obvious that you have attached that policy to a party's policy," he said.

After the exchange, Labor leader Anthony Albanese accused Mr Hunt of breaching ministerial standards, saying: "If the Liberal Party is paying for vaccines, then they can post with Liberal Party logos on it. But if it's the Australian taxpayer, then quite frankly it's an abuse, it's completely inappropriate."

Mr Hunt said he was a "very proud" member of his party, although he took pains in the on-air exchange not to mention the Liberal Party by name.

Rowland, who posted a clip of the interview on Twitter, denied he was politically biased and said Mr Hunt would be welcome back on the show.

"No, minister, I don't identify with the left," he wrote. "My job as an ABC journalist is to hold ALL sides of politics to account, as I was this morning."

Rowland was backed by Network Ten political editor Peter van Onselen, who said Mr Hunt had snapped "like a cornered animal".

"I guess that makes anyone who dares ask questions about (mis)use of partisan logos a left . . . so be it," he tweeted.

ABC 7.30 host Leigh Sales said Rowland's questions were relevant and valid.

"This practice by politicians of claiming 'bias' when they don't want to answer a perfectly legitimate question is repellent and needs to be called out immediately," she tweeted.

The Institute of Public Affairs free market think tank, which has complained about being banned from the ABC's The Drum current affairs show, said the questions reflected ABC journalists' obsession with Twitter.

"Good on Greg Hunt for calling out the ABC for its ideological obsessions that are completely detached from the concerns of mainstream Australians," director of communications Evan Mulholland said.

There is a long history of complaints from right-wing politicians and commentators about what they argue is the ABC's bias. In 2018 the Liberal Party's federal council voted in favour of privatisation of the national broadcaster, a non-binding decision that the Coalition government ignored.
direction: neutral to slightly bearish for political risk-sensitive assets

headline: Step One makes a good show on IPO
text:
Greg Taylor, the founder of bamboo fibre underwear company Step One, made an extra $150 million in an instant when the group he founded in 2017 made a barnstorming debut on the ASX yesterday.

But he is looking beyond a stellar debut to building a much bigger, long-term business that sells its products online using irreverent marketing and advertising.

"Obviously a bump on open is a good thing," he said. "It's a vote of confidence I think in where the company is headed."

Step One had an issue price of $1.53 in its initial public offering when the company raised $81.3 million via the issue of new shares, but the stock rose sharply in its opening trades and remained strong throughout the day. It closed at $2.75, a whopping 79.7 per cent above the issue price, after reaching an intra-day high of $2.84.

Mr Taylor was an elite rower who represented Australia in the Men's Eights and Men's Fours in world championships in the under-23s level almost two decades ago.

He created the designs after having problems with cotton underwear, with the bamboo fibre solving the problem of chafing, "riding up", and the need for constant adjustment for larger-framed people.

Mr Taylor had 100 per cent share of the company before the listing, and now has a stake of 66.4 per cent at the completion of the offer. His 123 million shares in the company are worth $338 million on paper.

He said he was transfixed on building Step One into a much larger business, but was chuffed that his parents, Colleen and Maurie, could come to Sydney from their Bendigo home in Victoria for the ASX listing ceremony.

Mr Taylor said the company would not be going over the top in celebrating and a small dinner was planned. "The real celebration is getting here," he said, referring to the journey to becoming a public company.

The chairman of Step One is David Gallop, a former CEO of both the National Rugby League competition and Australia's governing body for soccer, Football Federation Australia.

The company sources organically grown and certified bamboo from China. The underwear comes from China and Vietnam. Bamboo uses 65 per cent less water than cotton and Mr Taylor said he believed investors were also on board because of ethical considerations.

"I think people are really thinking about these things."

About $12 million of the fund raising is directed to advertising and brand expenditure for an entry into the US market. Step One started its US push last month and Mr Taylor said, while it was early days, it felt similar to Step One's expansion into Britain last year. "It's very similar to our UK entry. There's a huge opportunity for us," he said.

The company forecasts revenue to rise 20 per cent to $73.9 million in financial year 2022 from $61.7 million in the year ended June 30; net profit is expected to climb to $10.5 million from last year's $7.7 million.

Step One makes 45 per cent of its sales from repeat customers with a 6 per cent share of Australia's men's underwear market, and plans to enter the women's underwear category.

He said the group was carefully watching the shipping and logistics disruptions affecting industries worldwide. Step One had moved early and deliberately to build up extra inventory levels, with six to nine months' worth of stock in warehouses.
direction: Step One shares rise

headline: Melbourne
text:
SOCIETY

80 Collins Street, Melbourne

■ There were a few speed humps on the long road to Society's launch, not least of which was the shock departure of original chef Martin Benn. But here's the thing: judging by the big-spending crowds nightly filling up this second-floor fine-diner overlooking the Paris end of Collins Street, you could only conclude it was all grist for the publicity mill.

Le tout Melbourne is here. They're here for the super-glam bar, for the wow of the wine list, for the warmth of service from some of the city's best, and for the luxe, luxe, luxe menu from new chef Luke Headon. Patagonian toothfish, southern rock lobster, caviar, pearl meat, crystal snow crab, smoked wagyu prime rib - it's all here, and they're lapping it up. High society, indeed.

And also ... Society's two other restaurants, also at 80 Collins; Lillian Terrace, a casual all-day brasserie; and Yakimono, a 200-seat, charcoal-fired Japanese grill.

DI STASIO PIZZERIA

224 Faraday Street, Carlton

■ Most pizzerias are neighbourhood joints, not destination restaurants. But then, most pizzerias don't have the combined forces of Ronnie Di Stasio and Mallory Wall behind them. The third venue from the famed restaurateurs has a courtyard with 17th-century Roman fountain, significant artworks and custom-grown ingredients for "the perfect margherita". Worth crossing town for, we think.

NOMAD

Adelphi Hotel, 187 Flinders Lane, Melbourne

■ Nomad by name, nomad - finally - by nature, after the eight-year-old Sydney restaurant beat a string of mainly pandemic-related odds to set up camp in Melbourne's Flinders Lane. Like its Surry Hills sister, the new Nomad shares an executive chef (Jacqui Challinoor), a focus on cooking over fire and a commitment to dealing almost exclusively with small producers, in wine as much as in terrific, crowd-pleasing food. Outside that, we're assured it's a new venture entirely, with a proudly all-Victorian identity.

Restaurateur's take: "What we've all been through has given everyone a greater appreciation for what restaurants do. The joy on our diners' faces when a glass of wine is poured for them at the right temperature, in the right glass, really tickles our fancy." - Bec Yazbek, co-owner

AUTERRA WINE BAR

1160 High Street, Armadale

■ Sure, there's no shortage of wine bars in our capital cities and beyond as operators tap into our collective need for more impulse-driven dining out. But not many of them can boast a chef of the calibre of Amaru's Clinton McIver, nor a menu that amounts to a delicious procession of bonsai plates of food. Tiny tartlets of Great Ocean Road duck parfait; a single oyster with charred cucumber dressing. Plus, they even serve their red wines at the correct, coolish temperature. And there's definitely a shortage of that.L&L

●iAnd also ...

South Yarra's chic Yugen Tea Bar is part salon, part specialist café and part dessert degustation diner. As of February, it will also be a 150-seat Asian restaurant downstairs from the ground-floor bar.

Anchor Elwood is

the new, mainly Mediterranean home for restaurateur Rosalin Virnik,

ex-Curly Whiskers.

Prolific chef/restaurateur Scott Pickett is set to open another two ventures in the new year: Smith St Bistro in Collingwood followed by Audrey's, a new seafood-focused restaurant in the revamped Continental Sorrento.

We're joining the queues for the swank new eating bar at the just-opened butcher store Victor Churchill, in Armadale.
direction: supportive for hospitality and luxury consumer stocks

headline: AMP Capital warned of rule breach
text:
AMP Capital was warned by the $60 billion industry fund Cbus it may have been breaching fiduciary rules requiring the company to act in clients' best interests, as Cbus struggled to extract a $550 million stake in a key property fund.

Cbus was forced to wait more than two years to retrieve its stake from the $5 billion AMP Capital Diversified Property Fund, despite being at the head of a queue that became a $2.5 billion line of redemption requests.

The friction between AMP Capital and its client is a further indication of the soured relationships that triggered a 92.94 per cent investor vote last month to merge the fund with rival property management group Dexus, rather than continue under the management of AMP.

AMP Capital did not pay out the super fund, which made the request at the start of 2019, until after February this year and before it had sold a $71 million business park site in Macquarie Park and a separate $300 million office park in the same location.

This was despite Cbus noting that the gearing ratios in the Diversified Property Fund were low enough to allow the redemption without necessarily affecting other investors in the fund.

After publicly raising the issue in May last year, Cbus was paid out half its stake in the Diversified Property Fund, but the remaining $250 million was not realised until after February.

Before it received the remainder of its money, Cbus was compelled to write to AMP Capital warning the fund it may have breached its fiduciary responsibilities as it believed the fund had no real reason not to pay out the stake, according to sources familiar with the situation.

"That was the problem with AMP Capital - they were acting like it was their own money and not the money of their clients," one source said.

The independent AMP Capital Funds Management Board is responsible for making decisions around capital management of the fund and aims to act in the best interests of all investors in a fund. AMP contends that the fund's capital management strategy required waiting for property markets to stabilise to allow the sale of non-core assets to finance the string of redemptions.

An AMP Capital spokeswoman said: "At all times the responsible entity of ADPF and AMP Capital acted in accordance with the terms of the fund constitution and its fiduciary obligations to act in the best interests of ADPF unit holders as a whole."

Ming Long, chairman of AMP Capital Funds Management and a regular participant on ABC talk shows, last month revealed clients were pulling their funds from the Diversified Property Fund on the advice of consultants "citing their concerns in relation to perceptions about AMP's culture and corporate governance" following the promotion of controversial executive Boe Pahari.

Takeover discussions for AMP Capital with US-based asset manager Ares Management were troubled by a spat between investors in AMP Capital's Diversified Property Fund, the fund's independent board, and rival property group Dexus, which lodged a takeover offer for the fund.

The merger of the fund with Dexus has since focused attention on the future of AMP Capital's $28 billion real estate business.

The $7 billion AMP Capital Wholesale Office Fund is expected to come under pressure next, with investment bank Jarden Australia already appointed to consider its options. Major fund managers such as ASX-listed Charter Hall, Lendlease, GPT, Mirvac and Canadian-backed Investa could all be expected to present their credentials.

Former AMP Capital head of property Carmel Hourigan left the group to join Charter Hall after the promotion of Mr Pahari to the head of AMP Capital last July. He was demoted in August and was last month said to be poised to depart the company.

Dexus remains an interested party, as it takes control of $584 million in AMP Wholesale Office Fund units held by ADPF. The merger agreement will also bring under Dexus' wing a $140 million investment in the AMP Capital Shopping Centre Fund, a vehicle that also might be pushed into play.

Dexus has earmarked $2.1 billion series of assets for sale - including stakes in trophy malls such as Indooroopilly in Brisbane - and committed another $400 million in liquidity by acquiring units in the other AMP Capital-run funds that are held by ADPF.
direction: bearish for AMP Capital shares and property sector

headline: New rules if you put too much into super
text:
Expert view It is almost always better to take the refund option with excess contributons, writes Meg Heffron.

It's well-known that there are limits on how much can be put into super every year. We call these limits "contribution caps" and the size of the cap depends on the type of contribution.

"Concessional contributions" include things such as employer contributions, salary-sacrifice amounts or amounts people contribute themselves for which they claim a tax deduction. The concessional contribution cap increased to $27,500 from $25,000 on July 1, which is a good thing for those wanting to put more into their super fund.

But there's also a more subtle change that has just been passed by Parliament (for contributions made on or after July 1, 2021) that relates to the consequences of going over the concessional contributions cap.

Until this change, there were three consequences:

■The excess (ie, the amount of the contribution over and above the cap) was retrospectively added to the individual's personal taxable income for the year in which the contribution was made and taxed like other income, less a special tax offset of 15 per cent. This usually resulted in an extra tax bill being issued to the individual;

■There was a special additional amount to pay called the "excess concessional contributions charge". Conceptually this was like interest on the extra tax bill; and

■Unless the excess (with some adjustments) was taken out of superannuation, it would count towards the individual's other contribution cap - the limit on non-concessional contributions. (These are the contributions people make from their own money without claiming a tax deduction.) If this in turn caused the individual to exceed their non-concessional cap, more consequences applied, which generally meant the money was taken out of super.

Conceptually the three make sense together. Someone going over the concessional contributions cap is effectively getting too much into super on a very tax-preferred basis.

So the first step was designed to reverse this and make sure the money is taxed just like it would have been if received directly by the individual. The 15 per cent tax offset reflects the fact that the super fund would have already paid tax on this contribution - so taxing it again at full marginal tax rates would be unfair.

The second step was to provide an extra incentive not to exceed the cap - by charging interest on the "underpaid tax" (and the interest is calculated in a fairly harsh way).

The third step was designed to ensure that excess concessional contributions didn't provide a roundabout path to getting more into super than the law allowed. One way or another, it would be limited by a cap.

The change will remove the second step - there will no longer be an excess concessional contributions charge.

This doesn't suddenly make it enormously attractive to exceed the concessional contributions cap. There is certainly no real tax incentive to do so (the first step takes care of that). And it won't allow a whole lot more money into super that wouldn't otherwise be possible (the third step takes care of that). It just removes an extra sting in the tail for people who do it by accident.

It does mean there's an opportunity to use excess concessional contributions as a mechanism to delay paying tax but I suspect this is more theoretical than practical.

For example, imagine Ivy who is normally on the highest personal tax rate (45 per cent + Medicare levy of 2 per cent). She deliberately asks her employer to salary-sacrifice $50,000 extra (over and above her concessional contributions cap) into super. That means she initially avoids the personal income tax on this amount ($23,500) and the super fund pays tax at only 15 per cent ($7,500) - an apparent saving of $16,000. After a while (but possibly not for a year or two after she made the contribution), she will receive the extra tax bill for $16,000. The fact that she won't also be charged interest on this $16,000 means she has had a benefit of sorts - she has managed to hold on to an extra $16,000 until the ATO caught up with her. But it's unlikely to be an enormous benefit.

One particular word of warning on these excess contributions is that when the ATO first flags that one has arisen, it will make it clear that the money can be refunded from super (rather than being left in the fund and counting towards the non-concessional contributions cap).

It is almost always beneficial to take the refund option. Some people will need it to pay the extra tax bill!

But even if this isn't the case, there are downsides to just leaving the money in super. In this example, it would mean that the whole $50,000 would count towards Ivy's $110,000 non-concessional contributions cap. This is despite the fact that only $42,500 of it is left in the fund (after the 15 per cent tax paid by the super fund) and even more tax will have been paid by Ivy personally. The whole $42,500 (plus earnings) will also be part of her super that is taxed if it is taken out before she turns 60 or if it is inherited by someone who is not a dependant - such as an adult child.

By contrast, refunding the $42,500 out of super and then putting it back in as a non-concessional contribution gets a totally different result. Ivy only uses up $42,500 of her cap and it goes into the "tax-free" bucket in her super - so it can come out tax-free when she is eligible to draw on it no matter who ultimately receives it or how old she is. SI

Meg Heffron is managing director at SMSF specialist firm Heffron.
direction: bearish for high-net-worth individuals and superannuation fund contributions

headline: Only connect: e-gift cards get personal
text:
These are the top five innovations in the retail sector.

Prezzee

Innovation: Personalised e-gift card

Staff: 20-99

A lack of human connection because of lockdowns was just one of the casualties of COVID-19 - but people still wanted a way to mark special occasions. With that in mind, e-gift card business Prezzee launched a feature that allowed the giver to record a personalised video or audio message for no additional cost.

It was Prezzee's way of transforming the somewhat impersonal gift card into something special, and helped to connect people when they were unable to connect in person.

Recognising that this was an international issue, the company also launched in the United States and Britain.

To support corporate gift-giving, Prezzee introduced a co-branded card and personalised message template for businesses to connect with their customers and employees.

Chief executive Tony Karp says: "During the pandemic we were bowled over in terms of how corporates want to take care of their people and send care packages."

Companies shared everything from a virtual morning tea with coffee and pastry delivered to people's homes to hampers and cocktails.

"Realistically if we stop thinking about it as giving a gift card, we are allowing one human to connect to another, whether company to customers or staff, or person to person," says Karp.

Winner of the Best Innovation in the Retail Sector.

Hunt Hospitality

Innovation: Shoeless Jack

Staff: 100-499

Australia's accommodation and food services sector employs about 881,000 people, or 6.7 per cent of the total workforce, the Australian Bureau of Statistics says. The government's labour market information portal forecasts that 139,000 more people will be needed to work across the sector by 2025 as businesses get back on their feet.

It's a sector that has taken a huge knock from the pandemic - the number of hospitality workers fell 44 per cent in the last quarter, the ABS says. But as venues start to open again, there will be a rush to find staff. Online recruitment business Seek says job listings in the hospitality and tourism sector leapt 28.5 per cent in September as businesses geared up to reopen.

This is a sector that regularly deals with both labour and skills shortages. Shoeless Jack, created by Hunt Hospitality, has been designed to provide workers with instant access to expert advice that can help people new to the business.

Jack can answer an array of hospitality industry questions, with hundreds of internal processes converted to a step-by-step process for best practice. From cash-handling routines to learning to pour the perfect beer with just the right amount of head, Jack can provide advice and support.

Jack has also been adopted as the basis for a small bar franchise model, also named Shoeless Jack, that is under construction, where Jack's digital step-by-step processes will be put to the test in a standalone venue.

Coles Group

Innovation: Click&Collect Rapid

Staff: 500+

Customer demand for more convenient shopping options is growing - and took a big leap during the pandemic. In response, Coles introduced its Click&Collect Rapid service to 405 stores over two weeks in February - allowing customers to order from an expanded selection and pick up groceries in 90 minutes, compared with the previous best turnaround time of about four hours.

To use the service, orders must be placed online (with a minimum spend of $30 and as many as 40 items) for as many as 40 items between 1pm and 3.30pm. For a flat fee of $5, the order will be picked, packed and ready for collection at a specified time between 2.30pm and 5pm. Customers are alerted when the order is ready by email and SMS.

"Click&Collect Rapid has taken the stress out of running to the supermarket, with over 70 per cent of our customers satisfied with the ease of the ordering and collection experience and the wait times of less than five minutes," says James Geddes, Coles Online head of network development and customer delivery.

"The 90-minute order-to-collection time means customers can place an order and pick it up on the way to school pick-up or home, taking the pressure off busy parents and workers, as well as opening the door to a range of quick, convenient, and most importantly, healthy meal options, like our hot roast chicken - giving them an alternative to fast food when they need food fast."

Ikon Services

Innovation: Eco Bin Washing System

Staff: 500+

The two-metre headroom at the Crown Casino car park meant that when the on-site bins needed cleaning, Ikon Services had to send a buggy and tug, with lots of manual bin handling. To streamline things, Ikon bought a Hino 616 truck, lowered it so that it could fit into the car park, then modified the truck to create an eco-friendly bin-washing system.

Careful engineering made sure the bin lift function did not hit the car park roof during operation but still kept the truck high enough to clear speed humps and ramps. The Hino came with a diesel engine but can switch to electric mode in idle while cleaning is under way. The modified truck is equipped with an electric pressure washer, lithium battery pack, rotary spray jets, a water tank, motion and light sensors. Ikon has patented the design.

While site-specific, the innovation could be applied at other places with similar needs, says Estelle Lewis, group executive general manager, strategic partners at Ikon Services. "It's an excellent example of our ability to solve problems specific to the client and our commitment to R&D for all clients," says Lewis.

Muffin Break

Innovation: Lower carb gluten-free muffin

Staff: 20-99

Research suggests Australia's gluten-free food and beverages market will grow 7.8 per cent a year until 2025. The once-specialty niche is now considered mainstream and demand is climbing, Mordor Intelligence says.

Muffin Break and PBCo, responding to that trend and growing general interest in healthier foodstuffs, have developed a lower carb, gluten-free muffin mix, which is the base for Muffin Break's lower carb gluten-free muffin range available nationwide in its stores. The mix is also available in a retail pack for customers to bake at home. Containing 50 per cent less carbohydrates than a usual Muffin Break muffin, the mix has been designed to be 97 per cent sugar-free and gluten-free.AFR
direction: bullish for Prezzee and the retail technology sector

headline: Forrest's AIP gets grant for gas-fired plant
text:
Australia's richest man, Andrew "Twiggy" Forrest, has been formally awarded a $30 million grant from the Coalition government for early works on Australian Industrial Power's (AIP) Port Kembla gas-fired power station.

The gas plant, which is also the site for a proposed LNG import terminal, is being considered by the federal government for its Underwriting New Generation Investments (UNGI) program.

Although the federal government has awarded the $30 million grant, first flagged in the budget in May, it is a disallowable instrument that means the money could be challenged by Labor and the Greens in the Senate.

The grant will be used to help AIP doing early works on the project as it moves to a final investment decision.

AIP is owned by Twiggy's private company, Squadron Energy, and critics question why the second-richest Australian after Gina Rinehart needs a government handout to get the project off the ground.

The 650-megawatt Port Kembla plant will start as a gas-fired facility, but will have the potential to switch progressively to hydrogen down the track.

The AIP project would play a crucial role in reducing market volatility risks in NSW by supporting reliable electricity supply and keeping prices low, Energy Minister Angus Taylor said.

Progressing the AIP gas power station would support the development of the Port Kembla import terminal and help boost gas supply on the east coast, the minister said.

"This is what the government's gas-fired recovery is about - helping Australia bounce back strongly from the COVID-19 recession" Mr Taylor said.

"New gas supply and generation will help re-establish a strong economy and make energy more affordable for families and businesses, while complementing the world-leading levels of renewables in Australia. Gas supports jobs in our important energy intensive industries that are the lifeblood of our economy and our regions."

The construction of the terminal and power station will create about 850 direct jobs, and many more indirect jobs.
direction: bearish for AIP/Squadron Energy due to political and funding uncertainty
