headline: Challenger dials down risk ahead of bank approval
text:
Challenger has dialled down its risk-taking as its commits to holding more regulatory capital and lowers its profitability target ahead of approval from the prudential watchdog for its purchase of a small, deposit-taking bank.

The decision to lower its return-on-equity target to 12 per cent above the Reserve Bank of Australia cash rate, down from 14 per cent, and to increase the upper band of its prescribed capital amount (the amount of regulatory capital it holds) to 1.7 times, up from 1.6 times, comes less than two months after Challenger revised its full-year profit forecast to the "bottom end" of its target range in April.

In February Challenger said it would miss its then-target return rate of 14 per cent over the cash rate as investors digested a decision by the company to cut its interim dividend in half.

In an address to investors yesterday, Challenger chief executive Richard Howes said increased capital holdings and a lower profit target would give the company increased flexibility in periods of market volatility.

"This is good for shareholders. It aligns with our purpose, which is all about financial security," he said.

"Naturally, the lower risk profile leads to lower returns.

"It reflects an appropriate risk-return trade off."

It is the second time in two years Challenger has lowered its ROE target, after Mr Howes in 2019 abandoned the 18 per cent target (irrespective of the RBA cash rate) that the company had held for more than a decade.

Mr Howes reaffirmed Challenger's full-year underlying profit would come in at the lower end of its $390 million to $440 million guidance.

He also lifted guidance for the 2022 financial year, flagging an expected underlying profit of between $430 million and $480 million.

Mr Howes said the decision to lower the risk-reward plans was a "conscious" decision that did not count as an earnings downgrade.

Challenger in December announced the purchase of Catholic Super's banking arm, MyLife MyFinance, for $35 million in a bid to crack open the trillion-dollar Australian term deposit market.

While the company expected to have the deal completed in late March, the Australian Prudential Regulation Authority is still yet to grant Challenger approval for the purchase.

Presentation notes released by Challenger yesterday said it has targeted completion of the transaction by June or July and the APRA process was "progressing well".

Mr Howes said APRA had a lot on its plate, particularly in the neobank sector. Xinja recently came to an untimely demise after APRA got the bank to hand back its banking licence after a period of struggling, while there has been a flurry of corporate activity, such as the takeover of neobank 86 400 by National Australia Bank.

"We originally expected an earlier completion for this transaction," Mr Howes said.

"We remain optimistic that we will complete in July."

Challenger, which is Australia's largest provider of longevity insurance products known as annuities, offers customers the opportunity to trade a lump sum for a guaranteed income for life by matching liabilities with investment returns.

Challenger is well known to APRA, which oversaw the company's risk-taking investment strategy that backed its long-dated annuities.

Challenger operates a $20 billion investment portfolio for its life insurance business, which backs the annuities operations, of which about $6 billion is invested in corporate bonds and $7 billion in asset-backed securities.

Challenger shares are about 60 per cent lower than the peak above $14 hit in late 2017.
direction: downward pressure on Challenger shares due to lower profitability targets

headline: Amadeus MARCO POLO
text:
Our experts take a look at a global stock.

Shares in air travel booking company Amadeus IT continue a slow ascent after a plunge during the pandemic.

The vaccine rollout is progressing well in most of the world and this is allowing countries to ease travel restrictions and reopen borders. Having made significant cost cuts during the downturn, Amadeus is in a good position to continue to accelerate earnings as vaccination rollouts complete and the world economy reopens.

In the meantime, its results remain poor, albeit with an improving trend. First-half revenue in 2021 was down 60.3 per cent to €1121 million compared with the first half of 2019, while earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortisation (EBITDA) fell 83.2 per cent to €199 million.

By comparison, revenue fell 64.7 per cent and EBITDA declined 91 per cent in the first quarter of 2021 compared with the first quarter in 2019, so the company's performance is slowly improving in line with better conditions in the travel sector.

Notably, June was the best month so far this year and also the best month since the start of the pandemic.

In terms of Amadeus' 2021 second-quarter results in distribution, which provides indirect travel services distribution between travel agencies and providers, all regions saw an improvement in volumes compared with the first quarter.

Distribution revenue fell by 66.4 per cent, to €267.6 million, compared with the second quarter of 2019, an improvement on the 77.1 per cent revenue decline in the first quarter of 2021 versus 2019.

The IT solutions division provides mission-critical passenger management solutions to travel providers, with airlines the main client base. Growth rates for passengers boarded improved each month in the first half of 2021, with June again being the best month, where the number of passengers boarded was down by 63.4 per cent. Most regions saw a better second quarter versus the first.

This resulted in IT solutions revenue being down by 42.8 per cent to €356.8 million versus the second quarter of 2019, helped by business not directly linked to airline traffic or driven by transactions, including airport IT and hospitality. Again, this is an improvement on the 46.3 per cent fall in revenue in the first quarter of 2021.

Second-quarter revenue fell by 56 per cent (to €624.4 million) and EBITDA by 75.3 per cent (to €145.3 million) compared with the second quarter of 2019. EBITDA was €53.7 million in the first quarter of 2021 so despite the poor headline figures, things are slowly improving.

The company continues to sign renewals and win new business while also continuing to pursue cost reductions, with total fixed costs falling by €167.9 million so far in 2021 compared to the year before.

Amadeus incurred an adjusted loss of €106.7 million in the first half. Free cash outflow was €121.8 million, although this includes €74.8 million in implementation costs incurred as part of the company's cost-savings programme.

Net debt is €3.26 billion, but Amadeus has €3.4 billion in liquidity including €1.5 billion in cash, €871 million in short-term investments and €1 billion in unused credit facilities.

JON MILLS

Jon Mills is a research analyst at investment research and funds management house Fat Prophets. To try our daily market commentary, call 1300 881 177 or email info@fatprophets.com.au
direction: bullish for Amadeus IT shares

headline: Virgin Money gets its act together
text:
Virgin Money, the challenger brand that started with white-labelled credit cards almost 20 years ago, is finally a full service bank with a technology platform as good as anything offered by rivals.

This digital bank is arguably better than any of the retail neobanks launched in Australia over the past couple of years. Its range of products includes deposits, mortgages, superannuation and insurance.

In other words, it is a full service financial institution with a wider array of products than other neobanks as well as having the backing of a well capitalised parent.

The release this week of a suite of Virgin Money digital banking products, which ride on the back of the Temenos cloud-based platform, is a tribute to the persistence shown by Bank of Queensland.

"I think what's most exciting about Virgin Money is it brings together an entirely new cloud-based core banking platform with a native mobile app, payment system and card management," says Virgin Money chief executive Greg Boyle.

"This will be the foundation for, not just the Virgin Money brand, but all the retail brands of the BoQ group moving forward."

BoQ took 100 per cent ownership of Virgin Money Australia in 2013 after its strategy as a credit card business using Westpac Banking Corp's card processing failed.

Initially, Virgin Money was the responsibility of Matt Baxby, who was also in charge of BoQ's retail operations.

It was the poor cousin in the BoQ portfolio and did not get the attention it deserved mainly because BoQ specialist - the business banking arm focused on professionals - was far more profitable and offered higher growth.

Shortly before BoQ bought Virgin Money from Richard Branson's Virgin Group, the credit card relationship was switched to Citi.

Boyle, who has been the CEO of Virgin Money since 2016, says the jewel in Virgin Money's crown is its mortgage portfolio.

The portfolio grew by $459 million in the half year to February 28, representing annualised growth of 28 per cent on the second half of 2020 and taking the portfolio to more than $3.7 billion.

Boyle says Virgin Money conducted extensive market research before relaunching the brand, including into consumer attitudes to rewards programs.

He says Virgin Money consumers were cynical of rewards programs that offered chunks of points up front or points rewarded for large-scale transactions.

"The research told us that consumers felt that most traditional financial services rewards programs were just an add-on at the end of an experience," he says.

"So, what we sought to design and co-create with our customers is a reward program that has features and benefits that are not just a one-off acquisition hook at the beginning but a program that continues to reward customers throughout the life cycle of their relationship with us.

"It's the things that a traditional financial services provider wouldn't necessarily do such as helping save and building towards their goals, like home ownership, travel or whatever that might be, and the sort of surprise and delight features that you'd expect from a challenger brand."

Boyle says BoQ is utilising the sort of behavioural science and nudging behaviour that is recommended by leaders in financial literacy.

"It's about building their financial fitness, which is about saving more and meeting certain financial goals," he says.

"At the heart of it there are various goal-tracking devices built into the app and that help with saving to buy a home, which is one of the most prevalent ones, or it could be doing renovations, or a car loan if you're younger."

Branson took the time to give his two bob's worth about the local arm of Virgin Money. "The new Virgin Money Australia digital bank is the antithesis of a traditional bank: it embraces simplicity and rejects the notion of financial complexity," Branson said in a statement.

He will be meeting Virgin Money staff later this week by way of a Zoom call and will travel to Australia as soon as possible following his second COVID-19 vaccination jab.

Virgin Money Australia should not be confused with Virgin Money PLC, which was formed from the merger of Clydesdale Bank and Virgin Money, which previously bought Northern Rock.

The Virgin Money rewards program includes benefits from other members of the Virgin Group including Virgin Wines and Virgin Active. At this stage it does not include the Velocity airline rewards program owned by Virgin Australia.

Some of the features of the Virgin Money digital platform include biometric login, digital account creation in a few minutes, a transaction account, market competitive saving accounts and access to Apple Pay, Google Pay and Samsung Pay.

The rewards program looks to be more generous than those offered by rivals, with eight Virgin Money points for each transaction on everyday purchases, direct debit and BPAY.

BoQ's mortgage

focus is working

Bank of Queensland's focus on mortgage growth is providing the regional bank with the momentum needed to hold onto a sharemarket rerating that has helped its shares rise 78 per cent over the past year.

The stock has benefited from a combination of low interest rates, a surge in demand for housing on the east coast, a change of chief executive to George Frazis and no more skeletons coming out of the BoQ cupboard.

Yesterday, it lifted its interim dividend by 11¢ to 17¢ a share, which suggests the stock is trading on a 3.8 per cent yield before including the benefit of franking credits.

In the latest half-year results, BoQ said its housing loan growth was 1.6 times the system and accelerating. The bank said home loan application volumes had increased significantly "through increased branch and broker productivity".

Its customer satisfaction among mortgage borrowers has risen to third place this year from 11th in 2019.

At the half-year balance date of February 28, BoQ had $32.27 billion in residential mortgages, or about 58 per cent of its total loan book of $54.99 billion.

Frazis picked a good time to change banks, leaving Westpac in 2019 to become BoQ CEO in June 2019. This timing coincided with a cyclical low for the valuation of regional banks.

BoQ's trailing price-earnings multiple hit a low of 8.7 this time last year. The trailing P/E is now 34 and the prospective P/E is now 13.75, Bloomberg data show.

But it still trades at a discount to the big four banks and is a long way off catching the Commonwealth Bank of Australia, which has a forward P/E of 19.5.

The bank was positive about the economic outlook, thanks to government stimulus and the lower likelihood of "downside scenarios on unemployment".

Frazis is promising further above-system growth in lending in the year ahead.

But he did warn of cost growth of 3 per cent as the bank invests in a change program that includes switching the technology platform used by ME Bank, acquired this year for $1.2 billion.

Earnings per share rose 3 per cent in the half, compared to a 9 per cent rise in cash profits. The difference is explained by the dilution caused by $339 million capital raising in November 2019.

BoQ's shareholders could enjoy significant earnings upside if Frazis can reduce the cost base of recent acquisition ME Bank, which had the highest cost-to-income ratio of any major bank in Australia, at 75 per cent, in the year ended last June 30.

Most acquisitions target a 30 per cent cut in the costs of the acquired company. If Frazis achieves that, he will be cutting ME Bank's costs by $100 million, a move that would significantly boost profits.

The higher profits will be needed to cover the dilutionary impact of the shares issued to pay for ME Bank. BoQ's shares on issue have risen from 453 million in August 2020 to 667 million now.

Frazis promised the ME Bank acquisition would be accretive in the year to August 2022, including $70 million to $80 million in synergy benefits.

TONY BOYD

tony.boyd@afr.com
direction: bullish for Bank of Queensland shares

headline: Fair Work has 'gone off the rails' in jobs ruling: Deliveroo
text:
Deliveroo says the workplace umpire went "completely off the rails" in a landmark ruling that found one of its riders was an employee and has warned it has entered "foreign territory".

Lawyers for the on-demand food delivery company slammed the Fair Work Commission ruling yesterday in an appeal hearing, where they questioned how employers could be expected to pay minimum hourly rates in response to findings that employees can work for multiple employers simultaneously and still be employees.

The appeal could have broad implications for whether the gig economy must pay minimum pay and conditions and will test last year's full bench decision that an UberEats' rider was not an employee.

Commissioner Ian Cambridge ruled in May that terminated rider Diego Franco was an employee due to the control Deliveroo exercised over his deliveries and because new technology meant employment relationships can now contemplate employees working for competitors at the same time - what the gig economy refers to as "multi-apping".

Barrister for Deliveroo, Justin Bourke, QC, told the bench that the Transport Workers Union's suggestion that the commissioner had merely taken an orthodox approach to the employment question "defies reality".

He said the commissioner's findings did "not withstand scrutiny" and had "gone completely off the rails", including that Deliveroo's capacity to collect riders' data and metrics indicated significant control over work.

"It's pure speculation as to what may have occurred because we've got a technology that can collect data," he said. "This is uncharted waters."

The commissioner had also wrongly "diluted" the traditional point that working for competitors - in Mr Franco's case, logging on to DoorDash and UberEats while also on Deliveroo - was a powerful indication that a worker was not an employee. "The commission is clearly contemplating an arrangement ... where you are potentially working for three multiple employers at the same time, being logged on to three different apps ... but no explanation on how that works on a national minimum wage system - how you get hourly pay from that," Mr Bourke said.

"This demonstrates the foreign territory that the decision has moved in."

All this was done, he argued, without a "deep analysis" of the UberEats ruling to justify departing from its principles in "very similar" circumstances.

Instead of reinstating Mr Franco with compensation, Deliveroo has reached a deal to pay him $300 a week until its appeal of the decision is determined.

TWU lawyer Mark Gibian, SC, representing Mr Franco, told the bench the fact a worker can work for two or more employers at the same time should no longer strongly point to them being a contractor.

He gave the example of an administrative staffer who works part-time for two employers from home, rather than in different offices on separate days, and interrupts work for one employer to do an urgent matter for another employer.

"There's no fundamental difference in the nature of that relationship merely because the tasks are more intermingled than historically may have been common," he said.

He argued that employers' control over employees was primarily about their control over the manner in which work was done rather than when it was done.

The bench has reserved its decision.
direction: bearish for gig economy and delivery sector stocks

text:
You’d be forgiven for thinking you need to be golf-mad to call Fingal home. Playing host to the Moonah Links and St Andrews Beach golf clubs, this small Mornington Peninsula community has more greens than main roads.

But while Kay & Burton Flinders agent Andrew Hines agrees the area is a golfer’s paradise – there are, in fact, 18 golf courses on the Mornington Peninsula – he says Fingal’s very central location is one of its key drawcards.

“It’s 15 minutes from Portsea and Sorrento and about 20 minutes from Flinders and Red Hill,” Hines says. “So, for a lot of people who move here, it offers flexibility for where friends and family might be, and what they might do when they’re down here.

“The wineries and bushwalks aren’t far away, you’ve got sailing and beaches, and it goes without saying that you’re surrounded by all of the golf courses.”

Country meets coast

Retired builder Homer Koster chose Fingal for its country-like ambience.

“We have lovely country views in Fingal – we don’t look over the golf course, but out over farmland and our one neighbour is hidden behind trees,” he says. “Every day we count the cattle roaming around. It’s a lovely place to live; you do feel like you’re in the country away from it all.”

Koster splits his time between a Melbourne apartment and the peninsula, spending two to three days in the city each week. This, Hines says, is a popular approach taken by residents, who become enamoured of the lifestyle and want to spend more than just weekends on the peninsula.

“What we’ve learned in the past 12 months is how to work successfully from home and that we don’t need to travel so much,” he says. “People are only travelling into town two or three days a week, [turning] the Mornington Peninsula into an economy opened all year round.”

Growing amenity

Hines says there’s very little on offer in Melbourne that you can’t get down on the peninsula, from live music, great pubs and restaurants to art galleries, day-spas and nurseries.

This month an impressive $37 million aquatic centre, Yawa, opened in Rosebud, and popular pizzeria 400 Gradi has just opened a sprawling venue in Main Street, Mornington, with a restaurant, bar, gelateria and delicatessen.

Of course, the region’s growing popularity means real estate stock across the whole of the peninsula is in high demand, and the limited number of properties is driving prices north.

“It’s a real luxury when you’ve got the flexibility of [owning] a property on the Peninsula, work from home and enjoy the lifestyle,” Hines says.
direction: supportive for Mornington Peninsula residential property values

headline: Megapub in western Sydney sells for $70m
text:
A megapub in western Sydney has changed hands for almost $70 million in the biggest deal this year, as watering holes spring back to life following the end of the city's 106-day lockdown.

Highlighting the strong appetite for pub real estate after a record volume of sales in the prior financial year, the Vineyard Hotel in the outer north-western suburbs sold within 24 hours of an expression of interest campaign closing. It was put up for sale in August by the Stanford family, which had owned it for more than four decades.

Occupying a huge 7.7 hectare site on the corner of Windsor and Boundary Roads in Vineyard, about an hour from the Sydney CBD, the hotel includes 57 motel rooms and development approval to add 84 rooms on unoccupied land on the site.

The buyer, who has yet to be identified, owns multiple large format hotels in Sydney and elsewhere, according to Dan Dragicevich, Andrew Jolliffe and Sam Handy from HTL Property, who brokered the deal.

The sale smashed expectations of about $60 million and topped the $55 million paid by Iris Capital for Manly's Ivanhoe Hotel in February.

Mr Jolliffe said his agency received multiple offers above $60 million.

"The sale of the Vineyard Hotel speaks to a new benchmark in terms of both pricing and yield," Mr Jolliffe said.

In addition, he said there was half a billion dollars of unsatisfied under bidder capital seeking investment opportunities.

The Stanford family will soon start the sale of another large-format venue as it exits the sector.

Price expectations are understood to be above $50 million for the 17,000 square metre Carousel Inn in Sydney's Rooty Hill, which will also be marketed by HTL. The family also owns the Pub@Rivo in Riverstone, which will also come to market later this year.

The sale of Vineyard Hotel follows more than $1.3 billion of pubs changing hands over the 2021 financial year - a record, despite the disruption caused by the pandemic, according to HTL Property figures.

In another NSW deal brokered by HTL, the Loaded Dog Hotel in Tarago in the Southern Tablelands north of Canberra sold for $2.5 million on a yield of 6.64 per cent to a Sydney-based investor.

Featuring a bar, bistro, beer garden, gaming room and 11 rooms, the Loaded Dog was sold by long-term owners Mark and Nicole Ryan.

The pub is leased to long-time managers Tim and Alannah Long.

Selling agents Blake Edwards and Sam Handy said the sales campaign yielded multiple offers to purchase.

In Beenleigh, 35 kilometres south of Brisbane, ASX-listed fund manager Charter Hall paid $6.62 million for the Imperial Hotel, which is leased to Woolworths-backed operator Endeavour Group. It sold on a 4.9 per cent yield through Leon Alaban and Michael Harcourt of Savills.

The pub was sold by ALE Property, which is due to be acquired for $1.7 billion by the Charter Hall Long WALE REIT and industry fund Hostplus.
direction: bullish for pub real estate and hospitality REITs

headline: Treasury Wine buys luxury Californian winery for $434m
text:
The chief executive of Treasury Wine Estates has promised the high quality of the wines produced by luxury chardonnay maker Frank Family Vineyards in California's Napa Valley will be maintained as it looks to step up volumes after a $US315 million ($434 million) acquisition.

Tim Ford said the quality would not be diminished as it sourced extra grapes from its own vineyards in California and contracted growers to expand the volumes of the No.2 player in the luxury chardonnay market in the United States.

"We simply won't do it," Mr Ford said. "We are not going to compromise the quality of these wines. We'd be silly to adjust the formulations."

Frank Family Vineyards was established in 1992 by Rich Frank and sells wines in price brackets from $US38 to $US225 per bottle across grape varieties including chardonnay, cabernet sauvignon, pinot noir and sparkling wine.

The flagship wine is a luxury chardonnay that is No.2 behind market leader Rombauer Vineyards.

Mr Ford said the acquisition would sit well with the company's existing brands in the US and was a high-margin business delivering 37.9 per cent earnings margins, compared with the current Treasury Americas operations that generate overall margins of 17.2 per cent.

The Frank Family Vineyards business had increased revenues every year since 2009 and was largely unaffected by the pandemic, selling all the product made by its winemakers.

"They have sold what they've made," Mr Ford said. "The chardonnay is the big prize for us."

Treasury Wines expects to unlock more potential as it brings the business into its own operation, which has a much larger distribution network in the US and lifts volumes by making more wine. It also aims to pull out $US5 million in cost synergies.

Frank Family Vineyards sells about 50 per cent of its wine in independent liquor stores, 40 per cent in restaurants and bars, and 10 per cent direct to the consumer.

Treasury Wine Estates has been steadily exiting from lower-priced commercial wine operations in America and its strategy is to shift further into the premium end of the US market. Its once lucrative China business has been hammered by tariffs imposed by the Chinese government, which have undermined overall profit growth.

Mr Ford said the US wine market more broadly was gathering momentum as the economy opened up and the on-premise trade returned.

Frank Family Vineyards marks the biggest acquisition in the United States in six years for Treasury Wines, which acquired most of alcoholic beverages giant Diageo's wine division for $754 million in late 2015, under previous chief executive Mike Clarke.

Frank Family Vineyards produced net sales revenue of $US54 million in 2020-21 and US$21 million in earnings before interest, tax and the SGARA agricultural accounting standard.

The assets being acquired include the Frank Family Tasting Room in Calistoga, the Benjamin Vineyard in Rutherford, the S&J Vineyard in Capell Valley, and the portfolio of luxury wines. About 30,000 visitors attended the cellar door in 2019, before the pandemic.

Under the deal, Rich and Leslie Frank will maintain the ownership of Winston Hill Vineyard in Rutherford and the Lewis Vineyard in Napa, Carneros. Both vineyards will continue to source grapes for the Frank Family reserve tier of wines.

The acquisition is being funded by the combination of a new $US240 million underwritten acquisition bridge facility and cash from proceeds of US commercial wine divestments.

Mr Ford said Treasury Wines had largely completed its program to divest non-priority US portfolio brands and assets, with total net cash proceeds of around $300 million in Australian dollars. Some of the brands offloaded include Beringer Main & Vine and Beringer Founders Estate.

One of Treasury Wines' fastest-growing brands in the US is 19 Crimes, which sells at a much lower price than the Frank Family Vineyards portfolio.

The 19 Crimes brand, fronted by a big marketing and advertising push featuring American rapper Snoop Dogg, has grown into a 5-million-case-a-year business around the world, but generates most of its sales in the US.
direction: Treasury Wine Estates shares higher

headline: Lang Hancock Mining 1950s -1990s
text:
On a stormy day in 1952, West Australian grazier and mineral prospector Lang Hancock was piloting a light plane through the Pilbara region, about 1300 kilometres north of Perth. Forced by bad weather to fly low through a gorge and "alerted by the rusty looking colour", he chanced upon a huge iron ore province.

Hancock's "discovery" confirmed the view of WA government geologist Harry Page Woodward in 1890 that the Pilbara was "an iron ore country" with "enough iron ore to supply the whole world".

Born into a wealthy farming family, educated at the exclusive Hale School in Perth, a one-time owner of an asbestos mine in WA, and a believer in small government, Hancock demanded an end to the ban on iron ore exports - a legacy of pre-war fears about Japan using Australian iron ore to manufacture steel for re-armament.

Hancock lobbied furiously for a decade and in 1961 revealed his "discovery" and staked his claim. A consortium including the Rio Tinto majority-owned CRA secured mine development capital by negotiating long-term contracts with Japanese steel mills to export iron ore from the Pilbara's Mount Tom Price. Hancock negotiated a 2.5 per cent royalty on future sales, which he initially shared with his partner, Peter Wright.

Successful pressure for the lifting of all federal and state restrictions on iron ore mining was an arduous process, largely undertaken by Hancock. But mine development work was well under way by 1964, and in 1966 the first shipment of iron ore from Mount Tom Price arrived in Japan.

In 1970 Trevor Kennedy, then a senior journalist on The Australian Financial Review and later managing director of Kerry Packer's Consolidated Press Holdings, reported that Hancock - "probably the single most important figure responsible for the burgeoning developments now occurring in WA" - was planning to float a public company, Hanwright, with Peter Wright.

Hancock "showed the Financial Review the unbelievable massive deposits which exist in this area", Kennedy wrote.

Hancock held strong conservative views, although he had a falling out with WA's powerful minister for industrial development, Charles Court, who was then in charge of developments in the Pilbara.

His daughter, Gina Rinehart, is now executive chairman of Hancock Prospecting, and in a 50-50 partnership with Rio Tinto in the Pilbara's giant Hope Downs iron ore mine - named after Rinehart's mother and Hancock's second wife. Rinehart is reported to be the biggest single landholder in Australia. According to the Financial Review Rich List, she is worth more than $30 billion.

ANDREW CLARK
direction: bullish for iron ore miners and the mining sector

headline: High achievers from the archives of our People in Business column
text:
Gap-toothed, 27-year-old Ronald J. Walker, managing director of Brooke Barmer Chemicals, had returned from an "eight-week tour of the US, England, France, Germany and Japan studying trends in synthetic lattices".

So The Australian Financial Review column People in Business told its readers in November 1966. Four decades later, Walker became chairman of the Fairfax company that owned the newspaper that publicised his early overseas trip.

In between, he became Lord Mayor of Melbourne, chair of the Melbourne Grand Prix, and co-owner of Melbourne Crown casino developer Hudson Conway that made him wealthy.

Like black-and-white ghosts from the pasts, the headshots from People in Business column provide glimpses of early lives of those who went onto bigger things.

There's the still-living Mr John D. Kahlbetzer, director of steel merchants Coutinho Meth and Company (Australia) who left Sydney in November 1961 for a two-month tour of the company's export markets in the Far East and Europe.

After emigrating from Germany in 1954, Kahlbetzer ventured out of steel into farm investment, founding Twynam Agriculture, becoming one of Australia's biggest landholders and expanding into Argentina. He and his family ranked 105th on this year's Financial Review Rich List with a fortune of $1.15 billion.

In April 1961, People in Business reported that Ian Potter and Co's Mr Keith W. Halkerston had been elected a member of the Stock Exchange of Melbourne.

The 26-year-old Halkerston, a grocer's son who later worked for Morgan Stanley on Wall Street, designed Australia's first cash management trust, was appointed to the Campbell Committee inquiry that led to the financial deregulation, and joined up with Mark Burrows in investment banking. Known for his formidable intellect, Halkerston died of lung cancer 30 years after this item, even though he never smoked.

In November 1960, the Financial Review reported that Herbert H. Baer had become the first member to be elected to the Melbourne Stock Exchange under a recent rule permitting people not already engaged in the securities profession to apply for membership.

In fact, as we separately reported, Baer was the first Jew to be elected to the Melbourne exchange after arriving in Australia in 1940 as one of the interred "Dunera boys" from Europe.

"Tolerance - at last", headlined the Financial Review commentary, which added that his admission to the Stock Exchange was "only one step" in the right direction.

A significant increase in the merchant banking interests of Sydney stockbroking firm Ord Minnett led to the Financial Review noticing a young James Wolfensohn, the Australian who grew up in a two-bedroom flat in Sydney's Edgecliff before becoming a young lawyer at Allen, Allen & Hemsley, the most prominent Australian on Wall Street and president of the World Bank. And playing the cello at New York's Carnegie Hall.

In June 1965, the Financial Review reported that the then 27-year-old Mr J.D. Wolfensohn was resigning his membership of the Stock Exchange and joining the board of Darling and Co., the merchant banking associate of Ord Minnett.

After having created the "Louie the Fly" jingle for Mortein, Mr Bryce Courtenay, creative director of Hansen-Rubensohn-McCann-Erickson was made a director of the advertising agency in August 1967. By 1989, he graduated from jingles to the best-selling novel The Power of One.

In June 1971, H.J. Heinz Co Australia Ltd promoted Mr P. Scanlon as general manager of marketing. Not long after, John Elliott took over the Henry Jones IXL jam company and hired Scanlon for Heinz to help turn it around.

Elliott was backed by fellow McKinsey man Roderick H. Carnegie, who in June 1968 was elected a New York-based director of McKinsey and Company Inc. after having set up the American consultant's Australian outpost. Now 88, Carnegie was later managing director and chairman of CRA, now Rio Tinto.

Victorian country sales manager Mr Kevan Gosper was appointed Shell's PNG and south-west Pacific manager for the Shell group in February 1969. By then he had won a silver medal in the 4 x 400 relay at the 1956 Melbourne Olympics. Later he became head of Shell's Pacific operations out of London and a vice-president of the International Olympics Committee.

In November 1961, James T. Dominguez was admitted to partnership in A.C. Goode and Co, following the Stock Exchange of Melbourne's decision to admit non-member partners. Dominguez became a resident Sydney partner with particular responsibility for underwriting and research.

His stock broking firm Dominguez & Barry became Dominguez Barry Samuel Montagu and later SBC Dominguez Barry, with him as chairman through the changes. "Oh, to be 31 again," Dominguez says now.

The head of the Financial Review's Melbourne office Mr Robert Norman Gottliebsen was admitted to the partnership of Sydney share broking firm Patrick Partners. But, before Patrick went bust, Gottliebsen returned to the Financial Review to start the daily Chanticleer column in 1974 and then start BRW magazine.

Before that, People in Business noted that Mr Michael Ehrenfried Baume, the former London correspondent and investment editor of the Financial Review, had been approved as a partner in Patrick.

Now aged 91, Baume later served as a Liberal Member of the House of Representatives, a Senator and Consul-General in New York.

In March 1971, Mr N.F. Stutchbury was appointed staff inspector at the head office of the Mutual Life and Citizens' Assurance Co Ltd. That's the 130-year MLC, in recent years owned by Lendlease, NAB and now IOOF.

It was half a century ago, but someone in the extended Stutchbury tribe should be able to place him.AFR
direction: no specific market direction implied

headline: Brookfield sinks $1b into Intellihub
text:
Canada's Brookfield has beaten rival bidders including BlackRock to a $1 billion stake in Australian smart meters business Intellihub, coming in alongside existing part-owner Pacific Equity Partners to fight for a bigger share of a market that is getting a new lease of life with the boom in rooftop solar.

The giant Canadian infrastructure investor is buying the 20 per cent of Intellihub held by Landis+Gyr as well as a 30 per cent stake from PEP's Secure Assets Fund, which is reducing its holding from 80 per cent to 50 per cent.

In a related deal, PEP is selling its community energy services business, WINconnect, to Origin Energy. The deal will add 87,000 embedded electricity and hot water customers to Origin, involving apartment buildings mostly in Victoria and NSW.

The Intellihub deal extends a busy few weeks for Brookfield on the acquisition front, most recently winning electricity infrastructure owner AusNet Services in a $17.8 billion deal including debt.

In October, it agreed an $8 billion deal to acquire global lotteries services business SG Lottery, which was lining up to list on the ASX, and it is set to divest its 50 per cent stake in GeelongPort.

The smart meter sector has recovered from a patchy early few years amid a consumer backlash against them in Victoria in the mid-2000s when they failed to deliver the promised benefits amid a mandated rollout. Now, the rise in rooftop solar has helped open up a range of other competitive behind-the-meter energy services that providers can offer customers.

Intellihub chief executive Wes Ballantine said that helped attract a quality line-up of bidders for the stake on offer, which is understood to have attracted interest from Morrison & Co and Stonepeak Infrastructure as well as BlackRock.

"We were delighted by both the quality and quantity of parties that turned up," Mr Ballantine told The Australian Financial Review.

He pointed to "a really nice melding" of energy transition dynamics on one side through new products that support decarbonisation and decentralisation of energy, with the sort of long-term high credit-worthy cash flows that attract infrastructure investors.

"Those two elements, I think, were quite attractive to a range of sort of infrastructure, core plus-type investors." he said. Mr Ballantine described the deal as "transformational" for Intellihub as it sought to further build on its 45 per cent share of Australia's smart metering installations and expand into related behind-the-meter services against major competitors such as Ausgrid's Plus ES business and New Zealand's Vector.

The company had about 1.3 million meters installed across Australia and New Zealand, with an order book of at least 1.7 million.

In the past 12 months it had won large contracts with Aurora Energy and ActewAGL and secured expanded commitments from major energy retailers.

"We were delighted at Brookfield coming in ... obviously for us, such a headline, quality partner to come into the business, they've got stated ambitions around the energy sector and more broadly, in the Australian and New Zealand context," Mr Ballantine said.

Though the exact value of the deal was not disclosed, the transaction is understood to put an enterprise value on Intellihub of about $3 billion, and an equity value of $2 billion, signalling Brookfield is investing about $1 billion for its half share.

In the separate but related transaction, Origin will pay $42.4 million to acquire WINconnect, after accounting for some amendments to its agreement with Intellihub for the supply of metering services. Intellihub is also acquiring Origin's and WINconnect's electricity embedded network meters.

Origin CEO Frank Calabria described WINconnect as a "strong fit", complementing the company's strategy to expand in embedded networks.

"This transaction allows us to add considerable scale to our customer base, while providing further upside potential through the sale of a broader suite of behind-the-meter products and services to CES [community energy services] customers," he said.

Origin's number of community energy services customers will rise to 367,000 on completion, which is targeted for the June half of 2022.
direction: bullish for Intellihub, Origin Energy, and the broader energy infrastructure sector

headline: Telstra not keen on Digicel without government backing
text:
Chairman John Mullen says Telstra would never have considered buying Pacific telecommunications provider Digicel without a government offer of taxpayer support, and it would sign a deal only if it was in the interests of shareholders.

His first public comments on the potential surprise deal came as it emerged that the proposal for taxpayers to inject more than $1 billion to support the Pacific deal continues to face tough scrutiny from the Finance and Foreign Affairs departments.

Scott Morrison would not be drawn when asked about government backing for Telstra to buy Digicel, but said he expected the company would act in its commercial interests.

"They are market matters for companies. I will leave that to the managing director of Telstra," the Prime Minister said, adding: "I wouldn't expect them to be doing anything that wasn't in their commercial interests. That's what shareholders look to."

Mr Mullen said any deal must make financial sense for Telstra shareholders. "If we can help government we will, but [not] at the expense of shareholders," he told an Australian Council of Superannuation Investors event.

"If something does come of this, it will be because we can meet both government expectations and financial returns for the company. If we can't do both of those, then it won't happen."

Mr Mullen admitted Telstra would not be in talks to buy Digicel without the approach by, and support from, the Morrison government.

His remarks may placate shareholders and analysts alarmed that Telstra may be used as a Morrison government foreign policy tool to outbid China after some pundits suggested there was not much in the Digicel deal for the company "except a lot of trouble" and that it was too much risk for shareholders.

Within the bureaucracy there are concerns over the potential purchase price, Digicel's ageing 3G and 4G infrastructure, and the entry of rival network operators in Papua New Guinea and other Pacific islands, sources familiar with the discussions said.

They said there was ongoing departmental concern that Digicel's Irish billionaire founder, Denis O'Brien, was asking too much for the company. There is also a large gulf between the government and Telstra over what the telco should contribute amid Telstra's insistence on a minority stake.

Digicel may also face competition, with Fiji-based Amalgamated Telecom Holdings signalling it planned to launch in PNG under the Vodafone brand.

But national security officials are concerned that if a Chinese state-owned enterprise were to acquire Digicel, the telco could be used to spy on neighbouring countries and visiting Australian ministers, control media communications to disseminate propaganda for China-friendly Pacific political leaders, and be deployed as a patronage vehicle to corrupt the region's political elite.

While the key structuring points of the deal are taking shape between Telstra and Digicel, there are still outstanding details to be bedded down with the government.

Investment bankers from Macquarie Group are advising the government on structuring the debt for the transaction.

Digicel Pacific and Mr O'Brien are understood to have demanded about $2 billion. Some observers believe Mr O'Brien has an incentive to exaggerate China's interest in the assets to maximise competitive bidding tension.

Digicel is the single largest owner and operator of 3G and 4G mobile phone networks in Papua New Guinea, Fiji, Tonga, Vanuatu, Samoa and Nauru.

The Biden administration has been in talks with the Morrison government about working together to stop China buying Digicel's mobile phone networks in the Pacific and Caribbean, The Australian Financial Review reported yesterday.

Digicel Group's global business has been under cash flow pressure. Its Pacific's business is in healthier shape and generated earnings before interest, tax, depreciation and amortisation (EBITDA) of $US235 million in calendar 2020.
direction: downward pressure on Telstra shares due to deal uncertainty and government scrutiny

headline: Wealth platform's shares fall as spending plans flagged
text:
Shares in Netwealth dropped almost 10 per cent yesterday as the fast-growing wealth management platform flagged larger than expected spending, before the stock mounted a recovery.

The $3.5 billion leader in platforms servicing financial planners reported a full-year net profit after tax of $54.1 million yesterday, a 24 per cent increase from the previous year that was slightly below analyst forecasts of $55 million.

Total revenue increased by 17 per cent to $144.9 million and Netwealth said it expected its funds under administration, which are just shy of $50 billion, to grow by $10 billion in the current financial year.

Founder and joint managing director Michael Heine said the 50 per cent growth in funds under administration over 12 months was "very significant".

"We've coped with it extremely well. If you go back to our listing, I don't think our forecasts would have had funds under administration anywhere near where they are," he told analysts.

While the forecast growth in funds was ahead of most analysts' expectations, the decline in revenue margins of 36.8 basis points, compared to 43.7 per cent in the 2021 financial year, weighed on the share price.

This was a result of a repricing of the back book of existing clients.

Mr Heine told The Australian Financial Review the repricing was "appropriate". As Netwealth enticed new clients with lower prices it "also looked out for our existing clients that had been with us for a long time".

"That was always going to lead to a flattening of revenue in the second half," he said.

Netwealth flagged a ramp-up in spending as it recruits staff in technology to develop its systems, forcing analysts to revise their forward-looking estimates.

The strategic expense increase in the 2022 financial year was expected to normalise in 2023 and beyond.

"It is a one-off as we really try and capitalise on both the market opportunity, but also make sure that we're really well-placed to cater for the growth that we've been experiencing," Mr Heine said.

Netwealth shares opened almost 10 per cent lower yesterday at $13.56 but had recovered to $14.29 by the close of the session, a 4.5 per cent decline.

Another force weighing on margins over the past two years has been a sharp decline in interest rates.
direction: downward pressure on Netwealth shares

headline: Tech uptake key to pandemic outperformance
text:
Small businesses that took advantage of the pandemic to invest heavily in technology outperformed their less tech-savvy competitors, but Australian firms still underinvested compared to their international peers.

Investment in technology lifted 13 per cent from March 2020 to June this year, as the COVID-19 crisis forced investment spending higher, according to data collated by global cloud accounting software provider Xero.

But across the Tasman in New Zealand investment was 25 per cent higher, while the United Kingdom recorded a 20 per cent uplift.

"There's still work to be done to help more Australian small businesses realise the benefits of tech adoption. This will be important for the recovery ahead," Xero Australia managing director Joseph Lyons said.

Locally, small businesses on average invested 2.4 per cent of their budgets on information and communications technology (ICT), while in New Zealand it was 2.9 per cent, and in the UK, 4.4 per cent.

The findings were developed in partnership with consulting firm Accenture and are based on aggregated anonymised data from hundreds of thousands of Xero customers across the UK, Australia and New Zealand.

UK retailers recorded significant productivity gains during the pandemic. Unit labour costs in wholesale and retail trade in the second quarter of 2021 were 3 per cent below the 2019 average but the output per hour worked was about 10 per cent higher.

According to the UK Office of National Statistics, "the rapid uptake of online shopping instead of store-based retail may have contributed to the increase", as less labour was needed to generate sales.

Australia does not produce quarterly data. However, multifactor productivity in 2019-20, which included the first national lockdown, showed retail up 3.6 per cent for the year compared to an overall fall of 0.68 per cent.

The Australian Bureau of Statistics noted a 5.3 per cent fall in hours worked was "likely indicative of a shift to online retailing and shorter trading hours due to COVID-19", while overall output remained high.

Data for 2020-21 will be released later this month.

Small businesses in the top 25 per cent for ICT investment showed sales on average almost $35,000 higher than 2019, while those in the bottom 25 per cent saw sales fall on average about $6000, according to Xero.

Businesses that invested the most in apps experienced 2.2 per cent year-on-year sales growth compared to an equivalent fall for non-app users.

Xero economist Louise Southall said the data clearly showed businesses that planned and invested in technology solutions outperformed their peers.

"In addition, the impact on productivity growth means it is also a positive for the national economy as we rebuild in 2022," Ms Southall said.

The federal government on Friday released legislation that, if passed, would allow businesses to self-assess the depreciable life of intangible assets such as software, licences and patents.

Depreciation will occur in line with the effective life of the asset, which Treasurer Josh Frydenberg said would further encourage the digital transformation push that occurred during the pandemic.

"We want Australian businesses to capitalise on the accelerated digitalisation of the economy that has occurred during this pandemic," Mr Frydenberg told The Australian Financial Review.

Under the change, intangible assets such as software and IP will be depreciable based on the value producing life of the product rather than against a pre-defined Tax Office mandated schedule.

The change is expected to cost $170 million over the forward estimates.
direction: supportive for technology and productivity-focused small-cap equities

headline: Ben Quilty's savage van to test market resolve
text:
Contemporary art Eyes also on appetite for six-figure Banksy prints.

After setting an auction record for Ben Quilty in March with a painting of a burly budgerigar, Menzies is banking on the Sydney artist's popularity and selling no fewer than four Quilty works in its second sale for the year next Wednesday night.

Quilty has been given cover status again, this time with a far tougher work than the budgie painting, Beast 2, 2005, which sold for $220,000 (hammer price) in March, against an estimate of $35,000 to $45,000.

It's fair to say that Quilty's Skull 3, 2006, estimated at $120,000 to $160,000, is a little less easy on the eye. Quilty's Toranas, Falcons, and other cars with grunt beloved by suburban boys are popular with collectors, but Skull 3 is a menacing, anthropomorphic minivan, fang-toothed and hollow-eyed, which nonetheless continues to probe the artist's principal theme of troubled masculinity. One for die-hard fans perhaps.

"It's not a Torana, it's a minivan, but it has real teeth, it has real grit," says Menzies' new head of art, Brett Ballard. "Quilty has hit the mark with unresolved masculinity as one of his themes. It's about being a father; it's about growing up in north-west Sydney. We are on the road and the ideas are being carried forward. I like his voice and I like the directness in the application of paint, and the concepts he deals with."

Ballard will take part in his first auction with Menzies next week after his departure last month from rival firm Smith & Singer, where he was the last senior specialist standing (other than company chairman Geoffrey Smith). His colleague, Fiona Hayward, departed before him, joining Deutscher and Hackett. Asked about the move to Menzies after 10 years with Smith & Singer (including its previous incarnation Sotheby's Australia), Ballard said there was the sense of "liberation and elation" that comes with leaving a long-held position, quickly followed by the reality of getting back to the hard work of consigning and marketing sales.

"I am coming into a company that has been here for 23 years and has an enviable record, having sold the two most expensive artworks at auction, Sidney Nolan's First-Class Marksman that sold for $5.4 million in 2010, and last year, Henri's Armchair by Brett Whiteley which made $6.1 million," Ballard said.

"Those are wonderful benchmarks, not a benchmark we will reach in this sale, but we have a good range of price points and media, with a renewed interest in sculpture and print making.

"There is a hunger not only for name artists but also women artists and international names such as Banksy and Warhol and more cutting-edge artists such as [American street artist] Retna and [French street artist] Invader, so it's shaping up to be a really interesting sale."

Menzies will auction two colour screenprints by British street artist Banksy, whose works sell for extravagant sums on the international secondary market and who is rising on the Australian market as well. Last month, Smith & Singer set an Australian record for a Banksy in a single-lot, automated auction of an editioned print of Love is in the Air, 2003, which sold at its high estimate of $150,000 (hammer). The week after Smith & Singer's sale, an unsigned edition of the same print sold for ¥18,000,000 (about $219,000) at a Mallet auction in Tokyo.

The two prints coming up at Menzies are not as iconic as Love is in the Air but, with Banksy's mounting success, they have been priced accordingly: Donuts (Chocolate), 2009, has an estimate of $100,000 to $150,000 and Very Little Helps, 2008, an estimate of $90,000 to $120,000.

In his original street stencil of Very Little Helps, on Essex Road, London, Banksy incorporated a real-life cable and power box as the flagpole upon which a stencilled child raises a flag - in the form of a plastic bag from the UK's biggest supermarket chain, Tesco - while two other children watch in awe.

Both prints were originally bought for less than $500 from London's Pictures on Walls, which was founded in 2003 by a loose group of artists, graffiti writers and illustrators who had been "shunned" (their word) by the art establishment. To the collective's distaste, many of its artists found mainstream success, their art becoming a tradeable commodity, so the collective called it quits in 2017.

Banksy's images have become so popular that the artist is grappling with how to prevent them being commercially exploited on goods such as greeting cards. As an artist who remains anonymous, he is not able to claim copyright over his work.

Another work originating from Pictures on Walls being auctioned at Menzies next week is by French artist Invader, who is known for his mosaics of arcade-game inspired characters. Invader started out by installing his small mosaics on the streets of Paris, later taking his work to the world, including Banksy's home turf in London. Invader's colour screenprint Home Neptune, 2010, a pixelated image that addicts of the 1970s arcade game Space Invaders will easily recognise, has an estimate of $10,000 to $15,000.

Overall, the 129-lot Menzies sale is worth $3.5 million to $5 million, which Ballard describes as "well suited to the current market".

So far, the market is tracking significantly better than last year, when it thrived despite the pandemic, with $38.83 million in sales compared to $10.67 million this time last year.

The most expensive lot in the Menzies sale is by market faithful Sidney Nolan, and has made several appearances at the auction house. The painting Kelly, 1964, with an estimate of $500,000 to $700,000, was last offered at Menzies in 2018, with an estimate of $600,000 to $800,000, but failed to sell. It was also auctioned at Menzies in 2016 when it sold for $480,000 (hammer). Ballard declined to reveal whether the Nolan work was owned wholly or in part by Menzies' owner Rod Menzies.

"As with all items in the sale, ownership is confidential," Ballard said.

Another Nolan work, Kelly Guarding Camp, dated 1968, has been withdrawn from the sale as its authorship could not be confidently established. "We did our due diligence and made the decision to withdraw the work," Ballard said.

Ballard began at Menzies five weeks ago when the June auction had already been consigned. His vision for future sales is for fewer lots of higher value - "but not to the detriment of content", he says - and for an increased focus on sculpture and print-making.

"We will be staying with known 20th-century artists - Nolan, Roberts, Streeton, Tucker, Boyd - but I certainly feel strongly that we do want to bring in more sculpture, international and Australian, and go into more depth with printmaking, which is very accessible, even with artists such as Picasso, Warhol and Banksy," Ballard said.

"There are so many good things in Australia that clients have bought while abroad and one of the attractions of the job is to find those things."
direction: weak demand for non-essential luxury assets

headline: The budget is not socialism, it's ideology on pause
text:
Canberra Observed Shutting the border until next year is in keeping with a budget that put off the deficit and debt heavy lifting until after the next election.

On Wednesday night, a global executive based in Sydney emailed to say she and her young family were packing up and heading back to Britain. (The woman asked that her name not be used, so we'll call her Sally.)

She had noted with some cynicism the package of budget measures designed to attract global talent. These measures, a response to the review led by Peter Verwer, focused on regulatory relief and changes to tax treatment to entice companies and individuals to set up shop in Australia.

The idea is to strike while the iron is hot, given Australia's successful suppression of COVID-19, the political uncertainty in rival jurisdiction Hong Kong, and plans by the US and British governments to increase taxes on business.

Sally, who fell into the global talent category, said there was one glaring omission. "I have been unable to see my elderly parents or other immediate family overseas since the borders slammed shut on March 20, 2020," she wrote.

"I now understand - through the budget, rather than any formal road map from the government (heaven forbid!) - that this is likely to be extended for another year. This is not something I am willing to endure, and am currently in the process of moving my young family out of Australia indefinitely.

"My point - put simply - is that no one in their right mind with any remaining interests overseas would move to Australia in the knowledge that the government can deny them the ability to see loved ones. This goes for both skilled migrants and students."

She added that she was "by no means alone" in her plans to leave. One other colleague had already left and others were contemplating doing the same.

"This country faces an enormous brain drain as a result of the current draconian travel restrictions and seemingly zero appetite from the federal government to provide an exit strategy from the COVID-free corner it has painted itself into."

Stinging stuff and not an isolated view, coming after the budget assumptions pushed back until mid-2022 any meaningful reopening of the international borders.

The airline and international tourism sectors are once more despairing. NSW Premier Gladys Berejiklian, whose stewardship throughout the crisis left her state well poised to cash in on the recovery, is frustrated. Closed international borders are costing NSW $1.5 billion a month and opportunities to capitalise are slipping away, she says.

Despite all the promising ideas to speed up travel, many of which emanated from the recently decommissioned National COVID-19 Commission led by Nev Power, nothing so far has come to pass.

There was no mention in the budget about business- or university-sponsored travel and quarantine, the potential for rapid testing and truncated quarantine periods, or expanded quarantine facilities.

As it stands, the joint will stay pretty much locked up until mid-2022, unless the vaccine rollout gets a rattle on.

While there are clear health reasons behind the assumptions - the virus is far from being contained in many nations - the political imperative cannot be ignored.

As we noted last week, after watching the political rewards heaped on populist state leaders, the federal government, too, has decided to err on the side of an abundance of caution until the election, due by early next year.

For every Sally out there, there are

dozens of punters content with living like a hermit kingdom for another 12 months.

Tuesday's budget was very much in that vein. As has been written a hundred times, the government shifted the fiscal rules two weeks out and set a new unemployment target of below 5 per cent. This gave it licence to keep spending on job-creation measures rather than shift to budget repair.

Consequently, Treasurer Josh Frydenberg unveiled a pandemic budget that put off the heavy lifting in terms of debt and deficit repair until after the election. It did spend big, but it was not all about the pandemic.

The $17.7 billion for aged care would have been necessary regardless of whether there was a pandemic. Scott Morrison called the royal commission, and would have been crucified had he ignored it.

Overall, it was a Labor-style budget, which has made the opposition's task trickier. This was evident two days before the budget when Labor reached peak opposition.

In the age-old tradition that every silver lining must have a cloud, shadow treasurer Jim Chalmers played down what was evidently a much faster economic recovery than had been forecast in December when the budget was last updated.

"The point we're making is that even if the recession wasn't as bad as many economists feared, it doesn't mean that the recovery can't be better," he said.

Anthony Albanese's big housing response last night showed Labor was not going to be pushed to the right.

Morrison took some heat from the fiscal conservatives in his ranks, who were uttering such foul oaths as "Whitlamesque" on Tuesday night. The PM reiterated how when the pandemic struck and the economy was forcibly closed down, economic ideology had to be suspended so billions could be shovelled out. Even John Howard said so, he pointed out defensively.

All the government did this week was to extend the ideological suspension for another 12 months or so, he contended.

He also reminded the true believers that Labor was still the gold standard in terms of spending, by pointing out how he had ceased JobKeeper at the end of March despite complaints from the opposition.

He noted the Coalition eschewed a recommendation of the royal commission to fund aged care with a new tax. Labor has yet to discount the idea.

These nuggets, Morrison trusts, will keep any insurrection at bay until the next election, by which time the government will be banking on the belief that the voters will entrust the debt to the party that created it.

Still, this week, both sides promised to spend plenty. Amid the cacophony of reaction from interest groups, rent-seekers and all else who festoon the Parliament in budget week, just one party raised concerns about how it was all going to be paid for.

It was the Greens, with a proposition to tax the nation's 122 "pandemic profiteers" - the 122 billionaires whose wealth expanded between March last year and this year.

Slightly batty, but that was it.

Phillip Coorey is The Australian Financial Review's political editor.
direction: bearish for Australian equities and property due to talent drain and border uncertainty

headline: Migrant recovery needed to rein in 40 years of deficits
text:
Australia will be mired in debt and deficit for at least the next 40 years unless it boosts skilled migration, workforce participation and productivity, and embarks on budget repair, Treasurer Josh Frydenberg says.

Mr Frydenberg will flag the four-pronged approach today when he responds to the latest Intergenerational Report to be released by Treasury.

"A well-targeted and sustainable skilled migration program. Lifting participation, particularly for women and older Australians. Boosting productivity, through broad-based reforms. And strong and sound budget management," he will say.

The IGR, the fifth to be released and the first since 2015, attempts to forecast the state of the budget 40 years hence. It finds the pandemic has exacerbated the long-term challenges posed by an ageing population and a dwindling workforce.

The most enduring economic effect of the pandemic will be reduced population growth.

The IGR says Australia's population, currently about 25 million people, will reach 38.8 million by 2060-61. The last IGR released in 2015 forecast a population of almost 40 million 2054-55. Slowing population growth is largely responsible for the IGR downgrading economic growth forecasts from the 3 per cent average annual rate of the past 40 years to 2.6 per cent over the next four decades.

"This is the first time there has been a downward revision of the long-term population projections in an intergenerational report," Mr Frydenberg will say in his speech today.

"This means the economy will be smaller and Australia's population will be older than it otherwise would have been, with flow-on implications for our economic and fiscal outcomes."

In March, Prime Minister Scott Morrison spoke of the need to revamp skilled migrant visas as soon as international borders open and Mr Frydenberg will reinforce this.

"A well-targeted, skills-focused migration program can supplement our stock of working-age people, slow the transition to an older population and improve Australia's economic and fiscal outlook," he will say.

Based on existing settings, the IGR forecasts no return to surplus and for the nation to remain in debt over the next 40 years.

It will come close to balance in 2036-37, reaching 0.7 per cent of gross domestic product before falling away again to 2.3 per cent of GDP by 2060-61, a consequence of increased spending on aged care and health, interest on debt and an inability to raise taxes due to the constraints of the government's self-imposed 23.9 per cent tax-to-GDP ratio.

However, there is some leeway for tax increases in the shorter term. The IGR notes the tax-to-GDP gap will be reached in 2036-37, four years later than the 2015 IGR forecast.

Before the pandemic, the budget forecast a surplus in 2029-30 equivalent to 1.7 per cent of GDP. The IGR now predicts a deficit of 1.7 per cent of GDP at the same time, a differential of $109 billion.

As for debt, 2019-20 budget plans for Australia to have eradicated net debt by early next decade have evaporated and the IGR anticipates a net debt of 34.4 per cent of GDP by 2060-61.

The forecasts, gloomy as they are, are underpinned by upbeat assumptions that the productivity growth rate will return to its 30-year average of 1.5 per cent over the next decade.

"Historical trends in growth are a reasonable guide to future prospects for productivity," the report says, noting, however, that productivity growth has averaged just 1.2 per cent over the decade from 2010.

Economists are highly sceptical of the level, noting the 30-year average takes into account huge gains made in the 1990s and 2000s as a result of wholesale economic reform and the rise of the internet.

"To a large extent, Australia's future productivity growth will continue to reflect shifts in the global productivity frontier, as domestic firms adopt the latest technology and business practices," Mr Frydenberg will say.

"Nevertheless, no matter what path global productivity growth takes, we must continue to do everything possible to lift productivity."

The government will use the IGR to promote an economic repair agenda that keeps taxes low and growth high.

"IGRs always deliver sobering news. That is their role. It is up to governments to respond," he says.

"To improve the fiscal outlook, it will be important to address the structural challenges facing the economy and take advantage of opportunities to lift growth."

He again, however, rules out austerity, and also plays down the prospect of a bold reform agenda to drive productivity growth. "I understand there will always be calls for big bang economic reforms and grand bargains but we should never let that blind us to other important reform opportunities."
direction: bearish for growth-oriented sectors due to lower long-term GDP forecasts
